text: **Title:** Record of Strange Events (夷堅支志) **Author:** Hong Mai (洪迈) **Dynasty:** Song (宋) The "Record of Strange Events" is a collection of strange tales and anecdotes compiled by Hong Mai during the Song Dynasty. This particular section, titled "支志," focuses on various unusual and intriguing stories that reflect the cultural and social aspects of the time. Hong Mai, a prominent scholar and official, used these stories to illustrate moral and ethical lessons, often drawing from historical and literary sources. The text provides valuable insights into the beliefs, customs, and values of the Song Dynasty, making it an important source for understanding the period's cultural landscape.

The Record of the Immortal Rock, Volume Jia Preface to the Record of the Immortal Rock The Record of the Immortal Rock was compiled by Mr. Hong Mai of Fanyang. He was a man of vast knowledge and a keen interest in the unusual and extraordinary. Having served in various regions, he gathered and collected numerous events and stories, compiling them into this work. The book is divided into four sections: Jia, Yi, Bing, and Ding. Each section contains twenty volumes, with each volume featuring between eleven and twelve stories, or sometimes thirteen to fourteen. Though these stories may seem like minor tales, they are still worth reading. Upon examining the preface, it becomes clear that there were multiple editions of this book, including the Sichuan edition, the Wuchang (Jiujiang, Jiangxi) edition, and the Fujian edition. There was also an edition printed in ancient Hangzhou. Mr. Hong Mai had the book printed wherever he served. Today, the editions from Sichuan and Zhejiang are no longer available, but we are fortunate that the Fujian edition is still preserved in the school. However, upon checking the volumes, we find that many are missing. The local official Zhang Shaoxian, who oversaw school affairs, urged me to search for old copies to fill in the gaps. Unfortunately, the Fujian edition is long missing, making it difficult to find a complete set. Fortunately, my friend Zhou Hong still had a copy of the book in his study. This was the edition printed by Mr. Hong Mai in ancient Hangzhou. Although this edition is divided into ten sections from Jia to Gui, it seems to differ in detail from the current Fujian edition. The stories it contains are mostly similar but have some variations. I have used the Zhejiang edition to fill in the gaps where the Fujian edition is missing. Recently, Mr. Chen, a high-ranking official, arrived here and provided additional motivation for the restoration efforts. We immediately ordered the printing of forty-three volumes, completing the book. Hopefully, this will remain true to Mr. Hong Mai's original intentions. Thus, the Record of the Immortal Rock can be passed down to future generations as a complete work. —— Assistant to the ancient Hangzhou office, Shen Tianyou

**Re-carving the Song Edition of the Four Collections of the *Eji Zhi*: Preface** The *Eji Zhi* (Records of the Unusual) consists of 200 volumes from A to K, 100 volumes from Branch A to Branch K, another 100 volumes from Third A to Third K, and 10 volumes each for Fourth A and Fourth B, totaling 420 volumes. This is recorded in Chen Zhensun's *Shulü Jieti* (Catalogue of Books). After the Ming dynasty, it became very rare. Hu Yinglin, who was well-versed in many books, only knew its structure and examples from Wang Jingwen's preface to the *Eji Zhi* (a different version). The *Siku Quanshu* (Four Treasuries) includes only 50 volumes from Branch A to Branch E. It is quite difficult to obtain in the common people. The most widely circulated version is a Ming dynasty edition that imitates a Song dynasty original, containing 50 volumes of *Eji Zhi* classified by genre. This is likely a compilation by Song dynasty scholars. There is also a commercially printed edition of 20 volumes, which, although excerpted from the original, is less comprehensive than the classified version. Thus, not only is the complete book lost, but even the main collection of 200 volumes has been in a state of uncertainty for several hundred years. Yuan Wen Da (阮文達) obtained 80 volumes from A to D of the Song dynasty edition, had them reproduced, and presented them. Yuan obtained these from Yan Jiuneng in his hometown, who later passed them on to Huang Yaopu in Suzhou, who then passed them on to Wang Langyuan, and finally to Hu Xinyun. I acquired them from Hu. There is a seal from the Yulan Hall, indicating that they were once part of the collection of the Wen family from Hengshan. As *Liezi* says, "When Yu the Great traveled, he saw it; Bo Yi knew it and named it."

Yiji heard it and recorded it. The name "Yiji" is derived from this. Since ancient times, books about strange things have existed, none older than the *Shanhaijing*. When examined in terms of reason and trend, they are mostly largely absurd. Those who follow this tradition include Wang Jia's *Shiyi*, Gan Bao's *Soushenji*, Jing Shu's *Yiyan*, Xu Xuan's *Jishenji*, and Cheng Shi's *Zazu*, which were most popular in their time. However, the most extensive of these books contained no more than several hundred stories, while the least had only a dozen or so. There has never been a book as voluminous as this one. Although the stories it contains are quite similar to biographies, they often involve fabricated stories and plagiarized content, used for conversation. Zhi Jia's preface has already mentioned this. As for the writing, it is profound and inexhaustible, indeed surpassing what later generations can achieve. From *Jia Zhi* to *Si Jia*, there are thirty-one prefaces in total, each one is original and does not repeat the others. Zhao Shi's *Bin Tui Lu* excerpted parts of the text and highly praised it. Indeed, it is the pinnacle of literary achievement and the ocean of short stories. Qin Xi Hongjun, who diligently searched for and carved his ancestors' lost books, wrote a letter encouraging the publication. So he entrusted the work to the printer to fulfill Hongjun's wish. In the fifth year of Guangxu, the month of *Tuwei Danyue Yangyue*, compiled by Lu Xin Yuan of Guian.

The *Yijian Jia Zhi* Volume Nineteen Sun Jiuding Sun Jiuding, courtesy name Guozhen, was from Xinzhou. In the year Gui Si of the Zhenghe era (1123), he resided at the Imperial Academy. On the seventh day of the seventh lunar month, he went out to visit his fellow townsman Duan Junyi in the Bamboo Fence Alley. As he walked along the northern bank of the Bian River, he suddenly saw a high-ranking official with an impressive entourage. The official called out to him in the crowd, and quickly dismounted from his horse, saying, "Guozhen, it has been a long time since we last met, and you must be well." Sun Jiuding looked closely and recognized him as his brother-in-law, Zhang Qing. Zhang Qing pointed to a wine shop north of the street and said, "We can invite ourselves here for a brief, leisurely visit." Sun Jiuding replied, "You are a wealthy person; how can I, a poor scholar, buy wine for you?" Zhang Qing said, "My money is not suitable for use," and they sat down in the wine shop, where he drank wine at ease. After a short while, Sun Jiuding realized that Zhang Qing had been dead for a long time. He asked, "You have been dead for a long time. Why are you here? If I see you, would it not be harmful?" Zhang Qing replied, "No, your fortune is very strong. I will tell you about my death and the funeral you attended, and there is nothing I do not know. Last Mid-Autumn Festival, I visited my home, and your sister and others were drinking wine as usual, but they did not look at each other. I was angry and resentful, poured out the wine jug, and hit my young daughter before leaving." Sun Jiuding asked, "Where are you now?" Zhang Qing said, "I am now the Registering Salaries Judge of the Imperial City Office." Sun Jiuding was pleased and asked, "Is that so?" Zhang Qing replied, "No."

This matter occurs every ten years. The names are not yet known. It mostly happens after the age of thirty. Their official positions are not lowly. Sun said, "You have indulged greatly in wine and women throughout your life. How could you reach this state?" He replied, "This is my mark. In all matters, one should examine the heart. If the heart is not corrupted, then nothing is impossible." Before he finished speaking, a follower entered to report, "The path is straight." Zhang then rose and went with him. Pointing to the passerby, he said, "These are our kind. It is just that the world does not recognize them." At the Li Chun Gate, he bid farewell to Sun, saying, "From here, you must not look back. If you do, you will die. You are now being invaded by yin qi. Tomorrow you will have a severe diarrhea. Do not take other medicines. Taking Píng Wèi San is sufficient." After bidding farewell, Sun became greatly afraid. Upon arriving at the Bamboo Fence Lane, he met Duan Jun, who was surprised by his pale complexion. Duan offered him wine. At dusk, Sun returned to his studies. The next day, he had a severe diarrhea over thirty times and was cured by taking Píng Wèi San. Sun later faced continuous setbacks and achieved nothing. He spent over a decade in the Jin Kingdom before finally passing the imperial examination as the top scholar and becoming a Secretary of the Imperial Academy. He had previously studied together with my father in the Tonglei Study Hall. Upon reaching the North, they met several times, and he recounted this story.

General Zhang Zhang Jing, courtesy name Shuming, was from Yixing. He served as the magistrate of Anren in Raoyang. The county had many idolatrous temples, which he ordered to be destroyed and thrown into the river. He also banned the common people from offering sacrifices. In total, he demolished three hundred shrines. The only exception was the Temple of General Liu, which was considered the most sacred and was spared from destruction. The temple courtyard had a large pine tree with a broad canopy of shade. Zhang intended to cut it down. One day, he lay resting in his office during the day and had a dream. A strange man in armor rode a horse and descended the steps. He bowed and said, "My surname is Mu, and I have lived in this place for a long time. I am grateful for your protection and will not forget your kindness. In fifteen years, I will return." Upon waking, Zhang realized the man was a god, but he did not understand what "司成" (sic) referred to. He was greatly astonished and decided not to cut down the tree. He also repaired the temple. After completing his term, he visited the temple to bid farewell and left a poem on the wall. The poem read: 梦事虽非实。将军默有灵。 旧祠从此焕。古桧蔚然青。 甲马霄中见。琴堂卧正冥。 留诗非志怪。三五扣神扃。 The poem is still inscribed on the stone today. Fifteen years later, Zhang was appointed as an imperial censor and stationed in Shouzhou and Jiangning, overseeing the region of Jiangdong, Anren, which was under his jurisdiction. He later entered the Imperial Academy as a high-ranking official and died as a scholar-official in the Xianmo Hall.

The Mantra of the Precious Pavilion Yuan Kejiu once taught his brother Chang the mantra of the Precious Pavilion. Chang did not deeply believe in it, but he would recite it thirty to fifty times every morning. At first, he did not know of its efficacy. In the summer of the third year of Shaoxing (1133), while studying at the local academy, during the aftermath of a major military campaign, the city was in ruins. A man named Wang Cheng, who served as a watchman in the dormitory, would always have nightmares every time he stayed there. The nightmares would last until dawn, and he never had a night of peaceful sleep. Wang Cheng found this very distressing. When someone asked him about what he saw in the dream, he said that he was grabbed by the hair and was about to be beaten, so he shouted and resisted. Chang moved Wang Cheng to his own room, but the problem continued. The other students in the dormitory hated the disturbance to their sleep and discussed the idea of expelling Wang Cheng. Chang tried writing the mantra on a pillar, and that night everything was peaceful. From then on, all supernatural disturbances in the entire hall disappeared. The mantra is known as the "Om Mani Padme Hum" eight-syllable mantra. However, it was commonly misinterpreted or mispronounced, often written with phonetic characters, and the correct pronunciation was also unknown. One should consult the original translations in the Buddhist canon for verification. The mantra had significant effects. Chang then regretted his past negligence and began to sincerely follow the practice, keeping the matter secret. These three events were recounted by Sun Jiuding, who also wrote extensively about this in his books, all of which are recent accounts. A villager from Sanhe

Zhang Wei, courtesy name Zhenglun, was from Sanhe, Yanshan. His father, when he first went on an official mission to Taiyuan, had Zhang Wei serve as the local official responsible for entertaining and accompanying guests. Zhang Wei once recounted that in the year Xuanhe Yisilue (1125 AD), there was a villager in his hometown who knew how to read and write, and whose occupation was farming and raising silkworms. He was over sixty years old. One night, he suddenly woke up from a nightmare, trembling uncontrollably. He said to his wife, "My fate is sealed." His wife was shocked and asked him why. He replied, "Just now, I dreamed of walking in the fields and saw seven Jurchen riders on the road. One of them, dressed in white and riding a white horse, angrily told me, 'In my previous life, I was a commoner during the Tang Dynasty, and you were a soldier from the city of Cai. When Wu Yuanji rebelled, I was executed for my loyalty. I have harbored this grudge for a long time. Now that I have seen you again, even over generations, I must be repaid with your life. I drew my bow and shot you in the heart, and you fell dead.' I must die. Tomorrow, I will flee far away to avoid this calamity." His wife said, "Nightmares are not worth believing; it's just your imagination." The old man became even more frightened. Before dawn, he got up. His family was very poor, so he only let his grandson carry a blanket and intended to go to a distant relative's house sixty miles away to escape. They walked through a grassy path for over thirty miles before reaching the main road. Another two miles later, they met several people and walked together. Suddenly, a rider galloped up, shouting at everyone to stop. All the walkers stopped. The old man looked back and saw exactly seven riders, one of whom was dressed in white and riding a white horse, just as he had seen in his dream. He was greatly frightened and cut off the path, rushing away. The rider shouted angrily, trying to stop him, but the old man did not listen. The white-clad rider became furious and said, "This is a trickster!" He whipped his horse and chased after the old man. When he reached the old man, he drew his bow and shot him in the heart. The old man fell dead on the spot. The seven riders were all Jurchen.
Iron Tower God

Within the city of Yuezhou, there was a deity housed in an iron tower within a stupa, renowned for its miraculous efficacy. The officials of the prefecture treated it with great respect. As the Khitan were about to fall, some of the city's people saw the deity running outside the city. They immediately went to the temple to see it. The deity's image was sweating all over its body. Though they were quite surprised, no one could understand the reason. That night, the deity appeared in a dream to the temple's chief lecturer, saying, "I am following the heavenly mandate, to apprehend and count those who should die in the city." The deity had run around for several days and finally completed the task. The next day at noon, the Jurchen soldiers arrived and broke through the city. Those who were destined to die in the city numbered one thousand three hundred and some. Among them were more than forty monks from this temple, including the abbot. The deity, who had lived here for a long time, said, "On a daily basis, I respect the teacher's moral teachings. I will replace him with another name. The next day, I will leave from here. Perhaps I can escape." The lecturer woke up and told the monks of the temple. All laughed at his foolishness. Then he alone carried his bag and climbed to the top of the mountain behind the temple to avoid it. He walked about five miles when he suddenly remembered the white gold bowl he had left behind. He returned to the temple, just happening to be someone preparing offerings. Everyone competed to hold him back, saying, "The abbot is so clever, but now he believes in dreams. Now that you are here, we want the abbot to ascend the hall and preach. To leave without reason, then this temple cannot be maintained. Moreover, there is no news of an alarm on the border. Reluctantly comply with the wishes of the crowd. After the meal, he left. It is not too late." The monk had no choice and then ascended the hall and finished his lecture. Everyone went to eat, halfway through when there was a report that the Jurchen had come from the grasslands. They immediately surrounded the city. The city was usually unprepared and could not be defended. It collapsed in an instant. The monk was in a state of panic and confusion, having no time to run away. The soldiers had already looted the city. The number of people and monks who died matched the deity's prediction. The lecturer also could not escape.

The Verses of Guanyin Zhang Xiaochun had a grandson who, at five years old, could not walk. Someone told him: Recently, in the Huai region, there was a farmer whose legs and feet had been sick for a long time. However, he recited the name of Guanyin daily without interruption. As a result, he felt Guanyin's manifestation. Therefore, Guanyin left a four-line verse: Great wisdom arises from the heart. In the heart, there is no need to search. All virtues are accomplished. Neither past nor present. The farmer recited the verse for a hundred days, and his illness was suddenly cured. Thus, Zhang Xiaochun then taught his grandson and the nurse to fast, purify, and recite the verse. Within two months, the grandson walked like a normal child. Later, those with sick legs and feet who recited the verse were all cured. Additionally, in Tingzhou, a layperson named Dingguang, who taught and transformed, also left a verse: Great wisdom arises from the heart. In the heart, where should one search? All virtues are accomplished. Neither past nor present. Whenever ordinary people came to ask, the verse was always written and given to them. It was written with these four characters later, and there was no case where it did not work. It is completely beyond understanding.

Liu Xiangshi's Wife In the capital of the Jin Dynasty, Zhongdu, there was an official named Liu Xiangshi, a Han Chinese. He and his wife were both in their forties. They had two children, a son and a daughter, and several slaves. One day, he freed all his slaves and allowed them to become free people. He spent all his wealth to build an orphanage for the elderly, but the matter was not yet completed. His wife donated her left eye. Using an iron spoon, she scooped it out, about two to three inches away from her face. Just as she was raising the knife to cut the tendons, as if something suddenly pulled the eyeball back in. Her eye remained intact. This happened three times. Blood flowed over her body. The crowd tried to persuade her to stop. The next day, when she raised the spoon again, her eye had disappeared. She could not scoop it out. The following day, it returned to normal, extraordinarily clear and bright. Everyone was shocked and pitied her. People competed to give money and silk, and the orphanage was eventually completed. At that time, it was the first year of the Huangtong era of the Jin Dynasty, which was the tenth year of the Shaoxing era of the Song Dynasty (1140 AD).

In the year 1157 during the Southern Song Dynasty, the official Huang Cheng of the state of Qi in Jizhou died and revived after three days. He claimed that several people had pursued him, and he was taken to a public hall where a person dressed in red and green said, "I appoint you to escort 500 monks to Mount Wutai." Huang Cheng declined on the grounds of poverty and many young dependents, saying it was impossible for him to go. The officials on either side said, "You can appoint Li the County Magistrate to take your place. Besides, it is not too late to have someone else appointed." Then, the original pursuer was sent back to return him, and thus he survived. Two days later, the county of Shanguan in the state reported to the military governor that Li the County Magistrate was appointed to go to the state to inspect finances. He stayed in a county inn and died suddenly one night after falling asleep. Huang Cheng knew that Li had died in his place and performed the full funeral rites for him. After living for a year, Huang Cheng suddenly bathed and changed his clothes, telling his wife and children, "I must now leave you. The officials have appointed me to go to Mount Tiantai to fetch scriptures. The thing that has been most beneficial to me in my life is that I have read the *Avatamsaka Sutra* once." After speaking, he closed his eyes and passed away.

### Ice Turtles In the fifth month of the summer in the year of the horse (1238 CE), a severe thunderstorm struck Taikang County in the capital of the Song Dynasty, Bianjing (modern-day Kaifeng). During this storm, ice turtles descended, spanning dozens of miles. These turtles varied in size, and each had fully formed heads, feet, and trigram patterns. ### Emperor Taizu of Liao Shoots a Dragon Emperor Taizu of Liao, Yelü Abaoji, once resided in the Western Tower. One night, he slept in a felt tent. The next morning, he saw a black dragon, over a thousand feet long, coiling above him. He drew his bow and shot at it. The dragon leaped into the air, disappearing gracefully. It fell west of the Yellow Dragon Fortress, a distance of over fifteen hundred miles away, and was only a few feet long. Its remains are now kept in the treasury of the Jin Dynasty. The eldest son of the high-ranking official of the Jin Dynasty, Prince Chen Wang Wushi, once saw it. The dragon had a tail, mane, and limbs, and possessed two horns, which had been cut off. It was said to be very similar to a dragon emerging from water, as depicted in a painting by Dong Yu, owned by his family, except that the dragon's back had a mane that did not resemble that of a fish. ### Cold Mountain Dragon Cold Mountain is located about three thousand miles from the Yan Mountains and five hundred miles from the capital of the Jin Dynasty. It is a barren land. In the year of the wood rabbit in the era of Shaoxing (1155 CE), two dragons, indistinguishable by color or name, appeared. Each was over ten feet tall, and they died a few steps apart. The cold, pungent, and fiery aura they emitted was so intense that people could not approach them. One dragon had no horns, as if they had been cut off. The other had a hole on its forehead, about the size of a three-qian coin, resembling the mark of an axe or chisel. Prince Chen Wang Wushi wanted to have someone cut off the horns of the second dragon, but others believed it would be inauspicious, so he did not proceed. The place where my ancestors lived was also called Cold Mountain, and it is another four hundred miles from here.

### Xizhou Dragon During the summer of the year 1148, a dragon appeared in the wild waters of Xizhou for three days. Initially, a blue dragon was seen on the water surface, and after a long time, it submerged. The next day, a golden dragon was seen holding a baby with its claws. Although the baby was being played with by the dragon, it showed no signs of fear. On the third day, the golden dragon remained the same, and a figure resembling a young imperial attendant on a white horse was seen. There were six toads in front of the horse. After three periods of time, it submerged. The people of the county competed to go and see it, and they were very close to it, yet there was no harm from wind and waves. Xizhou once showed a drawing of this event to Liu Qi, but Liu Qi was not pleased. Zhao Bolin had also seen it. ### Jade Wine Vessel and Fragrant Turtle Emperor Huizong had a jade wine vessel, which was four inches in size and could hold several liters of wine. He also had a fragrant turtle as small as a fist, which was shiny like purple stone. Every time he burned incense, the turtle's mouth would receive it, and the smoke would enter it. The two items were sealed with yellow wax. When traveling, he would carry them along. Removing the stopper and the wax, the vessel would pour out wine, and the turtle would emit fragrance. Such items were not common in the palace, and it is said that Lin Lingsu presented them. ### False Qi's Ominous Signs At the beginning of the false Qi state's receiving the imperial investiture, a tablet for heavenly announcement and blessing was prepared. An official mistakenly wrote the era name as Jingkang. The ritual vessels used were purely from Zhao Ye's family temple. Those who understood it considered it ominous, and eventually, it was abolished by the Jin people. They also made paper money, ranging from one string to one hundred strings. The right side of the text says, "After eight years, it will no longer be in circulation." Until that year, it was abolished, and the number was already foretold.

**Strange Incident Involving Dogs** In the 14th year of the Tianhui era of the Jin dynasty, in the month of April, light rain fell in the Central Capital. A great thunderclap resounded, and dozens of dogs rushed toward the earth river and died. Only one or two could be saved. **The Daughter of the Shi Family** A man from the capital named Shi opened a tea shop and sent his young daughter to serve tea. Once, a beggar afflicted with madness, dirty and ragged in appearance, went directly to the shop and asked for a drink. The girl gave him tea respectfully, without taking any money. For over a month, she continued to select fine tea each morning to await him. When her father saw this, he became angry and did not send her away, but instead beat her. The girl barely took it to heart and served him with even greater care. After several more days, the beggar returned and said to the girl, "Can you drink my leftover tea?" The girl somewhat disliked it for being unclean and slightly spilled it on the ground. Immediately, she smelled a strange fragrance and drank it quickly. At once, she felt clear-minded and strong-bodied. The beggar said, "I am Master Lu. Though you have no karmic connection, if you finish my tea, you may have whatever you wish—whether wealth, nobility, or longevity." The girl, from a humble family and unaware of value, only asked for longevity and that her material needs would never be lacking. After he left, she fully informed her parents, who were surprised and searched for him, but he was already gone. When the girl reached marriageable age, she married a commander of a military camp. Later, she became the nursemaid to the granddaughter of Prince Wu of Yan and received a title of honor. The girl she nursed married Gao Zunyue and was enfeoffed as the Grand Lady of the Kang state. The Shi woman lived to be 120 years old.

During the reign of Emperor Shenzong of Song, there was a wealthy man named Wang Tianchang, who was the son-in-law of the Prince of Gaolou. One night, he dreamed that two messengers chased him to a place and ordered him to close his eyes and sit exposed, forbidding him to peek at anyone. The messengers said they would return after checking some documents. After they left, Wang Tianchang looked back and saw a grand gate with a sign reading "San Kun City." Below the gate, many people were shackled, including monks, Taoist priests, nuns, and prison guards. He sat there for a while when the messengers returned and ordered him to follow them. They hurried into a government office where the official was seated in formal attire, and other officials stood around him. The official asked where he came from, and Wang Tianchang replied that he was from the capital. An official then said, "That's a mistake. The Wang Tianchang we are looking for is not from the capital. We should quickly send this person back." Wang Tianchang recognized another official as an old friend who had died over a year ago. The official carried a large ledger and greeted him on the steps, reminiscing about old times. He said, "You should leave quickly. This is not a place for ordinary people." Wang Tianchang asked what was in the ledger, and the official replied that it recorded the births and deaths of people in the world. Wang Tianchang tried to see his own entry, but the official refused. Eventually, the official opened a page and showed him that he would die by a knife on a certain date. Wang Tianchang quickly closed the ledger, and the official escorted him out. After waking up, Wang Tianchang told his relatives about the dream, fearing that he might not die a natural death. He became so worried that he fell ill and eventually died from overwork. Later, people thought that "one stroke" referred to the character "labor" (劳), indicating that his death was due to overwork. This story was told by Zhao Boping.

Black Wind King The Posthumous Earth Shrine in Fen Yin is located forty li south of the Fen River, facing the great river and surrounded by mountains, forming a temple complex. It is the site of the ancient Han and Tang dynasties. The palace is grand and magnificent. During the reign of Emperor Gaozong of Song, the region was occupied by the Jurchens. The Jurchen military leader known as Black Wind King led an army of tens of thousands, intending to invade Liang and Yi. He stayed at the shrine, where it was filled with foul odors and filth, accumulating like a hill. Without cleaning, one night he, drunk, wanted to enter the sleeping chamber to view the true image of the Earth God, with disrespectful intentions. Those around him strongly advised against it, but he did not listen. He took more than ten servants and went directly there. Before he could even look up, a burst of fire rose, mingling with smoke and fog. The cold air pressed upon him, and he could not stand still. Frightened, the general rushed out, and the temple gates closed automatically. Several people followed him, and their feet were cut off by the threshold. The general bowed and prayed a hundred times, asking to move his camp the next day. On that day, the sky was clear, and the sun was high in the sky. Suddenly, a cloud rose from the shrine, and thunder and lightning accompanied heavy rain. In an instant, the water was several feet deep. The filth and excrement that had previously accumulated were washed away without a trace. The general offered a ritual sacrifice while maintaining purity and donated 50,000 strings of coins to atone for his sins. About 20-30% of the soldiers died.

During the reign of Emperor Xiaozong of Song, the Duke of Han, after stepping down from his position as chancellor, lived a leisurely life at home. He often wore a simple hat and rode a fine donkey, traveling around the lakes and mountains. He was accompanied by only a few private attendants. At that time, Li Ruhui, a local government official from Chu, came to the capital for a promotion but failed to secure a higher position, feeling distressed and having no solution. On a spring day, several of his colleagues went together to the Tianzhu, but Li declined, saying he felt listless and uninterested. They said, "This is the perfect time to go out into the countryside and dispel your boredom." They forced him to go along, and each rented a saddled horse. They passed by the scenic spot of Jiu Li Song when suddenly a heavy rain came. Everyone scattered to avoid the rain, but Li ran to the Cold Spring Pavilion, where his clothes got wet, and he sat in distress, sighing deeply. He met the Duke of Han there, who greeted him with a bow and felt sorry for his appearance. The Duke spoke in a Qin accent and asked, "What troubles you, sir, that you look so depressed?" Li, though he did not recognize the Duke, admired his imposing appearance and told him the truth. The Duke said, "Do you lack any documents? Is it not your duty?" Li replied, "It is a regular position." The Duke, named Shi Zhong, said he had a piece of paper and would give it to him the next day. He ordered a young attendant to inquire about Li's name, rank, and place of residence. Li bowed in gratitude and wept. The next day, an official brought a recommendation letter and said, "The Duke of Han sent it and also provided three thousand coins to help you." Li was then promoted to a high-ranking position in the capital. He wrote a letter and visited the Duke's residence, intending to show respect as a student, but he was not seen again.

The Twenty-Fourth Chapter of *Yiji Jia Zhi* **Lady Zheng** Lady Zheng, the wife of Zhang Zine, was beautiful and charming. Zhang held the position of Taichang Doctor. Lady Zheng died of illness. On her deathbed, she bid farewell to Zhang, saying, "You will certainly remarry, and will no longer remember me." Zhang wept and said, "How can I bear to do this?" Lady Zheng replied, "Words of others cannot be trusted. Why not swear by heaven?" She said, "If I break the promise, I will transform into a eunuch and will not have a good end." Lady Zheng added, "After my death, I will undergo a terrifying transformation. You should place my body in an empty room, and do not let anyone guard or watch over it. Wait several days before burying me." She repeated these words again and again. A short while later, her breath ceased. Zhang could not bring himself to follow her instructions and still sent an elderly woman to set up a bed beside her until midnight. Suddenly, the body let out a long sigh, revealed its face from the cloth, sat up abruptly, and soon stood up. The old woman, frightened, covered her head with a blanket. She felt the body move awkwardly and peered at it secretly. Suddenly, it was a night demon. The old woman, unable to escape, shook with fear and despair, and shouted loudly for help.

The family dug a hole in the wall to watch. They summoned several guards on duty and had them hold staffs and sit in a circle outside. The night spirit circled around repeatedly for a hundred times before stopping. It then returned to the sleeping chamber, lifted the blanket over itself, and lay down. After a long while, the family dared to open the door and look inside, only to find it had reverted to its original form. Three years later, Zhang became the Grand Master of the Imperial Academy, and Deng Xunren became the Right Chancellor. Deng wished to marry off his daughter to Zhang, but Zhang strongly declined. At that time, Deng was in favor with the emperor, who issued a decree ordering the marriage to proceed. On the night of the wedding ceremony, the emperor granted them a canopy adorned with pearls, worth fifty thousand strings of cash. Yet from then on, Zhang was often depressed and unhappy. One day, while he was taking a nap, he saw Zheng Shi descending from the window. She scolded him, saying, "What has become of our old agreement? How could you betray me? I am fortunate to have two daughters. Even if you have no son, why did you not simply buy a concubine? Why must you insist on marrying? Calamity is about to befall you!" She quickly climbed onto the bed and struck his private parts with her hand. Zhang felt pain and urgently called his family, but saw nothing. From that moment on, he was as if castrated. In the end, he fell victim to an extraordinary and strange disaster.

Zong Liben, the Son Zong Liben, a native of Huang County in Dengzhou, was a traveling merchant by profession. He was old in age but had no children. In the summer of the year 1138 during the Southern Song Dynasty, he traveled with his wife selling silk and cloth, arriving at Weizhou. They were about to go to Changle when they encountered the night and drove the carriage outside, taking shelter in an ancient temple. Several servants beat drums and held weapons for guard. The next morning, after finishing a simple meal, they set out on their journey. They encountered a child about six or seven years old who blocked the way and bowed in front of them. His speech was clever and pleasing. Zong Liben asked who his family members were and where he came from. The child replied, "I am the son of a public official in Changyi County. My father's name is Wang Zhongyan, and my mother both passed away. I was raised by others. They secretly left me and went away. Now I have nowhere to go and will surely die at the hands of wolves, tigers, and demons." Zong Liben comforted him and said, "Will you follow me?" The child bowed twice and wept with gratitude. Thus, Zong Liben took him in and raised him, naming him Shen Shou. The child's nature was alert and quick-witted. Every time he read documents, he could remember them after one reading. He could also hold a large brush and write characters one zhang wide.

Seal script, clerical script, and cursive script can be mastered without formal study. By seeing the calligraphy of famous sages and ancient models, one can slightly imitate and copy them, and surely capture all their subtleties. Lìběn is just a commoner from the marketplace; he abruptly abandoned his old trade and took this child with him on a journey, making him learn the lowly ways of a peddler to support himself. In the spring of the following year, they arrived at Zhangqiu in Jinan, where they encountered a foreign monk with a divine and heroic appearance. The monk pointed to the child and asked Lìběn, "Where did you find him?" Lìběn stared in disbelief and replied, "My wife really gave birth to him; why do you ask such a frivolous question?" The monk smiled and said, "He is one of the five hundred little dragons from Wutai Mountain. He has been lost for three years, and I have only just found him. If he stays too long, he will surely bring great disaster. I have already secretly applied a spell to restrain him, and he will no longer be able to wreak havoc." Then he fetched water and sprayed it, and the child immediately transformed into a small red snake, twisting and turning on the ground. The monk took the vase, called out to the spirit, and gave it a name. The snake immediately leaped into it. The monk, wearing his hat, left without saying goodbye. Lìběn and his wife missed him and never forgot him. Wáng Yìzhī, the military governor of Huaidong, witnessed this strange occurrence.

Qi Yige Saves His Mother During the reign of Emperor Shaoxi, in Jiangyin, there was a woman named Ou, the wife of Qi San. Her pregnancy and childbirth were fraught with difficulties, and she nearly died before finally being spared. She gave birth to a son named Yige, who was six years old. Yige was perceptive and understanding, and he could not bear his mother's suffering. He sought advice from an elder, asking what method could help escape this misfortune. The elder replied that the Daoist scripture "Nine Heavens Birth of the Divine Chapter" and the Buddhist mantra "Sutra of the Buddha's Top of the Head" were the best. Yige immediately sought out these two texts and studied them under a Daoist scholar. After reciting them for three days, he was able to memorize them unconsciously. From then on, every morning, he recited each text ten times, burning incense and looking up to the heavens, expressing his sincere devotion. Over the course of two years, in the first year of the Shaoxi era, Ou became pregnant again, and she was free from illness and suffering. When she was about to give birth in the tenth month, as Yige was reciting the texts, he saw ten divine beings standing beside him, and a strange light illuminated the room. Soon after, he was born (the rest of the text is missing, with eighteen lines per page, each line containing eighteen characters). Lying down and traveling to any place always harms the spirit. Now it is also absent from the collection.

Two Governors Chen and Miao Chen Zhongyu, whose courtesy name was Zhongyu, was a native of Zhengzhou and a descendant of the Wenhuigong (Wen Huigong). During the Zhenghe period, he served as the governor of Cai Prefecture. Upon assuming office, he visited the Temple of Pei Jin Gong and read the Stele of the Pacification of Huai Xi, which was composed by Duan Wenchang. Finding it strange, he asked the locals, who told him that since the stele of Han Wengong (Han Yu) was erected, it was later accused by the soldiers of Li Su, who claimed that the stele did not describe Li Su's achievements but instead praised Pei Jin Gong exclusively. Emperor Xianzong of Tang had ordered Duan Wenchang to rewrite it, and the matter had been settled for a long time. Chen Zhongyu felt it was unjust and, on the same day, had the old stele ground down. He entrusted someone skilled in calligraphy to write Han Yu's text and carve it. Miao Zhongxian, whose courtesy name was Ziyue, was from Tongzhou and served as the governor of Xuzhou. In Xuzhou, there was an old stele of the Huanglou built by Su Dongpo. During the party suppression of the Chongning period, it was supposed to be destroyed. The people of Xuzhou cherished it and placed it in the shallow waters of the Si River. At the end of the Zhenghe period, the restrictions were somewhat relaxed, and they pulled it out and re-erected it in its original place. The people who were carving the stele were bustling about, and the sound of hammers striking was incessant. The building and the government office were connected, and Miao Zhongxian disliked the noise. He ordered it to be dragged into the deep water, and it could no longer be retrieved. The two events were so opposite. As told by Zhu Xinzhong.

Turtle's Retribution** Chengjie Lang Huai Jingyuan was from Qiantang. In the early years of the Xuanhe era, he worked at the Dabao Temple in Xiuzhou for Cai You, setting up a bureau for service. He had a strong preference for eating turtles. A soldier skilled in cooking would cut the turtle's neck first to drain the blood, claiming that this made the turtle fully flavorful and delicious. Later, Jingyuan developed scrofula, his head becoming so large and heavy that he could not lift it. He had to drag his head while walking. Over time, the condition spread, causing his skin and flesh to decay, and his head eventually fell off, as if it had been cut by a knife. Jingyuan no longer dared to eat turtles. **Three Taoist Priests of Yujin Garden** During the Daguang era, the scholar Qian Jun from Suizhou, along with his brother, was studying at a prestigious academy. In the spring month, they were preparing for an examination and took a break to visit the Yujin Garden. They encountered three Taoist priests who came to greet and converse with them. The priests had dignified brows and spoke in a clear and pleasant manner. After a while, they took their leave, saying, "We have a small amount of fine wine and would like to share it with the two of you. It is too late today." They promised to meet again the next day at noon, adding, "We can still enjoy it, but if we wait too long, we might miss each other." Qian agreed, and the youngest priest laughed and said, "If you are late, you can dig the ground to find us." They all took it as a joke and laughed as they parted. The next day, Qian was delayed for other reasons and arrived at the meeting place late in the evening. There, he found the food and wine scattered, and no one was there. He felt disappointed for a long time. His brother said, "Could they be immortals? Let's try digging with a shovel and a spade. We dug only a foot or so and found a stone box. When they opened it, they saw the images of the three Taoist priests, their hats and headbands still intact, just as they had seen them the previous day. Outside the box, there was a medical text describing the process of refining mercury into white gold. The brother said, "Brother, take the book."

The younger brother wished to obtain a Daoist image and return home to worship with incense and fire. The elder brother happily agreed to this. After the examination, the younger brother was selected, and the elder brother returned home. They tested the formula, and nothing was left unfulfilled. Not many years later, the younger brother bought several tens of thousands of acres of land and became a wealthy person. One day, while sitting under the eaves, a report came from outside that three Daoists had come to visit. After they met, one of the Daoists stood up and said, "Do you remember the gathering at the Jade Spring of the past year? You obtained my elixir formula but did not use it to help the poor and needy, but instead were greedy and insatiable. You received more than your due, and heaven's decree shortens your lifespan. If you correct yourself today, you will still have three more years. If not, you will die soon. I was demoted to human form for revealing heavenly secrets and will come to take charge." After they left, Mr. Qian began to deeply regret his actions. He immediately burned the formula and destroyed the altar, closed the door tightly, and never opened it again. The next day, a young Daoist came again, but before he could sit down, he heard that his concubine had given birth. He hurriedly went to see it, and a boy was born. The guests left without seeing anything, and when he asked the servants, none knew. Mr. Qian died within three years.

Lu's Betrayal of the Pact** A man named Zheng from Quzhou was young, broad-minded, and skilled in writing. He married a woman from the Lu family in Kuaiji, who was also charming and bright. Their marital relationship was close and harmonious. Zheng once spoke to Lu in bed, saying, "We two have been very happy together. If I am unfortunate enough to die, you must not remarry. If you die, I will do the same." Lu replied, "We should promise to live together for a hundred years. How unfortunate or ominous is this?" They lived together for ten years and had two children. Zheng fell ill and, in front of his parents, reiterated his promise. Lu merely bowed her head and wept. Zheng eventually died. Not long after, matchmakers came to Lu. Lu interacted with them, but her in-laws scolded her. She did not listen. Soon after the mourning period, she took all her possessions and moved to Suzhou to marry Zeng, an official. They were married for only seven days when Zeng received a summons for a漕 (transportation of grain or goods by water) and had to conduct an examination in another prefecture. He traveled for two nights. One evening, Lu walked in the hall, and a hurried footman bowed before her, saying that Zheng had a letter for her. She ordered a servant to fetch it. The outer envelope bore three characters addressed to Lu. The handwriting was unmistakably that of her former husband. The footman had already disappeared. She opened the letter and read its contents: "Ten years as a newlywed couple, one life as the main sacrificial offering. We spent our days and nights in joy together, shared our surplus income. Suddenly, I departed for the great beyond. Who would you now remarry? You have abandoned the fields I left behind, moved your wealth to a new household. You do not care about my child, nor do you think of my father. You lack the virtue to be a wife and the compassion to be a mother. I have already appealed to the heavens above and will face judgment in the underworld." Lu lamented and regretted, not expecting to die within three days. The letter was found by Zheng's younger brother, Dian, who showed it to Hu Xiosran.

Zhang Yanze's Divination In the fourth year of Shaoxing, Li Shaoyu, a political official, returned to serve as the governor of Jiangxi. He dispatched General Yang Weizhong to suppress the rebels. On the day of Ren Shen in the month of April, at the hour of Yin, they marched out to Poyang. Hu Xianran escorted him across the river. Li then visited his Daoist friend Chen Sheng. A Daoist named Zhang Yanze, from Luoyang, who had previously served Master Xu Shen, often resided in the homes of those who revered Daoism in the Western Mountains. He happened to come to join in the conversation. Someone asked who had chosen the date and time. Hu Xianran replied, "Mu Maocai." Zhang Yanze said, "How wrong was that choice! Fortunately, if it were not the hour of Yin, it would be acceptable. If so, the rebels would be captured, but the general would be in danger. Predicting at noon, if there is no heavy rain, it would be good." At that time, the sky was clear and bright, with a slight heat. After the three had eaten, they walked around the temple. Soon, dark clouds gathered from all sides, and rain fell heavily, filling the ditches and gullies. Zhang Yanze clapped his hands and said, "It must be the hour of Yin. General Yang is in danger." The rebel forces numbered 12,000, while the official army was only two-thirds of that number. The vanguard commander Fu Xuan fully displayed the flags of the five armies to strengthen the morale of the troops. The rebel spies knew this and said, "Even the vanguard is like this. If the entire army comes, how could we stand against them?" They then sent envoys to surrender. The next day, the black steed that General Yang rode suddenly died, and Yang also fell ill. He immediately returned to Yuchang and died the next day.

**Xie Yuquan the Physician** Yang Weizhong fell ill, and his face turned red like fire. Many doctors could not treat him. His son-in-law Chen You was worried and asked Hu Xianran for advice. Hu Xianran introduced Xie Yuquan, who had been a scholar-physician for generations, to examine the patient. Upon entering, Xie Yuquan did not perform a physical examination and said, "The symptoms are already visible." Yang's wife, Lady Teng, asked the doctors for their opinions on the medication. Zhu and Zhang, two physicians, said, "We have already given him Zhengyang Dan, Baize Yuan, and added Zhonglu Fu Zi." Xie Yuquan said, "This is a case of Fu Shu (latent heat) syndrome. We should use Dahuang Huangbai (rhubarb and phellodendron) and other herbs. I have written a prescription." The advice did not agree. At the time, Yang Weizhong was over sixty years old and had recently taken a concubine who was very favored. Lady Teng thought this might be the cause of his illness and did not follow Xie Yuquan's advice. Xie Yuquan then said to Hu Xianran, "Go and listen to what the others are saying. As soon as you step through the door, they will criticize me, saying, 'This is a formula from the *Qianjin Yaofang* for treating summer heat, using seven herbs. He only remembers five of them and wants to treat a nobleman's illness with this?' Hu Xianran told Xie Yuquan about the criticism. Xie Yuquan said, "Five herbs are used to treat summer heat, but we are concerned they might be too strong, so we added two more to control them. Now, Yang Weizhong's illness is severe. We should focus solely on the five herbs. There should be no further control. If he indeed takes the previous two herbs, he will become agitated and thirsty by noon tomorrow, and he will definitely die by the hour of the sheep (1-3 PM). I come to help you all mourn and lament." Hu Xianran told Chen You, who did not reveal this. The next day, Yang Weizhong died, just as Xie Yuquan had predicted. All four events were recounted by Hu Xianran.

The Report of Zhao Biao's Son Zhao Lingjin, courtesy name Biao, was appointed as the Chief Clerk of Nankang in the fifth year of the Xuanhe era (1123). He passed through Qizhou and visited Wuzu Mountain. In the midst of rain and wind, he ascended to the summit alone. Upon reaching the White Lotus Pool Pavilion, he rested on a stone slab. It seemed as if he were in a dream when he saw an old monk leaning on a staff and speaking to him. The monk said, "You will have no trouble going to Lushan, but when you reach Jin Prefecture, you will have the sorrow of losing a son. In the past, while serving in Jin Prefecture, you were involved in an incident that led to the imprisonment of a citizen's mother, and thus you lost your own son. This is now your retribution." After saying this, the monk disappeared. Zhao Biao confirmed that it was not a dream and had never considered such a thing before. He thought that Jin Prefecture was in Hedong and that he would serve there in the future. He returned home and told his family, who were amazed by the story. From Wuzu Mountain to Huangmei County, the next day, due to rain, he did not proceed. His young son, Shanglang, suddenly fell ill. The county magistrate Wu Yu arrived and happened to mention the changes in the county. He said, "In the Tang dynasty, it was once Nanjin Prefecture. Few people knew about it." Zhao Biao was astonished and realized that the monk's words were true. He suspected that his son would not survive long and decided to allow him to become a monk. Four days later, Shanglang died at Bailing Post Station, which was only about thirty miles from the county. Zhao Biao personally recorded this event.

Divine Prescription A man from Jianchang named Huang Xiyun had a fellow villager who was a merchant. The merchant moored his boat at Xunyang and, under the moonlight, vaguely saw two people conversing. One said, "Last night, a grand offering was made at Mount Jinsha. I went to attend it. The food and drink were all bloody, and I could not approach them." He then said, "I was angry at the cook for being careless. His hand was soaked in the pot, and now it is severely infected." The other replied, "He certainly had a fault, but blaming him too harshly was excessive." The first one said, "I regret it now, but it's too late to do anything about it." The other said, "What's difficult about it? I have a remedy. Just crush fresh rhubarb, mix it with good vinegar, and apply it to the wound. It will not only heal the pain but also remove the scar. This formula is very good, but there is no way to make it known." The merchant, who was just heading to Mount Jinsha, heard their words and thought that in some mysterious way, they were being guided by divine intervention. Later, he visited the temple to inquire and learned that the previous night, a water and land offering had been set up. The cook had accidentally cut his finger while preparing food, and blood fell into the dish. In his daze, his hand was pulled into the pot, causing him unbearable pain and cries of agony. The merchant treated him according to the divine instructions, and he recovered in two days.

Poetic Riddles During the Yuan You era, scholars and officials with particular interests took the names of high-ranking officials and turned them into poetic riddles. For example, "In clear skies after snow, a rainbow is seen. A thousand miles of mountains and rivers meet the imperial capital. A white jade is held in the emperor's hand. A scholar refuses to wear a coarse robe." This refers to Han Gongjiang, Feng Gongjing, Wang Gonggui, and Zeng Gongbu. Another example is, "Everyone wears a Zizhan hat. Junshi has recently been promoted to a new post. A petition is returned to Wang Jiefu. Luxi has never felt cold." This refers to Zhong Changtong, Sima Qian, Xie Anshi, and Wen Yanbo.

Wu Chenggui Wu Chenggui, courtesy name Zizheng, was from Chang'an. In the seventh year of the Zhenghe era, he served as the supervisor of wine taxes in Xianzhu Town, Ninghai County, Taizhou. He frequently invited Daoist practitioners to his home, and there were often several people who ate at his door daily. At the time, my father was the chief clerk, and he warned him, saying, "My position is humble and my salary is meager, yet we have so many mouths to feed. How can we afford this?" Wu replied, "I do not provide them with fine wine and meat, but I simply go with the flow. When there is wine, I get drunk; when there are sea fish, I have a full meal. I do not spend anything else. Those who are unable to contribute will leave on their own within ten days. How do I know that I might not encounter a sage?" Later, he was again advised not to continue this practice, but he did not listen. One day, he appeared with a radiant and joyful demeanor, as if he had gained something. He said, "You laugh at my connection with Daoist practitioners. Recently, someone taught me the 'Inner Exchange Method.' Every day at noon and midnight, I manipulate the energy of the tiger and the dragon, rubbing them against each other. For a while, it is pleasant and smooth, not inferior to the joy of being in a room. Yet it causes no harm. Although it may not lead to immortality, it is also a unique and enjoyable practice." My father said, "Your wife is very young, and you do not have any children. What will you do about that?" Wu replied, "I also have a method similar to this that I can teach her. She will also find her own joy."

My younger brother has many sons. The sons of my brothers are like my own sons. It is enough for a woman to have a successor. My father wants to hear a brief account. I said, "You are currently serving in office. You also have parents, wife, and children. It is different from what Chenggui has. After the age of sixty, if we meet again, then it will be possible. Come back in ten days." He said, "Chenggui wants to go to Fujian to visit you. He will send your wife and children back to serve me day and night. If there is someone suitable to marry, then marry her." His father, Shen, was a general in Yue Prefecture. My father said, "Since I have entrusted myself to you, how could I bear to do such a thing? I have already given up on such desires. It is possible to live in separate rooms." "It will still be a burden. Without this person, I will feel lighter. If I want to leave, I can leave." "Why not return once to say goodbye to your family?" "The affection between kin. I will surely stay if I meet them. Ultimately, it is not possible to escape." When we met again, he said, "My wife has already left." Chenggui's replacement period has already arrived. All official duties have been fulfilled, but the replacement has not arrived. I wish to inform the prefecture and county. Send a military officer to the border. As he said, the county officials just arrived. His room is empty.

Fanzu Wu Fanzu Wu was a military man from the Wei Sheng region of Hedong. During the year of Gengsi in the Zhenghe era, he studied alongside his father at the Tonglei Hall of the Imperial Academy. He himself recounted that he had a youthful fondness for beauty, indulging in pleasure-seeking activities every day. At the age of twenty-six, he fell ill with a wasting disease, close to death. A Taoist practitioner named Niudao came to him and said, "If you can abstain from desire, I will save you." His parents, seeing their son on the brink of death, said, "If this child can be saved, what harm is there?" The Taoist practitioner then gave him medicine and taught him qigong practices, instructing him to separate from his wife. His illness gradually subsided. After three years, they resumed their marital relations, but his desire did not arise again. At the age of thirty-five or thirty-six while studying, he had a robust and healthy appearance, with a dignified and handsome demeanor. He was also amiable with others, though he was led to visit the demimonde without firmly refusing, but without any lustful thoughts. He avoided excessive drinking and eating, saying, "Excessive drinking and eating are the most harmful to one's health." It required sixty days of cultivation for him to fully recover. Later, he returned to his hometown, and his ultimate fate remains unknown.

*The Collection of Strange Tales from a Lonely Studio, Volume 39, Part 1* **The Elixir of Ten Thousand Years** In Huizhou, in the county of Wuyuan, in the village of Huajin, there lived a man named Cheng Bing. He sought profit through risky means, storing medicine to harm people. He killed many snakes, buried them in the ground, covered them with straw, and watered them. Over time, fungi grew from the heat and moisture. He harvested and dried these fungi, then mixed them with other herbs. The first batch of this concoction, if eaten by a person, would cause immediate death. Fearing the consequences, he dared not use the most potent batches. Instead, he often used the less effective ones, first testing them on frogs by observing how much they jumped. He then refined this mixture, giving it a beautiful name: the Elixir of Ten Thousand Years. A foolish man, wishing to kill his enemy, secretly purchased several thousand gold coins' worth of this elixir. Once, a guest arrived and intended to use it to poison someone. By mistake, he poisoned his father-in-law. Upon returning home, the father-in-law realized what had happened, but it was too late to save himself. Cheng Bing had a younger brother named Zhengdao, who considered this practice wrong and dared not advise his brother. Eventually, Zhengdao moved his family away, living dozens of miles from Cheng Bing. As Cheng Bing grew older, he began to regret his actions and stopped making the elixir. He started using fake ingredients instead, and the medicine no longer worked. No one would buy it, and after his death, he became very poor, leaving only one son who begged for food and eventually died. His line then came to an end. Once, a local official came to collect taxes and insulted Cheng Bing. Angered, Cheng Bing poisoned the official’s drink. Shortly after drinking it, the official suffered severe headaches and vomiting blood. He quickly returned and collapsed at Cheng Bing’s door, crying out for mercy. Cheng Bing gave him water to drink, and the official recovered. It is said that something in the water neutralized the poison. This account was told by a local man named Dong You.

**Li Xin's Vengeance** At the end of the Xuanhe era, Li Xin of Yushu in Raozhou was a corrupt and tyrannical official, regarded with fear and suspicion by the people of the district. One day, during a heavy snowfall, he entered the local government office. On the road, he met a man, and, drunk and relying on his strength, he struck the man down with his fists, killing him. A crowd gathered around in astonishment, but fearing that they might incriminate themselves, they said nothing. Li Xin left the scene. The street official, thinking the man had died suddenly, called his family to bury him. Li Xin grew even more reckless. He lived outside the city and often returned at night by climbing over walls. After three years, he suddenly met the dead man, who said, "I have been searching for you for a long time. Is it that you are here?" Li Xin returned home and told his wife, who was very frightened. The next day, he died. Li Xin’s family had raised several deer, and whenever he struck a bamboo pole against a pillar, the deer would come running in response. When the official in charge of household affairs, Bai Sheng, ordered him in July to buy a deer, he could not obtain one. So, he called for the one he had raised. As soon as he struck the bamboo, the largest of the deer arrived. He then killed it, took the meat to fulfill the order, and called upon his acquaintance Hong Duan to share the rest with him. A day later, Li Xin died. Everyone thought he had been poisoned, not knowing that he had been killed by a vengeful ghost. Hong Duan said.

Chen Shi, the daughter of a high-ranking official, was married to a man from the Shi family in Kuaiji. They had a son, but the man fell ill and was on his deathbed. He took his wife's hand and said, "I have been happy with you, more so than an ordinary couple. Please take good care of our son and do not remarry to repay me." Chen Shi hesitated in her response. The man became angry and said, "It is good for a new husband, do not think of your former master." He then died. Chen Shi wept bitterly and was deeply grieved, her body wasting away. Soon after, her father took her to Guangdong, where he arranged for her to marry a man from Putian named Wu Shuai. Chen Shi reluctantly accepted the marriage proposal and accepted the betrothal gifts. After a year of marriage, she suddenly saw her former husband appear. He scolded her, saying, "You treated me like this, how can you serve another man? I will first take our son, then come for you." That night, their son died. Ten days later, Chen Shi also fell ill and died. Chen Huan Shiming told this story, noting that Chen Shi and Wu Shuai were on good terms.

Li Shangren In the year Xinyou of the Shaoxing era during the Southern Song Dynasty, Wang Chengke served as the Inspector of Tea and Salt in the Eastern Zhejiang Region. His office was located in the eastern part of the inner city of Kuaiji, which was the site of the ancient Longxing Temple. Wang Chengke's third son, Wang Wei, once dreamed of a man dressed in a purple robe who said, "My bones are buried under a peach tree, and my wandering spirit has no place to rest. I hope you will pity me and allow me to be reburied." Upon waking, Wang Wei told his father, who then inspected the area and found a large peach tree near their residence. They dug beneath the tree but did not find any bones. The following year on the last day of August, Wang Wei had another dream where a man introduced himself as Caoqing Doctor Li Shangren. This man was the same one Wang Wei had seen in his previous dream. He bowed his head, looking sad, and reiterated his request. He presented a poem to Wang Wei, saying, "Hidden in the peach grove, disliking the pure fragrance. Last year, my wandering spirit met you. An eighty-year lifespan, unmatched by anyone. A presence and demeanor in the hall unheard of in the world. Reforming the times and correcting corruption with loyalty as the guiding principle. Saving people and mourning the dead, acting with virtue and kindness. The white bones are pitifully buried nearby. I hope you will lift your hand to repay me boundlessly." Wang Wei woke up and quickly wrote down the poem by candlelight. Coincidentally, Wang Chengke was about to return to his former position. Believing that they could not辜负李君的精爽（spirit），they quickly gathered officials and soldiers to search thoroughly under the peach trees in the west wing. After several days of searching, they found nothing. Wang Chengke personally supervised the digging and, after excavating a few feet, they discovered the remains. There were two small combs that had rotted away, and a black cap measuring just over an inch square. Four large nails were found beside the bones. They then relocated the burial to a spot beneath three towering pines behind the Yu Temple and held a ritual offering of food and drink. Scholars from Wu County, Mo Shou, and Zhu Xi from Luoyang both recorded this event. They believed that the poem in the dream was a sign of good fortune. Fourteen years later, Wang Wei was exiled to Guangdong, where there was already a resident named Li Shangren.

Segment's Concubine Segment, a man living in a temple in Pujiang County, Jinhua Prefecture, had a wife who once stood at the door. She saw a young woman begging. The woman was quite strong and asked about her name and background. She said she had no husband and no relatives. Segment's wife asked, "If that's the case, why don't you become a concubine and beg for food? Will you follow me?" The woman replied, "I'm not unwilling, but people won't accept me because I am poor and lowly. If I can serve in the role of cooking, it would be a great honor." So, she was taken in, bathed, and given new clothes. The cook taught her how to prepare meals, and after ten days, she was proficient. Then, she was trained in music, and within a month, she was excellent. After long training, her appearance became very beautiful. Segment named her Yingying and made her a concubine. For five or six years, he feared she would leave. One night, long past midnight, everyone in Segment's household was asleep. Suddenly, someone called at the gate, saying, "I am Yingying's husband." The gatekeeper responded, "We have never heard that Yingying has a husband. Even if you say so, it is not late to come tomorrow. Why must you come at midnight?" The person became angry and said, "If you do not open the door, I will enter through a crack." The gatekeeper was very frightened and immediately knocked on the main door, telling Segment what happened. Yingying heard this and seemed pleased, saying, "He is coming." She quickly went out. Segment suspected she had run away and lit a torch to chase her to the hall. But all he heard was a loud noise, and the lamp went out. His wife sent a maid to check, and they found that Segment was dead, with blood flowing from all his orifices. The outer door was locked as usual, and there were no signs of forced entry. No one knew what strange thing had happened. The story was told by He Shuda from Pujiang, and I heard it from Cheng Zizhong.

Sage Dou Gui Zhen, courtesy name Yan Li, was from Guixi in Xinzhou. His residence was only about thirty miles from Mount Longhu. Taoist practitioners frequently passed by his door, and the Gui family would always give them money. Gui Zhen had a long-standing hernia condition, and every episode brought him close to death. A doctor advised him to learn breathing exercises from a Taoist master, which he took to heart. On the twenty-third day of the sixth month of the year Gengshen during the Shaoxing era, after bathing in the evening, Gui Zhen took a stroll down a small path. An old Taoist man approached him. The old man was in his eighties or nineties, with white hair and a hunched posture. Gui Zhen greeted him and said, "Please come to my humble abode to receive the money for tea and soup." The old man replied, "It is already late, and I cannot go to your house. If you have the intention, can you host me for ten days?" Gui Zhen did not respond and continued walking. The old man turned back, called out to Gui Zhen, and motioned for him to follow. They entered a grove and sat at the base of an ancient pine tree. The old man introduced himself, saying his surname was Dou, and his voice resembled that of someone from Shandong.

Engaged in serious conversation for a long time. The dialogue was quite intrusive to Zhen. Zhen saw that the person was old; though outwardly respectful, inwardly discontented. Looking closely at his eyes, they were clear and sharp, reaching the temples. Wearing a blue headband, he did not sweat even in the heat. Zhen did not dare to leave hastily and asked again about breathing techniques. The Taoist priest said, "I have practiced breathing for two hundred years. Curing diseases is easy for me. I recited the learned texts of over a thousand words, including astronomy, geography, military strategy, and the essentials of the Dao, interwoven among them, barely understandable." Zhen said, "Master, please teach me. This is beyond my ability. Why not speak of the basics?" The Taoist priest said, "You seem teachable. I have a book hidden in Mount Heng. I will go and fetch it now. In another thirty-three years, I will give it to you." Zhen said, "Is it not the secret of movement and guidance?" The Taoist priest replied, "Not yet. Temporarily use the medical texts to help the people, gradually accumulating merit." Zhen said, "If it coincides with the time."

Master, where are you going? He replied, "It is not for you to know. I will come to visit you." He then invited Zhen to accompany him on his journey. Zhen used his parents' advanced age and his family responsibilities as an excuse. The Daoist became displeased and suddenly disappeared. Zhen was both terrified and afraid. He hurriedly returned home without telling anyone. A few days later, a Daoist came to the door and asked, "Where is the eighty-third official?" (Note: "八十三承事" is unclear and may require further contextual interpretation.) Zhen's father's family said he had left. The Daoist angrily said, "I am not asking for anything. The Master sent me to deliver a scroll to you. Why won't you come out?" He threw the scroll on the steps and left. Zhen took it and looked at it—it was the *Lü Dongbin Biography*. Zhen began to regret it. In the year of Ren Xu, he was promoted to the position of magistrate of Poyang. On his way back to Yan and Qu, his illness became severe. He could not travel by sedan chair for several miles without having to stop. He stayed at an inn and lay down by the outer door. A merchant passed by, leaned on his burden, and asked,

The official had a disease or ailment. He said, "Yes." The guest asked, "At the onset, was it impossible for you to walk, sit, stand, or lie down? Was there a particular spot that was most painful? Could you not even wish for death?" The official replied, "Yes." The guest then asked, "How do you know this?" The guest said, "I am from Yuzhang. When I was young, I also suffered from this illness. Today, I can carry a hundred catties without any harm, because I have a medicine to treat it." He then reached into his bag as if searching for something, and found a bundle wrapped like finely chopped mulberry leaves. He instructed the official to soak and drink three dou of wine with the medicine. The official, who was not accustomed to drinking, hesitated to take it. He thanked the guest with a thousand gold pieces and left. Upon returning home, his illness worsened. He tried many other medicines, but none were effective. He decided to follow the guest's advice and put the medicine into the wine. As soon as he took a sip, it was as sweet as honey. He drank whenever he felt thirsty. By morning, the wine was finished. His illness improved by eight-tenths. After two nights, he was completely recovered. He did not suffer from it again. After careful consideration, he realized the merchant was the same Mr. Dou he had met before.

Zhu Da Bo Gui Zhen Zu Anshi, from a young age, had a strong inclination towards the Dao. At the age of twenty-four, he left his wife and children behind, carrying gold and silk to seek immortality in famous mountains. After ten years, he returned. Whenever Taoists passed by his door, he would invite them in. Daily, he fed several dozen people in his hall. His family wealth was depleted, and all the family members blamed him, yet Gui Zhen's determination became even stronger. Zhu Da Bo, a simple-minded and cautious servant, performed tasks such as fetching firewood and water. One day, he returned from outside, behaving unusually. He said, "I just met a Taoist who gave me medicine. After taking it, I can stop eating." When tested, it was indeed true. When asked about the method, there was no answer. Sometimes in midsummer, the sun was extremely hot, and in winter, he would cover himself with ice, yet he was unaffected by cold and heat. He continued to serve as a servant as before. All the people of the Gui family revered and served him, calling him "Zhu the Immortal." They wanted to invite him with guest courtesy, but he declined, saying, "I should remain in the world as a servant. I will naturally leave after a year." This continued for three years. He told Gui Zhen, "Someone at White Flower Rock has invited me. I wish for you to go with me." They both went. Before reaching the foot of the rock, they heard the sound of music, clear and melodious, filling their ears. Colorful clouds gathered, covering the mountains and valleys. Gui Zhen was still marveling at the wonders when Zhu Da Bo suddenly bowed and took his leave, and he disappeared. Gui Zhen did not expect this and soon passed away. This was during the second year of the Da Guan era. White Flower Rock is ten li away from where the Gui family lived.

Zhen Obtains a Son Li Churen, also a native of Gui Xi, had a wife named Zheng. Once, she dreamed of reaching the foot of a high mountain where a green-clad little boy was playing on the peak. She quickly embraced and took him, then woke up. Shortly thereafter, she became pregnant and gave birth to a boy. She named him Songlao. As he grew older, he became extremely sharp and intelligent. His father ordered him to study for the Jinshi examination, and he was renamed Song, with the courtesy name Mengfu. At the age of eighteen, in the fifteenth year of the Shaoxing era (1145 AD), he passed the examination on his first attempt. Five years later, he became the magistrate of Jianyang, Jianzhou. Bandits entered his county, and all his relatives were killed. Zheng's dream was not auspicious either. This story was told by three people named Gui Zhen.

Shao Nan's Divine Art Shao Nan was a man from Yan Prefecture. He had some familiarity with official documents and enjoyed reading books on astronomy and the Five Elements. He had deep expertise in the art of divination (Tun Jia) and his divination was like that of a god. However, he drank heavily and was proud, and liked to criticize people to their face. Everyone called him mad. In the fourth year of the Xuanhe era, he traveled to Lin'an, the capital of the Southern Song dynasty. At that time, Cai Shao Ji, the Secretariat Scribe, was serving as the Transport Commissioner of the Two Zhe regions. He heard of Shao Nan's name and summoned him to perform a divination. Shao Nan said, "Within sixty days, you will retain your current position as the Grand Commissioner and replace a man surnamed Chen." At that time, Grand Marshal Zhong Xun was the military governor of a certain region. He wanted to imitate the system of three routes and determine the rank of officials. Cai Wenyao, the Governor of Hangzhou, often suppressed him. He had to bow to him every day at the steps before he could sit. Unable to bear his anger, Zhong Xun petitioned for retirement. He also summoned Shao Nan to decide the matter. Shao Nan said, "Wait until Cai Shao Ji is appointed as the Transport Commissioner. After forty more days, the Grand Marshal will also receive a position in the northern regions, with the title 'Anfu' in his official designation, but he will not be a military commander." Zhong Xun said, "I have already petitioned for retirement. How could there be such a thing?" Only fifty-seven days later, the Transport Commissioner Chen Hengbo was summoned, and Cai Shao Ji took his place. Zhong Xun prepared a meal to entertain Shao Nan and asked him again. Shao Nan replied, "The omen is the same as the previous one. There is no sign of retirement. My previous words were not false." After the matter was settled, Zhong Xun retired with his current rank. He asked Shao Nan again, and Shao Nan answered as before. Zhong Xun became angry and showed him the official document. Shao Nan was not pleased and said, "If that is the case, then I cannot understand either." Coincidentally, Tan Zhen, who was friendly with Zhong Xun, recommended him. Within ten days, he was appointed to know the state of Dai with his old rank, and also handled the affairs of the border pacification office. Wang Duan Chao, the Governor of Jinling (Nanjing), visited Lin'an to see Cai Shao Ji, and Shao Nan happened to be present.

A man from Shaoji spoke of strange events in the middle of the night. An old man asked how it was in Qiantang (Hangzhou). The person named Nan wrote in large letters on the table: "Fire." The old man said, "It has already become hot recently." He said, "The disaster has not yet ceased. It will be verified within three days." The Governor must see it. Some other day, he will return to govern this place. The old man dared not reveal it. It was the fifth day of the twelfth month.
