The next day, Governor Cai held a grand banquet on his birthday, gathering soldiers from the capital region who were returning home, who should receive rewards in the form of silk. Governor Cai posted a notice in the market, not allowing the purchase of official positions, and took them at a low price. They were all greatly angered. At night, they set fire in several places, wanting to lure Governor Cai out to save them and then kill him. Governor Cai was already drunk, knowing the situation was dire, climbed over the wall into the patrol camp. His family rushed to the Zhonghe Hall to avoid the danger. As a result, the state administration building was reduced to ashes. Duan Chao had not yet left. He saw Governor Cai and said, "Two days ago, I saw Mr. Shao who spoke about this matter. I did not dare to believe it. It turned out to be true." Governor Cai usually did not like divination. He tried to call and ask him. He replied, "Within fifteen days, he should be transferred to another capital." Governor Cai said, "Is it not to be a deputy? Why so soon?" After two days, he happened to be criticized by a speaker. He was dismissed and appointed as the director of the Hongqing Palace in Nanjing. Not long after, he was again demoted to the position of Zhexue Shi in the Longtuguo. He accepted the order on schedule. And Duan Chao was transferred to Hangzhou as the director of the Changping office. Xu Zida's nephew was transferred to the capital for an official post. He had not returned for a long time. His niece told Xu Zida, ordering him to go to Nan for divination. Nan wrote, "Your nephew has already left the capital. He met his maternal uncle who invited him to go to Xiluo for a posting. He entrusted two firemen to receive the official post. The state he should go to will be by the water. It must be Binzhou. It is not a county official or a court official, but one who also oversees the prison. It must be a Shicao Yuanyuan." Xu Zida said, "Mr. Shao often speaks accurately. However, this is also too fantastic."

My nephew sent a letter last month. He said that he had already left the city gate by the river and encountered my uncle, who strongly invited me to go to Luoyang for an official post. He entrusted the message to a letter shop, and I was already surprised by its accuracy. Soon after, I received news that he was indeed appointed as the magistrate of Binzhou and the left censor, a position that my uncle Yan had previously held. I and Zheng Dian, a man from Quzhou, were drinking companions. Zheng Dian likes gambling, but his wins and losses do not exceed several thousand. I said, "Your small wins are of no help; you should prepare to bet ten thousand." I suggested calling in gamblers who can match him and offered to choose a day for the gamble. Zheng Dian replied, "My purse is empty; how can I do that?" I said, "I will lend you something." On the appointed day, we gathered to gamble at the Cold Spring Pavilion in front of Lingyin Mountain. I went into the temple and lay down. Suddenly, I went out and called out to Zheng Dian, saying, "You should stop; your count has already exceeded the limit." He quickly looked and saw that it was exactly eight hundred and eighty thousand. I once went to Tongzhou, where the official Fan Zhicai, a censor, was blamed for saying that there was a tripod in Chaohu Lake. He came to ask about auspicious and inauspicious signs. I said, "In ten more years, you will meet in the constellation of婺女 (Dipper)." Fan asked, "Is it a transfer?" I said, "You will be the governor of a prefecture." Later, when Fan was pardoned, he indeed became the governor of婺州 (Wuzhou). He heard that I was in Hangzhou and sent someone to summon me. At that time, it had been nine years. I did not want to go and wrote back saying, "Although we had an agreement last year, I divined that two people would enter the city but not leave. If I go, I will die." Fan sent messengers repeatedly with wine and liquor, and his requests became more urgent. After the New Year, I finally set out. I passed through Yanzhou, where the governor Zhou Gefei asked where I was going to serve. I said, "I will soon become a false dragon, and in my second term, I will have the same fate as Fan in Wuzhou." He asked, "What will happen afterward?" I said, "I will die after one more term." Zhou became very angry.

The messenger was sent away quickly. Upon arriving in Wuzhou, Fan was very pleased. Nan said, "You and Zhou, the governor of Yanzhou, are both acting as governors." One day, he came again and said, "I stayed awake all night yesterday. After careful consideration, you will receive your appointment tomorrow. However, I am about to die. If you arrive by the fourth watch, you can still congratulate me. If you delay until noon, it will be too late." Fan said, "Why are you so eager to die?" The next day, he visited Fan again and spoke privately, saying, "The appointment notice is about to arrive. By chance, the messenger has not yet reached the city, twenty miles away, and has been injured. I would like to select a swift messenger to go and fetch it." Fan replied, "I am merely holding the position of governor. Why should I do this? Wouldn't this be laughed at by the people? Moreover, the official bulletin has not yet been heard, so it should not come so quickly." Nan said, "I am enduring this hardship to wait for it. What is there to hesitate about?" Fan immediately ordered a soldier, "Go twenty miles outside the city. If you meet someone carrying written documents, bring them back quickly." Then, he loosened his belt and engaged in a friendly conversation, ordering food to be prepared. After some time, the sent soldier arrived, sweating profusely, and bowed in the courtyard, shouting, "Congratulations, Dragon Graph!" He took the notice and read it, which was an official notice of appointment to the position of "Directly in charge of the Dragon Graph Pavilion." At that time, Wang Fu was the prime minister, and he had hastened the delivery of the appointment notice to Wuzhou, ordering the messenger to proceed at full speed. Therefore, the outside world did not know about it. Shortly after, Nan hastened the preparation of food and ate. After eating, Fan entered to thank his relatives. Nan quickly went to the guest room, lowered the curtain, and warned, "Anyone who dares to come here will be reported to the Dragon Graph and punished." Fan's family was overjoyed, competing to hold cups of wine to toast him. After a long time, he finally came out and hurriedly summoned Nan, who had already passed away while seated. Nan was in Hangzhou and was close to the author's father, who had once wanted to pass on his books to him. However, the author's father did not accept them. Nan had no children, and after his death, his knowledge and teachings were lost.

During the reign of Emperor Xiaozong in the fourteenth year of the Shaoxing era, on the fourth day of the third month, Zheng Lin, a stable boy under the Jiangdong Constitutional Office, fell ill for a long time. He dreamed of two messengers pursuing him, saying, "The great king has summoned you." They walked for several miles, and the buildings were majestic. The messengers led him up the steps and into the red gate. Below the courtyard were arranged men, women, monks, Taoists, chickens, dogs, cattle, and sheep. In front of the hall, a large mirror was hung, reflecting the innermost thoughts of people clearly. Soon, the king emerged. The two messengers escorted Zheng Lin and bowed respectfully, stating that they had brought Zheng Lin. The king asked where he was from and what business brought him there. Zheng bowed his head and replied, "Originally from Xinzhou, I was summoned here. I do not know why." The king ordered that the record of his entire life be brought. With a brush, he pointed out a word and looked at the official and said, "It turns out to be this Zheng Lin's name. Is there any mistake?" The judge carried the book forward and said, "The one who should be summoned is Zheng Lin from Songyang, Chuzhou." The king said, "If so, then it has nothing to do with this person's affairs." He ordered to return and report. The register of life and death was checked, stating that Zheng Lin's life span still had one decade left. Halfway, the king called Zheng Lin forward and said, "See, you are a good person. Have you recited scriptures in your lifetime?" Zheng replied, "I have."

Silently reciting the *Gao Wang Jing*, he looked at the book and recited the *Guanyin Jing*. The king said, "You see these idle villains not doing good deeds." The neighbor raised his head to look at the iron pillar below the hall. There were many people tied up, with five types of torture applied to their bodies. They were emaciated, naked, and standing, with no human appearance. There were white plaques on the pillars recording their crimes: some person cursed, some person killed life, some person fought to the death. The prison door was secured with golden nails, with a picture of the sea and a beast with its mouth open, holding it. Both sides of the hall were filled with judges, among whom there was one wearing a cow-horn headgear, who looked around and then turned. The king said, "You have seen it. When you return to life, continue to do good deeds. If you see those being punished, just recite the names of Amitabha and Guanyin. Let them be reborn. You will be rid of disasters and receive blessings." The neighbor understood the imperial decree, then took a few steps back. When he turned back, he saw nothing. There was only an old man in white clothes holding a cane. The neighbor asked the way to Raozhou. The old man pointed with his cane and said, "Go left from here. Once you find the path, hurry. If you delay, you will encounter the venom of tigers, wolves, insects, and snakes." The neighbor, worried and troubled, hurried back and woke up, drenched in sweat. It was the night of the sixth day.

Young Gentleman Wu Zhào Yìngzhī, a member of the royal family from Nanjing, accompanied his brother Mào to the capital city. Together with the wealthy young official from the Wu family, they indulged in pleasure trips daily. In the spring, they went to Jinming Pool, walking along a small path where they found a wine shop. The place was adorned with lush flowers and bamboo, with utensils and dishes neatly arranged, creating an extremely elegant and charming atmosphere. It was quiet and devoid of human voices. The woman serving behind the counter was quite mature. The three of them stopped to buy wine. Yìngzhī pointed to the woman and said to Wúshēng, "How about calling her to serve us wine?" Wú was very pleased and flattered her with words. She happily agreed and they then sat down. Just as they were raising their cups, the woman saw her parents returning from outside and quickly stood up. The three of them had already lost interest and all left. By then spring had ended, and they did not visit again. But their longing continued, manifesting in their dreams. The following year, they went together to revisit the old place. When they arrived, the doors and windows were desolate, and the barmaid was no longer there.

After taking a brief rest, he asked for a drink. He inquired about their family. They replied, "Last year, we passed by here and saw a young woman. Where is she now?" The elderly man and woman looked sad and said, "That was our daughter. Last year, the whole family went to the ancestral tomb, and she was left behind. When we returned, three disrespectful young men were drinking with her. We criticized her for behaving in such a manner while unmarried, asking how she could marry. She became depressed and died within a few days. Now, there is a small hill beside our house; that is her tomb." The three young men were too afraid to ask further questions and hurried to finish their drink and leave. As they walked along the path, they felt sorrowful. It was already dark, and they were approaching the gate when they met a woman with her head veiled, walking slowly and hesitantly. She called out, "I am the woman you saw by the pond last year. Didn't you come to our house to visit me, sir? My parents wanted to make you lose hope by lying that I had died and setting up a false tomb to deceive you. I also searched for you throughout the spring and am glad to meet you again."

Now, they moved to live in a narrow alley of the city. The room was extremely spacious and clean. Could we go together? The three of them were delighted. They dismounted and walked together. Upon arriving, they shared a drink. Wu Sheng stayed for the night. Over the next three months, Wu Sheng's complexion became increasingly haggard. His father scolded the two Zhao brothers, saying, "You lured my son somewhere. Now he is sick like this. If he does not recover, I will report you to the authorities." The brothers looked at each other in fear and sweat. They also began to doubt him. They heard that Master Huangfu was skilled in exorcism. They went to pay their respects to him. He invited them to view Wu Sheng together. Master Huangfu was greatly surprised upon seeing him and said, "The ghostly presence is very strong. The haunting is deep. It is advisable to quickly flee to the west, three hundred miles away. If it reaches one hundred and twenty days, he will surely die. It cannot be treated." The three of them immediately ordered their carriage to go to Xi Lu. Whenever they ate, the girl was always in the room. At night, she would occupy the bed. They had not been in Lu for long when it happened to be exactly one hundred and twenty days.

At a wine pavilion, he gathered, feeling both sad and fearful. Huangfu, riding a donkey, passed by. He bowed and pleaded for mercy. Huangfu set up a ritual platform and performed rituals, handing a sword to Wu and said, "You are destined to die. Now return and try to keep the door tightly shut. At dusk, there will be someone knocking. Do not ask who it is, just kill them. If you happen to hit a ghost, perhaps you can survive. If you unfortunately kill a person, then you must pay with your life. It is all a matter of death, but there is still a chance of escape." Following his instructions, by dusk, indeed, there was someone knocking at the door. He threw the sword at him, and he fell to the ground instantly. He lit a candle to see him, and it was a woman. Blood flowed profusely, and he was taken by the street officers. Along with two others named Zhao and the teacher Huangfu, they were all imprisoned. The interrogation did not succeed. The prefecture sent officials to investigate the family by the pond. The parents reported that he had already died. They opened the grave to verify, and only his clothes were left behind; no body was found. Thus, he was released. As told by Jiang Xuzhi. ### Mouse Report In the year of Bing Yin during the reign of Emperor Xiaozong of Song, during the summer and autumn, many states in Lingnan did not receive rain. In Qingyuan, Guangzhou; Wengyuan, Shaoguan; and Zhenyang, Yingzhou, the three counties suffered greatly from a mouse infestation. Even fish, birds, and snakes transformed into mice, appearing in groups of dozens, leaving the fields completely barren. A farmer from Baoren Temple in Zhenyang caught a mouse with a snake-like pattern on its chest. A fisherman set a net at night and in the morning found hundreds of fish that had turned into mice. He took them to look at, and they were all mice. After several months, the infestation ceased. As a result, the price of rice soared, and it was not until the following autumn that prices stabilized. As told by the monk Xici.

Li Yi's Rebirth** Li Yi, courtesy name Shenshu, was from the capital city. His original name was Xiangxian. During the Zhenghe era, he served as the Deputy Magistrate of Chizhou. He recounted a story told by the abbot of Meishan Temple, Kejiu. Two years ago, due to a severe illness, he dreamed of a person (the text is missing one leaf here). It was the 26th day. I therefore recount the story. **Song Shuhai's Dream Missing** The inner text is missing. **Jiang Bao's Deceased Mother** The servant Jiang Bao of the townsman Ma Shujing once returned home at night. He encountered a white-clad person who walked with him to the riverside and invited him to bathe together. Jiang Bao had already undressed and was about to enter the water when he suddenly heard someone calling his name. The sound was very far away, but as it got closer, he listened and realized it was his deceased mother. She shouted loudly and urgently, saying, "The person walking together with you is not a good person. Definitely should not bathe with him." Soon after, his mother arrived and immediately carried Jiang Bao. She quickly crossed the water to the shore, where they encountered a house. She then threw him into the bamboo grove. The residents heard a sound outside and went out to see it. They only saw Jiang Bao there, while his mother and the white-clad person had both left. Ma Shujing's younger brother Deng recounted this story. **Yu Yigong** Yu Yigong, courtesy name Yanfu, was from Wuyuan, Huizhou. He was domineering and bullying the villagers. The common people feared the law and were afraid to compete with him. He would always use tricks to take his wealth. As he grew older, he did not repent. In the year Ren Xu of the Shaoxing era, he fell gravely ill and would make the sound of a horse neighing. One day, his family was not around. Yanfu suddenly got up and closed the door. People outside heard the sound of roaring and throwing and hurriedly went in to see him. Then Yanfu's hands and feet had all become horse hooves. His body and head had not yet transformed, but his waist and spine were already soft. He would repeatedly rise and fall, unable to speak. His family feared that the evil sound would become known and carried him into a coffin and buried him.

Fang Ke, a man from Wuyuan, was a salt merchant who traveled to Wuhu. There, he encountered bandits. The bandits first tied up his servant, stabbed him in the abdomen, and threw his body into the river. When it was Fang Ke's turn, he bowed and cried, begging for his life. The bandits said, "Since we have already killed your servant, we cannot let you go." Fang Ke said, "I wish to speak one word before I die." The bandits asked the reason. Fang Ke replied, "I have liked burning incense since I was young. There are still a few catties of water-sink incense in my box. Allow me to open the box and take it out, burn it to thank heaven, earth, and the gods, and then it is not too late to die." The bandits agreed. After some time, the incense burned out. The bandits said, "Since you are pitiable, I will spare you one cut." They only bound his hands and feet, tied a large stone to him, and threw him into the water. At the time, Fang Ke had been away from home for several months. His family was surprised not to hear any news. One day, he suddenly returned. His wife scolded him, saying, "Since you have returned, why didn't you send a message first?" Fang Ke said, "Do not be afraid. Since I have returned, why didn't you send a message first?" His wife replied, "You must quickly inform the authorities." Then he disappeared. The family reported the entire matter to the prefecture of Taiping. The bandits were captured as he said. These two stories were told by Mr. Li Yong, a county official.

Water Spirit Judge Qi Ju, courtesy name Zhongyu, was from Dexing in Raozhou. He was warm-hearted and devoted to learning, but his family was extremely poor. He supported himself by teaching students. In the year Dingmao of the Shaoxing era, he stayed at the home of Dong Shimin in the same county. The agreement was set, but he did not arrive on time. Dong sent a letter to urge him. Just as he arrived at the door, he heard the sound of crying. It turned out that Ju had died two days ago. Wang Yaochen, who was close to Ju, said that Ju fell ill in the last winter of the previous year and dreamed of a person bringing a document. The person said, "A certain king requests the scholar to serve as a judge in the realm of water spirits." When Ju opened the document, it said, "You cannot look after your parents, nor can you be attached to your wife and children." Ju made an agreement to leave on the 13th day of the first month. Upon waking, he told his family, "I will die on the 13th day of the first month next year." From then on, he refused medical treatment and medication, and completely stopped eating and drinking. He often said to himself, "There are great benefits there. How can I stay here for long?" The family initially secretly worried about him. By the time it arrived, there was no other issue, but from then on, he became increasingly weak and stopped speaking. After another eight days, he did not get up. Wang Yaochen recounted the story.

The Report of Chen Wu and the Yellow Croakers** People of Xiuzhou enjoy drying yellow croakers. They say that among aquatic animals, it has the warmest nature. Even pregnant women and the sick can eat it. Chen Wu, the best seller, is highly regarded. People compete to buy his product. His disciples try various methods to learn his technique, but he refuses to reveal it. Later, he falls ill and stumbles around the bed. As soon as he sits on the bed, he cries out. They support him to stand up, but the pain becomes even more severe. After ten days, he dies, and his whole body rots. His wife says that when her husband was alive, every time he caught yellow croakers, he placed them in a container. Using the usual method with ash and salt, outside, he also collected many pieces of pottery fragments. The yellow croakers were stung by the salt and could not bear the pain. They writhed and rushed around, and their skin was injured by the fragments. The salt flavor slowly penetrated them, so they were especially delicious. Now his illness is exactly like when the yellow croakers died. **The Poem of Hou Yuangong** Hou Yuangong, a native of Mizhou, was a middle-ranking official. From a young age, he wandered through the examination halls. At the age of thirty-one, he finally became a local candidate. People did not respect him because he was old and slow. A disrespectful person drew his image on a kite and released it with a string. Hou saw it and laughed loudly. He wrote a poem titled "Linjiangxian" on it, saying: When not recognized, who would believe in one's actions? Now, one's name and deeds are revealed. A skilled craftsman drew the image without reason. Taking advantage of the wind, it gains strength. It soars into the high sky in one leap. Once it receives the wind, its body becomes stable. It seems to be heading far to the moon palace. At the time after rain, the sunset is red. How many people are on the ground? Looking at me in the blue sky. Hou passed the examination in one attempt and was over fifty years old. He then became a high-ranking official.

The Strange Occurrence at the Inn Hou Yuan Gong from Mizhou traveled with three other scholars to take the provincial examination in the eighth year of the Yuanfeng era. They stopped at an inn by the roadside. The room had beds in each of the four corners. The four scholars were very tired from their journey and rested separately on the beds. They all slept soundly. Two servants sat near the fire. They heard a sound coming from the northwest corner. Suddenly, the lamp went out. A creature with fur and four legs, resembling a pig, climbed onto the bed and sniffed from the scholar's face to his feet. The scholar was startled awake from a nightmare. After a while, he calmed down. The creature then climbed onto another bed in the same manner. The other scholar also cried out in surprise. Finally, the creature reached Hou Yuan Gong's bed. It did not have time to sniff before it seemed as if someone was chasing it. It hurriedly descended and quickly fled away, disappearing through the northwest corner. Hou Yuan Gong also woke up. He called the other three to wake up and eat. They all said there was a strange beast pressing on their bodies in their dreams. They did not know what it was. The servant then described what he saw. Hou Yuan Gong felt particularly pleased and confident. After they entered the capital, Hou Yuan Gong was selected and ranked, while the other three were dismissed. All of them died as guests in the capital. This story was told by Gao Sida.

Sun Zhu's Official Positions Sun Zhu, courtesy name Juyuan, was fourteen years old when he followed his father, Sun Xi, to serve in the Eastern Capital of the Song dynasty. Once, he traveled to Dengzhou and visited the Temple of the Sea God, where he secretly prayed to the deities, seeking to know his future success in the imperial examinations and the rank he would attain. That night, he had a dream in which someone told him, "You will achieve fame in one attempt, and your position will be above that of the杂学士." Upon waking, he was greatly pleased, but being still young, he did not know what the杂学士 was. He asked others, who told him, "It is a good dream! You will surely become a Scholar of the Dragon Pool Pavilion." Later, after passing the imperial examinations and entering the court, he held various high-ranking positions, receiving the emperor's favor and extraordinary treatment. People spoke of his success in relation to the dream. In the second year of the Yuanfeng era (1079), he was appointed as a Scholar of the Hanlin Academy. His guests all congratulated him, but Sun Zhu looked solemn and said, "I was told before that the Hanlin Academy ranks above the杂学士. Is this all that awaits me? Today's appointment is something to mourn, not to celebrate." Just a month later, while visiting an old friend outside the city, he fell ill while sitting. Emperor Shenzong sent eminent physicians to attend to him, hoping for his recovery. They predicted he would recover and suggested he be appointed to a high office. Indeed, he did recover, and the emperor was pleased. He sent a message saying, "When can you enter the court? You will be greatly employed." Upon hearing this, officials came in succession to pay their respects.

Crowds of hats and carriages filled the door endlessly. Sun privately spoke to his family, saying, "I pointed to the day when I would reach the Two Palaces, but the gods' words, how can they deceive me?" On the day of the court appearance, he turned to those around him and said, "I have been ill for a long time. I fear I may not be able to kneel and rise. Prepare a mat for me, and let me practice it." Just as he was about to bow twice, the illness flared up again, and he could not rise. They quickly supported and looked at him, and he had already died. When Sun was alive, once he was locked in the examination hall, the messenger arrived at his home, but he had already left. Dozens of people followed his trail and found him at the house of Li Duan Yuan, the Grand Marshal. At that time, Li had recently taken a concubine who could play the pipa. Sun drank and did not want to leave, but under the pressure of the imperial summons, he dared not stay and entered the examination hall. After drafting three edicts, he again composed short and long sentences, expressing his feelings of resentment. He sent it to Li at dawn, and the words said, "There are still three beats of the drum on the tower. Why must you insist on driving me away? Mounting the horse is so hurried. The pipa melody is not yet finished. Turning back, I see fine rain. Even if the jade hall is made of jade, the jade hall is long tonight." Some say this was written when Sun was about to die, but that is not the case. It is the story of Li Yi Qian, the grandson of the Chancellor, who was also Sun's great-grandson.

**Hu Keji's Dream** Hu Keji, courtesy name Shuping, was from Wenzhou. In the year Gengshen of the Shaoxing era, he took the provincial examination. He told his wife, "I dreamed that the examination hall opened early in the morning, and while others were still not ready to enter, I alone entered and sat in the main hall. I must be the top candidate this time." His wife replied, "Not so. Don't you remember the *Analects*? 'Advanced Disciples' is the eleventh chapter." When the results were announced, it turned out as his wife had said. **Xiang Songying** Xiang Songying was from Wenzhou. During the Xuanhe period, he wandered aimlessly in Wuzhou. A local man named Xiao Deqizhen, who was an official in charge of ceremonial duties, invited him to stay in his study. They talked until late at night, and Xiao left, leaving behind a bottle of wine, saying, "I must return now, so feel free to drink." Xiang, who was tired from his journey, lay down to sleep. Soon, a woman appeared and spoke to him, urging him to drink a large cup of wine. Xiang suspected she might be Xiao's concubine and hesitated, but she insisted, and he drank. Feeling uneasy, Xiang shouted for help. Xiao's younger brother, Xiao Kuo, heard the commotion and rushed in, asking what had happened. The woman then disappeared. Xiao Kuo entered and found the bed and bedding soaked with wine, which turned out to be the wine Xiao had left. The next day, Xiang asked around and learned that the official had once buried his daughter in that room. Xiang moved to another room, and he never encountered the woman again. In the eighth year of the Shaoxing era, Xiang took the exam in Nanjing and stayed at an inn in Lin'an. One night, he heard someone speaking to him all night as if in conversation. His fellow traveler asked him about it, and Xiang replied, "It was the same woman I saw in Wuzhou. But there was no harm." He died ten years later.

Jiangxin Temple Earthquake In the year 1146 CE, during the reign of Emperor Gaozong of Song, several dozen common people from Wenzhou went to Jiangxin Temple to attend a Buddhist recitation assembly. Someone who had come from outside said that the river water was extremely clear, not its usual color, and they all rushed out to see it. The monks were just sitting in meditation when they saw smoke and flames in the corridors. They were afraid and dared not move. After a short while, black mist gathered inside, and they could not see each other. Lightning and thunder illuminated the sky, and this lasted for two kē (units of time). Five of the spectators died in the mud, while the rest did not feel anything. A layperson was washing dishes in the kitchen when a spirit with an extremely tall and fearsome body appeared and led him out by the hand. As they were about to reach the door, another spirit arrived and said, "Don't make a mistake, don't make a mistake," and immediately let him go. It then went back into the kitchen and led another person out, who also fell outside. In total, six people died. All three accounts were told by Lin Xizai Hongzhao.

**Strange Tales of the Yi Family, Volume 58** The Elder Monk Zonghui The monk Zonghui had a long-standing career as a teacher and was renowned for his high moral character. He finally settled at the Western Rock in Nanjian. The temple had a large tea plantation, and Zonghui ordered the pruning of excess branches to ensure the tea plants would grow more vigorously in the future. He had no ulterior motives. However, a monk who was dissatisfied with his position in the temple went to Jianpu County and complained, saying that Zonghui was concerned about the implementation of the land measurement system and feared that the tea tax might increase. The county magistrate knew this was false and beat the monk, driving him away. The monk then appealed to the prefecture, but the prefect, who had also heard of Zonghui's reputation, did not believe his words and beat him again. The monk, unable to bear his anger, went to the office of the governor-general of the transportation system and claimed that all his complaints were true and that he had been unjustly treated by the county and prefect. He requested that the case be investigated in another prefecture. The governor-general sent the matter to Jianzhou, where officials were dispatched to arrest Zonghui. When the officials arrived and urged him to leave, Zonghui said, "Please give me one more day. I will repay you generously." The officials agreed to let him stay. Zonghui then told his disciples, "This monk has already been beaten twice. If I go to clear my name, he will be punished again. How could I bear to do that? If I do not speak up, the blame will fall on me, and I cannot accept that. It is better for me to leave." The monks thought he wanted to flee, and some began to pack their belongings, preparing to leave with him. The next day, Zonghui ordered the drum to be beaten and ascended the platform. After consoling and thanking the congregation, he recited a verse: "The imperial messenger has come to summon me, and I cannot delay. It is better to pass away now so that everything is clear. Since the beginning, there has been no birth song. I am happy today that I have achieved this." With that, he closed his eyes and passed away. This occurred in the 19th year of the Shaoxing era (1149 CE).

The Righteous Eagle In the sixteenth year of the Shaoxing era, Lin Xizai traveled from Wenzhou to Fuzhou to serve as a minor official in the local government. On his way, he passed through Zhi Jue Temple in Pingyang. There, he noticed that one corner of the hall lacked a ridge beast (鸱吻). He asked the monks about it. The monks replied that in the past, a pair of cranes had nested there. Recently, it was struck by lightning, and a very large snake skin was found. They found this strange and dared not repair it. The monks then explained that the temple had always had many cranes. On the large pine tree in front of the hall, three cranes shared one nest. Several years ago, a large snake climbed the tree and ate the chicks. The cranes could not defend themselves and all left. Soon, they led their kind to circle in the air, mourning and lingering until dusk, when they dispersed. The next day, they gathered again. Then, a strong eagle flew straight from the edge of the sky, directly into the nest. The snake had not yet left. The eagle struck it with its claws, the sound being like a crackling. After a short while, it flew up, then descended again, repeating this several times. The snake split into three or four pieces. The eagle did not eat it and left. Lin Xizai recited Du Fu's poem "Righteous Eagle" to them. Thus, the words of the poetic historian were verified. True and verified. These two events were told by Xizai.

In Taizhou's Huangyan County, there stands a tower before the Yue Hall of Dingguang Temple. A stork has built its nest on top of the tower. One day, a large and short snake appeared, devouring the stork's chicks. The stork mother wailed in anguish. She swiftly flew to the edge of the sea. Soon after, two hawks arrived, directly approaching the base of the tower. They seized the snake and flew away. This story is told by Chen Guan. Chen Guo Zuo, a man from Taizhou, was known as Chen Gong Fu. His father, Zheng, was a high-ranking official in the prefecture. After retiring, Zheng lived in the Huidi Alley of the city. At the time, Chen Guo Zuo was studying at the Imperial Academy. One day, a monk visited Zheng and pointed to the opposite gate of the Pujing Temple, saying, "Wait until this temple becomes a pond. The candidate will achieve the top rank." Zheng replied, "One temple so magnificent; if it unfortunately burns down, then it is possible. How could it become a pond? You know my son will never succeed; it is just a joke." The monk said, "Not more than a year. My words will certainly come true." Pujing Temple was low-lying, and every spring rain and the humidity of the plum season reached it, causing the water to flow and making it impassable. The temple had long suffered from this problem. They occasionally found vacant land behind the county granary and moved the temple there. The old site finally became a pond, in agreement with the monk's words. In the year Zhenghe, year of the Rabbit (1113 CE), Chen Guo Zuo became the top scholar in the Imperial Academy, the first to be granted an official robe, and later became a Deputy Minister of Rites.

Jin Mountain Mushroom** A monk named Juesheng from Zisheng Temple in Taizhou built a hermitage on Jin Mountain. One day, he went out early in the morning and saw a large python blocking the path. He ordered his servant to carry it away. On that day, while walking through a pine path, he saw several mushrooms that were fresh and lovely. He picked them and brought them back. While cooking them, they were not yet fully cooked when hundreds of snakes surrounded the pot, coiling and huddling. Juesheng was greatly frightened and quickly entered the room, sitting on a couch. Just as he was about to go to bed, the entire couch was filled with snakes, and he could not escape. The other monks in the room saw nothing. Juesheng died. **Xu Shuwei** Xu Shuwei, courtesy name Zhike, was from Zhenzhou. His family was always poor. In a dream, someone told him, "If you wish to pass the imperial examination, you must accumulate good deeds." Xu realized that his own efforts were insufficient and decided that only by engaging in medicine could he achieve this. He then focused on medical texts. After a long time, he saved countless lives. He had another dream where the previous person came and gave him a poem. The poem said, "Medicine has hidden benefits. In the quiet place of Chen and Lou, in the hall, calling out six, making it five." Upon waking, he wrote it down. In the year of Xining Renzi (1132 AD), he ranked sixth in the imperial examination. Due to the grace of the imperial favor, he received the same rank as the fifth place and was granted an official position. The person above him was Chen Zuyan, and the person below him was Lou Cai. The dream had already determined this. "Calling out six" refers to the meaning of the announcement.

**Chen Liangqi** Chen Liangqi enjoyed giving food. In the eleventh year of the Shaoxing era, his son Chen Guan became the magistrate of Wuyi in Wuzhou. Chen Liangqi accompanied him to take up his post. Once, they traveled together to the county, but Chen Guan forgot to bring food trays. That night, he dreamed that two old friends, Xia and Lü, came to him and said, "We have been waiting for you for several days, but we didn't know you were here." Upon waking, Chen Guan told about the dream. He then realized the reason and hurriedly provided food at the inn. The above four events were all recounted by Chen Guan. **Human Born with Turtles** One of my clan members enjoyed hunting. Whenever his enthusiasm was high, he would not stop even in the midst of severe cold or heat. In his old age, he fell ill, and three things grew on his back, hidden between the skin and flesh. After several days, heads and feet were all present, clearly three turtles. They could move, and if someone lured them with fish, their heads would suddenly appear as if wanting to eat. After a while, they would nibble on both sides, causing unbearable pain. He died after about a month. Five days after his death, his spirit possessed the wife of Zi Yue and said, "I because I enjoyed hunting, suffered a painful retribution in life. Today it is still not over. The underworld is sending someone to catch my hunting tools as evidence. Before it arrives, you can quickly burn the fishing nets and other things. Do not increase my sin." Yue did as he said and left. It was the seventh year of the Shaoxing era.

#### Huang Pingguo Huang Heng, courtesy name Pingguo, was from Pucheng, Jianzhou. In the tenth year of the Shaoxing era, he was promoted from the position of Proofreader in the Secretariat to Deputy Military Governor of Shaowu. He died before assuming office. Three years after his death, a villager who had died in Xuancheng on business was unknown to his family. His spirit returned and spoke to his family, saying, "I died on a certain day in Xuancheng due to illness. A servant accompanying me actually buried me. Due to the haste of the burial, I left behind one shoe. After entering the underworld, I met Huang Xingyuan (Huang Pingguo) who pitied me for walking barefoot and gave me a pair of shoes. He also sent someone to bring me back. That is why I am here." The family asked, "Where is Huang Gong now?" The spirit replied, "He is judging in a very important and powerful position in the underworld." The family was still holding back tears when the spirit suddenly departed. The next day, his son went to Xuancheng to retrieve the coffin and intended to cremate it. Upon opening the coffin for inspection, they found that one foot was indeed bare. #### Deputy Magistrate's Hall Pillar On the twenty-fifth day of the second month of the year Shaoxing Gishi, there was a heavy thunderstorm in Fuzhou. Deputy Magistrate Xue Ronggong got up before dawn and heard the sound of thunder very close. By morning, one pillar of the hall had been split into three pieces. The attached beams and rafters, along with the mud, all fell and shattered into soil resembling claw marks, imprinting on the writing desk and the disarrayed western veranda. At the time, they were about to welcome the new deputy magistrate, and items such as folding chairs and rain umbrellas were leaning against the side of the pillar. They expected it to be shaken, so they moved them to the wall. There was no significant impact. Previously, Xue's son had seen a green snake enter the base of the pillar. He tried to pull its tail but could not get it out. After the earthquake, everyone suspected that the creature was a dragon. Xue the Deputy Magistrate said this.

The God of the Leather Market Xi Dan, courtesy name Jinzhong, was from Henan. He served Emperor Huizong as a censor in the imperial court. Later, he governed two regions in Shu (Sichuan). In the sixth year of the Zhenghe era (1116 AD), he died in Chang'an (Xi'an). His son, Daguang, was transferred to serve in the capital after completing his mourning period. At that time, the temple of the God of the Leather Market was very famous for its miraculous effects. The people of the capital offered gold and silk day and night. Daguang once entered the temple and recognized his father's shoe from the time of his burial. He was deeply moved and saddened. After returning home, he dreamed that his father said to him, "After I died, I became a god. I now hold great power and influence, no less than when I was alive and served as a general. I know you are struggling with financial difficulties. Tomorrow, I will give you five hundred thousand coins." Daguang woke up in a state of shock. He heard a very urgent knocking at the door. He went out to see and found several soldiers pulling a cart. On top of the cart stood a small yellow banner, which read, "The God of the Leather Market leaves a message for Xie Xianggong with three hundred strings of cash." They left the cart on the ground and departed. At that moment, it was dark and he could not see clearly, so he remained skeptical. When it became light, he saw that it was real copper coins. Daguang then felt very confident and believed that he would surely be promoted to a high position. In the early years of the Shaoxing era, he served as a vice-premier. Later, he served as a university scholar and governor of Sichuan. The people of Sichuan all called him Xie Xianggong. Soon after, he observed mourning for his mother, the Empress Dowager Fu Guo, and died before the mourning period was over. Yan Kang explained this through his son Qi.

The Daughter of Jiang, the Deputy Magistrate Qian Fu, courtesy name He Fu, was the Deputy Magistrate of Taizhou in the thirteenth year of the Shaoxing era. He went to Ninghai County to adjudicate cases. On the twenty-sixth day of July, he rested at the Miaoxiang Temple, leaning on the table and casually writing. Suddenly, someone pulled his writing brush. When he turned back, he saw nothing. That night, after waking up from sleep, he felt as if there was something in front of his bed. He called the attendant to light a lamp and made vows, questioning the presence. Then, it disappeared. The next night, it came again, standing in the same place. Fu asked it, "If indeed you are a ghost, can you hit the screen?" Before his words were finished, it struck the screen from top to bottom several dozen times. Fu was greatly frightened and ordered to light two torches in front. Then, a large flying moth flew into the lamp and extinguished it. The object sat on the bed and stepped on it, facing away and remaining silent. Upon closer inspection, it was a woman wearing a round hat, a light blue shirt, and a bright yellow skirt, looking extremely short and small. She remained motionless for a long time. Fu silently recited the Tianpeng incantation several times, immediately opened the curtain and left. The night guards repeatedly shouted in alarm, asking the reason. One said, "There was a woman who came out from inside, walking very quickly, stepping on people's faces as she passed. She described her clothes, which were the same as those seen before." Fu thought it had gone, and since it was late and he had no time to move, he returned to bed. He dreamed that the previous person directly climbed onto the bed, resting on his left shoulder. She felt cold like ice and stone, saying herself that she was the daughter of Deputy Magistrate Jiang, who died here due to childbirth and forced Fu to join. Fu strongly resisted, then woke up. The next day, he asked Guo Yuanzhang, who was staying in the temple, about the details. Guo said that the account matched exactly what Fu had seen. The place where the bed was set up was indeed the place of her death. Fu recounted the story.

Ye Ruogu Ye Ruogu, a Gentleman of the Rank of Chengxin, was from Hongzhou. He served as an official overseeing the minting bureau, and his official residence was in Qianzhou. Ye did not bring his family with him and lived alone in the minting bureau's office. On the sixteenth day of the first month in the year 1154 CE, just before dusk, a woman knocked on the door and entered. She had a leisurely and beautiful demeanor. She spoke to Ye first, and he initially thought she had wandered in by mistake. He hesitated to respond. He fell asleep without realizing it, and the woman also fell asleep. Ye attempted to engage her in conversation, and she initially reacted with modesty and avoidance. They then engaged in intimate relations. She was clearly a virgin. They enjoyed themselves for a prolonged period. An old woman entered from outside, carrying a money box. She sat on a cross-legged stool, in a relaxed and informal posture, as if no one was present. She approached the bed and pulled back the curtain, placing her hands on the floor and saying, "You two are good." Ye suspected that the old woman was related to the woman and was very afraid. The woman shook her head and covered Ye's mouth, instructing him to remain silent. The old woman then left. The woman left after midnight. She visited regularly, either daily or every other day, and always stayed for a short time. Ye still assumed she was from a neighboring house. They continued to meet for several months. Gradually, Ye became increasingly weak and emaciated. He then fell ill and became very weak. He moved to another place to seek medical treatment, and she no longer visited. When they first met, she was wearing a light green shirt and dark red trousers. Over a long period, she did not change her clothes, but they always appeared new. This was also unusual. Ye Ruogu recounted the story.

**刘氏冤报** High Junzhi was from Fuzhou. He passed the imperial examination and became a Jinshi, but he was treated as a subordinate husband by the Tan family. He had a son who grew up, and he married a woman from the same prefecture as his wife, the Liu family. They had two sons and a daughter, but one of the sons died. High Junzhi served as a Cao Sanlang (a low-ranking official) and also died. His eldest grandson was mentally disabled, and his second grandson was young. Only the Tan family and the Liu family lived together. The Liu woman was still strong but lost her proper conduct as a wife. She engaged in promiscuous sexual acts with a monk at home. When the grandmother saw this, she scolded her. The Liu woman was angry and fearful. When the grandmother fell ill and was not being cared for, she hoped she would die. She set a trap with poison to kill the grandmother's two maids, but before they died, she forcibly gathered and burned their bodies. A few months later, the Liu woman fell ill. Every day, she called out the names of the maids she had killed. She said, "My jaw is extremely painful. Do not pull my hair." She also said, "I have been beaten enough. Please be a little kinder to me." Her family asked her about it, and she said, "My grandmother and the two maids are beating me." She died after ten days. Her son obtained an official position through the favor of his grandfather's retirement, but he also failed to establish himself. Now the family is destitute. This story was told by High Junzhi's nephew, Jieqing.

A Citizen of Jiangyin Lin Shuming stated that in the sixth year of the Shaoxing era (1136 AD), he was residing in Jiangyin. At that time, the price of mulberry leaves in the Huai region had risen sharply. There was a villager living on an island in the river near Rugao County in Taizhou. He raised silkworms in dozens of reeling frames. He discussed with his wife and children: "I have spent a lot of money on silkworm farming in recent years, but the profit is not enough to cover the cost, and it consumes a lot of time. It would be better to get rid of them all. I will load the mulberry leaves and sell them in Rugao. It would take no more than three days of labor, and I would gain substantial profit with no harm." His wife and children agreed. So, he used boiling water to kill the silkworms, and all of them died. He buried them under the mulberry trees and gathered all the leaves, loading them onto a boat heading north. On the way halfway, a carp jumped into the boat. The man took it, cut open its belly, and filled it with salt. Soon, they reached the shore. The customs officer boarded the boat to inspect the taxable goods. He opened the leaves and saw that they were dead. The man went to look at them and found that it was his own son. He was shocked and cried. The officer thought he had killed someone and detained him. He interrogated the other people on the boat, but none of them knew. He asked why he had come, and the man explained the whole story. The county sent officials to Jiangyin to investigate. They went to his home, but the door was closed. They broke through the wall to enter and found no one inside. They opened the burial site to examine it and found that it was his wife. Her body was already decayed. This further proved that he had killed his wife and children and fled, but he could not prove his innocence. The officials dared not make a judgment, and he died in prison. This incident is similar to the story of Wang Gongzhi recorded in *Sanzhui Xiaodu*.

Snake and Dog Retribution It is said that dogs can restrain snakes. Whenever a dog encounters a snake, it will circle, bark, and leap around it, much like a witch or shaman performing the *yu bu* ritual. If people mistakenly chase the dog, they will be bitten by the snake. One night, a dog belonging to Lin Mingfu barked. With the light of a candle, they saw a coiled snake. The dog circled and barked several dozen times. The snake died, but its body was not harmed. It must be due to a certain skill to restrain it. Lin Hongzhao said that at the Zifu Temple on Daoyuan Mountain in Pingyang County, Wenzhou, there was a dog named Hua Zi. This dog was skilled in controlling snakes, regardless of their size. Any snake that encountered it would die. In total, it killed hundreds of snakes. One day, a large python appeared in the kitchen. Those who saw it ran away, and the monk urgently called Hua Zi to bite it. Before the dog could do anything, the snake quickly approached and bit its jaw. The dog howled and writhed, and shortly died on the steps. The snake also disappeared. Is it not the work of its ghost? Retribution among creatures is indeed like this. Jiang Ningzu Jiang Ningzu, the son of Dazhi Jian, was forty years old and held the rank of Caoqing Lang. He should have been promoted to Daifu but refused to accept it. His parents pressured him, and reluctantly, he listed himself for the promotion. After accepting the appointment, he immediately requested retirement. From then on, he no longer wore court attire and often wore a wooden *shù* cloth Daoist robe. He requested from (the missing text suggests incomplete information).

**Li's Wet Nurse** **The Main Text is Missing** **Shanwen** In Jinxi County, Fuzhou, there was a very powerful and revered temple. People who visited the temple offered gold and silk, and there was no day that went by without offerings. The accumulated wealth reached two thousand strings of cash. A nobleman named Shanwen passed by the temple and was envious of its wealth. He burned incense and prayed, saying, "Reduce what is surplus and make up for what is lacking. Humans and gods are one in essence. I am extremely poor. I wish the deity would lend me twenty thousand strings of cash. If not, I will report this to the authorities and completely record all the possessions and burn the temple. What can you do to me?" After praying, he immediately called the temple priest to give him the money. The priest had no words to refuse, but said, "If the deity agrees, then it is possible." Shanwen took a pair of divination sticks and threw them, and they consecutively showed auspicious signs. He bowed twice in gratitude and carried the money out. This continued for ten years. One night, he dreamed that the deity came and said, "The money you borrowed in the past is now due. In your dream, you agreed to repay it in strings of cash. The deity said, 'This is just a nominal amount.' Shanwen wanted to double the amount, but the deity still refused. Shanwen was at a loss and pleaded, saying, "I acted rashly at the time. Now I truly have nothing to repay. I hope the deity will show mercy and forgive me." The deity thought deeply for a long time and said, "If you have no money to return, you can recite the Diamond Sutra. Each volume can be counted as one thousand. There is nothing else to do." Upon waking, he was afraid and immediately fasted and abstained from worldly affairs. He took the Diamond Sutra and recited it, completing two hundred volumes in three days. He silently prayed in gratitude. After that, he no longer dreamed. This story was told by Chen Yinbo Ming.

**Lin the County尉** At the beginning of the Shaoxing era, Lin Diguang of Putian served as a county尉 in Jiangxi. Upon completing his term, he was rewarded for capturing bandits and was transferred to an official post in the capital, but had not yet been assigned to a new position. At that time, Lin’an was plagued by frequent fires, and scholars and officials who resided in their mansions always carried with them imperial edicts and related documents whenever they went out. Lin was especially cautious and fearful, placing the edict in his sleeve and constantly checking on it. At first, it never fell or was lost. But every time he returned, it was missing. He then offered a reward of thirty thousand coins for its return, and within a day, someone would always find it and bring it back. This happened four times in total, yet Lin could not determine the cause. One night, he stayed alone in his room, and often heard people speaking outside. He found this strange, but dared not ask. One evening, there was a loud debate; after a while, it grew quiet. The next morning, the door was not opened. The innkeeper gathered those who lived in the same residence and broke through the wall to enter. They found Lin already lying on the bed, with a piece of a pair of scissors beside him. It was clear that he had used it to stab himself. When Lin first captured a thief, two men were somewhat suspicious. Lin desired the reward and, in order to claim it, fabricated evidence and had them executed. Thus, he received this retribution.

The Sixthty-Third Case of Yijian Jia Zhi The Chancellor Shi's Dream of Receiving Gifts When Chancellor Shi passed the imperial examination, he was just forty years old. Before he gained fame, he was extremely poor. Once on New Year's Eve, he offered sacrifices to his ancestors with whatever resources he had. Afterward, he went to bed and had a dream in which he seemed to be in the capital city. Two high-ranking officials arrived on horseback and summoned him urgently. He followed them into the courtyard of a large hall, where the king was seated. Attendants in gold and purple robes stood around, and the court was grand and splendid. The officials led him to perform a formal greeting, kneeling and bowing, which resembled the ceremonies of the imperial court. On both sides of the hall, tables were set up, adorned with gold and silver vessels arranged in a dazzling display. Shortly after, someone from the hall announced, "By the emperor's command, bestow four hundred and seventy gold and silver vessels upon Chancellor Shi." Chancellor Shi was in a hurry and shocked, too afraid to accept. Two officials supported him to bow, and he knelt to thank and left. The officials then escorted him back.

Crossing a great river and a high bridge, he just began to ascend the steps when he lost his footing and fell into the water, frightening him awake. On the day of the New Year, he told this to his wife, Be. She laughed and said, "Last night was the New Year's Eve, a time of great importance in our culture. Our family still had no cups of wine or slices of meat, and I felt we had wasted the year. How could there be such wealth as you described in your dream? It must have been a trick by evil spirits." The historian also smiled at this interpretation. Later, he was promoted to the rank of 28th in the imperial examination in the year 1155. After more than 12 years, he began serving as an official of the Imperial Academy. By the year 1169, he was appointed from the position of Secretary to the position of Registrar and served as a lecturer to Prince Jian. Only three years later, he encountered the grace of the dragon soaring in the sky, and thus ascended to the position of minister. He became extremely wealthy and noble, holding this high rank for more than 30 years. Calculating the rewards and grants he received before and after, they exactly matched the number 470 from his dream. What was bestowed upon him at that time far surpassed his peers. It was certainly not accidental; the gods know it.

Yu Yilang, the Releaser of Life Yu Yilang was a man from Jingnan, though he was a commoner from the marketplace, he was particularly fond of releasing animals and engaged in making or repairing statues of deities and Buddhas. In the fifth month of the third year of the Shaoxi era, he fell gravely ill and was near death. Two ghost officials pulled him out and led him through desolate wilderness until they came to a river. Many people were crossing the water to get across, but he alone was able to cross via a bridge to the other side. There were other ghost officials leading countless birds and beasts, all of which came to greet him. After a while, he reached the next road and encountered over a thousand monks and a gatehouse. The officials led him inside, and he saw ten people sitting in a row on the dais, wearing the attire of kings. They asked what place he was in, and they replied, "We are the Ten Kings of the Underworld." Two judges stood by, holding scrolls of records. Soon, they took him down to the hall and examined his deeds in life. The king asked about his good deeds that could lead to his release. The judge said, "This person has one more decade left according to his destiny, and he has also redeemed many lives of animals. Over three thousand have already been reborn as humans. Thus, he should be granted two more decades of life." The king then decreed that Yu's original lifespan was only sixty-three years, but since he had added two decades, he is now to be escorted back by a boy today. Soon, two boys in green clothes led him along a path of green grass to a wall with a gap, and they pushed him over the gap. He suddenly came back to life. There were several lines of red writing on his left palm, which he could not read. These were likely the words of the judgment.

Li Si Li Ziye had six sons. The eldest was named Mi Xing, followed by Mi Lun and Mi Da. All three had participated in the local examinations but had not passed. Li Ziye proposed to change their names, as he needed to report to the Ministry of Rites. They were then allowed to do so. First, he changed the name of the fourth son, Mi Yuan, to Zhenglu. Zhenglu was sixteen years old and entered the National University. In a dream, a person told him, "Mr. Li, you have already passed the examination." He then took out a piece of paper about two inches wide, which had the characters "Mi Xun" written on it, showing it to him. Li said, "My old name was Mi Yuan. Now it's Zhenglu. Is this me?" The person replied, "This is truly your son. What reason do you have to doubt?" After arguing for a long time, he finally woke up. He was quite pleased but feared his father's strictness and did not dare to tell him. He then told his mother, Lady Liu. She informed Li Ziye, and he then changed his name to Mi Xun, with "Si" as his courtesy name. A few years later, his brother, Si Ju, who was a Shangshu (Minister), was in charge of the county of Yuanju in Caozhou. Li Ziye retired from his position as the governor of Yan and went to live with his son. After finishing the upper house examination, Mi Xun also returned to serve his father. A person who reported the list of successful candidates arrived first and said that Mi Xun had already topped the list. He looked for the list but couldn't find it. However, he took out a piece of paper from his pocket, which had the characters "Li Mi Xun" written on it. He was unsure and didn't believe it at first. Mi Xun said, "This is what I dreamed of five years ago. Could it be true today?" The width of the paper and the size of the characters were exactly the same, but in the dream, there was no family name. It must be true. And indeed, it was true. It was the year Dazheng Wuzi. This is also according to Su Cuizhong.

Hú Zǐwén In the Fudong Mountain Temple of Changshu County, Suzhou, there stands a temple dedicated to the God of Mount Tai, its appearance being very solemn. One day, a scholar named Hú Zǐwén, under the influence of alcohol, entered the temple. He saw the two judges, one representing good and the other evil, facing each other. In a playful mood, he pulled the pen of the evil one. His companion thought it inappropriate and tried to stop him, but he returned the pen. On his way back to the boat, a messenger soon arrived and said that the judges had ordered him to be taken away. By then, Zǐwén had sobered up and remembered the drunken incident, which filled him with great fear. Along the way, he silently recited the Diamond Sutra. Upon arriving at the temple, the two judges sat facing each other. The one facing west was very angry and shouted, "You are a scholar; you should know when to stay and when to leave. How dare you insult me?" Zǐwén replied, "I was driven mad by strong drink and was completely unaware. I beg for a small chance to return home." The judge did not respond. Zǐwén continued to silently recite the sutra until the third chapter. Both judges stood up. After another two chapters, they raised their hands and bowed their heads. The one facing east explained, "This scholar made a mistake due to drunkenness. Considering his circumstances, it seems he can be forgiven." The angry judge said, "It's because the other judge is too lenient, making others dare to act like this." Zǐwén knelt and said, "I can recite the Diamond Sutra. If you grant me another life, I will recite seven scrolls of the sutra to repay you." The angry judge said, "If so, you should also receive a minor punishment." He poked Zǐwén's back with the pen he held and said, "Go." Zǐwén felt his whole body turn cold and then woke up. A boil grew where he was poked, and the pain was unbearable. It took a hundred days to heal. From then on, he recited the sutra seven times a day, even if very busy, he did not stop. This was told by Yè Píngfǔ.

The Exorcism of the Monkey Spirit by Elder Zongyan In the Nengren Temple of Yongfu County, Fuzhou, there was a guardian spirit of the mountains and forests. It had captured a live macaque monkey, molded it with mud, and named it the Monkey King. Over time, the spirit became a nuisance to the local residents. The temple was located at the border of four prefectures: Fuquan, Nanjian, Xinghua, and others. The villagers feared hearing the name of the spirit. Those who encountered it initially suffered from severe chills and fever, then gradually became mad and refused to eat. They would climb fences and trees, throw themselves to the ground, and often died. Children were especially harmed by this spirit. As a result, the number of worshippers increased, and the sacrificial blood never dried up in a single day. When the worship did not heal, the villagers would call shamans at night to the temple. The shamans would beat gongs and blow horns, calling it "capture." The monks of the temple, upon hearing this, would also strike the bell and drum in response, claiming to assist the god in battle. Superstitious practices became more severe each day, and no one changed them. Elder Zongyan, upon hearing of this, sighed and said, "You can be called extremely suffering. Those who killed you have already received retribution, but your wanton behavior has affected ordinary people. Your accumulated karma has become deeper; when can you escape?"

To chant the Sanskrit Great Compassion Mantra for the sake of salvation. That night, he sat alone. He saw a woman with a human body and monkey feet, her left armpit stained with blood. Beside her was a small monkey. A chain around her waist restrained both her hands. She hugged a girl and bowed twice in front of him. She said, "I am the disciple of the monkey king. I have long suffered from deep injustice. Now, thanks to the power of the Dharma, I have achieved liberation and been reborn in heaven. Therefore, I come to express my gratitude. I also request the release of the small monkey's chain." He followed her request and said the verse: "The monkey king has long endured deep suffering. Through the power of the Dharma, he has been reborn in heaven. One must believe that the original nature of the self is Buddha. Spiritual light shines everywhere without boundaries." After hearing the verse, she bowed again and settled down. The next day, he opened the hall and applied three layers of locks. It was because a few years ago, a sorcerer had shot him in the left armpit, and therefore, he often kept it closed. The monkey carried the small girl as seen, and he crushed it. He also crushed several dozen of their followers, all of whom were the work of crows, hawks, owls, and other such creatures. He threw them into the stream, and the strange occurrences ceased.

The Lanterns of the Two Judicial Offices in Fuzhou The left and right judicial offices in Fuzhou. Every year during the Lantern Festival, they must clear the prisons and hold a ritual. They then display large numbers of lanterns to compete in extravagance and splendor, making it the most splendid place in the entire prefecture. In the past, everything was arranged by Buddhist monasteries. During the year 1150, when Zhang Gong Yuan Dao, a high-ranking official, was the governor, he ordered that the monks should not be disturbed. The prison officials had no idea what to do and were ashamed to fall short of previous years. They all worked hard to do it. The night before, the head of the left judicial office, Chen Guan, dreamed of a red-robed official wearing a flat-topped hat bowing in the courtyard. The official said, "The money for the ritual has already been transferred from the right office." The next morning, a messenger arrived with a letter and ten thousand coins. Chen Guan took it and read it. It was a letter from the magistrate of Minqing to help the right office. As he was about to return it, the officials said, "Tonight's ritual. We are short of funds. What harm would it do to keep it?" Chen Guan also understood his dream. He replied to the magistrate's letter himself and took the money. Outside the ritual banquet, there was no extra expense. This was an error that happened at one time, but in the unseen realm, it was already predetermined. Chen Guan recounted this story.

The Elder of Jinyang County Zhou Gongcai, courtesy name Zimei, was from Wenzhou. In the early years of the Zhenghe era, he served as the county magistrate of Jinyang County in the prefecture of Jiangzhou. On an official mission to Jin Prefecture, he passed through Gu Yeshan and paid respects at the temple of the True Person. As he was descending the mountain, he saw a man dressed in rough clothing with a topknot sitting by the roadside. The man glanced at Zhou and said, "Your position is very good, but you will only be truly happy after you turn sixty." Zhou was then in his thirties. Moreover, he shared the same surname as the governor of Jiangzhou, who had written several recommendation letters for him, leading Zhou to expect a promotion in his next position. Upon hearing what the man said, Zhou became very angry and continued to speak, becoming even more furious. He took out his sword and wanted to strike the man. Suddenly, the man leaped up to the top of a tree and then jumped back down into a hole at the base of the tree. Zhou raised his sword to strike the tree, and the man called out, "I am an azure sheep. I spoke truly with you. Why treat me so harshly?" Zhou left, and as it was getting late, he stopped at an inn at the foot of the mountain. A Taoist priest was already there.

With one crane and a servant iron ghost accompanying himself, the Taoist bowed to Zhou and said, "The weather is slightly cold. Can you drink a cup?" The wine was too cold to drink. The Taoist drew the character for "fire" on the table and placed the cup on it. In a moment, it became warm. After drinking, he held the remaining drops and sprayed them on the wall, then sprayed them on Zhou's face, saying, "To cleanse you of misfortune. You will definitely see a strange thing today." Zhou told him about the previous events, and the Taoist said, "Yes, yes. But it is not surprising. Do you know this? This is exactly what I encountered before with Lü Dongbin and the old tree spirits." He also took some dried fish and ate together. At the time when the sun was setting, the light illuminated the dried fish on the tray, and they all turned into five colors. He laughed and said, "A glimpse of Zhang Hua's skills." Until night when they went to sleep, and at dawn they set out. The Taoist had already risen and said, "I want to have a long talk with you, but the journey is very urgent. What shall we do?" That day, they entered the county boundary, and in the evening, they did not find an inn to stay in.

Zhou rented a room from a commoner's home. Suddenly, the iron ghost appeared. He said, "The gentleman from yesterday did not make a proper reception, so I will come to you now. Let me bring wine and fruits first." Zhou asked, "Where is the gentleman?" The iron ghost replied, "He has arrived." Zhou went out to greet him. From afar, one saw the Taoist riding a crane, flying several feet above the ground. After he arrived, the commoner led his wife, children, and others to prostrate themselves. The Taoist also comforted and greeted them. He asked, "Are all of your family well?" The commoner knelt and replied, "The county magistrate has arrived. I was just worried about having no companion, and the gentleman happened to come. My home has wheat flour, and I just obtained some donkey meat. How about making a simple meal as a meal?" The Taoist nodded. The commoner bowed and sat facing east, and Zhou was the guest. After the meal, they walked to the wall and drank together. Zhou repeatedly filled his cup and became quite drunk, not noticing and falling asleep sitting. When he woke up, the iron ghost was beside him. He said, "The gentleman could not wait and has already left."

A very large peach was presented. He said, "Master, if you eat this peach, you will remain healthy for life. We will meet again after eighty years at Luofu Mountain." Zhou politely declined and offered 200 coins as a gift. Laughing, he replied, "What use do I have for it?" He then bowed formally and left, and in an instant, he had vanished. The people said, "He is the ancient elder from Jiaoxian. Now he is a terrestrial immortal. He is occasionally seen by travelers. Those who recognize him live over a hundred years. I first saw him when I was young. Now I am eighty years old." Zhou then began to regret, and indeed, he suffered setbacks for over twenty years. Finally, he obtained a position in the capital and later became the supervisor of the imperial message bureau. In the sixteenth year of the Shaoxing era, he fell ill after paying respects on the first day of the new year. He called upon a local man named Lin Lianggong for a meal and spoke in detail about his life experiences, including this incident. Three days later, he died. He lived to be sixty-eight years old. The prophecy of meeting again at Luofu Mountain remains unclear. Lin Lianggong said so.

Huang Zifang Huang Cong, courtesy name Zifang, was from Putian. In the early years of the Xuanhe era, he served as the county magistrate of Minqing in Fuzhou. He usually ate a vegetarian diet, but bought four ounces of meat daily to provide for his mother. He was upright and strict, not afraid of powerful people. At the time, the Daoist canon was being compiled, and the county magistrate Huang Mianzhong, a high-ranking official, ordered the twelve counties to collect donations from the people. Huang Cong alone refused to comply. After hearing that other counties had collected several million, he went to the county himself and paid with his own salary for four months. Huang Mianzhong was dissatisfied but, recognizing his integrity, dared not question him. An imperial censor sent to investigate local conditions frequently tried to extort him for personal gain, but he always refused to answer. The censor often harbored resentment and sought revenge. When Huang Cong was summoned to the capital to report on affairs, he would use exaggeratedly evil words to slander Huang Cong whenever he met officials. Once, when he entered the court to serve Emperor Huizong, the emperor asked him if he knew of any virtuous county magistrates in Fujian. The official, taken aback, could not answer. He only remembered the name of Huang Cong and praised him highly. That very day, an imperial edict was issued promoting Huang Cong to a prefectural governor in Zhangzhou. After the official left, he was filled with regret and shame, realizing that the reward for a virtuous person can turn misfortune into good fortune. This is according to Liu Tunan's account.

Zhang Qianzhong, the Seal Script Master Zhang You, courtesy name Qianzhong, was a Taoist priest from Wujiang. He was renowned throughout the world for his seal script calligraphy. He was a quiet, reclusive man who loved antiquity. If a character was not found in ancient texts, he would omit it or leave it blank. During the Xuanhe era, he was already over seventy years old. Lin Yanzhen, a Minister of the Secretariat, lost his mother, Lady of the State of Wei, and returned her body for burial in Lake Hu. He planned to inscribe a burial inscription and asked Zhang Qianzhong to write the title. Zhang Qianzhong wrote the character "魏" with a specific stroke order. Lin Yanzhen found it unfamiliar and ordered it to be changed. Zhang Qianzhong refused. Despite his displeasure, Lin Yanzhen respected Zhang Qianzhong's skill and tried to persuade him with great effort, offering a generous reward. Zhang Qianzhong firmly stated that the modern form of "魏" was not part of his method and that Lin Yanzhen should return it. He absolutely would not change it. Realizing he could not force Zhang Qianzhong, Lin Yanzhen stopped the request. From then on, people regarded Zhang Qianzhong as even more virtuous. My uncle on my mother's side, Shen Zuren, was a local official in Guian and was friends with Zhang Qianzhong. He was intimidated by Zhang Qianzhong and did not dare to ask for his calligraphy. One day, while drunk, he rushed to Zhang Qianzhong's door and asked for a piece of silk. Zhang Qianzhong wrote several large characters, which were particularly high and ancient in style and lovable. These characters are still treasured today. Zhang Qianzhong also wrote a work called "复古编" (Restoration Compilation) that is widely circulated.

Phoenix Pool Mountain Phoenix Pool Mountain is located fifteen li east of Min County, Fuzhou. On the mountain, there is a pool that never dries up in winter or summer. Folklore has it that at the end of the Tang Dynasty, a woodcutter once saw a group of colorful birds bathing there, and thus the name was given. To the south of the mountain lies Gu Mountain, on the slopes of which stands the Yongquan Temple. Phoenix Pool Mountain is subordinate to it. During the Xining era, Yuan Zhang Jian Gong Jiang, a high-ranking official, came to govern. He visited the Yongquan Temple on Gu Mountain. The monks were disturbed by his frequent visits and transferred the name to the Beishan Bao Ci Temple. The abbot, who was quite clever, happened to please Yuan Gong's intentions. He carved a phoenix out of wood, placed it on a small pond, and made it appear to spit water with its beak. When Yuan Gong arrived, he was greatly pleased and composed a poem. Several years later, he participated in major policies, and the events at Phoenix Pool became a foretoken. Later, Wen Zuo Cheng Yi, another high-ranking official, also came to govern. He too enjoyed this excursion and composed a poem in response to Yuan Gong's. Soon after, he also reached the two places. However, it was not the true Phoenix Pool Mountain. Yet, such a good omen was not by chance.

Chen Zhu, also known as Menghe. A native of Putian. In the early years of the Chongning era, he passed the imperial examinations. He served as the magistrate of Gutian in Fuzhou. He was infatuated with the county courtesan Zhou. Zhou was skilled in poetry and wrote a quatrain for Chen Zhu: "Menghe, the waning moon reaches the western tower. The moon passes the western tower, and the dream is already confused. A call brings heartbreak, and the place of sorrow is where the oriole sings on the fallen flowers." The first line plays on Chen Zhu's name. Another poem, "Spring Sunny Day," reads: "Suddenly, a swallow flies past whose house? Suddenly, the fragrance comes from where the flowers are? In the deep courtyard, the long day is idle, and a bottle of spring water is brewed for tea." Later, they tied a red ribbon symbolizing marital harmony and committed suicide in the Southern Mountain Bliss Temple. Their followers rushed in to save them, but they survived. However, the affair was eventually exposed, and Chen Zhu lost his official position and left. Zhou was still alive at the beginning of the Shaoxing era, but by then she was old and unattractive, and her social status and reputation had declined significantly.
