Wily officials engage in corruption. An old clerk from Fuzhou named Xia Hua, who governed himself during his time as an official, obtained an official position due to years of service during the Zhenghe era. He served for a long time, approximately 96 years. He said that he had encountered many officials, and they were all deceived by their associates. Only two people could not be deceived: one was Cheng Gongbi, and the other was Luo Chou. Luo was fond of learning. Whenever he read, he would always study the meaning. If he had any insight, he would be delighted and let out a long sigh. If he did not understand, he would scratch his head and hesitate. Officials would wait for his long sigh and immediately bring in documents. Even if they had ulterior motives, he would not inquire. If he scratched his head, even minor deceptions were all uncovered. Therefore, they could deceive him. He liked reading, but he was still deceived by us. How much more so others? The above three matters, all said by the local scholar Zheng Dongqing.

Zhou Shiqing Zhou Shiqing was a native of Pucheng, Jianzhou. In the early years of the Yuanyou era, he traveled to the capital to take the provincial examination. On his way, he encountered a hermit who spoke to him, and he immediately returned home with his wife and children to practice alchemy in Youguo Mountain. His reputation spread widely, and no scholar or official passing by the mountain failed to visit him. Lu Jifu, who had moved from Jian'an to Xuanchou, suffered from a foot ailment that prevented him from walking and came to visit Zhou. Zhou asked Lu to stretch his feet forward to breathe air, and had someone fan him with a fan. After a while, the soles of his feet became hot, and the heat penetrated to the heart. After a long time, the pain subsided. Zhou spent twenty years in the mountain. The elixir was almost complete when, one night, a great wind and thunderstorm occurred, and the thunderclaps were very loud. In the morning, he looked at the alchemy furnace, and the elixir was already lost. Zhou did not expect this and then went out of his body to search for it. He told his wife, "I will go briefly and return in seven days. I am not dead. Do not burn me." His wife followed his instructions.

Zhou Pingsheng was on friendly terms with a monk. The monk also had a hermitage on another mountain. Upon hearing of Zhou's death, the monk came to mourn him and strongly advised Zhou's wife, saying, "One who practices the Way regards the body as worthless. Since he has departed, how can one grieve?" The wife believed the monk's words and wept as she burned Zhou's remains. The next day, Zhou returned, but he had no physical form. In the air, he rebuked his wife angrily and then left. On another day, the monk came again, and the wife told him what had happened. The monk said, "I just heard of his passing and came. I did not visit before." Then he realized that the previous visit was a deception by a demon. Later, Zhou's family placed an image of Zhou in the monk's hermitage, and a monk named Xing performed daily rituals to worship it. Every day, four coins would mysteriously appear on the ground, and a jar of vinegar was left behind, which has remained unspoiled to this day. People often take the vinegar, but it has never run out. A local person named Liu Xiangyun commented, "The Fruit Mountain is quite shallow and narrow, with a desolate and barren appearance. It is not a place where immortals would reside." Liu Xiangyun's words.

**Chapter Seventy-Seven of *Yijian Ji*:** **Jiang the *Yuanwai*** There was a gentleman of means named Jiang from Dinghai County, Mingzhou, who was known for his generosity and emphasis on righteousness. Whenever he heard that his nephews or younger relatives were unworthy and had sold their land and property, he would necessarily buy it from them at the same price. After a long time, when he judged that they could no longer support themselves, he would return the property to them without taking any money. They would then sell it again, and he would buy it again and return it once more. This cycle occurred as many as four or five times. Once, Jiang set out to cross the sea in order to travel to the prefectural capital. He went to the stern of the boat to relieve himself when he was struck by a sudden gust of wind and fell into the water. The boatmen tried to pull him back by his clothes, but he could not be controlled. The boat moved as if flying. While he was shouting for help, he saw a person slowly standing on the water, who followed the wind to the boat’s location. Looking at him, they saw it was Jiang himself. They quickly pulled him aboard and asked how he had survived. He replied: "When I was drowning, I felt something like a reed beneath my feet. Just as the wind blew the reed toward me, it carried me to the boat." People believed this was a reward for his accumulated virtue. This story was told by Li Yuguangzhu.

Li Shaoyu, who returned to the position of Censor-in-Chief, was from Jiankang. His residence was by the Qinhuai River. At the age of two, because the family was paying respects and boarding the boat, the wet nurse held the child carelessly and accidentally dropped him into the water. The water was too swift to search for him, and the entire boat mourned loudly. By the next day, a fishing boat heard the cries. They asked about the cause and immediately took a child from the boat and returned him. It was Li Shaoyu. The person said, "Last night, I saw several people on the shore near the fire. When I went to look, I saw an infant lying on the ground, surrounded by fire on all sides. I thought the shadows or spirits had taken him, so I picked him up." This account was told by Lin Lianggong. On the twenty-first day of the third month of the sixth year of the Shaoxing era, there was a monk named Fadao from Changzhou in Pingjiang who fell ill and entered Yanshou Hall. Suddenly, he transformed into a hungry ghost. His head and eyes were extremely large, his neck was narrow, and his throat was green. He spat out fierce fire. When people gave him food, he shouted, "Iron balls! They are not edible." This continued for seven days. The magistrate of Changzhou invited a Fashi to save him. The Fashi said, "You must have hidden sins from your past life. Speak them quickly. Buddhism allows for repentance. I will recite spells to cleanse you." After a long time, the sick monk finally spoke, saying, "In the past, I served as a steward at Huiri Temple on Mount Lu, stealing vegetables from the temple to exchange for one catty of wine each day. Later, I became a deputy abbot at Nengren Temple in Jiangzhou, using surplus rice to buy wine. These are my two sins." The Fashi said, "Since you know your faults, I will save you." He then pulled open the monk's mouth and poured holy water into it. The monk fell asleep and woke up the next morning, already asking for soup and porridge. He gradually began to eat and recovered after several days. This account was told by a monk named Rizhi, who witnessed it at Tiger Hill Temple.

In the tenth month of the second year of the Shaoxing era (1132), the monk Rìzhì was dispatched to the western village of Huangyan County in Taizhou. He lodged temporarily in a mountain temple. The next day, the temple monks offered a meal. Two elderly villagers named Zhang and Chen came to request the head monk to give a Buddhist precept. Zhang said, "My granddaughter Fó'ér, who is fifteen years old, died suddenly last night. At dawn, we were about to prepare her body for burial. Her grandmother, unable to bear it, held her and wept. Suddenly, Fó'ér opened her eyes and called out, 'I am all over wet, and all my limbs are in pain.' When asked why, she said, 'Last night, two officials came to arrest me. They took me over to a place called Chālǐng, which is about ten miles from here. I couldn't move further, so they hit me on the back with iron hammers twice, which was extremely painful. At the bottom of the hill, there is a pool, and in the pool, there is a bridge. They ordered me to stand on the bridge. Another person was seen entering with two people wrapped in black blankets. These two officials also wanted to wrap me in a floral blanket. They said, 'You owe someone else 1,500 units of currency, and now you must pay it.' I pleaded with them, saying, 'Please allow me to return to my grandmother to ask for money.'"

The envoys did not allow it. A person standing by in green clothes said, "This person once heard about the *Prajnaparamita*. Can he be forgiven?" The two envoys had no choice but to throw me into the water and leave. The pool water was very shallow, and I climbed out of the water. I hurriedly returned. I was astonished by the matter and immediately went to Chaoling to verify it. Indeed, I saw Mr. Chen, whose house had a pond. I visited the owner of the house and asked, "What did your household give birth to yesterday?" He said, "A dog gave birth to three puppies." Two were black, and one was spotted. The spotted one was taken by the mother dog and placed in the pond, where it died. Only the two black ones survived. I told him everything my granddaughter said and compensated him with fifteen hundred gold pieces. Mr. Chen said, "There was no money in your daughter's possession," and refused to accept. I realized I could not repay this debt, and my granddaughter would also be in trouble in the future. So I brought Mr. Chen here. The chief monk then performed a ceremony of repentance and used the gold and silver to perform the ceremony. Dai Zhi asked about the reason he heard the *Prajnaparamita*. He said he once went with his mother to Hongfu Temple in the county to listen to Master Jingxiang give a lecture.

Zhang the Butcher's Father In the city of Pingjiang, there was a butcher named Zhang Xia'er who lived near a grass bridge. In the eighth year of the Shaoxing era, he went fifteen miles to the village of Huangdai to buy a dog from the Liu family. When the dog saw Zhang the butcher, it showed a joyful expression and dragged him forward. Zhang lifted its ear to gauge its weight and paid three thousand coins for it. The dog did not wait to be tied up and followed Zhang directly back home. Upon reaching the Qi Gate, Zhang was afraid it might escape, so he tied it with a rope. Suddenly, the dog spoke in human words: "I am your father. I do not owe you any debt. Do not kill me." Zhang was drunk and tired and did not understand what the dog said, so he took it home. He instructed his wife to prepare food. The dog then spoke to his wife: "New wife, come. I am your father-in-law. It has been seven or eight years since I saw your faces. Now I am fortunate to return. I only owe three thousand coins to the Liu family, which has already been paid. Please do not kill me. Your husband's life is very short; only one or two years remain. You should quickly change your occupation. In future generations, you cannot be human anymore. I am very hungry. Bring me some food." The wife quickly gave half of her husband's meal to the dog. Her husband did not know about this. After he finished eating, he asked for more, but there was none left. He became very angry. The wife said, "I gave half of the food to the father-in-law." She explained everything the dog had said to her husband, who then began to be greatly afraid. He kept the dog to feed it and did not dare to kill it. Three days later, the dog went out to the Jiang family and bit someone, and was killed by them. Zhang the butcher then changed his occupation to work as a servant in an oil-selling family.

The Mother of Chen Chengxin A villager from Wuxi County, Changzhou, named Chen Chengxin, originally made his living by selling pigs. Later, he became extremely wealthy. His mother, throughout her life, especially enjoyed raising pigs. She died in the fourth year of the Shaoxing era. Seven days after her death, while his family was performing Buddhist rituals, they heard a sound coming from the coffin. They thought it was a rebirth and were very pleased. They hastily took an axe to open the coffin, and it had already transformed into an old female pig. They quickly covered it up again. The next day, they invited the head monk of Taiping Temple in Changzhou to perform a funeral rite and then buried her. At the time, the weather was clear and pleasant. As soon as the funeral carriage left the door, a heavy downpour began. The mourners could not proceed and all returned. When they reached the grave, the pit was already full of water. They then sealed it with stones. (The following section is missing twenty-five lines.)

### The Missing Dragon Soarer The internal text is missing. The words of True Person Cai are missing. The internal text is missing. The official record of Liu Canmin is missing. The internal text is missing. Luo Gong's Divine Retribution Luo Gong, a native of Shaxian County in Nanjian, was studying at the Imperial Academy during the Dazhao era. There was a shrine at the academy that was very active and effective. Luo Gong often prayed silently for his future prospects, both morning and evening. One night, a deity appeared to him in a dream and said, "You have incurred the wrath of the underworld. You should return to your hometown immediately. Do not worry about your future prospects." Luo Gong had always maintained a high moral standard and had few faults. He asked the deity, "Please tell me the reason for my sin." The deity replied, "You have no other wrongdoing. It is simply because you have not buried your parents for a long time." Luo Gong said, "My family has brothers. Why is the blame placed solely on me?" The deity said, "Because you are a Confucian scholar who practices ritual propriety and righteousness, so you bear the responsibility. Your other brothers are ordinary and not to be blamed." Luo Gong, having realized his mistake, hurriedly packed his belongings and returned home. When his fellow villagers and roommates asked him why he was leaving, he told them about his dream. He had not even reached his home when he died. As told by Cao Ji, Luo Gong was the son-in-law of Cao's grandmother. ### Failing to Bury One's Father and Failing the Imperial Examination Chen Gao, courtesy name Henming, was from Fuzhou. He came to the capital as an examination candidate and went to the Temple of the Two Officials to pray for a dream. One night, he dreamed that a deity said to him, "Your father has died and you have not buried him. You cannot expect to pass the imperial examination." Chen Gao was doubtful and did not believe it. The following year, he was indeed dismissed from the imperial examination hall. He then sent a letter to inform his family and urgently prepared for the funeral arrangements. After retaking the exam, he finally passed. As told by Li Shuchang, a native of Ningde.

Misfortune and Fortune Are Unavoidable According to Li Si's Attendant, since the time of hardship, the misfortunes and fortunes of scholars and officials have been determined by fate. In the Jianyan year Dingwei, Fu Guohua, the Grand Secretary known as Moqing, served as the governor of Shucheng. He heard that rebels had risen in Wuchang, and that they advanced from Wuchang, passing through Qizhou and Huangzhou, to reach Shucheng. Fearing their invasion, he also had previously twice been sent on a mission to Korea and had a very heavy load in his bag, which he regretted. He then ordered his younger brother to take the family to avoid danger in Jiangning. After arriving and anchoring by the river, the boatmen reported that there were many thieves outside, and it would be better to enter the lock. At that time, Yuwen Zhongda was the governor of Jiangning, and he was friendly with Fu Gong. The family immediately sent someone to inform Yuwen, who borrowed the key to open the lock, allowing the boat to enter. They believed they were safe and free from harm. That night, the bandit Zhou De rebelled, raiding their boat. The entire family died, leaving only one old maid alive, while Shucheng remained peaceful. Wu Fang and Gu Yancheng were the governors of the Two Zhe regions. A soldier named Chen Tong in Hangzhou had accumulated anger against officials and was planning to cause trouble. Just when Gu Jun went on an inspection tour to Wu County, Chen Tong forced the crowd to remain calm and waited for him to return. After one month, Gu Jun returned. The officials and the governor's office in Hangzhou all came out to welcome him. That night, the rebellion broke out, and all the officials were killed. Gu Jun, however, took his family to a temple outside the city to perform Buddhist rituals. Before entering the city, he heard of the chaos and fled to Huzhou again, thus escaping. Fu Gong had the intention to avoid misfortune, but his whole family was not spared. The soldiers in Hangzhou waited for Gu Jun for a month, but he managed to escape. All these events were beyond human control.

Island Woman A monk named Ben Chang from Quanzhou told of his cousin, who was a maritime merchant. He intended to go to Srivijaya. According to the rules, one should sail south for three days and then turn east; otherwise, he would hit Jiaoshang, and the ship would surely be destroyed. When this person was traveling, he happened to encounter a swift wind. After sailing for two and a half days, he thought he should turn east, so he turned the rudder, but it was too late, and he landed on Jiaoshang, and the entire ship sank. This person alone found a piece of wood, floated on the water for three days, drifted to the shore of an island, thought he would surely die, left the wood and climbed ashore, walked dozens of steps, found a small path, the path is very smooth and clean, as if it is frequently used by people. After a while, a woman arrives, her body is completely naked, her speech is unintelligible, she is very happy to see foreigners, she takes his hand and leads him to a cave, at night, they sleep together. In the morning, she lifts a large stone to block the entrance, the woman goes out alone, she returns at the afternoon, she always brings exotic fruits, the taste is very precious, all of which are unknown in the world. Stay for a while longer, finally allows him to go his own way. Like this for seven or eight years, they have three children. One day, he walks to the sea, just then, a boat arrives at the shore, also a person from Quanzhou who was blown off course, it is an old acquaintance, he quickly boards the ship. At that time, the woman follows, he thinks she cannot catch up, he calls out to the people on the ship and curses them, he cries bitterly, he falls to the ground and nearly faints. The people on the ship raise their hands from the bottom of the boat to apologize, he also wipes away his tears. The ship has already set sail, he is finally able to return.

A Taoist Priest from Chashì In the market area of Changde Prefecture, there lived a wealthy household known as the Old Man Yu. His family harvested ten thousand stones of grain annually, and he was known for his benevolence and frugality. He often sold grain at reduced prices and, for every stone of grain purchased, would add an additional half measure. His other practices were similarly benevolent. In the sixth month of the first year of the Qingyuan era (1195 AD), while reciting scriptures in his study room, a sudden thunderstorm erupted during the day. A woodcutter seeking shelter stood outside the door. Suddenly, a Taoist priest appeared, wearing a green headband and a simple robe. The priest led the woodcutter into the house and knocked on the study room to see the old man. The priest said to him, "You should make this village elder crouch under the scripture table to temporarily avoid the thunder." The priest then sat down. Shortly after, lightning flashed into the room, circling it several times before subsiding. When the rain stopped, a servant reported that there were new red characters written on the lintel. The old man went out to see them and read that they referred to the woodcutter named Dong Er. It stated that in his previous life, he had committed five grave sins, his sins were so numerous they had reached their limit, and God had ordered his punishment. However, the true person Chen had placed him under the scripture table of the Bodhisattva Cixi for protection, and the gods dared not approach. There were a total of thirty-nine characters. After reading them, the Taoist priest disappeared. Shortly after, the old man died while sitting.

An official from Renhe County, during the reign of Emperor Gaozong (1165-1173), was suffering from premature aging and wasting away due to illness. His teeth were falling out continuously. He obtained a simple prescription from a merchant of medicines. The prescription called for grinding raw sulfur into fine powder, filling the powder into the pig's intestines, boiling the intestines until they were soft, grinding them together finely, using stale steamed buns to form pills, and taking them as desired. After two months, his drinking and eating doubled, his walking became light and nimble, and he was over ninety years old but without any signs of old age, performing his duties as usual. Because he accompanied the county magistrate out of the village, he drank and ate beef blood while drunk, then had several dozen bowel movements. The excrement was like gold water. From then on, he became weak and emaciated, and died shortly after. Li Juyuan learned this from the imperial physician Guan Fan of Lin'an and once spoke of it to Wang Shushi. Wang said, "I only heard that pork fat can neutralize sulfur. Using the intestines is even more reasonable. It should also be taken for a long time, and one will see the effects."

Zhou Shiheng, the Chief Magistrate of Poyang, after fulfilling his duties, served the Bodhisattva Guanyin with great reverence. At the beginning of the Qingyuan era, he made a vow to write 200 scrolls of scriptures by hand and distribute them for others to recite. Due to delays, he fell ill. He then prayed to the Bodhisattva for protection and healing. After experiencing slight relief, he paid the papermaker Jiang three thousand coins and a stone of rice to make paper for copying scriptures. However, Jiang used the money to make different kinds of paper and sold them in the city. Zhou saw this and reprimanded him. Jiang explained his poverty, and Zhou then increased the payment. When Jiang sold the paper, each sheet was cut into six or seven pieces. Feeling afraid, he hurried home and worked hard to repair and deliver the paper to Zhou. Zhou asked a monk to bind it into volumes and fasted and wrote with reverence. Just as he reached the 20th scroll, he was writing when dozens of crows cawed loudly on the roof. He chased them away but they did not leave, so he stood up and prayed in front of the image. Upon going out to look, he saw that one crow was hit by an arrow and bleeding. The other crows tried to pull the arrow out but could not, and they cried out in distress. Zhou continuously recited the sutras of the Buddha of Victory and the Bodhisattva Guanyin, pointing his pen at them. The arrow fell out on its own, and the crows flew into the air. As Zhou was praising the event, the arrow was thrown down from the skylight and landed in the Buddha's shrine. Such was the miraculous power of the Bodhisattva.

The Gold Hairpin Repels Ghosts** A woman from Yundang Village, Rui'an County, Wenzhou, had been ill for a long time. One evening, while taking her medicine, she suddenly disappeared. The neighbors were called urgently, and they brought candles to search the mountains for her. They came to a very deep cave. The crowd suspected she was inside and noisily entered. They reached the deepest part of the cave and saw a woman's face floating on the water. They took her back. She said that several people had invited her to go with them. At first, at the cave entrance, she saw torches coming and quickly pulled herself inside. Her collar had a gilded hairpin, and fearing it might be lost, she often raised her hand to feel for it. The ghosts showed a fearful expression, and thus her face did not sink. **The King of Mountain Searching** Wang Juchang, a Daoist from Rui'an, Wenzhou, had the courtesy name An Dao. Later, he returned to secular life and lived in East Mountain. Because of maritime trade, he went to Shandong but was detained by the false Qi regime. He escaped by land to return home and arrived in Kaifeng. At night, he dreamed that someone told him, "You will die tomorrow. If you meet someone riding a white horse, wearing military attire, and carrying bows and arrows, that is the person who will kill you. You should urgently call out to the King of Mountain Searching to beg for your life. If he laughs, you can live; if he is angry, you will die. It is because in a past life you once killed someone, and therefore you are now receiving retribution." The next day, as he was walking in a desolate marsh, he indeed saw a person riding a horse exactly as described in the dream. He immediately bowed and called out to the King of Mountain Searching to beg for his life. The person laughed and left. Thus, he escaped. Later, he returned to his hometown and painted his image and worshipped him. These two stories were also told by Zhu Hengsou.

The Bright and Radiant Light Mantra** Cao Que, a scholar from Rui'an, was known by the name Juelao. When he was young, he left home to become a monk. His family had been plagued by the disease of "transmission of the corpse" for generations, and those who managed the household all died suddenly, leaving no one to perform the necessary sacrifices. Cao Que then returned to his secular life. Several years later, he also fell ill. Fearing he had no way to deal with it, he began to recite the Bright and Radiant Light Mantra day and night. One day, he recited it up to ten thousand times. He then sensed three worms emerging from his body: two on his neck and back, and one on his abdomen. The worms moved all around, as if trying to flee. Frightened, Cao Que dared not look at them and continued to recite the mantra. Suddenly, a bright light like electricity appeared on his head. The worms disappeared, and his illness was cured. This story was told by Dai Hongzhong, a resident of the prefecture. **The Great Sea Fish** In the Dunzhao Salt Field of Zhangpu County, located by the sea, a military officer named Chen Min arrived there. He bought a shark from a fisherman to make thread. When he cut open the shark, he found a person lying flat across its belly. The person's skin was as if still alive, indicating that they had been recently swallowed by the shark. In the eighteenth year of the Shaoxing era, a sea turtle rode the tide into the harbor. When the tide receded, the turtle could not leave and lay motionless in the harbor. The water was fifteen feet deep. People used a long ladder on a large boat to climb onto its back, which was still more than ten feet tall. At the time of a famine, villagers rushed to cut open and carve the turtle. On that day, they took approximately hundreds of loads. The turtle remained motionless. The next day, someone plucked out its eyes, and it finally felt pain. It turned and moved in the water, causing nearby boats to capsize. Fortunately, nothing was lost. It took about ten days to finish carving the turtle. Many people relied on it for sustenance. Its backbone was all used for grinding grains in a rice mortar. **The First Volume of the Yi Jian Ji, Chapter Eighty-Seven

Wu Gongcheng** Wu Gongcheng, a native of Xinghua and courtesy name Junyu, was seventy years old when he retired from his position as a high-ranking official. In a dream, he was told that he still had 700,000 units of salary remaining in the government. Upon waking, he collected and tallied the receipts, and found that the accumulated and unclaimed amount matched the number mentioned in the dream. He then told his sons, "What I have received is only this amount. Let us set it aside and not claim it, perhaps to slightly extend my lifespan." His sons followed his advice and sealed the matter, no longer discussing it. A year later, Wu Gongcheng passed away. It was calculated that the half salary he received after retirement amounted to exactly 700,000 units, though it was not from his previous savings. After the mourning period, his sons, who had a connection with the local governor, were fully reimbursed for what they were entitled to receive. **Jin Si Captures a Ghost** In the southern part of Fuzhou, beneath the Xiyoutang, there was a public lotus pond spanning several acres. A local man named Jin Si managed the profits from the pond. His residence was in Nantai, seven miles away from the pond. Concerned about potential thieves, he would patrol the area every night. One evening, he encountered a man walking down a side path. Jin Si questioned him, and the man replied, "I was elsewhere on business and just returned home tonight." It was already the second watch of the night. Jin Si, known for his courage, observed that the man's behavior was unlike that of a human and that he was not on a path commonly used by people. Jin Si then said to him, "My home is in the south of the Yangtze River. I drank too much and feel drunk. I cannot return home. I wish to have you carry me. You should first carry me to Heshamen, two miles from the city, and then I will carry you to Mapu, four miles from the city. You will then carry me across the floating bridge." The man agreed to the arrangement and left. When they reached Mapu and the man tried to put Jin Si down, Jin Si held him tightly and called out to his family for candles and fire to come and see. The man had already transformed into an old hawk. Jin Si then bound and burned the hawk.

Buddha Saves an Ancient Grudge In the Lin'an region lived a citizen named Zhang Gongzi. Once, he visited a temple and saw an ancient Buddha statue in a ruined house, which lacked hands and feet. He took it home, adorned it, and worshipped it. After a year, the statue began to show signs of divine presence, foretelling all matters of good or bad fortune in his family. This continued for about twenty to thirty years. During the Jianyan era, the Jurchen invaders attacked Lin'an. Zhang hid in a dry well. In a dream-like state, he saw the Buddha he worshipped come to bid farewell, saying, "You are facing death. I have no way to save you. Because in a past life during the Huang Chao rebellion, you once killed someone. That person has now reincarnated as Ding Xiaoda. He will come tomorrow to kill you as retribution. There is no escape." Zhang was terrified. The next day, indeed someone came with a spear to the well, shouting at Zhang to come out. Once out, he was about to stab Zhang. Zhang cried out, "Are you not Ding Xiaoda?" The person was startled and asked, "How do you know my name?" Zhang told him what the Buddha had said. The person sighed and threw the spear on the ground, saying, "Grudges can be resolved but not perpetuated. You killed me in the past, I am killing you now, and in your future life, you will kill me again. When will this cycle end? I will release you to break this cycle. But if you stay here, you will be killed by the pursuing soldiers. Come with me." He let Zhang stay with him for several days, ensuring his safety, and then sent him away. Ding Xiaoda was originally a man from Hebei, conscripted by the Jurchen army. All three events were recounted by Chen Jiruo.

A Strange Woman in the Capital During the middle of the Xuanhe era, a scholar from the capital went out to enjoy the Lantern Festival. He arrived at the foot of the Meimei Building, where the crowd was so dense that it was difficult to move forward. After a short pause, he saw a beautiful woman who appeared anxious and seemed to have lost something. When he asked her, she said, "I was following the crowd to watch the lanterns, but I just happened to encounter a very crowded area and thus lost my companion. Now I have no place to go." He tried to comfort her, and she happily said, "I have been here for a while, and I am afraid someone might take me away and sell me. It would be better if I went home with you." The scholar was delighted and took her hand, leading her back to his residence. They lived together for half a year, and he treated her very favorably. No one else came to inquire about her. One day, he invited a close friend to drink with him and asked the woman to serve the wine. A few days later, his friend came again and asked, "Who did you get that woman from last night?" The scholar replied, "I bought her with gold." His friend said, "That's not true. You should tell me the truth. Last night, when you were drinking, I noticed that every time she passed behind the candle, her face would change color. I suspect she is not human. You should investigate this." The scholar said, "We have been together for several months, and there is nothing unusual." His friend could not persuade him and said, "There is a Taoist priest named Weng Wenqing at Baizhen Palace who is skilled in talismans. You should go and see him. If there is something wrong, he will be able to tell you. Even if there is nothing, it won't hurt to check." So, they went to Baizhen Palace. Weng Wenqing, upon seeing the woman, exclaimed, "The demonic aura is extremely strong, and this is a very strange case. It is not like ordinary ghosts or spirits. In the future, all of you will serve as witnesses." Those present were all frightened. The scholar, having already heard what his friend had said, did not dare to hide anything and told the whole story.

The Daoist master said, "What are this woman's usual habits?" The scholar replied, "She has a very fine and exquisite money box that she usually wears at her waist and never shows to others." The master then gave him two red talismans to write. "Return home and wait for her to sleep. Place one on her head and one in the money box." The scholar returned home, and the woman had already loudly scolded, saying, "I have entrusted myself to you for a long time, and you cannot be trusted. Why did you order a Daoist to write talismans? Why treat me like a ghost?" At first, she still tried to evade with excuses. The woman said, "My servant told me that one talisman is meant to be placed on my head and one in the money box. Why hide it?" The scholar could not explain. He secretly questioned the servant, who initially did not speak, but the scholar began to suspect. Waiting until night when she was asleep, he lit a lamp and made clothes, not stopping until dawn. The scholar became even more embarrassed and again went to consult the master of Daoism. The master happily said, "She can only endure one night. Tonight she will surely sleep. Just follow my instructions." That night she indeed slept soundly, and as instructed, the talismans were placed. The next morning, nothing was seen, and the scholar thought she had gone. After two days, Kaifeng sent a court official to arrest the master of Daoism and put him in prison. The official said, "A woman in a certain family had a wasting disease for three years. When she was near death, she suddenly shouted, 'The Daoist master Wang from Baizhen Palace killed me.' Then she died. Her family bathed her and saw talismans on her head and in her money box. They then went to the government office to file a complaint, claiming that Wang used magic to take her daughter." The master explained the reasons, and immediately summoned the scholar and the guests present at the time. All testified to the same effect. Only then was he released. The master of Daoism was from Jiangan. Lin Lianggong said. Lin and the scholar's friend were fellow students.

A Dog in the Village Temple of Yongfu In Fuzhou's Yongfu County, there was a village temple. Two monks, the elder and the younger, shared a room. The elder monk loved a dog and would call it forward every time he ate. The younger monk, however, hated the dog very much and would scold and chase it away whenever he saw it, sometimes even beating it. This went on for many years. One day, the elder monk went out for ten days. When he returned, he did not see the dog and scolded the younger monk, saying, "You have always hated the dog especially. Taking advantage of my absence, you must have killed and eaten it." The younger monk strongly denied it, but reluctantly admitted that he had killed it by mistake because it stole food. He buried it in the back garden, not for eating. The elder monk did not believe him at all and secretly went to the burial place to dig it up and see. Hurriedly returning, he told the younger monk, "Though a dog is a different species, its heart is the same as a human's. You have been at odds with it for a long time. Just now when I looked at its body, its head had turned into a snake. It will surely seek revenge on you. You should not go. You can ask someone you know to take another look." Later, another person went to see, and the snake's head became even longer. He was greatly frightened and asked for a way to resolve the grudge. The elder monk taught him to sell everything he owned, confess to the Buddha, and seek forgiveness. He entered the confession hall and did so day and night without stopping for several years. One night, while burning paper money, he felt there was something in the bowl. Thinking it was a mouse, he poked through the ashes to look, and it was a snake. Taking advantage of the younger monk opening his mouth, it quickly rushed into his throat, and he died. The elder master of wisdom, Weixue, from this county said.

The Miraculous Power of the Diamond Sutra A man from Qingzhou named Chai Zhu served as the judicial official of the Shouchun Prefecture. While investigating a robbery and murder case, a prisoner confessed that he had a lodging house located thirty miles from the city. He often killed guests who arrived with luggage and stayed alone, throwing their bodies into the White Sand River. The number of victims was unknown, but he had failed to kill one elderly woman. When Chai Zhu asked him why, the prisoner replied that a few years ago, the elderly woman had stayed alone at his lodging house. He and his brothers had discussed that it was a good opportunity that night. They waited until deep into the night, then sent his eldest son to knock on the door. The son returned after a long time, saying that if anyone stood against the door, it should not be opened. The prisoner did not believe him and took a knife himself. He reached the door, dug a hole in the wall, and peered through. He saw a large deity in a red glow, the size of a room, standing with its back to the door, with a very angry appearance. The prisoner was so frightened that he lost his voice and almost fell over. Just before dawn, the door opened, and the woman was just getting up to comb her hair, continuously reciting scriptures. When asked what scripture she was reciting, she said it was the Diamond Sutra. Thus, it was known that the deity seen the previous night was the guardian of the Diamond Sutra. A Poem by a Woman at the Nanyang Inn In the first year of the Jingkang era, a woman from Nanyang left a poem at the Nanyang inn in Dengzhou. The poem reads: "Wandering south, one can only lament. Avoiding people, she dares not apply makeup. But pity the story of Yingying. Standing alone in the spring breeze, her hair slightly disheveled." The handwriting is delicate and weak, truly a woman's writing. Followers filled the walls with poems in the same meter.

#### Wang Yanchu's Dream Poem Wang Yanchu, a native of □□□ Prefecture. In his youth, he dreamed of composing a poem: "After pounding rice, chickens □□□ walk, dogs bark at the fence. The stream is deep with strong water, and the heavy frost makes the oranges plump." During the reign of Emperor □, he was in charge of grain transport in the western part of the capital region. When bandits arrived, Wang Yanchu was wounded in a mountainous area. He ran through the villages, hearing the sound of pounding rice in a farmhouse, just as the scene from the poem composed in the dream. This was recounted by Huang ▖. #### The Son of the Liu Family Liu Minqiu, courtesy name Hao Gu, lived outside the suburbs of Kaifeng. He had a son who fell ill at the age of two. As he was about to die, Liu Minqiu could not bear to see it and moved him to a neighbor's house. He waited until the child died and then prepared the funeral. A wet nurse was holding the child and weeping when a Taoist monk passed by and saw the child. He said, "The child is not dead." He took a pill and gave it to the child, who then revived. The monk then asked for paper and wrote ten characters, sealing the paper and giving it to the wet nurse. He instructed her to keep it carefully and not to open it. If she opened it, the child would die. The wet nurse secretly peeked at it and could recognize nineteen characters, but she did not understand the rest. From then on, the child gradually recovered. His mother thought he would not survive until the age of nineteen. In that year, she abstained from meat and offered prayers to prolong his life. Later, he remained healthy. By the nineteenth year of Shaoxing, Liu Minqiu was serving as an official in Jiankang, and his son was forty-three years old. He fell ill on the twenty-sixth day of the third month and did not recover. The wet nurse was still alive and opened the sealed paper, which contained nine large characters. The text read: "The nineteenth year of Shaoxing, the twenty-sixth day of the third month." This was recounted by Liang Hongfu.

Pan Jing, a famous physician known by his courtesy name Wen Sou, treated three women who had been unable to conceive for extended periods. The first was the wife of Zhang Yan, a Wu Bu Yuanwailang, who had been pregnant for five years; the second was the wife of Fu Changling, a Nanling Wei, who had been pregnant for two years; and the third was the concubine of Liu Yi's grandson, who had been pregnant for fourteen months. All had not conceived. Pan Jing examined them and said, "It is a disease. All doctors had mistakenly believed they were pregnant." He then prescribed a large dose for them to drink. Zhang Yan's wife vomited out over a hundred pieces of flesh, some with eyebrows and eyes. Fu Changling's wife dreamed of two children with a pitch-black complexion, and they were terrified and panicked, running away in a hurry. Liu Yi's grandson's concubine vomited out a large snake that was still alive and writhing. The three women were all safe and sound. In another case, the Gujiang Ling Wang Ji dreamed at night of singing, drinking, and being with a woman, and he could not eat during the day, like this for three years. Pan Jing treated him, and his illness was weakening. The women he was with became increasingly disheartened, and he found it easier to get drunk and less joyful in his singing. After a long time, he no longer saw them. Pan Jing said, "The illness is weakening, but it is not yet gone. If he dreams of a man wearing a blue headband and white clothes, then he will recover." Indeed, he dreamed of such a man and was able to eat. The historian Wu Zeli of Beihu recorded this story.

A Man from Huangshan He was bestowed the title of Grand Tutor by the state, and was originally from Jinyun. He served as the military governor of Jiande in Muzhou. In his prime, he had no son. He consulted an astrologer named Huang on his fate. Huang said, "Your heir will be of great status. He will hold the position of military governor. However, this will be after the age of thirty. If he is born quickly, he will not be a great person." Tian You was not pleased. Later, he served in Gongzhou. Huang returned, and Tian You instructed him to consult the *I Ching* for divination. He received the *Bì* hexagram. Huang said, "Today's element is earth. Earth added to *Bì* forms the character *fén* (坟), which means 'tomb.' You will have a son, but there will be grief over the death of a loved one." Indeed, after several years, his son was born, but shortly after, his wife, Zhao Fu, passed away. His son was named Shao Yun. After passing the imperial examinations, Shao Yun became the son-in-law of Zhou Tong, the Censor of Eastern Huai. Zhou once invited a man from Huangshan named Shao Yun to consult the *I Ching*. He received the *Jìn* hexagram. Huang said, "In three years, you will give birth to twin girls. The *Jìn* hexagram has two yin lines at the bottom, and the character *Jìn* (晋) consists of two mouths. The hexagram's text says, 'Three meetings during the day,' which symbolizes the passage of three years."

Awaiting the verification of this matter, I will inform you of your future prospects. Shao Yun deeply detested his words. Yet, indeed, it came to pass. After returning from Weiyang to Wuxing, he met him again. Shao Yun said, "Your words of the past have indeed come true. What will be the outcome of your future? Why don't you tell me?" Huang replied, "You are a noble person. From now on, you will hold many important and prestigious positions. You will ascend to the highest governmental office and end your career as a military governor. You should take good care of yourself." Shao Yun was amazed and told his father about it. The father said, "I remember thirty years ago there was a guest who also had the surname Huang. He told me about the time when you would be born and predicted that you would establish a military governorship. Could this be the same person?" He asked Huang to be summoned. It turned out to be the same person he had seen before. The father and son looked at each other and laughed. They treated Huang like a deity. During the Jianyan period, Shao Yun became the Left Chancellor of the Shangshu Province. In the 16th year of the Shaoxing era, at the age of seventy, he submitted a petition to retire. He was appointed as the Military Governor of Chongqing and retired. He died two years later. Ultimately, it was as Huang had said. Huang, the Left Chancellor, spoke about it.

A Government Office in Raozhou South of the main gate of Raozhou stood a government office, which had long been known for its strange occurrences. In the eleventh year of the Shaoxing era (1141 AD), the official in charge of the granary system, Han Can, resided there. He invited Hu Jia, a scholar from LePing, to stay as a guest. The prefect, Cheng Jindao, also sent his son to study under Hu Jia. Coincidentally, Cheng Jindao was replaced in office, and Hu Jia accepted a bribe from Han Xiu, a government slave, to free him from official records. Hu Jia informed Cheng Jindao of the deal, and Cheng agreed to it. One night, a ghost impersonating a courtesan sneaked into Hu Jia's study room, carrying wine and food. She convinced Hu Jia to allow her to act as she pleased. On subsequent nights, she would visit again, rewarding Hu Jia's servants with the remaining wine after drinking. She visited frequently, often staying until after midnight. One night, after the third watch, the neighbor in the west saw Hu Jia's study room still lit. He called out to Hu Jia, who seemed to be responding. At dawn, Hu Jia was found dead in his bed. The owner questioned the servants and night watchmen, who all reported seeing a woman taking wine and food from the main hall each night. They believed the people in the house were too afraid to speak about the supernatural occurrences. The woman left at dawn, and the night watchmen heard Hu Jia shouting, suspecting he was having a nightmare. They did not realize he had died. It turned out that the ghost had deceived Hu Jia by impersonating a courtesan, and Hu Jia was unaware of the deception. Three years later, Ren Liangchen, a deputy governor, occupied the same place. His young daughter, about ten years old, often saw two people walking together. She fell ill and was quickly moved out of the place. Later, the office was converted into an inn for travelers.
A Woman with Two Heads and Four Arms During the reign of Emperor Ling, a woman from Luoyang was born with two heads and four arms.

**Unfilial Son Struck Dead by Lightning** In the village of Xiaocheng in Poyang, there was a man named Wang Sanyi. Initially, his parents had bought two coffins made of fragrant wood to prepare for their deaths. Wang replaced them with cypress wood from Xinzhou. Later, he sold the cypress wood and exchanged it for other planks. When his mother died, he wanted to keep the planks for himself but only purchased a pine coffin for her burial. After ten days of the burial, he was struck dead by lightning. His body was placed on its side. Someone ran to inform his son. The son hurriedly went to cry over his father's body and supported it as it fell to the ground. At noon, a thunderbolt struck, suddenly carrying the son to another place, about five li away. When he returned, his father was standing on his head. They buried him twice, each time interrupted by lightning. Finally, they cut a small hole in the coffin, marked it with bamboo, and covered it. Only then did they find peace. **Mei San's Dog** In a village near Donghu Lake in Raozhou, there lived a man named Mei San. On the eve of the Chinese New Year in the 20th year of the Shaoxing era (1150 AD), he tied up a female dog to kill it. He had already cut its blood and cooked it, but suddenly, the dog disappeared. That night, he dreamed that the dog said, "I am a dog. I do not object to being killed, but you still owe me a few of your dog's offspring. Please be lenient with me." Mei agreed. The next day, when he returned from outside, he was unharmed and decided to raise the dog again.

An Account of An Changqi An Changqi was a native of Gongcheng, Zhao Prefecture. When he was young, he passed the imperial examination. During the reign of Emperor Renzong, the court pacified the uprising led by Nong Zhigao, and extended favor to the two Guang regions. All those who had taken the imperial examination were specially tested in the court. Changqi, as a result, obtained the position of Magistrate of Yongding, Heng Prefecture. He resigned from office due to some matter and thereafter did not serve again. Alone, he traveled with a young boy to Guangdong, wandering freely in the mountains and waters. A contemporary named Hu Jun from Qujiang served as the magistrate of Haifeng, Huizhou Prefecture. Changqi visited him and stayed for a long time. During wine drinking, he often performed playful tricks to entertain the guests. Once, he tied several pieces of paper and covered them, chanting over them. After a while, the objects began to move. Slowly opening them, they were all mice, chewing and moving as if they were real. Covering them again, they were still tied pieces of paper. Sometimes, he chewed and spat out mountain yam to show others, the saliva sticking to the medicine, all like honey. Sometimes, he stayed awake all night. Pointing to his young boy, he said, "Do not underestimate this boy; one day he will accompany me in seclusion." In the second year of Zhiping, he visited the Xianyuan Temple in Qingyuan and told the monks, "I had long heard that there was a cave called HeGuang in the mountains, so I came to visit it." Then he went there with his young boy and did not return for several days. The monks suspected that he was eaten by a tiger and searched everywhere but found nothing. On the stone wall in front of the cave, they found a poem: "The fragrant tent is about to be left. The monkeys cannot bear to cry. Music and books are self-entertainment. Who will accompany friends? The alchemy furnace is not without medicine. There is another ladder to the blue clouds. In Xianyuan, I temporarily retreat. No one can match Yi and Qi." Below the poem, it was written, "Written by An Changqi, former Magistrate of Yongding, Heng Prefecture. Said by the mountain monk."

In the eighth year of the Shaoxing era, in the coastal area west of Guangzhou, near a place called Shanggongwan, at night, a sea creature appeared. It looked like a horse, with red hooves. It approached a nearby village, and the villagers gathered to kill it. As dawn approached, a sound like a vast army marching in the sky was heard, and people were calling out to find the horse. A knowledgeable traveler recognized the event as unusual and quickly advised the villagers to evacuate. The next day, the sea overflowed, drowning over a hundred households surrounding the village.

#### The Dream of Zhuo Yi Zhuo Yi was a man from LePing, Raozhou, who sought to become a Jinshi (imperial scholar). During the early days of the Three-Stage Examination System, he went to the local city god temple to request a dream for guidance. That night, he dreamed of going to an official office where he saw a couplet on the wall that read: "If Zhuo Yi becomes the top candidate in Raozhou, Zhu Yuan will be the third-place winner in the imperial examination." Upon waking, he was greatly pleased, believing that he would surely win first place in the provincial examination. At that time, the examination rules were strictly enforced, and the penalty for cheating was severe. Zhuo Yi was the first to commit such an offense. Zhu Yuan, a tea merchant from Huizhou, was punished for illegal trading and was indeed the third-place winner in the imperial examination. #### The Two Doctors Doctor Li, whose name is forgotten, was from Fuzhou. He was highly skilled in medicine and had amassed a fortune of millions within ten years. A wealthy family in Chongren County fell ill and invited Doctor Li to treat them, promising to pay him five million coins as a reward. After ten days of treatment, the patient showed no improvement. The family then asked him to leave and called for another doctor, saying that other doctors were unsuitable, but only Doctor Wang could help. At that time, Doctors Wang and Li were equally renowned, both being excellent physicians. The family also allowed Doctor Li to leave because his prolonged stay had not been effective. Doctor Li left with several prescriptions. On his way back, he met Doctor Wang. Doctor Wang asked where Doctor Li was going, and Doctor Li explained the situation. Doctor Wang said, "Even if I go, I won't be able to cure him. My skills are far inferior to yours. It would be better if we both return." Doctor Li replied, "No, I diagnosed the pulse very accurately and prescribed the medicine very appropriately. However, I could not succeed because I felt that my luck was at an end. I did not expect to receive the reward anyway, so I asked to leave. You should go instead."

The king gave all of the medicine to Li and instructed him to use it to treat the patient, assuring that the illness would certainly heal. The king had always respected Li and followed his advice. Upon seeing the patient, Li used all of the medicine he prescribed, making slight adjustments to the decoction and administering it in sequence. After three days, the patient showed signs of improvement. The wealthy family was delighted and fulfilled their agreement by thanking Li and dismissing him. The king returned to the prefecture and prepared a lavish feast for Li. He said, "In the matter at Chongren, I did nothing significant; it was all due to your guidance. I dare not take the money alone. Now I offer half of it as a gift to you." Li strongly refused, saying, "I should not receive this. Because the owner's illness was not cured, the reason for the recovery now is due to your efforts. What merit do I have? To treat the disease and I receive thanks—it is absolutely not possible." The king could not force him. On another day, the king presented items worth several thousand strings of cash under the guise of a gift, and Li finally accepted it. The two doctors, originally ordinary people, adhered to righteousness and valued giving and taking in such a manner, which scholars and officials might not be able to do. Now, after several decades, people from Linchuan still enjoy talking about this.

Unusual Flowers and Fruits** In the fourth month of the year 1151 during the reign of Emperor Xiaozong of Song, at Dinglin Temple in Jiande County, Chizhou Prefecture, a mulberry tree bore plums, and a chestnut tree bore peaches. The fruits were extremely sweet and delicious, unlike anything usual. In Shimen Village, Poyang, the household of Zhang Er Gongpu saw a large branch of peony with double petals grow on a bamboo fence. At the home of Wang Ershiyi, a farmer from the narrator's family, a golden lotus appeared in the pot, with a monk standing on it. From the eighth to the tenth day, it did not disappear. The family cooked a dog, and the phenomenon disappeared. It is said that from Pengze to Shimen Village, many pots have shown similar phenomena, but there were no monks. This is an unusual occurrence that has not been heard of before. That year, the rain came at the right time, and the elders of the village believed it was an omen of a bountiful year. **Huang Lüzhong Prays for a Child** Huang Yuancheng, courtesy name Yuanshou, was from Jianchang. He passed the imperial examinations under the board of Wang Yingchen. He spoke of his ancestor Huang Lüzhong, who had no children. Huang Lüzhong prayed at the Junshan Temple. He dreamed of a person carrying a colorful cage with three five-colored phoenixes and a separate bamboo cage with one bird, and gave them to him. Later, his main wife gave birth to three sons, all of whom passed the imperial examinations. His concubine gave birth to one son, who was not very capable.

絇纺三梦 絇纺, courtesy name Gong Su, originally had the surname "句." To avoid a taboo name, he changed his surname to "絇," which sounds like "句" (as in "章句之句"). In the year Xuanhe Jiachen (1124 CE), he took the provincial examination. In a dream, someone told him, "Quickly obtain [something] and you will get to纺." He was pleased, thinking that "quickly obtain" meant "to get," but he did not succeed. In the year Jianyan Wushen (1128 CE), he took the examination in Yangzhou, and he had the same dream. He thought, "Quickly obtain" refers to "絇," and since he already has the surname "絇," and he was taking the examination in Yangzhou, he must succeed. But he did not. After some time, he had the same dream again. He told the person in the dream, "I am not yet 48 years old." The person in the dream replied, "You will pass the examination at the age of 48. You are now 38, so you still have 10 years." Since he thought he would not succeed, he did not dare to pursue the examination. He lived in Daozhou. A wealthy man invited him to teach his son. In the year Shaoxing Jiayin (1134 CE), he was 45 years old. He thought he would not succeed and did not want to go. The host insisted, saying, "I invited you to bring my son to the examination field. You must go." He secretly instructed his son to submit a false application to take the examination. 织纺不得已，只好随行，与富家子一同被推荐参加考试。第二年，他交了公据，向礼部报到。他随意打开看，发现填写的年龄误写为47岁。这一年他正好48岁。他默喜，认为这是神助。但他不明白“逢州便得”的意思。等到成绩公布，他的名字左边是冯姓，右边是周姓。这一年他终于考中了。从开始到成功，前后十二年，共见了三次梦，曲折明白如此。

In the Yuanfu Wu Yin year, Huang Siye, a scholar from Jiande, Muzhou, lost his four-year-old son. He cried day and night. In a dream, the son said, "I have already been reborn. There is no need to remember me. In my previous life, I was a prime minister, but due to falsely accusing good people, I was demoted to become a commoner's child. There was also a minor mistake, and I was further demoted. Now I am only a few miles away, in the home of Mr. Si Shi. One day I will have an official position, after this lifetime, and then I will be reborn in a good place." The next day, Huang visited Mr. Si Shi and indeed found the child, who was born on the first day of the twelfth month, coinciding with the day the Huang family child died. Huang asked to see him, and the child leaped up with great joy. When given things, he immediately raised his hand as if to take them. Huang returned home, and no longer cried. Mr. Si Shi, whose name is Yi, reached the rank of Chaoying Lang, and his son named Xu passed the imperial examination in the twelfth year of Shaoxing. However, his career ended as the magistrate of Changshan County, and he lived to be fifty-three years old. These three matters were specially told to me by my teacher.

Old Man Yu and His Skill in Physiognomy Old Man Yu of Junwu was skilled in physiognomy, especially adept at interpreting the sounds of objects to predict good or bad omens. His ancestors had served the Southern Tang dynasty as Grand Historians. After the last emperor surrendered to the Song dynasty, the entire Yu family moved to Taiping in Shaowu. By the time Old Man Yu was of advanced age, people respected him and called him "Old Man." When Ye Zuchang was a child, he liked to play riding a sheep. Old Man Yu saw him and said, "Young Master will become the top scholar in the country. Encourage him, don't play around." Ye Zuchang then changed his behavior and started studying diligently. When Huang Youcheng, the Right Chancellor, was in mourning and living in his hometown, Ye Zuchang and other local scholars treated him as a teacher, following the etiquette of disciples. Once, while passing by Xiaoshan Temple, Ye Zuchang met Old Man Yu. Old Man Yu greeted him and said, "The top two scholars, how did you come here?" The two men looked at each other and laughed, saying, "How could that be?" Old Man Yu replied, "It's not just you; it will also be the same year. I will choose a topic for you to prepare for." The two men did not believe him and jokingly asked, "What is the topic?" Old Man Yu pointed to a bundle of bamboo in the courtyard and said, "It will be this." The two men laughed and left. In the third year of Xining (1070), the imperial examination for Jinshi was held, and the poetry, prose, and essay questions were abolished, replaced with policy questions.

Zu Qia was chosen as the first, followed by Jun. Only then did he realize a bundle of bamboo, which is the character for "strategy" or "plan." Zu Qia's father, Ke, was unlearned in his youth. Once, he passed by the old man's door, where a gathering of scholars from the county had assembled. None of them met the old man's expectations, except for Ke, whom the old man pointed out and said, "This person will be sixty years old, hold a seventh-rank official position, and wear silver and red robes." Everyone was in a state of disappointment or confusion. Later, Ke was honored due to his son's success, bestowed with multiple ranks of courtier, and granted a red sash. Indeed, he reached the age of sixty. The old man once walked in the fields and heard the sound of flowing water. He said, "The flowing water is sad, the land will change hands." As expected, this came to pass. On another occasion, the old man entered the market and heard the sound of music. He said, "The sound of metal is high, there will be soldiers. It will occur between the hours of Shen and You. But I will not be harmed. Four soldiers will drown to their deaths." On the day, indeed, there were soldiers returning from Tingzhou. They gathered in the market and drank together, quarreling over a woman, drew their swords, and killed each other. Feeling uneasy, they crossed the river in the evening in disorder. Just as the spring tide was raging, four of them drowned. Someone asked the old man the reason. He replied, "The sun is in the zodiac sign of Zi, which belongs to water, and water is strong in Zi, so metal dies here." His clever predictions and accurate hits are like this. Today, people in Shaowu still pass down a copy of his book on physiognomy, but it has been a long time since the old man's time.

Zong Ben Encounters a Strange Person Zong Ben, a monk from a farming family in Shaozhou, encountered a Daoist while walking through the mountain gorges to tend to his fields in the first year of the Xuanhe era (1119 AD). The Daoist was wearing a hemp robe, had a topknot hairstyle, and was begging for food. Zong Ben said, "My father has not yet eaten lunch. Can we go to my home to get some food?" The Daoist became angry and spat on Zong Ben's left thumb. He then drew a sword and threatened him, but Zong Ben responded as if nothing had happened. The Daoist laughed and said, "You barbarian can be taught." He removed his belt, took out a small gourd, and gave three red pills to Zong Ben. As Zong Ben raised his hand to take the pills, two fell to the ground and could not be retrieved. He swallowed one. The Daoist laughed again and said, "This is all that can be given." Suddenly, the Daoist disappeared. Zong Ben did not return home. Instead, he entered a village and went to the Shuanglin Temple, where he stayed on the Buddha hall. He could immediately talk about hidden matters among the monks, which surprised everyone. People ran to tell Zong Ben's family, but he dismissed them and did not allow them to enter. The next day, the news spread throughout the village, and people came to ask about good or bad omens. The path was continuously crowded with people, and officials below the level of the prefect sent letters asking for blessings. Zong Ben grasped the brush and closed his eyes, writing the blessings immediately. His writing style was clear and vigorous, making it lovely. The monks pointed to Zong Ben as a living Buddha and wanted him to stay long to strengthen their reputation. However, Zong Ben said, "My destiny is not here. I should go to Tingzhou to meet the Buddha of Enlightenment." He shook his arm and immediately set off on his journey. He arrived at the Fengyan in Taiping, where he enjoyed the beautiful and secluded mountain and water scenery. He also dreamed of a person in purple robes and gold seals pulling him to stay. Therefore, he decided to stay and not leave. The people of the county collectively contributed money to ordain Zong Ben. He obtained the title of the abandoned Danxia Temple and inscribed the rock. Not long after, an old man named Luo Ji Chou from Shaxian sent a message inviting Zong Ben. With joy and willingness, Zong Ben went to meet him.

During the reign of Chancellor Li Boji, who was demoted from his position as a historian to a lower administrative role in a county, Ben and Song said, "Stand together in harmony, like rice without husk. At this time, it is very glorious." Li Boji could not understand the meaning of these words. When the Jingtang era began, he gained the emperor's favor and was rapidly appointed as a high official. He finally understood the significance of the words they had spoken. Deng Su, with broad aspirations, met Ben as a student. Ben pointed to Li Boji and told Deng Su, "You will become great through this person." When Li Boji rose to power, Deng Su, in plain clothes, became the Left Censor. Ben stayed in Shaxian for over a year and then returned to Danxia. In the fourth year of Jianyan, Li Boji returned from the south and met Ben. Ben wrote large on the loom and wrote the characters "Shaoxing." The next year, it indeed changed. Ben told Li Boji, "This place is filled with bloody stench; there will be a military uprising. You should stay in Fuzhou." Li Boji followed his advice. In February, bandits rose in the surroundings, and villages were burned, leaving Liu Wuyu. In the sixth month of the second year, Li Boji led the army in Changsha and passed through Shaowu, detouring to visit Ben. Ben sent him to Jianning and urged him to hurry, warning him as in Taiping. Again, Ben wrote large on the hall wall, "Burned east, burned west," and wrote seven seven numbers. Just as he left the territory, bandit Li Dunren from Jiangxi entered the town and set fire, exactly on the seventh day of the seventh month. Ben initially lived in Danxia, where a flying sparrow stood and transformed on the incense burner before the Buddha. The old man wrote a eulogy for the auspicious sparrow, and people believed it was the master's influence. In the sixteenth year of Shaoxing, Ben predicted that on a certain day he would depart. On the day, he departed without illness. Ben later became skilled in poetry, continued to accumulate wealth, and was especially stingy, viewing spending one coin as pulling out a tooth. His disciples often advised him, saying, "This is my karmic debt."

Blessed and Auspicious Unusual Skill Monk Huì jí, whose surname is Zhang, was from Yujian, Raoyang Prefecture. He was young and reckless and served as a county official. One day, because he was pursuing a debt collector in a village community, he rested briefly at the foot of a mountain. There, he encountered a woman riding a bamboo sedan chair, wearing nothing but a piece of cloth to cover her body. He was surprised by her beauty and the strange way she was dressed, so he bowed and asked her. She replied, "This is beyond your knowledge." She then gave him a scroll of writing and said, "Encourage yourself. You will become a monk in the future." After saying this, the sedan chair left as if flying. Two servants slowly walked in mid-air. Upon returning, Zhang could suddenly talk about matters hidden in people's hearts. He gave up his wife and children and went traveling. Passing through Yihuang County in Fuzhou, he behaved and stopped as if crazy, and no one knew him. At that time, there was a severe drought, and the county people made a clay dragon to pray for rain. Zhang submitted a petition to pray for himself, promising that it would rain by noon the next day. If not, he would burn his body as an offering. He sat cross-legged on a pile of wood. People who were light-hearted and prone to mischief competed to add more wood. By morning, the sun was intense, and thousands of people gathered to watch. They held torches waiting. As expected, it rained heavily, and all the surrounding areas were thoroughly soaked. The people of the county began to treat him with respect. Zou Bing lived in this county and hated that he was misleading the people.

Zhang went to see him. He said, "I have long owed you a beating. Please forgive me." It happened that Zhang was repairing the roads and paths for the people of the county. He collected several million coins. Someone slandered him to Zou, saying, "He embezzled half of it and sent it secretly to his wife and children." Zou, angry, reported him to the county magistrate. The magistrate arrested and flogged him. Later, he regretted it and went to Zhang to apologize. Zhang said, "I had already told you that before. There is no harm." In the third year of Xuanhe, he arrived in Shaowu, Taiping. He told the county official Huang Wenfu, "I and you were separated by life but were both monks on Wutai Mountain. You once fell ill, and I spent my medicine on you. Now I should have a place to stay as repayment." Huang built a hermitage for him on the top of Xianglu Peak. He bought a monk's certificate and shaved his head. The master could cure diseases with water, and people came from hundreds of miles away in a steady stream. Tongzhi Lang Ye Wu was the magistrate. He dreamed of a woman holding fire burning the hall and pavilion. She again burned the drum gate. He woke up in shock. By dawn, the master came to the county. Ye Wu greeted him and asked, "Were you not afraid last night?" Ye Wu was startled and told him the whole dream. The master ordered the wooden statue of the land god to be carried to the courtyard and chopped it. Blood oozed out. Initially, there was a malevolent spirit in the county, which transformed into a beautiful woman.

The night-duty officer was bewitched, and up to this point, the bewitchment has ceased. Jiang Dingguo, the county magistrate, had a mother named Lu Shi, who suffered from dizziness. Every time it occurred, her head felt as if it was dripping and could not be endured. She consulted the master. The master said, "There is no other reason; it is because of Yin'er's misdeeds." Dingguo was frightened and fearful. Yin'er was a former concubine of his father, whom Lu Shi secretly killed. Therefore, she begged for an exorcism and thanksgiving. The master drew hundreds of kinds of birds and beasts on paper, ordered him to hold a torch, and set up fruits and melons. The host and guest arranged themselves on a couch, instructing all family members to go to bed and not look back, leaving only one or two servants behind. By night, the master entered Lu Shi's room, searched for it, and found her in the dressing room. The servants all saw a beautiful woman, about sixteen or seventeen years old, dressed in a yellow skirt. She wept while facing him, as if unwilling to comply. The master slowly explained, and she eventually nodded in agreement. He then gave the drawings and paper money to her, sent her out the door. The next day, Lu Shi's illness did not recur. A wealthy man named Jiang Jingyuan once fought with someone over land and lost. He used a plan to kill him. Suddenly, he contracted a spleen disease and went to the master to ask for water. The master recounted his faults, and Jingyuan knelt and pleaded earnestly.

To the place where he lived, he ordered the digging of a pit about a zhang deep. They found a black dog with fangs bared and glaring, which frightened everyone. Upon closer inspection, it turned out to be a large stone. The master struck it with his staff, and it crumbled into pieces. Jingyuan was immediately cured. Another time, a former courtesan who had given up her profession and a scribe came to visit the master together. The master's residence was at the top of a mountain, surrounded by dense trees; visitors did not know they were approaching until they reached the door. The master warned his disciples in advance: "A couple will arrive shortly. Do not let their maid enter." When the two arrived, they had no maid with them. The master described the situation, and the former courtesan wept and begged for help. It turned out that in the past, she had forced a maid to jump into a well and die. The scribe was unaware of this. Once, while in the market, he saw someone being arrested and standing on the side of the road. He called the person forward, felt under his neck as if removing something, and said, "You must not do this again." People asked him why, and he explained that the person had lost a bet the previous night and hanged himself, but was saved. The master captured demons during the day and foretold many fortunes and misfortunes. There were too many to recount. He died in the fourth year of Shaoxing. To this day, people in Tai Ning paint his image, but do not call him by name; they only say "Zhang Gong" or "Zhang Monk."

The Standing Pen Peak Fifteen li east of Taian County lies the Standing Pen Peak. There is the Immortal Coffin Stone. It is said that in the past, due to wind and rain, the sky became dark and obscure. People heard music in the air. When they arrived, they saw a coffin in the rock. The place is sheer and inaccessible, and no one could climb it. People doubted that the immortal's bones were sent here, and it was called the Sound Mountain. It is also known as the Holy Stone. When there is a great drought, praying for rain is immediately answered. Jiang Yingshu, the governor of Fujian, passed by and wrote a poem about it, renaming it the Standing Pen Peak. In the fifth year of Xuanhe, there was again great thunder and lightning, wind and rain, and mist and haze blocked the sky. When the storm cleared, another coffin appeared beside the first, with a title and arrangement of planks that were not unlike those of ordinary people. The following spring, villagers saw carriages, horses, banners, and flags, riding and following, ascending on clouds to the place, making music and then departing. The music sounded cold and clear, not of this world. Villagers who could climb like monkeys once climbed the peak and said that the coffin did not use nails and could be opened to see. The bones were greenish-blue, and the burial items were all of ancient style. Only a small knife with a long, slender blade was found, similar to those used by ordinary people. They were about to take it down when suddenly a thunderbolt struck the cliff, and a large snake appeared, causing the villagers to be terrified and fall to the ground. Their bodies were unharmed, but they became mad, recovering after half a year. They told the villagers about it. These five events are all recounted by Huang Wenyu, a scholar from Shaoyang.

Zhang Qi's Envoy's Dream Zhang Qi, a Left Martial General and Governor of Rong Prefecture, was relieved of his military duties in Jiankang in the sixteenth year of the Shaoxing era. He was then stationed in Raozhou as the military commander of Jiangdong. Three years later, he fell ill. Zhang Qi had land in Jiande County, Chizhou Prefecture, which he entrusted to an imperial envoy to manage. That year, the envoy dreamed of several men in yellow robes who held a red-painted wooden tablet, announcing the arrest of Zhang Qi, the military commander of Raozhou, and Zhao Bo, the magistrate of Changsha County in Tan Prefecture. Upon waking, the envoy thought that Zhang Qi had been summoned by the emperor and went to Poyang to congratulate him. By then, Zhang Qi was already gravely ill and could not see him. The envoy's family feared that his dream was ominous and dared not speak of it. Meanwhile, Zhang Qi repeatedly asked his son if Zhao the magistrate had arrived. The son thought his father was delirious and did not dare to answer. After more than a month, indeed, Zhao the magistrate resigned from his post in Changsha and returned home. He arrived at Raozhou and stopped at the city gates, where he died in a boat. At that very moment, Zhang Qi also passed away.

Zhou Bin Studied the Book of Changes Zhou Bin, courtesy name Donglao, was a native of Fuzhou, Fujian. He was a fine scholar. Chen Lioweng married his sister to him. Zhou Bin received teachings in the Book of Changes from Chen Lioweng and seemed to have gained some insight. Chen Lioweng enjoyed participating in Zen discussions. When he saw Zhou Bin discussing the concepts of life and death, which were beyond the reach of Zen practitioners, he was greatly surprised. Zhou Bin passed away due to illness during the Xuanhe period. On the day before his death, he wrote a poem to his nephew Cai Shi, saying: "Uncle reports the impermanence. Zhou Bin walks the third among the nephews, their hands and feet in a hurry. Beat three black sticks skillfully. Boil a pot of soup until it's mushy. Dirt and grease are for you to wash. The body can be carried by anyone. Six nails make a silent sound. The path of a thousand years is vast and boundless." Pai Zhen Gained Insight into Three Aspects of Death Pai Zhen, courtesy name Ziyu, was from Min County. At the age of twenty, he followed the local scholar Zheng Dongqing to study the Book of Changes. Suddenly, he gained insight into the principles of life and death. His family lived at the foot of Gu Mountain. In the seventeenth year of the Shaoxing period, he heard about Zheng Qiao from Putian entering the mountains to consult with an old monk on Zen. Pai Zhen wrote a letter to Zheng Qiao discussing Confucian and Buddhist studies. Zheng Qiao, seeing his youth and profound views, suspected that Pai Zhen's ideas were not his own but borrowed from others. He personally went to inquire and found it even more astonishing. He then visited Zheng Dongqing and asked about Pai Zhen's studies. Zheng Dongqing replied, "I don't know." In the fourth month of the nineteenth year, Pai Zhen came to visit Zheng Dongqing and asked about the *Yugong* chapter of the *Shangshu* (Book of Documents). After falling ill, he returned home and his condition worsened. He ordered his wife to leave and called his younger brother Liu to inform him of his impending death.
