He instructed Liu to hold a pen and composed a poem orally, saying: "Wait for the fellow students to come and mourn. You may show them this poem." The poem read: "Life is not superfluous. Death has no lack. Move forward with the times. It is a different kind of scenery." He finished the poem and passed away.

**许氏诗谶** When the Grand Marshal Xu was still a scholar awaiting his success in the imperial examinations, he lived in the Zengpu Lane in Fuzhou. One night, a tiger broke through the city wall from the East Mountain, entered his garden, injured the pigs in the pen, and then left. By morning, the entire household feared it would return. However, the Grand Marshal did not consider this unusual, and he recited loudly, saying, "Last night, the tiger entered my garden. Next year, I will be the top scholar." His aunt playfully continued the poem, saying, "Mad dog, don't bark wildly. And stay low in the house." The neighbors passed this around as a joke. The following year, the Grand Marshal became the top scholar among all scholars and later entered the government. His aunt's son, in particular, received an official position as a high-ranking official due to the Grand Marshal's favor, and was known as Xu Gongbu. The old house where they had lived, the Grand Marshal gave it all to him. Later, Xu Gongbu developed a mental illness, and his family confined him, not allowing him to leave. The words "mad dog, stay low," which his mother had written, were indeed a prophecy. This story was told by Zheng Dongqing.

The *Eji Jia Zhi* Volume Ten, Nineteenth Tale The Elder Man from Tongcheng The elder man from Tongcheng County, Shuzhou, was rich and powerful in the village. He loved drinking and women. At the age of fifty, he contracted a wind disease, and his right hand and foot became paralyzed. He was carried to the same county's renowned physician Li Baiquan, a Taoist, for treatment. After over a month of treatment, his illness was cured. Before leaving, Li Baiquan invited him to drink wine. Halfway through the drink, Li Baiquan asked him, "Which is better, death or life?" The elder man was startled and said, "You are a doctor. Your profession is to save lives. How could you not know that death is worse than life? Why ask this?" Li Baiquan replied, "I thought you did not fear death. If you can understand how terrible death is, that is very good. You have now gained life from death. You should permanently abstain from sexual relations. If you do not repent, you will surely die again. We will not meet again." Upon hearing these words, the elder man had a great realization. As soon as he returned home, he built a thatched hut at the top of a mountain and secluded himself there, refusing to see his wives and concubines. He handed over all family affairs to his sons. After two years, he became as strong and healthy as a man in his thirties. He walked into the city on foot and could walk 120 miles in a day. When Li Baiquan saw him, he said, "You have indeed followed my advice. If you continue to adhere to it without wavering, although you may not become a god or immortal, you will certainly become a person of virtue." From then on, the elder man became even more diligent. He brewed a lot of wine. Whenever guests came, he would play chess and drink with them, engaging in clear and profound discussions day and night. He lived this way for twenty-five years.

During the early years of the Jianyan era, bandits rose in the Jianghuai region, and Li Cheng attacked the Huai West. The old man foresaw that they were about to arrive and told his sons, "Hurry to escape and you can still survive." Some of his sons were attached to their families and wealth and were still packing their belongings, unable to leave immediately. The old man then carried a staff and had several thousand coins in his belt, walking alone to the riverbank. The bandits were still far away, and there were still boats available to cross. He secretly hid in the temple on Dangtu Mountain. His sons did not have time to escape, and when the bandits arrived, they were all caught by their weapons. The old man was in the temple and was friendly with a passerby from a neighboring room. One day, he called the passerby and said, "I want to buy a coffin. Please go with me to get it." The passerby asked, "Why do you need this?" and smiled without responding. The old man then bought the coffin and brought it back, placing it in the room. He frequently wiped it clean and said to the passerby, "I will finally trouble you. I have stored firewood bought from the market behind the house. Please burn my coffin tomorrow. I fear that my family might come. Just tell them." The passerby was both skeptical and trusting, secretly observing what he did. At dusk, he lay in the coffin, covering himself with the lid. The next day, he was seen dead. He was seventy-nine years old. After a year or so, the old man's nephew also escaped from the bandits and searched for the old man's traces. It was only then that the temple learned of his death. The old man had a close relationship with the mid-level official Zhu Xinzhong, who was his relative. Zhu once recorded this story and gave it to me.

庞安常针 Zhu Xinzong, whose ancestral home was in Tongcheng, had a friend who was pregnant and about to give birth. After seven days, the child had not been born. All kinds of medicines and talismans had been tried, but they were of no use, and the woman was on the verge of death. A famous physician named Li Jidao happened to be staying at Zhu's residence. Zhu invited him to examine the woman. Li said, "No medicine can be applied here. Only acupuncture can be used, but my skill has not reached this level. I dare not take any action." He then returned home. Coincidentally, Li's teacher, Pang Anchang, passed by the door. Zhu invited both of them together to his house. Zhu explained the situation to Pang, saying, "Her family is too humble to trouble you, but human life is precious. Would you not spare a moment to save her?" Pang agreed and went with Zhu. As soon as they saw the pregnant woman, Pang called out, "She will not die." He instructed the family to warm the area around her waist and abdomen with hot water. Pang then gently rubbed her with his hands. The woman felt a slight pain in her intestines and, while moaning, gave birth to a boy. Both mother and child were unharmed. The family was overjoyed and thanked them profusely, treating Pang as if he were a god, though they did not understand how it happened. Pang explained, "The child had already come out of the womb, but one hand had accidentally grabbed the mother's intestines and could not be freed. That is why the medicine had no effect. I just happened to feel the position of the child's hand through the mother's abdomen and inserted a needle into his palm. When he felt the pain, he immediately withdrew his hand, which is why he was born so quickly. There is no other method." He then had the child examined, and a needle mark was found on the right hand's palm. Zhu Xinzong remarked on the marvel of it.

### Red Elephant Divination In the second year of the Shaoxing era, after the imperial examination, Dong Liangshi of Luzhou visited the Taoist master Red Elephant to perform a divination to determine his rank. He wanted to know his standing. After the divination was completed, a poem appeared: Black monkey pulls a long bow, Walks to the edge of the sky and stands. Tied child flies alone high, Average people sigh in envy. Dong Liangshi could not understand the meaning. The diviner said, "The meaning will only be clear when events unfold." When the names were announced, Zhang Zishao became the top scholar. Zhang was born in the year of the Monkey (Renshen), which is why the poem refers to the black monkey. The long bow represents Zhang's name. Dong Liangshi was in the third tier. Above him was Sun Xiongfei, who is referred to as the "tied child flying alone high." Below him was Zhong Bing, who is referred to as "average people sighing in envy." Dong Liangshi then understood the meaning. ### The Virtue of Tan's Wife Wu Qi, a man from Qujiang Village in Zhenyang County, Yingzhou, had some knowledge of books. His wife, Tan, was born in the leap month of the second year of the fifth year of the Shaoxing era. In the same month, bandits rose up in Guanyin Mountain and attacked the villages. Wu Qi fled, and Tan and her daughter were captured along with several women from neighboring villages. Among the captives, Tan was particularly fair and pure. The bandits wanted to take her as a wife. She scolded them, saying, "You are thieves! Government troops will arrive soon and crush you. I am a virtuous woman. How could I become your wife?" They forced her repeatedly, even beating her, but she continued to curse and insult them. Eventually, she was killed by their cruel hands. After the bandits were defeated, the women who were captured with her were all released. They said, "If Wu's scholar's wife had not insulted the bandits, she would have been released too." They then described in detail how she died. Wu Qi learned of her fate and was deeply moved by her virtue. Those who heard about her praised her high moral standards. I once wrote a biography about her.

Herbal Medicines Should Not Be Taken Lightly In the third month of the nineteenth year of the Shaoxing era, a monk named Xici from Yingzhou went to the Guangkou area, thirty li south of the state, to sweep the pagoda. A guest boat arrived from Panyu, and the servant of a scholar on board had weak legs and could not walk. The boatman felt sorry for him and said, "I have a medicine that treats this illness like a miracle. Those who take it and recover are too numerous to count. I will give it to you." After the temple sacrifice was completed, the boatman drank the sacrificial meat and became quite drunk. He went into the mountains to find the medicine, soaked it in wine, and gave it to the sick person, telling him to take it before dawn. As soon as the medicine entered his mouth, the sick person groaned and said, "My stomach and intestines are extremely painful, like being cut with a knife." He died before dawn. The scholar blamed the boatman, and the boatman angrily said, "How could there be this?" He immediately took the leftover medicine from last night, soaked it in wine, and drank it himself. He died within a short time. It is said that there are many plants that cut off the intestines in the mountains, and people who eat them die immediately. The medicine the boatman took was entangled with the roots and vines. He was too drunk to choose carefully and directly put it into the wine. This led to disaster. Thus, it is known that herbal medicines should not be taken lightly.

Nanshan Temple Zhang Shaozhang, also known as Zheng Liang, was from Yingzhou. During the Xuanhe period, he held the position of Right Literary Editor and Transport Commissioner for the Guangdong and Guangxi regions, accumulating wealth that made him the foremost in the Lingnan area. After being sent on official missions to these regions, he built a grand mansion in Yingzhou, decorated with colorful plaster, which was extremely luxurious and ornate, surpassing anything seen in the southern states. In the first year of the Jingkang era, someone reported his misdeeds to the court. The court dispatched Chen Shu, who held the title of Directing the Dragon Pavilion, to investigate Zheng Liang's affairs. Chen Shu arrived in Yingzhou, and Zheng Liang, unaware of the reason, warmly invited him. Chen Shu also treated him kindly and they drank together, forming a friendly relationship. Upon arriving in Guangzhou, Chen Shu sent someone to arrest Zheng Liang and investigate his corruption. Zheng Liang was severely punished, and the case was reported to the court. He was released from prison but died the next day. By the time the verdict was issued, Zheng Liang had already died, and he was only demoted and exiled. Zheng Liang's family could not bury him immediately, so he was temporarily interred at the Nanshan Temple in Yingzhou. A large amount of treasure was seized from Zheng Liang. Chen Shu then temporarily assumed administrative duties and returned to his position in the second year of the Jianyan era. Chen Shu was again impeached by Governor Xu for other matters and was sent to the court of justice, where he was removed from the official register and exiled to Yingzhou. The prefect placed Chen Shu in the Nanshan Temple. By then, Zheng Liang had been reburied, and the temple was empty. The monks told Chen Shu the truth. Chen Shu said, "I investigated his case for the sake of the state. He is already dead, what have I to fear?" He stayed in the temple, but after three or four days, he saw Zheng Liang in broad daylight, shocked and said, "How dare you come here?" and immediately fell ill and died. This occurred in the second year of the Jianyan era. Zheng Liang's mansion is now divided into the Tianqing Temple, the prefectural school, and the postal station. His family moved to Jiangxi. The monks of Yingzhou provided commentary on the three matters.

**The Verification of Madam He** Madam He was from Shuixin, Jizhou. She married Jiang Anxing, a scholar from her hometown, and had two sons. After her husband's death, she abstained from eating meat and recited the *Avatamsaka Sutra* every day, continuing the mourning rites without ceasing. When someone advised her to recite other sutras, Madam He said, "To know the true nature: it is originally perfect and enlightened. If one is not enlightened or not perfect, that is the state of an ordinary person. I do not recite sutras to cover my eyes and ears." Her eldest son, Ying, passed the *jinshi* examination. In the sixth year of the Shaoxing era, he served as the *qianpan* (deputy official) of Hezhou and brought his mother to the official residence. Madam He calmly spoke to her daughter-in-law, saying, "Since I began reciting the sutras, I have had no dreams. In the past, on the night of the year, I often saw a lion seated in a radiant light, and I wished to ascend to it but did not succeed. Now, even with my eyes closed in broad daylight, I see the image of the Buddha." On the day of the fifth month, Jia Xu of that year, she bathed and changed her clothes. The next day, after eating, she washed and brushed her teeth as usual. Suddenly, she raised her feet and sat upright, her two middle fingers forming a mudra, then she closed her eyes and passed away. Her family rushed to call a physician, but it was too late. The county governor, Fan Zhiqing, led his subordinates to pay their respects. He sighed, "A great man cannot be like this." He ordered the painter to draw her image. When the image was completed, the eyes had not yet been painted. He then prayed, "Her spirit is entirely in this. May you grant us an opening to see." Soon, her eyes gleamed brightly. Her descendants supported her and looked at her, all saying she had been reborn. After the eyes were painted, she closed her eyes once more. She was seventy-seven years old. This was recorded by Fu Yu Yanji. A Merchant of Changguo

During the Xuanhe era, there was a merchant from Changguo County, Mingzhou, who sailed to a large island and anchored his boat. Several of his crew went ashore to cut wood, but they were spotted by the islanders and hurried back to the boat. One man, who was still in the middle of his task, did not have time to get back and was captured. He was bound with an iron chain and forced to farm the land. After one or two years, he gradually became accustomed to his new life and was no longer bound. When he first arrived, the islanders prepared wine to entertain their neighbors and called this man to the banquet. They burned his thigh with a red-hot iron chopstick. Whenever he stamped his feet and cried out in pain, everyone in the hall would laugh heartily. When the villagers heard about this, they would always invite him to their feasts and use him as a source of amusement. Later, the man realized the intent behind this and, when he was being burned, he bit his teeth to suppress his pain and made no sound. The guests were no longer amused and he was finally spared from this torment. He stayed on the island for three years before finding an opportunity to escape and return home. His thighs were marked with scars like the lines on a tortoise shell. Zhang Zhao, who was the county magistrate at the time, reported the story to the authorities. The *Pan Gu* Stele's Misfortune In Jiyuan County, Mengzhou, there is the *Pan Gu* Stele, which was erected by County Magistrate Cui Jia during the Yuanhe era of the Tang Dynasty. Over time, it was buried and used as a well lining. In the third year of the Zhenghe era, County Magistrate Song Gong, while patrolling the area, discovered it. He cleaned it and exclaimed, "This is a treasure!" The villagers, thinking it was a real treasure, waited until Song Gong left and broke it. Finding nothing of value, they threw the pieces on the road. A man from Gao Mi named Meng Wenshu, who was the county magistrate, heard about it and had the pieces carried back to the county. He placed them in a hall used for administrative purposes, which had been built by County Magistrate Fu Junyu during the Yuanyou era. Qin Shao wrote a record of the hall and inscribed it on stone. During the Chongning era, the stele was taken away by onlookers. Meng Wenshu obtained an old copy from the people and had it re-engraved, but he did not reveal his name. He was also a well-intentioned and knowledgeable gentleman.

Meng Wenshu Meng Wenshu, the magistrate of Leizhe County in Puzhou, was respected by his subordinates who dared not deceive him. Once, a mute person submitted a blank document to lodge a complaint. Those around him were all startled. Meng Wenshu had the person bound and said, "He relies on his disability to mock me." He ordered two officials to escort him out and display him in the main street. He then secretly dispatched a cautious and honest person to observe the area, instructing, "Report anything heard." Indeed, someone reported that the mute person was employed by a certain household, had owed wages for many years without paying, and thus went to the magistrate to lodge a complaint. He simply could not speak. Now he has been punished, what use is there for a magistrate? The officials informed Meng Wenshu, who sent the informant to interrogate the mute person. Thus, the truth was uncovered. The entire county called him a god. Guo Shumi San Yi wrote the epitaph for Meng Wenshu, recording this event. Respect for Su Dongpo In the second year of the Shaoxing era, the rebel Xie Da captured Huizhou. The houses of the people and government offices were burned and destroyed completely, except for Su Dongpo's former residence with the white crane, which he and his followers repaired and maintained. They cooked a sheep to offer a sacrifice and then left. The following year, the pirate Li Sheng attacked Chaozhou, completely destroying the city walls and setting fire. When they reached the nearby residence of Wu Ziyi, Li Sheng climbed the tower of Kaiyuan Temple and saw it. He asked his subordinates, "Is this the place where Su Neishi (Su Shi) kept his books?" He ordered his troops to save it and then took care of the Wu family's Yearly Cold Hall. The nearby houses of the people depended on this and were not severely damaged. Both were notorious bandits, yet they knew and respected Su Gong (Su Shi) in this way. Those who wanted to burn his books, can they not feel ashamed?

**Ghost Calls the Scholar** Fan Tang, courtesy name Hongfu, was from Pucheng, Jianzhou. When he was a commoner, he had no food to cook by noon, and the villagers did not find this unusual. One cold night, after returning from the countryside to the county, he took a nap on a bridge by the stream. In the middle of the night, he heard people's voices coming out of the bridge, as if someone was asking about him. Someone replied, "The scholar is sleeping here." Fan Tang did not suspect it was a ghost and listened carefully. They all crossed the water. At dawn, Fan Tang returned to Pucheng. People called those who taught children "scholars," and this was what was meant by the term. Soon after, Fan Tang passed the imperial examinations and eventually became a scholar at the Dragon Pavilion. It turned out that on the night he stayed at the bridge, a family five miles away had set up a feast and called out to the scholar, who was actually a ghost. **The No Response of the Guard** Huang Jianke, courtesy name Songhan, was from Changxi, Fuzhou. During the reign of Emperor Gaozong of Song, he was appointed as the governor of Huizhou. When the welcoming soldiers arrived, a fortune-teller passed by the door and heard the sound of a servant saying "no." He told Huang, "The servant's voice does not contain the character 'earth.' You will not go to Huizhou." Huang did not travel there and indeed was not appointed. This story was told by Huang Wenyu. **Liao Yongzhong's Playful Poem** Liao Yongzhong, the imperial censor, was appointed as a recorder at the Imperial Academy at the beginning of the Chongning era. Later, he passed the imperial examinations. During the Xuanhe era, he was again appointed as a recorder at the Imperial Academy. At that time, Cai Jing was in power. Liao Yongzhong once wrote a playful poem for someone he knew well, which said, "Twenty years ago, I was a recorder at the Imperial Academy. Now, my official position is the same. How long will it take? To catch up with the Duke of Qi and Lu." People spread this poem as a topic of conversation. This story was told by Zheng Qiao.

**观音医臂** A woman from Huzhou suffered from a long-lasting arm ailment. One night, she dreamed of a white-clad woman who said, "I also suffer from this. If you can heal my arm, I will heal your arm." The woman asked, "Where does the lady reside?" The woman replied, "I dwell in the west corridor of Chongning Temple." Upon waking, the woman went to the city and told the monk Zhongdao about her dream. The monk thought for a moment and said, "It must be Guanyin. Our room has a white-clad statue of Guanyin. I accidentally damaged its arm." They led the woman to the room and saw that the Buddha’s arm was indeed damaged. The woman then instructed the monk to repair the arm. When the Buddha’s arm was restored, the woman’s illness was immediately cured. This story was recounted by Wu Jia from Huzhou. **李八得药** In the seventh year of the Zhenghe era, a man named Li Ba from Weitang Town, Xiu Prefecture, suffered from leprosy for three years. He tried countless medicines, but none worked. Suddenly, a traveling monk came and gave him a pill to take. Li Ba hesitated and said to his family, "This monk who stayed for a while left many medicines, but none of them ever worked. I won’t take this one." Before Li Ba fell ill, he had recited the Great Compassion Guanyin Sutra three times. That night, he dreamed of the monk who gave him the medicine and said, "You were willing to recite the sutra three times, but you wouldn’t take my medicine." Upon waking, Li Ba took the medicine. After seven days, the skin peeled off all over his body, and his beard and eyebrows regrew. This story was recounted by Wu Jia from Huzhou.

**佛还钗** The wife of Xu Shuwen, a commoner from Pingjiang, encountered the fall of the city to the Jin invaders. She managed to escape their hands and fled into the water, venturing beyond the city walls. All she did was chant the name of the Bodhisattva Avalokiteshvara. Worried that the gold hairpin in her hair might become a burden, she threw it into the water. Halfway through her journey, she lost her way. A white-clothed old woman appeared on the shore, calling out to her and instructing her to climb up. She pointed out the path and said, "Stop when you meet a monk." The old woman also mentioned, "I fear you have no shoes to protect your feet, so I will give you this gold hairpin." Upon seeing it, she realized it was the one she had previously discarded. She arrived at a grove and saw a temple, so she stopped there. It was the Temple of Blessings. The following day, her son-in-law, Jiang Shiyong, happened to meet her and took her back home. **佛救翻胃** The monk Huigong of Pingjiang suffered from a condition of reversed stomach, unable to eat or drink. One night, he dreamed that a weasel cat entered his body from his neck and back. From that day on, his condition worsened. Whenever he passed by the market and saw fish, he developed a strong craving. He then resolved to chant the name of the Bodhisattva Avalokiteshvara a million times and recite the Great Compassion Mantra 108 times each day. In another dream, he found himself in the mountains and met a Taoist recluse, who inquired about his well-being and said, "I will give you medicine." Soon, a green-clothed child arrived with a cage containing a chicken. The weasel cat emerged from the monk's mouth and directly entered the cage to catch the chicken. The monk was startled awake, and his illness was instantly cured.

### 欧十一 湖州民欧十一。坐误杀人。配广中。其妻在家斋素。日诵观音。欧在配所。见一僧。呼曰、汝家妻孥极念。汝欲归否。曰、固所愿。遂出药。擦其腕。初无痛楚。腕已堕地。血流不止。僧曰、可持以告官。当得归。收汝断手。勿失也。欧如言得放还。及中途。复见僧曰。汝断手在否。曰在。取而续之。吻合如初。三事皆李柽与几说。 Ou Shiyi, a man from Huzhou, was exiled to Guangzhong for unintentionally killing someone. His wife at home fasted and recited the name of Guanyin daily. While in exile, Ou encountered a monk who called out to him, saying, "Your wife and children are very much in your thoughts. Do you wish to return home?" Ou replied, "That is indeed my wish." The monk then gave him a medicine and rubbed his wrist. Initially, there was no pain, but soon his wrist fell to the ground, and blood flowed profusely. The monk said, "You can take this to the authorities and they will allow you to return. Keep your severed wrist safe." Following the monk's advice, Ou was released and returned home. On the way back, he met the monk again, who asked, "Do you still have your severed wrist?" Ou replied, "Yes, I have it." The monk then took the wrist and reattached it, and it fit together perfectly as before. All three stories were told by Li Chen and Ji. ### 夷坚甲志卷第十一十八事 ### The Eighteenth Story of the First Volume of Yi Jian Ji ### 梅先遇人 予宗人庆善郎中兴祖绍兴十二年。为江东提刑。治所在鄱阳。王元量尚书鼎从假二卒往夔峡。既回拜于廷。其一梅先者。独着道服。拜至十数不已。庆善讶之。答曰、伺郎中治事退。当请间以白。少顷庆善坐书室。梅复至曰、初至夔州数日。有道者历问所从来。令某随之去。某应曰、诺。道者曰、汝当有妻孥。安能舍而从我。某曰、惟一妻一子。今得从先生。视彼如涕唾耳。道者甚喜。曰、汝能若此。良可教。吾将试汝。 My relative, Qingshan Lang Zhong Xingzu, served as the Inspector of Judicial Affairs in Jiangdong during the twelfth year of the Shaoxing era. His seat of governance was in Poyang. Wang Yuanliang, the Shangshu Ding, borrowed two soldiers to go to Kuixia. After returning and paying his respects in the court, one of the soldiers, named Mei Xian, was seen wearing a Taoist robe and repeatedly bowing to the ground. Qingshan was surprised and asked him why. Mei replied, "I will wait until the inspector finishes his duties and then I will ask to speak privately." A short while later, Qingshan sat in his study, and Mei came again and said, "When I first arrived in Kuizhou a few days ago, a Taoist asked where I had come from and ordered me to follow him. I replied, 'Yes.' The Taoist said, 'You should have a wife and children. How can you leave them to follow me?' I said, 'I only have one wife and one son. Now that I can follow you, I regard them as tears and spittle.' The Taoist was very pleased and said, 'If you can be like this, it is good that you can be taught. I will test you.'"

In the dirt, he picked up discarded, broken shoes and was commanded to eat them. Initially, they were extremely foul and dirty. He forced himself to bite but could not swallow. The Daoist laughed and quickly ate them himself, saying, "Eating according to my method." After several days, he found that they no longer smelled foul and tasted increasingly sweet and soft. Then he asked what the purpose of coming here was. The response was, "Following the master's command to collect rent for the Minister Wang." The Daoist said, "If so, it should be completed. This public money if not settled, cannot follow me. It's not too late another day." The person replied, "My home is in the east of the river, separated by thousands of miles. How could I come again?" The Daoist said, "If you think of me, I will come." He then gave three prescriptions, saying, "If there is a shortage of funds, you can mix and sell these medicines. Keep what is used in a day, and discard the surplus on the road to benefit the poor and needy. If there is no food, then eat grass and wear shoes. If given food and drink, simply enjoy it."

It cannot be done with intention. In general, one must be without intention to attain the Dao. I bowed to him dozens of times. He also gave me Daoist clothing. He said, "When you return and see the Lord, bow to him as you would to me. Just wear this robe, do not change it." Qing Shan said, "If it is indeed like this, do not become a mere foot soldier." He commanded the immediate writing of a contract to bring oneself closer. Once he summoned me to sit and take off the straw sandals to test them. Mei stretched out his feet and sat on the ground. He cleaned the sandals and then ate. Every few days, he drank a little water. After a long time, he spat out the residue. The sandals were as smooth and glossy as green jade. He showed them to Qing Shan. Qing Shan returned them to him. Mei took them directly and put them in his mouth, eating them until he finished. At the time, he was twenty-four years old. He slept in separate beds with his wife. He said, "The world is like this, it is quite disgusting. Why not learn Dao with me? Otherwise, go wherever you wish." His wife initially tried to comply, but within a year, she remarried.

Qingshan was granted leave to retire. He was ordered to go to Mount Maoshan in Danyang to attend a gathering of cranes in March. The mountain has a cave. Ordinary people wishing to enter must carry a candle, but the cave is so short that they can only walk a few dozen steps before stopping. Mai took hold and entered without any obstruction. He heard sounds like someone reciting or practicing in the stone walls. Reaching the deepest part of the cave, he found a stream with water a few feet deep. Upon closer inspection, he discovered several scrolls. He retrieved them, which were only partially wet, and identified them as a talisman received by a Taoist priest named Liu during the Yuan佑 period. Later, he sent Qingshan back to Danyang. Qingshan's maternal uncle or brother-in-law was sick and would vomit after every meal. Mai said, "The medicine in the gourd is exactly for treating this condition." He gave a few grains to the patient to consume. After one day, the patient started thinking about food again, and after ten days, the illness completely disappeared. Mai left Qingshan's residence, inquired about a substitute, and obtained a document to remove someone from military records. He then departed. Several years later, he once returned to his hometown, but now no one knows where he has gone.

### The Report on Eating Crabs Hong Qingshan's aunt loved to eat crabs and usually prepared them with rice wine lees. One day, while she was eating, she saw live crabs scurrying about on a stool, which greatly frightened her. She called for a maid to remove them. The maid, being ignorant, took the crabs back to eat. One of the crabs clamped onto her cheek, and despite her best efforts, she could not remove it. Her cheek was pierced as a result. From then on, she no longer dared to eat crabs. ### The Dream of Tile Roofs Hong Qingshan's wife, Ding, was from Wenzhou. Although she lived by the sea, she did not enjoy killing. Later, she came to Jiangyin and received over a hundred tile roofs as a gift. Unable to bear to eat them, she placed them in a basin, intending to release them into the river the next day. That night, she dreamed of many beggars, naked and emaciated, each covering themselves with a tile in front and behind, all with happy expressions. A separate group of about ten others looked sorrowful and said, "You all seem very happy, but what a miserable life I lead!" Upon waking, Ding reflected on the dream and realized that the tiles covering their shadows must have been the tile roofs. She could accurately remember the number in the dream and checked the crabs. They had already been eaten by a concubine, and the number of those saved was the same as in the dream.

#### The Mysterious Cricket When Hong Qingshan was a professor in Huzhou, during the late autumn, he was sitting leisurely in the hall when he heard a very clear cricket chirping in the courtyard. He went to listen, and the sound seemed to be coming from outside the room. He then went outside the room, and the sound was now in the courtyard. He found this very strange. He sent someone else to listen, and the sound moved to under the bed. He then went to under the bed, and the sound was now near his daughter's bed. He could not figure it out. That year, his wife Ding died, and the following year, his daughter also died. #### The Official Transformed into a Dog Teng Sheng, a clerk in Huating County, Xiu Prefecture, was corrupt and had accumulated evil deeds over time. He always carried a small pouch where he wrote down all his plans. After he died, he dreamed to his family members and said, "I am now a dog at Xianshan Temple in Huzhou." His family members were shocked and sad, and they rushed to the temple to inquire. A dog heard the guests arriving and quickly hid under the monks' beds. They called out repeatedly, but it did not come out, seeming embarrassed. His family had no choice but to return. After they left, the monks told them, "The person from Teng Sheng's house has left." The dog wagged its tail and came out. This dog had a square-shaped hanging object under its belly, looking like a small pouch, with a leather belt tied all around its belly, still faintly visible. Hong Qingshan once went to the temple with Cao Changzhi, the Vice Minister, and saw it. They asked the monks, who confirmed it. #### Cai Heng Eating Raw Fish Cai Heng, a historical figure, was known for his love of raw fish. He often ate it with great enjoyment, finding it both delicious and refreshing. His fondness for this dish was well-known among his contemporaries, and it became a part of his reputation.

Cai You's son, Heng, served as a scholar of the Baohe Pavilion. He was about to enter the court when his family called him, but he did not wake up. They thought he was just sleeping deeply, so they requested leave for him. He did not wake up until the transition from the third to the fourth hour of the day. When he finally awoke, he told his family, "I was not sleeping; I went to the underworld." At the beginning of his sleep, someone said, "An official has summoned you," and he followed them. They arrived at a government office, and that person entered and reported, saying, "Pursuing Cai Heng has arrived." Once inside, a prison official asked, "What did you kill recently?" He replied, "My whole family avoids killing; there is no such thing." The official said, "Here, denial is not allowed." He then thought about it slowly and recalled that he had recently caught a fresh carp by the pond and eaten it as sashimi. "But this is the only crime," he said. The official confirmed, "Yes," and immediately took an iron hook and pierced his chin, hanging him between the trees. Several warriors cut off his flesh, and in a moment, it was all gone. About the time of a meal, his body returned to its original state. The chief official invited him to ascend to the hall, and with equal courtesy and clasped hands, he asked, "Do you know Baohe?" He replied, "I am a disciple of the Grand Tutor, Shen." The chief official then asked, "Is the Grand Tutor well?" Heng replied, "I just received punishment; the pain is not yet over. After a brief rest, I will tell you." The chief official ordered him to drink soup, and immediately the pain subsided. He then asked about his brothers and other matters in great detail. As he was about to leave, he prayed, saying, "Heng has done so much evil. I don't know how to redeem myself." The official said, "Sacrifice everything you own for a chance to save yourself. You can take all your clothes, belts, saddles, and horses, and donate them to Huilin Temple. Feed hundreds of monks to atone for your sins." On that day, Hong Qingshan happened to be visiting the temple and saw the chief monk, telling him, "This could serve as a warning." Not long after, Heng redeemed the donated items with six hundred thousand coins and eventually died that year. All six events were recounted by Hong Qingshan.

Li Bangzhi's Dream Sun Juyuan and Li Bangzhi studied together for the imperial examinations when they were young. During the Xining era, Sun governed the state of Haizhou, while Li served as the deputy governor. The deputy governor's office was connected to the state garden. Sun's youngest daughter often played in the garden, and Li saw her and watched her with his eyes. Later, whenever he went out, hearing her voice, he would always get out of his carriage and turn around. Lady Han, Li Bangzhi's wife, saw this many times from the window and asked him the reason. Li told her the truth. One night, he dreamed he came to the garden. He tried to follow her but could not. When he hurriedly chased her, he stepped on her shoes and also placed a flower in her hair. He suddenly woke up in a start and told Lady Han about it. Lady Han cried greatly and said, "The flower in her hair is a sign of a fixed fate. The shoes mean harmony. You will marry Sun's family. I will die soon." Li said, "It is just an extreme thought that entered your dream. How could it be so?" Soon after, Lady Han indeed died. Li then slowly asked a matchmaker to propose to Sun. Sun angrily said, "I and Li have been friends since we were young, and we are of the same age. Could my youngest daughter marry him?" Li dared not speak further. Later, Sun returned to court as a Hanlin scholar and fell ill, nearing death. A guest visited him and mentioned that Sun had not yet arranged a marriage for his daughter. The guest said, "Today, among the literati and officials, none is more virtuous than Li Bangzhi. Why not let him marry her?" Sun replied, "But their ages don't match." The guest said, "But if she finds a suitable match, why worry about other things?" Before they could arrange the marriage, Sun died. His family eventually married her to Li. Later, she was ennobled as Lady of Lu. Li Bangzhi wrote the epitaph for Sun's tomb, which stated that Sun had three daughters, the eldest married Li Gongyan, and the other two were still unmarried. This indicates that when the epitaph was written, the second daughter was not yet married. Li Bangzhi's biography was written by Chao Wujiao, and it is said that Li Bangzhi remarried Sun's family. This is the account given by Qiang Xingfu, who is known as You'an.

Zhao Dunlin, a student from Ningbo, visited the Temple of the Two Grand Officials to seek a dream in the year 1118. In his dream, he heard the message: "The top scholar of this year is just born." In the year 1155, Zhao Dunlin finally passed the imperial examination. The top scholar of that year was Wang Shengxi, who was born in 1118 and was 18 years old at the time. Indeed, the dream from 1118 was fulfilled.

The Daughter of Governor Zhang** In the eastern part of Nan'an City, there was a temple called Jiaoyu Temple. During the early years of the Song Dynasty, there lived the daughter of a local governor named Zhang, who had been married to a man who went to the southern regions and never returned. She died young and was hastily buried. At night, she would appear, and many people saw her. Over time, even the monks at the temple no longer found her appearance strange. When travelers passed by, she would always approach them. She would collect money or silk and leave it for the monks. Once, two military officers returning from Guangdong planned to stay at the temple. One of them knew about the apparition and did not want to go, but the other, being rather stubborn, decided to stay alone. As he was putting down his load, the woman appeared and said, "It is not easy for a high-ranking official to travel far." The officer was greatly frightened and tried to persuade her to leave, but she let him go. He immediately fled back to the city. The official Jie Qian, who had been exiled and died, had a grandson who was burying him. The grandson rested in the temple and was gradually affected by the apparition, falling ill almost to the point of death. In the year 1150, the governor Dou Shengyu led the magistrate of Dayu County to move the apparition to a place five miles away in the mountains. Now, it still occasionally appears and interacts with villagers. I once visited the temple, and the old monk told me about the events surrounding her death. **The Thunderstruck Officials of Dayu** On the last day of the second month in the year 1151, the magistrate of Dayu County, Lian Qian, was handling official business at noon. Clerks holding documents stood around him. Suddenly, black gas entered from the courtyard, and in a moment, the entire hall became dark. Thunder and lightning struck with great force, and all the clerks fell to the ground. The magistrate was very frightened, and his hands and feet grew weak, so he also fell to the ground. In a short while, the phenomenon dispersed, and the crowd helped the magistrate up. Four clerks died, two of whom were clerks and two were prison officials. It is said that they were all former land surveyors. The magistrate Lian Qian recounted this incident.

A Deceased Friend of Zhang Duanque Zhang Duanque, a native of Chuzhou, once practiced Daoism and was devoted to alchemy and the pursuit of immortality through the use of furnaces and fire. Initially, he set sail with a fellow townsman to Quanzhou, but the sailors intended to evade customs duties by sailing far out to sea. They eventually reached Fanyu (Guangzhou) and docked their boat. Due to the sailors' intentions, the two men were forced to stay longer than planned. His fellow townsman fell ill and died. Zhang prepared the body for burial and had it placed in a Buddhist temple. One night, as he was about to fall asleep, his friend appeared to him in a dream. The friend called out to him by name and said, "Zhang Duanque, you have a strong interest in alchemy and furnaces. What are you doing?" Zhang realized that his friend had passed away and responded, "I continue to pursue my own interests. Why should this concern you?" He then closed his eyes and silently recited the Great Compassion Mantra. After reciting only a few verses, his friend said, "I just came to visit. Why are you doing this?" and then left. After some time, Zhang had another dream in which his friend said, "We have been together for a long time, but now we must part ways. We will not meet again. Please accompany me for a few steps to bid farewell." Zhang agreed, and they walked together for a short distance until they reached a red bridge. His friend went ahead and said to Zhang, "You should stop here. This is not a place you should cross." They then parted ways, and Zhang woke up feeling confused. A few days later, the military governor Wang Chengke, a high-ranking official, issued an order that all bodies stored in Buddhist temples should be burned. Zhang, remembering his deceased friend, instructed a monk to conduct a proper Buddhist funeral ceremony with all the necessary rituals. After the ceremony, Zhang collected the bones and threw them into a river at a bridge. This was the same bridge he had seen in his dream. The events took place in the 18th year of the Shaoxing era (1148 CE), as recounted by Zhang Duanque.

**Six Carp Begging for Their Lives** Wang Tingjun, the Chancellor, during the Xuanhe era, served as the Director of the Imperial Works. Zheng Shen Dao was his colleague. One day, while sitting in the office, Wang Tingjun obtained six fresh carp and planned to prepare them for slicing. Zheng Shen Dao did not know this. Just as he was about to fall asleep, he had a dream where six figures stood at the foot of the steps, introducing themselves saying, "We are Mr. Li, begging for your mercy to save our lives." Zheng Shen Dao asked, "What crime have you committed?" They all replied, "It depends on your mercy." Zheng Shen Dao promised to help them. Upon waking, he informed Wang Tingjun. Wang Tingjun said, "I just got six carp and was planning to prepare them for slicing. Could this be why?" He then released the carp. Zheng Shen Dao never ate fish again. Deep Dao said.

### Five-Lang Ghost In Qiantang, there was a female witch named Si Niang. A ghost possessed her and was recognized as Wu Lang. Those who asked about good or bad omens received responses in human language from the ghost. Some asked about their ancestors, and the ghost verified the truthfulness of their claims. Even if the inquiries came from a thousand miles away, the responses were immediate and accurate, always matching the truth. Therefore, Prince Han of Xian'an and his brother Shiliang particularly believed in her. The prince ordered the medium to be summoned. When the witch arrived at Han's mansion, Wu Lang did not appear. The witch felt uneasy and restless, so she left. After several days, she accidentally arrived at Lingyin Temple, where the ghost immediately called out to her. The witch questioned why it hadn't responded to the summons earlier. The ghost replied, "The door gods prevented me from coming in, so I couldn't reach you." ### Su Dongpo's Copy of the Diamond Sutra When Su Dongpo lived in Huangzhou, he hand-copied the Diamond Sutra. His penmanship was most satisfying. However, he only completed up to the fifteenth section. He then moved to Linru and later entered the Yutang, where he could not finish the entire scroll. Soon after, the manuscript became scattered and lost. Later, he was exiled to Huizhou and thought the previous copy could not be found again. He then took the sixteenth section and continued writing. He placed the completed work in Li Shi's Secret Treasure Pavilion. Li Shaoyu, a political advisor, received the earlier part of the sutra but regretted that it was not complete. Wherever he went, he would look for the missing parts, hoping to reunite them. At the beginning of the Shaoxing era, he sheltered in Luofu and met Li Shi's son, Hu. Hu showed him all of Su Dongpo's writings, including the remaining parts of the Diamond Sutra, which he kept secret. Li Shaoyu did not know about it. One day, they happened to discuss it, and they compared the two parts. The size and height of the characters, the depth of the black ink, were not differing by a hair's breadth, as if they were completed at the same time. They looked at each other in amazement. Hu returned the work to Li Shaoyu, and thus the complete sutra was formed. According to Huang Wenyu.

Chancellor He Zhong When Chancellor He Zhong of Jinyun was a commoner, he was very poor. He was recommended to take part in the local examinations and was about to go to the capital. Without any means of support, he went to seek a loan from a wealthy family. The gatekeeper did not let him in. He presented his visiting card and sat upright, waiting for a response. The host was taking a nap when he dreamed of a black dragon coiling outside the door. He woke up in surprise and went out to look, and there was Chancellor He Zhong standing there. He asked, "Why are you here, Fifth Brother?" He was the fifth son in his family. "Fifth Brother" was what people called a scholar. He told him what he wanted. The host gave him a large sum of money and said, "When you return home in the future, whether successful or not, you must visit me." After passing the examinations, He Zhong returned to the host's home and was hosted in an outer hall. As evening approached, he held a book and stood leaning against a pillar. The host seemed to see a black dragon coiling down again, wrapping around a pillar. He looked closer and it was Chancellor He Zhong again. He was surprised and privately told He Zhong, "You will become very powerful in the future. Do not forget me." Later, He Zhong passed the examinations with distinction and was appointed as a judge in Taizhou. There was a fortune-teller who could predict fortune or misfortune by listening to sounds. He heard the drum and horn sounds at the city gate and said, "There is a noble person among them. Who will it be?" Some thought it might be the governor or deputy governor, but it turned out not to be. Over several days, no one matched the prediction. One day, He Zhong came out in a sedan chair, and the fortune-teller confirmed his prediction. He Zhong eventually became a chancellor through the favor of Emperor Huizong and was posthumously titled Prince of Qingyuan.

#### Pan Jun's Dragon-like Encounter Pan Jun, a wealthy man from Jinyun, was poor when he was young. He once engaged in trade in the city. As evening approached, it started to rain heavily. He hurriedly sought shelter at a house by the roadside and could not return home. He begged for a place to stay. He did not know that the house was a brothel. That night, the prostitute had a dream of a black dragon circling the left side of her door. In the morning, she saw Pan Jun lying under the eaves. She found this strange and invited him in, treating him with great respect. She wanted to have relations with him, but Pan Jun, considering himself very poor, refused repeatedly. Despite her insistence, he could not be persuaded. One day, she got him drunk and they consummated their relationship. From then on, she used all her wealth to help him, without asking about his expenses. Pan Jun used her money to become a merchant, and wherever he went, he made substantial profits. He accumulated wealth exceeding tens of millions. He married the prostitute and brought her home. They had a son who passed the imperial examination and became a county magistrate. His family remains wealthy to this day. #### The Fireman of Hengshan Temple A fireman at Hengshan Temple in Changzhou. During the hot summer months, he drew water from a well and found a piece of ice. There was a frog standing on it. He was just about to pick up the ice with his hand when the frog jumped away, and he ate the ice. He then stopped eating food entirely. Initially, he did not know how to read, but from that point on, he became enlightened. Later, he disappeared, and no one knew where he went. This happened during the reign of Emperor Huizong (1119-1125), as told by Li Mize. #### The Carp of Songjiang Wang Zijian of Pingjiang arrived at Songjiang on the eighth day of the fourth month. He bought fish and shrimp to release as an act of mercy. He caught a large carp to make sushi. The cook cut off the tail and removed the scales, but the head and belly remained intact. Suddenly, the carp leaped back into the river. Soon after, Wang Zijian asked for sushi. The cook reported this to him. Wang Zijian did not scold him, but he thought the fish was dead. The next year, he returned to Songjiang on the same day and caught another carp like the previous one, but this time the scales and tail were missing. The cook examined it and realized it was the same fish from the previous year. They ended up eating it.

**Volume Twelve, Fifteen Stories of the Yijian Zhi** **Ling Ji’s Accumulated Virtue** Ling Ji was from Nanjian. When he was young, he went to the capital. He arrived at Pizhou and stayed at a traveler's inn. He felt something crawling on his back between the bed sheets. He lifted the mat and looked at it, and saw a cloth bag containing a silk bag. Inside the silk bag were hundreds of northern pearls. The next day, he asked the innkeeper, "Who stayed here the night before?" The innkeeper told him that it was a wealthy merchant. Ling Ji said to him, "This is one of my old friends. If he comes again, please have him come to the academy to visit me." He then wrote his name on the wall of the room, saying, "On a certain day of a certain month in Jianpu, Ling Ji stayed here." He then left. The merchant arrived in the capital and tried to sell the pearls, but they were nowhere to be found. He hurried along the old route, searching everywhere. When he reached the inn in Pizhou and saw the sign, he immediately returned to the academy to visit Ling Ji. Ling Ji told him everything and said, "The original pearls are still here. However, you cannot simply take them. You can submit a petition to the government office, and they will be given back to you." The merchant followed his advice. Ling Ji went to the government office and gave all the pearls back to the merchant. The governor suggested dividing them equally. The merchant said, "That is what I desire." Ling Ji refused and said, "If I wanted them, I would have taken them yesterday. I have not taken even a single hair." The merchant could not force him. He donated several thousand coins to a Buddhist temple to hold a grand ritual to pray for Ling Ji's good fortune. Later, Ling Ji passed the imperial examinations and became a Middle Doctor. His son, named De Xin, became a Deputy Minister of Personnel.

The Lin Family's Prosperity** A man named Wu Gan from Suzhou, who held the title of Dianzhongcheng, completed the construction and plastering of his house. The next day, crane claw-like marks appeared all over the walls, resembling the character "Lin." After several months, strange occurrences began to happen, often until midnight, when several white-clad figures would weep and appear. Wu passed away, and his family moved elsewhere. A man named Lin Maoxian, who held the title of Daqing, bought the house from the same prefecture. He settled there for just one day and saw several dozen white-clad children in the courtyard. They disappeared when they reached the corner of the house. He then ordered the ground to be dug, and not even a few feet deep, he found several dozen silver children, all inscribed with the character "Lin." He sold them all, and from then on, he became very wealthy. **Thunderstruck Shi Baoyi** In the summer of the sixteenth year of the Shaoxing era, a heavy rain and thunderstorm struck Zhenjiang, wrecking houses and pulling down trees. Several dozen fireballs rolled on the ground. There were giants, too numerous to count, all over ten feet tall, wearing red clothing and blue sleeves, holding large axes. They entered a butcher's house, and the butcher died. They then entered several more houses, asking about the location of Shi Baoyi, a patrol officer at a relay station. They arrived at a military camp and found his residence. Shi was just holding his child when the giants flew away, and he died under the axe. This was said by Zhao Shan of Jiaoshan. **Warning Against Eating Eels** In March of the year Wu Chen in the Shaoxing era, a common person in Pingjiang, while drunk, ate eels and mistakenly swallowed a hook. The line was still near his mouth. He quickly tried to pull it out with his hands, but the line broke, and the hook could not be removed. He suffered extreme pain and almost did not survive. After ten days, he could finally eat.

**Jin Yun Ghost Immortal** A ghost immortal from Jinyun, Zhejiang. Her name was Ying Hua. She had an exquisite beauty, with soft and graceful skin, resembling a figure from heaven. She resided in the office of the magistrate's assistant. During the Jianyan period, a man named Qi Sheng, who was the cousin of the magistrate Wang Chuan, became intimate with her, their relationship akin to that of a married couple. The magistrate's family also occasionally saw her, questioning Qi about it. Qi laughed and did not respond. One day, while they were sitting together, the magistrate arrived, and Ying quickly entered the tent. The magistrate tried very hard to see her, but she said, "My appearance far surpasses that of ordinary people. If you see me, you will surely be tempted. You have a wife and family, and I fear that a rift could form. It is not like with your younger brother. It is absolutely impossible to see me." Not long after, the magistrate's wife fell gravely ill with heart pain, and many doctors were unable to cure her. Ying gave Qi a single dose of medicine, saying to give it to his sister-in-law, assuring him that she would recover. She said, "No medicine in the world could cure her illness. If you do not believe me, she will die." Qi first informed the magistrate, who asked, "Why would someone with an illness take medicine from a ghost?" Although his wife was gravely ill, she faintly heard the words and quickly took the medicine. She soon recovered, and the next day, she could walk on the ground. The entire household was greatly amazed. After a year, Qi left to return home, and Ying accompanied him to the outskirts of Lin'an (Hangzhou), saying, "The imperial city is full of divine beings, and it is not safe for me to enter." About to bid farewell, Ying cried and said, "We have been together for so long, I cannot bear to say goodbye. I foresee that you will die in battle. I will give you a stick of incense. Please keep it carefully. If you encounter danger, burn it."

I heard the fragrance of incense. It came to save you. But fate is already decided; I fear it cannot be avoided. After parting, Qi Sheng joined Zhang Wang Jun's army on the Huai River and fought against Li Cheng, ultimately dying in battle. After a long time, other bandits attacked Jin Yun, causing officials and people to flee. When the bandits left, a certain official named Zhong Feng occupied the office of the chief clerk, while the chief clerk himself lived in the mountains. Ying went to the mountains and asked the chief clerk's wife why she had not returned to the county, and she told her in detail. Ying said, "I can drive them away." She dressed up and went to Zhong Feng's house, pretending to be a maid of the chief clerk, and shouted harshly, scolding him. Suddenly, she disappeared. Zhong Feng was greatly frightened and hurriedly moved out. Once, a regional official came to the county, and he was majestic and awe-inspiring. Officials stood on tiptoe and sat upright in the hall. A woman walked slowly under the eaves, stepped up the steps, and went up, surprising everyone. They asked the attendants, but all dared not answer. When the county official reported matters, he said to them, "Your maids and concubines were not guarded, so they could come here." Everyone thought Ying was the solution and was very afraid. They prepared to leave immediately, later moving to the county magistrate's office. The county magistrate was influenced by this and followed the official document to inspect the boundaries, with Ying also accompanying him. The county magistrate died shortly after. The county magistrate Zhao Daozhi wanted to remove the harm and fasted for several days, preparing to present a petition to the gods. Ying already knew about it and told the magistrate, "I am not a low-ranking ghost; what can you do to me?" Suddenly, the fasting room shook, and everyone in the magistrate's family fell ill. So he dared not present the petition. It still exists to this day. Lü Qiu Ning and Sun Shuyong said.

During the Xuanhe period, a palace woman fell ill with delirious speech and wielded a blade, moving wildly and uncontrollably. The emperor ordered a Daoist priest from the Bao Lu Palace to treat her, but it was ineffective. He sought out all Daoist practitioners in the capital, but none could help. Consequently, she was locked in an empty room without food for several years. Later, a Daoist named Cheng came from Longhu Mountain, and his name was brought to the emperor's attention. The emperor summoned him and said, "It is absolutely not advisable to open the door. She is holding a blade and may harm someone." The Daoist requested to bring hundreds of guards with weapons and surrounded the room three times. He conversed with her through the door and threw a talisman for her to ingest. The palace woman laughed and said, "I have consumed many talismans. What can you do to me?" She then swallowed it. Afterward, she became somewhat calm and said, "This talisman. I have obtained it." The Daoist then opened the door. The palace woman continued to chatter incessantly, but she could not leave because she was controlled by the talisman. The Daoist drew a prison on the ground with a knife and wrote the character for "fire" in each corner. He shouted at her, saying, "What spirit are you possessed by? Tell me everything. Otherwise, I will burn you with fire." She refused to speak. He took fire and extended it to the corners. She then cried out, "Please be a little lenient with me. I will reveal the truth." The Daoist extinguished the fire in two corners. She said, "I am also a Daoist from Longhu Mountain. I died and became a ghost. I have always practiced alchemical spells, incantations, and talismans, so I can understand them. I did not expect the Daoist master to have a genuine talisman. Now I dare not stay. I wish to stay for a few more days before leaving." The Daoist angrily said, "In the palace, it is not appropriate to stay for long. You must leave quickly." He immediately reported to the emperor, saying, "This ghost, if not punished, will surely cause trouble elsewhere. Only I can deal with her." He bound straw into the shape of a person, wrote a memorial, and presented it to heaven. He then beheaded the straw figure. The palace woman then revived.

The Daoist Master of the Capital** In the capital, there was a Daoist master who lived outside the city. One night, he dreamed of a divine general who told him, "The Emperor has sent us five hundred of us to serve you day by day on your left and right, and to follow you in performing rituals and teachings." From that day forward, his power greatly increased. Once, while riding a donkey into the city, he saw a villager and quickly dismounted to speak to him. He said, "There is an evil spirit following you, and it must be driven away." He commanded them both to go to a tea house, where thousands of people gathered to watch. The Daoist master then sent the divine general to beat the villager. The villager cried out in pain, and after the beating, the Daoist master made him drink a talisman. The villager immediately returned to normal. An evil young man then said to the crowd, "You can only beat those who have spirits, but you cannot beat me." The Daoist master became furious and ordered the divine general to beat the young man two hundred times. The young man cried out just as the villager had, and then apologized before being released. Shortly after, the Daoist master dreamed again of a divine being who told him, "The Emperor has ordered that you have been beating ordinary people without cause, and I have been instructed to take away all the divine generals." Upon waking, his power ceased to function, and he fell gravely ill, nearly dying. These two stories were told by Qiang You'an. **Quick Thinking in an Emergency** In the household of a scholar-official in Xiuzhou, there was a five-year-old boy. While playing, he stuck his head into a mortar used for grinding herbs and could not get it out. The whole family was at a loss. Someone suggested holding the boy's feet and pouring fresh water quickly over him. The boy cried out in surprise and shrank, and he was able to get out. Another boy was watching the threshing of rice and put a grain stalk in his mouth. It stuck to the area between his throat and tongue and could not be removed. Someone then ordered that the saliva of a goose be poured into his mouth, and it came out. It is said that the saliva of a goose can dissolve grains. Both of these incidents were very urgent, and without quick thinking, it would have been difficult to resolve them. These stories were told by Wen Ren Maode.

#### Wang Yanzhang's Preface In Qiantang, Guan Jingren, also known as Zikai, served as a tax official. His subordinates accused him, and the local governor had him imprisoned in chains. Dong Zhu, one of Zikai's disciples, went to Kuaiji to report the emergency to Vice Minister of War Wang Yanzhang. Wang quickly sent a letter instructing the governor of Hangzhou, and the matter was eventually resolved. Zikai intended to write a letter of thanks to Wang, but he died before the letter could be delivered. Dong Zhu delivered the letter on his behalf. Wang wrote in the margin, "Releasing the carriage horse of Yanzi, this act was once extended to a wise person. Hanging the sword of Xu Jun, I now feel moved by this writing." #### Liuhe County School In Zhenzhou, Liuhe County, after the turmoil of war, the school buildings were completely burned down. The students together built several thatched houses to live in. After some time, they discussed the idea of moving. Originally in the county, there was an abandoned temple. When the bandits had ceased, a monk spent effort and begged for money to manage the temple. Once, he took stones from the outskirts and found two large, quite flat stones. He placed them on the ditch in front of the hall, like a bridge. Over several years, the temple was nearly completed, but the head monk died, and no one continued. The county then used its site as a school. When gathering workers to repair and renovate, they removed the stones from the ditch. On the reverse side, large characters were carved, reading "Liuhe County School." No one knows what time period this was. Thus, this temple was meant to be a school, as if by destiny. This account was provided by Cui Yan, a local resident.

High Jun's Descent into the Underworld Once upon a time, when Mr. Dongpo was residing in Dan'er, there was a young woman who fell ill and died. She later revived and reported that she had been chased to the underworld. The people who were bound there were mostly from Dan'er. Recently, there was a similar incident involving Gao Jun, a soldier stationed in Kuizhou. Could this not be the so-called underworld, where each place has its own affairs, entrusted to people to transmit, serving as a warning to the world? Gao Jun was from Suizhou, where his family had served as soldiers for generations, and he was part of the Xiongwei Army. On the day of Xin Hai in the first month of the 22nd year of the Shaoxing era, he climbed a high mountain in Kuizhou. There, he encountered a man with disheveled hair holding a staff. The man showed Gao Jun a talisman and said, "I have been ordered to pursue you." Gao Jun was terrified and hurried back home. The man followed him relentlessly. When Gao Jun reached home, he threw a food container at the man. The man, enraged, strangled Gao Jun's throat, causing him to fall to the ground. Gao Jun immediately felt himself being dragged westward. As they walked, the man continued to show him the talisman, which had several large lines of writing and a signature at the end, which Gao Jun could not read. After walking for a long time, the path was dark, but suddenly it became bright. They saw a city with severe walls and very high iron doors at the four corners.

A Woman with Two Heads and Four Arms During the reign of Emperor Ling, a woman from Luoyang was born with two heads and four arms. In all writing, it is like riding a swift steed — though it may possess a wild and unrestrained spirit, one must control it with bit and bridle, and must not let it run wild, straying from the proper path or indulging in aimless wanderings. From ancient times, wise kings and sage emperors still required diligent study; how much more so, then, for common people! This matter is found throughout the classics and histories. I cannot elaborate further, but I will briefly mention a few essential points of recent times in order to enlighten you. As for writings, their origin lies in the Five Classics: edicts, memorials, and military proclamations arise from the *Shujing* (Book of Documents); expository and argumentative writings arise from the *Yijing* (Book of Changes); songs, odes, and praises arise from the *Shijing* (Book of Poetry); ritual sacrifices, mourning, and elegies arise from the *Liji* (Book of Rites); memorials, admonitions, and inscriptions arise from the *Chunqiu* (Spring and Autumn Annals). Court regulations, military oaths and decrees, the proclamation of benevolence and righteousness, the elucidation of virtue and merit, governing the people and establishing the state—all are indispensable. As for cultivating the mind and spirit, offering gentle admonition in a relaxed manner, and savoring the flavor of such writings, these too are pleasant pursuits. When one has spare energy, then one may study them. 四顾廛市列肆。如一郡邑。其中若大府。两庑囚系几满。一女子悬足于桁。吏曰。前生妄费膏油以涂发。故悬以沥之。又一女反缚。以钳钳其舌。吏曰、生前好摇唇鼓舌者。后所识宁江都将。荷铁校。曳铁锁。狱卒割剔其股文。血肉淋漓。形容枯瘠不类人。左右破脑者。折胫者。折肱者。穴胸者。百十人环守之。吏曰、生前贼杀无辜者也。一部将亦同系。棰掠无全肤。次则市之鬻面者。曰冉二。死已数年矣。前烈一大瓮。畜腐水败泔。其七已空。吏曰、是尝弃面与水浆。今积于此。日使尽三柸。 A Woman with Two Heads and Four Arms During the reign of Emperor Ling, a woman from Luoyang was born with two heads and four arms. There was a candy seller named Huang Xia'er, who also served as a prison guard. He kindly asked Jun, "When did you arrive?" Along with Jun, there were about three hundred others from the same department being pursued. Zhao Hong, the magistrate, had died the previous night and was aimlessly wandering in the courtyard. The high-ranking official with a yellow sash called out to Jun from the hall, "What is your birth date and time?" Jun replied, "I am twenty-five years old. I was born on the twenty-fourth day of the sixth month at the third watch." The official checked the records and said, "The ones I am pursuing are those born in the same hour. Have Jun wait here for further instructions." He then questioned the others in the same manner. Some were immediately ordered to wear wooden collars and driven eastward, while others were locked up in the wing. A strong man in golden armor holding an axe stood in the courtyard. Jun bowed and saluted, saying, "The official has detained me, but has not given me any instructions. What should I do?" The official said, "I will go inside and report on your behalf." After a short while, he came out and said, "You may go." He instructed a child to hurry and accompany Jun, warning that if they were buried, it would be too late. The child led Jun back along the path he had come, and they reached the darkest part of the journey.

Suddenly, he lost the child. He looked westward and proceeded. He climbed a mountain almost several miles up. Below, there was a river, and those who drowned could not be counted. Officials sat on the bank, ordering soldiers and servants to push passersby into the river. Those who entered were devoured by fish and dragons. Those who could cross and reach the shore were only one or two in a hundred. He became increasingly terrified, running to the heavy hills, then traveling east to the plain. Two paths crossed, and he did not know where to go. He rested on the riverbank, waiting for someone passing by to ask for directions. A dog came to pull at his clothes, and he hurried to the left path, about seven miles. He lost the dog again, proceeding alone over the front hill, reaching a large stream. Just as he crossed the bridge, it collapsed. A rider came afterward, urging to fix the bridge and shouting, "Hurry and repair the bridge." Soon, there were four or five people carrying large logs across the stream, but the rider could not cross. Jiuyu quickened his pace further, after some time, he reached the eastern ferry of Kuai. Looking at his body, he was naked. Some scolded him, beating his back. He then woke up in shock, having been dead for two days. His family was just planning to bury him. Chao Gong Su wrote this account. ### Rat's Destruction of the Sutra as Retribution A monk named Yougui from Ruyun Temple in Taiping, Shaowu, adhered to the precepts very strictly. Once sitting in the abbot's quarters, a few newly born rats fell in front of him. Upon close inspection, they had no feet. He ordered to fetch a ladder to explore the hole. It turned out the mother rat had used the Diamond Sutra to make a nest. This is the retribution he received. Dong Wenyu reported this.

Chanting the Titles of the Heavenly Venerable to Stop Fear Chen Jiruo said that he often dreams. He was fearful and unable to sleep alone. Every time he fell into deep sleep, he would be startled by nightmares. He was very troubled by it. He dreamed of someone instructing him: "Just hold the titles of the Primordial Heavenly Venerable and the Sacred Treasure Guardian of Life Heavenly Venerable." He was to burn incense and chant the two titles each morning when he wakes up, repeating each thirty times. After a long time, it will be beneficial. As he said, not within a year, fear no longer arose. Even sleeping alone in an ancient inn at night, he no longer suffered. Up to now, he has not been lax. The Monk Becomes a Woman The monk named Shanmin, from Changsha, served as abbot of the Guanyin Temple in Hongzhou. Later, he retired to the West Hall of Guangxiao Temple. In the autumn of the twenty-third year of the Shaoxing era, he fell ill. Dong Shu from Poyang was a Deputy Magistrate of Household Affairs, temporarily serving as the Magistrate of Xinjian. Living near the temple, he pitied his illness and prepared porridge and medicine for him daily. Shanmin always thanked him repeatedly after each meal. The abbot of Guangxiao Temple, Zuxuan, mocked him and said, "You are a person outside the world, and yet you are cared for by a layperson like this. You have a strong desire to repay his kindness. According to our Buddhist teachings, you will surely be reborn as a child of the Dong family." Shanmin was moved by his words but could not control himself. At that time, Dong's wife, Wang, was pregnant. Zuxuan secretly worried about it. And Dong provided food morning and evening, his feelings were as if he were a close relative. Shanmin's illness worsened. He died at the hour of Si on the second day of the tenth month. The temple was just ringing the bell and chanting Buddha. Those who entered from outside said, "Dong's wife gave birth to a daughter." Comparing the time of her birth, Shanmin just died, they said. The girl died after several months. Zuxuan said.

Ancestral Temple of the Xiang Family The wife of Xiang Ziqian, the nephew of Empress Gao, the esteemed and virtuous Lady Zhou, was a woman of great virtue. When she first married into the Xiang family, she felt that she had not been as well cared for by her in-laws, so she devoted herself to showing utmost filial piety in the ancestral temple, performing the rituals of visiting her parents as if they were still alive. She never neglected these duties for a single day. During the seasonal festivals, especially the winter solstice and New Year's celebrations, she personally took charge of cooking and cleaning, doing everything herself. During the reign of Emperor Huizong (1111–1118), she lived with her husband in a house in Kaifeng, the capital of the Song dynasty. One day, she fell ill and had a vivid dream in which she clearly saw five or six people, figures like those painted in temples of the world. One of them took the box she wore, took out a small piece of paper, and wrote all over it. The characters were not recognizable. Then the paper broke and became a pill, like the medicine she took. The figure took the soup from the table and advised Zhou, saying, "Take this and you will be safe." Zhou took the pill without doubt. When she woke up, she immediately felt a choking sensation in her throat and could not swallow food or drink. Her condition was almost fatal. Zhou thought to herself that this was beyond the ability of a doctor.

While the world at large practices rituals to avert disasters, I have never believed in them. Instead, I quietly prayed to our family temple for protection. A few days later, after taking medicine, I vomited severely and could only start eating porridge. Gradually, I returned to eating meat. After some time, I had a dream in which a celestial official rode in a carriage with a feathered canopy, slowly descending from the sky. The official's entourage was grand, and he ascended the hall and sat down. He took the previous five or six ghosts and beat them in the courtyard, as if interrogating them. The ghosts presented the medicine given by the doctor and the leftover porridge and meat, each holding and presenting them. The official said, "This is all I see." Among the ghosts, one who had written on paper and made pills trembled with fear, retrieving the pills and writings from the spittoon. Unfolding it, it revealed a small scroll, with the writing clearly the same as before. The official ordered the execution of documents to bind the ghosts and send them to prison. He then slowly adjusted the carriage and departed. I woke up feeling relieved and walked on the ground, returning to normal. Later, I lived to be seventy years old. The celestial official was the divine spirit of the family temple. This story was told by Zhou Zhongzi Fang.

The *Yijian Jia Zhi* Volume Thirteen, Eighteenth Tale **Divination by Di Chēng** Di Chēng, the grandson of Di Wǔxiāng, obtained the *Fèi Xiàoxiān's Book of Divination* and began selling divination in the marketplace. Xiānglín Xiàng Bògòng's son, Yīn, who had retired and was appointed to the position of Deputy Minister of Finance, commissioned Di Chēng to write a divination image. The image depicted a large boat sailing on a clear river, with singing and dancing women aboard and many flags and attendants leading the way. On the shore, there was a long pole with a flag at its top, fluttering in the wind. The accompanying poem read: "By the water, the pole is dangerous. Dividing the talisman, he received special favor. The tide recedes, and the waves are like a mirror. Let me entrust your body to it." Xiàng Yīn read the poem and was very pleased, believing he would surely be granted permission to retire and return home in a peaceful manner. However, he did not fully understand the meaning of the divination. One day, he stayed in the palace for a long time to divinate. The Secretary of the Central Secretariat, Pān Zǐjiàn Liángguì, served as the recorder and stood beside him. Pān called out in front, saying, "It is late, and I fear it is tiring for Your Majesty to listen." Xiàng Yīn stepped back, but the emperor's words were not yet finished, and he did not stop. Pān returned to his position, and a little later, he came out again, saying the same as before. Xiàng Yīn then hurriedly went down. The next day, they awaited punishment, but the emperor treated them equally. Later, they each requested to be transferred out. Xiàng Yīn submitted a second memorial and was appointed as a scholar-official to govern Pingjiang Prefecture. He was granted an official rank for more than three months and strongly requested to retire. He was granted a favorable edict to advance his rank and return home. Finally, he fully understood the meaning of the divination. The pole by the water referred to Pān Gong. And he was appointed to govern a neighboring prefecture. The emperor's favor became even greater, as indicated by the line, "Dividing the talisman, he received special favor." The tide receding referred to returning from the court, and the waves being like a mirror referred to Pingjiang. Let me entrust your body to it meant temporarily residing in the prefectural office, ultimately returning to rest. Jiá Cìnán said.
