In the year 1138 of the Southern Song Dynasty, Chancellor Lu lived in Taishan. His brother-in-law, Li Xiuwu, was staying at a guesthouse in Kuaiji owned by the Yu family. One day, while Li was having a meal with his wife, a soldier arrived with a letter from the Chancellor. Li took the letter and began to read it. The soldier said, "The Chancellor's deputy, a certain Commander, is already at the Great Good Temple. He has sent me to invite you." Li agreed and said he would come. However, he took too long to change his clothes and did not leave for a while. His wife went to look for him and found him in the garden, waist-deep in the pond water. She immediately called the servants to rescue him, and they managed to save him. When asked what he had seen, Li said, "Just now, I was drinking plum blossom wine with the Commander, and we were enjoying ourselves. But then you all came and took me away, so I couldn't finish my meal. It really spoiled my mood." Around the pond, there were dozens of peach and plum trees. They checked on the soldier, but he had already disappeared. About half a month later, someone came from Taishan and said that the Commander had died several months ago. The soldier who delivered the letter was the one sent by the Chancellor to the Li family. He had died on the way to Sheng County, and when the body was examined, the document was found, but the Chancellor's letter was missing. That day was the same day Li received the letter. The dead soldier managed to deliver the letter to the living, which is indeed a strange occurrence. This story is told by Fu Shixiu.

Fú Shìxiū's Dream Fú Shìxiū, a man from Kuaiji, failed in the local examination. He dreamed of entering the provincial examination hall and was given the topic "On the Virtue of the Emperor and the Stars." The next night, he dreamed the same thing again. In his exam paper, there was a large hook drawn, with a bearded dragon beneath it. The dragon used its claw to obscure the work of Li Bisheng, covering five or six sheets of paper. Fú found the dream somewhat unusual and recorded it. Three years later, he passed the local examination. The following year, during the provincial examination, the topic was "The Great Virtue of Heaven and Earth is Life," and he was asked about horse management. He passed the exam and finally understood his previous dream. He explained to himself: "The virtuous one refers to the emperor; the stars are called life; the horse in the scroll refers to horse management, but I did not understand the meaning of the bearded dragon." After being named as a successful candidate, he paid his respects to the examiner and met Gou Long Ting Shi, the proofreader. Gou told him that his exam paper was among those selected. Gou had a majestic appearance and a full beard, and Fú finally understood the meaning of the dream. This was during the twelfth year of the Shaoxing era.

A Dream of the Birth of a Child in the Fan Family Fan Guojun, a man from Quzhou, recounts the events of the Jianyan era, specifically the year 1128 CE. His father, Fan Cha, was appointed as the Censor of Xuanchou Prefecture, replacing a local person named Xu Changyan. Fan Cha was due to take up his official duties in August of the following year. On December 7th of that year, Fan had a dream in which he saw that on the 25th of the same month, someone from Xuanchou had died and brought a letter to welcome him. In the dream, the person was holding a small child and bowing in the courtyard. Fan was surprised that there were no ceremonial items accompanying the person. The person replied, "There were bandits on the road, so I dared not bring any vessels. I only have a green canopy and several sedan chairs." Fan asked why the person was holding the child, and the person said, "There is no wife in the house. This is only one son, and I love him, so I brought him along." The next day, Fan informed his father about the dream. His father said, "I was thinking about taking up the official post, so the dream was like this." At that time, Fan's wife, Cai, was pregnant and was due to give birth in January. Before the end of the year, she suddenly suffered from abdominal pain and was about to give birth. Just then, Zhang De, who had been sent from Xuanchou with a letter from Xu Changyan, arrived. The letter said that Xu Changyan had received a ceremonial salary and returned home to meet. Soldiers came, and Fan looked at the person, who was exactly like what he had dreamed. However, the person was not holding the child. When Fan asked about the items the person had brought, the answers were the same as what he had said in the dream. By evening, Cai gave birth to a son. After a month, they both went to Xuanchou. Zhang De came to report and said, "When I was assigned, I had a son who was only six years old. Because there was no mother, he was left with his aunt to raise. Now that I have returned, he has died." When asked about the timing, it was the same as when Cai gave birth to her son. Therefore, the auspicious sign in the dream probably meant that the child should be the son of the Fan family.

Yang Datong Yang Datong was a man from Huazhou. Before he passed the imperial examinations, he followed his elder brother on official business. Once, while playing with his elder brother's young son using a sedan chair as a toy, the child had already gotten off the sedan chair. Yang pulled aside the curtain and saw a woman holding a young girl sitting in the sedan chair. He was greatly surprised. Immediately, he gave the brother's son back and hurriedly went out to the outer room, thinking about a way to entice her. In an instant, the woman was already in the bedroom, smiling and saying, "I am here waiting for you." Yang then engaged in intimate relations with her. When he asked her why, she said, "I am a woman from a certain family. My husband has been away on official business for many years, and I have been living alone. Therefore, I have come to follow you. Do not reveal this or marry another. Although I have been here for a long time, no one knows about it." From then on, they lived together, sharing meals and sleep. After several months, Yang's complexion became increasingly pale and gaunt. His elder brother's family began to suspect something was amiss. They had also heard the sound of someone in the bed at night, suspecting something sinister. They called a Taoist priest to use the *Tianxin Liu Ding Fu Lu* talisman to ward off evil spirits. The woman suddenly transformed into a frightening appearance and wanted to kill Yang. Yang cried out in distress and begged earnestly, saying, "Please don't kill me now." The woman then returned to her normal appearance.

In the early years, a young girl wept and left, saying, "I am your wife from three lifetimes ago. This girl is your daughter. You went to another state as a merchant and were attached to a courtesan, forgetting to return. I was poor and could not support myself, so I took this girl and jumped into a well to die. I appealed to the Emperor, who ordered the Heavenly Court to decree, 'You pursued profit and forgot your family, causing your wife and children to die by misfortune. Even though you have other good deeds, you should pass the imperial examinations, but you will not be able to enjoy it. You will receive retribution over the next ten years. I have not yet severed our past connection, so I came to seek reconciliation. Now that my time is up, I must leave, and I will also be reborn from here.' As soon as she left, she was no longer seen. No one knew about her previous life." In the fifth year of the Shaoxing era, Yang passed the imperial examinations and was later appointed as an official in Guangxi. He was involved in matters in Liuzhou and visited the Temple of Literature. The temple priest asked him to enter, but Yang refused. The priest said, "If you do not enter, the god will be angry." Yang shouted at him and went directly to see the governor. Before he finished drinking soup, the bowl fell from his hand, and he died. It was said that he was angry with Lord Liu, but no one knew about his previous life. This event occurred exactly ten years after the girl's death, as recorded by Fu Shixiu. Dong Bai'e In the village of Baishi, LePing county, Raozhou, there was a man named Dong Bai'e who made a living by trading cattle. He had killed countless cattle. In the autumn of the twenty-third year of the Shaoxing era, he fell ill. Whenever he had an episode, he needed to be tied to a pillar with ropes around his head and limbs, and then beaten with a stick until he was in pain. Only then would he feel relieved and forget about his illness. This continued for seven days before he died. Dong Bai'e had always killed cattle using this method. His death was no different from that of the cattle he had killed.

Dewu Snake Eggs In Huizhou Prefecture's Wuyuan County, on the third day of the seventh month in the twenty-third year of Emperor Xiaozong's reign (1153), a great thunderstorm occurred. In the county, there was an ancient tree with a circumference of several zhang. The tree was struck by lightning and split into several sections. Inside the tree, over ten dou of snake eggs were found. Some people broke the eggs, and each shell contained a creature that was twisted and resembled a eel. Chickens and pigs died when they ate the contents, and common people who ate the dead pork also died. The eggs were about the size of a bullet, like small oranges. Fifteen li away from the county, a giant python was also struck and split at the same time. All suspected it to be the mother of the snakes. My clan member, Bangzhi, was then the county magistrate. He once broke the eggs and confirmed that they were indeed so. Lightning Strike on a Vang Family Woman In Wucheng County of Wuzhou Prefecture, there was a woman named Zheng Hengzhong, a government official. She had three unmarried daughters, who were sisters. The eldest was eighteen years old, the second was fourteen, and the third was twelve. On the sixth day of the second month in the twenty-fourth year of Emperor Xiaozong's reign (1154), the family had a gathering. The three girls went to watch. After the gathering, relatives gathered to play games. Suddenly, a heavy rain and thunderstorm struck. The three girls left and took a shortcut through a small house to return home. Before they arrived, the fire went out. They stopped at a small pavilion. A relative sent a maid with a lamp to check on them, and found that they had all fallen to the ground. One of them had already died from the lightning strike, lying naked in the rain, with her clothes sticking to the pillars. Another was half burned, with her clothes all torn. The third was unharmed. She was carried back home and regained consciousness the next day. When asked about it, she said, "While I was walking, suddenly everything went dark, and I saw nothing. I was shocked and fell asleep." The others did not know what happened. The one who was half burned could speak after a few days, but she did not die. This was reported by Liu Banghan in Xuancheng.

Zhang Sheng Enters the Underworld Zhang Sheng, a native of Quzhou, was a medical officer at the Imperial Military Council during the Xuanhe period. After living in Huzhou for several years, he once traveled to Lin'an (Hangzhou). In his carriage, he encountered a messenger carrying a document. Upon reading it, he found it to be a summons from the underworld, listing over twenty officials and nobles, with Zhang Sheng at the end of the list. After finishing the document, he became disoriented and dizzy. Returning home, he fell ill. The messenger arrived, called him, and led him away. The path they took was half bright and half dark, like during a lunar eclipse. They arrived at the underworld at night. The messenger went in first, and Zhang Sheng peered through the window. He saw prisoners lined up on both sides, their heads bound with mud. Shortly after, he was called inside. The judge asked, "You are supposed to die. Have you done any good deeds in secret?" Zhang Sheng replied, "No." The judge then asked, "Have you ever served in the military?" Zhang Sheng answered, "Yes." The judge continued, "During the Xuanhe period, you followed several generals to Yan Mountain. There were two soldiers who offended their commander and were about to be executed. You advised against it, and they were spared. Also, while you were in the capital, you often gave medicine to others."

Is it possible? Zheng replied, "I vaguely remember it." The official said, "With these two beauties, I will allow you to return and take the original." The edict read, "I will specially grant you an extension and let you return." Zheng bowed and thanked. After leaving the door, he asked the messenger, "How many years will I live after my revival?" The response was, "I don't know." As he was about to leave, the messenger said, "You have always enjoyed drinking. The leftover drops have soaked the table. They have accumulated already in the tens. You must finish drinking before you can leave." He then picked up a very smelly jar and forced Zheng to drink. Zheng drank about three dou, but he could not continue. He accidentally dropped the jar and woke up. He was sick for a month before recovering. He thought that his fate was unclear and wrote down the number of years. He often feared death. He then bid farewell to those he knew and returned to his hometown to prepare the burial ground. The next year, those he knew, including Xing Huizheng, the filial and solemn official of Quzhou, saw Zheng's son, and then Zheng had already died. Calculating his revival, it was only ten days or so. Xing Huizheng said.

Huang Shiyi In the county of Houguan in Fuzhou, there was a girl named Huang Shiyi, the daughter of a scholar. She stood under a curtain to watch people passing by. Suddenly, a messenger burst in and said, "An official is chasing you." The girl returned to her room and immediately suffered from severe heart pain. She died after a day but then revived. She said, "The one who was chasing me walked with me for several tens of miles. Suddenly, he showed a fearful expression and said, 'What I was chasing is actually Wang Shiyi. I mistakenly called you. Now that I see the great king, just say you are from the Wang family. If you tell the truth, I will beat you to death.' I forced myself to agree. When we arrived at the government office, I saw three people sitting in a triangular formation. The one in the middle was my father. He looked at me and asked, 'Why did you come here?' I replied, 'I was in the curtain, and someone chased me here. On the way, he said he was supposed to chase Wang Shiyi but mistakenly chased me. He warned me not to say anything.' My father returned to his seat and said to the person facing east, 'The one being chased is actually from the Wang family, and it is a mistake.' The person facing east asked, 'How do you know this?' My father replied, 'This is my daughter.' The person facing east then ordered the clerk to check the records. He looked at me and said, 'Indeed, it is a mistake.' He laughed and said, 'The royal law has no favoritism, but today it does.' Everyone laughed. Then they released me and let me return. This story is told by Zheng Yanhe, who knows it well.

Xie Xidan's Reincarnation Dong Siyong, a man from Xu, lived in Hongzhou. His wife, Zheng, was pregnant. On the first day of the twelfth month in the leap year of the 23rd year of the Shaoxing era, Dong Siyong dreamed that Hongzhou tax collector and military officer Xie Xidan came to him. Xie continued to bow, but Dong Siyong dared not accept. In the dream, he expressed regret. He awoke at the hour of Hai. That night, his wife gave birth to a son. Soon after, he heard the temple bell ringing. When he asked about it, he learned that Xie had died at that very moment. Dong named his son Yige. The Xie family later learned about it and confirmed that Xidan's nickname was indeed Yige. Thus, Dong's son was clearly Xidan's reincarnation. Xidan was from Shaowu and also knew books. Dong passed the imperial examination in the 24th year of the Shaoxing era and had connections with my wife's family. He heard this account. Lu Xiong's Mother's Dream Lu Xiong, a man from Shaowu, was the son of Cui, a librarian. In the 21st year of the Shaoxing era, he went to take the imperial examination in the Southern Palace. His mother, Fan, dreamed that several people carried a coffin to the central hall and said, "This is your mother." She woke up crying. She told Cui, "People say that dreaming of a coffin means one will get an official position. If this is true for your third son, who is third in line, there might be a sign of success. For you, there might be a promotion. But now, it is my deceased mother. What does this mean?" Cui did not immediately understand. Early the next morning, he went out to attend to his duties. When he returned, he had a happy expression and called out to his wife, "I have explained your dream to you. What is your mother's surname?" Fan suddenly realized that her mother was indeed a member of the Xiong family. Thus, they knew that Lu would surely pass the examination, and indeed, he did. Lu was pleased.

Fan You's Wife During the fifth year of the Shaoxing era, Zhang Yuandao served as a Right Censor. A soldier named Fan You lived near his residence. His wife died on the twenty-fourth day of the ninth month. She had been wrapped but the coffin lid was not yet placed. The following day at dawn, Zhang Six entered the court. Just as the herald was calling out, Fan's wife suddenly raised her hand and shook her husband from the coffin. Her husband was startled and asked her what had happened. She replied, "Just now several ghosts came here. One of them was a judge in green robes, with a face covered in pig bristles growing against the grain. He asked about my whereabouts. I answered, 'My husband, Fan You. Originally a soldier guarding the Yellow River embankment, he was assigned to be a waterman when Zhang Langguan went to Shu. Later, he followed him to the imperial court and is now a servant.' The judge nodded in agreement. While he was hesitating, suddenly someone called out the Right Censor. All the ghosts fled, but the judge sighed and said, 'The vital energy is not fully depleted.' Just as he was leaving, he was not far away." The wife revived and could eat and drink, but after ten days, she died.

A Woman with Three Rows of Teeth During the reign of Emperor Ling, Zheng Gong, the Right Chancellor and nephew of Ouyang, resided in Gongzhou. At that time, there was a famine in the eastern part of the empire, and refugees passed by his door daily. One day, a woman with a dusty appearance but somewhat attractive features approached. Zheng wanted to keep her as a concubine. The woman said, "I am starving and exhausted here and cannot move on. I will surely die here. It would be fortunate to be a servant." Zheng then summoned a matchmaker to draw up a contract and paid the agreed sum to buy cosmetics and clothes for her. The woman was intelligent and beautiful, and Zheng favored her. They lived together for several months. One night, during a heavy thunderstorm, Zheng heard a voice calling from outside his bedroom, saying, "The woman from before has been returned. This is death by starvation, and it is not meant to live." Zheng initially engaged in conversation with the voice but later realized it was strange and refused to respond. The next morning, he thought about it and wanted to send her away, but he was reluctant to do so. Unable to make a decision, another night, the same voice returned. Zheng shouted, "What kind of strange ghost dares to do this? I will not send her away, no matter what you do!" They argued for several nights. Suddenly, the woman complained of severe toothache and groaned all night. When Zheng looked at her the next morning, he saw that she had three rows of teeth, which were extremely alarming. Zheng and his family were all terrified. They sent her away that very day. Her appearance was already strange, and nobody dared to take her. She eventually died among beggars. In the region of Kuaiji, there was a person named Tang Men, who was known for his faith in the Dao. Zheng came out and said that when he was young, his mother told him about this story. However, he lost the name of his maternal uncle.

**Ma Jian's Vengeance** Ma Jian, a native of Qin Prefecture, was originally the son of a farmer. One day, while reaping millet in the fields, a woman secretly took some of his leftover grain. He beat her, and she broke her leg and died as a result. The village official took her to the government office. Ma Jian was over six feet tall and had a robust build. The governor found him extraordinary and said, "If you are willing to become a soldier, I will forgive you." Ma Jian agreed, and he was then branded and made a soldier. Later, Tong Guan selected strong and skilled men to form the Shengjie Army, and Ma Jian was assigned to the cavalry. After being discharged, he became a servant to Zhang Yuandao, the Attendant. Zhang, who was the governor of Guilin, once had a painting exposed under the eaves. Ma Jian took a three-legged stool and climbed it. He just reached the first step and fell. The bystanders thought he was unharmed. Ma Jian stood up and groaned in pain, saying, "My foot is hurt." He drew his small knife and wanted to stab himself. People rushed to look at him, and they saw that his shinbone was already exposed. The wound was bleeding profusely. Ma Jian said, "When I was climbing the ladder, I felt dizzy, as if someone pushed me down from above. That's why I fell." He then recounted his past, saying, "It must be this injustice that caused it." He died several days later.

Chen Sheng Becomes an Official Chen Sheng, a soldier of the Shao Wu Wei Guo regiment, loved drinking. Once, he became so drunk that he felt moved by his lowly status and sighed, "When will I escape this misfortune?" A short while later, he was in a dream-like state, and someone told him, "You will become an official tomorrow. Why are you sighing?" The next morning, Chen Sheng woke up and remembered the words. He said, "The spirits are playing tricks on me like this. How can I become an official?" That evening, he wanted to visit the quarters of a military officer. He heard a soldier whispering to the officer. After the soldier left, Chen Sheng followed him and went to a wine shop with him. He also gave the soldier some money. After getting slightly drunk, he asked the soldier, "What did you just tell the commander?" The soldier explained, "There are people in the camp plotting rebellion. They plan to burn the watchtower gate at midnight. They will wait for the prefecture governor to come out to put out the fire and then kill him to cause trouble." Chen Sheng hurriedly went with the soldier to report to the military officer. The three of them listed their names and went to report to the prefecture. The prefecture governor urgently summoned the military officers and secretly gathered soldiers from other camps according to the number of people mentioned in the report. They captured all the conspirators, and after the trial, they were all executed. Those who reported the plot all received official positions. Chen Sheng became a Chengxin Lang. This was in the 13th year of the Shaoxing era (1143 AD).

Lieda Saves the Mice Lieda, the elder monk of Longqing Temple in Jizhou, once stayed at Yanshan Temple in Yuanzhou. He planned to travel with several fellow monks to another prefecture for spiritual practice. He took an umbrella hat to pack his belongings. Inside the hat, he found a mouse nest filled with scraps of silk and paper. There were five newborn mice whose eyes had not yet opened, chirping softly. Lieda wanted to remove them but feared they would die. He excused himself to his fellow monks, claiming another reason and did not go. A few days later, the five mice were able to move. Lieda fed them porridge and housed them in the hat every night. After more than ten days, they were no longer seen. The hat remained clean and free of filth. Lieda found a clean hat and a piece of tea, which he believed the mice had brought. He hung them in the temple hall. After three days, no one claimed them. He then informed the temple master and the congregation, using the tea as an offering for their travels. From then on, wherever he went, he did not keep cats, and the mice did not cause any harm.

Fish Looking Back at Its Offspring Jing Du served as the waterway administrator of Chengdu. On an official tour of inspection, he traveled to Xinzhen, Shuzhou, where he bought a fish from the river. The fish weighed several pounds. He ordered the cook to slice it thinly. Just as the cook was holding the knife, the fish leaped back into the water. The cook feared punishment for the loss. A fishing boat passed by, and Jing Du earnestly entrusted the task to the fishermen, offering them a thousand coins if they could catch a fish of similar size and weight. The fishermen asked where the fish had been purchased. Jing Du replied, "About a mile from here, in a cave by the riverbank." The fishermen rowed to the indicated location and cast their net. They caught a large fish and returned it. The cook examined it and realized it was the same fish that had jumped back into the water. It was spring, and the fish had laid its eggs in the reeds. The mother fish came back and forth to care for them. Only when the young fish grew into adults did the mother leave. If the mother was caught, the offspring could not survive. Therefore, fishermen use this behavior to catch them, as recorded by Du Xunle in the *Yijian Jia Zhi* (Volume 14, Chapter 18).

The Lady of the Source Palace Liu Yun, courtesy name Houzhong, was from Haifeng, Chaozhou. He passed the imperial examination in the fourth year of Shaoxing (1134 AD). In the year Xuanhe Jia Chen (1124 AD), he was appointed as the governor of Xunzhou. Upon receiving the order, he immediately requested retirement. Coincidentally, the court issued a general pardon to commemorate the recovery of Yan and Yun regions. Although he had already retired, the court allowed him to return to public service. However, Liu refused to take up the position and instead spent his days in the local community, maintaining his usual routine of eating, drinking, and living, showing no signs of decline. His old friends and family members repeatedly encouraged him to resume his duties, but he remained resolute. The following spring, Liu contracted an illness after the death of his mother, which he was mourning. One day, he suddenly got up and called his son Fang, saying, "There is an imperial decree appointing me as a Scholar of the Kuiwen Hall." Fang was unsure and asked for confirmation. Liu took his hand and wrote the characters "Kuiwen," saying, "You must prepare a memorial to decline the appointment. If you cannot get permission, then prepare a thank-you letter." A few days later, he said, "The Celestial Official has already appointed someone else. I am relieved." The family rejoiced and he gradually recovered. However, he never ate meat again. On April 1st, he said, "I have recently been appointed as the Lady of the Source Palace, a position among the most esteemed in the celestial hierarchy. I am very pleased." The family asked, "Is this a dream?" He replied, "No, someone just informed me clearly. I will not be here for long." He immediately asked for paper and ink to write down a few matters, mostly warnings against excessive mourning and funeral practices. Three days later, he got up, dressed properly, and called his two sons, Fang and Jing, to tell them about his final wishes. He passed away in the middle of the night. A few nights before his death, a local man named Li Zhengfu dreamed of visiting Liu and saw officials and servants filling the door, saying they were coming to welcome their new master. His neighbor, a woman surnamed Xu, also dreamed of seeing banners and canopies flying in the streets. Soon after, Liu slowly left with his guides. A few days after his death, his body remained soft and smooth, as if he were still alive.

During the hot and humid summer, all four limbs could stretch and bend, and his complexion did not change much. When Liu was young, on the Mid-Autumn night of the year Bingyin during the Yuan佑 era, he dreamed of visiting a cave temple. There, he saw a statue dressed in Daoist attire, and a young woman beside him pointed and said, "This is his former incarnation." After waking up, he wrote eight poems to record it. By this point, it was quite appropriate to say, and his poems read: Silver-built terrace and jade-paved floor, Five clouds deeply reflect among myriad flowers. The sound of a beast ring echoes through the heavy doors being opened, An infinite number of young women welcome with joyful laughter. A girl with a bun leads the way around the inner hall, A curtain rolling up to reveal an old courtyard hall among clouds. Pine and cassia trees fill the courtyard, with turtles and cranes present, Clearly dressed in the attire of a Daoist master. Gradually entering the eastern hall, over a hundred steps, The empty hall still remembers the old residence. Window screens partially covering exquisite scrolls, All were books read thoroughly at that time. The gentle rays of the sun illuminating the corners of a building, The smoke of good fortune flows and trails far into the roof beams. Pine and cypress trees understand human tastes and preferences, The wind comes and makes a sound like the *Buxu* chant. An old twisted pine tree beside a golden altar, A long ribbon-like plant grows by a jade-paved pool. Chanting while picking purple ginseng, the fragrance fills the hand, Several calls of a phoenix in tall bamboo groves. The smoke from a beast-shaped incense burner mingling with the fragrance of a hundred flowers, Jade leaves and jade branches lean against both sides. A melody played on the cloud harp, with phoenixes and cranes dancing, Encouraging people to vie for the cups of nine-colored clouds. Looking at old inscriptions between cloud-mother screens, Drunkenly chanting under the green peach branches of the Mother. The group of immortals point to places not yet inscribed, Further asking for a poem about a white crane soaring in the void. Stepping out of the red palace as the sun gradually moves, The girls in buns prostrate themselves and ask about the return date. If the worldly ties are severed, it's like breaking off from the human world, Look at the peaches of immortality when they are ripe. Chen Anguo from Chaoyang once narrated this story. After this, he changed his name to Dan. He served up to the position of Junior Minister of Rites. During the year Gengwu of the Shaoxing era, he died as the Director of the Dragon-Graph Pavilion and Governor of Tanzhou. Jing once served as the governor of Taizhou.

The Man of Zhangzhou Who Married a Mountain Ghost** A man from Jianzhou, Fan Zhouhan, served as a judicial officer in Zhangzhou. A villager near the city, who made a living by gathering firewood, had no wife. After a long time, he found a woman and brought her home. He carried two baskets with him. His family was all happy, but his sister alone noticed that the woman had only one foot. She dared not speak. At night, they slept together. By the time the sun was high, they did not open the door. Their parents broke through the wall to enter and found only white bones on the bed. They opened her chest and found only pottery shards, stones, and paper money. It was likely a mountain ghost. **Wang Kan’s Exam Papers** A soldier from Liangshan, Wang Kan, with the courtesy name Mengxi, was originally named something else. He once dreamed of entering a grand official office and saw a large plaque hanging on the wall with his name, "Wang Kan," written on it. He then changed his name. Later, he was selected for the imperial examination. In the fifth year of the Chongning era, he encountered a yellow-robed officer on a main street during the day. The officer gave him three sets of exam papers. Wang Kan felt ashamed and thanked him, offering only three hundred coins as a reward. The officer said, "If I pass the exam, I will repay you generously." The officer nodded and left. Wang Kan then used the exam papers he received and passed the examination that year. He never knew who the officer was. Wang Kan eventually became a low-ranking official. **Yang Hui’s Journey to the Underworld** In the twenty-second year of the Shaoxing era, Qi Shu, a rebel from Gan Prefecture, led an uprising. While the rebellion had not yet been quelled, a villager from Jishui County in Ji Prefecture named Yang Hui dreamed of being summoned to the underworld. He saw hundreds of people tied with three wooden restraints in the courtyard. The judge scolded Yang Hui, saying, "How dare you associate with Qi Shu in rebellion?" Yang Hui replied, "I am a villager from Jishui County and have no connection with Qi Shu." The judge said, "Then it was a mistake." He then sent Yang Hui back.

Wu Zhonggong - A native of Zhengzhou, Wu Zhonggong, served as the governor of Guiyang during the end of the Jianyan era. At that time, the regions of Hunan and Hubei were plagued by banditry. Wu enforced strict laws to suppress the bandits, and many prisoners died in jail. When he fell ill, he developed severe abscesses around his neck. After a long time, the sores burst, and his throat became visible, as if he had been beheaded. One day, he ordered his family to prepare steamed duck for him to eat, but he did not get the chance, as he died soon after. Two days after his death, officials from the judicial office suddenly began speaking to themselves, saying, "Officials are pursuing me to testify about the affairs of Governor Wu of Guiyang." They died shortly thereafter. At the time, Liu Shi of Hengzhou was serving as a judicial official and witnessed these events. Ghost on the Banana Leaf - In the early years of the Shaoxing era, Lian Nanfu led the military in Guangdong. Cao Shen, serving as an advisor under the title of Xuanyi Lang, was involved in many military campaigns against pirates. Lian killed countless pirates, sometimes executing over a hundred in a single day. Cao handled all these matters personally, without taking the time to investigate further. For his contributions, he was promoted to the rank of Chaofeng Dafu and later became the deputy governor of Guangzhou. His residence was located in the Jinghui Temple. Shortly after taking office, he fell ill. Whenever the officials were on duty, the maids in his household heard voices in the banana grove behind the temple. Sometimes, they saw a man sitting on the banana leaves, who did not seem alarmed by the maids. When asked who he was, he replied, "I have come from the deputy governor to demand your life. We are twenty-six in total, divided into four groups to search for you. Six of us have arrived here first." Hearing this, Cao was filled with fear and prayed earnestly, offering to perform water and land rituals to appease the spirits. However, the spirits did not respond, saying, "Only by following us can you be saved." Cao eventually died. Before his death, one of his concubines gave birth to a child who was covered in hair all over his body. The child was buried at the foot of a mountain. After three days, when they opened the grave to check, the child was still alive, which was a very strange occurrence. It is said that this was the result of vengeful spirits. These five events were recounted by Zhang Kejiu.

The Dong Family Prays to the Arhats A local resident named Dong Guan Yanming, over thirty years old, had no son. He and his wife traveled together from Fanyang to Yuntong Temple on Mount Lu. They offered tea to the Arhats and promised to donate five hundred hats to seek an heir. Dong personally carried a pot and brewed tea. They reached the 124th Venerable. Just as he finished pouring the tea, the cup was already empty. Dong prayed, "Could it be that the Venerable has intentionally responded to our karmic conditions?" He vowed to adorn one hat with pearls and offer it. After returning home, after several months, his wife made a hat by hand and ordered a relative, Daoist Priest Dong, to carry it to the temple. Upon his return, Dong's concubine saw it first and greeted him, asking, "Has the Daoist Priest returned?" That month, she became pregnant. Before giving birth, family members often dreamed of a monk wearing a hat moving back and forth in the room. After twelve months, she gave birth to a son. Just over a month later, the child occasionally heard the sound of reciting sutras and would stop crying even if he was in the middle of crying. Dong recited one volume of the Diamond Sutra every day. Later, every time the child heard the sutra, he would always want to go forward, as if listening attentively. After a hundred days, Dong casually asked him, "Do you deeply love this?" "Could it be that you once recited it in a past life?" The child quickly opened his eyes and spoke in the voice of an old man, "I once recited it." Dong was startled and asked again, but the child did not answer. From then on, the child did not eat much milk. Then he fell ill and was on the verge of death. His eyes opened and closed several times, as if unwilling to leave. Dong patted him and said, "Since you are a person outside the world, you can freely choose to go or stay. If you want to go, then go. Why need it?" The child then closed his eyes, and when Dong felt his body, it was already cold. It is said that he lived exactly one hundred twenty-four days. Dong recounted this story.

Lady Wang, the wife of Li Zhen, the grandson of Princess Xianmu, had been married for over ten years without having a child. One evening, she took a leisurely stroll in her garden when she faintly saw a yellow bird flying and dancing among the trees. Playfully chasing it, the bird suddenly disappeared into the ground. Suspecting something unusual, she quickly called a child to dig up the spot and look. They found a piece of gold as large as a winnowing basket. Lady Wang exclaimed, "This is a gift from heaven to me." Although she thought, "Though it is a mysterious and obscure object, I dare not accept it. I only wish to have one child." She then returned home. The next day, she tried to dig it up again, but it was already empty. That month, she became pregnant and gave birth to a son named Jingzhi. By the end of the Chongning era, Jingzhi served as a Deputy Minister of Works. This account was narrated by Jingzhi's younger brother, Jingyu.

The Villagers of Shu Kill Four Tigers In the twenty-fifth year of the Shaoxing era, Wu Fupeng, who was appointed to govern Anfeng Commandery, sent a soldier from Fanyang to summon officials and soldiers. On his way, he reached the border of Shu Prefecture and saw a crowd of villagers gathered in groups of ten or a hundred. He loosened his load and watched them. One of the villagers said, "In our village, a woman was carried off by a tiger. Her husband, unable to bear his anger, went alone with a knife to investigate the tiger's den. After a while, he did not return. Now we are planning to go and save her." After a long time, the man returned carrying his dead wife. He said that he first followed the trail to the den and found that neither the male nor the female tiger was there. There were two cubs playing at the rock crevice. He immediately killed them and hid himself in the crevice. After a short while, he saw the female tiger carrying a person to the den. She fell into the den, not knowing that a person was hidden inside. He quickly grabbed her tail and cut off one of her feet. The tiger dropped the person she was carrying and fled unsteadily. He then cautiously went out and saw that it was indeed his wife, but she was dead. The tiger dragged its foot for dozens of steps and fell into a ravine. He then went back into the crevice to wait for the male tiger. Soon, the male tiger roared and leaped to the den. It also entered with its tail first. He killed it in the same way as before. The wife's grievance has been avenged, and there is no regret now. They then invited their neighbors to come and see. They took the four tigers back and divided and cooked them.

Master of the Subtle and Tranquil, Chen Qiongyu Master of the Subtle and Tranquil, Chen Qiongyu, was a woman from Jinhua, Wuzhou. At the age of seventeen, she invited her brother to travel to the sea of Si Ming. While her brother rode in a boat, Chen Qiongyu walked on the water. For several days, her clothes remained dry. After returning, she told others that she had met the Star Lady of Weizhou in the water, who guided her to Penglai. She then realized that she was originally the Thirteenth Cave Master. Thus, she became enlightened. From then on, she abstained from food and began composing poetry. She also gained knowledge of human affairs, both good and bad. Officials and commoners alike came to seek her counsel daily, and her doorstep was always filled with visitors. In the seventh year of the Zhenghe era, the local governor Liu An, along with the inspectors Lu Tianji and Wang Ruming, reported her to the court. They summoned her to the capital, where she was granted an audience. After the audience, Master Chen Qiongyu requested to return to her mountain retreat. Her dwelling was situated in front of Ge Xian Peak and backed by the Xian Gu Tan altar. She lived alone in a room. The local official Ke Tingjian wrote a poem in her honor: "For years, I have abstained from food and dwelled in spirit. Closing the door, I spend my days in tranquil ease. My noble character is in silent accord with Mazu. My marvelous teachings are personally transmitted from the Infant Lady."

The master's merits and deeds long surpass the three realms, and his name and reputation are clear through the nine layers of heaven. Who can ask the Queen Mother of the West? Is the master the nth immortal of Jinhua? Many have gifted him with poetry, but he particularly favors this piece. The master has composed poems before and after, numbering in the thousands. His disciple Zhaos once said, "The words of the poems respond like echoes." How can one know this? The master replied, "The sound of their home. That is, the officials of the heavens hold the records. Over five hundred years, the past and future are known. Fearing the revelation of heavenly secrets, he temporarily uses wind, flowers, snow, and moon as themes for poetry. Yet, good and bad fortune are hidden within them. Not just knowing, but also managing them. Yesterday, he temporarily served as the magistrate of an unnamed county, managing human life and death. Later, he temporarily served as the recorder of the underworld, managing the six kinds of offenses: officials, money, kings, rebellion, filial impiety, theft, excess, and murder. Those who offend often face a harsh fate. Those who do not commit these offenses will become immortals in three generations. Recently, he has taken charge of the registry of the lunar platform immortals. All wise scholars and officials are recorded. If one has good deeds, they can become a lunar platform immortal. Generally, he encourages people with loyalty, filial piety, and honesty. Even at eighty or ninety years old, one's appearance does not decline.

### Zhang San Gong's Missing Text The internal text is missing. ### County Magistrate Chu of Wuhu During the Jianyan era, Lu De, a rebel in Taiping Prefecture, rebelled, burning and looting the homes of the people and killing officials. Chu Sheng, the county magistrate of Wuhu, was unable to flee in time and was captured by the rebel forces. Lu De personally came to execute him, having removed his clothes and forced him to sit. Seeing three rays of light emanating from his head, Lu De released him and ordered him to take charge of the county affairs, entrusting him with the management of the warehouses. After the bandits were quelled, Chu Wei used this merit to change to an official in the capital. The monk Zusheng from Xuancheng said that Chu Wei recited the *Yuanjue Jing* once a day and the name of the Bodhisattva Guanyin a thousand times, considering it routine. Therefore, he escaped from violence and misfortune due to his good deeds. ### The Tiger of Guankeng Village In Luoyang, there is a hill that is not very high, with a flat top. The residents called it "Liao Shang." A woman from a farming family once returned to her parents' home. Passing by the hill, she saw a tiger crouching in the grass. Fearing she could not escape, she stood up and called out to it, saying, "Ban Ge, I am now visiting my parents. I have no grudge against you. Go away quickly." The tiger lowered its ears and listened attentively, then quickly dragged its tail and moved towards the dangerous area. The woman was able to escape. The world considers tigers to be spiritual beings that do not harm people without reason. However, this woman, seeing a fierce beast without fear, was able to reason with it, which is also a rare ability. ### County Official Cai and the Treatment of Inch-White (Translation incomplete due to missing text)

Cai Dingfu's son, Kang, suffered greatly from an infestation of inch-long white worms. The doctor advised him to grind betel nut into a fine powder and boil water with the roots of the pomegranate tree that grow towards the east, mixing the powder with the water for consumption. First, roast a large piece of fatty pork and swallow the juices and fat from it. This is because the worms' heads are only upward for three days of the month, making them susceptible to the medicine. On other days, their heads are downward, and the medicine is ineffective. The worms are attracted by the smell of the roasted meat and rush to devour it. When the worms are in the stomach, it feels as if a thousand arrows are piercing it. At this point, drink the medicine. Cai followed the instructions and, within two or three moments, his stomach began to rumble loudly. He quickly went to the toilet, and the worms came out in a stream. He ordered a servant to use a pole to pick them up. The worms were all connected in a string, nearly several meters long, and still wriggling slightly. He threw them into a stream, and the long-standing illness was immediately cured. This remedy is also recorded in Yang's Collection of Verified Remedies. Cai traveled to Lin'an and told Qian Zhongben about it, wanting to spread this remedy to help future generations. Xu Yuanhuaiqing, a scholar from Le Ping, had a dream where a black-clothed guest came to him and said, "I borrowed 300 coins from you yesterday. Now I am returning them." Before he could ask who the guest was or when the debt was incurred, he woke up. The next day, he thought about it but could not understand. He usually kept about ten ducks. On that day, when he returned home, he found an extra black duck. A young servant thought it belonged to someone else and let it out. The duck circled around and rested nearby, laying an egg before flying away. This happened every day for a month, and a total of thirty eggs were laid. After that, the duck never returned. He could not figure out who the owner was, but the value of the eggs totaled exactly 300 coins.

**The Huang County Magistrate's Pet Bird** Huang Zhu Shaoxian served as the county magistrate of Poyang. In the second year of the Qingyuan era (1196), a thief entered his home, picking up clothes and dividing them into two bags. Just as the thief was about to leave, Huang's family had a well-trained and tame pet bird. The bird could understand human speech. That night, the entire family was sound asleep. Suddenly, the bird began to pace restlessly in its cage, calling out incessantly. Those who heard it mistakenly thought the bird was being attacked by a cat and quickly got up to see it. The thief, startled, fled in panic, leaving behind one of the bags. Huang also noticed the thief and sent a servant to chase after him, but the thief had already escaped. Despite the bird's small size, it carried the gratitude for being raised and knew how to repay it. People felt ashamed by this. **The Tide Ghost** Shen Fu, a soldier from Mingzhou, lost his father when he drowned in the Qiantang River. At the time, Shen Fu was only five or six years old. His mother raised him, but he often suffered from illness and misfortune. She consulted various shamans, who all said that his father had become a ghost. The mother poured wine and prayed to him, saying, "You died, leaving only one son. I rely on him for my life. Why do you keep causing trouble? If you need anything, tell me in a dream." That night, the father appeared in a dream and said, "I died and was recorded by the river god, becoming a ghost of the tide. My duty is to push the tide, and the labor is extremely hard and painful. I urgently need straw shoes and pine boards. You should burn many of them to help me. I will seek a replacement and leave after the year is up." The mother followed his words and burned the two items for him. From then on, Shen Fu never fell ill again.

A Ghost of Jiande A man from the Wang family of Qimen. He traveled from Fanyang to Chizhou and intended to stay overnight in Jiande County. Before reaching a place about one li away, he passed by the old house of a relative and stayed to drink. His entourage had already departed. After drinking, he rode his horse alone and became lost, no longer meeting up with his companions. He ventured deep into a side path covered with dense undergrowth. As the day was about to darken, several people suddenly appeared and seized him. They walked about ten li and brought him to an ancient temple in a deep mountain. He was bound with his hands tied behind his back to a pillar. The several people all burned incense and poured wine, worshipping in front of the deity's statue, showing a self-satisfied expression. They prayed, saying, "Please, Lord, take him for yourself." Then they locked the temple door and left. Wang only then realized that they were killing people to worship ghosts. He was overwhelmed with sadness and fear. He usually practiced the Great Compassion Mantra. At that point, he only recited it silently, seeking supernatural help. In the middle of the night, there was a heavy rain and strong wind. The trees vibrated, making a thunderous roar. The door creaked open wide. Something entered from outside. Its eyes shone like torches, illuminating the hallway. When he looked, it was a large python. It struggled and opened its mouth, wanting to rush towards Wang. Wang trembled with fear. He recited the mantra more intensely. The snake was more than one zhang away, as if there was an obstacle in front of it. It retreated and advanced three times. Then it lowered its head and went straight out. At dawn, people outside came playing drums and flutes, wanting to drink the sacrificial offering. Seeing Wang still alive, they were greatly frightened and asked for the reason. He told them in detail about the matter. They looked at each other and said, "This gentleman has good fortune. We should not be able to offer him." They unbound him and apologized. They escorted him out onto the official road and warned him not to dare speak of it. Wang had already escaped, but he could not fully investigate the bandits. Wang Jiagou said.

**Volume Fifteen, Seventeenth Story of the Yi Jian Jia Zhi** **The Wife of Xue, the Judicial Officer** Xue Du. In the early years of the Shaoxing era, he served as a judicial officer for the Kui Road Circuit Intendant's Office. His official residence was in Gongzhou. His wife fell ill, and he summoned the doctor Liu Taichu to treat her. The treatment was ineffective, and she died. After some time, she opened her eyes again. She asked the doctor's name and hometown in great detail. And then she died. Several years later, Liu moved to Jingnan. During the day, a red-clothed woman appeared, covering her head as she entered the door. She said she was sick and sought treatment. Liu was not at home, and the family told her the truth. The woman went straight inside and reached the main hall, sitting upright and waiting. Someone uncovered her head covering, and it turned out to be a skull. In the midst of the shock and shouting, she disappeared. After this, Liu's medical skills gradually declined, and his family became increasingly impoverished. At the time, Xue served as the magistrate of Hengshan in Tan Prefecture. Upon hearing of the matter, he wept and said, "She is my wife." **Lightning Strikes Two Barbarians** The governor of Yongzhou, who also served as the commander of the regional military, visited the Hengshan Military Camp each year to engage in trade with the people of Jiao. In the twenty-third year of the Shaoxing era, Zhao Yuan served as the governor. When he arrived at the camp to buy horses, more than a thousand barbarians were present, bustling about. Two civilians passed through a section of the province, and the barbarians killed them. They also kidnapped one woman and took her away. Zhao Yuan could not capture or interrogate them. The next day, with no clouds in the sky, a loud thunderclap struck, killing two of the barbarians. Their bodies lay one face up and one face down, just as the two civilians had died. The barbarian chief, fearing the incident, learned of the event and immediately sent the woman back to Yongzhou. Liu Xiangzi, who had heard the story, commented on it.

Ma Xian Gu** Ma Xian Gu, a spiritual maiden from Guozhou, achieved enlightenment as a woman. She was once seduced by a rogue Taoist who drove her to drink with medicinal wine. Afterward, she became suddenly and crazily disoriented. On the twenty-fifth day of the twelfth month in the leap year of the first year of the Jingkang era, she wore mourning clothes and carried a mourning staff, weeping in the marketplace. She said, "Today, the Heavenly Emperor has died. I am performing the mourning rites." The people of the city spat, cursed, and chased her away. Later, it was learned that the capital had fallen on that day. Yang Pugong Quan, who was then serving as a clerk in the Department of Works, recounted this story. **Chen Zunzhe** Chen Zunzhe, a monk from Langzhou, usually lived carefree and wild, but he often foresaw events, which no one could predict. On the fourteenth day of the fourth month in the first year of the Shaoxing era, he suddenly dressed in mourning clothes and wept at the gate tower. Someone asked, "This is the city gate, how can you do this?" He replied, "I am crying because the Buddha has passed away." Those who heard him thought it was absurd. A month later, the posthumous edict of the Empress Dowager Longyou was received. The day he cried was indeed the day of ascension to immortality. My father-in-law recounted this story. **Jia Sicheng's Horse Dream** Jia Sicheng, courtesy name Yanfu, served as the governor of Kuizhou in the seventeenth year of the Shaoxing era. In a dream, he was assigned to a government post and saw a soldier leading a horse to greet him. As he was about to take the reins, he carefully examined the horse and saw it had thirteen legs. He murmured in amazement and woke up. The next day, boils broke out on his back, and he died thirteen days later. Jia was born in the year of Gengwu. It is said that this was a horse disaster. Zhang Da recounted this story.

The Dragon of Jingju Cave In the northwest of Hengshan County, there is a cave called Jingju, where a dragon has made its lair. The monk Zongyu, upon first arriving, enjoyed its seclusion and planned to build a hermitage. However, a few women frequently disturbed him, so he did not dare to stay and avoided various temples. In the year 1141, the monk Shantong came to live there and built only a few thatched houses. The wandering monk Miaoyin, who was staying in another house, was approached by a woman who came to have relations with him. From the waist down, he felt cold like ice and died after several days. The lay practitioner Zuyuan went into the mountains to gather wood but later became lost and did not return for five days. He was found in Tiger Cave, where he said that a woman had ordered him to stay there. Now, she goes out to find fruit and snacks to feed me. The cave entrance is very narrow, only enough room for a person, but inside it is quite spacious—it is where the dragon has made its lair. Zuyuan returned and also fell ill. In the month of April of that year, near the full moon, a sudden storm arrived, and the entire mountain turned black. Lightning and thunder crackled outside the house. Shantong usually did not sleep and sat in meditation in the niche. Nearly midnight, he lit a lamp, heard a sound, and went out of the niche. The sound was like the sound of a bellows. Looking at it, it was a giant python coiled several times, with its tail still outside the door. Shantong called the other monks, and they hit it away with sticks. After it left, it returned, and they hit it again. Finally, it rushed into a rock crevice, but before it could fully enter, it died from the earthquake. A large amount of water came down, flooding most of the rooms and houses. Later, the moon and stars shone brightly. The next morning, they saw the dead python, about two zhang long, with a circumference of several chi. Its body was covered in black square patterns. Zuyuan immediately went mad, mourned and lamented for several months before dying. A total of eight monks and servants were killed by it. Previously every night, the mountain would become dark, even when the moon came out. Since the python died, the night began to brighten. Now there are dozens of houses, and ten monks say, Shantong said.

**The Ancient Vase of Yiyang** Zhang Yuqing, a descendant of Wen Ding Gong Qi Xian, lived in the small town of Xiaoshui in Yiyang County, Henan Province. He found an ancient clay vase buried in the earth. The vase was very dark in color, and he cherished it greatly, placing it in his study to grow flowers. During the extremely cold winter, one night he forgot to remove the water from the vase and intended to do something with it. All other things with water froze, but this vase did not. He found it strange. He used hot water, and the vase remained warm all day. Zhang sometimes went out to the suburbs with guests, placing the vase in a box and pouring water to brew tea, all as if freshly boiled. From then on, he began to treasure it secretly. Later, a drunken servant broke it. When he looked inside, he found it similar to ordinary pottery, but the bottom was thick, about two inches. There were ghosts holding torches to burn it, and the carvings were very fine. No one could identify what period it was from.

The Wife of Zhao Anzai The Zhao family of Dengzhou is a prominent lineage. It is said that they have lived in Nanyang since the Han dynasty. Emperor Liu Bei once borrowed a large sum of money from them, with Zhuge Liang acting as a guarantor. The contract still exists in their family. In the second year of the Jianyan era, the people of Deng were devastated by the Hu army, with some being captured and others dying. Hundreds of men and women of the Zhao family were taken captive and taken north, reaching Qinghuishan in Fenyang, where they were attacked by Red Turban leader Shao Bo, and lost everything they had taken before fleeing. Zhao Anzai's wife, along with her daughter and wet nurse, were taken captive by Wang, a minor officer in the Right Army, who was part of Shao's forces. Chancellor Zhang came to pacify Shaanxi and Sichuan, and Shao led his troops to surrender. Wang was together with Zhao's wife in Langzhong, where there is a temple of the Spirit and Manifest God. The wife and her wet nurse went on the second day of the month to burn incense. The wet nurse saw a beggar on the road, sick and covering himself with tattered paper, his appearance very emaciated. She looked at him carefully and told the wife, saying, "There is a beggar who looks exactly like my eleventh son." She sent someone to inquire about his hometown, family name, and lineage.

Indeed, it is a place of safety. The wife remained calm. The husband ordered an old woman to give her a gold hairpin and agreed to meet again on the sixteenth day, warning her not to change clothes. They met on the agreed date, and he gave her two taels of gold, saying, "Use half to go to the Pacification Office to file a complaint, and use the other half to buy a boat and set up a place to wait for me." After the wife had filed a complaint with the Pacification Office, military officials arrested Wang Sheng. Just as Wang went out hunting, the wife took all her belongings worth several thousand coins, along with the old woman and her daughter, and went to the boat at Anzhai, traveling downstream. Wang Sheng's family was very wealthy, but he did not take a single coin. Wang returned late and did not see his wife, and a new warrant arrived. He saw that all the treasures in the house were still there and sighed, saying, "I had always heard that she was a wife of the Zhao family. Now she is going back to her husband, which is normal. I do not care at all." The Zhao couple separated and then reunited as before. A woman who does not forget her former husband, even among beggars, can be sought after, and one can find ancient heroic women. It is a pity that her name and surname are not recorded. Wang, though a warrior, also understands righteousness, which is admirable.

Dogs Bite Zhang San's Head The wife of Zhang San, a low-ranking official in Fangcheng County, Tangzhou, was originally a courtesan. She was cruel and brutal, treating her female servants harshly for even minor mistakes. She would hang money on their hair to make them touch each other and create a sound, beat them if they became slightly lazy, prick them with needles or claws to make them scratch the ground, or put them in a cloth bag and prick them with a needle. In total, she killed several concubines. Her husband was afraid of her and dared not speak up. Later, she killed her daughter-in-law. The family of the daughter-in-law went to the county to report the matter. The county issued an order to the magistrate to inspect the body. A young servant girl then called out, saying, "There is another dead person under the bed. It can be examined together." The case was settled, and because she was not a legitimate wife, it was treated as if a common person had committed murder. The body was displayed in the market of Tangzhou. Zhang San also fell ill after this, and all his limbs on the left side became paralyzed. Tears and snot flowed uncontrollably, and he had to bring his head to the table to eat. He died after three years. After he was buried, wild dogs bit his grave, lifted the coffin, and carried his head away, throwing it out of the county gate and leaving. All three events were told by Zhang Zongyi, the husband's uncle, who followed the Daoist path.

Snake King San Wang San, a resident of Fangcheng, was skilled at catching snakes. Whenever he visited a household, he could sense the number of snakes present and their locations. He would then capture and eat those that posed a threat. People called him Snake King San. One day, the magistrate of Fangcheng obtained a snake and summoned Wang San to eat it. The snake injured the magistrate with its claws, pulling out two of his teeth. Nearby villagers were troubled by the appearance of venomous pythons, which were causing harm. They raised ten thousand taels of silver and ordered Wang San to devise a method to capture the snakes. Wang San drew three trenches on the ground and told the villagers, "If it were a common snake, it would die after crossing one trench. At most, it could cross two. But if it could cross three trenches, then I would be bitten by it." Soon after, the snake moved forward fearlessly, intending to approach Wang San. Wang San was very distressed and quickly tore his pants, splitting them. The snake split into two and died. Once, Wang San visited Maobei Village and told a wealthy household, "There is a large black snake in your home. It is not advisable to catch it while it is still prosperous." The wealthy household wanted to test his words and forced him to catch the snake. Wang San wrote a piece of paper and instructed the person to throw it into the water hole at the left corner of the kitchen wall. He called out, "Snake King San calls you. Immediately run away, do not look back, or you might be injured." The person did not believe him and threw the paper. After a short while, the person stayed to observe and saw that the large black snake had already appeared. The person was shocked and fell down. The snake came out from the side and reached Wang San, who carried it in his sleeve. The household indeed broke apart afterward. My wife lived in Maobei Village and often called Wang San over. During the Jianyan period when bandits rose up, Wang San's final fate was unknown. Some say he was a snake spirit.

Responding Worm Mao Jing, a military officer in the Yongzhou deputy governor's office, contracted a strange illness. Every time he spoke, something in his throat would make a sound in response. A Taoist instructed him to recite the names of herbs from the *Bencao* (Herbal). When he reached the herb *indigo* (蓝), he became silent. He then took the indigo, squeezed it to extract the juice, and drank it. After a short while, he vomited out a piece of meat-like matter about two inches long, with all human features intact. Liu Xiangzi, who served as the deputy governor of Yongzhou, witnessed that Mao Jing had been suffering from this disease for over a year and personally saw his recovery. I recall that an earlier book mentioned the *ying sheng chong* (responding worm), and it was cured by taking *lei wan* (a type of medicine). This case is similar to that one.

**Vice-Chancellor Xin** In the eighth year of the Shaoxing era, Xin Qiri, also known as Ciyang, was promoted from the position of Right Censor to the post of Inspector of Criminal Affairs in Hunan. When his boat arrived at Wuchang, the general Yue Fei came to meet him at a pavilion on the river. Xin offered the excuse that he could not see guests on the road, but Yue would not leave. After a long while, Xin reluctantly agreed to see him. He then proposed to prepare a meal for the next day, and Yue's intention was very sincere. Xin could not refuse. After the banquet, after three rounds of wine, he invited Xin into a small room and showed him all the imperial edicts he had received throughout his life, amounting to hundreds of documents. He spoke at length about the imperial favor he had received. Taking Xin's hand, he said, "Last night, I dreamed that I was summoned to the Supreme Court of Justice for a trial. The jailer said, 'Vice-Chancellor Xin is ordered to investigate.' I woke up in a cold sweat, feeling deeply afraid. I dared not tell anyone. But just as I was wondering, the ferryman reported that you had arrived. Since you were transferred from the position of censor to an external post, you will surely hold a position of sole authority in the future. If I, unfortunately, were to be imprisoned, I hope you will come to my aid." Xin was startled and did not know what to say. As soon as the wine was finished, he immediately set sail. Several years later, Yue Fei was dismissed from his position as Deputy Chancellor and was ordered to attend court. His former general Wang Gui, following the wishes of the current prime minister, accused him of plotting rebellion. Yue was imprisoned in the Dali Temple. The new Vice-Chancellor He Boshou was ordered to investigate the matter. It dawned on him that the dream he had had the previous night was about the new Vice-Chancellor. Later, He Boshou declined and avoided taking up the position, and the case was entrusted to Chancellor Wanqi. These two events were recounted by Liu Xiangzi.

In the spring of the tenth year of the Shaoxing era, Ma Yuan Yi from Le Ping was serving as a guard at the Dali Temple. He traveled with his maid Yi Nu to the road leading to Shangrao, where they both visited a shrine to beg for blessings. In the sixth month of that year, Yi Nu dreamed that she and Ma Yuan Yi arrived at the shrine they had visited, where several officials called out, "The Great Minister requests your presence." They pointed to a tall building ahead and said, "The Great Minister is there slaughtering a pig for a celebration and has invited his subordinates." The next day, Ma Yuan Yi told the temple official Zhou Sanwei about the dream. Zhou interpreted that in the tenth month of the lunar calendar, there would be a promotion or advancement. The following winter, the temple established a special court to investigate Yue Fei. At night, Zhou often secretly went to the court. One night, under a dimly lit moon, he saw something resembling a pig with horns under an ancient tree. Zhou was startled and took a step back. The creature moved slowly and disappeared into a small shrine beside the prison. Several nights later, the moon was very bright, and Zhou saw the same strange creature again. On its head was a piece of paper with the word "release" written on it. Zhou believed that when the case was resolved, there would be clemency. Later, he heard from the monk Hui Qing, who was a disciple of Yue Fei, that when Yue Fei was young and living in Xiang Tai, he worked as a market patrol. There was a man named Shu Weng who was skilled in reading faces. Whenever he saw Yue Fei, he would serve him tea and food. Shu Weng once privately told Yue Fei, "You are a pig spirit. Your spirit is in the human world, and there will be unusual events. One day, you will command an army of ten thousand, establish achievements and a career, and reach the highest rank of officials. However, pigs rarely have a good end; they are always slaughtered by humans. If you achieve success, you should retire early." Yue Fei laughed and did not take it seriously. It was only now that the prediction came true. Ma Yuan Yi reported this story.

Woji Mountain Temple During the reign of Emperor Shenzong, a monk set out to visit Tiantai Mountain but became lost and entered Woji Mountain in Xinchang County, Zhejiang Province. He encountered a large Buddhist temple, which was eerily quiet and devoid of human activity. The monk marveled at the orderliness of the monastery. After entering the main hall, he noticed that the officials were handling their duties with great seriousness. He wondered why such strictness existed in a deep mountain retreat. He slowly made his way into the Dharma Hall, passing through two buildings, and finally saw several senior monks sitting in silence. The monk approached to ask a question, but they waved him away, and he dared not inquire further. He then left the monks' quarters and stood to one side, observing. After a while, he heard that the first senior monk was being called up to the main hall. The monk began to weep as he took his seat, and officials in purple robes and gold seals stood before him. In an instant, fire erupted from the monk's body, spreading to engulf him, and even the officials in purple were consumed, leaving no trace. This process repeated itself, with monks being called up one after another, over and over again. The monk asked the officials what was happening, and they explained that the monk had lived a life without discipline, falsely claiming to be the abbot, and had slandered the true teachings of the Buddha. Therefore, he was receiving this punishment. The officials in purple were the leaders. Fearing greatly, the monk quickly left, reaching the middle of the mountain. There, he encountered several soldiers driving an elderly woman. He recognized her as his mother. She turned back and called out, "Because you used to frivolously talk about Prajna, you have caused me to be in this state." She hurried away, and the monk could not speak further. He descended the mountain, searching for his way, and asked the locals what temple was on this mountain. They replied, "The path is deserted, how could there be a temple here?" They pointed to another road, saying it led to Tiantai Mountain. When he asked how long he had been gone, they told him it had been three days. He did not continue his eastward journey and returned home directly. His mother had already died. At the time, the story spread, and several retired senior monks, including Guan Zidong and Qiang You'an, all wrote articles to record the event.

The Immortal of Luofu Lan Qiao, style name Zisheng, was from Longchuan, Xunzhou. His mother Chen had no son. She prayed at Luofu Mountain and became pregnant. When the time came, she dreamed of immortals' cranes gathering at her residence. That night, Lan Qiao was born, and his room was filled with a strange light. At the age of twelve, he was already capable of writing poetry and prose. A fortune-teller told Chen, "Your son has extraordinary bones. If he serves in office, he will rise to the rank of general or prime minister. If he studies Dao, he will become an immortal." Lan Qiao replied, "Being a general or prime minister is not my goal. What I desire is to ascend lightly." From then on, he sought out Daoist texts to read. Worried about learning alone without teachers or friends, he bid farewell to his mother and traveled to the Jianghuai region, eventually reaching the capital. After seven years, he returned and told his mother, "I was originally wandering the rivers and lakes. I returned because I miss you." He gave her a pill that emerged from his gourd, saying, "Take it, and you will enjoy a long life without illness." He stayed for over a year and then departed again, leaving several catties of gold for his mother. "This gold was formed by the breath of true qi," he said. His mother treasured it, but he did not return. A man from Chaozhou named Wu Ziye met him in the capital. It was the height of summer. Together, they climbed Bian Bridge to buy melons. Lan Qiao said, "Dust dirties my melon. I should eat it in the water." He threw himself into the river, and Wu Ziye watched in astonishment.

A melon rind would sometimes float to the surface of the water. The gnawing was also performed with a serious demeanor. Until night, he did not emerge. Wu went to visit his residence, but found him already sound asleep, with the air between his nose like thunder. Slowly opening his eyes, he said, "I am waiting for you to eat melon in the waves." For a long time, Wu did not come, and he asked, "Why is that?" Wu then realized that Qiao had attained the Dao. He bowed twice in shame and gratitude, and then assisted in cooking. Later, traveling to Luoyang, he wore tattered clothing, entering wine shops every time, always drinking several dou. He often placed a hundred sheets of paper under his feet, having people pull them one by one, and none of them were broken. It was because his body was so light. He said to others, "I am the immortal of Luofu Mountain." From this, he ascended to heaven. One day, selling medicine outside the city, he again placed paper under his feet, having viewers take them. When the paper was all taken, his feet floated, the wind and clouds moving freely and unrestrainedly. Stepping and rising, he ascended. Immortal cranes gathered, coming from the south to welcome him. Looking at them, they were faintly visible. Clearly, the sound of flutes and pipes could be heard in the air. He still longed to recite Li Bai's poem: "Gazing down, I cannot reach the sage; my heart, yearning, is filled with sorrow." His mother lived to the age of ninety-seven and then passed away. On the day of her burial, woodcutters heard crying sounds between the ruins and tombs. Those who recognized it claimed that she had returned. The biography was written by Zheng Zong of Yingzhou.

Mao's Ancestors Mao Xuan, a scholar from Quzhou Jiangshan, was in school during the time of the "舍法" system. Unable to manage his livelihood, his family affairs had fallen into disrepair, and he considered selling his house. Before he could sell it, he woke up one morning to see his deceased grandparents and parents sitting in the hall. They wore dignified clothing and had appearances similar to the living. Mao Xuan was shocked and bowed to ask them, "You have been dead for a long time. How can you be here?" They did not answer, but only his father said, "I see that your situation is not good." He then looked up at the house and sighed, saying, "Your future is still long. You should relax." Mao Xuan asked about hell, and his father replied, "Only those with sins enter. I have no sins and will be reborn, but it is not yet my turn." Mao Xuan asked, "If you have not yet been reborn, are you still in the grave?" His father said, "Not so. During the day, we wander aimlessly, but at night, it is not appropriate to discuss." He then mentioned that Mao Xuan had recently participated in a ritual at the graves of the Ye family, and he was there too. Pointing to the Five Path Gods outside the door, he said, "Their divine power is very great. Wild spirits cannot enter." He then pointed to the God of the North and said, "Serve him diligently. After death, you will not go to hell. You should become a disciple of the Big Dipper." Mao Xuan said, "The elders are still here. Should I call my older brother?" His father stopped him, saying, "The time is pressing. I must leave." He told Mao Xuan to go inside, and the others descended into the courtyard, flew away like birds, and disappeared into the sky. Mao Xuan looked longingly, and a servant arrived from outside. The spirits did not wish to interact with the living, so they left quickly.

Fang Dian, courtesy name Dachang, was from Putian. He repeatedly tried for the imperial examinations but failed. Many fortune-tellers said he would not obtain an official salary. A man named Liu Zhongmin from the same county, who served as the magistrate of Tong'an in Quanzhou, advised Fang Dian's elder brother, who was his deputy, saying, "Your younger brother should not seek an official position. If he stops taking the exams, perhaps he can extend his life by several years." Fang Dian's brother did not believe this. In the 15th year of the Shaoxing era, Fang Dian took the imperial examinations and passed. When the list of successful candidates arrived in Tong'an, his brother took it to ridicule Liu Zhongmin. Liu said, "Passing one exam is not enough to be happy about. I fear he will not obtain an official salary." Fang Dian was appointed as the magistrate of Jinjiang. After returning to wait for his next appointment the following year, there was a spring examination in Putian. By convention, students were examined by local school officials, and the local government assigned Fang Dian to this task. After entering the examination hall, he received over a thousand coins daily. Within ten days, he gained over ten thousand coins. He suddenly died in the examination hall. This story was recorded by Chen Yingqiu.

The Record of the Immortal and the Wondrous, Volume Sixteen, Fifteen Stories **Wei Dakě's Rebirth** Wei Zhongda, styled Dakě, was from Huating, Xiuzhou. While serving in an official capacity, he fell ill and was taken to the realm of the dead. He waited for orders in the courtyard, where four people were seated above him. The young man facing west called out, "Let's check it out." The other three hesitated. The young man said, "If we don't check it, how will we proceed?" They replied, "He is already destined to return. Why check? We fear we won't be able to release him."
