The young man was unwilling to let go and called back a red-clothed official to convey his intention. The official held up a toothpick tray, on which there were two red and black plaques. The red one had gold-inscribed good characters, and the black one had white-inscribed evil characters. The young man pointed to the yellow plaque, and the official took it away. A short while later, several people held books that filled the courtyard. A scale was placed horizontally in front, with trays on both sides. The official lifted the books and placed them on the eastern tray. The tray was so heavy it pressed to the ground, causing the ground to shake. Wei could not stand still, and the three people all turned pale. They said, "Earlier, we said it couldn't be checked. Now what will we do?" The young man also looked sad and regretful. After a moment, he said, "Let's check the good ones again." The official took the red plaque away. Suddenly, the northwest corner was slightly bright like the setting sun. A red-clothed Taoist priest held up a jade tray, and the four people all stood up. The Taoist priest arrived.

Sitting in the center, he looked at the documents in the jade plate, which were as thin as chopsticks. An official held it down and placed it on the western tray, which was also pressed to the ground. However, the eastern tray was raised high towards the sky. Suddenly, a great wind arose, rolling up the paper and covering the sky, like crows and kites flying in disorder, leaving none remaining. The four people got up to congratulate each other, inviting Wei to sit on the seat. Wei bowed his hands and said, "Zhongda is not yet forty years old, and has never dared to commit grave sins. How could there be so many records filled with such things?" The young man replied, "Those with good hearts have light sins, while those with bad hearts have heavy sins. Even a single thought not right is recorded. Why must they be truly committed? But they are already completely extinguished and gone." Wei thanked and said, "This is indeed so. I dare to ask what good deeds are." The young man replied, "The court initiated a project to repair the stone bridge of the Three Mountains. You once wrote a letter to advise, which is a memorial." Wei said, "Although you did write a letter, the court did not follow. How beneficial was it?" The young man said, "Your duty was fulfilled. If your words are used, how many tens of thousands of lives could be saved. You will reach the highest position among officials, but there are many sins recorded. You will still not lose the position of Eight Seats. Strive." Then he sent someone to guide him back. Wei later became the Minister of Personnel, and Xu Zhuan heard this from Wei Zhongzi Zang. 郁老侵地 (Note: The phrase "郁老侵地" appears to be an incomplete or separate element in the text, and its meaning is unclear without further context. It is included as is, with no additional interpretation provided.)

A Woman with Two Heads and Four Arms During the reign of Emperor Ling, a woman from Luoyang was born with two heads and four arms. In the village of Wugan in Jintan County, Zhenjiang, the Zhang and Wu families were neighbors. Later, a fire destroyed the village, and everyone dispersed to other places, leaving only an empty site. A man from the same county whose family name is Tang fell ill with a fever and died. He told the officials that he would come back to life. The officials ordered him to go out, and someone accompanied him. When they reached the gate, they saw bustling markets and shops, no different from the human world. They then sat in a tea house. At that time, the elderly of the Wu family had been dead for over ten years. They met as if they had known each other all their lives. The elderly man said happily, "I heard that you would come soon, so I waited here. Now that you know you can return, I will entrust you with something. Do you remember our old house well?" Tang replied, "Yes." The elderly man said, "When we were alive, we were neighbors with the Zhang family. My house's column foundation is within my territory, but the rainwater dripping from the eaves falls on Zhang's land. I secretly benefited from that place and slyly sued the officials to take it. I invaded about three feet of land. Zhang's old man died, and he appealed to him in the underworld. I have already confessed my past wrongdoings and agreed to have the house returned. But both houses have already been reduced to ashes. Zhang has already moved away, and my son has also wandered to Jianchang, where he became the son-in-law of Fu from Nanfeng. The paths of the living and the dead are different, and this intention cannot be conveyed. You kindly pity me and trouble yourself to tell my son to quickly return it to the Zhang family. Make a deed and burn it. If I get this, then the matter will be resolved, and I will be reborn again." Tang agreed. Soon after, the escort arrived, and he bade farewell. When he revived, he called Zhang's son and told him the reason. Zhang's son replied, "In the past, we did indeed dispute it, but now we have moved away. It is useless." Tang, due to Wu's request, could not bear to betray it and sent a message to his son. He took the deed and gave it to Zhang, and wrote a copy and burned it. One day, he dreamed that Wu came to thank him. Tang was the eldest son of a distant relative.

Cai Yuanlang, upon passing the imperial examinations and becoming a new official, was appointed as the magistrate of Qiantang County. During his patrol, he arrived at a village called Tang Village in the late afternoon and decided to rest for the night. A man of extraordinary appearance requested to see him. Cai Yuanlang, who enjoyed associating with Daoist practitioners, promptly invited him to speak and offered him wine before the man left. The next day, Cai Yuanlang stayed elsewhere, but the man returned again. On the third day, they stopped near a village, and the man came once more, drinking several dou of wine. He earnestly requested, "I cannot return home tonight. May I stay here for the night?" Cai Yuanlang initially refused, but the man persisted until Cai, finding no other option, agreed. They shared the same bed, with Cai sleeping on the outer side and the man on the inner side. The man warned, "If anyone comes looking for me in the middle of the night, do not say anything." Cai Yuanlang suspected that the man might be a criminal or a fugitive, and as a magistrate, he felt it would be inconvenient to hide him. However, he had no choice but to agree. As the night wore on, Cai Yuanlang remained wide awake, unable to sleep. He heard voices outside the house, and soon a crowd gathered. They forced their way in, shouting, "Car Four Yuan is here! How can we tolerate this?" They were about to grab him from the bed, but someone said, "We must be careful not to harm those outside the bed. The emperor will certainly be angry, and we will be punished." Cai Yuanlang was extremely frightened.

A man rose and called his servants, but none responded. The Daoist monk slept peacefully as usual, and shaking him did not move him. An outsider remarked, "He has been in a trance for sixty years." It was indeed strange. He sighed for a long time and heard the sound of tearing thousands of sheets of bamboo paper inside. It became quiet at the sound of the rooster crowing. The servants finally responded, but when asked what they saw, they all said they did not know. The monk suddenly woke up and thanked, saying, "I am Chē Sì. Thanks to you, I have escaped this great calamity and can live another sixty years. I have already transcended the world three times. From now on, I have no worries. You will be granted a high rank, and that is why I was spared. If not, both of us would have died. Thinking there was nothing to repay, I have a medicine that can transform paper into iron, iron into copper, copper into silver, and silver into gold. Do you want it?" Cai refused to accept it, but the monk forcefully taught him the art of alchemy, saying, "Use it when you are in need." As dawn approached, the monk left. He was never seen again. Cai only passed on this story to his middle son, Xiu. After Cai died, Xiu's family fled to Guangxi and relied on this knowledge to survive. Cai's guest, Chen Bing, once served as the governor of Xiang Prefecture and said so.

A Woman Who Urinates Pearls Wang Gai, a man from Huzhou, was traveling to Jian'ning in Shaowu as a minor official in the 16th year of the Shaoxing era (1146 CE). He stayed overnight at the Yushan Post Station in Xinzhou. After urinating, he was about to go to bed when he saw a beautiful woman beside him. She reached into his urinal and picked up three or four blue-green grains that resembled pearls. She strung them with a red thread and hung them around her neck. Startled, Wang Gai asked, "Who are you?" but she had already disappeared. From then on, every time he urinated, a crack would appear beside him, and a woman dressed in fine clothes would emerge, either from within a container or from the latrine. She would only disappear after obtaining the pearls. Wang Gai became increasingly weak and exhausted, and doctors and sorcerers were powerless to treat him. This continued for nearly two years. The pearls she had accumulated were numerous, wrapped around her neck and abdomen dozens of times. Later, the number of pearls decreased until only one or two remained, and they turned gradually white. The woman, with a sorrowful expression, said, "I have received great favor from you. My task is accomplished. But I regret harming your life. There is nothing I can do to repay you. I will slowly find a way to do so." She bowed twice and left. That night, Wang Gai did not urinate again. The next day, he died from profuse sweating. The three events were also recounted by Xu Bo.

Li Zhiming Li Zhiming was a man from Jianchang. In the eighth month of the twenty-fourth year of the Shaoxing era (1154 AD), he stayed in a village of Yuzhang. He lay down to sleep but was not yet asleep. The moonlight was bright and clear, and he saw people coming and going outside the window. A moment later, he turned back to face the window and saw a man standing with a black headband and a sweatshirt. In a daze, the man had already entered the room. Li suspected him to be a thief and carefully observed his actions. Soon, the man came forward and walked around the bed. The northeast side of the bed was against the wall, but the man walked through without any obstruction. Just like a ghost, he lifted the curtain and grabbed Li by the neck. Li had courage and strength and raised his hand to support him. He also came with his left hand and resisted again. He wanted to shout loudly, but his throat was blocked as if choking. After a long time, he was able to call out. The two servants replied in unison, "Yes." Li said, "Usually call you at night. Not responding to several calls. Why are you so cautious now. Since you are not sleeping, why didn't you wake me up earlier." They all said, "Seeing a man come before the master. Struggling against each other with great force. Wanting to get up but unable to move their feet. Wanting to shout but unable to breathe. Just hearing the master's voice. The man finally left. We were finally able to speak."

Guangzhou Tomb Mystery A scholar from Guangzhou named Kong Yuanju lived several miles outside the city. Whenever he entered the city, he would pass through a place of random graves. Every day, he would go to the state school in the morning and return in the evening, always passing through the same area. One evening, he returned slightly later than usual, and the sun was still clinging to the horizon. He heard someone repeatedly reciting the line "the leaves are lush, and the yellow birds are flying" from the *Book of Songs*. He determined that the voice was coming from the path he was on, and after a short while, the voice seemed closer, coming from among the graves. He turned his head to look and saw a creature resembling a squatting gourd, covered in hair, with red eyes and a pig-like snout. It was staring at Kong Yuanju and shouted loudly, "The leaves are lush, and the yellow birds are flying." Kong was greatly frightened and hurriedly walked back. He immediately fell ill and died within ten days.

**Bilan Tang** In the home of a commoner in Jianchang County, Nan'gang, the worship of the Purple Lady was very effective. Whenever they asked about future benefits, the spirit would inform them. For example, it would say that tea in the lower Yangtze region was expensive and could be traded, or that there was a shortage of rice in a certain place and it could be transported there, and these predictions were always accurate, bringing great profits. One day, the spirit wrote a message saying, "A distinguished guest will arrive tomorrow. You should treat him well." The family had been preparing their children, servants, and others to wait at the door, but no one came all day. Just as they were about to close the door, a beggar arrived. They invited him in and had him bathe and change clothes. The beggar was overjoyed but also feared that the family might kill him because of the spirit. He pleaded, "Although I am a beggar, I am very lowly. I value my life. Please spare my life." The family explained the reason from yesterday. The beggar said, "If that is the case, please pray again. I will then be able to ask the spirit myself." They burned incense, and the spirit arrived, writing nine characters on the paper: "Alas! Have you forgotten the matter at the Blue Wave Pavilion?" The beggar fainted upon seeing this. After a long time, he came to and wept, saying, "When I was young, I was originally a rich man's son. I had a lifelong promise with a courtesan. Fearing my parents' disapproval, I took her and fled. As we became increasingly destitute, I also feared that our secret would be exposed. So I went to Wuxing and visited the Blue Wave Pavilion. Drunk, I pushed her into the water and went into exile, becoming a beggar. The person you brought here is my wronged soul." He spoke and wept again. The family gave him several hundred gold coins and sent him away. From then on, they no longer worshiped the spirit. This story was told by Li Shaoyu.

Wuxi, Changzhou: The Dai Family Mansion The Dai family of Wuxi was a wealthy household. Thirteen郎, a member of the family, constructed a grand mansion in the town. The mansion was extremely well-crafted, with iron molds used to measure the rafters. Any slight inaccuracy would result in replacement. Additionally, a carriage or vehicle traveled smoothly without obstruction. After a year, the mansion was nearly completed. Thirteen郎 had a dream in which a scholar sat facing east on the main hall and looked at him, saying, "I am Mr. Li Mo, a talented scholar." Upon waking, Thirteen郎 despised him greatly. Several years later, Mr. Li Mo from the town passed the imperial examinations. Thirteen郎 married his daughter to Mr. Li Mo. Before his death, Thirteen郎 instructed his two sons, "You have never been successful and will not be able to properly preserve our family legacy. This mansion will be sold by you. It is better to give it to Mr. Li than to others." As his father had said, the mansion was given to the Li family. During the period of disorder in the Jianyan and Shaoxing eras, bandits frequently raided the area, and many houses in the town were burned down. Only the Li family mansion remained stable and unchanged. It is still occupied today. Mr. Li Mo, courtesy name Ma Jia, once commanded the western part of Zhejiang and held the rank of Middle Doctor in the Treasure Text Hall. This was narrated by his maternal uncle.

Two Rabbits Demand Payment My wife's uncle, Zhang Zongzheng, lived near the wheat fields of Fangcheng. He had a fondness for hunting with traps and nets. Near the side of the ancestral tomb of his father and grandfather, there was a vast forest and large hills, where wild animals gathered in large numbers. Daily, he engaged with his companions in these activities. A dense network of nets covered the mountains, which they called the sky-net. In one net, without counting, there were hundreds of catches. Too busy to pick them up, only a few bad young men rushed and chased on top of them, crushing and killing them, each taking some away. Even if it was snowing or windy, they persisted in their activities. During the chaos, he crossed the river and moved to Wuxi in the ninth year of the Shaoxing era, following his brother, the Censor. He also often shot for entertainment. Once, he found a very small rabbit near Mingyang Temple, with a missing ear, like a mark from being grabbed. Soon after, he felt ill as if mad. He himself took the hunting tools and burned them, built a hermitage, and lived alone. Suddenly, he saw two rabbits speaking human language. One said, "I have been a rabbit for three hundred years. I was at the eastern cemetery of the Zhang family. The Zhang family cemetery in Fangcheng is called the Western Cemetery. My father was buried separately, called the Eastern Cemetery. You killed me." The other rabbit said, "I have been a rabbit for one hundred and eighty years. I hid near Mingyang Temple, coming and going with woodcutters. Once, I was caught by an eagle, struggled, and escaped, injuring my ear. All hawks, dogs, and nets, I can all avoid. I never expected you to use a crossbow. Now we demand payment with our lives." Zhang humbly pleaded for mercy. Everyone around heard it. He was ill for several months and slightly recovered, but remained listless and like a fool. He died ten years later.

Pu Dashao's Ink Pu Dashao, a man from Langzhong, obtained the method of making ink from Ouyang Xiu (Shangu). The ink he made was very fine, and it was favored by scholars in the southeast. Once, a high-ranking eunuch presented it to the emperor. The emperor was paying attention to calligraphy and writing, and upon seeing the inscription "Jinping Pu Shunmei," he asked who the person was. The eunuch replied, "It is the name of Pu Dashao, a Sichuan ink maker." The emperor immediately threw it to the ground, saying, "A mere ink maker dared to use a false name. This is punishable." And so, it was no longer used. His fate was so unfortunate. From then on, the seal only said the name. After Pu Dashao's death, his son Zhimei inherited the method, and he and his fellow townsman Shi Wei were both famous. The governor of Kuizhou, Han Qiu, ordered him to make several thousand pounds of ink. He failed to complete the task on time, and was arrested. The boat capsized in the river, and both craftsmen died. What is sold now is all made by his workers, who stole Pu Dashao's name to make themselves appear important. As told by Du Qixin.

Shengping Lane Official Residence In Shengping Lane, Hongzhou, there was an official residence that was plagued by strange occurrences. In the year 1151 of the Song Dynasty, the house was empty and uninhabited. One day, a merchant selling hats and earrings passed through the back door, and two women called him in. They thoroughly examined his goods and bought two hats, paying half the price first and instructing him to take the remaining money from the main gate. The merchant believed them and waited in front. An old soldier guarding the house asked about the reason, and the merchant told everything. The soldier said, "This is just an empty room. How can there be such women?" He then led them in together. The hall and rooms were covered with accumulated dust, and the two hats were hanging high on the wall. The merchant realized they were ghosts. When he looked at the paid money, there was also none. A year later, the author's cousin, Guan, who lived in the house as an official in Jiangxi, had his nephew, Cheng, who, at night after drinking, went to the toilet. He saw a man with white hair and a disheveled appearance under a peach tree, whose body was very large, sitting with legs spread apart like a winnowing basket. Cheng was drunk and did not ask. When he returned from the toilet, it was still the same. Gradually closer and gradually smaller, the figure was only a few inches tall. When Cheng scolded it, it disappeared. Guan recounted the story.

**The Old Lady Yan of the Yan Family** In the Yan family, there was an elderly female servant named Yan, who had served them for several decades. She was treated with great respect by the family. Even after her death, they continued to honor her with seasonal sacrifices. She appeared to them in a dream and said, "In the afterlife, I am very happy, but I am suffering from a lack of servants." The family drew two female figures and burned them as offerings. She appeared to them again in a dream and said, "You have given me much, but what to do about being weak and useless?" The family, moved by this, instructed the craftsmen to make the figures with sturdy paper bones and painted two beautiful maids. The next day, she appeared to them and said, "These new maids are very pleasing. I am no longer lonely." The following year, during the Cold Food Festival, the family returned from paying respects at the grave. She appeared to them again in a dream and said, "The maids I received have now left me." The family asked, "Why did this happen?" She replied, "I initially did not want to say anything, but the young maids were seduced by Yan San and left." The family, understanding, decided to take action. The next day, they discussed the matter and laughed together. Yan San, her nephew, was usually undisciplined. Since the old lady died, no one had heard whether he was alive or dead. The family decided to draw two elderly figures and burn them as offerings. She appeared again to thank them, saying, "I now have two elderly maids. Thank you." This was the fifth dream she had, and she received two elderly maids as a result.

Zheng Jun's Wife Zheng Jun, courtesy name Minshu, was from Fuzhou. He was the son of Hongzhong, a literary advisor at the Imperial Library. His first wife was Lady Wang, who bore him a daughter named Tai娘. As Lady Wang was dying, she held Zheng Jun's hand and instructed him, "Do not remarry. Take good care of my daughter Tai娘." After her death, Zheng Jun bought a concubine to live with him. After some time, there was a woman from the Teng family in the capital who was about to marry. Zheng Jun heard of her beauty and broke his promise to pay the dowry. One day, as he was about to go to court, he had not yet risen when he saw Lady Wang enter his room. She took a stool and sat by the bed, holding the curtain with her hand. She consoled Zheng Jun and spoke about parting and reunion in life and death, and asked the reason for his remarriage. Zheng Jun said, "Household affairs are entrusted to a concubine, which are not managed at all. I could not help but do so." Lady Wang said, "Since the agreement has been made, what more can I say? If you can take care of Tai娘 as if I were still here, it would not be harmful. I will no longer worry about it." She then talked about other things in great detail. Suddenly, she said, "The grand favor has come to call you. It is time to mount the horse." And then she left. Zheng Jun quickly asked, "When will we meet again?"

He said, "To meet again after ten years on a boat by the river." The next day, Zheng spoke with his younger brother, expressing grief and lamentation without joy. Nevertheless, he ultimately married into the Teng family. During the early Jianyan era, Zheng was dismissed from his position as Supervisor of Tea and Salt in Hunan. He then bought thousands of large cypress trees and went to Weiyang (Yangzhou). At that time, the temporary capital's government offices were being established, and the price of wood soared, yielding ten times the profit. Not long after, the Jin invaders attacked Yangzhou, and many people fled. Zheng was burdened by his wealth and was reluctant to leave. He then planned to buy a boat and sail down the river, but the river was crowded with boats like a web, and he could not proceed. He heard that the enemy had arrived and urgently returned to the city, buying over a hundred taels of gold. Just as he was leaving the city, Jin cavalry was already behind him. Zheng rode a horse and fled, but one rider shot at him from behind. Zheng turned around and said, "I am Zheng, the supervisor. Do not harm me." The rider recognized him as an official, caught up with him, and threw a knife at him, causing him to fall off the horse. The rider took the gold and returned. Zheng was severely wounded and lay exhausted in the grass. His servant rushed to see him, but it was already too late to save him. He died two days later. Zheng had no son. Ten years after what Wang said, these two events are still according to Dingguo's account.

Hua Cheng Temple By Shen Chi Yao Shen Chi Yao, serving as the magistrate of Pengze County in Jiangzhou, received an order to travel to Linjiang in the twenty-fourth year of the Shaoxing era. After passing through Hu Kou County, sixty miles away, he stayed at the Hua Cheng Temple. He went to the guesthouse and, that night, visited the temple's chief monk. The monk invited him to stay in a separate room in the master's quarters. As they began to discuss the strange occurrences in the guesthouse, the monk said, "There was an old coffin in the room last year. A guest who stayed there saw light inside the coffin and was greatly frightened. He sat up, thinking deeply and observing carefully. He saw the light as if it contained a person moving. He became even more afraid. The room was adjacent to the Buddha hall. Knowing that the situation was urgent, he decided to open the door and rush directly to the hall. Just as he began to step out, the ghost in the coffin lifted the lid and extended its head. The guest stepped out, and the ghost followed suit. The guest then stepped back, and the ghost did the same. This happened several times. The guest, now in a state of panic, knew he could not stay and rushed out. The ghost chased after him. The guest ran around the hall in circles, shouting for help. A group of monks came to his aid, but before they arrived, the guest, exhausted, fell to the ground, almost being caught by the ghost. Suddenly, the ghost collided with the hall pillar, making a clear, resonant sound, and then everything fell silent. The monks arrived, helped the guest up, and examined the remains. They found the bones scattered on the ground. Later, the family of the deceased came to the temple, suspecting that someone from the temple had disturbed the coffin, and they sued the temple in court. After several months, the case was resolved.

Wu Kui, courtesy name Gonglu, was from Jianzhou. During the reign of Emperor Huizong (Zhenghe period), after completing his studies at the Imperial Academy, he returned home. Passing through Qiantang, he dreamed that officials greeted him and led him into a grand hall, where a high-ranking official with a golden seal was present. The official gestured for Wu to sit in the upper seat, but Wu declined, saying, "I am merely a commoner. To suddenly be seated here would give rise to suspicions of improperly seeking office. I dare not." The official then left. Wu sat facing forward on the bed, and a clerk brought a stack of documents to him, which he read with his hands. Wu thought that the local clerks were trying to overwhelm him because he had just taken up office, but he had no way to resolve the situation. Looking down, he saw several prisoners, all handsome men and women, standing with shackles on. The cases they were being punished for were all related to adultery. Wu wrote in large letters, "According to the law, those who commit adultery with someone else's wife will be deprived of descendants; those who commit adultery with someone else's unmarried daughter will have their descendants become licentious." The clerks looked at each other in awe and admiration for his quickness. "The case is now resolved," they said. Wu then awoke. Returning to the capital, he told his fellow student Jin Yanxing Anjie about the dream. Jin, the Gold Attendant, recounted the story.

**Volume Seventeen, Entry Fifteen of *Yijian Ji*** **Clay Figure Fetus** In the Chaojue Temple of Xianjing, there was a Nine Mothers Hall located at the top of a mountain. A caretaker named Huang, who was in charge of providing incense and fire, looked at the clay figure of a nursing maid. Her breasts hung outside, and he found her pleasing. Each time he came, he would stroke and fondle her, showing great care. One day, the clay figure’s eyes moved, and she then got up and walked, taking his hand and leading him behind the screen for intimate contact. From then on, it became a daily occurrence, lasting for several months. Over time, he accumulated illness, but still struggled to climb the mountain despite being ill. The abbot secretly watched him, and when he reached halfway up the mountain, a woman greeted him with a smile. The next day, the abbot followed behind him, and the woman came again. She hit him with a walking stick, causing him to fall to the ground with a clanging sound. In the broken earth, a fetus was found, like one several months old. The abbot ordered the caretaker to take it back, crushed it into powder, mixed it with medicine, and ate it. The caretaker then recovered.

A Courtesan of Yongkang In the Yongkang Commandery, there was a courtesan. She visited the Temple of Lingxian Wang. Outside the temple, she saw a soldier, tall and slender, with a majestic and robust appearance. His legs were adorned with intricate and dynamic patterns. She looked at him intently and admired him, becoming so attached that she could not leave. By dusk, her family forcibly took her home, and she felt as if she had lost something, restless and unhappy. That night, a guest arrived to ask for lodging. His appearance matched the man she admired. The courtesan was overjoyed beyond words, thinking she had found this guest too late. The next morning, the guest left early, but returned at dusk. He stayed for several nights, then suddenly wept and said, "I am not a human being; I am a soldier from the temple. Because you found me pleasing, I violated the rules and came to be with you. I failed to attend night duty, and was reported and punished by the authorities. Tomorrow, I will be flogged and exiled. When I pass by your home, kindly buy many paper money to give me." The courtesan also wept and agreed. As promised, the soldier carried a heavy iron chain, his body covered in blood, and was branded on the face, saying he was to be exiled to a certain place. Two strong soldiers accompanied him. As he passed by the courtesan's home, he bid farewell. The courtesan prepared an offering and burned paper money, crying as she sent him off. On another day, when she visited the temple, the statue of the soldier had fallen to the ground.

**A Person Dies and Becomes a Cow** A person named Wang from Daojiang County, Yongkang Army, was known for his severe and harsh nature. In the fifth year of the Shaoxing era (1135 CE), he served as the official responsible for handling various administrative tasks at the Transport Commission of Sichuan. He was ordered to oversee the salt monopoly in the Tongchuan Circuit. He personally went to the salt well and forced the people to enter into an agreement, ordering double the usual quota to be recognized. Those who were supposed to produce 5,000 pounds of salt were instead required to produce 10,000 pounds. The following year, those who failed to meet the quota would have their property confiscated. Wang knew that the people could not fulfill the agreement, so he planned to seize their property and had officials personally supervise the boiling of salt. After returning to report, he claimed that the salt quota had doubled and was recommended to the Pacification Commissioner. He was appointed as the Transport Commissioner of Lixzhou Circuit but died shortly after. Yang Shixin, from Pengshan County, Meizhou, was awaiting appointment as the governor of Hezhou while in the fields. He dreamed of Wang coming to visit, emerging from behind his official robes with a cow. Just as he was in shock, his maidservant also woke up from a nightmare, saying she saw Wang, the transport commissioner, come.

A man had a cow's tail growing on his back. He was still talking to others when someone reported that a calf had been born. He quickly lit a fire to see it, and the calf lifted its head and shed tears. The matter soon became widely known. There was a man named Ma from Zizhong who also served as an official in the imperial financial administration. Whenever he went out to supervise the collection of money in various counties, he valued only the quantity, without considering whether the quotas were realistic or not, and insisted on strict deadlines. He took great pride in this approach. The people of Shu, due to his harsh and relentless nature in collecting money, nicknamed him "horse brush." Someone warned him about the story of the man with the cow's tail. Ma replied, "Even if the world were to produce two-tailed creatures, why bother to ask?" Soon after, a boil broke out on the left side of his back. The wound healed slightly but then broke out again on the right. The two boils faced each other, looking like whip marks, and were several inches deep, with the diaphragm clearly visible through the sores, filling the room with a foul odor. His colleagues came to visit him when he was ill, and Ma said, "One should take someone as a warning, and the other regrets it too late." He died just one year after the man with the cow's tail.

Ni Hui, a native of Chengdu, was skilled in the art of divination. In the spring of Dingwei during the Jingkang era, the royal family was in turmoil, and Shu was far from the imperial court, with the postal service cut off. Those who understood the situation considered it a cause for concern. When Deputy Governor Yu Qian, Qi Duan, and Bian Tong visited him, Ni Hui asked, "Given the state of the nation, you must be aware of the situation." He replied, "This is precisely what the ancients referred to as 'the time when there is no ruler for three months.' Calendar experts consider the intercalary month as ordained by heaven. Last year, the leap month was placed in November, and the north has become even stronger, while the element of fire has weakened and faded. If the capital is not held, it will certainly fall in this month. If the calendar officials had foresight, they could have moved the leap month to May to support the element of fire, which would have still allowed for a chance of revival. But it is too late now. However, based on my calculations, the celestial mandate of the nation will only last until the year Bingwu, with one more cycle remaining. On May 1st this year, the cycle will be reborn, and it will continue infinitely. But there are still two months until now."

It is uncertain whether this will be achieved by this day. Therefore,虞 and窦 were asked to cast their fortunes.虞's divination resulted in the trigrams for申,酉, and戌.窦's divination resulted in the trigrams for戌,酉, and申. Upon completion of the divination, the reader expressed joy, saying, "There is no need to worry." The initial part of the divination was extremely difficult, but it stabilized in the middle and became excellent in the final part. The fate of the Song dynasty will become even more enduring from this point. However, the divination showed signs of a royal pardon, and within a hundred days, there will be a significant event (likely a pardon), which can be verified. The two officials were both joyful and fearful. Later, it was confirmed that the capital was indeed captured in the leap month. On May 1st, the emperor ascended the throne in Nanjing. The pardon reached Chengdu, and the date of the divination was about 95 days before the event. In the winter of the second year of the Shaoxing era,虞's son,并甫允文, visited辉.辉 said, "There will be no more days to see you." Next year, I will enter an unlucky period, known as "father and son not meeting." I wanted to send a child to another city to ward off bad luck, but it was already too late. I will surely die. On the day of the beginning of spring, he indeed died.

#### 解三娘 In the spring of the twenty-seventh year of the Shaoxing era (1157 AD), Zhao Feng, the leader of the rear army of Xing Prefecture, was ordered by the commander to inspect troops in various prefectures. He arrived at Guozhou and stayed at the Nanchong post station. He ordered officials to place a bed in the central hall. A post station official approached and said, "This hall has a spirit. At night, one must hear crying sounds. Usually guests come here, but they often avoid staying and dare not stay. Instead, they stay in the west room of the hall." Zhao Feng laughed and said, "Am I afraid of ghosts?" He finally slept in the hall. At night, the crying sound came from outside, as if something went directly to his sleeping place. Zhao Feng said, "Do you have a grievance? Do you want to speak? Speak out. I will make things right for you. Otherwise, leave quickly." Indeed, it left. After a while, it came again. All his followers heard the sound of footsteps. The next day, he told the prefect Wang Zhongfu. Wang thought it was nonsense. That night, he went to a banquet in the prefecture and returned home late at night. When he was drunk and could not sleep, he leaned on a folding stool to rest. A woman with loose hair stood in front. She said, "I am Lian Nu, the daughter of the magistrate of Jie County."

A woman from the Central Plains. She fled to Shu during the chaos and lost her virtue to the tea and horse official of the Qin state, Ji Min. Her home was actually located in this inn. Li had a daughter who married the son of the prefectural governor, Ma Daifu, named Shaojing. The woman was given as a concubine. Unfortunately, she was privately favored by Master Ma due to her beauty. Li informed her father, who beat her to death with a stick. Her breath was still not completely gone, so they immediately ordered a large pit to be dug, and her body was thrown in and buried. It has been thirty years since then. Fortunately, the general pitied her and allowed her to be reborn. Feng said, "You have been dead for a long time. The scholars pass by here every day. Why didn't you come forward sooner to clear your name?" She replied, "My remains long to be buried. I have never forgotten for a moment." There is a deity guarding this place, and it does not allow her to come out frequently. Ten years ago, she cried out at night and came out to complain. The earth deity told her, "Later, when General Zhao comes here, that will be the time for your wrongs to be righted." She has been waiting day and night for the general's arrival, so she dares to ask. Feng said, "If that is true, I will remember you." The woman thanked him and left. He sent someone to follow and watch her. They arrived at the hall, outside the outer wall, and she disappeared.

The next day, Feng summoned a monk to recite Buddhist scriptures and perform a memorial service. Afterward, he set off on his journey. In the evening, he arrived in the eastern county of Tongchuan and stopped at the county post. The woman appeared again in front of him. She had already tied her hair into a high bun. Feng said, "I have already performed Buddhist rituals for you. Why do you follow me?" She replied, "The favor you have shown me is indeed great. But my bones are still outside the hall. Who else but the general can retrieve them?" Feng said, "I am a guest here, and I have already left that place. How can I exert my strength for you? Why don't you appeal to the prefectural governor Wang Lanchong?" She said, "It's not that I don't know. The gate of the spear has a divine presence, and it does not allow entry without permission. However, my grievance can only be resolved by Wang Lanchong. Without the general's support, how can it reach Wang Lanchong? If my bones do not come out, then I cannot be reborn. If my bones are released and I am reborn, it depends solely on the general's words and efforts." Feng agreed again and once more prepared the matter, sending an intermediary to inform Wang the prefect. Wang then sought out the former subordinate of Li the Minister of Household.

Only Tan Yong remained. He was entrusted to find his bones. Tan Yong led a dozen soldiers to dig at the base of the wall in search of them. For two days, they were confused and could not find the location. Tan Yong summoned a witch mother to ask about it. The witch called herself a holy woman and spoke in ghostly language. She called out to Tan Yong and rebuked him, saying, "You buried me with your own hands. How could you truly have forgotten where I am?" The place where you are digging is correct, but it is still too shallow. When you laid me down, you used a wooden bed. The wood is still there. If you find the wood, the bones will follow. The skull bone is the lowest. Please, at all costs, retrieve it for me. If I do not get my skull bone, I cannot be reborn. Tan Yong was terrified and submitted. The next day, they indeed found the body. The county moved the burial to a high plateau. At that time, Shao Jing was the magistrate of Linshui County in Qiu Prefecture. Soon after, he was transferred to the position of judicial officer in Puzhou. When he met the Jie family, he recounted the events of that day. Shao Jing also died shortly after. Guan Shouqing's eldest grandson first became a teacher here and once wrote a record of it. Yu Bingfu was the governor of Qiu Prefecture, and Shao Jing was still serving as a magistrate at the time. **Dream Medicine Formula**

虞并甫。绍兴二十八年。自渠州守被召至临安。憩北郭外接待院。因道中冒暑得疾。泄痢连月。重九日。梦至一处。类神仙居。一人被服如仙官。延之坐。视壁间有韵语药方一纸。读之数过。其词曰。暑毒在脾。湿气连脚。不泄则痢。不痢则疟。独炼雄黄。烝面和药。甘草作汤。服之安乐。别作治疗。医家大错。梦回尚能记。即录之。盖治暑泄方也。如方服之。遂愈。 虞并甫。In the 28th year of the Shaoxing era. He was summoned from his post as governor of Quzhou to Lin'an. He rested at the Reception Hall outside the northern suburb. Because of the heat on the journey, he fell ill, suffering from diarrhea for months. On the Double Ninth Festival, he dreamed of a place resembling a dwelling of immortals. A person dressed like an immortal official invited him to sit. He saw a poem-like prescription on a piece of paper on the wall and read it several times. The words said: "The heat poison is in the spleen. Moisture affects the feet. If not diarrhea, then dysentery. If not dysentery, then malaria. To refine realgar alone, steam and mix with medicine. Make a decoction with licorice. Taking it brings peace and joy. To treat differently, a great mistake by the doctor." Even upon waking, he could still remember it, and immediately recorded it. It is a formula for treating summer diarrhea. As prescribed, he took it, and then recovered. 孟蜀宫人 陈甲、字符父。仙井仁寿人。为成都守李西美璆馆客。舍于治事堂东偏之双竹斋。绍兴二十一年四月。西美浣花回得疾。旬日间甲已寝。闻堂上妇人语笑声。即起映门窥观。有女子十余。皆韶艾好容色。而衣服结束。颇与世俗异。或坐或立。或步庭中。甲犹疑其为帅家人。以主人翁病辄出。但怪其多也。顷之。一人曰。中夜无以为乐。盍赋诗乎。即口占曰。晚雨廉纤梅子黄。晚云卷雨月侵廊。树阴把酒不成饮。识着无情更断肠。一人应声答之曰。旧时衣服尽云霞。 The Palace Lady of Shu Chen Jia, style name Yuanfu. From Xianjing Renshou. He was a guest of Li Ximei Qiu, the governor of Chengdu, and stayed in the Double Bamboo Study to the east of the Hall of Affairs. In April of the 21st year of the Shaoxing era, Ximei returned from washing the flowers and fell ill. Within ten days, Jia had already retired to bed. He heard the laughter and voices of women upstairs and immediately got up to peek through the door. There were more than ten women, all beautiful and graceful, and their clothes and attire were quite different from the secular world. Some were sitting, some were standing, and some were walking in the courtyard. Jia still suspected they were the families of the general, as the master was ill and they had come out. He was only surprised by their large number. Soon, one of them said, "In the middle of the night, there is nothing to do for fun. Why don't we compose a poem?" She then recited: "Evening rain is fine and plum fruits are yellow. Evening clouds roll and the moon invades the corridor. Under the tree shade, drinking wine does not make for a drink. Recognizing the absence of feeling is even more heart-wrenching." Another woman answered immediately: "All the old clothes are all clouds and mists."

If you haven't reached the fairyland, you haven't truly come home. Today, the pavilions are all unfamiliar. Only because of the ancient trees, the Xuānhuá Palace is remembered. Others are just beginning to think. Jiǎ found his poetry words not human-like. He finally realized it was a ghostly entity. Suddenly, it became silent and nothing was seen. Later, he told the elders of Shu Prefecture. They all said that during the time when the Wang family ruled, they once built the Xuānhuá Palace on the Mokho Pool. Its name is mentioned in the Five Dynasties History. The Meng family followed this tradition. The current prefectural hall stands on its original site. The ghost who wrote poetry was likely a palace concubine. Ximěi's illness then did not improve. In the old Shu Prefecture, the drum was not beaten at the afternoon mealtime. If it was beaten, the sound of dozens of women crying in groups was heard. It is said that the Meng family used to kill palace women at the afternoon mealtime, using the drum sound as a rhythm. Therefore, the ghosts would cry upon hearing it. The envoy Cao Wò, acting as military governor, wrote an offering to the spirits. He ordered the drum to be beaten according to the ritual, and the crying also stopped. Later, the practice was discontinued. Jiǎ passed the imperial examination in the thirtieth year of the Shaoxing era.

Buddha Heads in a Fish's Belly Fan, the wife of He Ci from Zizhou, worshiped Buddha very respectfully. One day, her family cooked a fish. After cutting open the belly, they saw something extremely tough wrapped in fat. When they cut it open, it turned out to be two Buddha heads. Her family carved wood into a whole body to support them and has worshipped them ever since. He Ci passed the imperial examinations in the year Jia Chen of the Xuanhe era and later became the governor of Kai Prefecture. All eight matters were reported by Yu Bingpu. Fan was his cousin. Xu Guohua Xu Guohua from Jian'an entered the Imperial Academy during the Xuanhe era. He dreamed of climbing a high building. A large golden bell hung from the building, and a magnificent man in golden armor stood beside the bell. The man looked at Xu, struck the bell, and said, "Twenty-seven ranks." He struck again, saying, "Your position will not exceed that of a junior official." He struck a third time, saying, "You will be part of the seventh category." Xu understood and said, "One must pass the imperial examinations. Becoming an official up to the level of an external attendant is enough." He wrote it down in a document but could not understand the meaning of the seventh category and twenty-seven ranks. In the year Bing Wu of the Jingkang era, the Jurchen cavalry attacked the city. Many students in the Imperial Academy died from foot disease. Xu also died from this disease. The local man Dong Zongju wanted to bury him in the eastern city cemetery, but there were no more plots left. All were buried outside the wall. Dong marked the place with a sign, which was exactly in the seventh grave of the twenty-seventh row. Xu returned to comfort his father and showed his handwritten note, which was the message from the divine in his dream. There was no mistake. It was said by □ De Sheng from Ningguo.

Qinghui Pavilion Guangxi Zhaozhou, the most perilous land of plague and poison, yet its mountains and waters are quite clear and graceful. The garden of the prefecture has a pavilion named Tianhui. During the Jianyan period, the prefect Li Pi, whose name coincided with that of the Jin state, wished to change it. He requested a name from the guest scholar Xu Shichuan, but after a long time, he did not receive one. A person named Fan Zi changed the name to Qinghui. The banner was already raised, and Xu visited Li, sitting together on the pavilion. After a while, they took their walking sticks and walked to the four corners of the garden. They saw a piece of stone in the accumulated earth, clearly like writing. They ordered it to be taken and cleaned, revealing it to be a record written by Qiu Jun. The record roughly states: "I chose this pavilion for its beauty and named it Tianhui, taking its natural scenery as inspiration. On a certain day in the future, there will be a common person who will rename it Qinghui, which can be a source of amusement." Investigating the initial naming day of Fan Zi, there was no mistake.

The Banana Spirit In Xinghua, there was a man named Chen Chen. During the Chongning period, he was exiled to the self-reflection hall at De'an Prefecture for offending the court by submitting a memorial. He shared a room with two students, Liu and Li. Li was on the inside, Chen in the middle, and Liu on the outside. One night, Liu woke up and felt heat beside him. He saw an object wrapped in a red blanket lying next to him. He was greatly frightened. The next night, he went to bed before the others, rushed to the inner part of the bed, and switched places with Li. Li saw the same thing. They were all too afraid to speak. At night, they competed for the more comfortable positions. Chen said, "Are there something to be afraid of? I will face it." After he fell asleep, he heard a sighing sound outside, as if someone wanted to enter but was hesitant. On another night, Chen went to bed first, Liu went to the toilet, and then returned to his original position. He saw the same thing again. Finally, he told Chen the truth. Chen bravely confronted the entity. He heard a sound again and shouted out, causing the entity to run out of the window and disappear into the banana plants. The next day, they cut down all the bananas. They dug the ground more than a zhang (about 3.3 meters) deep but found nothing. From then on, the strange occurrences ceased. All suspected it to be the spirit of the banana plant. This story was told by Huang Ziheng Yanzi, a man from Huangde'an.

Yao Zhong and the Four Ghosts Yao Zhong, originally a general in Wu Jie's army, once fought a minor battle with the enemy and suffered a slight defeat. Wu Jie wanted to execute him, but Yao Zhong said, "Because four subordinate generals led the troops to retreat first, this is why we lost." Wu Jie summoned the four generals and had them executed, but released Yao Zhong. Several years later, Yao Zhong led troops to stay at a mountain inn. There, he saw four headless people, all about two feet tall, bowing in the courtyard. They said, "We failed in our mission and should die. However, if you do not speak up, we can be spared. Now we are all dead, so we come to claim our lives." Yao Zhong replied, "Previously, we retreated in defeat. I should have been punished by military law. Having already shown leniency, but was criticized by the younger teacher. If I do not clarify myself, then I will die in your place." The four people said, "At that time, the retreat. But select one person to retreat first. Would be sufficient to cover the responsibility. Why go to such extremes?" Yao Zhong had no response. The four ghosts gradually became loud and aggressive, wanting to ascend. Suddenly, an elderly man with white hair emerged from the ground, also about two feet tall. He asked them, "You all failed in battle and should face the law. It is your fate. How can you appeal again?" He shouted them away, and they disappeared with a sound. The old man also disappeared. People thus knew that Yao Zhong would surely become important. Ten more years later, he was appointed as the military governor and commander of the Xingyuan army. Told by Lu Binzhifu.

Chen Maolin's Dream Chen Maolin, a scholar from Changle, Fuzhou, dreamed of arriving at the grand hall below. He paid respects alongside many others in a ceremonial manner. The official tablet recorded that he was just beginning an official position and was unfamiliar with court etiquette. Upon waking, he believed that he would surely top the imperial examination, appearing before the highest authority. He then changed his name to "Dream Omen." In the 17th year of the Shaoxing era (1147 AD), he became the top-ranked candidate in the provincial examination and attended a celebratory banquet. Along with his peers, he paid respects at the Grand Accomplishment Hall. By old custom, the eldest in age was considered the leader, but Chen refused, saying, "I am the top candidate and should lead the many scholars." None dared to argue with him. After burning incense, he was supposed to perform two bows, but he mistakenly performed three. Someone who had heard of his dream laughed and said, "This is what they meant by 'just beginning an official position, unfamiliar with court etiquette.'" Chen was also bewildered, wondering if his dream had come true, as he did not pass the examination. Lin Zhiqi, a young and insightful scholar, commented on the matter.

Zhang Dezhao A man from Jianyang named Zhang Dezhao. He spent his entire life pursuing the jinshi degree, an honor that eluded him despite his efforts. By special imperial favor, he was appointed to an official position. He contracted a severe illness, likely typhoid fever, and was taken away by a yellow-robed person holding a talisman, arriving at the underworld. Standing up boldly in the court, he declared, "I have been pursued to Zhang Dezhao of Jianzhou." The ruler of the underworld, angered, said, "I ordered you to pursue Kong Zhaode of a certain state. How could you make such a mistake?" He then sent officials to punish his crime and ordered Zhang to return. Zhang humbly pleaded, "I have devoted my life to Confucian studies and am now old. I have only obtained this lowly position. Today, I have come to this place. I wish to know just once how long my life will be and what my fortune will be. I hope you will have mercy and grant me this knowledge." The overseer replied, "The mysteries of heaven cannot be revealed. It is difficult to predict one's lifespan." Zhang then bowed and asked about his future official position. After pondering for a while, as if consulting records, the overseer said, "You will reach the position of a county magistrate." Zhang then returned. On his way, he encountered a servant and asked where she had come from. She replied, "I have been here for several days. There is no trouble at home." Continuing on, he reached the edge of a deep valley and fell, waking up. When he inquired about his home, he learned that the servant had died shortly after he left. Zhang's condition improved, and he visited Liu Yancong and his son Liu Hui in the mountains of Chongan. He recounted his experiences to them. Liu Hui said, "You are old now. Do you still have ambitions for glory? You are currently serving as a county clerk or minor magistrate. As you predicted, you will become a county magistrate, but it will take another ten years. Be content with your current situation." That year, Zhang was transferred to serve as a county magistrate in Qingliu, Ting Prefecture. After a year in office, the county magistrate resigned, and Zhang temporarily took charge of the administration. He then passed away, just three years after his encounter in the underworld. Liu Hui recounted this story.

Xiasan Pine In the eastern Xiasan Temple of Qingyuan County, Guangzhou, the mountains and rivers wind and twist, and the forests are lush and dense. There stands an ancient Flying-to-Here Hall. About ten steps southwest of the hall, a large pine tree grows by the cliff, with a sprawling and spreading canopy. In the tenth month of the first year of the Dazheng era (1107), Qian Shiyu, the Imperial City Envoy from Nanchang, resigned from his military post in Guangzhou and returned north. He moored his boat at the temple. His followers chopped the roots of the pine tree to extract resin for lighting at night. The following year, Qian Jilao, a palace guard, traveled from Guangzhou to Lianzhou. Passing through the temple, he dreamed of an old man with white hair and a face showing signs of sorrow. The old man said, "I have lived here for three hundred years. Unlucky to encounter a relative of the public, who could not restrain his followers, leading to the chopping off of my knee as a substitute for a candle, causing me to bleed until now. If you can inform the abbot of the temple, exert effort and repair, perhaps the blind wind will act, without the worry of being shaken, to reach the end of one's natural lifespan, it would be a great favor."

The Elderly Monk asked about the entity's name and place of residence. The entity replied, "I am not a human with a round head and square feet. I am a being with spiritual qualities among plants. I came from the southwest. That is where I am." The Elderly Monk was instructed not to forget this. He awoke and suspected it might be a pine tree. Due to the miraculous and vivid nature of the event, he needed to wait for the temple gates to open before entering to report it. At dawn, the morning bell had not yet rung, and he fell back into a peaceful sleep. By morning, the boatmen had already untied the ropes and sailed several miles away. The Elderly Monk was deeply troubled and could not forget the event. Passing through Hengguang, he told the official of Jian'an, Peng Gui, about it. In the second year of Zhenghe, Peng Gui resigned from his official position and traveled to Guangzhou. Passing by the temple, he inquired about the event as described by the Elderly Monk. Indeed, he found a massive pine tree with a stump about a foot in diameter, its bark damaged and peeling, with sap continuously flowing. It had been this way for seven years. He then reported to the head monk, who mixed soil to fill in the gaps and surrounded the tree with large bamboo to protect it from the outside. Qujiang native Hu Yu wrote a record of the pine tree dream, documenting the event. I have often visited this temple, and the pine tree still stands to this day.

Yiji Jia Zhi, Volume 18, Sixteenth Story Yang Jing's Repayment of a Wrong Yang Jing, a man from Lin'an, initially served as a low-ranking military officer in charge of transporting flowers and stones to the capital. He gained favor with Tong Guan and rose to the rank of Wugong Daifu (Military Officer of the First Rank). He became a state military commander and was nearing the end of his term of office. He crafted three highly exquisite mother-of-pearl inlaid vessels. He bought a local craftsman named Chen Liu and instructed his son, Eleven, to bring them to the capital. One vessel was to be presented to the inner palace, one to be given to Old Cai (a nickname for Cai Jing), and one to Tong Guan, in hopes of securing a reappointment. However, Eleven only presented one vessel to the emperor and sold the other two at Xiangguo Temple, earning hundreds of coins for entertainment expenses. He failed to return on time, and Yang Jing waited for him for a long time before deciding to travel north on official business. They met somewhere between Su and Si. Eleven, fearing his father's blame, said, "All the gifts were sold by Chen Liu. I almost couldn't escape punishment." Yang Jing believed him. Upon arriving in the capital, he called for Chen Liu to question him. Chen Liu answered disrespectfully, and Yang Jing beat him. After three strokes, Chen Liu cried out "Long live the emperor" and was released. Chen Liu returned to his boat and told his wife, "General Yang cannot discipline his son. He blamed me for his words. I cannot bear it and drowned myself in the Bian River." Yang Jing was appointed as a state military commander and returned home. The Chief Transport Commissioner Wang Fu, who was in charge of the Yìngfèng Office, appointed Yang Jing as an additional official and often had him stay in the office. It was the seventh year of the Xuanhe era (1125 AD). On a certain day in April of that year, Yang Jing was in his office when a group of laborers pulling a large cargo ship got drunk and fought, breaking noses and causing bleeding. They rushed into the transport office, causing chaos. Yang Jing looked around in surprise as if he had seen something. He quickly hid behind a screen and then fell to the ground. He was carried home and immediately fell ill, becoming speechless and confused.

Not eating. At the time, there was a monk in Linping Town who could use the filth trace method to control ghosts. He was acquainted with Jing. The monk was sent to summon the ghost. Upon arrival, the monk saw the ghost and said, "I am Chen Liu, a carpenter. A few years ago, I was unjustly killed by Dr. Yang. I petitioned the Eastern Peak. The Peak Emperor ordered me to hold the document and pursue the killer. For years, I could not get close. I returned to report. The emperor was angry and immediately sent me back again. He said that Yang Jing had not arrived. You need not return. It has been more than a year now. There are many deities in the public office. I have been obstructed for a long time. A few days ago, several people were covered in blood. The land deities and their ilk all fled in fear. I took the opportunity to advance and finally arrived here." The monk advised him, "In a past life, you had a grudge with this person. In this life, he killed you. You now seek revenge. This cycle of revenge is endless. When will it end? I will order the Yang family to feed ten thousand monks, hold a grand water and land feast to thank you. What do you say to letting it go?" The ghost bowed and replied, "If I had heard these words from the monk before, I would have happily left. Now, I have incurred the anger of the supervisor. I fear that if I return, I will have no chance of release in the depths of darkness. I cannot do without the Yang public." The monk had no strategy to help. He saw a lock on Jing's neck and said, "The matter is as it is. Let me unlock it. Let him eat his fill. Can he also handle his family affairs?" Jing agreed. The monk then unlocked the lock, and Jing immediately rose as usual. However, as soon as he was away from the monk, he became drowsy and confused again. He died after a few days. Wu Xingju from Fuyang, who was formerly a servant in my household, personally witnessed Jing's illness and his death.

Yang Gong Quanpu's Dream of His Father Yang Gong Quanpu, a native of Zizhou, had his father die in the year 1113 of the Zhenghe era. His father had not been buried yet. In the following spring, he dreamed that his father returned home. Yang Gong Quanpu asked his father when he would be eligible to take the imperial examination. His father replied, "There is a bureaucrat in the underworld who is in charge of the records. He is an old acquaintance of mine. I once took the register to read it. You have no name in the three halls. You can only be eligible through the imperial examination." He also said, "Do you know that the court has implemented the Five Rites?" Yang Gong Quanpu answered, "I do not know." His father then asked many questions about family matters in great detail. After speaking, he left as swiftly as a bird. In the eighth month of that year, the new Five Rites were officially announced. Scholars whose parents had not been buried were not allowed to enter school. Yang Gong Quanpu understood his father's words and completed the funeral arrangements that winter. In the year 1127, he became a candidate for the imperial examination, believing that the dream was not fulfilled. However, he did not achieve success initially. In the year 1121 of the Xuanhe era, the shafe system was abolished, and the imperial examination system was reinstated. He finally passed the imperial examination in the year 1124.

The Red Earth Cave Thirty miles outside the city of Zizhou, on the side of a red earth mound, there is a cave. It is said to be deep and unfathomable. A man from Puzhou named Liang Ziyin, who boiled salt from a well in Rongzhou, often passed by the cave entrance. Once, he and several companions prepared three days' worth of dry food and carried a torch made of birch bark and entered the cave. At first, the path was completely dark, covered with fox droppings and with bats flying around. After passing more than a hundred steps, the ground was as clean as swept, with stalactites hanging from the stone like pearl tassels. After about half a day, they heard the sound of a water mill coming from above. It was the Jialing River. Fearing this, they quickly exited and finally could not explore its source. A Hat Covering the Head Wang Longguang, courtesy name Tianchong, was from Zizhou. He went to the capital to take the higher-level examination and passed through Jianshou, Zitong County, Seven-Bend Mountain. He visited the temple of Ying Xian Wu Lie Wang, commonly known as the Temple of Zhang Xianggong. He dreamed of someone holding a list, with no names on the front but names on the back. There was also someone holding a hat covering his head. Upon waking, he was pleased, thinking that scholars who passed the exam would wear a hat. That year, he was exempt from the examination and did not pass, but was promoted to an inner hall. The next time, in the eighth year of Zhenghe, he finally passed the exam. He had already understood the meaning of the list on the back. At that time, it was forbidden to use names like Longtianjun, Yujwang, etc. On the day of the announcement of results, he was personally given the name Chongguang, with a hat placed on his head. This means this.

Lin Xiangyong, courtesy name Tianhe, was from Mingzhou. In the seventh year of the Zhenghe era (1117), he was admitted to the Imperial Academy and was about to take the highest-level examination. When he was young, he had been recommended in advance and was eligible for an exemption from the regional examination. Some advised him to take the exemption test first. If he failed, he could stay for this year's admission and wait for the next year. Lin did not listen to this advice. His fellow student, Yang Gongquan, asked him why. Lin said, "I am just twenty years old. I was recommended by my hometown and dreamed of answering questions in the grand hall, sitting in the southwest corner. As I was about to leave, a small eunuch came to me to ask for my writing tablet. I felt quite proud of myself and believed that I would surely be selected. I consulted a diviner, who said that I would get an official position at the age of forty-eight. Now it is not the time. I was very unhappy about it. I was eventually dismissed by the Ministry of Rites. Since then, I have been in a difficult situation for nearly thirty years. Now I am forty-seven years old, and I can hope for success. I will not use this as a second chance." That year, he was indeed selected. After the palace examination, he told Yang Gongquan, "On the day of the exam, I sat in the southwest corner, and a small eunuch came to me to ask for my writing tablet. Everything was exactly as I had dreamed thirty years ago. The prediction of the diviner and the dream were perfectly accurate."

#### Cao Yingchen In the sixth year of the Xuanhe era (1124 CE), several ten thousand candidates from various regions took the provincial examination. Six high-ranking officials were in charge of the examination, and there were sixty officials below them to assist. An edict was issued to pass through the outer gate of the examination hall and personally write one's name to prevent impostors from entering. After compiling the list, there were sixty-one candidates. The chief examiner suspected something and gathered all the examiners together. They counted each one individually and examined the candidates at the gate, saying, "Each person must dismount and write their name; how could there be two names?" Upon reviewing the records, they found one more name, which was Cao Yingchen. Checking with the personnel department, they found that there was no such name inside or outside the court. It was then known that this was done by a divine entity. The chief examiner then discussed with the officials, saying, "Cao is the name of a state, and the name Yingchen suggests that it is the work of a divine force. During the examination, one must be cautious." That year, 850 candidates passed the examination, making it the greatest event of the era. Yang Gongquan was among the top candidates, and he heard this from the official in charge of the examination, Wang Tang翁繩. #### The Crane of Zizhou In Zizhou, there was a military officer named He. His younger brother loved hunting with a bow and arrow. One day, he went to the mountains with a crossbow and a sling, and every creature he saw was unable to escape. Once, he sought shade under a large tree and looked up to see a red crane's nest, which was very large, with several chicks chirping. Soon, the mother crane returned, resting on a branch and carrying food to the nest. The younger brother shot the mother crane with his crossbow, hitting her abdomen. She was about to fall but still managed to stretch her neck and regurgitate food to feed her chicks before falling to the ground. Despite his usual lack of conscience, the younger brother felt a pang of pity and immediately discarded his bow and arrows, vowing never to hunt again. These six events were all reported by Yang Gongquan.

#### Suspicious Case in Chengshi In Chengshi County, Xingren Prefecture, there was a son of a famous family named Fu. He traveled annually to Dezhou to trade silk and gauze. During one of his trips, he became acquainted with a courtesan and they had been together for many years. However, the courtesan's mother did not approve of their relationship and forbade it. The courtesan, grieved and resentful, hanged herself. Fu did not know about her death. One day, he met her in Chengshi and she told him, "I was mistreated by my adoptive mother and could not bear it any longer. I sued her and became a free person. I managed to escape and come to meet you. Can you take me in?" Fu was pleased and worried that his wife might be jealous and refuse to accept her. He built a separate room for her outside the house. The following year, he returned to Dezhou to check on her. He learned of her death and was very shocked. However, he was deeply in love and could not resist the temptation of her beauty. He remained in a daze, unable to think clearly. Rumors began to spread, and his wife eventually found out. She feared that her husband might die from a ghost, so she and her brother planned to kill him. They set a specific day to carry out the plan and prepared wine and food to drink at night. They waited outside while Fu sat in the east side of the room, and his wife sat in the west side. After they had settled down, Fu's brother-in-law, wielding a knife, rushed towards the west side. Just as he was about to reach her, he accidentally touched the lamp and extinguished it. In the darkness, he stabbed Fu and stepped out. When the lamp was relit, Fu was found dead with a sword through his armpit. His wife did not see anything. The county officials arrested both of them and imprisoned them, accusing them of killing their husband and brother. They were also questioned about their affair. Despite a year of investigation, the case remained unsolved. Ren Xinru, who had close ties with the Fu family and had personally witnessed the events, reported the case to the prefecture as a suspicious case. The prefecture sent it to the imperial court, but it was during a time of turmoil in the capital, and the case was not properly handled. In the end, no one knew what happened. Ren Xinru said so.

Shao Yu's Water Calamity Shao Yu, a native of Pei County in Xuzhou, lived with his father-in-law, Ren Xinru, in Quzhou. During the Dingmao year of the Shaoxing era, Zhang Jiushan, a high-ranking official, was in charge of the prefecture. On the Dragon Boat Festival, there was a grand dragon boat race, and many people went to watch from the floating bridge in front of Fushishi Temple. Shao Yu had entered the temple with several friends earlier but returned alone. As he walked halfway across the bridge, the iron cable broke, causing the boats to drift and the bridge to split into pieces. Hundreds of people in front of the bridge drowned. Shao Yu fell into the water but felt something supporting his feet, so his head remained above the surface. In a daze, he saw the drowned people appearing and disappearing intermittently, their forms transformed into creatures with crab heads and human bodies, human heads and fish bodies, or resembling dolphins, turtles, and tortoises. Several large deities stood by the bridge pillars, each about thirty feet tall, holding axes. Two more deities descended from the clouds, one with a crab head and another resembling a ghost. One of them spoke from the air: "We have just counted three hundred people. Look at you, Shao Yu. You are not meant to die. We will lift and throw you onto a broken boat." They did so, and Shao Yu barely managed to reach the shore. After returning home, he did not dare to tell anyone about his experience. The following year, he traveled with Ren Gong to Ningbo, passing through Xiang Pavilion in Yuyao. They waited for the tide before continuing eastward and climbed the pavilion to read the inscriptions on the walls. Someone called out from behind: "You did not easily escape the water calamity from last year. It was not due to your accumulated good deeds that you survived." Shao Yu turned around and saw a tall and imposing Taoist monk wearing a white hemp shirt and with very dark skin. Shao Yu asked, "Who are you?"

The respected teacher asked, "Are you not here to help me escape this calamity? How do you know me?" The hermit did not reply but said, "In the year of the pig, you will face a great disaster. You should be cautious in all matters. If we meet again, you can avoid it." Yu recognized him as a strange person and immediately bowed down. As he rose, the hermit was already on flat ground, moving as swiftly as flying. His long beard was light and flowing, reaching down to his waist and thighs. Then he disappeared. Yu often feared he could not escape and was always cautious and self-disciplined. In the year of the pig, he dreamed that several soldiers carried a sedan chair to him and invited him into a mansion, similar to where Zhang Jushan lived in life. They traveled about ten or so li, with the weather overcast and as if it were about to snow. They arrived at a large city with marketplaces and were then carried inside. Yu realized it was not Quzhou and remembered that Zhang Jushan had already passed away. He thought he was going to die and felt very sad and discouraged. They walked to the courtyard below, where a curtain hung in front of the hall. He heard two people talking to each other. The pursuers and the messenger went together to the corridor. A clerk held a register and reported to the master, who scolded angrily, "How can you arbitrarily pursue someone?" One person replied, "Han Jun has already received the order." The clerk came down again, carrying a cup of water, intending to spray it at Yu's face. A bystander stopped him, saying, "It's not right to do so." The clerk couldn't put his hand out and had no choice but to send the pursuers to send Yu back. The sedan chair traveled to a deep shore. The messenger fell and woke up in shock. The rooster had already crowed. The hermit was never seen again, and Yu also had no other problems. Nine years later, Yu, appointed by Governor Ren to serve in Xuanchou, was on a mission. He carried a memorial to congratulate on the new emperor's enthronement and to request an official position. He changed his name to Kan. I personally asked him for the details, and he told me as such.

Li Shuchang and His Servant Li Shuchang, courtesy name Jichang, was from Ningde, Fujian. In the early years of the Zhenghe era, he traveled with five other people from his hometown to take a supplementary examination in the capital. They hired a servant together, named Chen Si. Chen Si was kind and simple, but often slow and inefficient. The other four people frequently scolded him, but Li Shuchang was different; he often gave Chen Si money for alcohol and comforted him. After they arrived in the capital, the other four all passed the spring examinations, but Li alone was rejected. By autumn, he finally entered school, and two years later, Chen left. A few days later, Li visited, arriving at Baokang. Inside the door, he heard someone calling for Li Shiyi, the Secretariat Officer. Looking back, it was Chen Si. Chen invited Li to a restaurant. After eating, Li wanted to leave quickly. Chen asked why. Li said, "In these days of scarcity, I plan to sell a small item." Chen gave Li a piece of silver, saying, "Use it for now, there is no need to look for it elsewhere." A few days later, they met again in the horse market, and Chen invited Li to drink at a restaurant. Chen said, "Considering your destiny, you should not pass the examination. If you study the Dao, you will surely achieve something." Li replied, "I have traveled thousands of miles to study."

You must obtain an official position. Returning home will bring honor to your parents. What is meant by learning the Dao? And you are just a servant. How can you know it? Chen said, "Since we parted last year, I followed a Taoist who provided me with sustenance. The Taoist took me to Mount Kongtong, taught me essential methods, and instructed me to look for suitable people. I told him about your lifelong actions, and he showed interest. Would you like to join me? He then orally taught me the essence of health preservation, all of which was simple and direct yet profound. But Li finally refused to follow, and the Taoist gave him a silver tablet and left, never to be seen again. Since then, Li has also lost interest in worldly affairs. Due to the deep favor of my cousin, the Chancellor Yu, I was granted an official position and retired, not serving in office. Once, I traveled to Zhiti Mountain in the county and visited the Thousand-Buddha Pagoda with a Heavenly Crown. While walking deep in the mountains, I had to relieve myself, and there was no water to wash my hands. Just as I was breaking off grass to wipe my hands, a person stood nearby holding a copper basin full of water to offer. He also held a cloth towel to hand over. I saw that his hands were green, and his face was also green. I couldn't help but look at him and laugh, and the green-faced person also laughed. And then he disappeared. It was probably done by the mountain spirits."

The Chancellor of Fu Prefecture, Mr. Yu, was a high-ranking official during his prosperous times. His family had accumulated a great deal of gold, which he usually stored in caches of a hundred silver ingots each. These caches were covered with earth and bricks were placed on top. After Mr. Yu's death, his son, also named Yu and holding the position of Attendant, intended to purchase land and dug up one of these caches. The bricks and mortar covering it remained undisturbed, but the silver was nowhere to be found. There was a shaman in the prefecture who lived on Jinjiu Ridge and was known for communicating with spirits. The family went to consult her. The shaman said, "Your father's silver was not lost originally. It was moved to the shrines of the land gods, which caused the gods' anger, so it was hidden away. If you can prepare sacrificial animals and wine to apologize and hold rituals for water and land, you should be able to retrieve it.
