However, I must first go to explain this to the gods and offer silver for an incense burner and other offerings. You should come back in three days to inquire whether it is possible." As agreed, the Yu family returned after three days. The shaman said, "The gods have allowed me to retrieve the silver. You can go back and take it, but do not break the agreement." After returning home, they dug up the ground again, and the cache was still completely present. They were greatly surprised and amazed. That day, they held a ritual to honor the gods as the shaman had instructed. Mr. Li Jichang witnessed this event.

A Beggar in Tianjin Wang Guai was a man from Shaowu who traveled to the capital for official duties. Passing over Tianjin Bridge, he encountered a beggar who was being severely beaten. Moved by the scene, Wang asked why this was happening. The beggar replied, "He owes me 500 coins and has not paid me for a long time." Wang felt compassion and used the money in his bag to pay off the debt, then left. On another day, Wang returned to the bridge, and the beggar took a steamed bun from his chest and offered it to him. Wang disliked the beggar's dirty and greasy clothes and the drool dripping from his chin, so he politely declined the offer. On a third occasion, the beggar invited Wang to visit his home. Wang went with him, and they found themselves in a narrow alley with a poor courtyard, the entrance blocked by a tattered mat. The beggar also prepared wine and fruits as a gift, but Wang again refused to eat. After Wang obtained his official position and returned to the south, he was walking along the embankment of the Bian River when a heavy rain and strong wind suddenly arose, making it impossible to move forward. Spotting a small pavilion with a flag on the road, Wang quickly got off his carriage and stopped briefly. The owner of the pavilion came out to greet him, and upon seeing his appearance, Wang recognized him as the beggar from Tianjin. They met with great joy, and the beggar offered him a cup of wine. Reflecting on the past, Wang thought to himself, "How filthy he once seemed."

The king finally refused to drink. The man said, "The weather is bitterly cold. Without wine, there is no way to withstand it." He urged the king, "Please finish this cup for me," and repeatedly held the cup to persuade him, but the king finally did not touch the lips to the cup. The man was extremely disappointed and then packed several types of fruits as a gift, saying, "Please accept this as a parting gift." The king could not bear to repeatedly refuse and reluctantly accepted it. After getting into the carriage and walking a few steps, he wanted to give the gift to his servant and felt it was very heavy. When he opened it to see, the peaches, plums, and pomegranates were all made of gold. Only then did he realize he was a strange person and greatly regretted it. With his hands, he pounded his eyes and cried. The beggar came again and said, "This is because the official lacks the bones of a sage. In twenty years, I will visit you again. Do not hate." In the blink of an eye, the tavern and the person were all gone. Twenty years later, he died from a boil on his back caused by taking mercury pills. All three events were told by Zhu Hanzhang. The king once served as the deputy governor of Kuaiji and personally told the story to Zhu Gong.

Sao Liangchen Sao Liangchen was a man from Jinyun. In the 15th year of the Shaoxing era of the Southern Song Dynasty, he studied with like-minded scholars in a temple on Jinsi Mountain, which was fifteen li away from the prefectural capital. Late in the afternoon, he returned to the capital. On the road, he encountered a woman dressed in blue-green outer clothing and a red inner dress, who was weeping bitterly. When he asked her why she was crying, she replied that she was not tolerated by her stepmother and had been beaten day and night, unable to endure it. She was seeking death but could not bring herself to do it, hence her tears. Sao asked, "If so, can you come with me?" The woman wiped away her tears and agreed. They then followed him home. He told his wife, "I just now passed through the fields and saw a girl who had nowhere to go. I happened to bring her with me. Our household is short of servants. We can treat her as a servant or concubine." His wife was also gentle and submissive, without any jealousy. He had her brought into the house. The Sao family had always been poor, and their room contained only one bed, so the three of them slept together. The next day, they ate from the same dish. Sao's wife said to her, "At night, when I touched your body, it felt unusually cold and sharp. Why is that?" The woman did not answer, but her expression showed shame and anger. She put down her chopsticks and left straightaway. Sao blamed his wife for her words and got up to call her back. His wife stopped eating and waited until noon. She opened the door and looked, but no longer saw her husband. She then informed the neighbors, and they searched together. Three days later, they found his body at the edge of a stream outside the gate, half submerged in water and half on the sand, already dead. The fellow students carried his body back. They still did not understand what strange thing this was. Some said it was the spirit of a fish or a dragon. This story was told by Zhu Xizai and Shun Zici.

In the first month of the 24th year of the Shaoxing era, Shen Taixu Xuzhong, serving as a provincial examination examiner, went to the toilet at night. Upon returning, he saw the clerk lighting a torch and leading the way. They reached the dormitory when suddenly the clerk fell to the ground, and the lamp went out immediately. Shen was greatly alarmed and shouted loudly. Everyone in the examination hall had already gone to sleep and all got up to look. They found that the clerk who was guarding the house had hanged himself between the beams, with his feet six or seven feet above the ground, which could not have been done by human hands. An old imperial guard said, "This must be the work of a ghost." He advised them not to panic and to take several tables and stack them up. They slowly untied the clerk, prying open his mouth and pouring hot water over him. After some time, the clerk could speak and said, "The examiner was reading the exam papers, past midnight. I was napping with the clerk. Suddenly, a figure entered from outside, wearing a blue headband and a plain robe, resembling a Taoist monk. The figure said to me, 'Why are you here?' and used its head to push against something on both sides and left. Then, this clerk followed the examiner out of the door. I was left alone. The figure came back and said, 'There are many good things outside. Don’t just sit here.' It took my hand and we walked together. We saw lights and candles shining brightly outside, with carriages and horses moving around, like a bustling market. I peeked through a crack and saw the door getting narrower and my vision getting darker. Soon, I lost consciousness. The next day, I was mute and seemed like a fool. Shen sent me out, and after a month, I returned to normal. Liu Gongsu personally witnessed this."

East Court Taoist Priest A scholar from Quanzhou named Chen Fangshi was friendly with a Taoist priest at Dongting Temple. Chen once inspected his rural estates and had a dream in which he arrived at a government office. He saw in the courtyard the review of prisoners' complaints. An official loudly announced, "Pursue the Taoist priest from Quanzhou." When Chen looked at him, it was the Taoist priest from Dongting Temple. Another official handed over a scroll of official documents from the side and made him read it. Chen did not understand the words, but he heard one thing mentioned: on a specific date, a certain amount of grain from the temple's storage was taken to brew wine. After reading through the document, the official asked, "Is this correct?" The Taoist priest confessed. The official then wrapped the document around him, from head to toe, and pushed him to the ground. As he turned over, he transformed into a large water buffalo. Chen woke up in a startle and hastily visited the Taoist priest. It was exactly because of that night's death. Chen's courtesy name was Jiyě, and he was a descendant of a prominent family.

The Youngest Son of the Huang Family Huang Ru-neng, a man from Yixian County in Huizhou Prefecture, served as an official in the northern suburb of Lin'an (modern-day Hangzhou) during the 17th year of the Shaoxing era in the Southern Song Dynasty (1147 AD). His youngest son, who was seventeen years old, had never been able to write poetry before. Suddenly, as if possessed by a spirit, he composed ten or so poems, feeling light and airy with the aspirations of a deity. He often talked about the story of the Fairy of Mount Wushan. Among the people Huang Ru-neng was associated with, there was once a young boy who also died in a similar manner. Huang felt worried about this and gently advised his son, saying, "Are you not involved with someone from the common people's household? To the point of such foolish thoughts?" Huang continued, "I am in office here. If there is any incident, it will trouble me." The boy replied, "No." One day while eating with his parents, the boy went directly to the fence, turned his head and looked intently, as if looking at something but not reaching it. His mother chased him back and firmly questioned him about the reason. He answered, "Just thinking about something. Nothing else." From then on, his expression became like that of a madman, and day by day, it became more severe. Huang wanted to have his nephew take the boy back to their hometown, but his nephew was waiting for the examination at the Imperial College and did not leave immediately. So, Huang locked him in a room and ordered several servants to watch over him day and night. The servants became slightly lax over several nights, and the guard fell into a light sleep. By then, the boy was already missing. He was found kneeling under the window, having hanged himself with his belt. This account is provided by Cheng Tai-zhi.

**Yijian Ji, Volume 19, Part 14** **Portrait in a Buddhist Temple** A scholar from Pingjiang named Xu Geng studied at a Buddhist temple. He saw a portrait of a woman hanging above a shrine in the room. He found it attractive and, as soon as he returned to the room, began to dream of a woman coming to join him. From then on, this became a regular occurrence at night. Not long after, he died. Some family members who had heard about the matter went to the temple to trace and find the portrait. They discovered that the image was mounted on a bamboo shaft. When they cut it open, they found it filled with spirit or essence. The exact nature of this discovery is unclear, but it likely refers to some form of record or explanation.

En Zhi Suo Zhi Yuan Wáng Shīdào, courtesy name Shēn Zhī, was from Mianzhou. In the 28th year of the Shaoxing era, he took his wife and children from Shu to the imperial court. The following year, on the last day of January, he had a dream in which a man resembling a high-ranking official from the Three Departments appeared and said, "You have a new appointment." He presented a yellow imperial edict, which stated that Wáng Shīdào was to be appointed to manage a certain institution. Wáng Shīdào did not have time to read it carefully and said, "I have already served as a deputy governor. Now I am about to become a prefectural governor. Why should I take on a minor administrative position?" The man replied, "This position is not easily obtained. It is also a command from the Heavenly Deity. How can you refuse it?" He added, "The welcoming official is almost here. The administrative office is not far. You may go there immediately to take up your duties." A short while later, all the attendants arrived. Wáng Shīdào quickly climbed into a carriage and traveled for just one or two miles, arriving at a large administrative bureau. The doors were wide open, and he saw the five-character title "En Zhi Suo Zhi Yuan" above the entrance. A clerk said, "This institution oversees all living beings in the world. It contains countless species of birds and animals, beyond number, with sparrows being the most numerous." Wáng Shīdào had not finished viewing everything when he woke up. He told his family about the dream and swore never to kill any more living beings. Worried that he might not be able to keep his vow forever, he took care of his afterlife affairs and warned his son to visit all the local people who were in office. Half a month after the dream, he was appointed as the prefect of Dazhou. After about ten days, he went out to pay respects and returned. He fell ill in a sedan chair and died on the boat. It was the fourth day of March.

The Jade Belt Dream Zhang Zishao, a high-ranking official, was exiled to Dayu and contracted an eye disease. Later, he became the governor of Yongjia and suffered a stroke, which left him paralyzed and unable to lift his hands and feet. His eyes then developed inner opacity. He requested a temple position and returned to his old hermitage in Yangguan. On the night of the full moon in the third month of the twenty-ninth year of the Shaoxing era (April 14, 1159 CE), he dreamed of a man in green leading him to a large temple. The gate had eight characters inscribed in gold on a plaque, but only two were remembered, which read "Kāifú." A monk like a guest master at a Zen temple saw Zhang and was very pleased, inviting him to sit. Zhang asked who the head monk was, and the monk replied, "It is Shen Yuányòng, the Attendant in the Inner Court." Zhang said, "I have not seen Mr. Shen for a long time and urgently want to pay respects to him." He ordered for official robes to be fetched, and upon speaking, they immediately appeared. Seeing Shen, Zhang bowed twice, and Shen returned half a bow and greeted him as if they were old friends. Shen said, "Your father is here." He ordered the green-clad man to lead to the eastern small hall of the abbot's quarters. Zhang's parents were sitting opposite each other and long-sighing. Zhang rushed to bow and weep. A bystander scolded and said, "This is not a place to cry." Then, Zhang went to the front of the Dharma Hall and asked, "Why is there no Buddha hall?" The green-clad man said, "This is the ten thousand dharmadhatu as the Buddha hall." Zhang said, "I am sick and disabled, and have lost my sight; I wonder if I will have eyes to see the Buddha and a mouth to recite sutras in the future." The man replied.

The official had never failed to see the Buddha, nor had he ceased reciting sutras. Walking up to the side of the gate, he came upon a small, clear, and cool pool, surrounded by a railing. A young servant said, "This is the water of merit." He poured himself a cup and drank it, feeling its coolness penetrate his skin and bones. In the western annex, there was a room that was extremely clean, with a portrait hanging inside. Upon looking at it, he realized it was a portrait of Zhang. Greatly shocked, he exclaimed, "How did I come to possess this?" The young servant replied, "One day, you will preside over this place, but you must first see the jade belt. Then, you may come." After this, he woke up. Hastily summoning his disciple, Lang Ye, he had him write down the story. All considered the jade belt a good omen, and if his illness healed, he would be promoted to a high position. By the second day of June, both of his illnesses suddenly disappeared. That very day, he went to visit his ancestors' graves, and then visited his close relatives' home for a banquet, continuing this for five days. One day, while reading with his students from Jiang Shaoyu's collection of historical facts, he came across the account of Ding Jinfu taking the jade belt during Emperor Zhenzong's eastern tour. He said angrily, "Ding Wei is truly wicked. Even things belonging to the emperor, he obtained through trickery." Feeling unhappy, he put down the book and went inside. His illness returned, and he could not speak. The next day, he died. People finally understood the meaning of the jade belt dream. Zhang Shou, aged sixty-eight, said, "Dong Siyong told me this. At that time, he was a salt official."

Mao Lie in the Dark Prison In the village of Zhao City, Hejiang County, Luzhou Prefecture, there lived a villager named Mao Lie. He became wealthy through unjust means, plotting and scheming to acquire the good farmland and houses of others until he obtained them. A man from Changzhou named Chen Qi was friends with Mao Lie. Chen Qi had three younger brothers who were all young, and he worried that they would grow up and divide the property. Therefore, he mortgaged all his farmland to Mao Lie, accumulating thousands of strings of cash. After his mother died, he divided the visible farmland into four parts. He then carried the money to Mao's house to redeem the mortgaged land. Mao Lie had the intention to embezzle the money and agreed to take the deed at a later date. Chen Qi said, "To have a single written document as proof is sufficient." Mao Lie replied, "Do you and I treat this as such?" Chen Qi believed him. After several days, he went to Mao's house, but Mao avoided coming out. Chen Qi sued in the county, and the county official accepted a bribe from Mao Lie. The official said, "The official uses documents. How can there be a transaction of thousands of strings of cash without a deed?" He would tell the magistrate. The magistrate decided the case according to the official's suggestion. Chen Qi was beaten for false accusations. He appealed to the prefecture and the transport commissioner, but all appeals were unsuccessful. He then prepared offerings of meat and wine to curse at the altar.

In a dream, I met with a deity. The deity said, "This is not something I can handle. Why not go to pray at the Eastern Peak Palace? They will grant your request." Upon arriving at the palace, in the midst of the curtains and veils, a faint voice seemed to say, "Hurry out, pray quickly." When night came, I went in again to pay respects. I put the petition on the table, and another faint voice was heard saying, "Go out." I then ran away and escaped. I died at home. Three days later, a monk who was a mediator died, and a slave who was on the left also died. Finally, the one who prayed also died. A short while later, he revived and told his family, "I went to meet with Mao and Zhang for a major matter. Guard me well for seven to ten days. Do not bury me." The one who prayed entered the underworld. Those who pursued led Li and the monk to meet. Li still tried to excuse himself by saying he had no payment voucher. The jailer pointed to his heart and said, "What I rely on is only this. What use is a voucher?" They took the mirror of karma to reflect it.

Witnessing Li and his wife sitting together accepting prayers for money, someone said, "Indeed, it is so." They were led into a large courtyard where the guards were very numerous. Above them, a person in imperial robes angrily scolded the officials and had Li restrained with shackles. Fearing this, Li confessed to his guilt. The master then said, "The county magistrate's judgment was unjust. He has already been demoted by several ranks. Those officials who accepted bribes had their homes burned down, and their lifespans were shortened by half." Li then went to jail. On the way, he wept and told Qi, "I will not return. Tell my wife to perform many Buddhist rituals to save me. Your original contract is in a certain box. Also, I obtained thirteen pieces of land through deceit during my lifetime. All the contracts are beneath the pile of money in the room. Please call the families of the thirteen people and repay them to reduce my crime." The king then ordered a monk to be brought forward. The monk said, "I only see the situation when the land was first mortgaged. I do not know about other matters." Both Qi and Li were released. After they came out, passing through the houses of the village, most of them were prisons. The senders pointed and said, "This is where those who killed surrendering soldiers are detained. Those who are disrespectful to their parents, those who practice witchcraft and build illicit shrines, and those who evade Buddhist practices are also detained here. There are many such cases. Since the Zhou and Qin dynasties."

All, regardless of rank, ethnicity, or region, are treated equally. There is no discrimination. The monk then said to Qi, "You have been here for seven days. You should return home quickly." He returned home and woke up. He sent his son to see the county official. Then his house was burned down. He looked at the monk, who had been cremated for three days. He went to Ma Shi to recount the events. Ma Shi's son followed his father's instructions and retrieved the bond and returned it. That night, the monk came and knocked on the Ma family's door, shouting, "I am in this situation because of your father. I was captured and returned, but my body has already been burned. What will you do with me?" Ma Shi replied, "It is already this way. There is only one thing left to do: perform a Buddhist ritual." The monk said, "I should not have died. The records of the underworld do not accept me. And I cannot become a human again. Even if I receive blessings in the afterlife, they are useless. Until the end of this cycle of life, I will be reborn in another form. Now I will only guard your door. I cannot leave." From then on, he came every night. After a while, his voice became distant, and he said, "Because of your good deeds, I will gradually withdraw. But ultimately, there is no way out." Several years later, the Ma family's decline finally ended. According to Du Qixin, at that time, Liu Yishu lived in Luzhou and wrote a biography.

邢氏补颐 晏肃，字安恭，娶河南邢氏，居京师。邢生疽于颐，久之，颐颔连下腭及齿，脱落如截。自料即死，访诸外医，医曰：“此易耳，与我钱百千，当可治。”问其方，曰：“得一生人颐与此等者，合之则可。”宴氏惧，谢去之。儿女婢仆辈相与密货医，使试其术。是夜以帛包一物至，视之，乃妇人颐一具，肉色阔狭长短，勘之不少差。以药缀而封之，但令灌粥饮，半月发封，疮已愈。后避乱寓会稽，唐信道与之姻家，尝往拜之。邢氏口角间有赤缕如线，隐隐连颐，凡二十余年乃亡。

**Mistakenly Entering the Underworld** Li Chengji Zhaoshi, in his youth, fell ill with a severe fever. For several days, he did not sweat, and he was restless and uncomfortable. He thought to himself, if he could escape the bed, he might enter a cool place. He imagined soaring onto the top of the canopy. Then he thought this was not enough; if he could go outside, it would be even more refreshing. So, he imagined himself leaping out and wandering aimlessly. He felt unusually at ease as he strolled through the open fields. Soon, he arrived at a large city, with bustling marketplaces, towns, and houses, like a human state or prefecture. Li strolled leisurely in the street when he encountered an old acquaintance, an old woman who sold silk. She had been dead for a long time. When she saw Li, she was surprised and asked, "Why are you here? This is the underworld!" Li was afraid and sought help. The old woman said, "I can do nothing. Fortunately, I often sell silk and come and go at the Right Judge's house. Try to ask him." She then stopped Li outside and said, "Do not move randomly. Take one step beyond this, and you will truly die." The old woman entered and returned after some time, saying, "The matter is settled. But we must still discuss it with the Left Judge." Soon, the sound of a horse being saddled was heard. When they emerged, it was a young man in green clothes. The old woman called Li and had him follow behind him. They arrived at the house of the Left Judge. A man in red clothes came out to greet them.

A wandering soul from the mortal world had just arrived here. It was necessary to send someone to return it. The red-robed official said, "Who made it come here on its own?" It had already arrived, and it was not summoned from here. Why must we send someone? Li leaned in to listen, becoming increasingly fearful. The green-robed official said, "Let us check the records. Perhaps it has an official position." He repeated this several times. The red-robed official finally held his ground, saying it was not possible. Reluctantly, he ordered an official to fetch the records. The official read and said, "Li Zhaoqi's position reaches that of a Palace Attendant." The green-robed official exclaimed, "How so? How so?" It should have such a high official position. Could we arbitrarily keep it? The red-robed official felt somewhat ashamed. Then they jointly made a talisman and sealed it. After completing the seal, they gave it to a little ghost and instructed it to return Li. Li heavily thanked the old woman and started to leave. If asked, he would show the talisman. The little ghost had sores all over its head, with pus and blood, and a foul smell. It sang and shouted continuously. Every several dozen steps, it would claim foot pain and sit down. Li pitifully prayed to it, and it continued forward. They arrived at an open field. The little ghost said, "I should only arrive here." It returned the talisman to Li, throwing it on the ground. Li bent down to pick it up and fell, waking up. It turned out that he had been drowsily sleeping for a day. Since then, the Li family set up a place for the old woman and worshiped her. Indeed, Li ended up as a Palace Attendant.

Vajra with Filthy Tracks People from the regions of Zhangzhou and Quanzhou often practiced the method of Vajra with Filthy Tracks, which was used to treat illnesses and ward off calamities. When the deity descended, it spoke through a child. In the twenty-second year of the Shaoxing era, Monk Ruochong resided at the Guangfu Temple on the western hills of Quanzhou. One night, a monk came to see him, and Ruochong was surprised by the unexpected visit. The monk explained, "I am very poor and only had a few taels of silver in my robe and bowl, but someone stole it. I just then invited a Daoist to perform a ritual. The deity said it needed the elder monk to speak, so please come." Ruochong went with the monk to his room, where a village child stood on a chair with a sword. Seeing Ruochong, the child greeted him and said, "Master, please sit. It is too late at night to trouble you." Ruochong replied, "I did not know the divine being had descended. I forgot to burn incense. May I ask why the deity wishes to see me?" The child responded, "I am a divine being from heaven. There are lost items in the temple, and we need the owner's testimony. This is very easy to know, but we are concerned about causing disputes, which would go against my original intention. If you do not report it to the authorities, I will help find it." Ruochong repeatedly thanked the child and said, "I will strictly follow the precepts." The child said, "The ritual has begun. I will immediately draw the sword and leave." The child jumped and walked, then suddenly threw himself into a well. After some time, he jumped out and moved to the pile of cow dung outside the temple, jumping around everywhere. He struck the ground three times with the sword, and suddenly fell to the ground. After some time, the child woke up but did not know what had happened. Ruochong dug into the pile of cow dung and found a brick standing out unevenly. When he lifted the brick, silver was underneath. It was where the thief had hidden it.

Flying Night Demon Chancellor Zhao Qingxian's nephew, Guo Da, traveled to a small town outside Qing She during the height of summer. Riding under the moonlight, his horse suddenly became startled and refused to move forward. Turning his head to the left, he saw an object about a zhang (about 3 meters) tall in the melon field. The creature had a shape like a bat, a head like a donkey, and wings as broad as a mat. One claw rested on the ground, and the other held a melon, which it was eating. Its eyes shone brightly, and Guo Da lost his courage. He turned his horse and galloped back. Within several dozen steps, he looked back and saw that the creature had not yet left. Another day, he entered a shrine and saw a painted flying night demon, confirming that it was indeed the same creature he had encountered.

The Moonlight on the Last Day of the Month Zhao Qingxian was granted a residence in the government office lane of the capital. His eldest daughter married into the Shi family. One summer night, unable to sleep, she opened the door to let in the cool air. She saw the moonlight filling the courtyard as bright as day and exclaimed, "What beautiful moonlight!" Suddenly, the courtyard began to darken, and the moon's outline gradually diminished. Soon, the light disappeared entirely. Looking up, she saw the stars shining brilliantly. It was then that she realized it was the last day of the lunar month. She still did not know what kind of light it had been. All four events were narrated by Wang Ju, also known as Jia Sou. Seizing the Opportunity to Take the Imperial Examinations Shen Chi-yao, from Anji in Huzhou, was invited by his sister's husband, Fan Yanhui, who supervised the office of petitions, to take the imperial examinations at the Imperial College. Upon arriving, they learned that only same-clan relatives were allowed to take the exam, and outsiders were not permitted to participate. Some of Fan's relatives wanted to help Shen by having him pretend to be a temporary resident of Lin'an. This would require summoning guarantors, and the cost would be twenty-five thousand units of currency. Shen refused. The relatives tried to persuade him to stay, and they collectively decided to pay the fees to accomplish the matter. An agreement was reached, but Shen was very unhappy. Meanwhile, the examination in Huzhou was scheduled for the 15th day of the 8th lunar month, and there were only two days left. Even if he wanted to return, it would be too late. That evening, he suddenly saw dozens of tall figures in his room, all resembling gods or deities. They scolded him, saying, "This is not your place. You should leave quickly. Otherwise, we will kill you." Shen became frightened and fell ill. He quickly sent a servant to buy a boat and return home.

Walking to the riverbank, I saw a small boat. I called out to the boatman to consult him. He said, "I am a native of Anji, here to sell rice. The officials need the boat, so I dare not return. If I get an official, I will not take the rental fee. But who is the person you wish to carry?" I replied, "Mr. Shen, the scholar." He then asked about his residence, and I said, "He is my neighbor. Though he is sick, he cannot be left behind." The boatman immediately led the people on the boat to lift him aboard. Late in the afternoon, we left the house. By the next morning, his illness had completely disappeared. On the sixteenth day, early in the morning, we arrived at the foot of the walls of Wuxing. I saw people in white robes coming and going in great numbers. I asked them, and they said, "Yesterday, we entered the examination hall, but the exam papers were heavily soaked by heavy rain. They had to be changed, and the supplementary exam was moved to the seventeenth day." Mr. Shen was thus able to take the supplementary exam. Those close to him came to congratulate me, saying, "The matter of the supplementary exam was especially arranged for you." After the exam, the results would be posted. That night, I dreamed of a loud thunderclap and woke up. Going out into the courtyard, I looked up and saw the moon and stars shining brightly. I felt deeply confused.

The Intention to Decide by Divination. At dawn, a diviner who practices the art of the I Ching passed by the door. The divination yielded the Zhen hexagram, which depicted a woman lying sick in bed. One person hurried forward, and beside the depiction, the word "run" was written. The phrase contained words of transformation like a dragon. The diviner said, "Your decision regarding official documents is very auspicious. However, there will be illness among women in your household, but it will not cause harm." The diviner then left. The official reported that a person had arrived, whose name was Bensheng, pronounced as Ben Shen, who was selected as a top scholar. Upon returning home, the wife indeed lay sick. The following year, he went to the provincial examination, where Fan was appointed as the examiner. To avoid any conflict of interest, he entered a separate courtyard. On the first day, he was tested on the classics. As he was leaving, a patrol officer from the department stood guard and would not let him go. At that time, the prohibition against carrying books or cheating was very strict. Shen was quite surprised by the officer's scrutiny and asked, "What are you doing?" The officer replied, "I saw some very good candles in your chest. Are they not from Huzhou?" If they were not needed, the officer requested them. Shen gave them all to the officer. The next day, he was tested on poetry and prose.

The man returned and said, "I just went to the transcription office and saw the chief examiner copying a test paper—perhaps five or six of them. They were extremely similar to what you wrote. You will surely pass with distinction. Do not finish it tonight. I have already set up a lamp outside." Shen thought he wanted a candle, and so gave him one. He received it and returned another, saying, "Please keep this to illuminate yourself." "Once every three years," he continued, "you cannot afford to be careless in the details." In the evening, he left through the middle gate, extended his hand to invite Shen to sit, set up a small table, and looked around—no one was there. Shen wanted to submit his scroll and leave, but was persuaded to read it again. He read until he reached the family's collected *Classic of Filial Piety* and poetry, then realized he had mistakenly written two characters in the wrong place. He quickly corrected them. The next day, when he went to visit the man, he was never seen again. Only then did Shen realize that the divine being had been lenient because of his mistake, showing him favor. That year, he was selected for advancement. It was indeed strange—what he saw while traveling, the neighbor who took a boat, the rain that stained his test paper, the changed omen, and the words of the patrol guards—all were silently helping him. How strange!

**Elaodao** Wen, the daughter of a family involved in the leather trade, married a man from the Chen family in Xiuzhou. After a while, they divorced. She returned home and began to pursue spiritual cultivation. She lived with another woman named Elaodao, who was also a Daoist. In the twenty-fourth year of the Shaoxing era, Wen was appointed as the governor of Zhangzhou. On her way to Quanzhou, she stayed at the residence of a high-ranking official. Elaodao and her two servants were in the west room. In the middle of the night, there was a sudden cry of "Catch the thief!" Wen, holding a sword, went to investigate. She saw one of Elaodao's servants with her hand raised toward the ceiling, with no visible means of restraint, but it was firmly stuck in place. Beside her was a boy dressed in blue, about fourteen or fifteen years old, wearing a medical bag at his waist. Wen shouted at him, "Who are you? How dare you come here at midnight?" The boy replied, "I am from the capital. Elaodao owes me a million in medicine money. I have come to collect it. What does this concern you?" Wen continued to argue with him, but he suddenly disappeared. Wen then called in a Daoist priest from the Tianqing Temple named Zheng Faxun to resolve the situation. When Zheng arrived, the servant's hand was released, but he had no way to apply his magical arts. At the time, Elaodao was still in her thirties, born in Jiangnan. The claim that she owed a million in medicine money from the capital was interpreted by some as a karmic debt from past lives.

The Wife of Chen Wangyou's Son Chen Wangyou, a native of Chaozhou, served as the governor of Meizhou. His son's wife died there and was buried on the northern hill of the prefecture. Her spirit returned every night to sleep with her husband. Fearing this, her husband slept on his mother's bed. The wife came to him again and could not be repelled. Even when family members saw her, there was no place to hide. She had a son who was several years old, handsome and lovely. She often wanted to take him away, and the whole family competed to take the child from her. The wife went in and out as if nothing was wrong. The Chen family was very frightened and called upon a Taoist priest to perform rituals and pray to the gods, but they could not send her away. It was the third month of the year Gengwu during the Shaoxing era. Three months later, Chen Wangyou died in the prefecture. The Ghost of Hao Guangsi Hao Guangsi served as a lower-ranking official in the Guangzhou government. A ghost troubled his family, entering every room, including bedrooms and kitchens. Once, a fire broke out in his clothes chest. Hao went to save the burning clothes, and his hands were severely burned. An official document he held was reduced to just the text and seal, the rest being burned. His court robes and fur coats were all worn through and could not be worn. One day, he opened the chest to retrieve the seal, but the lock was intact, yet the seal was missing. At first, he ordered a shaman to investigate and treat the problem, but it did not work. Then, he cleaned a room and strictly performed rituals with incense and fire. For twenty or so days, the seal was lost. Officials in Guangzhou who needed to report their salaries and grain supplies were all troubled by the missing seal. Suddenly, they heard a sound like a large stone falling in the room where the rituals were being performed. It struck the desk three times and stopped. When they looked, the seal was there. Initially, Hao had covered the desk with white paper because it was not good. He placed three seals on the desk and left. After that, seven days later, Hao Guangsi died. His family moved out, and the ghost followed them but did not leave. It ceased only when Hao returned north. It was the year 1150 AD, during the Shaoxing era. All three events were reported by Mr. Xie Zhi.

Wáng Quán, the Commander of the Capital of Jiankang, had a fondness for archery when he was not yet prominent. His crossbow bolts never missed their mark. In the early years of the Shaoxing era, he followed General Hán Xián'ān Shìzhōng to Jianzhou to campaign against Fàn Rúwéi. Once, he carried his crossbow into the mountains and saw a magpie's nest in a tree. He immediately shot at it, unsure if he had hit the target. He heard a voice behind him say, "If your eye were to be hit by an arrow, what would happen?" He turned back but saw no one. Wáng Quán realized the strangeness of the voice and quickly climbed the tree to look. He found that one magpie had been hit in the eye, twisted in the nest, and died immediately. Wáng Quán was shocked and regretful, so he drew his sword and broke his crossbow. Soon after, he fought the rebels, and flying arrows gathered around his nose and eye, not even an inch away. He suffered from wounds from metal weapons and took a long time to recover. This account was told by Hán Wángzǐ Yànzhí, his son Wēn.

The *Yiji Jia Zhi* Volume Twenty-Twelfth Story Wooden Master Wang Zhidao Shu Zhan was from She County, Huizhou. In the eighteenth year of the Shaoxing era, he served as a low-ranking official overseeing financial matters in Hubei Province. Once, he attended a banquet hosted by General Tian Shizhong. He arrived last, and the governor of Ezhou, who was already there, was playing chess with Tian. A Daoist priest named Wooden Master was also sitting nearby. Upon seeing Wang, Wooden Master bowed and said, "It has been a long time since we last met. Are you well?" Wang was startled and replied, "We have never met before. How can you say it has been a long time?" Wooden Master said, "You are now a person of high status. Have you forgotten? Do you not remember the conversation we had at the roadhouse in Xuanchou about the story of Niu Qizhang?" Wang was startled and stood up to thank Wooden Master. The Daoist priest then left. Wang told his guests, "In the fifth year of the Chongning era, I passed the imperial examinations for the first time and became a professor in Xuanchou. In the winter, I traveled alone on official business and sought lodging at a small inn in a village. There was only one room, and a scholar had already taken it. It was very late, and a heavy rain prevented me from moving on. I had no choice but to push open the door and enter. I said, 'It is late, and I have come here. May I share this room with you?' The scholar was sitting by the fire and replied, 'Yes.' After a long while, he suddenly said, 'Have you read the *Tangshu*?' I was angry and said, 'I may have little learning, but I am not so vulgar.' He laughed again."

Remember the biography of Niu Sengru? I did not answer. The scholar said, "My words mean nothing else. You are the reincarnation of Niu Sengru. In your past life, you were the military governor of Wuchang. Your karmic connection is not yet completed. In this life, you should return. In the future, your official position and salary will mostly be in that place." I was surprised by his words and suspected he was a fortune-teller. I asked him his surname and given name. He slowly replied, "Do you know of someone named Yong Xiaowen? I am." Since the beginning of the Chongning era, he had been rejected and dismissed from the imperial court, wandering aimlessly in the mountains and forests. He happened to meet me. When I questioned him, he would not speak. We faced each other all night, discussing literature alone. He left at dawn and was never seen again. Just now, I saw the appearance of a Taoist, who turned out to be Mr. Yong. His bearing was not much different from forty years ago. He is truly one who has attained enlightenment. The guests were all amazed. Wang served as the transportation commissioner of Hubei again, and also governed Ezhou, serving as the general commissioner. For many years, he was in Wuchang. Mu Sheng, whose name was Guangmo, traveled back and forth between the Han and Mi rivers. When he met people, he only talked about literature and writing. He did not mention other matters. None knew him as a strange person. Shen Daoyuan Jun also knew him and said, "During the Zhenghe era, he entered as a Taoist to preach. Emperor Huizong said he obtained half of Lin Lingsu's abilities. Therefore, he used 'Mu' as his surname." Wang said.

Lingzhi Temple In the twelfth year of the Shaoxing era, after the imperial examination, Tang Xindao stayed at Lingzhi Temple by West Lake. It was already May. Two servants were seeking coolness by the lake when they called out urgently. Tang went to see what was happening. The two servants were pulling a monk, saying that the monk was running to jump into the water, with one foot already in the water. They called him but he would not turn back, and they pulled his clothes with all their might but could not stop him. They brought him back to the room. People at the temple said that during a recent invasion of Lin'an, two monks had died in the lake, and this was one of their ghosts. When asked what he had seen, the monk said, "Two monks came to tell me that a grand bath was being set up on Solitary Hill and invited me to go together. One foot was already in the water, but someone was pulling me from behind, so I could not go. I was very angry about it." After sitting for a while, he opened his chest and put on new clothes, and changed his shoes and socks, as if he was being guided. He then hastened directly to the lakeside. Several monks chased to save him. After returning, he scolded the rescuers, saying, "I just had a very pleasant experience, and you all seem to dislike me. You had to force me to come back. I will go away one last time." The abbot sent three people to guard him in the room and locked the door from the outside. The room where Tang stayed was next to the monk's, separated only by a rush mat.

Heard that he was possessed by a ghost. He composed a poem, which went something like this. Tang only remembered one line: "Every day, I move my bed to the wind below." It seems he had stolen a phrase from Su Shi. Tang mocked him, saying, "You were born as a monk, regarding your body as mere earth and wood. Though unfortunate in death, you should transcend and leave behind. Instead, you willingly linger as a wandering spirit or lingering soul. Truly shameful!" The monk replied, "I am not a harmful spirit. I wish to save this monk, so I stay with him. What concern is it of yours?" Tang retorted, "Can one look at someone about to die and not help? Moreover, since you cannot save yourself, you unjustly cause the death of one person. What benefit does this bring you? It only adds one more ghost to the lake!" They continued their exchange until midnight, when the ghost grew angrier and shouted, "Even you are not one who understands the cycle of birth and death!" Tang laughed and said, "I will die when I am supposed to, and I will not be trapped in the underworld. I will not follow your example of harming living people unjustly." The ghost seemed to understand what Tang said and no longer spoke. After a while, the monk fell asleep. In the morning, when asked, he said he was from Kuaiji. The temple master ordered him to be sent back home. Later, Tang saw him at the Jinding Temple on West Lake, where he only remembered the initial incident of drowning and nothing else.

Wang Bi Recommended as the Top Candidate** Wang Bingwen, from Mingzhou, was recommended as the top candidate in the examination in the first year of the Jingkang era. He traveled to Chuzhou in Huainan to take the examination and stayed at Longxing Temple. Inside the main gate of the temple, someone had written: "The light of the east wall shines down upon the constellations of Dou and Niu." It was predicted that this year Wang Bi would be the top candidate. Wang asked the monks and the gatekeeper, but none knew who had written it. That year, Wang indeed became the top candidate. All three events were said by Tang Xindao. **Lord of Mount Tai** Sun Dian, also known as Yu Zhi, was from Zhengzhou and a descendant of Wenjing Gong Gu. In the fourth year of the Jianyan era, he served as the magistrate of Jinjiang County in Quanzhou. He maintained his integrity and uprightness in office. In the seventh month of that year, the rebel general Yang Qing invaded the borders of the prefecture from Jiangxi. Sun Dian went out to defend against the enemy. Upon his return, he developed a boil on his back. The chief clerk entered his inner room to examine him, and several clerks were standing by. Sun Dian looked out the window and asked, "Who brought a letter?" No one saw it. After a while, Sun raised his hands on both sides, mumbling in his mouth, as if he were reading a letter quickly. The chief clerk asked what the letter was about. Sun replied, "A summons calling me to be the Lord of Mount Tai." He looked at the clerks and asked, "Are there two people named Shi Ni and Xu Kai here?" The clerks replied, "There is a Professor Shi living in another village, but there is no Xu Kai. There is only Tu Kai, the top scholar." Sun said, "Why use a scholar for this?" The clerks found his words strange and dared not ask further. Three days later, Sun died. Shi Ni, whose courtesy name was Dechu, was waiting for his turn in his hometown in the third year of the Shaoxing era.

Because the official term had not yet arrived, he traveled to Lin'an, wishing to make some changes. He contracted an illness in the house of Bojian and died in the seventh month. Tu Kai, also known as Zhengfu, was at the time the prefectural school teacher. His fellow students often teased him, saying, "You will go to Taishan. In the future, friends will visit Dai, relying on you for guidance." Upon hearing that Ni had died, Tu Kai was not very happy. He went to the abbot of Tianning Temple, who intended to oversee the in and out of the granaries. The person was first recorded and placed in prison, where after several days of torture, he died. His family begged for a proper burial, but it was not allowed. They stripped his body to examine it. Shao was afraid and, whenever asked, confessed. Like this, for ten months or so, dozens were linked together, and those who died were nearly ten. Zhou Yanjue, the Censor of Criminal Affairs from Jinyun, knew of his innocence and urgently went personally to the prison in Jia Prefecture to review the case. Shao was then released, and upon reviewing, it was found that he had no crime. But it was discovered that he had given wine to travelers and used official paper beyond the allowed amount. Just as the case was being prepared, Yang died immediately, and several prison officials died in succession.

The decree was issued the following year. Shao was demoted by three ranks and returned to the western mountains of Jianwei. In the autumn, Shi Junzheng, a scholar from Meishan, was resting in his quarters. He was invited out by many people. There were over a hundred attendants, all wearing embroidered robes and flowered hats. The horse handlers rode magnificent steeds, extremely swift and powerful. They mounted the horses and galloped away, so fast that one could hardly keep their eyes open. They arrived at a grand mansion, where the three red doors were wide open. The horses entered through the central gate. Shi wanted to go to the guest quarters, but the horse handlers would not allow him to proceed directly to the main hall. Several officials in red and green robes sat facing east, all bowing to Shi. They said, "I am just a commoner. How can I sit opposite such esteemed guests?" One of them replied, "Today's affairs are under your governance. Why should you refuse?" Another official explained, "The emperor has summoned you to handle the case of Deng Anmin. It has not happened yet. You will be summoned after you pass the imperial examinations." Shi reluctantly took his seat, yawned, and woke up. He did not tell his family about the experience but wrote it down secretly. The following year, Shi took the imperial examinations in the capital, passing through Jingnan, where Wu Jun was the military governor and fell ill.

A man saw spirits moving before him. He avoided the main hall and dared not stay there. He died shortly after. The official returned home. Upon arriving at Kuixia, he fell slightly ill and told his traveling companion, "I will die. You should inform my family. Have them fetch the document written last autumn to see it. It will be clear." That night, he indeed died. Two years later, the so-called important guest suddenly died in the inn in Chengdu. In the eleventh month of the following year, Shao saw Anmin with his head uncovered, holding a document and reporting, "Anmin's wrong has been righted. The case in the underworld is ready. It is necessary for you to come and testify. You are not guilty." Anmin pointed to the end of the document and asked Shao to write his name. Afterwards, Anmin again said, "There is a name but no signature. It cannot be used." Shao then wrote it in a floral script. He then left. Shao knew he could not escape. He prepared a feast to entertain close relatives and guests with joyous drinking. After six days, during the meal, he felt a slight pain in the abdomen. He refused medical treatment and dressed in formal attire, waiting for the end. He died in the middle of the night. The person from Chengdu named Zhou Shi with the courtesy name Xingke said, "When Shao was the governor of Mei Prefecture, Xingke was the magistrate of Qingshen County."

The Missing Text of Deng Anmin The internal text is missing. The Virtuous Woman of Yanguan In the leap June of the 29th year of the Shaoxing era (1159 AD), a thunderstorm struck Yanguan County. Several days before the storm, Zhang, the wife of Gu Deqian, who oversaw a salt field, dreamed of a divine being who blamed her for her past lives, saying, "You will die under the thunderbolt tomorrow." Upon waking, she was filled with great fear, crying and sobbing. Her mother-in-law asked her about it, but she did not tell the truth. Her mother-in-law angrily said, "Is it because I lent you something and it hasn't been repaid? Why such extreme behavior?" Zhang finally revealed the dream. Her mother-in-law did not believe her at all. The next day, a violent storm arose, and the sky became dark. Zhang knew she would die. She changed her clothes and stood under a mulberry tree outside the house, silently thinking that death by thunder was unavoidable. She was concerned for her old mother-in-law, wondering what could be done about her fear. Soon, lightning and thunder obscured the darkness. A voice called out to Zhang from the air, saying, "You truly deserve to die. But because you just thought of filial piety, Heaven has pardoned you. You should return and do good deeds." The following text is missing.

Cao Enters the Underworld Jin Shiyi. A native of Jizhou. His father, Shouzhong, held the position of Shangshu Lang. In the 29th year of the Shaoxing era, Jin served as the county magistrate of Yuhang. His wife, Cao, fell ill and died in June. After she had been buried for a night, one of her feet suddenly bent and straightened. Jin was startled and looked at her, noticing that her face was wet with tears. He wailed in grief, saying, "Could it be that the funeral arrangements are not yet complete?" What else did she want to say? As he touched her body, it gradually warmed. She then sighed and said, "I need money." Jin ordered several bundles of paper money to be burned. She said, "Not yet." He burned more as before. After a long while, she slowly revived. He helped her sit up, and she wept profusely, saying, "My grandmother called me. I remember when I was ill and unconscious, two women came and said that Lady Cao was calling. We left together, going very quickly in a sedan chair. We arrived at an official residence. Inside the gate, there were four departments. I only remember one, called the Southern Military Department. I was wandering aimlessly, and then I met an old soldier who had served my uncle when he was alive. He blocked my way and bowed, asking, 'How did you get here?' I replied that it was my grandmother's command. He then led me in. In the side halls, there were many prisoners, their moans and groans filling the air. I ascended the eastern steps. My uncle wore a golden crown and a purple robe, like a king today, and was engaged in important discussions with officials dressed in purple and white."

Let us prepare wine. I heard my uncle say that the officials had changed, and there were unresolved matters. Soon after, he sent two guests back. I rushed out from a secluded place to bow. My uncle was startled and asked, "Who called you here?" I replied that it was my aunt. My uncle and I went in together. The aunt was seated on the main hall, wearing a ceremonial headdress and shawl, resembling a goddess in a shrine. Many maidservants stood in a circle around her, each holding a peacock fan. My uncle scolded, "Your family has many children; how could you have invited her?" The aunt said, "It's because we lack money." I bowed again and asked, "What do you need the money for?" The aunt said, "My eldest daughter killed a female servant out of jealousy and has been imprisoned in a dark dungeon for a long time. The prison officials demanded bribes, and we had no way to obtain them. Unable to avoid it, I am asking you for it." She also said, "For you, perform the ritual of the turning wheel relic. It has been used up. Now, recite the Liang Wu's Confession to save my daughter." After a short time, my uncle urged me to return, and I was sent back. He instructed the bearers of the sedan chair to eat, and they said they had already eaten. Then he sent me out, warning me, "Do not reveal this, for it may not be good for you." They sent me to the carriage, accompanied by over ten people, all wearing yellow clothes and golden armor. They moved like the wind. Once I arrived home, the yellow-clad ones demanded gold. After burning two piles of money, they left. From then on, my illness improved. However, after only ten days, I died again. People said that revealing the secret was unavoidable.

**The Case of the Envious Dragon King** De Guo Sanyi's wife, Lady Lu, was from Haisi, Xiu Prefecture. She was known for her dignified and calm demeanor, with strong principles and character. On the fifteenth day of the sixth month of the twenty-eighth year of the Shaoxing era (1158), she called her son Zhao and warned him, saying, "I will die in a few days. Do not prepare my body for burial immediately." She repeated this warning several times. Zhao was deeply concerned but did not fully believe her. When the time came, she passed away without any illness, her heart still slightly warm, and she was barely breathing. Ten days later, she revived and said, "A certain dragon king in Suzhou had a favored concubine. The dragon king's wife became jealous and beat the concubine to death. The case was brought to the heavenly court, where it remained unresolved for many years. God commanded me to investigate the matter. With a single question, I uncovered the truth. The report has been submitted, and I will be executed within a few days. At that time, there will be a violent storm. On the fifth day of the seventh month, a fierce wind drove the tide, causing floods that devastated hundreds of miles, destroying fields and houses. This story was told by the Three Important Matters, Sin Siyong.

Chaste Widow and Loyal Husband In the fifth month of the Jianyan era (1130), rebel soldiers led by Yang Qin attacked Nanjian Prefecture. They passed through a small village and seized a common woman, attempting to defile her. The woman, resolute in her virtue, swore to die rather than be defiled and thus met her death. Her body was left by the roadside. After the rebels retreated, people gathered to bury her. The spot where her body lay remained marked, and this mark did not fade. It dried in the rain and remained wet in sunshine. Those who passed by all marveled at it. Some tried to erase the mark, but it immediately reappeared. Covering it with other soil made the mark even clearer. To this day, the mark still exists. There was also Fan Wang, a military officer from Shunchang County. During the time of Fan Ruwei's rebellion, bandits like Yu Sheng also secretly rebelled. A local military officer named Chen Wang, who was prone to causing trouble, wanted to join the rebellion. Fan Wang shouted to the crowd, saying, "Our parents, wives, and children all depend on the state for survival. Now that we are unable to fight the rebels, if we help them instead, how could we face our conscience and the heavens?" The rebels, angered by his sharp words, quickly killed him. His son, Fosheng, aged twenty, was known for his bravery. The rebels pretended to summon him under his father's name, and upon arrival, they were both killed. His wife, Ma, upon hearing that both her husband and son had died, wept on the road. The rebels tried to force her to submit, but she refused. She was crucified and dismembered. Several months later, when the rebels were defeated, a brick was found where Fan Wang died.

A mysterious corpse was found, its traces subtly lingering. Not much was hidden. The townspeople together lifted the bricks, gathered, and built a shrine to it. Later, they also depicted it on the city god's temple. In the sixth year of Shaoxing (1136), Wu Kui, a person from Jian'an, who served as the deputy governor of the state affairs, reported the matter. The imperial decree bestowed the title of Chengxin Lang on the deceased and allowed the establishment of a temple. Su Hao, the magistrate of Shunchang, oversaw the construction. In a dream, Wang appeared, wearing official attire and paying respects, expressing gratitude for being entrusted with supervision. He said, "When I was first attacked, the villains gouged out my left eye." He led Su to see it. There was also another Wang's corpse on the ground, wearing a short white cloth shirt. He pointed again to the southeast corner of the temple and said, "The remains are still there. I have already conveyed the meaning to the county magistrate. Please remember it." The next day, Su entered the temple and asked about Wang's state at death. All said yes, but no one knew about the gouging out of the eye. The southeast corner was the site of the brick shrine. Thus, five bricks were found and placed in the temple. The county magistrate Huang Liang heard about it and told his wife Cai. Cai was surprised and said, "Last night I also dreamed of a purple-robed person paying respects to you in the court. You bowed to him, ascended the hall and the steps, expressed humility, and left. His name was Fan Wang. Isn't this what the magistrate meant by conveying the meaning?" Wang, a soldier, died in loyalty, and the woman died in chastity. They died but did not decay. He does not believe it, however.

The Giant Snake of Kuishan Mountain Wang Lüdao, the Left Chancellor, was buried in Kuishan Mountain in Quanzhou, about forty miles from the city. The mountain was known for its abundance of snakes. The tomb guardian, Zhang Yuan, raised over ten sheep, which were often swallowed by the snakes. One day, Zhang Yuan took a sickle and went out to track and catch the snakes. He saw a large snake catching one of his sheep, coiling around it several times. The snake first bit the sheep's skin and sucked blood, then sprayed poison inside. The sheep gradually shrank and became soft as if it had no bones, and the snake finally swallowed it. Zhang Yuan stood by, waiting for an opportunity, and then drew his blade and rushed forward. The snake raised its head, about five feet high, waved its tongue, and became angry, showing a fighting stance. Zhang Yuan threw his blade, but it fell short. He ran back and called his son, who took another knife and went to the scene. The snake was still in the same place. Zhang Yuan faced it and stabbed it, cutting off its head and killing it. The snake's tail had two branches, sharp like hooks. They weighed its meat, which came to sixty catties. The back skin was as wide as fifteen inches. The tomb guardian monk said, "This is just the smaller one. There is another one that lives in a cave in the mountain, with a body as thick as a jar. Every time it comes out, large trees all shake."

The Strange Snake of Rongzhou Ma Kuozi, also known as Zi-Chong, was exiled to Rongzhou. He resided at Tianning Temple and built a toilet among the bamboo. Once, he held a spear to strike the toilet and heard as if someone was scolding him. Looking around, there was no one. Again, he heard a scolding sound. There was a snake at the corner of the house, with its mouth open and tongue out, and its head was as big as a winnowing basket. Ma struck it with his spear, and the blade entered the beam. He quickly went out to call the servant to see together. The snake was already dead, but they could not see its body. Looking carefully to search, it was only as thin as a fine rope, wrapped around the rafters and beams several times. They took it to show the locals, but even the white-haired elders could not identify what kind of snake it was. The One-Legged Woman In the seventeenth year of the Shaoxing era (1147), there was a woman in Quanzhou selling medicine in the market. Two little girls followed her. For several days, curious people secretly followed her to see where she stayed. She entered the temple hall of Fengchong Temple. The hall was empty with no one inside. Only the three women were together. People heard the sound of pounding medicine every night. In the morning, she would go out again. At first, no one had ever seen where she slept or ate. One day later, the temple monk secretly observed her. They all had one leg. He exclaimed in astonishment. The woman seemed to have heard him. The next day, she was not seen again. The story was told by Wang Jiashuo, who heard it from three sources.

*Yijian Yi Zhi* *Preface to Yijian Yi Zhi* The initial compilation of *Yijian* was completed. Scholars or officials may have spread it. It has been engraved on boards in Fujian, Sichuan, Wuchang, and Lin'an. Thus, every household has the book. People think I am curious and appreciate the unusual. Whenever I get a story, sometimes they send messages from a thousand miles away. Thus, within five years, I obtained another collection of stories, the number of which is equal to the first. I named it *Yijian Yi Zhi*. The two books, *Yijian* and *Yijian Yi Zhi*, together contain six hundred stories. All the strange and wonderful things in the world are all gathered here. The *Qi Hua* records strange things, Zhuangzi discusses the heavens. Illusory and vague, they cannot be questioned. Up to Gan Bao's *Soushen Ji*, Qi Zhang Gong's *Xuan Gui*, Gushenzi's *Boyi*, the records of Hedong, the records of Xuan Shi, the records of Jishen—all cannot avoid including allegorical stories. If my book is like this, not more than sixty years ago, within sight and hearing, all are clearly based on evidence. If you do not believe me, go see Mr. Wu You and ask him. On December 18, 1166, Fanyang Hong Mai Jing Lu wrote the preface. In May of the eighth year, re-engraved from the Kuaiji version in Gan, removed five stories, changed two stories, and other parts were also revised. Re-engraved in Jian'an in July of the seventh year of Chongxi.

The Second Volume of Yijian Records, Chapter Fourteen The Reborn Buddha A resident of Lanchi Village in Xianjing County named Xian Qiu fell ill and mistakenly took medicine, which made his condition severe. He became dazed and unconscious and saw three officials in yellow robes holding a summons to arrest him. There were also two officials in white robes screaming on the beams. Xian Qiu ordered his family to burn paper money and pray, saying, "My son bought medicine and has not returned. I wish to delay for a moment." The three officials were pleased and took the money and left. By evening, the son returned, and the three officials followed him inside. Xian Qiu then died and traveled with the two officials in white robes, who were also being arrested. One was named Chuchong, and the other was named Suizhongding. After traveling for a long time, they entered a large city with three gates and grand palaces and buildings. They met an old friend named Cao Weiji, who had died several years ago. Cao Weiji asked Xian Qiu why he came. Xian Qiu said, "I was being pursued to this place. I do not know what for." Cao Weiji said with joy, "There is a fellow villager here. Do not worry." He asked, "Who is it?" Cao Weiji replied, "Yu Taibo, who now judges the path of rebirth. Tomorrow you will be reborn as a Buddha. You should go quickly." A little later, officials led him to the palace, where the king, wearing a crown and flowing robes, sat on the throne. First, they called Zhongding and Chong, who were all released. They were quite far apart, and Xian Qiu did not know what was said. Then, they asked Xian Qiu, "What good deeds have you done in your life?" He replied, "My family is poor and lacks resources. But I once visited Wawushan Mountain and visited a Bodhisattva. The aura was very beautiful. And I donated a piece of wood to the temple of the Sky Father." Officials gave him paper and ink to record what he said.

The document was held up. The king wrote on the back: "Release." Qiu prostrated himself in the courtyard. After taking several steps back, someone called out to him. The king approached the steps and said, "Tell my family on my behalf. Set up a rebirth ceremony and recite the name of the rebirth Buddha." After finishing speaking, white light ascended, and the room was suddenly illuminated. Qiu prostrated again and went out. Arriving at the base of a large building, he saw a green plaque with golden characters inscribed: "Great Compassion and Great Mercy Rebirth Buddha." As soon as he stepped out, he revived. His wife and children were crying, having already prepared the coffin for burial. It was the 26th day of the sixth month in the 18th year of the Shaoxing era. The next day, he rushed to describe what he saw to虞祺. On虞祺's anniversary day, he said, "虞祺's name is祺, and his courtesy name is齐年. He never read Buddhist scriptures. He once served as the漕运官 in夔州 and潼州. During the time of military mobilization, various regions prioritized collecting and hoarding resources, but only虞祺's jurisdiction remained calm and undisturbed. Finally, he was in潼川. In the 17th year of the Shaoxing era, he was suffering from a minor illness. On the 27th day of the sixth month, he leaned on a table without speaking. He suddenly glanced at the seated guests and said, 'Ancient Buddhas have all come. I will also return.' 子允文 stood beside him and wept. He looked around and said, 'If I can become a Buddha, what is there to object?' The guests found his words strange. He then smiled and passed away. When Qiu's story was told,虞祺's proof of becoming a Buddha became even more evident. The name of the rebirth Buddha is found in the Mahāparinirvāṇa Sūtra. The magistrate of新宁,桀璜, wrote a record.

Odorous Ghost A man from Kaifeng named Zhang Yan recounted that during the end of the Zhenhe era on the Qingming Festival, a scholar from the Imperial Academy, along with his fellow students, went out to the outskirts to drink freely. On their return, they followed the Bian River embankment, and they saw a white-clad person behind them, about ten or so steps away. It was a tall and dignified man, but the foul smell was offensive to the nose. At first, they thought it was just a coincidence. Soon after, the person followed them into the school. When they asked their fellow students, none of them saw anyone. They found this very strange. When they returned to their room, the person stood to the left and right of them. When they asked it, it did not respond. When they scolded it, it disappeared. Suddenly, it reappeared. It followed closely without stopping, and the smell became twice as strong. The scholar was unable to bear the fear. Someone advised him, "Perhaps your good fortune is shallow, or you are being tormented by a grudge. Why not return home to care for your parents? Do not think of fame and achievement. If it can be avoided, then do so." He followed this advice and left the capital. The person gradually became farther away and eventually disappeared. The scholar lived at home for many years, but he could not help but feel depressed. His parents urged him to resume his studies, and he forgot about the previous strange encounter. He then went to the capital to seek advice. After a month, while sending off a guest to the old place where they had drunk, he met the person again. The person spoke sternly, "This time, I will not let you go." It followed him as before, but the smell was even worse. The scholar suddenly became delirious and fell ill for ten days, after which he died.

庄君平 According to Li Boji, the Chancellor's younger brother, Ji Yanlun, there was a Taoist in Fuzhou who had no other skills but was adept at fortune-telling and divination. He said that a person with the Dao is different from others because they have green eyes. Once, he saw an old man in the market whose hair and beard were as white as snow, but whose face was rosy, and whose pupils were deep green. He secretly followed the old man and found that he was staying at an inn. The next day, he moved in and observed the etiquette of a disciple very strictly. They lived together for over a year, but there was no connection between them. One night, it was very cold, and the old man got up to go to the bathroom. Ji Yanlun held the urinal for him, and the old man was surprised by its warmth. Ji Yanlun replied, "I feared the cold air would harm you, so I warmed it in my bedding." The old man was greatly moved and said, "I didn't know you had such a kind heart. I must tell you the truth. I am Han Zhuang Junping. I have traveled the world for a thousand years and have never seen someone like you. I will give you a book from my bag. Read this and you can attain the Dao." The old man left the next morning and never returned. The book contained one hundred five-character poems, all about self-cultivation and living in the world. Ji Yanlun could recite many of them. Today, he only remembers a few lines: "Achievements and fame are not worth a cup of water. And another line says, 'Standing alone by the autumn river.' That's all. The Taoist stayed in Fujian for a long time but eventually disappeared.

Chess of the Immortals** A villager named Lin Wu from Fuliu Village in Youxi County, Nanjian, was sixteen years old when he went to chop wood in the mountains. He saw two people playing chess. Leaning on his burden, he watched them. Beside them were two cranes pecking at waxberries. Two berries fell to the ground. The chess players looked at Lin and told him to pick them up. He bent down and ate them. Suddenly, the two people disappeared. Lin returned home and immediately began to abstain from food and drink, never eating again. His ultimate fate is unknown. **Crab Mountain** The mother of a physician named Sha Zhujiao from Huzhou loved to eat crabs. Every year during the crab season, she would buy dozens of them, place them in a large jar, and sit with her grandchildren to watch. Whenever she wanted to eat, she would select some and place them in the pot. In the 17th year of the Shaoxing era (1147 AD), she died. Her son set up a ritual at the Tianqing Temple. All the family members went there. One ten-year-old grandson saw an old woman standing outside the temple gate, her entire body covered in blood. The old woman spoke to the boy, saying, "Because of my addiction to eating crabs, I was driven to Crab Mountain immediately after my death to receive punishment. The crabs piled up like a mountain. The jailer pierced me and made me stand on them. The crabs fought with their claws, inflicting pain on me without pause. The suffering is indescribable. Just now, officials of the underworld brought me here to receive offerings, but the local deities do not allow me to enter." The boy told his father, who then wept and prayed to the local deities. Soon, the old woman appeared at the altar, saying, "The pain is unbearable. Please print and burn the *Nine Days Born Divine Scripture* for me. Distribute it among the crabs so they can hold it and be reborn. Perhaps this will save me." She then disappeared. The family immediately carved the divine scripture onto a board and burned a hundred sheets each night until the end of the mourning period.

**Assistant Minister Li Xiliang** During the Zhenghe period, Li Xiliang served as a low-ranking official. His neighbor, who was very poor and made a living as a teacher, once borrowed a horse to go out of the city. Upon returning, he said, "A month ago, I dreamed of a noble person who said, 'I am your sixth-generation ancestor, a loyal minister who assisted in the founding of the nation. My tomb is located in a certain village outside the capital, about ten miles away. There are fields designated for the maintenance of ancestral worship and rituals, which can yield two hundred dou of rice annually. I have been gone for a long time, and I do not know that the descendants have become so few. These fields still exist, but they are now taken over by the person responsible for managing the tomb. You should go and manage them; it will be enough to make a living.' After waking up, I did not dare to go immediately. Last night, I dreamed again, and the noble person scolded me. I said, 'Since I was young and orphaned, I do not know where my ancestors' tomb is, and I do not know the person responsible for managing the tomb. Moreover, I have no contract or deed of ownership. How can I reclaim the fields?' The noble person replied, 'What you say is very reasonable. There was once a stele with detailed records, which is now buried in the grass outside the gate. Just as I have said, investigate it, and you will certainly find it.'"

Thereupon, Xiliang proceeded that day and found a large tomb garden, indeed a good place, but the weeds and dirt were extremely severe. He called out the caretaker and accused him of the crime of neglect. The caretaker replied, "No one has come to worship or clean for a long time, therefore it has reached this state." Xiliang asked about the location of the land, and the caretaker falsely claimed there was none. Xiliang ordered to bring the tombstone as proof. The caretaker said, "I don't know where it is anymore." Xiliang then ordered to dig the ground with shovels and mattocks, and indeed, it was found near the door in the grass, about a foot away. The caretaker was shocked, fearful, and ashamed, and then fully explained the location of the land. Some of it had also been swallowed up by powerful landlords. Now we will sue in Kaifeng to request justice. Xiliang was greatly surprised by the matter and commended the officials, and they fully recovered the land. After several months, the caretaker again told Xiliang, "At night, I dreamed that my ancestor told me, 'You will get an official position.' One of my contemporaries who assisted in founding the dynasty has become a chancellor. To entrust you to him, he will not forget our old friendship." Not long after, Zheng Dafu was appointed as chancellor and first requested to recognize the descendants of those who founded the dynasty. Thus, they were all given official positions. Wang Jiagou recorded the names of the scholars.

The New Case in the Underworld In the early years of the Dazhong Era, the official responsible for rewards and punishments, Guo Quan, died and then came back to life. He claimed to have visited the underworld extensively, where he often encountered prominent people of recent times. Among the prisons he saw, one held a large number of prisoners. When he asked about it, they told him, "This is the new type of case that has been established." This was said by Chen Fangshi. The Wandering Woman Dong Guoqing, with the courtesy name Ziyang, was from Dexing, Raozhou. In the sixth year of the Xuanhe Era, he passed the imperial examination and became a Jinshi. He was appointed as the main clerk of Jiaoshui County in Laizhou. When there was military action on the northern border, he left his family in the hometown and stayed alone in his official position. The central region fell, and he could not return. He gave up his official position and fled to villages, frequently interacting with innkeepers who pitied his plight and bought him a concubine. She was intelligent and perceptive, and she had beauty. Seeing that Dong was poor, she took it upon herself to manage their livelihood, using all the family's resources to buy seven or eight grinding donkeys and several dou of wheat. Every time she got flour, she rode the donkey into the city to sell it, returning home late at night with money. She went out every few days, and in this way for three years, the profits increased more and more. They had land and houses. Dong had been separated from his mother and wife for a long time, and there was no news or communication. He felt distressed and worried, and his mood remained unsatisfied. The concubine often asked why. Dong loved her very much and did not hide anything. He told her that he was originally an official in the south, and his entire family was living in the hometown. He alone was wandering, with no definite time for his return.
