Each time I deeply reflect, I am almost heartbroken to the point of wanting to die. I said, "Why didn't you tell me sooner? I have an older brother who likes to plan things for others. He will arrive soon. Please help you plan it." After ten days, indeed, a merchant arrived, tall with a curly beard, riding a large horse and driving over ten carriages past the door. I said, "He is my brother." I went out to greet and bow, and had Dong meet him. We discussed family ties and drank until night. I began to tell the guest about the previous day's matter. At that time, the enemy ordered that those who fled from the Song government should confess, and those who concealed themselves and did not confess would be executed. Dong had already leaked the information and suspected that the two were plotting against him. He greatly regretted and feared, so he denied it, saying it was not true. The guest raised his beard, angry and laughing, and said, "I entrusted my younger sister to you for several years. We treated each other like family. I risked breaking the law to bring you back to the south, but you doubted me like this. If something happens along the way, it would also involve me. I will take your official document and use it as proof. Otherwise, I will tie you up and report you to the authorities in the morning." Dong became even more afraid, convinced himself that he would surely die, and searched his bag and handed over all the documents.

The woman wept all night, listening to the guest. The guest departed. The next day, he arrived on horseback and said, "Let us go." Dong called his concubine to join him. She replied, "I have something urgent. I must stay for a while. Next year, I will come to find you. I have made this robe by hand to give to you. Please wear it carefully. Only follow the direction of my brother's horse. If you return to your country, my brother might offer tens of thousands of coins as a gift. You should not accept it. If you cannot refuse it, then show him the robe. He has received my favor. Now I am sending you back. It is not enough to repay the kindness. He will then protect me as I leave. If you accept his gift, then he will feel his obligation is fulfilled, and he will no longer care about me. Guard this robe well, and do not lose it." Dong was startled and puzzled by her words, which seemed unrelated. In the morning, he feared that the neighbors might notice. He wiped away his tears and mounted his horse, galloping to the seashore. There was a large ship ready to cast off. The guest gestured for Dong to board. They bowed to each other and parted. The ship immediately sailed south, with no provisions or preparations for the journey. Dong was bewildered and did not know what to do. However, the people on the ship treated him with great care, providing him with food. They did not ask him any questions. Soon, the ship reached the southern shore.

The guest had already arrived by the water's edge and invited Dong to a pavilion. They exchanged greetings and expressions of concern. The guest presented twenty taels of gold and said, "This is for the longevity of your esteemed mother." Dong remembered the words spoken by his wife when they parted and strongly refused it. The guest said, "Do you want to return to the country empty-handed and starve with your wife and children?" He forced Dong to keep the gold and left. Dong caught up with him and showed him the robe. The guest laughed in astonishment and said, "My intelligence is indeed inferior to his. My affairs are far from complete. Next year, I will bring your beautiful lady. Leave directly without looking back." Dong returned home to find his mother, wife, and two sons all safe. He took out the robe and showed it to his family, letting it be unfolded. The yellow color was faintly visible. When he folded it open to see, it was filled with gold leaf. After going to the palace to clear himself, he was appointed as a magistrate of Yixing. The following year, the guest indeed brought his concubine. Chancellor Qin had a past with Dong when they were both captured and recounted their past experiences. He was promoted to a position in the capital handling military audits and died a few months later. Qin ordered his mother, Madame Wang, to appeal to the court, and Dong was posthumously promoted from an educational official to a court official. His son Zhongkan was also appointed. This was in the third month of the tenth year of the Shaoxing era. Said by Fan Zhineng.

The Dream of Cattle Eaters and Its Warning Zhou Jie, courtesy name Shengqing, was a native of Taizhou. He temporarily resided in Sian Town, Huzhou. During the reign of Qin Chuci, the governor of Xuancheng, Zhou was summoned by edict to serve as the magistrate of Nanling County. Due to an illness or epidemic, he returned home. In a dream, he was arrested and brought to an official's office. A man in red robes sat at a desk handling prisoners, and there were also several officials in red and green robes who greeted him with courtesy and sat in a circle in the hall. An official led Zhou and asked him, "How can you indulge in beef so excessively?" He ordered a whipping on the back, and several soldiers dragged him away. Zhou looked back and pleaded for mercy, saying, "From today onward, not only will I not eat it, but I will also make this prohibition known to my entire family." The officials all stood up to apologize, and the main official's anger subsided, allowing Zhou to return home. Upon waking, he was sweating profusely all over his body, and his illness suddenly recovered. To this day, he strictly adheres to this prohibition and often speaks of it to others. In the 30th year of the Shaoxing era (1160 AD), Zhou oversaw the salt warehouse.

The Sheep's Grievance** Wu Daofu told his wife's younger brother. He served as a county clerk in Huai West. The county was very poor, and there was no one to slaughter sheep. The clerk and the magistrate and the deputy discussed and jointly contributed money to buy sheep from neighboring counties to raise them. They agreed that the sheep should not be slaughtered for private reasons unless for sacrifice, important guests, or public use. The agreement was already made. After a long time, the clerk's wife's younger sister came from afar. They met and were very happy, so they bought wine to entertain. In a hurry, there was nothing to prepare for the meal, so they immediately slaughtered one sheep. After the wine, the two women stayed together, while the clerk slept alone in an outer room. At dawn, he heard the sheep cry under the bed. Its sound was angry and sad. He patted the bed to scare it, but it did not stop. A moment later, the sheep climbed onto the bed and hit the clerk with its horns, bit him, and cursed, speaking human words and said: "You bought me to be slaughtered, it was your plan. You made an oath with others and broke it first. I was supposed to die on a certain day for the village sacrifice. Now killing me suddenly is unjust, I must be compensated for my life." The clerk said: "It is my fault, I dare not escape death. Please allow me to enter the room to say goodbye to my wife and children, and to make arrangements for after my death, can I?" The sheep said: "When you killed me, would you spare me a little?" The clerk quickly entered and knocked on the bedroom door, calling his wife. The wife was drinking with her sister and did not respond. The clerk said: "I am being urgently pursued by my guilt, so I want to say goodbye to you. How could you not respond, I am dying." The wife must not remarry, otherwise I will become a ghost to seek revenge on you." The wife woke up in shock and opened the door, and found her husband already lying dead in blood. The night watchman said: "I only saw the clerk arguing about matters, but saw nothing else." The wife was still young, and her parents wanted to marry her off. Every time a matchmaker came, she would dream of her late husband blaming her. She finally remained faithful. **Zhao Zixian's Dream** [Note: The second part of the text, "赵子显梦," is not provided in the source text, so it is not included in the translation.]

Zhao Gongcheng, courtesy name Zixian. His ancestral home was in Quanzhou. In the 28th year of the Shaoxing era (1158 CE), he served as the governor of Ganzhou. His relatives came to rely on him due to poverty. He lodged them in another inn and provided them with daily meals, always instructing them not to overstep. Once, while napping during the day, he dreamed of his old home's courtyard, which was covered in blood and gore. He ordered it to be cleaned, and it immediately returned to its original state. A neighbor came to tell him that several cattle had been slaughtered. He awoke suddenly, startled. A guard came to report on patrol duties and said that a certain prince from Quanzhou, who was acquainted with the governor, had slaughtered several cattle for the market. Zhao Gongcheng realized it was a divine warning. He arrested and thoroughly investigated the matter, holding the prince's servant responsible for the crime and expelling him from the territory. He then strictly enforced a ban on such activities. Zhao Gongcheng said, "Sanzhong." ### Dream Reading a Strange Book Shen Jun, courtesy name Daoyuan, was a man from Qiantang. He was known for his clear and virtuous character and did not speak falsely. He lived in Xiantan Village, Huzhou. Relatives and friends in the county often invited him to the city for weddings. He attended many feasts and could not return for several days, feeling quite tired of it. In a dream, he visited a friend named Lu Weizhi. He saw a table piled with dozens of books. The host was just tying his belt. Shen Jun casually picked up one volume. The chapters varied in length, generally resembling the *Zhenhao* (True Declarations). He chose the shortest chapter to read. The text said, "People enjoy eating peaches and plums, but peaches and plums should not be eaten in excess. Eating crabs is laughable. Any crab must be killed to be eaten. Similarly, in learning the Dao, one must eliminate the negative to achieve purity. If it is unavoidable, one can eat a mouse in a carriage or a dung beetle in a toilet, but only as a last resort." He had not finished reading a few sentences when Lu Weizhi looked at him and said, "The writing is quite strange. You should finish reading it." They both laughed and then woke up. Shen Jun tried to find the reason for the dream but could not determine it.

After a long time, he finally understood. About six months ago, a monk named Huai Zheng from Wucheng came. They stayed at the same temple called Huìtōng Temple. Zheng made a type of porridge called Dongpo Yusan Geng. He invited Chen and Lu to join him. When Lu arrived, the porridge was already finished. Zheng then joked and said, "Just arrived by the river. Saw a woman in a boat cooking crabs for washing hands. I happened to compose a poem and gave it to you as a gift: Purple-bearded crabs with shells as thin as paper. Grapes made of flesh and amber marrow. The host rolled up his sleeves and ground the crab claws. Added vinegar, rubbed with orange, and served with new wine. A foolish monk born without this taste. Eating a single vegetable root would be enough to satisfy him. Called him to try and scrape the bottom of the pot. To exchange his tongue for another reference. Everyone passed it around and clapped in appreciation. Silently moved. Slowly said, 'The poem is beautiful, but how does it compare to the great works?' Wei was surprised and thanked him. Chen never ate crabs again. Gradually proving the absurdity of the dream. Two years later, because of taking cangzhu (a type of herb), he was forbidden to eat peaches and plums. Finally fully understanding a chapter that says, "Chen has written an article about this." As for Li Sanying's poem: It is said that Zhou Shihou, who passed the imperial examination under Zheng Xue, ranked fifth in the fifth division. His talent surpassed one person named Chen Chuan. Shihou jokingly said, "The top scholar cannot be compared to Zheng Xue. Turning back, one still likes to see Chen Chuan." In the twenty-seventh year of the Shaoxing era, Wang Shipeng from Yongjia ranked first among the scholars. His fellow townsman Wu Yizheng ranked last. Li Sanying, who was specially recommended, was able to enter officialdom and ranked below Wu. Wu followed the previous saying and said, "One dare not look up to Wang Shipeng. Stretching one's legs can still step on Li Sanying." Its structure is almost naturally formed.

Mr. Xiao Xi** Li Cizhong and Mr. Xiao Xi were traveling in Jiankang Market. They entered a teahouse and saw a beggar walking slowly in front of them, his legs covered in sores and filth. Li said to Xiao Xi, "This person has such a terrible disease. I wish you could save him." Xiao Xi replied, "It’s not difficult. I’m just afraid that such a strange act might shock the crowd." Li persisted, so Xiao Xi asked for a piece of paper, spit saliva on it, and applied it to the beggar’s legs. The saliva was thick like glue. He gave the paper to the beggar and instructed him to apply it to his legs. After some time, Xiao Xi asked, "Do you feel hot?" The beggar replied, "At first it was very painful. Then it became extremely itchy. Now it’s so hot I can’t stand it." Xiao Xi peeled off the paper and asked Li to look at it. The new flesh had grown completely, and all the scars were gone. The townspeople came in a crowd to watch. Xiao Xi slipped away among the crowd, and Li hurried to look for him but it was too late. **Record from the *Yijian Zhi* Volume 22** **A Jar Found in a Tree** The Hu family of Piling wanted to expand their hall and house, but the朴tree in the courtyard was an obstacle. They cut it down and found a clay jar inside, capable of holding three dou of rice, with the bark and knots still intact. That very day, a mountain spirit was disturbed and caused trouble. A passerby who was skilled in reciting the Dragon Tree Spell was called to deal with it. The family asked a child to watch. The child saw tiny figures, each a few inches tall, being chased away by the Dragon Tree Spell. The spirits entered a woman’s bed and spoke through her. They then set up a ritual platform, performed ceremonies, and drove the spirits away. It took six months of disturbance before the situation was finally settled.

**Yixing Man** A man from Yixing, who had long been known for his wit and humor, had a mountain ghost enter his room. The ghost extended a foot from the ceiling to the floor, with black hair hanging down. The man playfully said to the ghost, "If you truly have supernatural powers, drop another foot." The ghost could not respond. After a while, it retracted its foot and left. Since then, it never returned. (Told by Jiang Chengxiang) **Jiang Professor** A man from Yongjia, Jiang, passed the imperial examination in the second year of Shaoxing and was appointed as the chief clerk of Jinyun in Chuzhou. He was later transferred to teach in Xinzhou. Returning home to wait for his next assignment, he was not yet a hundred miles from home when he was walking in the mountains. He heard two people crying bitterly on the hill. Upon arriving, he saw an old man holding a young girl with braided hair blocking the path and crying. Jiang was moved by pity and asked the reason. The old man said, "I served in the army for twenty years and was fortunate to be granted leave. Unfortunately, I encountered robbers who took away my official documents. I intended to go to the Ministry of Personnel to handle the matter, but it would cost fifty thousand coins to resolve. I love this girl very much, but now I have to sell her out of love. I will leave soon. That's why I am crying, unable to part with her." Jiang said, "Give the contents of my bag to the old man. How about delaying this plan a bit?" He immediately gave the rest of his belongings to him, which were only worth ten thousand coins. The old man said, "Thank you for your noble deed, but it will not help me." Jiang said, "If you truly do not doubt me, then I will entrust the girl to you to bring back home."

The old man was instructed, "Please take this money to Lin'an." If the matter fails, return to my home and take more. I will treat your daughter well; I dare not make her a concubine. Do not worry. The old man replied with thanks, "Yes." They agreed to meet again next year during late spring. The old man handed over his daughter to Jiang, wiped away tears, and parted. Jiang got off the carriage and loaded the daughter onto it, following behind on a staff. As they approached home, Jiang placed the daughter in an outer guesthouse and went alone to see his mother and wife. Mrs. Zhou greeted and said, "Hearing that there was someone following the carriage, where is she now?" Jiang told the truth. Mrs. Zhou said, "Then it is a good thing. What harm is there in completing it together?" She sent someone to call the daughter back. Jiang's mother, Mrs. Ke, loved her as if she were their own daughter, and at night, they slept together with her. The daughter occasionally went to the outer guesthouse to play with Jiang, sometimes teasing each other. When they first met, she was still an ordinary woman. By then, her appearance became increasingly beautiful, and she became lovely and beautiful. One night, she was drunk and could not control herself, so she stayed and they became intimate. And the old man never came again.

As he was about to take up his official post, his wife refused to accompany him. She said, "There is already a beautiful person; what use am I?" His wife, Lady Ke, also said, "You have been entrusted with someone else's child. If you act this way now, one can predict what will happen in the future. I am old and will die in my hometown. I cannot follow you." Jiang tried to persuade her but failed. He finally went alone with his daughter to Xinzhou. After living there for several months at dusk, he called his daughter to comb her hair. The daughter held the comb and kept wiping her tears. When he asked her, she did not answer. He exclaimed, "Do you miss your father? Are you planning to leave?" The daughter replied, "I am not sad about anything. I am sad about the master." She continued, "Human life is unpredictable. Now the time is almost up. I wish you would quickly write a letter to inform the master's wife." Jiang became angry and scolded her, saying, "How can a child and a woman say such ominous words?" The daughter said, "The situation is critical. In a moment, it will be too late. My words are not reckless. Look at the minor official in the courtyard. Order him to fetch a pen and paper." The daughter hastily took the comb, held the pen, and forced Jiang to write.

Jiang became angry and laughed, saying, "What should be written?" The woman replied, "Just say that he died suddenly of a severe illness today." Jiang had no choice but to write down ten or so characters. He then asked again, "How did you know?" The woman suddenly changed color and spoke loudly, "Do you know about a person named Yinghua in Jinyun? I am she." With that, she clapped her hands and vanished. Jiang immediately fell to the ground and died, with blood flowing from his ears, nose, mouth, and eyes. A minor official saw a fox emerge from the room, passing through the window and climbing up to the roof before leaving. People all said that Jiang's righteousness did not last until the end. Some said that when Jiang first arrived in Jinyun, someone told him about the matter involving Yinghua. Jiang said, "I must kill him." After serving as an official for several days, he walked in the garden and discovered a hidden area where he found a large well covered by a boulder. He found it strange and ordered it to be opened. Inside, he saw a huge white earthworm, more than a zhang long and as thick as a pillar. He pierced its head with a needle or awl, and the earthworm disappeared immediately. When Jiang died in Xinzhou, people suspected it was this creature. This story was told by Jiang Zili from the Tang dynasty.

### 陈氏女 A Woman from the Chen Family A man from Wuxi named Chen Yanheng lived near Nanchan Temple. His wife, Bian, was pregnant. She dreamed of a woman dressed in a red robe and a plain skirt, who scooped water in the courtyard and looked up, saying, "I am the daughter of a Tiaoju (an official title) from Kunshan County. I came to Nanchan Temple to attend the water and land assembly. If your merits and virtues are complete, you will be born into your husband's family as a son. If not, you can also be a daughter." Bian saw this woman as very beautiful and replied, "To have children is not something I dare to hope for. I hope you will visit and drink tea with me." The woman smiled and left. After waking up, she told Yanheng about the dream. He went to inquire and found that indeed there was a Tiaoju named Chen Yanwu from Kunshan, who had come to Nanchan Temple to set up a water and land assembly for his deceased 18-year-old daughter. The next day, Bian gave birth to a son. ### 张梦孙 Zhang Mengsun A man from Piling named Zhang Rujie Weiji served as the magistrate of Fenghua County in Mingzhou during the 13th year of the Shaoxing era (1143 AD). His daughter-in-law, Li, was pregnant for the full term. Weiji dreamed of an old friend, Chen Yuyu Wenqing, who said, "We have been apart for sixteen years. How would you like to have a grandson?" Weiji was delighted. The next day, he told the monk Rizhi, "Wenqing is a fine gentleman. I will surely have a virtuous grandson. You can congratulate me." Later, Li gave birth to a girl, and he named her Mengsun. When she was a few years old, she played beside her grandfather. She happened to see Wenqing's writing from when he was born, picked it up and looked at it, saying, "It is my writing." Wenqing was from Wuxi and had both passed the imperial examinations under the list of Shen Yuanong, serving as a judicial officer in Yangzhou. During the Jianyan era (1127-1130 AD), the Jurchen invaders invaded Huaidian, and he died in office.

**A Woman Transformed into a Dog** In the household of Scholar Zhai in Suzhou, there was a wet nurse named Wang. She had never done any good deeds and would laugh at and mock people who recited Buddha. When she was forty years old, a growth appeared on her buttocks, causing her great pain. She applied ointment to it, but it grew larger and larger, eventually becoming a dog's tail, measuring more than a foot long. From then on, she could not walk and had to crawl on her hands and knees. She would wait for dogs and pigs to eat their fill and then follow them to eat whatever was left. At night, she would sleep with them. After half a year, she died. **Another Case: Officer Xu Zhong** An officer named Xu Zhong also developed a tail due to a disease. He told his wife and children, "I have sinned by throwing away food. In a dream, I entered the City God Temple and was ordered to go to the court to provide a statement. From now on, I must not eat human food, but only bran and chaff. Bring me some bran and chaff." Once he received it, he crouched and ate it, just like a dog. His family performed Buddhist offerings and rituals to repent for his sins. He died after a few days. This occurred during the 30th year of the Shaoxing era (1160). **The Wife of Zhang Shi** The wife of Zhang Shi, a commoner in Wujiang County, loved to kill living beings and treated her in-laws poorly. She was sixty years old when she fell ill. Two sores appeared on her legs, filled with worms that bit into her bones and marrow. She writhed in pain and moaned, and her cries could be heard by her neighbors. After a long time, whenever she excreted, she would eat it, and even the mats and cushions she ate. She died after a year. This was recorded in the *Si Shi Ri Zhi Shuo* (Four Matters Daily Wisdom).

承天寺 滕恺，字南夫，婺源人。绍兴五年登科，调任信州司户。赴任后，他梦见自己前往其他郡县，游览僧舍，匾额上写着“承天寺”。寺庙建筑宏伟，但无人居住。独有一老僧出来接待客人，说：“此寺乃本师所建。建成后，因缘事未了，舍之游方，逾期不还。众僧亦悉委去，惟某仅存。老病无力，不得供扫洒事也。”问：“去几何时？”答：“二十七年。”又问：“何时当来？”答：“今岁归矣。”当时滕恺二十七岁，醒后以为不祥。恰逢当年秋试考校南康军，行至中途，日薄西山，投宿民家，不肯收留。民家指一曲折小径，说：“此处佛刹颇洁，士大夫来者多就馆，盍过之。”行数十步，果然发现一座野寺。查看匾额，正是“承天寺”。入门后，寺庙寂静无声，廊庑殿宇积满尘埃。徘徊良久，只见一人出来，问答间，全如梦中所言。滕恺戏登禅床，作长老说法，以为梦已应验，无他矣。随后，老僧引他至上层，开门拂榻，室内所藏器玩戒规，皆历历可识。滕恺始觉不祥，不能留，强宿于旁舍，明晨离去。自此以后。

Spiritual energy was often gloomy. After entering the examination hall, his daytime food intake decreased, and nighttime sleep was forgotten. Interacting with fellow candidates, there was no more laughter or conversation. Surprised and asked him, he finally told the reason. He said, "I fear death, how can I find joy?" Fellow candidates tried to comfort him, but he could not be consoled. After finishing the examination, he returned immediately. Arriving at the Leping Post Station, a Taoist came to pay respects and said, "I want to see the Master of the Household Department." The attendant reported inside, but Kai refused to see him. The Taoist entered directly and looked at Kai and said, "Hurry to prepare. In three days, you can still say goodbye to your family. If delayed, it will be too late." Without bowing, he left. Kai became even more afraid and sent a messenger to inform his family. He then became weak and fell ill. After three days, he arrived at Dexing and urgently summoned the county magistrate to meet. He said, "Kai is about to become a ghost. No time to talk with you." On the road, he encountered a mad Taoist who said, "Your life will end today." If it is as he said, leave my after-death affairs to you. The magistrate said, "How could there be such a thing? You are suffering from hard work and illness." He will go back and fetch wine to drink with you. Stay together here tonight. Do not be afraid. Just as the magistrate got into the carriage, Kai indeed died. His brother Chunfu was in the countryside. After receiving the letter from Leping, he was already worried about it. That day, he stood idly by the gate and saw a monk wearing a warm hat and walking with a staff coming. Thought he was from the temple. Greeted and spoke with him. The monk did not answer and covered his face with his sleeve. Directly went to Southfu's study and looked around, seeing no one. Chunfu cried out in tears. And the funeral procession from Dexing arrived, in a sedan chair, he returned home, wearing a warm hat on his head. Then the monk seen was Kai. As said by Cheng Tai.

Three Official Man Wang Fei, a scholar, was from the Eastern State. During the early years of the Jianyan era, he fled to Wu Xing to avoid the turmoil. He stayed at the Kōngxiàng Temple. His nephew, Wén Lǎo, was walking outside the temple late in the afternoon when he saw a man wearing a blue robe and riding a horse, passing by. The man greatly resembled Wén Sān Xiōng, his close relative. Wén Lǎo followed and called out to him. The man turned back and said, "Last night I arrived here and moored my boat under the Bai Ping Pavilion. I had to leave the city urgently because of some urgent business. I will pay respects tomorrow. If not, please come to see me." Then he galloped out through the Qingtang Gate. Wén Lǎo had not seen him for several years. The next morning, he found the man's boat and called out to his servant, saying, "I want to see the Three Official Man." The servant replied, "He has been dead for over a month." Wén Lǎo said, "He rode past my door yesterday and spoke to me. How could he be dead?" He then described what he had seen. The servant was shocked and reported it to the family. They all cried out and came out. His wife wept and said, "My husband died when he was in dire straits and could not be properly dressed for burial. Therefore, he was buried in his everyday clothes. What you saw was him." Wén Lǎo returned home and remembered that there was a Cígǎn Temple outside the Qingtang Gate. He went there directly and asked the monk, "What guest came here last night?" The monk replied, "There was no important guest. Only a patron set up a water-land assembly." Wén Lǎo then realized that he had come to attend a gathering in the afterlife. This account was written by Xu Chunli.

In the fifteenth year of the Shaoxing era, Xu Zichong returned from Danyang to Wudun. His boat arrived at Buniu, and he docked alongside the coffin ship of the former Governor of Guangzhou, Zheng Tongpan. It was nearly evening when a purple-clad official introduced himself as Lin Tixia and requested an audience. He said, "I am a servant of the Zheng family. The favored concubine, Mo, was originally the daughter of a concubine from Wudun. Her legitimate mother did not accept her, and when she was pregnant, her mother was expelled. She was born in an outer house and, after growing up, became a concubine. When the main wife's position became vacant, she actually managed the household affairs and was called a minor lady. The master died in the south, and one son was too young to return. Relying on the good relationship with Wang Shilang of Pingjiang, she sent someone to bring back the coffin and wanted to place it in a temple within the territory. The family's wealth was extremely abundant, and all of it belonged to Mo. She planned to return to her father. Hearing that you live in Wudun, she asked you to deliver a letter and send someone to come and welcome her." Xu agreed and said, "I will do it." He traveled several miles.

Tomorrow, they met again. Lin said, "Miss Mo wishes to see you. Please bring your bags first." Xu feared there might be other objections and refused to accept. Shortly after, Lin came again and said, "I don't have time to write. Just convey this meaning to Mr. Zhi Lu, and that will be enough." Xu went home. One day, he went to see Mr. Zhi Lu and told him about the matter. Mr. Zhi Lu was surprised and said, "There is no such thing." After several months, Xu met his cousin Gao Gongru. They spoke about it, and Gao Gongru was surprised and said, "I almost fell into his trap." Then he recounted what he had seen. At first, he docked his boat at the Su Gu Pavilion and also met Lin Sheng. His words were roughly the same. Finally, he said, "Miss Mo wants to return to her father. She thinks about her life and never heard of him. And she has been defiled by someone. She will certainly not be treated with respect. She wants to marry as a wife, but it is unacceptable for scholars and officials. Common people and marketplaces are not suitable for her. She wants to re-enter a wealthy family as a concubine. The person is extremely beautiful, with personal wealth worth millions. Do you have any interest in her?"

Gao consulted his wife. His wife coveted his wealth and agreed to accept him. Lin asked, "Do you want to see her first?" Gao was pleased and stayed to drink. He stood outside the boat waiting. After a while, a woman in green clothes and a red skirt walked along the dike. She ordered a child to cover her face with a small green umbrella. Her waist was graceful. The guest paused leisurely and was intoxicated in heart. Lin smiled and said, "Does she suit your taste?" However, this was a good family's daughter, and it was difficult to formalize the agreement. You should present some gifts like a dowry. He immediately gave her a bundle of silk. By dusk, she came and said, "It is agreed. I have taken all the items from my pouch and placed them in your boat. We will meet tomorrow at a certain temple, and then the ceremony will be completed." Before the conversation ended, she carried more than ten boxes, all of which were gold, pearls, rhinoceros horn, ivory, and musk. By the appointed time, Lin led Gao into the temple. They arrived at a room outside the temple. They saw several women laughing and talking behind the curtain, and the woman in red was there. When they turned to see the outsiders, all of them turned away. Lin said, "You should wait a moment."

I should first inform him. After half a day, there was no further response. A monk reciting sutras in the hall below was surprised that Gao had been standing for so long and came to ask what had happened. Gao fully described what he had seen. The monk said, "This mountain temple is desolate. How could there be such a thing?" Gao still thought it was absurd and sternly rebuked him. An elderly monk then came out of his room and sighed, "Surely this is a strange occurrence. I have recently frequently seen it." He led Gao inside to see. Behind the treasury, there were more than ten funeral halls arranged, all with wooden plaques inscribed with the names of the deceased. One read "Young Lady Mo," and the last one was "The Head of the Guard Lin Chengxin." Just as they were shocked and hurried out, a servant rushed to report that the boat was about to sink. Another servant then said, "Fortunately, the boat was unharmed, but all the items entrusted were not as expected." They quickly looked at them. Items like ivory, elephant tusks, incense, and medicines had all turned to white and black paper money ashes. The supposed gold, pearls, and vessels turned out to be skulls, animal bones, and horse and cow dung. Both of them encountered such things. Gao barely escaped. The imperial college student Qian Zhiwang said, but it was not confirmed by Xu. Wu Qi's Dream Wu Qi, the original scholar. During the Zhenghe period, because of his study and performance in music at the Imperial College, he passed the examination and received a degree, becoming a teacher at the Zhenjiang Prefecture, replacing Li Boji. He had already moved into the government quarters, but Li Boji's library and study room had not yet been vacated. One night, Wu Qi dreamed of a ghost in a purple robe and gold belt bowing in the courtyard and saying, "In fifteen years, you will become the Chief Military Councillor." He awoke and was very pleased. From this, he became even more self-assured, thinking that becoming a high official was within reach. But later, his career did not advance as expected. In the first year of the Jingkang era, he died as the magistrate of Huolu in Dingzhou. Li Boji became the Chief Military Councillor in that same year. Said by Zhe's nephew Yi.

Zhao Shiwang Xu Zhe, the chancellor, resided in Suishui. He was friendly with Nanwai Zongzheng Zhongpa. Until he became a general (this part is missing from the original text), he asked, "What is your father's name?" The person replied, "Ba." He could not say what Zhongpa's courtesy name is. He pondered for a long time and said, "Yuquan." But his father's courtesy name was Maoshi. Dunyi was regretting talking to a ghost. Taking advantage of his mistake, he shouted at him and said, "You are a lowly ghost possessing him. You are not the true Zhao Fugan. How could a son not know his father's courtesy name?" He ordered him to be quickly taken away. The official sighed and said, "He invited me but did not show respect. And when he chased me, it was without the intention of an old friend. So it makes me ashamed to see others." After returning home, Dunyi's mind was still not convinced. He again wrote a letter to inquire where he was after death, who was holding him, and how he could come here. He also asked about the stories of retribution and reward that are circulated in the world and what they are like. The official said, "There are many things asked. Let me report slowly." He asked for paper and was about to write. Suddenly he shouted several times and wrote in large characters, "I was ordered to take him to see the Emperor Tianqi Ren Sheng." He fell to the ground suddenly. For three days, the official then revived. The ghost had been there for about half a month. When he left, the gate god with a halberd accused him. Perhaps fearing the leakage of secret matters, so he said. Dunyi died the following year. The three Xu brothers wrote the same letter. But Dunji and Dunli were not recorded. Is it not because their longevity and fortune were not yet exhausted? A cunning ghost could not spy on them? Shiwang died at the age of thirty-seven. Dunli said.

Yiji Yi Zhi, Volume Thirty-Four, Stories Frogs Begging for Their Lives Commandant Kang Xu of the Western Zhejiang Military District resided in Baolian Mountain near Lin'an. On a summer night, as he was about to sleep, he was disturbed by the croaking of frogs. He ordered a young servant to catch them. While Kang was deeply asleep, he dreamed of thirteen people begging for their lives. Kang said, "Although my position is that of a military officer, I am not one who can arbitrarily take life. How can I spare your lives?" They replied, "If you grant us permission, nothing is impossible." A short while later, he woke from a nightmare. He told his wife, "Is it not the frogs?" His wife said, "Did you not catch them?" He called the servant to question him. The servant had already placed them in a bottle. Kang checked the number and found there were exactly thirteen. He immediately released them. This was in the 29th year of the Shaoxing era. As told by Zhang Caifu.

**Boatman Wang Gui** In the 31st year of the Shaoxing era, envoys from the north, Gao Jingshan and Wang Quan, came to congratulate on the Heavenly Birthday Festival. The emperor ordered the high-ranking official Huang Shu to carry fans and silk handkerchiefs to welcome and bestow gifts upon them. By custom, the ships used were provided by the Two Zhejiang Canal Office. The boatman Wang Gui, who served there, died of illness in Hongze, Chu Prefecture. He left behind two sons. His wife wept and told Huang Shu, "My husband has died, and the boat should be returned to the government. With our family of several mouths, we are on the brink of starvation. If my eldest son can continue the service, then we can continue to eat. I wish to ask the canal official for a word." Huang Shu agreed. When they returned to Zhenjiang, they met the canal official. The wife made her request, and on that day, her eldest son was recruited as a soldier to replace Wang Gui. When Huang Shu arrived at Danyang late in the evening, Wang Gui sat in a small boat, looked afar, and bowed. He said, "The whole family is grateful for your kindness, but we cannot repay it." Huang Shu called him forward, and Wang Gui replied, "The paths of humans and spirits are different, so I dare not board your boat." Huang Shu suddenly realized that Wang Gui had died. He called his attendants, but they saw nothing. **Poems by the Daughter of Chen Shugǔ** Chen Shugǔ's daughters were all skilled in poetry and prose. One of them married a man named Li, who served as a judge in the Jin Ning Military District. The provincial inspector knew that his wife was the most talented in poetry. He presented her with a small screen depicting geese and asked her to inscribe it. The wife wrote two short poems in Huang Tingjian's small cursive script. The first poem read: "Through the winding waters, reeds lean and water lilies are pale, A pair of birds glide gracefully against the west wind. Our small boat is blocked from going to the river village, Yet we rejoice at meeting in a dream within a dream." The second poem read: "Who painted this curved screen depicting the small Xiang River? Geese fall in the autumn wind, and water lilies turn half yellow. The clouds are light, the rain sparse, and the solitary island is far away, This will surely make a clear dream reach Gao Tang." The two poems are so clear and elegant, it is as if one does not need to see the actual painting.

韩蕲王诛盗 韩蕲王宣抚淮东，捕获了数十名凶恶的盗贼。将他们带到金山，在庭下陈列刀剑，依次斩杀。众盗贼皆因恐惧而颤抖，最终被处决。唯有一名盗贼跃出，向韩蕲王行礼，指着其中一把最大的刀说：“愿相公赐我这把刀。”韩蕲王笑道：“甚好。”当时有宦官前来传达旨意，在座的宦官说此人临死不惧，似乎也可用。韩蕲王说：“他是在用计谋试图逃脱。”最终还是将他杀了。 浦城道店蝇 在浦城永丰境内，有村民开设了一家旅店。有一位来自严州的客人带着一担丝绢前来，租住并停留数日。店主的妻子性情淫荡，引诱客人与其通奸。不久后，她告诉丈夫说：“这位客人携带的货物不少，而且单独出行，可以图谋。”丈夫便用酒灌醉他，半夜持刀砍杀。客人大声呼救，声音传到了邻居那里。那里住的人很少，仅有一位邻居老人跑来。妻子走到门口，右手挡住不让老人进入，左手却递给他一把客人的丝绢。老人接过丝绢高兴地离开了。客人因此而死。夫妇二人一起抬着尸体，埋在了百步外的山坳里。他们仓促慌张，挖的坑非常浅。店主自以为不会泄露此事。过了几个月，客人的儿子惊讶父亲长期在外未归，以前曾一起做生意，凡是在路上停留的地方，都十分熟悉。

Following the journey, he meticulously investigated the area. Upon reaching the inn, there was no sign of the guest's presence. He stopped to search for clues. During the day, he sat in a daze. A large fly landed on his arm. He waved it away, but it kept coming back, up to five or six times. His concern for his father was intense, and he became extremely suspicious. He wished to the fly, "Isn't it that the divine has sent you to tell me something?" He asked the fly to just lead him. Suddenly, the fly flew up. He followed it, and the fly buzzed as if speaking, flying directly to the place where the guest was buried. There were countless flies there. He extended his head to look, and the body was clearly still there. He ran to report to the village officials. The culprit was arrested and brought to the county. The old man's guilt was also revealed, thus becoming clear evidence. The innkeeper and his wife were both executed, and the old man was flogged on the back. The official destroyed the evil house into ruins. Zheng Jingshi traveled from Putian to Lin'an, passing through that place. He saw all the houses and buildings in ruins, and the remains were completely empty. It was during the eleventh or twelfth year of the Chongxi era. The guest's冤 (wrong) was resolved by the fly, similar to the case of the deer fawn in Xin昌. It is said that they were punished by the ghost.

张夫人婢 张稽仲叔夜枢密之夫人。宗室克敞女也。有小婢。常侍左右。每出必从。在海州时。因侍夫人夜如厕。将还。呼之不应。至于再三。他妾闻之。亟往视。乃俱归。将笞责此婢。而是日以疾卧。元未尝出。始知先携灯者鬼物耳。夫人不淹旬遂病。逾月而卒。张才文说。 Zhang's Lady's Maid The wife of Zhang Ji, a high-ranking official known as Shuye, was a princess from the imperial clan, specifically the daughter of Zongshi Ke Chang. She had a young maid who always attended her side and followed her wherever she went. When they were in Hai Prefecture, the maid accompanied the lady at night to the toilet. On returning, the lady called for her but she did not respond, even after repeated calls. Another concubine heard about it and quickly went to see what had happened. They all returned together, and they were going to punish this maid. But on that day, the maid was sick and lying down, having never left. They then realized that the one who had brought the lamp was a ghost. The lady did not last long before falling ill and died after more than a month. Zhang Caiwen said. 窦氏妾父 徐州人窦公迈。靖康中买一妾。滑人也。未几虏犯河北。妾父母隔阔。不相闻。忧思之至。殆废寝食。忽僵仆于地。若有物凭依。乃言曰、某、女之父也。遭兵乱。举家碎于贼。羁魂无所归。欲就此女丐食。而神不许。守窦氏之门岁余矣。土地怜我。今日始得入。窦氏曰、汝不幸死。夫复何言。吾令汝女作佛事。且具食祭汝。汝亟去。许诺。妾即苏。窦氏如所约。阴与之戒。勿令妾知。又再岁。其父乃自乡里来。初未尝死也。前事盖黠鬼所为。以窃食云。 The Concubine's Father in the Duan Family Duan Gong Mai from Xuzhou bought a concubine during the Jingkang era. She was from Huai Prefecture. Not long after, the barbarians invaded Hebei, and the concubine's parents were separated and could not communicate. She was so worried that she almost neglected sleep and food. Suddenly, she fell to the ground as if something was possessing her. She said, "I am her father. We were caught in the chaos of war and our whole family was killed by the rebels. My soul is wandering without a place to return to. I want to ask this daughter for food, but the spirits did not allow it. I have been guarding the Duan family's door for more than a year. The land god has pitied me, and today I finally got in." The Duan family said, "You are unfortunate to have died. What more can be said?" They will let your daughter perform Buddhist rituals and prepare food to offer to you. You should leave quickly. She agreed, and the concubine immediately recovered. The Duan family followed the agreement and secretly warned her, telling her not to let the concubine know. After another two years, her father came from his hometown. He had never actually died. The previous events were likely caused by a cunning ghost, who wanted to steal food. 王夫人斋僧 Wang's Lady's Observance of the Monks (Note: This part of the text is incomplete and does not provide enough context for a meaningful translation. If more information is available, please provide it for a complete translation.)

Prince Zhong Lie of the Qiong royal family had a son named Shizhou. He married the granddaughter of General Wang Jinqing, a military officer. Shizhou died in his youth, twenty-two years prior. During the year Ding Chou of Emperor Gaozong's reign, Shizhou served as the defense commissioner of Fuzhou and attended court. He lived in the Miluo Cang Alley in Lin'an (modern-day Hangzhou). On the twelfth day of the fifth month, before dawn, his concubine Yang dreamed that someone urged her to get up, saying that a monk from Tianzhu (India) was coming. When it was light, a monk from Shangzhu Temple named Zhongzuo came to pay respects. He said, "I have been ordered to feed the monks. May I ask your intention?" He presented a note written on a piece of paper. The words on the note said, "In accordance with the order of the Taiwei, on the fifteenth day, a vegetarian meal will be prepared for the monks at this temple. Messenger Chen Xing delivers this message." Shizhou was startled and said, "I never had this intention, and there is no one named Chen Xing among my servants." Zhongzuo felt ashamed and left. As he went out, he met another monk named Qingfu from Zhongzhu Temple and a monk named Liexin from Lingyin Temple. They all said they came to inform about the vegetarian meal. They all entered and paid another visit to Shizhou. Just as he was refusing their explanation, he heard a commotion in the air. He went inside to see and found a maid named Laixi from his son Buqian's household, possessed by a spirit, speaking as if a member of the Wang family. She said to Shizhou, "Do not question the three monks. The one who did this is me. Because I used an excessive amount of water for washing my head and feet and the sin of wasting silk and shoes, in the underworld, there are five large jars of dirty water. The command is to drink it. The wet nurse also drank it on my behalf, but only three jars were consumed. Then I was sent away, not allowing others to drink on my behalf. I cannot bear the suffering. I want to seek Buddhist merit to save myself, but there is no way to achieve it. I heard that Prince Zhong Lie presides over the Long Rui Palace, with hundreds of attendants. My concubines and servants, such as Wan Gongren, Wang Gongren, and Xia Qitong, are all around him."

Only I, because of my own filth, could not proceed. Recently, I borrowed a large coat and a special headdress from someone else, and only then could I enter and pay respects in the courtyard. The king pitied my poverty and gave me Tchen Bao-yi. Therefore, he had me perform a loose fast at three temples. I myself requested thirty thousand in expenses, which was the amount for husband and wife. Now the monthly salary is ten times that. How can you not help me? Then he called an old wet nurse and said to her, "You have seen me before. Why didn't you say something?" The old woman replied, "I really saw the lady standing in front of the Marshal's bed the other day. I was afraid the Marshal would be afraid, so I didn't dare to speak up." He then criticized the family for giving their daughter to the Hu family, saying the dowry was too meager, to the point where they had to pawn clothes and couldn't redeem them. He then instructed them to marry the widow's sister and broke into loud weeping. He also urged the family to do good deeds and not to kill living things. His words were sincere and earnest, and those who heard him all wept. Shi Zhou agreed to provide a vegetarian meal for the monks at three temples and set up a water-land assembly at Xianlin Temple. The Wang family was quite pleased.

He joked, "Manage my money for the sake of merit. Do not speak of this afterward." The three monks spoke of Chen Xing, who was very dark in appearance, wore a robe of four shades of black, and carried an old green canopy. Whenever people spoke to him, he would immediately turn away. When offered tea and food, he would only lift it to smell it. At first, they suspected he was full. They gave him two hundred coins, but he strongly refused half of them. He then went to the supervising monk to obtain a notice of commission and left, telling them where Shizhou lived. He said, "If you receive the money and divide it among the attendants, you need not wait for me. I am now going to Pingjiang." Shizhou then gave the money to the three monks. Two nights later, the one who was glad had another dream about the Wang family, saying, "I am now sitting in a lotus basin, and I will not return." Longrui Palace is located at the foot of Kuaiji Mountain. Prince Qiong suspected that he was the deity. Zhang Luncai’s father was the son-in-law of Prince Wang. He once saw the writings on the study room’s wall. (The following text is missing three characters and fourteen lines.)

The Records of Xingyuan are incomplete. Missing text within. A Daoist from贺州 is missing. Missing text within. Yang Daming Yang Daming, a resident of Nankang County, Nan'an Army, buried his father at the foot of Huanggong Pit Mountain. He built a hut by the side of the grave. His white chicken was caught by a fox, which he then hid in a stone cave. The next night, there was a great thunderstorm. The stone shattered, and the fox died. People believed this was a manifestation of filial piety. A Daoist came to the hut and saw this. He praised Yang Daming's pure filial piety and pointed to a Daoist robe on the shelf, saying, "Give this to me, and I will have something to repay you." Yang Daming gave it to him without hesitation. The Daoist unbuckled a small gourd from his waist and stored the robe inside. The mouth of the gourd was very narrow, yet the clothes fit without hindrance. Soon, he took a small black stone from the table, caressed it, and breathed on it for a long time. Then it turned into purple gold. He then transformed medicine powder into pills and gave them to Yang Daming. Yang Daming thanked him, saying, "I live in poverty and am currently in mourning for my father. I dare not hope for wealth and longevity." The Daoist found him even more remarkable.

A man retrieved a robe from a gourd and returned it to him. He said, "I just wanted to test you." He then wrote a poem on the eaves, saying: "Yang Jun is indeed a man of principle. His filial piety moves heaven and earth. The emperor pities his hardships. On the Qixi Festival, he sent him back. He hesitated to medicate a stubborn stone. He left a son as a gift. If you do not accept, I will not force you. The dust and wind are hard to stay long. I hope your resolve does not waver. When the time comes, remember the moment of hesitation. Achieve your lofty goals with ease. He then departed. The villagers who heard of this gathered to watch. The place where the poem was written was several zhang above the ground. It was first written in light ink, but then the ink color became bright and the characters flew and moved. All suspected that the person was a celestial being. It was the thirteenth year of the Shaoxing era. The village official reported the matter to the county magistrate. The county magistrate, Li Nengyi, informed the prefectural governor, who in turn reported it to the imperial court. The following year, the emperor granted ten bolts of silk. He ordered the local officials to visit him periodically. The matter is recorded in the *Diary of the Emperor's Daily Activities*.

Ding Ruoxu A man from Qiantang named Liu Shi, whose courtesy name was Ruoxu, had spent many years taking the imperial exams without success. In the fifth year of the Shaoxing era, he took the provincial examination and stayed in a monastery on North Mountain. His servant, Wang Gao, who had served him faithfully for many years, knocked on the door at night and called out, "I just had a dream that the list of successful candidates would be posted tomorrow. Fan Guangyuan would be the top candidate, and Liu Ruoxu would come second. It was clear in the dream. You must be selected highly." Liu Ruoxu was also delighted. As expected, the list was posted, and Fan Guangyuan topped the list, but Liu Ruoxu was rejected. Wise people analyzed his dream and said, "Ruoxu is Liu's name. The list did not mention Liu Shi, but mentioned Liu Ruoxu. It was a sign of no fame." Seven years later, he finally passed the imperial examination through a special list and was appointed as an assistant teacher in the fifth rank. He declined the appointment. When Empress Xianren returned from the north, Liu Ruoxu and his fellow examinee Shen Lianggong both offered praises. An imperial edict allowed him to take up an official post, and he was transferred to serve as the local official in Taihe, Jizhou. His family had someone skilled in the Five Elements, who said Liu had no sign of holding an official position. A year later, the time for his official appointment arrived, and the county sent one official to welcome him. Liu was a scholar, and he was already very happy and full of hope. He held a banquet and invited his family members, asking them, "You always said I would not hold an official position. Now the official has come." His family members only apologized and expressed regret. After the banquet, he returned home and again calculated and speculated. He privately told people, "If Liu Ruoxu obtained an official position, I will no longer talk about fate." He died the day before he was supposed to start his official duties. As told by Ling Jiwén.

**混沌灯** Lu Nongshi, the Left Chancellor of Kuaiji, had a young son named Bao. They resided in Wuxi County. Lu Nongshi invited the respected scholar Mr. Chen to tutor his sons. The youngest son, just six years old, was naturally intelligent and had already achieved much. Upon entering school, he immediately approached Mr. Chen, asking to practice composing paired phrases. Mr. Chen gave him two- and three-character phrases to compose. The boy smiled and said, "That’s not enough; I can handle five-character phrases." He then wrote, "The egret roosts on the sandbank under the moon." Mr. Chen responded, "The crow flaps its wings in the wind at the treetop." Next, Mr. Chen asked him to compose a phrase for "the dense frost and the formation of geese in the cold." The boy replied, "The waning moon and the rooster’s crow at dawn." Whenever he spoke, he astonished everyone, yet he did so effortlessly. His parents were delighted, believing that their son would grow up to surpass his father. The following year, on the eighth day of the first lunar month, Lu Nongshi ordered his servant to buy large bamboo to make a lampshade. He covered it with black paper and hung it beside his desk. When asked what it was, he said, "This is called the Chaos Lamp." The next day, he pierced one hole in it. He continued this for seven days, and on the fifteenth day, all seven holes were completed. That very night, the boy passed away. As the saying goes, "Each day a hole was pierced, and on the seventh day, Chaos died." How strange! This was recounted by Chen Fuqing, who was the son of Mr. Chen.

**The Temple of Wang the Straightforward Official** Wang Chun of Fuzhou, whose courtesy name was Lianggeng, served as the magistrate of Chong'an County in Jianzhou in his capacity as a Tongzhi Lang (Assistant Official). While handling official affairs, he had not yet finished eating a steamed pancake when he suddenly rushed home and fell to the ground, dying immediately. Two days after his death, monks were chanting in the hall when suddenly a maid from Wang’s household opened her eyes and scolded the monks, saying, “All of you, go out. I have something to say.” Her manner and voice were no different from Wang Lianggeng’s. She then sat on the couch and sent a minor clerk to summon the county magistrate, the recorder, and the county constable. When they arrived, the recorder also came. The maid was filled with fury and ordered her attendants to seize the clerk and bring him down. She beat him a hundred times with a rod and said to the county officials, “This is the one who killed me. I have the power to kill him, but because he is involved in something strange, I entrust this to you.” She continued, “Several days before my death, I discovered one of his serious crimes. I confronted him and said, ‘I will investigate you thoroughly.’ He was angry and afraid, so he bribed the cook to poison me. The day before, after eating half the pancake, I realized something was wrong. In a panic, I rushed home to tell my wife and children, but before I could finish speaking, I died.” Fortunately, the coffin was opened and examined, and the truth became evident. The county magistrate and his subordinates wept, then called in a craftsman to open the coffin. The entire body had rotted and turned to black liquid. Only then did they interrogate the clerk, who knelt and confessed. Along with the cook, he was sent to the prefectural government. The authorities, finding no clear name or identity, did not wish to carry out a formal punishment, so they secretly executed him in prison. The county magistrate then ordered a temple to be built in honor of Wang, calling it the Temple of Wang the Straightforward Official. This account is recorded by Wang Jia.

**Book of Yi Jian, Volume 41, Section 41** **Dreaming of Climbing a Black Ladder** Yu Shunkai, a man from Huizhou, took the provincial examination in the eighteenth year of the Shaoxing era. In his dream, he saw two ladders, one red and one black, leaning against the eaves. Someone urged him to climb the red ladder, but Yu looked at the steep steps and, citing his weak feet, declined. Instead, he climbed the black ladder and reached its summit before waking up. That year, one person was admitted through the special list, and Chu Zhi, also from Huizhou, was among them. **Zhang Wen Gui** Zhang Wen Gui, courtesy name Zhengfu, was from Gaoan. He entered officialdom through the special list and was later transferred to serve as a judicial officer in Yingzhou. In Zhenyang County, several villagers, including Zhang Wu, stole a cow. Local residents Hu Da, Zhu Gui, Zhang Yun, and Zhang Zhousun led a group to pursue and capture them. The thieves scattered, but Zhang Wu resisted and was killed by Hu Da, who then took the cow. The thieves, unable to achieve their goal, falsely reported being robbed to the county magistrate. The magistrate, Wu Miao, eager for credit, arrested all twelve of them and charged them with armed robbery and murder. After intense interrogation, they all confessed to the crimes, and two of them, Zhu Gui and Zhang Yun, died in prison. The case was then sent to the judicial office, where Zhang Wen Gui examined the prisoners' statements and found them suspicious. Through further questioning, he uncovered the truth and captured accomplices to corroborate the evidence. The case was resolved, and Hu Da was sentenced to flogging on the back, while the others received lighter punishments. Zhu Gui and Zhang Yun were exonerated. This occurred in the seventh year of the Yuanyou era. Wu Miao's plan failed, and he returned to Fanyu in anger, where he died of illness. Zhang Wen Gui cleared the wrongful convictions of ten people and was entitled to a higher rank in the capital, but the local governor, Fang Xijue, did not recommend him for promotion due to his lack of connections.

But the person who was promoted to the position of Chief Clerk of Linchuan took office in the fourth year of the Shaoxing era. In the following summer, on the day Gui Mao of the fourth month, he became ill due to the stress of examining a corpse and became severely weakened. He did not drink even a single spoonful for a month, and he was unconscious and unaware of his surroundings. His family gathered around him, weeping as they waited for him to die. On the day Xinwei of the fifth month, he suddenly made a faint sound, asked for water to drink, and his body gradually began to move. He loudly said, "Quickly send someone to fetch the luggage from the ship." By midnight, his spirit and energy finally stabilized. He then said that while he was sick in bed, he heard someone calling out, "Documents from Yingzhou were issued." He immediately went out to see it. There were three or four officials who said, "We are here to take official statements." He told them that he was very ill, too weak to walk, and did not have official attire. The official said, "There is official attire there. It has already been prepared at the dock." He had no choice but to go with them. In a short while, they arrived in Yingzhou. He saw the townspeople and their activities, everything looking as it did before, except for the tavern in the market, which was missing. He asked those around him, and they said, "It was burned down." The official stopped him and told him to wait for the official documents. He returned shortly. He asked what kind of documents they were, and they said, "It is the case of Wu Miao solving Hu Da's case." He thought that Wu Miao had been dead for a long time and wondered why they were pursuing him. He then realized that he was dead.

Proceeding forward a little, we entered a grand official residence. The gatehouse and corridors were imposing and severe, with guards holding spears and halberds standing in strict formation. There were about ten companions with us. As we were about to enter, a guard brought clothing and a cap and handed them to us, which we put on before entering. Someone then warned us, saying, "Do not drink any water that is brought to you. If you drink, you will not be able to return." Proceeding further to another gate, the guards became even more numerous and imposing, with several dozen strong men all holding axes and halberds. Indeed, someone brought water, and all the companions drank it. I declined by saying I was not thirsty. Then, they brought tea instead, and I declined again. The person became angry and said, "Why is it so difficult to serve?" We continued forward, and those who were following us entered the gate first. They led us all inside. The palace buildings and towers were adorned with gold and jade, shining brightly. A curtain hung down from the hall, and we were all too afraid to look up. I secretly asked who was on the dais behind the curtain, and was told, "It is a king." Soon, a command was issued to bring us forward, and immediately, they seized and led away the others. Finally, they reached me. I heard that the person inside the curtain asked about Wu Miao's case, and I answered truthfully one by one. The king said, "I also know the details, but it is necessary for you to come to confirm it, to ensure the truth." I replied, "I have personally investigated this case and saved ten people from death, but I alone have not received any reward from the court."

I dare to ask for the explanation. The king replied, "The position of County Magistrate in Linchuan is a reward for merit." I said, "If we follow the reward criteria, it should be promoted to an official position. But now, only the recommendation system based on seniority is used." The king said, "How could two recommenders suddenly receive the position of County Magistrate in a large county?" When I first received two recommendation letters, after going to the Ministry, the Guangdong Censor Wang Pengnian was no longer usable. I did not expect the underworld to know it so precisely. Then he presented a petition saying, "Official positions have their predetermined roles. I wish to extend my lifespan slightly through minor merit." Immediately, I heard the emperor call for the records. The official loudly said, "Has already been judged by the king." Then I saw the document come out from behind the curtain and was handed down to the guards, who led me to the office. From afar, I saw Wu Miao wearing shackles behind the curtain, and Zhu Gui and Zhang Yun standing beside him. I wanted to borrow a book to read, but the guard did not allow it, saying, "You will see it at the office." He pointed to the office clerk and said, "This is a candidate from Pu Prefecture. He conducts himself uprightly but failed the legal examination. Therefore, he died and was judged here." At the office, I bowed to the clerk and asked about the judgment. The clerk showed the paper, which had added "one year" at the end. I pretended not to understand and asked the clerk, who said, "You are a well-learned old scholar."

Do you not understand its meaning? One cycle is twelve years. You have the merit of saving ten people from snow. Therefore, the king has rewarded you with this. This person rarely has any business. He happened to be in the king's presence and heard your response. The king was very pleased. The Supreme Deity favors life and abhors killing. The *Book of Documents* says, "Rather than kill an innocent person, it is better to let a guilty one go free." It also says, "The virtue of favoring life is pleasing to the people's hearts." I cited dozens of such examples, but cannot remember them all. I calmly said to him, "Are you originally from Puzhou?" The official was startled and asked, "How do you know this?" I smiled and said, "Have you ever heard of the great bell of Puzhou in your life?" People in the capital city joke. There is a bell in Puzhou. The official looked displeased and said, "This is not a matter of jest. Do not speak lightly." Then he led him out to the palace steps. He knocked on the curtain and finished his report. The official raised his hand and ordered him to step back. I then stepped forward and said, "I just received a judgment to increase my age by one cycle. I am now sixty-seven. Calculating the amount added, it should reach seventy-nine. But my father's lifespan was only seventy-eight. Is there a son who lives longer than his father?" The king said, "Not so. The length of a person's life depends on what they cultivate. Even though father and son are close, they are not necessarily the same."

He then bowed and expressed his gratitude before leaving. Below the corridor was a large gate, guarded with strict vigilance. An official said, "This is the main gate of the prison." Within, there were various prisons. All forms of corruption, lust, murder, and harsh laws; slandering loyal and virtuous people; destroying good people—regardless of rank, status, or duration—all suffer punishment here. Wishing to enter and observe was not allowed. Seeing a monk inside holding a tray, the official said, "This is the monk who guides souls." All human souls are guided by this monk. Along the corridor were railings, like what counties call "sandboxes." There were many prisoners there. A girl around seventeen or eighteen years old called out, "Hearing that the official will return to Fuzhou, please convey to Governor Xu Zhaosan that Twelve-Niang has not yet attained heaven. I wish to perform good deeds to save myself. Governor Xu will also be able to recommend officials. I silently thought that Governor Xu's recommendations for this year have been exhausted. How can he reach me?" Suddenly, I heard that Zhang Wengu was called to communicate with the prisoner. He was driven to the king's presence. The king asked him, and he told the truth. The king said, "Can you speak on my behalf? There is no obstacle in reason. There will be mutual benefit." I then proceeded, afraid of forgetting the girl's words. I went to the office and borrowed a pen from an official.

A person wrote twelve characters on his arm, then hurriedly rushed out. He saw Yuan's pursuers leading him onto a boat. They traveled to a city, which turned out to be Nanxiong Prefecture. A person in yellow clothing came to report that Magistrate Fang had died, and they had pursued him to this point. It is said that Governor Fang Xijue of Ying, who was seen as the magistrate of Jiangxi's regular grain supply, was still believed to be in Ying at that time. He did not guarantee to report on the investigation of the case, and a low-ranking officer falsely spoke to please him for gain. When asked where he was, the officer said, "He is at a certain place." They went to look for him but did not find him. They reboarded the boat and immediately reached the shore. The senders pushed the boat away, and he then woke up. Looking at the twelve characters on his arm, they seemed faintly present. At the time, he had been ill for a month, and the flesh between his waist and hips had rotted, exposing the bones. A skilled doctor applied cinnabar powder to it, and the muscles immediately regenerated. Xu Chaosan, the governor of Linchuan, was Xu Zhongfu. Shier Nü, the daughter of his elder brother, heard of the matter and recited Buddhist scriptures, feeding the monks to recommend him. As for Fang Xijue, he died after being disciplined by a written warning, when his breath was not yet extinguished, but his spirit had already departed. The written warning was issued for several hundred days, and the canal office believed he was not competent, issuing an edict to the prefect to investigate, with the intention of dismissing him. Xu the prefect presented the facts to prove his competence.

The illness has healed, and Wen Gui is now capable of resuming his duties. He then realized the woman's meaning regarding recommendations and the king's words about mutual benefits. Later, a guest came from England, saying that the market building had indeed been destroyed by fire. The following year, Wen Gui retired as a Tongzhi Lang. By the second year of the Daguang era, at the age of seventy-eight, he dreamed of a man in a feathered robe who said, "A decade has been added to your life, and now the count is complete." The Lord of Yin, because Wen Gui had served as a temporary judge in Yingzhou, had sentenced the woman Cao to death for her original crime, but to preserve her head, he decided to sentence her to death. After leaving office, the Ministry of Justice questioned and found it to be an error in sentencing, but coincidentally, the matter occurred before the amnesty, and Cao had already died, so there was nothing to rectify. Only a stamped document needed to be signed. He died in the fourth year of the Zhenghe era, at the age of eighty-three. Examining his rebirth and dreams, it added a total of a decade and a half, which should have been eighteen years, but only sixteen years passed. This was because from the year he returned to life until the time he had the dream, totaling a decade, and from the year of the dream to his final year, another half-decade. The person from Linchuan, Wu Ke, once wrote a biography, which Wen Gui's grandson Ping transmitted.

Xu Yi, also known as Yan Zhou, was from Gongzhou Xiangyi. In the year 1125 during the reign of Emperor Huizong, he visited his relative Zheng Heshu in the northern part of the city and stayed there. That night, he dreamed of walking on a main road, with cold sand up to his ankles. Alongside the road were mounds, tombs, and thorny bushes. A woman wearing black clothes with white skirts was walking in the fields. She said, "There is no sand here, making it easier to walk." Xu Yi followed her, but his feet were weak and he could not climb. The woman helped him up by pulling his hand. The moon was very bright, and there were no trees. All that could be seen were wild fields, and the wheat was lush and green. The woman led him to lie down on the grass. In a low place, there was a small platform with paper and ink. Xu Yi wrote a poem on it: Idle flowers and wild grass in spring, Migratory geese and social swallows return year after year. Dew falls from the blue sky, wetting the wheat seedlings. The ancient road is cold under the moon, with few human tracks. As he hit the pen on the platform, a sound startled him awake. The dream was still vivid in his mind. He suspected that it resembled a tomb area, and it was an ominous event. That year, he fell seriously ill and nearly died.

The Servant of the Leftover Prince During the reign of Emperor Xiaozong in the Southern Song Dynasty, when the year was 1158, the Vice Minister Shen Chuan Yao, alias Zhaoyuan, was transferred from Jiangxi to command Hunan. On his journey, he passed through Yizhou, where an innkeeper ordered a young servant to run errands for him. The servant was about thirteen or fourteen years old, with a calm and composed demeanor, lacking the rustic or childish behavior typically associated with village children. Shen Chuan Yao was pleased and asked him, "Would you be willing to follow me?" The servant replied, "I would be very happy to." He then asked to return home and inform his parents. Shortly after, he returned and joined Shen Chuan Yao, accompanying him westward. In the household, the servant was extremely careful and orderly, anticipating his master's needs and performing all tasks with precision. The entire family loved him. After six months in Tan, the servant suddenly requested to leave. Shen Chuan Yao said, "You are just becoming familiar with this place. Perhaps you should stay for a while longer." The servant replied, "I have a specific duty. I must leave when the time appointed by the Leftover Prince comes." Shen Chuan Yao was surprised by his words and asked what his duty was. The servant replied, "I am currently serving as the servant of the Leftover Prince. My duty is to manage the shoes and footwear of people. Whether new or old, all shoes are recorded meticulously. For example, all the shoes that Vice Minister Shen Chuan Yao wears daily are recorded without omission. I took out the ledger from my sleeve and showed several dozen pairs of old shoes, then bowed twice and left. Shen Chuan Yao was initially surprised and realized that the servant was not human. A few days later, Shen Chuan Yao passed away. This story was recounted by Zhang Shi, alias Qin Fu.

A Veteran of Luzhou Mr. An Zhi, a high-ranking official, died tragically due to matters in Huai West. People in Luzhou sometimes claim to have seen him. To this day, the main hall remains vacant and unoccupied. In the 26th year of the Shaoxing era, Wu Kui became the prefect of Luzhou. During the spring, his family wished to go out for a spring outing. There was a Daoist temple outside the city, which had been a traditional site for spring outings and banquets. Wu Kui prepared his servants and set out early in the morning. It was just before dawn when the night-duty veteran soldier woke up and saw that there were already lights and candles in the hall. He peered through the curtains and saw Mr. An sitting at his desk handling affairs, with officials and clerks lined up to serve him. The usher held up a guest card and announced, "Certain officials are passing through the hall." Mr. An stood up to greet them, and several guests bowed respectfully and took their seats. The etiquette between host and guests was no different from what the prefect usually showed to his subordinates. After the guests left, Mr. An turned around and saw the veteran soldier. He ordered him to be called out and said, "If you don't show respect, how dare you peep secretly?" He then ordered five men to beat him twenty times with a stick. The veteran soldier bowed and thanked him, then got up. He saw nothing. In the morning, he looked at his wounds and found that he had indeed been beaten. It took several months to heal.

**The Dream of Zhang Yü** Zhang Jin, courtesy name Ziwen, in the year Wu Chen of the Shaoxing era. After completing his term as the recorder and judicial officer in Changzhou, he went to the Ministry of Personnel for evaluation. In the same lodging was Zhang Yü, an official from Jiankang, who had recently been dismissed from his post as a subordinate official in Kuilu. There were also five officials recommended for promotion, but one of them had previously been implicated in a crime, and the Ministry of Personnel had doubts about the recommendation. The matter had just been submitted to the higher authorities for approval, and the decision had not yet been made. Yü was deeply troubled by this and nearly lost his sleep and appetite. Suddenly, he saw Jin arrive, verified his name, and was greatly pleased. The lodging official asked why he was so happy. He replied, "In the past, I went to Jiangshan Mountain to visit Master Bao and asked for a dream. The deity told me, 'There will be water by your side, and you will be promoted.' When I awoke, I consulted dream interpreters, but none could understand it. Now I have met a relative, and his name is Jin. The character for 'Yü' contains the character for 'water,' and 'Jin' is the character for 'water.' The deity has already told me. This is why I am so happy." At that time, Chancellor Qin was in power, and because of Yü’s hometown connection, he expedited the matter. Just on that day, the reply was received, and the two were summoned together for an audience. Jin was scheduled to be third, and Yü was placed fourth. However, the military office mistakenly swapped their positions. When they arrived at the hall, Yü stood above Jin, exactly fulfilling the omen of "water by one’s side." Ziwen said.

**Great Dagu Dragon** Deputy Minister Guo Sanyi was on his way to Changsha. As he passed by the foot of Dagu Mountain, the sky was clear and there was no wind. The river water was clear and cold. When the boat reached the middle of the river, it stood still as if something was holding it. The boatmen dived into the water and looked around but found nothing. Suddenly, they saw a child on the steering pole, about five inches long, with all body parts intact. With two legs hanging down, sitting on the steering pole, the steering pole became unstable. When they looked up at the person, it did not change color. They quickly reported it to Guo. Guo ordered clothes, hats, incense, and wine, and prayed to it. After a while, it transformed into a long snake, with its head raised, it entered the water. The boat then could move.

**Zhang Ji's Wife** Zhang Xuanyi, from Poyang, had a wife named Wang Shi. She was pregnant for twelve months. Before the child was born, Zhang Ji died. Wang Shi wept, and within a few days, the fetus disappeared. She felt no pain or discomfort. According to Lu Heng's account. **Zhao Shizao** Zhao Shizao, during the Shaoxing era, served as a temporary tax official of the southeast route of Guangdong. After his term ended, he traveled with his fellow officials, Liu Ling and Sun Wei, to Lin'an by boat. Zhao Shizao brought his wife and children, a total of six people. They first arrived at the Guangli Wang Temple. The boatman said that according to custom, they should prepare sacrificial animals and wine to offer and pay respects. Zhao Shizao wanted to go, but Liu Ling and Sun Wei disagreed. That night, Zhao Shizao dreamed that he and the two others entered the temple. The god of the temple was very angry and scolded them, saying, "You are scholars and officials. You should know how to act appropriately. Generally speaking, when passing through a city or town, one should show respect to the local deities. Now you are sailing across the vast sea and being arrogant by not paying respects to me. Is this proper conduct?" Zhao Shizao explained his initial intention to pay respects. The god forgave them and ordered the execution of the two others. Soon after, a servant brought two pig heads in a silver tray, which were covered in blood. Zhao Shizao woke up in a state of shock. He saw that Liu Ling and Sun Wei had also woken up and were sitting up, looking terrified. They told each other about the dream. It turned out that Liu Ling and Sun Wei were both born in the year of the Pig. The next day, the three of them went to the temple to pay respects and apologize. Zhao Shizao alone prayed to the god, asking for guidance on whether to stay or leave. The divination using turtle shells indicated a favorable outcome. They then returned to their boat. That night, Liu Ling and Sun Wei shared a bed. A snake as thick as a chopstick passed through Sun Wei's belly from around midnight until nearly dawn.
