In the morning, Zao looked down and saw a creature coiled and twisted, like a rope hundreds of feet long. It remained for half a day, then disappeared. All were greatly alarmed, but preparations for departure were already complete. There was no time to stop. That evening, flames in the sea flashed like lightning. The sailors were greatly afraid and hastily entered a small harbor. A huge wave followed, and in a moment, the boat was submerged. Zao stood near the side of the boat. The guard carried him onto a small boat. He took out his dagger and cut the ropes. He barely made it to the shore and entered a temple. He said to the monk, "I have no attachment to other things, but I mourn the loss of my official documents and my wife and concubines. That is truly painful." There was a strong man who claimed he could enter the water without getting wet. They offered him a generous reward and sent him. At that time, the boat had already sunk, but the mast and sails could still be recognized. The man took two hours to emerge, and he was dazed and didn't know what was happening. After a long time, he could speak and said, "I encountered a huge black dragon whose head and tail were not visible. Its body filled the boat. There was no space to enter. I trembled and emerged, almost getting swallowed." Zao stood by the water and wept bitterly. The next day, the waves calmed. At the place where the boat had sunk, he found his official documents and a bundle of Lingling incense. He then returned to the prefecture. At first, Zao, a guest traveling, gained a position and accumulated wealth through bribery. He first married and bought concubines. By then, he was again alone and poor, no different from the beginning. He then sold the remaining incense and traveled by land back to Zhejiang.

Two Scholars from Yueqing Yueqing County in Wenzhou was divided into two parts, known as West Yueqing and East Yueqing. Scholar Jia Ruyu lived in East Yueqing. He took the provincial examination and dreamed that his name was posted on a list of successful candidates on a building, reading "Chen Qi." Jia Ruyu could not understand the meaning and told a local man named Xie Quanfu. Xie said, "You will surely be selected. 'Chen Qi' means that you are from East Yueqing, and you are the seventh in line. It is clear that you will be 'Chen Qi.'" The next day, the results were announced, and he was indeed selected. During the year of Bingyin in the Shaoxing era, Wang Guiling, along with his younger brother, took a supplementary exam at the Imperial Academy and stayed at the Nine-Bend Temple by the lake. He was very anxious about passing or failing. Suddenly, he dreamed that his name was on the list as "Wang Er." Upon waking, he thought his younger brother would be selected. His brother said, "Wang Er" means that you will be the second place. Indeed, it turned out to be true. In the year of Jia Xu, Wang Guiling took the provincial examination. He had no dreams or omens beforehand. Once, while walking alone by the window, he saw a pile of old paper. He silently prayed for a sign and picked up a half-sheet of torn paper at random. The paper resembled a divination using the five elements, but all the characters were erased. Only the characters "Ding Chou" could be read. That year was not favorable for him. However, in the year of Ding Chou, he became the top candidate in the imperial examination. Wang Guiling said so.

The Temple of the Dead and the Cookies In the spring of the first year of the Jingkang era, the capital Kaifeng was besieged. Yao Shunming, a censor, had a son named Hong who wished to return to Yue. Leaving through the South Wind Gate, he secured a boat and intended to re-enter the city. Just as there was an order prohibiting entry through any of the gates, Hong was at a loss. He led two of his close friends and traveled east by land. Following the Bian River for several days, they arrived late in the evening at a small temple beside the road. The monks were all absent, and the temple rooms contained many shrines for the dead. The three men could not proceed, so they decided to stay overnight. They sent their servant to a village store to buy wine and also brought back pork. The temple kitchen had not been used for a long time, and the utensils were missing, so although they had meat, they could not prepare it. One of the scholars laughed and said, "I have a plan." He placed the meat on a coffin, sliced it finely to make a stew, and read the inscription on the coffin, which indicated it belonged to a woman. The scholar joked, "In the middle of the night, when it is quiet and empty, you can come to me." The three men became drunk and slept past midnight. The scholar suddenly woke up, vomiting profusely. His mind was hazy, and when morning came, he saw that what he had vomited were all cookies and cakes, though he had not eaten any the previous night. He claimed that while he was sleeping soundly, a beautiful woman came to him, and they drank together. She fed him cookies, and he went to the coffin to look for her. It turned out that the deceased's family had arranged cookies as offerings, and the entire plate was full of broken pieces.

Section 52 of the Yi Branch of the Yi Jia Collection The God of Life and Death Yu Si, styled Zhao Zu, was from Luo Yuan, Fuzhou. He held the rank of Chaosan Lang. In the eighteenth year of the Shaoxing era, he lived in his hometown and had a close friendship with Xue Zhilao, the governor of Fuzhou, as they were both successful in the imperial examination in the same year. Yu Si requested to travel to the capital with a silver convoy to seek imperial favor and hoped for a promotion based on his years of service. The following year, due to the imperial sacrifice, he received the privilege to recommend his son for an official position. On the fifth day of the ninth month, he arrived in the prefecture and stayed with his relative Lin. On the nineteenth day, he went to Dazhong Temple and had a drink at the home of his cousin Han Zhigang Jie. By the time he returned, it was already past midnight, and he was very tired and went to bed. The moonlight was very bright, and it felt like a dream. He saw a person push open the door and enter, dressed in a Daoist robe and wearing a small cap, holding a banner. The person stood in front of the bed and called out, "The God of Life and Death summons you." Yu Si asked for the warrant and order, and the person replied, "I have received a strict imperial order directly, and there are no written documents." Yu Si immediately got up, put on a purple narrow-sleeved robe, tied his belt, and went out. When he looked back at himself, he saw that his bed was still as it was. He sighed and said, "I must die! How can I manage in a place of temporary lodging?" The person leading the way was always a few steps ahead. Yu Si wanted to speak to him but could not. As soon as they left the eastern gate, he realized that the path was not the one he usually took. Tall trees lined the road, and the shadows were dense and dark. The sunlight was dazzling, and it seemed like it was between the hours of Chen and Si. They traveled for several miles and did not encounter any people. Yu Si was very frightened. Suddenly, he saw a grand city ahead. Two people stood on either side of the gate, wearing soft headbands and tied belts, dressed in clothing reminiscent of the Tang dynasty. The person leading the way said, "I am an introducer from the God's court."

Wait here. It is now permissible to proceed. The two envoys bowed and entered the gate. Inside the gate was a pavilion, with abundant provisions and arrangements. A person adorned with a flowered cap and a coiled hairstyle, wearing a red silk robe. The descendant ascended the pavilion. The two envoys both sat down, not exchanging a single word. They drank soup and then withdrew. They were again led in, walking about three or four li. The surroundings were resplendent with gold and jade, and the ground was paved with琉璃. The descendant privately rejoiced, knowing it was certainly not a bad place, and his worries were somewhat alleviated. They turned into a corner and entered a more magnificent and splendid building. The envoy said, "This is the place where the True Official of Fate conducts affairs." The descendant asked, "If we go there, what etiquette should we use to greet him?" The envoy replied, "You have no court attire. You should only perform a respectful bow. When summoned, you should immediately ascend the hall." Upon entering, they unveiled a golden plaque inscribed with "The Hall of the True Official of Fate." They proceeded according to the proper etiquette. They were then led up, and the descendant looked closely at the True Official's attire, which was similar to modern court attire. It was said to be a certain official from Yue Prefecture during the Jianyan period. The descendant did not wish to mention it. Some said it was the sacred action of Zhang Du. The envoy smiled and said to the descendant, "This year's examination here selected twenty people. Your name was seen, so we especially went to summon you." The descendant felt greatly alarmed and thanked, saying, "I hold a low position and lack ability. There is not a single merit to record. How could I possibly be included in the examination?" The True Official spoke sternly, "Here, one's status does not matter. One's rank does not matter. Only the correctness of one's thoughts is considered. We have an old acquaintance. I wish for you to know about your future. You will have all the necessary qualifications, but the longevity you will enjoy is only seventy-four years. If you can decline honors and accept a modest position."

One generation can be extended. From this point onward, by accumulating merit and good deeds, there will be further rewards and calculations. The number obtained will not be limited to this. Do you wish to pursue it? The heir replied, "How dare I not obey?" The True Official said, "Today, without reporting to the Heavenly Lord of the Celestial Court, I cannot summon you. However, you must not stay longer than three hours. You should return quickly." The two attendants led him out, and he then departed. Traveling along the original path, he passed through a palace gate and heard a cacophony of voices, with people moaning and weeping. The messenger said, "This is the True Lord of Record-Keeping Palace." The True Lord was seated on the throne, questioning prisoners. The heir asked, "What is the gravest sin in the human world?" The reply was, "Filial impiety is the greatest sin. Deception comes second, and killing living beings is third." Upon reaching the outer gate, a person wearing a flower crown emerged and clasped his hands, saying, "This official is truly extraordinary. I have been here for half a year and have seen many people enter, but none have ever left. This official is indeed extraordinary." He then bowed and invited the heir to sit and drink a soup before descending the steps. The messenger said, "Usually, one only reaches this point. Because of your connection with the True Official and your benevolence, you are being specially escorted today." After exiting, the heir asked, "Who was the person with the flower crown just now?" The reply was, "He is a person from the 33rd heaven, demoted to guard the gate for a minor offense. After one year, he will return." The heir asked, "What kind of soup did you drink?" The reply was, "When entering, it was nectar; when leaving, it was dew." The heir pleaded, "Now that I have returned safely, what advice do you have for me?" The reply was, "There are methods of warding off evil. When you return home, you should practice them."

Removing the peachwood charms from the door. Personally, using a sharp blade to cut them into pieces. Storing the cut pieces in a clean basket. At night, during the second watch, sending someone away from the house by one li. Digging a hole three chi deep in the southeast direction and burying the pieces. After the person leaves, sitting quietly and reciting a spell: "Heavenly Emperor, Earthly Emperor. The Three Bonds and Five Constants. Hastily as the imperial decree." Waiting until the person returns before stopping the spell. It is also said that upon returning home, one should eat separately and sleep on a different bed. Eating should involve offerings to ancestors, and sleeping should involve mental rest. All of these are essential for spiritual cultivation. The practitioner said, "This journey leaves me with no way to repay your kindness. What do you need?" The two messengers looked at each other and smiled, shaking their heads and saying, "There is nothing of value here." Firmly asking, they replied, "You should recite the Diamond Sutra daily and dedicate the merit to yourself or others, one or two scrolls being sufficient." The messenger who spoke was the only one to respond; the other remained silent. Walking another one or two li, they departed, saying, "There are no other paths; returning home is straightforward." The practitioner walked alone and, as if reaching the eastern gate of the city, fell asleep. It was already the third watch of the night, and it felt like daytime, as if he had been out during the day. He then called a servant to light a lamp and prepare a formal letter. Waiting until morning, he visited Xue Bai and mentioned his intention to retire. Returning home, he followed the instructions to perform the ritual, though he did not understand the reasoning behind it. Eventually, he resigned and received a new appointment the following year. He finally told others about the entire story and wrote it down. People believed that the practitioner would live a long life and enjoy continued good fortune. However, seven years later, he died, which was quite different from what he had dreamed.

In the thirty-second year of the Shaoxing era, Liu Zi'ang was appointed governor of Hezhou. When the chaos in the Huai region had just settled down, he traveled alone to take up his post. One day, he saw a beautiful woman entering and leaving the government office, and his mind was bewitched by her. He invited her and they became intimate, staying together for several months. Then, he went to the Tianqing Temple to pay respects. An old Taoist monk asked him, "You, sir, did not bring your family. Your complexion is pale and dark. There seems to be an ominous aura. What is it?" Liu initially hesitated to answer, but after the monk repeated his question several times, Liu finally admitted that he had taken a concubine. The monk said, "She is not a human. It will be difficult to deal with her. Here are two talismans for you. At night, hang them outside your door. She will not dare to enter." Liu took the talismans back. Not yet midnight, the woman arrived, angrily scolding, "We treated each other like husband and wife. What kind of Taoist monk are you, meddling in our affairs? If I leave, I will leave. Do not miss me." Liu could not bear to part with her, quickly got up, took the talismans, and destroyed them. He never understood why living beings would fear talismans. They resumed their intimate relationship as before. After several more days, the Taoist monk came to the office to inquire, saw Liu, was shocked and said, "You will not survive. What can be done? However, I will let you see her." He ordered dozens of buckets of water to be poured over the hall. In one corner, about five or six feet square, the water immediately dried up. They dug there and found a large body lying on the ground. It had no coffin or shroud. It was stiff but undamaged. Liu examined it and realized it was the woman he had been intimate with. He was greatly repulsed. Within ten days, he died. Wang Jiagou said. He got it from Han Jin's son, Ji Ming.

Dreams of Zitong The Zitong God's dreams are known for their miraculous nature. This has been recorded in earlier texts. Luo Yanguo from Chengdu failed repeatedly in the imperial examinations, even after his fourth attempt. He then observed a period of fasting and prayer to seek a dream. In his dream, Cai Luguang, a high-ranking official, told him, "I have already recommended you to be appointed as a Censor and Scholar." The following year, he failed the provincial examination again. However, due to his repeated attempts, he was granted the position of Literary Officer in Muzhou. Shixu's man, Shao Yundao, was recommended by his hometown in the seventh year of the Shaoxing era. He also sought a dream from the Zitong God. In his dream, the god told him, "I have arranged for you to rank first in the imperial examinations." When he took the examination, he indeed ranked first. He then had a stone record engraved in the west wing of the temple to commemorate this. Later, he was dismissed from his position as a secretary in Meizhou and went to Lin'an for an official assignment. While traveling by boat to Zhagou Town, he fell ill and died. It was only then that the meaning of the god's words about "arranging for you to rank first" became clear, as "first" in Chinese can also mean "gate." Wangren's Land Zhang Jiu from Guangdu managed the property of a fellow clansman. Not long after, the owner wanted to add more collateral and instructed an official broker to make a fraudulent contract, described as a "bone-breaking contract," to defraud Zhang Jiu. The next year, the owner came to sell the property again. Zhang Jiu then showed the earlier contract to the owner. The owner was silenced and unable to speak. Zhang Jiu slowly told him, "I wish your descendants will be as speechless as I am, unable to speak." The owner left in tears. That autumn, Zhang Jiu had a grandson who died without being able to speak. By winter, his son fell ill with a severe cold and lost his voice, eventually dying. Another year later, Zhang Jiu himself also met the same fate.

**The Retribution of Song Gu** Song Gu, a native of Chengdu, served as the county's *literati* official. A village elder fell ill and lay in a weakened state at the border of the district. It was the fourth year of the *Daguan* era (1107 CE), when the imperial court was enforcing the *Anji* policy, which required local officials to be responsible for the care of the sick. When a sick person was found, the *li zhang* (village head) was held accountable. Gu, fearing official notice, lured the elder to cross the Niuyin Bridge in Shuangliu County. He noticed the elder had something hidden in his robe, and upon searching, found over ten taels of silver. He took the silver and then pushed the elder off the bridge into the water. He warned his disciples not to speak of it. Not long after, Gu returned to the same spot and, losing his footing, fell into the water and drowned. His body was later found fifty li downstream, in the sandy riverbed, merged with the body of the sick elder, as if they were embracing. This account was reported by Wang Shi亨 and two other officials. **The Woman's Encounter with the Spirit World** Zhang Yuandao, the wife’s father-in-law, had once served as a *Shangbu Shilang* (Minister of War) but had since retired and was granted a *fengci* position, living in the Nan Chan Temple in Wuxi County. His second daughter had married Liang Yuanming and had come to visit her parents. On the seventh day of the first month in the *Jiwei* year of the *Shaoxing* era (1149 CE), she visited Huishan Temple. She ate a somewhat cold pancake, then returned home and suffered chest pain, which worsened through the night. While she was sleeping and had fallen off her pillow, Yuanming helped her sit up, but she only wept silently, pointing to her mouth and saying, "It cannot be spoken of or asked." When Yuanming asked what she had seen, she replied, "Zhang Wu is here." Zhang Wu was Yuandao’s uncle, who had died in battle, and later appeared as a spirit to his family, claiming to be an official messenger of the Taishan Temple. The family suspected that he was a malevolent spirit and summoned a Daoist priest from the Dongxu Temple to investigate. The priest burned incense and performed a ritual, asking the family to watch. All present saw a shadow on the paper, as if a man were wearing a *fuxiu* (a traditional headpiece). The priest said, "Then it must be a righteous spirit."

This is not a spirit. It must be a matter of the underworld's pursuit. Write a talisman and make her swallow it. At dawn, she began to recover slightly, still in pain, dazed and confused, only in the evening could she speak. When she first fell ill, someone held a talisman and came under the bed, saying the officials are pursuing you. The woman said, "I am a daughter of a gentleman's family. How can you call me without permission?" They replied, "It is like this in the world of the living. The underworld does not inquire about such things." Suddenly, she felt her body being taken by this person. Arriving at the back wall gate of the temple, about to go out, a person about ten feet tall pushed her in, scolding the pursuer, saying, "The young lady of Minister Zhang. Who are you to call her?" The pursuer did not answer. She was already outside the wall, with a very ornate canopy. She was made to climb onto it, carried by two people on their shoulders, traveling about hundreds of miles, crossing the Qiantang River again. After a long time, she entered a large hall. The red doors were shining brightly, with golden nails above, and nine rooms of the hall were all draped with curtains.

In the middle of the hall, the curtains were drawn back. The king, wearing a red robe and a green jade crown, sat on his throne. A messenger announced that official business had arrived. The king stood up straight and leaned on the desk. He asked, "Did Chancellor Zhang kill Zhao Zhe in Shaanxi? Was your father, who was a senior advisor, involved in this matter?" The daughter wanted to say she did not know, but feared it might implicate her father. She replied, "My father was not initially involved in the plot, but he did advise against it, only to be ignored." The king said, "If you advised against it and were ignored, why didn't you leave?" She answered, "I once requested a prefectural post, but my request was not granted." The king turned to his attendants and ordered them to go to the office to provide a statement. As she was answering, she saw a man in the western wing listening and laughing. There was also a man in the eastern wing, both of whom were tall and dignified in appearance. When they arrived at the office, the office clerk pointed out, "The laughing man is Zhao Zhe, and the one to the east is Qu Duan." The officials below were all tall, each about a zhang in height, wearing iron hats, brown cloth robes, and carrying writing tools.

The ghost was commanded to recount her experience. It was said that she should pursue her eldest son, but because of his lack of wisdom, he was spared. The eldest son of Yuan Dao was named Tong. He had been ill since childhood and was not mentally capable. Soon, he brought a very abundant dish of food. Someone said, "Do not eat it," for if she ate it, she would not be able to return. Under the eaves, there were many doors lined up, some inscribed with "Hell of Boiling Cauldrons" or "Hell of Millstones." There were many rooms, all locked, and no one was seen. She saw from afar the mother of her former in-laws, the Song family, who sat at a table and smiled at each other. Someone nearby said, "She was seen in the good department." Soon, the statement was completed, and the king ordered her to be released. There was no longer a carriage to ride, so she went alone with the pursuers. When they reached the riverbank, they saw a nobleman in official attire riding a horse, with a large retinue. Someone asked, "It is Mr. Lü, the prime minister." At that time, Prime Minister Lü Zhongmu was already ill, and a month later, he died. It seems that his spirit had already departed.

A Painting Student Wang Daoheng, from Pixian, Chengdu, showed exceptional talent in painting at the age of seven. His brushwork and intent were already surpassing those of others. During the Zhenghe era, the painting academy was established, and painters from across the empire were recruited and tested using the methods of the Imperial College. Wang Daoheng was the first to enter the examination, which used two lines of Tang poetry as the subject: "In a dream of butterflies, my home is ten thousand miles away; on the branch, the cuckoo sings under the moon at the third watch." Most of the other candidates produced rather shallow works, but Wang Daoheng depicted Su Wu, the Han envoy, tending sheep in the northern sea, wearing a fur coat and holding a ceremonial staff while sleeping. Above him, two butterflies danced. The painting captured the appearance of hardship and sorrow in the desert during wind and snow, with every detail fitting perfectly. He also painted luxuriant trees, with a cuckoo perched on a branch, the moon high at noon, the shadows of the trees on the ground, and pavilions, terraces, and towers, all barely visible. The painting fully captured the scene of one couplet, and he was chosen as the top candidate. The next day, the painting was presented to Emperor Huizong, who was amazed by it and appointed Wang Daoheng as the head of the painting academy. Another time, the academy used "In June, walking with a cane on a stony path, listening to the flowing water in the shade at noon" as the subject. Most of the other candidates painted tall trees overlooking a clear stream, with a guest sitting by the water. One artist, however, painted a long forest and deep gorges, a path through rocky steps, a person standing in the shade of the trees, listening intently, while the water was down in the mountains, though not seen. This work appropriately captured the meaning of listening to the flowing water and was also among the best.

Zhou Qiang, a man of virtue, served as the Judicial Officer of Qizhou. He used the post station as his office. In the evening, he took a walk outside the middle gate. Feeling slightly tired, he noticed a board leaning against the side of the hall. He asked someone to fetch it so he could sit on it. When he reached that place, there was nothing there. The back of his house was supported by the Wan Zhi Hall of the prefectural government. The hall had a pond. During the day, he saw a bald-headed person sitting facing the water. He scolded the person to get up. The person stood up with a startled look, his back like someone carrying a large jar. He jumped into the pond, making a sound like a drum. Those who knew about it thought it was the spirit of a turtle or crab. He also once went to Mount Lu, where he sat in a small room with Elder Gui Zong. He saw a person walking back and forth under the window, wearing a black headband. The person was just as tall as the window. He was surprised at how short he was. He went out to look at him but saw nothing. Zhou Qiang said.

Thieves in the Tree The family of Wang Shen Zhi lived by the river. Whenever they lost bowls, plates, bottles, and other items, as well as clothes, they would immediately blame the servants and maidservants, but they could not find the lost items. One night, a strong wind blew up, and a large soapberry tree on the east side of the house was blown down. The broken part was hollow, and all the items lost by the Wang family over the years were stored inside, half-damaged. The tree is still there today, according to Guo Die Jie's account. The Wife of Hu Si Hu Hu Si Hu, the son-in-law of Wang Shi from Fenni, Hongzhou, bought a concubine from the capital city. She was very beautiful. He took her back to his wife's home. His wife's mother said to others, "Hu Lang's concubine is indeed beautiful, but her voice can barely be heard. Ten sentences are only understandable in one or two. Her face is extremely stern and cold, and she rarely interacts with others. Her feet are extremely small, and they can be hidden in a skirt. She is not like an ordinary woman. If she stays for a long time, it is not good for the Hu family." Hu Si Hu heard this and suspected that his wife could not tolerate her, which is why his mother spoke so. He did not have the heart to part with her. When the concubine came, she was accompanied by a white dog, which followed her everywhere, eating and drinking without leaving her side. At night, she slept under the bed. After a year, the concubine entered the Buddha hall to pay respects and suddenly cried out for help. People went to see her, and it turned out that the dog had bitten off her arm. She lay in the blood and died. The dog also died shortly after. According to Li Shao Zu's account.

A Strange Monk's Talisman In the south of Yuzhang, about ten li from the city, there is a place called Shengmi Ferry. On the first day of the Ganzhi era in the year of the Rabbit, on the eighth day of the third month, a monk arrived early in the morning by crossing the river. He was about to disembark when he told the ferry official, "In a short while, you will see five people in yellow robes carrying baskets. Do not let them cross. If they do, strange calamities will follow." The monk took a brush and wrote three characters. They looked like a talisman but were not one, and they were completely unreadable. The characters were: (车足)□□. He handed the characters to the official and said, "You must not refuse them. Show these characters to them." After speaking, the monk left. The official did not fully believe him but found the event strange. At noon, as predicted, five people in yellow robes, resembling government messengers, arrived, each carrying two bamboo baskets. They boarded the boat directly. The official refused to let them board, and they all angrily cursed, almost starting a fight. The situation lasted for a long time without resolution. The official then took the written characters and showed them to the people. As soon as the five people saw the characters, they ran away in a disheveled manner and disappeared quickly. They left ten baskets on the shore. When the official opened the baskets, he found 500 small coffins inside. The official burned the coffins and spread the characters. Every household in Yuzhang drew and worshipped the characters. That year, there was a widespread epidemic in Jiangnan and Zhejiang, but this region remained peaceful. Knowledgeable people said that the five people were spirits of pestilence. I passed through Jiangzhou and Quzhou and heard different accounts from the locals, but I did not know which one was true. I added this story to my records.

Li Nanjin A scholar from LePing named Li Nanjin passed the imperial examination in the 27th year of the Shaoxing era (1157 AD). After the announcement of the successful candidates, he returned to his inn. That night, he dreamed of two women holding plates and singing songs to accompany the wine. They said, "You are like a willow in the garden; how long can you remain green?" "The golden spring light is still good. Enjoy leisurely feelings with fine wine. In future years, when you return, you will find a place in Strong Mountain; a pillow of morning mist will be clear." Upon waking, he remembered their words but did not know what Strong Mountain was. After being assigned an official position, he was appointed as a professor in Guanghua Army but did not take up the post. He came to visit Li Zhi, the supervisor of mining and smelting, and presented a newly discovered iron mountain. He supervised the workers himself to smelt the iron. One day, he saw a giant snake raise its head towards the furnace as if it had something to say. Li warned the miners not to harm it, and then killed it. Later, another snake as large as a pillar came to the smelting site, followed by thousands of small snakes. They formed a large cluster. The giant snake leaped up to a height of over ten feet. Li still ordered his servant to beat it with a stick, but the servant dared not approach. Then, he sent someone back home to fetch an imperial edict, placing it on the ground. The snake walked straight past without looking back. Li was greatly frightened and immediately felt unwell. He then returned home. Before this, his family had a dream where an old woman came to look for Professor Li, saying, "You wrongfully killed my son." And then realized that he could not recover. He died after several days.

### Events from the *Yijian Yi Zhi* Volume 63 #### The Woman in the Stone Coffin In the early years of the Shaoxing era, Lin Shi, the magistrate of Jiangling County, was pursuing bandits and came to a village. There, he saw farmers digging the ground and found a stone coffin without any cracks. He called for craftsmen to open it. Inside was a woman who was more than three feet tall, with closed eyes and naked, her appearance and complexion red and moist as if she were alive. She held two swords in her hands and had one sword in her mouth. Lin Shi immediately wrapped her in an oil-soaked umbrella and buried her. He did not know what kind of being she was. #### The Case in Yizhou Xiang Dangzhang, a long-time official, served as the judicial officer of Yizhou during the Yuanfu period. He was testing the military district of Nan'an and was accompanied by the magistrate of Xinchang County, Huang, and a judge from another state, Zheng. After completing their tasks and preparing to return, Zheng had a younger sister who was married to an official in Yichun Prefecture. Zheng invited Xiang to go together, and Huang reluctantly agreed. Zheng said jokingly, "You can quickly forget your feelings for someone in the entertainment world." Huang had no choice but to agree. However, he was not happy in his heart. Upon arrival, Huang wanted to stop outside the city, but Xiang insisted on going into the government residence. After sitting down, Xiang went to visit his parents, and Huang was invited to a private room. Huang seemed not to hear, and when called, he stared blankly without responding. Soon, Xiang pointed to a copper tray and asked, "What is the price of this? Can we stop buying it?"

When I first heard his words, I was quite pleased. I summoned a minor attendant and sent him to the place where he was staying to ask him. He replied, "This is but a common thing. Why such haste?" I said, "He is going to place it in my coffin." At this, I began to feel doubt and fear. I took his hand and tried to make him rest a little, but he did not move. I urgently summoned Mr. Zheng to view it together. I supported him to the bed. After a while, he let out a loud cry, as if the pain was unbearable. Then he began to discharge blood and excrement, filling the room with filth. He climbed onto the bed and then descended again, crying out all night without stopping. I and Mr. Zheng then said to him, "Your illness is very severe. Why not leave something behind for us?" He nodded and said, "I wish to see my mother and wife." That very day, I wrote a letter and hurried to Xinchang to inform his family. I then told them, "Originally, he did not wish to come, only because of the two of us. Now, in his condition, if your esteemed wife is unable to come, and he should pass away, it would be the two of us who have killed him. How can we prove our innocence?"

I hope you can summon your strength to tell me the reason why you do not wish to come and the state of your illness being so severe. Huang opened his eyes to listen. Enduring pain, he said, "I was serving in this position at the time. The magistrate of Yichun sent three archers to buy chickens and pigs in the countryside. After forty days, they did not return. The wives of the three men appealed to the prefecture. The prefecture governor had old friendships with the magistrate and ordered the magistrate to handle it himself. The magistrate deceived the prefecture and said, 'There was a bandit uprising in the area. We have already found the roots and lairs of the bandits. We sent the three men to investigate, fearing that their accomplices would reveal this plan. We used the excuse of buying goods. They are likely to be killed by the bandits. I hope to combine the efforts of all counties to catch the bandits. Officials and soldiers will capture them together.' The governor agreed with his words. The magistrate led them himself. They stayed in the mountains for two months and could not report back. Just then, four villagers were farming in the fields, looking very stupid. He sent a subordinate with 20,000 coins, called them over to speak, and said, 'The three archers were killed by the bandits.'"

The magistrate came to pursue and capture. For a long time, he did not succeed. Unable to return home, I asked the four of you to pretend to be bandits to fulfill the order. In the future, when the case is concluded, it will be named as a death sentence, but in reality, it will not be more than a dozen strokes of the cane. I will then release you. You are so poor, now each of you will get five thousand coins to give to your wives and children. And there is no danger to life, what is there to be afraid of? If you go to the authorities and they ask you if you killed someone, just say 'yes.' Then you can eat well and sit in jail, and you will be released in a few days. The four agreed. They were then bound and taken to the county. Coincidentally, the county magistrate was absent, and the clerk took charge of the matter. The clerk questioned the prisoners and found that their statements matched the magistrate's account. He sent them to the prefecture, where I happened to be in charge of the case. There was no disagreement. I then prepared the case and submitted it to the higher authorities. The response was that they should all be executed. The day was set for their execution in the market. I looked at the four of them and saw no signs of guilt. I suspected that perhaps they were innocent. I dismissed the jailers and questioned them privately. They all said they were not wronged. I then carefully said to them, "If you really did it, you will be beheaded tomorrow."

Head and body separated. It cannot be restored. The prisoners looked at each other and wept. They said, "Initially, we thought that after death we would return to life, to earn money for our families. We did not know that death was real." Then they fully explained the reason. I was greatly shocked and completely untied their bindings. The military officer had already discovered this and secretly reported to the governor, saying, "The prison official accepted bribes from the prisoners and led to a change in the situation." The next day, I will go to the government office to report. The governor was extremely angry and shouted at them to step down, saying, "You have completed the case handling, and it has been reviewed by the higher authorities. And then accepted bribes? You want to change it recklessly?" I replied, "Having obtained their innocence, how dare I not defend them?" The governor had no choice and transferred the case to the recording office. Then it was transferred to the county, but they could not decide. According to law, it should be submitted to the constitutional court, then the case would be reopened. The governor said, "If so, then the governor of the entire prefecture would bear the blame for wrongful convictions. How could there be a case already decided and then changed?" He completely burned all the transferred case documents and handed it over to the legal department.

If one were to follow the initial agreement, I would argue based on principle and righteousness. After several days of arguing without success, I resigned. The county magistrate ordered the head of the household register, who had previously governed the county, to take over my duties. On the verge of executing the prisoner, the magistrate then regretted and said, "If Judge Huang does not sign the case record, in the future, he will surely sue me in court." The magistrate instructed his colleagues to advise me, saying, "The prisoner will definitely die. Even if you insist, it will be of no use. Now, you must sign your name at the end of the document. Everyone will know that this is the decision of the county magistrate. What crime have you committed?" I reluctantly signed the document, and the four prisoners were executed. Two days later, a yellow-robed person with a stick came to chase the two clerks in the courtyard, hurrying to get the case file. The clerks were talking, and the yellow-robed person struck them with the stick. The four clerks all entered their rooms and did not come out. I went to check myself, and I saw the room door was not open, with the case files scattered inside. After a while, the four clerks suddenly died. A few days later, the acting magistrate died. The county magistrate was promoted with another reward.

On one day after I had finished my duties, I saw four prisoners bowing before me. They said, "We were wrongfully killed. We appealed to the Heavenly Emperor and obtained his approval. They want to arrest you." I pleaded, "The reason I know this injustice and can speak out is due to the efforts of Mr. Huang, the judicial official. Now seven people have died. It is enough to atone for our minor lives. I beg that you do not pursue this further." The Heavenly Emperor said, "If this person had not signed, then the four of you would not have died. It all stems from one signature. To determine guilt based on the circumstances, this person is the leader." We cried and bowed in the Heavenly Court for forty-nine days. They finally allowed a three-year delay. Immediately, the trousers were stuffed and the knees were exposed, with blood flowing through the holes. "We continued to bow without stopping," they said, "until this point. If the great deadline is reached, they will come to find you here." They bowed again and left. I just entered the door, and the four prisoners were already there. They said they had been waiting for a long time, fearing that the deadline had passed.

Let him quickly fetch his mother and wife to say goodbye. The reason I did not want to come is because of this. What more can be said now? Xiang asked, "Where are the ghosts?" Huang pointed and said, "They are all standing here in a respectful manner." Xiang and Zheng set up a table, burned incense, and dressed formally to pray. They said, "The four of you are so clear and discerning. Mr. Huang is dying, and there is no way out. Since you have already allowed him to say goodbye to his mother and wife, why add a serious illness? Why make him suffer so much?" After the prayer, Huang happily said, "The ghosts have listened to you." The pain will stop, and the suffering will not recur. Yet he was listless and devoid of vitality. After ten days, he told Xiang, "My mother has come. Please prepare a sedan chair to meet her." Xiang said, "The messengers I sent have not returned, so how could she arrive so soon?" Huang replied, "The four of them have already come to inform me, so I left." They indeed met outside the courtyard gate. He lifted the curtain, bowed once, and then died. Xiang was from Yuleping, and his son Yuanbo, a Lang, recounted this.

In the fifth year of the Xuanhe era, Xiang Yuanbo was the magistrate of Kaifeng. Cai Zhongxiang, who had already retired, once held a Daoist ritual at the Ningshuang Palace outside the city. Yuanbo went to pay respects to him. Cai Zhongxiang invited him to stay overnight. The next morning, Yuanbo saw Cai Zhongxiang's son Cai You and his brother Cai Heng, along with ten others, come to greet their father. They all wore gold belts and silver-embroidered robes, and many carried parasols. Yuanbo was amazed by their grandeur and recalled his uncle, He Zhitong, a high-ranking official, and was even more astonished. Among the guests was Jing Jie, the Director of the Imperial Inspection Bureau, who had a conflict with Cai Zhongxiang and spoke disparagingly of him. Later, he regretted his words. He Zhitong said, "Do not talk too much. Master Qi happens to be here. He is respected by the Grand Tutor. You should see him." They invited Master Qi to join their table. Master Qi said, "I have always been favored by Cai Gong. He treats me very well and keeps me in his garden. I should not speak ill of him. I just heard what the Director said. It is not appropriate to say such things." Jing Jie apologized for his previous remarks. Master Qi said, "There is no harm. Cai Gong spoke to me about his own situation, but he only asked about his descendants. I replied, 'They are good.' However, I often feel ashamed of my own words. You see how grand his mansion is, reaching up to the sky. In three years, not a single tile will cover his head. His golden saddles and silver-embroidered horses, which shine brightly in the market, will also disappear. In three years, not even a lame donkey will be left. This is truly like autumn wind and falling leaves. Alas!" At that time, the Cai family was at its peak, and no one dared to speak. Three years later, the Cai family fell. Master Qi was from Zizhou. Yuanbo told this story.

Vice Minister Cai In the seventh year of the Xuanhe era, Cai Juhou, the Vice Minister of Revenue, was appointed as the governor of Qingzhou but declined the post due to illness. He returned to Jinling, where a boil developed on his back. He ordered Taoist priests to perform a ritual and asked his close friend Wang Sheng to write a green paper. Shortly after, Cai Juhou died. Soon after, Wang Sheng also died suddenly. Three days later, he revived and called out, "Please bring the wife of the Vice Minister." The wife arrived, and Wang Sheng said, "At first, I was like a dream, being chased by someone. I refused to go, but they took me by the bed. I looked back and saw that I was still lying in bed. I thought I was dead. So we went together. The sky was like a dense fog. My feet were always three feet off the ground. We traveled about ten to fifteen miles to a public court. The judge asked why I had written a false green paper to deceive the heavens. I realized what was meant and replied respectfully, 'It was all the Vice Minister's intention. I only wrote the text.' The judge's anger slightly subsided and ordered me to stand aside. Soon, a small door on the west side opened. Prison guards escorted a prisoner, whose shackles were linked together, standing in the courtyard. Two others carried a bucket of blood and poured it over his head. The prisoner screamed and suffered great pain, as if unable to bear it. Upon closer inspection, it was the Vice Minister. The judge withdrew and again escorted him into the small door. Looking back at me, he said, 'You now return. Tell my wife. Quickly perform good deeds to save me. Now I am only dealing with matters in Yunchou.' The wife wept bitterly and said, 'The Vice Minister last year when he was the military governor of Yunchou. There were 500 bandits from Liangshan who surrendered. But then they were all killed. I repeatedly advised against it, but he did not listen. Now I am suffering this. How painful! Then she called Lu Shizhong to perform a yellow paper ritual to atone for the sins and pray for forgiveness.'

The Ancestral Spirits of Yao Village Pu Rui, the chief monk at Guangxiao Temple in Ganzhou, once traveled on a boat belonging to a local government official (通判) in Jiangzhou. They passed through Chizhou and docked at a village shore. He heard people calling out to each other to worship the ancestral spirits by burning incense. Pu Rui went to follow them and saw hundreds of people bustling back and forth. There was a house with three rooms, and inside the main hall was a small room, similar to a place where people worship Buddha. There were two elderly people, each about three feet tall, with bald heads and a very small knot of hair at the back of their heads. They wore 衣衾 that covered their lower bodies, revealing only their heads and faces. They were motionless like wood or stone, but their eyes could move, and they had smiles on their faces. People held incense, lamps, and wine to offer to them. Pu Rui returned and told the official about what he had seen. The official and his family then went to pay their respects. They offered money, candles, tea, and wine. They made small packets of 衣衾, dipped them in wine, and placed them in the mouths of the two elders. They also extended their tongues to lick them. Sometimes they reached out to touch the chests of the two elders. Their skin was tightly adhering to their bones, and it was said that they were hundreds of years old. One of the people said, "The entire village is named Yao, and these two elders are the village ancestors."

**建康伍伯** Tian Bangguang, the governor of Jinling, intended to beat a low-ranking official. The Wu Bo on duty demanded a thousand coins, but the official only agreed to half. The Wu Bo, enraged, spat out saliva and laughed, saying, "I haven't beaten anyone in a long time." He took pleasure in doing so. Rubbing his hands against the wall in anger, he suddenly saw a broken porcelain plate nearby and struck it. Blood flowed down to his elbow, and immediately, the severe pain began. He took leave and returned home, and after more than a month, the initial pain subsided. **刘叉死后文** Commander Wang Qingchen of Baode Army invoked the Purple Lady Spirit. He then wrote hundreds of words, claiming they were authored by Liu Cha, a Tang Dynasty Jinshi. The text read: "When I was young, I was a knight-errant, traveling all over the world. History calls this 'fleeing one's fate,' but that is not the case. Du Fu gave me a hundred measures of gold, but I refused to accept it. History calls this 'stealing,' but that is not the case. In Luoyang, a group of unruly youths forcibly took the wife of a good family. Later, they even destroyed her household. I could not bear to see the innocent suffer without recourse. At night, I gathered them and fought them, cutting off the necks of more than ten of them. I then minced their livers and ate them. Day and night, I roamed the market, unrecognized by anyone. History calls this 'killing innocent people and fleeing to the mountains,' but that is not the case. For several generations, I have been a man of integrity, sacrificing myself to help others. I taught and guided people, seeking to right injustices and follow the path of boundless virtue. Because of this, I reached the level of a deity. As for poetry and songs, they are merely the byproducts of my deeds."

I briefly write to you. Reflecting on the erroneous statements in historical records, the unusual actions of the past, I am filled with shame and regret. I ultimately passed away in Zhongnan, and my disciples buried me on the sunny side of the mountain, near a clear stream. The tomb still exists today, but people do not know it is my tomb. Because I had no military achievements for the country, thus the historians listened to the words of the wicked, and the records do not know where they ended. Suppose figures like Guo Ziyi and Li Guangbi, later generations all know their great achievements, but at the time, the historians' words were greatly praised and embellished. The world's sentiment is like this. Alas, to completely trust history is worse than having no history. If they cannot get to the truth, but only strive for elegance to flatter the emperor, they can certainly consult those who know and write it down. Why should they allow false words to slander the righteous and virtuous in inappropriate places? If I had heard of it at the time, I would certainly make this flatterer's head and feet be in different places. Since I have become a deity, I no longer compete. I will keep it hidden for now. May the wise of later generations take this as a lesson.

Pig's Hoof Talisman Nie Jingyan lived in Hengyang. A commoner wanted to borrow money and brought a pig's hoof as an offering. Nie accepted it and had it prepared in the kitchen. The kitchen maid took a knife and cut into the hoof. She saw a small piece of paper with a talisman inside. She hurriedly came out to report it and had the person called back. The person said, "I just bought it from a butcher. I was in need of your help. How could I dare to use a talisman for evil purposes?" They took the talisman back to the butcher and scolded him. The butcher said, "Today I just slaughtered a pig. How could there be such a thing?" They took the intact pig's hoof and gave it to the commoner, while taking the talisman home and cooking it. The entire family of four died. These five events were all mentioned by Jia Zisun. Temple Deity Stops the Offering Duan Yuansu lived in the capital. A neighbor had a sick person who was troubled by a spirit. The treatment was ineffective. They wanted to invite a Daoist priest to offer a petition to the emperor. Duan's ancestor dreamed of a deity like a divine being who said, "All deities who have served people for a term must move on. I have been in charge of this place for several years. Now I am about to move on. Your neighbor's ghost is currently under my jurisdiction. I am going to deal with it myself. I heard that your family was going to offer a petition. I am afraid it will cause trouble for you. Please plead with them to stop it. The sick person will recover on their own. I pleaded with them repeatedly. Duan promised. He asked where it was located. The deity said, "It is also a neighbor of your family." The next day, he thought about it and realized it was the Leather Temple. As the deity said, he told the neighbor to stop the offering. The sick person recovered on the same day.

**Heron Nests in Banyan Trees** Outside the Yimen Gate of Fuzhou, banyan trees were planted on both sides. Each tree was home to thousands of egrets, which nested among its branches, calling and chattering as they moved about. Their droppings filled the road with filth, and all who passed by covered their noses. Xue Zhilao Bi, the governor of the region, once rode out in a cool carriage, only for his clothes to be soiled by the birds’ excrement. He took this as an ill omen and wished to have all the trees cut down, though he had not yet spoken of it. That night, Zeng Wu, an official of the Anfu Office and Canshi, dreamed of a man clad in armor who earnestly said, “I have been ordered to protect the governor’s seat. My hundreds of subordinates all live among the banyan trees. Now the governor wishes to cut them down, and I have nowhere to go. Please speak on our behalf.” After waking, Zeng Wu, having heard nothing of the governor’s plan to fell the trees, paid little attention to the dream. The following night, he dreamed again, and the armored figure said, “Please speak now, or it will be too late.” Zeng Wu then understood that the governor’s dislike was only of the filth caused by the egrets, and that this was an easy matter to resolve. He asked for three days, promising that all the egrets would leave. Zeng Wu agreed. The next day, he went to the governor and spoke of it. Xue was startled and said, “I did indeed wish to cut them down, but I had never spoken of it aloud, and yet the spirit already knew. That is admirable.” By dusk, it began to rain heavily, and it rained for three days before stopping. When the rain ceased, the egrets had all vanished, and the trees stood clean and fresh as new.

The Seventh Mission of Zhao Zi Ju of the royal house, courtesy name Sheng Zhi, lost his wife in his prime years. His heart remained deeply attached to her. He decorated a small room in his house and treated her as if she were alive. At night, he slept alone in that room. One night, he felt someone coming out from the room and was frightened, calling his maidservant. The maid went back to sleep. In a short while, she had already come to the bed, pulling the curtains and whispering, "Don't be afraid, don't be afraid. I am here." His spirit suddenly became dim, and he no longer felt the boundary between life and death. So he slept with her, and they were as happy as before. From then on, she came every day. Every time they ate, she would sit opposite him. The servants and maids looked from the side but saw nothing. But the food in the dishes seemed to have been touched by someone. Their affection grew stronger over time, and he felt confused and disoriented. He gradually lost his appetite, and his movements and speech became weak. But he never told anyone about it. A Taoist monk passed by begging for food and happened to see him. The monk sighed and said, "You willingly consort with ghosts. Do you not consider your own life? I can perform the Heavenly Heart King's method. I will teach it to you now. Try your best to do it. The ghosts will retreat on their own." Zi Ju suddenly understood and immediately bowed twice, accepting the teaching.

He painted images of the Six Jia and Six Ding, and observed strict fasting and worship. His wife remained in her old state, but she was also not happy, often sighing deeply as if she were not achieving her goals. After half a year, she cried and bid farewell, saying, "Staying too long might corrupt your law. I must leave." She then never came again. From then on, Zi Ju observed the law with even greater diligence and treated people's illnesses with consistent success. In the second year of the Jianyan era (1128), my wife's family, the Zhang clan, fled south from the capital and resided temporarily in Longxing Temple in Yangzhou. Previously, an aunt married into the Zhao family, whose husband was the governor of Jiangzhou but did not take up the post and remained in Taiyuan. When the Jurchen cavalry surrounded the city, the aunt died under the bombardment. There was also an uncle who was captured by the bandits and cut into pieces and eaten. That year, my wife's grandmother, Lady Tian, fell ill and vaguely saw these two people outside the window. Zi Ju happened to be staying in the temple at the same time. My father-in-law informed him about the matter, and Zi Ju burned incense and prayed. After a long time, he said, "One man and one woman. Both died by unnatural means, but they are part of your family. They should not be punished. They should be sent away with wine and gifts." As he said, the illness also soon recovered.

Demon Fighting with the Law** Bo Wu of Zhao, the son of Zi Ju, emulated his father in practicing the Tianxin method. However, he had not yet mastered it. One day, someone brought a carp to their home. The fish leaped out of the basin several feet high, as if dancing. At the time, Zi Ju was out, and the family hurriedly called Bo Wu. Bo Wu brandished a sword, recited spells, and faced the fish with righteous law. The fish leaped even higher, almost reaching several feet. Frightened, Bo Wu quickly fled. On another occasion, Bo Wu and his companions were drinking at the Shuangxi Pavilion in Yanzhou. A maidservant lay by the railing and suddenly burst into tears. When asked, she did not respond. Bo Wu knew she was possessed by something and also performed a ritual to counter it. They fought from the afternoon until the third watch of the night, but Bo Wu could not bear the fatigue and hardship and gave up, leaving. This account was told by Bo Wu's younger brother, Bo Ti. **Taoist Priest of Mengcheng Temple** In the Mengcheng Temple of Bozhou, there was a Jade Register Hall that was strictly locked and guarded, only allowed to be opened at specified times. During the Xuanhe period, the Taoist priest Zhang Chongjun was in charge of the temple. One night, he heard the sound of a stick being straightened and dispatched within the hall. It continued for twenty beats before stopping. The next morning, he called everyone to open the lock and inspect. Inside, they found a Taoist priest who regularly practiced the Tianxin method, tied to the beam with his feet upside down. There were two marks on his back as large as bowls, and he was already dead. His feet hung loosely around the beam, and there were no ropes binding him. This account was told by Guo Di, who was accompanying his father as a magistrate at the time.

### The Story of the County Magistrate's Daughter from the *Yijian Zhi* Volume 71 Lu Shizhong, whose courtesy name was Dangke, was renowned among the literati and officials for his ability to exorcise ghosts using talismans. He was nicknamed Lu Zhen Guan. He always carried records of ghost cases with him. In the first year of the Jianyan era (1127), he traveled eastward from the capital to Lingbi County. The county magistrate, Bi Zao, had already been replaced but had not yet departed. Upon hearing that Lu Shizhong was coming, Bi Zao came to greet him and said, "My second daughter has been afflicted by a ghost. We have invited Taoist priests and exorcists to treat her, but instead, they were insulted and cursed, and some were even beaten away. Her condition has worsened. Only a true exorcist like you can save her. I humbly request that you come aboard my boat to take a look." Lu Shizhong agreed and entered the boat. The sick girl rose immediately, dressed herself, and came out to bow. She stood quietly by Lu Shizhong, showing no signs of illness, with a joyful expression on her face. She said, "Sister, it is a great blessing to see a true exorcist. All my pent-up frustrations that couldn't be expressed, I can now speak them out. Sister was born to my previous mother, and my second sister was born to my current mother. My second sister,恃母钟爱，每事相陵侮。顷居京师。有人来议婚事垂就。唯须金钗一双。二姐执不与。竟不成昏。心鞅鞅以死。死后冥司以命未尽。不复拘录。魂魄漂摇。 恃母钟爱，每事相陵侮。顷居京师。有人来议婚事垂就。唯须金钗一双。二姐执不与。竟不成昏。心鞅鞅以死。死后冥司以命未尽。不复拘录。魂魄漂摇。 恃母钟爱，每事相陵侮。顷居京师。有人来议婚事垂就。唯须金钗一双。二姐执不与。竟不成昏。心鞅鞅以死。死后冥司以命未尽

A Woman with No Place to Return Having nowhere to return, she met the Queen Mother of the West, who was out on a journey. Moved by her plight, the Queen Mother taught her secret methods. The method was about to be completed when it was again ruined by the second sister. The eldest sister was unfortunate, and life and death were entangled with this younger sister. Now, she must go with her to the grave to repay the great injustice and to thank the Queen Mother of the West. The true official should only deal with spirits or ghosts. There is a grudge that wants to be avenged, and the situation cannot be stopped. I wish the true official would not speak further. Lord Lu pondered for a long time and said, "His words were strong." Then he said to Bi Ling, "You should pray and thank her with good deeds. The method cannot be used to treat it." The woman suddenly fell to the ground, and he supported her up. She was again weak and exhausted as before. The one who came out to bow was the body of the second sister, but her words were those of the eldest sister. She has been dead for several years. The next day, the second sister died. Lord Lu came to mourn. Her father said, "Yesterday's events are full of twists and turns that I do not understand. The Queen Mother of the West taught the method, which was an after-death matter. How could the second sister ruin it? There must be some influence from your family. Please do not hide it. In my method, I will thoroughly understand its beginning and end." Bi Ling said, "There was indeed a strange event before. Now thinking about it, it must be this. The eldest daughter had already died and was buried in a temple outside the city. During the Cold Food Festival, a memorial service was held, and the entire family went."

Beside a thatched room, there lived a scholar. When he left, he locked the door. His family accidentally opened the seal and entered the room to look around. The middle daughter saw a copper mirror on the table and exclaimed, "This is my elder sister's coffin item. How did it end up here? It must have been stolen!" She thought that objects could be similar, and there were many such items for sale in the capital. The middle daughter argued, "When we bought the mirror, each sister got one. The strap and edge were all made by me. The paper used was for a certain official's visiting card. You can see that it is true." Just as they were lamenting, the scholar returned. He angrily said, "What is there to see in a poor scholar's temporary lodging? To enter without permission, what is the reason?" The middle daughter replied, "You robbed graves for items, and the stolen goods are all here. I came to catch the thief." She then bound him. The scholar then said that half a year ago, he was sitting by the light reading at night when a woman knocked on the door. She said, "I was sent away by my grandmother's anger and was ordered to return to my parents' home. My home is in the city, and I have nowhere to go. I wish to stay for one night." She wept and pleaded bitterly, and he reluctantly let her in. They became deeply entangled in love, and since then, she visited every night, sometimes even during the day. One day, as she was near the water combing her hair, the woman saw her and laughed, "No mirror?" She just happened to have one. She then took it to give to her. This is the item.

The spirit often carried clothes to mend, but refused to say who it was a family member of. Yesterday, it said to me, "Tomorrow my family will have a gathering with relatives and guests. We must mingle. You must not go to your place. I will come back at night." Then it left. Today morning, I was alone and without joy, so I strolled in the fields to pass the time. I did not expect you to enter my territory. My family heard about it and all wept. Only the second daughter said, "This man is definitely lying. It must be verified to be true." She ran to the burial place to trace it. There was a crack that could fit a hand. She opened the bricks to see the coffin. The large nails were all pulled up several inches. When the lid was removed, the eldest daughter was sitting cross-legged, sewing a man's headscarf. From the waist down, the flesh was all newly grown, the skin was warm and soft. Above the waist, it was still dry fat. She began to regret, then covered it again, and released the scholar to leave. Since then until now, it has been more than three years. So-called Xuan Nu's theory—Isn't it what Taoists call 'reviving the dead'? Must a living person be with it for a long time to revive it? The matter has become public, and cannot be continued. And the white-haired one told the story, all coming from the second daughter. Isn't the one who broke the law this one? Mr. Lu was also astonished. On the way to Shanyang, he told it to Guo Tongsheng. Guo Tongsheng's son De said, "Created characters to express the Dao."

The Ossuary of the Western Inner Palace In the fourth year of the Zhenghe era (1114 CE), an imperial decree was issued to renovate the Western Inner Palace. The project was entrusted to the Jingxi Transfer Commission, which was ordered to oversee the work. The Transfer Commissioner, Wang, was dismissed for administrative inefficiency and causing disturbance, having been impeached by Cai Anchi, the Governor of Henan. Cao, a former official of the Huizhou Pavilion and currently in mourning, was appointed as the Chief Transfer Commissioner. He was exempted from handling routine documents and was solely responsible for the renovation of the palace buildings. Cao was eager to accomplish tasks and frequently urged his colleagues, saying, "Complete it quickly. Rich rewards can be obtained immediately." The Deputy Transfer Commissioner, Sun Kuang, however, believed that the project was too large to be completed. He replied jokingly, "Have you heard the story of the Fox and the Tiger? The fox had a daughter who chose a tiger as her husband. On the night of the wedding ceremony, the officiant blessed them, saying, 'May you have five sons and two daughters early.' The fox stood respectfully and replied, 'Five sons and two daughters is not what I dare to hope for. But it would be a blessing to release us from our troubled lives early.' Today's matter is just like this." Cao was unhappy with this response, and Sun immediately claimed illness and resigned. The imperial city was extensive, covering sixteen li, and the imperial corridors were established with four hundred and forty rooms.

The palace was excessively decorated with red lacquer. Generally, it was hastily prepared. There was a need for cow bones mixed with ash, but it could not be provided. Twenty miles outside the city of Luoyang, there were dozens of hills of mass graves where thousands of people were buried. An official named Han proposed a plan, saying, "These are all ownerless, decaying bones. Let us dig them up and burn them. Their bones will never run out." This method had already been used during the time of Wang Cao. Song approved of it. The official in charge, Prefect of Chengzhou Guo Lianrong, and the assistant envoy Peng Qi, along with more than ten others, all came together for the task. There were no objections. Song was appointed to the position of Xianmu Ge Xueshi for his achievements and was summoned to serve as Dianzhongjian, where he died. During the reign of Xuanhe, Sun Kuang died of illness and arrived at the Temple of Mount Tai. The inscription on the outer gate read "Gate of Purity and Tranquility." A prison guard seized him and took him inside. He was commanded to provide a statement of family extermination. Sun said, "What crime have I committed?" A voice from the dais shouted loudly, "You dug up the ancient tombs of Luoyang to gain favor and reward. It is you who did this. How can you deny it?" Sun asked to speak with the others. He saw two prisoners carrying iron shackles standing under the eaves, each held by a soldier who blocked their faces with an iron fan, constantly swinging it.

A nail was applied to each fan. Blood flowed over their bodies. They were brought forward, and it turned out to be the two lords of Song and Wang. They still supported each other. Sun Li cited the stories of Fox and Tiger, along with the reasons for his resignation. All the people in the court laughed. The two men submitted and left. Sun recovered. One day, Han also dreamed as Sun had seen, and the statement was completed. He was about to be dismissed, and he looked up and said, "My crime is too great to escape punishment. But my ancestor Wei Gong had great merit to the state. It should not be the case that one grandson's crime leads to the extermination of the entire family." The judge thought deeply for a long time and said, "Only the family should be destroyed." The statement was as such. From then on, he died after several months. Within a year, his wife and children were all gone. Now only a son from the same clan is taken to continue the line. I heard this from Linchuan's Wu Hucheng, who got it from Han Zicang. I cross-referenced it with the records of the National History Bureau and found the dates and official positions to be as such. Shaozhou's Li Yuguang said, "There was a court official who also advanced in rank due to this matter. Later, he lived in Dengzhou and contracted a strange disease. A carbuncle grew on his buttocks."

It was about an inch long, with a bone inside that made it impossible to sit or lie on. The doctor treated it with medicine, and after a long time, it fell to the ground, curled up like a piglet's tail. After several days, it returned to its original state. The doctor then treated it again with the same method. Like this, it lasted for more than a year, and a total of thirty-six times it fell off, and then it died. Wang Rizheng said: When Song initially discussed with his officials, some thought it inconvenient. Song went home to think about it and was determined to proceed, writing a document himself and handing it to the office. The clerk, worried that he might be sued in the future, stuck the document into the official record. Several years later, the underworld summoned him to trial, and he saw a man with a bull's head leading someone out of flames—it was Song. The clerk complained, "All matters were due to the waiting official, and his handwriting is still there." The king ordered a soldier to go and retrieve it, and it arrived in an instant, showing it to Song, who then confessed. The clerk was then allowed to return. The next day, he went to the office to review the old records, and all entries from beginning to end were present, but only Song's document was missing. He then claimed illness, left his position, and returned home, giving up his family to become a practitioner of asceticism.

Tingzhou Mountain Spirits In Tingzhou, there are many mountain spirits known as shanxiao. Among them, those residing in the administrative center of the prefecture are called the Seven Nuns. Behind the deputy magistrate's office stood a massive soapberry tree, its trunk splitting into three branches, directly shading the main hall. It was said that a spirit also dwelled within it. Chen Jilao served as the deputy magistrate. His daughter had already been married, and both she and her husband came to visit. At midnight, while the daughter was sleeping outside the bed, she felt something shaking her table. Fearing for her safety, she moved into the inner room, where it was as if someone was climbing up. The mats and cushions were all shaking. The husband and wife shouted loudly that there was a thief. Mr. Chen quickly got up, along with his eldest son, who was the recorder, and they went to investigate. They saw nothing. Surprised, they said, "The public office has strict guards. How could a thief get here? If it's a ghost, how dare it do this?" An elderly man named Ma Ji, who was on night duty, was ordered to go to the kitchen to warm wine. The kitchen was connected to the hall. As Mr. Ma reached the door, he screamed loudly. The recorder, who was usually brave, lit a torch and looked at it. Mr. Chen's servant fell to the ground, foam everywhere. He gave him good medicine, and after a long time, he could speak again. He said, "A big, blurry black man."

A figure came out of the house and moved directly toward me, but I did not know why. Old Ji still did not believe it. The recorder saw a white-clad person seven feet tall, who came out of the kitchen and rushed to the hall, opened the door and went out. The recorder truly thought it was a thief and hurriedly chased after it, but the hall door remained closed as usual. He opened the door again and saw the object open the hall door and leave. He chased after it again, but the door remained closed as before. When he reached the hall, the white-clad person went directly to the east room where the guards were on watch, causing one guard to fall into a state of shock and fright. The others tried to save him but he was already dead. Several years later, Zhao Zizhang served as deputy governor of the prefecture. At that time, bandits were active in the subordinate counties. The great general Cheng Shihui from Jiangxi came from Ganzhou to chase and capture them, was about to return to his camp, and took a short rest in the deputy governor's hall. He brought out the two concubines he was carrying and drank with Zhao. Just as they were drinking, there was a concubine about two feet tall, wearing a brown shirt and a plain skirt, walking slowly forward. Cheng hit it with a stick, and it turned out to be a cat jumping out. The clothes were all lying on the ground. Zhao Zizhang's son, Bo Ti, followed his father to his official post, and Old Ma Ji was still alive, having heard the story like this. Bo Ti said.

Huanglian Mountain Temple The Huanglian Mountain Temple in Lechang County, Shaoguan, was a renowned place. In the winter of the second year of the Jianyan era, the prefect invited the Zen master from Linjiang to preside over the temple. The temple guardian deity was known for its supernatural abilities. The people of the county would pray for success and perform sacrifices by slaughtering animals and pouring wine, after which they would drink the wine and return. When the master first arrived, he made an agreement with the deity: "The deity was entrusted by the Buddha to protect the temple and should not accept this offering. From today onwards, prepare more pure food offerings." The deity agreed, and from then on, no one dared to bring alcohol or meat into the temple. The following year, on the last day of the eleventh month, a benefactor completed a Buddhist ceremony and wanted to drink wine. Three servants carried a barrel past the deity's shrine. Suddenly, a dog appeared and collided with the barrel, instantly breaking it into pieces. There was no wine left. Those who saw this could not help but express their amazement. This story was told by Gai Cinan.

The County Official's Servant** In the Ningdu County of Ganzhou, there was a county official named Li, who supervised the collection of taxes in nearby villages. He was accompanied by one servant. The servant asked for money from those who owed taxes, but was not satisfied with the amount. He tied one of them to a mulberry tree and urinated on him with feces. He received a thousand coins. On that day, thunder and lightning suddenly arose, and the servant was killed in front of the Pu'an Temple in the village. The money was still in his waistband, and four hundred coins were driven into his flesh, with skin covering them. This happened in March of the fourteenth year of the Shaoxing era. At that time, the county was called Qianhua. The monk Zuyi of the temple said. **Du San's Disrespect** In the north of Chongzhen Ward in Hongzhou, there was a man named Du San who sold water from a well. He drew water to sell, and in summer, he sold mosquito repellent to support himself. He lived with his mother and one younger brother. His younger brother worked in a bakery. Only the elder brother fed their mother with two meals. But he was a heavy drinker. If anything displeased him, he would insult and beat her. Those who saw it would clench their fists in anger, and they urged his mother to file a lawsuit, but she did not do so. One day, after getting very drunk, he returned home and beat his mother again. Soon, he became suddenly frenzied. He took the mosquito repellent, added arsenic and sulfur, and swallowed it. He ran into the market and asked his companions for water to drink. People in the market thought he was drunk, but they did not know that the poison had already taken effect. He died shortly. Is this the retribution for his filial impiety?

The Family of Zhang the Broker In Xing Prefecture, there was a wealthy man named Zhang, who was originally engaged in the business of trading cloth goods with small merchants. One evening, after closing his teahouse, he heard a groaning and moaning sound outside. He went out to see and found a prisoner who had been beaten to death by the authorities during the day in the market square. The prisoner said, "I have revived after being dead. With water, there is still a chance of survival. Fearing to be seen by the patrol officers, I will die again." Zhang immediately pulled him into the door, slowly untied his bindings, and helped him lie on a bed. He set up a mat for him to sleep and fed him porridge and medicine. Even his son and wife were unaware of this. After two months, the wounds on his ribs had healed, and he could walk. Zhang gave him money for the journey and, before dawn, personally sent him out of the city. He never asked about his hometown, family name, or given name. After ten years, a wealthy traveler riding a horse with attendants arrived, carrying five thousand bolts of cloth into the market. The leading brokers competed to welcome him. The traveler said, "Is Zhang the broker here? I want to have him sell my goods." The crowd laughed, and someone called Zhang over. Zhang replied, "The wealth I have is not enough to amount to several ten thousand coins. This is a large transaction. I wish you would choose another wealthy and capable person."
