The traveler said, "I certainly want to trouble you."

But visit good merchants and borrow from them. Give me a contract. Wait until I return home. It will not be too late to come back and collect the money. Zhang Yan followed his advice. After several days, the guest told the old man to prepare wine for me. Do not invite other guests. Upon arriving, he invited the old man's wife to drink with him. After drinking, he got up and said, "Do you recognize me?" I am the person you kept under your bed ten years ago. I have always been a bandit, traveling through over ten prefectures, never losing, until I was captured in Xing. I owe you a debt of life. As soon as I left the house, I immediately swore by the sky, saying, "From today onward, I will no longer kill." But if I can find a good master with money, I will repay Zhang Yan, and I will no longer be a thief. As soon as I reached Taihang Mountain, I met a lone traveler, and I robbed him and got more than a thousand strings of coins. I then became a merchant and traded. Now I have land and property in Jinye, and I will repay the old man and woman with this cloth. Originally, I agreed to give it back to the old man. You can take all the money to start a business. I will not come back. He bowed and left. The Zhang family thus became wealthy, with their wealth reaching ten million. People in Xing called them the Cloth Zhang family. The three events were also recorded in Jia's Southern Records.

He Wenzheng, the Chancellor, was studying at the Imperial Academy. Along with his fellow student, Mr. Huang, he visited the fortune-teller Sun An to inquire about his destiny. Sun An sat cross-legged and wore only a loose upper garment. The Chancellor asked first. After laying out the numbers, Sun An straightened his clothes and bowed, saying, "Your destiny is extremely noble. Not only will you be the best in the world, but you will also reach the highest rank among ministers." The two looked at each other and laughed, saying, "Why are you mocking us?" Sun An became angry and said, "I am old and barely make a living. Now I am flattering a scholar, what will I gain? How can the destiny be so fitting?" The Chancellor replied, "Then when will you become the top scholar?" Sun An said, "In the year of Yi Wei." "When will you become a chancellor?" "Within twelve years. But there is one unusual thing. After you become a chancellor, you will die in a foreign land. Ordinary envoys sent to distant lands are usually just attendants. How can a chancellor enter a foreign country? This is not understandable." Initially, the Chancellor came from Xianjing. At that time, he passed through Tongbei and wrote a petition in the temple to ask for a dream.

That night, a person in a dream reported that Huo Lang was coming to see He Zhuangyuan. He then went out to meet him. Huo said, "In the future, you will be asked about the imperial examination questions." When he arrived in the capital, he again sought a dream at the Temple of the Two Princes. The dreamer told him as Huo had said. After waking up, he tried to write several hundred words for the imperial examination, and showed them to Mr. Huang. Mr. Huang thought it was not good. At the time, the Chancellor was a guest at the office of Prime Minister Deng Xunwu. He then dreamed of someone reporting that Huo Lang was coming again. After sitting down, Huo said, "Your draft for the imperial examination yesterday was very wrong." The emperor's interpretation of the *Daodejing* will be given to the two government offices in three days. You should read it thoroughly first. As expected, Deng Gong indeed received the gift. He immediately recorded it and read it morning and evening. In the year of Yiwu, during the imperial examination, the question indeed came up. He answered everything with classical texts and became the top candidate. Twelve years later, in the year of Jingkang Bingwu, he was appointed as the Deputy Chancellor. He followed the two emperors to the north and died in captivity. All happened as An had said. Huo Gong was probably the top candidate in the previous two examinations. The Method of Heavenly Heart Li Shimai, the Chancellor's eldest son, named Henglao. When he first learned the Heavenly Heart Correct Method, in eating, drinking, sitting, and standing, he never failed to maintain discipline. He was staying in Guilin. At night, he went to the toilet. He saw a lampstand outside. He was already surprised by it. He then took it and placed it in the middle. Soon it was outside again. It then climbed onto it. It then became even higher. The lampstand was upside down, but the oil did not spill. It rotated and filled the room. It was about to reach his head. Henglao was squatting on the toilet. He could not perform the ritual. He was terrified and shouted out, running away. From then on, he dared not lightly practice it. Some say that when first practicing talismans, spirits do not enjoy it. Therefore, they often set up strange phenomena to test him. Jiashou said.

虞并甫奏章 虞并甫，侍奉其父在潼川担任漕运官。因父亲生病，他斋戒了一整天。他命令道士刘泠然，向皇帝上奏章请求帮助。刘泠然一向以精确著称。从子夜开始在坛上伏拜，直到天明才起身。刘泠然说：“我刚刚去了皇帝那里，看到桌上的一份奏章上有两句说：‘乞求减少我的寿命，增加父亲的寿命。’皇帝指示我说：‘虞允文非常孝顺，可以参与执政，但他没有提出请求。’不久之后，虞并甫的父亲去世了。十八年后，虞并甫参与了国家的重要政务。宇文仔说。 孙尚书仆 孙仲益尚书住在毗陵。他派两个仆人前往平江。其中一个仆人在路上突然去世。另一个仆人买了一张竹席覆盖尸体，并回到家中报告。经过一夜，死者竟然复活了。他说：“我们正一起走着，我在路边停下来解手。遇到一个穿黄衣服的官员拿着藤棒驱赶我说：‘官府叫你拉船。’果然有一艘船接上了我们。我们在运河中行进，只有我一个人被强迫拉船。船像飞一样快，不知道去了哪里。我心里想，没有县文引在手，凭什么擅自叫唤我？我等他稍微松懈的时候，把他推到河里。我急忙沿着原来的路返回。到达时，我发现自己躺在竹席下，没有办法进去。我徘徊不忍离开，于是坐在上面。天快亮时，一个行人经过，看到我并责备说：‘你怎么独自坐在这里？难道不是鬼吗？’我吓得失去了意识，不自觉地回到了自己的身体里，这才醒来。我才知道自己已经死了。李耆俊说。

The *Yijian Zhi* Volume Eighty-Three, Story The Ox Ghost Gao Shiling, a junior clerk, lived in Huangyan, Taizhou. In the fourth year of Shaoxing, he was temporarily stationed in Bashi Town, Wenzhou, to collect taxes. On the nineteenth day of the second month, after he had gone to bed, he heard two voices outside calling out: "A strange creature is coming to kill you. You must avoid it. Firmly block all five senses and do not engage. Perhaps you may escape." Upon listening to the voices, he recognized them as those of Li Lijian, his former colleague and supervisor of Mingzhou, and Zhao Lu, the patrol inspector of Taizhou, both of whom were now dead. Gao Shiling was greatly frightened and immediately covered himself with a blanket, sitting upright in fear, waiting for the creature. After a short while, he heard someone cursing Li and Zhao, saying, "I will kill Gao Shiling. What business do you have with this?" Another person walked past the bed, leaning on a staff, as if he were blind. He whispered, "When he called you, do not respond." Gao Shiling then heard the cursing voice say, "Blind beast, why do you also meddle in human affairs?" Li and Zhao tried to mediate, saying, "Killing one Gao Shiling will not benefit you." Soon after, an insect buzzed through the window crack and entered the tent. It flew around the blanket, making several passes. Gao Shiling looked closely and saw that the insect's color was like gold, with a red thread hanging from its back. He reached out to grab it, but Li and Zhao shouted, "Trouble! Trouble! Killing it will only bring more harm." Gao Shiling then let go of the insect, which continued to fly around all night.

Finally unable to achieve his goal, he left. A minor official peeped out of the window and saw a young man standing face to face with an old woman. The young man said, "I must seek revenge from the old man." The old woman replied, "It should be so." At dawn, they opened the door and found two cows lying under the fence. Tracing their origin, they discovered that the cows were from a farm five miles from the town. The town patrol heard of this strange occurrence and invited Gao to drink, releasing him afterward. Soon after, he requested to return, saying, "The old woman and the young man are both under the table." Gao's wife and children were all in Huangyan. That night, he saw his concubine and said, "When you came, I was already pregnant. Now the fetus has died. Yesterday, I sent shoes and socks to you." While crying, Li and Zhao entered by lifting the curtain and talked about things as if they had known each other for a long time. Gao thought he would surely die. All night long, holding a candle, he wrote letters to his relatives and friends to say goodbye. He wrote eighty letters, some of which were mixed with verses and hymns. It seems as if something was possessing him. He repeatedly put on his hat and belt and walked out, intending to go to the river in front of his house. He heard a voice from the air saying, "Do not give him shoes. If you give them, he will leave." Those around him hid the shoes. He did not eat or drink. After five days, he woke up. His family came to see him. The so-called pregnant concubine was actually four months pregnant. She miscarried after eating beef. He was not actually dead. Gao was also unharmed. Wu Chuanpeng said.

Song of Han Palace Spring In the fourth year of the Shaoxing era, a special examination for the Jinshi degree was held in the Sichuan region. A certain envoy from Chengdu prayed to the God of Zitong, wishing to know the name of this year's top candidate. That night, he dreamed of arriving at the temple and seeing two scholars walk out holding hands, singing the lyrics of "Han Palace Spring." He asked the deity, "How does the Jade Hall compare to the humble thatched cottage with sparse hedges?" The deity pointed and said, "This is it." The next day, he returned to the temple to verify the dream from the previous night. Scholars arrived one after another without end. After a long time, two people came out together, walked out holding hands and sang, indeed the words from the dream. He examined their appearance and found them to be the same, immediately stepped out and bowed, saying, "One of the two must be the top candidate." He told them about the dream, and both were greatly pleased. They argued back and forth, saying, "I started it," and "I am singing this." One of them was Huang Gong from Xianjing, who boldly said, "This is my family's old dream. What does it have to do with you?" His father, when he first passed the examination, dreamed that the deity gave him a poem: "The news from the Jade Hall is near. The names on the golden list are high." He woke up and was happy, thinking he would surely become a Hanlin scholar. But he ended up as a professor in Chengdu. Reflecting on it now, it was set for me. What is meant by the news from the Jade Hall, refers precisely to the words in the song. That year, the candidate indeed came in first. Two generations confirmed the same dream. Even though it was a moment of laughter and song, it was already determined decades ago. The deity is indeed very spiritual. Told by Guan Shouqing.

In the second year of the Ganda period (1166), the magistrate of Lin Gui in Jing Jiang, Guo Ziying, had a dream in which a person told him, "You have been newly appointed as the head of the Wan Shou Temple." Guo was troubled by local affairs but was very happy in his dream upon learning of his sudden appointment as a temple official. The next day, the转运判官 Zhu Xiong summoned him to attend and receive the payment of "folded rice money" from various states, instructing him to use a separate seal for this purpose. In the "Xin Zi Treasury," he found a seal. After several days, he took it out to look at it, and the inscription on it was the record of the Yu Qing Wan Shou Temple in Gui Zhou. This was said by Zhang Yin, the丞 of Lin Gui.

The Three Wonders of Master Li Master Li, the abbot of Miaoguo Temple in Raoyang, was a young man when he began his travels in search of spiritual enlightenment. He arrived at Fuyan Temple in Hengshan in late April of the summer. One evening, he strolled to the Doushui Bridge in front of the temple and saw a steep cliff overhanging the pond. On the cliff stood a strange monk, his back against a stone. Master Li had long heard that there was an Arhat in this place, appearing and disappearing irregularly. He also remembered the story of Bodhisattva Chiti entering a stone wall as recorded in the scriptures. He looked at the monk with awe and reverence. Suddenly, the stone wall opened slightly, and the monk entered. The wall then closed without a single crack. Ten days later, after completing his studies, Master Li walked with two fellow practitioners to the Tiger Paw Spring Pavilion behind the temple. A sudden gust of wind arose, and the two monks wanted to return. Master Li stayed behind a little longer. The two monks said, "We have long known that there are many strange things under this pavilion. There is no need for you to stay." Master Li, feeling bold and unconcerned, remained. Soon, someone began knocking on a pillar at the southwest corner of the pavilion. Then, a hand reached inward, gradually extending until it nearly reached five feet. Master Li playfully asked, "Can you retract it?" The hand immediately withdrew. A short while later, he heard the sound of knocking on the pillar again. He said, "Are you a saint? Then you should hide. If you are a mountain spirit, you should show yourself." After a meal, a hand emerged from the pillar, its fingers initially as large as beams, gradually shrinking to the size of a newborn infant's fingers. Master Li was greatly alarmed and hurried down the mountain. It was the year 1140 CE. Three years later, when returning home, he passed through the White Cloud Hermitage on Mount Lu. One clear night, he was paying his respects to the Buddha when he saw something moving outside the window, resembling a cow or a tiger. Opening the door, he saw a huge black cow wandering back and forth. He thought to himself, "I saw nothing during the day. Could it be a ghost?" The next morning, he went to the spot and found a large green stone lying down, exactly where the black cow had been the previous evening. Master Li recounted these wonders.

The Ghost Who Blows Out the Lamp The son-in-law of the Wang family, a relative of my wife, lived in Maibei, Fang County, Tang Prefecture. He was close friends with a local monk, who had died several years ago. One night, the monk appeared in his dream as if he were still alive. They talked and laughed for a long time, and the monk then gave him a poem before departing. After waking up, he could remember two lines of the poem: "Parents' graves are now complete, and descendants sigh because of their own fate." He could not recall the final couplet, so he wished aloud, "If you truly have a spirit, do not hesitate to visit me again in a dream." Soon, he remembered the last two lines: "The green mountains are infinitely beautiful, but when returning, do not linger." The next day, he pondered the meaning of the poem and suspected it was an omen of misfortune. On another occasion, after returning from the county, he was riding alone late in the evening, slightly drunk, with his servant following behind but not yet arrived. After traveling twenty miles, he saw a group of seven or eight people gathered around a fire in a thicket. He went to join them, and they were all beggars. They sat in a circle, not speaking. Upon closer inspection, their appearances were somewhat human, but some were missing arms, some had missing eyes, and some had conjoined necks—none were fully formed. Seeing Wang, they leaped up and blew out the lamp he was holding. The lamp was made of a pig's bladder and did not go out. Wang was terrified and fled at full speed. The ghosts chased him relentlessly. After traveling another twenty miles, he finally reached home. He hurriedly pounded on the door, shouting, "The ghosts are chasing me!" The people inside the house rushed out, and the ghosts dispersed. Wang then fell ill and died.

A Ghost Without a Chin A villager from the White Stone village was weaving silk for someone ten miles away. Carrying the loom, he returned home at night under the bright moonlight. A man approached and said, "I am very timid and fearful. I have heard that there are ghostly creatures that come out at night in this area. I would like to travel together." The villager agreed. The man said, "If we see anything, what should we do?" The villager replied, "If I see it, I will strike it with the loom. I have a large sickle tucked in my waist, which can also be used to kill it." The man was startled and walked slightly behind. He called out again, "People say that ghosts have no chins. Look at my face." The villager realized that the man was a ghost. He raised his knife and turned around to strike, but the ghost's chin was connected to its chest, and it had two glaring eyes. The ghost then disappeared.

Long People's Country A man from Mingzhou set sail across the sea. He encountered dense fog all around. A strong wind arose, and he did not know which direction his boat was heading. The sky cleared slightly, and he found himself under an island. Two men carried knives and went ashore to cut wood. They saw a bamboo fence about a hundred paces away and entered it. Inside, they saw vegetables arranged in neat rows, suggesting that people lived nearby. They squatted and picked vegetables when suddenly they heard clapping sounds. Looking up, they saw a giant, standing three to four zhang tall, moving as fast as flying. The two men hurried back, but one was slightly slower and was caught by the giant. The giant used his finger to make a hole in the man's shoulder, passed him through a large vine, and tied him to a high tree before leaving. Soon after, the giant returned with a pot on his head. The man, looking down from the top of the tree, realized they were going to cook him. He was greatly afraid and remembered there was a knife at his waist. He took the knife and cut the vine, enduring the pain and using all his strength, just barely managing to cut through. He quickly boarded the boat and cut the ropes. By then, they were far from the shore. The giant entered the sea to chase them, moving as if on flat ground. The water only reached his belly. He finally reached the boat and grabbed it, firing a powerful crossbow but not retreating. Someone held an axe and chopped at the giant's hand, cutting off three fingers and dropping them into the boat. The giant then left. The fingers were as thick as pillars. Xu Jing Ming Shu said he had seen such a giant, and He De Xian also claimed to have seen one. Shouzhou Shilu Hall

The office of the Sili in Xiu Prefecture is full of strange occurrences. There is often a figure wearing a blue cap and a coarse robe, short and broad in appearance, walking slowly and heavily. There is also a woman who emerges every night, confusing the night watchmen. When my father was in office, my elder brother, who later became chancellor, was nine years old. One day, he seemed to see something, opening his eyes wide and staring, repeatedly saying "water, water." After some time, he recovered. Two days later, my father returned from the prefecture in the evening. His attendant servant was holding his official robes behind him when suddenly he cried out and fell to the ground. My father had heard that ghosts fear leather belts, so he took one and tied it around the servant, then helped her onto the bed. After a long time, she spoke, saying, "This person usually disrespects ghosts and spirits. He just held something in his right hand that was very frightening. I thought it was a belt. I dared not get close. I didn't know I came from the left side. I was just lucky to be caught. But then the official person's bell馗 array stopped me. I will leave now. Please do not trouble me." When asked who she was, she refused to say. After repeated questioning, she finally said, "I am Zhi Jiu, a farmer from Jiaxing County. Along with a fellow villager named Shui San, we were nine people in total, all of whom died due to the flood the previous year. While the officials were distributing relief food to the living, I alone died first. Now I live on a large tree behind the house. What the young master saw the other day was Shui San." My father said, "I worship Zhenwu very diligently, and there are also images of Buddha and the land god and stove god. How could you come here without permission?" She replied, "Buddha is a good deity, but he does not meddle in trivial matters. Zhenwu every night has his hair loose, wields a sword, and flies over the roof. I carefully avoid him. The land god behind the house is not very diligent in his duties. Only the small temple in front of the house is always reprimanded when seen. Just now, I entered the kitchen, and the stove god asked where I was going. I answered, 'just wandering around.' He scolded, 'You must not cause trouble.'"

The official said, "I dare not." Thus, he had come to this point. The official asked, "What are the two things that usually appear?" The entity replied, "The one with the green headband is a stone spirit, called Stone Elder Brother. He is located outside the window of the study, beneath the fence, and enters the ground by about three feet. The woman is Qin Second Lady, who has lived here for a long time." The official said, "I offer paper money to the Great Earth Deity every month on the first and fifteenth days. Why do you allow external ghosts? Go for me to inquire. Tomorrow, I will destroy its shrine." The entity replied, "Do you not know? Even if there is money to use, what to do about the hunger in the belly? I enter people's homes and gain something, and necessarily divide and leave it to them, so they have tolerated me silently until now." After a meal, the entity said again, "It has been as warned. I reported to the Earth Deity, who was angered by my talking too much and used a staff to drive me out." The official asked, "Have you seen my family temple's ancestors?" The entity replied, "Every festival when offerings are made, I necessarily go to watch. I hear the fragrant food, want to eat but cannot. There are also empty seats among the seats. Only the Lady in the Yellow Robe sees me and is always angry." The official then sent the entity to spy again. Soon, the entity breathed heavily and changed color, then slowly said, "Just as reaching the door, I was chased by the Lady holding a staff. I quickly turned to run and just barely escaped." The Lady referred to is my grandmother-in-law, Lady Ji Guo. The official asked what was needed. The entity replied, "The ghost realm is suffering from hunger. I wish to have a full meal, good wine and fat goose, to share with everyone. Not like the usual treatment of thin chicken." After speaking, the entity suddenly listened intently, as if someone was calling him. He suddenly said, "The Earth Deity was greatly angered and forced both of us out. Now temporarily staying on the city wall, no place to return to. I wish to quickly let me return. From now on, I dare not come again." Then, the entity untied his belt. The concubine slept through the day and then woke up.

A Seamless Boat In the seventh month of the twentieth year of the Shaoxing era, in the port of Gantang, Fuzhou, a boat drifted in from the southeast. It carried three men, one woman, and several thousand pounds of sandalwood and agarwood. One of the men was originally from Fuzhou, living in Nantai. He had once lost his boat at sea and, by chance, found himself on a piece of floating wood that carried him to a large island. He was known for his love of playing the flute, which he often kept at his waist. The islanders introduced him to their ruler, who had long enjoyed music and was greatly pleased to see the flute. The ruler kept him as a guest, providing food and drink, and gave him a house to live in. Later, the ruler also gave him a wife. He stayed on the island for thirteen years, during which time he could not communicate with anyone and did not know what country they were from. However, the people on the island seemed to know that he was Chinese. Suddenly, they prepared a boat and invited him to travel with them. After two months, they finally reached the shore. The patrol inspector of the Gantang Garrison, suspecting the boat to be an illegal vessel, sent someone to escort it to Min County. The county magistrate, Qiu Duowen, called me to examine it. The boat was carved from a single piece of wood, with no seams, and had only one opening for entry and exit. Inside, there was a small storage area about three feet wide, said to be where the woman lived. The other two men were his brothers, who wore cloth to cover their bodies, tied their hair with a belt, and went barefoot. When given wine, they would kneel and sit, placing their hands on the ground as if in worship, drinking it all at once. The woman had teeth as white as snow, and her eyebrows and eyes were sparse and elegant, though her complexion was somewhat dark. At the time, I was a scholar-official of the prefecture, dispatched to examine candidates in Linzhang, and I intended to ask them in detail upon my return. However, the county sent them to the maritime customs office in Quanzhou, and I did not return. To this day, I regret it.

#### 詹林宗 The scholar from the village, Zhan Linzong, during the 32nd year of Emperor Gaozong's reign, studied at the Miaofu Pagoda Temple in Chengxi. In the morning, while grooming himself, he found a small snake sitting on his hairbrush. The snake remained there for some time before finally leaving. Later, he passed the imperial examination and ranked fifth. In the first year of Emperor Xiaozong's reign, when it was time for the imperial examinations, he went to a nearby village's Big Pond Lake temple to study. He silently prayed, saying, "Three years ago, I received a divine sign and ranked fifth. Now, if I am selected as the top candidate, I will be grateful to the snake as a sign." The next day, as he was alone, writing an essay on the *Shangshu*, a snake appeared from somewhere, coiling by his side. It extended its head and looked around. When startled, it did not move, and after a while, it quickly left. Zhan was very pleased, and when the results were posted, he was indeed first. #### 葛师夔 Ge Shikui, serving as the deputy magistrate of Wuning in Hongzhou, entered the provincial capital to report matters. He stopped at Shanglan Temple, planning to pay respects the next morning. It was June, and he disliked sleeping with the attendant in the same room. He set up a separate bed, locked the door, and slept. Only a small attendant was outside, while the others stayed in the Water and Land Hall. As he lay down, he heard someone snoring on the bed. He scolded it slightly, and it stopped. As soon as he was about to fall asleep, the sound became loud again. Ge reached for a chamber pot and hit the snake's body, becoming very angry. He planned to deal with it at dawn. When the rooster crowed, someone outside reported that it was time to get up. After getting out of bed, the snoring person was still not moving. Ge went out, called the small attendant with a lamp, and entered to check. The attendant exclaimed, "It's a dead man!" He ran out and called all the night watchmen and the temple's head monk to come and see. They found a man lying peacefully on top, barely breathing. The monk recognized him.

Exclaimed in surprise, "This is someone I've been acquainted with at the temple. He died five days ago. Yesterday, his body was temporarily stored behind the temple. How could he be here now?" He immediately invited his son to see the burial site. The coffin was empty. On another day, he returned to the temple and rested in the abbot's quarters. The chief monk met him at night for a conversation. Ge happened to mention the old acquaintance, County Magistrate Liu, whom he hadn't heard from for several years. The monk was close to Liu and pointed to a bed, saying, "The magistrate died in another room. His wife passed away on this very bed." Ge was initially unaware of this and felt very afraid, but he couldn't move. After lying down, he tossed and turned, unable to sleep, and heard the bed screen being knocked three times. His heart jolted with fear. He called out loudly, "If you are my old friend, why not openly tell me?" Immediately, there were several more knocks, and someone shouted Ge's name, "Mingdao, are you safe and sound?" Ge, covered with a blanket, quickly ran out. The next day, he moved to a new residence. In the year 1134 of the reign of Emperor Gaozong of Song, Ge served as the county magistrate of Yudan and spoke to me about this. Now, I am recounting it. I have forgotten the surname and name of County Magistrate Liu.

Ganzhou City Wall Tower In the summer of the 17th year of the Shaoxing era (1147 AD), my father moved south. I accompanied my younger brother on this journey. On August 2nd, we arrived in Ganzhou. We moored our boat under the floating bridge and climbed the city wall tower to rest briefly. The local magistrate, Zeng Qing, known as Duanbo and Zao, came to see us. He said, "This is not suitable for lodging. Only the Yugu Tower would be appropriate, but Zhou Kangzhou has already occupied it. We should leave tomorrow. For tonight, you can stay here." That night, my father set up a bed in the center, and my brothers and I slept beside him. At midnight, I got up to change my clothes and went out through the northern side gate. I saw a man combing his hair, with his hair hanging down to the ground. At the time, two servants were staying inside the gate, named Wang San and Cheng Qi. I thought they were the two men, but when I called out, they did not respond. Returning to look inside, they were sleeping as before, which made me suspicious. I went out again, and the man was still combing his hair. He faced the parapet, with most of his feet hanging outside. The wind blew through his hair, making it stand up. My heart was stirred, and I returned to my bed. The next day, my father asked where I had gone at night. He heard from the gatekeeper that the tower was known for its strange occurrences, and every night it was said to be haunted. I recounted what I had seen the previous night. That day, we moved to the Yugu Tower, but I could not sleep all night. I also heard that when Zhou Kangzhou was staying in the tower, someone opened the double doors of the room and walked out. They thought it was a thief and called their son to chase after it, but the doors remained as they were.

A Poem by a Minor Official Li Mo, residing in Wuxi, was just drinking with his guests when a Taoist monk knocked on the door and said, "I have come from Mount Qingcheng. Master Liu Gao Shang has sent me to see you. He wishes to speak with you." The gatekeeper replied, "Master Bao Wen is currently drinking and does not wish to be disturbed." The monk persisted, but Li Mo refused to see him. The monk, displeased, said, "Give me a pen. I wish to write down my name." The gatekeeper handed him a pen, and the monk wrote on the door: "The sun turns the shadows of the courtyard locust trees, moving gradually. Through the heavy doors and multiple rooms, the calls are delayed. It is better to wave the sleeves and pass through the clouds. Tell the falling flowers and flowing water." After finishing, the monk left and said, "This is what I meant by 'Minor Official.'" Upon hearing this, Li Mo was filled with regret and self-doubt for several days. Recorded by Li Lun.

The Son of the Hu Family A man from Shuzhou named Hu Yongfu recounted that his uncle, Hu Qin, served as the deputy governor of Shu. After arriving at his post, Hu Qin visited the back garden one day, where he saw a small house at the corner of a wall, with curtains hanging as if it were a shrine. An old soldier emerged and bowed, saying, "The daughter of the previous deputy governor, who was eighteen years old, died unmarried and was buried here. She has now left to serve in another official position." Hu Qin asked about her appearance, and the soldier replied, "I have no knowledge of her appearance, but I have heard from people that since the time of the previous governors and down to the officials of various households, no woman has been as beautiful as she was." At the time, Hu Yongfu, the youngest son of the Hu family, was just coming of age and had not yet married. Hearing this, his heart was stirred. He pointed to the incense on the table and said, "This is also too cold and desolate." The next day, he took an incense burner and a flower vase to offer there. He privately poured wine and made an offering, hoping to see her. From then on, he went there every day, driven by his utmost sincerity, which manifested even in his dreams. After more than two months, one day he went there again, and the curtain of the house moved slightly, as if someone were calling out. Suddenly, a woman dressed in splendid attire emerged, her beauty captivating. Hu Yongfu knew what was meant and went straight to her. She said, "Do not fear me. I am the one in the room. I am moved by your affection and so have come." Hu Yongfu was so surprised and delighted that he almost went mad. He immediately accompanied her into the room, and they did not leave until after midnight. From then on, this became their daily routine. Hu Yongfu neglected his studies entirely, and his family rarely saw his face. He no longer ventured into the garden, and only his spirit was depleted, and his appetite diminished. His parents secretly worried about him and confided in the soldier who guarded the house. The soldier reported that at night, Hu Yongfu was whispering and laughing with someone. When called upon, Hu Yongfu dared not hide the truth and told them what had happened. His parents said, "This is a ghost. We must deal with this for you."

Confucius said, "That is not so." Since we met, I was initially quite skeptical. Now it has been some time, and I have observed her daily activities and behavior. Her speech and actions are not a single thing different from others. How can she be a ghost? The parents said, "Then what is unusual about her?" She replied, "But every time food is set out, she never picks up a chopstick. She only drinks alcohol and eats fruit." The parents said, "Wait until she returns and make her eat. I will observe it myself." The son went back inside, and the woman arrived. He ordered food to be prepared and invited her. Even after repeated invitations, she would not. She said, "Usually coming and going without any hindrance. Now eating this, the body is somehow affected. Cannot return." The son insisted again. Reluctantly, she picked up a chopstick. The parents came in from outside. The woman suddenly stood up and tried to hide, but her form could not be hidden. Feeling embarrassed and awkward, she cried and bowed to apologize. The Hu family gathered around her and asked about her situation. She said, "Also did not realize it herself. Before, if she wanted to come, she came. If she wanted to leave, she left. Did not expect it to be like this now." They asked, "Since she cannot leave, is she now a human or a ghost?" She said, "The body is still here. If she stays, she is a human." If you do not believe, please open the grave to verify. As they said, they broke the tomb. Saw that the coffin had a gap that could fit a finger. Was completely empty. The Hu family was very happy. Said, "Destiny is like this. She should be our family's wife." Arranged for her to stay in a separate room. Chose a careful and kind servant to serve her. Sent a messenger to inform her family. And presented the dowry. Her father sent his eldest son and family members to see her. It is truly my daughter. And the wedding ceremony was completed, and she left. Later, she gave birth to several children. And they still exist. The woman's surname is Zhao. Said by Li Deyuan. Forgot the name of the state and the name of the Hu family son.

**The Street-Roaming Tiger** Zhao Qingxian, whose father was Yuanqing, served as the magistrate of a certain county in the eastern region. There was a woman who was notorious for filing frivolous lawsuits, causing great trouble in the county. She was called the "Street-Roaming Tiger." She regarded beating as scratching, showing her callousness. Although Zhao knew about her, he had never intended to deal with her. One day, she came to the court with a lawsuit. After questioning, she was found to be at fault, and Zhao struck her with a stick. She died after eight strikes. That day, she appeared as a malevolent spirit, following him wherever he went, whether he was walking, sitting, or lying down. Even in his food and drink, her presence was visible. Zhao found this extremely distressing. After he was dismissed from his post, he passed through Mount Tai to pay homage. The female ghost followed him as before. When he reached the temple, he burned incense and bowed twice, but she still stood beside him. Zhao held his tablet and prayed, saying, "Yuanqing was appointed to govern the county, and it is my duty to hear lawsuits. This woman acted recklessly, and according to the law, she should have been beaten. She died before the punishment was completed. This happened by chance, not because I was excessively cruel and killed her. However, she has become a malevolent spirit, causing trouble for years. Even before a great deity, she shows no fear. The deity is wise and upright. I hope that the deity can make it clear who is at fault. If the fault lies with me, I will not escape blame. If not, then the deity should not allow her to cause me such prolonged suffering." After finishing his prayer, he bowed again and stood up. The ghost then disappeared. This was recounted by Zhao's grandson, Tian.

In the second year of the Zhenhe era, Li Xiaoshou served as the governor of Kaifeng. He governed with a strict and severe hand, and within the capital city, no one dared to criticize his policies. There was a wandering scholar staying at an inn by the Bian River who suddenly fell ill and remained unconscious for several days. He then abruptly woke up and asked someone, "Is the governor well?" The person replied, "He is fine." The scholar said, "I am going to die." He then recounted that while he was delirious, he saw an indistinct door on the wall, which gradually became clearer. The door was adorned with golden decorations and vermilion pillars, and he found himself standing beside it. He pushed the door open and entered a grand and splendid courtyard, resembling a fine government office, but it was eerily empty. He wandered there for a long time and heard music playing in the hall. The sound grew louder, and hundreds of female entertainers emerged from behind the screen, each holding musical instruments, dressed in splendid attire. They surrounded a nobleman dressed in gold and purple, who was riding in a cool carriage, directly to the main hall. The music played loudly, filling the air with noise. The nobleman sat down, and the female entertainers arranged themselves around him. Suddenly, a clap was heard, and they all transformed into beings with ox heads and other monstrous forms, with strange and ugly appearances, terrifying and shocking to behold. The chair he was sitting on turned into a large iron bed, and the musical instruments made of gold, stone, strings, and bamboo became weapons and tools for stabbing and drilling. A hundred ghosts rushed forward, stripping and tearing his clothes, cutting and burning him.

Suffering the utmost torment, I called out in pain, twisting and writhing, unbearable to look at. For some time like this, I clapped my hands, and the ghost transformed back into a woman. The bed turned back into a carriage, and the spears turned back into musical instruments. The respected person was dressed in fine clothes as before, and music was played as they entered. I had no direction to go, so I went alone to a small room under the eaves to stay, no longer knowing day or night. I estimated it to be about a day. What I saw was again like this, and this happened three times. Gradually, I became accustomed and less fearful, and began to observe from the side. A ghost suddenly looked back and said, "Who are you? Always come here. Will you trouble me?" The ghost drove me out and closed the door, and thus I regained life. The respected person I saw was actually Mr. Yin. At that time, Mr. Xiao Shou was still well, but soon after, he fell ill and was then appointed as the head of the Liquequan Temple. He died exactly one month later. When Mr. Xiao Shou was in charge of the capital, he especially paid attention to thieves and bandits. There was a very fast white horse that was about to enter the court, but it was stolen by someone. Guards were dispatched to watch at the city gates, and after five days, someone posted a notice on the door saying, "The white horse has been dyed black and has already traveled a thousand miles." Because the thief had already obtained the horse, he dyed its fur and mane and rode it out, so it cannot be captured. The following year, the R family from Puzhou sent a messenger with a gift. When the basket was opened, the horse skin was inside. How cunning and deceitful the thief was.

**Eight Pieces of Brocade** In the seventh year of the Zhenghe era (1117), Li Simu, also known as Mida, served as the Chief of the Court Records. A man wished to become a personal attendant. The staffing of the two ministries (likely the Secretariat and the Chancellery) was traditionally limited, and he could not accommodate the man. He refused him and sent him away. The man said, "I have my own means of livelihood and can support my wife and children. I am willing to wait in the palace, but I do not need the salary." He was then allowed to serve. He arrived early for court and left late, never leaving his post for even a moment. Even on days off, he remained at his post. No one had ever seen where he ate or slept. Li Simu praised his diligence and called out to him, saying, "Personal attendants in the two ministries are called 'fetchers and bearers.' Whenever they dismount from their horses and return home, they scatter and do not look back. How is it that you alone remain so steadfast?" The man replied, "I do not enjoy playing and idling. Moreover, I am already a low-ranking official. In my duties, I must act as I do." Li Simu, who was naturally sparing in his enjoyment of music and color, often stayed alone in his residence. He imitated the practices of Daoist adepts, such as the bear-hugging and bird-stretching exercises. The man found this amusing and could not stop laughing. Li Simu was surprised and asked him why he was laughing. The man replied, "I am dull-witted and rustic. I have never seen such things before, so I cannot help but laugh. It is nothing else." Later, at night, the man laughed again. Li Simu thought he was mocking him and scolded him, saying, "I am learning the methods of longevity and peace. You do not understand, so why do you laugh?"

Why have you laughed so often? This person merely apologized. Later, it reached three times, and his laughter was the same as before. At this point, I began to suspect him. I got off the bed and asked seriously: "Since your arrival, I have always known that you are different from others. Now, the reason for your laughter—there must be a cause. I wish you would clearly tell me." He replied, "I am just a fool. What do I understand?" I persisted in asking. After hesitating for a long time, he finally said, "I am not one of the common, mediocre people who seek food. My teacher is Master Wang of Mount Song. He pities that fewer and fewer people in the world seek the Way and pursue the True. He wishes to teach the sincere, simple, and respectful people. He sent me to the capital to search for you. Three years have passed since then. Yesterday, I saw the *sheren* on horseback. His bearing was elegant and graceful, and it seemed he had the bones of the Dao and was teachable. So I pretended to be your servant, to observe what you did. When I watched what you did at night, it was the way to quick death, yet you think it is the way to long life and peace. Is this not greatly laughable?" Sili Ju listened to his words and was so ashamed that he broke out in a sweat. He put on his formal clothes and bowed twice to him, treating him with the rites of a teacher. This person accepted immediately without hesitation. After sitting down, Sili Ju raised his hands in greeting and asked a question. This person gave only a general overview. As to the profound and subtle parts, he said, "This matter is beyond my ability." He said, "I will return to report to Master Wang. I will return in half a year." At dawn, he left without saying goodbye. The next May, Sili Ju was appointed governor of Guangzhou. He never saw him again in his lifetime. Shen Du, the public official, said this in a refined manner.

**The Indestructible Body** The physician, Chief Medical Officer Nè, lived in a high street of the capital city. He was renowned for his medical skills and accumulated a fortune in the millions. In his later years, he bought a famous garden outside the city and kept ten concubines, completely neglecting the principles of health and hygiene. However, every morning, he would recite several scrolls of the Diamond Sutra. Three years after his death, the Jurchens invaded the capital, opened his tomb, took out his golden belt and clothes, and left his body on the side of the road. When the chaos was over, his son Nè repaired the tomb and saw a mummified body lying to the left of the tomb, which appeared as if still alive, with hardly any damage. Thus understanding that the saying of the indestructible body is not an empty claim. Nè specialized in obstetrics and held the position of a military governor in a distant prefecture. He was captured by the enemy and taken to the household of Prince Chen Wushisi, where he told this story to his father.

Huang Shijie Huang Shijie, a native of Jiangling in Jianzhou, had a mother named Yu. She dreamt that a person held a provincial examination list and announced, "Your son will get an official position." The mother replied, "My son does not read books. How can he get one?" The dreamer said, "It is already determined by fate." He then showed her the list, which revealed the name Huang Guangbi. The mother said, "My eldest son, Shian, has already become a Daoist. The younger one is named Shijie, but there is no such person on the list." The dreamer replied, "Change his name and use the character 'Huan' as his style name." The mother wrote this on the wall but did not say anything. In the fourth year of Shaoxing (1134), Huang Shijie wanted to take the autumn examination. His mother said, "If he has never studied, there will only be the cost of traveling back and forth. It is not possible." Huang Shijie told his uncle about this. The uncle spoke on his behalf. The mother said, "If he must go, he must change his name. If the name is not changed, he cannot take the exam." The uncle said to Huang Shijie, "Your mother's vision is like this. It cannot be disobeyed." He then prepared paper and pen to ask his mother. The mother immediately wrote "Huang Guangbi" with the style name "Huan." Huang Shijie was indeed recommended and passed the examination the following year. His eldest brother, Shian, later became known as Dacheng. I once met him in the southern regions. Dacheng said.

Two Thieves Died Themselves My cousin, Guaan, was an inspector for the Mining and Smelting Office during the eighteenth year of the Shaoxing era (1148 CE). He traveled from Poyang to Xin Prefecture with a certain low-ranking official from the county. When they reached Yugan, my cousin's relative was the magistrate. He entertained the attendants with wine and food. The low-ranking official, having eaten nothing, drank several cups and became drunk, unable to stand. My cousin continued on ahead, waiting for him all day without his arrival. After three days, he sent a messenger back to inquire, but the official could not be found along the way. The official had a large stone inkstone that he cherished greatly, often keeping it at his waist. On that day, while drunk, he was walking when two people saw the object at his waist and suspected it was silver. They killed him, only to find that it was not silver. They then wrapped his body in a rush mat and threw it into the river. No one knew about it. The following year, the two thieves were together in one place, causing disturbances during the day as if they were arguing with someone. They said, "We indeed mistakenly killed you. We were wrong." They told the bystanders about the incident from the previous year, and upon returning home, they both died.

Liu Zhengyan In the early years of the Xuanhe era, Liu Fa, a great general of Shaanxi, fell in battle against the Western Xia. The imperial court showed great compassion to his family, granting them a residence in the capital. His son, Zhengyan, after completing his mourning period, moved his family from Hezhong to live there. The house had a hundred rooms, and one room in the western wing had long been haunted by ghosts. Whenever the side gate opened, one would always see a man in a purple robe and golden seal, wearing a headpiece in the style of the Tang dynasty, wandering about inside. A small child stood respectfully behind him. The ghost also frequently appeared in the main hall of the house, appearing and disappearing, causing harm to people. A certain cousin by marriage of Zhengyan, who had always valued courage and boldness and was fearless, decided to investigate the mystery alone. He wrote a letter of introduction and went to visit, placing it outside the gate. After a short while, the door opened by itself. The purple-robed figure held a tablet and invited the guest in, serving tea and conversing with him. His manner and conduct were remarkably dignified. The guest asked, "What dynasty are you from? Why do you live here?" The ghost replied, "I have lived here for three hundred years. In the Tang dynasty, I was actually the military governor of Bianzhou and Songzhou. Because I did not uphold my loyalty as a minister until the end, my entire clan of three hundred people were executed here. Until now, I still lament it, but even though I regret it, it is too late." The guest said, "So many years have passed. Why do you still remain in the ghost registry?" The ghost replied, "My sins are very grave. Rebirth is truly difficult."

Not getting a traitor like me to take my place, it is not easy to escape. The guest said, "For your sake, I will seek blessings from the Buddhist tradition. We will perform the Water and Land Dharma Assembly to save souls and provide assistance on the path to the afterlife. What do you think?" He replied, "It is of no use." Nevertheless, let’s try it. The guest returned and told Zheng Yan. On another day, Zheng Yan summoned a monk to build a Dharma assembly hall in the main hall. As soon as night fell, the person in purple robes sat on a trapeze-like chair and observed, while the young boy stood by the table. No one failed to see. Only the monk was afraid. He shook a pestle while reciting the spell to subdue ghosts and spirits. As soon as he spoke, the person in purple robes had already noticed. Shouting loudly to the young boy, he said, "Take the life and go." The young boy hurried forward, and the monk fell to the ground as if struck by something. He then said, "I indeed killed you. I burned your bones, stored the ashes in a bag, and hung it in the gap below the third level of bricks in the pagoda. No one knew about it. Now I dare not hide. I wish to let me go." After a while, he woke up. The person in purple robes and the young boy were both gone. When asked, he did not know what he had said. This young boy was probably beaten to death by the monk. After death, he followed the person in purple robes. The monk saw him and therefore feared. Until the Jianyan period, Zheng Yan was finally executed for treason.

Wang Dunren During the reign of Emperor Gaozong, the Director Hu Ruming, whose courtesy name was Shunzhi, commanded Guangxi. He had disagreements over official duties with the Transport Commissioner Lu Yuan. A government official named Xu Yu incurred Hu's displeasure and was beaten and expelled. Xu secretly sought out Hu's faults to inform on him to Lu. He discovered a case involving financial discrepancies in the purchase of horses in Yongzhou and impeached Hu to the court. The former prime minister Qin Hui inserted his words. In the thirteenth year of the Shaoxing era, the Chief Judge Yuan Nan and Yan Yang were sent as imperial commissioners to investigate the case. In that same month of June, Hu was arrested and sent to the authorities. All the witnesses of the time were also arrested. Xu Yu faced trial as well. Only a few days later, Hu died in prison. The two commissioners feared the consequences and kept it secret, preventing it from being said. They pretended to summon a doctor into the office and instructed the doctor Wang Dunren to certify that Hu's illness was severe. They carried Hu out. Xu Yu was also allowed to return home. Before he arrived, he suddenly gathered his clothes, bowed his body, and bowed to the air, saying, "The Director is here." He became ill immediately and died upon arriving home.

Three years later in the sixth month, Dunren died of a boil on his back. According to his family's statement, he said, "I recently entered the prison to visit Hu Dazhi. At that time, he was already dead. I feared the responsibility of the Sicheng, so I falsely claimed that his illness was very severe, to the extent of eight-tenths. I should have taken Zhongru, but when the medicine arrived, there was no one to give it to. I drank it myself and left, which led to his冤 (injustice) not being made right. Now I must face him in the underworld." Lü Yuan succeeded him and lived in Hengzhou. Just before his death, he warned his children to arrange affairs after his death. He pointed to his coffin and said, "Enter here to see Hu Dazhi. At that time, great expense was involved in explaining this." He finally did not recover. Furthermore, when Hu Gong was in prison, he was allowed to accompany himself with a maid. Later, she married in Guilin. During the day, everyone saw Hu coming from outside. He said, "You must urgently prove my冤 (injustice)." The maid replied, "The official has ordered it. How dare I not follow?" Hu was pleased and left. The maid fully informed her husband. She was about to change clothes and take a bath, but before she could, she died.

Fangbo's Verse Fangbo, the wet nurse of the Liang family from Dongping, was a native of Zizhou. She served as the wet nurse for Xuanyi Lang Yuanming. Throughout her life, she ate only vegetarian food and was extremely simple-minded, unable to argue with her peers. The wife of the household, Lady Zhao, was interested in Chan Buddhism, and Fangbo was always by her side. She could only recite the name of Amitabha Buddha, but she was very sincere and did not stop, even though she did not use prayer beads. No one knows how many millions of times she recited it. In the eighteenth year of the Shaoxing era, at the age of seventy-two, she fell ill with diarrhea and could not get out of bed. Yet her recitation became even more sincere, and suddenly, as if nothing was wrong, she chanted a verse: "The path to the West is good for cultivation. There are no mountains or pits above or below. When you leave, you don't need to wear shoes or socks. Your feet tread on lotus flowers, step by step." She recited it continuously. Someone asked who said this, and she replied, "I composed it." They asked, "When can I go, grandma?" She said, "At the hour of Shen." Indeed, she died at that time, on the fifth day of the tenth month. She was cremated according to Buddhist rites until completely consumed, but only her tongue did not burn, like a lotus flower. This account was recorded by Yuanming's son and friend.

The *Yijian Yi Zhi* Volume Ten, Story Twelve Zhang Rui the Physician Zhang Rui, a military commander and physician from Chengzhou with the courtesy name Zijiang, was renowned for his medical skills and lived in Zhengzhou. During the Zhenghe period, a pregnant granddaughter of Lord Puyu fell ill at the expected time of delivery. The official physicians diagnosed her condition as a yang syndrome of typhoid fever and were afraid of miscarriage, so they hesitated to administer cooling medicines. Lord Puyu secretly invited Zhang Rui to treat her. Zhang Rui said, "The child is due to be born soon. How could any medicine harm it?" He administered medication according to standard procedures and doubled the dosage. Within half a day, the child was born, and the illness disappeared. The next day, the woman experienced severe diarrhea and had difficulty swallowing. Other doctors pointed out the flaws in the treatment and argued that the two conditions were as contradictory as ice and fire, especially since she had just given birth. Even if the legendary physician Bian Que were to be reborn, there would be no way to save her. Zhang Rui reassured them, saying, "There is no need to worry. The patient will recover on the same day." He gave her several pills, and her throat immediately returned to normal, and the diarrhea stopped. By the time she had reached her full month, Lord Puyu hosted a banquet to celebrate the successful treatment.

From among the sons, grandsons, daughters, wives, and sons-in-law, there were sixty people in total. They invited Rui as a guest. The host personally poured wine to wish him a long life and said, "Your skill is divine. I dare not comprehend it. I dare to ask, how can one medicine cure two ailments?" Rui replied, "This is not recorded in the classics. It is simply done by intuition. What was used earlier was the Fu Zi Li Zhong Wan, wrapped with Purple Snow. When the throat is closed and not passable, only extremely cold medicines are effective. Once it has been swallowed, it completely dissolves and goes. What reaches the abdomen is the power of Aconite. Thus, one dose cures both ailments." The host greatly admired and was amazed, and he collected all the gold spoons and chopsticks from the table and gave them as a gift. Murong Yanfeng, when he was an official in charge of the emperor's daily affairs, had his mother fall ill. He also summoned Rui to Zheng. By the time Rui arrived, she had already died. It was hot at the time. Rui wanted to enter and examine, but Murong could not bear it, thinking that he might be seeking money. He said, "The cost of travel."

When all debts are fully repaid, there is no need for you to come in. Rui said, "According to the methods of treating fevers, there are those who die and then revive after a day and night. Why not take a look?" Reluctantly, he was invited in. Rui lifted the face cloth and examined closely. He called the undertaker and said, "Have you ever seen a dead person in summer with a red face?" The undertaker replied, "No." "Was the mouth open?" "No." "Then it was no sweat and fainting. He will not die. Do not rush to bury him." Hastening out, Rui fetched the medicine. He ordered to boil half of it with two liters of water and administered it to the patient. He warned, "Guard it well. Until midnight, there will be a major diarrhea, and he will live." Rui stayed at an inn outside. At midnight, the caregiver felt a sound of bubbling. The patient had excreted, filling the seat with waste, and expelled impure substances in a quantity of more than a dou. The whole family rejoiced. They knocked on the door and called for Rui. Rui responded, "I am too tired today to get up. But it is not necessary to get up. Only tomorrow can medicine be administered." It was almost dawn.

Directly ordered a carriage to return to Zheng. Murong visited his room. He only left a single prescription of *Pingzhou San*. The mother took it. After several days, she recovered well. Likely due to suspicion of sharp anger and money, he did not inform and left. During the Southern Song period, he entered Sichuan. Wang Ju, Uncle Jian, asked him, "How many of your skills are truly the ancient so-called 'ten perfections'?" He replied, "Not yet. I can only manage seven or eight." My eldest son was ill. I diagnosed by examining the complexion, and all were extremely hot. I ordered to boil *Chengqi Decoction*. I wanted to drink it, but drinking again made me doubt, up to three times. I was about to drink, as if someone were pulling my elbow. I temporarily held the cup and waited. The child suddenly experienced trembling and palpitations. I covered with a cotton quilt several times. Only then did it stabilize slightly. Sweat flowed like washing. The next day, he was completely recovered. If my medicine had been taken, he would have died. How could it be considered a miracle? The world's mediocre doctors, learned only a fraction of the medical texts, thought themselves sufficient. How frightening. Wang Gu, Uncle Jian, said.

A Princess from Yuhang In the fifth year of the Xuanhe era, Tang Xindao traveled from Kuaiji to Qiantang to take the imperial examination in the Two Zhe regions. He stayed at Puji Temple. In a vacant room behind the temple, there was a traveler's coffin. Tang wished to see it, but the monk warned him, saying, "Inside is a woman. The coffin is half-open and half-closed, and she often comes out to interact with people. It is not safe to enter without several people." Tang replied, "How can there be a scholar who fears ghosts?" He finally went alone. The inscription on the coffin read, "The coffin of Princess Ji of a certain king." It had been forty years since then. Inside was a young woman, around twenty years old, with makeup and cosmetics as if freshly applied. Her appearance was no different from a living person. Tang was amazed and left. He returned to Kuaiji and told Wu Yù Cái Lǎo about the experience. Wu said, "What is there to be surprised about? I stayed in a temple in Yuhang County, and there was also a princess's coffin in the temple. Every night, she drank wine and sang with the monks, as if no one else were present. She engaged in intimate relations and returned to the coffin in the morning. The monk would always send her back. This continued for two years."

The matter gradually became known to his father. The father was furious and planned to burn him/her up. The mother dreamed that her daughter was weeping and pleading, saying, "Alas, I have died. But my fate is to unite with a monk. I know I am lewd and have brought shame upon my parents. However, I am already pregnant. If I cannot give birth to a child, I will sink into the depths of the underworld and never have a chance to escape. I wish to delay for three months to fulfill this destiny, and then I will be burned. It will not be harmful." The mother also wept upon waking and told her husband. The husband became even more angry and said, "The child is already dead, yet she associates with an ordinary monk and wishes to give birth to a child. I cannot bear this shame. We must burn her." That night, the mother and the entire family all dreamed that the daughter came to them, repeating the same plea but with greater urgency and intensity, saying it several times. The next day, they spoke together to the father. The father, a resolute man, became even angrier and did not wait for the chosen day. He immediately called the executioners, brought firewood, set fire, and chopped open the coffin to burn her. Her belly was full and rounded. Soon, it broke and split apart. Indeed, there was an infant inside, already fully formed. The story is credible.

A Golden Horse Foal Deputy Commander Guo Ziming from the capital served the household of Prince Taizong and was rewarded with the position of governor of Puzhou. He was granted a residence in Carbon Workshop Lane. One night, he heard the sound of galloping armored horses on the roof and found it strange. He sent someone to investigate and saw a horse about the size of a cat but slightly taller, running without stopping. A soldier caught it with a broom made of reeds. They brought it down and found it to be a small yellow horse, a living creature. They kept it at home, and over time, it became tame and fearless. The Guo family cherished it. During meal times, women would cut fresh grass into thin strands and feed it by hand, calling it the "Golden Horse Foal." Later, someone accidentally struck its foot, causing a slight injury, but it continued to neigh, drink, and eat as usual. One evening, someone knocked on the door and said, "Return the Deputy Commander's horse money." The guard reported this, and upon checking, they found the horse had died. When they opened the door, five hundred thousand coins lay on the ground. The Guo family took the money and buried the horse. After several years, they unearthed it and found that it had turned into a golden horse, which then transformed into copper. The injured foot had fallen off. It still exists to this day. His great-great-grandson Hui, who lives in Xinzheng, Zheng Prefecture, actually keeps it. His younger brother's son, Diao, says so.

The Lake Kou Dragon In the prefecture of Chizhou, every year, three thousand soldiers were dispatched. One general was sent to oversee the garrison in Jiangxi, and they would typically assemble in Yuzhang in May. After their rotation and rest, they would return. In the twenty-fifth year of the Shaoxing era, the commander Zhao, taking over the command, departed. After two days of travel, they docked their boat at the temple of Shunji. After completing the sacrifice, they brought courtesans into the temple to drink, as the boat was extremely hot. He ordered to set up a bed in the western wing of the blessing hall, planning to depart early the next day. The temple priest knew the god was not pleased but dared not speak plainly. He only said, "The dragon king is not in the temple; he has gone out to patrol the river. He expects to return west in one or two days. If the large army does indeed depart, there is fear of encountering each other, which would be inconvenient." Zhao, always brave and drunk, hearing the priest's words, did not believe them at all. He shouted, "What do we fear in our march?" On schedule, he beat the drum and set sail. Traveling not yet thirty miles to Lake Kou county, far away, it seemed as if a mountain was blocking the way. The boatmen were terrified, standing upright to watch it. Soon, a strong north wind arose, and white waves surged up like houses. Seeing what was previously called the mountain, it was actually a large red-spotted dragon, without head or tail, its length exactly the same as the width of the river, carrying water southward. Zhao still ordered to shoot at it. Hundreds of arrows were fired, and it came closer. Zhao began to fear, urgently turning the boat and rushing into a small harbor to avoid it. The mooring lines were just finished when the dragon passed directly in front. A cold wind blew, and in the height of summer, everyone felt as if they were wearing warm clothes. After a long time, it calmed down. Other boats capsized, dozens of them, and dozens of soldiers drowned. A wealthy merchant traveling with his relatives also drowned. At that time, my father-in-law was stationed in Jiangxi. Zhao detailed the events, only omitting the mistake in the temple.

Feng Xinshou, during the reign of Wang Gongjian Gong Shiheng, served as a judicial officer in the administrative system of the Southern Song dynasty. In the year 1157, he was retired from the position of Right Historian and returned to his hometown. He rarely interacted with guests or visitors, but only accompanied Feng in his daily walks. Feng and Feng Xinshou were very close. One day, Feng Xinshou invited Feng to sit and asked, "Your ancestral tomb is opposite ours. There should be three peaks standing in front, with a stream flowing in front of them, is that correct?" Feng was surprised and said, "Yes, how do you know this?" Feng Xinshou replied, "I am not a blind historian, but I have practiced quietness for a long time, and my mind is calm and tranquil. Sometimes I can foresee things. Not only this, but your future official positions can also be predicted. In ten years, you will hold a position in the imperial academy, serve in the imperial court, and teach in the royal palace. You will rise from a low-ranking official to a high-ranking advisor, and eventually hold the highest position in the government. Liu Dazhi is a good official, but his lifespan is nearly over, and he will only serve in this position. Wang Qianpan is an ordinary official, and neither of them can compare to you. Although I have always respected you."

Yet doubt and belief were equally divided. It was said that he had already ranked second in the imperial examination and passed. If he were to be recalled to office, he could hope to enter the Hanlin Academy. It should not have been ten years later. Soon after, Liu died in Yining. Wang, the signatory judge, also became stagnant in his career. Chu took over his position and was reassigned to a new post, and only then was he appointed as a professor in Hongzhou. He had to wait for a long time before taking up the post, which he finally did in the year Bingzi. At that time, Xin Shou was appointed as *Gishi Zhong*, and he recommended Chu. Chu was summoned and appointed as *Xiaoshu Lang*. After two months, he was promoted to Assistant *Zuo Zuo* and concurrently served as a tutor in the two princely houses. Meanwhile, Xin Shou was promoted to *Lishibu Shi Lang*. Chu went to congratulate him, and they stayed to drink, full of affection and lingering feelings. As Chu was about to mount his horse, Xin Shou said to him, "This will be the last time I see you." Chu was surprised by the ominous nature of the words. Later, he became the examiner for the *Quanshi* examination. While in the examination hall, he heard that Xin Shou had been dismissed from his post as governor of Piling due to discussing official matters. Immediately, he left the country, and thus had already missed the chance to see him. After leaving the examination hall, he sent an envoy with a letter to inquire after him. When he reached Xiaoshan, he learned that Xin Shou had already died at the county relay station. Indeed, Chu later rose from the position of *You Shi* to *Yechuan*, was sent to govern the Sichuan region, and upon returning to court, became *Tongzhi* of the *Miyuan*, where he died. When one examines what he had said, not a single detail was incorrect. Wang Gong said.

Mr. Wang In the state of Pu, there was a Mr. Wang Laoshi, who was renowned for his Daoist arts. There was a scholar in Pu who was skilled in verbal debate and wished to challenge him with his eloquence. He went to visit him. Mr. Wang's dwelling was surrounded by high walls on all sides, with only a small dog's hole for entry and exit. The scholar crawled through the hole and approached him. As they engaged in conversation, it was as if clouds were spoken. Suddenly, a whirlpool-like crack appeared underground, and soon it had grown to a foot deep, revealing a creature with scales and armor as large as a basket. Mr. Wang told the guest, "You should return quickly. If you delay, a strange disaster will surely befall you." The scholar hurriedly left. After walking less than five miles, thunder, lightning, rain, and hail suddenly arose. The horse crouched and refused to move. By chance, he found a clay room and entered to take shelter. He looked at Mr. Wang's hermitage, prostrated himself a hundred times, begging for his life, and barely escaped. Other events are mostly recorded in the *Guoshi Houpu* (National History Supplement) by Cai Tao. Ancient Pottery Vessel from Yiwu Yuming Yu Baoguang, whose courtesy name was Ruhui, was from Yiwu. In the third month of the first year of the Shaoxing era, he ordered his servant Jiang Lu to plow the garden in front of his residence to the south. Before the plowing was finished, there was a hollow sound in the earth, and the ox was startled. Jiang Lu thought there might be a hidden cellar below. He stopped plowing and dug the ground. At a depth of two feet, he found a pottery jar, six inches wide and one inch thick, with a very ancient shape. Beneath it was a round pot, which could hold three pecks of millet. It had four ears attached to the rim, each four inches in diameter. When he looked at it, its color was a deep, earthy hue. When he tapped it, it made a clear, resonant sound. He opened the jar and looked inside, but it was empty. After washing off the dirt, he placed it on a table. No one could tell what dynasty it belonged to. When guests came, he would use it to serve wine. Yu Baoguang's son, Yu Liangneng, once wrote an essay about the ancient pottery vessel, which still exists today.

Dreaming of a Woman Engaging in Literary Duels On the thirteenth day of the leap tenth month of the year Ding Si during the reign of Emperor Gaozong of Song, the night of the thirteenth, I, Yu Shuqi, stayed at the southern study of my residence. In a dream, I was taken by a friend to a place where the windows were veiled in clouds and mist, and the rooms were secluded and beautifully decorated, charming and elegant, like the quarters of a courtesan. A young woman, just reaching the age of marriageability, had a graceful and alluring beauty, and her attire was elegant and refined. The bed was covered with a soft carpet and cushions, and the fragrance of orchids and musk was pervasive. The screens, tables, and other furnishings were finely arranged, all beautifully proportioned and harmonious. We sat there for a long time. The woman looked at me and said, "I have a riddle for you. Would you like to engage in a literary duel?" I agreed. She then said, "The supreme deity gave birth to a person of 'Xizhun.' Seeing a fish, one touches it." After she finished, she handed a piece of paper to my friend to write down the riddle. She then changed the character '人' to '才.' I asked how to write 'Xizhun.' She replied, "It is written with the character '酉' and the character '寸.'" She then asked, "What does 'Xizhun' mean?" I replied, "It means a person who is elegant and refined." She then asked, "What does 'seeing a fish and touching it' mean?" I replied, "It is similar to saying 'seeing a trick and hitting back.'" Since I was preparing for the imperial examinations and had the ambition of achieving success, I did not want to focus on such matters. Instead, I responded with another riddle: "Primordial energy is concentrated in the legendary sword 'Tai'e.' Encountering a tiger, one must fight." The woman looked at me intently and smiled. She then wanted me to compose a poem, but before she could speak, I woke up. The rooster had already crowed. These two events were narrated by Yu Shuqi.

In the strange and unusual place of Minqing County, Fujian Province, there is a large stream near a village. North of the stream stands a temple, while south of the stream lies a vast mountain and a long valley. The grass and trees stretch out in all directions. According to the elders, since ancient times, no human has ever ventured into this area. Those who do encounter strange and unusual things. There were three monks who came from elsewhere; all of them enjoyed seeking out secluded and beautiful places. They were eager to go there and prepared dry rations together. They took a small boat to cross to the other side, planning to stay for three nights. Soon after they began their journey, they encountered snakes and poisonous snakes everywhere, which they had to step over to pass. Strange birds with ugly appearances chirped and chattered loudly, causing fear upon sight. Within half a day, two of the monks wished to return. One monk alone stood up and said, "Monks regard death as trivial; how much more so, then, for fear?" He decided to go alone and took what the other two had brought. He walked through the grass and slept under the open sky, going further south. On the second day, the number of snakes and birds decreased, and a path could be found. On the third day, he also felt tired and suffering. From afar, he saw smoke rising from the tree tops at the foot of the mountain, indicating that there were people living there.

The monk retraced his steps to the previous location. He found a thatched house and, finding no one there, took a rest. Gathering fallen leaves, he lit them as a fire. Shortly after, a person arrived from outside, carrying a hoe, placed it over the door, and moved closer to the fire. Upon seeing him, it was clear that he was a person. They did not engage in conversation. From his sleeve, he pulled out ten yams, cooked them, and offered half to the monk, eating the other half himself. By evening, he lay directly on the earthen bed, and the monk also stayed for the night. They never inquired about each other's identities. At dawn, the person had already left, and the monk returned the same way. Along the path, he marked every place. Upon reaching the temple, he told the two people everything he had seen. The two regretted returning empty-handed the previous day and decided to search again together. After three days, nothing differed from what was previously marked. When they reached the large tree, the thatched house had been burned down. Only the bark was cut off the wood for a few inches, and a poem was written on it: "Happens to meet with the clouds and water, does not communicate with the clouds and water. The clouds disperse and the water flows away, the sky and earth become vast and empty. Returns with a sense of disappointment. Afterward, no one can go there." This was said by He De Xian.

Nest, the Hermit In the eighth year of the Shaoxing era, there was a Taoist hermit in Wuxi County named Nest from Meishan. He lived to be 117 years old. In his youth, he had associations with the famous brothers Su Shi and Su Zhe. Although he appeared very old, his face was not wrinkled or darkened, and his eyes shone with a bright blue light. He could drink and eat more than an ordinary person. His maternal uncle, Doctor of the Imperial Academy Shen Ti Ren, lived in a village called Gao Village, about ten miles away from the county. Nest would often visit his uncle, staying for two nights each time. He claimed that when he was 30, he met a strange person who predicted that he would not live long and that his lifespan would end at 55 years. The strange person then taught him a secret method, instructing him to keep track of the specific date and time. When the time came, he was to enter a quiet room and walk in the shape of the Big Dipper constellation, lying down with his hair loose under the constellation of the Big Dipper. This, he believed, would save him from death. From then on, he experienced significant hardships every 15 years, and he had to go through this cycle five times. If he reached 120 years old, he would achieve immortality. He began practicing in the Tianqing Temple in Suzhou, believing he would die on the night of the 16th day of the first lunar month. Following the strange person's instructions, he fasted for one day and borrowed an empty room from a Taoist priest, claiming he was practicing breath control.

Hiding oneself in a secluded place, during the daytime, one already sees a multitude of ghosts. It seems as if they are being pursued. By midnight, the visitors become more frequent, moving around inside the room, to the point of stepping on hair and skin. Yet their bodies are very light, and they cannot press down. People all sigh and shout, saying, "They must be here. Why can't we see them? If we don't capture them tonight, our sins will not be forgiven. What shall we do?" That night was restless until almost dawn, quiet and nothing heard. Then dared to come out. All four times like this, what was seen was the same.
