This year will be the same, do not know if we can finally escape. By November 21st, informed all the good people in the town. Returned to the lodging and closed the door. After three days, people were surprised he did not come out. Opened the door to see him, he was already dead, with a white streak on his nose. Do not know on what day he died. Is it not the great limit of fate, cannot be escaped after all? Su Huangmen wrote the biography of Cao Gong, stating that he died in Lingnan. Is this the same person? It is a pity that no one asked about it. My uncle said.

Pine Cones In the winter of 1138, my brothers and I were staying at the family temple in Wuxi, located in the Wudianshu area, to mourn our deceased mother. The temple was surrounded by 20,000 large pine trees. The following spring, two of these pines each produced a pine cone. The pines were about 15-20 meters tall, and the pine cones grew at their tops, surrounded by lush green leaves, forming a naturally beautiful scene. The temple monk, Shao Ming, remarked that pines near the tomb of the Bì family in the nearby village had also produced pine cones in the same manner, though smaller. The grandson of the Bì family, a scholar named Anye, had been recommended for an advanced position. Now, the two pine cones on our temple's pines were larger, leading to the question: Could this be a sign that the Sēn family would have two sons pass the imperial examinations? At the time, my brothers-in-law, Sēn Zìqiáng and Sēn Zìqiú, were just about to take the imperial examination, but they did not succeed. However, my older brother and second brother passed the special examination for scholars with extensive knowledge and eloquence in the year 1142 (Rénxū). It was during their preparation for this examination that they were studying at the temple. Thus, the two pine cones were seen as a good omen.

Liang Yuanming My son-in-law, Liang Yuanming, once had a dream where he entered the underworld. An official of the underworld ordered him to go to a department to answer charges. He was warned, "When you return home, do not reveal this to anyone, not even your parents or wife. If you break this command, your entire family will be destroyed." Liang bowed twice and accepted the command. Those who followed led him back, passing through the gate of Hell. They led him to a cauldron of boiling water, where they saw jailers prodding prisoners with long forks and placing them in the cauldron. The bones and flesh of the prisoners were rotting, and the foul odor offended the nose, much like the smell of gourd soup in the human world. That night, he woke from the dream and told his wife. She also dared not ask what he had been accused of. Since then, Liang has refused to eat gourd soup, saying that the smell makes him vomit. Three years later, while traveling from Guilin to Mount Heng, he passed through Lingling and encountered another person's funeral procession. The funeral banner read, "Liang Xuanyi, Military Secretary of Hanyang." When asked about his hometown, the person said he was from Dongping. It turned out that Liang Yuanming had recently been transferred to the position of secretary in Hanyang. The details of his hometown, official title, and surname matched. He deeply disliked this coincidence. He failed to reach his post and died. This occurred in the fourteenth year of the Shaoxing era.

*Yijian Zhi, Volume 11, Story 13* **Jade Flower Attendant** A man from Putian, surnamed Fang and known as the Censor, lost his name. In the early years of the Jinghe era, he served as the magistrate of Wuyuan County in Shezhou. Suffering from a fever, he lay exhausted. Suddenly, he heard the sound of heavenly music in his ears. Soon, twenty-four girls arrived, each holding flags, banners, and pennants. Suddenly, clouds of various colors rose from their feet, and they flew and ascended gracefully. In an instant, they arrived in a city. The city had a grand and clean building. The door was inscribed with golden characters saying "Taihua Palace." A couch was set up in the center for him to sit on. Maids stood in rows. A long-bearded Taoist priest arrived on a cloud, wearing a blue crown and a colorful robe, holding a jade tablet. He bowed twice in front of the man. The man woke up in surprise and wanted to respond. The priest bowed with clasped hands and said, "I am a servant of the gentleman." He asked the man to sit calmly and accept. After finishing his respectful bow, he knelt and said, "The Jade Flower Palace in the Blue Void Cave respectfully greets the gentleman. It has been a long time since we last met on the Jade Terrace. Many years have passed on Earth. We hope to meet again soon and look forward to hearing good news." The man was confused and did not know what to say. The priest said, "The earthly world is impure and corrupt. It can change a person's heart and mind. The gentleman should have forgotten his past. Now I will recount the details. The gentleman was a contemporary of Empress Wu Zetian of the Tang Dynasty. He was born in Jizhou and was able to write."

He was fond of wine and unrestrained in his drinking, drifting up and down in the neighborhood. At a time when a great plague struck Hebei, the dead lay like tangled hemp. The scholar wrote down the prescriptions he had obtained and posted them in the main thoroughfares. Those who followed the prescriptions were immediately cured. From this, his reputation spread even wider, and the number of people he saved was beyond counting. In a dream, someone told him, "Your hidden virtue has reached heaven. The Supreme Deity has praised your deeds and will summon you to the ranks of the immortals." The scholar had always been destitute and, believing himself destined to become a celestial being, became even more reckless and indulgent. In the end, he died from falling into a well while drunk. After a long time had passed since his death, his previous merits were recognized, and he was summoned to appear at the White Jade Pavilion — a place where the famous Tang poet Li He once recorded a similar tale. At that time, four people were summoned together, and they were to compose one essay. The Emperor himself set the topic: "On the Great Way of Non-Action," a regulated prose essay (fu). The scholar had a striking line that read: "The Emperor bored holes and lost his soul; the snake drew extra feet and lost the cup." The Emperor read it and was greatly pleased, elevating him to first place and appointing him as the Literary Official, dedicating him to the task of writing. He was later given the title of Jade Bloom Attendant, joining eighteen fellow officials, all senior immortals of the Upper Clarity school. He would serve the Emperor's side, and when going out, he would accompany the golden chariot. Once in the morning, he visited the Purple Mist Palace.

The palace attendants did not know the sedan chair had arrived. Some might have woken up late, having just drawn one eyebrow, they rushed out to greet and pay homage. The emperor looked at them and smiled, ordering the officials to compose poetry. The scholar's concluding lines said: "In the morning makeup, unaware the imperial carriage had arrived. Only drawing one eyebrow on the wall of the human world." The emperor recited and praised it enthusiastically. The scholar was eventually demoted to the outer supervisor of the Jade Pavilion due to his arrogance and reliance on his talent and favor, which caused jealousy among many. After bidding farewell at the palace steps, the emperor said, "The Jade Pavilion is my repository of books. Not because of your extraordinary literary talent, it is difficult to reside here." After that, the banquets and audiences became less frequent. One day, the emperor and the immortals toured the Jade Garden, thinking of the scholar's talent, and sent a messenger to summon him. The scholar excused himself with illness, and alone with the maidservant Song Dao Hua, they boated on the pond, holding hands affectionately, with thoughts of human husband and wife. They were accused by the messenger, and the emperor responded in his edict: "The man becomes a neighbor's son, the woman becomes a neighbor's daughter. Both were demoted to the human world." Song Dao Hua was born in Shu, while the scholar was from Min. The scholar had already passed the imperial examinations and served as the magistrate of Shaowu. The emperor ordered his return, but some who did not enjoy it reported, "In the celestial region of Shaowu, the fiery energy is intense."

The need for this person's divine essence to calm it. Only then does it stop. Recently, there was an edict to restore the former place after one generation. Mo Zhenjun, who replaced the master as the Attendant of the Imperial Academy, feared that the mortal world was easily corruptible. If there were other faults, then the staircase to immortality would become even more difficult to ascend. Therefore, he sent a disciple to come, earnestly conveying the message. Song Dao Hua, who had already returned to the right path, held a precious banner and stood by the side, bowing and saying, "The world of mortals is truly full of confusion and suffering. If one could enter again into the mythical place of immortality, to see the golden-haired lion, for thousands of years, one would never have idle thoughts." Cao Daohua heard the words of the two people and started to be startled as if having some realization. The Daoist priest and the group of women all left in gratitude. Sweat all over his body, he then woke up. It had already been three days. He immediately summoned local officials and his descendants, describing what he had seen, and then submitted to the prefecture to request retirement. At the age of sixty-two, his later fate is unknown. My father recently obtained this story from a fellow villager named Hu Linqing. There are also detailed records, but they were lost over time. Inquiries with the sons of the Hu family and people from Wuyuan revealed that no one knew. They could only describe the general outline as such. Now I record it, and there may be omissions.

The Yongping Tower In the Yongping Supervisory Tower of Raozhou, which overlooks the Pan River to the south. In the 32nd year of the Shaoxing era (1162 AD), Lu Ying of Kuaiji and Zhang Yi of Piling, officials residing in their official residences, were slightly intoxicated after dinner. They ascended the tower together and leaned against the railing. There were no attendants present. They were engaged in a lively conversation filled with laughter. Suddenly, a woman appeared without knowing her origin, standing between the two men. She also leaned against the railing and laughed, asking, "What are you two discussing here?" Before they could answer, she had already disappeared. Both men were greatly shocked and frightened, quickly descending the tower. Later, they dared not go again. Someone named Guo has already spoken about this incident.

唐氏蛇 唐信道在会稽居住时，整理松棚完毕后，低头看见地面上伸出约两寸长的短枝，以为是松树的枝条，准备捡起来丢掉。那东西微微动了一下，显示出生命的迹象。他用力拔它却拔不出来，于是叫来童子挖土取出，发现它逐渐长大，深入土中数尺深。原来是一条奇异的蛇，尾巴细如筷子，身体粗大如同人的手臂，头部却很小，与尾巴相称。越地的人们从未见过这样的蛇。我之前记录过融州的蛇的故事，与这个情况相反。唐信道说。 巩固治生 方城有个叫巩的人，用机智的方法谋生。他的邻居周家一向富有。一天，周家的男人们相继去世，只剩下一位老妇人和一个十岁的孙子。巩摆酒宴请老妇人，用好话劝说她，向她说明利害关系，说：“您和孙子独自生活，依靠田地、房产和财富自保，这就像开门迎盗贼一样。为什么不趁您还健康强壮的时候，把所有东西卖给我呢？我会供养您到老，并且抚养孙子长大成人，怎么样？”老妇人大喜，以低价出售。实际价值无法估量。巩才买到这些东西，就赶走他们，让他们离开原来的职业，全家搬到新的住处。搬家那天，他请了几位僧人准备道场庆祝和感谢。到了半夜，从井里传来巨大的声响，声音绕着整个房子转，直到天亮才停止。巩最终住在那里。刚过一年，敌人侵犯了唐州，巩氏家族数十口人，都在那里丧生，没有一个人逃脱。

Dui's Burial Liu Yanqing, the Shao Bao, was orphaned at a young age. Later, he lost his grandfather. He divined a burial site in Baowen Army. Someone told him, "The site you have chosen is very beautiful but does not have the right spot. The tomb master thought it was unfavorable for himself, so he kept it hidden. If you open the tomb at that time, just take the place where he stood. Then your family will enjoy wealth and prosperity for generations." Liu Yanqing followed his advice. The tomb master wept and said, "Who told you this? It's already done this way. There is nothing to be done." After the burial, within a hundred days, the tomb master died. He said, "Take good care of my family. I will choose a good day and auspicious time to repay you." On a certain day, he could carry the coffin here. Wait until you see a man riding a donkey. Then lower the coffin. Regardless of when it is. Liu Yanqing heard this and felt sad, but he doubted the story of the donkey rider. On the day of the burial, they delayed until noon. It turned out that a donkey had given birth to a foal in a small village house. The foal had very unusual fur color. The villager carried it on his back to show it to the owner. They buried the coffin at that time. Three months later, the tomb master indeed died. Liu Yanqing rose to the position of Jiedushi. His son Guangshi became Taifu Yanggong. Liu Yaoren Shanfu said. Shanfu is the son of Yanggong.

米张家 In the capital city, there was a soldier named Kan Xi who served in the Imperial Construction Office. Due to his old age, he was discharged from military service and became a peddler, selling fruits to support himself. His wife, Tang Shi, had previously worked in the imperial harem and married Kan Xi later in life. In the sixth month of the second year of the Xuanhe era (1120), Kan Xi was selling melons outside the East Water Gate along the Bian River, near a dense grove of willows. The spot where he sat was about a hundred steps away from the main road, and the shade of the willows was especially thick. It was the height of the midday heat, and there were no travelers around. Suddenly, he heard a voice calling from the treetops, followed by another voice responding. The caller asked, "Is the thing there?" The responder replied, "Yes." This exchange happened several times. Kan Xi looked up and all around but saw nothing. Frightened and uneasy, he wanted to go home, but his wife brought food just then. He told her everything about the matter. She said, "The old man has a weak stomach and deaf ears. He must have heard something by mistake. There's no need to be afraid." The next day, he did the same thing and told her again. She said, "Then on the next day, I will take your place here. You should bring me food." Tang Shi was a wise woman. She waited for the right moment and heard the response. She immediately said, "If it's there, why not show itself?" She threw gold coins and silver ingots from the trees, and they gleamed brightly. She looked all around and saw no one, so she quickly gathered and placed them in the gourd basket. Before she finished, Kan Xi arrived and laughed in surprise, saying, "I don't have time to eat now!"

A Man and His Wife Discover a Strange Object Xi, a man, and his wife came across a yellow object with a very unusual shape. They were unsure what it was. His wife said, "This is probably a type of gold." They gathered melon peels and the broken mat they were sitting on to cover the basket containing the object. Together, they carried it home, but could only walk a hundred steps before being too heavy to bear. They temporarily left it at Zhang's tea house. They then posted a notice seeking someone strong enough to carry the object. Zhang was surprised by their urgency. When they uncovered the object, he exchanged it for tiles and gravel. Xi and his wife did not check to see what he had given them. Upon arriving home, they realized what had happened. His wife said, "Let's just endure this for now and not say anything. Tomorrow, we'll try the same strategy again, and we might still get it back." Later, when they sat under a tree, they waited for a long time without hearing anything. They then imitated the call they had heard earlier. A small ghost responded, "Yes." His wife said, "Bring back the object from yesterday." The ghost replied, "It's gone." They asked why, and the ghost said, "I already had it delivered to Zhang's house." Xi wanted to take the matter to court. His wife said, "The ghosts and gods are not on our side. Even if we complain, what good will it do? We should consult with Zhang's family instead." Zhang said, "The object has already been given to me. There's no evidence to support your claim. How can you take it back? Besides, you two are old and childless. What use is money to you? Why not stay as guests in my house? I'll provide you with whatever you need. You can stay here for the rest of your lives." Xi agreed to this arrangement. Zhang's family became richer as a result. They moved to live in the north of the city, and people began to call them "Zhang's Rice Family." This story was told by Lu Zhi.

The White Rat of Yongjin Gate A man from the capital named Lu Zhi was in Lin'an during the eleventh year of the Shaoxing era. He was sending a close relative to the north gate and forgot to bring money with him. He unfastened his clothes and pawned them at a pawnshop. He saw the owner as if an old acquaintance, but he could not recall who he was. He then visited the customs official Zhu Ziwén at the northern gate and mentioned the incident. It turned out to be a beggar he had seen several years ago. The man was originally a wealthy commoner who had suffered misfortune and lost his family due to war or disaster. He and his wife were begging in the market when he sent his three sons to pick up loquat seeds. They pounded the seeds to extract the fruit to sell. One of the sons once saw a white mouse among the seeds and told his father. The father warned him, saying, "Tomorrow, go and catch it. If you sell it to bird keepers, it will be worth several hundred coins. Do not miss it." By dawn, the mother and the son went to the same place. They indeed saw the mouse and chased it. They reached the wall of the Yongjin Gate, and the mouse disappeared into a hole. The mother stood there and did not leave. She sent her son back to fetch a spade to dig the ground. They dug about two feet deep and still saw the tail of the mouse. Soon, they found a green stone and removed it. Below was a large jar filled with white gold. The son rushed to tell his father. The father arrived but dared not open it. He hurried to the office to report the discovery, offering to give half to the government and asking the local official to guard and retrieve it. The head of the office followed his words, and they obtained silver totaling five thousand taels. They took what they obtained and sold it the same day, bought a house to live in, and used the money as capital for their sons. Thus, they became a wealthy family, and the man became the owner of the pawnshop. Lu Zhi recounts this story.

A Nun with a Beard and Mustache During the Song Dynasty, there was a Buddhist nun named He at the Chuanfa Nunnery in Pingjiang. She was originally a servant in the household of Zhang Zihou. One of her disciples was named Jinshi, who was also a former concubine of Zhang Zihou. One day, while Jinshi was resting in her room, a Taoist knocked on the door and asked for food. Jinshi said, "The temple is quite quiet, and we lack good provisions." The Taoist replied, "Whatever is available is sufficient. I just came from the Miaozhan Temple and wanted to stay a while, but the smell of a corpse was too strong and unpleasant, so I came here instead." Jinshi then prepared some food and invited him in. After they finished eating, the Taoist was about to leave. Jinshi had been suffering from a long-term illness and often felt weak and short of breath. She casually said, "I have been ill for a long time. Do you have any medicine to treat me?" The Taoist replied, "I just have one grain of medicine, which is just right for you." They went to the Buddha hall together, and the Taoist instructed her to drink some water facing east and swallow the medicine. Jinshi asked where he was from. The Taoist said, "I am from Hedong, and I have many family members. I do not wish to reveal my name." Before leaving, he gave her two instructions: "After taking my medicine, you must observe two things: do not attend funerals or participate in funeral rites, and wait for twelve years. I will return then." The Taoist then left. When Jinshi returned home, she immediately smelled the food. For two days, she did not eat. Her master, He, scolded her, saying, "You ate poisonous herbs from a wild Taoist. You have damaged your digestive system and will die soon." She forced Jinshi to eat, but as soon as she swallowed, she vomited. From then on, Jinshi stopped eating altogether. Over time, her beard and mustache grew, becoming dark, glossy, and smooth like a man's. After twelve years, when she attended a funeral at a relative's house, she suddenly felt hungry. It was exactly twelve years since she took the medicine, but the Taoist did not return. Jinshi later suffered from the calamity of being captured by enemies and died in battle. He, who narrated this story, described seeing Jinshi with a beard and mustache.

The Yangshan Dragon Mother Temple is located within twenty miles of Pingjiang Prefecture. It is said that the son of the dragon would visit the temple every April. He would always take the path through the wilderness, and many people from Wu often saw him. However, one year he entered the city alone. On that day, lightning and thunder surrounded the room of Cao Yun, who was temporarily living in the office building behind Zhang Jidao's residence. Something pushed him towards the wall, and the pine canopy in the courtyard was lifted from the air. The dragon rose from the air, and all the boxes and chests in the room were moved to another place. Zhang Jidao and his nephew He Defu looked up and saw the firelight in the clouds, with the large scales of the dragon clearly visible. There were monks, Taoist priests, nuns, and courtesans crowded in front of them, walking on air and clouds in a posture of welcoming. The dragon then left through one corner of the city. This event was narrated by He Dexian.

Encounter with the Immortal Pavilion Wu Pang, a man from Yiyang, Xinzhou Prefecture, had a residence named Jiezhucun. His young son, Datong, was born mute and with shriveled hands. In the 17th year of the Shaoxing era (1147 AD), when Datong was eleven years old, during the autumn, he played with village children at the foot of the mountain. An old man passed by and asked about the residence of Wu Runfu. The children pointed and said, "It is over there." The old man asked, "Why does this child not answer me?" They replied, "He cannot speak." The old man said, "Then I will first treat this ailment and then proceed." He picked a stem of wild grass and took its thorn, pricking Datong under his ears. Immediately, Datong cried out. Then he pricked his elbows, and Datong immediately reached out and grabbed the old man's clothes, saying, "Why did you prick me?" The children were all amazed and went back to Wu Pang's house together. The old man said upon entering, "Your family has another person with a disability. Can you carry him to the county? I will treat him soon. And I will agree on a certain day." It turned out that Wu Pang's elder brother Jun's eldest son, who could not walk, was forty-five years old. After several days past the agreed time, the old man entered the county to look for him but found no trace. Later, Wu Pang and Datong went to the county and saw a beggar with disheveled hair and tattered clothes. Datong pointed and said, "This is the one." Wu Pang gave him money, but he did not accept, saying, "Buying wine for me is enough." At the wine shop, just as he was preparing the cups, he threw them away.

He said, "This is not enough for a single drink." He then took a large jar from the storage. Two people could not lift it, so he carried it out alone. Its value was a thousand coins. He lifted the jar and drank it all, then left. He said again, "Your family has many trees from the hemp cart source. They can be cut down. Build a tower for me in the bamboo grove where I live." The hemp source is seven miles away from the bamboo grove and produces large trees. Pang built the tower as he said and named it "Meeting with Immortals." He often cooked sheep and poured wine for celebrations. From then on, the Taoist did not come again. Datong sometimes went somewhere alone and sometimes returned after several days, without telling his family where he went. At first, his body was very short and small, but now his appearance is grand, and his demeanor is noble and dignified. After several years, he came again and said, "Wait until your parents pass away, and your wife and children will also pass away. You should visit me at the Purple Bamboo Rock in Gui County." Now Pang and his wife are both dead, and Datong's wife and children are in a splendid palace. After traveling, he returned. With red plumes and returning phoenix wings, he carried the earnestness of three cups of fragrant wine to nourish me, the true immortal guest. Red mist floats in the air, and auspicious clouds spread far. This is the trace of my arrival. He frequently repairs to sail together on the blue autumn clouds. When he finished writing, people asked, "How can we know when you descend?" He said, "When the red clouds fill the sky, then I have arrived." On another day, when he came again, it was indeed so. Therefore, it is mentioned in the words.

A Daoist Practitioner with a Title [Text missing] A White Marmoset Liu Gongzuo, a low-ranking official and former governor of Hengzhou, was traveling by boat back to the capital. During the journey, he fell ill. His wife Zhao visited him every night, checking on his condition and administering medicine. It was the height of summer, and the horse gate was left open. One night, Zhao came to the bedside. Liu Gongzuo was asleep and did not wake up. Suddenly, a creature appeared, resembling a monkey and having a pure white color. It went straight from the sleeping chamber, rushing out through the people, passing through the outer door, and jumping onto the shore. Zhao was afraid of frightening the sick person and dared not speak. She called her son out to see it. The creature was still on the shore, looking around with a wild gaze. After a long time, it finally left. Liu Gongzuo was born in the year of the monkey (Bing Shen). People believed his spirit had departed. He died in Sizhou. Tianyishan Li Chudu, a person with the title Pingzhong, lived in Kuaiji. In the eighteenth year of the Shaoxing era, he suffered from an illness that was not very severe. The state granary supervisor had just released him. Accompanied by several guests, he went to visit him. Li was engaged in leisurely conversation, speaking about the errors of doctors. Suddenly, he looked and said, "Recently, I received an order to buy tens of thousands of pounds of silk. I did not know the price." The guest was surprised by the unrelated speech. Soon, he called the guard to convey a message to Tang Yunshi, saying, "I am happy to be a colleague. Now let's go ahead and wait for you. You can prepare your luggage." Then, he spoke to the guest, "I got a government office building in Tianyishan. It is very clean and bright." The guest dared not respond. Then, they led away. That night, he died. Tang Jun's name was Hong. His residence was near Li's. At the time, he was ill and living at home. He was very afraid upon hearing it. The next day, he also died. Li's burial was in Tianyishan. Fang Zizhang said.

*Yijian Yi Zhi, Volume 12, Section 11: A Strange Monk in Zhenzhou* In the second year of Jianyan, Fan Maozai from Jinhua served as the acting official of the Jianghuai Transport Department with the title of Tongpan of Xiuzhou. His official residence was in Yizhen. At that time, the notorious bandit Zhang Yu was raiding the Huaidian region, and the common people were just celebrating. Fan moored his family's boat and went to the office daily to handle affairs. His wife, Lady Zhang, had always been deeply devoted to Buddhism. Whenever a wandering monk came to the door, she gave all the things she saw to them without any hesitation or reluctance. In the county, there was a monk who beat cymbals and begged on the shore. He shouted, "There is a monk named Zhang in Sizhou, collecting money to repair the outer wall of the city." Zhang invited him to the boat. The monk took out about ten carved wooden figures from his sleeve and pointed to them, saying, "This is the Great Saint Sengga, this is Muqia, this is Shancai, and this is the Earth Deity." When he commanded them to laugh, the wooden figures happily opened their mouths, with a joyful expression on their faces. He took a carved wooden figure of a child sleeping on a pillow and gave it to Zhang, saying, "This is a likeness of the Great Saint Sengga when he was born."

A monk gave another pill to Zhang and warned him to swallow it. Zhang presented him with one piece of purple silk and one piece of black silk. The monk had just left when Mr. Fan, who had just come from outside, was informed by his younger son. Mr. Fan asked where the monk was, and his son replied that he was not far away. Mr. Fan sent someone to catch up with him and intended to punish him severely, but the monk remained completely unperturbed. The monk asked for paper to write two sets of ten characters and wrote nine characters and the character "徐" below. He handed it to Mr. Fan and then left. Zhang took the pill and wanted to take it, but it was as large as a bullet and could not be swallowed. He then ordered his maid to grind it into powder, mixed it with water, and drank it. The next day, the monk came again and asked if Zhang had taken his medicine. Zhang answered truthfully. The monk exclaimed, "Why didn't you swallow it whole and break my medicine? But it is also harmless." Two days later, hundreds of pirate ships crossed the river to the south, intending to attack Jingkou. The last ten or so ships alone returned and docked at Zhenzhou, killing and looting. At that time, there were too many boats along the shore to count, lined up in a row, not even a step could move.

All the people of the Fan clan, regardless of age, fled to the ferry and dispersed. Zhang, however, was too ill to move and stayed behind with his wife and two concubines. He silently recited the Sutra of the Bodhisattva Avalokiteshvara. It was the fourteenth day of the first lunar month. Suddenly, one bandit climbed onto the boat and stabbed a spear into the back of the sail, aiming for where Zhang was sitting. The four-layered quilt covering him was all pierced through, but the blade passed under his armpit, causing him no harm. Surprised, the bandit released his weapon and asked, "What magic have you used to survive?" Zhang replied, "I have been ill since giving birth, so I was waiting to die here. I was only reciting the name of the Buddha. How could I have any magic?" He then said, "I have a small box of gold and silver at home. I will give it to you as a gift. Please spare me." The bandit took the money but left his clothes, saying he would use it to buy porridge. Not long after he left, another bandit arrived, carrying a firecracker and lit it, intending to burn the boat.

Before the device was released, it fell into the water. They also left. Soon, a great fire broke out on both banks, spreading to the water. The ropes of Fan's boat were already burned through. It seemed as if someone was pulling it, moving among thousands of boats without anyone steering it. It emerged from the great river, completely lost, not knowing east or west. Only Yi Nu was holding the rudder, moving leisurely in whatever direction. By the time morning came, they were in Yangzhou. Fan's younger brother, Maodirect, was a Sinao Chancellor. He followed the imperial entourage and immediately took them. On that day, the family of fourteen scattered in different places and all gathered in Yangzhou, no one was missing. Only then did they realize the saying that breaking the medicine was harmless. If they had swallowed it as the monk said, there would have been no suffering of being scattered. Fan returned to his hometown. Because of drowning, he fell ill and died, exactly thirty-nine years old. He was buried in Wuyi, where he bought a mountain from the Xu family. Everything matched what was written on the paper. The monk was no longer seen, and the wooden object he left could not be moved. After Lady Zhang fell seriously ill and left her body, she lived to be seventy and then died. Her son Yuanqing and Duan Chen said.

Zhang Huizhong's Encounter with a Tiger Zhang Huizhong, a man from Chengdu, along with his sister's husband, Qiu Sheng, passed the provincial examination in Sichuan in the twenty-sixth year of the Shaoxing era. They traveled together to take the imperial examination. Before they could leave the gorge, their boat capsized in the river, and Qiu died in the accident. Zhang barely escaped. After being granted a position, he was assigned to serve as the magistrate of Jingyan County. On his return journey to Xia Prefecture, he received a letter informing him that his younger brother had died of illness. Bearing his grief, Zhang hurried westward, riding a weak horse and asking a Sichuan soldier to carry his bag and follow him. As he passed through Wan Prefecture, the sun was setting, but he continued to travel forward. He fell off a cliff, landing more than ten zhang away from the shore, with wounds all over his body and unable to rise. Soon, a tiger approached, leaping forward to bite his hair bun and attempting to eat him. Zhang was terrified and called out to the tiger, saying, "You, tiger, if you have a spirit, please listen to my words. My mother is eighty years old, and I have two sons and one daughter. Last year, my sister's husband died in the water."

This year, my younger brother died at home. Only I remain. I will use my meager salary to support myself. Now you eat me. It is also fate. What about my old mother? The tiger, having heard my words, released the hair. It lowered its head in a listening posture. After speaking, he left. It circled around me, as if guarding or protecting. Midnight had passed. Chang's pain subsided slightly. He slept on the stone. He dreamed of someone saying, "The sky is about to dawn, we can go now." He woke up and it was already light. He climbed the dangerous tree step by step. When he reached the shore, the horse still stood still. He then rode it. The edicts were all on his person. But the bags and pouches were taken by the soldiers. Chang completed his term in office and his mother died. Soon after, Chang also died. It was then known that one thought of filial piety escaped from the brink of death, only for the sake of his mother. Even a different kind of creature understands righteousness in this way. And those who fall into a trap but do not help and instead push them down with stones, how far apart they are. Can people be worse than tigers?

The Old Man of Dazhanguan Pass At the end of the Jinghe period, Zhang Wei, a scholar from Hanzhou, accompanied his fellow townsman Wu Ding to take the provincial examination in the capital. They walked out of Dazhanguan Pass and encountered a sudden downpour. Their umbrella had been taken by a servant ahead of them, leaving them with no protection. They hurried into a whitewashed house to take shelter. The house was dilapidated, with holes and leaks, barely standing. They spotted several new houses to the left of the road and rushed over to seek refuge. An old man came out to greet them, his expression very respectful. He closely observed the guests' appearance and behavior, his eyes not leaving them for a moment. The two men asked the old man together, "Could you possibly tell us our fortunes?" The old man replied, "Yes, yes." Zhang Wei first pointed to Wu and asked about him. The old man smiled and said, "Very good, very good," but he did not explicitly state his fortune. Wu then pointed to Zhang Wei and asked, "What will be the future of this scholar Zhang?" The old man stood up and replied, "This gentleman has a bone structure of the highest rank, unmatched by anyone else. In the future, when there is turmoil in the Central Plains, it will be his time of great opportunity. He will be a general when leaving and a prime minister when returning, the pillar of the state. Such a destiny is beyond what you can compare to." The two men did not believe him. When the rain stopped, they left without further ado. The following year, Zhang Wei passed the examination, while Wu failed. Zhang Wei saw Wu off at the western outskirts of the city. Before parting, he said to Wu, "When you pass through Dazhanguan Pass again, please visit the old man by the roadside." Although Wu was not pleased with the old man's words, he still wanted to revisit to inquire about his fortune. When he arrived at the place from the previous day, only the whitewashed wall remained; there was no new house. He asked the people passing by the pass, but none knew anything about the old man. After Zhang Wei rose to power and became the Cuanxi Pacification Commissioner, Wu remained a commoner. Through Zhang Wei's favor, he obtained a minor official position but never achieved prominence. These two events were recounted by Huang Zhongbing.

Clay Figurines of Zhenzhou Zheng Angong served as the governor of Zhenzhou. There was a night watchman who, every midnight, saw a light in the pavilion on the city wall. He went to look at it and found ten or more people and a few children gambling. The watchman had the courage not to be afraid and playfully extended his hand to ask for money. The people competed to give him money, and he almost got three thousand coins and returned. The next day, he verified it, and they were genuine copper coins. He did not tell anyone. The next night, it was the same. He then bribed the guard leader to request exclusive duty during the third watch. He gained more wealth, and this continued for more than two months. Coincidentally, there was a loss of funds from the military treasury, and several hundred taels of silver were missing. They posted a notice to investigate and capture the suspect. Someone reported that this soldier had recently wasted a lot of money and his clothes were brightly colored, making him suspicious. They tried to capture him and questioned him about the theft. He could not hide it and spoke the truth in full. Zheng suspected that the clay figurines were involved. He then tied up the soldier and sent people to search all the temples. At the City God Temple, there were clay figurines whose appearance was similar to what he had seen. He broke them and found a silver tablet in their belly. Cutting all of them revealed the same. He then excavated the ground, and underneath all the figurines, each contained several thousand coins, matching the amount the soldier had spent. There was no discrepancy. He then destroyed all the idols, and the strange occurrences ceased. Zheng Angong said.

The Head of Han Xin During the Zhenghe era, Censor-in-Chancellor Xi Zhongchen named Jin Zhongdan. He was the commander of Chang'an. Because the public treasury building had collapsed, he ordered the workers to rebuild it. During the reconstruction, they found one stone box underground. Its appearance resembled jade. The lid was engraved with four characters: "The head of Han Xin," written in seal script. It was empty inside, so they moved it to a high plateau, offered a sacrifice, and covered it up. This was told by Zheng Shimeng, a title of office, who was related to Xi by marriage. The Guest House of the Eastern River Transport Office The Eastern River Transport Office is located in Jiankang Prefecture. The offices of three subordinate officials are situated within it. The northernmost one is said to be haunted. Many who have lived there before and after have not been at peace. In the second year of Longxing, Chen Fuqing was the prefect. Fang Shizhi, the deputy magistrate of Huzhou, sent his daughter to marry his son. They stayed in this house. They saw a crowd of shadows on the eastern window wall. They thought it was just people walking outside the wall and did not think much of it. This continued all day. They tried to go closer to see, and found that the figures were no more than a foot tall. They were riding and following in a grand procession, like the dignified appearance of a provincial governor. They were constantly running and walking. Fang Shizhi was frightened and moved to another place. Zhao Shanren alone did not believe it and went to stay there. In the middle of the night, he heard his name being called. In the morning, he looked for his headgear and clothes, but they were nowhere to be seen. He hung them all on the beams and left in panic. The people of the prefecture said this place used to be a temple. Fang Shizhi said so.

Wáng Shǎng and the Omen Wáng Shǎng's spirit tablet. When he was in the capital, he consulted Master Miàoyìng, a Buddhist monk, about his fortune and received two verses: "His name will not be remembered beyond the Chengjiàdù ferry. Even after leaving the city, he will still have to travel ten miles into the countryside." In the year Bingzi of the Shòusòng era, he was dismissed from his position as the governor of Dangtu and was in Yíxīng County. He then sought a poem from Master Dázhēn Huáng Yuándào, whose last line read: "Straight down the Xùn Pass is Méijiādiàn. Fortune and prosperity will not last into the spring of the following year." Coincidentally, the Jiangdong Inspector, Lǚ Chénzhōng, accused him of misconduct during his time in Xuānchéng and set up a case against him in Guangdé Army. The investigation found no substantial evidence, and the case did not succeed. It was then transferred to Huīzhōu for trial. Upon exiting the south gate of Guangdé, he passed through a pass and asked its name. It was called Xùn Pass. He was already displeased. Reaching a ferryhead guesthouse to rest, it turned out to be Méijiādiàn. He was startled and feared it, shedding tears involuntarily. A fellow scholar, Wèi Bó, comforted him, and he felt slightly relieved. He ordered his servant to prepare wine. An old soldier pounded dried deer meat at the door threshold. Wáng Shǎng criticized him for being unclean. The soldier, angry, said, "This is different from Jiankang Prefecture. What is there to fuss about?" Wáng Shǎng, angered by his disrespect, became extremely furious. The wine cup fell to the ground, and he immediately fell ill and did not recover. It was the ninth day of the first month of the Dingchǒu year. The ferry was called Chengjiàdù, exactly ten miles from Guangdé. Said by Sūn Lián.

Qin Changshi and Qin Changling were both nephews of Grand Tutor Qin Hui. In the 23rd year of the Shaoxing era, Qin Changling's term in office was completed, and he was about to take up a new post. He met with Dazhen Huang Yuandao and was warned: "Your lifespan is not very long, but you should especially avoid being an official in Xuanchou Prefecture. If you get such a position, you must not accept it, for accepting it will lead to death." Soon after, Qin Changling was appointed as a military judge in Ningguo Commandery. He did not want to go and reported the matter to his uncle, who scolded him. He then accepted the appointment and arrived home on the 18th day of the ninth month. He died five days later and did not have the chance to take up the post. Qin Changshi came from the position of Censor-in-Inspector of Zhejiang East to attend his brother's funeral. He heard that Dazhen was in Liyang and went to see him. Dazhen said, "This year we will bury the military judge, next year we will bury the Censor-in-Inspector. I will go to Kuaiji to see them off." Qin Changshi was angry and afraid. The following year, on the 12th day of the 12th month, he indeed visited Dazhen in Kuaiji and took paper to write a poem, which included the lines "two and five meet again on the path, lost once more." Qin Changshi asked, "Is your lifespan limited to two or five years?" Dazhen replied, "No." Qin Changshi then asked, "Then it's just two or five days?" Dazhen replied, "Perhaps so." At the time, the governor of Kuaiji was Zhao Shican, and the Director of the Equalization of Grain Administration was Gao Bai, both of whom were present. Qin Changshi asked privately, "The Censor-in-Inspector is just forty-five years old, and he looks so healthy and spirited."

Why is there such a statement? It was said, "Last year, I saw it in Liyang. The spirit has departed from Gan. We once agreed to send him to his burial. The length of life is determined by fate. What is there to be surprised about? Now it will not be more than seven days." On the eighteenth day of this month, Changshi prepared a meal and summoned Bai Zhi and his son-in-law Feng. Dazhen was present. While Changshi was sitting, he took Yongjia yellow oranges and selected them by hand himself. Dazhen followed and ate them immediately, eating several, and then peeled the rest and threw them on the ground. Changshi explained the situation to him, saying, "My uncle's birthday is not far off. I want to use them as a gift for his birthday. I hope you will not be troubled by this." Dazhen laughed and said, "I don't care about my own death day, but I care about other people's birthdays." Those around him saw that his words were sharp and all stuck out their tongues and shrunk their necks. Changshi was not happy and looked at Bai Zhi and his son-in-law Feng, inviting them out. He locked the door and wrote a letter, instructing the guard to say, "If Mr. Huang comes looking for me, just tell him I am sleeping." The guard stood outside the door. Suddenly, he heard the pen fall to the ground and went in to see. He had already fallen onto the trundle bed, with saliva blocking his throat with a gurgling sound. His family called doctors and sorcerers in a continuous stream. His wife, Zhan, wept and knelt before Dazhen, begging for help. Dazhen laughed and said, "I said this last year. Today I am here specifically to send you to your burial. My fate ends here. Even if it were Bian Que, it would be of no use. Take good care of him. I will leave at the third watch." At that time, he indeed died.

In the early Zhenghe era, there was a hairdresser in Chengdu. One day, while he was out in the bustling marketplaces, his wife was left alone at home. An elderly man with a bun hairstyle, resembling a Daoist or hermit, came to their door and asked to have his fine facial hair plucked. He initially gave two hundred coins as payment. The wife politely declined, saying, "The work isn't much; only ten coins would suffice." The elderly man replied, "Just take it. Be patient with me." She then sat down, first plucking the hair on his left side, then moving to the right. After finishing, when she turned back, the hair on the left side had already grown again. She plucked it again, and the same happened on the right side. This process repeated several times. By midday, the wife could not bear the fatigue and disgust any longer. She returned the money and dismissed him. When her husband returned, she told him everything. The husband was angry and said, "This must be Zhongli, the immortal. Why did you refuse him? Even if I pluck your hair all day and night, what harm would it do? It's my bad luck." He then ran wildly through the market, calling out, "Where did the master leave me?" Night and day, he ignored hunger, thirst, cold, and heat. Like this, for three or four years, he traversed many towns until he met a woodcutter in the mountains. The woodcutter asked, "What are you doing?" He told him the reason. The woodcutter said, "This is a person from the realm of immortals. If he comes to find you, then it's fine. You running around your whole life is of no benefit."

Although I am extremely foolish, yet I have heard that those who attain the Dao must accumulate great hidden virtue and righteous deeds, and it cannot be hoped for lightly. I possess a secret technique that I will impart to you. You should use this to assist your path, and refine it over time. If you manage to fulfill your wish, playfully pluck a single blade of grass and blow on it, and it will turn into a golden hairpin. I told the craftsman, "Try using my method to do it, and it will be helpful." The craftsman said, "All these are mere illusions and not worth learning. What I desire is to see the master." The woodcutter said, "You have not seen the person, how can you recognize them when you meet them?" I replied, "Ask my wife to describe his appearance. I have drawn it and placed it in my sleeve." The woodcutter said, "Then you should bow to me three times. I can make you see him." The craftsman performed the bowing, and upon rising, the woodcutter asked, "How does my face look?" The craftsman said, "It looks like what I just saw." He bowed again and asked, "Up to three times." When he looked at him, he no longer looked like a woodcutter. He looked exactly like the drawing. "You are someone who sincerely seeks the Dao. You have cried for many years, and your voice has reached the heavens. The Heavenly Deity deeply sympathizes with your resolve. Therefore, He has instructed me to call you indirectly. You should follow me." And we entered the mountains together. After two years, we returned to the village. Farewell to those you know and leave. And he has never come out since.

A Taoist Hermit of Wuyi Mountain In Chong'an County, Jianzhou, lies Wuyi Mountain, a place of secluded and extraordinary beauty. Overlooking a clear stream that winds and twists its way through nine curves, travelers row boats beneath it, looking up and gazing to the utmost. The Taoist priests merely point out where ancient relics are located, saying no one has climbed it. In the early years of the Song Dynasty, a Taoist hermit came to the Chongyou Temple. He alone wished to enter and visit the celestial realm. After several months, he explored almost all of it, finding nothing. Suddenly, in a mountain ravine, he found a thatched cottage. There was a Taoist nun living in seclusion, with long eyebrows and a rosy cheek, and no attendants were present. He asked her about her reason for coming. She said, "The celestial realm has a name but no form. As is passed down, I have lived here for a long time. I have not seen it." The hermit replied, "I have long desired to go there. I must search for it with all my life." At that time, the sky was overcast, and it was already evening. The nun invited him to stay in the cottage. The hermit declined, saying, "You are a woman living alone. It is not right according to propriety." She explained, "It is not for any other reason. There are many tigers and wolves in this place. I am afraid they might harm you." He still refused to enter.

Sitting upright outdoors, he had not been there long when indeed a tiger roared and came forward. The nun hurriedly opened the door and called to it. The tiger replied, "Rather die at the hands of a tiger than enter." Soon, another tiger appeared, and the roars grew even louder. The nun spoke to it again, saying, "These two black tigers are gentle and kind, but the rest will attack without mercy. You will be ground into powder." The Taoist monk remained steadfast and unshaken by these words. Soon, five tigers gathered together and carried him by the head and feet. Only a dozen steps away, they threw him down the slope and left. His body was not harmed in the slightest. He then sat firmly until dawn. The nun invited him to sit and praised him, saying, "You have such determination. It is beyond my reach." The celestial realm is not far from here, but it is still separated by a deep chasm. The chasm is over ten zhang wide, with surging, turbulent waters. Only a single bamboo pole crosses over it, and one cannot cross without wings. Sometimes a person with two braids (a monk or hermit) crosses back and forth. You should try to go. If you happen to meet, you should bow and ask for directions. Otherwise, there is no other way. Once you arrive.

The stream surges and roars, shaking trees and stones. The slender bamboo sways, making it impossible to stand on. Happening to meet a woodcutter coming out, the monk bowed twice respectfully. The woodcutter startled and retreated, saying, "A wild person from the mountains. I cut firewood to support my family. How dare I do this?" The monk fully explained what he wanted in a respectful manner. The woodcutter initially kept it secret and did not speak, but after a while said, "Who told you this?" The monk replied, "I heard from the nun in the temple." The woodcutter angrily said, "That talkative old woman! She carelessly leaked my affairs." He ordered the monk to close his eyes and pulled his clothes to walk. The monk felt like flying through the void, with clouds and dragons appearing and disappearing. Soon, they passed through the space between the ears, and once on the ground, they were on a flat hill. The palaces towered high, with golden door knockers and jade doors. A person with a blue cap and red shoes looked around and said, "How can there be common people's qi (vital energy)?" The monk rushed out and bowed deeply. The blue-capped one scolded and said, "Who brought you here?" The woodcutter reported, and the blue-capped one immediately sent someone to chase him back. The monk exposed his back, and he was beaten with an iron staff for three hundred and sixty strokes. Blood and flesh separated, bones broke, and marrow spilled out.

The hermit also trembled with fear. The blue-capped one said, "The Heavenly Abode is where high immortals gather. Who are you? How did you manage to come here? I pardon your sin. You should return quickly. Accumulate good deeds and merits. Perhaps you can come again in the future." He ordered a cup of water to be brought for him to drink. There was a single grain of sesame rice in it. After drinking the water, he chewed the rice. He chewed and swallowed for a while. He could only eat one-third of it. His stomach was already very full. The blue-capped one laughed and said, "You ate one grain of my rice, but you couldn't finish it. How could you stay here?" So he returned. He reached the foot of the cliff and saw someone being beaten and groaning in the grass. "It's because of you that I ended up here," he said. "I was just demoted and fell. I don't know how many eons have passed. I have finally been released. You go now." On the way back, he no longer saw the stream. He walked leisurely through the long forest, but his feet were always a few inches above the ground. Looking back, he saw high mountains and deep valleys. It was no longer the same as before. The nun and her hermitage were also lost. So he took shelter in a rock crevice. He is still there today. About seven or eight years ago, Huang Yuandao saw him. He said he was from Shandong.

A Son from the Zhang Family of Longquan A son from the Zhang family of Longquan County, Quzhou Prefecture, at the age of fifteen, once carried a basket of fresh fish. By the stream, he broke open the fish. The fish splashed and struggled. The knife accidentally cut his finger, causing extreme pain. He stopped to rest briefly. Suddenly, he thought to himself, "I cut my finger and it hurts so much. Imagine the pain of the fish being skinned, gills removed, gutted, and tails cut off. They just can't speak." He threw all the fish back into the stream. That day, he entered the deep mountains and lived in a cave in the rock, refusing to eat or drink. His parents were puzzled that their son did not return and thought he might have drowned. The next year during the Cold Food Festival, people from the village who were hiking in the mountains first saw him. His body was as dry and shriveled as a mummy, extremely thin with bones protruding, but his face was still recognizable. They quickly reported to their parents to come, wanting to call him back home. He turned his head away without looking back and said, "I am not your family member. Do not worry about me." The parents cried and left. Ten years later, they went to see him again, and then his body had returned to its normal state, with a smooth and lustrous complexion. No one knew why. Now he has lived in the mountains for over twenty years. All four stories are told by Huang Dazhen.

*Yijian Zhi Yi, Volume Thirteen, Thirteen Stories* Liu Ziwen Liu Zong, courtesy name Ziwen, served as the magistrate of Linjiang in Zhongzhou at the beginning of the Shaoxing era. Now, after completing his term, he resides in the neighboring county of Dianjiang. He has a son named Six, who is six years old. One day, Ziwen suddenly saw a child beside his wet nurse. The child's height and build were similar to those of Six. Ziwen suspected that the child was born from an illicit relationship between the wet nurse and an external servant. He blamed his wife for this. His wife, Li, was foolish and weak-willed, unable to manage the household. However, she knew that the accusation was false. She replied, "There is no such thing." Ziwen became angry. At the time, he already suffered from thigh pain and often used a wooden staff. He immediately beat her back and sent her out. He then went to tell his mother. His mother said, "The child must have mistakenly heard it. How could there be such a thing?" Ziwen exclaimed in anger, "Even my mother is still like this. What more can I expect?" Returning home, he used fruit to lure Six and said, "Your wet nurse slept with whom at night? Who is the child?" Six was startled and could not respond. Ziwen quickly stepped forward and grabbed his hand.

Ziwen forcefully grabbed her and would not let go. Those around him rushed to save him. Still, his face was disfigured and blood was flowing. He then called the old woman and ordered her to drive her away. He said, "You have been in my home for many years. The child has also grown up. You have ruined yourself with immoral acts. Because of my son, I cannot punish you. Go away." The old woman wept and bowed before leaving. Ziwen watched her leave. Laughing, he said to his attendants, "Her son has already followed her out. All the shameful deeds have been revealed. Yet the family collectively concealed it. Why is that?" Everyone knew he would fall ill. Within a short time, he indeed fell ill and died. While ill, he often said to himself, "I often argued with the governor but could not win. You are not unaware. Why did you stay and not leave?" Later, his younger brother Xie said, "When Ziwen was the magistrate of Kuizhou, there was a prisoner in the jail, on the brink of life and death. The governor wanted to kill him. Ziwen did not strongly oppose it. The prisoner eventually died. Then what he saw while ill was suspected to be a supernatural occurrence. Ziwen is my aunt's brother."

In the ninth year of the Shaoxing era, Zhang Yuandao, a scholar-official, lived in Nan Chan Temple in Wuxi County. His daughter invited a great immortal, who suddenly wrote: "The Nine Peaks Immortal Descends." When asked who she was, the immortal replied, "I am the goddess of Mount Wu, as the world knows her." She composed a long poem titled "Xinu Jiao Daqu," consisting of nine sections. [The first section reads:] In the splendid palaces of jade and crystal, I lie comfortably on the twelve peaks of Mount Wu. I see the Qutang Gorge for thousands of years, with shimmering waves boiling in the river. Endless wonders abound. I enjoy leisurely poetry and drink from golden cups. I recall the time when King Xiang of Chu came to meet me in a dream. My hair is tangled, and my hairpins are disordered. I gather my robes of clouds and morning glow. Bowing respectfully, I ask about my divine duties. Peach and apricot blossoms are in full bloom, and green grass covers the ground. Swallows arrive, and I travel through rain in the morning and clouds in the evening in Mount Wu. When there is free time, I just lie comfortably in the hall. [The second section, "Yao Tai Jing," reads:] Ascending the winding cloud ladder, I reach the high heavens and beyond the clouds. I drink with all the immortals, remaining drunk in eternal spring. Tigers roar and apes call. The fragrance of the jade peach is unusual, and the wind carries it in fine and rare ways. It is difficult for mortals to recognize such delights. Chang'e plays the flute, and there is divine music of unique quality. It is naturally clear and crisp, stopping the clouds from flying. The waves are clear and calm.

A Stream of Fragrant Water. [Third Scene of蓬莱] Mountains dyed green like a snail's shell. Floating in the world, hard to reach. Illuminated by the light of precious jewels. Day and night without rest. An endless fairyland. When I want to speak, what do you know? And cultivate your mind. To view and travel is not very difficult. Looking down on the fleeting life. Still competing for fame and profit. Don't you realize that next year there will be chaos and war? A gloomy sky and sorrowful clouds. What should be done when times are declining? Causing us to cry all day in the palace of蓬莱. [Fourth Scene of Persuasion] I will again address all of you. A hundred years pass like lightning. High reputation and prominent position. In an instant, like a bubble on the water. In a moment, it is hard to stand long. A candle burning in the wind. How can it last long? Quickly go to茅峰, cut off love, abandon fame, and avoid the world. When the task is accomplished, there must be a higher truth to guide. Pointing out how to die and live, applying good medicine. The merit is unparalleled. Remember this wonderful formula first of all. [Fifth Scene of Eating the Peach of Immortality in the Palace of the Queen Mother] Fruit clusters forming abundantly. Green branches intertwining and reflecting each other. Each peach of immortality, truly fragrant and beautiful. Then he ordered双成 to cut it with a magical knife. Eating one piece will make me live for ten thousand years. It is laughable for the Eastern people. To have selfish thoughts and steal bait. Causing the fairy companions in the palace. To blame each other endlessly.

Despite the efforts, Shuang Cheng could not help but report the matter to the Queen Mother in detail. It was then pointed out that it was you who stole the peaches from the Queen Mother's garden. [Sixth Chapter: Jade Clear Palace] Purple clouds and crimson mist surround the palace. Highly surrounded by jade-paved terraces, the dawn light reveals an infinite arrangement of intricate details. The heavenly immortals are neatly and orderly assembled. There are also unique sounds, as if they were about to raise their voices but then stopped. After the court session, a fragrant scent spreads throughout the world. The carriage of the deity just started, and the highly revered immortals all retreated. There are jade-like flowers as white as snow, scattered and floating freely in the air. The jade maidens and golden boys hold the red text and transmit divine teachings. They encourage all the immortals to rise early, but the words are meant to be heard but not necessarily acted upon. [Seventh Chapter: Palace of the Sun] Time in the peculiar palace of the sun is always day. The scenery is bright and clear, lovable and without darkness or gloom. The Great Emperor frequently inspects the Jade Pool beyond the red railing, riding a phoenix to fly. The Master of Teachings smiles and commands everyone to drink, and the precious music is blown together. All are beautiful and skilled heavenly performers. Every three cups, the Holy Mother must come and bow. There are thousands of kinds of exotic fruits and famous flowers, filling the sleeves with fragrance. Intending to return, one rides a phoenix-drawn carriage with phoenix wings. [Eighth Chapter: Palace of the Highest Clarity] Bright and radiant clouds, ten thousand feet of auspicious clouds are highly spread. The sight of the immortals' attire, swaying as they approach the fragrant terrace. The jade beasts burn together, the sky filled with the image of a coiling dragon. The Great Emperor rises, and the jade maidens and golden boys serve all around. Following the command, they proclaim, greatly appreciating the kind intentions of all the immortals. They then return to report.

With deep gratitude and reverence, I bow. After presenting the report, I return to the palace. The scene remains as mysterious and extraordinary as ever. If not for my lord, who would hear it? [Return to the Ninth] I am returning. The fairy palace has been long departed. The cave door is unattended, awaiting my return. I wish to light the golden fire. Diligently cultivate yourself. Do not forget what has been said. Lili. There is no way to return again. Affairs are too complex to be fully expressed. You must understand for yourselves. Your return is as I intended. Generally, the heart is calm and free from worry. Even if changes are made, what is there to fear? The words are completed without any revisions. Another large inscription reads, "I will return." Thus, he departed. The next day, another person, claiming to be a song and poetry deity, said, "Last night, the goddess of Mount Wu saw me and invited me. She said she would compose a poem at your home, but feared it might not be in harmony with the music. She asked me to refine it. Upon reviewing it, I only made a few changes. The third verse speaks of the coming year, when chaos and war will arise. At that time, the enemy was retreating from Henan. People considered this prediction unfounded. The following year, Yuan Dao was appointed from a minor official position to oversee tea and horses in Qin. Crossing the Huai River to the north, he reached Canyang. The enemy army arrived in large numbers. In a state of panic, he rushed back. The entire family narrowly escaped. Henan was recaptured. Examining the lines in the poem, the spirits must have known it.

In the fifth year of the Shaoxing era, during the summer, a severe drought occurred. The imperial court prayed to all the mountains, rivers, shrines, and temples, but there was no response. They then sent the governor of Lin'an to Mount Tianzhu to welcome the Guanyin of Inspiration. At the Fahui Temple, a religious ceremony was held, and after three weeks, there was still no response. By the time the sixth month had passed halfway, the monk Pan Fahui, who practiced severe asceticism, silently prayed to the Buddha, begging to burn his right eye as an offering. He took an iron ball and threw it into the fire, heating it until it was red-hot, and placed it in his eye. He then lit incense on top of it. As soon as the incense flame rose, clouds filled the sky, and heavy rain poured down, thoroughly soaking the entire region. Fahui's eye immediately dried up, turning deeply and brightly red. It was frightening to look at, but since his wish had been fulfilled, he was very pleased. Three days later, he dreamed of a white-clad woman coming to him, wanting to borrow a pearl. He refused to give it to her. Two monks beside him said, "Giving it to her would not be a problem. She will return it herself six times six (36) times." Upon waking, he did not understand what this meant. On the twenty-first day of the seventh month, he dreamed again of two monks inviting him to attend a Six-Portal Feast. The white-clad woman also came, leading the way. Fahui asked who she was, and the monks replied, "We are your donors." Fahui said, "The woman might not know the way. Why don't you lead her along?" The monks replied, "She knows the way well." As they slightly advanced, the mountains and forests were lush, and all the fruits were ripe, falling one after another. Fahui picked up the fruits and ate them, feeling a sense of coolness in his heart, unlike any other day. As he bent down to pick something up, the woman suddenly turned around and threw an iron ball, hitting the eye that was burning. He screamed and woke up. There was something in his dry eye that looked like a goose's eye, and he could see as well as before. It gradually returned to its original size. Counting the time when his vision returned, it was exactly thirty-six days. He finally understood the sign of 'six times six' being returned.

**Guanyin in the Clam** Yu Ji, a native of Lizhui, served as a minor official in Xinghua during the Xuanhe period. He traveled with his family by boat, and as they passed through the Huai River region, which was abundant with clams, the boatmen would buy them daily for food. Yu Ji, however, would always stop the purchase and release the clams into the river. One day, he found a basket that was unusually heavy. The boatmen wanted to cook and eat it, offering double the price, but Yu Ji refused. He placed the basket in a pot, and suddenly a loud voice rose from the pot, accompanied by a brilliant light. The boatmen were terrified, but upon closer inspection, they saw a large clam split open, revealing the image of Guanyin within the shell. Beside her were two bamboo poles, standing tall and lifelike. The Bodhisattva's features were beautiful and dignified, adorned with a crown, robe, and jewel ornaments. The bamboo leaves and branches were also intricately crafted from fine pearls. Yu Ji instructed the boatmen to recite Buddhist scriptures and repent, and then he took the clam shell back home. The *Chuan Ding Lu* records a similar event involving Emperor Wenzong of Tang, who also encountered a miraculous appearance of Guanyin while enjoying clams. However, this story is unique in that it includes the two bamboo poles. The tale was told by Song Kuang, known for his humility.

A Daoist from Xuyi In the thirtieth year of the Shaoxing era, Yang Yizhi Kang was the governor of Xuyi. There was a Daoist whose origins were unknown. He could speak loudly and predict people's fortunes and misfortunes, sometimes accurately. Yang treated him with reverence like a deity and accommodated him in the eastern study of the prefectural administration. He often invited officials and subordinates to drink together with him. The Daoist would frequently look around sideways, as if seeing something. Late in the spring night, Yang's son Xun's wife was about to give birth. Xun went out to call for a midwife and passed by the eastern study. He heard the Daoist talking to guests inside and saw him send the guests out upon returning. He noticed a dark shadow going south, which already seemed strange to him. Suddenly, the Daoist bowed and said, "Your father has already come out of the hall. I am going to pay my respects." Xun replied, "He was just waking up from a deep sleep." The Daoist exclaimed, "Lamps and candles are arranged, and guests are filling the room. Why are you playing tricks on me?" Xun could not stop him and returned to his room. The next day, he told his father, who still believed he had met a strange person and warned him not to speak lightly. After half a month, the night watchman got up early, and the study door was already open, but the Daoist was nowhere to be seen. They searched frantically and found him hanging himself with a belt in the bamboo grove to the north of the study. Only then did they realize that what they had seen before were all ghosts. Jiang Decheng, during the time of Tianyou, was the deputy prefect and saw it personally.

**A Cow Strikes a Courtesan** Twenty li north of Guilin is a place called Gantang Pu. In the sixteenth year of the Shaoxing era (1146 CE), Fang Wuzi served as the transport commissioner of Guangxi. Officials from the Gui Prefecture all went out to greet him. Courtesans from the military camp also gathered in front of the pavilion, then dispersed to rest in the homes of the local people. Suddenly, a young yellow calf escaped from its enclosure. The group of courtesans fled in panic. The cow moved directly through the crowd and struck one person, using its horn to push the man’s abdomen against a wall. His intestines were forced out, and he died immediately. The cow went mad, dragging itself into the mountains. The village head and dozens of soldiers took up bows, crossbows, spears, and staffs to chase it. After two days, they shot it dead. The name of the courtesan was Gan Mei. After that, on rainy and overcast nights, people all heard her cries of injustice. The phenomenon lasted for several years before it finally ceased.

Beggars of Yan Prefecture In the east of Yan Prefecture, there was a beggar sitting under a large tree. His body was dirty and covered in filth. Passersby all covered their noses as they passed by. Only Li Cizhong suspected him to be an extraordinary person. Dressed in formal attire, he went to pay respects. The beggar cursed him loudly. Li stood respectfully and dared not leave. Suddenly, the beggar laughed and said, "I have a poem to give you." He then sang, "Looking for fish in a tree is rare in the world. Who knew there would be fish flying at the top of the tree? Encountering obstacles while following the flow is a common affair for everyone. After three lines, he said, 'You are out of order.' Li earnestly asked for the last line. The beggar cursed again and the poem remained incomplete. In the following year, during the year of Jiazi in the Shaoxing era, a great flood hit Yan Prefecture. Many people in the prefecture drowned. However, Li's home was the highest, and he alone avoided the disaster. He then realized the meaning of the poem and the phrase "you are out of order." Li Cizhong said. Poem about Eating Beef A man from Xiu Prefecture named Sheng Zhao lived in Chao Guo Temple in Qinglong Town. He liked to eat beef and was friendly with a son of the Chen family. The Chen family once sent a servant to invite him. They were to meet for a meal the next day. When he looked at the invitation, there was no mention of this. Instead, on a piece of uniform green paper, it was written in large characters, "All things are transformations of the mind. Only oxen are the most laborious. Look at those who die suddenly. All are those who eat beef." Sheng was amazed and called for his servant, who was already gone. In the morning, he asked the Chen family, who said they had never sent anyone. Sheng was afraid and from then on, he did not eat beef. Zhao Gangli Zhenfu said.

**Island Bamboo** In the prefecture of Mingzhou, there was a Daoist priest who begged in the marketplace. He carried a section of a large bamboo, about three feet in diameter, with bloodstains soiled on it. He claimed that he was originally a merchant from Shandong who had once sailed across the sea and encountered a storm. The ship was blown to an island, and upon landing, he looked around and saw a towering bamboo, its green color almost dripping. He was amazed by its strangeness and began to wander and admire it. Soon, two men in black robes approached and said they were urgently searching for him. "Are you here?" they asked. He replied, "I just came from the boat and still do not know where this place is. Why are you looking for me?" The men did not respond and grabbed him by the arms, dragging him forward. The path was full of sharp, large thorns, which stabbed his feet with extreme pain, making it impossible to walk. He asked one of the men, "What are these?" They replied, "These are cattle horns." He found this even more strange and continued to follow them. They eventually arrived at a place where the master scolded him, saying, "You liked to eat beef. You should receive punishment for it." He was greatly frightened and begged for mercy, saying, "Please, I will not do it again." The master said, "Since you have repented, I will release you. You can go back and tell the world to take this as a warning." He said, "If they do not believe, what can be used as proof?" The master looked around and ordered someone to cut the bamboo, saying, "Take it with you and show it when you return." The two men carried a large saw and rushed into the forest. After a short while, the bamboo was brought back, and it was covered in fresh blood, dripping down and staining their clothes. They said, "We were in the middle of cutting the prisoner and did not have time to clean the saw." He then carried the bamboo back to the boat. After returning home, he immediately abandoned his wife and children, left his hometown, and went elsewhere. He eventually mixed himself among the beggars. Zhao Zhenfu saw him many times.

**Three Wonders of Mount Song** Dong Ju Zhong, a native of the capital, lived in seclusion on Mount Song during his youth. He resided in the deepest part of the mountain summit in a place called Konghe An. Initially, he lived there with two others, and every one or two months, he would descend the mountain to seek food. Climbing was extremely difficult and arduous, often requiring him to climb up vines until the end of the day. The two others could not bear the hardship and left, but Dong Ju Zhong remained as if nothing had happened. This continued for twenty years. During a period of chaos, he moved south. In the Shaoxing era, he was once summoned to the imperial court and granted the title of Chongjing Chushi. He now resides in Donghu, Nanchang, and often speaks of his past experiences. He said that on the steepest part of Mount Song, there was a level area of about a hundred acres that could be used as farmland. There were also small mountains, caves, and cliffs, and the clouds and rain usually appeared only halfway up the mountain. There were hundreds of large lizards, each about three to four feet long. People would feed them by hand, and touching their bodies, which were smooth like fat. One day, the lizards gathered around a water pot and each drank water. As they drank, they would spit out the water, which would then form round and solid balls like marbles, accumulating on the side. Suddenly, a thunderclap sounded, and the balls disappeared. The next day, people from the mountain below reported that it had rained heavily with hail in the middle of the day, and they realized that the lizards had caused this phenomenon. He also heard of an old man reading books in a stone cave. When he listened closely, it was silent, but after he left, the sound returned. Later, the stone wall collapsed, and he found many strange books. These books contained various esoteric and alchemical practices, including the study of yin-yang, techniques, and the pursuit of spiritual and alchemical knowledge. After he descended the mountain, he selected a few dozen complete texts and burned the rest. He also once experienced a mysterious fragrance that filled the room and lasted for several days, but no one knew where it came from. Dong Ju Zhong was born in the seventh year of the Yuanfeng era, the year of Jiazi.

The Huangbo Dragon The Huangbo Monastery is located sixty li south of Fuzhou, and on the mountain there is a dragon pond. It forms a cave between the cliffs and rocks, which goes straight down without a bottom. The mouth of the pond is about five feet wide. The monks of the monastery say that this is a benevolent dragon. When it returns from bringing rain, one often hears the sound of music welcoming and guiding it. Sometimes, in the mist, one can vaguely see a pattern of flowers guiding it. The monk Qinglao from Quanzhou heard about it and was delighted. He and several of his fellow monks went to the edge of the pond, burned incense and silently prayed, reciting the mantra of the White Umbrella, wishing to see its form. First, they took some paper money and threw it into the water. Immediately, something from below pulled it, and it disappeared suddenly. They were already frightened. It was daytime, and black clouds rose like fans. Soon, the sky was filled with clouds. People could not recognize each other across the room. Gradually, the clouds began to disperse.
