Something rose from the pond, resembling a lotus with red stems and petioles. Then, two eyes like the sun appeared, shining brightly, and suddenly appeared above it. The monks were terrified and quickly ran down the mountain. Thunder and lightning followed behind them. After some time, it stopped.

Poem by Qing Lao Qing Lao, courtesy name Gui Nian, was skilled in composing poetry. He first met Li Han Lao, the Censor-General. When he submitted his work, it included the line "looking together at the crows perched on trees." Li Han Lao found it very remarkable, thinking it captured the style of Wei Yingwu from Suzhou. Qing Lao lived at the foot of North Mountain, where there was a horizontal stone at the top of the mountain that looked like a boat. He called himself Boat Peak. Li Han Lao renamed it Shifan Hermitage and wrote a poem: "Feathers for clothes, iron for liver, The old man entrusts his life to the cold rock. The heavenly offerings are still abundant, The birds and mountain flowers have already stopped singing and blooming. Meditation rises with the coolness of the moon and water, The poem is as cold and beautiful as a sealed cloud. Who steers the painted boat on the mountain top? I want to see you make the stone sail." He once visited but did not meet Qing Lao, leaving behind a poem: "Huiyuan should have sent Lu when he crossed the stream, Yuchuan entered the temple but did not meet the sun. The sunset on the half-mountain, crows roosting on the trees, Walking with a cane, step by step, slowly." Afterward, Qing Lao died, and Li Han Lao wrote an essay to commemorate him: "Now Hong Juefan, Ancient Tang Huixiu. Also once followed Fojirizonggao in Zen meditation. Zonggao did not approve, Saying, 'Just like water droplets on a stone, Cannot enter even a little bit.' Because of the curse of words." Qing, the Secretary of Baoren Temple in Quanzhou, was also skilled in poetry. Li Han Lao praised one couplet of his: "People leave from the edge of the morning moon, The path enters the thin places of the cloud-covered mountains. Thought it could be painted."

A Snake at Mount Jiang During the early years of the Shaoxing era, Wang Gui, the military supervisor of Quanzhou, recounted an incident. Zhang Xunwang, the prominent military leader, was stationed in Jiankang. One of his subordinate officers, Miao Tuanlian, was inspecting a campsite at the foot of Mount Jiang. Suddenly, his horse became startled on the road without any apparent reason. Miao found this strange and saw a large snake in a mulberry field. The snake wrapped its body around a tree, causing the tree to bend. It then stretched its head into a well to drink water. Frightened, Miao dared not advance further and whipped the horse to return. Meanwhile, the fourteenth officer, who happened to be leading fifty riders behind, heard Miao shout, "There is a strange creature ahead that is alarming. It is advisable to return quickly." The officer, young, strong, and brave, and relying on the numbers of his men, whipped his horse and went forward alone. He asked Miao to explain the situation and immediately drew his bow to shoot at the snake. The first shot missed, but the second hit the mulberry tree directly. The snake turned its head and stuck to the tip of the tree, opening its mouth toward the people and exhaling breath like black mist. People and horses all retreated more than a hundred steps, their faces pale. Within a few months, Miao, Zhang, and all their followers died. These events were told by Wang Jiagou.

The *Yijian Zhi* Volume 14, Part 15 **Poison from Bamboo Shoots** A local man named Nie Bangyong once visited Jiansu Temple. He roasted and ate two bamboo shoots in a bamboo grove. Upon returning home, he felt confused and distressed in his stomach. When the pain struck, it was almost unbearable. This continued for five years, during which he became extremely thin and emaciated. He would only recite the name of Guanyin to seek help. His brother, Hui Lian, was a monk at Yongning Temple. Nie Bangyong lived in a place called Lichi, which was 30 li from the county. Whenever he entered the city, he would stay in his brother's room. One night, he dreamed that someone told him, "Tomorrow when you leave the temple gate, you will meet a seller of obscure medicine. Go ask him, and he can cure your illness. If the disease is cured, you will pass the imperial examination next year. But only if Maitreya descends to earth." When he woke up, he told his brother about the dream, and they both found it extraordinary. The next day, he went out of the temple gate and indeed met the seller of medicine. The seller immediately said, "Your illness is very strange; it must be due to eating the bamboo shoots. During mating, snake sperm entered the bamboo shoots. You did not notice and ate them, and now there is a snake fetus in your belly, and it has already grown. Fortunately, the snake has not yet opened its eyes, so it can be removed. If it had opened its eyes, it would have eaten all five organs before leaving. Even I cannot save you." The seller then gave him two measures of medicine and instructed him to drink it with alcohol. Soon after taking the medicine, he vomited and had diarrhea, expelling a large amount of waste. A snake as thick as a finger was found in the waste, with its eyes still closed. Nie Bangyong was happy that his illness was cured, but he still doubted the prediction about passing the imperial examination. At the time, Yongning Temple was the examination venue. When the autumn examination came, Nie Bangyong took his place, and he was seated right under the sign of the Maitreya Temple. Indeed, he passed the examination.

Dong Suoyi He Ziyin, the Chief Magistrate of the Jiangdong region, traveled on inspection duty to Jiankang in October of the second year of the Longxing era. He entered Mount Mao to pay respects to Mr. Zhang Dadao. He heard that Dong Suoyi, a recluse, also lived in the mountains. Usually, he did not interact with scholars or officials. Upon seeing the approaching entourage, Dong would quickly climb to the top of a hill to avoid them. Ziyin cleared away all his officials and attendants, traveling alone with only one guard. He walked with a staff to visit Dong. When Dong asked who he was, Ziyin replied that he was an idle person. Dong invited him to sit together and pointed to his forehead, saying, "The Chancellor of Peace, Zhang Tianjue. The idle man of the four seas, Lu Dongbin." Ziyin was surprised by this, stood up, and said, "Chancellor Zhang. He is my grandfather. How did you know this?" Dong replied, "Your bone structure resembles his. I happened to mention it." He added, "I am very familiar with the Chancellor. When you return to the temple, you can see the inscriptions he left behind."

Zi Ying asked about his methods. He smiled and said, "I don't really understand anything. However, there are also very difficult aspects to grasp. You only know this much." Zi Ying then inquired about the essentials of health preservation. He replied again, "There are also very difficult aspects to grasp." Yet, he still refused to speak clearly. Zi Ying took his leave. He also asked what was needed. The person said, "Everything here is available. I am from Shaanxi and like to eat noodles. If you can provide enough of them, that would be sufficient." He said, "Next year, if there are no obligations, I hope you will visit me again." Zi Ying walked a few steps and looked back, only to see that the person had already started climbing the mountain. His movements were as swift as flying. When Zi Ying returned to Poyang, he told me that the next spring, he went to Jiankang again, intending to visit the person. But he died in Dangtu. What he said about next year having no obligations—wasn't that a premonition of his death? Zhe Dong Constitutional Office, Lei In the Zhe Dong provincial office, the governor's residence had five rooms to the south of the main hall. When Xie Chengfu Zuxin was in office, his younger brother Changfu lived there. On a summer day, there was a violent storm with thunder and lightning. It seemed as if the thunder and lightning were right in the room. Changfu was dressed formally and sitting upright, calmly observing the storm. He heard the sound of beams cracking. Before he could move to avoid it, they had already broken. The boxes and chests that were originally under the east wall had moved to the west side by the time the storm stopped. Their positions and heights remained unchanged. During the storm, it seemed that no one was in the room. This was reported by He Dexian.

#### Changzhou's Top Scorer In the tenth year of Shaoxing, during the autumn examination in Changzhou, a diviner predicted the name of the top scorer, stating that it should contain elements of grass, wood, and mouth. Those who heard this all thought that many people's names contained these three elements, and wondered if the prediction would come true. When the results were announced, Li Jian was the top scorer. His name "Jian" can be interpreted as "Xin Ke." His surname contains the element "wood," his given name contains the element "grass," and his courtesy name contains the element "mouth." Others did not have all these elements in their names. #### A Better Act of Charity Than Buddhist Rituals Tang Zhiyuan, a Privy Councilor from Jin坛 in Zhenjiang, was known for his stern and narrow-minded personality. He had few friends in both official and civilian life, and many villagers were afraid to interact with him. His son Tingzhi died first, but his two grandsons were both pure and cautious, able to reverse their grandfather's actions, and were highly praised by the clan and relatives. In the second year of Longxing, Tang Gong (Tang Zhiyuan) died. Several months later, he appeared in a dream to his eldest grandson, saying, "I lived without major faults, and after death, I did not fall into a bad state. There is no need to perform religious rituals. The year is suffering from famine. If you can release grain from the granary to help the people, it is far better than performing Buddhist rituals. It would also benefit me." That night, many villagers in the village dreamed that Tang Zhiyuan had come, and their words were the same. The eldest grandson then took five hundred hu of rice and gave it to the magistrate of Jin坛, to save the starving. He then gave another three hundred hu. As told by Yuan Zhongcheng. #### Wang Junming [Note: The provided text does not include any content about Wang Junming, so there is no translation for this section.]

A Man from Shu Named Wang Junming, Who Had Profound Knowledge of Future Events Wang Junming, a man from Shu, had profound knowledge of future events. Although he was blind in both eyes, he could predict celestial omens and disasters. During the early years of the Xuanhe era, he was in the capital city. He told others, "The royal aura of Bianzhou has come to an end. At night, you can look down upon the constellation of Di and Fang using a basin of water. There is not a single star shining over the celestial region of Bianzhou. Dig two feet into the ground outside the Xuande Gate and take a piece of soil and smell it. The soil is dry, barren, and desolate, no longer having any vitality. The stars do not shine, and the earth's veins are cut off. Can it be the capital of a powerful ruler?" He immediately submitted a petition, requesting to move the capital to Luoyang. At that time, China was at peace, and the ministers spoke together about his madness. An edict was issued to expel him from the jurisdiction, and he resided in the area between Zheng and Xu. During the Jingkang era, when the era name was changed, people thought highly of his words. An order was issued to send him by water wherever he was, and he was summoned into the palace and asked him. He still explained his previous statements, saying, "Changing the plan now is not too late." A man from Xianjing named Yuzhanyuan, who was at that time a Doctor of the Imperial Academy, was told by Wang Junming, "State affairs cannot be spoken of. Only Shu is a blessed land. It will not suffer from war. You should return to the west. Do not let your family test fate." Yuzhanyuan asked, "What will happen to you, sir?" Wang Junming replied, "My fate will end this year. I will surely die here. Only regret that at the time of death, I will not see my wife and children." Yuzhanyuan truly believed his words and hurriedly visited the local official He Wenzhen and requested to leave. He obtained the position of Deputy Governor of Chengdu. On the day the capital city was about to fall, an order was issued to send four guards in a sedan chair to urgently summon Wang Junming. They reached the palace gate and heard that the barbarians had already climbed the city walls. They abandoned him and left. He crawled out of the sedan chair, and no one knew where he went. It was suspected that he was pushed into a ditch or ravine. His family wept as they searched for him for several days but could not find him. They eventually considered the day he left home as the day he died. Yuzhanyuan also said this.

Nan Chan Bell Deity In the eleventh month of the eighth year of the Shaoxing era, a guest named Ma from the Ma family stayed at Nan Chan Temple in Wuxi County, Changzhou. He lived in a room under the bell tower. His wife gave birth to a son. A few days later, one of his concubines, for no apparent reason, began to speak in a divine voice. She scolded him for being indecent and dirty, saying, "Move out quickly. If you do not, there will be a great disaster. The dining utensil under the cooking stove broke the previous day. It was caused by me. You mistakenly whipped the maid." The Ma family considered this to be an act of witchcraft and called for a monk to recite the *Shou Leng Yan Zou* (Heart Sutra) to exorcise the evil spirit. The spirit spoke loudly, saying, "I am the principal deity of the temple, the one in charge of the bell. How can I be pressured? This bell was originally cast by a woman of the Chen family. It is now over a hundred years old. I guard it very carefully. All temples use the bell sound as a command. Every time it rings, the heavenly dragons gather together. Now, the officials who receive also strike it. I suffer it in place of my head. I cannot bear the pain. Why not tell the temple monk to make a different small bell? When the official arrives, then strike it. If you do not believe me, use the three future things as evidence." From this, two nights later, a courtesan came to set up offerings. Then, a merchant named Liu Shun gave a flagpole. Again, after ten days, the Xuanzhou monk Master Rizhi came and set up three great water and land gatherings. Master Zhi is a person in the ten stages. With great compassion, he does charitable deeds. One should add respect and worship. The spirit finished speaking silently. The Ma family feared and immediately moved out. The so-called three things, all as he said. The county person Bian Zhichang wrote a record.

Hong Cui Zhong A scholar from Le Ping named Hong You, with the courtesy name Cui Zhong, was handsome, lively, and talented. However, he enjoyed making criticisms and remarks through language. He once wrote a *Shan Ju Fu* (Mountain Dwelling Ode), which was composed entirely of colloquial language. It recorded all the gossip and news of the neighborhood, leaving nothing unrecorded, and thoroughly described all the affairs of a village. He was particularly friendly with his cousin named Pu. In the eighth year of the Zhenghe era (1118 AD), he passed the imperial examination but died before receiving an official salary. He had no children, and all the expenses for his burial were provided by Pu. Several years later, Pu sat up late into the night with a doctor named Ye Junli. Ye went to sleep first, but Pu suddenly got up and bowed to someone, then invited the person to sit and converse. The family secretly listened to it, and it was the voice of Cui Zhong. He said sadly, "I miss you as if it were yesterday. I wish to see you once more and reminisce. Thank you for the kindness in burying me. But I have come to your door many times, but were always blocked by the gatekeeper. Now I have come with your brother Seven Censhì from Zhouyuan. Zhouyuan is where Seven Censhì is buried. That is why I was able to enter. I remember that it was not my brother's high righteousness that saved me. My bones would have been left in the ditches. I only realized I was dead when it happened. But saw officials come and say, 'To lead me to the official realm.'"

He immediately followed along. Now, in the underworld, he is making a judgment. He is completely at ease and without any worries, but especially suffers from the darkness of the environment. The atmosphere is gloomy and sorrowful. Even if one were an official for a hundred years, it is not as good as living in the human world for a single day. The underworld official spoke to him, saying, "One should have been a high-ranking official in life, but due to one's speech sins, one spoke falsely about others' faults, therefore, everything is nullified. Now it is too late to regret." He was reminded of a collection of writings from his lifetime, which was in the possession of the twelfth son. He asked his brother to go and fetch it before he wakes up tomorrow morning, as it was only in the box at the side. Suddenly, in a dazed state, he did not remember that he was already dead. He called someone to make tea, and then disappeared. At the time, the lights were set up, but there was no more light. The doctor named Ye was surprised and asked him, and only then realized. The next day, he went to the house of the twelfth son and obtained the book. Cui Zhong had always been at odds with his wife, and later remarried to Ye, also often coming to talk. Ye asked him, saying, "All his life, I heard that Hong Cui Zhong was very learned. If it is true, he can recite the *Rites of Zhou*. Immediately, he read aloud with a loud voice, not a single word was wrong." The twelfth son is his nephew.

Fever Demon of Yu Bi A clan member named Hong Yang returned to his residence from LePing. By then, the day had already turned dark. Two servants were carrying a sedan chair, and one was carrying a load. They desperately wanted to reach home in the middle of the night. Twenty li south of the town was WuKou Market, and another five li further south was Yu Bi Field. When they arrived, it was already the second watch. There was faint moonlight. They heard a loud sound coming from the mountains, as if dozens of large trees were being broken. The sound was getting closer. Hong Yang thought it was a tiger, but the sound was not like that of a tiger. He knew it was something unusual. He quickly got out of the sedan chair and consulted with the servants about where to hide. He intended to return to WuKou, but it was no longer possible. He wanted to continue forward, but the nearest human habitation was still far away. He had no plan to proceed or retreat. He saw a small stream on the left side of the road without water, which could provide cover. He immediately went down. The object was already standing in front of him. It was about three zhang long, and from top to bottom, it was entirely made of lamps. The two sedan chair bearers were terrified to the point of death. The load bearer hid in the sedan chair and held his breath. Hong Yang usually held the Great Compassion Mantra of Guanyin. He urgently recited it several hundred times. The object stood still and did not move. Hong Yang was also extremely frightened, but he continued to recite the mantra. The object slightly retreated, and the distance between them became somewhat greater. It shouted, "I am leaving," and went straight to the field below for about one li, entering a small household. Then it disappeared. Hong Yang returned home and fell ill. He recovered after one year. The load bearer also became ill. The two sedan chair bearers all died. Later, when they visited the small household in Yu Bi Field, the entire family of five or six people had all died from an epidemic. They finally realized that the unusual object was likely a fever demon.

Fuller's Vajra Ji Xian, a man from LePing, lived in the town of JiuDun. Later, he bought the Cheng family's mansion and moved in. After several years, the house was under the watch of spirits, which sometimes appeared in form. The spirit introduced itself as Huang Sanjiang, saying, "We were both merchants trading silk and brocade, and we both ended up here. Now I will share this house with you." Initially, this was not considered strange. However, the spirit later entered the room of Ji Xian's son. The couple slept as usual, but by morning, their hair was tied together, and they were moved to another house. While eating rice, it suddenly turned into wheat. While eating early rice, it suddenly turned into late rice. Sometimes, guests eating together would transform while eating, and everyone was frightened and left. Ji Xian invited sorcerers to perform rituals to drive the spirit away, and he also invited Taoist priests to perform rituals to thank and appease the gods, but none of it worked. Near his residence was a monk named Quanshi from FengLin Temple, who could recite the Huiji spell. Ji Xian wanted to summon the spirit. At that time, his son's wife was already ill. The spirit told her, "I have heard that your family will send Quanshi to deal with me. The Huiji Vajra, though it has a thousand hands and a thousand eyes, is still a formidable force."

A Ghost's Mischief Only during the great fasting offering does the ghost often snatch the sour dumplings. How could it harm me? The monk, having accepted the invitation, first set up a ritual site in the temple and chanted spells for seven days and nights. When it was about to finish, the ghost again told the woman, "The bald-headed one has indeed come. I will cautiously avoid him." But it won't be more than a few months before it comes back again. What is there to fear? I have never brought disaster to your family. If I had known it would be like this, I would have regretted not making plans earlier. When the monk arrived, he ordered a young boy to stand in the room and watch for signs, calling it "opening the light." The boy saw a great god entering with spears, halberds, flags, and banners. One god held a large banner, inscribed with "filthy trace divine troops." The god circled around a hundred times. The ghost rushed and crouched under the woman's bed. When the god left, the ghost came out. Its head was suddenly several times larger than before. Soon it was captured and carried away by someone. The monk said, "It should be seen again behind the sick person's bed. Only then will it truly go away." The next day, two cow horns emerged from beside the large cabinet behind the bed. After a long time, they disappeared. From then on, it never came again. All the disturbances lasted from spring until autumn. The Xu family was left desolate. Three matters as told by Hong Fu.

A Ghost of Jiezhucun During the reign of Emperor Ling, in the village of Jiezhucun in Yiyang County, Wu Qingchang sent his servant to guard the rice in the fields at night. There was someone with a sickle secretly cutting the rice. The servant chased him with a spear but failed to catch him. The next night, the same thing happened again. In the morning, when he looked at his rice, it was as if nothing had happened. The servant, who always had courage, thought to himself, "The spear is too short to be effective. He should prepare a longer spear." That night, the thief came again. When he saw someone coming, he would run away. The servant ran after him in big strides and stuck the spear into the ground, then caught him. Holding a torch to see, it turned out to be a piece of old pine wood, taken from under the bed. The next day, he planned to burn it and told the village witch doctor. The witch doctor said, "It can transform. You cannot burn it whole. You should break it into pieces and put it in a small pot, then boil it with water." The fire was just getting hot, and the smell was unbearable. He heard from the pots, cries of distress: "Please forgive me. I won't bother you again. If not, he will surely come to the witch doctor for revenge." The servant broke the pots and threw them in the fields, and indeed, the thief did not return.

A Poem Found in a Post Station in Xin'an Ni Juji's second son, Ni Ye, served as a magistrate in Xinjian, Jiangxi. He requested leave to send his wife back to visit her parents-in-law. On his return journey, he reached the Xin'an area. He sent someone ahead to reserve a post station. Upon arrival, he tried to enter but heard voices from inside. Approaching to listen, he heard laughter and conversation. Outside, there were no servants or attendants. He attempted to inquire about the identity of the people inside but failed. He peered inside upon entering and heard the sound coming from the main hall. Upon entering the main hall, he then heard the sound in a room. Feeling suspicious and fearful, he quickly left. He visited all the residents outside the station, and one person said that once a little boy had been sent to borrow writing tools but was not seen leaving. Ni Ye then, with a strong servant, forced open the door and entered directly. In the west room, he saw a small poem written on the wall: "Frost wind withers the orchid. Silver screen brings morning chill. Lightly brushed eyebrows and red cheeks. Xiao Xiang Bay, Lotus Bay. Longing, a few sorrowful sighs. Leisure in a painted building with wine. The ink is still wet. Writing tools are on the ground. No signs of human activity." The Ni family did not dare to stay overnight and left. The story is told by Chun Nian.

During his service in Beijing, Zhao Qingxian, the Chancellor and Prime Minister, fell ill with diarrhea. After more than a month, he passed away. His body was washed and dressed in new clothes, preparing for burial. Suddenly, a letter arrived from the capital, but it contained no official documents. Instead, it brought a medicine from a noble household that proved to be a divine aid. They immediately gave it to him, and shortly after, he revived. They quickly sent messengers to the capital to inquire at the imperial office. It turned out that one of his family members had been suffering from severe diarrhea and had mistakenly placed the medicine in a postal box. Later, Zhao Qingxian fell ill with jaundice, a condition that was life-threatening. An elderly woman carrying a small box came to the door. When asked what she was selling, she replied, "I am good at treating jaundice." They called her in, and she took a heated iron spoon and applied it to several parts of his body. The yellowing of his skin and eyes quickly receded, and he fully recovered the next day. Afterwards, Zhao Qingxian served as the Deputy Magistrate of Xuzhou. When he was about to leave his official post, he fell ill again with severe diarrhea. He had a longstanding friendship with a Daoist hermit named Liang. One day, Liang suddenly arrived and asked about his illness. Zhao Qingxian said, "There is no harm." Liang then asked for a cup of water, placed it on a table, and sat down to recite a spell. Soon, the water rose as if it were boiling. He gave the water to Zhao Qingxian, and his pain immediately subsided. Zhao Qingxian felt grateful but had no way to repay him. He once received a silver bowl from a Goryeo envoy and wanted to give it to Liang. Before he could speak, Liang laughed and said, "What difference does Goryeo silver make from copper? You don't need to give it to me." He then bowed and left, and Zhao Qingxian could not catch up to him. In Su Shi's collection of poems, there is a poem dedicated to Liang the Daoist: "Seeking herbs, Old Man Hu wanders everywhere. Laughing, he strokes the golden statue with his hands. Your grandfather knew you for a long time. What can the child of creation do to you? In the cold mountain, there is no calendar. On the rain-soaked river, a fisherman wears a straw cloak. The gods and immortals protect the many officials. They have not yet grown tired of the joys of earthly life." This is the same Daoist hermit.

### Ghost of the Daming Warehouse Wang Lüdao, the Left Chancellor, was supervising the Daming warehouse gate in the Chongning warehouse of Daming Prefecture during the early years of the Zhenghe era. His official residence was inside the main gate. One night, dozens of guards and soldiers on watch duty called out simultaneously, and the sound reached the outside. The Left Chancellor got up in alarm. A soldier said, "There is a monster that is very terrifying. Do not come out, sir." He then hid behind a screen to watch. A large ghost straddled the warehouse gate and sat, its feet hanging down to the ground. It kicked its legs contentedly, and the tiles on the roof all shook. After a while, it took a wide step out and entered the home of Scholar Li, where it disappeared. Scholar Li died immediately. ### Xing the Great General Xing the Great General was from Baizhou and lived near the frontier. He became wealthy through unrighteous means and accumulated minor services to become a high-ranking military officer. Once, on the Cold Food Festival, he led his family to visit their ancestral graves. After the sacrifice, they drank wine. They saw a white mouse entering and exiting among the pine and cypress trees. They chased it, and when the mouse saw them, it touched its head to the ground and did not move. So they took it back. The mouse's body fur was all white, and its eyes and paws were bright red and lovely. Xing held it and placed it on his horse. When he got home, it ran away and was no longer seen. That day, all kinds of strange phenomena appeared. Pots and tripods were seen embracing each other and walking, utensils changed places, cats and dogs spoke human language, and could not be scolded. A small piece of wall near Xing's bed fell off, and a small person appeared, with a face like a clay or wooden doll. Five days later, it had grown and became a head of a barbarian, with long, tangled hair. It was very ugly and disgusting, and its voice was not much different from that of a human. It demanded wine and meat. Xing dared not refuse and gave it whatever it asked for. If it was delayed, it would get angry. The entire family, old and young, served it with utmost caution. After a year, Xing died, and all the strange phenomena disappeared. This was told by the old man San Shi.

The Records of the Strange from the East, Volume Fifteen, Fourteenth Story The Dyeing Craftsman Dong A son of the Dong family from the village of Hongyuan. His family was originally in the dyeing trade. He had a particular fondness for catching flying birds. Upon catching them, he would break their brains and thread them with bamboo. Upon returning, he would burn the rice stalks and thatched huts, roasting the feathers and fur until they were clean, and then sell them. The number of birds he killed in his lifetime was incalculable. When he grew old, he contracted a strange disease, causing rough skin all over his body, with scales like a tree. When he experienced severe itching, it could not be relieved by scratching alone; he would only take reeds and burn his body, which would then calm down after a while. He then suffered from headaches and did not take medicine. When the pain was severe, he would have someone hit his head with a bamboo stick dozens of times until it subsided slightly. People believed it was a retribution for killing living beings. In this way, he suffered for three years, paying for his sins each day, and then died.

A Witch from Linchuan In Linchuan, there was a witch who served a deity named Muheng Sanlang. She specialized in chasing and capturing ghosts and spirits. Her powers were highly effective and well-known, and people from far and near sought her out. She feared that these spirits might harm her, so she warned her family every day: "If someone from outside visits me, no matter how close or distant they are, just tell them all that I am not at home first, then inform me." One day, a villager was plowing a field and saw two guests carrying something and walking along a side path. They lifted their skirts and walked cautiously, as if something was blocking their way. The villager asked, "Why are you acting so strangely?" They replied, "The water is deep and the road is slippery. The path is full of mud, and we are eager to move forward but cannot." The villager laughed and said, "There is no water on flat ground. How could you say such a thing?" The two guests realized their mistake and apologized, saying, "We were dizzy and delusional. Thanks to your guidance, we now understand." They happily moved forward without any obstacles and went straight to the witch's door. They introduced themselves as officials from Jianzhou who had recently been troubled by a spirit and were saved by a monk. They sent them with new tea to express their gratitude. The family was happy and led them in, comforting them. They finally told the witch. The witch asked where they were. They replied, "They have already entered." She was greatly surprised and said, "Why did I always warn you? It's too late now." She sent someone out to look for them, but saw nothing. The witch knew she would die and was just giving instructions for her after-death affairs. Suddenly, someone hit her back, and she fell to the ground. Saliva congealed in her throat, and she died soon after. Told by Li Deyuan.

**The Daoist Practitioner of Shangyou** During the fourth year of the Xuanhe era, the local official Dong Pu served as the Chief Magistrate of Shangyou in Nan'an Commandery. A Daoist practitioner arrived from the mountains, standing over six feet tall. He said he would travel from here to pay homage to Mount Heng in the south and mentioned having magical arts. The Chief Magistrate prepared a banquet and invited guests. He then had the Daoist practitioner come forward to demonstrate his skills. The practitioner carried a bottomless bamboo basket, full of mud. Hundreds of people gathered in the courtyard to watch. The Daoist practitioner instructed each person to take a small amount of mud, like a bean, and put it in their mouth. He asked everyone what they wanted to make, whether it be fruits, dishes, or candy, regardless of the season or the land. Each person expressed their wish. The Daoist practitioner then took a deep breath from the sky and blew the air into their mouths. Each person's desire was fulfilled, and the mud transformed accordingly. He warned them not to chew or swallow, and they could change it into another thing. Thus, what was meat could become fruit, and what was fruit could become meat. The transformations were countless and endless, but the mud remained unchanged. Some of the Chief Magistrate's descendants did not believe it and sent a local servant, Hu Man, to verify the trick.

He admonished him, saying: "You also claimed to desire something. Even if it were true, you should provisionally say it is not. Try to see how it will be handled." The servant, holding mud in his mouth, called out, "I want cherries." The Daoist scolded him and asked, "Is that so?" The servant replied, "It is not." The Daoist asked repeatedly, and each time the servant answered the same. Laughing, the Daoist said, "Are you trying to play a trick on me? I shall torment you." He then exhaled into him, and it became garlic. The pungent odor spread outward. The servant still insisted it was not so. The Daoist then informed the crowd, saying, "This man has been insulted greatly. I shall let all of you hear it." Pointing at his mouth, he said, "Feces is coming out." As he spoke, foul air filled the space, spreading throughout the courtyard. The servant quickly spat it out, took water to rinse and clean himself, but even after a long time, the smell still lingered. The onlookers laughed heartily, and became even more respectful of him. The Daoist also requested to leave. When offered money, he refused it, but asked only for wine. He drank several jin and then departed. In the end, no one knew who he was or what his surname was. Dong’s grandson, Hong Yingxian, was present under official service and witnessed this marvel with his own eyes. Yingxian said.

In the capital city, Wang Xiwén encountered a man pushing a small cart. On the cart was a large jar, adorned with floral decorations on the outside. A small sign was placed that read "Various Dye Shops." Hanging from the rack were several strips of assorted colored silk. People peered into the jar, which contained only a few liters of murky liquid. Someone handed a piece of silk to the man and said they wanted it dyed blue. The man received the silk, threw it into the jar, and a short while later took it out. It had turned into blue silk. Another person handed a piece of gauze and asked to have it dyed with cinnabar red. The man also threw it into the jar, and when it was taken out, it had become cinnabar red gauze. Other colors could be produced as well, such as yellow, red, black, or white. The dye could turn red into green and purple into deep red. Whatever color was requested, the dye responded instantly. Yet, despite the constant use, the jar never ran dry. The dyed colors were always bright, clean, and of high quality, as if they had been dyed in a shop specializing in silk for days. No one could figure out what method the dye used.

**Zhao Shanguang** In the 29th year of the Shaoxing era, Zhao Dunben Buwei served as the Deputy Magistrate of Lin’an. His son, Zhao Shanguang, was by his side. That night, Shanguang dreamed of a man holding a summons chasing him. The man said, “The Lord of the Office is calling you.” Shanguang refused to go, saying, “My father and the Lord of the Office were colleagues. I know only the duties of a son and heir. Why are you calling me?” The man holding the summons seized him and dragged him along. Shanguang asked, “What attire should I wear to appear before him?” The man replied, “Wear the official robe, and that will suffice.” When they arrived at the official’s hall, the courtyard was filled with stern attendants, and the solemn presence of the master was fearsome. The official, seated behind his desk, looked angrily and said, “Zhao Shanzuo, in your past life, why did you dare kill a pregnant woman?” Shanguang fell to his knees and replied, “My name is Shanguang, not Shanzuo.” The official turned to the officer who had brought him and said, “Is this a trivial matter, and have you mistakenly summoned the wrong person?” He ordered the man to be dragged to prison, but then released Shanguang. When Shanguang returned home, he saw only darkness before his eyes and could not feel his body. He thought to himself of how he had recited the Lotus Sutra all his life, and wondered why he could not see anything now. Suddenly, he felt the scripture he had recited in his hand, glowing brightly. He realized he was lying on his bed. He threw himself into the bed and awoke. His family had not noticed anything. Seven years later, he became the Household Official of Raozhou, and then he died.

**A Wrongful Dream in Xuanzhou** Li Nanjin was a guest in Xuanzhou, where he became friends with a courtesan. In the eighteenth year of the Shaoxing era (1148 CE), Qin Di was the governor of the region. During a musical gathering for guests, Li, in plain clothes, secretly observed the event and used his hand to signal the courtesan he was friendly with, attempting to speak with her. Qin happened to see this and flew into a rage, ordering Li to be shackled and sent to prison, intending to bring charges against him. In the same prison were four serious criminals, who had been detained for robbing a wealthy man's property. The officials, having accepted bribes from the people, wished to sentence them all to death. After a month of intense interrogation and torture, they searched desperately for a reason to kill them, but could not find one. Li Nanjin, who was skilled in litigation, devised a plan for the officials, instructing them to retrieve the case records and legal statutes. He repeatedly studied them and even drafted the interrogation questions for the officials. They questioned the four prisoners in turn, but none could say anything. When the case was concluded, all four were beaten to death. The officials indeed received a heavy bribe and, in return, arranged for a secret passage to redeem Li Nanjin from prison. Two years later, Li returned to Le Ping and, accompanied by his uncle, Shi Yin, went to De Xing to visit the land survey official Wang Bing. They stayed overnight at a guesthouse in Xiangtun. In the middle of the night, Li was startled from a nightmare. His uncle called to him, but he did not respond. After shaking him many times, Li could only make a choked sound from his throat. His uncle then ran out and called for help from the neighbors. Together, they shouted until, after a long time, Li finally awoke. He sat up and told his uncle, "Evil deeds are truly not to be done. I did this out of necessity before, but now I dare not hide anything." He then fully recounted the events. He said, "I just had a dream in which I was in Xuanzhou and met four men on the road. They pulled at my clothes and pleaded with me, saying, 'You have no conscience. You used your cunning to kill us. We never wronged you, and now we must repay you with death.' Then they took a large iron basin and placed it over my head, which is why I could not speak. Had it not been for my uncle's rescue, I would have truly died from the nightmare." Ten years later, Li finally met his end by encountering a snake demon. Hong Fu said.

The Wronged Concubine of the Horse A woman named Chang from the region of Shu was first married to Chu Chunqing from Yiyang, Hunan. She had a favored concubine named Ma, who was jealous of her favor and envied her status. Taking advantage of Chu's absence, Chang beat Ma to death. Chu served as a county magistrate before his death. After Chu's death, Chang remarried Cheng Xuan from Poyang, Jiangxi. In the second month of the second year of the Ganda era (1166 AD), three days after giving birth, she could not deliver the child. During the day, she saw the ghost of Ma holding a stick and whipping her abdomen. Cheng called upon the Taoist priest Xu Zhongshi from the Tianqing Temple to perform a ritual to treat the haunting, and also gave her sacred water. She eventually gave birth to a daughter, but because the child was not viable, the ghostly disturbances became more severe. Chang cried day and night, addressing the heavens: "The ghost is beating me with the stick used to kill her. I cannot bear the pain." The ghost replied, "I was not killed by you; it was a maid who used the stick improperly and took my life." The ghost added, "All of this was the will of the mother-in-law. What more can be said?" Cheng called upon another Taoist priest, who commanded divine soldiers to pursue and capture the ghost.

The ghost claimed that the deity had wronged it to the point of death. Even though the priest was highly respected, what could they do against my just cause? All present saw Chang lying on the bed, engaged in a prolonged and difficult discussion with someone. The Taoist priest thought that it was ultimately impossible to apply the law, so he used kind words to persuade. He recited many scriptures and spells for spiritual assistance. The ghost nodded and then left. After five days, it reappeared and said, "The power of scriptures and spells can only help me be reborn, but killers must pay with their lives, which certainly cannot be avoided." Chang replied, "If so, I must die. Even if I regret it, there is nothing to be done. However, when this concubine died, she had hairpins, earrings, clothes, and other valuables worth hundreds of thousands. Now I will repay all of them, to avoid causing trouble in future lives." She called out and asked, "Do you want copper coins or paper money?" The ghost laughed and said, "I am a ghost, not a human, so why would I need copper coins?" She then bought a hundred bundles of paper money, prayed, and burned them. After the smoke cleared, Chang died. It was the sixth day of the third month.

Water Fight In the village of Hechong, LePing County, the Cheng family resided. To the north of their settlement, there was a field, a walled enclosure of dozens or even hundreds of acres. In the fourth month of the summer of the fourteenth year of the Shaoxing era (1144 AD), after a long rain had just cleared up, the sun was directly overhead with no clouds in the sky. The water in the fields, as if gathered by some unseen force, all converged into one mass. It flowed directly westward to a point called "Cedar Post" and stopped there, reaching a height of several tens of feet. There were no embankments or dams, and it did not overflow or break its banks. To the south of the village, the house of Cheng Bo Gao was about three hundred steps away. Suddenly, the water in his well rose, also reaching several tens of feet high, twisting and turning like a rainbow and roaring like thunder. The water flowed northward, penetrating through the wall of Cheng Cong's house and destroying the northwest corner of the tower before passing through. Villagers looked out and saw something with two horns like a sheep, leaping and jumping within it, accompanied by several boys dressed in green. They headed directly to the side of the Cedar Post. The water in the fields rushed to meet them, fighting against them, advancing and retreating. After ten moments, the conflict resolved. The northern water dispersed back into the fields, and compared to before the fight, there was no reduction. The southern water also followed its old path into the well. That day, the entire village was in a state of panic, suspecting a water disaster. But later, there was nothing else. Cheng Bo Gao, who was originally wealthy and prominent in his village, gradually declined in status. Not long after, he died. Now the fields are all owned by others, and Cheng Cong also quarreled with his brother over dividing their property, taking several years to settle. Thus, it was not a good omen.

**The Tavern of the Capital** Lian Bu, the grandson of Xuanzhong named Tan Xiao Zhi, was studying at the Imperial Academy. On the Lantern Festival, he and three fellow students from the same dormitory took leave and went out to tour the city. They viewed everything in sight, their eyes filled and their feet weary. Yet in their hearts, they were constantly preoccupied—with women. At the fourth watch of the night, the streets were nearly empty. Suddenly, they saw a single rider approaching, accompanied by several attendants. They drew near and looked at her stealthily—she was a beautiful woman. They followed her, wanting to see where she was going. Soon they arrived at a secluded alleyway tavern. She dismounted and entered. She bought wine and drank alone, frequently laughing and chatting with her attendants. The three men also entered and sat facing her. They sat at the table and ordered wine. Unable to control their emotions, they called out from afar to the woman, saying, “How about sitting with us?” She immediately replied, “Yes.” All of them were delighted and hurried to join her. They even urged Xiao Zhi to sit close to her knee, thinking she must be a famous courtesan. The woman covered her head with a cloth, so they could not see her full face. The guests joked and pulled the cloth away. To their horror, it was a large, ugly ghost—extremely frightening. They all shouted together, “There’s a ghost!” The tavern servant came out to see, but found nothing there. He laughed at their foolishness and they told him of what they had experienced. The servant said, “I only saw three scholars enter the tavern. How could there be such a thing?” The three men trembled all night long, and did not dare to return until dawn.

桂 Bǎixiáng, a man from Kuaiji, was renowned for his ability to summon and control the Six Jia and Six Ding. He was famous for adhering to the righteous method and was called the True Official. Previously, under the Chang Bridge of the Wu Song River, many boats were damaged when the tide came, and it was believed that this was caused by the evil nature of a dragon. The county people collectively hired a messenger to carry a petition to ask Guì. Guì said, "If I use my method, I must prepare a petition to be submitted. Then this dragon will surely die. The matter is too great, and I cannot bear to do it. I will do something easier instead." He then gave a judgment to the messenger and instructed him, "You go back and take it to the usual place where boats sink. Tell them, 'Guì Bǎixiáng asks the river dragon why it harms people. It should quickly repent and reform. If it commits again, I will fly a petition to heaven. It will be captured and punished according to law.'" This person carried it back and reported to the elders. They hired a different fisherman to wait for the tide to come and report from the fourth bridge. The fisherman welcomed and threw the judgment document, fully reporting Guì's words. In an instant, the tide head reached the place, and it immediately flowed back. Since then, it no longer caused harm. These two stories were told by Zhao Gong Mào Yuángōng.

Tang Huisi, a man of high rank, served as the governor of Jiangxi. On his inspection tour of the region, he sailed past the Wu Cheng Temple. He disembarked to perform rituals but did so without due respect. By evening, a sudden storm arose, causing both masts of his ship to break. Despite all efforts to save the ship, they barely managed to reach the shore. The next day, as they set sail for Nankang, the boatman suggested offering a pig to the temple. Huisi said, "Considering what happened yesterday, would I dare to value even one pig?" He allowed the offering but felt deeply resentful. As soon as the ship left the shore, the wind carried it back, and they had to turn around several times before they could continue their journey from dawn until noon. On the following day, they arrived at Dagu Mountain. The boatman again proposed making an offering, but Huisi angrily responded, "We have been feeding the dragon with our pigs for days; it must be satisfied. Let us continue northward without looking back." Just a few miles later, the sky and earth grew dark, and thunder, lightning, rain, and wind all came together. People could not see each other's faces, and white waves reached the sky. A huge dragon emerged from the water, as tall as the masts, blocking the river. Its mouth spewed fierce fire, brightly illuminating the people. Hundreds of spirits and mysterious creatures, with strange shapes and forms, surrounded the ship from all sides, too numerous to count. The people on the ship knew their lives were in danger, so they tied their clothes together with belts, hoping that if they drowned, their bodies would be easier to find. The palace guard official Niu Xin, accompanied by an attendant in a different boat, was the most fearful, lying face down on the board. He saw a man with white hair, no head covering, and a small bun at the top of his head. The man said, "Do not fear; this does not concern you." Huisi, dressed in formal attire, bowed down and asked for forgiveness, offering many Buddhist scriptures to appease the dragon. The dragon gradually distanced itself and then disappeared. The darkness also cleared, and the paddler, calmed down, resumed rowing. They realized that they were not in the right place; it turned out they were going against the current, forty miles south of Dagu. Initially, they were not aware of this. The magistrate of Nanchang, Feng Xishu, recounted this story.

Hu Fu Zimu, having been dismissed from his position as Tongpan of Rongzhou and assigned to a new post, traveled from Changsha by boat on the river. It was June, and the heat was intense. Hu Fu Zimu was on the boat, and those traveling with him were all unclothed and not wearing shoes or hats, passing the time with chess. Suddenly, the chessboard tilted, and they thought it was just a coincidence, continuing to play without stopping. The wife of the boatman shouted, "Burn incense quickly! A dragon has entered the boat!" Startled, they looked and saw something entangled, rising above the water surface, right in front of the boat's entrance, pressing down. The boat sank several feet, and the stench and saliva filled the boat. The scales were as large as a basin, and its light could be used for reflection. Hu Fu Zimu hurriedly put on boots and clothes, praying and bowing many times. The boat was about to sink completely, and the dragon suddenly leaped into the water, the sound like the collapse of a house. It caused several large waves, but the water calmed. The boat was already far away. Hu Fu Zimu arrived in Wuzhou as an official and died. Wang Gengsou said, "It was his relative."

程师回 燕人程师回，归国后担任江西大将。绍兴十二年，朝廷派遣他返回北方。他在乘船经过大孤山下时，船夫告诉他，凡是经过这里的船只，不得奏乐或煎油。有人违反了这个规定，菩萨必定会发怒。程师回问道：“菩萨是谁？”船夫不肯说，但在多次追问下，终于告诉他那是一条龙。程师回笑道：“它怎么敢如此？”他说道：“龙生活在水中，我无法控制它的行为；我在船上，龙又怎能控制我？”于是，他命令随从敲鼓、吹笛、演奏异域音乐，并烧油炸鱼，香味弥漫到船外。他自己取出胡床坐在船尾，将弓箭、剑戟等兵器摆放在身旁。船夫们彼此看着，捶胸长叹道：“我们都被这个胡人所累，命不久矣，怎么办？”当时天气晴朗，忽然狂风大作，浓雾四起。一声雷鸣后，一条形似金盘双目的生物出现在烟波之中，距离船只仅数十步之遥，它盯着船想要靠近，气势非常凶猛。程师回说：“这就是所谓的菩萨吗？”他拉弓射出一箭，正中对方的一只眼睛。那生物退缩了，转身潜入水中。不久，风浪平息，它缓缓流走。人们都佩服他的勇气。从此，江上的行人都以烹饪油为戒，相传蛟龙之类听到油香就会出现，常常跃入船只，导致船只翻覆，有时甚至会冲破堤岸离去。程师回所射杀的，大概就是这种生物。

Xu Si, a man from Yongkang, Wuzhou, was known for his literary skills and was highly respected in his community. He was often sought by locals to compose inscriptions for their ancestors. Throughout his life, he never ceased reading. After serving as a magistrate in Jianzhou, he returned home. In his old age, he suddenly fell ill with forgetfulness. He could not distinguish any objects in the world. Even when facing his guests and old acquaintances, he did not recognize them. His wife and children, when in front of him, were treated like strangers. While eating meat, he did not know it was meat; while drinking alcohol, he did not know it was alcohol. He was no longer aware of changes in hunger, thirst, cold, heat, day, and night. His hands could not write a single character. He lived like this for three years before passing away. It is said that due to his intense study and deep thinking, he lost his conscience. This is according to Yu Liangneng. From the "Yijian Zhi" (Records of the Supernatural), Volume 16, Item 15: Liu Gu's Daughter In the jurisdiction of Fangcheng County, there is a Flower Mountain near Maibi Market. The townspeople collectively raised funds to build a Daoist hall to accommodate Daoist nuns. A villager named Liu Gu abandoned her family to enter the Daoist order and lived in the hall. Her daughter was already married. One night, she dreamed of her. Her daughter wept and said, "I fought with my husband yesterday, hitting each other. I mistakenly stepped on the threshold, causing damage to both breasts, and died." Liu Gu was shocked and woke up. She immediately went down the mountain to visit her daughter's house and inquire. It turned out that her daughter indeed died yesterday. After inquiring about the details, Liu Gu confirmed that the dream was accurate. She wanted to take her son-in-law to the county office. The villagers persuaded her to stop, saying, "Although you have left home, you still have a daughter to rely on. It would not be wise to do this." She stopped. The village official also hoped for no trouble and kept it secret. The daughter's injustice was never rectified.

A Woman from the Wang Family in Yunxi In the seventh year of the Zhenghe era, during the autumn, a woman from the Wang family in Yunxi, Wuyuan County, died. After a day, she revived. A local scholar named Zhu Qiaonian, who was studying by the river, hurriedly went to ask what she had seen. She said, "Yesterday, I was just entering my room when I saw two officials waiting outside the door. They then led me away. We walked through the sandy wilderness. The weather was dim and hazy, and I could not distinguish morning from evening. Soon, we entered a large city. The markets and towns were very prosperous. All the deceased relatives, neighbors, and acquaintances were there. We met and were surprised and sighed. They asked why I had come here. The pursuing officials led me into the government office. We passed by the west wing and stood in a respectful manner in the room. The official checked the records and pointed out, 'Are you the daughter of the Yu family from Wuyuan County, She Prefecture?' I replied, 'Yes.' He asked, 'What are your father's and grandfather's names? What is your hometown?' I replied, 'No.' He pinched my ear and said, 'You are mistaken.' He scolded the pursuers and sent them out. After a long time, another woman was brought in. Her body was covered in blood, and several infants were pulling and tearing her clothes, circling around her. The official then asked about her surname, family background, and hometown. All matched the records. He ordered her to be imprisoned and turned to me, saying, 'We have the same surname. So we were mistakenly brought here together. This woman killed five children. The children's grievances were very severe. Although her life span was not yet complete, the underworld authorities had no choice but to record her first. You are now returning to the world of the living. You should tell the world what you have seen. Do not kill your children without reason.' He sent someone else to escort me out and pushed me into the river. Then I woke up. Qiaonian immediately went with his family to inquire about the family of the woman who was pursued. Indeed, she died on that day. Qiaonian wrote an article to record it.

Red Flags in the Sea During the time when Chancellor Zhao resided in Zhu Ya, the military governor of Guilin sent an envoy to deliver wine and rice as gifts. From Leizhou, they floated south across the sea. After three days, they raised the sails and set off early. The wind was very strong. Looking out, they saw in the vast waves, the red flags fluttering, following each other downwards, visible to the utmost extent without end, from afar, it could not be clearly discerned. Suspecting it to be sea bandits or foreign troops, they called out to the boatmen. The boatmen shook their hands, signaling not to speak, their fearful expressions were evident. They quickly entered the boat, with their hair loose and holding a knife, standing on the back of the boat, cutting off their tongue, blood dripped into the water, warning the envoy to close his eyes and sit inside the ship. For about two hours, they heard the boatmen call out, "Reborn, reborn!" Then said, "What was seen this morning, it was a giant fish. Never seen in one's life. The so-called red flags were actually the fish's scales. The fish that can swallow a boat, what is worth mentioning. If this fish had met our boat, within ten or so miles, if the fish moved just a little, we would already have been drowned in the whale waves. How terrifying it is." At that time, the boat was heading south, while the fish was heading north, facing each other for two hours, they traveled several hundred miles apart. Calculating its body length, it must be more than a thousand miles. The story of the giant fish and bird in the *Zhuangzi* is not a fable. At that time, my father-in-law Zhang Yuandao was the military governor. Zhang Zisi heard it from the envoy. My father-in-law did not know.

Three Mountain Tailgate East of Ninghai County in Taizhou, there is an island reached by crossing the sea. It is called San Shan Town, where a garrison of one hundred patrol and inspection soldiers is stationed. Only after two tides can one reach there. When my father was the chief clerk, he once went there on official business. He climbed to the top of the mountain with the patrol inspector and looked around to see that all sides were vast oceans. The water on the northern side of the mountain was particularly steep and swift. Looking down from a high place, the water swirled into eddies, and there were dozens of places where the center was sunken and not full. This is what is called the tailgate that drains water.

A Collection of Frosty Elegance by Dong Ying Dong Ying, a scholar from Deqing County in Raozhou, was known by the courtesy name Zhongda. Throughout his life, he developed an obsession with writing poetry, to the point where he would neglect sleep and meals whenever he was in the throes of creative thought. Once his poems were completed, he would always show them to everyone. Among his works, there was a striking phrase: "Cloudy ravines brew rain across a thousand peaks; wind blows the reeds old on a single autumn shore." This line earned him high praise from Han Zicang, who suggested that Xu Shichuan change the character "汀" to "川." Wang Yanzhang remarked that this single character change had significant implications. He named the collection "Frosty Elegance" and wrote a preface to introduce it. Despite his deep passion for poetry, Dong Ying was extremely poor. One day, while composing a birthday poem for Chancellor Qin, his thoughts became overly intense, and he fell into a state of madness. He attempted to throw himself into the river, but someone sent a messenger to call his son, who arranged for a boat to bring him back home. After several days, Dong Ying passed away. His family was impoverished, and his son was young, so they could not afford a proper burial or Buddhist rituals. After more than ten days, a relative named Dong Yingmeng dreamed of him. Dong Ying said, "After my death, due to our family's poverty, I did not receive the power of the Buddha and have not yet escaped the suffering of hell. My elder brother, if you would show clan favor and perform seven days of Buddhist rites to aid my journey in the afterlife, and publish 'Frosty Elegance' for posterity, then I will rest in peace in the underworld and repay your kindness." Dong Yingmeng followed his request, first offering food to the monks and conducting the rites. He then dreamed again, and Dong Ying thanked him, saying, "I am grateful for your assistance; I have been freed from suffering. The collection 'Frosty Elegance' will be my final gift." He offered a poem as a token of gratitude, and one line of it was remembered by the villagers: "At dusk, people pass through the gates of hell." The rest of the poem, however, was not widely known. Dong Yingmeng, who ran a bookshop, eventually published the collection.

In Hangzhou, on Wansong Mountain, there were many residences of high-ranking officials. Chen, an inner attendant, lived in the highest of these. In the 15th year of the Shaoxing era, during the height of summer, he sought to cool off. By the fourth watch, he had not yet slept, and the streets were deserted. Suddenly, he saw a prison guard in a yellow robe leading three people from north to south. One was dressed in gold and purple, another in a purple robe, and the third in a cool robe. They arrived at the gate of Liu's residence, ascended the steps, and were about to enter. The person in gold and purple hesitated. The prison guard said, "They have already taken responsibility. Why not go in?" It was late, so he urged them to proceed quickly. They bowed their heads and entered. (The remaining two pages are missing.)

Sun entered the main bedroom. The door and lock were very secure. Sun called the postal official to open the door. The official replied, "This room is inhabited by a strange ghost. It has been like this for several decades now. No one dares to enter." Sun was young and an official of a high-ranking government office, with a hundred attendants. Relying on his bravery and temperament, he finally opened the door and entered. At night, he lit a candle in front of him and took a sword, placing it on the table. After the second watch, he sat alone and his heart was moved, unable to lie down to sleep. Suddenly, he heard a sound from the beam. He looked up and saw a green ghost about two feet tall, sitting on the beam and clapping its hands while laughing. Sun quietly called the attendants outside, but all were deeply asleep and did not respond. After a while, the ghost slowly descended and stood beside Sun, dancing around and around. Soon it took the sword and held it, keeping dancing. Sun became even more afraid and just sat there, listening to fate. Soon a woman with a hat appeared behind the screen, her clothes very neat. She smiled and said, "Little ghost, don't bother the official," and then returned. After she finished speaking, they all disappeared. The window paper was already bright, indicating it had been restless all night. Zhao started his career in Yu and became the Deputy Minister of Personnel. He was sent to govern Di Prefecture. Due to a severe drought, he used the method of the barbarians to pray for rain. He was held sitting in the scorching sun, with several buckets of water being pulled and alternately poured on his body. Eventually, he died of illness.

A Dream of Zhu Xizhen Missing internal text Ghost at Zouping Post Station Missing internal text Great Wind in Jinxiang In the Jinxiang County of Jizhou, the city walls were very strong. The county fell into the hands of the northern barbarians. In the year Renxu of the Shaoxing era, someone knocked on the city gate at midnight wanting to enter. The gatekeeper refused. The person became angry and cursed. After a long time, he said, "Even if you don't open the gate, I have my own plan." Suddenly, a great wind shook the sky. The city gate broke apart, and the wind blew the gatekeeper out of the city. All the houses in the county flew out. From the county magistrate down, their bodies moved like they were riding the wind. They could no longer control themselves and fell to the ground when they reached outside the city. That year, the state was submerged by the river. The entire city became fish, but Jinxiang remained intact and became the state capital. These two events are said by Zhao Bujun.

### The Ghost of the Yan Mansion The Prince of Yan resigned from his position as chancellor and built a mansion on the east side of Qinghu Lake in Lin'an. One evening, his daughter arrived late at the back garden. She saw a woman wearing a round hat and a brown robe, standing with her back turned. Thinking she was a sister, she called out to her. The woman turned around and stabbed her chest, causing her to fall to the ground immediately. Despite the attack, the girl was still able to speak about what she had seen, but she soon nearly suffocated and was on the verge of death. The prince summoned the Taoist expert Song Anguo to examine her. Song Anguo lifted the curtain and carefully observed, saying, "Although there is a ghostly presence, it is not harmful." He described seeing a young woman around eighteen or nineteen years old, dressed similarly, and an elderly woman over fifty years old, both standing around her. He then said, "Now I will send them away." He ordered to fetch a large bamboo pole and hung paper money on it. He had a little boy hold it and instructed the sick girl to blow air into it. Song Anguo put his mouth to the bamboo and blew into its tip, doing this twice. The bamboo bent as a result. Song Anguo remarked, "The evil energy is so strong, how could it not harm people?" He then fetched water and sprayed it on the bamboo. The boy's strength could not withstand it, and both the boy and the bamboo fell. The girl then woke up. Previously, a woman from a certain family had committed lewd acts. Her parents, along with her wet nurse, threw her alive into a well, covered her with a large green stone, and carved her sins on the underside of the stone. What is seen now is these two ghosts. The ghost spoke to Song Anguo in this manner. Song Anguo, courtesy name Tongfu, treats ghosts without using talismans or summoning rituals. His methods are simple yet ingenious, unmatched by others. The Yan Mansion is now the Left Treasure Warehouse.

Ghost Entering the Grain Mill** **Wáng Shèng, the Military Commander, Walked Alone in the Rear Garden.** Wáng Shèng, the military commander, was walking alone in the rear garden. He saw someone with a bull's head wearing red clothes standing opposite him. Wáng Shèng shouted and asked, "Who are you?" The bull-headed person also asked, "Who are you?" Wáng Shèng picked up a brick and threw it at him. The bull-headed person also threw a brick back. Frightened, Wáng Shèng left without further confrontation. His wife had been married three times: first to a minor military officer, then to Chén Sīgōng, and finally to Wáng Shèng. She once saw her two former husbands sitting in the hall as if they were still alive. She told Wáng Shèng about this, and he said, "If they come again, tell me immediately." The next day, the two men appeared again. Wáng Shèng went out, and they sat there as usual. He quickly chased them away, and they ran to the western wing of the building and entered the grain mill, disappearing inside. Wáng Shèng tried to hit the mill with his hand, but all five of his fingers were injured. That year, Wáng Shèng died. These two stories were told by Hán Ziwén. **Narrator: Zhang Fugan

A person from Yanping named Zhang Fugan possessed the skill to command ghosts and spirits. Zhong Shixian Shiming was suffering from malaria and sent a note requesting medicine. Zhang did not give medicine and did not reply to the note, but he wrote his signature on the note and instructed, saying, "Lick it with your tongue and it will heal." Indeed, he was healed. Zhong's father-in-law, Lin, was a wealthy man who used a thousand coins to buy a beautiful concubine. Lin went to Fuzhou, but the concubine was seriously ill and not eating. Zhong invited Zhang to treat her. Zhang said, "The situation is urgent. I estimated that it could prolong her life for three days. Lin will return as scheduled, then he can see her." Zhang then blew air into the concubine's mouth. After a short while, her eyes opened and her body moved. She asked for porridge to drink and could speak somewhat. After two nights, Lin returned, and the concubine also died. Zhang also went to Fuzhou with a scholar named Deng. Deng was weak and unable to manage affairs. Zhang said, "I will lend you some strength, how about that?" Deng replied, "You don't have a servant yourself, why are you teasing me?" They passed by a shrine previously, and Zhang pointed to a yellow-robed attendant and said, "Lend this person to you." Deng thought it was just a joke and they went their separate ways. They reached the crossing at the front of the creek, and the boatman tied up the boat and waited. He said, "You are not Scholar Deng, are you?" Just then, someone on urgent business passed by and ordered to prepare a boat to carry him. They were already surprised by this. In the evening, they arrived at a village market. They saw a notice at the inn saying "occupied by Scholar Deng." They asked about it, and it was indeed him. For the next three days, it was the same. They reached Futang, and Zhang dreamed that a yellow-robed person came and said, "Accompanied you for several days, it was very tiring. Did you have no money to thank me?" Zhang replied, "Because I was eager to get there quickly, I injured two fingers. I should quickly treat it." Zhang awakened and found it strange. He immediately burned several thousand sheets of paper and offered them. On the way back, he passed by the shrine and saw the yellow-robed person's feet, and two fingers had been amputated. Zhang mixed mud to treat it.

Zhao Lingzu lived in an alley near the Temple of Mount Tai in the capital. One day, his servant reported that there was a skull near the well outside the book window. Zhao Lingzu said, "It must have been dropped by a bird of prey. Get rid of it." The servant tried to remove it with a broom, but the skull did not move at all. As he approached, the skull suddenly jumped into the well, making a plop sound. Zhao Lingzu said, "You were afraid and could not control yourself, and you accidentally pressed it into the water. Temporarily block the well with a stone and do not draw water from it." The next day, when Zhao Lingzu went to check, the skull was again on the stone. When he got closer, the skull moved around and lifted the stone to enter the well. The servant was very afraid. Zhao Lingzu still did not believe it and said, "I will observe it carefully tomorrow. I will see what happens." He observed through the window crack and saw the same thing as the servant. He also became afraid. On the Lantern Festival, when lanterns were hung, Zhao Lingzu climbed a ladder to roll up the curtain but had not finished when he suddenly cried and descended. When asked, he did not answer. He then developed a mental illness and became weak and foolish. His wife suggested moving to a new place to avoid misfortune. They then found a new house in the western part of the city.

He sent his son Ziché ahead. The wife and the influential family shared one sedan chair. After Ziché finished sweeping and cleaning, he returned to welcome them. They met at the eastern corner of the building. Lifting the curtain, he asked if they were well; the influential family's expression suddenly became clear. But they would often look eastward, until they could see no further. Upon arriving at the new residence, he suddenly became enlightened. He could recount what happened during their illness. He said, "Remembering when I first climbed the ladder, I saw a woman with disheveled hair covering her face, coming out from the hall crying, her voice filled with sorrow. I could not bear the sorrow and also shed tears. From then on, she never left us, but I never saw her face. Today, we stepped up into the sedan chair together, sitting side by side, with disheveled hair as before. Looking towards the eastern gate, she hurriedly descended and headed east. I immediately felt my spirit gradually returning to normal. Watching her emerge into the main street, amidst the crowd, I could not distinguish her, and then stopped. With today's enlightenment, thinking back on yesterday's confusion, fortunately, we did not fall into the ghost's trap. The influential family was spared. Subsequently, another imperial relative, the Five Inspector, came to live there. He died within half a year. This was during the Xuanhe period.

The He Village Case Qin Di served as the governor of Xuancheng Prefecture. In the village of He within the prefecture, a wealthy family was brewing alcohol. The patrol inspector was sent to capture them. Leading several dozen soldiers, he surrounded their home at midnight. The family, being a wealthy clan, saw soldiers with weapons at night and assumed they were bandits. They immediately beat the drum to gather their neighbors, and combined their servants and slaves to fight back with weapons. The patrol inspector had no other concerns and was not prepared for such resistance. Both he and his men were captured. The villagers reported their capture of the bandits to the county. The county magistrate, having heard of the matter, transferred it to the county magistrate. The magistrate, thinking it would be difficult to argue against the powerful family, rode out lightly and gently said to them, "I heard that your family captured bandits. Let's share this together." The villagers, not suspecting anything, were greatly pleased and handed over everything they had captured to the magistrate. Along with his son and grandson, a total of three people, they escorted the captives to the capital. Upon reaching the capital, Qin Di released the patrol inspector and his men but arrested the three villagers. He tied them up with hemp ropes from head to toe, from shoulder to foot, and then beat each of them a hundred times. When the ropes were untied, the three men died. Qin Di's brother, who was in a high position, dared not speak up, and the vice-magistrate Li Ji, fearing the powerful family, immediately requested to retire. The next year, Qin Di died in the capital. The year after that, Yang Yuanzhong took over as the governor. During the day, he saw several people dragging a prisoner, chained and clinking, to the bottom of the stairs. One person stepped forward and said, "Use the He Village case as a precedent." Yang, having just taken office, did not know the origin of the matter. He was just examining it when it already disappeared. He called the officials and told them the reason. The officials said, "This must be the case of the wealthy family's brewing dispute during the time of Qin Di, the vice-magistrate." They brought the completed case file. Yang, upon reviewing it, was greatly shocked. He quickly ordered the scribes to write it down in neat calligraphy and bought ten thousand pieces of paper money to burn together. Zhao Bujing heard it from Li Cizhong.

A Concubine of the Yao Family In Kuaiji, Yao Hong bought a concubine. She was skilled in embroidery and cooking, and had good looks. She was also clever, witty, and cautious, and able to please people. From the mistress down, all loved her. She lived there for several months. One night, the entire Yao family felt a cold air fill the room, sharp and pressing. Soon after, they heard a ghost whistle once and then come out through the window. The family was terrified. After a while, they calmed down and lit candles to check on each other. Only this concubine was missing. When they looked at her bed, her clothes and furs were all there. There was a small hole in the paper window as big as a coin, and no one knew what kind of monster it was. This story is told by Guo Tang the Elder. From the *Yijian Zhi* (Strange Tales from a Chinese Studio), Volume Seventeen, Section Fifteen.

### Zhai Ji Obtains a Son A man from the capital named Zhai Ji lived in Siquan County, Huzhou. At the age of fifty, he had no son. He painted an image of Guanyin and prayed earnestly. His wife was pregnant. She dreamed of a white-clad woman holding a child in a tray. The child was very beautiful and graceful. His wife was greatly pleased and wanted to take the child. A cow lay across the tray, preventing her from doing so. Eventually, she gave birth to a son, but the pregnancy lasted a month longer than usual. He prayed again as before. Someone who heard about her dream told Zhai Ji, "You have a strong fondness for beef. Could this be the reason?" Zhai Ji was startled and immediately swore that his entire family would never eat beef again. He then dreamed again of the previous woman bringing the child to him. He took the child and his wife gave birth to a healthy son. They both said it was a wonderful outcome. ### Zhang Ba Shu Bian Zhi Bai Gongshu lived in Pingjiang. His grandmother, Madam Wang, was ill. Several doctors had been consulted without success. One day, a guest knocked on the door. The guest wore a blue cap and a black robe, was fair-skinned, and had a beard. He said, "I am Zhang Ba Shu, the weaver of silk from Fan Gong Bridge in Runzhou. Scholar Yuan Ershiwu in the front alley asked me to come and take your pulse." Gongshu went out to see him. The guest said, "There is no need to take the pulse; I have already understood the condition of your wife. I will leave a prescription called Wu Jin San. Have her drink it immediately." In the Bian family, there was a small yellow dog that had just been born a few days ago. The dog had a black ribbon pattern on its back.
