The guest said, "Please give it to me; I will come back in three days to collect it." Gongshu laughed and did not answer. Three days later, the dog suddenly died. Madam Wang's illness also healed. Gongshu then visited Scholar Yuan to thank him. Yuan was greatly surprised. There was a painting beside him. Looking at it, it was an image of Lü Dongbin. It was exactly the same as the one he had seen before. The painting was actually obtained from Zhang Ba Shu's house. Bian's nephew Wei Yue said.

**Wang Xin's Reincarnation** Wang Xin, courtesy name Henzhi, was from Jiangyin. In the year 1168 (Wu Chen of Shaoxing), he passed the imperial examination. He was waiting for a vacant professorship in Yangzhou but did not take up the post. He died at home in March of the year 1175 (Yi Hai). In the tenth month of the same year, his farmhand saw a man riding a horse, with two soldiers serving as drivers. Upon closer inspection, he recognized the man as Professor Wang. Startled, the farmhand asked where he was going. Wang replied, "I want to go to the house of Scholar Yin Qian'er in Penghao Village." The farmhand said, "It is more than ten miles to Penghao Village, and it is already late in the day. I fear we will not reach there." Wang said, "Distance is not a concern. It is enough for you to guide me." They then set off together. By the first watch of the night, they arrived at the gate of Yin's house. Wang dismounted and left a wrapped gift for the farmhand, saying, "Thank you for coming with me." He then swiftly entered through a crack in the door. The farmhand was greatly alarmed and hurriedly returned. When he opened the wrapped gift, he found fifty copper coins inside. He did not dare to tell anyone about the incident. The next day, he went back to ask and learned that Yin's wife had given birth to a son that night. According to the account of Yan Kangzhao.

The Giant Ghost of Hézhǎo In the village beneath Hézhǎo Mountain in the Línjiāng military district, there lived a wealthy family named Zhang. During the fourteenth year of the Shòuòng era (1144 CE), one morning, a servant opened the door and saw a figure over ten feet tall, completely black, who directly entered the main hall. When questioned, the figure did not respond. When dragged, it would not move. The servant quickly reported this to the master, and when the other servants arrived, they struck the figure with sticks, which made a metallic sound. They tried to pierce it with a spear, but the blade curled like a hook and could not penetrate. They tried to wash it with hot water, but it remained completely dry. The figure moved freely and showed no signs of anger. In the region, there were often bandits and thieves, so many households kept large drums to gather people in times of emergency. But when they tried to strike the drum, it would not sound. The Zhang family realized that they could not compete with the ghost's power and so knelt down to plead for mercy. The ghost ignored them. It slowly rose up and walked through the main hall, the wells, the stoves, and the latrines, examining everything. The Zhang family's treasury was locked with large locks, but the ghost easily pulled them open. After thoroughly exploring the house, the ghost returned to sit. As night fell and the family was about to light candles, the fire would not ignite. The entire family was terrified. They climbed up to the Wángsì Pavilion on the mountain and held a yellow register ritual for the nine depths, ordering a Taoist priest to present a memorial to heaven. After seven days, the ghost finally disappeared. From then on, the Zhang family declined and is now reduced to poverty. This story was told by Wáng Jièrán from Shítíao.

**The Magistrate of Xuanzhou, Meng** In the first year of the Gandao era, in July, Wang Shiwu, a servant of the Wang family from Shitian Village in Wuyuan, was working in the fields when he suddenly collapsed. His family arrived, found him dead, and carried him home. He still had faint breathing, so they dared not close his body. After eight days, he revived and recounted what had happened. He said that while he was in the fields, he saw over ten people coming from the west. They were all dressed in Daoist robes and carried boxes, chests, and large fans. As he looked at them, they seized him and threw him to the ground, beating and striking him, then forcing him to carry a burden and walk. They brought him to the County Temple of the Five Lords, where a man emerged wearing a hat and sash, dressed like a modern *tongyin* official. He conveyed the Lord’s orders, asking what they needed. They replied that they intended to carry out a plague in Wuyuan. The man in formal attire went in and came out again, saying that the Lord would not allow it, and ordered them to leave quickly. The men hesitated, but soon a voice was heard from within the temple calling out: “If you do not leave immediately, there will be other arrangements.” They then abandoned the temple and entered the mountain temple, where they were again driven away. They then went from Zhe Ling to Xiuning County, visiting the City God Temple and the Temple of King Yingji. They made the same request as in Wuyuan, but were refused again. They then traveled to Huizhou, visiting three temples there, but were also refused. The ten men became disheartened and unhappy. They gradually made their way to Xuanzhou and entered a large shrine. As soon as they reached the gate, several people had already come out to greet them, as if they had known they were coming. They met with great joy. They entered and reported to the deity, who gave his permission. The deity then ordered swift messengers to inform all the local land deities, and also sent one ghost as a guide. The journey was to begin at the home of Magistrate Meng in the northern gate.

After arriving, he hid the items he carried under the stove, transported large timber, and set up a camp fence outside, much like a modern military encampment. Until dawn, each person held their item and patrolled the hall. The two sons came out first, striking their brains, and they fell to the ground. Next, he encountered the servants and maids, striking or fanning them, and none survived. They stayed for two days. One of his followers entered and reported that fire was seen to the southwest. Fearing that rescue forces were coming, they quickly climbed the city walls and looked to see where the fire was coming from, shooting arrows at it. The fire was extinguished. Another two days later, they reported again that the fire outside the camp reached the sky. When they reached the city walls, the fire was already raging. The camp fence was burned down completely, and they could not take action, so they dispersed. Only I remained, realizing that I was dead, and I followed the old path back, and thus survived. The local man Wang Gengxin, who was appointed as the military secretary of Guangde, witnessed the events. His brother-in-law Yu Yongguan, who was just appointed as the magistrate of Xuancheng, sent a letter to inquire about the matter. It was said that Meng was a doctor. In July, a great plague struck the entire household, starting with the two sons, and two maidservants died. They called a village sorcerer to treat it, but as he was performing the ritual, the sorcerer himself fell ill and died. The Meng family gathered all the sorcerers in the city and worked together to perform rituals, and only then did they recover. This is what was meant by the fire burning the camp fence. That year, the people of western Zhejiang suffered from countless plagues, but the east of Jiang was unaffected. The gods of She can be called benevolent. The stone basin man Wang Gong said, "Wang San was his family servant."

Tamed Turtle Dove In Yanguan County, there was Qingshan Temple. Master Liao Xuan, known as Mingyi, retired to live in a temple owned by a resident of the county named Zou. In the first year of the Longxing era, during the spring, in the morning, while walking along the path, he saw a young turtle dove fall to the ground. He picked it up and brought it back, personally feeding and caring for it. After two months, it was able to fly, and during the day, it was allowed to go wherever it pleased, while at night, it would roost in the screens and tables. In the tenth month of that year, his disciple Huimü once again took charge of Qingshan Temple and welcomed and brought the teacher to the western side of the main hall. When evening came and the turtle dove returned, the place was empty and no one was there. So it circled the room many times, continuing to mourn and cry. The caretaker felt compassion for it and said, "I will take you back to your teacher's place." The next day, he put it in a cage and gave it to Liao Xuan. From then on, it did not leave. It became tame and close to those around it, and when touched or caressed, it did not move, but when others approached, it would always startle and fly away. Alas, who would say that animals lack intelligence? Told by Dou Siyong.

A Female Ghost Deceives Chou Du The Purple Lady spirits often pretend. Sometimes they can harm people. I have often heard and seen this. Now I will record a recent incident to warn future generations. Chou Du, a scholar from Taizhou, was originally a descendant of a prominent family. He wandered the Jianghuai region and, at the age of thirty, remained unmarried. In the autumn of the first year of the Gandaodao era, he frequently invited the Purple Lady to compose poems and verses. He recited and played with the verses endlessly, and was thus deceived. Day and night, the ghost纠缠 without letting go, must see her true form to be husband and wife, and will also entrust herself to dreams. Chou Du was already confused but still feared death, still resisted with all his might. The ghost followed him more closely, to the point of holding his hand to write, without needing to move the divination tool. Those who were with him knew it, feared he could not avoid it, therefore went out to Taizhou City, directly entered the City God Temple, burned incense to plead on his behalf. When he first entered the temple, Chou Du's teeth clicked together, already showing signs of fear and trembling. Upon returning home, he immediately asked for paper to write as a woman, detailed the beginning and end, the words were extremely vulgar and redundant.

Now, I will select the main points and present them as follows: The disciple Jì Sānliù (纪三六) named Shuǎng (爽), who resides in front of the Four Holy View Temple within the capital city of the Great Song State, has a wife named Zǒng (张), also known as Sānliùniáng (三六娘). She was born on the twelfth day of the third month in the year Xīn Yǒu (辛酉) at the hour of Sì (巳) and died on the fourteenth day of the third month in the year Guǐ Sì (癸巳). In the ninth month of that year, she met Mr. Lǚ (吕先生) at the mouth of the Jī (箕), where she learned the techniques of cultivation and nourishment. Afterward, she traveled throughout the world. On the third day of August this year, she passed through Gāoyóu Army (高邮军) and met the scholar-official Chóu Duó (仇铎) in the inner courtyard of the Yanhong Temple's pagoda complex. She claimed that she was a true immortal from the great island of Penglai, saying that she had come to support and care for her because of her youth. She then wrote down words with a pen, falsely claiming that her sisters were in the mountains of Penglai. She advised him to be sincere and not to have wild or unrealistic thoughts, promising to continue to descend to him. After ten days, their interactions became more familiar. It was not appropriate for her to write lewd words to tempt Duó, and she also said that there would be a ministerial destiny in the future, confusing and disturbing his mind with this. On the seventeenth day, she arrived in Taizhou and wanted to meet with him, but he refused.

The narrator was about to enter a dream, but was prevented from doing so. He then told Doku, "Your father is angry with you for being unfilial. He has burned written petitions to the heavens, and the heavens have issued a decree that you will be struck by lightning within three days. You should prepare many offerings of tea, fruit, incense, and candles, and bow deeply to beg for your life. I will pray to the heavens to avert this disaster for you. Additionally, I demand ten thousand volumes of the *Duhu Jing* (Scripture of Deliverance). After three years, I will marry you. My intention is to instill fear in you and make you comply with me. I also falsely claimed that Lü Dongbin was at the door, and instructed you to meet him before dawn at the stone tomb outside the eastern gate. Doku wanted to go, but was stopped by others. The narrator then wrote the characters 'cloud room' and instructed Doku to eat half a liang of musk, hoping that he would become desperately thirsty and die by drinking water. Even when Doku struck his head against a pillar or broke a clay pot and scattered its contents, he did not die. The narrator then claimed that the heavenly deities had descended and would burn Doku's left arm. He instructed Doku to hide in a mat and lie under the bed, and spoke as if Lü Dongbin was intervening to save him, saying, 'The heavenly deities have fortunately spared this person because of Lü Dongbin. If this person were to die, Lü Dongbin would no longer be a god.' This continued for two hours.

I cannot kill the bell-ringer. By evening, I finally spoke to him. I am not a fairy from Peng Island; I am a white dog spirit. Today, I will die in your place, and I will become a low-ranking ghost forever. We should have a cup of wine to say our farewells. The next day, I returned and said, "I am the white snake spirit from A Mu Mountain in Xinghua." I have killed over 3,700 people in the past. The people called a priest to deal with the situation. I spoke to the bell-ringer again and said, "I am only afraid of the Zhang Tianshi of Longhu Mountain. I am not afraid of anyone else." Because Sanliu Niang originally intended to be obsessed with killing the bell-ringer, she became lost and did not return. She must entangle the person and harm his life. Now, I am reporting to the city god of this prefecture on behalf of the bell-ringer and requesting punishment from heaven. I am humbly submitted to the written account of my responsibility. What I have stated is true. If there are any discrepancies later, I willingly accept severe punishment. What I have written totals 1,500 characters. It was immediately recorded and burned. The bell-ringer woke up three days later. He was trapped for nearly a month. The woman claimed she died in the year Gui Si. By this time, it had been 53 years. The state of being a ghost has lasted for a long time indeed.

Zhang Chengxian, known by his courtesy name Weiyong, served as the supervisor of the grain supply office in Chen Prefecture. At the time, the magistrate of Wanchu was on leave, and Zhang temporarily took charge of the affairs. He captured two types of bandits, totaling fifteen individuals, and sent them to the county for trial. The case had not yet been submitted to the authorities when the magistrate returned and reported the matter to the prefecture governor. The magistrate requested that the two cases be combined into one, hoping that the total number of criminals would meet the quota for a higher rank, which would allow him to be promoted to a prestigious position in the capital. The prefecture governor, who had always been friendly with the magistrate, agreed and informed Zhang of this plan. Zhang replied, "If the magistrate desires a reward, it is not impossible. However, if you allow the official documents to be altered, combining the two cases into one and handing them over to the officials to manipulate and adjust, then I would not dare to do so." The prefecture governor could not persuade Zhang otherwise, and the magistrate became very angry, almost becoming enemies. Twelve years later, Zhang served as an official in the Jianghuai Transport Office. He set up an incense offering at Mount Mao and stayed overnight at the Jade Celestial Palace. In his dream, his uncle told him, "The matter in Chen Prefecture can be guaranteed to be without worry, but you cannot be promoted to the rank of Zhenglang." Later, he went to the hall, where the king asked, "Can you still remember the matter in Chen Prefecture?" Zhang replied, "I remember them all clearly, but there are no official documents to prove it." The king said, "The records in this place are very clear. There is no need to promise." After coming out, Zhang saw two direct officials, each carrying a brocade cushion and giving it to him. They said, "This is our way of repaying you." Zhang had never had a child before, but in that year, he had one son and one daughter. Seven years later, he was promoted to the rank of Daifu and was appointed to the position of Zhi Mige, where he eventually passed away. This story was told by Bian Weiyue.

**Ghost Transformed into Firelight** When the Marquis of Han lived in his old mansion, he had twenty young female entertainers. His son, Ziwēn, who was twelve years old, and his concubine, Ning'ér, were playing in the eastern wing of the building late at night. They saw a person walking ahead of them. The person's appearance and demeanor suggested that he was also a young female entertainer. When they called out to him, he did not respond. Ziwēn then ran after him. Ziwēn ran very quickly, but the person walked gracefully and slowly, showing no urgency. However, Ziwēn could not catch up. As they approached the outer door, Ziwēn called out loudly. Suddenly, the person appeared in the courtyard and transformed into a ribbon-like form, bursting into flames and disappearing into a ditch. Ning'ér confirmed that she had seen the same thing. They returned to tell the Marquis of Han. All believed there should be a hidden corpse, or perhaps treasure. They wanted to dig up the ground to verify it, but then because the labor was too great, they stopped.

Canglang Pavilion In the city of Suzhou stands the Canglang Pavilion, originally the residence of Su Zimei. It is now owned by Han Xian'an. During the time when the Jin invaded, people sought refuge in the back garden, but all died in the pond. Because of this, the place was often unsettled. Later, Han Xian'an moved into the pavilion. Every moonlit night, he would see hundreds of people appearing and disappearing by the pond—some were monks, some Taoists, some women, and some merchants. They sang and shouted in a chaotic manner, and after a long while, they would lament and then disappear. One night, an old guard, who was just about to sleep, was carried off by dozens of people to the pond. The guard, a native of Shaanxi, was known for his bravery. Knowing they were ghosts, he showed no fear and sternly told them, "You all died here a long time ago. I will speak on your behalf to the master. I will collect all the bones, rebury them on high ground, and perform Buddhist rituals to save you. Why not leave this stagnant cave and stop harming ordinary people?" All of them expressed shame and gratitude, saying, "Thankfully, we will let go and retreat." The next day, the guard reported this to the master. The master immediately ordered ox carts to move the pond water, dug through the mud, and collected the decayed bones, placing them in large bamboo baskets. Altogether, they filled eight baskets, which were all placed in a large coffin and buried. That evening, another man led the old guard into a bamboo grove and said, "Everyone else has gone, but I still have two arms here. Please continue to help me." They retrieved him as well and buried him east of the city. Then, they set up a water and land offering at Lingyan Temple. From then on, the strange occurrences in the house ceased. These two events were recounted by Zi Wen.

During the Chongning period, there was a mad monk in Pingjiang (present-day Suzhou) who loved alcohol and was good-for-nothing, but he enjoyed composing poems and gathas, which he could create spontaneously. The people of the prefecture called him Lin the Wine Immortal. They often belittled and mocked him, but the Guo family treated him with great respect. When the mother of the Guo family fell ill, the monk gave her a cup of medicine, saying, "Drink until you are satisfied, do not force it." Afterward, she drank two-thirds of it. The monk took the remainder and threw it on the ground, and it turned into a golden color. The mother's illness was cured, and the monk also left behind red pills and powders for the Guo family. The Guo family sold them according to the formula and thus became wealthy. Some people in Suzhou who could transmit his poems included the following: At the gate, green willows without singing birds; in the courtyard, moss-covered stones with fallen flowers. Let us discuss matters with the East King; whose family does the full beauty of spring belong to? In autumn, the mountains are cold and the waters are cold; in spring, the willows are green and the flowers are red. A single point of Dongting Lake undergoes myriad changes; the village is shrouded in misty rain. With a golden wine cup, he enjoys leisurely drinking; the jade fairy still desires to indulge. Do not let the night pass quickly; enjoy the moonlight and return. All his poems are similar to this.

**Zhengshan Arhats** The ancestral tomb of the Bian family is located on Zhengshan in Pingjiang. In the first year of the Xuanhe era (1119 CE), Bian Gongshi, who held the position of Taixuelu (a high-ranking official in the imperial academy), obtained sixteen stone and wood statues of Arhats from Wudongqing. He sent his household servant to bring them to the front of the tomb temple. One night, the monk Duliupin had a dream in which ten or so monks came with a letter from Bian Gongshi, asking to stay temporarily. He reported this to the chief monk Huigong, who found it difficult to accommodate them. "The resources at this small temple are thin and ordinary. Even for three or four monks, we struggle to provide for them. How can we house such a large group?" One of the monks stood up and took a pen to write a poem on the left side of the door, saying, "There are divine beings deep in the pine and lichen. I do not remember the other words." The next morning, when they were discussing this dream, the stone statues arrived. Bian Gongshi completed the poem, saying, "There are divine beings deep in the pine and lichen. A small temple can still accommodate a thousand worlds. To stay temporarily here is surely a long-term arrangement. To sing and recite poetry is fortunate to be passed down. Taking a letter from his sleeve and casually responding, the poem on the door was not accidental. I have not yet overcome the habitual nature of my distress. I will establish a future connection with you."

Shen Nineteen A man from Kunshan named Shen Nineteen was skilled in assisting others with painting and calligraphy. His family also made a living by cooking crabs. A local man named Qian Wubai had recently painted an image of the Bodhisattva Ksitigarbha and asked Shen to decorate it. While he was working, the sound of cooking crabs continued without pause. That night, Shen dreamed of entering the underworld. The guards he saw were all demonic beings with ox heads. On either side, large cauldrons were set up, and people were being lifted and placed inside to be cooked. As they were about to reach Shen, suddenly a monk appeared with a staff, and Qian Wubai was also present. The monk instructed the guards, saying, "Just let this person enter the cauldron to cleanse his feet." Shen was still terrified, but the guard ordered him to undress and enter. Soon, he emerged from the boiling cauldrons and fierce flames. The other prisoners were crying out in agony, but Shen felt no pain. He felt cool and comfortable, as if he had just taken a bath. He felt very refreshed. He turned over and woke up. From then on, he renounced his previous profession and began selling candy to make a living. This story took place during the twelfth year of the Shaoxing era. It was recorded by Bian Wei'ya.

Eighteen-Year-Old Wife Ye Shenyi, a high-ranking official, was not yet successful in the imperial examinations. He befriended Ma Minyi, a scholar from Quzhou. Ma Minyi was always poor and simple, but later he married Xu, a woman from Xiasan, who brought wealth through financial means, making him quite wealthy and well-provided. They called her the Eighteen-Year-Old Wife. In the 32nd year of the Shaoxing era, Ye Shenyi retired from his position at the Western Palace and returned to his former residence in Shouchang County, called Shekan. It was winter at the time. Two village men carried a sedan chair and brought an old woman, claiming she was the wife of Mr. Ma. Ye Shenyi ordered his daughter to invite her into the main hall. When they looked at her appearance, she had been fat before but was now thin, completely different from the Eighteen-Year-Old Wife. They asked her why this was so. She replied, "Old age brings many troubles, and my body has become emaciated. There's nothing surprising about it." Everyone was suspicious. They questioned her servant, who said, "We only saw her coming out of a store. We were instructed to bring her here. We don't know where she came from." Ye Shenyi's guest, Xu, noticed that the old woman's face was dry and black, and suspected she was not human. Additionally, a small servant carried a box made of paper, which was old and tattered, indicating they were funeral objects. Xu shouted and entered, saying, "This is a ghost!" She ordered them to expel her. The old woman still looked angry and said, "Calling someone a ghost is very disrespectful." After she left, the sedan chair did not take the usual path but went into the mountains on the side, and they disappeared. Several days later, Ma Minyi arrived and said his wife had never left home. Xu's neighbor confirmed this.

The Resurrection of Qian Rui In the first month of the Gandaoyuan era (1165 AD), a major epidemic broke out in Xiu Prefecture. Qian Rui, a minor official, fell ill for over a month. Suddenly, he began to speak incoherently, as if he had seen something. He claimed that he had been summoned to an official building and, looking up, saw Yu Changji, the Justice of the Supreme Court, seated on the dais in his official robes. Qian Rui had previously served as an official at the Supreme Court and recognized him. He immediately knelt and bowed, standing respectfully. Yu Changji said, "I called you here to handle a case." The attendants led him into a side room to examine the case files, which belonged to the Judicial Inspectorate of Zhe West. There were about fifty to sixty people involved in the case. Qian Rui concluded the case and presented it with great joy. He then pleaded to be allowed to return home. Yu Changji did not permit it. With no other options, Qian Rui stood at the side of the corridor. He saw an old friend, Ning San, standing with his head bound, and greeted him. Ning San, who had previously served as an official at the Transport Department, explained that he had made a mistake in a previous case and had been arrested and brought here. The documents related to the case were in a green silk bag in a certain kitchen. He had tried to retrieve them multiple times at night, but his family thought bandits had come and prevented him from doing so. He asked Qian Rui to return home and tell his son to retrieve and burn the documents, then send them to him. Qian Rui agreed. After seeing Yu Changji finish his duties, he again pleaded to be allowed to return home. Finally, he was permitted to go. Someone escorted him back, and as soon as he stepped outside, he was ordered to board a large boat. The boat was on land, and Qian Rui found this unbearable. In his dream, he cried out, "Pour water on the ground." He was exerting all his effort to call out when the boat suddenly reached the shore. He woke up in a panic, finding himself covered in black stains as if he had been washed. At that time, Yu Changji, who was the governor of Xuyi Army, had just died. Qian Rui is still alive today. This story was told by Jing Pei's younger brother.

The *Yijian Zhi* Volume Eighteen, Thirteen Stories Zhang Dan, the Daoist A man named Xu Fengyuan from Quzhou lived in the mountains of Xia. In his youth, he enjoyed the company of reclusive figures. One day, Zhang Dan, a Daoist, passed by him and stayed in his guesthouse. Zhang wore simple and unadorned clothing, dressed only in a green cap and a robe, with nothing inside. Even in the coldest winter, he did not add more clothing. Every full moon night, he carried an iron flute and went into the mountains to play it until dawn. Xu Fengyuan studied the *I Ching* and once closed the door to perform the *Da Yi* divination but did not understand the method. Zhang called to him from another room and said, "A scholar, this is not something you understand. I will tell you tomorrow." The next day, Zhang taught him a method of divination and calculation. This method could determine the time of a person's birth and death, as well as the lifespan and fate of various objects and living things. Xu Fengyuan tested the method and found it to be accurate. Zhang loved drinking the most and often went to the market all day, returning only when drunk, with no money in his bag. People said he could burn silver to support himself. Xu Fengyuan wanted to test Zhang's capacity and summoned four good drinkers. They took turns drinking with him from morning to evening, and all got drunk. Zhang remained as usual. At night, he entered the room, and people outside saw him standing upside down against the wall, with his feet hanging on the wall, his hair loose and placed in a clay pot, wine dripping from his hair. Xu Fengyuan's grandfather, Dequan, was over seventy years old. Zhang said, "Old Man Eighteen, you will have a great calamity next May. Use my method to avert it, and you can extend your life by ten years." The Xu family did not believe him, thinking that the monk was good at scaring people with words and should not be listened to.

As soon as the words were spoken, Zhang already knew it, and immediately left. He entered the Lohan Temple in the city. It was May of that year. Dequan was ill. Feng Yuan originally intended to go and invite him, but he refused to go. As a result, he died. One of his disciples had a person with a head, and also secretly kept a paper-drawn cow. Every time he played with guests, he would take the drawing and hang it on the wall, cutting fresh grass and placing it beside it. After a long time, the grass was either eaten up or half eaten, with feces left on the ground, which could be swept away. Later, he gave the cow to the person with a head and instructed him to buy forty-nine catties of hemp fiber, twisting it into a large rope. He told him, "I am about to die. When I die, do not bury me in a coffin. Only wrap the rope around my body from shoulder to foot. Dig a pit in the empty ground behind the temple and bury me there. After seven days, check the grave every seventh day." Headman carefully followed his instructions. Seven days after his death, when they opened the grave, his face was as red as cinnabar. By the forty-ninth day, after seven checks, only the hemp rope remained, along with a pair of worn-out shoes. His body had vanished. Feng Yuan once gave him a poem that said, "I love to play the iron flute on moonlit nights. My warm clothing can withstand the cold of snowy and frosty days. I asked to see his age, and he laughed and pointed to the pine and bamboo, saying they were not as strong." Zhang wrote it on a large piece of silk, and his handwriting was very grand. He also wrote the mercury method on a large piece of silk and taught it to Feng Yuan. After Feng Yuan died, many villagers sought his calligraphy. His son Mengliang did not want to reveal it, and lifted it up and burned it. The method of track analysis. The descendants of the Xu family roughly knew the general idea but not in detail. Feng Yuan's grandson Qinning said.

**The White Gold of the Imperial Academy** Ren Ziliang was a student at the Imperial Academy. One night, as he passed by the study hall, he saw more than a hundred pieces of silver in a bamboo grove, shining brightly under the moonlight. Ren Ziliang silently prayed, saying, "Heaven knows I am poor and in need of this wealth. If it is meant to be a great blessing, I will not dare to take it. I wish to return it to the gods. I hope to receive it again in a more appropriate manner in the future." He then left the silver behind and went away. The next day, as he returned to the same place, he noticed that the white object was moving and stretching. Surprised, he looked closer and saw that it was a large white snake, about ten feet long. He quickly ran back to his room. The next day, the silver and the white snake were no longer visible. Ren Ziliang was unsure whether the silver was affected by strange things or if the white snake had caused mischief and wanted to harm him. Both possibilities were not clear to him. His son, Liangchen, told this story.

**Tianning Monks** In the Tianning Temple of Guangce County, Shaowu, there were several temporary lodgings for monks. Seven or eight monks lived there. All of them eventually fell ill with a mental disorder, exhausted and died from overwork. Only one monk survived, but he also became seriously ill. He thought he would not survive, but he eventually recovered. He then told others, "I was often lured by a woman into a secret room. The room had a dim window and was deep and secluded. The bed and bedding were bright and beautiful. We formed a marital bond. All the clothes and shoes I wore were made by her. This went on for almost a year. One day, the local land god appeared and called the woman to account, saying, 'All the monks in the temple were killed by you. Why not leave one to take care of the temple's cleaning and sweeping tasks? From now on, you must not call him again.' The woman bowed and apologized, crying and saying goodbye. After this, the monk's condition improved. He remembered the room and the woman clearly. He discovered that the room was a private chamber in a house in the county. He told his parents, who opened the room to find the woman's body, which appeared fresh and lifelike. By her side, there was a pair of monk's shoes that were already finished. In her hands, she held one shoe, and she was still sewing. A black silk cap was still by the pillow. Her parents cried and changed the burial arrangements.

Zhao Butha served as an auxiliary tax official in Tingzhou. He left his family in Shaowu and went alone to stay at the Kaiyuan Temple in the city. He had a relationship with a government-sponsored courtesan and often brought her to the temple to stay. One night, during the fifth watch (approximately 3-5 AM), Zhao Butha was just beginning to sleep deeply when his father-in-law called out from outside, saying that it was the judge's birthday and they needed to get up quickly to pay their respects. Zhao Butha rushed out in a hurry. He was still deeply attached to the courtesan. The courtesan returned and said that she had instructed her father to give hundreds of coins as a bribe to the camp commander, so they did not need to go. Zhao Butha then returned to bed. The next morning, Zhao Butha planned to prepare food. Zhao Butha's secret friend, Feng Baiguan, came. The courtesan hid inside the house, saying that Feng Baiguan had visited her before and that she did not want to admit him because of Zhao Butha. She was still holding a grudge and did not want to come out. Feng Baiguan liked pomegranates and had already left two pomegranates in the kitchen. When Feng Baiguan entered, he drank with Zhao Butha and ate pomegranates, then left. The courtesan came out to eat. By evening, she asked for hot water to wash her feet. They slept together at night. Zhao Butha's nephew happened to arrive to ask about his well-being.

The courtesan instructed Zhao to stand facing outward. He had already hidden inside. His nephew hid under the bed. Without lifting the curtain, they also left. The two were intimate and laughing. Zhao suddenly fell asleep, dreaming of holding hands and walking out of the temple into the market. They reached the lower district. The courtesan pointed to a place and said, "This is my home." After passing the gate, she asked, "Could you stay for a moment?" Zhao thought to himself that he was a public official and it would be difficult to go to a brothel. He strongly resisted. Then he woke up in a panic, sweating profusely. He realized he was sleeping alone. He called his servant to ask where the courtesan was. The servant replied, "Someone returned home before dawn and has not come since then." He asked about the meal and foot-washing. The servant said, "You ordered a meal for two people, but there were no other guests. In the evening, you also ordered hot water for washing, but it was never used." Finally, he realized it was a ghost. From then on, he fell seriously ill, with his skin peeling off all over his body. He recovered after a year. He said he was fortunate not to have entered her home; if he had, he would have died. These two things were told by Uncle Guangji.

**Lü Shaoxia** In the twentieth year of the Shaoxing era, Xu Changyan was the governor of Jiangzhou. His nephew, Yan, observed guests consulting the Purple Girl Spirit. He asked, "May I inquire about the name of the divine spirit? Which dynasty does this spirit belong to?" The response was, "Tang Dynasty, Lü Shaoxia." Yan then said, "I hope for a promotion. Can the spirit foretell the future?" The spirit replied, "Heavenly secrets cannot be revealed. The poem says, 'Just write about the classics and history, or include a couplet of poetry. The meaning lies within. You should explore it yourself.' It then wrote down a poem by Wei Yaqiao from Suzhou: 'After writing, I wish to inscribe three hundred characters. Dongting Lake must wait for the full forest to be covered in frost.' The guests passed the poem around, but could not understand its meaning. Fifteen years later, Yan finally obtained a position in the capital and was appointed as the magistrate of Wuxian County. Only then did he realize the meaning of the poem. Dongting Lake is indeed part of Wu. Yan recounted this. **Gong Tao's Reincarnation** Gong Tao Zhongshan said that his mother was pregnant in Quzhou. When the time came for her to give birth, they sent for a wet nurse. It was already midnight. The doctor lived south of the city administration. On the way, he passed by the judicial office and saw people bustling in and out, saying that the official was gravely ill. By the time they arrived at the Gong residence, Tao was born. After the birth, the doctor returned. By then, the judicial official had already died. The next day, the doctor informed the Gong family. The judicial official was named Zhou. He was a clean and upright person who liked to carry a cane and wear a hat. Whenever he went out, he always called for a young servant to accompany him with these two items. When Tao was three years old and could speak, he often called for a hat and a cane. His family then realized that he was the reincarnation of Zhou.

### 超化寺鬼 In Quzhou, there is a temple called Chaohua Temple, located in the northern part of the city. Surrounding the temple are hundreds of acres of lotus and water chestnut ponds. The temple is situated in a secluded and quiet area, and many scholars and officials lived near it. Adjacent to the city wall, there is a pavilion called Yunshan Pavilion. Beneath the pavilion is a three-room sleeping hall where many strange and supernatural phenomena were reported, and no one dared to go there except for Ceng Tongpan, who lived there with his family. He often saw signs of the supernatural but dismissed them as the idle talk of his servants and maids. One day, his two-year-old son was crawling on the ground, and the wet nurse turned to speak to someone else. Suddenly, the child disappeared. The entire family searched the temple and prayed to the Buddha and monks, but they could not find him all night. The next morning, they heard a crying sound coming from a chest. When they opened the chest, they found the child, who had just woken up from a deep sleep. That very day, they moved out, and the room has remained empty ever since, according to the account of the senior monk. ### 嘉陵江边寺 Feng Zhongda, courtesy name Mingzhong, was from Xingzhou. His residence was about several dozen miles away from the prefectural city and faced the Jialing River. One evening, after drinking and becoming slightly intoxicated, he walked alone along the riverbank and took a short rest on a large, flat stone. Looking to the left, he saw rows of lush pine and cypress trees, and the moonlight cast shadows on the ground. He was delighted by the scene, recalling that he had never seen such a view before. Inspired, he walked for about two miles and came across a desolate temple. The Buddha hall stood tall and proud, with an eternal lamp flickering faintly. The temple was eerily quiet, and no one was in sight. As he walked further, he reached the main hall, where an old friend, a monk, greeted him with a bow. They sat down and chatted for a long time. Suddenly, Feng realized that the monk had died many years ago. He asked, "Master, you have been gone for many years. How are you here?" The monk replied, "Yes, I am here." They continued to talk and laugh as if nothing had changed. The monk inquired about Feng's friends and acquaintances, asking where they were now. It was almost midnight, and Feng felt tired and wanted to sleep. The monk led him to a small room on the western side of the temple and told him to lie down on the bed.

The monk warned him, saying, "This place is fraught with danger. Do not rashly venture out. Wait until it is almost dawn. I will come and call you to rise." Then he left. In the morning, he wandered aimlessly in the room, feeling that the surroundings were desolate. He could not sleep. Looking down at the window, he saw the shadows of bamboo trees unevenly cast, which made his heart grow even more restless. He got into bed and turned restlessly, his eyes not meeting his eyelids. He did not have time to wait for the monk to call, and so he rushed out of the door. From afar, he saw the Buddhist temple's lamps and candles burning brightly. He hurried toward it. The crowd was sitting in rows, and several servants were ladling out porridge. Inside the bowl, there was a fiery glow. He pressed closer to look, and it turned out to be molten copper. The hot, foul smell was unbearable. He returned in a panic, and met the monk from yesterday again. The monk said sternly, "Warn you not to go out. Are you not afraid?" He ordered a servant to hold a torch and escort him back. The torch went out in the middle of the road. In the morning, he stumbled and fell to the ground, awakening in a start. Then he realized that he was still on the stone, having not actually left. It was almost like a dream. Huang Zhongbing recounted this story.

Sage Zhao Xiaoge In the sixteenth year of the Shaoxing era, Li Duanyan, the magistrate of Quanzhou, recounted that he encountered a Daoist named Zhao Xiaoge in Xiu Prefecture. Zhao Xiaoge, whose courtesy name was Jindao, had once been registered in the military records, but his given name was unknown. He claimed to reside in Xianping County. Zhao Xiaoge was short and stunted in appearance, with dim and hazy eyes covered by a white film. He was known for treating illnesses with fruits and herbs, using remedies that were not found in orthodox medical texts. For instance, he would mix rouge powder with cold water to treat hemorrhoids, or use dogtail grass to treat urinary tract infections caused by sand and stones. His treatments were often effective. Zhao Xiaoge enjoyed drinking alcohol, and after drinking, he could vaguely discuss people's fortunes and misfortunes. The magistrate Zhu hosted him on a boat. One day, Zhao Xiaoge fell seriously ill with a heat-related ailment, became delirious, and jumped into the water. He accidentally fell into a fishing net and was rescued. After sweating profusely, he revived. Three years later, he arrived at the home of an official named Sun Minxiu in Lin'an. Sun was gravely ill, having not eaten for seven days and suffering from severe vomiting and diarrhea, nearly dying. Two messengers arrived with a letter from Zhao, the military supervisor of Hongzhou, to the home of a woman named Tao in the market area, reporting that Daoist Zhao had died in Hongzhou. These messengers had always been close to Zhao. Tao reassured them, saying that Zhao was still well and was actually at Sun's residence. They immediately went to visit Zhao together. Upon hearing this, Zhao quickly got up and left, appearing as if he had never been ill. The two messengers were greatly startled and bowed to him repeatedly. Zhao merely recited silently from the *Zhen Gao* (True Decree) and did not respond to their words. He then went to the home of Chang Zhiban on the market street.

Good people competed to burn incense as a sign of respect. Zhao bowed his hands and fixed his gaze. He raised and lowered his hands. He did not utter a single word. By night, outsiders left. His family sent a son to serve as an attendant. Until dawn, all the front and back doors were open. By then, he was no longer there. After a long time, he returned to the lake. Passing by Li's tomb temple, he met with Duan Yan. His body was covered in dust and dirt, as if he had traveled ten thousand miles. Asked where he had been, he would not say. But he said that he had been oppressed by people and avoided it. Now he dares not enter the city again. Six months later, he announced his departure. He said, "An epidemic has broken out here. I will make medicine to save people. I will leave for a year and then return." Duan Yan asked, "You are a Daoist. Do you also fear epidemics?" Zhao replied, "Natural disasters cannot be avoided." From then on, their interactions became more distant. In the thirtieth year of the Shaoxing era, he returned to Lin'an. He stayed at the home of a small cavalry officer named Wang. An official named Liu, suffering from rheumatism, sought medical treatment. Zhao taught him to mix mint juice with powdered aconite and take it. Liu took too much of the medicine and died. His son blamed Zhao and wanted to sue him in court. Zhao said, "It is not necessary." He instructed the son to take all the remaining medicine. The son also died. The Wang family bought a coffin and buried him outside the small dam gate. The laborers finished sealing the grave. Zhao returned to rest at a nearby soup shop. Seeing Zhao in front, the others called out and greeted him, saying, "It was very painful for you all to see me off." The others found this strange. They quickly returned to the burial site and opened the coffin. There was nothing inside.

### The Hunter of Xiuning In Xiuning, there was a villager named Zhang Wu, who made his living by hunting with bows and arrows. His household affairs were barely sufficient. Once, he chased after a deer, which was accompanied by its two young ones. The deer could not run fast, and thus it was caught. Realizing it could not escape, the deer led its young ones down to a place with loose soil. It then covered them with soil and threw itself into the net. Zhang Wu's mother, seeing this from afar, rushed to the net and told her son what had happened. She then broke the net to release the deer, and both the mother and her two young ones survived. Zhang Wu and his mother looked at each other, regretting their previous actions. They burned and discarded all their nets and snares and decided never to hunt again. Xiuning had many monkeys that enjoyed destroying people's crops. The villagers captured them using traps, setting up hundreds of cages. They would slightly open the boards, just enough space for one monkey to pass through. They would call out to the monkeys, saying, "Release one, and I will let you go." The monkeys would then hold a small one and push it out. The man would hit it with a mallet, and it would die in the cage. The other monkeys would look on and cry out, even shedding tears. The man would then request another, and this continued until all the monkeys were captured. The locals say that when the wheat and rice were ripe, the monkeys would gather in groups of a hundred or more. They would stand upright like humans, forming a fish-like formation, moving from east to west, jumping around several times. The crops would all fall over, and the monkeys would seize them. The rest would be trampled and left behind, leaving the fields empty. Thus, the villagers hated them and killed them. But it was also unkind. This was said by Zhu Xiyan.

**Two Dreams of Wei and Chen** Shi Chengxiang Zhiweng succeeded Wei Chengxiang Nanfu as the magistrate of Yuyao. Just before taking office, Wei dreamed that he and Shi arrived at a place together and were both called chancellors, but Wei was wearing a red robe. Upon waking, Wei told Shi about the dream, but Shi did not understand the significance of the red robe. Fifteen years later, Shi became the right chancellor, and Wei, as a minister of works, presented a memorial to the emperor. After the chancellor's report, Wei was called to the throne and was wearing a red robe, which reminded him of his dream. Wei thought that he would fulfill the role predicted in the dream. Three years after Shi left his position, Wei was appointed as the right chancellor, fulfilling the role he had seen in the dream. Chen Fuqing, as a minister of personnel, dreamed of handing over duties to Wang Deyan, who later became the head of the Imperial Council. Chen was pleased because he had recommended Wang and thought that he would soon enter the political arena. However, not long after, Chen was appointed as the governor of Jiankang and remembered Wang's death place. He strongly disliked it and, upon arriving, avoided all the rooms and private quarters, not daring to go there. He died within a month. The two dreams, one of prosperity and the other of decline, illustrate the contrasting fortunes of the individuals involved.

Poetry of Master Zhang Master Zhang Shanren came from Shandong to the capital city. He composed poetry with seventeen characters, becoming famous during the Yuan佑 and Shaoxing periods. Until today, people can still talk about him. His words are vulgar, but often sharp and insightful, containing sarcasm and criticism. Everywhere he went, people feared his tongue and competed to give him food, drink, money, and silk. As he grew older, he became somewhat tired and weary, and decided to return to his hometown. Before reaching his destination, he died on the road. People by the roadside were also his acquaintances. They pitied him for having no children and bought reed mats for him. They wrapped him and buried him in the fields, putting up a wooden signboard with his name on it. After a long time, a frivolous person came to the side of the inn and heard someone talking about this. Boldly, he said, "Master Zhang was always bold in his poetry. Now he is dead. It cannot go unrecorded." Immediately, he ordered a pen and wrote on the signboard, "This is Master Zhang's tomb. Passersby should feel sad. Two reed mats wrapped. Properly buried." People believed it was a retribution for his words, Wu Fupeng said.

**The Divine Lord of the Green Child** Dragon Dàyuan Shēnfù. At the beginning of his service in the prince's residence, he fell ill with a severe fever known as *shanghan* (a classical term for a serious cold or feverish illness). After five days, he had no sweat, his knees were cold to the bone, and a white, sticky substance formed on his tongue. The physicians were at a loss, considering it a grave illness. That night, after undergoing moxibustion and retiring to sleep, he dreamed that he had arrived below the halls of the heavens. Looking all around, he saw no one—only the wet nurse of his second son standing by. Just as he was standing there, a procession of attendants came from the east, numbering in the hundreds. Each was tall and wore plain, wide robes. Their carriage was veiled, with curtains hanging down, and everything was white. They moved far away toward the northwest, their sounds gradually fading. Then others followed behind. The attendants were all young girls dressed in green, each holding a lotus flower, waving banners and flags, lined up on either side. One person rode in a ceremonial carriage like a king, wearing a jade crown with swirling cloud patterns, dressed in green robes, with sashes hanging from the top of his head down to his waist. His appearance was clear and refined, with a slight beard, and he looked to be about thirteen or fourteen years old. Deep-Father gazed at him, raising his hand to his forehead in reverence. After the carriage had passed, a young girl beckoned Deep-Father to come forward and said, "Do you recognize the revered deity in the carriage? Have you ever paid him reverence?" He replied, "The carriage passed too quickly; I could only raise my head to look at him." She said, "That is very good, very good. This is the Divine Lord of the Green Child. If you had encountered the man in the white carriage, you would already have been reduced to dust. However, he will return again, so you must not fail to avoid him." She then gave the flower in her hand to Deep-Father.

Looking back, the warrior was instructed to lead them to the double doors across the street. He said, "You should enter quickly; delay will bring disaster." They hastened to the door, and the person inside asked, "What object is used as proof?" The warrior showed them the flower, and they were immediately led in. The wet nurse followed, but the doorkeeper stopped her. The warrior took a small flower from the base of the flower and placed it in her hand, and she was also allowed to enter, ascending to the high tower. The tower had protective railings, and outside the railing, there were flying pavilions that seemed to form in the air. Looking out in all directions, they saw a panoramic view. After a short while, a white carriage came from the northwest, rumbling back, with people in front and behind dressed in white, in disarray. Gradually, it transformed into a long white mist, hundreds of feet long. A thunderbolt rose from it, shaking the heavens, and it departed looking northeast. All that it passed through—houses, walls, hills, and trees, regardless of size, height, or location, whether in pits or valleys—all turned to dust. Only the door and the tower remained standing. Deep Father was terrified and could not calm himself. Looking down from the pavilion, there was a clear, bright pond like a large round mirror. Just as he was standing by the water, someone pushed him, and he fell into the pond, suddenly waking up. Sweating profusely, the bell had already rung. He urgently called his son to record the name of the deity and set up an altar with incense and fire. The next day, he improved and could finally recount the event. The Taoist said, "This is the Green Child of the Eastern Sea. The white carriage is suspected to be Ruxiao, a white tiger." How terrifying it is!

The *Yijian Yi Zhi* Volume Nineteen, Thirteen Stories Jia Chengzhi Jia Chengzhi was the son of Bao Wen Ge Xueshi Dang. He served as a deputy magistrate in Hengzhou, known for his administrative skills. He was proud and unwilling to be subordinate to others. The governor of Poyang, Wang Han, did not engage in disputes with him and entrusted the affairs of the county to him, gaining his favor. They were colleagues for four years. Later, the new governor Zhao Chi arrived. Upon his arrival, he immediately became an enemy of Jia Chengzhi. He arrested all the officials and subordinates of the county, imprisoning them with chains, and forced them to list their illegal acts. The officials could not withstand the beating and flogging, and were coerced into admitting guilt. They claimed that the county official regularly collected the tax or revenue and took a third of it as a bribe for himself. Zhao Chi reported this to the transport judge Zhu, who knew that this was not true and issued an order to stop the investigation. He also summoned Jia Chengzhi to the governor's office. Zhao Chi feared that Jia Chengzhi would harm him and conspired with his friend Professor Deng. He sent the military officer Huang Ci to gather poisonous plants, mixed them into a poison, and prepared a feast to invite Jia Chengzhi. In the middle of the meal, he went to change clothes and called his son, giving the poison to the official slave Ruan Yu, who poured it into the wine and held it up for a toast. The magistrate of Ningpu, Duan Yan, was present. The wine entered Jia Chengzhi's mouth, and he immediately felt severe abdominal pain, with blood flowing from his eyes and nose. He quickly ordered a carriage to return home, but by the time he reached home, he was already unconscious. His wife and children sat around crying. Jia Chengzhi opened his eyes and said, "Do not cry. I was outsmarted by someone and lost my life. I do not wish to go through the legal process. I will appeal to the underworld. It will take five days if it is far away, or three days if it is close. First, I will take action against Zhao Chi. Next, I will take action against someone named Deng. Then, I will take action against others like Duan Yan and Ruan Yu." He died the next day. On the way to being placed in the coffin, his face and head were split open.

In the county, the people saw the vice-magistrate riding with his entourage as usual. They hurried to the government office. The gatekeeper went in to report. He held something like a bucket of water poured over his body. The next day, he went out to attend to affairs. Before reaching the hall, sand was scattered from above. Wherever it touched, it ignited into flames. The official standing beside him, one grain of sand splashed on him, also suffered burns. It took a long time for the burning to stop. The following day, while sitting on the hall, an eight or nine-year-old grandson was playing. He exclaimed, "Judge Jia pulled Grandfather's hat into the air." He touched his head, and the hat was on the ground. He then fell ill. He often held his chest and said, "Inspector, please untie me; wait for the professor to come, then I will leave." He died three days later. It was the first month of the seventh lunar month in the Ganda era (1165 CE). Professor Deng was examining candidates in Xiangzhou. He was with the examiner-in-chief, Judge Wang Canran, and the examiner, Lu Jue. Suddenly, he rose and bowed to someone, then looked back and said, "Judge Jia is guarding me; we must go together. Please have the locals arrange my funeral." The locals were Lu Jue. The two said, "In broad daylight, how could such a thing happen? Could it be that you are delirious from exhaustion?" Professor Deng pointed to the veranda and said, "He has been standing there precariously for a long time." He hurried into the room, fell on the bed, and a minor official called to him, but he was already dead. Huang Ci and Ruan Yu died within a few weeks. Liu Yan, after being dismissed from office, went to Guilin. While sailing up the Li River, he saw Judge Jia pressing down on his boat. He fell ill and died. He then revived, and this happened twice. It is unknown what is happening now. The son of the deceased protected the funeral procession to Guizhou, where he also died suddenly and was revived, but remained confused as if drunk. Wang Han said.

Ma Shiyuan Ma Shiyuan, courtesy name Yanda, was from Dongzhou. In the sixth year of Xuanhe (1124), he won first place in the military examination. In the third year of Jianyan (1129), he served as the governor of Shouchun. When northern nomadic tribes invaded and passed by the city, Ma Shiyuan recognized that the invaders knew him from his previous mission during the Jingkang era. They shouted at the city gates, saying, "Governor Ma and I are acquainted. Why don't you open the gates?" The people of Shouchun spread rumors that the governor had dealings with the invaders. Fearing this, Ma Shiyuan did not dare to come out. He handed over the seal to the deputy administrator. The deputy administrator, who originally had different intentions, immediately wrote a surrender letter and opened the city gates to welcome and bow to the invaders. The invaders did not enter the city but invited Ma Shiyuan to join them in their camp. The deputy administrator wanted to claim credit for the invaders' retreat and submitted a report stating that the governor had surrendered the city, thus preserving it. After the report was submitted and Ma Shiyuan returned, he stayed with the northern army for only three days. The deputy administrator, feeling cornered and fearful, incited a group of unruly young men to take Ma Shiyuan and kill him. Many of his family members also died. The court praised the deputy administrator's actions and promoted him to the position of governor of the same city. Overjoyed beyond expectation, he held a banquet on the day he received his appointment, enjoying music and feasting with officials and soldiers. After only three toasts, he fell ill on the spot. He seemed to see something and knelt down, crying and confessing, "I indeed surrendered the city. I falsely claimed credit for saving it, and thus caused you to suffer an unjust fate. I regret it deeply." He then collapsed and died. By the tenth year of Shaoxing (1140), the Henan region was recovered. Guanwen Dianxueshi Meng Fugeng served as the governor of the Western Capital. He appointed ten assistants, and they met for meals every day. Chengyi Lang Wang Shanggong suddenly fell ill and did not attend.

The official sent the master of ceremonies to invite him. For a long time, he did not return with a message. The official then sent another person, and this was done up to four or five times, but none of them came. Everyone present found it strange. After a while, several people came together, all looking pale. They said, "Wang Zhi was staring at the ground on the ground. His head looked like a basket, and his appearance was extremely terrifying. When they saw him, everyone was shocked and fell, losing their breath. After a while, they regained consciousness. This is why he was late. Meng Gong led his officials to see him on foot. Wang Zhi was still able to speak. He said, "Please summon a Taoist priest from Mount Song." At that time, the Taoist priest happened to be in the official's residence. Immediately, he set up an altar to summon spirits. Suddenly, there was a strong wind that arose in the courtyard. When the wind stopped, one person about a foot tall appeared, wearing a purple robe and a golden belt. His eyebrows and eyes could all be seen, and he slowly appeared in the air. He questioned the Taoist priest, saying, "I complained of my injustice to the Heavenly Deity and came after being granted permission. I am not an evil spirit. How can you punish me by law?" The Taoist priest dared not answer. Meng Gong personally burned incense and asked him. He began to say that he was Ma Shiyuan. He said, "When I was governing Shouchun, Wang Sheng was a legal officer. Once, he visited me at night and persuaded me to welcome the enemy. Ma Shiyuan refused and could not. Then, he plotted with the circuit intendant to rebel. He also falsely wrote a surrender document and proclaimed it to the people, leading to the disaster of death and the ruin of the family. The circuit intendant already seized the seal of the prefecture and possessed it. Wang Sheng also received a reward for preserving the border. Alas, how unjust! He cried and said, "The Heavenly Deity has allowed me to avenge the guilty." He vanished suddenly. Wang Sheng died the next day. The first account was heard from Ma Shiyuan's son, Ma Yan, and the second account was heard from Chen Jue, who passed it on. Some mistakenly believed it to be a single event.

The Imperial Granary Office The Imperial Granary Office in Lin'an. It is located at the foot of Yangsha Pit. Initially, it was an official residence. Wu Xin once lived there. His wife took a nap during the day. Sand kept falling on her face. She wiped it away, and it happened again. Surprised, she said to herself, "How can there be sand falling from the roof?" Then someone from the roof replied, "The place is called Yangsha Pit. What is there to be surprised about?" Wu Xin's wife was afraid and moved out immediately. Jiang Anli served as the Deputy Director of the Imperial Granary Office. He stayed overnight in the temple quarters. He sneezed, and his nose mucus fell onto the table, turning into small wooden figures. The craftsmanship of the carvings was extremely fine. When he picked them up, they disappeared. Soon after, they appeared again. A total of thousands of wooden figures fell. Jiang Anli fell ill and never recovered. The people of Hangzhou said that it was formerly the residence of the false Duke of Fu. It had magnificent houses with red gates. Many maidservants and concubines were killed there, and they were all buried within the house. This is why there are many strange occurrences. No one dares to live there now. Wang Jia said.

Qin Nu Flower Spirit Liu Ze, courtesy name Mu Zhong, was the younger brother of my mother-in-law. In his youth, he studied Taoist talismans under a Taoist priest. Later, he accompanied his father-in-law to govern Suzhou and, along with his family, visited Lingyan Temple. One night, they stayed in a monk's lodging and heard a voice calling out "Liu Er Gongren" from the mountains. After a while, the voice grew closer, and the people in the lodging also woke up. Liu Ze asked, "Did you hear this sound?" They all laughed and said, "We are protected by the righteous power of the heavenly heart, so it should be like this." The next day, Liu Ze composed a petition to admonish the land god, saying, "I have sincerely practiced the Taoist arts and have never violated any precepts or prohibitions. How dare the mountain ghosts insult me?" That night, he dreamed that the god told him, "I have already ordered my attendants to search, and it was the flower spirit causing the disturbance, not a ghost. It will be dealt with soon." Liu Ze returned home and dreamed that his former concubine Qin Nu came to him, saying, "The one calling you from behind the temple is me. If you do not forget me, do not let the temple god persecute me." Liu Ze then composed another petition, similar to a formal complaint in the world, and sent someone to present it at the shrine. A few days later, he dreamed that Qin Nu came to bid farewell, saying, "You have already submitted the petition. I dare not stay any longer." She wept and left. Qin Nu was a woman from the capital, who died in Lin'an. By this time, it had been six years since her death.

During the Xuanhe period, there was a eunuch named Yang Jian, who was highly favored by the emperor. One night, while his wife was asleep, she saw a red light enter through the window, illuminating the tent so brightly that it outshone everything. A Taoist priest, about a foot tall, moved around the tent in mid-air. He slowly took a bowl from his waist and a small ladle from his hair, pouring wine into it. The fragrance was so strong that it caused her nose to flare. The priest smiled and looked at Yang Jian's wife, asking, "Can you drink this?" The wife, feeling uncertain and fearful, did not respond. The priest circled the tent several times, asking again and again, but she remained silent. Finally, the priest said, "If you cannot, then I will drink it myself." He drank the wine in one go, then suddenly left in a red light, disappearing completely. The family smelled the wine's fragrance for several days before it finally dissipated. Yang Jian had recently built a new study room, which was especially magnificent. Inside, there was a bed, and the door was locked from the outside, preventing anyone else from entering. One day, while Yang Jian was on duty, a young servant reported that a woman had been seen in the room. Yang Jian's wife rushed out to see, and she found a woman with a beautiful appearance and graceful demeanor, something she had never seen before. The woman smiled back and acted naturally. Yang Jian returned home, and his wife scolded her, saying, "Why did you buy a concubine in secret without telling me?" Yang Jian tried to defend himself repeatedly, and they went outside together to look at the room. When they opened the lock, the woman quickly climbed onto the bed and covered her head with a blanket, making it impossible to pull away. After a long time, she turned her face to the wall and leaned back slightly, seeming to be tired. Yang Jian and his wife, along with their concubines, tried to pull the blanket off, but it was too tightly held. After a while, the woman turned her face to the wall and leaned back slightly, seeming to be tired. Yang Jian and his wife, along with their concubines, tried to pull the blanket off, but it was too tightly held. After a while, they lifted the blanket again and saw a huge white snake coiled in several layers, as big as an arm, lying stiff and motionless. The family all fled in terror. Yang Jian sent ten tough soldiers to carry the bed out and threw it in the grass outside the city, not daring to look back. Not long after, Yang Jian died. This story was told by Wu Yuanmei Zhongshi. The previous story was told by Jia Sou.

Wu Zushou Wu Kai, also known as Zhengzhong, married the daughter of Liu Zhongfeng, the Chief Military Commissioner. They had a son named Zushou. During the Jianyan period, Wu followed his father into exile in Shaoyang Prefecture. One night, he dreamed of someone wearing Tang dynasty attire, as if an old acquaintance. The person came to visit and said, "I have been seeking you for two hundred years, traveling the ends of the earth, visiting nearly everywhere. I never expected to meet you here." Zushou asked, "Who are you? What brings you to seek me so urgently?" The person replied, "You were a county magistrate at the end of the Tang dynasty. My entire family of ten died unjustly at your hands. The years have passed long since. Have you forgotten?" He then invited Zushou to a place where they became more relaxed. Zushou asked, "Since you have been in the underworld for so long, you must be able to foresee future events. I wish to know my fate—whether I will live a long life or face misfortune. Please tell me." The person replied, "Your fate is limited to this. Your official rank, longevity, wealth, and blessings will all be as they are now. There is nothing worth mentioning." Zushou became sad and unhappy. In his dream, he became angry and developed a respiratory illness. A tumor grew on his shoulder. He awoke in shock, feeling something beside him on the pillow. When he touched it, he found a small tumor on his shoulder. The next day, it grew larger and soon became a large goiter, as tall as his head. The pain was so severe that he could not lie down. Liu's wife called in doctors and shamans, and invited Taoist priests to perform rituals. They tried every possible remedy, but finally, Zushou did not recover. Zhengzhong's concubine Chunying later returned to her father-in-law. The story is as follows.

The Ghost of a Monk at Mount Lu Monk Wenxiu, whose family name was Chen, was traveling to Mount Lu. Intending to go to Donglin Temple, he found himself at dusk with a light snow falling, unable to proceed. He then entered a small temple by the roadside to seek lodging. The attendant said, "There are no vacant rooms. Only the monk's hall is quite clean, but a guest monk died there by unnatural means in the past. Sometimes it appears as a ghost. Most people who pass by are not brave enough to enter." Monk Wenxiu, considering that he could not go elsewhere, and suspecting that the temple might not be welcoming, decided to stay alone. The attendant lit lamps and stoked the fire, and told him the name of the monk, comforting him before leaving. Until nightfall, he sat cross-legged on the ground stove, covering his head with a patched robe, and quietly recited sutras and mantras. Lightly dozing but not fully asleep, he vaguely saw another monk facing him, also covering his head and reciting sutras. Knowing it was a ghost, he shouted sternly and asked, "We are both brothers of the Dharma, but our paths of life and death are different. Please go well." The ghost did not respond nor stand up. Monk Wenxiu closed his eyes, joined his palms, and recited the Great Compassion Mantra, also chanting in harmony with the same tone. Moved, he called out the ghost's name, shouting at him, "Are you so-and-so?" The person suddenly stood up, spitting saliva at Monk Wenxiu's face, covering the paper quilt he wore with fresh blood. Then it disappeared. The attendant heard the shouts, knowing there was a ghost, hurried to see, and found the paper quilt remained white as before. He then invited Monk Wenxiu to return and stay together. At dawn, they descended the mountain, and Monk Wenxiu told the story.

The Temple of the Two Young Lords The Temple of the Two Young Lords in the capital is located at the foot of the inner city wall to the west. Scholars who come to the capital must go to pray for dreams. They usually place money in the hands of the two children, saying that it has the most divine power. In the second year of the Chongning era, Huo Duanyou from Pi Ling, Hu Xianke from Tonglu, and Zhai Tianyin from Kaifeng sought dreams. They all received two lines of poetry. Huo's poem said, "I have received new news, and I will be the only one to be announced." Zhai's poem said, "One throw and I become the king of flowers, and the spring wind carries fragrance for ten thousand miles." Hu's poem said, "The yellow umbrella stands tall, and the imperial guards are close. The red silk is faint, and the phoenix's sheath makes a soft sound." Later, Huo topped the list, and Hu and Zhai both passed the exams. A local man named Yu Guoqi sought a dream in the fifth year of the Chongning era to prepare for the provincial examination. His father, Shi Yue, took him to the temple, where they burned incense and wrote a prayer. That night, Yu dreamed of a child about thirteen or fourteen years old who rode a horse to the gate of his lodging and told him, "Here is the provincial list." When he woke up, the list was posted, and he was indeed selected. There are many other miraculous events, too numerous to recount. Shi Yue says.

Wang Xian Yan In a certain state of Guangxi, across the river stands a towering and steep cliff. On this cliff, there is a rock known as Wang Xian Yan (Wang Xian Rock), which has never been reached by anyone. Opposite the cliff is a place called Wang Xian Pu (Wang Xian Pavilion). A soldier named Rao Jun, who is old and only fond of drinking without restraint, lived in this area during the late Xuanhe era. One day, a Daoist passed by and was already drunk. He stayed at Rao Jun's place for the night. Late in the evening, the Daoist vomited profusely and called out to Rao Jun, saying, "Get up. Lend me the bed you are sleeping on." Rao Jun did as he was told. By midnight, the Daoist called again, saying, "I am very hungry. I would like to eat a chicken. Please give me one." Rao Jun had only a long-crowing rooster, which he killed and gave to the Daoist to eat. At dawn, the Daoist left, leaving behind a poem for Rao Jun: "Rao Jun, Rao Jun, listen to my words. The immortal realm is just a short distance away, beyond the cold and heat of this world. I have told you everything, but it ends here. Do not cling to this fleeting life and refuse to leave." In the blink of an eye, the Daoist soared to the top of the cliff, sitting calmly and smiling. Rao Jun looked up at him and saw him as if he were in the clouds. He shouted, "Master, why didn't you take me with you?" The Daoist did not respond. Rao Jun then jumped into the river. His fellow soldiers shouted in alarm, "Rao Jun has fallen into the water!" They rushed to save him. Rao Jun appeared and disappeared intermittently, sinking deeper into the waves, almost drowning. Suddenly, the Daoist flew like a bird, reaching the surface of the water and pulling Rao Jun by his hair. Bystanders saw auspicious clouds rising, and they reached the cliffside just in time. Later, Rao Jun returned home, bid farewell to his wife and children, and told them, "This was Lü Dongbin, the Daoist." According to Huang Dao Ren, the name of the state is uncertain.

The Mother and Son of Ma Wang'er** Ma Wang'er, a singer from Tangzhou, was renowned for her ability to sing the ci poetry of Liu Yong. She was famous in the entertainment register. Zhang Erlang, a man from Fangcheng, frequented her home for many years. Eventually, he took her back with him. Later, when enemy cavalry invaded Jingxi, Zhang's family fled to Baxia. Ma Wang'er died in Yidu County, Xiazhou, at midnight. Her body had not yet been collected for burial, so it was placed on a stretcher in an empty room. The next day, a coffin was brought to the location. Only her clothes were scattered on the ground like a snake shedding its skin, and her body was nowhere to be found. They searched for her, and discovered that her body was leaning against the wall between the door and the wall. From head to toe, not a single thread of clothing covered her body. People said that during her time as a prostitute, young men came to visit, and sometimes the payment was unsatisfactory, so they stripped her of her clothes and other belongings. This was the reason for her punishment. She had a son named Yun, who lived in the fields of Yidu. In the sixth month of the twenty-seventh year of the Shaoxing era, Yun and his servant crossed the river to see sesame plants. Several farmers were working in the fields. It was very hot, and the sun was shining brightly. Suddenly, a small cloud appeared from the middle, covering Yun's body. Shortly after, the shadow was as dark as ink, and they could not recognize each other. Bystanders only heard Yun calling out, "Bodhisattva, help me!" After a mealtime, the weather cleared up again. Yun fell to the ground, and his clothes were burned, but his undershirt and green skirt were unharmed. He was still alive but barely. The crowd helped him walk dozens of steps and entered a civilian's house. He continued to moan and cry out, and died after a few more cries. He was thirty-four years old at the time.

Se Chen Sees the Underworld Officials A man from Poyang named Shen Chen, who was renowned in his village as a good person, lived near the fifth milestone outside the northern gate. At the age of forty, he contracted a severe cold and had not yet recovered after eight or nine days. He was lying exhausted on the bed, exactly at dusk. Suddenly, a yellow-clad person holding a rattan stick entered straight from the outside and came up to the bedside. The person completely resembled a county official's subordinate, standing upright without speaking, and frequently looking back at the bedroom door. Another person wearing a black hat and green robe, holding documents in hand, was about to enter but not yet entered. The yellow-clad person shook their hand and said, "Good good."
