The green-robed person took a pen from their sleeve and spread out a ledger, striking out a line. The two then followed each other out. Chen was greatly frightened for a long time. He asked his wife and children, who saw nothing. The fear grew even more intense, and he immediately broke out in a sweat like washing. He recovered after one day and later died of old age.

Medicine for Treating Snake Venom In Linzhou, there was a man who made a living by performing snake tricks and selling medicine. One day, as he was about to perform, he was bitten by a venomous snake (viper). He immediately died, with one arm as large as a thigh. A short while later, the skin all over his body swelled and turned yellowish-black, and he died. A Taoist monk was watching from the side and spoke, saying, "This person is dead. I have medicine that can treat it, but I fear the poison will deepen further, and he might not survive. Can you all witness and confirm this? I dare to exert myself." The crowd all stood up and encouraged him. He then asked for twenty coins to go. After a meal's time, he ran to arrive, ordered fresh water to be fetched, unwrapped the medicine, and prepared one liter. He used a stick to pry open the injured man's mouth and poured it in. When the medicine was finished, the injured man felt a rumbling in his stomach, and yellow water came out of his mouth. The stench was revolting, and his limbs shrank back to normal. After a long time, he returned to his original state and could already stand up, no different from before he was injured. He bowed to all the spectators and thanked the Taoist monk earnestly. The monk said, "This medicine is not hard to obtain. It is also very easy to prepare. I do not hesitate to pass it on to others. It is simply one thing, fragrant white芷 (a type of herb). It should be mixed with Ophiopogon (McMenomin) soup for consumption. But in this urgent situation, there was no time, so I used water instead. I have now saved one person. I can go now." He brushed off his sleeves and left. Duke Guo of Shaozhou said he had obtained the formula. A border guard from Poyang, on duty at night in a watchtower, was bitten by a snake on the abdomen. The next morning, his abdomen was swollen and red, almost bursting. He drank this medicine and immediately recovered. Clean and pure in saying.

The Folklore of the Han Family In the Jiangsu and Zhejiang provinces, there is a custom of believing in witchcraft and spirits. It is said that when a person dies, their spirit returns. On the day of their death, it is believed that the spirit will return, so the entire household leaves to avoid evil spirits. They leave a strong servant or a monk to guard the house and place ashes on the ground. The next day, they observe the tracks and determine if the deceased has been reborn as a human or transformed into another form. A woman from the Han family in Poyang died, and she asked her relative, a monk named Zongda from Yongning Temple, to stay at her home. Zongda closed his eyes and recited the *Shurangama Sutra*. In the middle of the night, he heard a sound coming from the room where the woman had died, a low moaning that grew louder over time, as if something was trapped in a jar. It kicked and scraped against the walls, showing no sign of stopping. Zongda was also afraid, but he continued to recite the *Shurangama Sutra* several times. As dawn approached, the son of the Han family also came. They still heard the sound of something touching the door, which did not stop. Zongda told him what had happened, and they both entered the room with sticks. They saw a creature about four feet tall, wearing a jar on its head, coming straight towards them. Zongda struck it, and the jar broke, revealing a dog that ran out. It turned out that when the door was first closed, the dog was already in the room. The jar had a narrow opening, and the dog could not get out, so it wore the jar and cried out in distress. The saying goes, "Excessive doubt can create imaginary fears," and this story is a good example of that. This is what Zongda said.

The *Yijian Zhi* (Strange Tales of the East) Volume Twenty, Twelfth Story The Silverworker of Tonglin Market in Le Ping There was a young silverworker from Tonglin Market in Le Ping. He was crafting silverware for the residence of Zhang, a scholar-official from Dexing. Every night, he worked. One evening, a woman in her twenties appeared. She had an admirable appearance and brought out wine and food to drink together. After drinking, they slept together. She left just before dawn. All the utensils she carried came from the household of the master. He suspected she was a servant but dared not refuse or speak about it. She came and went for more than a month. Those who knew about it said to him, "I have heard that in the past, a young maid of the Wang family hanged herself here. She often caused strange disturbances. Is what you see not this ghost? Please consider your life." The young man was very afraid. That night, she brought out wine again. He welcomed her and said, "People say you are the ghost of the woman who hanged herself. Is that true?" The woman was startled and replied, "Who said that?" She quickly climbed onto the beam, extended her tongue to two feet long, and disappeared. The young man dared not stay any longer. The next day, he left.

A Stone of Heaven Moved In the Daoping Township of Xiuren Village, Fuqing County, Fuzhou, there is a mountain known as Shizhu Mountain, which is locally called Frog Mountain. It is fifteen li from the county town. On the night of the third day of the third month in the second year of the Ganda period (1166 CE), after midnight, residents such as Zheng Zhouran heard a sound on the mountain that was like a thunderclap. The sound lasted for some time before it ceased. Some saw the stars outside their doors shining brightly. Tracing the sound and its momentum, they determined that it originated from the Shizhu Mountain behind the Ruyun Temple. The next morning, they went together to investigate. On the southeastern slope of the mountain, they found a large stone, about nine zhang in size, that had fallen halfway down. The path it had taken was four chi wide, and the trees and rocks on the mountain were slightly undamaged. A local scholar named Li Huai mentioned that there used to be a stele at the foot of the mountain, inscribed with a prophecy by the monk Miaoying Shi from Nangshan. Recently, due to a flood, the stele was lost but has now been found at the county bridge. The prophecy reads: "A stone of heaven moves, the top scholar arrives. The dragon's claw flower blooms, the top scholar comes from both east and west." There is a stone dam in the county, built during the Tianbao period of the Tang dynasty, known as the Tianbao Dam, which is about ten li from Shizhu Mountain. In that month, the imperial examination was held at the Jiying Hall, and Xiao Guoliang from Yongfu won the top position. Yongfu is located to the west of Fuqing. People in Fujian believed that the prophecy had been fulfilled. Three years later, Zheng from Xinghua, located to the east of Fuqing, also won the top position. The prophecy that the top scholar would come from both east and west has proven true without fail.

Zu Sicheng During the winter of the eighth year of the Zhenghe era (1118 AD), Zhao Gong Shihui, a scholar-official, was serving as a professor in the Wuwei Army. There was a deputy governor named Zu Aow, who was from Jinan and originally a legal expert. He had served as a Deputy Judge at the Supreme Court and conducted himself with integrity and caution, taking the law as his responsibility. Zhao once dreamed of wandering through a small temple. Beside the temple was a pond, no more than a few zhang in size. The pond was surrounded by three layers of red railings, with the innermost layer being about two chi high, the middle layer three chi high, and the outer layer about four chi high. Zhao was inside the innermost railing, only a few steps away from the water. He saw a floating corpse in the pond and found it repulsive. As he tried to step over the railing to leave, he found it extremely difficult. Suddenly, the corpse rose and chased him. Zhao kicked it into the water. He tried to leave again, but the corpse rose once more. He kicked it again, and this happened three times. The corpse's movements became slower, and its appearance was sad and distressed, as if it had something to say. When Zhao asked it, the corpse replied, "In the past, my crime did not warrant death. I was wrongfully killed by Deputy Governor Zu Sicheng. I have carried this grudge for several years." Zhao said, "Zu Sicheng is well-versed in the law and especially detailed and respectful in matters of criminal cases. He would never kill someone unjustly." The corpse answered, "This matter was not necessarily the intention of Zu Gong, but it was due to his suspicion."

He was then sent to another place, where he was ultimately sentenced to death. Thus, the injustice found its resolution. His lifespan could not be prolonged; he was nearing his end. I merely wish you to know this. After speaking, he leapt into the water. Zhao looked at the heavy railing, which was even higher, except for the four corners, which were slightly lower. He found it very easy to climb, but ultimately could not surpass it. The corpse pointed to the clouds from the water, saying, "It is very easy to pass from a high place." Zhao followed his words, took a stumbling step, and already found himself out of the flat ground. He then congratulated himself, saying, "Having passed this railing, there are no obstacles ahead." He woke up, surprised by the experience. At that time, Ao happened to be out in a neighboring town. Until his return, only five days had passed when he contracted an eye disease called cataracts, which grew more severe day by day until he could no longer see. He then requested to be assigned to a temple. Another month later, his eyes suddenly recovered. Suddenly, he suffered from wind-dampness, and his hands and feet became paralyzed. When he was granted leave and returned, he passed through the mouth of the Liangshan Lake. The boat broke, and water entered. The boatman urgently rescued him, but he barely managed to reach the shore. Ao died suddenly from fear. Not many months after Zhao's dream, alas, the matter of the prison, how deeply to be feared! Zhao did not ask for his name in the dream, nor what crime he committed, which is a pity. Zhao Gong recorded this matter himself.

Dreaming of Two Rabbits Long Shenfu. Born in the Year of the Rabbit (1071 CE). At the age of twenty-five, he dreamed of entering a grand palace. Upon reaching the gate, warriors stood there. Beside them were four rabbits. One of the warriors looked at Shenfu and said, "Give one to you." Shenfu bowed and took the first one, which had a brown body with a purple back. He held it in his hand. Another warrior called out to someone behind him and gave them the next rabbit. Soon, the warrior called out to Shenfu again and gave him another, which had a white belly and purple fur. Shenfu carried it on his shoulder and returned home. Then he woke up. At that time, his wife was pregnant. He immediately told her, "I dreamed like this. We will surely have a child without doubt. But it will be twins. Do not be afraid." His wife heard this and became frightened, crying and telling her mother-in-law. The mother-in-law scolded Shenfu, saying, "A woman who has not yet given birth, and you frighten her with these words. What is the matter?" After three months, she gave birth to only one boy, in the Year of the Rabbit (1095 CE). Shenfu already understood the sign of the first rabbit. His son was named Pang. At the age of twenty-five, Pang also had a son, again in the Year of the Rabbit (1125 CE). Thus, getting another rabbit, it indicated the sign of having a grandson. All three generations were born in the Year of the Rabbit, which is indeed unusual.

Long Shiqing's Dream Long Shiqing served as the military governor of Quzhou during the Jianyan era. He temporarily took charge of the state affairs. After him, the prefect Liang Yijin arrived, and due to the relationship of courtesy and reciprocity, all matters related to the treasury were entrusted to Long. He was also in charge of the public treasury. When a guest arrived at the prefecture, Liang provided the guest with 300,000 coins. The officials reported that it was not customary, but Long dug underground passages and divided the money into three installments to give to the guest. Liang took office for three months and lost control due to the enemy's arrival, leading to his dismissal. The successor, who had a grudge against Long, accused him of requesting excessive supply funds and immediately put him in jail, thoroughly investigating all officials in the prefecture who were slightly involved. Long was also detained, fearing he could not escape. At night, he dreamed of entering a desolate wilderness and climbing an ancient tomb. Looking inside, it was deep and dark, frightening. He clutched the grass on the tomb, on the verge of falling but not quite. A person came from nowhere, grabbed his hair, and threw him to the ground, then looked at him and said, "I am Gao Jin." Long woke up in a panic. Two days later, the Judge of Wenzhou, Gao Minxin, arrived, set up an investigation, and upon seeing Long's defense statement, said, "The prefect himself provided the guest with treasury funds. What does it have to do with others?" He was released. Then Long knew that the person in the dream was Gao Jin. When the investigation was complete, Liang lost his position, and those who were implicated were all demoted, but only Long was spared.

**Xu San, the Soul-Escort** Xu San was a villager from Xunxi in Wucheng County, Huzhou. In the middle of July in the fifteenth year of the Shaoxing era, he suddenly died. Four days later, he revived and recounted that he had been taken to the Underworld. The official in charge sat at his desk, and black-robed attendants surrounded him. After questioning him thoroughly about his entire life, he was given an iron staff and appointed as a jailer. He stood beneath the hall, and whenever the names of other prisoners were called, he would accompany his colleagues in driving them forward. The officials would then list his offenses, and he was ordered to strike them with the staff. At the touch of the staff, blood flowed from them. Then, water was sprinkled upon them, and they would return to being human once more. This happened more than once. After a long while, when affairs had grown quieter, he walked freely beneath the eaves. Passing by a room, he saw a sign reading "Office of the Judge." Inside, curtains, tables, and shelves were arranged. He looked closely at the person inside and realized it was his former master, Mr. Wang Yun, the tax official. He inquired why he had come there, and Xu San fully explained the beginning and end of the matter. He then earnestly begged to return. Wang Yun agreed, and together they went to another office. Xu San was ordered to sit outside. A short while later, Wang Yun came out and called to him, saying, "You were not meant to come here. Now you may return to life." He handed him a written document by hand and ordered him to return quickly. He also said, "I am here and it is not bad at all, but I lack money and paper and writing utensils to use. When you return, tell my family."

Quickly burn a million coins. Use two hundred sheets of paper and twenty brushes. Send them to me. In the world of the living, burning money carelessly often results in it becoming scattered and disordered. In this place, there is no one who can properly manage or organize it, making its use extremely difficult. It is advisable to wrap it in a handkerchief before burning it. Do not forget this. Also, take the head ornament and tie it to your left arm. Say, "I fear my family will not believe you. This is an object I possess at my end. You can hold it as proof." Immediately cry and thank, then enthusiastically go out. On the road, ghosts and spirits frequently shout and block you. Show them a document, and you will be spared. Run even faster. Climb a high mountain. Fall and wake up. Have no time to visit the Wang family. After some time, die again. The next day, the Wang family sends a letter to scold, saying, "Last night, I dreamed that the tax collector spoke about the events that happened before. Why did you not tell me earlier?" From then on, revive again after three days. Say that a certain god blocked and detained you, ordering you to participate in a boat race. See that the object tied to your left arm still exists, with the seal and mark exactly as before. After seven years, arrive at Weitang in Xiu Prefecture, working as a hired farmer for the Fang family. After another seven years, because you owe rent and grain and cannot repay, flee by boat and return to your hometown. Pass through Tai Lake, where the whole family drowns and dies. The children, including Jingpei, say that he is the son-in-law of the Fang family.

The Merchant of the Shenxiao Palace A person named Dai Que, from the capital city, was twelve years old. At that age, he accompanied his father and elder brother on a trip to Changzhou. They entered the Shenxiao Palace to visit a Daoist but did not meet him. When they exited to the gate, a merchant spoke to the gatekeeper, saying, "I wish to see the master of the palace." At that time, Daoism was highly respected, and there were strict prohibitions on entering and exiting. The gatekeeper asked for the merchant's name and letter of introduction, but the merchant refused to provide them. They argued for a long time, and the merchant threw the gatekeeper to the ground and beat him. He then directly entered the hall. The others were afraid he would drag them down and all left. The merchant entered and immediately disappeared. In the kitchen and house, all over the walls, he had written the four characters "Lü Dongbin arrived." The master of the palace heard about it and sighed deeply, saying, "The immortal passed by me, but I did not get to see him. It is fate." The next day, the joy spread throughout the state. Three days later, the Dai family had a meal at a temple. Que went to the toilet and returned to wash his hands at the stone basin. A person beside him bent down to clean the chopsticks. A guest spoke to him. Que looked at the guest's appearance and realized it was the one he had seen at the Shenxiao Palace. He rushed forward and bowed twice. The person was surprised and asked why. Que said, "You are Master Lü." He told him about the previous events. The person smiled and ordered him to take a cup of water from the jar. He drank half of it and gave the other half to Que, who drank it.

Qu had to clear up the matter with his father. He rushed to the toilet to visit him, but it was too late. When Qu was grown up, he could perform divination using the method of Gai Ge, which he named "Gu Xiang." Later, he settled in Sanqiao, Lin'an, to run a divination shop. There was a beggar dressed as a Daoist, ragged and emaciated, who earned a living by cleaning sewers. Once, he cleaned the latrine behind a brothel. Qu secretly felt pity for him. The next day, he invited him to sit down and prepared food for him. He said, "You call yourself a Daoist, but you must have someone to serve and venerate. Today, you work in the sewers, earning very little. Moreover, entering a brothel, your clothes and hands are not clean. Do you not fear inviting misfortune?" The Daoist admitted that it was true, but he was preoccupied with making a living and had no time to worry about it. Qu gave him two hundred coins. Suddenly, the Daoist smiled and said, "Do you remember me?" Qu was startled and did not understand. The Daoist said, "When I first saw you here, you did not remember me." Qu asked, "Fifty years ago, you met Lü Weng at a temple in Changzhou. At that time, there was someone sitting on a stone washing counting rods. Do you recognize him?" I am the same person," the Daoist said. Qu was surprised and thanked him. He was about to ask for his name, but the Daoist bowed deeply and left. Qu never saw him again. Since Qu drank the leftover water, he lived to be over seventy years old without a single day of illness or suffering. Zhao Li said. **城隍门客** The City God's Guest Qu had to explain the matter to his father. He rushed to the toilet to meet him, but it was too late. When Qu grew up, he became skilled in the divination method of Gai Ge, which he named "Gu Xiang." Later, he settled in Sanqiao, Lin'an, to run a divination shop. There was a beggar dressed as a Daoist, ragged and emaciated, who earned a living by cleaning sewers. Once, he cleaned the latrine behind a brothel. Qu secretly felt pity for him. The next day, he invited him to sit down and prepared food for him. He said, "You call yourself a Daoist, but you must have someone to serve and venerate. Today, you work in the sewers, earning very little. Moreover, entering a brothel, your clothes and hands are not clean. Do you not fear inviting misfortune?" The Daoist admitted that it was true, but he was preoccupied with making a living and had no time to worry about it. Qu gave him two hundred coins. Suddenly, the Daoist smiled and said, "Do you remember me?" Qu was startled and did not understand. The Daoist said, "When I first saw you here, you did not remember me." Qu asked, "Fifty years ago, you met Lü Weng at a temple in Changzhou. At that time, there was someone sitting on a stone washing counting rods. Do you recognize him?" I am the same person," the Daoist said. Qu was surprised and thanked him. He was about to ask for his name, but the Daoist bowed deeply and left. Qu never saw him again. Since Qu drank the leftover water, he lived to be over seventy years old without a single day of illness or suffering. Zhao Li said.

A scholar from Jiankang named Chen Yaodao, with the courtesy name Deguang, died three years ago. His fellow scholar Guo Jiude dreamed of him as if he were still alive. Guo said, "You are already dead, how can you come back?" Chen replied, "I am now a guest of the City God, responsible for writing official documents, which is very laborious. Today, the host is attending a banquet at the Yanshan Temple, which is located ten miles south of the city gates. I have just had some free time, so I came to see you. I asked about his family, parents, and brothers, and he cried for a long time. Guo said, "Since you are a guest of the City God, you should know the names of the candidates who will take the autumn examination this year and those who will pass the next spring examination." Chen replied, "This is not my duty. There is someone else who oversees the register of successful candidates. I will ask him when I return." A few days later, Guo dreamed again and Chen said, "You will surely pass the next spring examination. I have a close friendship with you, so I am telling you. Others may know, but they dare not reveal it." Indeed, Guo passed the imperial examination the following year. Another scholar named Liu Zigu, who lived in the same neighborhood as Chen Yaodao, had a sister-in-law named Huang Sen, who was virtuous and well-versed in literature. Her father was an official who owed the government money, leading to his death and the destruction of his family. Huang Sen also died due to his lack of success. Several months after his death, his wife, while visiting her brother, suddenly dressed in the clothes Huang Sen used to wear, greeted her brother with a bow, and her demeanor and voice were just as they were in life. Liu Zigu was shocked and sad, calling out his nickname, "Yuangong, where are you now?" She replied, "Huang Sen studied hard all his life but could never obtain even a low-ranking official's robe. Recently, I was recommended by Chen Deguang to serve as a judge for the City God, with a green robe and official documents, which is far better than when I was alive. I am worried that my wife might miss me, so I came to inform her." Liu Zigu asked who would pass the next spring examination in their hometown, and she replied, "Only Guo Jiude will pass." After a short while, she left, and her words matched those of the previous dream. Fang Deshuo

The Ghost of the Lu State The Lu State's Judicial Hall was located to the west of the prefectural government office. It was said that a powerful ghost resided within it, and no one dared to live there. Instead, it was used as an oil and medicine storage for the city defense. Wang Shenyi, a judicial officer, was from Anyang. During the spring, he and his colleagues, including Shao Qikang, carried courtesans and wine and went to visit the hall. They also went to the back garden to practice archery. After finishing their practice, they drank heartily in the hall. Suddenly, they heard laughter coming from behind the screen, like that of a noble man. Everyone was startled. One of the guests, who had a bold spirit, called out and asked, "What are you laughing at?" The ghost replied, "I have lived here for a long time, feeling depressed and unable to comfort myself. I see that you are on a spring outing, envying the joy of human life, and I couldn't help but laugh unintentionally. Can you drink?" The guest replied, "Very good." The guest stood up and poured a large cup of wine, flipping it and placing it inside the screen. Someone inside the screen received it. They then heard the sound of drinking to the brim and exclaiming joyfully. Soon, an empty cup was thrown out. The guest asked again, "You are a brave man, skilled in archery. Can you shoot one arrow?" The ghost replied, "How dare I not be happy for you? I should take a small step back." After the bow and arrow were brought in, everyone sat with their backs against the wall. Soon, an arrow broke through the screen paper and hit the target accurately. Everyone was initially respectful and amazed. They all stood up and asked, "Dare I ask what era you are from? What is your name? Why do you end up here?" The ghost replied, "My surname is Helan, and my name is Wu." Before he finished speaking, someone criticized his name, saying it was not elegant or refined. The ghost became angry and said, "Why don't you learn? Have you not seen the line 'black reins with white metal attachments' in the *Xiaorong* chapter of the *Shi Jing*?" He then said, "I was born during the Dali period of the Tang dynasty because I came to Zhao Yi."

A Man Who Was Killed and Decapitated I paid homage to the military governor Li Baoren. I presented a strategy to pacify Shandong, but was slandered by malicious tongues and killed here. My body and head were in different places, and my bones were scattered and not collected. For several hundred years, every time I met someone, I must make a complaint, and I was often treated as a ghost, frightened and left. Thus, I have remained in obscurity until now. You gentlemen are talented people. Will you show some pity? All the guests were grave. Those who ask about auspicious and ominous signs asked one by one about their official titles. Slowly, he said, "The coming household official will reach the position of attendant, but the longevity will be great, but not as good as Wang the judicial official." At that time, officials, soldiers, and government slaves, seeing this, all ran back home, joyfully spreading throughout the state. The governor Ma Shao Zhongyu alone did not believe, thinking that the officials were drunk and made false statements, all chained and imprisoned his followers, and will soon prosecute them. The crowd was afraid, each dispersed. The next day, Zhongyu went to the place himself and examined it. The hole in the screen paper was still there. He ordered to open the hall door lock. The lock was already open, but the door was closed as before. He called strong soldiers to push the door together, but it could not be opened. Soon after, a loud voice arose from the beam, shouting, "How dare you do this? Do you not remember the incident when you were the magistrate of Xingzi?" Zhongyu hurried out. From then on, no one dared to go there again. The same household came to it. Shao indeed became the Deputy Minister of Works. Shen Yan was appointed as the Governor of Laizhou with the rank of Senior Official, living to be seventy-five and died. Wang Gongming said, "Laizhou was his great-grandfather." I passed the imperial examination. The *Kuo Yi Zhi* also records this matter very briefly, mistakenly taking Shen Yan as Wang Pi. The rest are all different.

Wang Zude Wang Zude, a Chengdu native and holder of the title of Chengxin Lang, arrived in Lin'an in the thirty-first year of the Shaoxing era. He obtained the position of overseeing the construction bureau in Anzhou. After taking up his post, he heard that Yu Bingfu, the Minister of War, was sent to Shaanxi to announce imperial decrees. Wang Zude then requested a letter from the Sichuan Military Governor's office, under the pretense of consulting on matters, to pay homage to Yu Bingfu in Qin Prefecture. Before he could use the letter, he died as a guest in Shaanxi in the sixth month of the year. Yu Bingfu dispatched soldiers to escort Wang Zude's coffin back to his hometown and sent a message to his family. The Wang family immediately held a funeral and set up a place for mourning in the hall. On the 15th day of the lunar month, they performed the ritual of setting up a table for offerings and conducted a memorial service. Just as the ritual was about to conclude, a soldier carrying a folding stool entered from outside, drenched in sweat. He said, "The official Wang Zude has a very hasty nature. He traveled day and night from Qin Prefecture, arriving here after four days and nights. He is coming soon." Soon after, six men carrying a sedan chair arrived, all looking exhausted. The person in the sedan chair ascended to the hall and sat on the eastern couch. It was Wang Zude himself.

Called to his wife and said, "I have wanted to return home for a long time. I was forcibly detained by the Minister Yu, and only recently managed to escape. Exhausted from traveling, I heard that people think I am dead, and they are trying to ruin my livelihood. You should believe it now." His wife replied, "I recently received a letter from Minister Yu, reporting that you died in Qin. The funeral bier arrived the day before. What is this?" He began to smile and said, "Do not be afraid. I am truly dead. I heard that the family is discussing selling the house. The house is ancestral property. How can it be sold? The ten scrolls of Huang Quan, Guo Xi, Li Cheng, and their cold forests that I treasure, I heard they have taken them out to discuss the price. How long ago did I pass away? Not yet in dire straits. How could they act so hastily? I miss home very much, but there is no way to return. Today, because of the Mid-Autumn Festival, the underworld has granted me leave, so I can come for a short while, but I cannot stay long. Also, I called my beloved maidservant, showing full affection and care." At that time, the whole family was in a state of disbelief, unable to distinguish between life and death. Suddenly, green smoke rose from the ground, and in a blink, they were unrecognizable. When the smoke cleared, there was nothing to be seen. Guan Shouqing, an elderly grandson, lived in a house between two streets. He saw commotion outside the door and hurried to see it, but it was already gone.

A Woman from Shuzhou Su Yanzi, a man from Pengzhou, was a low-ranking military officer in Shuzhou. He had a daughter who was about eight or nine years old. One day, while playing by the corner of her bed, she noticed a small, bright hole in the ground. She inserted a tube into it and got stuck. She ran to tell her father, who held a long pole to measure the depth. It was so deep that the pole could not reach the bottom. When he finally pulled it out, there was a tattered red silk hanging on it. He was greatly surprised and called laborers to dig the ground. They dug more than a zhang (about 3.3 meters) deep and found a dried-up skeleton with head and feet intact. They immediately buried it in the fields. The next day, a beautiful woman appeared in the room. The family members tried to ask her questions, but she always avoided by hiding in a crack in the wall. They never managed to question her. At that time, there was a scholar named Chen Yu in the prefecture who came from Langzhong. He was good at physiognomy and could use Daoist techniques to repel ghosts and spirits. He was called to examine the woman. Soon, a woman arrived and said, "I was originally a daughter of the Duan family in Han Prefecture. I was promised to marry a man from the same prefecture named Tang. I was about to be married, but Tang's family suddenly became poor and broke the original agreement. I could not remarry, so I sold myself as a concubine to the official in charge of expenses in this prefecture. Unfortunately, I was favored by my master because of my appearance and was buried alive underground by the mistress. For several years now, if not for Mr. Su reburialing me, I would be a lingering spirit. But when I first came out of the ground, the laborers were not careful and nearly cut off my shin bones with their shovels. Now I cannot walk, so I had to stay here. There is nothing else to it." Chen Yu said, "It is not difficult to leave. I will help you. Take some paper and cut it into a human shape. Say that it will carry you." Then she smiled, thanked him, and left. That night, Yan Zhi's older sister-in-law dreamed of a servant carrying this woman. Bowing twice, she said goodbye and left. Both stories were told by Huang Zhongbing.

**Taboos in Diet** Eating yellow catfish should not be followed by consuming perilla. Eating honey should not be followed by consuming salted fish. Eating pufferfish should not be followed by taking wind-relieving medicines. All these taboos are credible and have evidence. A man from Wu named Wei Jidao, with the intention of visiting his wife's home, ate a dish of yellow fish soup and then picked perilla and mixed it with tea to drink. Shortly afterward, he experienced an unusual itching in his feet that extended up to his heart and lungs. He ran barefoot in the sand, rushing wildly like a madman. The skin on his feet was all broken and ready to tear. He quickly sought and took detoxifying medicine, and it took nearly two days to stop. On the side of the Yuehua Temple in Shaoguan, a household prepared offerings for monks. The new honey had just been made, and the group of monks ate it heartily. Two senior monks from a different branch of the temple returned halfway and encountered a village selling salted fish. They could not resist the temptation and bought and ate half a pound of it. That night, they all died. A medical officer named Li Fubo passed through Changzhou, where Prince Wang Ziyun served as the governor of the region. He was invited for breakfast, and pufferfish was prepared as a dish. Li usually did not eat pufferfish, so he sent it back to his wife. His wife was taking medicine early in the morning and did not consider it a concern. She drank it and found it very delicious. Immediately, blood flowed from her mouth and nose, and she died. Li had not finished his meal when the news of her death arrived.

#### Yijian Zhi: Volume III (丙志) ##### Preface to Yijian Zhi: Volume III Initially, I compiled the first two volumes of *Yijian Zhi*, focusing solely on collecting strange and extraordinary things, with no intention of writing about human affairs. However, I found myself criticizing people's faults, often doing so easily or out of a desire to fill the pages quickly, thus deviating from my original purpose. For example, in the first volume, there is a story about a person turning into a bird, and in the second volume, there is the tale of the injustice suffered by the Huang family of Jianchang involving Feng Dangke and Jiang Maoxin. These stories are not accurate, and ultimately, they lead to the defamation of good people. Additionally, the story of the chivalrous woman from the Dong family is not entirely as described. This is due to the inaccuracies in the accounts or my own lack of careful listening, which has left me feeling deeply ashamed. After revising and correcting these inaccuracies, and organizing the materials stored in the miscellaneous section, I gathered enough content to create the third volume. Despite my efforts to stop, my habitual tendencies kept me from doing so, and well-meaning gentlemen encouraged me. I justified this to myself by saying that it is enough to talk about ghost and god stories, and there is no need to mention other things. Thus, I compiled the third volume, *Yijian Zhi: Volume III*, which consists of twenty volumes and includes a total of 267 stories. This was written on the 18th day of the fifth month in the seventh year of the Ganda era. By Hong Mai, courtesy name Jinglu. #### Yijian Zhi: Volume I, Eighteenth Story ##### Nine Saints and the Strange Ghost [Note: The specific content of the eighteenth story is not provided in the source text, so it is not included in this translation.]

Xue Jiexuan of Yongjia, courtesy name Shi Long, was the son of Left Counselor and Attendant Huiyan. In the autumn of the second year of the Longxing era, the mother of a neighboring Shen family was ill. Xue Jiexuan sent his son Yun and two nephews by marriage to visit her. The family had just summoned the shaman Shen Anzhi to deal with a ghost. Yun and the two nephews all saw a god-general dressed in military attire, several inches long, appearing on a tea tray. The ghost ate, drank, and spoke just like a human. It took a deceased concubine from the Shen family and left with her. It then pursued the spirit of Shen's mother, who arrived within moments. The ghost appeared alive, transformed into a beam of light, and entered her head. Her illness improved slightly. Yun returned home and boasted to his family about the supernatural event, marveling at it. At the time, the wife of one of Yun's cousins was troubled by a mischievous spirit. She had been suffering from a lingering ailment and invited Shen Anzhi to investigate. Shen Anzhi captured two such spirits, which were monkey-like but lacked limbs. The god-general said that three others had fled far away and requested permission to pursue them. Soon, hundreds of armored soldiers arrived, bearing flags with the Big Dipper and the Eight Trigrams painted on them. The flags emitted bright light, and the soldiers were fully equipped. Their crossbows had eight dragon heads, with mechanisms hidden in the handles. When one mechanism was triggered, the dragons opened their mouths to receive arrows, which were then shot and leaped. Shortly after, the three remaining spirits were captured. Shen Anzhi also seized two more individuals, one wearing a blue headband and the other a bun, both covered in leaves. He ordered them imprisoned and interrogated. The tortures included poisons, boiling water, and crushing, all administered on demand. The spirits were repeatedly tortured and revived, but they did not confess. Shen Anzhi called in a blue-faced general on horseback to interrogate them. The general ordered the torture to continue and personally broke the spirits' limbs, impaling them on spears. The torture was as severe as before, but the spirits finally confessed. They admitted that they were trees from which a scroll had been found after their bellies were cut open.

Said, 'This is the soul of the girl.' Threw it into her mouth. It also entered her crown. That night, she slightly recovered. The next day, the god said that the three generations of xiao demons, with great strength, were reluctant to be captured and were just being resisted by soldiers. Requested to attack them. Immediately dispatched tens of thousands of soldiers and summoned the city gods and the armies of the Five Peaks. Scouts and spies were continuously arriving. Reported defeat. Some were cut off or mutilated and fled and returned. Said, 'The entire city became a battlefield. Our army fought in the alleys. All were unfavorable.' Again sent the iron-capped general with ten times the number of troops. Also failed. An's expression was not pleased. Burned the talisman to summon the soldiers of the Three Thunder Temples for reinforcements. By sunset, the decision was not made. Two days later, there were those who came holding flags to report victory. Like the flags of victory in the world. And then carefully reported the two words. Captured a chief. Crowned and robed, with red tassels, and bound. The large blue ghost claimed to be from the Thunder Department.

A ghost appeared, standing empty in the air. Cloud vapor covered its body, and it roared in the clouds. The thunder sounded again, and a golden snake several zhang long rode the lightning into the dark prison. Yun and his nephew said it was no different from ordinary thunder and lightning, while the others did not notice. That night, the spirit general said, "I heard that distant divine beings have taken over the domain of the ghosts. They are about to seize our prison." It ordered a cage vehicle to imprison the prisoners inside and arranged armored soldiers to guard. Anzhi burned thousands of paper money to reward the soldiers. After burning them, they were distributed, and each person received seven coins. Several days later, the woman's illness remained unchanged. Anzhi again led the spirit general and said, "The woman's soul has been taken by the ghosts again." Then, he unbundled his hair and performed the Yu step, holding a sword and cursing. Every time they captured someone, they imprisoned them. His nephew no longer saw anything. Yun saw the spirit general's form gradually grow larger like a human. He bowed to Ji Xuan and invited him to sit, and they discussed matters of ghosts and gods. The spirit general said, "There are not truly any."

All things originate from the human heart/mind. The human heart/mind exists and possesses them. There is no non-existence, there is existence. There is no need to question further. Further speaking about learning, one should read the collected works of the two sages, the Wise and the Manifest. Told that there is no such book in the world, the book has been burned and destroyed by the policies of Qin. The successor of Mr. Chenglie is the son of Mr. Xianmo, and his intention probably refers to Emperor Yao and King Wen and King Wu. Again he said, "A person cannot stand without trust. If one truly knows self-confidence, then the ways of the ancient kings can be attained through learning." Xuan's nephew has been suffering from malaria for a long time. His sister saw this and was respectfully surprised. The spirit looked around and said, "I heard that there is a ghost malaria among relatives. It can be treated together." Anzhi did not agree. The next day, his sister came to the borrowed room to treat her nephew's illness. Three spirits descended. One of them was like Left Sir Gong. He called Xuan by his nickname, "Tiger Boy," and said, "I am your father."

He is now the Heavenly Bright and Powerful King, ranked above Yue Fei. My brother, the Minister of Personnel and the Righteous Ally, who holds the title of Secretariat and is the Righteous Chancellor, commands the Five Thunder Armies and is also known as one of the Three Bright Ones. He should meet with you, and you should prepare a feast to welcome him. All seven ghosts were captured and imprisoned. Until the night watch, the prisoners were called, roughly as in the human world. The next day, many gods arrived. From this point on, they did not leave the hall or the door. Some called them the North Star, South Star, True Warrior, and Emperor of Mount Hua. Others called them the God of Guankou, Cheng Tang, King Wu Ding, Yi Yin, Duke of Zhou, Chen Tuan, and Sima Guang. It was also said that Yao and Shun were in heaven as left and right ministers, King Wen was in charge of the Military Council, and Confucius resided in the Imperial Academy. Their words were mostly rustic and laughable. The King Yama arrived later. Bow twice to greet the gods. Order the underworld officials to search for the deceased members of the Xue family. Sixteen men and women were found.

Xuan's parents and father-in-law Grandson Sun were all present. All were dressed in official attire and robes. All the household servants were present. After the banquet, it was said that the legal case was not yet concluded, and he would come again tomorrow. Today's food was unusually thin and poor, but it will be more abundant in the future, sufficient to complete the ritual. He then left, leaving only two side generals to patrol. Xuan's brother Ningzhong secretly found it strange and said aloud, "This is a strange ghost attaching itself. It is not worth further worship." Ghosts and spirits are indeed difficult to know, but since it claims to be our ancestor, how can we not worship it? The spirit will gradually become displeased and may accuse Ningzhong and others of being unrighteous, asking the Emperor to reduce their lifespan.

Upon receiving the imperial decree, they were permitted to proceed. The intention was to instill fear in Xuan. On the following night, sixteen people gathered again. They arranged their own provisions and transformed the innermost part of the house into a spacious courtyard. The curtains and canopies were all adorned with brocade, and the utensils and objects were made of gold and jade. The men wore formal attire with caps and robes, while the women donned ceremonial dresses. The maidservants were adorned with pearls and jade. All types of music were prepared, including ancient instruments like the xun, chime, and bells. Yun had never seen such instruments before. After the wine was drunk, the entertainers performed dances and plays, including the "Po Han," "Man Yan," and "Long Jue" performances. There were thousands of strange and varied forms, and the melodies seemed to come from afar, carried by the wind. The entertainers often made prophecies about future events. Some continuously criticized these predictions, and those who believed them were considered coarse and vulgar. The reactions were indecisive and contradictory, and the person felt some criticism. A man from the left office cried and said, "Do you think death means a lack of consciousness? There might be those who are confused or misled. It is not your fault. You can paint portraits of me and Grandmaster Sun, and set up a shrine in the room for the gods and generals I serve."

The elder said, "When a great man dies, he becomes a god in heaven. This is very good. His descendants will be blessed. It is not appropriate to be offended. Do not invite improper offerings. Now let us sever all connections. Do not come back." The younger man replied, "Yes." At dawn, the elder had not yet risen. His wife, named Shu, was the daughter of a secretary. She also suspected that it was not appropriate to continue the rituals. The elder had not yet responded. The person known as the Left Department Secretary had already cried at the bedside. He said, "Are you really cutting me off?" Shu said, "Uncle and father-in-law, please come. Why come to the bed of a young woman?" Everyone was greatly ashamed. He said, "You are right. I will leave immediately." He then mounted a tiger and left. Shu said that her husband, father-in-law, and father were all upright men. They would surely not do such a thing. Perhaps they are using his name to steal food. After finishing speaking, two people came. They said, "All of this is false. The true flying god has sent me to capture him."

宣叱曰：“汝辈魑魅亡状，又欲以真飞天诳我。”拔剑击之，复其本质。少焉，尽室皆魈。移时乃没。明日，沄诵书堂上。又有启户者曰：“二魈已伏诛，吾来报子。”宣以剑拂其处，血光赫然。它奇形异状者踵至，皆计穷舍去。其一盘辟于廷，曰：“昼日吾无可奈何，夜能苦子耳。”及夜径来逼沄。宣抱之于怀，魈将以物置沄口，宣掩之。沄于手中得药，投诸地，有声堕宣指间，疮即隐起。已又投食器中，淑取食之，无伤也。夜半不去，沄困急闷闷不自持。

He silently recited the Qian hexagram of the *I Ching*. It seemed to bring a slight calm, but soon returned to its previous state. Su took an image of Zhenwu and hung it beside him. Yun felt as if water was being sprayed into his body, cold as ice and snow. The ghost transformed into a luminous vapor, passing through the window and disappearing. Finally, his spirit found peace. The Xue family discussed calling a Taoist priest to perform proper rituals. The ghost pointed out the shortcomings of others, but did not mention Zhang. Yanhua happened to arrive upon being summoned. The ghost pretended to be an old servant named Chen Dehua. It was commanded to reveal the truth. "I am one of the Five Peaks and Nine Saints from the Western Temple," it said. "All the affairs of Shen Anzhi are under my jurisdiction. The people of this region treat me with respect, except for the Xue family. That is why I used Shen's medium to deceive them and intend to harm their son. Now my hands and feet are exposed. Please let me part ways from now on." The next day, after Yanhua left, the evil spirits returned, attacking Yun even more fiercely. Yanhua then ordered the summoning of spirits. Yun saw a divine figure with loose hair flying through the air, riding an iron fire wheel.

The spirit used a magical vessel to confront and resist them. All the people were lost. The nine holy beings called themselves divine generals, wearing gauze caps and reddish-brown robes, alongside Taoist priests. They walked and spat water, showing no fear. Huai returned, burned a document, and reported to the emperor. The room was swept to make it a prison, and ashes were placed there. The next morning, they examined the traces of the ashes. One ghost and one woman were captured. A prison guard in a red robe stood beside them. A ghost in the air called the righteous god a thief general, saying, "Do not stab me with a spear. I am Wang Bangzuo, a man with an iron heart and stone intestines. What can you do? Hasten to repair my temple, and it will be done." The emperor would not ask further. Leading the servants, they destroyed the temple, cutting off the heads of all the clay figurines. Initially, Yun dreamed of being carried into a cave by a group of monkeys. A blue ghost led a tiger, looking stern. Therefore, their image was defaced. The temple was destroyed. Wang Bangzuo took responsibility and requested from Yun. The emperor ordered him to return home. Seven people continued to arrive.

One of them named himself Xiao Banggong. Yun called out, "Why does the god not capture this one?" Immediately, a large star appeared in the courtyard, and clouds gathered below it. Three ghosts ascended spirally and were soon brought to the ash room. The fourth one escaped. Fire wheels and stone axes intersected in the clouds. A total of twenty-one captured ghosts were all beheaded. Fifteen of the bodies had fire script imprinted on their backs, saying, "Mountain ghosts are wicked," and "Heaven's command to punish them." Six of the bodies had imprinted text, stating, "Ancient buried corpses without tombs, those who harmed ten people, their heads were impaled on poles and displayed." That night, the prison was opened, and the ash traces were scattered and chaotic. But only five were restrained. They were to be sent to the North Hell. A golden-armored god held a yellow paper talisman and showed it to Yun. The top had nine stars arranged, the middle had a black killing talisman, and the bottom said that all small and large ghosts, gods, and evil paths would be punished. Yun recorded it and showed it to Hua.

Hua Xi said, "Heaven has decreed it." At dawn, he went to the prison to inquire of the officials. The officials reported that the preparations had been finalized. Execution can proceed. The primary culprit is not Wang Bangzuo, but it is Xiao Wenzuo. Xiao Zhongyan and Li Budai are also involved. The rest are too numerous to count. Their names are not worth asking about. The soldiers arranged wooden donkeys, stone blades, fire seals, and wooden balls in the courtyard. The officials had prepared the case files and legal texts, filling the desk. They called the military officer to enforce the law. One official brought a decree and said, "There is a special order. There is no need to follow the laws and regulations. First, list the crimes on a lacquer board. Replace it with a red banner. Fill it with gold. Set up a large flag. Write 'Taiqing Tianshu Yuan' on it. Below the flag, a plaque was placed saying, 'In accordance with the order of a certain divine general, execution will take place.' The official showed the order to Yun, saying, "There is an order to execute all the demons and their companions. All shall be punished according to the case." The Five Thunder Judge approached and said, "The primary culprit will be killed by dark thunder. All will experience three lives and three deaths."

The next fifteen people were dismembered. The rest were struck by a hidden thunderbolt. Three demons were summoned and shaken before. Water was poured over their heads, and they quickly revived. After being struck three times, they died. The bodies were placed in baskets and taken away. Three red banners were placed behind them, named Nine Saints, Mountain Demons, and Five Passages. All their crimes were documented, and they were paraded through the temple. In turn, they were nailed to donkey beds with two men, four women, and six demons. The executioner wore a red cap and a tiger-patterned robe, and their crimes were also written down. One was an old servant named Hua Nu, who died from being struck by lightning and became a malevolent spirit. Another died prematurely and became a tree spirit. A man died violently and spread disease. Demons who are supposed to be gods but act wickedly. Those who falsely claimed to be Nine Saints. Those who stole the food offerings of the gods. They did not uphold righteousness in life and became evil spirits in death. They killed and harmed the country, doing everything they could to cause harm. And others like someone whose traces are mysterious, all were first fed.

Those who swallowed wooden balls and were then cut into pieces. Those who died from lightning or fire numbered another twenty-two. After the executions, all the bodies disappeared without a trace. A military official holding a document from the Heavenly Pivot Bureau declared: "The punishment of the mountain demon was not within the authority of this bureau to violate heavenly law, but was carried out in accordance with the request of the officials and specifically ordered and enforced." He requested notification through the document. Initially, the county had been involved in a cult dedicated to nine saints, which had long been a source of trouble for the people. By the time the light and sound had ceased, since the witch doctor had treated the girl's illness, from the seventh day of the tenth month to the twenty-eighth day, the matter was completed. The entire affair lasted over two weeks. The heavenly beings descended by Yan Hua were no different from the strange phenomena associated with the witch doctor. The younger brother's voice was like bells, chimes, gold, and jade, as fine as a baby's voice, while the strange sound was heavy and dull, like a human voice. Xuan regretted that he initially brought disaster upon himself through blind trust. He wrote an article saying: "A Record of Mistakes," in which the beginning and end were recorded in great detail. I have selected the main points and recorded them here. Yun was around fourteen or fifteen years old at the time.

Chen Shunmin During the reign of Emperor Gaozong, Chen Shunmin, the magistrate of Jinjiang, was summoned to Fuzhou. He had not yet reached the third post station when he arrived at the inn. All his attendants went out, leaving him alone in the hall. Suddenly, a woman emerged from the eastern side room, wearing a light yellow dress and looking very attractive. She wandered around the hall, singing two stanzas of a new water poem. Chen Shunmin recognized her as a ghostly being and silently recited the Tianpeng spell. She paid no attention to the spell and walked slowly, singing softly. Her appearance remained unchanged. Chen Shunmin then recited the spell more quickly, and his voice gradually became louder. The woman spoke angrily, "Why must you do this?" She rushed into the room and was no longer seen. This story was told by Liang Shuzi, a political figure. Gongyuan Ghosts The examination hall in Lin'an was known for its many strange occurrences. Officials and soldiers often encountered these phenomena. In the first year of the Ganda era, during the autumn examination, Huang Zhongbing, Hu Changwen, Rui Guorui, Hui Chang, Yu Gong, and Yong were the examiners. Liu Rong, the head of the imperial college, was alone in a room, suffering from a fever and lying down during the day. A man and a woman entered the room, holding hands. They called out to Liu Rong, "The view outside is extremely beautiful. Why are you sitting alone here?" Liu Rong did not respond and tried to push them away. He got up and began to recite the Tianpeng spell. Just a few sentences in, the two people rushed out. Yu Gong's servant went to the central hall to fetch some soup and felt someone running quickly behind him, which made him quite nervous. He looked back at the hall lights and saw a group of white geese. When he shouted at them, they disappeared. Hu Changwen's young official passed through the middle of the hall and encountered a woman with a high bun and elaborate clothing, sitting by the railing. Her feet were not visible. When he moved closer to look, she had already vanished. The night watchman said that this ghost was seen every night.

### East Bridge Land Li Yunsheng, having passed the imperial examination as a Jinshi, was recommended by the Grand Councilor Wang Mingyuan and appointed as the magistrate of Shangyuan. He returned to Yixing to wait for a vacancy. In a dream, he saw the land of East Bridge sending someone to welcome him, saying that he should take over the position. Li Yunsheng excused himself by saying he was going to take up his official post and did not wish to hold this position. The clay figure became very angry and said, "You must first complete your term in Shangyuan." Li Yunsheng served for two years, then left office due to some matter. Finally, he was accused of corruption and exiled to Lingnan. ### Yama, the King of Hell Lin Heng, courtesy name Pingfu, considered himself to be stern and quick to punish evil throughout his life in officialdom. He once served as the governor of Xiuzhou and was over eighty years old. Summoned through recommendation, he was appointed as an attendant in the Imperial College. After which critics argued that he should not have been dismissed and returned home. He returned home and fell ill. Near death, he saw officials carrying documents coming. On the bottom of the paper was written "Yama, Lin." They asked Lin to write his name. Lin, upon waking, told his family that he had once dreamed of holding this position, kept it secret, and dared not speak of it. Now he could not avoid it. His family was worried about it. A few days later, he died. On the night of his death, more than ten monks from the Jingyan Temple in Xiuzhou dreamed together of going out the south gate to welcome Yama. The person sitting in the carriage was clearly Lin. Lin lived outside the south gate of Xiuzhou. It was the second year of the Gandaoduan era. These two stories were told by Fang Wude.

**The Wen Family Girl** In the fourth month of the third year of the Ganda era (1167 AD), a girl from the Wen family in Yongzhou reached the age of fifteen and was ready for marriage. Two nights before the wedding, she dreamed that a man in yellow robes led her to the court of an official. The judge, dressed in green robes and wearing a hat, greeted her and said, "We have finally managed to get you here. There has been a major mistake in this case. A fifteen- to sixteen-year-old lawsuit has caused much suffering. Go home now and come back tomorrow." She woke up and told her parents, who did not understand her words. The next night, she dreamed again and found herself at the base of a palace hall. A king sat on a table, and the judge presented documents to him. The king declared, "Correct it." Suddenly, someone brought a cup of soup, which she drank in the courtyard. It was extremely foul. When she woke up, she had transformed into a boy. Her parents were shocked and sent someone to inform her fiancé. The fiancé's family believed she had never been a girl and had been deceiving them. They filed a lawsuit in the state office, and the investigation confirmed the truth. The case was then closed. Her voice and demeanor were still similar to those of a girl, but she had changed into male clothing. The fiancé's family decided to marry her to their daughter. **The Deity Asking for a Curtain** In Yongzhou, there was a small temple opposite the city gate. The temple deity appeared in a dream to He, a recorder and military officer, and said, "I am merely a local land deity, not a king or prince. Whenever the county governor passes by, I must hide. This makes me very uncomfortable. I ask you to provide a curtain to shield me. As he said, the next day he dreamed of the deity thanking him. This story was told by He You, the son of the recorder.

The Official of the Southern Mountain Li Zhe, courtesy name De Cui, was from Jinan. In the early days of Jianyan, he crossed the river and settled in Jinyun. He was appointed as a professor in Taizhou. He traveled alone to take up the post. He had a good relationship with the military governor Zhao Shiyao of the state. Because the official residence was far from the school, he requested from Zhao to change the location. Zhao agreed. After moving his family to the new residence, Li Zhe slightly repaired the headquarters of the military governor. He then requested leave to return home to welcome his wife and children. He had not returned. Inside the professor's residence, there was a small tower. A person from Zhao's household went up to it. They heard the sound of galloping horses and shouting, and fearing this, they descended. Music and singing alternated, like a grand musical performance. They immediately reported this to Zhao. Zhao returned to his former residence the same day. Li Zhe also only stayed in the original residence. After a long while, he calmly told Zhao, "In my previous life, I was a recorder in the Heavenly Court. Because of a mistake, I was exiled to live among humans. And my conduct in life was also not good. Therefore, my enjoyment was not long-lasting. Within half a month, I will die from a malignant sore. I dare to trouble you with my afterlife affairs." Zhao replied in horror, "There is certainly no such thing. Do not speak foolishly." Within ten days, a tumor formed on Li Zhe's brain. In a few days, it spread and exposed the bone. Indeed, he died. Several days after his death, the family was just about to offer a meal to monks. The kitchen maid was in the room. Her behavior suddenly became different from usual. She claimed to be the professor. She sent a servant to urgently invite Zhao. Zhao arrived. The maid wept and said, "Li has died. Because of his hidden sins in life."

The reprimand was severe. It was permissible for my family to set up an offering tonight to atone for sins before heaven. Zhao immediately called upon a Taoist priest as requested. The servant, dressed in a blue robe, holding a tablet, and wearing a headband, bowed gracefully. Those outside heard of it and rushed to watch. The servant lit incense and knelt before the altar, no different from an official. After the ritual was completed, she told Zhao, "I have been freed from suffering thanks to the power of the Tao. I am still an outsider, only under a certain bridge in the city. In three days, please come to see me." Three days later, Zhao went there and saw a huge black python coiled in the earth, half-exposed. Zhao poured wine to it. Another day, the servant returned and came to Zhao, saying, "The snake's curse has been lifted. Now I am an official of the Southern Mountain. My power and experience are beyond those of a mortal official. I have asked heaven to allow the family to be moved. The remains fill the room. Only you can rely on me." Zhao reproached her, saying, "You are a scholar. How can you not know that 'there are three kinds of filial impiety, and none is greater than failing to have an heir'? You were unfortunately taken too early. Will you let the whole family be recorded in the book of the dead?" The servant did not respond. After a short while, she revived. Soon after, Zhe's wife also died. They had three young sons. Zhao took care of all the funeral arrangements. Zhe's elder brother, De Sheng, was promoted to a high position and later lived in Tian Tai. He began to take care of the orphan. Zhao's son, not named, said.

The Temple of Duke Yu of Wuyang During his tenure as the governor of Wanzhou, Feng Dangke discovered a temple dedicated to Duke Yu of Wuyang (Fan Kua). The locals revered the temple greatly. Feng believed that Duke Yu had followed Emperor Gaozu of Han into Sichuan and Shu, but soon returned to pacify the Three Qins, defeated Xiang Yu, and never ventured west again. Since Wanzhou was deep in the south and adjacent to Qianzhong, it was unlikely that Duke Yu had ever visited the area. Therefore, he concluded that the temple must be honoring evil spirits masquerading as Duke Yu, seeking blood offerings. According to law, such a temple should not be maintained, so Feng ordered it to be demolished immediately. Shortly after taking office, he encountered a tall, well-armed man in the court, dressed in armor and holding a halberd. The man had a very martial appearance and sat on the dais. Realizing this was an unusual occurrence, Feng rebuked him. The man raised his beard and angrily said, "I am Duke Yu of Wuyang of Han. I have been venerated here for a thousand years. What have I done to you that you would destroy and remove my temple? I have nowhere to return. Now I will coexist with you here." Feng questioned the man about his identity, but the man insisted he was the true Duke Yu. Feng retorted, "Even if you were the real Duke Yu, what is there to boast about?" He proceeded to criticize the man's life, but the spirit was not intimidated. Unable to counter the criticism, the spirit gradually faded away. After this, the spirit no longer appeared in physical form, but continued to trouble Feng's family. Feng's seven- or eight-year-old son was often tied up at the top of a tall tree. This continued for several months until Feng left the prefecture due to official duties, and the disturbances ceased.

Wei the Scholar In the Double Streams County of Chengdu, the Yuwen family was a prominent clan. They had established a spacious school within a temple. They invited a scholar named Wei to teach their nephews and younger brothers. On another day, there was a wedding ceremony at home, and music and performers were summoned. The performances by actors and musicians were very grand. All the students went to watch, and even several servants left. Wei remained alone in the room, his heart quite agitated. He went upstairs to look for a monk, but the monks just happened to be out at a layperson's house performing Buddhist rituals. The entire temple was quiet. He returned to the room and lit the lamp to sit. The summer night was very hot, and the windows and doors were leaking. The pines and bamboos sounded mournful, and the bright moon filled the courtyard. A woman came and went several times, and he knew it was a ghost. The outer door was still not closed, and he dared not get up. He added more oil to the lamp and often adjusted it. He lifted his hand, trembling, and accidentally touched the lamp, causing it to go out. He was too scared to bear it and quickly climbed onto the bed, pulling the curtain to hide himself. He often looked at the woman in the courtyard, who remained calm. Then he touched the curtain string and it broke, causing the curtain to fall down. The bed was empty, and there was nothing to block it. He became even more afraid and fell asleep without realizing it. When he woke up, the woman was already beside him. Wei was in a panic and had no plan. He threw the pillow at her, but the woman was disappointed, surprised, and got up, no longer going outside. She just walked around the room, laughing and crying. The rooster crowed, and she suddenly rushed out. Soon, all the monks returned, and they called out and told the reason. It was because three days ago, a family in a civilian house buried a coffin here. What they see now is probably its ghost.

Red Plum Spirit of Shu Prefecture** - **Old tradition in Shu Prefecture. There was something called the Red Plum Spirit.** - **During the reign of Emperor Gaozong of Song, Wang Xiangzhi was the prefect of Shu Prefecture. He invited Li Shi, a young and talented scholar from Zizhou, as a guest official. Li Shi, bold and eager to see the unusual, playfully composed two short poems and inscribed them on a screen to tease the Red Plum Spirit. The next day, the Red Plum Spirit responded with a poem, engaging in a poetic exchange.** - **One day, during a prefectural banquet for guests, the banquet lasted until late at night. Li Shi had already retired to bed when he saw a woman sitting on a trundle bed with her back to the bed. He was unsure who she was, suspecting that she might be an official slave sent by the magistrate to provoke him, or perhaps the concubine of the prefect who had left secretly because the master was drunk. Alternatively, she might be a ghost. He thought to himself, "One of these three must be true. It would be better to kill her. It would still be enough to establish a reputation for purity in the world." He drew his sword and rushed forward. The woman stood up and walked away. When they were a few steps apart, Li Shi chased her out the door. She suddenly leaped onto a high tree and vanished slowly. Li Shi was greatly frightened and wanted to return to his room.

Shi had weak feet and could not move. One night, a night watchman with a bell came forward. Shi returned with him. The next night, he came again. At first, in the dark, it seemed like a small round light. Gradually, one could faintly distinguish people, and it became human form. Though he dared not speak to it, whenever he closed his eyes, he would see it. His friend Zhao Zhuang Shu and a couple of others, including Kuai, joined together for the imperial examinations and came to stay with him. Shi instructed them saying, "You must call me together, do not let me sleep deeply, to avoid falling into the ghost's trap." But from then on, he could not escape. Later, when he went to Chengdu, it followed him there too. Someone advised him saying, "The Qingcheng Zhenren Temple is a place where immortals reside. You just go there, and it will not dare to come." And indeed it was so. Shi regretted his earlier playfulness, leaving it to fate. After a long time, he returned to Dongchuan, passing through Lingquan County, near Zhu Zhenren's Fengdong Mountain. As he was about to enter Jianshou Prefecture, he no longer saw it. It was after more than a year that it finally disappeared. Shi's courtesy name was Zhiji, and during the Ganda period, he served as a Lang of the Shangshu Province.

Liu Xiaowulang, a man from Deyang, Han Prefecture, had already gone to bed when he heard people outside arguing and shouting. A soldier entered and called him. Unconsciously, he followed him. When he looked back, his body was still on the bed. He realized he was dead and felt deeply sad. He saw an old woman carrying a young woman, who had a sorrowful and angry appearance. There were two large deities, who said they were the city god and the village god. They took a large mirror and showed it to him. A cold air pressed upon him, and his hair stood on end. It seemed like there was a scene of people killing and injuring each other. The two deities said, "No," indicating that this woman was killed by Liu Wulang of Nanjian Prefecture, not by him. He was Liu Xiaowulang of Han Prefecture and had no connection to this. They knew it was a mistake and came early, but they insisted on entering his door, which is why there was a dispute. They tried to stop them but they wouldn't listen. Now just return safely without fear. The woman heard these words and tears welled up in her eyes. She sighed and said, "Aimlessly searching and not finding, returning to the long night." Then they left. Liu Xiaowulang then revived.

Luo Chijiao, whose name was Yan, was from Langzhong. As a youth, he met a strange person who took him away, and upon returning, he gained some understanding. During the Xuanhe period, someone spoke about him to the court, and he was granted the title of Jingying Chushì. Zhang Weigong, the governor of Shaanxi and Sichuan, invited him to join the army. The Jin invaders attacked Rao Feng Pass, sending out their best troops repeatedly. General Wu Jie defended against them, inflicting and suffering similar casualties, yet they still held their ground and did not retreat. Zhang Weigong was concerned, but Luo said, "Minister, do not be afraid. Tomorrow, the enemy will retreat. If it is not so, I will accept the penalty to bear the guilt of misleading the army." The next day, they indeed retreated and returned, and Zhang Weigong then began to respect and marvel at him. He was repeatedly recommended to become the Master of Taihe and Chongyi. He enjoyed traveling in Hanzhou, and whenever he arrived, he was always hosted by the family of Wang Zhixing, the official. The Wang family has seen him for three generations, and he treated Zhixing and his wife with great respect, saying, "They are my former parents." In the Bingchen year of the Shaoxing era, there was a great famine in Shu. Wang Zhixing bought a concubine from among the displaced people. She was very beautiful, and Luo saw her and was startled, saying, "How can this person be in your household? If she is kept for a while longer, she will suffer great misfortune. I will help you get rid of her." He ordered to boil several pots of water, took a basket of ash from under the stove, called the concubine forward, covered her head with a cloth, and poured water over the ash. The smoke rose vigorously, and the concubine immediately fell to the ground, turning out to be a single skeleton. Luo said, "Did she come through a nunnery? Where is she now?" He said, "She is in a certain place." Luo quickly summoned her, and when she arrived, he covered the skeleton with a cloth, and it became a human form again, with her manner and appearance unchanged from before. He then handed her over to a nun and received payment. Wang Zhixing's younger brother, Zhijiu, returned home after passing the imperial examinations. Luo met him elsewhere and gave him a book.

Seal the outside. It warns, "If you return home and encounter something unsatisfactory, then open it." Upon arriving home three days later and hearing of his mother's death, he tried to open the book. It depicted an official in green robes riding a horse, followed by guests offering congratulations, and finally a coffin was carried, with those in mourning clothes following and crying. The Longhua Temple in Guangdu is the merit temple of the Yuwen clan. Luo sits with the chief monk. Suddenly, he stands up and says, "Someone from the Fang household is coming." The monk is surprised and asks where they are. Luo says, "They have entered the ancestral hall." The monk considers it strange and absurd. The next day, a messenger from the Yuwen household arrives. His wife, Lady Fang, died the previous day. Once, he went to Yangzhun Town and stayed at Chen's residence. At night, he went to the toilet and ran back, saying, "Strange things, strange things." Just now, four white-clad people climbed over the wall into the garden. Chen's household is all afraid. Luo says, "It has nothing to do with you. You will know tomorrow morning." By dawn, the gardener reports that four lambs were born. In the thirtieth year of Shaoxing, he fell ill in Yanting. He sent a message to Wenjiang, asking to be picked up by the family of Li Zhi, the censors. Li sent several servants. Luo's illness improved significantly, and he immediately set out. He warned his servants, "From here to the left, only the Jinmen road is close and easy to travel, but I don't want to go there. I wish to follow the Guanghan road, or some other route to the west. Please don't make a mistake." The servant replies, "Yes." Later, he went against his words. He reached the ancient town, and Luo was sullen and unhappy, saying, "You must be trying to kill me. I specifically told you not to come this way. It's too late now." That night, his illness returned. The ancient town is a district of Jinmen. And he died in Wenjiang. The people of Shu believed he was over a hundred and seventy years old. Scholars went to ask about his success or failure in the imperial examinations, and his responses were as accurate as if by divine inspiration. This is not recorded.

Zhao Suoshou Zhao Suoshou, whose name is unknown, was originally a scholar from Puzhou. In his youth, his parents gave him money to buy books in Chengdu. On the way, he had an encounter with someone outside the ordinary, and thus he abandoned his family to travel. By the end of the Shaoxing era, he was over a hundred years old. He enjoyed visiting the area between Peng and Han. When walking, he would tuck his two hands into his chest, which is how he got his name. People would invite him to eat, and he would finish whatever amount was given, regardless of the quantity. He would drink wine, from one cup to a hundred cups, without refusal. Sometimes he would go an entire day without eating or drinking, but he remained content and happy. Once, while drunk, he spoke freely about Wen Lügong's entry into Shu, with clear details and a complete account. On another day, he was asked again, and he replied, "I do not know." Xiao Zhongbingjun's family wrote down the true story of Zhao. A person named Fang Wei from Chengdu wrote a poem in praise of Zhao, saying: Cultivating qi is close to a technique, and discussing the Dao is close to Zen. Wearing coarse clothing and carrying jade, his joy is as natural as the sky. He can leave whenever he wants and stay whenever he wants. He keeps his hands in his sleeves, and no one can guess the reason. Zhao saw this and laughed, saying, "How can cultivating qi be called a technique? Zen and the Dao are one and the same. How can there be two? I tuck my hands into my chest, not into my sleeves." He took a pen and continued to say, "Like a donkey without a mouth, like a cow without horns. Even Manjushri and Samantabhadra cannot grasp him." He then praised himself, saying, "In the midst of the world's dust and the realm of purity, a good life for a Daoist. Without any purpose, going east and west. Sometimes half awake, sometimes half drunk. When they meet, they laugh loudly and talk freely. Not like the songs of barbarians or the noise of captives. Only those of the same path can understand. How many people in the world share the same path?" Mianzhu native Yuan Zhongju, who had been long-suffering and recently recovered, also wrote about him.

Encountering Zhao at the door, he was invited in to drink wine. The man asked, "My illness is like this. What will you do, master?" Zhao did not answer but sang a song in one stanza.
