He said, "I have a house with three rooms, columns made of eight mountains. Surrounded by four walls like the sea, the myriad phenomena are like the bracket sets. The tiles cover the blue sky without any leaks. Accumulated many years of karmic connections, practiced successfully for three years. Tamed the fire dragon and subdued the tiger, reaching immortality on land." He said this was written by Lu Dongbin. "I also have one," he added, and sang another song: "Destroy a room, good to support. Do not let wind and rain easily deceive. Find a path that is both repaired and stable. Do not let others know. Must be advantageous, rotate temporarily. Know the danger in the midst of peril. Pass through the mysterious gate and return home, then walk the cloud ladder." After singing, he drank several cups full and left without saying anything. Zhong Bing was walking with him, and slowly asked the reason. Zhao said, "The meaning of my words is visible." After ten days, Yuan indeed died. In Shifang County, it is a custom every year in the first month to celebrate the birthday of Wei Zhenren. All the Daoist practitioners gather, and Zhao also went. He stayed at the residence of Mr. Xie. The night before, he told them, "Staying at your home is not convenient. Lend me this bed, I will go somewhere." At dawn, he directly went to the small temple across the street, found a room, and sat on the bed in lotus position. People around him found it strange that he did not speak, and when they looked, he had already died. Thousands of people gathered, rushed to see, paid respect with incense and fire. After three days, cremation, and his bones were linked like a chain.

Longdao Fisherman A man from Changdao, Xingzhou, made a living by fishing. His home was north of the Jialing River, and every day he would take a small boat across to the south bank, cast his line on the rocks, and return at dusk. Even in his old age, he continued to work tirelessly. One evening, he did not return home. His family, looking from afar, saw him sitting on the rock as usual, but when they called out to him, he did not respond. They suspected he had fallen ill, and his son quickly rowed the boat to see him. Upon arrival, they found a straw cloak covering his body. It had not rained that day, and the man usually did not carry a straw cloak or hat when he went out. By the time they reached him, he was already dead. However, earthworms were all over his body, sucking and biting without stopping, as if he were wearing a soft and fluffy straw cloak. This was because he had used earthworms as bait for fishing all his life. Elder Shouyue In Yangcun Village, Hanshou County, there was a temple called the Three Saints Temple. Elder Shouyue, from Pengzhou, had studied under a master in White Deer Mountain of the state. After his death, his disciples in the mountains dreamed of him. He said, "I have entrusted my body to another kind and am now in a certain family's home at the foot of the mountain. You should come to see me." Upon waking, the disciples cried. The next day, they went to visit and found a dog with a body that was entirely black, except for a patch of white fur under its belly, clearly forming the two characters "Shouyue." They then bought the dog and brought it back.

A Taoist Immortal In the northern part of the city of Chengdu, there lived a family named Li. Once, a beggar came to their door. He was dirty and repulsive to look at. They gave him money, but he wouldn't leave. They scolded and chased him away, and he disappeared without a trace. The Li family was surprised and couldn't guess who he might be. Three days later, a different Taoist priest arrived. He looked at the family and said, "Your family's appearance has suddenly changed. There must have been a divine being passing by." The family replied, "No, there wasn't." The Taoist priest pointed to the left door and said, "This is the image of the Immortal Zhu from Lingquan." The family then carefully examined it and saw the face and attire clearly. The Taoist priest said, "The Immortal came, and you didn't recognize him. Isn't that fate? I can add some painting to it. I will help you, so that the people of the city can admire it." He then took out pigments from his bag and started painting. In a short while, he finished. The painting showed a man with a beautiful beard and long eyebrows, radiant and lustrous, as if he were a person from the realm of immortals. The Li family was delighted and called their wife and children to bow and worship him a hundred times. The Taoist priest said, "There is still one place that is not finished. I am only at the Tianqing Guan across the street. For now, I will go back. I will come again in the evening." He left without bowing. After the time passed, he did not come. The family asked about him, and it turned out that he had never existed. The Li family regretted not having met him. They displayed the painting in the temple. Zhang Zhongding, a high-ranking official who served as the governor of the region, built a small hall to honor it.

聂从志，是仪州华亭人，是一位高明的医生。有一次，县丞的妻子李氏病危，聂从志治好了她的病。李氏不仅美丽而且放荡，她仰慕聂从志的容貌。某天，县丞前往邻近的郡县，李氏谎称自己生病，派人邀请聂从志来家中。等到聂从志到来时，李氏对他说：“我差点儿就死了，全靠您救了我。但世间没有什么东西足以报答您的恩德。我愿意以我的身体来侍奉您。”聂从志感到惊恐害怕，只是用委婉的话拒绝了她。李氏流泪坚持请求，言辞中充满了哀伤。聂从志不敢回应，急忙离开了。他直接回到了家中，即使再次被邀请也不再前往。到了晚上，李氏打扮得花枝招展，敲门来找聂从志。她抓住他的手说：“你一定要跟我走。”聂从志割断了自己的袖子，坚决地离开了。从此以后，他也没有再向任何人提起这件事。一年多后，仪州的推官黄靖国生病了，被阴间的官吏带到阴间作证。即将返回时，一个官吏请他稍作停留，说有事要见他。他们走到河边，看到狱吏抓住一个女人，用刀剖开她的肚子，拉出她的肠子清洗。旁边有一位僧人说道：“这是你的同事某人的妻子。她想与医生聂从志私通。聂从志拒绝了她，面对美色而不动心，可以说是一位正人君子。因此，他的寿命只有六十岁，这是因为他做了这件阴德之事。”

In ancient times, decrees were issued to extend for twelve years, and each generation was granted an official position to one descendant. Women's lifespans were reduced by the same number of years that Nie had added. The reason for cleansing the intestines was to eliminate lust. Jingguo, who had always been close friends with Nie, secretly went to ask him after he had recovered. Nie was surprised and said, "When we were speaking privately, no one heard. But on the night of the sudden arrival, I was alone in the room. This is known only to this woman and me. How could you have heard?" Jingguo fully informed him. Thus, the story spread among the people. It was during the early years of the Xining era. Wang Minzhong recorded this story in his *Record of Encouraging Virtue*. The details are very detailed, but there are some minor differences. Moreover, there is no mention of Nie's name or the surname Li. After Nie's death, one of his sons passed the imperial examination. His grandson was named Tuna. During the reign of Emperor Gaozong of Song, he served as the county magistrate of Luoyang in Han Prefecture. Xianjing Yudi was commissioned to write a poem about hidden virtue, consisting of hundreds of words, to reveal hidden virtue. The poem reads: The eight realms of the vast void originally had no distinction. In the midst of it, a hermit always has a clear view. Asking what kind of person this hermit is, his name was recorded as Nie and his surname as Li. In the spring in full bloom, peach and plum blossoms are in competition. Fragrance enters the curtain and wakes people from sleep. Graceful steps over water cross through the busy world. Jasmine flowers with a heart convey secret intentions. The hermit remains unchanged like clear water. Seeing through the young appearance, turning around to the man, resisting abandonment. Gazing with worry at the moonlight, shedding tears in vain. Shyly changing appearance, spring's myriad charms.

As for me, my mind was initially at rest. The world at large was unaware of this. Therefore, there are records in the books of the underworld. There was an official from Yi State named Lin Ying. At night, a yellow-robed official appeared in the fields, holding a faint ink in a state of haste. Outside the door, a host of dark soldiers stood in a strange and unusual formation. At the break of dawn, the grand hall loomed imposingly. The Jade Belted Lord, a deity of high authority, stood with a stern demeanor. I bowed twice and called out to the official, who then stepped forward. A clerk at the desk held a multi-layered paper, on which several lines were written about a virtuous deed. Nie, a man, prevented a woman named Li from engaging in immoral behavior at night. From the beginning, his heart was free from impure thoughts. Despite the myriad distractions, his mind remained calm. In a room, he sat quietly, his mind focused. Before his eyes, wild flowers and petals scattered chaotically. His resolve was firm; he did not consider the trinkets of love. He did not permit the union of joy with the fire of the sun. By preventing the immoral act, he caused a mysterious and hidden effect. The woman's youth was extended by a decade. As he left, he asked a purple-robed old man. The punishment in the afterlife was no different from that in the world. In the books of the underworld, there is a classification of souls. Disloyalty and filial impiety are punished by the gods. What is the consequence of taking life? It is like the fleeting life of a mayfly. The courtesan, walking in the garden, was pitifully disciplined with a whip. At the edge of Fang Gong's lake, in the autumn scenery, a person named Sun bowed and knelt before someone. He bowed his head and recounted the events to me. I wished to record these events in a detailed manner. I heard that the south and north departments are close in distance. The gods and the earth are intimately aware of everything. Not only does a miraculous medicine penetrate the five organs, but the consequences of actions also extend far and wide.

In the hidden depths, surely there are ghosts who can speak. In secret places, beware that the walls have ears. Though the conduct of scholars may seem minor, the movement of thought and the observation of the heart are truly profound. I know well the records of the heavenly calendar of Wu Shen, which document all the injustices of the world. The neighbors to the east and west know nothing of it, yet the ghosts and spirits know every detail. Obstacles may be like walls, yet they can become void; only this heart is hard to conceal. How is it that within this heart there are both light and darkness? The highest virtue communicates with the divine, following one principle. The Daoist has eyes without redness or disease, discerning the truth and falsehood of the world. Zhao Hua has passed, Ma Yuan is dead; in the county, there are those with hidden virtue like gentlemen. Alas, we scholars suffer through cycles of rebirth, chasing color, sound, and taste. Among them, the greedy and the wicked are especially secretive and cunning, collecting and carrying things into the mouths of the greedy. The children Dōu and Ài mock together, laughing at officials who constantly dream of filth. Though it is said to be in darkness and skillfully avoided, even the servants are ashamed of them—how could they not feel shame? Alas, the treacherous and cunning Wang Ji Gong, how can he face Qi Rui with such a face? I love the people of old, especially those who were simple and noble, who had few desires, were pure and sincere, and had a noble spirit. They were open and calm, living only in truth; could there be any worldly burdens left in their hearts? They understood life and death, the seen and unseen, without expectation; this heart silently aligns with the divine. Wang Chen descended from the hall with embroidered robes, Li Yue kept the precious pearl in his robe. If the nine original places could be reborn, I would return with you, folding my knees and contemplating the profound and secluded beauty. Yú Gōng’s poetry is rather strange and obscure; some parts may not be understood. This scroll is all said by Huáng Zhòng Bǐng.

The Records of the Strange, Volume 31, Story 31 The Bandit Ghost of Yellow Flower A man from Chengdu named Yang Qi, courtesy name Cheng Weng, traveled with his fellow townsman Ren Gao during the Zhenhe era. They set out for the capital to take the provincial examination. After leaving the Scattered Pass, they entered the dangerous region of Yellow Flower Right Boundary. This area was notorious for banditry, so they dared not slow their pace. Both the horse and the rider were exhausted, and it was the height of summer, making them weary and drowsy. They stopped at a temple by the roadside for a brief rest. The long corridor was quiet and empty, and they did not encounter a single monk. The two travelers took a short nap on the hall. Yang had not yet fallen asleep when a young boy dressed in green, about two feet tall, with a pale and black complexion, appeared from outside. He held a single sheet of white paper and approached Yang, intending to cover his face. However, it seemed as if someone was restraining him, preventing him from moving forward. The boy stood back, sighed, and after a while, covered his face with tears and left. Yang felt it was an omen of ill fortune, shed tears, and mourned to himself. He also dared not speak of it to anyone. That night, they stopped at an inn in a village. As they were about to lie down, the boy appeared again and went directly to Ren Gao's side. He covered Ren Gao's face with the paper he was carrying. The boy then danced and leaped with a triumphant air. Ren Gao was unaware of what had happened. The next day, after traveling thirty miles, they came upon a clear stream. The two men went to wash their feet. After finishing, Yang climbed back up first. Ren Gao, still enjoying the feeling of cleanliness, hesitated to leave. Suddenly, he let out a loud cry. Yang turned around to see that Ren Gao had already been taken away by a tiger. It was then that Yang realized the boy he had seen was likely a bandit ghost. That year, Yang passed the imperial examination.

The Venerable Nujuna In the Zhongyan Mountain of Qingshen County, Meizhou Prefecture, there stands the Venerable Nujuna's place of worship. Below the mountain are three stone shoots, towering and standing tall. Pilgrims who fast and abstain before staying overnight often witness visions of magnificent canopies and radiant light as auspicious signs. Once, the traveler Lin'an Song Sisi passed through this place and encountered a monk ahead of him. The monk was heavily drunk and unrestrained, hanging three new shoots on his staff. It was midday, and the heat was intense, which the traveler found extremely unpleasant. However, he did not speak to the monk. The monk turned back and scolded, saying, "I do not drink alcohol. How can you break a precept and defame me?" Song was angry but remained silent, restraining himself due to the monk's drunkenness. The monk then said, "I know you are the government official Song Sisi. You have a slight connection to purity, which is why you have come here." Suddenly, Song realized that the monk must be carrying three shoots, and wondered if he was a manifestation of the Venerable Nujuna. He got out of his carriage to show respect, but saw nothing.

Li Buiwei Li Buiwei, a native of Dongzhou, entered Sichuan during the Jianyan era. Later, he became the magistrate of Jiangyuan, Shuzhou. He befriended a local man named Hu Sheng. Hu Sheng's concubine was the daughter of the governor of Sichuan's transport commissioner. She had been captured by enemy forces and later married Hu. Li Buiwei often mocked her, even writing short poems to mock her. Eventually, Hu Sheng could not bear it anymore and gathered evidence of Li Buiwei's public misconduct. He spread malicious words and sold the information to the transport commissioner. A friend of his, Zhang Jun, who happened to be a subordinate official, confirmed that it was true. The matter was reported to Meizhou, and the prefect appointed Recorder and Judicial Officer Yan Min to handle the case. Yan Min arrested more than ten local officials and imprisoned them, determined to find evidence of embezzlement. Li Buiwei was known for his integrity in office, and there were no faults to point out. However, they found that he had once bought an iron kettle for a price of seven hundred and fifty coins and claimed that it was sold at a loss. Yan Min believed he was innocent and found it difficult to arrest him. The prefect, fearing the transport commissioner, did not follow Yan Min's advice and immediately sent officials to arrest Li Buiwei. Li Buiwei could not bear the anger and committed suicide by cutting his throat. A month after his death, the officials in charge of the case and the prefect died one after another. The transport commissioner and Hu Sheng also died. Yan Min was dismissed from his post and transferred to Chengdu. Passing through Shuangliu County, he stayed overnight in a civilian's house outside the city walls. In the middle of the night, he heard someone knocking on the bedroom door. He asked who it was, and the voice replied, "It is Li Buiwei." He asked again.

弼违回答说：“我的名字叫李弼违。你不记得了吗？虽然你不开门，但我自己能从缝隙中过去。”说完，他已经站在床前。忞非常害怕，转过脸对着墙躺下。弼违说：“你不想见我，是因为我脖子上有脏东西。我现在自己遮住它。”他立刻解开腰间的布条，缠绕在脖子上。忞无可奈何地坐了起来。弼违说：“我之前的冤屈已经昭雪，没有什么遗憾了。但是牵连的人很多，没有你的证词是不行的。你本来就知道我，现在我的命运即将结束，所以敢麻烦你再来一次。还有很多人没有到，天符在这里。你可以看一看。”他拿出手中的文书给忞看，文书像是浅绿色的黄纸，上面都是人的姓名，墨色浓淡不一。弼违指着说：“这卷中都是将要死去的人。墨色最浓的离死期很近，最淡的也不超过十年。泄露天机的原因，是想让你传给人间，知道幽冥中有鬼神，像这样可以相信而不怀疑。”说完作揖告别离去。忞大概能记住所写的文字。后来，那个人生病了，预先告诉家人他一定会去世，果然如此。这是根据所见验证的。忞不久也去世了。

Fèi Dàoshū, also known as Dàoshū, was from Guangdu. In the year 1120 during the reign of Emperor Huizong of Song, he traveled to the capital, Bianjing. As he approached the area near the capital, he stayed at an inn by Yanzhipo. When he unloaded his burden, the sun was setting behind the mountains. The innkeeper's wife, with a charming smile, leaned against the door and greeted him with a smile, speaking words of kindness. In the middle of the night, she came alone to him and said, "I secretly admire your demeanor. I wish to share a brief moment of joy with you. Can you allow it?" Fèi Dàoshū was startled and asked, "Who are you? How did you come to be here?" She replied, "My father is a merchant in the capital. Our family is from a certain neighborhood. I was married to this innkeeper. My husband has passed away, and I am poor and have no means to return home. I cannot bear to stay alone. I risk shame to approach you." Fèi Dàoshū said, "I do not wish to violate proper etiquette. I understand your situation. I will visit your father and arrange for someone to bring you back. Do not be resentful." The woman left in shame and embarrassment. Fèi Dàoshū arrived in the capital and, on another day, passed through the neighborhood where the innkeeper lived. He found the merchant's house and introduced himself, hoping to meet the man. The merchant asked, "Who are you? How do you know me?" Fèi Dàoshū replied, "I am from Sichuan, Fèi Dàoshū. I passed through the capital recently and met your daughter. I have something to ask of you, hence my visit." The merchant hurriedly came out to greet him and said, "That night, I had a dream where a god told me my daughter would lose her virtue to someone. If not for meeting the talented Mr. Fèi, she would have been lost. Your name and character are indeed true. I wish to hear your story." Fèi Dàoshū told him everything. The merchant wept and bowed in gratitude, saying, "The god said you would become a noble person. It must be true. I will remember the dream and send my eldest son to bring her back and arrange a new marriage." The following year, Fèi Dàoshū passed the imperial examinations and reached the rank of Grand Master, serving as the governor of Badong.

杨希仲 杨希仲，字季达，是蜀州新津人。在未考中进士之前，他曾在成都某家做馆客。这家主人的小妾年轻且放荡，常到学舍调戏客人，想要与他亲近。杨希仲严肃地拒绝了她，她便离开了。杨希仲的妻子在乡下，当晚梦见有人告诉她：“你的丈夫独自在外地，能够自我约束，不欺骗暗室，神明都知晓，应当让他成为众多学子中的佼佼者以作回报。”妻子醒来后，不知这是何意。到了年末，丈夫回家，才说起此事。第二年，全蜀地区举行科举考试，杨希仲考中了第一名。 张四郎 在卬州南十里处的白鹤山上有张四郎的祠堂，相传他是神仙一类的人物。山下的碑刻非常古老，字迹难以辨认。当地人说，张四郎设立此碑是为了抵御邪灵，拯救疾病和瘟疫。后来有人能够辨认出碑上的字，便可以学习成仙。青城的唐耜担任卬州的太守，喜欢游历此地，希望能有所遇合。他常常站在碑下仔细摩挲阅读，忽然认出一个字，说道：“难道不是这个字吗？”旁边有人应道：“是的。”唐耜厌恶他插话，呵斥他离开。不久，唐耜又后悔了，但已经不见那人了。他曾经外出游玩，遇到一个道士站在路边，开玩笑地喊道：“大人，请接受我赠送的一面土镜。”他命令随从取来，却发现是一块普通的土块。唐耜以为这是对他的侮辱，打算带回去。仔细看那块土，果然发出微微的光芒。他开始怀疑这是个异人，但很快那人也不见了。唐氏家族至今仍珍视这块土。唐耜，字益大，官至秘阁修撰。

Monk Changluohan In the city of Jia, there was a monk named Changluohan, an extraordinary man. He often encouraged people to set up vegetarian feasts in honor of Arhats, and thus earned this name. An elderly woman surnamed Yang had a strong liking for chicken. Throughout her life, she had killed countless chickens, perhaps thousands or even tens of thousands. After her death, her family prepared a series of seven vegetarian feasts and conducted a Taoist ritual involving the Yellow Register. The Taoist priest was just about to offer a written document to a deity when the monk suddenly arrived and told her son, "I will make amends for you." The Yang family was very pleased and invited the monk in. The monk instructed his servant to go to the house next to the east courtyard and buy a rooster and hen. As he said, they were obtained. The monk ordered the chicken to be killed and prepared for the meal. The Yang family’s son wept and pleaded, "Respected master, you have come. I do not mean to show any affection for the chicken. Today is the day for the Taoist ritual, and our entire household has been abstaining from meat for a long time. Please give it to a neighbor." The monk refused and insisted on cooking and eating the chicken. Once it was cooked, the monk sat down in the hall, cut the meat and filled the plate, dividing it and placing it in the nine positions of the highest deities, then ate the remainder. After the feast, the monk left without bowing. That night, the chicken seller and the Yang family all dreamed that the old woman came and said, "Due to the sins I committed in life, I was rebuked and transformed into a chicken. Thanks to Monk Changluohan’s repentance and offering, I am now liberated." From then on, the people of the county performed Buddhist rituals to recommend the deceased, hoping for his arrival, believing that he would help in the afterlife. Monk Changluohan died at the end of the Shaoxing era, but his physical body still exists today.

On Qingcheng Mountain in Yongkang, every year on the 15th day of the second lunar month, a Taoist gathering is held. People from all directions come together, and the large families, the Zhangs and Tangs, take turns hosting it. Once the attendees have gathered, the temple gates are closed and remain so until the fasting ends. One day during the fasting period, a Taoist monk knocked on the door wanting to enter. The gatekeeper stopped him, but the monk kept shouting and cursing. The gatekeeper went to inform the Zhang family member, who was concerned that the monk would disturb the crowd and firmly refused to let him in. The monk was unhappy and went down the mountain to a tea shop where he stayed for a while. He asked for a pen and wrote on the wall, then took off his straw raincoat and hung it on the wall. He told the owner, saying, "Watch over this for me. I will come back soon." He had not left for long when the straw raincoat started spinning like a wheel, rotating around the wall. Those who saw it were amazed and ran to tell the people in the temple. They all lifted the straw raincoat to see it and found a poem. The poem said: "I happened to ride with the Green Emperor out of the Fairyland of Penglai. With spears and swords, I roamed the nine heavens. Green shoes and yellow hats, none of them recognized me. For me, leave this straw raincoat so it won't sink and be buried." The people only looked at each other, feeling regret, but it was too late.

Yang Wangcai, courtesy name Xi Li, was from Jiangyuan, Shuzhou (modern-day Chengdu, Sichuan). From a young age, his vision was already unusual. Once, he borrowed money from a fellow student and predicted the number of items in the student's basket. When the student opened the basket, the prediction was accurate. As he grew older, he became known for his skills. People in Sichuan called him "Yang who draws horses," referring to his ability to predict fortunes. His appearance was ugly and bizarre, with eyes that resembled those of a ghost. The things he said were extremely strange or extraordinary. One day, a large tree in front of his house suddenly fell during the midday hours of the following day, causing a commotion in the street, but the surrounding houses remained undamaged. Initially, his methods were somewhat like those of a sorcerer or a liar. He would carry silk to the market and sell it, sometimes claiming it was three zhang long, sometimes four zhang. The owner would verify the length and pay him accordingly. He would then leave.

A fortune of a few feet of cloth. Sometimes, he would ride a donkey to visit people, and then feign an excuse to leave temporarily. He would tie the donkey in the courtyard. He went for a long time without returning. The donkey also made no sound. Upon seeing it, it was found to be made of cut paper. Someone went to the prefecture to report the supernatural, saying that during each ritual sacrifice, six seats were set up, with the two eastern seats left empty. The Yang couple faced each other, and a monk and a Taoist sat below. Heresy deceiving the masses, according to the law, they should be executed. Because of this, they were arrested and sent to prison. The jailers always feared and respected them, dared not put shackles on them, and also feared they might escape. Yang knew their intention and told them, "Do not fear me." I will be punished again, the number is already determined. I will accept it with a smile. The things I did the other day, the law does not forgive, it is the result of magical deeds. After enduring these two calamities, then one becomes a Daoist. The magistrate Yang Chen decided the case at night. Yang said, "Did my respected uncle send a message?" It is very regrettable.

Chen remained silent. When he went out of the door, people from Chengdu came, reporting the death of his uncle. On another day, someone told Chen, "Next year, there will be good news in your family. Four people whose names are linked with the character '望' will pass the examination." Chen had a daughter who was sixteen or seventeen years old. She suddenly fell ill, and tried many doctors without effect. Then, someone else told him, "Your daughter has been ill for a long time. Doctor Chen used a certain medicine, and Doctor Li used a certain medicine, but none of them were correct. This is only a snake haunting the back garden's plain tree. Quickly discard the medicine. Wait until I receive the beating. To treat it with a talisman. The daughter will be safe. Do not worry." Chen returned home and told his wife, half believing and half doubting, because he often saw small snakes crawling around the tree. And what he said about changing doctors and medicines, all of it was not false. After Yang received the beating and returned, he wrote a talisman and left it for Chen, instructing him to hang it on the tree. The daughter immediately recovered. The following year, Chen's cousins and brothers took the examination, and indeed, four people were selected, named Congwang.

A certain Yang, who lived in the regions of Minwang, Songwang, and Taiwang, first married a courtesan as his wife. One day, he summoned two individuals who wielded official, large sticks to his residence. He gave them three thousand coins and ordered them to use the official sticks to beat himself and his wife, each twenty times. Both were too frightened to refuse and left. When the case was finalized, he and his wife both received the punishment, as he had wished to ward off. And the ones who carried the sticks were exactly the two he had summoned. Later, he came to Chengdu, where his door was like a market. Scholars asked about their fortunes, and he answered promptly. Sometimes he composed a poem, or a poem of dozens of rhymes, or a long song with a preface, writing freely and completing it at once. Each time he took an exam, he would first compose a poem to show others. The words were secret and could not be understood. When the results were announced, the names of the top performers were inevitably reflected in his poem. Or the entire list of over a hundred people, he had already written them down and sealed them, with many missing strokes or half-characters, forming incomplete characters.

The hierarchy is complete, and nothing is out of place. The Sichuan Military Governorate sought documents from thirty years ago but could not find them. They informed Yang, who said, "They are in a certain room, a certain chest, the nth bundle." Following his instructions, they found them. A man named Shi Chen from Meishan visited Yang's home, where locals were gathered. A new horse had been acquired, a black body with a white nose. Yang said, "Give me this horse, and you will suffer misfortune." The guest angrily replied, "Mr. Yang, you rely on your skills to take my horse. I paid a hundred thousand coins for it, and it hasn't been a month. Can you really force me to give it up?" Yang said, "I want to save you from this misfortune, but if you do not believe me, it is fate." He added, "Next May 20th, the official will report the matter. Remember it carefully. Do not look at its feed. Take good care of the left side. After this day, perhaps we can meet again." The guest became even more angry and refused to listen, forgetting Yang's words. On that day next year, he personally fed the horse, and suddenly, the horse ran and leaped.

Kicking her left rib, she died immediately. Guan Shouqing's elder grandson was a professor in Guozhou. He wrote a letter to inquire about good or bad omens from his colleagues. When I arrived, Yang Zai, who was in the room, told his wife to prepare a meal to entertain the servant of the professor. The meal was ready, and I had just reached the door when he came out to greet me and asked, "Why did you come for someone else instead of asking about the matter?" I was startled and bowed, but I did not know why. I frequently interacted secretly with a wealthy family's son in Huayang. Once, I borrowed twenty thousand coins from him, but he was stingy and did not give it to me. One night, I spent the night in an outer room and heard the sound of knocking on the door. She said, "I am the daughter of the eastern house. My husband, drunk, sent me away. It's not possible to go far away at night. Please allow me to stay." The wealthy son joyfully invited her in and slept with her. Worried that her parents would find out, before dawn, he called her to wake up. She did not respond. Only the sound of blood filling the tent could be heard. Lighting a lamp to see, the woman's body was cut into three parts, and blood flowed everywhere, as if she had just been executed.

The rich man was terrified and almost died. He thought to himself that a strange disaster had occurred. Without Yang, he could not be saved. He rushed to Yang's house and knocked down the door to report an emergency. Yang said, "We have been friends for a long time, and in times of need, we should share it together. The other day when I asked for a loan, you refused to give it to me. Now that you are in trouble, why do you ask me for help?" The rich man wept and took responsibility, saying, "If it is indeed true that you can help me, this is easy, there is no need to worry. Take my token back, place it in the room, quickly close the door, and do not tell anyone." The rich man thanked him, saying, "If I indeed received help, I would repay with a million." Yang said, "There is no need for such a large amount. Just give me the two thousand coins I need." The rich man then took the token back and returned home, trembling all night. At dawn, he secretly entered the room and found no body. The bed was clean as if nothing had happened. He was overjoyed. The next day, he brought the two thousand coins and wine and food to Yang's house. Yang said, "My home is too crowded and narrow for drinking. Why don't we go out to the countryside instead?"

He proceeded to visit a wine shop, where he was seated and began to drink. He looked at the woman tending the wine shop and found her strikingly similar to the one he had encountered the previous night, except her complexion, which was pale and yellow, was different. The woman also frequently glanced at him, seeming to have some doubt. He called out to ask her, and she replied, "Two days ago, I dreamed that someone summoned me to a place. A young man kept me company all night until I woke up. My body felt very unwell, and blood flowed like a stream, nearly two dou before stopping. Now I still feel weak and short of breath. I have never felt this illness before. I began to realize that it was caused by her soul." Grand Councillor Yu was summoned back from Jing and Xiang, and Zi Gongliang sent a letter inquiring about his future. Yang replied, "If I get Su, I won't get Su. After half a month, I will become a deputy signatory." Grand Councillor Yu thought that the title of deputy signatory without the "tong" prefix had been used for a long time. After that, he will govern Suzhou. After fifteen days in office, he was summoned to be a deputy signatory of the Military Council. At that time, Qian Chuhe was already a deputy signatory, so the "tong" prefix was added. There are many such cases, too numerous to recount. Wang Kongmu Wang Sheng, an official in charge of the registry in Chengdu, lived outside the Great Peace Gate. Every day at the fifth drum, he would hurry to the government office, always reciting a spell of great virtue and invocation once. As he approached the gate, he often encountered a woman drawing water. This continued for a long time. One day, he had a troubled mind and stopped reciting the spell. Suddenly, the woman appeared before him and said, "Every morning as I pass by here, my master must rise early, as if he is paying respect to someone. Therefore, I dare not delay in drawing water. But today, is there something you are neglecting?" Wang Sheng was startled and left, not understanding her words. Later, as he returned home and passed by the temple of the river god, his heart was moved, and he hurriedly entered to pay his respects. There, he saw a mural of a woman holding a water vessel, which was the same one he had seen before.

Tang the Eighth Tang the Eighth was originally the son of the Zhao family from Qingcheng. His father was called Zhao Lao. They lived at the foot of a mountain and enjoyed meeting Daoist practitioners. When Tang was around ten years old, it seemed as though he had encountered something unusual. His family lost him, and after two months, he was found on a boulder at the back of the mountain. They took him back home. From then on, he spoke wildly and impulsively. Once, he climbed to the top of a tree and shouted, "Water is about to come to the city of Qingcheng." The next day, there was a great fire in the county. He also once rubbed a large tree and sighed by its side. When asked, he said, "This will be my father's coffin." After some time, Zhao Lao indeed died. Later, Tang told others, "Zhang Tianshi is at the Well of Immortals. I will go to visit him." He left home and traveled to the Well of Immortals. Every night, while sleeping, white vapor covered his body like the moon, and everyone could see it. In the county, there was a man named Yan Yancai, who was old. He believed that his life's course was similar to that of Chancellor He Wenzhen and that he would surely become the top scholar among many. In the year of Gengwu during the Shaoxing era, he went to take the imperial examination. After he left, Tang visited his family, took all their utensils, and placed them upside down. He said, "The scholar will go out, and the top scholar will return. This is something to celebrate!" The whole family was delighted. However, Yan Yancai, while taking the examination, mistakenly marked something on his white robe, which was exposed by the eunuchs, and he was dismissed and sent back.

Due to the grace of promotion to the top rank, he was specially listed at the bottom of the top five ranks. He then understood the intention of being placed upside down. A dozen or so scholars, who were about to take the imperial examinations, came to visit Tang. Tang said, "None of you can compare to Yu Ren. Yu Ren is the one who is Yu Yu." That year, Yu Yu was exempted from taking the exam, and all the scholars failed. Yuan Xian Dao and his family made a cake with pickled meat, ate it, and left four pieces. That evening, Tang came to ask for food. Xian Dao said, "Just now there was nothing, but it can serve as your offering." Tang laughed and said, "There are still four pieces of meat cake, why be stingy?" All that was seen was similar to this. In the early years of the Longxing era, a villager from Chengdu pulled a cart into the market, met a Taoist, and left behind two thousand jiaozi. The Taoist gave him a letter and said, "Ask you to deliver it to Tang Ba Lang in Xianjing." The villager took the letter and left immediately. A slightly cleverer companion exclaimed, "I heard that Ba Lang is a strange person. Is there not a strange medicine or secret book in the letter?" They opened it to see, and it was just white paper. They quickly sealed it again, and just arrived at Xianjing. Tang met and scolded, saying, "Why didn't you return my letter?" The villager prostrated twice and apologized. Tang held the letter and read it repeatedly, and said with a sigh, "It delayed me by twenty-four years." He left in a bad mood, and still exists to this day. This scroll is all said by Yuan Xingzong and Xian Dao.

The Strange Tales of Yi Jian, Volume Bing, Section 44 The Baker's Daoist During the time of a Daoist assembly in Qingcheng, thousands of people gathered. Local residents often built temporary shelters near the mountain to sell tea and fruits. A baker who sold pies obtained a shop. On the first day of opening the shop, a drunken guest arrived at his place. The guest spoke drunkenly without restraint, lying naked on the left side of the door. The baker was greatly troubled by him. Giving him money, he refused. Offering him a cake, he did not accept. Previously, a strong wind had broken a large tree, and the residents split it into two benches, placing them right next to the door to wait for passersby. The shop was far from the river. Just when he was fetching two containers of water, before he could use them, the guest suddenly stood up, tied a bundle of straw, and dipped it in water. He washed the wood with a scraping sound, doing so for more than two hours, then lay on top of it again. Those who saw him pass by all hated him. As soon as they reached the door, they turned back. The bakery could not sell all day long. The guest also left, apologizing to the owner, saying, "Do not be angry with me. I will bring money tomorrow to pay you. The sales will double." And he left. Some people went to the bench and wanted to sit. They saw a bright and dazzling light and noticed the four characters "Mr. Lü has come" written in thick ink. Taking a knife to scrape it off, the more they scraped, the clearer it became, penetrating deep into the wood, as if it were one piece. The spectators were like a wall. From then on, the cakes and fruits sold very well. It was the second month of the thirty-second year of the Shaoxing era. Guan Shouqing personally saw him washing the wood, and he said it was a thin and slender Daoist.

In the Qingcheng Mountains, which are thirty li away, there is a Lady Magu Cave. It is said that this is also where Lady Magu practiced spiritual cultivation. Kou Zilong, a Taoist priest at the Zhenren Pavilion, went alone to pay homage. On the way, he encountered several village women who were carrying radishes from the mountain. They relaxed their loads and pulled up their skirts, preparing to wash vegetables at the clear spring by the roadside. Seeing Kou Zilong, they asked, "Where is the master going?" He replied, "I am going to visit Lady Magu." One woman laughed and said, "Lady Magu is not in the mountain today. There is no need to go." She took a radish and gave it to Kou Zilong, saying, "You can eat this." After eating it, he continued on his journey. He thought to himself, "They are all rustic and foolish women. How could they know who Lady Magu is? Is this not a joke on me?" Suddenly, he was enlightened. He turned back to look, but saw nothing. From then on, his spirit was clear and his qi was complete. He remained healthy in old age, never suffering from illness. Whenever he performed rituals for others, he called himself the Minister of Fire. He lived over a hundred years and died during the Longxing era.

Old Marsh of Qingcheng Eighty li outside Qingcheng County lies the Village of the Old Man. Locals call it Old Marsh. It is said that the person who did not eat salt or dairy and lived over a hundred years, as recorded in Su Shi's collected works, is none other than this place. Usually, no one visits there. Guan Shouqing and several friends, about seven or eight in total, intended to go there for a visit in late spring. They had not yet reached twenty li when the sun was approaching evening. Birds were chirping and monkeys were crying, creating a bleak and desolate atmosphere. Those traveling together looked at each other, as if their mundane thoughts were swept away. They walked slowly with canes, proceeding step by step. After a while, the mountain moon began to appear, and the fragrance of flowers filled the air. They looked closely and saw that all over the mountain were peonies. It was nearly the second watch of the night when they finally found a simple household. The old man had not yet gone to sleep. Seeing the guests arrive, he gladly invited them in. He spread out a mat made of reeds and they sat down. The guests apologized, saying, "We are unexpected guests in the middle of the night. The cooks and servants are still far away, and we have nothing to eat. We hope to borrow a meal from you. We will pay for it tomorrow." The old man replied, "I am fortunate that you do not despise me for offering coarse food. How could I dare to discuss the price?"

In a short while, a bowl of wheat porridge and a basin of vegetable soup were set out. Bowls were arranged around the table, and the guests were invited to eat together. The old man sat alone in the center of the bed. Soon, a steamed item resembling a small child was placed before him. None of the guests dared to take a bite, except for Shouqing, who broke off a small piece. The old man said, "I have stored this taste for sixty years, preparing for old age. Today, I am honored by distinguished guests and dare not be stingy, yet you all ignore it. Why is that?" Shouqing took the entire item and said, "This is ginseng found under pine roots." The next day, the old man led them to a nearby house, where everyone was happy and shared more food and drink. He said, "This place has no taxes or rents. I have dug mountains to create ridges, just enough for sowing, to provide for the needs of the year. The county officials never come here, and sometimes for years there are no human traces. Why do you scholars visit me?" They stayed for three days before being sent off the mountain. All those seen there were hundreds of people, and even the younger ones had long white eyebrows and hair, with no young children or grandchildren in sight. Later, I did not have time to go again. These three things are all related to what Shouqing said.

孙鬼脑 眉山人孙斯文，是文懿公抃的曾孙。他生来就有着美丽的容貌。曾经去拜访成都的灵显王庙。看到庙中夫人塑像端庄美丽，心中十分羡慕。自言自语道：“若能娶到这样的妻子，真是幸福啊。”当天晚上回到家，梦见有人用锯子锯掉他的头，又另外给他安上了一个头。醒来后摸自己的脸，发现面貌大变，感到非常惊恐。于是取来蜡烛照镜子，叫妻子来看，妻子看到后惊恐万分，当场死去。绍兴二十八年，孙斯文来到临安。我在景灵行香处多次见到他，他的样子丑陋吓人，脸庞极大，眼睛深陷，鼻子高挺，嘴唇厚实，舌头宽大，头发杂乱如蝎子。每次吃饭时，他都会伸出舌头卷起食物，咀嚼的声音如同风雨声。看起来就像一个突然出现的泥塑判官。画家们画下了他的形象，在市场上出售以供人取笑。孙斯文对此事深感羞耻，不愿意提起。有人问他时，他总是说：“这是我的相貌造成的。”杨公完全认识他未换头时的样子，说：“现在的样子与以前大不相同。”四川人称他为孙鬼脑。

The Son of the Prefect of Langzhou The son of the prefect of Langzhou was often sent by his father to carry goods to the market. Once, he carried a ceremonial tablet to the home of a wealthy citizen. The citizen questioned him, saying, "This tablet comes from my house. How did you get it?" The minor official truthfully replied. The citizen then searched his box and found the tablet missing, proving that the official was a thief. He was arrested and taken to court. At that time, several households in the same prefecture had been robbed, and many valuables had been lost. A reward was offered to track down and capture the thieves, but no one could find them. When they heard about this matter, all submitted petitions to the government office. The officials interrogated him, and his response was as before. The prefect, Han Jun, told the deputy prefect about the case. The deputy prefect suspected his son in his heart and secretly entered his study room. He saw clothes, utensils, and luxury items displayed, none of which belonged to him. Greatly startled, he called out and asked his son. The son admitted to being a thief. The father became greatly angry and said, "Unfortunate for me to have a son who commits petty theft as a crime. This is the greatest disgrace in the world. How can it be worse than this?" He ordered his son to be arrested, bound, and taken to the government office. The son showed no sign of fear.

The official lured him with flattering words, saying, "I share a professional relationship with your father. I will help you secure your position. But first, return the lost items to their owners. I will definitely not pursue this matter further." He then sent a military officer to supervise him to his room and take everything he had hidden. The son detailed which items belonged to which families, and each was returned to its rightful owner. However, only one pair of leather socks remained, with no owner identified. The son bowed twice and humbly requested, "I wish to receive it as a gift." The official asked what he would use it for. The son replied, "Recently, when I climbed the city wall, I saw this item at the base of the wall. I tried wearing it, and it felt as if I was walking on air. From then on, I could enter homes without anyone noticing, even during the day." The official became even more skeptical and returned the socks, challenging him to prove it. The son happily put them on, and as soon as he did, he leaped up to the roof, moving without any hindrance. He flew away like the wind and was eventually lost. My wife's nephew Zhang Yin, who served as the magistrate of Lingui, heard this from Wang Kun, the magistrate of Lingchuan. Wang Kun said this was a recent event, but he did not wish to reveal their names, and it is not verified.

**Poem of Luzhou** Since the calamity of Li Qiong, Luzhou has suffered greatly—some of the dead have become malevolent spirits. The commander and governor died one after another from illness. Zhang Jinyan of Leye composed a poem of a thousand words, urging the people of the state to build a temple to honor them. The people of Luzhou followed his advice, and the county seat was finally at peace. His poem reads: Amid the flat lake to the south of the city, The long Huai River winds around the west. How mighty is the form of the golden basin! The Wu people built the city of Hefei. Cao Cao once looked back with wolfish suspicion—this was his land. The Later Qin dynasty fell into chaos here. The six palanquins of the emperor halted in Wu’s capital. Heavy troops were stationed at the frontier. In the year DingSi of the Shaoxing era, A scholar took command of military affairs. Li Qiong seized his men and rebelled, Crossing the river to join the false Qi. In the sudden chaos of forced submission, The sword was raised against them. The officials in office at the time Did not waver in their integrity when faced with danger. The *Shangshu* (Minister) gave his life for the state. He died with his own body, having sacrificed himself. His words of cursing the traitor were sorrowful and resolute. The sound of his throat being crushed was a low, choking cry. Alas, Zhao, the magistrate! His loyal blood splattered upon the roadside. Zhao Kangguo, the governor of Luzhou, Qiao Zhang was a great general. His gate still rests upon the steps of the hall. Even now, his remaining troops still speak of him, And all weep and cry when his name is spoken. Commander Qiao Zhongfu, Zhang Jing, who refused to follow the rebellion, Was killed in the state capital. By law, he should have been granted a posthumous title. The historical records should preserve this clear precedent. By law, a temple should be built for him, That his spirit may be honored and rest in peace in the Huai region. How can it be that later generations Have so carelessly forgotten us? The official buildings stand grand and empty, Yet the spirit does not protect the humble thatched roof. The wronged spirit and the soul Wander restlessly, with no place to rely. Therefore, within the governor’s residence, Ghostly things are strange and unusual. In moonlight beneath the court’s hall and eaves, It seems as if someone is peering. The sound of breathing is heard, And the clothes and bearing of a person seem to appear. The scholars and common people grow weaker day by day, And officials suffer from misfortune. In twelve years, eight commanders were removed. Five times were there funerals, and three times mourning for a wife. Zhang Zongyan, the military governor, Lost both his husband and wife.

During the reign of Emperor Ling, Chen, a high-ranking official in the imperial government, and Li, a junior official, both died. Han, a high-ranking official, and Bao, an official in the Left Department, also passed away. Du, an official who inspected local administration, and Wu, a high-ranking official, both lost their wives. Zhang, a high-ranking official, and his wife, as well as his concubine, all died, leaving their bodies covered in sores, their skin painful to the touch. A mad villager took control of the main hall, speaking as if he were the wife, cursing the household and expressing a desire to take a black horse and a young concubine. Soon after, everyone died. Chen, known for his upright character, and Li, known for his literary talent, both had grand plans to build a city, but their ambitions were thwarted, and they ultimately passed away. Du, while in office, was beaten by a ghost during the night. He drew his sword and drove the ghost away, but then left the door, saying, "Respectable Second, you have good fortune." Shortly after, his wife died, and he was left to mourn. Du, while in office, was beaten by a ghost during the night. He drew his sword and drove the ghost away, but then left the door, saying, "Respectable Second, you have good fortune." Shortly after, his wife died, and he was left to mourn. Bao, known as De Zhang, was transferred to Jiahe, where he fell ill and became weak. During the scorching summer, he wrapped himself in a woolen cap around his chin and jaw, but his remaining days were numbered, as his spirit had already departed. Wang, a high-ranking official, was ill and requested to be transferred to Jiahe, where he died. He was known for his teachings on governance and his ability to soothe the people. During his time in the east, his policies were simple and beneficial to the people. After his death, the entire community mourned. Han, known as Shi Shuo, and Wu, a high-ranking official, both died. Their funeral carriages were sent out, and the guards fell in front of their residences. Their chariots were destroyed, and their horses were covered in old cloth, marking a tragic end to their lives.

The people on the road heard it and were startled. The whole family was shocked and nearly stunned. When Wu Shang’s wife’s mourning carriage was about to depart, a tea and wine officer died together with a horse. In ancient times, there was a governor of Ye who avoided using the surname Weichi. He died in a national crisis during the Northern Zhou dynasty, yet his loyal and valiant spirit had not yet received a shrine. By the time of the Kaiyuan era of the Tang dynasty, many magistrates faced great dangers; many were exiled and died in office, grieving even before they arrived. How benevolent was Zhang Jiayou! Upon taking office, he discerned the root of the matter. He established a solemn temple and strictly observed the rites of worship. After completing his term, he was transferred to the capital. His brothers held high office, and the Jinwu Guards shone with their prestige. Wu Jing succeeded him in governance, and the spirit was then adorned with a crown and ceremonial robe. From that time on, the governor had no misfortune. History indeed confirms this. Bo You once held power in Zheng, indulging in extravagance and revelry. He fled, only to cause further chaos, and died in disgrace in the marketplace. His vengeful spirit became a source of harm, killing people as if plucking them from the air. Later, a virtuous man named Liangzhi was appointed, and sacrifices were made by the descendants of the family. His punishment was self-inflicted; fortune and misfortune can still be changed. A large family has strong support; could Zi Chan have deceived us? There are five kinds of malaria caused by cold and heat, and there is the mythical one-legged creature夔, who limps with a single foot. These may bring illness or supernatural harm. Prayers are made and roosters are sacrificed to appease them. How much more so should we offer reverence to righteous and loyal heroes, whose rank and position are not lowly. They are solemn and full of life; how can one doubt that they have become spirits? If even a ladle of water is not poured to the ground, how can one dare to hope for a pot and a horse’s hoof? If not even a single tile covers the roof, how can one dare to expect carved beams and inscribed rafters? The lord of the state entrusts the people and the land. To whom is this responsibility entrusted? The past cannot be blamed, but the future is still within reach. If we follow the example of Bao Zheng or the Earth God, we may build a modest house of several rooms, with its wealth or poverty depending on public or private means. In the city, there is an abandoned shrine to the Earth God and the former residence of Bao Zheng, both of which are dry and elevated, suitable for use as a shrine. Paintings and statues may be arranged, and incense and offerings made according to the seasons. The position of the Shangshu is of great prestige.

The main hall may yet be used. Lady Lü, in loyalty to her husband, followed him in burial. A virtuous wife strictly observed the inner chamber. There was one who brought back the silk used to bind the hair of the Shangshu Lü, returning it to Wu. Lady Wu took it and committed suicide to accompany him in death. The virtuous and wise are arranged in the two side halls, the later and earlier ones ranked in degrees of mourning. Those who shared the same trials in that time—their features cannot be forgotten. Zhang, Chen, Li, Bao, and Han—inevitably followed one another. De Zhang fell ill and departed; whether to leave or remain depended on the moment. The ritual vessels were arranged in solemn elegance, the swords and ornaments gleaming in varied brilliance. On the day the construction was completed, the sacrificial rites were performed to purify the state administration. Green script was offered to the Supreme Deity, and the imperial scroll and incantations were presented to the gods to inform them. As if to say, things have different inclinations—how can humans and spirits dwell together? Here, a new dwelling was chosen by divination, and two bows were made to welcome him back. The sorrowful flute sounded desolate, the wind-driven carriage moving unevenly. The vast sky too took on a different hue, and the roads were all desolate and sorrowful. The grand temple of literary and martial virtues stood unshaken for a thousand years. The magistrate enjoyed peace and stability, the high hall filled with joy and harmony. Kindness and benevolent policies were spread throughout, good fortune bringing abundant joy. Thus, the imperial decree in purple clay was issued, and he entered to serve in the white jade steps. The people gained future blessings, the harvests protected through prayers. Thumping drums at night, joyful offerings of sacrificial animals in the morning—on which both humans and spirits relied. In times of peace, all things were free of blemish. The Son of Heaven in the era of revival was sage, the great officials relying upon him. Moral virtue reached both the hidden and the visible, gentle winds spreading to both Han and barbarian lands. The regulations and institutions shone with literary governance, clearly like the sun and stars hanging down. No minister failed to respond, the regular sacrifices maintained and brightened. The four ears of the ruler were unblocked, all state affairs carefully consulted. Admonish you, the officials of Huai West—why wait if you do not ask? The open letter conveyed the central meaning, how dare we delay in implementing it? The Grand Master of Rites determined the temple's name, the golden scroll bearing a splendid title. Especially inscribed to honor those who died for their principles, large characters carved on a grand stele. On the back of the stele lay a firm stone, upon which was engraved my poem from Lu Zhou.

Monk Zhao Monk Zongyin, originally a scholar from Shaanxi, surnamed Zhao, abandoned the secular life to become a monk. During the Jingkang period, he resided in Chang'an, living in a large temple. He was fond of discussing worldly matters, and his words were as sharp as clouds. When the Jin invaders attacked the capital, Fan Qianshu, the Left Chancellor, commanded Jingzhao and controlled the five routes of army. Upon seeing Monk Zongyin, he was greatly pleased and invited him to return to wearing Confucian attire. Monk Zongyin immediately went to visit the Temple of Mount Hua and spoke of his intention to save the world with his body. He then followed Fan Gong. Fan appointed him to an official position due to his convenience. Despite the difficulties, Monk Zongyin made significant contributions and was promoted to the position of Director of the Dragon-Graph Pavilion. Later, he became a member of the Pacification Commission of Sichuan and Shaanxi, also leading several thousand soldiers. When speaking to guests, he often spoke grandly, saying that he paid attention to Buddhism, acquired great eloquence, and was among the ancient Buddhas, equal to the pure and clear Vimalakirti. He was knowledgeable about both ancient and modern times, experienced in official business, and was truly the best among all the civil officials in the world. He was adept at anticipating and adapting to enemies, making strategic decisions on the battlefield.

Among all military officials in the world, he is the first. If there is unrest in many regions, with smoke and dust still rising, he would be the most notorious criminal or rebel. One dare not be overly modest. The guests fear his words, and no one dares to respond. He is harsh in his criticism of others, often speaking with malice, which is due to his innate nature. After achieving his ambitions, he ordained five hundred monks, all of whom were named "Zongyin," making them stand in for himself. At that time, he was over sixty years old, and he did not remarry, only buying twenty concubines. After retiring from military service and living in leisure, after several years, he fell ill with his internal organs continuously leaking. The concubines abandoned him and did not look after him. Zhao took his own excrement and ate it. Someone who saw this and found it strange asked, "How could you know this taste?" After ten days, he died. Those who understood it believed it was a retribution for his speech. When Xi Daguang was the governor of Hedong, he once shared his efforts. When he was the governor of Hunan, he fed a thousand monks to gain merit. Although Zhao was prominent and influential, people still called him "Monk Zhao." The Residence of the Jing Family In Dazhou, outside the Yangtze River, there is a large residence of the Jing family. Next to it stands an ancient tomb. Ghostly apparitions frequently appear there, causing those living there to be unsettled. They moved into the city to avoid it. My wife's family rented it to live in when they moved to Sichuan. Her mother-in-law's younger brother, Zongzheng, slept outdoors in the summer night. After the third watch of the night, he saw a large hairy creature approaching with a fierce look. He raised his hand to pat its neck. Zongzheng suddenly jumped up and shouted at it fiercely, "Do you not see the Big Dipper above? How dare you do this?" The creature responded by retreating, and he slept peacefully until dawn.

Purple Qi of Shuzhou** In the third year of the Chongning era, a man from Chengdu named Ling Kan went to the capital to report that a resident of Ruiying Township in Xinxin County, Shuzhou, named Cheng Yu, whose name offended the taboo of Emperor Guangzong. His family had buried his parents, and there was often a purple aura and firelight on the mountain where they were interred. The emperor issued an edict to the local prefecture, ordering them to quickly relocate the graves to another place. He also commanded that the grave site be dug up to form a pool, and within a three-mile radius of the mountain, burial would no longer be permitted. The prefecture was ordered to inspect the area every quarter by appointing officials from the county. The following year, the emperor decreed that the place, which often displayed such auspicious sights, should be designated as a site for honoring the divine and cultivating merit. A Taoist temple was then built and named Yinfu. The emperor granted ten acres of land and ordained two children as novices annually. Two years later, Emperor Guangzong, the Taishang Emperor, was born, initially being enfeoffed as the Duke of Shu. Ultimately, the former princely domain was elevated to the rank of Commander of Chongqing Army, thus fulfilling the prophecy of the purple aura and firelight. Cheng's son's name happened to be the same as the emperor's taboo name, clearly demonstrating the will of heaven. **Strange Phenomenon of the Chazhi Family's Pies** The Chazhi family of Jingnan has lived for generations in Shatou. They had a daughter who, from a young age, loved to eat pies. Whenever she ate, she would only chew the parts with sugar and sesame, discarding the pastry crust. This was a common behavior among children. In the second year of the Ganda era, the daughter was fourteen years old. One day, while walking in the courtyard, it suddenly started raining. Something carried her into the air, and a thunderbolt struck her, causing her to fall to the ground and die. It rained pastry crusts from the sky, and it stopped after some time. Dogs seized and ate the pastry crusts, which were no different from the real ones. As told by Zhu Zhiyuan.

The Concubine of the Magistrate of Xiaoxi A scholar from Shu (Sichuan), unnamed, traveled east on a convoy. He left Chengdu and anchored his boat at the Jiangdu Temple. Before dawn, he entered the temple to pay respects. He saw a woman already inside the main hall and suspected she was a ghost. He was very frightened. After calming down slightly, he leaned against the door and peeked inside. The woman burned incense and hurriedly knelt down, crying and praying: "I am originally from the capital. I lost my father at a young age and followed my mother to enter Sichuan. I married a man from Chengdu named Mr. So-and-so. Now for seven years, I have had two children, a boy and a girl. My husband went to serve as the magistrate of Xiaoxi in Sichuan last year. He did not bring his family along and also did not send any letters. Recently, I heard that he broke the promise and remarried. I am poor and alone, it is hard to stay here long. I looked around and found myself completely alone. There are no relatives to rely on. I thought about it day and night. There is only one way out, death. I hope the great king will witness my heart. I immediately used a razor to commit suicide and fell to the ground." The scholar was surprised and frightened and feared that this dark deed would bring trouble upon himself. He hurriedly boarded the boat and left Xiaoxi. The magistrate mentioned was actually a fellow villager. He met him at the riverside pavilion and casually talked about his family affairs. The magistrate said, "I recently bought a concubine to keep at home. I haven't had time to take her yet." The scholar briefly described the woman's appearance and actions. The magistrate was shocked and said, "That's all true. How did you know this?" The scholar then told what he saw. The magistrate looked startled and lowered his head without speaking. Soon after, he left. He called for a servant to bring hot water, but could no longer hold a cup. He said, "As soon as you finished speaking, this person was right beside me. I cannot escape this." Then he climbed into the carriage and returned. He died upon reaching the county seat. This was the first year of the Gandaodao era. It is also according to Huang Zhongbing's account. It is said that the scholar from the convoy wanted to investigate further but could not.

In the waters of Yingzhou, there was a persistent problem with an old turtle that kept appearing and disappearing, causing harm to the riverbanks and boats. The local government offered a reward of a hundred thousand units of currency to anyone who could kill the turtle. A fisherman came forward to accept the challenge. He asked for only one thing: a ferry boat, two people to row it, a large jar, a piece of pig liver, and some iron hooks and chains. On the designated day, he boarded the boat and made a hole in the bottom of the jar. He hung the pig liver inside with the hooks and set the trap. He then moved the boat downstream. After some time, the turtle came out to eat the liver and swallowed the hooks. Its head could not retract, and it became very angry. It extended its neck out of the jar, trying to attack the boat but being hindered by the jar. Unable to move forward or backward, the fisherman hit the turtle's head with a stick. The turtle sank with a sound, and the fisherman then released the rope to follow it. He let the turtle move where it would, estimating that it was already exhausted. He pulled the hooks again and hit the turtle three or four times. The turtle died, and the fisherman then rowed the boat to the shore. The people of the country gathered like a wall, happy that the threat had been removed, and competed to give money to the fisherman. It turned out that the turtle loved pig liver, and the fisherman knew this. He also had the skill to manipulate the situation, making the task very easy. This is what Zhong Bing said.

Peach Source Stone Inscription In the fourth month of the third year of the Jianyan era, a great flood occurred in the Peach Source Cave of Dingzhou. A large stone was carried downstream. The stone bore writing that resembled heavenly script, and the characters were clear and distinct. It was possible to read thirty-two characters, which read: "Non-action is the great way. Heaven knows human feelings. Non-action in the profound darkness. The gods see human forms. Thoughts, words, and intentions. Ghosts hear human voices. Breaking prohibitions and being filled with sin. The earth receives human souls. Though these words are brief, they serve as a warning to the world." Scallion Yellow Chicken Eggs Zhang Jun, the Hui Gong, resided in the capital. He attended a guest's meal and was served a dish of scallion yellow chicken eggs. The official did not wish to eat them, but the host insisted. Reluctantly, he ate three of them, and his mood turned foul. He left the banquet before it ended. That night, he suddenly experienced unbearable foot pain. Holding a candle to inspect, he saw three chickens pecking at his feet—one rooster and two hens. A divine being in golden armor stood by his side. The deity asked, "Will you make a vow?" Zhang replied, "I vow to abstain from eating chicken eggs for the rest of my life." The deity said, "The vow is light." Zhang then said, "If I do not break this vow, my mother will live an infinite life. Breaking it would be a sign of filial impiety." The divine being nodded in approval. In an instant, both the chickens and the divine being disappeared. By dawn, the place where the chickens had pecked was still red and swollen, about an inch thick. From then on, he no longer ate chicken eggs.

*Yijian Bing Zhi* Volume 53, Story 53 **Li Mingwei** Li Mingwei, a Taoist priest, was from Fuzhou. He was renowned for his strict adherence to the Taoist precepts and his elevated spiritual status. He was skilled in offering petitions and incantations on behalf of others, and the results of his rituals were often strikingly evident. In the fifth year of the Shaoxing era, the governor of Jianzhou, Yuan Fuyi, sent him along with the Taoist priest Ye from Tianqing Temple to perform a joint ritual. After the ritual concluded, Li Mingwei told Ye, "Just as we offered the petition, I reached the Three Heavens and saw the Taoist priest Zhang from this prefecture. He was also offering a green ritual text for someone, but the envelope was sealed very carelessly and was already damaged. The Celestial Master said, 'This cannot be presented to the divine, so it was thrown away.' Ye replied, "Zhang is a fellow Taoist in the temple, but I don't know which household he is at tonight." The next day, Zhang returned from outside. Ye asked him where he had gone. Zhang said, "I was performing a ritual at Ye's house twenty miles away. The villagers' home was unfamiliar, and the paper for the green ritual text was not good at all. When it was time to burn and present the text, the altar tilted, and the text fell to the ground. I quickly used my hand tablet to catch it, and it was not severely damaged. However, my Taoist robe was burned. Ye then told Zhang what Li Mingwei had seen. Zhang was very frightened and immediately prepared a ritual sacrifice to atone for his sins."

Goulou Posthouse During the Xuanhe period, a new official arrived as the military commander of the Goulou prefecture, but his predecessor had not yet left, so he temporarily stayed at the posthouse. Before sunset, while gathered in the dining hall, a white vapor rose from a well in the courtyard, thick like fog. Soon, a woman dressed in green emerged from the well, ascended the steps, and surveyed those seated. Her hair was dense and bushy, and her eyes were bright enough to reflect. She then entered a small room on the western side, climbed the wall, and disappeared. The entire household was terrified, and they dared not sleep that night. After the second drumbeat at midnight, the doors and windows opened inexplicably, and figures entered from outside in a disorderly manner. No one suspected anything unusual. Upon closer inspection, the faces and attire of these figures were indistinguishable from those of the family, but the family members saw figures resembling the military commander. People came and went in a confused manner, unsure whether they were human or ghosts. Though they had swords, they feared injuring someone and did not dare to strike. The disturbances ceased only at dawn. The elderly and children were terrified and dazed, and they moved out on the same day. A month later, Chen Ruxi from Jinyun arrived as the deputy governor of the prefecture. While renovating the official residence, he also temporarily stayed at the posthouse. The military commander recounted the previous events to him, but Chen did not believe them. Three days later, all the maids had nightmares. Some saw very large figures that did not speak, and others were carried from their beds to the floor by ghostly figures. The disturbances ceased only at dawn, but there were no other unusual occurrences.

A Poor Scholar Named Ye Yi In the second year of the Shaoxing era (1132), two officials from Xiaoshan County, Quzhou Prefecture, named Pan Fu and Lu Qiu Guan, were staying in the same temple. A poor scholar named Ye Yi, whose mother was elderly and his family was impoverished, came to rely on them. During the day, he ate at the officials' homes, and at night, he slept in a monk's quarters. At that time, there was a palm reader from Sanqu named Cai Sheng, who could read hand lines and predict fortunes and misfortunes. When he examined Ye Yi's hands, he was shocked and said, "Your hand lines are very poor. According to the method, you should either kill someone or be killed by someone. This will happen within the next three days. Do not go out recklessly; you might not escape this fate." Ye Yi was usually timid and cowardly, and he was currently a temporary guest, not yet familiar with the people around him. He assumed there must not be such a thing and temporarily agreed, saying, "Yes." Three days later, at dusk, the two officials invited Ye Yi to drink with them. He returned drunk in the middle of the night. The monk in the same room had already gone to sleep. A thief was outside and followed him in. When the thief opened a chest, it made a sound. The monk woke up, sneaked up, and was about to take a stick to hit the thief. Just as he encountered the thief, the thief wounded the monk with a knife. The monk shouted loudly and ran away. Ye Yi was deeply asleep and heard the shout, suddenly waking up. The thief happened to be right in front of him.

A man named Ye was holding the sleeve of a thief. The thief, fearing he could not escape, grabbed his arm and said, "Let me go. Otherwise, I will kill you." Ye, being very drunk, held onto him even tighter. Fearing that others would arrive, the thief then stabbed him and fled. Ye died immediately. The two magistrates heard of this and feared they would be held responsible for it. They tried to trace and capture the thief but failed. They saw Ye enter the monk's room from under the eaves, covering his abdomen, and no one on the left or right saw it. There was a village witch in the area who could communicate with spirits. They sent her to ask about it. Just as she reached the door, the witch's manner and speech were like Ye's when he was alive. She cried bitterly and said, "On my behalf, thank the two magistrates. Because of my past karma, I died unfortunately. Now I have caught the villain. In a few more days, I will be captured. I have no regrets. Only think of my old and poor mother. The money I have in my bag, about a hundred thousand. Hope to burn my bones. Collect my remains and the remaining money for my mother. Then, both alive and dead, I will be grateful." The magistrates followed all the instructions. Five days later, the thief was indeed caught. The thief said the next day after killing Ye, he saw Ye hundreds of steps away. Then gradually got closer. Yesterday, he slept and woke up with him. Knowing that he would surely be defeated.

A Villager Named Xiao Ling In Qingtian, there lived a villager named Xiao Ling. His wife was in her twenties, foolish and ugly. She was possessed by a spirit that allowed her to speak with people, but only her husband could see the spirit's form. They used large paper to write all over it, but the writing could not form words. They pasted the paper on the inner wall of her room and still set up a table with an incense burner, like people who worship gods and Buddhas. They burned incense more than ten times a day, using sandalwood, camphor, or other fragrant woods. No one knew where the spirit came from. A wealthy man named Xu Mian, who was usually strong, heard of the matter and decided to verify it personally. Just as he reached the door, the spirit spoke from the air, saying, "Good guest, come. Can you set up tea?" Xu Mian was already startled. After sitting down, he asked the villager, "Have you heard that your family has a ghost? Why don't you let it come out to see me?" Before the words were finished, an object fell on his back and was very heavy, then he fell to the ground. Looking at it, he saw it was the upper part of a tea mill. His back did not feel pain. Xu Mian was terrified and left. The spirit chased and sprinkled feces at him. There was a traveler who was good at reciting the filthy trace mantra, which could drive away ghosts and spirits. Xu Mian invited him to the villager's house, but before he could perform the ritual, a large scythe for cutting grass flew and danced down from the air, swung and whirled around, like a person holding a blade, almost touching his clothes. He quickly fled and barely escaped. Later, the spirit stole small things to benefit his family. A shepherd boy in the mountains once saw it, which looked like a child aged twelve or thirteen, all over his body were yellow hair. He suspected it to be a monkey or ape. It still exists to this day.

**青田小胥** During the Jianyan period, there was a low-ranking official named Mr. Chen in Qingtian County. One day, while on duty, three of his colleagues secretly left, but Mr. Chen, who was usually cautious and fearful, remained alone in the office. The next morning, the door did not open, and the chief clerk knocked on the door and called out to him repeatedly, but Mr. Chen did not respond. The clerk then reported this to County Magistrate Chen Yanchai. The magistrate broke through the wall to enter the office and found that Mr. Chen's clothes, bedding, headgear, and shoes were all present, but he was nowhere to be seen. The windows and walls were as neat and tight as usual, and no one could understand what had happened. Mr. Chen's father wept day and night, and they searched the hills and rivers extensively. Just then, a man named Zengcheng, a high-ranking official, happened to be passing through Yongjia and Qingtian. He pitied the old father who had lost his son and prayed on his behalf. Zengcheng ordered the preparation of a written statement and filed a complaint with the Exorcism Bureau. In the judgment, it was ordered that the true officials of the land and domain should appear the next day at noon to find out the whereabouts of Mr. Chen. If it was due to evil spirits causing harm to living people, they were to be arrested and brought to the authorities for investigation. The rest would be handled according to the laws of the Qing Dynasty. Additionally, a picture of a jade maiden was drawn and burned at the City God Temple. The next day, five miles from the county, at a place called Xiapu, fishermen were just reeling in their nets when suddenly the pond water boiled, and the sound was like a thunderclap. They quickly moored their boats on the side of the shore to avoid the disturbance. In a moment, something rose over ten feet and then fell back, and the water also became calm. When they looked at it calmly, they saw that it was the body of Mr. Chen. It was still hot in autumn, and he had been dead for ten days, but his face looked as if he were alive. No one could understand what kind of evil spirit it was, and no one could explain how his body could have come out of the house.

A Long-Lived Cow In the first year of the Shaoxing era, the imperial entourage was stationed in Kuaiji. At that time, most affairs were just beginning, and there was an imperial decree strictly prohibiting private slaughter of cattle. However, the guards often violated this prohibition. One day, a water buffalo charged with a spade blade, rushing into the city from the side of Yu's temple. Those who saw it were startled and retreated. The city guards, fearing it would trample and cause destruction, wanted to stop it. It nearly killed them by its impact.
