At that time, the government office was temporarily located in the Dashi Temple. The cow slowly entered through the three gates, passing through the west corridor. A horse was tied under the corridor. When it saw the cow coming, it kicked it with its hooves. The cow angrily butted its belly, causing it to split open, and its intestines hung from its horns. Its anger grew even more, and it chased anyone it met, directly going to the courtyard. The prefect Chen Ruxi was handling affairs. Upon seeing him, the cow slowed down and lowered its head, bellowing mournfully, and then lay down at the bottom of the steps. Chen ordered strong soldiers to remove the blade and apply medicine. The cow remained motionless. He also announced a reward for capturing the butcher and ordered it to be taken to the Yuantong Temple to be made a long-lived cow. It was immediately tied up and taken away, and it was no different from a normal cow. It lived for several years before dying.

The Tortoise Pursues the Person** - **Chen Di, a magistrate in the Department of Criminal Affairs, loved turtles from a young age.** He lived in Qingtian Mountain City, where it was hard to obtain turtles. Whenever he got them, he ate them, never having any thoughts about it. - **In the year 1162 of the Song Dynasty, he dreamed of a busy street.** He saw over twenty turtles emerging from the water, moving very quickly, and about to bite him. He ran back quickly, reaching the door, and the turtles followed closely behind. Then he rushed to the hall, and they pursued him more urgently, becoming very distressed. He jumped onto the dining table, and the turtles scrambled up the four legs of the table. Chen Di was greatly frightened and told them, "I never had the intention to eat you. Why are you pursuing me?" He scolded them and woke up. - **The next morning when opening the door, a village servant brought a letter from his friend Liu Yuanzhong, with a bamboo basket containing twenty-eight turtles.** He opened and looked inside, finding them exactly like what he saw in his dream. - **At that time, Liu Yuanzhong had just hired a new servant who was good at catching turtles.** The servant walked into the water with bare hands, observed the sand and stones, and knew where the turtles were hiding. He caught dozens every day, and as a result, his relatives and friends also benefited. - **Chen Di released the turtles into the stream and from then on, he no longer ate them.

A Fish Dish from Jinyun Flies In Jinyun County, the streams and ravines are shallow and sluggish. Ordinarily, there are no large fish. Once, a fisher caught a giant carp. Finding it unusual, he presented it to the county. The county magistrate, who was just engaged in governance, doubled the compensation for its value and handed it over to the cook to prepare the minced fish. He invited local officials to a banquet to enjoy together. Several rounds of wine were served, and stringed and wind instruments were in the lineup. The minced fish arrived but had not yet been eaten when suddenly the fog made the day dark. A thunderstorm suddenly arrived. The minced fish threads on the plate danced and jumped out. A strong wind passed through the roof ridge, and tiles fell like a collapse. Midsummer was chilly, and guests' hair all stood up. A fireball, like a large basketwork basket, flew and gathered between the tables. Guests rushed to avoid inside the bookcase, and the fire also followed in. Giants alternately came and went in the lightning. After several moments, the rain stopped, but inside the house was still dark. Holding a candle, they looked at the magistrate, who and two courtesans had already fallen to the ground. For a long time, they then revived. Guests and servants' clothes and skirts were many burned. Rivers overflowed, and after ten days began to calm down. Those who know believed it was of the kind of dragons and chimeras.

**The Temple of the West** Hou Hanchen of Yongjia had lived for generations in the West. Suddenly, he was troubled by a malevolent spirit. At first, it threw sand and struck stones; the stones it struck passed through the corridor and into the sleeping chamber, making a clear, ringing sound, yet the walls and doorways suffered no damage. After some time, the spirit began to speak from the air, and would sometimes place filth in the family’s food and drink vessels. Even when they bought ready-made meals, the same thing happened. The family found this very troubling. One day, their young daughter, who had just begun to wear two braids, saw a man in white clothes holding a pair of scissors and coming forward. He called out, “Little lady, give me the horn on your head.” The children cried in fear, and one of her braids was already lost. Hanchen arrived from outside, took the girl onto his knees, and was just about to weep and recount the incident when the spirit called again, “That man has come again to cut my braid!” He quickly shielded her head, but the other braid was also lost. He then summoned Taoist priests and shamans, who used every possible method to ward off the spirit, but none worked. They planned to move away to escape it, but all their furniture and belongings were stuck fast to the ground. Even the lightest items could not be lifted with effort, and they could not leave. In this way, they endured for nearly two years. One day, Hanchen drank heavily at a relative’s house and returned very drunk. When he reached the entrance of his alley, he saw a small temple and suspected it to be the source of the haunting. Drunk, he went to a neighbor’s house to borrow a large axe, then smashed the clay figures and the incense altar and other items inside the temple, locked the door with a padlock, and from then on, the strange occurrences ceased.

Meng Bingjun, the county magistrate of Yongjia, had a daughter named Shiqin. She was exceptionally intelligent. She died before her coming-of-age ceremony. Her mother, Feng, was inconsolable. One night, she dreamed that her daughter was sitting in the courtyard, playing with gambling tools. She remembered that her daughter had died and called out to her, saying, "Since your death, I have not stopped thinking about you. How can you be here?" The daughter replied, "There is no need to remember me. I have been reborn as a boy." She took some dice and showed them to her mother, saying, "This is the game of yèzǐgé. Gamblers play it by placing two dice facing each other, and this is where I was born." She then said, "Another time, go to the rice shop near Huangtu Mountain to visit me. That family will also become officials." After speaking, she woke up. She told Meng about the dream. Meng lived in Anxi Village and did not know where Huangtu Mountain was. Someone said it was a small market outside the southern city gate, about five li from the city. Meng went there and found a rice shop. The neighbors were scholars. He asked them, and they said it was the home of Scholar Yeyu. The details matched the dream. Meng presented his visiting card and paid a formal visit. He met Yeyu's family and said, "A few days ago, we had a son. The date matches the night of Feng's dream." He told them everything and asked to see the child. The child's eyebrows and eyes resembled those of the girl, and Meng had a joyful expression. Yeyu named the child Yi. The following year, he indeed passed the imperial examinations. When Yi was over ten years old, he still visited the Meng family occasionally and often called Feng "Auntie from Anxi."

Jiang Anshi Jiang Anshi was a man from Lanxi. He was fond of Taoist teachings and received his talisman from Master Zhang Jingying at Longhu Mountain. He also received the Taoist teachings from Master Huang Bimei at Nanyue. His residence was called Yuantan Village, and he built a small room beside his hall as a place for worship and service. One day, after the rain had just cleared up, several rays of multicolored light appeared under the paving stones, emerging directly between the eaves. Some were as large as rafters, others as small as bamboo, and no one knew where they came from. He suspected there was a hidden treasure and ordered his servant to dig it up. After digging over a meter, nothing was seen, so he filled it back in. However, the light reappeared as before. He tried to deal with it using rituals, but it was still ineffective. Master Huang came to his house and performed a ritual of the Yellow Talisman, burying a golden dragon under the paving stones. The light finally disappeared. One morning, he entered the Taoist room to burn incense and saw a stone in front of the incense table. All around it was covered with green moss, and the stone was still wet, as if it had just come from the stream. Jiang Anshi usually only used two young boys to sweep and clean, and others were not allowed to enter. He thought the boys were playing a trick, but the stone was very heavy, not something the two boys could lift. He did not ask further and just ordered them to carry it to the pond outside. The next day, it was back to normal, so he moved it again to a large pond three miles away.

Jiang shut the room. The next day, he personally opened the door, and the stone was there again. He silently prayed to the gods, wrote a talisman on it, and threw it into the stream. The following day, the stone was gone. Jiang was very happy, believing that the talisman's power had protected him from strange occurrences. As he was eating with guests, something struck the tiles on the roof, making a loud noise and falling into the courtyard. Upon inspection, it turned out to be the stone he had seen before, with the talisman still on it. A message was written beside it: "This talisman is not quite correct." Jiang looked at the back of the stone and found another talisman, which was slightly different from the one he had written. Realizing he could do nothing, he placed the stone back in the room. After some time, he found a small red paper on the table, which said, "You have no way to deal with me. Why not draw the forms of my brothers? I will help you perform rituals." Jiang prayed, "What kind of god are you? How many brothers do you have? What do they look like? If you can really help me perform rituals, please clearly inform me." Another piece of paper appeared, stating, "I am a three-spirit official." It provided a detailed description of its appearance and attire. Jiang had no choice but to draw the images and place them beside the altar. His family also encountered them frequently, leading to a sudden decline in his livelihood. Two years later, Jiang died, and the strange occurrences also ceased.

The Case of the Zhu Family in Lanxi The Zhu family of Lanxi was a wealthy household. Their residence was located thirty li from the county. One of their sons had just come of age and was somewhat educated. Beside their house, they had dug a large pond covering dozens of acres. At the turn of autumn and winter, when the water dried up, a dried-up corpse was found by the side of a tree near the bank. No one knew where it had come from. The neighbors dared not hide the discovery and reported it to the village headman. Before this, a Taoist had come begging to the Zhu family, demanding without limit. Zhu became angry and drove him out. The Taoist spoke disrespectfully, and Zhu beat him. The Taoist pretended to die. Zhu was in a hurry to report the incident to the authorities but it was too late at night, so he didn't succeed. The Taoist knew he could not be deceived, so he apologized and left. The village headman had a long-standing dispute with the Zhu family over land. He then claimed that Zhu had once beaten someone to death, and now the body was found in his pond. He reported it to the county. The county magistrate believed it to be true and arrested the Zhu family, throwing them into jail. All witnesses, officials, and historians who defended their innocence, the magistrate believed they were all bribed. He punished them even more severely, beating and interrogating them without a break. Zhu was a wealthy person and a scholar, having never been involved in government affairs. He could not bear the pain and torture, so he falsely confessed. His mother feared he would not be spared. She brought the spirit of the dried-up corpse back home, burned incense and prayed, crying day and night.

A reward notice was posted, and people were recruited to capture the real thief. The county prison was prepared, and the case was about to be reported to the prefecture. The beggar mentioned earlier, who had been active in the region between E and Yue, intended to cross the Xiang River and climb southward to Mount Heng. In a dream, someone told him, "You should not rush to go. The next day, there will be someone coming to chase you." Awakening and finding it strange, by morning, he met another Daoist separately. They bought wine and drank together. The beggar asked where the Daoist came from and what new things had happened. The Daoist replied, "I came from Wuzhou. When I arrived at Lanchi, I heard the townspeople talking about the injustice in the Zhu family. So I told him in detail." The beggar suddenly exclaimed, "I am the one who deceived them. I am guilty of this crime. I have already been condemned by the underworld. Now he is imprisoned, and he will die soon. If I do not go to clear this up, then he will truly die because of me. When will the debt of injustice be settled?" So he forced the latercomer to go east with him, traveling day and night to reach Wuzhou. He presented himself to the county. The county magistrate still did not believe him, and the doubt remained unresolved. Later, a thief was caught in another county. During the interrogation, the thief claimed to be a butcher. He once bought a customer's cow on credit, and the customer demanded payment very urgently. He could not repay it, so he secretly killed the customer and placed the body in the Zhu family's pond at night. The Zhu family was finally released.

Tongchuan Wine In the twenty-fifth year of the Shaoxing era (1155), Shen Dehe Jie served as the governor of Guangde. He issued an order to Shi Li □ Di, who was in charge of judicial and financial duties. At that time, the wine brewing had been completed, and the jars were stacked. One day, the warehouse clerk removed some wine and reported that a few hundred jars had disappeared from the second stack. Di went to inspect and found it to be true. He suspected that it was a trick by the servants and ordered them to investigate any thieves. After ten days, more and more wine disappeared. The upper layers remained intact, but the jars were lost from below. He thoroughly inspected the walls, windows, doors, and locks, finding no trace of any leakage or break-in. He found it very strange, but he did not immediately believe it to be the work of supernatural entities. The soldiers patrolling the city walls found a jar between two bamboo poles. Upon examination, it bore the mark of Tongchuan wine. They could not figure out how it had appeared there. A few more days later, Di went with his colleague Shen Wen, the magistrate, to inspect the site. The number of missing jars was too great to count. Shen feared there might be a hidden pit in the ground. He lit a candle to examine it, but the ground was flat like a palm. Below the first layer, there was nothing left. They were just about to report the matter to the prefecture when the clerks and soldiers whispered that the warehouse had an old shrine. The previous official had removed it. Could this be a sign of misfortune? They secretly bought sacrificial wine and offerings, and prayed and made offerings, promising to rebuild the shrine. The next day, when the crowd arrived, the missing wine was all gone. Later, when the wine was distributed, they checked the original numbers and found that only one jar was missing—the one from between the bamboo poles. They then built a shrine. This account was narrated by Chen Di of Jinyun.

*Yijian Zhi* Volume 63, Story **Fan Zimin** A Daoist priest from Quzhou named Fan Zimin was fond of drinking and lived a disheveled life. He initially traveled from Yandang Mountain to Tianmu Mountain. On his way to Shaoxing, he found a strange stone and treasured it, admiring and playing with it constantly. Later, a fellow Daoist priest stole the stone, leaving him feeling as if he had lost something. His speech often became illogical, and he frequently talked about matters beyond people's expectations, sometimes hitting the mark unexpectedly. He was exceptionally skilled in painting, creating scenes of misty rivers and cold forests that reached a high level of mastery, with cows being his most proficient subject. People in Zhejiang East called him Fan Niu because of this. However, he enjoyed playing with filth, often holding it in his hand, wetting his clothes, placing it in his hair, or defiling the statues of gods, Daoists, and Buddhas. He would write with dirty fingers on the windows and walls of people's homes, yet he did not feel any foul smell. He begged for money and rice from people, specifying the amount he needed first. If they gave the exact amount, he accepted it; if they gave more or less, he rejected it. What he received, he often threw into the toilet. The deceased official Liu Guang of Qingtian County, whose family was poor and could not afford a proper burial, was temporarily buried in front of the City God Temple. The following year, the grave was damaged by rain, exposing part of the coffin. Fan passed by the side of it, took some broken tiles and stones, and knocked on it, saying, "Do not grieve. In three days, a relative will come to accompany you." Liu Guang's brother Ju also became an official.

After three days, he died suddenly. His children were still young, so the officials buried him beside the tomb of Guang. A Daoist from Suichang named Ye set up a hermitage in the mountains. Fan visited him, but lost his way in the middle of the road. He encountered Ye’s servant and asked for directions. The servant, fearing that Fan would disturb him, deceived him by saying, “Go left.” Left led to the end of the mountain, a place not meant for people to walk. Fan knew about it and raised his hand, pointing to the servant and saying, “You go back this way.” Then, taking another path, he arrived at the hermitage. Ye wanted to prepare food but waited for the servant who did not arrive. Fan explained the reason, and Ye went to look for him himself. The servant was sitting upright on a large rock, his spirit like that of a fool. Ye called out to ask him, and the servant became awake. He said, “It was not right to deceive Master Fan. Master Fan instructed to go back this way. I immediately felt something pulling me along, blurred as if in a drunken dream. Without the respected teacher calling, I could not return.” Ye was also afraid and ordered the servant to apologize. Later, Fan went to the Chisong Temple in Wuzhou, where he saw the people in the temple and did not hesitate to mock or insult anyone. He drank at least one dou every time, bought beef, and cooked it in the Daoist chamber. After drinking and eating, he fell asleep. Then, he left feces all over the ground. Slowly getting up, he reached out to play with it, stamping his ten fingers on the wall. The entire room was filled with his marks, and everyone in the room left.

No one dared to challenge him. They would wait for him to leave, then draw water and clean it. Only Chen Yuetian detested him, and he often scolded the crowd in public. Fan laughed and angrily said, "You dare to slander me!" Chen hurriedly went to the Three Pure Ones' hall and bowed twice, whispering some prayers before leaving with a sweep of his robe. Two days later, Chen Yuetian died without illness. Because of this, the Daoists became even more cautious in their service. In front of the temple, a small stream crossed, troubling those who had to cross it. A Daoist named Shi, accompanied by one disciple, donated money from his purse to build a stone bridge. The workers and materials were ready, but Fan said, "Do not build this bridge; you will be at a disadvantage." Shi replied, "I used my private funds to do a public good. What is there that is not possible?" He proceeded with the construction, and Fan did not forcibly stop him. Instead, he smiled and said to Shi, "This is also very good. I just have two red boxes. I will give them to you as a gift to help with the expenses." Shi respectfully replied, "Yes," but did not know what they contained. On another day, Shi returned without anything to bring. He asked about the boxes, and Fan said, "I have already promised you. I will not speak falsely." Three months later, the bridge was completed, but two Daoists died in succession. The craftsmen carried two red coffins to bury them. Meanwhile, General Cheng Min, who was stationed in Wuzhou, once visited Fan.

A report came that Pan Chengxuan was coming. Min was about to go out to greet him. Fan said, "Do not see this person. I fear the official’s family cannot escape disaster." Min had a son who married the daughter of the Grand Princess of Qin, and she was Pan’s sister. Because of this marital connection, Min finally invited him in to sit. Fan said, "Disaster has occurred! Disaster has occurred!" He urged them to urgently buy paper money and bring the clothes of the official and his wife. "I will dispel the evil spirits for you," he said. After everything was prepared, Fan burned incense and recited spells, burning the clothes and paper money together. After a short time, the Grand Princess of Qin died. Min and his wife went to pay their respects. Both fell ill. The wife was wrapped in a white curtain, and she suddenly suffered from epileptic convulsions, becoming unconscious. Min had severe diarrhea and was extremely exhausted. They were forced to carry him home. After a short time, he recovered, but his wife only slightly improved after a year. It was then known that when the clothes were originally sought, the maidservant had only taken two pairs of Min’s pants, not the wife’s. In the second year of the Gande era, Qian Yu was the governor of Jinyun. Fan traveled from Qu to visit him. He said, "I owe the official four scrolls of paintings. So I have come to repay him." After the paintings were completed, Fan solemnly passed away. When they were preparing the body for burial, a piece of paper was found between the cushions. It was written, "On the day of Gengshen, Heaven and Earth decreed Fan Zimin." This was the day of his death, as said by Chen Tianyu.

Red Slave During the reign of Emperor Xiaozong, in the second year of the Ganda period (1166 CE), the magistrate of Qingyang County in Chizhou Prefecture, Hu Shi Jiang, completed his term in office and returned to Lin'an (modern-day Hangzhou). A county official named Liu Lushi was also on leave and staying at another inn. Hu visited Liu and they had a meal together. Just as they finished eating, Hu suggested they go to a shop to drink some noodles. Liu replied, "I am just in the middle of eating. It’s not possible to drink right away. Why don’t you return to the inn and rest for a while? I’ll come to join you later." After Hu left for a short while, Liu went to visit him and found him already ill and lying on the bed. Upon seeing Liu, Hu burst into tears. After a long pause, he said, "My time of death has come. Just now, on my way back from where you came, I was clearing the street with a sword or weapon. I encountered a woman who called out to me and said, 'You previously made an agreement with me, how is it? At first, you deceived me by not marrying. Later, you betrayed it. When I was ill, you barely paid any attention to me. How could there be such a cruel person like you in the world? Now that things have come to this, I will no longer say anything. But you will also get sick. Your symptoms will be very similar to mine. Even though I am here, I will certainly not go to see you. You should take care of yourself.' She then left. I walked a few steps and thought about it. It was the courtesan Red Slave, whom I had associated with in the past. She died three years ago. My heart felt lost and confused. When I returned to the inn, my mood was very bad. The disease condition was already apparent. I will not be able to rise. What can be done? Liu made porridge and boiled medicine for me. Until evening, I returned to the inn. Seven days later, I indeed died. His companions who could talk about his past events said that the complex symptoms all matched the ghost’s words. It was at the time when I was buying noodles that my soul had already gone dry. At that time, it was the second year of the Ganda period. This was told by Han Yantuan.

Sun Gong's wife, Gu, used to walk late at night on the bridge outside her home in Weitang Town, Xiu Prefecture. One evening, she called a young boy to come to her. The monkey rushed to pull at her clothes, attempting to have sexual intercourse with her. Gu was furious and ordered the servant to beat the monkey severely dozens of times. She then returned home. As night fell, she heard the window pane inside shaking with a sound, suspecting that a thief had entered. She got up to investigate and was suddenly grabbed by two hairy hands from the window, which held her arm tightly. Terrified, she screamed loudly and fainted. Her family heard her and rushed to help, lighting lamps to see what was happening. They found the monkey sitting outside, still holding her arm. They hit it with a stick, and it retreated. Gu was unconscious and needed to have her teeth pulled and be given medicine. She was supported and saved until nightfall when she recovered. When the situation was urgent, there was no time to tie up the monkey. The Sun family gathered their neighbors and surrounded the village to chase and shoot the monkey. It took three days to finally capture and kill it.

**Peach Blossom Spring Painting** Liu Pu, a man from Jinyun, was serving as the Deputy Governor of Chengdu when he encountered a mysterious stranger who bowed to him on the side of the road. The stranger carried a basket containing two boards, which were as strong and hard as iron. He said he could carve the scenes of Peach Blossom Spring but regretted not having a suitable owner. Liu Pu thought he could accept one of them and invited the stranger into his official residence. The stranger asked for a measure of wine and drank it, then entered the room for a short while before emerging with one of the boards. The picture was already completed. The buildings and figures were as fine as silk and hair, clearly visible. There were seventy-two female immortals, each holding musical instruments. Those who understand music will recognize it, as it is the complete *Niliang Faqu*. The rhythm and timing, the dance formations, were all in harmony with the music. A fisherman moored his boat by the shore, and the layout and scale of the elements, the exquisite carving, even those skilled in painting and carving in the world, could not achieve. Those who served as deputies at the same time.

He also desired to obtain one of them. Initially, he did not reject or close off the opportunity. He went to him immediately. The requirements were the same as before. It took only half the time. Suddenly, the board broke. Then he lost track of the person's whereabouts. At that time, it was during the Tian Sheng period. The Liu family has passed it down as a treasure through generations. During the chaos of the Jianyan period, it escaped into the common people. Now it belongs to the Hu family of Piling. The scholar Sun Xi from the county recorded it, saying: "The account of the Peach Blossom Spring by Tao Yuanming only mentions peach blossoms lining the banks. There are no other trees in the middle. The clothing of men and women is all like those outside. Old and young people are content and happy. Now this painting has palaces and halls, like a fairyland or Buddhist paradise. There is no peach forest, which differs from the record. It is suspected to be seen by a different person. It is about immortals and things beyond the mundane, and cannot be measured by ordinary reasoning. I have seen the ink version, all as mentioned above. Is it not the realm of immortals? Is there another place called Peach Blossom Spring?"

Li Xiucai Li Lun lived in Fuzhou. He enjoyed associating with people outside the conventional norms. There was a scholar named Li Xiucai from Mount Song. It is unknown where he came from. Upon meeting, they found each other agreeable, and Li Lun invited him to stay under his roof for several months. The scholar was prideful and unrestrained. He once struck someone and broke their teeth, and was arrested and sent to the government office. Li Lun spoke on his behalf to Governor Xue Gongbi, and he was spared. On another day, he struck someone again, and Li Lun scolded him severely. From then on, he did not leave the house. One day, it was very cold, and Li Xiucai never used a stove. Suddenly, he asked for fire and invited Li Lun to sit with him. He said to Li Lun, "You like alchemy. Have you made any progress?" He asked his servant to bring steamed cakes. When the cake arrived, he chewed it finely, spat out the residue into four parts, rubbed the iron chopsticks with it, and threw them into the fire. After a short while, red flames rose up, and he threw the chopsticks to the ground. The chopsticks broke in half, and they became white gold. Li Lun was surprised and shocked. He then mentioned that he had recently obtained a steamed cake from Mr. Xi, who is known for his alchemical skills. The scholar smiled and said, "This is not significant. I will give you a yellow one." Li Lun was very happy but dared not say it. His son, Sheng, was just a few years old. The family taught him to bow and asked for a trick. The scholar removed Sheng's silver arm bracelet, rubbed it with oil, and placed it in the fire.

When the items were taken out, one of them was pure gold, while the other had half of it transformed. Below the hall, a yellow chrysanthemum had withered. Shen picked a branch and asked to play with it. After blowing and spitting on it for a short while, it also turned into golden flowers. After several days, Luo requested the so-called yellow cake. The master said, "Your greed is so great. How can you achieve the Dao?" He then showed Luo the red one first. Taking a cake, he held a knife and divided it in half. He blew on one side and wrapped it in paper. After a long time, he looked out and saw that it had already become cinnabar, with the walls sharp and clear, bright and clear enough to be a mirror. He again requested two vessels of mercury. Drinking one of them, he stood on tiptoe and leaped, and pearl stars jumped out from his pores, brightly and clearly covering the ground, solid and able to be swept. He then used one vessel to rinse his teeth and immediately spat it out, and all turned into silver, like the shape of ink balls. Luo became even more respectful and amazed. When Luo was about to be transferred to Lin'an, the master sealed four pots of water and gave them to him, saying, "To drink farewell." That night, Luo returned to his house and no longer saw the master. Walking to the middle of the journey, he opened the water and found it all turned into fine wine. On the cover paper, he wrote the three words "Magu Wine." All the transformed things are now in Shen's place, with the silver at the broken place turning into golden clouds. Fan Yuanqing said.

Mr. Xu, the Attendant Mr. Xu, from Quzhou, served as the magistrate of Xin Yu. He was summoned by the Censorate to investigate a case in Luzhou. On his journey, he had not yet reached Jishui, thirty li from the city, when it grew dark and he planned to stay at an inn. An elderly man named Xu, a prominent local, insisted on inviting him to his home, where they enjoyed a feast of roasted lamb and wine. The magistrate thought the elderly man was only being hospitable due to their shared clan ties. That night, the elderly man confided in him, saying, "I have lived here all my life and have never had the chance to meet a scholar-official. Last night, I dreamed of a high-ranking official passing by our gate, with a plaque inscribed 'Mr. Xu, the Attendant.' Today, you have arrived. You must be destined for greatness. I entrust my descendants to you." The magistrate, who had recently passed the imperial examinations and considered himself quite accomplished, was pleased by the elderly man's words and promised to visit again on his return. He continued on to the city, completed his duties after a month, and returned eastward, visiting the elderly man directly. The elderly man greeted him with the same hospitality, but his respect had noticeably diminished. As they parted, the elderly man looked solemn and said, "During your journey, did you not betray your conscience? In my dream last night, a divine being told me that you accepted a bribe of five hundred thousand coins and deliberately failed to report the truth in the case. Your official rank will be stripped, and your lifespan will be shortened. Why do you not value yourself? You have disappointed my expectations." The magistrate was shocked and ashamed, unable to respond. After returning home, he was recommended for a promotion and traveled to Lin'an to take up his new position. Just after receiving his appointment, he fell ill and died in an inn. His father, originally a grain merchant, had accompanied him on his official duties and constantly encouraged him to act unethically. In the case in Luzhou, a wealthy man had beaten someone to death. The magistrate accepted a bribe from the wealthy man and forced the victim's servant to take the blame, resulting in the servant's execution. This led to supernatural retribution. Even after the magistrate's death, his father was still alive, and all the wealth they had accumulated was quickly spent, leaving them as poor as ever. As told by Liu Minshi Wenbo.

The Ten-Character Scripture A man from Wu named Zhou Ju. In the first year of the Jianyan era (1127), he returned to his hometown from the capital. At that time, China was suffering from military conflicts, and bandits were rampant everywhere. Zhou Ju encountered a celestial being dressed in a star-crowned robe who told him, "You will die by sword tomorrow. If you can recite the Ten-Character Scripture, you will not only avoid death but also resolve grudges and extend your life." Zhou Ju knelt and asked for the scripture. The celestial being revealed that it was the "Nine Heavens Supreme Thunder Sound Universal Transformation Heavenly Venerable" in ten characters. Zhou Ju bowed and accepted it. The next day, he indeed encountered bandits who chased him into a forest. In a state of panic and fear, he suddenly remembered the words from the previous day and hastily recited the scripture once. Not yet finished speaking, a loud thunderclap occurred, and the bandits were startled and fled. Thus, Zhou Ju escaped. This story was narrated by Zhao Xuelao.

Island of Tall Men A man from Banqiao Town in Muzhou set sail for Guangzhou. He encountered a severe storm with heavy fog, which left him disoriented and unable to determine his direction. He allowed the wind to take the boat wherever it would go, and after about ten days, their water supply ran out. The men feared they would die of thirst. They saw an island gradually approaching and rushed towards it urgently. Upon reaching it, they climbed onto the shore and found sweet water from a spring. They used all available containers to collect the water and brought it back to the boat. The island was covered with date palm forests, and red fruits hung down from the trees. Using poles, they knocked down some of the fruits and obtained several measures of them, intending to store them as food. They were extremely pleased beyond expectation and felt reluctant to leave. They entered a stone cave together to rest. Shortly afterward, four giants arrived. Each was over six feet tall, had long hair, and was naked, covering themselves only with leaves. When they saw the men, they were surprised and looked around, whispering to each other. Three of them left directly, moving like galloping horses. Under the cave was a large stone that would take more than a hundred people to lift. The remaining giant carried the stone to block the cave entrance and then left. However, there were still small openings on the sides that were large enough to enter and exit. The men rushed into the boat one after another and quickly untied the ropes. One giant came to pursue them, jumping into the water and grabbing the boat with his hand. The men on the boat pushed the oars with all their strength but could not move the boat. They quickly took a grappling hook to stop him, and using a sharp axe, they cut off one of his arms. They finally escaped. The arm was over five feet long. The men on the boat salted the arm and took it back to show others. Gao Sida, who lived in Banqiao at the time, had seen it. Mr. Shen had told me about it. My notes on the people of Changguo and the women on the island, as well as my notes on the country of tall people, are all similar cases. The sea is the largest thing in the world, containing everything, and it is truly awe-inspiring.

**Typhoon Disaster in Wenzhou** On the thirteenth day of the seventh month in the thirty-second year of the Shaoxing era, a fierce typhoon struck Wenzhou, shaking the ground. The houses and dwellings of the inhabitants, as well as the boats on Songjiang River, were blown away, drifted, and sank, with the extent of the damage too numerous to count. At Jingju Nunnery, three halls stood firmly, but two of them collapsed. The bell tower of Tianqing Temple also collapsed. Jiangxin Temple, located in the middle of the river, had two towers on the mountain peak that were very tall and steep, and they remained undamaged. Two days before the disaster, a wealthy merchant anchored his boat below the temple. In a dream, a deity appeared and said, "A big storm will happen the next day. It will cause significant harm. You should quickly move the things in your boat elsewhere. I will attend a water and land assembly at Ma Xing tonight. After the assembly, I will come to the temple and guard the tower." The merchant followed the deity's warning. Ma Xing is the name of a village. Later, he went to inquire and found that indeed there was a water and land assembly held that night. Initially, there was a woman in the prefecture who was about forty years old. She had no place to live and often begged for food in the market. She spoke in an unusual way. At night, she would stay at the foot of the statue of Vajra at Jingju Temple. The nuns all pitied her and could not bear to drive her away. The day before the wind started, she pointed to the clay statue and said to the people, "The body is so big, but I'm afraid it will fall over tomorrow." She did not stay overnight, and indeed, it happened as she said.

**Divine Responses of the Heavens** In the Yongjia period, there was a man named Xu Ji, the father of Shenfu. He was devoted to the heavens and experienced many miraculous events. Once, thieves attempted to enter his house at night, but the family did not realize it. Xu Ji had a dream where he saw bandits approaching, and a giant wielding a long spear chased them away. Startled awake, he quickly got up to check and found that the outer door was open, but nothing had been taken. The next morning, he discovered a spear outside the main gate, not knowing where it had come from. When he entered the room dedicated to the heavens and burned incense, the spear that the deity had held disappeared. It was then that he understood the dream from the previous night. **The Witch of Fuzhou** In Fuzhou, there was a witch known as the Great Compassion Witch. She was skilled in performing exorcism rituals using the "filthy traces" spell and was very effective in treating demonic possession and witchcraft. She was commonly referred to as the Great Compassion Witch. One day, a virgin from a village suddenly became pregnant. Her parents questioned her about the cause, but she did not know how it had happened. They summoned the witch to investigate. As soon as the witch arrived, a young boy entered the house, danced for a long time, and then jumped into the pool in front of the house. This boy was the son of a wealthy neighbor. He did not come out until evening. The next day, another boy did the same. The parents of the two boys gathered to accuse and physically assault the witch, wanting to take her to the authorities. The witch said, "Please give me a moment. Allow me to complete my ritual. Your sons will come out on their own. They will not be harmed." The crowd gathered around the pool, waiting. After a while, they heard a sound like a thousand people coming from the pool. Everyone was startled and moved back. The two boys came out of the water. One boy tied a large carp with a rope, and the other hit it from behind. They dragged it onto the shore, and the carp was already dead. The two boys acted as if nothing had happened, completely unaware of what had transpired. The witch then piled jars and bricks on the girl's belly and raised a staff to break them all. Afterward, she had a sudden diarrhea, and the pregnancy disappeared. It was then confirmed that the carp was the cause of the possession.

Zhang Ba and the Carved Image of Guanyin Zhang Ba, a resident of Wenzhou, had a guest bring a carved image of Guanyin made of sandalwood to sell. Zhang was afraid it might be fake and wanted to test it. The entire body was painted, and it could not be destroyed. He then used a small knife to scrape sandalwood shavings from the bottom of the foot and burned them. Shortly after, his left foot became greatly painful, as if a boil or poison was attacking from within. Medicine could not be applied, and the foot eventually rotted. Until now, he can only walk with a cane. The four stories on the right are all part of the wood's inner essence. Wang Zi and the Broken Divine Finger Wang Huan, a resident of Shuangdian in Raozhou, worshipped a good deity. He placed the image of the deity in a niche in the room. His five-year-old son playfully broke one of the deity's middle fingers. In a dream, the golden-armored deity said, "I have protected your family for many years and have never allowed any minor misfortune to befall your family. Why do you allow a child to break my finger?" Wang was shocked and apologized. In the morning, he looked at it and indeed saw that it was true. He quickly ordered a craftsman to repair it. This child immediately fell ill, with a very painful sore between the middle fingers. After it healed, the finger curled up and could not be straightened.

The Records of the Strange and Unusual, Volume 77, Story The Ancient Post Station of Dàyí Right Attendant and Prohibitor Jiang Di was from Xinxi, Puzhou. He served as the patrol commander of Dàyí Town in Tiāncháng County. The outpost was sixty li from the county. Once, Jiang Di had just returned from the county when it began to rain. He relaxed his burden on the road at an ancient post station and sent his attendants to prepare food. While drunk and going to the toilet, he saw a woman with a high bun and long skirt, dressed in a style reminiscent of the Tang dynasty. She held a red-handled copper halberd and came straight at him, stabbing Jiang Di. Jiang Di struggled to resist her and shouted loudly. The attendants gathered and only then did she leave. They searched the room but found nothing. That night, he was unable to continue his journey and moved to a small room in the west wing, instructing several soldiers to guard the door. When Jiang Di wanted to sleep, the woman was already there. She said, "I was just playing around. Why get so upset?" She pulled him to the bed. Jiang Di, unwillingly, shared the same bedding. He asked for her name, but she did not answer. Before dawn, she quickly left. When Jiang Di started his journey again, she held the halberd in front of him, guiding him until they reached the outpost. From then on, every time he visited the post station, she would come out and share his bed. When she left, she would guide him to the county gate and send him off. No one else ever saw her. Jiang Di became increasingly confused. He usually made a round trip every ten days, taking advantage of official duties. His colleagues gradually heard of the strange occurrences, and Jiang Di did not hide anything. One night, as he was about to sleep, two small hands strangled his throat very tightly.

Di shouted in surprise. A stranger had arrived. It was already lost. Immediately, he pulled down the tent and lit the candles, surrounding himself with servants. After a short while, everyone fell asleep, and the candles also went out. The woman came back and said, "It was just my sister playing with you before." Then there was a young woman, especially beautiful, who lay down on the bed. The next day, Di returned to the camp, and the two women both came forward angrily. This continued for more than a year, and Di's strength and vitality were exhausted. He gradually could not eat. One day, the official Sun Gu came, who had once received the Upper Pure Talisman and practiced the Heavenly Heart Method, which was very effective. Di's family invited him to treat Di. They set up an altar and summoned spirits, wearing magical talismans. The next day, when Di went out, the two spears did not appear. After walking several steps, he saw them by the roadside. The older woman angrily said, "We sisters have done nothing wrong to you. How could we have any intention to harm you? So why did you use magic to send us away?" Her anger and resentment showed on her face. The younger one explained from the side, "This person is as emotionless as wood and stone. But separation and reunion are all determined by fate. Why be so upset about this?" They suddenly disappeared. Sun Gu warned him, "Do not pass through this station within a hundred days." Di, because of illness, also resigned his position and returned home, lying in bed for many months, and finally recovered. Wang Han was the governor of Tianchang and was the elder brother of Yan Neihan.

**An's Grievance** An's daughter from the capital married the son of Li Weining, an observer. She was possessed by a demon and called upon a Taoist priest to treat her. The priest discovered that it was a small ghost from the White Horse Great King Temple. Using the Exorcism Bureau's method, the priest condemned and beheaded the ghost, and An's daughter revived. After ten days, she fell ill again. The priest treated her once more. The demon, possessing her, spoke and said, "The previous person did not deserve such a severe punishment. The master is too harsh. I will be questioned soon." It was revealed to be another temple ghost, and the priest beheaded it again. After another half month, her illness worsened. When the Taoist priest arrived, An's daughter spoke in the voice of a ghost, saying, "The previous two demons were mere ghosts. The master could have punished them. I am a righteous spirit and not someone the master can treat. Moreover, the master has already inflicted the ultimate punishment on two ghosts. What do I have to fear? Now I will compete with the master to see who wins." The Taoist priest, realizing he could not win, secretly fled. Li sought help from his relatives and chose a skilled practitioner to rescue her. When the practitioner arrived, An's daughter said, "Master, do not treat me. What I am seeking is an ancient grudge. I originally came from Shu and made a living as a merchant. An is my wife. When I went out, she committed adultery with others. When I returned, she secretly plotted to kill me. My vengeful spirit has wandered aimlessly, searching in all directions for twenty-five years without success. Recently, I came to the White Horse Temple and met two ghosts who told me the details."

The Former Wife The spirit of the former wife was here. Now that you have received a life to repay, you may go. The master should not witness your suffering. The Daoist priest said, "You have a grievance, and I will not punish you. But the events of the past have long passed, and the cycle of grudges and retribution—how can it ever end? I will now have the Li family make a meritorious connection to recommend you, so that you may fully release your past anger and be reborn in heaven. How does this sound?" An, rising from the bed, made a respectful bow in the Sichuan dialect and, appearing as a man, thanked him. Master Li immediately ordered two hundred thousand coins to be carried to Tianqing Temple, where a *jiu you* ritual was to be held for her. An again bowed in thanks, and suddenly she awoke. Master Li's whole family then observed a vegetarian fast, preparing for the ritual on a certain day. The night before, however, she fell ill again. Li became greatly angry and went himself to her room to reprimand her. Bowing respectfully, he said, "You have done everything with great effort. How could you not feel grateful for the kindness shown in the afterlife? But tomorrow’s ritual—what state and what person will it be offered for? What surname did you bear in your previous life? We failed to inform you of this yesterday. If you do not speak now, the merit will be lost without a proper recipient." Li was greatly surprised and asked her to explain everything. She then said, "There is also my younger brother, who has been traveling with me. Please include him as well in the offering, so that we may both be reborn in the next life." Li agreed to her request, and An was then free of illness. An’s sister had married Zhao Boyi, who lived in Wuchang, Huzhou. This story was told by Wang An.

### The Thunder Demon of Yangzhou Lian Yanheng, a vice minister, lived in Yangzhou. His wife, Lady Yang, was sitting in the hall during the day, gathered with her children. Suddenly, a great thunderstorm erupted, and a strange ghost fell from the sky to the ground. The ghost was only about three feet tall, with a blue face and flesh. A hat, like the headgear worn in the world, was made of flesh and connected to his forehead. He looked at people and covered his face as if smiling. Soon, the number of spectators increased, and the laughter did not stop. In a moment, a great thunderbolt struck the roof, and the clouds were dark and obscured the light. People could not distinguish each other. Suddenly, he flew into the air and disappeared. ### Tong Lang of New City Lian Shizhong was the magistrate of Lin'an's New City. The magistrate's office had a tower, and an ancient camphor tree stood outside the tower. The tree was so large that it could be embraced by two people, and its shade was very extensive. Lian Shizhong's daughter was of marriageable age. She once climbed the tower and looked out. Suddenly, it seemed as if she was talking and laughing with someone. From then on, she began to apply cosmetics daily and stayed in the tower, even in wind, rain, cold, or heat, she did not stop. Lian Shizhong found this very strange and called a witch to seek medicine to treat her, but it did not diminish much. The family only saw her talking and laughing with the camphor tree and suspected it was a ghostly possession. They ordered the tree to be cut down. The girl cried out in shock and grief, calling out "Tong Lang" several times. The strange phenomenon then ceased. The girl later recovered and was fine. When they asked about what had happened before, she said she had no clear memory of it.

Lady of Shouchang County Attendant Scholar and Vice Prefect of Chizhou, Ding Su, had as his wife Lady Shi of Shouchang County. She died of illness in the official residence. Fourteen days later, her son Yu dreamed that his mother was still alive, and she said to him, "I will be reborn in Huainan, but I will still be a woman, and my life will not be long. The reason for this is that I have unpaid debts from past lives. You should tell your father to arrange for good deeds so that I can be reborn as a man." Yu woke up and informed his father. A few days later, his grandson Bai Peng also dreamed of passing through the government office, where guards were arranged in rows and moving past. Someone called out from behind, "Lady Shi is here. How can we not pay respects?" Bai Peng hurriedly turned back into the government office and went to the east wing under the curtain. Indeed, he saw her there, and she said, "I feel lonely here without close friends or relatives, and I have daily and nightly labor of attending court. I am fortunate to have the title of Lady of Rank, so I can ride in a carriage. Otherwise, I would have to walk on foot and suffer from the hardship of being bound." Before she finished speaking, a clerk outside the curtain said, "You should hurry away. The judge knows about it, and it is not possible." Lady Shi also said, "You should go now." After leaving the office, someone else called out, "The judge has summoned you." Bai Peng then entered through the west wing and saw a green-clad person sitting at a desk. Upon closer inspection, it was the former Vice Prefect of Tan Prefecture, Li Gang, the Scholar of the Court. Bai Peng remembered that Li Gang had passed the same year's imperial examinations as his grandfather. He ascended the hall and bowed twice, saying, "You and my grandfather were the same year's successful candidates. We have a deep bond of friendship. I hope you do not return the bow." Li Gang accepted his bow. After sitting down, Li Gang asked in great detail about the health of the Attendant Scholar. A short while later, a clerk led Lady Shi to be questioned. Bai Peng stood up, and Li Gang asked, "Is Lady Shi related to you?" Bai Peng replied, "She is my recently deceased grandmother." Li Gang said, "So she is your grandmother."

It is fated by heaven. Bai Peng said, "I have heard that you already have a karmic connection for rebirth, but you have not yet transcended the female form. Do you believe this?" He replied, "Yes." Yesterday, the talisman arrived. Bai Peng wept and said, "My grandfather used to be your disciple. Now, my grandmother's karmic connection for rebirth is in your presence. If she can be reborn as a male, both the living and the dead will be benefited by great virtue." The Master said, "What can be done since the matter is already decided?" Bai Peng pleaded sorrowfully many times. The Master said, "Wait a moment. I will try to help." Thus, the Master went out, walked along the corridor, and ascended the hall step by step. For a while, he seemed to be absent. Soon, he came back down, supported by attendants, walking slowly and deliberately. He smiled and said, "Your request has been granted. However, you must return and recite the *Sutra of the Moonlight Princess* and the *Avatamsaka Sutra* to assist in her rebirth. That will be beneficial." Bai Peng bowed and thanked him before leaving. When he looked back, his grandmother was still standing at the bottom of the stairs. She said loudly, "Get many copies of these two sutras. Do not forget!" He then woke up and remembered everything. Xu was both surprised and doubtful. He said, "I have never heard of these two sutras. I sent someone to the Canming Temple to find them, and they were indeed found. The *Sutra of the Moonlight Princess* tells the story of a princess who attained enlightenment and was reborn as a male, and the *Avatamsaka Sutra* discusses the concept of the three realms and the non-dual nature of reality. Xu was amazed. He chose a wise monk and instructed his family members and women to fast, purify themselves, and recite the sutras. They recited up to a thousand volumes. He set up a *冥阳水陆斋* ritual to accompany them. After a hundred days, Xu dreamed that his wife came to him and said, "The merit of the Buddha is inconceivable. Because of your offerings and prayers, I am now reborn as a son in the Lu family in Huaiyuan." She bade farewell and departed.

#### Construction of the Liguang Embankment During the Zhenghe period, the governor of Taiping Prefecture ordered the construction of the Liguang embankment. The workforce was large, and suddenly, thousands of crows gathered noisily beside another dike. The project supervisor was surprised by this and sent someone to inspect the area. It turned out that a laborer had already died, and the crows were carrying soil to cover the body, nearly half of it. The supervisor then ordered the soil to be uncovered, and in the chest of the deceased, they found a small scroll. It was the Diamond Sutra. Everyone respectfully admired it, and it was moved to a high place and buried. There were many similar stories in the past. #### The Story of Qian Da's Wife Qian Lingwang, a high-ranking official, had a wife named Chen, who was naturally cruel. Even minor faults by maidservants would result in her beating them, and many died under her lash. Later, she fell ill and began speaking from the darkness, saying she was a deceased concubine named... She fully explained her intention to kill Chen. Qian put on formal attire, burned incense, and bowed to her, promising to recite scriptures and offer food to monks to help her transcend to a better life and redeem his wife's sins. The concubine replied, "I am just a lowly servant. How dare I accept the official's bow? But I have already appealed to the underworld officials. Only if Lady Chen goes to the underworld once will it help. Even if there are many merits, it is of no use." Chen eventually died.

**Cai Shijiu** In the 21st year of the Shaoxing era, Lu Wang Qian from the native place of Xiu Prefecture attended the provincial examination. On the first day, he remembered the seventh rhyme in the regulated verse but forgot to use the official rhyme. With no way to remedy the situation, the next day he wandered aimlessly at the desk, feeling lost and disoriented. A low-ranking official, seeing his distress, said, "I can steal this scroll for you. But my family is very poor. You should have something to repay me." He repeated this several times. Wang Qian agreed to pay 200,000 coins and then left, still doubting whether it would work. Soon after, the scroll was indeed brought to him, and he altered the text and gave it to the official. When asked for his name, the official said, "I am Cai Shijiu, living in a certain house in a certain lane in Dark Gate Lane. I am serving in the examination hall and cannot leave. Please ask Wang Qian to deliver a letter to my family." After the exam, Wang Qian took the promised money and the letter to visit the official's family. The wife saw him and wept, saying, "My husband died in the examination hall. He has now passed away twice. Can he still think of our poverty?" That year, Wang Qian passed the examination and provided generous support to the family, taking the official's son as a servant. In the 26th year, when examining in Huzhou, he took this servant with him. This event is similar to the story of Guo Chenggu in the Tang dynasty, but in recent years, it has been passed down by scholars and officials, possibly with some errors.

Zi Xia Kicks the Wine In the school of Huzhou, every year during the four seasonal months, the students were examined in the main hall. The exam papers were copied, sealed, and anonymized, following the same procedures as regular exams. The top performers received five jars of wine from the prefecture, the second and third place winners received three jars, and the fourth and fifth place winners received two jars. In the 21st year of Shaoxing, Tang Jiaoyou Yao was appointed as a professor. After the exam, the results were about to be announced. A traveling scholar from Fuzhou named Chen Yan dreamed of ascending the Great Accomplishment Hall, where Confucius bestowed upon him five jars of wine. Zi Xia's anger showed on his face, and he raised his foot to kick two of the jars. Upon waking, Chen Yan found it strange and told his fellow students. When the results were announced, his name was second. Tang Jiaoyou Yao told him, "You originally should have been the top performer, but due to mistakenly invoking Zi Xia's story, you were slightly demoted." Only then did the dream prove true.

Zhou Zhuangzhong Zhou Zhuangzhong. In the second year of Jianyan (1128), he passed the imperial examination and was admitted to the civil service. He dreamed of reaching the palace hall, where a person held a document and ordered him to sign it. Upon seeing the text, it resembled a petition or declaration, stating that he would become the King of Hell. Zhou declined the offer due to his mother's old age, and at the beginning of his career, he refused to comply. The messenger insisted repeatedly, commanding him to sign, and Zhou, unable to resist, complied. Upon waking, he felt deeply unhappy. The next day, he changed his writing style to a more decorative script. That night, he dreamed again of the same person who came to him and said, "You have already signed, how can you change it now? But the matter will not occur for another twenty years." In the seventeenth year of Shaoxing (1147), he became a minor official in the Ministry of Agriculture and Water Conservancy. He had another dream where a person held a yellow edict and asked him to accept the decree of the King of Hell. The person said he would return in two more years. Zhou grew increasingly displeased and kept the matter secret, not telling anyone. By the seventh month of the nineteenth year (1149), exactly two years had passed, and he had become a mid-level official in the Ministry of Finance. He believed that the matter would not happen and began to share his previous dreams with his family. That night, he dreamed of door gods and land deities coming to bid farewell, accompanied by a grand procession with golden drums and horse attendants. The next day, while he was about to eat at the Ministry, he suddenly felt dizzy and confused. He hurried home but did not have time to seek medical treatment and died.

Underworld Judge In the seventh month of the twenty-third year of the Shaoxing era, a professor named Zhao from Huzhou, whose name has been lost to history, had a dream in which a man presented his card to seek an audience. The man introduced himself as Mo Zai. After entering and sitting down, he stood up and said, "I am Mo Zai, from the southern part of the city. I have just heard that a heavenly decree has come down, appointing you as the Underworld Judge, with me as your deputy. It is a great honor to work together, and I dare not fail to pay my respects." Zhao was greatly startled and asked, "Who are you?" Mo Zai replied, "I am a wealthy man from this prefecture, ranked seventy-first. I once received the position of assistant teacher through grain contributions." Zhao woke up feeling repulsed. The next day, he went to the school and told the students about his dream. The students confirmed that there was indeed such a person, and that his name, family, and ranking were all genuine. However, he had already fallen into the ghost registry two years ago. Zhao looked sorrowful and, upon returning home, fell ill and died without taking medicine.

Señor Shěn, the Clerk In the winter of the twenty-seventh year of the Shaoxing era, a minor official named Shěn from Changxing County, Huzhou, was summoned to the county jail on official business. He was imprisoned for two months and was finally released. By then, the Winter Solstice was approaching. Shěn left the jail late in the evening, intending to walk back home all night. Despite the dimming light, he had no time to rest. He walked for more than forty miles, and by midnight, he encountered a humble dwelling. He stopped and stood outside the door. Soon, a young girl opened the door and asked who he was. Shěn explained his situation. The girl said, "This area is known for frequent banditry. If there's an emergency, trouble might arise. It would be better if you stayed inside for the night." Shěn also thought it was not safe to continue and agreed. The girl then said, "My mistress is staying alone in the rear room tonight. You might as well come in. There will be something good for you." Shěn did not respond, and she repeated her invitation. Shěn said, "I just came from official business. How dare I do such a sinful thing?" The girl replied, "It's no problem. She strongly invited him several times. Shěn asked for water to wash his feet. The girl went inside and brought out a large basin of hot water for him. After washing his feet, Shěn took out a small knife from his waist to trim his nails. As soon as the knife came out of its sheath, the house, the people, and the basin all disappeared. He found himself sitting on a grave. He quickly left and escaped unharmed.

#### Ma Shuyin Ma Shuyin. At eighteen years old, he followed his father, Zufu, who was transferred to the capital for official duties. He died of illness. He had an elder sister who married Li Shu, an official in charge of taxes in Changzhou. His mother stayed with his sister's family and did not know that her son had died. One of Li's maids suddenly went mad and spoke in a man's voice, saying, "I am Ma Shuyin. I died of illness on a certain month and day. It has been several months now. I wish to see my mother and elder sister once, so I have come by boat. I want to seek Buddhist merit to assist in my rebirth." His mother and sister were initially shocked and fearful upon hearing this. They questioned her and believed her story. They agreed to her request. The maid then fell silent. They immediately summoned monks from Taiping Temple to recite sutras and prepared offerings and prayers to honor him. The next day, the maid spoke again, saying, "I am grateful to my mother and elder sister for this. However, a certain monk stopped reading the sutra at a certain point, and another monk stopped at another point. The merit is incomplete; it is a pity." His mother did not fully believe her. She tried to call the monks and scold them, and they all felt ashamed and apologized before leaving. They quickly resumed their recitation. #### Ma Xianjue Ma Xianjue, the second son of Zufu. Once, he visited a shrine with his friend and saw a painting of a female performer holding musical instruments. He was particularly fond of a beautiful figure in the painting. He jokingly pointed to it and said, "If I could have this person as my wife, my simple wish would be fulfilled." That night, he saw the woman in his dreams. Long immersed in this, he regarded it as normal. Initially, he feared being known and kept it a secret. Later, he no longer had any fear or hesitation. He often whispered earnestly and secretly in the room. When others entered and encountered him, he would say, "Family members are here," to avoid detection. He was extremely deluded and did not realize it was wrong. His parents were worried and tried various methods to exorcise or treat him, but he showed no signs of improvement. Eventually, he did not recover.

Thunderfire Melting Gold The ancestor of Xu Jianshu from Suzhou. While living in the village, a thunderbolt struck between the buildings, causing smoke and fire to block the view. After some time, it dispersed, but the beams and pillars were broken, and dragon tracks remained. Later, when they opened the wooden chest to retrieve the silver vessels, most of them were found to be pierced and corroded, having turned into pearl-like grains that flowed and scattered below. The lock and bolt of the chest remained unchanged, but inside, the silver had melted and liquefied. It was the fire of a divine dragon, especially skilled at destroying gold and stone. Two-Headed, Four-Armed Calf A wealthy man named Xiu Ershisan from Changzhou lived in the village of Dadu. In the third year of the Shaoxing era, he died of illness, with no other abnormalities. Soon after, in the eastern part of the neighboring county of Kunshan, a farmer's cow gave birth to a white calf with black hair on its side forming seven characters: "Xiu Ershisan once served as a jailer." It was when Xiu was poor that he once served as a county jailer, and it is said he had hidden vices. His son wanted to redeem it for 20,000 coins, but his family did not allow it. Fly-Tiger Retribution The wet nurse of Li Shu's wife, a military officer, loved to use a night-pastime painting for gambling. Whenever she painted, she often caught fly-tigers to extract their blood, mixed it with ink, and painted it. It was a popular method of warding off evil, to make herself win more often. She became accustomed to this practice. Later, when she was old and ill, nearing the end of her life, she told people, "An endless number of fly-tiger children are biting me to death. Catch them for me." But others saw nothing. Knowing she would not live long, she eventually died. This scroll was all transmitted by Wang Rizheng, who often obtained it from his younger brother.

**The Yijian, Volume 3, Story 82** **The Woman Without Feet** Guan Zi Dong recounted to his brother, Doctor Yan, who was in the capital, about a woman he saw begging in the marketplace. Her clothes were tattered and her body was dirty. She had no feet, and instead walked on her hands. Yet her appearance was exceptionally beautiful. A court official saw her and was taken with her beauty. He stopped his horse and asked, “Do you have parents?” She replied, “No.” “Do you have in-laws or relatives?” He asked. She answered, “No.” “Can you sew clothes?” he inquired. She said, “I can do it somewhat.” The official then said, “Would it not be better to be someone’s concubine than to wander about begging?” She lowered her eyebrows and sighed, “In this state of body, I cannot even care for myself. If I were a servant, I would be serving others. How could I make others serve me? And who would be willing to employ me?” The official returned home and told his wife about the woman. His wife was moved with pity and had the woman brought to their house. They bathed and clothed her, looked after her meals, and taught her needlework. She was quick and skilled in her work, and the whole household came to love and cherish her. The official also gradually grew close to her. After living with them for about a year, he went out and visited Xiangguo Temple, where he met a monk. The monk was shocked and said, “You have a very strong demonic aura. What will you do?” The official thought the monk was lying and angrily refused to respond. Some days later, when he met the monk again, the monk said, “The supernatural affliction is urgent. You must truly tell me. I have no request of you. Does your family have any ancient objects like broken-footed cauldrons or tripods?” The official replied, “No.” But the monk kept asking, and the official could no longer hide the truth. He finally confessed, “She is my concubine.”

Yes, yes! Flee quickly! Tomorrow you should ride as far as a hundred li away. If you cannot reach it, then temporarily stay wherever the day’s strength allows. The gates are strongly closed. If in the middle of the night you hear someone knocking, do not open them. Perhaps this may save you. There is no other strategy but this. The scholar was at first frightened, did not consult his family, borrowed a fine horse, and traveled all day with great effort. As dusk approached, he stayed at an inn. He had not yet settled down when dust rose on the road, and flags led the way. A tall and imposing man on a black horse also arrived. He paid a respectful bow and sat down, pointing to a room and telling him to stay there. He spoke very little. The scholar was even more afraid, closed the door, and dared not sleep. When the night was nearly over, a voice from outside called out: “Your family has suddenly suffered a calamity. I have come to deliver a message.” At that time, the lights were still burning. Peering through a crack, he saw a woman with no feet, carrying two fleshy wings, whose color was deep blue. The scholar was drenched in sweat from terror. The tall man quickly opened the gate and went out, swinging his sword and striking her. The woman let out a long howl and vanished. The next morning, the scholar awoke and saw the tall man. He bowed and thanked him, saying: “Had it not been for your esteemed office, I would not have known where I would die. Dare I ask who you are?” He said, “Do you recognize me?” “You are the monk from Xiangguo Temple,” the scholar replied. “I had already told you this before. I am your guardian deity, for you have always sincerely worshipped me, and so I have come to rescue you.” After he finished speaking, the man and his carriage disappeared from sight.

A Scholar Named Hu Jiang Bu, also known as Shizhong, was a student at the Imperial Academy. He shared a room with Scholar Hu. Scholar Hu had a benign tumor on his upper body and wanted to cauterize it with mugwort. Someone warned him that today, the deity associated with the fingers was present, and he should wait for another day. However, Scholar Hu did not believe the warning and proceeded with the cauterization. After seven days, the wound became inflamed, and the skin peeled off one layer, revealing a human face inside, as if seen in a mirror. He was repulsed by this and quickly covered the wound with ointment. After another seven days, the wound began to heal slightly, but it became very itchy. He scratched it, causing the skin to rise, and the human face remained the same. Over forty days, the wound grew larger and more painful, and eventually, he died.

A Man with a Wasting Disease and His Son Treated by Taoist Rituals Mr. Huang, a high-ranking official known as the Martial Virtue Grand Master, Gatekeeper, and Advisor to the Imperial Audience, and also serving as the Military Governor of the Eastern River Region, was nearing the end of his term in January of the 22nd year of the Shaoxing era. He was about to return to Yiyang when he passed through Chizhou, where he encountered snow and stayed briefly. The prefect lent him a teaching hall, and he met his former colleague Zhao Shie. Zhao was surprised by Mr. Huang's blue-black complexion and his incessant coughing. His speech and movements were no longer as they used to be. Zhao asked calmly about his suffering. Mr. Huang looked solemn for a long time and said, "Unfortunately, my family has inherited a wasting disease. Because of this, people have died generation after generation. For half a year now, this illness has begun to manifest. My second son Yuan is also like this. He is likely to die soon." He then became sad and shed tears. Zhao said, "I often hear that this disease is terrifying. Occasionally there are cases of recovery, but the root cause cannot be eradicated. I can treat it with the highest Taoist scriptures. But I am concerned that people do not understand and thus lose their lives due to neglect. If you truly have faith, let me try it on you." Mr. Huang burned incense and wrote talismans, which he gave to Mr. Huang and his son to swallow. Soon after, all over their fingers, inside and out, yellow hair grew, more than an inch long. Zhao said, "The illness is deep. If it lingers a bit longer, black hair will grow, and then it will be impossible to cure. But it can still be treated now." He chose a day, wrote a different talisman, submitted a petition to the City God, reported to the Eastern Mountain God, and presented it to the Supreme God. He ordered Mr. Huang to clean the small room in the west corner of his lodging, paste paper inside, place lime at the base of the walls, and set up a large oil cauldron. The father and son wore white clothes, closed the door, and sat facing each other on beds. After swallowing the talisman, the ritual was completed.

Luo Heng ordered several young men to hold candles and watch. After a while, four or five black flowered cicada moths flew out from the bodies of two people. There were other insects in the wall, making sounds and emerging, some like dung beetles, some like spiders, in total thirty-six in size. All were thrown into the boiling cauldron. The stench could not be borne, and the chirping still did not stop. Then one insect as fine as a hair, wriggling and moving, entered into the sleeve of the young man. It was quickly caught and also thrown into the cauldron. Immediately, all limbs felt at ease, completely free from suffering. The Huang family was filled with joy and astonishment, knowing that it was effective. They silently prayed to heaven, wishing to save those who died in the past because of this disease, and performed a great ritual of salvation for the nine abysses. Before the ritual had been performed for several days, Huang's wife dreamed of over ten deceased people, among them one wearing a black small floral robe, holding a white yellow talisman and a white tablet, came to bow and thank, saying, "You saved me, so I will save your wife." She woke up and told her husband. Huang wept and said, "The one wearing the small floral robe was my father. My father died in the midst of warfare, with clothes not complete, only managed to get one robe for burial. What was seen in the dream was truly real." So on the first day of the second month, they set up a ritual at the Heavenly Joy Temple. That evening, dark clouds gathered, and rain seemed about to fall. In the middle of the night, faint thunder was heard. The tea offered at the sacred positions was all white like milk. The Daoist congregation feared the rain would start, preventing them from burning the scriptures. Afterwards, it reached the fifth watch of the night, and the ritual was completed. The rain then came heavily. The evil diseases that plagued the Huang family for generations ceased from this point. Shi E, courtesy name Jin Chen, at that time, Wei Yanliang, Right Counselor and Governor of Chizhou, wrote an account.

**Xie Seven [Woman More]** In Yushan County, Xin Prefecture, at the Seven-Li Store to the south of the pond, there lived the wife of a man named Xie Seven. She was unfilial to her mother-in-law, giving her only barley for each meal, which was insufficient to satisfy her hunger, while she herself ate white glutinous rice. On the seventh day of the seventh month in the thirtieth year of the Shaoxing era, both the wife and her husband went out, leaving the mother-in-law alone to guard the house. A wandering monk passed by the door and asked the mother-in-law for food. Smiling, he said, "I myself have never been full. How can I have any excess?" The monk pointed to the glutinous rice in the bowl and said, "Give this to me." The mother-in-law shook her head and said, "This white rice is for Seven [Woman More]. I dare not touch it. If I return, I will be scolded and insulted." The monk persisted in his request, but she finally refused to give it to him. Soon after, the wife returned. The monk approached directly and asked for food. The wife became very angry and scolded him harshly. The monk pleaded even more urgently. The wife snapped, "Take off the robe on your body, and then I will give you something in exchange." The monk immediately removed his robe and handed it to her. The wife examined it carefully and, in jest, put it on. Suddenly, the monk vanished, and the robe transformed into cowhide, becoming firmly fastened and impossible to remove. A single hair began to grow on her chest, gradually spreading over her entire body. Her head and face became half-cow. Her husband ran to inform her family. Her parents hurriedly arrived, and there stood before them a full-grown cow. Now, it is unknown whether it still exists. The above four stories were also obtained from Wang Rizheng.
