### 白石大王 The father of Chen Zu'an from Fuzhou was appointed as the Deputy Transport Commissioner of Yan Prefecture. In a dream, a yellow-robed official arrived with a talisman and said, "The emperor has decreed that you shall become the White Stone Great King." When asked where this would occur, the official replied, "Not yet. Wait until you see a large stone with a blemished corner, then you will leave." By the appointed time, the official returned to welcome him. Upon waking, he greatly disliked the dream. After serving in his official position for two months, he visited Mount Tai and stayed at a temple at the foot of the mountain. He happened to see a large stone in the courtyard, one corner of which was missing. Feeling dejected and unhappy, he returned to the prefecture not long after. Soon, the yellow-robed official arrived again, and he died on that day. ### 莫东得官 Mo Bozhen from Wuxing was a Censorate Officer when his three sons had not yet been appointed to official positions. He once dreamed of being granted an official position for his second grandson, Mo Dong. Waking up, he said with joy, "Dong is the fifth among my descendants. I can extend my favor to him. That is enough." In the thirty-second year of the Shaoxing era, he was appointed as a Deputy Transport Commissioner of Tongchuan. During the enthronement, he was to send a representative to congratulate. At that time, his eldest son, Mo Cheng, had already passed the imperial examination. The second and third sons could not go due to mourning for their mother. He then ordered an official to carry a blank document to give to Mo Cheng in Wuxing, letting him decide for himself. Mo Cheng's eldest son, Mo Guo, and his second son, Mo Dong, both read extensively and became well-known. Mo Bozhen believed that Mo Dong would surely achieve fame and appointed him to the position. When Mo Bozhen heard about this, he recalled his previous dream and felt melancholy and unhappy. That year, due to the imperial favor and the inspection, he was promoted to the rank of Censorate Officer. He died before taking the oath. What he received in life was only that one grandson received an official position. These two stories were narrated by Wen Ju.

Huang Shisheng Huang Shisheng, whose name was Dayan, was from Pucheng and lived in Guangde Army. On the fourth day of the eleventh month of the twenty-seventh year of the Shaoxing era (1157), he was troubled by a long-term illness that caused his heart to race. A yellow-robed child called him out of the door. He walked down a wide street, with hanging willows on both sides. The pond water was clear and lovely, and lotus flowers bloomed as if in mid-summer. After traveling more than ten li, there were no more residents. He saw towering buildings and pavilions, with gold and green colors shining in the sunlight. The child led him into a gate, where thousands of criminals stood below the hall. On the throne were four people, each wearing a Tian-guan (Heavenly Crown) and a robe embroidered with gold threads, sitting separately. An official called Huang, and Huang said, "Your time has not run out; you were mistakenly summoned." He ordered a green-robed child to lead him out of the eastern gate. Looking back, he saw the others being driven northward. Outside the eastern gate, it was like a bustling market in the human world. They did not travel far before seeing another beautiful palace, with ox-headed A-pang (demon guardians) inside and outside. A king sat with a crown and held a jade tablet, with a stern and imposing presence. A purple-robed official asked, "What deeds have you done in the world?" Huang replied, "During the recent years of war, I was once robbed by two bandits. I was eventually captured, and the neighborhood guards wanted to kill me. I felt compassion for him."

To redeem his death with twenty thousand coins, and throughout his life, he had abstained from killing, held scriptures, and created statues on numerous occasions. Soon after, he held a large mirror and looked down, and found no karmic debts or grievances. He was then instructed to go to the provincial office for verification. The head of the provincial office declared that he should be released. His deputy, Wang Xun, who was an official from Guangde, had always been close to him and said to him, "You will be reborn into the human world again. If you see people, encourage them to cultivate virtue, revere heaven and earth, honor and care for their parents, seek refuge in the Three Treasures, practice an equal-minded heart, do not kill living beings, do not covet others' possessions, do not lust after women, do not harbor jealousy or envy, do not slander the virtuous, do not harm others. If evil deeds are committed, they will be exhausted in a single day, and one will fall into the great hell, with no hope of release. After fully receiving one's karmic retribution, one will be reborn in the realms of hungry ghosts and animals. The hundredfold exhortations and precepts in the Buddhist scriptures are certainly not false words." He also instructed him to deliver a message to his family: "I am in the public service, and how could I be without faults? But I have released thirty-one people from the death penalty, and because of this hidden virtue, I have become a spirit. You may make a set of clothing, recite many scriptures, convert money into seventeen thousand strings of cash, and submit a detailed report to the City God's Office."

To reach the point where I can redeem my own faults, I also speak of how the world recommends the deceased with merit and virtue. One must rely on the city god to certify this, only then can one gain blessings. If one performs sacrifices by killing living beings during specific times of the year, even the ancestors do not partake in these offerings. These two matters cannot be unknown. Two days later, the underworld gathers virtuous men and women at the Pavilion of No Worries. According to their good deeds, they are made to attain spiritual fruits. As for the prisoners in hell, they are also driven to this place. Like prisoners in provinces or counties who hear the pardon of their crimes, those with lighter offenses also escape suffering and are reborn. One should go to observe this. Upon arrival, one sees what is called the Pavilion of No Worries, built from many stones, rising high above the clouds, with auspicious light reaching the sky. Men and women are all beneath it. Those who are virtuous wear magnificent clothes, holding incense and flowers as they stroll through the alleys, wandering among the gathered clouds, and standing on jade steps and golden platforms. But the multitude in hell are all chained and imprisoned, weak and exhausted, kneeling and prostrating outside the gate, equally filled with joy and fear. Just as they look around and murmur, suddenly everything disappears. Mr. Wang, the chief manager, says, "Already relying on the protection of the Buddha today, they have escaped the suffering of hell, but all have lost their human form." Returning to the office of the chief manager, one sees many others dealing with affairs. Among them are acquaintances, who entrust me to ask for merit and virtue for their descendants.

The words entrusted to me were all the secrets of my private life, known only to myself and not to outsiders. After the matter was completed, a child led me back. I saw a mountain of iron, with flames burning fiercely, roasting the prisoners, who shrieked continuously. Another mountain had trees without leaves, with swords and knives hanging from them. The prisoners climbed up, suffering the pain of cutting and slicing, with countless accumulated corpses. I spoke loudly, clasped my hands, and recited the names of Guanyin and Dizang Bodhisattvas. Suddenly, a thunderclap sounded, and both mountains disappeared. Proceeding forward, I passed through a cave, where a foul-smelling river could not be approached. The child said, "People discard leftover food, drink, and tea in ditches, and all are collected and stored here by the earth deities, awaiting their death, when they are then made to eat them." After traveling several miles, I returned to the king's presence. The king commanded, "You will return to the world for five years. Convey my words to the people: those who do good will be reborn into the world and enjoy happiness and blessings; those who do evil will not return for countless eons and will endure the suffering of no respite. Let those who hear this pass it on by word of mouth." He then ordered a child dressed in green to lead me out through the Gate of Eternal Spring, where there were flowers as before. Crossing the bridge, I stumbled and woke up. It was already the eighth day. The old man Huang was eighty-five years old, and the county magistrate Qin Jiang of Chongren wrote the record.

A Man from Hengshan At the beginning of the Ganda period, a man from Hengshan village participated in the village god festival, drinking and getting drunk. By evening, he returned home alone, stumbling into a ditch filled with water. He was in a state of delirium, running along the ridges of the fields. Upon reaching his house, he found the door already closed. He knocked, but no one responded. He managed to slip in through a crack in the door. His wife was sitting on the bed spinning thread, and his two sons were playing in front of her. The wife often scolded her husband for not returning home late at night. The man shouted, "I'm here!" but the wife did not hear him. She continued to scold angrily, and he did not respond. Startled, the man thought, "Could he have died?" He quickly ran out of the house, passing by the ancestral altar where he saw his ancestors seated. He knelt and wept, asking for help. His father said, "Do not be afraid. I will pray to the land deity for you." Immediately, the land deity appeared, dressed in a coarse cotton shirt and straw sandals, looking completely like a farmer. The deity asked for the reason behind the man's plight and told a little boy to follow the man. The boy had a bald head and was barefoot, resembling a cowherd. They left the house together and returned to the original path, going back to the ditch. The deity instructed the man to hold himself tightly and shout several times. Suddenly, the man woke up. Meanwhile, the wife, worried about her husband being out late, asked a neighbor to hold a torch and search for him. The neighbor happened to arrive at the spot where the man was. They returned home together. The story was recounted by the man's son, wife, and nephew Zhang Yin.

The Guest from the Top Mountain A monk named Ciyue from Changshu County in Pingjiang built a hermitage on the steep cliff at the northern peak of the mountain, beside the Temple of the White Dragon. He lived there for over thirty years, serving the dragon with utmost sincerity and winning its favor. All his prayers were answered, and the people of the county greatly respected him. In the 32nd year of the Shaoxing era (1162 CE), when he was seventy-eight years old, he suddenly contracted a蛊 (gǔ) disease. Water floated between his skin and flesh, and he remained unhealed for months, moaning day and night with almost no hope of recovery. The coffin and burial shroud were prepared, and he awaited death. One day, a guest arrived, unknown from where. He wore a blue silk square-capped headband and a white hemp robe, with a lofty and dignified appearance, different from ordinary people. He came from the foot of the mountain to pay respects at the dragon temple and visited the monk's hermitage. Seeing Ciyue's illness, he asked, "How long have you been sick?" Ciyue replied, "I have been sick for months." The guest said, "This is edema. I have medicine that can cure it." Ciyue happily asked for the method. The guest ordered Ciyue to undress and lie down properly. He then used his fingernails to draw on Ciyue's abdomen and below the navel. Immediately, water flowed, overflowing onto the bed. The old swelling disappeared instantly.

A pill was discovered, about the size of a bullet, and it was pitch black. The instructions were: "It is advisable to take the root of *Phytolacca acinosa* and mung beans together, with ten bowls of water. Boil until it reaches a boil, then remove the residue. Drink it as needed. Once the medicine is finished, the illness will be cured. Additionally, the person's lifespan may extend to eighty-five years." The person named "悦" felt ashamed and thanked repeatedly. They also inquired about the person's name, surname, and hometown. The person replied, "I am a returning guest. I am from Lin'an. And I am a monk. Nowadays, people recognize falsehoods but not truths." After speaking, the person bowed and left. "悦" followed the instructions and drank the medicine. The taste was unusually sweet and delicious. Over two days, the medicine was finished, and the illness disappeared completely. "悦" no longer knew who the guest was. Two months later, another guest arrived, claiming to come from the capital, and came to observe the Guanyin statue at Putuo Mountain. The guest gave a scroll of painting to "悦" and said, "This is what I have done." Then the guest left. Later, "悦" unrolled and looked at the painting. It depicted a figure of Lü Dongbin covered in a vine-like pattern. It was then understood that the "returning guest" referred to Lü Dongbin. The county clerk Zhao Yanqing wrote a record of this event. ### 粉县主 粉县主, the granddaughter of Prince Zhongyu of Xun Kang, was a young lady around fourteen or fifteen years old. One day, she was having a gathering with her family in the hall. Suddenly, a strong wind arose from the courtyard, followed by thunder and rain. Fireballs flew around, and smoke and fog filled the air, making it impossible to see each other. Men cried out for mercy, women covered their ears and fell to the tables, and some fell to the ground. After a while, the storm stopped, and the sky cleared as if nothing had happened. When they checked the people in the hall, only the young lady was missing. After a long time, they found only her two eyes on the steps of the courtyard. Her body was nowhere to be seen. The young lady's father was named Shi Li.

In the Dazhao era, a medical official named Geng Yu bought a beautiful and clever servant. After a year, a child passed by and recognized her as his mother, clinging to her and reluctant to leave. The servant also affectionately cared for the child. The child returned home and told his father, "My mother is at someone's house." At that time, the child's mother had already passed away, and the father did not believe him. He went to the tomb to check, and it seemed that the tomb had been disturbed by thieves, with no body found. Returning home with the child, he visited the neighbors of Geng Yu and discreetly inquired about the servant's name and background. It turned out that she was indeed the child's mother. The father then pretended to be a peddler and lingered on the streets, waiting for the servant to appear. When he saw her, the mother called him forward and expressed their separation. She then wept and said to others, "This is my husband, and the small one is my son." Geng Yu heard this and became angry, scolding her, "When I bought you last year, you had no husband. The contract and the matchmaker can verify this. How dare you lie?" The husband reported this to the Kaifeng Prefect, who traced the origins of the servant. The servant was confused and could not remember anything, only saying that she had been walking near a bridge and got lost. A matchmaker led her away, and due to hunger, she sold herself. The matchmaker also confirmed that she had indeed met her at Guangbei Bridge, where she was seeking food and sold herself to pay off debts. The Kaifeng Prefect did not have time to investigate the full details and ordered the husband to pay the remaining money to Geng Yu and take his wife back. Geng Yu refused, and the husband again reported to the Yushi Tai (Imperial Censorate). The investigation was incomplete, and the servant disappeared again. Within a year, Geng Yu died, and his family's fortunes declined.

### Jiang's White Quail During the Xuanhe period, Jiang Xia served as the magistrate of Hong County. His eldest son brought his mother to the place where his father was in office. The family had a white quail and a white swallow, both of which were exceptionally clean and beautiful. They were kept together in a cage on the back of their boat. As they traveled down the Bian River for dozens of miles, they passed by the Temple of the Second Son of the Water and Rain God, Er Lang Shen. The boatman entered the temple and reported to the deity, saying, "The god has always loved such creatures. I wish to keep them secret." He then took the cage into his sleeping quarters. Suddenly, a maid who had come from the kitchen stumbled and accidentally knocked the cage, causing it to fall. When they looked, the white quail was dead. This story is recorded by Ming Yu.

The *Yijian Bing Zhi* Volume Ninety-Four **Shangzhu Guanyin** In the second year of the Shaoxing era (1132), the preliminary examination for jinshi candidates in the Zhejiang region was held in Lin'an, the capital of the Southern Song Dynasty. From Huzhou came Tan Yi, who, along with seven fellow candidates, visited the Shangzhu Guanyin Temple to seek guidance through dreams. Tan Yi dreamed that someone presented him with two plates containing six eggplants as a gift, which he found distasteful. Only Xu Yang dreamed of eating a large crab, which he found very delicious. By dawn, the candidates gathered and sat with a guest. They discussed the type of seafood known as "huang jia." Xu Yang asked about its appearance, and the guest replied that it was smaller than a "you mou" crab but larger than a regular crab. Xu Yang secretly rejoiced and shared his dream with the others. They believed it was a sign that he would surely pass the examination. When the results were announced, six of the candidates did not succeed, but Xu Yang alone passed. Two years later, Tan Yi, along with Zhou Yuan Te Cao, took another examination organized by the Cao Office. They visited the temple together again. On the night before, Zhou dreamed that he and the others climbed a temple hall together. Tan Yi drew lots first, and after three attempts, all three were inauspicious. The others then took turns praying. Zhou stood behind and said, "What we seek is only a dream. Why bother with the lot?" The others insisted, and he approached the lot-drawing area. He encountered a woman with her hair down as if freshly washed, who hurried out from behind the Buddha. They believed she was a member of the Guanyin's family and quickly fled. He woke up. The next morning, they entered the temple. The three lots Tan Yi drew were indeed inauspicious. The others either drew lucky or unlucky lots. Zhou simply burned incense and bowed twice, wishing for a dream. That night, he dreamed that a fellow villager named Xu Guang brought the imperial examination results. The candidates were ranked in three categories.

Zhou had already ranked first among the middle tier. Soon after, guests gathered in large numbers to congratulate him. Among them was a Taoist priest. The following July, after the provincial examination concluded, Zhou awaited the results. One day, as he was visiting the market, he heard someone call out from behind, "Xuan Te, I come to congratulate you." Turning around, he saw Xu Guangzhi. Xu said, "I just passed by the county gate and saw a person listed on the official list with the same name as you. I was just joking around." Zhou was about to scold him, but Xu added, "The provincial list has entered through the south gate." They all then dispersed. When Zhou reached home, the news arrived. The next day, several guests came to congratulate him, including a Taoist priest who stood solemnly among them. Zhou said, "I do not know you. Why do you grace me with your attention?" The Taoist priest smiled and said, "Do you not remember me? Last year, you came to me for divination. I calculated your five elements and knew that you would definitely pass the exam this year. And indeed, it is so. Therefore, I come to congratulate you to verify my skill. I am not seeking anything." He then hastily left. Zhou pondered deeply about the person and realized it was the diviner from Kaiyuan Temple. Finally, he verified yesterday's dream, with no slight discrepancy. Zhou indeed ranked in the middle tier, although not the top choice, he was the top person in Wu County. As for Xu Guangzhi's joking talk and the Taoist priest's formal visit, they were all coincidental trivial matters. But dreams and omens had already been seen several months before. Is everything in life not predetermined? Said Xuan Te.

The Official of Fengdu Palace Lin Yi, courtesy name Cai Chen, was from Gusu. He was upright and valued righteousness. In his hometown, he was known as "Lin without difference," because his name was close to the character for "difference." Later, he gained an official position through a special imperial recommendation. He was appointed as the magistrate of Jiaxing and, upon completing his term, returned home. One night, he dreamed that officials came to welcome him into an official residence. They led him into the main hall, where he took his seat. There were dozens of subordinates, some dressed in purple and silver insignia, who lined up and bowed before him. They took out official documents and asked him to write his name at the bottom. When he looked at the rank, it was a seal or title that was three inches wide, which he could not read. However, he recognized the five characters beneath it: "Official of Fengdu Palace, Lin." This happened on several pieces of paper. Lin had always read Daoist texts and was deeply interested in immortality and spiritual matters. He turned to the officials and said, "Those who study the Dao should become immortal officials. This position is under the jurisdiction of the underworld, and only those who have been punished for their sins would be sent here. Moreover, I have heard that those who hold this position are not promoted for about two hundred and forty years. It is not a desirable position. I do not wish to accept it." The officials replied, "This is the command of the Heavenly Emperor. How can you refuse? If you do, you may incur the wrath of Heaven, and you will be demoted to a lower rank. Even this position will no longer be available to you." Lin had no choice but to write his name. He then woke up, realizing that his life would not be long.

He informed his close Daoist friend, Master Lu Shanyou. Yì's younger brother's wife, Lady Yu, was the daughter of a scholar-official named Cè. She did not eat pork. Yì reproached her, saying, "Our family is poor and unremarkable, unlike yours. How can we always have mutton? Why not try to eat according to our family's wealth or poverty?" The wife replied, "How dare I? But since I was a young girl, whenever I smelled the smell of roasted pork, I got a headache that I couldn't bear. Now I fear it, not because I choose to avoid it." Yì said, "If I were truly an official of Fengdu, I would certainly make you eat it." The wife smiled and said, "Fortunately, I have been helped by your strength to add this food. It is a great wish of the new wife." After a long time, Yì was transferred to the capital as an official. On his return journey, he died in a boat on the Si River. Initially, Yì's father had moved the family across the Si River and visited the Pu Zhaowang Temple. Yì's mother had given birth to him in the boat, and it was the same way when he died. The news of his death had not yet reached Wu. The family made a broth with pork to prepare a dish. The younger sister-in-law asked, "What kind of feast is this? It smells so fragrant." The family replied, "It's pork." The wife said, "Try giving it to me." She took it and ate it, finishing a whole dish immediately. From then on, she could eat it. At that time, it had been half a month since Yì died. The record was compiled from the house of the mountain, but one thing is not very detailed. Also, he was considered brave.

The Temple of the Second Prince In the seventh year of the Jinghe era, a small child in the marketplace of the capital city rode a hunting dog and proclaimed to the crowd, saying, "My elder brother sent me here. Yesterday at 3 PM, the Guankou Temple was burned by fire, and they intend to rebuild it here." The child was just seven years old. When asked about his hometown and name, he did not answer. By evening, the deity descended at the city gate and spoke through someone, fulfilling the child's desires. The officials reported this, and thus the Shenbao Temple was built. The people of the capital have always been fearful of this matter. From spring to summer, all the men and women of the city carried soil to help with the construction, calling it offering soil. They dressed as ghost messengers patrolling the gates, urging the offering of soil. The scene was bustling with activity, and signs were posted on the main streets, saying, "Someone offered soil." The wise considered it ominous. Soon there was an imperial edict to prohibit it. Later, there was a report from Sichuan that the Yongkang Temple was burned, and the date was the same. This child was later raised by the temple caretaker and was a dull and ordinary person.

**The Dragon of Xuanhe** In the first year of the Xuanhe era, in the fifth month, the capital city experienced continuous rain for several days. When the rain ceased, a person at a tea house located in front of the Kaifeng county office, still before dawn, began to clean the tables and benches, and saw something that seemed like a dog crouching by their side. By dawn, they looked at it and saw that it was a dragon. It made a sound like a cow, and the person was startled and fell to the ground. The tea house was adjacent to a military armory workshop. At that moment, the soldiers were just going on duty, and upon seeing the creature, they killed it and divided its meat among themselves. The local officials, fearing repercussions, dared not report the incident to the imperial court. The people of the capital, however, drew and played with the image of the creature. The dragon was six to seven chi in length. Its scales were a dark, bluish-black in color. Its head was like that of a cow, and its cheeks resembled a fish’s head, a bright green in color. It had horns on its head, and its body was extremely long. From the junction of its body, it began to split into two branches, a form similar to the dragons depicted in the world. More than ten days later, a sudden great flood attacked the capital city, rising ten zhang high. It began at the northwest Mu Tu Gang and extended to the Ma Jian outside the Wan Sheng Gate, submerging all the homes in its path. At the time, people believed that the great river had broken its banks. However, the water was clear and clean, and the river had not in fact overflowed. In the end, no one knew where the water had come from. After several days, the water had already entered the Bian Canal. By dawn, it was about to overflow. The court recruited people to take advantage of the wind and water’s momentum, and they breached the downstream channel. The water then flowed into the Wu Zhang River from the north of the city and poured down into Liangshan Lake. The flood lasted nearly a month before it finally subsided. Thus, the common people passed it down as the dragon’s revenge. This account is recorded in *Hou Shi Bu* by Cai Tao.

A House Rented in Wenzhou In the city of Wenzhou, there was a house that was always evil and strange. First, Zhong, the tax collector, lived there, and the entire family died. Several years later, Lü, another tax collector from Huangqizhen in Fuzhou, came to Wenzhou after being dismissed from office and also rented the house. He often saw Zhong's spirit with a bald head and a shaved body wandering between the western houses. There was a girl aged twelve or thirteen who was the most troublesome. She would wait for guests to arrive, always peering at them through the wall and laughing. She would toss and turn household items, making the windows and desks dirty, making it impossible to catch her. Only an old woman was very respectful and cautious. Every time the girl left, she would definitely scold her away. Lü's wife was sick for several days without getting better. The old woman taught her, saying, "Lady County has no other illness. Just boil Wu Ling San and take half a Liuxiu pill, and it will be enough." Lü thought her words were reasonable and hastily followed her advice. After one dose, she was cured. However, humans and ghosts lived together, and all the household items would shake at any time. Sometimes an empty sedan chair would move on its own in the hall, greatly worrying the whole family. Another day, the old woman again told him, "We have discussed together and want to welcome you as the master, planning to use the day of a certain month next month. If this plan succeeds, you will definitely not escape. You should quickly move to avoid disaster." Lü told Hu Jigao about this. Xiang Jigao was a low-ranking official in Fuzhou, and he knew him at the time. He also advised him to leave. On the day of leaving, dozens of men and women from the west house shouldered together to watch, looking at each other with regret, seeming to regret not planning it earlier. Later, no one dared to rent the house again. After one month, it was not finished. The county official brought his family. Someone told him the reason. The official smiled and said, "I am a ghost among humans. They are just ordinary people. What is there to fear? I lived there without doubt, and the ghosts also disappeared."

Stone Person Appearing in a Dream Grand Marshal Xi, stationed in Shu, was mourning the loss of his mother, Lady Zhu. He had decided to bury her in Qingcheng Mountain. One night, he dreamed of two people entering to pay respects. They walked heavily and slowly, and their bodies were covered with loathsome sores. They said, "The burial place of the late lady is in Wenzhou. The place name is Xu Family Upper Ao, facing Gēng Mountain and Jiǎ Direction. You must go to seek it. When the matter is completed, please treat my sores for me." Xi had once lived in Yongjia and also wanted to return. However, he was hesitant due to the distance and had not decided. Upon waking up, he found it strange and wrote the matter down in a book. Immediately, he prepared a boat and went east, taking his father's coffin, the Chancellor, back to Wenzhou. The burial day was approaching, but the place for the burial was still not decided. He invited Zhang Zao from Xiaoshan to divinate it. They stayed in a mountain temple together. His nephew, Qilang, just bought a house and went to the owner of the land. He told him the reason. The old man said, "One li away from here, named Xu Family Upper Ao. There is a tomb site facing Gēng Mountain and Jiǎ Direction. Many people consider it a auspicious place. Many have offered good prices to seek it, but the Xu family has not agreed. You should go and see it."

As it was getting late, he could not go. He returned home and told his wife. Before he finished speaking, Xie said, "Is it not the one facing north from a south-facing hill?" He took the written record of his dream and compared it with the current situation, finding no discrepancy. The next day, he personally visited the place. An old woman came out and said, "I am the wife of Mr. Xu. My husband once wanted to use this land to bury his father. He dreamed of a golden-armored deity holding a stick chasing him away, pointing to the person sitting on the mat and saying, 'This is the land of the gentleman's family. How could you do this without permission?' Since then, for forty years, now I give it to you, without taking money. My son is currently the village head and can become a county official, to exempt him from service is enough." Xie was greatly pleased beyond expectation, but did not know who the person in the dream was. After burying his parents, he then built his own tomb on the left side. The laborers dug the soil, and there was a clinking sound. When he looked, it turned out to be two stone figures lying beneath, buried for a long time, their bodies were worn through. Xie offered them wine as a sacrifice, carried them outside, ordered the repair and restoration with mud, and built a shrine. The sign read "Stone Figures from the Dream." Zhang Dayou said.

The Old Monk's Dream** In the third year of the Gandaosui era, Wang Guan, a military officer of the rank of Wujinglang, and Jiang Can, an official responsible for administrative duties, were both involved in government affairs. Wang Guan's younger brother, Wang Cong, went to visit Tianzhu (a Buddhist temple) on the Winter Solstice. The day before, Wang Cong borrowed a horse from Wang Guan. Wang Guan ordered a groom to deceive him with a large black horse from the imperial stables. That night, Wang Cong dreamed that an old monk came to visit him. The monk said, "I am old and lack the strength. If I can accompany you, I hope you will be lenient with the whip and punishment." Wang Cong was surprised and thanked him before waking up. The next day, when the horse arrived, he immediately mounted it and set out. After leaving the capital city gates, the horse became reluctant to move forward. Just as Wang Cong was about to whip it, he suddenly realized, "Is this not the dream I had last night?" He quickly handed the horse back to the groom and returned to the temple. Upon hearing about this, Jiang Can's government also decided not to keep the horse and ordered it to be returned to its original place. Wang Guan later recounted the story. **Nèi Bēn Yuǎn's Poem** In the winter of the first year of the Jingkang era, Nèi Bēn Yuǎn, who held the position of Tongzhishi at the枢密院 (Chúmìyuàn, Military Council), was sent as an envoy to negotiate peace with the Jurchens. He ceded the Hedong region to them as a bribe. On the twelfth day of the eleventh month, he arrived at Jingle County. The city gates were already closed, and the local people climbed onto the city walls, gouging out his eyes and cutting his body into pieces. At that time, his father, Nèi Yòngzhī, was still alive. In the eleventh year of the Shaoxing era, Zhang Zhū, who had traveled from the north to the south, passed through the Jingle post station and saw a poem written in blood on the wall. The locals said it was written by Nèi Bēn Yuǎn's spirit. The poem read: A meteor streaks through the sky, piercing my heart five times; Lightning splits my eyes, and my ribs are torn apart. As carriages and horses trample me, my head turns to dust; Crows and eagles peck at my bones, leaving them as ash. My father and brothers feel sorrow but can only mourn in vain; My children, unaware, do not grieve. Looking back at the river, I cannot return; In the afterlife, I vainly build a platform to gaze upon my homeland. Zhang Zhū recorded the poem and showed it to his son, Zhang Áng, who included it in his biography.

Mr. Shen Mr. Shen was a Daoist priest from Huzhou. It is unknown how he first attained the Dao. He was usually silent and did not deeply interact with people. When he was at leisure, he would freely talk about future good and bad fortunes, always hitting the mark. However, he would not answer questions about it. People gave him food, and he accepted it without refusal. When he had no affairs, he might go months without eating. During the Xuanhe period, there were those who mentioned his name to the court. He was summoned into the inner palace, but he refused to bow. When asked about his learning, he vaguely said nothing. Not meeting the emperor's approval, he was still considered a virtuous and simple scholar and sent back to his hometown. In the first year of Jianyan, autumn, he suddenly dressed in mourning clothes and stood at the gate of the city, cried out with his hands on his chest, and after a long time, turned around, looked back into the city, and laughed. Three days later, it stopped. Soon after, the notorious bandit Zhang Yu attacked and broke the city. The prefect led the state troops to defend the inner city, and the bandits could not capture it and then left. All residents outside the city were harmed. Two years later, he visited every market, farewelled people, and told them, "If you have rice, don't make porridge. If you have money, don't build a house." People could not understand his meaning, and from then on, no one knew where he went. That year, the enemy invaded Huai West, and Huzhou suffered the most severe calamity.

Li Ji's Roasted Chicken Fan Yinbin was transferred from Changsha to Lin'an. He bought wine with his guests and went up to the Shengyang Tower. There was a man selling roasted chicken who bowed twice to Fan and offered everything he carried as a gift. Upon seeing the man, Fan realized he was his former servant Li Ji, who had been dead for several years. Startled, Fan asked, "Are you not Li Ji?" Li Ji replied, "Yes." Fan said, "You have died and become a ghost. How can you be here again?" Li Ji laughed and said, "There are many people like me in the world. But people cannot recognize us. Look at the person sitting upstairs and those passing by on the road. They are all of us. We live among people, engage in trade and labor, and never cause harm. Not only here, but even Madam Zhao, who is frequently employed by the household as a laundry woman, is also a ghost." Fan returned home and told his wife Han about it. Han said, "Madam Zhao has come to our house for twenty years. How can we treat her as a ghost?" One day, when Madam Zhao came, Fan jokingly told her, "I heard you are a ghost, is it true?" Madam Zhao angrily said, "I have been with your household for a long time. Do not play with me." Fan said, "Li Ji told me like this. Show these stones to her." Madam Zhao's face changed. Suddenly, a sound like tearing silk was heard, and she disappeared. This story is similar to what is written in novels, probably a trick of ghosts. Both stories are said by Tang Shao Liu.

The Nine Depths Daoist Ritual in Wujiang The stone embankment of Wu Song River connects to the west of Taihu Lake. Ships going and coming often face the danger of wind and waves, sometimes leading to capsizing and drowning. In the third year of the Ganda period, Zhao Bo Hǔ served as the magistrate of Wujiang. Thinking of the souls trapped in the underworld, with nowhere to appeal, he gathered Taoist priests to perform the Nine Depths Daoist Ritual in the county administration, to liberate them. A man from Bian, Xue Shan, was a guest at the inn. He then explained to them the reason why his old friend Huang Sheng and his son drowned. He set up two altars for their worship. Three days after the ritual, Zhao Bo Hǔ was appointed as the Inspector of Equalization and was tasked with inspecting the military farming lands. He traveled with Xue Shan, crossing the lake to reach the Nine Li Temple. In the middle of the night, he dreamed that Huang Sheng visited him as if they were still alive, with a respectful demeanor, as if he had something important to say. Xue Shan thought he was coming to bid farewell before taking office. He asked him slowly. He replied that he had been separated from Wu Gate and had been in a difficult situation here for a long time. He had received great favor from the county magistrate, which helped him move on. He was finally able to leave. Xue Shan said, "Why not visit him once to thank him?" He replied, "He had visited many times, but the gate was very strict. No longer possible to enter. He dared to entrust this to his old friend." After that, he took leave and climbed onto the horse. Hundreds of people stood below the hall. Xue Shan joked, "How splendid are your carriages and horses?" Huang Sheng replied, "Not so. These are all those who were detained during my time, who were released due to Zhao's grace." After they parted, Xue Shan woke up in shock. He told Zhao Bo Hǔ about it. He then realized that the lake they crossed the day before was where Huang Sheng and his son had drowned.

A Dog of the Zheng Family During the reign of Emperor Huizong of Song, a man named Zheng, who held the title of Fengyi Lang, served as the magistrate of Le Ping County. He had brought a dog from his hometown, and the dog was usually tamed and well-behaved, not biting people. In the county, there was a woman who sold items like flower powders for a living. She frequently came in and out of the magistrate's residence. Zheng held her in high regard. One day, during the daytime, the dog entered the main building and rushed to bite her breast, causing her to fall and nearly die. Zheng scolded his servants to tie up the dog. Considering the dog had traveled far, he did not want to kill it and instead gave it to a monk at the Bao Ben Temple. That night, Zheng was robbed. After about half a month, the thieves were caught and interrogated, and it turned out that the woman who had been bitten by the dog was involved in the theft, acting as a guide for the thieves. Only then did Zheng realize the dog's spiritual awareness. He called the dog back and the monk named De Tao spoke about the incident.

Dream of the Houtu Temple The Houtu Temple in Fuzhou is renowned for its spiritual influence. A scholar from Yihuang named Zou Ji, before passing the imperial examinations, prayed for a dream. In his dream, he entered the temple, completed the formalities of worship, and turned to look at the eastern wall. There, he saw a large poem written on it and glanced and read it. Upon waking, he could remember it clearly. The poem read: Heavenly Way is fundamentally non-action, Justice brings clarity from below. Warmth and yellow come together, Darkness leads the way inside. Ten mouths stop separately, Frequent sounds of crying. Both reach sixty, This is the future. Zou found the words mostly unfavorable and feared he might die from an epidemic. Later, in the third year of the Zhìpíng era (1066 AD), he was recommended by the local authorities for the imperial examinations. The topic of his essay was "Heavenly Way is non-action and things come to completion." The following year, during the provincial examination, the topic was "Justice brings clarity." The order of seating had scholars from Wenzhou in front and those from Huangzhou behind. At that time, the emperor's illness led to the suspension of court examinations, and the prophecy of the first two lines of the poem began to be fulfilled. Zou eventually served as the Jiangxi Censor, a high-ranking official. The phrase "ten mouths stop separately" referred to the character "路" (road), and "frequent sounds of crying" symbolized ongoing distress or sorrow, representing the nature of criminal cases. He and his wife both died at the age of sixty-five. The forty characters of the poem, though brief, predicted the topics of the two examinations, the seating order, the political events, the ultimate length of his official career, and the ages of his wife and himself. Truly, it was extraordinary.

Lord of Mount Tai Lei Du Lei Du of Lin'an. His courtesy name was Shize. He was of a straightforward and upright character, fond of reading. Although he was listed among those recommended for the imperial examinations, he refused to take the provincial examination. His nephew, Cai Zhifu, served as the Military Governor of Yongkang. After taking up his post, in the last day of the ninth month of that year, Cai's wife, Xu, had a dream in which someone handed her a scroll of writing, resembling an official edict from the central government. Xu read it through, and upon waking, she could only recall that at the end of the paper, in large letters, it said, "Lord of Mount Tai Lei Du signed." Fearing it as an omen of ill fortune, and not knowing whether Lei Du was safe, Xu did not speak of it. Within a few days, Cai died, and his wife and children carried his coffin back home. The following year, when they returned to their village, they learned that Lei Du had died in the eighth month of the previous year. Was the dream of Mount Tai's deity truly a sign? These two events were recorded by Wu Bishuo of Lin'an.

*Yijian Zhi* Volume 10, Chapter 11 **The Lady of the Fang Family** A young unmarried woman from the Fang family of Pujiang, Wuzhou, was bewitched by a demon. Every day after noon, she would adorn herself with flowers and go to bed, sleeping for two hours before waking up. She would always have a flushed face, as if she had been drinking, and look very happy and radiant. Her sister asked her why she acted this way, and she replied, "I cannot explain it. There is no such pleasure in the world." The Taoist priests tried a hundred different methods to cure her, but instead, she suffered humiliation. Sometimes, they revealed her hidden secrets, saying, "You have been associating with a woman from a certain family. Given your current situation, how dare you treat me?" They would slap her face and knock off her hat, and she would leave in a disheveled state. Local sorcerers heard about it, but none dared to go to her house. She cleansed the room and burned incense, prepared a formal complaint, and sent a servant to Guixi to report to the Celestial Master Zhang of Longhu Mountain. On the day the servant arrived, the woman was sitting in the hall when she saw two yellow-robed officials coming to arrest her. At first, she refused to go, but they reassured her, saying, "Lady, you are not suffering. Once the matter is resolved, you will return immediately." She then followed them away. All the places she passed through were familiar to her. Soon, she arrived at the Eastern Peak Shrine, where they led her into a small hall below. The hall faced north, and the master ordered her to ascend the hall.

The girl secretly looked at his attire. He wore a purple robe with a red belt and a fish-shaped pendant, completely like the attire of today's attendants. He warned her, saying, "You are a mountain demon. Your path is winding and twisting, but I have already fully known it. You should just state it directly, and I will release you." At the beginning, when the girl was afflicted, it was actually her deceased uncle who was the matchmaker. That day, he was first in the courtyard and winked at the girl, making her not speak. The girl ultimately concealed the matter and only spoke of the demon's appearance and those she drank and flirted with. The judge ruled, saying, "The chief villain and his ten accomplices will be flogged on the back and exiled far away, never to be released again, but without branding their faces. The remaining fifty or sixty people will also be flogged on the buttocks and placed under surveillance." The prisoners were sentenced and sent away, not much different from the world. Then, he ordered two soldiers to send the girl back. At that time, the family saw the girl lying on the ground for more than two hours, her mouth and eyes both closed. They pried open her teeth and administered medicine, applied needles and moxibustion, but she was unresponsive. However, her body was not cold, knowing that she was not dead. The servant returned, saying, "After submitting the petition, the Celestial Master ruled to send her to the Eastern Peak within a short time to resolve it. Therefore, it was so swift." From then on, the girl was safe and sound as usual. After a year, she married, as if she were still a maiden.

#### High Professor A local man named Gao Yu, courtesy name Guangsheng, was the son-in-law of Qin Changshi and lived in a town outside of Kuaiji. He was friends with Zhan Daozi, whose given name was Kangzong. During the year Xin Yi of Shaoxing (1151 CE), when the Huai region was under attack, Gao Yu entered the city and stayed at Zhan's house. He spent days with Zhan's guest, Chen Qian, and they got along very well. In the second year of Longxing (1164 CE), Gao Yu became a scholar at the Imperial Academy. One day, Chen Qian and his wife dreamed that Gao Yu came to visit, but he appeared very short. Chen Qian told his wife, "It has been only a few months since I last saw Gao Guangsheng, and he looks so short now." Soon after, he followed her into their bedroom. His wife was displeased and said, "A respected professor like Gao should understand proper conduct. Why would he come into our private quarters?" She chased him away and woke up in a panic. The next day, she told Zhan about the dream. At that time, Gao Yu was already seriously ill, and Zhan was worried about him. Upon hearing the news, Zhan was very unhappy. That night, the news of Gao Yu's death arrived. The following year, Chen Qian's wife had another dream where someone carried a coffin into the house. She asked who it was, and they replied, "It is Gao Yu." Upon waking, she told Chen Qian, who realized that Gao Yu had come to be their son. They prepared for childbirth, and on that day, a son was born. These two stories were told by Zhan Daozi. #### Plundering Magistrate (No additional content provided in the source text)

The Deputy Governor of Yangzhou, Shen, lost his name and hometown. While in office, he was known for his integrity and uprightness. Deputy Governor Rao Huaiqing, in particular, knew him well. When Huaiqing was relieved of his duties and returned to his hometown of Linchuan, the officials of the prefecture bid him farewell at Guazhou. The night before, Shen heard someone speaking outside his window, saying, "Tomorrow, your good fortune will end, and your horse will fail you." When he told his family, he looked solemn and unhappy. The next day, as he was about to mount his horse, his son held onto his clothes to stop him. Shen said, "I have a close relationship with Deputy Governor Rao, who is leaving for a long journey. How could I not send him off?" So he rode his horse straight ahead. The horse he was riding was borrowed from the army, and it was very bad. As soon as he left the city, the horse ran away and fell, and his foot got caught in the stirrup, and he could not get free. He galloped forty miles and only stopped at Guazhou. The driver caught up with him, and his face was all broken, and his flesh was bloody and blurred, and he could not be recognized. He was carried back to his residence, and he was barely alive, and he died after one day. Huaiqing felt sorry that he had died, and he gave him twenty thousand strings of cash as a gift, and the prefecture sent more than ten men to escort his coffin back home. The other officials on the road looked at each other, and it felt as if they were carrying ice and frost. If they were slightly careless, their heads would hurt as if struck by something. Those walking alongside saw a green-robed official sitting on the coffin, holding a stick and looking around. This continued until they reached home. After more than a year, his wife Yan, during the day, saw flags moving slowly in the air. A man riding a white horse descended slowly. When he arrived, it was Shen. He comforted and patted her for a long time, and then called out to their children, teaching them about reading and farming. He said, "I am now the Chief of Surplus, with great achievements and glory. Do not forget me." He flew away gracefully, and from then on, he did not come again. Yan's younger brother, Rong, recorded this story.

**Raw Meat as Part of the Drinking Ceremony** The Zeng family from Nanfeng was married into the Li family of Linchuan. When the groom first went to the bride's home for the wedding, his mother-in-law, Lady Zhang, sent him off. As the year drew to a close, she urgently requested to return home. The Li family held a farewell banquet. Lady Zhang returned home and was displeased, saying, "While I was at the Li family's home for several days, I was favored by their main courtesies, which were not light. But at the time of our departure, they suddenly served raw meat as part of the drinking ceremony, which made me feel very uncomfortable." When asked about the details, she said, "There was one plate of sheep, one plate of pork, one plate of duck, and one plate of chicken, totaling four dishes. Each dish was arranged on four large plates, all containing live animals. Although the food was abundant, it was not suitable for consumption." The family members also expressed their surprise, saying, "We never expected the Li family to be so rustic and crude." A village woman named Deng Ba, who was a guest at Lady Zhang's home, privately told others, "How could there be such a thing? Did the lady see something else?" Lady Zhang's husband was a military judge in Guanghua. He did not bring his family with him when he went there. After a long time, he received news that he had died. Later, his son returned and said that his father had died just before the end of the previous year. On the day he died, his colleagues prepared a sacrifice according to local customs, using live animals in four large plates. The names and types of the utensils used were exactly the same as those Lady Zhang had seen. It turned out that Lady Zhang had left the Li family's home on the day her husband had just died and was receiving his funeral rites. The events matched perfectly, as if guided by a talisman. How amazing! How amazing! The following two matters are all as told by Li Deyuan.

**Huang Priest's Ritual** Hui Dao Bi, a political figure, had a wife named Zhao. On October 16, 1151, Zhao died from illness. After seven days, her son-in-law Hu Changwen Yuanzhi invited the Daoist priest Huang Zai Zhong to conduct a ritual for the spirits of the dead. The effects of the ritual were remarkable, and Hui Dao Bi was deeply impressed. After another seven days, Hu Changwen Yuanzhi again invited Huang Zai Zhong to conduct a ritual for the spirits of the dead. Three days before the ritual, they performed a soul-calling and cleansing ceremony. The young boy Shu Jie, who was twelve years old, volunteered to enter the room to hold the banner and watch over the ritual. As he entered, he began to cry and said, "Mother appeared from under the white banner, sitting on a chair, with her feet dangling into the basin. On either side of the basin, her clothes were hung. She raised her head and looked around, and then suddenly disappeared." He wept bitterly. Later, when the soul was brought to the eastern side of the ancestral altar, Huang Zai Zhong saw Zhao sitting there. Shu Jie, upon seeing her, fell to the ground and said, "Mother is here." A servant named Xiao Nu, who had previously died due to illness and swelling, was also mentioned in the ritual.

A group of people followed, and they spoke for a long time. Huang was concerned that the ghostly energy might harm the child, so the deity spread qi and blew on his face, then took a cup of soup and ordered him to drink, after which he woke up. He said he had just gone to the city gate to see the welcoming of the fairy, and saw dozens of people wearing golden brocade robes, carrying a sedan chair adorned with golden phoenixes at all four corners, their mouths holding golden thread balls. Two fairy children led the way, carrying golden incense burners and spittoons. They arrived at his home, and the fairy stepped out of the sedan chair, saw Master Huang, and bowed twice to ask for a talisman. Only then did he receive the talisman, put it away in his sleeve, and then rode a golden-haired gazelle, with the two children leading him away. Huang then woke up. What he had seen were those who summon souls at the altar on the west side of the temple. It was already the fifth drumbeat, and he only then fell asleep. He then dreamed of entering the main gate, where a general about ten feet tall, wearing golden armor and blue boots, led the way. In the hall, people were wearing blue clothes, blue hats, and holding blue jade tablets, sitting on a dragon throne, saying it was the Lord of Salvation of the One Supreme.

Hearing the summons, the Second Division and the chief of the Nine Heavens' Life-and-Death Register were invited to sit together. Suddenly, blue clouds arose in the sky. Hundreds of jade maidens carried red banners and canopies. They welcomed the sixth position from the Palace of Purity. They shared immortal fruits together. Shu Jie observed the scene. A strange ghost in the form of a lion appeared and was forced to leave. The general shouted, "The Savior of Suffering has been summoned to address the sins. How can you just drive him away?" He ordered the prison guards to chop the ghost into pieces. Countless heavenly deities on either side played freely and without restraint, some wearing broken jade flower crowns that shook and made a sound, described as "wolf-mang crowns." A high-ranking deity from the heavens descended to inspect the underworld. The general said, "Each of the three realms has its own nature. The heavenly realm is free and unburdened, so it is filled with joy. Human society is governed by rituals and laws, so it values respect and humility. The underworld punishes human sins, so it values severity and might. They are fundamentally different." He also heard the summons for the judge of the underworld. Prisoners were pursued and lined up in the courtyard, approximately ten thousand in number, all carrying iron collars. The tenth person was called out.

A celestial officer, riding the cloud and holding a document, descended to fetch the person. The document was about two feet wide and ten feet long, and it reached directly to the ground. The officer took the person up to the cloud and left. Other instruments of torture, such as fire wheels, copper pillars, copper dogs, and iron snakes, were being forged in front of them. The pain and suffering were at their utmost. Three men in official robes were among the prisoners. The general said, "One was cruel and tyrannical in governance, the second was disrespectful to his father, and the third was corrupt as a tax official." The tax official was the general's brother, who had once served as the tax collector in Tanzhou but stole public funds and fled. He was still in prison. The cruel and tyrannical one faced the heaviest punishment. Shu Jie asked how they could be saved. The general replied, "Except to recite the 'Nine Heavens Birth of the Divine Chapter' ten thousand times, they can be rescued." He then led Shu Jie to various prisons, such as cauldrons of boiling water, hot stones, and sizzling iron. Shu Jie observed the prisoners there. He asked the general, "I dare to ask what your name is." The general replied, "In the past, I was named Wang in the human world. This question has no name here." He added, "Every time I see people setting up water and land offerings to the officials of the underworld, calling officials like Cui Judge and Li Judge, they all refuse to come."

It would be better to simply call the judge of the Xth bureau and Yth case. It was said that I got a very narrow tent. My body is not there, but I am always on the altar listening to commands. I dare not leave a step, and even a word or two must pass through my hands. Then I present it. I first look at the Three Pure Ones, then at the sincerity of the master. Even if it's just a bowl of plain soup, I would still present it. It is because the tent (or office) was not arranged properly, and there were many impurities around. The yellow-robed person's incense clothes were not clean, and the water-carrying person's body was foul-smelling. A green-shirted child brought a baby to play around the position of the Heavenly Venerable, which angered the Heavenly Venerable. They were all followed and locked up. The green words (ritual texts) were very good. The underworld was opened, and the pardon was also sincere. Especially because the judge's voice was strong, but the pronunciation was not accurate. One word was read as "tan," and several people could not clear it up. The head official reported and requested the release of six people.

The official secretly remarked that the decree of clemency was unclear. It would fall again among the four, but only two would be pardoned. One of them was from the Zhao family. The general said, "Your father often scolded you for being lazy and not studying. I will teach you a spell to enhance wisdom: *Unobstructed and wide-reaching wisdom, Yulü Shahe Wu Jinjie.*" He also recited a verse for wisdom: *Vast as heaven and earth, unobstructed and unimpeded. One realm, vast and boundless as the sea. One grind, two grinds, turning without awareness. Only when one awakens is there unobstructed emptiness.* He warned them not to reveal this. Every time a festival or seasonal event occurs, they should burn incense and silently recite it a hundred times. He also said, "The human mind is like a mirror; it must be constantly polished, and must not be stained by dust. Then wisdom will arise naturally." He continued, "I have five roles in one body. The first is the chief introducer under the Three Heavens' Gate. The second is Mr. Huang, who oversees written matters. The third is the master of Natural Mountain. The fourth is the overseer of the Ash River. The fifth duty is minor and cannot be spoken of." Then he led Shu Jie to the Ash River. The innocent crossed the bridge, while those burdened with heavy karma had their lower garments removed.

A Man Wearing Underwear While Crossing the River He was wearing a type of undergarment ("裈") and crossed the river. On the riverbank, there were several large, dead trees. Ghost soldiers hung his clothes on these trees. A vehicle followed the bridge as he continued his journey. Each piece of clothing had a name written on it. He looked at one of the labels, which read "Shu Shi Shisiang." Shu Jie was about to return home and bowed to the general, saying, "Since I arrived in the underworld, I have been grateful for your kindness." The general replied, "What do you have to thank me for? I should be the one thanking you. I recall that we once served together in the same office. Once, due to my position, I was in a difficult situation, and you helped me out. I have not forgotten this favor. Now, as I return, I will share everything I have seen and heard in the underworld with others, so they may be aware and cautious. Nothing should be hidden." They bid farewell and continued on their way. As he approached his home, he saw that his mother was in a room, having completed some preparations. She was led to a platform, and she said, "Mr. Huang does not allow a filial son to ascend the platform." The mother then ascended the platform alone, performing a ritual of respect to all the positions or individuals on the platform. She approached the tent of Mr. Huang, burned incense, and bowed, saying, "Thank you for saving me from suffering." Mr. Huang, a Taoist priest, then ascended into the sky gracefully.

He told them that all the grudges from past lives had been resolved, and he should not grieve or be distressed. He ordered a servant to take water from a bowl and sprinkle it on his uncle's face while shouting at him. He then woke up. It was just getting light, and from sleeping to waking, it had only been a few moments. The experiences and things seen or heard were so numerous that they could not be recounted over several days. Hui Gong observed the events with a certain attitude. The banquet hall had a dragon and tiger hall placed near the kitchen area, and there was a lot of noise and activity. The person lighting incense was an old soldier, and the soldier who was suffering from scabies was the one who drew water. Children in the garden often played in front of the sacred place, and everything matched what was said. He then informed the dragon and tiger gods and moved their positions to a quieter place, changing the people responsible for certain duties. He forbade the children from coming. He also reviewed the error in the word "潭." The old text mentioned that someone might die in the waves. A Taoist child wrote "涛" as "淘" and moistened his fingers with saliva, writing the character but not very clearly, causing readers to misunderstand. Hui Gong wrote a record of 5,000 words, and this is a summary of the main points. Zhu Xinzhong's Dream During the second year of the Xining era, Zhu Xinzhong, a censor, served as the governor of Yanzhou. In a dream, he reached the foot of a large mountain, and people pointed out that it was Kunshan. Soon after, he was transferred to Xuanchou. The map presented by Xuanchou included a place named Kunshan, and he thought his previous dream had come true. Another year later, he was transferred to Pingjiang, and Kunshan was indeed one of its subordinate counties. While in Pingjiang, he dreamed that an official reported that Hong Neihuan was coming. He rushed out to greet him, and it turned out to be his older brother. His older brother was a former Hanlin Academy scholar living in his hometown. After sitting down, he sat on the eastern side. Upon waking, he found it strange. Within two months, Zhu Xinzhong was dismissed, and his older brother indeed followed him.

### The Mason of Changshu Zhongdashi Wu Wenyi, from Dezhou, held multiple positions as a prefect. Later, he settled in Changshu County, Pingjiang. Upon completing the construction of his new mansion, he began to dream every night of seven people in white robes descending from the roof ridge. He informed his family, but no one could understand the meaning of these dreams. Soon after, he fell ill and did not recover. His son, suspecting something supernatural, ordered laborers to climb the roof and inspect it thoroughly. They found seven paper figures among the tiles. It turned out that the mason had been dissatisfied with his wages and had performed a curse to harm the owner. At that time, Wang Xian Dao Huan served as the prefect of the region. Upon hearing of the incident, he arrested all the craftsmen and sent them to prison, where they were flogged and exiled to distant states. The custom of the people of Wu was that during tile-laying, even in the height of summer, family members would climb the roof to inspect it, fearing such curses. Wu Wenyi, having moved north, was unaware of this custom, and thus his evil plan failed. ### The Child from the Tea Shop In the sixth month of the fifth year of the Gandaodao era, a family from a tea shop in Pingjiang lost their ten-year-old son. The parents searched for him for several days, leaving only their young daughter to guard the house. One day, a soldier in a yellow robe came to drink tea and said, "Your young master asked me to deliver a message. He will return you on a wooden raft soon." The daughter, pleased, pleaded with the soldier to stay a little longer so she could wait for her parents to return. The soldier firmly refused and, as he was leaving, added, "Tomorrow, your young master will send a letter from another place." He then departed. That evening, the parents returned, and the daughter told them everything that had happened. They could not understand why and worried that the message was not good news. The next morning, they heard that a body had been found floating beside a wooden raft near the Shengping Bridge. Rushing to see, they discovered it was their lost son. A bystander mentioned that a messenger had drowned in that spot a few years ago. The daughter's vision, it seemed, was of a ghost.

The Spirit of Le Bridge** A woman from a household in Le Bridge, Pingjiang, was married. Every night, she was troubled by a demonic entity. Her mother was deeply concerned and decided to sleep in the same bed with her to observe the situation. As night fell, a person leaped up from the ground, with two braids hanging from the back, wearing red silk that shone brightly. The sound was like a thunderclap, and the ground also closed. This happened for several nights in a row. The mother then told her husband, who dug into the ground to find the entity. He found a copper bell about two feet deep, with a red ribbon tied to its nose. They then remembered that several years ago, the court had strictly enforced copper restrictions, so they had buried the bell in the ground and forgotten it after a long time. They immediately broke it and threw it away. The girl's illness then healed. The above four events were all told by Zhu Sishu. **Liu Jingwen** Chengyi Lang Ren Suicheng was the nephew of Ji Sun, Liu Jingwen. He said that when Jingwen was governor of Xinzhou, he would visit the shrine of Duke Wen of Jin every few days. When he arrived, he would converse with the deity, and after some time, he would leave. The deity also occasionally entered the city. City officials saw Jingwen shut the door and talk with guests, and then the deity had come. One day in a large gathering, he told one of the clerks, "The Heavenly Emperor will come to summon you. You will then leave. I will follow shortly." The guests looked at each other in alarm. Not long after, the clerk indeed passed away without illness. Jingwen also died shortly after. After a night, he suddenly revived, asked for a brush to write three poems, and spoke the poems to his family. The poems said, "I now hold office in the Thunder Department. I am no longer a person of the world." He closed his eyes and died. One of the chapters says, "The central palace is in the middle of the sky. Above it is my home. Various phoenixes dance. Often the magic grass blooms. Waving goodbye to the world."

I leap into the clouds and mist. During my leisure, I compose poetry about the sky and sea. I am not part of the clamor of the world. The second chapter says: The fairy realm is not of this world. Heavenly gods surround the halls and palaces. The mist and clouds are evenly distributed at all levels. Coiling dragons are everywhere to the left and right. Cloud-capped mountains rise up. The wind makes the earth and sky tremble. From here, one receives old favors. Ten thousand things change at the tip of the brush. The third chapter says: From ancient times, heroes have been worn down. How many more things are laughable in the world. The hexagram *Gen* (Mountain) above and *Xun* (Wind) below signify advancement. A cart safely crosses the Milky Way. These words are all difficult to understand. According to the *Dongpo Ji*, Wang Anshi served as the governor of Xizhou and passed away there. This text mentions Xinzhou, which is likely incorrect. *Chunzhu Jiwén* records that Wang Anshi dreamed of taking the place of Confucius and passed away. The accounts differ. Su Shi praised Wang Anshi's poetry, saying: "The four seas know that my white hair is full. Did I ever place chrysanthemums on Double Ninth?" His poetry is so clear and alert. Now these three poems are like that. Is the separation of life and death as profound as this?

The Poem of the Woman from Yongxi In the Yongxi Temple in Suzhou, every night around midnight, a woman often appears in the corridors, singing short verses. She laughs and sighs, and those who hear her come to her, but she disappears. Her words go: "All around me are mountains and rivers, reminding me of old journeys. On the sandbanks, flowers and grass play with the softness of spring. At the long pavilion, the *Mulberry* boat rests. Good dreams easily fade away like water. Her tender heart is empty, following the morning clouds in sorrow. Travelers should not climb the tower looking towards the capital." Those who appreciate such things often record and keep it. A scholar named Mou Ruanqing saw it and was shocked, saying, "This was written by my deceased wife. No one else knows." The guest told the reason, and Mou Ruanqing grieved and sighed. This temple is where her coffin is located. These two stories are found in Zhou Zich's *Lesser Concealed Bamboo Slope Poetry Talk*.

Yijian Zhi Volume Eleven, Sixteen Stories** **Li Tie-di (Iron Flute)** Cao Yu-shan, a Taoist priest from Raozhou, was a scholar of Daoist learning and was sent to the capital as a tribute during the reign of Emperor Huizong (1111-1118). He became acquainted with Li Tao-zhen, a Taoist priest from Hebei who loved to play the iron flute and was a man of unrestrained and free-spirited nature. After returning to his hometown, Cao served as the deputy of the Shenxiao Palace in 1121. Li came from the capital to see him. Li had a horse, which was kept at a civilian's house forty li away. At that time, the Jinfu Temple was used as a palace. Every time he played the flute at the palace gate, the horse would come on its own without needing a driver. They spent more than a month together. One day, they parted ways. Just as Cao entered the city, Li did not meet him. Li wandered around for a long time, turned to the Taoist boy Zhou Yong-zhen, and asked for a brush and inkstone. He wrote a poem on the wall: Since our parting, four springs have passed. Now we meet again at the Shenxiao Palace. The furnace's climate and the elixir are just beginning to mature. The sword in the scabbard has a divine glow. We have not yet ridden the phoenix chariot to the azure heavens. For now, we will follow our own path and disdain the mundane world. Riding the wind, we will pass by the Qiang Lushan temporarily. One day, we will meet again to pay homage to the Purple Palace. He wrote after the poem: "Hidden True Wanderer, visiting Cao the Deputy at the Palace in Xiaoxiang, left a message when he did not meet him." Just as he threw down the brush, Cao happened to return. Zhou Yong-zhen told him, but Li had already disappeared. The following year.

A guest, dressed in a white robe with black sashes, entered Cao's room and looked at the characters on the wall. He asked who had written them, and Yongzhen replied that it was Mr. Li Taizhen. The guest laughed and said, "Nine hundred Han." He then asked for a writing tool and wrote on the opposite wall, referring to himself as Daoist Li Baoyi. He wrote: "A single golden pill prolongs life, Who else but me? The divine turtle ascends to the clouds, The colorful phoenix returns to the Earth Mother's mount. In the chaos of the primordial void, I seek the pure white, In the Kunlun Mountains, I gather the precious branches. Only a thousand days of effort are needed, To cultivate a being with eight corners and long edges." After finishing, he left. Three years later, a man surnamed Cui came. He read the two poems and laughed. At that time, Yongzhen was also present. Cui stared for a while and abruptly said, "Did your teacher ever eat meat?" Yongzhen replied, "Yes." Cui said, "Wasn't it you who bought it for him?" How could he be like that? He grabbed Yongzhen's ears repeatedly and then slapped him to the ground, leaving straight away. Originally, Yongzhen was dull-witted, but by then, he became quite intelligent. Later, he changed his name to Yanzhao and became a Daoist. The poems of the two Li (Li Taizhen and Li Baoyi) were once engraved on stone in the palace. During the Jingkang era, the Shenxiao Temple was abandoned and later restored as the Jianfu Temple. The stone was destroyed by a monk. Cao and Shàn lived to be eighty-five years old and died in the fourth year of the Gandaodao era. Zhou said.

**The Wet Nurse of the Zhu Family** A man from the countryside named Zhu Hanchen. During the Xuanhe period, he served as an official at the Imperial Academy. His wet nurse died, and she was interred in a Buddhist temple. When the Zhu family returned to their hometown, they had no time to cremate her bones. Zhu's wife's younger brother, Li Yuanchong Jingshan, was staying at a guesthouse in the capital. One night, he dreamed of an elderly woman wandering aimlessly in his room. The next night, he dreamed again, and she wept and said, "I am the wet nurse of the Zhu family. I died away from home. Now I am lodged in a certain alley and temple, which is very inconvenient. I wish my uncle could carry me back. Li Bai, there are many coffins in the temple. How can I mark mine? It is on the western side of the temple. There are two bamboo poles planted on the grave. The one to the south is longer, and the one to the north is shorter. The inscription on the coffin is still there. If you search for it, you will find it." Li awoke and could not sleep again. He quickly took paper and a pen to write down the details. The next morning, he went to visit the place. Following the instructions, he found the temple and told the monk in charge. The monk removed the coffin and cremated it. He wrapped the ashes and gave them to a servant. The monk then said that there were hundreds buried there. When they first arrived, every night they heard singing, shouting, laughing, and joking. Finally, there was much weeping. Especially on moonlit nights, it was so intense that they could not sleep. Now it has been a long time, and they no longer fear it. Li returned to Fanyang. Three days before he arrived, the Zhu family dreamed of the woman coming, with a happy expression. She said, "I have been in a strange place for a long time, feeling very lonely. Thanks to my second uncle Li, I am being carried back. I am almost there." The whole family was filled with sorrow and sent someone to meet him on the road. The monks properly buried her.

The Ghost of Huayan Well Liu Yanshi, after passing the imperial examinations and returning home, arranged a water-land offering with his younger brother at the Sizhou Hall of Yongning Temple. After the gathering dispersed, they stayed in the temple. The master monk Ji Deng, supervising his disciples to gather the offerings, noticed that the outer door was not closed. He questioned the servants, who all said that Liu Yanshi and his brother had been asleep for a long time. Holding a candle, he inspected the room, which was empty and no one was there. The bedding was also missing. Suspecting they had gone elsewhere, he noticed that the three gates were also locked. Ji Deng exclaimed, "This must be the ghost of Huayan!" He quickly ordered to fetch the bell and mallet to investigate it. Previously, a monk from the Huayan Temple in the west corridor had hanged himself from a chestnut tree near the well in the courtyard. He sometimes appeared as a ghost. Ji Deng passed by the west side and found a discarded blanket. At the wall of the Huayan Temple, he also found a single shoe. The temple monk was sleeping deeply. Ji Deng pushed open the door and entered, going straight to the well. Liu Yanshi and his brother were indeed sitting facing each other on the well, alternately raising their hands to push and invite, showing a posture of politeness and yielding. Ji Deng immediately helped them back. After they woke up, they asked about the cause. Liu Yanshi said, "We were tired all night. Just as we were getting into bed to sleep, the monk came to convey the master's intention, saying that the night was still early and he was just heating water to wait for us. Please have a leisurely conversation. So we followed him. Completely unaware of the wall's barrier, soon we heard the sound of women singing and laughing. A grand and luxurious house, shining brightly. Someone led us into the door. Thinking about walking together with his brother, it was hard to decide who should go first. As we were trying to avoid each other, we suddenly found ourselves in darkness and could see nothing. Without the master's help, we would have all fallen into the well and died." Liu Yanshi's courtesy name was Lidao.

Shi San [Female] Zhang Yuanzhong, a resident of a state, lived on a major thoroughfare opposite the residence of Governor Dong. Dong's household held a lavish feast that included both aquatic and terrestrial delicacies.
