Zhang dreamed that a deceased woman, identified as Shi San [Female], came to him and said, "I haven't been to your house for a long time. Today, I was invited by Governor Dong, but since the guests have not yet gathered, I wish to stay temporarily for a short while." Zhang remembered that she was dead and did not respond. She then said, "In the past, I bought a maid from you, and you promised to pay me 5,000 coins as a fee. I have not received this payment, and I have been waiting for it for a long time. I cannot come here unless it is the right time. Please pay me." Zhang woke up feeling uneasy. The next day, he bought a bundle of paper money and burned it at the bridge of Danshui Lake. That night, he dreamed again, and she said, "The debt of 5,000 coins that I owe you, you do not need to pay in full. If you do not give it to me, I will file a lawsuit against you. Do not regret it at that time." Zhang had no choice but to pay her the required amount, which he gave to a temple monk to recite sutras. Afterward, he sighed, "To speak with ghosts and to pursue an unnamed debt, how can I not have a shortened lifespan in the world?" Indeed, he died eight years later.

**The Carpenter's Festival** In Fanyang, the local custom is to sacrifice animals to serve the gods. Those who are too poor to afford a whole animal buy a pig's head and four feet to enjoy, which is called "head and feet wish." The carpenter Hu Six was ill, and his wife prepared offerings on New Year's Eve for the festival. She placed five items in a pot and awaited the shaman. During the festival season, there are many rituals. The shaman came at dusk. The wife sent her daughter to fetch the food. The daughter ran back and told her mother, "Mother, go and fetch it yourself." She said, "I want to see my father," and looked very frightened and discouraged. The mother went to the kitchen, removed the cover, and lifted the meat. One of the feet was missing. In a hurry, without time to investigate, she bought another piece of meat to replace it. Afterward, the daughter then said, "I was just about to enter the kitchen." She saw a black thing wriggling through the house, top to bottom, and couldn't tell what it was. So she was startled and ran out. Several days later, the carpenter died.

Zhaoshe Gets Recommended Li, an official of Poyang County, had a dream in the seventh month of the fourth year of the Gande era. In his dream, he left the city and passed by the Dongyue Imperial Palace. On the road, he saw an old colleague carrying official documents out from the center. The colleague said, "This is the list of those recommended for the autumn examination in our prefecture. I came here to write to the Lord of Mount Dongyue." Li asked, "Who among my acquaintances and fellow villagers has been recommended?" The colleague replied, "Only Zhao Zhe from your alley." In the dream, Li thought there was no such person and felt doubtful. The colleague said, "He is the son of Zhao Yishi, the physician." Li said, "This is someone from my nearby village whom I am familiar with. His name is Zhongxing, not Zhe." The colleague said, "I do not speak falsely. You will know this yourself." Then he left. At that time, this official had been dead for several years. Li found it strange. He went out to visit Zhao Zhe and wanted to talk about what he had seen in the dream. They met on the road. Zhao Zhe said, "I have already submitted a guarantee document. Last night, I dreamed that someone advised me, saying, 'The court is now promoting the cause of peace and prosperity, but my name is still Zhongxing, and it is also the same as the national surname, which is not good. If I can change it, that would be better.' I am very puzzled by this dream. I will consult with my friends." Li laughed and told him everything he had seen. He advised Zhao Zhe to change his name to Zhe and said, "If you are recommended and sent, I will marry my daughter to you." That year, Zhao Zhe was indeed listed by the Ministry of Rites. Li then accepted him as a son-in-law.

A White-Clothed Woman During the Xuanhe era, a man from the countryside named Dong, a scholar, was at the state school. While using the restroom, he saw a white-clad woman wandering in front of him. He asked her why she was there. She replied, "I am a woman from the vegetable garden. My husband has passed away, and I feel lost and have no place to belong." Dong kept talking with her and informed her about the location of the study hall. That night, she came to sleep with him. Soon after, he fell ill. One of his fellow students, who knew about the incident, reported it to the professor. The professor went to Dong's room and rebuked him, saying, "A scholar being preyed upon by a different kind of being, how did this happen?" He asked what Dong possessed. Dong replied, "I only had an undergarment left. I examined it, and it was dirty and without seams." The professor ordered it to be thrown into the fire and sent the students to trace its origin. An old gardener said, "Earlier, a child was herding sheep. A female sheep fell into the well in the west corridor and could not be retrieved. Now, a white-clad woman has appeared. Is she her ghost?" They called a Taoist priest to perform a ritual, chanting and throwing black beans into the well. The strange occurrence ceased completely, but Dong also died.

The Brocade Sachet Wang Dao, a man from Shiti in Dexing County, invited Gong Pang from Shangrao to stay as a guest during the sixteenth year of the Shaoxing era. The guesthouse was originally set up with two beds. Gong occupied the eastern bed, while the western bed was left empty to wait for other guests. On an autumn night, Gong had already gone to sleep, but the lamp was still lit, and he heard a rustling sound from the west bed. Soon, a woman pulled the curtain aside and stepped out. She wore an ornate headdress adorned with pearls, jade rings, and pearl earrings, and her strange and splendid clothing was unlike anything seen in paintings. Her beautiful and graceful appearance was mesmerizing. She came forward and approached, and Gong was filled with both joy and fear, asking with a serious expression, "Who are you, and how did you get here?" The woman replied, "You don't need to ask, Minister of Justice." Gong said, "I am a commoner, how could I deserve such a title?" The woman responded, "You will be listed in the provincial examination next year, and you will be promoted to a higher rank, securing your future." She then stayed for the night. At dawn, she wept and asked to leave, taking off the brocade sachet she was wearing and giving it as a parting gift. She said, "Keep this item secret, do not show it to anyone else, if even one person sees it, it will no longer be fragrant. After forty years, I will come back to retrieve it." She lingered affectionately for a long time before finally departing.

Gong and his wife took hands and went out of the door. They looked up at the Milky Way and she pointed to a large star, saying, "This is me." While carefully observing, something like a piece of white silk appeared, rising from the star and hanging down to the ground. The woman immediately ascended it. After going a few feet, she looked back and said, "Husband, quickly return to the room. If anyone asks, do not respond. If you disobey my words, you will bring great disaster." Then she slowly rose and disappeared. Gong remained standing and gazing with longing, unable to bear to leave. Suddenly remembering the previous warning, he quickly returned and closed the door. Not even a breath's time had passed when he heard someone knocking on the door. He refused to answer, and the visitor, angered and cursing, left. By morning, he saw the left bag that had been left behind. It was exquisitely embroidered brocade, not of this world. Inside contained a box like tortoiseshell, filled with fragrant incense, emitting a pungent fragrance that could not be described. He told Wang Gong in detail. Wang's son-in-law, Wang Qing, repeatedly asked to see it, but was not allowed. Under the influence of alcohol, he opened the box and played with it secretly, and the fragrance disappeared from then on. Gong indeed passed the imperial examination from then on, what is called the auspicious sign of the Censor-in-Chief, whether it is true or not is unknown. My relative Fu replaced Gong as a tutor and recounted this to Wang Gong. The Cow Plague Spirit In the sixth year of the Shaoxing era, in the village of Zhang in Yudan, the family had already gone to sleep. A shepherd boy was in the cowshed when he heard someone knocking on the door. He quickly got up to look and saw hundreds of strong men, all wearing five-flower armor and red helmets, suddenly entering. They then disappeared. By morning, fifty cows in the shed were all dead. It was said that they were plague spirits.

#### Cow Old Woman Dream In LePing County, HangQiao Market, there was a family named Cheng involved in dyeing. One day, they dreamed of an old woman who said, "I owe your family a certain amount of money. Except for what has already been repaid, I still owe a certain amount. Fortunately, there is one house left, which can make up the number. I will now take my leave." After bowing twice and taking her leave, she vanished. Upon waking up, they found a cow had died in an empty house. Selling the cow brought in the exact amount of money as told in the dream. #### Cheng the Buddhist Devotee In DeXing County, XinJian Village, there lived a family named Cheng. Behind their house, two hundred steps away, was a stream. Every morning, Cheng the Elder would carry his fishing gear, sit on a stone like a panther, and set his net. On the thirty-first year of his life, during the full moon of the first lunar month, he dreamed that someone told him, "Go to your fishing spot tomorrow and you will catch a fish large enough to swallow a boat." Waking up, he found it strange and went immediately after the rooster crowed. For a long time, he saw nothing. He thought, "Could the dream be deceiving me?" Suddenly, light rose from the water, illuminating the stones. He washed his face with water, calmed his mind, and observed carefully. There was only a large egg-shaped stone, white like snow, shining brightly. With one net, he caught it. Taking it home, his wife and children were all surprised and said, "How did you get firelight all over your body?" He took it and placed it on the Buddhist altar. The room was as bright as day. His wife looked at it and saw it turn into a dry red color. In an instant, it turned into a belt three feet long, no longer having the form of a stone. They were even more surprised and amazed. When he was about to burn incense, it had already grown as tall as a pillar, its length proportional. Fearing this, he went out and led his family to bow in a line.

Soon after, they heard a rumbling sound coming from inside the house. Looking through a small gap, they saw it as if people were throwing scattered coins. The wife carried a bamboo basket and casually stored more than ten coins. As she was carrying them away, the basket was already full. The little children used other objects to pick them up, and the same thing happened. For a long time, this activity spread throughout the entire house, and the coins were thrown into small ponds. The gold remained in the room for days. The old man knelt and prayed, saying, "We are so poor and lowly. Heaven has granted us this gold, more than we ever hoped for. I wish the gods would quickly return it, so as not to disturb the village and cause a great disaster." By evening, the gold was no longer seen. Instead, a cow's head rose from the pillar, wagging its ears and moving its eyes, as if alive. The next day, everything was silent. Nevertheless, the Cheng family became wealthy as a result. Every year, on the sixteenth day of the first lunar month, they held a vegetarian meal for Buddhist monks and Taoist priests, named the Dragon Assembly Festival. The patriarch was very charitable, distributing wealth to the poor, and the villagers regarded him as Master Cheng. In the 29th year of the Shaoxing era (1159 AD), he lived to be eighty-three years old and died. His grandson also studied and took the imperial examinations.

**The Ghosts of Zhishan** Zhishan is located one mile north of the city. All around it, in front and behind, are burial grounds. The side rooms of a Buddhist temple stand there, and coffins face each other. In wind, snow, rain, and fog, one often hears chirping sounds. This is undoubtedly a ghostly area. In the sixteenth year of the Shaoxing era (1146 AD), Governor-General Ren Liangchen (courtesy name Bexian) lost his son. He entered the temple to set up a water-land ritual. Before midnight, the entire temple heard the sounds of people laughing and moving around in the area below the mountain, exchanging greetings and condolences. Cheng Cibu, a temple official guarding the tomb, returned from a branch road leading to a mud-covered gate. On his way, he encountered three people walking together. They shouted sternly, "We were invited to the temple for a gathering and came to attend. What are you doing here, and how dare you come to this place?" They chased and wanted to beat him. The servant ran away. Just then, there was a torch coming out of the temple, and he thus escaped. **Ye Boyi** In the third year of the Ganda era (1167 AD), Cheng Shikuo (courtesy name Hongyuan) from Fuliang, after resigning from his position as a secretary at the imperial library, returned home. His wife was pregnant and nearing her due date. His brother, Hongfu, went to Jingdezhen. On the fifteenth night of the twelfth month, Hongfu dreamed that Ye Boyi (courtesy name Qianheng), a former secretary, visited his residence and asked for a room to stay in. Hongfu said, "Our living quarters are not very clean. Only Shikuo's newly renovated study is suitable. Please take a look." He followed him in and saw that the furnishings were elegant and clean, as if prepared for a guest. Ye Boyi said happily, "This place can be stayed in for a long time. I am content with it." They sat together and asked for food. Ye Boyi also requested fire-cured meat, a local dish that he enjoyed when he was alive. They searched the rooms and found it in Shikuo's position. After the meal was prepared, the guest ate heartily and went to bed. Hongfu woke up from his dream. The next day, on his way home, he met a servant who told him that Shikuo's wife had given birth to a son. They named him Henhun. At that time, Ye Boyi had passed away exactly three years ago.

A Case of Lice Tumor Li Sheng from Fuliang contracted a back itching disease. The lump on his back rose up like an inverted basin. Although he experienced no pain, the itching was unbearable. His appetite gradually diminished, and no one could identify what the illness was. The physician Qin Deli examined him and said, "This is a lice tumor. I can treat it." He applied medicine to the area and wrapped a cotton band around the circumference. After a night, the lump broke open, and a large number of lice emerged, all alive and moving. The next day, Li Sheng felt lighter, but a small opening like the tip of a chopstick remained unclosed. Lice continued to emerge in large numbers. Eventually, he died. I remember a Tang dynasty novel that recorded the time when Duke Jia governed Huatai. A resident of the state died from a similar illness. Duke Jia said, "There is no medicine in the world that can cure it, except for the ash from a thousand-year-old comb and the water from the bath of a yellow dragon." This case is exactly the same.

Coins Made the Sound of Mice In my village, there once lived a simple and unskilled peasant. He had no other talents except for working for others. He was paid by his clan's ancestral home to pound rice for thirty coins per day. Over the course of more than a year, he earned fourteen thousand coins, which he placed in a corner of his bed and warned his wife and children not to use without his permission. Every morning, he would admire and caress the coins before leaving. One night, he could not sleep, and a group of mice were making noise nearby. He hit the bed to chase them away, but they did not stop. He blew out the lamp to look for them, but found nothing. The lamp went out and he relit it, and the disturbance continued all night. He woke up early and felt unusually unhappy. He walked out the door aimlessly and just happened to encounter two people fighting. They were fighting so fiercely that one of them broke a tooth and bled. There was no one around, and he pointed them out as witnesses. The village official arrested and sent them to the county, and they were all thrown into jail. The people are indeed foolish, and he did not know the cause of the dispute. He could not answer a single word and was beaten and returned home. All the expenses for the journey and the officials left him with nothing but washed-out money.

Zhang Er and His Son In the city of Fanyang, there was a man named Zhang Er who made a living by selling porridge. He had a son who was nineteen years old. The son was fond of drinking and was idle and good-for-nothing. Whenever he was drunk, he would even scold his parents. His neighbors all disliked him. In the second month of the seventh year of the Ganzhao era (1171 AD), the son lay on his grandfather's bed. At midnight, he suddenly startedle and stumbled, unable to make a sound. He was rescued and treated for over ten hours before he woke up. After a long time, he was able to speak and said, "A man in a yellow robe called me away." He was forced into a bathhouse where all around him was blazing fire. The heat was unbearable, and he cried out and tossed and turned. He felt someone helping him from outside, but he could not get out. Like this, some time passed, and he suddenly woke up. He thought it was a nightmare, but the scene was vividly clear in his mind. Soon after, the rooster crowed. His father went to the kitchen to make porridge. A female cat had just given birth to five kittens in the stove, and one of them died. The father suspected that this was where his son had fallen. From then on, the son began to feel remorse and fear. He made a vow not to drink and completely changed his old habits. All the events in this scroll are from our prefecture.

Yijian Zhi (Bing Volume), Book 12, Part 15** **The Carvers of Shuzhou** In the sixteenth year of the Shaoxing era (1146 AD), the Huainan Transfer Office began the engraving of the *Taiping Shenghui Fang* (a medical text). Half of the work was allocated to Shuzhou. The local government recruited dozens of craftsmen and established a workshop at the school. The daily drinking and noise made by the craftsmen troubled the scholars. Professor Lin reported this to the county magistrate Wang Xidan. Wang ordered the workshop to be moved to the top of the Kui Gate Tower in the south of the city and appointed the magistrate of Huaining County, Zhen Yi, to supervise it. On July 17th, a small pagoda next to the gate, 15 feet tall, collapsed without reason. The next morning, the sky was clear. By noon, dark clouds suddenly rose from the west, covering the tower. Strong winds and heavy rain followed. At the time, there were over a hundred craftsmen and market vendors. A thunderclap struck, and eighty of them fell to the ground. The rest were also shocked and lost their spirits. After a long time, flying ash rose from the ground, and fireballs burst and flowed on the ground, all with the smell of sulfur. After an hour, those who had fallen recovered. The foreman Hu Tianyou reported to Zhen, who ordered an investigation. Among the craftsmen, five were identified: Zhou Liang from Qizhou, Ye Jun from Jianzhou, Yang Tong, Zheng Ying from Fuzhou, and Li Sheng from Luzhou. They all shouted and fell dead, their bodies injured and broken. Upon inquiring about their crimes, it was found that these five were particularly fond of drinking and lazy, eager to finish the printing. They altered the characters, strokes, and measurements of the medicine formulas, changing them arbitrarily to mislead people. Therefore, they received this punishment.

In the second year of the Longxing era, Zhang Yu, the county clerk of Huaining County, Shuzhou, went on official business. His servant, Gu Chao, stayed overnight in the study room. He saw a woman dressed in green who said she had been chased away by her mother and had nowhere to go. Knowing that he was alone, she came to him. When asked where she lived, she replied that she was from the Purple Bamboo Garden in the city. They then spent the night together. After a few nights, Chao became dazed and seemed like a fool, looking pale and weak. Yu, puzzled by this, questioned him. Chao told him the truth. Yu said, "This must be a demon. It will surely harm you. Wait until tonight when it comes here again. You should grab its sleeve and shout for help. I will come to see." When the woman returned, Chao held onto her sleeve and shouted, "There's a ghost!" The woman suddenly broke free and fled. When they lit a lamp, they saw that it was a large banana leaf. Previously, outside the study room, the garden was full of purple bamboo. The banana plant, which was also quite large, had caused strange occurrences before. Yu ordered it to be cut down, and the leaves were wet with blood. Both the bamboo and the banana plant were cut down. Chao was then sent back home. From then on, Chao became deeply sad and depressed, and he eventually died from an illness.

Wu Wang's Appeal for Justice In the fifteenth year of the Shaoxing era, Chen Zu'an served as the magistrate of Wu County. His niece or daughter-in-law, Lady Lu, was ill and in distress, and she was possessed by a spirit. Chen wanted to invite a Taoist priest to perform an exorcism. The spirit said, "It is useless to treat me. I have held grudges in the underworld for nearly two decades without being resolved. Therefore, I wish to express my grievances." Chen asked the reason. The spirit replied, "My name is Wu Wang. I am from Nanjing. I fled south due to war and fire and lived near the city wall, earning a living by selling cords. I once drank with a fellow villager named Cai. Returning home along the river at night, Cai was very drunk and accidentally drowned. A patrol officer happened to see it and suspected that I pushed him into the river. He arrested and sent me to the prefecture, where I was imprisoned and interrogated. Unable to bear the pain, I confessed. The authorities imposed the law and beat me to death in front of the stone pagoda at Yongxi Temple. I have carried this grudge for a long time. Today, I will briefly tell you about it." Chen said, "Who was in charge of the matter at that time?" The spirit replied, "The prison official had no intention. The entire affair was handled by the prison clerk. The clerk was afraid of conducting a thorough investigation and wanted to quickly resolve the case. He did not allow for any defense or analysis, and the prison official, lacking clarity, simply accepted it as true. The case was thus brought to the extreme penalty. The spirit then listed the names of the investigating clerk, prison guards, and the executioner. Chen said, "If it is true, then..."

"Why do you alone accuse me?" he asked. "The temple is next to the county, and it is the place where the county makes prayers. Usually, you enter the temple, and I always see you. Therefore, I am very familiar with you. Now, the matter has been going on for a long time, and I cannot correct it again. I just want the world to know about it." Chen replied, "Where are your bones? I will find a place to bury you. Can you rest peacefully in the earth?" "My remains are scattered, and only about one-tenth remain. Burying them would be of no use. Please pity me. I wish to participate in a water-land assembly to aid in my rebirth." Chen said, "This expense is extravagant, and I am poor and cannot afford it." "Then just register me in the water-land assembly. Have someone secretly call Wu Wang at the stone pagoda so that he may know and receive merit. And thus be reborn." Chen asked, "Where is the best place?" "All have merit, but the one at Maple Bridge is especially excellent. Please do it there." Chen agreed, and the ghost bowed in gratitude. Chen then asked if the sick person could recover. "The Lu family's fate is exhausted. I fear it cannot be avoided. Medicine and prayers are all useless." After several days, the woman indeed died. The following year, Wang Bao Yan Guang went to Maple Bridge to hold a vegetarian feast. Chen used his salary to set up a seat for Wu Wang.

### Rain of Black Rice in Shuzhou In the spring of the fourth year of the Ganda era, heavy rain fell in Shuzhou. Both inside and outside the city walls, black rice fell from the sky. The rice was as hard as iron, and even when chewed, it was completely black. People suspected that the rice boats from earlier had capsized in the river, and the dragon took water to bring rain and carried it here. ### Zhu Er Kills a Ghost Zhu Er, a man from Changshu, Pingjiang, stayed overnight on a field embankment to guard his rice. One night, a woman came from outside and visited him. She came for several consecutive nights, and Zhu Er realized that she was not human because her body was as cold as ice. He searched the entire village but found no trace of her. Secretly, he sewed a bag from a quilt and intended to put the woman in the bag. The woman knew about his plan and came to his house that night, crying. Zhu Er asked her why she was crying. She said, "You have bad intentions. I will no longer come." Zhu Er was concerned that the cold might make the woman sick and assured her that he had no other intentions. He wanted to share the bed with her to keep her warm. The woman entered the bag. Halfway through the night, Zhu Er pretended that the bag was squeezing him and got up. He carried the bag on his shoulder and walked. The woman shouted to be let out, but Zhu Er did not respond. At first, the bag felt very heavy, but it gradually became lighter. When he arrived home, he lit a fire to see what was in the bag. The woman had turned into a piece of pine board. Zhu Er took an axe to chop the board, and it continued to bleed. The next night, the woman came back to demand vengeance. After a long time, she eventually stopped. The five stories are all told by Hu Ti from Xin'an.

**A Daoist from Hebei** On the night of the full moon in the first month of the seventh year of the Xuanhe era, lanterns were lit in the Taiyi Palace in the capital city. The crowd of spectators filled the streets. Two people fell into the pool. Palace guards rushed to rescue them, but they refused to get back on land, speaking wildly and incoherently. The Daoist community tried every kind of talisman and incantation, but none worked. The incident reached the inner palace, and an imperial decree was issued ordering the master of the BaoLu Palace to deal with it. Fearing he would be unable to handle the matter, the master personally went to the Daoist hall and bowed to all present, saying: "Among us, there must be someone with great skill. I hope we can all work together to help. Otherwise, it will bring disgrace to our religious community." Hundreds of people in the hall all remained silent. A certain Daoist from Hebei rose up alone and scolded them, saying: "You usually do not study diligently, but in times of need, you rely on others. I will go forth with my sword." He went to the edge of the pool, and the two drowned people both raised their hands in submission. The Daoist said to the crowd: "These are powerful ghosts. We must first remove their bones." The crowd did not understand what method this was. The Daoist circled the pool in the ritualized steps known as *Yubu*, reciting incantations for a long time. He then sent strong soldiers into the water to support the drowned men. When they reached the shore, their bodies were soft as flesh, and upon reaching the bank, they had become hardened lumps of meat. He scolded them and asked where they had come from. They answered in unison: "We are also Daoists. In life, we were skilled in magical texts, but we were guilty of crimes and punished. Though our paths are different, in the world of the living and the world of the dead, our lifelong practices remain. We believe that none of you in the capital can match us. We did not expect a divine master to arrive, and now we are at a loss. Our past sins are now clear. We are to be captured and imprisoned in the Hell of No Gaps. We obey your command. We accept your decree. If you show mercy and do not kill us, guide us toward a path of life, and allow us to escape from the realm of lower spirits, it will be your great kindness. We agree."

After a meal or so, they all rose up as usual. Their family members helped them leave. Two groups of Taoist priests, together, expressed their joy and gratitude. When asked about the reason, they said, "This spirit is not easy to control. If you were to fight it, even a thousand people could not defeat it." They explained that they had learned a method to extract the sinews of a ghost, and thus applied it. Once the sinews and bones were removed, the spirit could do nothing. They all sighed and said, "This is beyond our reach." Song Shanchang, a man from Fuzhou, worked as a hired laborer. He went to the capital to serve this Taoist priest. Song was cunning and intelligent, and he had a good understanding of what was being done. He once secretly opened the priest's bag and studied the essential techniques. Privately, he treated minor spirits for the common people, and his treatments were always effective. The priest was pleased with him and was about to teach him the secret teachings. However, Song was deceitful and lacked integrity, and he was also lazy, so he did not complete his studies. Coinciding with the Jiajing Incident, he returned west. Later, he became a Taoist priest and lived in the Xiangfu Temple in the state. His treatment of ghosts and demons was as effective as a miracle. For all those suffering from malaria or epidemic diseases, he would draw a line on the center of their face with his finger. In a moment, the left side would become hot as fire, while the right side would become cold as ice. He would breathe according to the temperature changes, and they were cured instantly. He had dozens of disciples, all of whom learned from him. One of his disciples once went to a villager's house, where the entire family was ill with the epidemic. The disciple could not cure them, so he went to the prefecture to invite Song to come. Song entered the Taoist chamber, took the spear of the god's vanguard, and struck the ground three times. Then, he took a sacrificial cake and split it in half, giving it to the disciple. He said, "There is no need for me to go. You hold this and give it to them to eat, and they will be able to rise on their own." The disciple returned to the villager's house, and the sick people had already risen. They said, "We were saved by the three thunderclaps of Master Song." For he had practiced the Five Thunder Method.

The Woman of the Rao Family In the village of Shu Bei, twenty miles from the city of Fuzhou, there was a family named Rao, a prominent household. The area was surrounded by sweet-smelling forests. One day, members of the Rao family went out to play in the forest. They saw a willow tree with a hollow trunk, and the water inside was clear enough to serve as a mirror. The young wife playfully peered into it and immediately fell ill. She returned home and fell into a trance-like state, no longer recognizing anyone. Then, there was a voice speaking from the air, interacting with people in a friendly manner. Whenever it heard a song, it could immediately harmonize, its voice graceful and varied, with a lingering charm. If there were small mistakes in pitch or rhythm, it would certainly mock and point them out. It discussed literature and poetry, generally making sense. It was very close, yet no one could see its form or appearance. If the wife had done something wrong, it would speak openly to the master. Even if there were fraudulent transactions involving land or property dozens of miles away, it would report it immediately, having no secrets or hidden things. Both the upper and lower classes were tired of the situation. Various rituals and prayers to ward off evil spirits or misfortune were performed, trying every possible method. Ultimately, achieving no benefit, this continued for several years. The Rao family then burned incense and prayed, saying, "We have received the favor of the respected deity for a long time. Humans and gods have different paths. We hope not to become disrespectful or careless, to shame the deity. We wish to build a new temple in a mountainous area, set up incense and images for worship, and serve collectively. We hope the deity would move there, and each maintain their proper role or position. Isn't that better?" The spirit promised. After this, there was no further disturbance. The Rao family was pleased with their plan and built a very splendid temple. They welcomed the spirit daily for worship. Five days later, the spirit returned, speaking as playfully as before. The elder Mr. Rao scolded it, saying, "Since you have a temple to eat from, why do you still torment us?" The spirit laughed and said, "Am I a fool? With such grand halls and rooms, why would I leave to go to a small temple?" The Rao family became increasingly fearful and despaired. Until the young wife died, the spirit finally departed. The entire family declined as a result.

Xu Shiyi and His Brother Xu Shiyi, a native of Fuzhou, passed the imperial examination and became a Jinshi. He served as a county-level official responsible for household registration and taxation in Jianchang. There was an unauthorized shrine behind his official residence. He wanted to remove it but did not succeed. Suddenly, he fell ill with a mental disorder, appearing dazed and stuporous like a fool. His eating, drinking, speaking, and laughing were all different from those of ordinary people. His brother, Xu Shijie, heard of the situation and went from their hometown to visit him. After arriving, before he could speak, Xu Shiyi spat in his face. Xu Shijie was startled and did not know what to do. He suddenly felt dazed, but Xu Shiyi returned to normal. Xu Shijie carried the illness back home and became mute and unable to speak. For daily needs, he would write down what he needed. He had a strong liking for Du Fu's poetry. Even though he abandoned worldly affairs, he only sought to read this poetry without ceasing. Later, his condition worsened. Whenever he went out, he would be naked. Family members locked him in a room, and he lived like this for twenty years before dying. Xu Shiyi served as a professor in Guangzhou and also died. Both brothers were highly regarded in Linchuan for their literary talents, but unfortunately, they were like this, which is strange.

蛇犬毙 林廷彦担任临川县令。上任不久便患病。一天，府中的人在白天看到有人坐在大厅的椅子上。他们以为是县令，以为他病愈了，可以出来。有人上前去看，发现竟然是州宅的一只母狗。旁边还有两条蛇盘踞着。人们拿起棍子想要打它们，蛇却消失了，不见踪影。但那只狗却被打死，卖给了屠夫。那一年，林廷彦去世了。另有一位名叫涂千里的人，居住在宜黄县。夏天，他与宾客朋友坐在他家的燕堂里。突然，一只狗叼着一条蛇径直走到他面前，咬死了蛇，然后把蛇放在地上离开了。他的客人以为这是杨震遇到的鹳雀衔鲤鱼的祥瑞之兆。涂千里悲伤地说：“我生于乙巳年，现在是巳运，却遭遇了蛇灾。我恐怕是逃不过这一劫了。”不到一年，他果然去世了。

**The Venerable Monk** In the Shushan Temple of Yihuang County, there was a monk named Venerable Monk who was skilled in conducting the Water and Land Assembly, a grand Buddhist ritual, and was adept at reciting mantras and verses. As he grew older, he developed a condition where he could no longer speak clearly. Whenever he entered the ritual hall, he would drink several cups of chicken broth, believing it could help his voice. If he was not satisfied with the offerings he received, he would often speak freely and curse. In his old age, he contracted an illness, and his tongue split into two parts, making three tongues in total. He became unable to eat, drink, or speak. The doctor applied medicine and attempted to cut off the extra tongue, but the pain was unbearable. Only after ten days did the condition recur, and he would again apply the same method. This happened several times without stopping. Finally, he became severely ill, and his disciples saw a green-faced big ghost during the day, which entered through the window and took him away. When they checked, he was already dead. **The Red Lizard** In the administrative center of Xinjian County, Yuzhang, during the seventh month of the fourth year of the Gaodao era (1168 CE), there was a loud thunderstorm at midnight. The tiles on the hall roof all rang, and the family gathered in one room. They heard a strong wind sound, and the window lattices made a sharp sound. They suspected there might be a collapse, but only at the fifth drum (dawn) did it stop. When it became light, they looked and found a huge willow tree pulled out from the garden. It was three zhang long and ten feet in circumference, cut into pieces like being chopped, and spread throughout the houses of the county magistrates and clerks. A lizard, colored like cinnabar, about a foot long, was stiff and dead on the ground. People suspected it was a strange creature. These six events were recounted by Liu Ming Shi of Linchuan.

In the tenth year of the Shaoxing era, a monk named Fa'en from Mingzhou was executed for plotting rebellion. Initially, Fa'en was renowned for his miraculous powers through the recitation of the "Filth-Trace Mantra." The people of the prefecture greatly revered him. A group of lawless individuals hoped to gain wealth and nobility through this and planned to take Fa'en as their leader on a specific date. They intended to raise an army and kill all officials and wealthy families, then sweep up the crowd and move towards Lin'an. If unsuccessful, they would escape to the sea. At the time, the prefect, Cao Dazhong, had already left, and the vice-prefect Gao Shiding took charge. The bandits believed their plan would certainly succeed and gathered to drink at someone's house, raising cups and toasting each other, calling each other "Taiguo." Just before the planned day, one of his followers wrote down the date of the rebellion and went to consult the fortune-teller Bao Dazhang about good or bad omens. As they were leaving, another person arrived, and by midday to late afternoon, more and more people came, all asking the same questions and saying, "We plan something. Can it succeed?" Bao was suspicious and deceived the last person, saying, "This is not your destiny. In my art, there is an unspoken nobility. Considering your appearance, it is not sufficient to match it. Where is that person? I will speak with him myself. I dare not reveal it to others." The questioner happily went to inform Fa'en, and they all went together to Bao's shop.

Bāo Xiàwéi bowed twice in reverence before him. He said, "What merit does my humble skill possess that I have been blessed by heaven? Today, I have encountered an extraordinary celebration. My household has a young daughter who is not unattractive. I wish for her to join the ranks of the concubines." Immediately, he invited her into the room and guided his wife and children to greet her. They set up a feast with music and dance, and a maid encouraged him to drink. Bāo Xiàwéi stood respectfully. After a long while, he feigned a need to buy food and hurriedly went out to inform Shìdìng. He quickly summoned the military officers, and on the same day, they all were captured. The trial concluded, and mercy was extended to the principal offender, who was executed by slicing in public. Dozens of followers were killed, and their bodies were displayed on the road. That night, the road inspector went out on patrol and saw a man struggling among the corpses. He was the one who had died from beating but had revived. The inspector lifted him up and asked him. The man said, "At first, I was taken to the market for execution. I only knew fear and did not remember anything else. When I was struck on the side, my spirit emerged from the top of my head and sat on the eaves, watching as my body received the blows. Then I was in a state of unconsciousness, like a dream, and do not know why I am alive now." The inspector said, "If you were destined to die, how could you live?" He kicked the man's wound with his foot, and the man died again. Shìdìng thus obtained the position of Secretary of the Secretariat, and Bāo Xiàwéi also received an official position. The people of the county pooled a million coins to give to him.

### The Master of Mount Qingcheng In the region of Xiangzhou, people performed a thousand-sutra ritual to honor the dead. Both monks and Daoists participated in the ceremony. Each person seated at the gathering recited a scroll of sutras at their own discretion. One Daoist simply recited the phrase "Great One Who Seeks the Sound to Save from Suffering" once. Afterward, he proceeded to eat. A neighboring monk teased him, saying, "You only recited it once. Didn't your tongue dry up?" The Daoist replied, "If it benefits the deceased, why recite more?" After the ritual concluded, he left immediately. Inside the lacquered plates, there were red inscriptions as if carved, reading "Master of Mount Qingcheng." When he tried to scrape them off with a knife, the inscriptions became even more visible. ### The Deputy Magistrate Li The Deputy Magistrate Li of Wuchang had a dream in which he was summoned to the realm of the dead. The judge asked, "When you were the son of the Zhang family in your previous life, how could you push your wife into the water?" In his dream, Li suddenly remembered the incident and replied, "My wife fell into the water accidentally and drowned; I did not push her." The judge then sent someone to summon the local mountain and river deities to testify. Their testimony matched Li's account, and he was released back to the living world. Later, while staying at an inn, Li encountered a woman who claimed to be his wife from his previous life. She clung to him, refusing to let go, and their bond was as strong as if they were still alive. His relatives and acquaintances were aware of this. After several years, she finally left. Li never married again. Although his life was free from other troubles, he often suffered from lower back pain. He carved two wooden sticks and hollowed out their centers. Every day, he struck them hundreds of times, which alleviated his pain somewhat. It was said that this pain was caused by ghostly or supernatural energy.

Wu Dechong Wu Gongcai, courtesy name Dechong, was from Yiyang. He entered the Imperial Academy. At the age of fifty, he had achieved nothing. He wanted to stop taking the exams and return home. He sought a dream interpretation at a temple dedicated to two deities. In his dream, a child told him, "You will have a very good year next year. From now on, you will be prosperous." Wu believed the dream and decided to stay. The following year, he was selected in the upper division of the exams. Reflecting on his increasing age, he once again felt the urge to return home. In another dream, the child said, "You will be successful in the imperial examinations. There is no need to return." Indeed, the following year, he was listed among the successful candidates in the imperial examinations. After being appointed to an official position, on the eve of leaving the capital, he dreamed that a previous person came to him and said, "You should not become a prefectural governor. In your past life, you were a prefect and mishandled a legal case, making a mistake in judgment. Although it was unintentional, the punishment from the underworld is severe. It has already caused you to lose an eye. Do not take this position again to avoid further harm." Upon waking, Wu thought to himself, "I am fifty-two years old. I have only one official position. I cannot reach a high-ranking position. Moreover, both my eyes are clear and healthy. How could there be such a thing as being blind in one eye?" He did not believe the dream. Three years later, he fell ill with red eyes and indeed became partially blind. Despite this, he continued to serve in local government and was promoted rapidly. He resigned from his position in Yudu, Yichuan County, and later served as a deputy governor in Hengzhou, Yongzhou, and Jiankang Fu. In the twelfth year of the Shaoxing era (1142 AD), he arrived in Lin'an (Hangzhou) and sought a deputy position. At the time, Wang Qingzeng, a high-ranking official, had a close friendship with Wu from their time in the same dormitory. Wang advised him, "You have served three terms as a deputy governor. Your rank and experience are high. There is no doubt that you should become a prefectural governor." Wang recommended Wu to the prime minister, who appointed him as the prefect of Yichun. Wu was unwilling to accept the position and died at home shortly after returning home.

The Thirteenth Volume of the Yi Jian Chronicles: Fifteen Tales Miss Lan In the twelfth year of Shaoxing (1142), Wang Zhijun, a man from the eastern part of the empire, resided temporarily at Qingni Temple in Xingan County, Linjiang City. The temple was far from the city, remote and plagued by bandits. Wang was known for his wealth. Once, he entertained guests with a banquet, which lasted until midnight before they dispersed. Both Wang and his wife were drunk and asleep. Suddenly, about thirty bandits broke in. They bound all of Wang's children and maidservants. One of the maidservants called out, "This is the master's household affair. Only Miss Lan is here. What do we have to do with this?" Miss Lan, who was favored by Wang, stepped forward from the crowd and replied, "All the master's possessions are in my hands. If you wish for anything, I will not be stingy. But the master and his mother are still asleep. Please do not frighten them." She held a large candle and led the bandits into a room on the west side. Pointing to chests on the bed, she said, "These are wine vessels, these are colored fabrics, and these are clothing and bedding." She gave them the keys and allowed them to take whatever they desired. The bandits tore the quilt into large pieces, took the vessels, and trampled them in the middle. When the candle burned out, they relit it. They were greatly pleased and overjoyed.

The bandits remained for about ten *ke* and then left. After a long while, the old man Wang also woke up. The servant girl began to tell the reason, and she released all the bindings. The next morning, she reported to the county, and the county reported to the prefecture. The old man Wang became melancholy and fell ill. Blue Sister privately said, "Why should you worry? The thieves are not difficult to catch." The old man Wang angrily retorted, "What do you know, woman? After giving all the family wealth to the thieves, then why do you say they are easy to catch?" She replied, "The thirty thieves were all wearing white cloth robes. When I was holding the candle, I used the soot from the candle to mark their backs. But if you verify it this way, they will surely fail." The old man Wang used her words and told the pursuers. Not two days later, they caught seven people in a cattle market. They traced the trail and not one escaped. All the stolen goods were found, and nothing was lost. This event coincides with the record in the biography of Zhang Chang in the *Han Shu*, where the leader of the thieves used ochre to mark the hems of the other thieves and caught them. A servant is loyal to her master, and it is not easy to find such loyalty. When faced with danger, she does not fear or shrink back, and she has extraordinary wisdom in a crisis. Even if included in the *Lie Nü Zhuan* (Biography of Exemplary Women), she would not be ashamed.

Changxi Man** A man from Changxi, a place in Fujian, lived as a son-in-law in a family by the sea, making a living by fishing. His mother, thinking of him, went to see him. The man was very unhappy. The mother sensed his feelings and told him to return home the next day. The man did not want to stay. His wife alone kept him, saying, "Aunt, please stay a little longer. Wait until I catch some fish to make a bowl of soup." Shortly after, the man returned. Upon reaching the door, he heard his mother's voice and quickly hid the fish behind the house. He again deceived his mother, telling her, "Today the wind is bad. I didn't catch even a single fish." The mother then left. After she had left, the man blamed his wife, saying, "What I just caught were all eels. They were both many and large. I've never caught such before. Why did you bother to keep me here?" The wife went to look and saw that the basket was full of snakes. She ran back to tell the man in alarm. The man did not believe her. He went himself and indeed saw a cluster of snakes coiled together. One, the largest, raised its head and bit his throat directly, and he died immediately. The snakes also disappeared. **Strange Pig in Fuzhou** In the first month of the Zhenghe era (1111 AD), a pig gave birth to seven piglets in a household selling soy milk in the northern part of Fuzhou. Only one was a pig, while the rest had human heads and horse feet, with bodies resembling humans, clean and hairless. At birth, they made cries like a baby. The family was afraid and quickly buried them in the backyard. Neighbors heard the crying and came to see them in the morning. They still saw two of the piglets and showed them to the village. Soon, the crowd was like a market. The local governor knew it was an omen of misfortune and ordered them to be killed quickly. At that time, auspicious signs were being reported to the imperial court, and he dared not report this to the court.

A son of a butcher in Fuzhou, aged sixteen, was gentle and only played around in the market, refusing to learn his father's trade. His parents told him, "You have grown up; you should learn the family business to support yourself and take care of your parents. What good is it to wander aimlessly?" He replied, "I see it every day and know it well. If I want to kill, I can do it. Why bother to learn?" His father gave him a knife and locked him in an empty room. Secretly watching him, they saw that from morning till noon, he just sat silently facing the sheep. Suddenly, he raised the knife and said to the sheep, "I will make you my enemy. There will be no end to this." He then cut his own throat. His parents rushed to save him, but it was too late. An old man named Lin Yongao from the water found a statue of Guanyin and brought it home to worship. After several months, he went boating into the sea. The boat broke and he drowned. In desperation, he called out to Guanyin, "I once saved you. How can you not save me?" As he spoke, his body floated up. He found a piece of wood to ride on. Surging waves reached the sky, and he traveled about a hundred miles, following the current into a small inlet. He found a basket of abandoned items and returned home. People believed it was the Buddha's help. These four stories were all told by the monk Jiang Bao of the Taiping Temple in Fuzhou. Jiang Bao had a book called *Ming Si Bao Ying*, which recorded these stories.

De Guo Duanyou Guo Duanyou, a man from Raozhou, devoted himself earnestly to Buddhist practice. In the winter of the year 1155 (the sixth year of Emperor Xiaozong's reign), he solicited materials for writing or copying scriptures. He exerted personal effort in the early morning to recite or write with a pure mind, focusing on the *Avatamsaka Sutra*. He set a goal to complete six copies and only stopped when he had finished. In the fifth month of the year 1163 (the eleventh year of Emperor Xiaozong's reign), he contracted a seasonal illness and suddenly lost the light in his eyes, with an obstruction by a film or membrane. Both medical and shamanistic treatments were ineffective. He believed only the power of the Buddha could save him. On the last day of the fourth month of the following year, he made a solemn vow to perform an act three times a day, worshipping the Bodhisattva Guanyin, hoping that medicine or a prescription would be given in a dream. On the sixth day of the fifth month, he dreamed of a person in black clothing who said, "You want to see clearly. Use a powder made from otter paws and bear gallbladders, and it will work." The next day, he sent someone to the market to look for the two medicines. He only obtained the otter paw powder, which was ineffective when applied. On the twenty-seventh night, he dreamed of going to Jianshi Temple for a meal. After finishing the meal, he returned home and passed by Tianqing Temple. He heard the sounds of Buddhist rituals (bells and gongs) inside. He entered the temple and saw more than thirty women. One person in the middle was eight feet tall, wearing a black silk robe, with ears hanging down to the shoulders, green hair and green eyebrows, and a garland of sandalwood flowers. He wore a crown as large as a five-dou container. Guo knew that this person was extraordinary and wanted to wait for the person to turn around and pay respects. Soon a purple-robed Taoist priest, holding a tablet and bowing forward, said, "I am the chief priest. I specifically came to welcome you for the *Avatamsaka Sutra*. Please come back to my place for tea." Guo followed him inside, passing through the west corridor. Two halls had long yellow banners hanging down. A woman was kneeling at the altar to worship Guanyin. Under a green cloth curtain outside the screen, sixteen monks were sitting cross-legged on their meditation cushions. The Taoist priest went down the steps to fetch the tea set, but before he could bring it up, the dream ended.

Guo departed without informing anyone and rushed to the hall of law. It seemed as if he was moved by something, and he became aware only at midnight. The next day, he told his wife Huang that there was a bear bile pill formula. It had come from the Daoist Canon, and he urged her to go and find it immediately. Before he had finished speaking, his nephew Zhu Yanming arrived and said, "Last night, I unexpectedly found the bear bile pill formula for treating eye ailments in the temple. The whole household was surprised and astonished, as it matched the dream. We followed the formula to buy the medicine, and within ten days, it was completed. After taking it for more than twenty days, the medicine was finished, and my eyesight returned to normal. By October of that year, I was able to see clearly as before. That very day, I wrote down the previous medicine formula, noting its remarkable effects, and expanded it into ten sections. Now, my eyes are clear, and many people suffering from eye diseases have been cured by taking the medicine. The medicine uses seventeen ingredients, with bear bile as the main component. One and a half taels each of Huanglian, Muminghua, and Zhuma; two and a half taels of Fangji; one tael each of Cao Longdan, Shetui, Diguapi, Daimu, and Xianlingzhi; half a tael each of Qumai, Xuanfu Hua, and Ganjuhua; one and a half qian of Ruisi; one qian of Qilinjie; and one he of Manganzi. All these ingredients are finely ground into a powder. One goat's liver is used, half of which is boiled and dried, and mixed with the other half, which is removed from its membrane, ground into a paste, and combined with the medicine. The mixture is then pounded into pills the size of tangerine seeds. After meals, take thirty pills with rice wash. There is no special method for preparing the other medicines, except that the nodes of Muzhi should be removed, the flesh of Ruisi should be used, Manganzi should be washed with water, and Shetui should be roasted and removed. Guo recorded the beginning and end of this experience himself, but the encounter in the hall was not told to others.

The Vice-Governor of Hongzhou A local man named Li Binwang, who used the courtesy name Yongli, served as the magistrate of Xingan County during the second year of the Shaoxing era. When the Xuanfu Shi (pacification commissioner) entered the region, he personally went to the villages to supervise the collection of taxes. His servant dreamed that his master had returned, and heard a shout and saw a sword beating, announcing the arrival of the Vice-Governor of Hongzhou. The servant told his master’s wife about the dream. When Li arrived, his wife recounted the dream to him. Li smiled and said, "I am so poor and destitute. When large armies were passing through the county, fortunately, they did not consider it a fault for being poor. To get enough supplies of food and grain is sufficient. Having a high-ranking official visit was not what was expected." The following August, at the end of his term, Li indeed became the Vice-Governor of Hongzhou, just as the servant had dreamed.

Jin Junqing's Wife The daughter of a certain governor of Jingnan, aged eighteen, had already found a fiancé and was preparing to choose a day for the wedding ceremony. In a dream, someone told her, "This is not your husband. Your husband is Jin Junqing." Upon waking, she did not tell anyone about the dream. Instead, she embroidered Jin Junqing's name in three characters on every inch of her belt. Her mother saw this and was puzzled, and she informed her father. The father searched the government offices, even down to low-ranking officials and clerks, but found no one named Jin Junqing. When he questioned his daughter, she revealed the content of her dream. Shortly after, the fiancé she had agreed to marry indeed died. Six months later, the new governor of Xianzhou arrived in the region. When a messenger was sent to the governor's office, it was Jin Junqing. At this point, they realized the truth of the earlier events. Upon meeting, they treated him generously and kept him for several days. They learned that he had recently lost his spouse and told him about the girl's dream. Jin Junqing replied, "I am forty-two years old, which is twice your age, plus six more years. Moreover, I have not been long bereaved, and it would not be right or just for me to marry you." However, the host pressed him, saying, "Fate or destiny has decreed this. How can you refuse?" Reluctantly, they eventually married. Thirty years later, Jin Junqing passed away. His wife bore several children. Jin Junqing had risen to the position of Censorate Minister and was from Fanyang. These two stories were told by Li Binwang.

The Iron-Hat Daoist Zheng Jiefu was a man of integrity. He was from Fucheng, Fuzhou. During the Xining period, he was exiled to Yingzhou for his frank advice. In the early Yuanyou period, Su Dongpo recommended him and he was reinstated. In the early Shao Sheng period, he was exiled to Yingzhou again. At that time, Su Dongpo had been exiled to Huizhou and they first met there. They became friends at first sight. In the 38th year of the Zhenghe period, Zheng was in Fucheng. He dreamed that a guest arrived, introducing himself as the Iron-Hat Daoist, and left a poem. Upon reading it, Zheng recognized it as Su Dongpo's. Su had once called himself the Iron-Hat Hermit while in exile. It had been seventeen years since Su's passing. The poem read: "A true person in the world, no matter what, remains true. The official worries about the ruler's shortcomings. The family suffered financially due to their strict observance of rituals. The gate and threshold are filled with dogwood and chrysanthemums. The pavilions and railings are all made of pine and bamboo. My friend who is plain and unpretentious, we regret not meeting more often. He also said, 'Zheng Jiefu will soon come.' Upon waking, Zheng sighed and said, 'I am about to pass away.' He was seventy-eight years old at the time. The following autumn, he fell ill. He told his grandson Jia Zheng, 'A person's body is composed of the four elements. If these elements disperse, what remains of this body?' He recited a poem: 'Throughout my life, I have relied only on fate. Still like a bird passing by at the edge of the clouds. Now, around me, there is nothing at all. Gaining only a pillow of sleep in an empty hall.' He passed away a few days later.

Zhang Ghost In the state school of Hongzhou, there was a principal named Zhang Mou, who was naturally harsh and stingy. As he grew older, his harshness only increased. Even when students requested leave, he would stubbornly refuse to grant it. The school official would give five days off, which Zhang would reduce to three days, and three days to two days. This pattern continued in all cases. The students resented him greatly. There was a man named Zhang Ghost, whose appearance resembled a ghost. The students made him impersonate an underworld officer to scare old Zhang. Zhang Ghost readily agreed, saying, "I will obey the command." However, he added, "The performance must be realistic. A document from the underworld is required." The students asked, "What should the format of the document be?" Zhang Ghost replied, "I have seen it done before." He then took paper and wrote faintly with white alum, signing his own name at the end. That night, he went to the state school. The school gate was already closed. Zhang Ghost slipped through a crack. The students were startled. Old Zhang saw him and angrily shouted, "What dare you, beast! Surely you were sent by others to scare me at night." Zhang Ghost smiled and said, "I am following a document from Yama, the king of the underworld, to summon you." Old Zhang demanded the document. Before he could finish reading it, Zhang Ghost revealed his headscarf, showing two horns on his head. Old Zhang was shocked and screamed, dying instantly. Zhang Ghost emerged and stood in the courtyard, saying, "I am truly a cow-headed jailer. I was sent to pursue this old man yesterday. I accidentally lost the talisman while crossing a river. I have been afraid and unable to return for twenty years. I relied on the efforts of the scholars to return with the message. Now the performance is realistic." He bowed and left. Chen Zhengmin, in his *Dun Zhai Xian Lan*, recorded a story about Li Anshi in the Imperial Academy, who was killed by a ghostly charm played by his classmates. This story is similar to the one above, but they are different incidents.

**The Prime Minister of Peace** During the Xuanhe era, the imperial garden of Genyue was a place of grand and magnificent scenery. It already included such palatial retreats as the Jiaoxiao Tower and the Huaxu Hall. To the east of the garden was the Jinglong Gate, where a grove of a thousand tall bamboo plants grew, their dense foliage forming a lush and shady expanse. Few other gardens in the capital could rival its beauty. The imperial concubines moved freely through this area, as if walking through the abode of celestial beings. Emperor Huizong ordered the construction of a pavilion to overlook the garden. Once completed, it still had no name. In a dream, the emperor saw a man dressed in gold and purple robes, who said, “The new pavilion at Genyue should be named *Yi Cui*,” taking the line from a poem by the Tang poet Du Fu: “On a cold day, the green sleeves are thin, at dusk she leans on tall bamboo.” In the dream, the emperor asked, “Who are you?” The figure replied, “I am the Prime Minister of Peace.” Awakening, the emperor was puzzled by the dream. The next day, the Hanlin scholar Li Bangyan entered the court. After presenting his report, he happened to ask, “Recently, a small pavilion was built within the garden, with tall bamboo below it. What name should it be given?” Li Bangyan, without hesitation, replied, “Yi Cui.” The emperor was surprised and delighted, saying that the name coincided with his dream. At that time, Li Bangyan was already highly favored, and the emperor had in mind a major appointment. Within a few days, Li Bangyan was promoted to the position of Right Chancellor of the Ministry of Works, and thus became the second chancellor.

路当可得法 During the reign of Emperor Huizong in the Zhènghé period, Lù Jūnbǎo Gùan served as the governor of Shangshui County in Chen Prefecture. His son, Shízhōng, was seventeen years old and had not yet married. He studied in the Four Illuminating Hall of the county garden. At the time, Liáng Zhōnglǐ was the magistrate, and his two sons, Jiùnyàn and Mǐnyàn, were both around ten years old. They often played together. One evening, a gardener reported that Shízhōng was missing. The governor sent soldiers to search the entire county but could not find him. After five days, Shízhōng returned. They assumed he had been wandering aimlessly and punished him with a beating. Shízhōng dared not defend himself, but he frequently spat up blood. He privately told the magistrate, "A Daoist hermit, whose origin is unknown, came to a small room and spoke with me. After a long time, he said, 'You are worthy of instruction. I will teach you the art of talismans and spells, which can control all ghosts and spirits. However, your internal organs are filled with impurities and filth, and they must be completely removed.' Initially, I was very afraid of his words. He smiled and said, 'There is no harm.' He then instructed me to take one pound each of raw oil, white honey, and ginger, and mix them to eat. He took me away with him, and we went to an unknown place. For several days, I did not feel like eating, but I only felt a continuous flow of blood from my mouth. Every night, he gave me more than ten scrolls of writing to read, but during the day, I saw nothing. Before leaving, he said, 'You have now attained the rank of a True Official, a position of the highest grade. Just follow my methods, and that will be enough.' From then on, he became famous for his mastery of talismans. A few months later, he told Liáng's son, 'I made a mistake in one of my talismans, and I incurred a significant punishment. I will be demoted several ranks and will also suffer from a severe boil. Soon after, a boil the size of a bowl appeared on my back, causing intense pain. It took forty-nine days to heal. These two stories were recounted by Liáng Jiùnyàn's son, Zǐzhèng."

Longle Sea Bandits In the eighth year of the Shaoxing era, Su Wen Guan of Danyang served as the magistrate of Changle in Fuzhou. He captured twenty-six sea bandits. Prior to this, there were twenty-eight merchants from Guangzhou and officials involved in transporting goods who had rented a boat together. The crew of the boat, including the boatmen and helmsmen, numbered roughly the same as the passengers. However, they were all strong, bold, and lawless, and upon seeing the valuable goods the passengers were carrying, they secretly planned to rob them. After traveling for several days, they drank heavily and killed all the passengers, binding them back-to-back and throwing them into the sea. They left only two servants to cook, and when they reached the border of Changle, the oars broke. The bandit leader sent two men to buy new oars in Nantai, and they anchored in the harbor to wait. They frequently went ashore to rob and also abducted local women, bringing them onto the ship. They were drunk every day. One of the two servants escaped and immediately went to the county office to report the crime. At that time, the magistrate was still in the village and had not returned. Su Wen Guan mobilized the local militia and led the troops himself, traveling ninety miles to meet the bandits. Finding them drunk, he bound them all. On their return journey, they encountered a small boat with oars blocking their way. They shouted at the boatmen, who dared not answer, and took them along. Not a single one escaped. At that time, Zhang Zijian, the Censor-in-Attendant, was the commander. He ordered the boat to be searched and noticed various objects entangled at the stern. Some went into the water to inspect and found the bodies of the victims all gathered at the bottom, stiff but not decayed, and also not harmed by fish or turtles. Zhang Gong was amazed and quickly had them buried. The stolen goods had only been in their possession for three days, and they had not yet used any of it. Zhang Tingshi Dehui said.

**Rituals to Ward Off Disaster in Cai State** When the Secretary of State Lu Anlao was young, he studied at the academy in Cai State, where he shared a dormitory with seven or eight fellow students. One evening, they secretly went out for a walk and did not return until midnight. Suddenly, it began to pour heavily, but they had no rain gear. At that time, the academy's rules were very strict, and since they had never requested leave, they dared not stay out overnight. They quickly borrowed a single cloth blanket from an inn, propped it up with bamboo sticks at each corner, and carried it with them as they hurried back. As they neared the academy wall, they saw patrolmen coming from the east, holding torches and shouting as they approached. They were greatly frightened and stopped about twenty steps away, too afraid to move forward. Suddenly, the patrol soldier turned and ran back, not looking back at all. Thus, they were able to climb over the wall and enter the academy. All night long, they were anxious, thinking that their actions would surely be discovered and that they would be reprimanded and expelled. The next day, the military official reported to the county office, saying that after the second watch last night, it had been raining heavily. While on patrol at a certain location, they suddenly saw a strange object coming from the north. Its upper surface was flat like a mat, blurred and indistinct. Below it, there was a rustling sound like people walking, and it seemed to have about twenty to thirty feet. As it approached the academy wall, it disappeared. The county magistrate and those below him could not determine what it was. The people of the region passed the story along by word of mouth, all believing it to be a great monster. They reported the matter to the officials, and in each district, three-day rituals were held to ward off disaster. The form of the creature was depicted, and it was worshipped and then dismembered. Thus, the strange phenomenon described in earlier histories as the "mat-hat and stick-walking" demon is probably of this kind. This account was told by Xuji, the son of the Secretary.

In the fifth year of the Gande era (1169), there was a robber in Xiangyang who was sentenced to death. However, a special imperial decree spared his life and branded him before exiling him. The state governor feared he might cause harm again, so after he received his punishment, raw lacquer was smeared over his eyes. The prisoner, blinded and unable to see, was taken to the county jail in Changlin, waiting for transport. At the time, the village head happened to be in the jail for some matter. Feeling pity, he advised the prisoner: "When you leave, ask the guard escort to go to the side of Meng Spring. Find a stone crab, crush it, filter the juice, and drop it into your eyes. The lacquer should flow away with the juice, and the wound will also heal." The next day, the prisoner bribed the guard escort to get a small crab and used the method. After two days, his eyes returned to normal, with no slight damage. My brother-in-law Zhu Xiyan, who was then the magistrate of Dangyang and acting as the county magistrate, saw this with his own eyes.

**Yijian Zhi (丙) Volume Fourteen, Thirteen Stories** **Zhang Wugū** Wugū, the younger sister of my father-in-law, was named Zōngshū. From a young age, she was bright, wise, and well-versed in books. After reaching the age of marriage, she married a scholar named Dǒng Èrshíbā from Xiangyang. Dǒng was weak and lacked character. Zōngshū had a high-spirited and assertive personality, and she treated her husband like a servant or inferior. She was deeply dissatisfied, which eventually led to a wasting disease. During the winter of the Jìngkāng era, the defeated soldiers of Guō Jīng attacked Xiangyang and Dengzhou. Dǒng died in the Han River. The following year, Zōngshū accompanied her mother, Tán Fūrén, to Nanyang. There, she drank, laughed, and played, showing no signs of sorrow. Her long-term illness gradually improved, and she was just feeling relieved and happy. One day, without any apparent reason, she vomited blood uncontrollably. Filled with fear and suspicion, she sent someone to call a person (the identity is unclear due to missing characters). The person said, "Zōngshū, you cannot remarry. If you do, you will be killed." The person used the character "hé" (和) as a reference to Zōngshū. Even her family did not know this. Zōngshū recognized the voice as that of her deceased husband. She shouted, "I have always been a burden to you. Now I am dead. Why should you still纠缠 (纠缠) me? Do not force me to remarry another man. It has nothing to do with you." The shaman remained silent, and Zōngshū recovered. She remained calm and composed. Her brother-in-law came to the south and took her along. They went to Yangzhou to find a husband for her. They planned to marry her to Wang Tì. Zōngshū said, "I will not remarry."

His whole life was troubled by being a civil official. He did not want to see it again. To get a military officer would be enough. He then met the military officer, a gatekeeper and herald, Mr. Xi. In the fifth month of the second year, the mourning period for Mrs. Dong had not yet ended. That winter, Mr. Xi died at the hands of bandits. Shu followed his mother and elder brother across the river. They stayed in Liyang. In the last day of March in the third year, he dreamed that Mr. Xi came from the window and grabbed his head. Upon waking, he felt pain in his neck. A red tumor grew on his left cheek. He was bedridden for over a month. He was dazed and could not recognize people. The second sister came to visit him. She smiled and said, "My uncle-in-law is here." He asked who it was. She said, "It's the 28th young man." From then on, he only communicated with Mrs. Dong. Until he died. The following year, his mother was in Dazhou. She dreamed that Shu was gambling with someone on a floor. Just as he was in life. His mother scolded him, saying, "Gambling from morning until night. Is this something a woman should do?" Shu, filled with anger, transformed into a whirlwind. He chased his mother to the bed. His mother cried out in fright, saying, "A ghost is pulling me." His wife quickly got up and looked, and saw that he had fallen halfway to the ground. The next day, he could not get up. He died after two months.

During the reign of Emperor Huizong in the year 1119, my father-in-law served as the magistrate of Yidu in Xiangzhou. In the height of summer, there was no rain, and the town prayed at all the shrines, but the prayers were not answered. The townspeople said that there was a shrine dedicated to the immortal Cui on a certain mountain, which was extremely effective and responsive. They decided to prepare offerings and visit the temple. As they descended the mountain, a small amount of snow started to fall on the mountain. Despite the scorching sun, suddenly there was a heavy thunderstorm, and the rain covered a hundred miles. The townspeople showed their gratitude to the deity and contributed money to repair the temple. However, no one knew whether the immortal was male or female. They consulted maps and historical records and asked the elders, but no one could decide. My father-in-law napped during the day and dreamed of a large carriage coming from outside, adorned with flags, canopies, and insignia, and the ceremonial items were very elaborate, towering high above the house. He privately thought that the county gate was humble and modest, and wondered how it could accommodate such a sight. In an instant, the carriage had arrived in the courtyard. He stood on tiptoe and looked inside, and saw a woman with a serene face and elaborate dress sitting inside. He was startled and wanted to pay respect, but he suddenly woke up. He then ordered the creation of a statue of the female immortal. Before he could request a name for the temple, he was transferred to another post and left.

The Late Husband of Liu** A man from Tang Prefecture named Zhang Wenji had passed away over ten years ago. His wife, Liu, who was nearly eighty years old, encountered her late husband during the daytime. He pulled her clothes and led her to walk, saying, "Let us go home together. Do not stay here for long." They held each other, unable to let go, and Liu suddenly fell to the ground. Her youngest son came forward and supported the old man, saying, "What are you doing to my mother like this?" He then helped the old woman stand up, and only then did the old man leave. The eldest son and his family, seeing the old man suddenly fall and then stand up, rushed to see him. They heard his words clearly. At that time, the youngest son had also been dead for a long time. All of them were fearful and knew it was an omen of misfortune. Not long after, the old woman died. **Ice Flowers in a Tin Basin** My father-in-law, the Duke of Qinghe, in the sixth year of the Shaoxing era, served as an Inspector of the Chancellery and Secretariat and concurrently as a Military Advisor to the Commandery. He went to Huai West to pacify and advise Zhang Shao-bao's army, leaving his family in Jiankang. On the 15th day of the 12th lunar month, which was his birthday, the family took a commonly used large tin basin for washing. They poured out the dirty water but not completely, and the water in the basin froze into ice, forming intricate patterns like carved designs. Looking closely at it, one could see a longevity star sitting on a stone slab, with a tall pine tree covering it, and a turtle and a crane standing on either side, just like what is painted in the world. My mother-in-law, Lady Liu, ordered the calligrapher to draw its appearance. The calligrapher lived far away, and by the time he arrived, the ice patterns had already melted. From then on, there was no day without melting and freezing. Beautiful flowers and trees, long forests and distant views, a myriad of emotions and forms—though the most skilled and ingenious people would find it difficult to replicate, these ice patterns were truly remarkable. Until the spring warmth ceased, and my father-in-law received an appointment as a Vice Minister of War. In the *Spring Churn Records*, there is a story about Ge □ that is very similar.

Wang Balang was a wealthy man from Bizhang, Tang Prefecture. Every year, he traveled to the Jianghuai region to engage in large-scale commerce. During these trips, he developed a close relationship with a courtesan. Upon returning home, he always showed disdain for his wife and had a strong desire to divorce her. His wife was a wise woman who had given birth to four daughters, three of whom were already married. The youngest daughter was just a few years old. She considered it not yet the right time to leave, so she used gentle words to respond: "I have been your wife for over twenty years. Our daughters have married and have children. If you divorce me, where would I go?" Wang Balang then brought the courtesan with him and stayed in a nearby guesthouse. His wife, at home, sold household items to raise money and stored all the money in a box. The house was empty like that of a poor person. The county magistrate allowed the divorce and divided their property equally. Wang Balang's wife appealed to the magistrate, saying, "My husband behaved improperly by abandoning me for a courtesan. If this daughter follows me, she will end up destitute." The county magistrate found her appeal just and fair.
