She eventually obtained a daughter and moved out to live in a different village. She bought jars and pots, arranged them at the entrance, as if she were selling them. One day, her former husband passed by the door and, out of old kindness, spoke to her, saying, "How much profit do you make from this? Why not change your plans?" Her wife scolded him and chased him away, saying, "We have already decided to part ways. We are now like strangers. How can you interfere in my family matters?" From then on, there was no further contact. When the daughter reached the age of marriage, she was married to the Tian family in Fangcheng. By that time, her mother's savings had accumulated to ten thousand strings of cash, which the Tian family inherited. Wang Sheng, however, only spent his days with prostitutes. Later, he died as a guest in Huainan. A few years after that, his wife also died. After her burial, they were about to change her grave. The daughter, thinking of her father's remains that had not yet been returned, sent someone to bring them back, intending to bury them together with her mother. They washed and dressed the bodies, placing them side by side on a single bed. The watchers, becoming slightly careless, noticed that the two bodies had turned to face opposite directions. They thought it was an accident and wept as they moved them back to their original position. A short while later, they were back to the same position. They then realized that even in death, the couple's feelings were so strong that they remained a couple of grievances. Nevertheless, they were eventually buried together. Yang Xuanzan The son of Yang, a man from Tangzhou, gathered at the Teng family, relatives of his wife. He held the position of Xuanzan Shiren. He enjoyed eating chicken throughout his life, killing countless chickens. In his later years, he developed sores on his temples, which did not cause much harm. One night, a chicken he kept at home suddenly crowed loudly in the middle of the night. He greatly disliked this and killed it the next day, roasted it, and ate it. Before he could swallow, the sores on his temples erupted, covering one side of his face with boils. After a long time, he gradually recovered, but the pus flowed down to his throat, gnawing through his flesh to form a hole, which was exactly the same as where the chicken had been wounded. Fresh blood dripped continuously without stopping. He eventually died. The above six matters were all heard from his wife's family.

The Judge of Loyalty, Filial Piety, Righteousness, and Chastity Yang Wei, courtesy name Wen Shu, was from Rencheng, Jizhou. He served as an observer of the Guangzhou administration. He died in office, and his funeral was delayed. His nephew, Xu, was in the village. One afternoon, Xu was drowsy as if drunk when he suddenly saw Yang Wei riding a horse with attendants arriving. Xu hurriedly greeted and bowed. After sitting down, Yang Wei looked serene and untroubled, just as he had in life. Xu knelt and asked, "Uncle, where are you going now?" "I am now the Judge of Loyalty, Filial Piety, Righteousness, and Chastity. My duty is to judge cases of loyal subjects, filial sons, righteous men, and chaste women." They talked until late into the night. The bystanders only saw Xu bowing and speaking, and they all found it strange. As they were about to leave, two officials in purple robes stayed behind and said, "Lord Yang favors the stone platform at Fan Mountain. Why not build a shrine there?" Xu suddenly woke up and told his family, "Just now, my uncle from Guangzhou appeared and said the following." The family was both saddened and astonished. They then called a craftsman to make an image. The craftsman's skill was usually poor, but when the image was completed, it was surprisingly similar to Yang Wei. They realized it was a spirit. However, because the position was not prominent and there were no clear signs, the local officials did not report the matter, and the shrine was never built. Yang Wei was a virtuous man in life. In his official duties, he focused on teaching the people about filial piety and righteousness. He was upright and kind, so after his death, he became a spirit. Examining historical records, it seems that such an afterlife position has never been recorded before. Recorded in the collected works of Chao Wujiu.

Long Keqian, also known as Zhongkan, was a profound scholar of calendrical studies and possessed the ability to foresee future events. During the late reign of Emperor Huizong of Song (1125-1126), Zhao Jiuling met him in the capital. Zhao had to return home urgently due to his father's illness and encountered Long Keqian at the gate. Long Keqian said, "There will be a major upheaval in the capital. I must leave as well." When Zhao inquired further, Long Keqian replied, "On the day of the fire dragon, snow will fill the sky." The following year, the Jurchen forces invaded the capital, and on the day of Bingchen, they breached the city walls. At that time, heavy snow fell continuously, all of which matched his prophecy. The Talisman of Water Moon Master In the 21st year of the Shaoxing era (1151), during the Southern Song Dynasty, there was a heavy summer rain in Xiangyang that lasted for ten days without stopping. The Han River was about to overflow, causing great concern among the officials and civilians. The magistrate of Xiangyang, Yan Jun, told his colleagues, "The situation is urgent. I have a plan that can stop the rain immediately. Although it may seem like a magical practice, I cannot avoid this name." He then ordered his carriage to leave the city and went to the riverbank. From his pocket, he took out a talisman and threw it into the river. After pouring wine and performing three sacrifices, he returned home. That night, the rain stopped. The next day, the water level returned to normal. The entire prefecture revered him as a deity. At that time, Li Deyuan from Linchuan, who served as an observer and censor, asked Yan Jun about the method. Yan Jun explained it to him, saying, "Just follow my method. Anyone can do it. There is no other trick." The method involves taking a square piece of paper measuring three inches on each side, writing a red circle in the center, surrounding it with nine layers, and ending with a single character. The four characters "Water Moon Master" are written on top of it. This talisman can cure all kinds of calamities, including floods, droughts, diseases, epidemics, wars, ghosts, and strange occurrences in mountains, forests, and rocks.

Encountering Haunted Houses and Demonic Lairs Writing them down and revealing them, all have remarkable effects. Deyuan returned to Linchuan. His niece-in-law often at dusk would be possessed by a spirit. She would put on new makeup and change clothes, sitting on a bed to wait. In a short while, she would laugh and play with people, until the whole night passed before she calmed down. Deyuan secretly wrote a talisman and stuck it inside the door threshold. The woman did not know. The next day on the bed, she saw a tall man in formal attire coming as usual. When he reached the doorway, as if he encountered an obstacle, he gestured angrily and shouted, "Wretched woman! How can you suddenly forget me?" The woman replied, "I never had such thoughts. Why did you say that?" The man lifted his foot to enter, but he could not proceed. He then left. The woman suddenly became clear as if waking from a drunken stupor. She then told people about it. She had been confused for several weeks, not knowing where her body was. From then on, she was at peace. When I was a Lang in the Ministry of Rites, Deyuan was a Zhubu in the Taichang Temple. We walked together to the斋宫. He wrote it for me, but he did not use it.

贾县丞 李德远在绍兴二十七年（1157年）被调任至临安任职，住在白壁营。他与福州的姚知县同住一处。当时正值盛夏，每天晚上，李德远都会在后轩纳凉。姚知县的老友贾县丞前来拜访，说他丢失了官凭。他们的住处相距不过百步，贾县丞每天早出晚归，必定会经过姚知县那里聊天。李德远因此得以认识了贾县丞。贾县丞是长安人，喜欢谈论骊山宫阙和故都的繁华景象，滔滔不绝，令人愉悦。他还曾担任过缙云县丞，讲述鬼仙英华的事迹，尤其有据可依。姚知县和李德远便买酒设果，与他热情交往。两人相处了两个月，才各自分别。又过了两年，李德远成为敕令所的删定官，在官署中与同僚唐信道闲谈时，聊到了奇异之事。唐信道详细讲述了英华的故事，李德远应答如流。唐信道惊讶地问：“你是怎么知道这些的？”李德远回答说：“我听别人说的。”唐信道更加惊讶，问道：“难道不是那个高大有胡须的人吗？”李德远点头称是。唐信道又问：“他是陕西人吗？”李德远再次点头。唐信道说：“这个人自从从缙云县丞任上罢官后不久就死了。他的哥哥把他葬在某个地方。我送他下葬后，到现在已经十年了。怎么会有像你所说的那样呢？”李德远顿时感到一阵恐惧，毛发直竖。他仔细回想，与贾县丞相处这么久，却从未见过他在白天出现，虽然同饮共食，谈笑风生，但他总是坐在远离灯光的地方，自己从未看清过他的面容。现在才知道，原来他是鬼。姚知县分别后一年多也去世了。

The Daoist Priest Zheng Jianchang Prince Wencheng was famous for his Daoist arts. His disciple, Daoist Priest Zheng, learned the Five Thunder Technique. He traveled to the regions of Jing and Fu, performing rituals to pray for rain and exorcise evil spirits. When he summoned thunder and lightning, it responded immediately, as if answering his call. In the early years of the Shaoxing era, he came to Lichuan. Several guests went to visit him, wanting to see the so-called god of thunder. Zheng refused them, but they persisted. He then proceeded as usual, reciting spells and writing talismans, wielding a sword and shouting. After a long while, a solemn wind blew, and mist obscured the surroundings. Suddenly, a god appeared, wearing a tall hat and holding an axe, standing before them. The god said, "I am your disciple, the god of thunder. I have been summoned by the master. I wish to know your intention." Zheng replied, "I summoned you for the others to see. There is nothing else." The god became angry and said, "Whenever I receive a command, I must first consult heaven before coming. After the task is completed, I will report back. Now you are summoning me for entertainment. How can I explain this to heaven? This axe cannot be carried out in vain. The master should bear the consequences." He then raised the axe and struck Zheng's head. The guests all screamed in shock and fell unconscious. After a while, they regained consciousness, but Zheng was already dead. The above three stories were told by Li Deyuan.

Huang Wuqiao In Shaozhou, there was a man named Huang Dunli. In his youth, he studied at school but failed to succeed in his studies. He was known for his boldness and playful nature, living comfortably in the village. The villagers, due to his dark complexion and cunning personality, called him Wuqiao. Ten miles away from his residence, there was a large temple that the villagers revered greatly. They placed many offerings outside the temple, which were managed by an attendant. Huang wanted to take the silk fabrics to marry his daughter. The attendant, knowing it was difficult to refuse, suggested, "Why not use the divination cups? If the gods grant you permission, there is nothing you cannot do." Huang bowed twice and prayed, "The silk fabrics are stored in the temple and are quite useless. Moving them to benefit people would be what the gods desire. But the attendant does not understand the gods' intentions and still speaks so. If the great king truly grants it, I wish to see the auspicious divination cups. If I get the yin divination cup, the lady shows mercy, which would be even better. If I get the yang divination cup, all the gods in the temple would agree." The attendant dared not speak and finally carried the silk fabrics back home. One day, Huang met with the villagers and was teased, "You are known for your courage. Why not take a hundred coins to the temple, place a coin in each statue's hand, and then return. We will gather to offer you wine and meat as a reward." Huang dressed himself and went immediately. Two young men, known for their bravery, secretly followed him. They took a side route and entered the temple first, mixing among the clay statues to watch what he did.

After a while, Huang arrived. He bowed and entered, saying, "Huang Dunli has come to distribute money. Please be informed, Your Majesty." He then groped for the idols, placing one in each hand. If he couldn't hold them with his hands, he would place them on his shoulders. Soon, he reached the spot where the young man was standing. He suddenly grabbed his arm. Huang thought it was a ghost and shouted, "Your Majesty cannot control your troops. I have come to distribute money, and yet this little ghost is so rude." He continued as before, showing no fear. After finishing his task, he locked the temple door and went out. His companions then began to admire him. In the past, there was a strange creature in the northern part of the stream. It would come at night to the riverside and, upon seeing anyone crossing, would carry them south. When asked why, it would reply, "I have vowed to do so. It is not for any request." Huang suspected that it must be causing harm to people. Pretending for another reason, he went there night after night. The creature behaved as usual, carrying people south. After three days, Huang said to it, "Reciprocity is valued in courtesy. I have troubled you too much. I wish to repay you with a small effort." The creature refused. Huang forcefully lifted and held it. He had already instructed his family servant to tie grass and light a large stone. As soon as they reached the shore, he threw the creature onto the stone. The creature cried pitifully and begged for its life. When the candle was lit, it transformed into a green-faced giant ape. Huang beat it to death and threw it into the fire. The stench could be smelled for several miles. The strange creature was thus exterminated. This story is told by Xu Bo.

Qiqi, after passing the imperial examination, rented a horse to pay respects. On his way, he passed through a narrow alley where he happened to meet an old man selling medicine. The medicine stand was very ornate and elegant, with dozens of white ceramic jars displayed, containing pre-prepared medicines. It seemed that the stand had been newly cleaned and decorated before being set up. Suddenly, the horse was startled and knocked the stand over, causing the old man to fall to the ground and several jars to shatter. Qiqi dismounted and apologized sincerely. The old man, a commoner, was light and arrogant, not asking where Qiqi had come from. He grabbed Qiqi's sleeve and counted his offenses, saying, "Have you ever seen the Grand Tutor come and go here? His attendants shouted in numbers in the hundreds, and street officers carried sticks and shouted ahead, making those sitting on both sides all stand up, and passersby looked at the dust and stepped aside. Have you ever seen the Chief Magistrate come and go here? Warriors and jailers shouted one after another, and we see his dignity and rush around without time to spare. Now you alone ride a worn-out horse, coming alone."

How can I avoid it? All sorts of insults and taunts, hundreds of words. The crowd of young ruffians was like a wall. Qiqi was known for his wit and eloquence, and he did not show any sign of being moved or shaken. He slowly responded, saying, "Grandfather, your criticism of me is very fitting. I have committed many sins. I am burdened by my horse, but there is nothing I can do about it. But wealth and honor come in their own time. Am I not willing to be a chancellor? Am I not willing to be a prefect? But I have just obtained a minor post. How dare I hope for more? Have you not seen the D Liu pharmacy in Jingzi? The grand entrance stands out prominently, with a large house having seven rooms in front. Although I am not good at riding, I would not accidentally ride a single horse into it and accidentally touch the equipment. The young ruffians all laughed and praised him, and the old man also felt ashamed and discouraged. He told Qiqi in colloquial language, "It's okay, it's okay." And thus let him go. Jingzi is where the Liu family's large pharmacy in the capital is located. Therefore, Qiqi used this to answer. Zhao Tian Jihe said. The Yellow Mountain Prayer of Huang Shixian During the year of Gengwu in the Shaoxing era, Huang Shixian traveled from Putian to take the provincial examination. He had agreed to travel with his fellow townsman Chen Yingqiu, but due to some matters not being completed, he set out a few days later. Passing through Jian'an, he visited the Li Hou Temple on Li Mountain to seek a dream. In his dream, the deity told him, "There is no need for me to tell you; just see that Chen Junqing has already said it." Huang arrived in Lin'an and met with Chen. He asked about his success or failure. Chen had not visited the temple before. He said he did not know. Huang pressed him repeatedly. Chen became angry and shouted loudly, "Shixian will be the top candidate, and Junqing will be second, which is enough." Huang was pleased that this matched his dream and told Chen about it. When the results were posted, it was exactly as Chen had said.

The Filial Piety of Zhou Changshi Zhou Changshi, a wealthy man from Linjiang, was known for his deep filial piety towards his mother, Lady Zheng. Lady Zheng had been suffering from a severe illness affecting her waist and legs for more than five years, making it extremely difficult for her to walk. Despite trying various doctors and medicines, there was no significant improvement. On the Mid-Autumn Festival night of the second year of the Shaoxi era (1191 AD), Zhou Changshi and his wife were attending to their mother, drinking wine and admiring the moon. Seeing his mother struggle to sit and stand, he couldn't help but shed tears. After the meal, they went to bed, and Zhou quietly got up, leaving his wife thinking he was going to the toilet. With a small knife in hand, he went to the courtyard and faced the empty sky, praying to the Big Dipper. He said, "My old mother has been ill for a long time. I have tried all sorts of medicines, but her condition has only worsened. Now I vow to cut open my abdomen and take out my liver to feed her, in order to repay her kindness in bearing and raising me. I hope the divine will show compassion and grant me this wish." After burning incense, he was about to apply the knife when suddenly he heard a voice behind him scolding and struck his back with a stick. Startled, he turned around, but no one was there. There was only a sealed envelope on the ground. He picked it up and looked at it, and inside was a piece of paper that read, "Zhou Changshi's service to his sick mother, his filial piety over many years. This medicine in three pills, given to Zheng Shi Ba Nü." Zhou held the paper, wept, and thanked the divine. He waited until the next morning to give the medicine to his mother, and her long illness suddenly healed. He then told his wife and children about what he had seen.

虞孟文的妾 衢州龙游人虞孟文，用十四万钱买了一个妾。这个妾颇有姿色和技艺，得到了虞孟文的专宠。不久，虞孟文去世了。他的弟弟仲文是个残忍的人，强行将妾给另一个女人。妾被带回家后，仅仅几个月，她梦见已故的主人来找她，责备她说：“你在这里睡觉，难道不觉得不方便吗？”妾醒来后感到害怕，告诉了仲文。仲文说：“他已经死了，怎么还能怕我？”妾在鸡鸣时起床去厕所，正经过大厅时，哥哥拿着棍子坐在大厅上，起来追赶她，打了她两次，她逃跑并逃脱了。后来，她因病去世。 鱼肉道人 黄元道，原是成都一个普通家庭的孩子。他生于大观丁亥年，得了风搐病，双手蜷缩无法伸展，膝盖顶着下巴，面部向后抽搐，又哑巴不能哭。父母想要他死，把他放在房间的一角，饥寒交迫，但他竟然没有死。只有祖母怜悯他，时常给他喂粥。他活到了七岁，遇到一个道士经过家门口，向他母亲要施舍的东西。母亲愧疚地说：“家里非常贫穷，哪里还有多余的力量。”道士说：“那么给我一个孩子也行。”母亲告诉道士那个生病的孩子，道士说：“这样就够了。”道士用布袋装起孩子，背着走了出去。孩子的父亲跟踪道士，发现他去了野外，道士把孩子放在地上，掬起清水给他洗，脱下身上的纸被盖在他身上，拿出一颗药丸放进孩子的嘴里，绕行五十多里，步履蹒跚（原文缺失九行又十个字）。

A single fish, one head, was prepared to be eaten raw. He then, having fallen into [something], stained his finger and tasted it. It was sweet and fragrant like liquor. He held the bowl and drank it all. A metallic sound entered his belly, clanging loudly. Suddenly, he was as if pushed down a cliff. What he saw was still the place of Yuan Mu. A cow was nearby chewing grass, no different from before. He felt his four limbs were not well, and jumped into a mountain stream to sit. The water was deep up to his shoulders. He rolled around in a state of deep satisfaction. After one night, he emerged. His spirit and vitality were now clear and unburdened. His mind was wide open. It was no longer the same as before. He did not know how long it had been. He led the cow back to Wang’s house. The master was surprised and said, “Where has the child gone? He has not returned for a long time.” From then on, he roamed the marketplaces, able to speak of the hidden truths in people’s hearts. Sometimes he would scold someone, saying, “You have acted against the gods, and you will be entered into the register of ghosts.” He would also scold another, saying, “You have deceived the common people, and you will face official trouble.” Later, these things indeed came to pass. The people of the market feared that he would expose hidden crimes, and warned one another to be cautious and avoid him. Master Wang bound him and locked him in a room, but soon he was released. He entered [a place] in Mei Mountain for many years. When Zhang Wei Gong became the governor-general, he came to tour the mountains, accompanied by his mother, Lady [name], and saw him. He took him out. Later, he followed the official out of Shu. Just as he descended the gorges, he left without saying goodbye. He passed by Wudang Mountain, where Master Sun Xu told him, “Huang Yeren of Luofu Mountain. During the reign of Wang, he was the governor of Huizhou. He abandoned his post to study the Dao. Now his rank among immortals is high. You should go to pay homage to him, in order to seek the method of prolonging life and transcending the world.” Happy and resolved, he set out on his journey.

To visit the Chongzhen Monastery on Luofu Mountain for guidance. The abbot said, "The mountain has three stone towers. The highest point is almost impossible to reach. One must climb vines and grab dead trees, like apes climbing. If one falls unfortunately, one will surely be crushed in an unpredictable abyss. If you are not concerned about your life, then you can go." Huang replied, "If I were attached to my life, how could I come here?" The abbot then told him where to go. Proceeding with a staff, he descended the stone tower. Starting from the cliff, he climbed, barely able to put his feet down. As he neared the middle tower, sudden rain and wind arrived. He hurried to a stone cave to take shelter. As dusk approached, he stayed overnight. At night, he heard the sound of the forest rustling. A large python entered the cave, followed by others endlessly. They coiled around him. Huang sat with his eyes closed. By dawn, the snakes left one by one. He continued forward. The cliff path suddenly ended. A single large vine hung down. Using it, he climbed up. Occasionally, he found a small path, but after just a few dozen steps, the path ended. Looking down at the river, he could see as far as the eye could see. Following the direction of the vine as it went up and down, the daylight was almost gone. Finally, he reached the upper floor. A round, bright opening in the middle. Crawling through it, he reached the edge of the cliff. He saw a wild man with green fur covering his body, sitting on a rock. Performing a bow, he stood with hands clasped. The person did not look at all. Huang dared not breathe. After a long time, the wild man suddenly asked, "Who are you? Why did you come here? And why do you want to see me?" Huang answered in detail. The wild man said, "I think you are both hungry and thirsty. I will rise and lift the stone I was sitting on."

At the base of the rock, there was a pool of crystal-clear spring water. Pointing to it, he said, "This can be drunk." Huang took a ladle made from a hornbeam leaf and scooped some of the water. It was about two dou in volume. Soon after, he experienced severe abdominal pain and rushed outside, where he had a violent diarrhea, lasting for over twenty episodes before it subsided. Returning to serve again, he was finally invited to sit down. Only then did he begin to speak, saying, "The fleeting glory and wealth of the world may seem enjoyable, but to an enlightened person, they are no better than a rotting mouse. One who can find contentment in this place is equal to someone living in wealth and status. Even through this life and into the next, there is no tedium. If there is even the slightest impurity, it cannot be retained for a moment. Do you see any other pleasant places here?" Huang replied, "In the courtyard of Mr. You, I am still too timid to look around. How could I know of others?" The rustic man said, "Try to see my place of enjoyment." He reached out and touched the stone wall, which suddenly opened. They entered together. Above, the ground was perfectly level, its surface as smooth as a mirror. Below, clear springs flowed over intricately carved stones, with exotic flowers and plants arranged in all directions. Two pools faced each other. He told Huang, "Stay here and manage my garden for me. The water from the eastern pool can be used for drinking, and the water from the western pool for irrigation. Do not make any mistakes." He then left first, closing the wall door behind him. Huang followed his instructions, finding that the area was about seven or eight zhang in size, yet contained everything. There were five-colored peonies, each flower a foot in diameter. The room was always brightly lit, making it impossible to distinguish between day and night. He lived there for a long time, and the flowers and leaves were always like spring. One day, the rustic man opened the door and entered, very pleased to say, "You truly have focused your attention on this place. You are truly teachable. For now, leave this place."

My method for attaining longevity and eternal vision differs from the path of extinction. One must exhaust all worldly connections before it can be practiced. In addition, you have consumed the precious spring water, and all impurities have been cleansed. One should have something else to eat. Take fish and meat from the bowl, as you would from the old mountain, and give it to him. Point to the cave where he urinates and make him drink it all. Send him down the mountain. Say to him, "When you return, give fish and meat to people you meet. Eat it at will. Until you no longer wish to eat, come back to see me." Huang bowed twice and left. From then on, he could eat raw meat up to ten pounds. Later, it gradually decreased. In the twenty-eighth year of the Shaoxing era, he was summoned into the palace. He was bestowed the name Yuan Dao and the title Da Zhen Xiansheng. He was forbidden from eating fish. A poem of praise from the emperor was bestowed upon him. It said, "Eating without fire, the people of the ancient past. Writing without thought, its profundity is beyond measure. Externally showing simplicity and rusticity, internally containing ultimate truth. White clouds without a trace, the Purple Palace always green." Zhou Kui, the vice-premier, was formerly friendly with him. Living in leisure in Yixing, Huang visited him. He wrote the ten characters "Bright Moon Double Streams Water Clear Wind Eight Songs Pavilion" as a gift. Two years later, Huang was exiled to Wuzhou due to a verbal transgression. Zhou, the vice-premier, happened to move to govern. The prediction began to be verified. All these accounts were mostly obtained from Zhou. In the second year of the Gandao era, I saw him in Poyang. He ate two pounds of meat and still drank a bucket of water. This confirmed the beginning and end of his attainment of the Dao, which did not differ from Zhou's account. Therefore, I compiled a summary of the main points. Another year, in Jiujiang, he was impeached and punished by the prefect Lin Li and the vice-prefect Huang Zhong. He was flogged and exiled.

The Tomb of Fang Xiang's Father Chancellor Lü Zhongmu, during the early years of the Zhenghe era, buried his father in Licheng, Jinan. He dug through a wall for two zhang and discovered a stone coffin. The tomb site was still in good order, but it was empty, with no grave goods or remains. Only one stone remained, inscribed with the words, "The tomb of Fang Yanqian, former Cishi of the State of Sili." This site matched exactly with the location predicted by Lü Zhi's family, not a single inch different. They then buried him there. Fang Yanqian was the father of Fang Xiang, who served as a chancellor during the Tang Dynasty. Fang Xiang was a virtuous chancellor of the peaceful era, and Lü Zhongmu was also a famous chancellor of the revival era. Five hundred years apart, yet the evidence aligns so perfectly. Remarkable indeed, as recorded by Zhao Bukui.

The Daoist Who Planted Fennel At the end of the Zhenghe era, Lin Lingsu gave a lecture at the Bao Lu Palace. Thousands of Daoists and laypeople gathered, all kneeling and bowing in respect. Among them stood a single Daoist, glaring fiercely. Lin was surprised that he did not bow and shouted, "What is your ability that you dare to act like this?" The Daoist replied, "I have no ability." Lin said, "If you have no ability, why are you here?" The Daoist retorted, "Sir, you have no ability you cannot perform, so why are you here?" Emperor Huizong, listening from the pavilion, found this remarkable. He asked the Daoist to demonstrate his true abilities. The Daoist bowed and said, "I can nurture all things." The emperor then ordered the Daoist to take some fennel seeds and plant them at the base of Mount Genyue. Two guards were instructed to supervise the planting and stay overnight at the temple. By the third drumbeat, the Daoist had vanished. The next day, the fennel was seen growing lushly in a cluster. The Vice-Chancellor Zhu In the third year of the Jianyan era, residents of Fengcheng County, Yuzhang, near the Ba Qi Pavilion by the river, heard someone calling out "Vice-Chancellor Zhu" for several consecutive nights, but no one was seen. Soon after, the new governor of Ganzhou, Feng Jizhou, a scholar-official, arrived at the pavilion to take office. His servant, Zhu Xiu, drowned on August 4th. These two stories were recounted by Wu Huchen.

Swallow Tower Behind the residence of the Tan Prefecture government offices stood the Swallow Tower. It was far from the main hall of the residence, and family members could not reach it. A certain military commander, known as "卿," enjoyed visiting it. Late in life, he took a woman from a respectable family as his concubine, and named her "Susu er." He favored and spoiled her greatly. One day, she also climbed the tower. He asked her why she had come, and she replied, "I wished to see my master. My heart did not fear the distance." He was even more pleased, and they lingered together for a long time. He then sent her away. Late in the afternoon, he returned home, and Susu welcomed him in the hall. He looked at her and said, "Did you have no other business when you returned?" The concubine was startled and replied, "Today I was in the room. My footprints have never left the house. How could that be?" The official became angry and said, "You came to the Swallow Tower to see me. I spoke with you. Why hide it?" Susu's face turned red and she said, "I have never known the path to the upper part of the tower. How could I have dared to walk there alone? You are just teasing me." The bystanders all testified that this was not the case. The official was confused and unhappy. He entered the sleeping chamber and lay down, suspecting that what he had seen before was surely a ghost or spirit. Soon after, Susu entered the room, patted his back, and helped him to sit up. She said, "I truly went to your place. I was afraid others would know, so I kept it secret. I also did it just to tease you. Why be so worried?" The official's mind finally eased, and they laughed together. Suddenly she said, "Now I will tell you the truth. I am not Susu." She asked him to look closely at her. When he looked, he saw a large, dark green-black face, extremely ugly and terrifying. The official struck the bed and screamed. Others rushed in quickly, but saw nothing. He then fell ill and soon died. This story was told by Wang Jiashou, and heard by Zhang Jingfu.

The Wife of Ruanyuan, the Governor of Chenzhou Ruanyuan, an official of the Ministry of Revenue, was from Jiujiang. In the final years of the Xuanhe era, he served as the governor of Chenzhou. His son's wife died of illness and was temporarily buried in Tianning Temple. As Ruanyuan was about to be replaced by another official, he told his son, "I am old now, and I am fortunate to be able to unseal my seal and return to my hometown. Old men have many taboos. I do not have the time to bring my wife's coffin eastward. You should instruct the temple monk to watch over it. You can come and take it at another time." The son dared not disobey. That night, Ruanyuan dreamed that his wife came to him, knelt down, and wept, saying, "I am temporarily buried in the temple, and I am a guest spirit. I am constrained by the temple's guardian deity. Although I can sometimes return home, every time the temple bell rings at dawn and dusk, I must rush to obey its command. It is extremely sorrowful. Now that I cannot return with you, I will never be free. I am afraid that the coffin will be a burden. Please burn the coffin and carry my bones. If I can be buried early in the hills, I will have no further regrets." Ruanyuan woke up moved by the dream and instructed his son to first escort the coffin back to Jiujiang for burial. That night, he dreamed that his son's wife came to thank him.

Yue Shilang's Transformation On the evening of the last day of the year in the 11th year of the Shaoxing era, Yue Fei, the Vice Minister of Military Affairs, died unjustly. His son, Shang Qing Lin, and his brother, Shang Zhen, along with their wives and daughters, were all imprisoned in Huizhou Prefecture, confined in a small room at the corner of a temple wall behind the military commander's hall. The brothers slept on opposite sides of the bed, barely enough space for them to lie down. All food and movement were subject to the orders of the military commander. After Qin Hui's death, the court exonerated Yue Fei, and an edict was issued to visit his family and restore his sons to official positions. Shang Qing had not yet accepted his appointment when, one night, he heard the temple bell ring. Feeling as if in a dream, he saw a soldier in a green robe, resembling a quick runner from the imperial guard, with his sleeves tied around his waist, carrying a bamboo basket in his hand, filled with various tools like knives, swords, hammers, and chisels. The sharp edges and blades were placed on the bed. The soldier bowed deeply once and shouted loudly, saying, "By the command of the Heavenly Emperor, I am here to transform the official into someone with divine bones." After finishing speaking, he climbed onto the bed. Shang Qing was terrified and sweating profusely, listening carefully to what was happening.

He then used the tools he brought, freely applying cutting and removing. Yet, he did not feel any pain at all. In a short time, the matter was completed. He gathered the tools and descended. He then sang, indicating that the bone replacement was complete. Bowing and informing, he left. Shang Qing lifted the curtain and looked at it. There was a skull of one body, lying on the ground from head to foot. He woke up in shock. It was already noon. Zhen, who was beside, heard his brother's very painful groaning sound. Calling and shaking him did not result in a response. Thinking there was no strategy that could be done, he only sat firmly and guarded. Until now, he still had not washed and combed. Shang Qing detailedly told the things he saw. Just finished, the sedan chair came to invite. Still transmitting joyful words, he then informed that the order had arrived. The unclear part. During the Chunxi period, he held the governor-generalship of Hubei. Poyang's Hu Jingde supervised the grain supply office. Talking about this with him, the blue-robed person transmitted the imperial decree and called him by his high official title. He did not want to speak for himself. Later promoted to vice minister of works and governor of Guangdong, he died.

#### The Strange Transformation of the Zhu Family's Silkworms In a village in Huzhou, there was a man named Zhu Fuda from the Zhu family. Year after year, he was engaged in sericulture. He practiced Buddhism very carefully every day, which is why he earned his name. In the fifth year of the Shaoxi era, the silkworms he raised reached their third molt and were nearing maturity. One of them suddenly transformed, with a body like a human, a face like a Buddha, a golden color, and distinct facial features. Zhu took it and placed it in a small box, worshiping it in the incense hall. Neighbors all came to see it. Li Juyuan was there and also borrowed it to view. It was truly indistinguishable from a Buddha statue. After several days, when the box was opened, it had already transformed into a moth and flew away. #### Setting Up a Water-Land Assembly at Jinshan Zhuang Anchang, a scholar and official, was from Yixing. Because his wife had died, he set up a Water-Land Assembly at Jinshan to offer merit to his deceased wife. The affair was completed late at night, and he temporarily placed himself on the bed. In a dream, his wife came, dressed in new, clean clothes with a joyful expression. She took off her shoes and stood on the void wearing socks, gradually ascending into the clouds and disappearing. He woke up in shock and told the monks and others, who all said it was a sign of rebirth into heaven.

The Sixteenth Chapter of the Yi Jian Bing Zhi Tao Tan's Son The magistrate of Jiaxing, Tao Tan, had a son who fell ill in a very unusual manner. His appearance, speech, and laughter were no longer like those of a normal day. Tao Tan was deeply troubled by this. He consulted shamans and priests, tried every method of exorcism, but none could cure him. Just then, the distinguished Buddhist monk Yuan Jing, a master of the Tiantai school and skilled in blessing water, happened to be in the region on official business. Those who drank his blessed water were instantly healed, and people from Wu greatly revered and followed him. Tao Tan had long heard of his reputation and immediately went to visit him. He presented a detailed account and said, "When my son first fell ill, a woman from outside came and teased him. After a while, they both left. Gradually, they walked to the water's edge, where she left behind a poem saying, 'Born as a person of the year of the wood rabbit, to die as a lonely ghost. The gate of the spring remains open, the bed and curtains await your arrival.' Since then, she has come frequently and said, 'In the middle month of winter, between the 14th and 28th day, on the night of the full moon, carriages and horses will come to greet you. Now the time of the demon's departure is near. I do not know where to go. I hope for your mercy.'" Yuan Jing agreed and, carrying his staff, followed Tao Tan to his home. He prepared a site for a ritual platform, set up an image of the Bodhisattva Guanyin, took a willow branch and dipped it in water, sprinkled it while reciting a spell, and circled the platform three times before leaving. That night, the child slept peacefully. The next day, Yuan Jing sat in the lotus position and called the child, asking, "Where do you live and how did you come here?"

The speaker replied, "East of Kuaiji, south of Bian Mountain. This is my dwelling. Surrounded by ancient trees." When asked about their surname and name, they answered, "In the deserted place on Wu King Mountain, I have often practiced dancing under the wind." The monk said, "Are you of the Liu family?" The speaker laughed softly. The monk continued, "Since the beginning of time, you have been deluded and chased after material things, bound by them, drowned in lust and evil, wandering through countless lifetimes, unable to liberate yourself, entering the realm of Mara, bringing about disasters that extend to the innocent. You should now know that Mara is not Mara, that Mara is the Dharma Realm. I will now proclaim the Secret Mantra of the Supreme Samadhi of the Lion's Roar for you. You must listen attentively, feel deep remorse, acknowledge past mistakes, and return to your original pure awareness." The speaker then wept and wailed, no longer speaking. That night, they told their child, "The merit of eloquence. Your father's devotion cannot be surpassed. I will depart." Two days later, they returned and said, "Having been with you for a long time, I cannot part so soon. Let us drink one cup together in farewell." They drank together to the brim, and after finishing, the speaker composed a poem: "The 14th day of the twelfth month is a good time. We have no chance to meet again by the river. Today, a cup of wine at the parting. Farewell, a thousand miles apart." They then departed and were never seen again. Qin Shaoyou recorded this event.

The Taoist of the Great Pure Palace Haozhou is the hometown of Laozi. Therefore, the Great Pure Palace was established to revere and serve him. There was once a Taoist who sold medicine, wearing tattered clothes and living in poverty, but with an air of arrogance and self-importance. Carrying a medicine furnace to the hall to burn medicine, he spoke boastfully and exalted himself, pointing to the sacred image and saying, "This is my disciple. I am the teacher of Laozi." The crowd gradually gathered. In a short while, fire emerged from the furnace, scorched his clothes, and flames erupted all over his body. He was shocked and ran away. The people around him tried to douse him with water, but it did not extinguish. He ran wildly through the courtyard. Wherever the fire passed, other objects were not burned, only his body was burned. Later, he faced north before the image, as if bowing his head in submission, and then died. Looking at his body, all were scorched and burned.

Wutai Mountain The Taoist priest Qi Xizhuang, whose origins are unknown, studied health preservation. He enjoyed traveling to famous mountains and came to Wutai Mountain, where he was so delighted that he could not bear to leave. He built a thatched hut and lived before the site of Yan Zhenren's rock. The mountain was abundant with chestnuts, yam, and various edible fruits and vegetables. He collected them seasonally for food. After living there for three years, monkeys entered his room and did not leave even when he tried to chase them away. They watched him sitting, standing, and doing various things and imitated him from the side. Xizhuang was greatly surprised and recalled when he first entered the mountain, a guest had taught him a method to chase away monkeys. The method involved taking monkey feces, hanging it up, and striking it. He tried it, and the monkeys left. Just a few days later, another large monkey appeared, like a child aged five or six years old, with hair hanging down to the ground. It looked at Xizhuang intently and imitated his actions as before. Frightened, Xizhuang did not dare to chase it away again. He intended to leave the mountain but was undecided. He heard someone calling him and went out of the door. He saw a child with a topknot, wearing a yellow single-layered robe and a green belt, with bright eyes and an appearance not entirely human. The child asked, "From where did you come to Malong Mountain?" Xizhuang pointed to it, and the child went away quickly like flying, crossing hills and valleys directly. It could not be seen from a distance. Since then, all objects around the house made sounds at night. One night there was heavy snow. In the morning, he saw countless footprints outside the door. Xizhuang felt frightened and could not stay there anymore. He went down the mountain and got a disease of muteness, taking several years to recover. These two stories are found in Zhang Wenqian's collection.

Wang the Shao Bao Wang De, the Shao Bao, was buried in a location several miles from Jiankang. In the thirty-first year of the Shaoxing era, his wife, Lady Li, visited his grave during the Cold Food Festival. She spent the night outside the city and left early in the morning. On her way, she stopped at a villager's house for a brief rest. Before dawn, the villagers recognized her as a member of the Shao Bao's family and said, "The Shao Bao passed by here last night. He hasn't gone far yet." Lady Li was surprised and asked why he had stopped. The villagers replied, "Last night, halfway through the journey, a group of horsemen passed by. Three high-ranking officials dismounted and knocked on our door, asking for five thousand coins to buy grain for their horses. After a long time, they left. Their intentions were not very warm." Lady Li secretly asked the horsemen about their destination and identity. They replied, "We are Prince Han, Prince Zhang, and the Shao Bao. We are leading troops north of the Huai River to defend against the foreign invaders." Lady Li then took the money they left, which turned out to be worthless paper money, and felt deep sadness and emotional distress. After completing the ritual, she returned home and fell ill, eventually dying. That April, I was in Lin'an and heard the story from a matchmaker named Liu. I did not share it with others but told it only to Han Ziwēn. By autumn, the foreign invaders indeed launched an attack.

Three Night Demons of Yuhang In the fifth year of the Ganda period (1169), a woman named Zhao, wife of the magistrate Yu of Yuhang County, gave birth to a child with a green face, a hairy body, and two flesh horns that were terrifyingly fierce. They killed it on the same day. Not long after, a woman from the same county named Wen gave birth to a child that was exactly like the previous one, with two faces facing each other, much more extraordinary than what is commonly seen or heard. They also killed it and bribed the midwife with thirty thousand coins to keep quiet, but everyone else already knew about it. Later, a woman from a garden keeper's family gave birth to a similar creature. Within two years, these three strange occurrences happened, all within one or two li of each other. Isn't this a result of the same evil qi? This story was told by Wang Sanshu.

Zhang Changxian, the third son of Jie Zhongshu, was arrogant and obstinate. Qin Guan, the chancellor, appointed him to a higher position due to his father's influence. In the twenty-fifth year of the Shaoxing era, he was appointed as the Transport Commissioner of Jiangxi. His residence was in Xunzhou. Before leaving, he followed the prefect Lin Jingdu to visit his family tomb, but he was angry that no silver incense burner was set up. He grabbed and beat Wu Chengzhong, the prefectural official, and Lin Jingdu dared not resist. After serving for three months, he was dismissed due to complaints. He was further punished for accusing Zhang Jun, a general, of writing inappropriate poetry for his birthday, and was stripped of his official status and exiled to Xunzhou. The Ministry of Justice ordered one official and ten soldiers to escort him. At the time, Zhang Changxian was returning from Yuzhang. He had not yet arrived at Xunzhou, and the prefect of Xunzhou sent someone to meet him on the road. Wu Chengzhong, who happened to be the person assigned to the escort, was just given the task and did not prepare any supplies. He immediately set out and met Zhang Changxian on the road. Zhang Changxian ordered him to get off the horse, took off his hat, and made him walk in front of the horse. After half a day, Wu Chengzhong was so exhausted that he could not bear it. He happened to meet his friend Huang Fu Shitong, who wept and explained the situation. Huang Fu Shitong bribed Wu Chengzhong with 200 taels of silver, and Wu Chengzhong allowed Zhang Changxian to wear his hat and ride in a carriage, and also took two of his concubines with him. Whenever they stayed at an inn, Wu Chengzhong ordered the ten soldiers to guard Zhang Changxian, and they shared a room, locking the door, while Wu Chengzhong took the two concubines. After two months, they arrived at Xunzhou. At that time, a deadly disease broke out, and about 14 out of 15 people in Xunzhou died. The prefect Zhang Ning provided a place for them to stay outside the city. Zhang Changxian fell ill due to the disease and his condition worsened after a few days. He did not have time to enter the city before he died. Wu Chengzhong also died soon after. It was said that his evil deeds had reached a point where they could not be tolerated anymore.

Huayang Temple Poem** In the spring of the 25th year of the Shaoxing era, while Qin Hui was chancellor, his son, Qin Xī, came to Jiankang to perform the ritual of burning yellow paper. He then visited the Huayang Temple on Mount Mao and composed a poem: "My ancestral mountain, a blessed place, is said to be the champion. The three peaks are resplendent with beauty in a single day. Where did the scattered treasures go? The southern cave of the green cliff is piled with white clouds." At the time, Song was the governor of Jiankang. That very day, the poem was engraved on wooden boards and displayed in the temple. By evening, Qin Hui went to see it and noticed faint white words on the side of the plaque. He ordered a ladder to be brought and climbed up to read them. They were a response to his poem: "Arrogance in wealth and status is the root of all evil. How long will youth and beauty last? Wealth and status are like a spring dream. Beauty and fragrance are reduced to a mound of earth." Reading this, Qin Hui was greatly displeased. At that time, the Qin family held immense power over the empire. On this trip, officials from counties and prefectures hurried to greet and serve them, fearing they would not arrive. Yet, no one dared to criticize them so openly. Qin Hui thoroughly questioned the source of the response but could not find out. Both Song and the Taoist monks were afraid, not knowing what to do. That winter, Qin Hui died.

Qin Changling Qin Changling had a portrait painted. It was hung in his study. A monk saw the portrait and inscribed on it: "A single step could lead to a precipice. If not moved, it will be buried in its place. The Buddha Maitreya resides in a palace with eight thousand floors. Striking opens all doors. Understanding the affairs within the purple silk tent. It is not wrong to linger while walking." It was the 23rd year of the reign of Emperor Xiaozong of Song. By September, Qin Changling was transferred to the position of Signatory Judge in Xuanchou Prefecture and returned. On the journey, he fell ill. His condition worsened in Lishui. He stayed in the empty house of Wang Jigao, the Chief Censor. Suddenly feeling very cold, he wanted a warm blanket. The county magistrate Xue bought purple silk and made a blanket for him. He died in it. The following spring, in front of the Mabu Mountain Temple, he encountered a man with eyes like a ghost, wearing a white cloth robe, carrying a pair of straw sandals and two buns, who sang as he passed by: "Forty-three. Forty-three. A full moon falling into a clear pond." Qin Changling was indeed forty-three years old. These two events were related by Tang Sanyi, a physician from Taiping Prefecture.

In the old instrument office of Kuaiji, there lived a temporary official from the Zhe Dong Pacification Commission named Yan Ling Jiang Gui during the middle period of the Song Dynasty. His two sons were both over ten years old. One morning, they got up early and went to the small chamber in the central hall. They saw a woman dressed in a silk robe and a white skirt, helping her mother to dress and comb her hair. Surprised that she was not a family member, they ran into the room and told their father. Gui quickly got up to look at it and still saw her back. The woman then entered the western room and disappeared beside an old woman's bed. Gui called the old woman to wake up and asked her about it. The old woman said, "Today before dawn, a woman outside the window picked a branch of peach blossom, inserted it into her hairpin, laughed, and entered." She fell asleep again in a daze and still did not know who she was. Gui asked the old gatekeeper about it. The old gatekeeper said, "Twenty years ago, Liu Yi Cao lived here. At that time, his wife died in childbirth in this room. The woman you saw today is probably a human being. The world believes that ghosts are afraid of peach blossoms, but that saying is wrong." This story was told by Jiang Mingyu.

Wang's Two Concubines In the spring of the second year of the Jingkang era, the capital fell. The enemy continuously targeted officials, soldiers, and civilians without respite. Women from the imperial family and entertainers were often not spared. One day, early in the morning, the gate of a certain official named Wang was opened. Two women were sitting outside. They hurriedly entered the main hall and wept, bowing and saying, "We have been sent to the front lines of the army. We managed to escape and return. We dare not go back to our former homes. We wish to become your servants to save our lives." Both women were beautiful. Wang accepted them. Wang had no official wife and favored them greatly. He made a promise not to remarry. Later, when Wang became a Shangshu Lang and was appointed to serve in the ancestral temple, he suddenly considered the idea of marriage. One day after a meal, the two women dressed up and came out to bow. Wang was surprised and asked them. They replied, "Formerly, with our lives hanging by a thread, we received your protection and thus were able to live again. We had a promise of a bright future. We did not expect your favor to end. Our future will be with someone else. We cannot live knowing this. We must part forever. So we come to bid farewell." Wang tried to comfort and stop them. They wept again and said, "It is already this way. But we cannot bear to die alone. In the morning's soup and noodles, we have already placed poison. We fear the poison will take effect soon. We hope you will handle our affairs. We will now lead ourselves to the underworld first." They bowed twice and left. Wang was greatly startled. He got up to see them. They then took each other by the hand and drowned themselves. Wang had no way to deal with it. He called his family to explain what happened. He urgently sought medicine to counteract the poison, but it was too late, and he died.

Wang Xingyuan A man from Linjiang named Wang Xingyuan, whose name is unknown. He lived in a village. When he had not yet passed the imperial examinations, his family was very poor. He went to the city to teach in a private school, earning a monthly stipend of two thousand coins. Once, a neighbor brought a letter from his family, requesting to buy something in the market. At the time, it was still several days before the next payment. Wang ordered the student to inform his parents and borrow the money in advance. The student took the money and went out, but just as he did so, Wang temporarily left the house. The neighbor then placed the money on the table but did not tell Wang about it. That night, Wang dreamed of two snakes coiling and dancing on a bed. He woke up in a panic and could no longer sleep. The next day, the neighbor was about to leave, and Wang told the student about the dream. The student then fully informed Wang. Realizing the meaning of his dream, Wang sighed deeply and said, "An income of two thousand coins is very small. Even if I receive it a few days early, how can I compare to the wealth of Tao Zhu and Yi Dun? I would rather wear straw sandals and return home, eat wild vegetables and coarse food, and spend the rest of my life in simplicity. Fame, official rank, and wealth are not for me." That very day, he abandoned his teaching position and left, no longer interested in pursuing advancement. When the imperial examination edict was issued, his friends urged him to participate, but he reluctantly entered the examination hall. He was recommended and passed, and the following year, he won first place in the provincial examination. He became an official and gained prominence. His elder brother, who was in the school, heard about this from fellow students.

A Woman from Guangzhou A woman from a civilian household in the foreign quarter of Guangzhou. Her parents deeply loved her. They arranged a marriage for her. The daughter was extremely rebellious and disrespectful, showing disrespect to her parents every day. In the seventh month of the twenty-fifth year of the Shaoxing era (1155 CE), she became drunk during the day. She then cursed her mother. She then went out of the house, making gestures with her right hand, speaking out in extremely foul and offensive language. The neighbors could not bear it and were about to report her to the authorities. Suddenly, a cloud appeared above her head, followed by a loud thunderclap. The daughter was struck dead on the road. Her body did not fall, and her hand was still raised as it was initially. I was in Nanhai at the time and heard of the incident. A Dream of the Mill At the end of the Jingkang era (1127 CE), a high-ranking official who did not want his name recorded was guarding a county between Qingzhou and Qizhou. He died tragically. More than ten years later, his son dreamed of walking on a wide road, lined with elm and willow trees, with no one around. He heard a loud noise coming from in front, as if it were hundreds of drums, muffled. As it got closer, he suspected it was a large army coming. He hurried to hide in a room beside the road and secretly peered through the window. When it arrived, he saw hundreds of ghosts carrying large mills, spinning without stopping. A human head protruded from the mill, with blood flowing profusely. Upon closer inspection, it was his father. He was shocked and in pain. Another sound like before occurred, and he looked closely. It was his mother. He couldn't help but cry loudly. He then woke up, fearing that the supernatural omen was terrifying. He hurried to Yanzhou, using several thousand coins to perform a yellow talisman ritual. He invited a relative of the imperial family, the military commander, and the supervisor of the ritual to preside over the ceremony. That night, everyone saw outside the bathhouse a person wearing a purple robe and golden belt, about a foot long, with clearly recognizable eyebrows and eyes, standing at the foot of the banner. After a while, he entered the bathhouse. The ritual was completed.

Zi Ju wrote a memorial to plead for his father's life. He said to his sons, "My father's affairs are too painful to speak about openly. You should let your brothers and younger siblings see it for themselves." He took a large box, filled it with ash, sealed it with mud around the edges, and left it for several days before opening it. When he opened it, there were written characters on the inside that resembled those used in the world, stating, "Someone who corrupts the state and harms the people. His sins are unforgivable." The sons wept bitterly and left. Officials in high positions were not known to have committed serious offenses, yet they still faced divine retribution after dying prematurely. Is this not a legacy of three generations? Zhang Jinyan happened to be there. He chanced to walk under the altar and met a man dressed as a woman who wept and said, "I am a relative of your father." During the Jiaqing era, she followed her husband to the north, where she was killed by the invaders, and her spirit had no place to rest. Thanks to the power of the ritual tonight, she has been able to come here. She asked about the families and relatives in great detail. Jinyan also inquired about the relatives who had passed away, and they exchanged words. It was almost dawn, and he could not leave. By morning, he ascended the altar, stood behind the monk, and disappeared when the sunlight became intense. Wang Jia's story was told to Jinyan by Wang Jia.

**The Formula for Treating Abscesses and Boils (异人疽方)** Hu Quan, the magistrate of She County, encountered a legendary figure in the capital city. The figure imparted to him the formula for treating abscesses and boils, called Neito Sanfang. He said, "My medicine can make those that have not yet formed disperse quickly, those that have already formed rupture quickly, and the pus will naturally drain out. Do not use hands to squeeze, and the diseased tissue will naturally fall off. There is no need for surgical instruments. After taking it, the pain will suddenly decrease." The method involves using two liang (a unit of weight) of ginseng, angelica, and astragalus, and half a liang of ligusticum, szechwan lovage, poria, platycodon, white芷, licorice, and cinnamon. All are finely powdered and mixed. Each dose consists of three to five qian (a unit of weight) and is taken with hot wine. If one cannot drink, it can be boiled in wood香 (woodruff) decoction instead. However, it is better to take it with wine for its remarkable effects. People from the capital city had over seventy abscesses on their backs, and many doctors tried their skills but were ineffective. Quan showed them this formula, and they looked at each other and laughed, saying, "We have never heard of treating abscesses and ulcers with such ingredients." Quan insisted, saying, "Ancient prescriptions have their own meaning. Consider these ten ingredients, all of which are gentle in nature."

Generally speaking, the fundamental principle is to promote blood circulation and strengthen the middle burner and qi. Even if it cannot completely cure the illness, it will certainly not cause harm. What harm is there? Then personally prepare the medicine and take it, with half a sheng of hot wine, taking six qian of the medicine. After a short while, the pain is reduced by seven-tenths. After taking it several times, the wound becomes severely ulcerated, with pus and blood flowing out, as if something is supporting it from within. After a month, it heals well. Another elderly man had a hard lump on his chest, with toxic qi spreading upward, like a large gourd placed obliquely, and the lump cannot be moved. After taking the medicine for one day, the lump disperses, with small lumps about the size of chestnuts remaining. The next day, they are as smooth and normal as usual. Another old man had a condition on his head and would not believe this prescription, dying at the hands of the doctor. The next year, his son also fell ill, with the same condition as his father. To learn from his previous mistake, he drank heavily and took the medicine, getting drunk all day, rolling around on the ground. When he woke up from the drunkenness, the disease had gone. There were many other cases of effectiveness, and it is truly a treasure for saving the world, like a divine being. When selecting medicines, it is important to be precise and discard the coarse, taking the clean and weighing it. I gave this to my two brothers and had it engraved in Xin'an and Dangtu counties.

**Wang Family Stone Inscription** A man from Shaowu named Wei, in the second year of the Da Guan era, buried his parents by the road at the lower part of the Ta Yuan Monastery to the west of the county. After a month, following a rainstorm, he noticed a faint mark on the side of the grave. Digging it up, he found two silver wine cups, one copper water jar, and one mirror. He also discovered a buried stone inscription. The inscription read: "A daughter of the Wang family of Langya, the wife of Xi Zai of Jiangnan, buried in the west of Shicheng in the leap month of the seventh month of the year Bing Shen. All the objects are ancient, yet their craftsmanship is exquisite, beyond the skill of even the finest artisans of the world." **Minister Feng** Huang Feng and Feng E, two distinguished scholars from Shaowu, once visited the Temple of Fu Hua Wang in their county together in search of a dream. In their dream, they heard the words "Huang San Yuan and Minister Feng." Both were pleased and proud of themselves. Later, Huang Feng passed the military examination as the top candidate and repeatedly topped the literary examination, yet still failed to pass the final imperial examination. Thus, the so-called "three yuan" were not as they had dreamed. Feng E took the provincial examination and ranked second. He passed the imperial examination and entered the first category (Jia Ke), becoming a professor in Lin’an Prefecture and acting as head of the Guozijiang (National Academy). He formed a close friendship with Lin Dainai, also known as Mei Qing, who was from the same examination year. Lin quickly rose to the position of Minister of Personnel and recommended Feng E to take his place. However, before this recommendation could be acted upon, Feng E died in office. Thus, the so-called "Minister" was not as the dream had foretold.

The *Yijian Zhi* Collection, Volume 17, Part 11 #### Shen Sees a Ghost Shen, a family from the region of Yue, has lived by the side of a mountain road for generations. The people of the county hold the Dongyue Temple in Zhuji in great reverence. Every March 28th, on the birthday of the God of Heaven, artisans and performers from several counties gather at the temple. Passengers traveling through the area must pass by Shen's house. In the year of the reign of Emperor Xiaozong (Shaoxing), three wandering Taoist priests returned to the TianTai Mountains. On this day, they stopped briefly at Shen's gate. One of them was old, wearing tattered clothes, while the other two were quite strong and well-groomed. They carried dried food and a horse scoop and similar items. After sitting for a while, Shen went out to see them. The three bowed deeply and asked for water to wash the rice and make a meal. Shen also gave them vegetables and cloudy wine. They all thanked him and finished eating. The older one then calmly said, "You will soon suffer from an eye ailment. I will give you three pills. Use them when the illness strikes." Shen nodded in agreement. Soon after, they left. The older one added, "On the Mid-Autumn Festival, I will come back here. Please wait for me at the door. If we do not meet, we will not see each other again." Shen nodded again. He placed the pills in a hidden and secluded place in the temple. He never told his family about this and no one believed him. In June, Shen indeed suffered from a severe eye infection, with swelling and pain so intense that he could not sleep or eat. He tried every possible medicine, but the condition worsened. Only then did he remember the hermit's words but forgot where the pills were. He ordered a thorough search, and after a day, found them in the temple's dust. He took one pill, washed it with water, and used a copper needle to apply it to his eye. It felt like ice and snow, cold and penetrating to the brain. The pain stopped immediately, and the swelling gradually subsided. That night, he slept soundly. The next morning, he woke up, and his eyes were as good as ever.

His residence was fifteen li from the city. There was a stone bridge outside the city called Kuahu. During the recent time of war and calamity, many people had been killed there. One day, he rode a donkey into the city and returned after noon. Passing by this bridge, he saw people with disheveled hair and flowing blood, others with severed heads and arms, three or two supporting each other—no one knew how many there were. Their forms were strange and bizarre, not even a single hair could be hidden. Startled, he fell to the ground. When he rose, he saw them again in their usual state. He was both startled and ran away, daring not to open his eyes. By the time he reached home, the sun had already set. That evening, he went out in front of his house and saw the same thing in the fields and by the water. Terrified, he returned home. A few days later, he went into the city again, and this time he returned earlier than before. What he saw was just as before, but he tried to steady his heart and clarify his thoughts in order to face it. His frightened spirit was somewhat calmed. From then on, he often saw them, and gradually he became less afraid. The villagers became aware of the matter and many went to visit him. Han the Governor had lost his beloved son and could not forget him. He summoned Shen and asked him about it. Shen said, "I only see ghosts and spirits, but I cannot summon, command, or drive them away." Han replied, "I am not doing this for that reason. I only fear that my son's spirit still lingers in the other world. I ask you to look at it once." He led Shen to the place where he had lived before. Shen did not recognize it at first, but when he was told in detail about the person's appearance, manner, and clothing, he realized it was exactly the same as when the son had been alive. Standing in the room, Han and his whole household wept greatly. After that, many came to inquire, and those who did could not be counted one by one. Most of them experienced the same as Han’s case. They then called him "Shen Who Sees Ghosts." Five years later, he gradually saw nothing more. As for the monk's appointment for the Mid-Autumn Festival, he had forgotten it entirely. Those who took an interest in such things lamented this.

#### 仙岩三羊 During the Jianyan period, many northern scholars and officials fled to the southern lands. Wang Xian Dao, a high-ranking official, brought his family to Guixi County in Xinzhou. They settled in a mountain temple near the scenic area of Xianyan. The local people frequently visited and interacted with the Wang family, gifting them three live sheep. The Wang family had always abstained from killing and could not bring themselves to sell the sheep. They released the sheep into the mountains, where they were left to fend for themselves. The sheep climbed to high places and eventually became trapped on a steep cliff. They remained there, living in a cave, eating and drinking as usual. After three years, the Wang family moved elsewhere, but the three sheep still remained. The locals came to call them the "Xianyan Three Sheep." They were not seen again after more than twenty years. Xianyan is not far from Longhu Mountain, and it is known for many miraculous sites, making it a place of spiritual significance. Zhang Nan Zhong recounted this story. #### 灵显真人 In the fourth year of the Jianyan era, Zhang Wei Gong was in Shu. At a time when there were setbacks in Central China, he secretly prayed at the Lingxian Temple in Langzhou. In a dream, a god spoke to him, saying, "I once held the rank of a king and corresponded with the world below. I was responsible for all matters of good and bad fortune. However, after the Da Guan period, my title was changed to True Man. Although the name is more exalted, I am now in a lesser position and do not concern myself with the myriad affairs of the world. I have been offering sacrifices of blood to this day, and I feel ashamed. The great plans of the state, how could I possibly know them?" Zhang Wei Gong woke up and was amazed. He immediately requested that the deity's original title be restored and performed a ritual to announce this. From then on, the deity's influence returned to its former state. The deity is commonly known as the Second Prince.

#### Xingyuan Dream In the third year of Shaoxing, Liu Yanxiu Ziyu served as the governor of Xingyuan. He went to visit the Temple of Lingxian Wang to inquire about the peace or unrest of border affairs during autumn and winter. That night, he dreamed of entering the temple. The deity summoned him to ascend the hall. Liu asked the deity about what he wanted to know. The deity said, "I have just now asked the Emperor. I also do not know." As he was about to leave, the deity had a woman hold a tray to show him. The deity said, "Congratulations on the downfall of Liu." Liu looked at the objects on the tray and saw only one pig lung and one pomegranate. Upon waking, he secretly rejoiced, knowing that Liu Yuxi would soon be overthrown. Four years later, Liu Yuxi was indeed overthrown. #### Geshan Snake In the year Ximao of the Gandao era, there was a prolonged drought in Raoyang. The river flow was all sluggish. Three fishermen from Geshan went into Fanjiang empty-handed to catch fish. Two of them came out first. One of them suddenly felt his legs turn cold as if they were covered in ice, and there was a slight trace of saliva. Fearing that the snake had dug a hole beneath him, he hurriedly left. One person alone did not return. His family was told to guard the place. By evening, he returned. Two days later, his body floated five miles away. There was a hole the size of a fist on his left thigh, and his entire body had turned white. It was because the snake had surrounded him and sucked his blood. The snake's appearance is entirely the same as that of a eel, and it can grow up to eight or nine feet long. It is also a type of dragon. A villager named Li Shí from Geshan once caught one.

The Deity of An Guo Temple At the An Guo Temple in Nanchang, a new abbot arrived. On the first night, he led his disciples to chant the Great Compassion Mantra around the corridor. The next night, he dreamed of five dignified men in formal attire. They stood in a row and bowed, saying, "Brother..." (the text is incomplete here). The Fragmented Text The text contains several fragments that are difficult to interpret fully: - "杂肉馒头缺" (a type of food or metaphorical expression, unclear) - "内文缺" (missing text) - "畏龙眼缺" (a fear or concern related to "dragon eyes," unclear) - "内文缺" (missing text) Wang Iron Face Wang Ting, a native of Sanqu, was skilled in physiognomy and did not rashly give approval. The literati and officials regarded him as Wang Iron Face. In the third year of the Ganda era (1167 CE), he came to Lin'an. On June 3rd, he visited me. At the time, I was serving as an acting Secretary of the Central Secretariat and also as an acting Director of the Imperial Academy. Wang Ting said, "Your forehead is very bright and moist. From this day, in 32 days and 49 days, you will have a joy of becoming true." The next day, I was in the palace room, and I mentioned this to the other officials. They all asked me to recommend Wang Ting. I told Wang Ting about it. He said, "What I said has not yet been verified. If you suddenly recommend me, how can I justify it? Wait until you are promoted. If you come again later, I will not miss this appointment." He finally did not come. Zhou Yuan, who was acting as a Deputy Minister of War, wanted to resign. He invited Wang Ting to his office. Wang Ting said, "In winter, you will be promoted. It is not too late to govern a state in the future." Mo Zimeng, a Minister of the Ministry of Finance, and He Xishen, a Minister of the Gold and Treasure Bureau, were also present. Wang Ting said, "In another month, Minister Mo will be appointed to lead the border, and Minister He will be appointed as a supervisor." Zhou Yuan said, "I am just seeking to resign."

There is no reason for a transfer to the north in winter. Even if the Censorate (莫郎中) is transferred to an external position, he should not have received an official title. The Censorate (何郎中) has been in Sichuan for ten years and has held the envoy's staff many times. He returned to the capital less than half a year ago. How can he leave so soon? Ting said, "I believe in my method alone. I cannot do anything about the human affairs you mentioned." He secretly told Yuan Te, "Howe's salary will be exhausted next year. Is it just a one-time departure?" Ting stayed for several days and then immediately returned home. On July 6th, I humbly received the appointment to the Inner Court. On the 22nd, he was appointed to the Inner Court and given the privilege of writing. There was no discrepancy with the two days mentioned. Zi Meng was appointed to the Inner Court and became the governor of Huaidong in August. Xi Shen was sent out to serve as the prosecutor of Fujian and died the following year. Yuan Te was appointed to the Ministry of Personnel in November. Two years later, he became the governor of Taiping Prefecture. All happened as he said. This is something I personally witnessed. And the several events that were transmitted are especially remarkable and worth recording. Xu Jiqing, the Censorate, was serving at the temple in Qu during the 31st year of the Shaoxing era. Ting saw him and said, "You will be summoned to serve in sixty days." Jiqing said, "We are from the same hometown. Don't play around with me." Ting said, "Ting has never flattered anyone in his life."

How can this be? Let's test it with two examples. A month later, I received news of a tragedy from relatives five hundred miles away. Then there was a disaster of falling from a high place. Then my words would be proven true. Later, Ji Qing's eldest daughter, who married Ma Xiyan, died in Lin'an. Ji Qing went to visit his ancestral graves, fell while climbing a mountain, and got caught in a tree but was not seriously injured. Just as the court was selecting an envoy to go abroad, they hurriedly summoned him, and the dates and months all matched. In the year I met him, I once visited Zhenjiang and told the prefectural judge Mao Qinwang, "You will eventually serve in the capital and get a nominal position as a prefect." The chief monk of Jinshan Temple was just entering the monastery. I said, "He will travel two hundred miles in a single day." The monk did not believe it. Just two days later, Fang Wude sent a messenger from Jiankang to invite him, and he left. He sought a decision from me. I said, "He will go there and return. On the day of his return, there will be a minor disturbance, but it will not affect you personally." When he returned, just as he was relaxing his burden, Xijin caught fire, and the rented houses of the temple were burned down. Qinwang completed his term and was appointed to Quanzhou but did not get to take up the post and retired instead. Then he passed through Suzhou and met Wang Junming, who said, "You will suffer the misfortune of losing one's spouse."

From this point, even if the noble person has a minor illness, it is also appropriate to take good precautions against it. Jun Ming dared not answer. His wife, Song, peeked through the screen and heard it. She hit the screen, scolded angrily, and entered. Not long after, she indeed fell ill with abdominal pain and did not recover. Fan Zhi Neng, who was just living in leisure, told him, "Even if you get an official position this year, it will not succeed. Wait until the new year, then it will be very good." A man from Wu named Geng Shi Ju obtained a literary degree through imperial favor. His appearance and behavior were always noble. He received help from Hu Chang Wen to become the priest of the temple of Mount Tai. The court official said, "This person cannot get an official position, but he can still live for several years. Once he starts receiving salary, he will die." Geng Shi Ju died very quickly. Fan Zhi Neng was appointed as the supervisor of the equalizing granaries in eastern Zhejiang. The order was not issued, and he went to sleep. On the day of the Start of Spring, he was appointed as the governor of Chuzhou. He arrived at the prefecture and stayed for several months. He was called back to serve as a court attendant. The court official agreed to see me again. I waited for him to come, but he did not come. I also resigned. Is it not that he knew it would be like this? There is no one who can tell me. All the affairs of Xu Ji Qing were heard from Hu Chang Wen. The affairs of Zhenjiang were heard from Huang Zhong Bing. The affairs of Gusu were heard from Fan Zhi Neng.

**The Dragon Spirit of the Taoshui River** Mor Zimeng was in Wu Prefecture. He took his family on a trip to the Taoshui River. It was the first ten days of June, and the lotus flowers were in full bloom. They drank wine, sang, and enjoyed the beautiful scenery to the fullest. As the sun began to set, they saw a bright light several miles away. Soon, it grew closer, and a cold wind brushed against their faces. The boatman said, "This is the dragon deity passing by.
