We should hurry to avoid it." Mor Zimeng and his family all got off the boat and went ashore, entering a small house. They were still not settled when a strong wind swept past the river, followed by thunder. Lightning flashed brilliantly, moving like a speeding arrow. Heavy rain poured down like water from a pot. It lasted for about two hours, and then the sky cleared, and the sun shone as brightly as before. Looking at the place they had just been, it was almost unrecognizable. The lotus and water chestnut plants were completely washed away, leaving nothing behind. The boat sank into the mud and could not be retrieved. All the items they had brought with them were damaged. If it were not for the boatman's prior knowledge, they would have been in a dangerous situation. Mor Zimeng recounted this experience.

In the twenty-ninth year of the Shaoxing era, during the sixth month of the intercalary period, the chief cataloger Ren Yuanli died suddenly. He held the position of Chief Cataloger and was a lower-ranking official. He should not have received posthumous honors. At this time, the Deputy Secretary of the Imperial Library Ren Xinru discussed the matter with his colleagues and petitioned the court. He explained that Ren Yuanli was the grandson of the former censor Ren Deong and requested that his heir be given special recognition to encourage loyalty and righteousness. At that time, Yu Gong ordered me to write the petition. The proofreader Liu Yishu, upon seeing the draft, said, "The current wording seems too narrow. We should add several sentences to indicate that this action also benefits talented individuals from all over the country, showing that the imperial library supports scholars even in unfortunate circumstances." I was surprised by this suggestion. Shortly after, the Ren family received an official position for one of their sons, just a month later. Liu Yishu, however, died suddenly from eating cold noodles, breaking his abdomen in one night. He also held the position of Chief Cataloger, thus fulfilling the same fate as Ren Yuanli. The Chancellor said, "If this action is repeated, it will become a permanent example, and those who criticize it may not agree." It was heard that Ren Yuanli had written many books. If all of his works were submitted to the government, it could be used as a justification. At that time, Yu Gong, who was the Secretary of the Imperial Library, ordered his son to compile his father's works, totaling several hundred volumes, and submit them. The matter was then reviewed by two ministries.

*Yijian Bing Zhi, Volume 18, Twelve Stories* **Zhang Fengzi** Zhang Fengzi, whose origins are unknown, came to Poyang during the reign of Emperor Gaozong. He stayed at the guesthouse of the Jia family. Every morning, he went out to tell fortunes and returned drunk in the evening. When speaking with others, he seemed to be making sense at first, but then suddenly fell into a state of confusion, making it impossible to understand him further. He kept a chicken and a canary. On a winter night, he sat on his bed with a brazier full of burning charcoal. He placed the two birds on either side of him. As the fire was about to die out, he would say, "The fire is warm enough. You can sleep now." He was particularly skilled at summoning mice. An elderly woman named Shen asked him to do so. Zhang scattered food on the ground, chanted a few Buddhist verses, and after a short while, a large number of mice came out in droves, some crawling through gaps and holes, numbering perhaps a hundred. They gathered in front of him, grabbing the food and eating it. After they finished eating, Zhang said, "Go now. Do not bite the clothes or damage the utensils." The mice then chirped and jumped about, moving eastward and westward, and were instructed not to run around aimlessly.

If you disobey the command, you will surely be killed. The rat silently withdrew, dared not make a sound. Some suggested getting rid of it, so a spell was recited and it was sent to the government granary, saying that the law does not permit killing. Its eyes were deep blue-green like a mirror. When urinating, it splashed straight up several feet before falling. It loved to sing the *Man Ting Fang* lyrics, saying: "Oh, ox! Strong-hearted and strong-bodied, how many can be restrained? Loving the tender grass on the meadow, just go invading the green and chasing the emerald. After running around, how could they care for the lost ones? How could they know that the mountains are far and the waters are distant. Looking back, returning home is late. The shepherd boy has wisdom, hold the long rope firmly, carry the short end high. Let it go into the mud and water, do not adjust for me, I will regulate myself. Walk steadily under the green pine, play the flute long and loud. When it's time to return, neither man nor ox is seen, it is the time of bright moon." All say it was his work. He stayed for more than a year before leaving. **Ear Ring of the Pig** Jiang Shi Lang Song Wei, a minor official, traveled from the Sichuan road through the gorge to Yun'an Pass. He killed a pig to worship at the temple and, when washing the sacrificial animal, saw a square ring on the pig's ear. The black color was still bright and smooth. It must have been a former person who committed theft.

Grand Marshal Han During the Shaoxing era, Han Gongyi, the Grand Marshal, was granted the ceremonial title of Observing Envoy. He suddenly fell ill, and the Retired Emperor, remembering his old retainer, sent the imperial physician Wang Jixian to diagnose him. Wang declared the disease incurable. At that time, Han's breath had already ceased, and the entire family cried out in grief. Wang Jixian reported back, and the emperor ordered three hundred taels of silver and bolts of silk to be given to Han's family. As Han was on the verge of death, a folk doctor happened to pass by the door and shouted, "Anyone with a rare illness should come." The sons of Han tried to invite someone. The doctor examined Han's complexion and took his pulse, then needled his body several times. Han's nasal breathing was faint but present, and he could barely groan. The doctor then ordered medicine to be administered. By evening, Han suddenly revived. The next day, the incident was reported to the court. Han returned the assistance provided and was again given money for medicine. Among the royal family, those who were good at joking even joked among themselves, saying, "Our family is so poor. If we could die like Grand Marshal Han once, it would be very good." Later, Han became a governor, and after thirty more years, he died. The Khitan Recite Poems When Khitan children first learn to read, they use vernacular language to reverse the order of the sentences and practice them. Sometimes they use two or three characters for a single character. Once, when I was on a mission to the Jin state, the deputy envoy and Secretary of the Imperial Academy, Wang Bu, often told me about this as a source of amusement. For example, when reading the lines "The bird roosts in the poolside tree, the monk knocks at the moonlit gate," they would say, "The monk's gate is beaten in the bright moonlight, the old crow sits on the tree in the water." It was generally like this. Wang Bu was from Jinzhou and was also a Khitan.

The Golden Key to the Star Palace The "Jia Zhi" records the story of a family in Jianchang who worshipped the Purple Girl deity. Another family in the same county, the Li family, also worshipped her very respectfully. One of their sons had not yet married. He often saw a beautiful woman coming and going in the house and thus became intimate with her. They often drank together at the same table, roasted lamb, and fresh fish, but no one knew where these things came from. The parents found out and forbade it, but they could not stop him. They then locked him in an empty room. The woman could still come. After several days, she said to him, "This is not a place of happiness. Why not come to my home?" They then took hands and went out, riding high horses and elegant carriages, with the entourage ready. She urged him to get into the carriage, covering it with curtains, so he barely saw anything. In no time, they arrived at a large city with jade palaces and pavilions, beautiful women lined up in rooms, the climate mild and pleasant, and they could not distinguish day from night. They often roamed to other places. There, they saw many pearl balls, radiant and colorful, hanging between the beams. He asked for their name, and she replied, "These are the stars you often see in the sky." They stayed for a long time. One day, standing by the railing, the woman said, "Today is New Year's Day in the world." The man suddenly realized and said to himself mournfully, "I am very happy here, but on the New Year's festival, I will not bow twice to my parents and wish them longevity. Wouldn't this cause my parents to worry?" The woman already knew his thoughts.

Disappointed, he said, "You have a heart that longs for your parents. I cannot stay any longer. You should return quickly. Our fateful encounter ends here." He ordered wine to drink and say goodbye, took a small bag and placed it in her hand, and warned her, "Just close your eyes and keep your hands together. Follow wherever your feet lead. On the road, you will encounter strange beasts and ghosts, various spirits and mysterious creatures. They will ask for things from you. Take something from your bag and give it to them. Do not exceed this number. If you do, there will be no more. Wait until your feet touch the ground, then you will arrive in the human world. Then make plans to return home." She cried and bid farewell. As she began her journey, she felt as if there was a collapsing cliff and flying rapids beside her ears, the sound shaking the heavens. The wind blew her clothes, and the cold penetrated her bones. A huge beast opened its mouth and bit her sleeve. She remembered what the woman had warned and gave the item and went away. Soon another creature came, and this happened about a hundred times. Feeling for what she was carrying, she found only one item left. Suddenly, she heard the noisy sounds of the market, and her feet also touched the ground. She opened her eyes and asked someone, and it was Si Prefecture. Alone with nothing but herself, she was completely at a loss for what to do. She opened the bag and looked inside, and there was a golden key. She sold it in the market and earned twenty thousand coins. She met a boat heading south and followed it back. Her family members met with mixed feelings of joy and sorrow, saying she had been gone for several months. Li Shaoyu's *Fushuo* (Treatise on the World) is a historical text, and he is a member of the Li family.

A Monk from Lángzhou In Lángzhou, there used to be many mosquitoes. People staying in the marketplaces or inns would not be able to sleep all night due to the mosquitoes. One day, a monk stayed at a guesthouse. The host treated him very generously, often inviting him for small drinks. Although the rent or payment might be short, the host did not check or verify it. The monk stayed for several months before leaving. Before departing, he expressed repeated gratitude to the host and then stood up. At the well, he said, "I have stayed here for a long time, and you alone were able to recognize me. There is no way to repay your kindness. Now your family will never suffer from mosquitoes again." He then took a single pill from a gourd and threw it into the well. He instructed, "Carefully cover it. After three days, it can be drawn." With that, he left. Indeed, as he had promised, every summer evening, mosquitoes would roar and swarm around the eaves but could not enter the room. When Zhang Wei Gong, the provincial inspector, was in charge of Sichuan and Shaanxi, he established his office in Lángzhou. Scholars and merchants came in large numbers, gathering at this inn. Some even spent the night outdoors. Calculating the earnings, it was several times more than other inns.

#### The Fried Shrimp Man During the Jianyan period, Envoy Xie Liang, a high-ranking official, was sent on a diplomatic mission to the Western Xia. Traveling down the Han River, he stopped for the night. He saw thousands of ants rushing into the water, and upon closer inspection, they had already transformed into shrimp. This phenomenon continued without end. Envoy Xie disembarked and traced where the shrimp came from, discovering they emerged from a small tomb. He asked the locals, who told him that an old man had lived there for thirty years, making fried shrimp for a living. The old man had died several months ago, and this was his burial place. It was then discovered that his remains were eaten by ants, and they fell into the category of shrimp. #### Mr. Xu In the early Shaoxing period, Han Shuxia Huang, a censorial imperial historian, returned from a mission to Hunan. He received an order to go to the central government office to report to the chancellor. At that time, the government was newly established, and the official protocols were still rudimentary. Mr. Xu, a provincial governor, was known for his simplicity and haughtiness. He was already in the guest quarters, where he saw Han wearing a green robe seated in a lower position and paid no attention to him. After a long while, he asked, "Where did you come from?" Han replied, "From Hunan." Mru Xu said, "It is not easy to get such an assignment today. Even if you see the court, what good will it do you?" Soon after, the court adjourned, and a provincial official passed by the veranda. Mr. Xu saw him and bowed respectfully, saying, "The order given yesterday for a certain matter has been followed." The official felt ashamed and thanked him, then hurriedly left upon seeing Han. Mr. Xu did not realize it, and another person soon arrived, repeating the same words. He also hurried away, and the chancellor dismounted from his horse.

The provincial officials spoke out boldly, requesting the inspector Xu, who was greatly startled. Xu quickly stood up, wanting to apologize. A brazier was in front of him, and he waved his sleeve over a teapot or kettle, sending ashes flying and filling the room, with no time to say a single word. That day, Han was appointed as the Right Censor, and immediately prepared a report based on his observations to impeach someone. He believed that the envoy or messenger was personally responsible for the mission but was flattering and currying favor with low-ranking officials, and thus was dismissed from office. Several years later, Han was appointed governor of Wuzhou. At that time, Liu Lida, whose given name was Dazhong, was the Minister of Rites, and was soon to hold power. His father was not happy to be in Lin'an and came to temporarily assume the role of a judicial official in Wuzhou. Because he reported matters slowly, Xu rebuked him, saying, "How old are you like this? Why not return?" Liu replied, "My son is not tolerated there, so I am here." Xu stared in surprise and asked, "Who is your noble son?" Liu replied, "Dazhong." Xu suddenly changed his anger into a smile and said, "Your spirit is striking. Although you are old, you are still healthy. The position of a legal official is not suitable for you. The position of a teacher is vacant. Encourage yourself to visit the students once. I will immediately appoint you as a temporary teacher at the state school."

A Legendary Elixir of Life Two stories from the Gui family of Guixi have already been recorded in the *Jia Zhi*. Gui Zhen recounted that his uncle, whose name is unknown, was especially devoted to the Dao. He often practiced breathing exercises to absorb the essence of nature and sought to transform base metals into gold, aiming to achieve immortality and ascend to heaven. One day, a guest visited him, claiming to be able to create the legendary *Jiu Zhuan Da Dan* (Nine-Transformation Great Elixir). Gui Zhen believed him without doubt and treated him with the utmost respect, offering him everything he asked for, spending beyond his means. After a year, the elixir was completed. The guest placed it in a clean room, sealed it with red clay, and adorned the outside with symbols of the eight trigrams, the twenty-eight lunar mansions, the ten days of the Chinese calendar, and the twelve earthly branches. The room was then locked away with great secrecy. The guest told Gui Zhen, "I am now going to visit two sacred mountains to meet my companions. I will return in three years. Only then will the elixir be ready for consumption. Do not violate my words." With that, he departed. Gui Zhen went to the elixir room, lit incense, and paid his respects. After more than a year, he suddenly thought, "Immortals often test people. Even if the elixir is consumable, they might withhold it from me if I am not worthy. What shall I do?" He secretly opened the storage and took the elixir.

Then the complete elixir was clearly present within it. Overjoyed, he did not consult his wife and children, and drew water to immediately consume it. As the medicine was swallowed, an outer report came that a guest had arrived. Just as he entered the door, he saw Gui Sheng and was startled, running away. Gui sent a servant to chase and pull him back. The guest said, "My elixir is complete, but the time has not yet been fulfilled. I never told him the method of taking the elixir. But he did not listen to my warnings. How could I really be traveling to the three mountains? I have never left here. Now it is like this; he will surely die within a few days. I am studying the way of the immortals to achieve longevity. How can I stay with a walking corpse?" He finally left. That day evening, Gui felt a burning sensation in his five organs. The next day, he could no longer bear it, and jumped into the pond outside the door. Within a short time, the pond water boiled, and all the lotus flowers wilted. A tall tree at the corner of the house, he could stand on the top of the tree. Soon after, he came down, rushing and screaming. After three days and nights, he died with blood flowing from all seven orifices.

Lin Lingxiu, a renowned Taoist master, was skilled in the art of commanding the Five Thunder Gods. The capital city had been suffering from a severe drought for a month, with no rain. The emperor summoned Lin to perform his magic. Lin replied, "The will of heaven does not wish for rain. All rivers and water sources in the world have been sealed and locked. Without the command of the Heavenly Emperor, I cannot access them. Only the Yellow River is not forbidden, but it cannot be used." The emperor said, "People are suffering from the heat. If we can get a little rain to wash it away, even if it is muddy, it will be beneficial." Lin accepted the command and immediately went to the Upper Pure Palace, where he instructed Minister of the Inner Academy Yu Wen Cui Zhong to oversee the ritual. Lin took a bowl of water, held a sword, performed the Yu step, and recited spells several times. He told Yu Wen, "Minister, you may leave. If you delay, it may cause trouble with the rain." As Yu Wen left on horseback, a cloud as large as a fan rose in the air, soon covering the sky. The sound of thunder rose from the ground, and the horse was startled, galloping home. Just as he arrived, heavy rain began, accompanied by rapid and powerful thunder and lightning, lasting for more than two hours before stopping. The gutters of houses were filled with mud, and water accumulated on the ground to a depth of more than a foot, yellow and muddy, not suitable for drinking. It was of no benefit to the crops. This account is attributed to Hong Qingshan.

In the first year of the Jianzhong Jingguo era, Huang Tingjian returned from Qianzhong and stayed briefly in Jingnan. There, he met a woman in the village who was graceful and beautiful, a sight he had never seen before. Regretting that she was already married, he composed a poem about the water lily to express his feelings. The poem read: "From mud comes the white lotus root; from manure, the yellow jade flower. It's a pity that the heavenly fragrance is not taken care of by heaven; it has flowed into a commoner's family by fate." He then asked his guest, Gao Zimian, to compose a poem in response. Several years later, Huang Tingjian passed away. The woman, living in a commoner's family, gave birth to two sons. During a famine in Jingchu, they were too poor to support themselves, and her husband sold her to the family of Tian as a servant girl. One day, Gao Zimian was invited to a gathering where the woman served wine. She appeared restrained and weary, no longer the same as before. During the gathering, they talked about their past, and Gao Zimian was moved. He asked the master of the house to name her "Guoxiang," using words from Huang Tingjian's poem.

To fulfill the aspirations of the valley. In the third year of Zhenghe, Zi Mian was a guest in the capital. He met with his cousin from Ruyin, Wang Xingzhi Yu, and they spoke about it. Wang Xingzhi patted his thigh and sighed, saying, "It can be recorded in verses. It will be a strange and remarkable event for future times." Zi Mian then wrote a long poem, which was very extraordinary. The poem reads: "The Southern Creek Grand Historian returned to court late. He rested his carriage in Jiangling and was quite friendly. The talented pen once praised the water lily. It is a pity that the heavenly fragrance is not cared for by heaven. To entrust the flower with meaning for Luo Fu. Seventeen years old but not yet fifteen and a half. The gate of Song Yu is distant and respectful. The courtyard of Lanqiao is strange and poor. Ten years ago, the gaze formed a distant place. The gentleman did not come to heaven again. The married neighbor's lady has a graceful appearance. Only the sincere words of the literati are passed on. Hearing that the separated phoenix and crane are sad. The soldier is useless and sells the eyebrows. After the wind blows, the peach blossoms bear fruit. When the clouds in the Wu Gorge are full of dreams."

The Goodwill of Mr. Tian Mr. Tian, known for his good intentions, has known him/her for a long time. He reciprocates with precious pearls, treating him/her like a close friend. Despite being worn out, he/she still retains the beauty of the goddess of the Luo River. His/her elegance is comparable to that of the beautiful woman of the Changtai. Her jeweled hairpin, ivory comb, and golden phoenix hairpin are exquisite. At the wine cup, he/she was first introduced to the famous courtesan Dong Jiaorao. Arriving late, Du Mu must surely regret it. The sadness of Suzhou is also understandable. Instead, he compares her to a water lily. Suddenly realizing that the scholar from the west is Huang Xueshi, he only then understands that he/she was once like that. Regretting not writing in the air to form the character "Huang," Prince Wang first heard this detailed story. He requests a poem, composed and given with a sense of melancholy. Now, there is no method to manage the beans. Only Mr. Tian is called the national fragrance. By nature, he uses the rhyme, especially sad and suppressed. He says, "Fallen flowers, the decline of spring, the sadness of late spring. The garden gate can no longer be visited. Waiting for the flowers to bear fruit until spring returns. In the end, only the east wind controls the situation." A woman from the Chu palace with blooming spring flowers, as rain and as clouds, all are abundant. Meeting her personally, one glance makes the nation fall in love.

Not understanding the welcome into a city of one's own. Having already agreed in eyesight before reaching the place of spinning. Future meetings are hard to rely on, as people have already departed. Pitifully, the heavens and earth excel in poetic sound. Not as good as Cui Hu's peach blossom line. Sitting here, one forever holds the sorrow of burying jade. A wandering son would not know the brows of Jingzhao. Hard to bear the separation of the cranes after they have flown apart. Still like the first time of seeing a startled swan. Close family joy and deep love should last long. Temporarily attending a splendid banquet to enjoy guests and friends. Dancing through the spring wind, strength is not enough. In exhaustion, the waist is like a whirlpool of willow. Who on the seat gave the green feather ornament? The style of Shuzhou is especially full of emotion. The joy is intense, the wine flush rises on the jade cheeks. The warm fragrance and red tenderness seem about to melt. Beautiful women with short lives are similar in ancient times. Before and after, one meets the best of the world. Only regretting the time of the full water. That year, no letters of longing were sent. The regret of Yizhou, you can understand. The misty clouds of ten thousand miles are empty and sorrowful. Infinite charm is casually parted. How many people can appreciate the fragrance of Huangxiang?

Zhang Kong Encounters a Taoist Zhang Kong, a man from Bianzhou, failed to pass the imperial examination for the jinshi degree. His family was very poor. His mother's family, the Gongs, had been practicing medicine for generations. Therefore, Zhang Kong also learned the art of medicine and set up a pharmacy behind the Yichun Gate in a market area. Despite this, his business did not thrive. One morning, he got up, put on his clothes, and combed his hair but had not yet washed his face. Suddenly, a Taoist approached from the direction of the rising sun. His eyes were bright and piercing, fixed on the sun without blinking. He directly entered the shop, looked at Zhang Kong but did not bow, and sat down, arranging his clothes. Zhang Kong was greatly insulted by the Taoist's arrogance and asked angrily where he came from. The Taoist replied, "You need not ask where I come from. I only want to see you." Zhang Kong thought the Taoist was a madman, as there were many such people in the capital. He threw a coin at the Taoist and waved him away. The Taoist smiled and said, "I have no request from anyone. I came to teach you because you have a spiritual quality. Why do you reject my gift so strongly?" Zhang Kong realized the Taoist's intentions and stood up, putting on his hat and headband, and went out to speak with him. As they discussed matters of the Dao, the principles and subtleties were profound, and Zhang Kong heard things he had never heard before.

At this point, he began to feel remorse and regret, saying, "I, a humble person with ordinary eyes and a confused heart, am now ashamed. Immortal lord, you have graciously visited. I wish for you to teach me completely." The Taoist said, "Where do you come from?" He replied, "My family is poor, and our rice and porridge are not sufficient. If I can be full without eating, that would be the greatest wish." Soon, a vendor selling steamed jujubes arrived. The Taoist took the first coin he had thrown and bought seven jujubes. He then turned to the person named Gong and said, "Immortals consider avoiding grains as inferior. However, if one abstains from grains, there is no residue or impurity. Without residue or impurity, there is no leakage. From this, one can also enter the path. Zhang Liang and others used elixirs to treat hunger, which is already a detour. If you wish to attain this path, can you refrain from indulgence from now on? If people cannot indulge, worldly thoughts will naturally cease. When worldly thoughts have ceased, then one possesses the talent for immortality. He then took the seven jujubes, looked at them carefully, and blew on them. He said, 'Eat these, and you can remain full for life without eating. If someone forces you to eat, it is not forbidden. If you again wish to abstain, it will be as before.'

But you have an elderly mother and a family. You cannot follow me. However, since you have eaten seven dates, you should then have seven dreams. I will tell you in advance. When you have fulfilled your filial duties and your marriage is complete, if you can already abstain from food, what else do you seek? You should leave and go to a famous mountain, find a deep and spacious cave in a steep and isolated place, build a wall of stones to block the entrance, without any thoughts arising, sit, stand, walk, and stand in it, and there will be delightful experiences. After half a century, then your body will be like a cicada shedding its shell, you will be free and at ease beyond the six directions. Beyond this, it is not something that can be explained to you today. After finishing speaking, he rose and put on his clothes. I tried to keep him, but he would not stay. He got up and went out the door, saw nothing. I then realized that he was not an ordinary person, feeling a sense of loss, for many months, whenever he smelled the smell of food or drink, he would vomit, so he stopped eating, after more than two years, he stopped defecating and urinating, his spirit was clear and bright, his steps were light and agile, he often tested his strength.

From dawn till dusk, he traveled around the outer walls of the city, making five circuits. This covered hundreds of miles. Before and after, he received seven dreams, as the Taoist priest had said, with little deviation. His mother had suffered from hemorrhoids for twenty years, and despite numerous medicines, none were effective. Haphazardly, he gave her the remaining cores of seven dates, and she healed overnight. Kong did not consort with his wife and treated her as a stranger. His wife, Guo, was strong-willed and resolute, and she died in anger. Family members became increasingly worried and suspicious, and they forced him to eat, though he ate the equivalent of several people. Later, he sometimes ate and sometimes did not. Friends suspected him of being fraudulent and locked him in a room to test him, but he did not find it a burden. Cheng called Jian, who then took medicine with him. Upon arrival, he sat on the sick person's mat and sat beside him. Even after ten days or a month, he did not need water or food. He enjoyed drinking and liked writing poetry. At the age of sixty, he looked as healthy as a young man. After his mother's death, no one knew his final fate. Li Fangshu wrote a biography.

The Strange Tales of the Eastern Wilderness, Volume 19, Sixteenth Story The Burial Site of the Song Family Song Wen'an, a native of Kaifeng, was buried in Zhengzhou in his second generation. A Daoist practitioner passed by the site and pointed to the water beside the tomb, saying, "This is an excellent location according to the Five Elements book. One day, it will produce an emperor." Hearing this, the Song family was afraid and ordered workers to block the water with all their might. The water was thus blocked, turning the area into flat land. From then on, the family's fortunes declined, and no one from the family passed the imperial examinations again. In the early years of the Chongning era, a great flood occurred, washing away the old gully and forming a small channel, which was only about a foot wide. The following year, Song Huan, Song Wen'an's great-grandson, passed the examination. This was exactly 100 years after Song Wen'an's death. Six years later, his brother Song Ju also passed. However, Song Huan only rose to the position of prefectural governor, while Song Ju became a doctoral scholar before dying. Afterward, the family remained obscure. Song Ju was the son-in-law of the author's father-in-law, who was also his fellow student.

Bakery Spirit Xiao Hong Zhang's mother-in-law-in-law was staying temporarily in Wuxi. She bought a few acres of vacant land and began constructing an inn. While the workmen were busy, an old veteran soldier named Liu Wen playfully picked up a piece of soil and said to the crowd, "I just found a piece of gold!" Everyone gathered to see it, but he laughed and threw it away. It turned out that he had actually found a pair of gold rings in the broken soil. He sold them for several thousand coins. That very day, he fell ill and remained so for half a year before finally recovering. At that time, the Zhang family was living at Nan Chan Temple. A spirit descended on the winnowing basket of a purple spirit and wrote in the ashes, saying, "I am an ordinary person living in the residence being built by the public. My name is Xiao Hong. I lived at the west gate with my two sisters. My father was a pie maker. Unfortunately, our stepmother was unreasonable and treated us cruelly. We could not bear it and both hanged ourselves. Now I am severely unfortunate, and my decayed bones were destroyed by a public servant. The objects in my grave are worth ten thousand coins. Old man Liu took everything. I have no place to return to. Now I only live outside the window under the walnut tree, relying on the public to stay here and cannot leave again." People asked, "You died because of your stepmother. Why don't you seek revenge?" The spirit replied, "I have already complained to heaven. It has already avenged me. I was offered Buddhist scriptures but refused to accept them." Someone said, "A great sage is coming. How can you stay here for so long?" The spirit answered, "As such, I will return to the tree. I will come again." The Zhang family offered many bribes with Buddhist rituals and burned money, setting up food offerings to worship it. The spirit then ceased to appear.

The Hawk of Tang Yin In the Tang Yin settlement of Fanyang, west-facing mountains were home to a hawk that nested nearby. It often swooped over the lake, catching ducks to eat. One day, using too much force, its claws gripped the duck's back and became stuck in the bone, unable to pull free. The duck, in pain, suddenly dove into the water. The hawk tried its best but could not escape. Soon after, both creatures floated dead on the water. People said that the hawk's strength could not suddenly fail against one duck, and it was probably a matter of karmic retribution. Xue the Scholar Wang Anshi, residing in the Half Mountain area of Jinling, also built a study hall on the road in Jiangshan, where he often stayed. Whenever guests arrived, he would always keep them for the night, lending bedding to poor scholars. When they left, he would give them whatever he had. Once, Xue An of Lin'an came to visit. That night, Wang Anshi sat with him, sending someone to fetch a blanket from home. Wang Anshi's wife was displeased by the delay and replied, "All the blankets are gone." Wang Anshi was displeased but soon said, "I have my own plan." He had a fur cushion hanging from the beam and took it himself with a fork, giving it to Xue. The next day, he invited Xue to dinner and played chess with him, agreeing that the loser would write a poem about plum blossoms. Wang Anshi lost first with a quatrain. Later, Xue lost and could not fulfill the agreement. Wang Anshi recited a poem on the spot in his place: Wild waters and desolate mountains by a lonely shore, Fragrant branches display their colors, most closely associated with spring. Desire to surpass the brightness and beauty of frost and snow, Not afraid of the anger of the jade maidens with green belts. Xue later passed the imperial examinations and became a high-ranking official in the imperial court. He even worshiped Wang Anshi at home, emulating his words, actions, and behavior in all things. He often wrote poems in harmony with the emperor's compositions.

Also using the method of character explanation. His son entered the Imperial University and boasted to his classmates, saying, "My father writes poetry in the presence of the emperor, certainly not by chance." Recently, while studying, he took a day off to travel, closed the door, and lay down during the day. In a dream, a divine figure in golden armor broke through the roof and descended, calling out, "You can learn to recite poetry. One day, you will sing and respond with the sages." And now it has indeed come true. A guest named Li Ji, who was known for his wit, responded with a furrowed brow and repeated, "Indeed not by chance. Indeed not by chance." The son questioned him repeatedly. Li Ji said, "The angel was already troubling your esteemed father." Han Zicang served as a literary official. Someone slandered Xue, saying that Han had altered Wang Zhixing's poem to mock or insult him. The words went, "Thirty years ago, a beggar. The Master of Jing once wrote a poem to replace the plum. Now you have lost, and no one can replace you. Do not go to Jinling to play chess again." Xue wept and complained on his bed. Han, after sitting, knew of Fenning County. Actually, it was not Han's work. Wu Fupeng said that the story of the golden-armored figure was obtained from Wu Hucheng.

The Tongpan Zhu In the ninth year of the Shaoxing era, Tongpan Zhu of Yongzhou completed his term of office. He returned home with his family, carrying very rich possessions. He also had charge of a government convoy of silver, amounting to twenty thousand strings of cash. Traveling by boat out of Guangxi, Zhu had a fondness for chess, often neglecting sleep and food to play with guests. He once wished to find highly respected monks and reclusive scholars who could play chess, and to spend one's life with them. On the way, the gatekeeper informed a certain Taoist that he wanted to see him. The Taoist claimed to have a very high level of chess skill. Zhu was greatly pleased and quickly invited him in. The Taoist was tall and had a beautiful beard, and his speech flowed like clouds. He arranged seating and set up the chessboard. In the late afternoon, he showed no signs of tiring. Zhu then stayed overnight. Calmly speaking about the intention to travel together, the Taoist said, "I am traveling as a guest here, often knocking on people's doors to beg for food. Being allowed to accompany the rear carriage is a fortunate wish throughout one's life." Zhu was even more pleased. When they untied the mooring ropes, the Taoist was placed at the stern of the boat. They ate together every day. Another scholar accompanied them, and all could drink heartily and sing loudly. He quite performed small tricks or performances, entertaining their children. All levels were pleased with him. They accompanied him for two weeks. Traveling to Heavy Lake, they happened to encounter heavy rain and wind, and could not proceed. Anchored at a different harbor, they continued drinking and playing chess as before. After the second drumbeat, the boat suddenly tilted. More than ten strong men rushed through the door, raised a pestle or a heavy club, and shouted. Zhu's children clung to the Taoist's robe to seek help.

The Taoist priest clasped his hands and said, "I bear the extreme favor and care of the public household. I had no choice but to come here. How could I possibly face you with a blade?" He ordered them to take him in turn and bind him, then threw him into the lake. The next day, they divided and took away the wealth. The county heard of this and sent officials to inspect. There were only floating bodies in disarray, and no one knew the names of the owners. However, they found a small calendar on the shore, which was a calendar commonly used by bandits for their daily meals. Names and numbers were listed, totaling seventeen people. They reported this to the prefecture. The matter reached the imperial court, which issued an edict ordering a widespread search along the routes. Whoever captured a bandit would be appointed as a Chengxin Lang and rewarded with two million coins. Several archers from Jianchang County, skilled in capturing bandits, were tracking the bandits. A merchant named Ren Qi who traded frankincense asked the magistrate Li Yong and expressed his willingness to respond to the call. He went west to Changsha and saw someone selling Guangzhou medicine in the market. He tried calling out their names and numbers, and the person immediately turned around. He ran to report to the military patrol and had them arrested. They went together to the inn. A room with a green silk partition and many utensils was described. When they sought the person, he fled through the back door. It was a fake Taoist priest. The case was tried in Linjiang, and the prisoner admitted to being Wang Xiaoge, who was one of those who killed Zhu Tongpan. Ten of his followers were captured in other places. The Taoist priest was named Pei San, and the scholar was named Wang Xian. All of them were fugitives and were a source of regret. Li Yong was promoted to the rank of Chengshi Lang and transferred to the judicial position in Raoyang Prefecture. He told me about the incident.

The Master of Xian'en Temple In Wuyuan County, there is a mountain temple called Xian'en Temple. The monk Juhui was its master. He only cared about wine, meat, money, and wealth. He paid little attention to the morning incense and night lamps. The house and barns were old and dilapidated, not repaired. The Shamen Tianwang Hall collapsed, and its pillars were used as cowsheds. He acted freely and unconcerned. This went on for over forty years, even as he grew old. In the first year of the Gandao era, a deity descended to the Dharma Hall and called out Juhui's name, rebuking and enumerating his sins. A little boy saw a giant with a large face glaring, wearing red clothing and a tall stature, showing great anger and a stern expression. I only heard his voice. From then on, all the monk's clothes, bedding, food, money, utensils, and equipment were all taken away and thrown into the mountains and forests. Villagers sometimes found them. The kitchen knife moved slowly and mysteriously from the kitchen. Boxes, chests, cabinets, and other items that could not be carried often saw smoke coming out of them. Quickly opened to see, all were burned to ashes. The monk could not bear the distress and anger, gathered all the remaining items, and scattered them among the donors' families.

At night, the god cursed its master, saying, "You are a hidden sinner. Misfortune will soon befall you." The person was so frightened that he dared not sleep. He waited until dawn and took it back. It was in a state of chaos and disarray until it was nearly all gone. Behind the temple stood hundreds of giant bamboo poles. Usually, they couldn't be sold for less than three or two hundred coins per pole. All were broken in half. Suddenly, the little boy disappeared. After two hundred steps, he returned, saying the god had seized him to his dwelling. The rooms and houses were magnificent and splendid, with guards surrounding him. Both adults and children, all wore purple and red clothes. There were also guests coming and going, and I was made to serve on his left and right. The next evening, a woman said, "Keeping this child here is of no benefit." She waved me away, and I was as if in a dream, then able to return. On another day, the god descended to the law hall and called out to the monk, saying, "Your sins reach up to heaven. You should leave this place quickly. Let disciple Zhiyuan take over. If not, I will impose a great punishment on you." The monk wept and nodded. He moved to a guesthouse in a nearby village and died within a few months.

Wang Dalang's Horse During the third year of the Chongning era (1104), there was a merchant named Wang Dalang from Wuyuan County. He obtained a fine horse that stood out with its exceptional appearance and spirit. He had a young boy ride it, and the boy was also good at training and taking care of the horse, following a regular schedule. The horse became even fatter and better. A sculptor from another county came, and the people of the county pooled money to have the sculptor make a statue of the horse at the Five Lords Temple. Someone jokingly said to the sculptor, "If you can make a statue that looks like Wang Dalang's horse, then you are a master. We will give you a generous reward." The sculptor wanted to showcase his skills and went to visit the boy, giving him fruits to eat and gradually befriending him. He would go to the place where the horse was kept and watch it every day, and he would also give the boy wine to drink. He led the boy to a mountain area and waited until the boy was drunk and asleep. He measured the height and size of the horse with a string, even measuring the details of the horse's ears, eyes, nose, mane, and tail in great detail. He did the same for the boy and had obtained all the details accurately. He then went to the temple to make the statue. Once completed, it looked exactly like Wang Dalang's horse and the boy. They chose a day to add the eyes. As soon as the work was finished, Wang Dalang's horse suddenly became wild and ran away. The boy chased and rode it, going straight to the pine wood pond in the south of the city, where both of them drowned and died. After that, the horse would go out every night to drink water from West Lake or go to nearby villages to eat crops. The next day, there would be traces of the horse on the lakeside and in the fields, and there would always be the tracks of the horse. Floating water plants would still stick to the horse's mouth, and the grain would scatter on the road. The boy also had a spiritual presence. People who came to the temple to pray often had dreams to report back. During the early years of the Xuanhe era (1119-1125), when Fang La rebelled, the temple was burned down, and the horse disappeared. Even now, old people can still talk about it. These two stories were told by Li Hui.

Wizhou Pig In the sixth year of Xuanhe (1124 AD), Qiang Xiufu served as the governor of Wizhou. A butcher presented a piece of pig skin to him. On it were six characters, each as large as a finger, which read: "Three years of filial impiety." The red ink was vivid and reddish-brown, penetrating through the pig skin. The people in the prefecture competed to see the pig skin. The butcher also immediately changed his occupation. Prince Zhao Bushi, who served his father as the head of the Ritual Department, saw the pig skin. Dongzhou Thunder In the sixth month of the sixth year of Shaoxing (1136 AD), Prince Zhao Bushi was in Dongzhou. He climbed the Baoning Military Building with several others to cool off. Suddenly, black clouds rose at the edge of the sky. In a short while, they filled the sky. The thunder and lightning were intense, and the rain sound was as if a river were being overturned. Six or seven rolling fireballs entered the building. Prince Zhao Bushi and his companions were frightened and trembling. They lay down on the floor of the building, covering their faces with their hands. They only heard an unbearable foul smell. Gradually calming down, they peeped at it. They saw three or four people who were seven or eight feet tall. Their faces were ugly and black, with short, blood-red hair, disheveled without a headband. They held clubs that were like bone-shaped clubs. Someone said "here," someone said "not here," and someone said "only here only here." After they finished speaking, they began to hear the thunderclap sound. For a long time, the clouds dispersed and the rain stopped. They got up to inspect and found that the large pillar of the building door had shattered, with a path from the base to the top that was straight like a line. Beside it, there were traces of dragon claws, indicating that it said "dragon's claw traces."

**The Thunder Demon's Fallen Headband** In the second year of the Shaoxing era, in the fourth month, a sudden downpour occurred in Yiwu County, Wuzhou Prefecture. Amidst the thunder and lightning, a green cloth headband fell from the sky into the village. It was not made in the manner of human craftsmanship, for it was sewn only with four straight seams. It was held to cover a three-dou water jar, fitting perfectly. The belt was three to four feet long and as wide as a palm. The villagers dared not keep it and placed it in a shrine. After several days of thunder and rain, it was lost again. The above three events were all obtained from Li Zeng, as told by Zhao Bushi. **The Heavenly Emperor Summons Duan Wang Xiang** Duan Wang Xiang, courtesy name Detai, was from Wanzai County, Yuzhou Prefecture. He knew a little about books and had a naturally honest and cautious disposition. He never offended anyone, yet when he encountered unjust matters, he would bravely step forward. During the Jianyan period, bandits were rampant, and dozens of members of the Duan clan were taken by force. Duan Wang Xiang bravely went to redeem them with money and silk. The bandits admired his righteousness and returned them all, sending them back. In the fifth year of the Shaoxing era, severe drought struck the southeast, and a dou of rice cost over a thousand coins. Duan Wang Xiang completely opened his stored goods, taking only the usual price, and also made porridge to feed the hungry. Those who depended on him for survival were countless. Later, he suddenly grew tired of human affairs and built a hermitage in the mountains of Yantian. On the walls, he wrote the words "open and honest." One day, he summoned his relatives and old friends and said goodbye to them, saying, "The Heavenly Emperor is soon to summon me." People did not believe it. After several days, he rose into the sky and disappeared. The villagers who went to see his dwelling found only his old clothes and shoes remaining. The event was reported to the court, but the county official was not pleased and stopped it. And so it ended.

The Man Without Boundaries Feng Guanguo, a native of Shaowu, was young and quick-witted. After finishing his studies, he seemed dissatisfied with his life and left his hometown to wander beyond the ordinary world. He met a strange person who taught him the techniques of internal alchemy and qigong. He possessed profound knowledge of astronomy, geography, life, fate, and fortune, and became skilled without formal learning. He called himself the Man Without Boundaries. He stayed in Yichun for two years, using his skills to sustain himself. His predictions about people's fortunes and misfortunes, as well as the changes in yin and yang, were always accurate. Some criticized him for his excessive drinking and strange behavior, but Guanguo did not argue with them. He wrote a poem in reply: "Having traveled through the world of human affairs in four hundred states, how many beautiful scenes are good companions. In the morning, one should laugh at the flushed old man, for our paths do not match those of wind and cattle." He also wrote another poem expressing his feelings: "Wandering aimlessly in the world, everything seems natural. Deeply concealing his marvelous uses, he spreads like a deity. Writing that is fluid and dynamic, like dragons and snakes. In the pot, the sun and moon are often suspended. Lightly using human relationships to mix with the world, only using wine cups to pass the years. Where does the hidden cultivation of virtue lead? Laughing and pointing to the mythical island of Yingzhou, returning to a fairyland." He had many more poems, all of which were detached from the mundane world and spoke of transcendent things that no one could understand. In the thirty-second year of the Shaoxing era of the Song Dynasty, in March, he bid farewell to all his acquaintances and wrote a poem to say goodbye. On the fourteenth day, he sat upright and recited a verse before passing away. Li Guanmin, the governor of Yizhen, heard of this and respected him, ordering a statue of him to be made in the Zhiping Palace on the east side of the city. These two stories were obtained from □ Zongshuo.

Qu Shī Releases the Carps Several miles southwest of Fānchéng lies a small village called Yuánshēng Village. There are over a hundred households in the village, all of whom make a living by fishing in the lakes and rivers. There was a man named Qu Shī who purchased a fish pond from elsewhere. During winter, he built a small dam around the pond and drained all the water, intending to catch all the fish by draining the pond. He saw two large black carps jump out beyond the dam and then jump back into the water. This happened repeatedly several times, which made him secretly find it strange. He traced the reason behind their actions and found that there were hundreds of newly hatched baby carps gathered in a hole, unable to escape. The male and female carps were moving back and forth, carrying and moving the baby carps, preferring to risk their own lives rather than showing concern. Qu Shī sighed deeply with emotion and used a basket to carry them all out. He abandoned his work and returned home. Several years later, he fell ill and died, entering the underworld. An official from the underworld told him, "You, as a fisherman, make a living by setting nets, yet you have a heart that values life. Your intentions are not like those of others. I will extend your life for a full cycle of twelve years. When you return to the world, tell people not to harm natural creatures." It is said that he died one night and was revived the next day.

A Son of the Qingcheng Tax Collector A man from Sichuan named Yang Digong. During the middle of the Xuanhe era, he studied at the Imperial Academy but did not achieve fame. Later, through imperial favor, he obtained an official position as a tax collector in Qingcheng County. He had a son named [name missing] who was diligent and invited the elderly scholar Mr. Jian to teach him. A local person named Guan Shouqing visited Yang, and Yang invited him to dine together and went to the study with him. His son suddenly addressed his father by name, bowed formally, and said, "Do you still remember the hospitality of our fellow students at the Imperial Academy?" "I am from the western part of Zhejiang, and my surname is [name missing]. I left the capital during the chaos and no longer returned. Several dozen people did not come back, but only you and I were close friends. Though father and son have been separated for so long, our appearances are unfamiliar, and we are no longer recognizable. But just now, we used to study together. Then he recited the classics we had studied, and the texts he had written, flowing out effortlessly, without any effort.

At the time, Old Master Jian was formulating a theory that all things are already within oneself. When asked about it, he replied, "This theory posits that heaven creates all things, and only humans are the most intelligent. From the vastness of heaven and earth to the grandeur of mountains and rivers, from the diversity of living beings to the myriad forms of life, all principles are fully contained within the six-foot body of a human. Thus, the value of the human body is equal to that of heaven and earth. It is truly a pity that uncivilized people undervalue themselves." Old Master Jian was surprised and asked for a more detailed explanation. He laughed and said, "Wait until you can drink up the entire West River in one gulp, then I will tell you. It is said in Sichuan that during the Qin Dynasty, a creature known as the West River Dragon caused harm. Therefore, I used this saying as a joke." Master Yang recalled past events, and everything he said matched the reality. In the first year of the Longxing era, Shouqing went to the imperial court, and the boy was thirteen years old. I do not know what happened to him afterward. Shouqing said, "Now I cannot remember his name accurately."

Signs of Death for the Bright** In the thirty-first year of the Shaoxing era (1161 AD), in Yangzhou, I was serving in the Secretariat of Military Affairs. There was a guest who came to the office and submitted a letter stating that the bureau of astronomy predicted a significant change within the palace before the Winter Solstice. On the eighth day of the Winter Solstice, the sky was heavily overcast. The official in charge of astronomy reported that at the fourth watch of the night, a large star suddenly fell from the northeast sky. The report arrived, and it indeed matched what the two people had said. At that time, the enemy was planning to harm their leader and wanted to send an envoy to negotiate with us. They found Zhang Zhen, a loyal military officer captured in Guazhou, and sent him to carry a letter to request peace. When Zhang Zhen returned home, his wife and children came out in mourning clothes, thinking he had been killed in battle. They were just ordering a Buddhist ritual for the dead. When they met, they were filled with a mix of sadness and joy. Zhang Zhen took the spirit table and burned it. **The Snake in the Mud Wall** A young man from the Yang family in Lianhe Village, Fanyang, bought a mud wall in Yonghe Town. He loved it very much and placed it outside a small room. In early spring, with a slight warmth, he opened the window and sat on it. He felt as if someone was pushing his clothes, but when he looked back, he saw nothing. A little later, it touched his thigh and caused slight pain. He got up and looked, seeing something wriggling inside the hole. The place it touched turned red and became a wound. He quickly called his family out and broke the wall to check. It moved, and they poured water over it. They all died and ran away. He died three days later.

The Great Man of Mount Jiuhua** In the 31st year of the Shaoxing era, enemy forces invaded the Huai River region and Chizhou. People from Qingyang went together to Mount Jiuhua to search for a hidden refuge. A certain scholar, entering a high and secluded mountain area, saw a clear stream with an axe placed in it. He placed the axe in his left arm, which was about a zhang long. It was a snake. Amid the chaos of battle, he fled but did not return. **Shi Wen Shi's Dream** Shi Wen Shi from Wuxing had a dream in a public temple. In the dream, someone said, "They were six dice, all showing four spots." He revealed a name on the third board and saw someone named Shi from Changxing in Huzhou, but the name was missing. He asked in doubt, "Is this it?" The next day, he told his fellow students, and they all congratulated him on his good dream, saying, "You will surely be ranked high in the exams and become a doctor." Dice are game tools. Another person outside the window responded, "The dream is not about today; the fulfillment is still far off." Shi was not very happy and went out to look, but there was no one there. Later, the capital was in chaos, and he returned to his hometown, where there were many troubles at home. He did not resume his studies. Thirty years later, Shi initially passed the exams and was appointed to a position in the imperial secretariat, which was on the third board. At that time, he was exactly twenty-four years old, in the 24th year of the Shaoxing era. He finally fully understood the meaning of the six dice, which he had not yet grasped.

Dong Xifan Changxing Liu (with fifteen characters missing) could not (with three lines and fifteen characters missing) in the middle of Liu (with fourteen characters missing) was treated with great favor, comparable to that of high-ranking officials. This □□ remains. A Witch from Jingnan There was a witch in Jingnan who possessed magical arts. She could bring blessings or misfortunes to people and acted arrogantly in the village. Those who resided in the prefectures and counties dared not inquire. Wu County's Gao, serving as the governor of Jiangling, could no longer bear it and captured the witch, intending to beat her. The senior officials wept and advised against it, pleading not to punish her, fearing that it would bring about a strange calamity. Gao became even more angry, dragged the official down and confronted the witch, beating her twenty times. The witch did not apologize, laughed, and left. After just a mealtime, Gao felt his face slightly swollen. Looking in the mirror, he saw it was swollen and puffed up, with only two eyes remaining, as thin as threads. He immediately called the official to ask about the witch's residence, agreeing to go with him. The official thought he would certainly pay respects and apologize, then told him the location. Gao rode his horse directly out the door, traveling more than thirty miles, arriving at dusk at a dilapidated house. The witch came out to greet him. Gao shouted at his subordinates to tie her to the pillars and ordered them to beat her with the sticks they carried. All the statues and scriptures were taken out. The witch remained calm and composed. Later, entering her room, they found a small box. Breaking open the lock and looking inside, they saw it was wrapped in multiple layers of bedding, containing a wooden figure. They broke it, and she finally showed a look of fear. But she was already beaten to a pulp. Gao's face returned to normal. He took her back. The next morning, he entered the government office and reported, saying, "The witch behaved improperly. I did not hesitate to sacrifice myself to eliminate the harm for the people. Fearing that if the news spread, she would escape, so I did not have time to speak beforehand. Now she is nearly dead. Dare to report." The prefect admired his decisiveness and instructed him to carry out his mission, and threw her into the river.

**Shi Shi Passed the Imperial Examinations** Shi Shi. The villagers of Xuzhou (the original text is missing thirteen characters) dreamt of him. They spoke in great detail about matters among friends. The villagers asked, "What about Shi Zhongheng?" He replied, "He should pass the imperial examinations under Liu Yu's list." Shi awoke and told Shi. Shi said that Liu Yu was from Jinan. He has already been appointed as a censor. He did not know where another person with the same name was, and he did not believe it at all. Fifteen years later, during the time when Liu Yu was usurping power, Shi passed the examinations (the original text is missing one leaf) with two heads that could not extend or retract. He hated it, gave it to a Taoist priest at Qianshan Temple, made him live in the mountains, and it was lost after a few days. That winter, Dong's wife Zhao died. He considered it an ominous sign, but it was just a coincidence. The above three stories were told by Wang Jiashou.

玉师子缺** The inner text is missing. **两头龟缺** The inner text is missing. **张朝女** During the tenth year of the Shaoxing era, Zhang (missing eight lines and thirteen characters). **郑司业庖人** Zheng, the head cook of the imperial kitchen. **郑明仲司业南福州(原阙五字)乡里□□□师至丹阳** Zheng Mingzhong, the head cook of the imperial kitchen in Nan Fuzhou (missing five characters), a teacher from the village arrived in Danyang. He met several old acquaintances. They traveled together on the same boat. The servant who accompanied Zheng could prepare meals. Everyone was happy. They wished to share meals together. Zheng called the servant and told him. The servant firmly said, "The reason I came is to serve one master alone. I do not wish to mix with other guests." Zheng advised him repeatedly. Even offered him a bribe. But he still refused. Zheng became angry and sent him back. The servant bowed twice and asked, saying, "Sending me back is indeed good. I fear that my fellow villagers will not understand and will think that I was punished for a fault. I wish to ask for a letter from one of your family to explain the reason." Zheng also wanted to inquire about his well-being. He immediately wrote a letter and gave it to the servant. The servant bowed again and left. (Missing six characters) The letter was written. The date is missing thirteen characters. (Missing eight lines) (Missing fifteen characters) The foul and stinking air from the sweat furnace. (Missing six characters) Rushing out, closing the door, cleaning, and going to bed. The next night he came again.

The sleep grew deeper. He lay on his side with his face upward, mouth wide open in a startled manner. Yu first placed a hammer in the fire and quickly took it out, inserting it into the man's mouth. The man immediately shouted and fled, his voice sounding like that of an old pig. The hem of his clothes dragged the remaining fire, spreading and burning the fallen leaves. By then, it was already dark, and no one dared to pursue or look at him. In the end, no one knew what kind of monster it was. After a month, a student was sitting under the window, hearing a rustling sound outside. He peered through a hole in the window and saw the frosty moon was bright and clear. There was a black thing like a monkey sitting on the small bridge over the water ditch. Another thing was pure white, like a three-foot-long dried tree trunk. They sat facing each other in a relaxed, cross-legged manner. After some time, they got up. The first one recited, saying, "The wind has settled, the frost has come. Six-Lang, how dare you a dead body擅自出现 (appear without permission). Six-Lang has more than ten thousand strings of cash in the treasury, which has never been used. If fate dictates your end, you will still become the master of the cave in Maoshan. You, you foolish and ignorant ghost, do not linger here. I will deal with you." (Note: The phrase "死尸何敢擅出" is translated as "how dare you a dead body擅自出现 (appear without permission)" to maintain the original meaning and tone, while "六郎有正库钱万余贯" is translated as "Six-Lang has more than ten thousand strings of cash in the treasury" to reflect the cultural context of the large sum of money.)

He scolded her repeatedly. The old woman wept bitterly. She crawled back to her original place, but the old man did not see her. By midnight, Zhu could only moan. After eating porridge for several days, he recovered. Bo Ying calmly recounted what he had seen. Zhu's expression changed. He then said, "When you were not at home, the old servant did not serve me and spoke to me with harsh words. I struck her with an iron whip, and she died. I secretly buried her in the bathroom." (The original text is missing twelve characters here.) A person named Yuan passed by. (The original text is missing nine lines here.) The body (The original text is missing sixteen characters here) was said to be the vessel of true spirit and energy (The original text is missing twelve characters here). She was beautiful and noble, but her speech was vulgar and coarse, lacking refinement. He strongly disliked her and hoped she would leave. He said, "Although I do not want the elixir, I will not give it to you." Due to his old age and illness, he lacked the ability to entertain guests, so he instructed his children to accompany the guest. The guest said, "I came specifically for you. You do not want the elixir, but I will take it back. However, the journey is very long, and I wish to stay for one night. If it is sunny tomorrow, I will leave. Otherwise, I must stay for a short while." Having no choice, he assigned the guest to stay at Pine and Chrysanthemum Villa. At that time, the weather had been clear for a long time.

At the fifth watch, heavy rain began. The family, because the top was warm, did not dare to bury [苏意]. By morning, the sons recorded the previous events. They opened the box to look at it, and the medicine was still there. They took it and put it into his mouth. In a moment, he was able to get up. He felt refreshed as if he had no illness. He drank and ate as usual. Again, he ordered to pick up the punctuated characters and the red paste, intending to burn them and drink the residue. However, they could no longer be seen. After several days, the eldest son went to Jingkou. According to what the guest said, he ordered to draw the yellow elephant. The armor suddenly emerged, causing terror and death. When my wife's family entered Shu, they passed by that place and stayed at a Buddhist temple. They accompanied the envoy Liang Heng in the sleeping and bathing quarters. They saw more than ten poor and emaciated people. They stretched out and asked who they were. The people said, "Gathering firewood and boiling water." Working day and night without rest. The text is missing fourteen lines and thirteen characters. It was stored in a new lock. The text is missing eleven characters. When opened, it should be given to Jiang San. They sat down and died. At the age of over eighty. During the Shaoxing era, five carriages were constructed. Two days after the Flower Day of the Geng Xu year, I finished reading the Bing Records. After several pages, it was broken and could not be read. It is a pity that the pearls were lost. Recognized by Yatai.

Missing content from the top mountain temple. Missing content about Xiao Liu Lang. Missing content about the immortal Daoist practitioner. Missing internal text. Missing content about the hot springs of Fangzhou. Missing internal text. Missing content about Wang Junyi. Missing internal text. Missing content about the poem from the Wax Sandal Pavilion. Missing internal text. *Yijian Dingzhi* Preface to *Yijian Dingzhi* The four books of *Yijian* (甲, 乙, 丙, 丁) consist of 1,150 stories, totaling approximately 300,000 words. Those who read it and laugh say: the Five Classics (*Shijing*, *Shujing*, *Yijing*, *Chunqiu*) do not exceed 100,000 words in total. The *Shiji* (Records of the Grand Historian) by Sima Qian covers thousands of years and contains over 800,000 words. You cannot play with the heart of the Bible; you cannot open the door to knowledge. Yet, over thirty years, laboring the mind, mouth, ears, and eyes, engaging in trivial matters related to the strange and fantastical, consuming ink and paper, almost half the size of the *Shiji*. Vague and disconnected, interwoven and brewed together. Something that sages do not speak about; something that Yang Xiong does not read. Having this book cannot bring even the slightest benefit; without this book, the world would not lack anything. It is already very laughable; yet it is examined as evidence. It does not necessarily come from contemporary virtuous officials and scholars. It comes from common people, wild monks, mountain guests, Daoists, blind shamans, vulgar women, low-ranking clerks, and foot soldiers. Whenever strange stories come, they also happily accept them, without questioning. Why would people need to verify it? Is this not even more laughable? I also laughed and said: the Six Classics were written by sages. How dare one comment on them? Like the words of Sima Qian, I will immediately use your words to confirm it. It records Duke Mu of Qin and Zhao Jianzi.

Isn't it wondrous? The divine spirit of Changling. The Yellow Stone at the collapsed bridge. Isn't it strange? The story of Jing Ke is recorded. The court physician Xia Wuju is verified. The appearance of Lü Hou is described. The painter is authenticated. Court physicians and painters. How is this different from what was previously said about the lowly, sorcerers, and slaves? To excel in learning from Grand Historian Sima Qian. There has probably never been anyone like me. You take this tongue of yours back home. Temporarily keep your laughter to yourself. One day, the *Wu* Chronicles will be completed (missing a leaf). The sacred image was exposed. Take the remaining three gates. Cover it with a house. And cover it with thatch. Not long after, it was destroyed again. A passerby bought a bamboo hat. Tied to the top of the image. To shelter from rain and sun. Yao Qing suddenly fell ill. For half a year. The four limbs stank and rotted. Blood flowed. Suffered immensely. Relatives and friends who came to inquire. No one dared to look into his door. On the verge of death. Saw a man in yellow clothes coming to chase. Clearly stated the reason for dismantling and viewing. Li Shou and Xiang Qiu were also involved. They died one after another. Only Yao Qing suffered the most severe misfortune. Li was probably misled. The villagers saw the guilt and proof like this. Together they collected money and grain. Built three rooms. Moved the image and treated it as before. It was the twenty-seventh year of the Shaoxing era. Five years later. I heard it from the military governor Xiang Zhongde Shiju. *Yijian* Volume D. This leaf is missing from the correct place. For now, I will keep it as is. Attached here for reference. Heart Source notes.

The 19th Entry of the Yijian Dingzhi Wang Langxian A hermit from Wenzhou, whose name is unknown, dwells in Taoshan of Ruian. His abode is deep and secluded. He sustains himself through farming, planting, and other agricultural activities. His divination using the I Ching is as if by magic. Every year, he descends the mountain to sell divinations. Each divination costs one thousand coins, and he usually stops after ten. He buys all the necessities for the year and returns home. Well-intentioned individuals sometimes carry gold and silk, waiting and hoping for a long time. However, before he has traveled ten miles, he has already completed his quota of divinations and no longer wishes to divine further. A man from the prefecture named Wang Langxian, originally a scholar, did not succeed in his studies and decided to follow the hermit to learn. When he encountered the hermit's departure, he knelt and bowed twice on the road, then followed him into the mountain. He performed the duties of a servant. Gradually, he explained what he desired. The hermit also explained roughly. He mentioned the ten divinations he had made that year and asked Wang to explore their meanings. Wang was exhausted in spirit and thought, as if he had gained something. The hermit did not consider him capable and said, "Your natural talent is limited to this. You cannot force yourself to advance further." He sent Wang out of the mountain. However, Wang's learning had already surpassed others. A litigant seeking a decision about tomb boundaries came to him. The divination encountered the hexagram of the I Ching. The hermit said, "The tomb lacks earth. This is an omen of defeat." After more than a month, the previous person came again. Another divination encountered the hexagram of the I Ching. The hermit said, "This omen is not comparable to the previous one. There is grass on the tomb. You will get the right immediately." Indeed, it turned out as expected. Wang then traveled west to Qiantang. At that time, the governor of Hangzhou liked various skills. Those who came were treated well. However, after a long time, they became disobedient, and he imposed punishments on them without leniency. Wang wrote a note saying, "The magician Wang Langxian." The governor invited him in. Greeting him, the governor asked, "You are named Wang Langxian. Have you ever heard the sound of drums and horns on the city wall at five drums?" Wang replied, "I have heard it." The governor asked, "How effective is it?"

He said, "Inside and outside, all is peaceful and tranquil." But tonight, after the second watch, there should be a woman coming to report an emergency. Wang returned to his lodging, but several厢卒 were already there. They said, "Why have you come here? Many have been exiled before—we do not know how many. Now that we are here, how can we escape?" The next day, before dawn, the governor called him and said, "Your words yesterday were very accurate. The night happened to be at the second watch, and the wife of the vice-governor went into labor. She knocked on the door to ask for medicine. This is precisely what is meant by 'a woman in distress.'" From this point on, the treatment at the inn became more respectful. He then inquired about auspicious and inauspicious signs. Wang replied, "On this year’s certain day and hour, the summons will be issued." The governor, a firm believer, counted the days with his fingers, waiting. When the time arrived, he invited his officials to a meal. Wang was seated at the table. The governor said, "Mr. Wang, you said today you would be honored with the summons. Gentlemen, please all bear witness." After the meal, by midday, there was still no good news. All the guests were anxious. After about four or five quarters of an hour, he pressed for an answer twice. Wang hurried to stand in the courtyard and observed the shadow of the sun. He said, "It is about to arrive." Soon, the postal station arrived. Opening the seal, he saw the letter. Indeed, he was summoned to the imperial court. The governor thanked him with a million coins and agreed to go to the capital with him. Wang said, "I am from a distant province, a humble person. I wish to see the capital once, hoping to rise through fortune. But my family is especially poor. After you are sent on your way, I will temporarily return home, take the gift given to my wife and children, and then proceed north at full speed. It would not be too late." The governor agreed. After he had departed, someone asked him the reason. He said, "Though the governor was summoned, his future prospects do not look promising. It would be difficult to meet the emperor." The governor had not yet reached the city gate when he was instead assigned to another prefecture. After a year, he died. Wang’s fate remains unknown.

A Monk Named Ruxing A monk from Yongjia named Ruxing traveled with fellow monks to Lin'an (Hangzhou). They rested at a roadside inn and saw a child selling divination services. Ruxing consulted the I Ching, and the divination indicated that there was jade buried in the ground, which would emerge on the sixteenth day of the ninth month. The child said it was a favorable omen. Ruxing received the omen and drew a picture of an official pulling a bow to shoot a monk. The first two arrows missed the target, but the third arrow pierced his foot, with a dragon coiled below. The child did not understand the meaning. Ruxing interpreted it himself, saying, "I will certainly recommend myself to become an elder. It will only be effective after three times. The dragon represents a ruler. I will travel to the capital city, perhaps having a fortunate encounter." Not long after, the magistrate of Zhenjiang prepared a letter and sent gifts to invite Ruxing to stay at Ganlu Temple, exactly on the sixteenth day of the ninth month. The fellow monk was also pleased, thinking that he would soon achieve his aspirations. However, for several years, there was no achievement. When Chen Tong, a soldier from Hangzhou, rebelled, the monk fled into the southern mountains. Once he went out to the waist of the mountain, sheltered by trees, he looked down and saw several bandits walking in the gorge. They shot arrows high up to search for hidden soldiers, firing three consecutive arrows. The last one hit the monk's foot. Another monk sat beside him and said, "The elevated seat." Only then did the omens in the divination come true, and nothing was left unfulfilled.

Left Deputy Commander Zuo Liang, a Military Officer of the Rank of Xiuwu. In the twenty-eighth year of the Shaoxing era (1158 AD), he served as the Deputy Commander of the military forces in Wuzhou. He attended a gathering hosted by a military official named Wang Zuode Rui Xu in the evening. Upon returning home, it was already night. Two figures followed him inside. Only when they reached the central hall did he realize their presence. Zuo Liang, enraged, shouted, "Who are you? How dare you enter here?" He seized one of them and beat him severely. The figure had two horns standing upright on his head. Zuo Liang recognized him as a ghost or spirit. Despite this, he refused to release him. The other figure grabbed Zuo Liang's waist from behind and made him fall. Zuo Liang then disappeared into the darkness. He regained consciousness just before dawn and recounted that he had been summoned to the underworld. The two messengers were about to report his crime of resistance. The judge examined his name and said, "Zuo Lang, the Deputy Commander of Wuzhou." The judge replied, "My order was to summon Zuo Lang. What does this have to do with you?" He was then released.
