Zuo Liang walked about ten steps before turning back and saw that the two messengers were tied up side by side in a corridor. The next day, his colleagues came to inquire about his condition. He explained the incident in detail. He had once served under Zhang Weigong as a subordinate and had a very strong and heroic presence. After this experience, he looked very weak and lifeless, as if he had been injured in a fight with a ghost.

During the winter of the Jingkang era, when the Jurchens crossed the river again, Xu Kang, the provincial inspector of Hebei, was punished for abandoning his post at Luokou and fleeing in disorder. He escaped to Jiyang. Coinciding with the chaos in Central China, he did not go to his place of exile but instead traveled with his two sons and more than ten followers through difficult terrain to Nankang. He did not wish to communicate with the provincial authorities and simply entered a small temple in Mount Lu to stay. One day, he picked vegetables from the garden and quarreled with a monk. The monk secretly reported to the provincial capital, claiming that they had been robbed by routed soldiers and had numerous accomplices. The governor Li Ding believed this report and immediately mobilized troops and equipped them with armor, surrounding the temple. They bound Xu and his sons and sent his followers to prison. When Xu arrived in court, he shouted loudly, claiming to be wronged, and fully explained his lifetime experience and credentials. Governor Li Ding asked, "How could there be a former provincial inspector who, upon arriving at a place, did not pay respects and instead hid himself?" He instructed the prison officials to investigate but found no evidence of the crime. Then he sent a clerk into the prison cell, pretending to speak kindly to investigate. They prepared wine and drank together, but there was no evidence of robbery. Xu hurriedly returned to the south.

Wives and concubines had fallen into destitution. The imperial edicts were not all preserved, and there was no way to prove oneself. The doubts could not be resolved. Kang's eldest son was skilled in dream interpretation. Kang told him, "I dreamed that father and son were holding umbrellas and walking in the rain. Soon after, a strong wind arose, and it blew all three umbrellas apart, tearing them into halves that flew away. What does this bode?" The son wept and said, "The dream is very inauspicious. It is an omen of father and son being separated into three parts." That night, the clerk came again, carrying a large amount of wine and food. He drank heavily with three people, and filled a cup to say to Kang, "The prefecture magistrate encourages a toast." A short while later, the two sons were moved to another house. After the gathering, each was imprisoned separately. By midnight, all were killed with iron hammers. Ding submitted a report claiming he had the merit of eliminating bandits. Before receiving a response, he died. All those involved in the matter died one after another within a month, with only the clerk remaining alive. The man from Yanling, Zhou Xirui, once served as the military governor of Nankang, serving before and after Ding, separated by politics. His son Jue heard it from the clerk, saying, "Kang obtained his official position through the military examination."

Summer Dice of the Xia Family Xia Jin, courtesy name Jidao, was from Jixian, Wei Prefecture. During the reigns of Emperor Huizong and Emperor Gaozong (Chongning and Dading eras), he studied at the Imperial College for a long time but did not achieve success. His family was poor, and he had no money at all. Sometimes, his fellow students would gather for gambling, and Xia would watch from the sidelines, asking the winners for a small amount of money, which was commonly known as "begging for heads." One night, he tied his belt, burned incense, and knelt before the dice, praying, "I have heard that dice have a spirit. I dare to respectfully seek your guidance. If I am chosen this year, I wish for a perfect outcome in the next ten throws. Otherwise, I will pack up my books and return to farming, never to advance again." After finishing his prayer, he rolled the dice. All six dice showed the same number. Xia was shocked and delighted, but he was not entirely confident. He prayed again, saying, "I have sincerely prepared myself and prayed throughout my life. I fear this might be a mere coincidence. I hope you can tell me more." He rolled the dice again, and the results were the same. He then knelt and bowed twice to thank the divine gift. That year, he indeed passed the imperial examination under the "Mochou" list. Later, he became a Zhongdashi (Middle Doctor) and served as an advisor to the Pacification Commission of Sichuan and Shaanxi. His family treasured the dice used in the divination, treating them with great respect. This is a story told by Zhou Jue.

The Method of Treating the Condition of Choosing Life A man from Putian named Chen Ke Da, who served as the governor of Zhaoqing, suddenly developed a swelling under his ribs. The swelling appeared like a boil or abscess and grew to the size of a bowl in an instant. Those familiar with such conditions said it was caused by the "choosing of life" toxin. At dawn, he chewed mung beans to test the condition; if the beans tasted sweet and fragrant, it would confirm the diagnosis. Indeed, this was the case. He was then instructed to grind *Cimicifuga* into a fine powder, mix it with cold boiled water using two large coins as a measure, and take it continuously. He soon had a bowel movement, expelling several bunches of scallions, each fully formed with roots and stems. The swelling disappeared immediately. He continued to take a decoction of *Pingwei San* for further treatment and ate plain rice porridge. After ten days, he returned to normal. In Leizhou, a woman named Kang Cai's wife was afflicted by the witch Lin Gongrong from the minority tribes, who used chicken meat in a ritual to cause the condition. A merchant named Yang Yi, skilled in medicine, treated her with a herbal remedy. After eating it, she vomited a large piece of accumulated meat. When the meat was cut open, raw flesh was found inside, already formed into the shape of a chicken, with head, tail, beak, and wings all intact. Kang Cai reported the incident to the local authorities, who arrested Lin Gongrong and put him in jail.

The doctor then called for Yang Sheng, instructing him to prepare a detailed account of the illness and the prescribed medication. The summary states: All foods and drinks, such as fish, meat, melons, fruits, and tea, can trigger the condition. In the initial stage of poisoning, one feels a slight pain in the chest and abdomen. The next day, the pain gradually increases and becomes more severe. After ten days, the poison starts to move within the body. If it moves upward, it causes chest pain; if it moves downward, it causes abdominal pain. Gradually, this leads to emaciation. These are the signs of the condition. If the poison is in the upper part of the body, it should be removed. The method involves using a cup of hot tea, adding half a coin's worth of alum to it, and waiting until the alum is completely dissolved. Drink it slowly, and after a while, probe the throat with a chicken feather to induce vomiting of the poison. If the poison is in the lower part of the body, it should be purged. Use rice water to take two coins' worth of curcuma powder, and the poison will be expelled. Then, grind half a catty each of ginseng and white atractylodes into powder, mix them with half a sheng of alcohol without impurities, and put it in a bottle. Simmer it over a low fire for about half a day until the alcohol is cooked. Take it out and drink it warm, one cup per day for five days. Then return to normal eating and drinking.

The Method of Picking Qi Mr. Chen Yu, a clerk of the Deking Prefecture, was a magistrate who investigated a case involving a sorcerer. The sorcerer was skilled in the method of picking qi. Initially, he had a grudge against someone and wished to harm them. He would then knock on their door at midnight and call out to them. When the person inside responded, he would use qi to manipulate them. Over time, the person's abdomen would gradually swell, and eventually, the skin of their abdomen would become as thin as paper, revealing their heart and lungs. They would experience labored breathing, with the root of the illness firmly established, and no medicine could cure them. The case was unresolved when the person died. Jiang Qiu, who served as governor, felt that the matter involved strange and fantastic elements and dared not subject it to the law. Instead, he sentenced the sorcerer to flogging on the back and exile to Hainan. This demonic art has several types. One involves cursing someone to grow a turtle inside their abdomen. Another involves removing the bark from a tree and cursing it, waiting for the tree to regrow, the bark to close, and the person to die. However, there is no known method of treatment for these conditions. The above two cases were recounted by Mr. Chen Yu.

A County Magistrate of Nanfeng In the early days of the Shaoxing era, there was a county magistrate named Zhao in a certain county. His youngest son, who was twenty years old, had not yet been married. He was staying with a guest teacher in the east pavilion. When the guest teacher returned at dusk, the son stayed alone in the academy. He heard a rustling sound outside the window and looked through the window. A woman was wandering under the bright moonlight. Just as he was in a state of shock, she approached the window and bowed. Startled, he asked, "Who are you? How did you get here?" She replied, "I am the daughter of the woman from the east neighbor. I admire your studies and have climbed over the wall to follow you. Would you allow me to listen for a while?" He gladly invited her in and kept her from leaving. From then on, she came every morning and returned every evening. The son's spirit became increasingly dull and weary, and his food intake suddenly decreased. His parents questioned him about it, but he did not tell them. They secretly asked those around him, who said, "We only hear her murmuring like private conversation every night. She sometimes laughs as well. We have wanted to report this but were afraid." Knowing that he was bewitched by a ghost, the parents moved to sleep in the same bed with him, and everything became quiet. After a month, his complexion and appetite gradually returned to normal. One day, while he was alone in the room, he suddenly cried out for help. It seemed as though someone was pulling his hair and dragging him out. He was driven away very quickly. The whole family did not know what was happening. The maids and servants tried to hold him back, but they could not control him. Slowly, he moved from the east of the academy to the back garden. As soon as he stepped out the door, he went even faster. He was about to reach the edge of the octagonal well when he suddenly fell to the ground and lost consciousness. The family carried him back. After a while, he could speak again and said, "It was actually that I had been meeting the woman for a long time."

When the household was moved, he did not return. This morning, his parents were in the hall when suddenly they saw him come in from outside. He was extremely angry, raising his hand and scolding loudly: "For a long time I could not find you. Originally, you were right here!" He then rushed forward and seized my hair. I struggled hard but could not escape. He went directly to the wellside and waved his hand into the well. Immediately, countless small ghosts emerged, each about two or three feet tall, and they all pulled me, dragging me toward the well. Soon, an old man with a white beard arrived, seated in a small cool sedan chair, accompanied by thirty attendants, rushing from the corner of the garden. He shouted, "Not allowed! Not allowed!" At this, all the ghosts folded their hands and stopped. The old man commanded, "Use the rod to strike!" His attendants raised sticks and beat them wildly, and all the ghosts returned to the well. The old man scolded the woman, saying, "I forbade you from coming out. How dare you act like this?" The woman lowered her head and clasped her sleeves, saying nothing. He continued, "There was originally a large stone placed to block the well. Where is it now?" The servant replied, "The people in the house carried it away to use for pounding clothes." The old man exclaimed, "It was not appropriate to move it!" He whipped the woman dozens of times and scolded her, saying, "How could you recklessly come out, endangering living people? Moreover, your husband has his own future!" He then threw her into the well and ordered others to carry a large stone and block it above. He told me, "I am the land deity, who came to save your husband. His life was nearly ruined by these ghosts. Go back and tell the people in the house that this stone must not be moved!" After saying this, he rose in his sedan chair and departed. This man later obtained an official position, eventually serving as the magistrate of Nanfeng.

*The Guesthouse in Jinling** In the early years of the Shaoxing reign, an official heading to Lin’an for his assignment passed through Jinling and stayed the night at an official lodging. His attendants unpacked and then dispersed, leaving him alone sitting in the hall. After a long while, the door of the eastern room opened by itself, and a servant with disheveled hair emerged, wearing a blue robe and white trousers. He stared at the official with wide eyes, raised a finger and pointed to his chest, saying, “There is a jade ring in my chest. Do you know or not?” Then, in a flash, he disappeared back into the room. The official was so terrified that he could not bear it, nearly falling to the ground. His startled spirit slightly calmed, and only then did he pull up his clothes and straighten his seat. Soon after, the door of the western room opened, and a woman in a blue robe and blue skirt emerged, holding an infant. She also stared at him with wide eyes, pointed to her child, and said, “You caused my death, official.” After speaking, she rushed back into the room. The official was shaken to the core; he wanted to run but his feet would not move, and he wanted to cry out but could not speak. After some time, the servant came from outside, and the official was hurriedly taken to a guesthouse. He remained confused and bewildered all day long. *From Volume 2, Story 20 of the Yijian Dingzhi

The Dog of the Zhu Family In the Xinchang County of Jun Prefecture, there lived a man named Zhu, who kept a dog that was extremely well-trained. Whenever the master returned from outside, regardless of the distance, the dog would always wag its tail and jump in front to greet him. One day, Zhu was imprisoned for owing rent, and after more than ten days, he was released. By the time he returned home, it was already late in the day. The dog was unusually joyful, and in its excitement, it accidentally scratched the master's clothes, tearing them. Zhu thought this was an omen and told his wife, "I just came out of prison, and the dog is like this." He then said, "The Liangshan Temple is building a house. I want to reward the craftsmen. We can kill the dog and cook it, and bring along five dou of flour." His wife followed his instructions. The next day, Zhu went to the temple and ordered a child to carry a small box with him. Upon arrival, the monk greeted him at the door and said, "Do not open the box. Are you not bringing the dog and flour?" Zhu was astonished and asked why. The monk replied, "Respected benefactor, please enter and sit in the main hall. This matter is too painful to speak of, but if I do not say it, I fail to fulfill the trust. Last night, I dreamt of your father, who said, 'Because of my attachment, I could not transcend. I was reborn as a dog of this family. That is why when I see my son return, I must go out to greet him. Just because he was released from prison and returned, he was very happy and accidentally tore my clothes. My son then plotted with his wife to kill me to use as a sacrifice.' Although I am facing death, I am also fortunate to leave behind my animal body. If I can avoid the suffering of being cut into pieces, the master's kindness is great. At the time of my life, I had some silver, which I secretly buried outside the stove, afraid that others might steal it. I often slept on it. Please instruct my son to dig it up and use it to perform Buddhist rituals to contribute to my posthumous blessings. With the remaining silver, he can still make a living." Upon hearing this, Zhu was deeply saddened and said, "The dog indeed sleeps there day and night." He then buried the dog at the back of the temple and returned home to dig up the hidden silver, finding the exact amount as mentioned. He then set up a water and land assembly at the temple.

Zhang Dun, a skilled physician from Luling, wandered in the remote areas and once temporarily resided in Chaozhou. One night, he dreamed of being invited by someone to a grand house, dark and solemn like a royal residence. He stood waiting on the left side of the door, and officials led him inside. Reaching the courtyard, he looked up at the bright canopy above, where a formally dressed person was sitting upright. A young man wearing light-colored clothes and a red headband led Zhang Dun to diagnose his pulse. Zhang Dun said, "Kidney deficiency with wind and emptiness, fearing that tinnitus would cause harm." The formally dressed person replied, "I have been suffering from ear pain for several days. You diagnosed it very well. Let's find some medicine. Consider giving the young man twenty thousand coins." Zhang Dun did not have time to give medicine and woke up in shock, unsure of where he was. He suspected it must be a shrine. The next day, he searched everywhere and finally arrived at the temple of the Southern Sea, remembering everything that had happened. The one who led him up was likely the prince of the eastern annex. Climbing to the main hall, he gazed at the divine image and saw a yellow bee nest in its left ear. He immediately carefully removed it, burned incense, and bowed twice before leaving. The next day, the tax official sent a note saying that a guest boat had failed to pay taxes and claimed that the items belonged to Zhang Dun's family. Zhang Dun thought he had never seen them and hurriedly went to prove it was false. Seeing the boatman tied up on the bridge, he called out from afar, "I am a tenant of Cai Xiucai, a relative of Liu Tiujǔ. Knowing that you have a close relationship with Liu Tiujǔ and also know the tax official, he fabricated this excuse." Zhang Dun arranged for his release. Later, Cai came to thank him and brought cloth and other items, exactly twenty thousand coins. The official Liu Tiujǔ was Liu Jing.

**Master Guan, the Chief Military Commissioner** When Master Guan Shirén of Jinyun was still a scholar-official, on the first day of the year he rose early and went out. He encountered several large ghosts, whose appearances were fierce and evil. He shouted and asked them, and they replied, "We are plague spirits. On the first day of the year, we are meant to spread illness among people." Guan asked, "Is my family affected by this?" They answered, "No." He then asked, "Then why are you unable to enter my home?" They replied, "Perhaps because three generations of virtue have been accumulated, or because the family is about to rise in fortune, or because they do not eat beef. If any one of these three is true, then we cannot enter. Your family is free from plague, so we have disappeared."

Xiaogu Temple** In the reign of Emperor Ling, a woman from Luoyang was born with two heads and four arms. **Fuchitemple** The Fuchitemple at the mouth of the Xingguo River is the shrine of Wu General Gan Ning. Its miraculous responses are well-known, and one dares not be disrespectful when passing by. Daily offerings of sacrificial animals are made without fail. During the Jianyan era, a large bandit named Ma Jin crossed the river from Qizhou and Huangzhou. He came to the temple to seek divination with a divination tool, intending to massacre Xingguo. The deity refused, even after being asked repeatedly. Ma Jin became angry and said, "If I get a winning divination, I will massacre the city. If I get a positive divination, I will massacre the city. If I get a negative divination, I will carry the temple and burn it." He then threw the divination tool himself. One fell to the ground, and the other disappeared. Soon, one attached itself to the temple wall, several feet above the ground, standing upright without falling. Ma Jin was shocked and fearful, and he bowed and left. To this day, the divination tools are protected in their original place, and those who pass by must pay respects. Inside the hall, on the high walls, there are also two large divination tools, hanging loosely from the beams. It is said they were thrown by Huang Chao.

**The Scholar of Jinan** Wang Sheng of Jinan was a relative of Cansheng Qing Zeng. After passing the imperial examination and leaving the capital, he traveled for dozens of miles before stopping at an inn by the roadside. The host was also a scholar, and he offered Wang Sheng wine to drink. Looking toward the back of the house, Wang Sheng saw several rooms with elegant and luxurious curtains and drapes. He asked who lived there, and the host replied, "I am a *tiju* heading to take office in Fujian. I went ahead alone in a single carriage, leaving my family here to await the official who will come to welcome me. It has now been months." From a distance, Wang Sheng glimpsed women moving about inside, and he saw a young and beautiful woman, upon whom his eyes lingered, unable to look away. As evening approached, he stayed the night at the inn. That night, the host spoke with him, and they discussed their hometowns and family backgrounds. Learning that Wang Sheng was unmarried, the host mentioned a daughter of his family, who was extremely beautiful and charming, and who was now being proposed for marriage. He asked, "Do you have any intention of taking her as your wife?" Wang Sheng was delighted and feared he might not be able to obtain her hand. The host arranged the matter. The next day, they made their agreement. The girl’s mother then invited Wang Sheng to meet her. She said, "My husband is far away on official business, and he deeply cherishes his daughter. We have been searching for a suitable match for a long time. Unexpectedly, we have found a fine groom. Since both of us are traveling, we cannot perform the full six rites of marriage. Why not simply go through a simplified version?" So they hastily prepared the betrothal gifts and chose a date for the wedding. They also allowed Wang Sheng to take the girl back to Jinan.

The husband was urgently summoned to Fujian to be welcomed by a letter. After their parting, they did not communicate again. He did not consider it suspicious. The wife remained as usual. Over four or five years, she gave birth to two sons. Her daily routines and hobbies were no different from those of ordinary people. However, the servants drew water only using the front bucket and discarded the back one, considering it unclean. She brought along a maid who handled all cooking, sewing, and other household tasks. At night, she ordered the maid to sleep under the bed. Suddenly, she told her husband, "My body is not well. I will lie down briefly. Do not enter the room to startle me." He agreed to it. In a moment, thunderclaps and lightning struck, heavy rain poured down, and flames blazed brightly. The entire household was in great fear. After some time, the storm finally settled. Both the wife and the maid had disappeared. Initially, when the husband entered the capital, he passed by a place called the Dragon Mother Temple. He went in to pay respects and saw the statue of the Dragon Maiden, which was beautiful and dignified. His heart was moved, and he silently thought, "In the future, if I could marry someone like this, it would sufficiently comfort my heart." When he left the temple, a large snake coiled on his horse's saddle. He tried to drive it away, but it did not leave. He began to be greatly afraid and returned to the temple to pay respects and apologize. When he left, he saw nothing. Later, he encountered this strange event, and knowledgeable people suspected it was done by the dragon.

### The Hermit of Haiyan Wang Guanfu, a censorial official, originally served as the magistrate of Haiyan during the early years of the Chongning era. In the spring, he opened the county garden for selling wine, and a large number of visitors flocked to the site. Wang's eldest son, Yue, with the courtesy name Bingyi, was around ten years old and also enjoyed the view. One day, he encountered a wild hermit who bowed with raised hands and called him a court official. They conversed and laughed for a long time before the hermit left. Yue disliked the title of official and returned home to tell his father, but his father could not understand what was meant. Ten years later, during the implementation of the Zhenhe official system, the title of Western Court Official was changed to Bingyi Lang. Wang finally understood the hermit's words and changed his son's name to Fu, giving him the courtesy name Chengke. ### The Turtles Reciting Poetry Wang Chengke, a junior minister, lived in a countryside house in Fenning at the end of the Jianyan era. He dreamed of thirty pairs of black-clothed men and women. Two people stood like a couple in front, and the rest lined up behind, weeping and kneeling, begging for their lives. In the dream, it seemed as if he agreed. The next day, he took a leisurely walk outside the gate and met a villager carrying turtles. The villager poured them out on the ground, with two larger ones in front and the remaining twenty-six behind. Suddenly remembering the previous night's dream, Wang bought all of them and released them into the stream. That night, the two black-clothed people came to thank him and recited two lines of poetry: "Wandering freely in the rivers and lakes, much better than being in low, wet places." They appeared very content and self-satisfied, and their joy was evident. Because they had previously been in low, marshy areas and were taken by people.

The Deputy Magistrate Zhang In the sixth year of the Gande era (1167), Zhang, a man from Jinyun County, served as the Deputy Magistrate of Shaoyang Prefecture. His servant and maid had an affair, which was discovered. They were arrested and delivered to prison. Zhang secretly instructed the Assistant Magistrate Wu to have them killed. Wu reported this to the Prefect Zhou Jimei. Zhou Jimei, however, thought it was not right and ordered the application of the law and the completion of the case. The servant and maid were punished with flogging and banishment to the northern mountains. Zhang was dissatisfied with this outcome and chose a military officer from his own hall to escort the prisoners, instructing him to kill them and return, promising a generous reward. The officer accepted the order and set out. After two days, he killed the prisoners near the border of Nanxiong. That night, Zhou had a dream where the servant cried and pleaded, saying, "I have committed a crime, but I am grateful for Your Excellency's grace in sparing my life. Yet, I was ultimately killed by the Deputy Magistrate. Please have mercy on me." The next day, Zhou thoroughly investigated the matter. The officer had already returned, and Zhou quickly dealt with him, also punishing him with flogging and banishment. Zhang, in his study, saw the servant standing in front of him. He was angry that the servant had not been delivered, but suddenly he saw nothing and fell to the ground. He then fell ill with a sudden diarrhea and died after ten days.

Sun Shidao Once upon a time, Sun Shidao, the inspector of the port of Fuzhou, encountered a strange man who taught him a method of treating illnesses using talismans and charms. This method was very simple, yet it had an immediate and accurate divine response. A woman, the sister-in-law of Magistrate Wang, contracted a disease caused by a ghost. The ghost would call out Mr. Wang's name and continuously curse him. This state persisted for over a year. Despite performing various rituals to expel the ghost, none of these efforts were successful in curing the woman. Upon hearing of Sun Shidao's reputation, they invited him to help. Sun Shidao asked the entire household to fast for seven days, then dressed formally and burned incense before personally submitting a petition to the Heavenly Pivot Bureau. The woman, already aware of this, said, "Inspector Sun can only treat evil spirits. What about my injustice?" When Sun Shidao arrived, he invited the woman to come out. Mr. Wang said, "Given her condition, anyone who calls out will surely be cursed. How can we expect her to come out?" Sun Shidao replied, "Let her speak." The woman happily agreed and said, "Yes." She soon went to wash and then came out. After a while, she dressed neatly and composed herself as usual. Seeing Sun Shidao, she said, "My family of four were all innocent and died by misfortune. I have now obtained permission from heaven."

The woman demanded compensation and would not stop until she received it. The monk kindly asked her not to say more. She then showed her chest to prove her suffering. She said, "I have suffered such cruel treatment. How can this injustice be righted?" The monk tried to enlighten and persuade her, urging her not to cause harm. She bowed and thanked him repeatedly before entering. The monk privately told the king, "Do you remember the events in Nanjian Prefecture?" The king could not recall. The monk had already written the names of four people on his palm and showed them to the king. The king nodded without speaking, feeling deeply regretful and fearful. It was said that in the past, when he served as the deputy governor of Nanjian, a theft occurred in a subordinate county. He went to supervise the capture and found a commoner who was a thief. He captured and executed the thief's husband and wife. The thief's daughter, who had married a nearby village, heard that her parents were killed and came immediately. She cried, wailed, and cursed angrily. The king was furious and then captured and executed her. She was pregnant at the time, and in fact, four lives were taken. The monk said, "This wrong cannot be rectified according to our law. It can only be temporarily pacified." If the illness recurs, do not call me. From then on, the woman became somewhat calmer. After two months, it happened again. Until the king died, the woman finally became peaceful.

In the third year of the Gandaod era (1167 AD), a woman from the western part of Chaozhou city gave birth to a child who was pregnant beyond the expected term. When she gave birth to the child, it was the size of a finger, with all five limbs present. Hundreds of them could move slightly. She loaded them into a basket and threw them into the river. The woman was also unharmed. There has never been such a strange occurrence in ancient or modern times. A person named Zhang Wang from Shaowu had a dream during the autumn examination in the Dingmao year of the Shaoxing era (1157 AD). In the dream, someone inserted chopsticks into her hair and said, "You want to be highly recommended. You should do it like this." After waking up and thinking about it deeply, she said, "My name is Wang. If a dot is added to the beginning, it becomes 'Zhu.'" She then changed her name to Zhu. That year, she was indeed recommended and sent, and she was about to take the imperial examination. She dreamed again of a green-clad child pulling her clothes from a cradle and said, "Do not go hastily. Wait for me." Later, it did not go well. By the Ji Chou year of the Gandaod era (1169 AD), she finally passed the imperial examination again. A person named Ding Zhaozuo from the same village also planned to go together. Both of them passed the imperial examination. Ding Zhaozuo was born in the Dingmao year. He finally understood the previous dream and jokingly told Ding, "For your little one, delayed me by twenty-one years." They laughed together and that was all.

Liu Daochang Liu Daochang, originally the son of a soldier from Yuzhang, knew only a few characters. He loved drinking and was a good-for-nothing, wandering aimlessly in the market. Once, he was beaten in the prefecture for a crime and, ashamed to see his peers, did not return home. Instead, he directly climbed up to Tengwang Pavilion to take a nap. In his dream, a Taoist held a scroll and placed it in his sleeve, saying, "Keep this secret. Practicing it can help people. Even if it is your father or brother, do not show it to them." The Taoist warned and instructed him very seriously. When he woke up, the book was in his sleeve. Suddenly, he felt his spirit become clear and light. He looked at the text and saw that it was a kind of magic spell. Returning home, he immediately painted the image of Zhenwu. He began treating people's illnesses and performing rituals. The talismans he wrote were very different from ordinary Taoist seal scripts. All the treatments he provided, whether drinking talisman water, scooping incense ash, or reciting spells over dates, were very simple. He also told people, "There will definitely be a response at night. Nothing will go wrong." He used his methods to treat cattle epidemics, and all were cured. The people of the county gradually came to respect him, and together they built a Zhenwu Hall for him to live in. Initially, he planned to dig a pool to get water for treating illnesses, but suddenly a spring emerged in the courtyard, extremely sweet and cold. When it was dredged and treated, it turned out to be an ancient well. Now his method is widely practiced, but the Taoist book cannot be seen. Only ten or so talismans were carved on stone.

**Li's Immortal Pill** Li Quan, a beggar from Yuzhang, was formerly enlisted in the military records of Jiankang. During the Battle of Shaoxing in the Xin Si year, he was injured in his eyes and broken in his feet, and was demobilized as a civilian. He became sick and unable to support himself, so he began begging in the market. He walked with two crutches, his vision dim, and found it very difficult to navigate his way. Every time he passed the gate of Wang Shilang's mansion, he was given some money. Suddenly, he stopped coming for several days, and everyone thought he would die. After half a month, he returned, with his eyes seeing clearly and his steps light and quick. He said that he had met a Taoist who gave him a prescription and warned him that it would be effective if taken, instructing him to sell it to help others and not to seek profit at the expense of others. He said that earning 700 coins per day was sufficient. When asked his name, the Taoist refused to say. Li Quan collected the money he begged and made the medicine. After taking it for ten days, his eyesight had improved to seven parts out of ten, and his foot strength was back to normal. He immediately used the prescription to sell medicine, carrying a large fan with the inscription "Li's Immortal Pill" and hanging two flags on a pole. All who took it saw results, but he never earned more than 700 coins, adhering to the ethical guideline. One day, he earned as much as 2,000 coins, and then he fell ill and could not go out, only feeling better when the money was spent. It was the year Jichou of the Ganda era.

Liu San娘 A madwoman from Yuzhang named Liu San娘. She suffered from a mental illness. She often held two wooden hammers and struck them together. She wandered all day in the market, her clothes torn and dirty, and loved to insult people. At night, she sometimes slept in temples. Even though she had a son who was a soldier, she treated him indifferently. Only Song Zhen, the Censor-in-Chief Pu, recognized her as an extraordinary person. Zhang Ruging, the Grand Secretary Cheng, served as the governor and often called her into the government office, where she stayed for two or three nights, sharing meals with her. Sometimes he left her in the courtyard, and sometimes he left excrement in her bedding. After a long time, she suddenly told those she frequently visited, saying, "On a certain day, I will die." Indeed, she did die as predicted. Her son buried her in the wild. After half a year, the county official went to Changsha to see her. She was striking the hammers as before. The official was shocked and asked, "Liu San娘, you are dead. How can you be here?" She smiled and said, "Convey a message to my son. I am very comfortable here." Repeatedly asked, she did not respond, and the official never saw her again. He returned and told her son, who then opened the tomb and found it empty. The Jail Guard of Xingguo In the Xingguo military court, there was a prisoner sentenced to death by decapitation. The jailer Li Zhen carried out the execution. The prisoner told him, "Death cannot be avoided. Please do not cut off my hands; it will harm your family." Li Zhen did not listen and first cut off both of the prisoner's hands at the market, saying, "See what you can do to me now." Two days later, Li Zhen's wife gave birth to a son whose wrists were cut off below the elbows. At the time, Wang Zhen, also known as Zhi Chuan, was the Deputy Governor and personally witnessed this.

#### The Disaster of the Pig in the Qiu Family In the sixth year of the Ganzhao era, the family of Qiu Yue, the deputy assistant teacher in Nanxiong Prefecture, was afflicted by an epidemic. A large pig in their household gave birth to several offspring, some with human heads, others with chicken heads, leopard heads, and horse heads, resembling statues or paintings of plague demons. They killed the pig, sacrificed it, and performed a ritual to ward off the disaster, but the calamity became even more severe. Qiu Yue and his wife both died. His eldest son, Qiu Gao, who was recommended as a top candidate in the provincial examination, also died along with his wife. A total of eight or nine deaths occurred. They tried every possible method to pray and make offerings, but after a long time, things finally settled down. This was a recent disaster attributed to the pig. #### The Dead Woman of Xuancheng Xuancheng experienced the turmoil of the Qifang Rebellion. The prefecture governor, Liu Longtu, was killed, and the people of the prefecture built a shrine in his honor. In the aftermath of the bloodshed in the city, there were often many ruins. A woman from a household was pregnant but died before giving birth. She was buried behind the temple. People living near the temple sometimes saw lights in the grass at night and heard the sound of a baby crying. One day, there was often a woman carrying a baby coming to buy bread near the street. No one knew who she was, and they were quite suspicious. They once waited for her to leave and followed her. She disappeared to the left of the temple. The next day, she came again, and they tried to talk to her. They secretly tied a red thread to her skirt and followed her again. The woman felt someone was following her, left her child, and disappeared. Only the red thread was left on the grave in the grass. They then took the child back home, found out about the child's father's family, and told them the reason. They dug up the grave to verify, and found the woman's body still intact, but the pregnancy was already empty. They burned her body and raised the child themselves. It is said that the child is still alive today. The *Jingshan Compilation* also records a similar but slightly different story.

The Ghost of Bai Sha Post Station** The Bai Sha Post Station, located on the eastern boundary of Nan Jian Prefecture, has always been known for its strange and supernatural occurrences. Travelers and their servants dared not stay alone there. In the year 1134 during the reign of Emperor Gaozong of the Song Dynasty, Fang Wude, a high-ranking official, was appointed to govern Fujian. Several officials from his entourage came to welcome him and all stayed at the post station. It was the beginning of summer, and they set up their beds in the hall. After a long time, they finally went to bed. One of the officials, Wang Xiao, the chief in charge of military affairs, suddenly had a terrifying nightmare and cried out loudly. The others got up and lit candles to see what was happening. He was still in a state of fierce struggle, as if fighting someone. After a long time, he woke up and said, "I was just falling asleep but not yet fully, when a white-clad woman came to the bed and pressed against me. I couldn't drive her away, and she kept pulling my clothes. Only when everyone got up did she leave." The others saw that his nightclothes were torn and tattered, like those of a poor man. They stayed up all night with candles lit, too afraid to sleep. **Li Yuanli** Li Yuanli, a native of Fuqing in Fuzhou, served as the magistrate of Longxi County in Zhangzhou in 1156. He was acting as the county magistrate and captured six bandits. According to law, capturing seven bandits would warrant a promotion to the capital. Li ordered his archers to search and find one more person to make up the number. All six were sentenced to death for full charges. Li received the rank of Chengwu Lang and received the official notification of his promotion. Immediately, he saw the wrongfully executed standing before him, and he became depressed and unhappy. When he was transferred to Lin'an (Hangzhou), a fellow guest in the same inn asked him about it, and he spoke quite frankly about it. He was quickly assigned to Tong'an County in Quanzhou and returned, packing his belongings and leaving the city. The ghost followed him without letting go. Only after walking ten li did he stay at the Dragon Mountain inn. That night, he died suddenly. This scroll is all said by Wang Zhichuan.

**Volume 37 of the Yi Jian Ding Zhi** **Wu Shi Liang** Wu Shi Liang, the county magistrate's assistant in Jinx溪 of Fuzhou, upon completing his term, settled his family in a nearby village at Longshou Temple. One night, tiles were thrown against his window. Suspecting the temple monks, he questioned them the next day, but they dared not answer. One of them finally spoke cautiously, saying, "This is the Third Young Lord of our county. The sounds and traces are clear and evident. Could it be that we have committed some offense?" Wu did not believe him. The next day, as he walked through the temple corridors, tiles and gravel rained down from the sky, falling continuously. At the time, it was snowing, yet the objects thrown were all dry, as if they came from an ancient tomb. Wu then grew afraid. He summoned the monks to recite scriptures and offer prayers of atonement, but the strange occurrences continued. Eventually, flying stones filled the incense burner. Wu's father took a brick, inscribed a message on it, and cast it into the air while praying, "If we have indeed offended the Three Saints, may you return again." Soon after, the brick returned to the spot where it had been thrown, clearly visible. With no choice, Wu was forced to move his family from the east wing to the west wing of the temple, in order to avoid the deity's wrath. However, his belongings had not yet been settled, and the disturbances were as before. So he moved his entire family into the county town, taking residence at the Miaoyin Taoist Temple. The supernatural phenomena grew even worse. He called upon Taoist priests to hold a ritual and show respect, but the disturbances did not cease at all. Wu became angry and called out the name of the deity, scolding it, saying, "You are a god, and should be wise and upright. Why do you treat me like this? I have treated you well enough. Not even a little have you relented. You act with malicious intent. From now on, I will no longer fear you." After he spoke, the sound and activity ceased. Earlier, the family's chests and boxes—even those without locks or keys—had seemed occupied by some force, making them impossible to open. On that day, however, they opened and closed as normal, and the harm from the stones finally came to an end.

The Wife of the Magistrate's Servant** Wang Tongpan, a magistrate from Fuzhou, lived in a temple at Shushan Mountain. His servant's wife was young and beautiful. A guest scholar named Zhou Shunchen had a deep affection for her but could not meet her. One day, Wang sent someone to light a fire and knock on the door, inviting Zhou for a night conversation. When the door opened, it was the servant's wife. She looked at Zhou, smiled, blew out the lamp, and followed him inside. She said, "It wasn't the magistrate who invited you. I came here on purpose." Zhou could not bear the delight and stayed the night. The next day, they met again, but she acted indifferently as if they had never met. Zhou found this very strange and suspected that the person from the previous night looked different in terms of weight. That night, she came again. Zhou did not dare to let her in, but she refused to leave. Before dawn, she suddenly disappeared. Zhou secretly questioned the temple monk, who revealed that a woman's coffin was in a neighboring room. The woman soon fell ill and recovered after a month. A teacher named Cai Zisi, upon hearing this, especially went to her room, burned incense, and prayed. He wanted to see her to inquire about her hometown and surname, hoping to visit her family, but found nothing. **Yunlin Mountain** Xu Yanzhang from Linchuan lived at the foot of Yunlin Mountain in Jinxi. His wife's relative, Ni, visited him and stayed in an outer room. It was raining heavily at night, and after midnight, something pushed the door open. The door opened, and the object entered, sat on the stove, blew the fire to light it, and sat down. Ni peeked from behind the curtain and saw something that looked like a bearded sheep, with its entire body wet. She got off the bed and shouted at it. The object jumped up and fell on Ni. Ni screamed and ran out, managing to escape. She did not know what kind of monster it was.

**Sun Guanglu** In the state of Zheng, the official Sun Yu, who held the title of Guanlu Daifu, passed away. His family chose a burial site. The eldest son, Ke, had a dream in which he accompanied his younger brother, Song, who was the magistrate of Hedong, and their father, along with a guest named Zhang Yanhe, to a mountain temple. Sun Guanglu ordered noodles to be cooked. Ke politely declined, saying he was already full. Zhang Yanhe also did not eat and stood up. Only Song ate with him. After finishing the meal, Sun Guanglu said, "This place is only four or five li away from Xiaomei Mountain." Zhang Yanhe replied, "It’s about ten li." Sun Guanglu said, "Indeed, it is. The village of Yang Fei is only four or five li away." Ten days after the dream, news of Song’s death arrived from Hedong. They also chose a burial site together, which happened on the same day as Sun Guanglu’s death. After passing by the temple where the tomb was, the temple monk prepared noodles to offer to the spirits of the two. Everything unfolded exactly as in the dream. The tomb is located to the south of Xiaomei Mountain, ten li away. Four li further is the village of Yang Family. **Jiang Zhiping** Jiang Zhiping had a good relationship with an old man named Neng Xiang. The old man suddenly told him, "What kind of deeds have damaged your moral virtue? You will die within a year." Jiang, a virtuous person, replied, "How could I have done such things?" The old man said, "Think about it." Jiang said, "Upon self-examination, I have no other evil deeds. But when I was an exam official, I placed a close acquaintance in a high position, and there was indeed a personal bias. That was the only thing." The old man said, "That’s it. You acted based on your personal likes and dislikes, granting heavenly rank to others. You cannot escape this fate." Soon after, Jiang died. Alas, there are countless people in the world who commit far greater sins than Jiang, standing shoulder to shoulder. How can they not be afraid?

Sungshan Bamboo Grove Temple At the Western Capital's Mount Song, there is a temple called the Vajra King Temple. Near it, there are vast bamboo groves that stretch endlessly into the distance. Every time the monks have their meals, the sound of the bell faintly emerges from beyond the forest. Because of this, the temple is called the Bamboo Grove Temple, or it is said to be a sacred place of the 500 great arhats. There was a monk who came from the western part of Shanxi to pay respects to Bodhidharma. On the way, he met another monk who said, "I am a disciple of the Bamboo Grove." The monk gave him a letter to deliver to the kitchen attendant, instructing him to knock on a large tree beside the temple, and someone would come out to respond. The monk took the letter and continued on his journey. Upon arriving, he found himself in a deep, dense bamboo forest with no one to ask for directions. He tried knocking on the tree, and a small novice came out to guide him in. They walked for several hundred steps and reached a stone bridge. After crossing the bridge for another hundred steps, they saw a grand temple with golden and jade-like splendor. The attendant came out to welcome them and asked to see the letter. The attendant said, "He just went to the heavenly realm for a meal and will return shortly." They sat for a long time. Soon, they saw more than a hundred monks in the air, riding on flying cranes, lions, dragons, and phoenixes, slowly descending. The monk held the letter and presented it to them, asking to stay for the night. They firmly refused and sent the novice to lead him out. He followed the old path back and reached the stone bridge. The attendant pointed to a side path and told him to go alone. After taking just a few steps, he turned back and saw steep cliffs thousands of feet high and towering trees. He no longer knew where the temple was located.

Lu Zhongju During the Daming era, there was a student named Lu Zhongju at the Imperial Academy. Because he submitted a memorial discussing state affairs, he was expelled from the academy. Later, he returned to the capital. In his dream, a deity told him, "You should achieve success. Why not write a memorial?" The next night, he had another dream. Lu Zhongju, having been punished before for this reason, did not believe it at all. He moved to another place to avoid it. That night, he had another dream, but he still did not believe it. He went to visit his old friend, the Minister Gao Sheng, and asked for financial assistance. They met with great joy, and Gao Sheng kept him for the night. The next morning after returning from court, Gao Sheng said, "Tian Jue is extremely annoyed by people. He wants to compile a political code and ordered me to be the proofreader." Lu Zhongju replied, "This is just one matter among the Six Codes of the Zhou Dynasty. Why not compile the Six Codes? Why only choose one?" Gao Sheng said, "You should write a book about this." Lu Zhongju then thought of the deity's words and quickly drafted a memorial about it. Gao Sheng ordered a clerk to transcribe it immediately and submit it. Thus, Lu Zhongju obtained the position of Di Gong Lang. At that time, Zhang Tian Jue was the Chancellor. A Strange Beast in Luoyang There was also a strange beast in Luoyang.

In the seventh year of the Xuanhe era (1119), a black beast suddenly appeared in the market of Xiluo. It resembled a dog or a donkey, appearing at night and disappearing during the day. There were rumors among the people that it could scratch human skin, causing sores and wounds. One person was sitting under the eaves at night when he saw the beast enter his house. He hit it with a stick, hitting it hard. The sound ceased, and it fell. He took a candle to look at it, and it turned out to be a young girl lying on the ground, already dead. Such incidents were not one-off. The following year, it was captured by the Jin invaders. Weng Qiyu's business friend lived outside the city walls of Jian'an, about ten miles from the county seat. On the Lantern Festival night, he invited two young men from his neighborhood to go into the city to watch the lanterns. They walked along a moonlit path through a pine forest but had not walked halfway when they met a villager carrying a hoe and singing. The two young men were very frightened and just wanted to stay overnight at a nearby peasant's house. Weng Qiyu asked them why. One said, "I just saw a ghost with an axe coming." The other said, "No, I saw a ghost with a red beard and leopard pants holding a wooden club." Weng Qiyu convinced them that it was not true. The next morning, they finally entered the city. The one who said he saw the ghost with an axe died without getting sick, and the one who said he saw the ghost with a red beard got sick and died at home. Weng Qiyu alone remained unharmed. Doctor Hu from Changzhou was the governor of Xinzhou. At the moment of taking office, a loud noise came from the large beam in the hall. He called a carpenter to climb up and inspect, intending to repair and reinforce it. The beam broke, and the hall collapsed, crushing several people to death. Not more than a few days later, Doctor Hu developed a boil on his back. The ashes in the stove in the hall suddenly flew up, filling the entire room. A giant snake hung its head from the beam, making a sound like a child crying. Doctor Hu fell ill for three days and then died. The above ten events were recorded by Sun Shen Yuanhan from Zheng.

other two monks also did not close their eyes. | | 闻有敲窗者。 | They heard someone knocking on the window. | | 问之不对。 | When they asked, there was no response. | | 以为小吏故作戏耳。 | They thought it was a small They looked at the torn window paper. | | 有妇人小面正可棂间。 | There was a small woman's face fitting through the lattice. | | 良久入卓上立。 | After a long while, she stood on the table. | | 形体悉具。 | Her body was fully formed. | | 仅高尺余。 | She was only about a foot tall. | | 僧唤仆不应。 | The monks called the servants, but they did not respond. | | 密相与计。 | They secretly discussed among themselves. | | 此亦无足畏。 | This was not something to be afraid of. | | 俟其至前。 | They waited for her to come closer. | | 则两人执之。 | Then two of them would grab her. | | 一人启门呼仆入。 | One would open the door and call the servants in. | | 五男子当一女鬼。 | Five men against one female ghost. | | 便可成擒也。 | They could capture her easily. | | 妇人稍下据倚坐。 | The woman slowly lowered herself and sat down. | | 已与常人等。 | She was now the same size as a normal person. | | 遂揭帐而登。 | She then lifted the curtain and climbed onto the bed. | | 僧始耸然如体挟冰霜。 | The monks were startled as if their bodies were holding ice and frost. | | 不暇施前策。 | They had no time to carry out their previous plan. | | 妇人忽趋而下。 | The woman suddenly rushed down. | | 自为掩帐。 | She covered herself with the curtain. | | 取钵便溺。 | She took a bowl to urinate. | | 其势如倾斗水。 | The force was like pouring a bucket of water. | | 退至火边。 | She retreated to the fire. | | 大声吼。 | She roared loudly. | | 雷从地起。 | Thunder rose from the ground. | | 物与灯皆不见。 | All objects and the lamp disappeared. | | 湍琴犹在膝。 | Miao Tuan's qin was still on his knees. | | 惊魄定。 | Their hearts calmed down. | | 方复起。 | They then got up again. | | 共坐达旦。 | They sat together until dawn. | | 明日告邑胥。 | The next day, they reported to the county official. | | 皆莫知何怪。 | No one knew what kind of strange occurrence it was. | | 其室今为吏舍云。 | The room is now a government office, they say.

Shaozhou East Inn Wang Xingzhong and his brother Kewen, from Jinxi in Fuzhou, took along more than ten servants and headed to Guangzhou to visit their father. Passing through the eastern part of Shaozhou, they were about to enter the inn when the inn staff told them, "There are so-called Seven Saints here who often cause harm to travelers. It would be better to stay at a guesthouse, where it is peaceful and trouble-free." Wang Xingzhong thought the staff was fabricating stories to scare them, as they were reluctant to provide accommodation and service. He reasoned that their party was not small and, if there were indeed spirits, they could resist them. They decided to stay at the inn. The other servants stayed outside, while three servants were in the hall. It was almost midnight when all the doors inside and outside suddenly opened wide, and lanterns and candles were arranged. Wang Xingzhong suspected it was a thief and held a sword on his knee, waiting for them to enter and kill them. Kewen simply sat with a blanket over him, reciting the *Lengyan* spell. After a long time, he heard the sound of weapons clashing in the hall, with shouts and cheers in response, completely like the skills practiced by wicked young men in the world. Wang Xingzhong peeked through the door.

I saw seven men with their hair loose and their bodies bare, each holding two knives. They were jumping and playing around, and I was suddenly very afraid. I quickly climbed onto the bed and hid behind my brother. The ghosts entered the room, carrying out all the chests and boxes, even taking the bed canopy with them. They took the food from the travel provisions and ate it, and they also secretly watched. They had already cut off the heads of three servants, along with their hands, feet, and internal organs, and hung them on the walls. I became even more terrified and dared not open my eyes again. Gradually, I fell into a drowsy sleep. Soon, the neighbor's rooster crowed again, and there was silence, no sound heard. My heart began to calm down. At dawn, I woke up. The canopy and other items were still in their original places, and the three servants were all unharmed. I briefly described what I had seen, which was mostly the same, but I did not deeply remember the details of the slaughter. Those who stayed outside also suffered from this harm. Although they all survived, their spirits became dull and their appearance withered, because their blood had been lost. Kong Zhong rose to the position of Tongpan in Zhaoqing, and Xing Zhong died as a Gan Guan in Guangxi.

**Salt Field of Haimen** In the salt field of Haimen, County of Haimen, Prefecture of Tongzhou, there lived a man named Liu who was the supervisor of the salt field. He had a son. One night, the child woke up crying. His parents went to check on him and saw two small clay figures shaped like steamed buns on his head. They waved them away, and the child immediately recovered. This happened again the next day. From then on, Liu would place the figures beside his seat, or when the child was with the wet nurse, the trouble would return. Liu suspected that a ghost was causing the trouble and scolded it, saying, "You can cause trouble, but why don't you cause trouble between my wife and me? Why do you only trouble the baby?" That night, Liu deliberately went to sleep in an outer room to test the cause. The next morning, he found over thirty such figures arranged on the pillow, bed, and footstool, of various sizes. Clothes, utensils, and other items had also disappeared without reason. Searching for them was fruitless, and the maidservants and concubines found it very troublesome. One day, the gatekeeper told the old servant, "Two young nuns have been in the house for a long time. They can be sent away." The servant went inside to report this, but there were none. A little later, the gatekeeper saw them leave and immediately followed them.

Passing by a small temple at the corner of a wall, Liu concealed himself. Liu prepared incense and wine, and went to the place to pray, saying: "Since I took office, I have not neglected any rites in serving the gods. How could such a demon have arisen? Now I make a pact with the deity: if I can fully change my past actions, I shall summon monks to chant sutras, prepare a water and land offering, to provide merit for the afterlife. Otherwise, I shall cast the idol into the sea, burn the shrine, and cut down the tree. You may choose whichever of these two options you wish." After bowing twice, he withdrew. Only upon returning home did he find that sixty different types of clothing and utensils that had previously been lost were now all there, intact. The illness of his child also did not occur. Liu died in office, having served well. His successor arrived at the prefecture. The prefect, Tian Shiqing, invited him to a meal. During the banquet, they spoke of this matter. By evening, when he changed clothes, he did not return. Tian Shiqing sent a palace servant to check on him, and found him already collapsed and unconscious. A physician was called to treat him, and he revived at midnight. After assuming office, Tian Shiqing's two sons both died young. Hearing that the great tree had caused calamity, Tian Shiqing ordered soldiers to cut it down for firewood. The Liu family was spared from the disaster, but the successor bore it instead. This was indeed pitiable.

In the second year of the Jianyan era, a man from Zheng named Sun Xuanzhongfu accompanied his father, Dafu Junke, to Yangzhou. They stayed at an inn, and another official, Zhou, also lodged there. One guest, drunk and crazed, entered the inn from outside, sat or squatted inside, and uttered filthy words. Zhou pointed to Sun Xuanzhongfu's room and said, "This official has a fierce and stern nature. He will arrest you. Why don't you leave?" The guest became even more boisterous and uncontrollable. After a long while, Dafu Junke went out to greet someone, leaving Sun Xuanzhongfu alone in the inn. The guest then entered the room and tied up Sun Xuanzhongfu at the table. At that time, all the servants had gone out, and there was no one to intervene. Zhou also closed the door. Suddenly, the guest left the inn, went to a high bridge, and spoke to passersby, saying, "I just went to a certain store and was insulted by the Sun family. I have no face to show myself." He then took out a small dagger from his waist, stabbed his throat, and died immediately. The patrol guard reported to the military officer, who quickly arrested Sun, Zhou, and others. They were about to examine the body of the deceased. Soon after, the guest revived and could speak. He wrote a statement, saying, "I was drunk and spoke wildly. I have never been insulted by the Sun family. What I said on the bridge, I cannot remember. Everything was done by me, and no one else was involved." Thus, they were all released. The guest died shortly after. It was not because of his statement that he had not been insulted by the Sun family; Sun was almost implicated.

**Magistrate of Haimen** The Magistrate of Haimen in Tongzhou’s Haimen County, who also acted as the County Magistrate, was patrolling the coast when he was startled by a huge tide. He contracted a heart ailment and told his wife, “You are young, and your children are still weak. What can we do about returning home?” His wife was surprised by his ominous words. The Magistrate said, “A woman stood beside me and asked for a red back jacket. You should give it to her immediately.” The wife made a red paper jacket and burned it. The next day, the Magistrate said the woman was very grateful but mentioned that it was just a larger one. The wife went to the place where she had burned the jacket earlier and found it still unburned in the ashes. She then made another jacket for the Magistrate. The Magistrate often spoke of seeing people descending from the chimney, though their residence was far away, and his eyesight could not reach it. For more than a month, he predicted his death date to his wife and suddenly passed away. His official residence was in a temple, and his wife, overwhelmed with fear, asked two nuns to accompany her for the night. As soon as they passed the funeral canopy, one nun suddenly climbed onto the table and spoke as if the deceased, ordering the wife to invite County Magistrate Sun Su. Sun came and had a detailed conversation with the spirit, who also pointed out the faults of a certain minor official and asked for him to be beaten. Sun followed the instruction and explained the reason, saying, “You truly were unfortunate to die, but it was fate. With such affection, how can one transcend? To invite Monk Huiyu to explain Buddhist teachings.” After a day, the nun woke up. When the funeral returned, the spirit spoke again to the crowd, ordering the wife to remarry if she wished but warning her, “Absolutely do not do anything that would disgrace the family. I will not forgive you.” People might suspect that it was because of a minor official, the story goes.

South Fung Magistrate A man from Fujian, whose name is withheld, served as a magistrate in South Fung County. He became infatuated with an official servant named Long Ying. He sent his wife and children back to their hometown and lived alone with Long Ying. The County Magistrate Sun Quan tried to dissuade him, but he refused to listen and eventually secretly took Long Ying and fled. Later, he was transferred to the position of Professor in Hunan, and Long Ying followed him to his new post. Their food, vegetables, and all other provisions were provided by the kitchen. One day, Long Ying brought porridge, which was unusually busy. Wang did not have time to eat it when suddenly, coal dust fell into the bowl. He ordered it to be removed, but Long Ying said, "Just remove the dirty part; why throw it away?" He forced Wang to eat it. Wang became angry and said, "Since you do not consider it a problem, you eat it yourself." Long Ying refused, and Wang became even more furious. Just as a dog passed by in front, he then poured the porridge onto the ground and allowed the dog to eat it. In a short time, the dog vomited black blood and died painfully. Wang questioned the matter, and Long Ying said, "The porridge came from outside; I did not know it would be like this." He ordered the cook to be called, and the cook replied that the porridge was actually provided every day, but Wang had refused it and said it was prepared inside the house. The original porridge was still there and could be verified. They then thoroughly searched the room and found the cooking pot. Long Ying's expression changed, and he was arrested and taken to the court for interrogation. He confessed to having an affair with a guard and intended to poison the master with medicine, and then use up all the family's wealth to marry. When discussing the punishment, since it was not completed, the punishment was reduced, and he was only given a whipping. This can serve as a warning to future generations. It was not a blessing from above; Wang could not escape.
A Woman with Two Heads and Four Arms During the reign of Emperor Ling, a woman from Luoyang was born with two heads and four arms.

They often roost in high trees. At that time, they were involved in banditry with Kang Qi. The matter was eventually exposed. They were ordered to capture a fire monkey. They went to the riverside, where monkeys and apes often roamed. Suddenly, a monkey passed by, and he hated it very much. The next day, they received the same order again. And the day after, it was the same. But the monkey had no feet. They knew there was no way to escape. Seeing Kang Qi coming, he suspected him and wanted to avoid him, but he was tempted by sweet words. He also thought about the ten years they had been bandits together and felt that he should not be betrayed so quickly. As soon as he got close, his right foot was cut off. He could still jump and walk several dozen steps. He found a herb to stop the bleeding and relieve the pain, and used it to wrap the severed part. He walked another hundred steps and collapsed from the pain. Now he has no way to escape death. That year, there was a general amnesty, and he was also released because of the amputation. He still exists, although he no longer appears. But he is now a teacher to the bandits, and the villagers suffer from him. These are the seven events recounted by Sun Ge.

The *Yijian Zhi* Volume Ding, Chapter 44 Sun Wuguo Sun Yu, a man from Zheng, was the nephew of Lady Wang. When he was around eighteen or nineteen years old, he visited his maternal home and met his cousin's daughter, Zhenzhen, by the railing. They had a mutual agreement to become a harmonious couple. After returning home, he often thought of her. One day, someone came to discuss marriage proposals, and his mother asked him about his preferences. He said that Zhenzhen would be sufficient. His mother loved him very much and immediately sought advice from his uncle. The uncle said that among his many sons-in-law, they were all officials, but Sun Yu was the only one who had not yet entered officialdom. If he could pass the local examination and be recommended for the imperial examinations, his daughter would be given to him. Sun Yu was fond of reading and thus became even more diligent and hardworking. He took the examinations in Suzhou twice but failed both times. Zhenzhen also grew up, and it was no longer possible to keep her. Therefore, she was promised to the son of the Shao Bao (a high-ranking official) Zhao Mi. Sun Yu went to Lin'an to visit his uncle and attend a gathering at his maternal uncle's house. Zhenzhen took the opportunity to cry and tell him, "I am already betrothed to someone else. Our relationship cannot continue." Sun Yu did not stay and immediately returned to his hometown in Kunshan. On the road, he met his cousin Ge and invited him to share a boat. He asked him, "Is there such a thing as love sickness, as people say? I have been feeling very weak and unable to rely on myself lately. My intestines are all pulled and cut like an inch-by-inch piece. I will surely die from this." Ge tried to comfort him gently.

And he scolded her, saying, "Young uncle with kind parents, yet showing such attachment without reason. Wouldn't this be laughed at by relatives?" After arriving home, he lodged Gé in an outer room. Yù slept on his mother's bed. In the middle of the night, he got up and called Gé to wake up. "Just as I was about to fall asleep, I heard someone calling 'Brother Wu.' Yù is the fifth. I looked around, and it was indeed Zhēnzhēn." He rushed out of bed, but saw nothing. What omen is this? Gé stayed for ten days and passed through Lin'an. Just as Zhēnzhēn was married into the Zhao family, the next day a combined banquet was held. Suddenly, I saw someone standing beside me. I was startled and said, "Brother Wu, why are you here?" He immediately fell ill. After a month, he recovered. At that time, Yù was already sick, emaciated and thin. He went with his mother to seek medical treatment in Suzhou. Upon arriving at the door, he told his mother, "This illness is most feared to cause vomiting and coughing up blood. If these symptoms appear, he will surely not survive." After finishing the conversation, he suddenly vomited, spitting out several clots of fresh blood and died. On the night when the woman had her vision, Yù was still well. Isn't this a sign that his spirit has already departed? Later, he had wild thoughts, not knowing good from evil. This is even worse. Therefore, I write this to warn. The woman still exists.

**Master of the Office of Destiny** Wang Quan, courtesy name Zizhen, was from Fengxiang Yangping. His father passed the imperial examinations, and his brothers were all jinshi (advanced degree holders). Wang Quan lived in seclusion, enjoying the Dao. One day, while walking in the countryside, he rested in a melon garden. A woman came to beg for melons, and her breasts were full. Wang Quan knew she was no ordinary person. He asked her surname, and she replied, "I am Xiao San娘." Wang Quan took the melons and placed them in a bag to give to her. The woman ate the melons and saved some for him. She said, "Would you like to try some?" Wang Quan took and ate them without hesitation. The woman said, "You are ready to be taught." She mentioned the immortal Hai Chanzi, who was residing there, and said he would guide Wang Quan in his spiritual journey. She promised to take him to meet Hai Chanzi the next day. When they met Hai Chanzi, he said, "You have met me because of a predestined connection." He commanded Wang Quan to kneel and receive the highest teachings. Hai Chanzi taught him the secrets of alchemy and advised him to accumulate merit through good deeds. Wang Quan then returned home to inform his mother, sent his wife away, and traveled to famous mountains. At one time, high-ranking officials recommended his wisdom, and he was posthumously granted the title "Chongxi Chushi." In the third year of the Yuanfu era (1099), he revisited Mount Mao. Prior to this, a stone cave on the mountain suddenly opened.

The *Zhenhao* refers to the convenient gate of the Huayang Celestial Abode. It has been closed for a thousand years. Moreover, sweet dew descended. The Taoist priest Liu Hunkang said, "There must be something unusual." Later, Quan came to receive the *Shangqing* talisman. That night, celestial music was heard above the empty floating clouds. He stayed for more than a year. During the day, he dreamed of two celestial beings accompanied by hundreds of yellow-robed attendants in carriages. They were greeted by a white tiger, and he rode the tiger, climbing dangerous and perilous paths. They entered the stone cave through the central peak, passing through the previously opened gate. Looking around, he saw a golden hall and jade chamber. Two green-clad children entered to report. He saw Master Mao and paid respects twice. The master inquired about his well-being with great kindness and said, "The Emperor has appointed you as the Vice Minister of Life and Death in the Huayang Celestial Abode." He then bestowed a golden ruler and returned. Upon waking, he bid farewell to Liu Hunkang and said, "My fate is coming to an end. And something has been bestowed upon me. I shall depart from here." He descended to meet the Taoist priest Ge Chong and said, "I dare to entrust my death to you." He prophesied that he would pass away on August 17th. At the appointed time, he dressed in formal attire, sat upright, and died. It was during the reign of Emperor Huizong of Song. He was about sixty-one years old.

Liu Shiyi Liu Shiyi, having been relieved of his post as the Tongpan of Muzhou and returning to the capital, moored his boat and stayed overnight in Si Prefecture. There he met a beggar, about seventeen or eighteen years old, with bright eyes and red lips, whose appearance was striking. He asked the beggar, "What do you sell?" The beggar replied, "I sell beans; each grain costs one hundred and twenty coins." Liu said, "I just lack money. I only have the cotton coat I wear; would it suffice as payment?" The beggar said, "Certainly. Let me fetch some beans." Using a piece of paper, he rubbed it between his two breasts, and suddenly, several black beans appeared. He took one and gave it to Liu, then threw the rest into the Bian River. Liu wanted to swallow the bean but was told, "Not yet." The beggar then reached into his armpit and pulled out several green beans, taking one and giving it to Liu before throwing the rest away. Liu swallowed both beans and gave the beggar the coat, who laughed and did not take it. Liu had been suffering from a disease caused by a parasite and could not eat. Immediately after swallowing the beans, he could eat as before and even more. Later, his face turned red like cinnabar, but the illness recurred once a year, causing him to drink several dou of water when thirsty. He felt two beans in his abdomen as large as dates. The beggar also agreed to meet again in a certain year in Huai West, but Liu did not know what it meant. The above two stories are recorded in the *Fuxiu Ji*.

A Horse Named Jiang Ji In the seventh year of the Ganda period (1167 AD), there was a severe famine in Jiangxi and Hunan provinces, with conditions being particularly dire. Many people died of hunger. In the following spring, the prefecture of Shaoshan sent an official named Jiang Ji to the market area near Mount Heng to purchase mirabilite and other items, traveling in armor and on horseback. Along the way, they trampled on people's wheat fields, and sometimes fed the horse with rice. On February 27th, they arrived at a place called Liugang within the jurisdiction of Mount Heng. Suddenly, the sky became extremely dark, making it impossible to distinguish people and objects. There was a loud thunderclap, and after a long time, the sky cleared up. Jiang Ji and his horse both fell to the ground and died. The prefecture of Shaoshan reported the incident to the transport office, and the transport judge Chen Conggu posted notices along the way to warn others.

In the year 1169 during the reign of Emperor Xiaozong of Song, a scholar from Wuyuan named Wang Sheng passed through Yixing, a city in the state of Changzhou. He was a guest at the residence of Zhou, a high-ranking official. On the evening of the last month of winter, a woman appeared from outside. She was dressed entirely in black clothes and had two braids in her hair. Her appearance was exceptionally beautiful. In her hands, she held a lacquered tray, which contained fruits and delicacies, and a silver cup filled with wine. She walked slowly towards Wang Sheng and said, "Lady, because it is cold and the night is long, thinking of the gentleman sitting alone, I have come to bring you wine." Wang Sheng was both pleased and suspicious. He wondered if it was not some mischievous person in the house who wanted to test him. However, her attire seemed too ancient to be fashionable. Both possibilities were suspicious, so he did not raise his head and did not drink. The woman said, "This wine is specifically for the gentleman. What do you have to be afraid of?" She repeated her words several times, and Wang Sheng finally drank, but only halfway through. The woman then took the fruit and ate it freely, and she laughed and joked, expressing affection. Wang Sheng began to feel ashamed and afraid, and he threw down the wine and walked out. After a while, he returned, but saw nothing. The next night, she came again as before, and this happened three times. Wang Sheng could no longer bear it and told Zhou everything he had seen. Zhou said, "I have been searching for the silver cup but have not found it. I was just about to blame the servants. Could this be a ghost?" He ordered a thorough search of the hidden places in the house. They searched until they reached the wine room, where they found an ancient tripod, very plain, with wooden containers inside. Zhou said, "It must be this object." They lifted its belly and looked at it, and it turned out to be made during the Tang dynasty's Gaofeng period (666-674). Zhou immediately broke it. From then on, the woman no longer appeared. Wang Sheng was relieved.

In a village temple in a certain county of Yuanzhou, there lived ten or so monks. Near the temple, there was a scholar who was skilled in sorcery and had the power to control the fate of the monks. Whenever the monks came and went, he would always inform them. If he received offerings from the monks, he would split them evenly; otherwise, they would suffer misfortune, even chickens and dogs were not allowed. In the thirtieth year of the Shaoxing era, a guest monk passed by in the morning, just as he was unloading his belongings.
