Just then, a villager from a neighboring village died, and he quickly called for a monk to recite scriptures and perform the funeral rites. At that time, all the monks in the temple had left, only this guest monk went. He received seven hundred coins and returned. Later, when the other monks returned and learned of the matter, they looked at each other in astonishment. By evening, they all left, and the guest monk was completely unaware. He was very hungry, so he went into the kitchen to get food and ate it. Then he locked the three doors himself, climbed up to the Buddha hall, and sat at the feet of the Buddha, covering his head with his robe. He recited the *Lengyan* spell. Midnight had passed, and a sudden clap of thunder sounded, followed by a crack of lightning. The moonlight through the window panes was as bright as day. Soon, he heard the sound of bells and cymbals, as if several strong men were carrying a large piece of wood, trying to lift it up and then put it down. This happened three or four times, and then, as if they had lost their footing and fell, there was silence. At dawn, he went out to look and found four paper figures at the bottom of the stairs. Next to them was a paper coffin. He casually folded them and put them in his bosom. Soon, the other monks arrived and were shocked to see it. They asked him about what he had seen at night, and he told them the whole story. He said, "That person practiced dark magic, but he could not harm others. He must have been harmed by it himself." Let's go together and check, he suggested. Indeed, the scholar was found dead, his body as if cut by a knife. The temple reported this to the county, and the county sent someone to investigate and report back. It was said that Wang Chong, an old man, had told the story.

**The Tree Demon of Deqing** Song Anguo, the governor of the western region of Zhejiang, resided in Huzhou. He practiced the Heavenly Heart Method but did not abandon it. A household in Deqing was troubled by a spirit, and they invited Song to their residence. The ritual was ineffective, and the ghost not only caused trouble but also humiliated Song. Song became furious and went to a nearby village temple. He fasted and abstained for seven days, writing talismans and reciting incantations with great concentration. Then, he drew his sword and went bareheaded to a large tree behind the house. He performed the Yu-step ritual, walking in a specific pattern. Suddenly, a thunderbolt struck from the sky. The tree, which was several meters tall and had a circumference of ten meters, split into two from the top to the root. There were several more thunderclaps, and the branches and trunk, no matter how large or small, all split like counting rods, lying on the ground in a pile. The monster was completely eradicated.

A Woman with Two Heads and Four Arms In the Baoguo Temple of Deqing County, Huzhou, there once was a signatory official named Guo. The coffin of his deceased daughter was placed in a temple room, and it began to interact with people, causing great mischief. After the burial was relocated, the supernatural disturbances continued as before. The temple locked the three-room house, and no one dared to stay there. After a long time, the imperial guard sent soldiers to the outskirts to herd horses. A member of the imperial family named Zhao Da visited the temple to borrow a room for buying wine. The monk said, "There is no vacant room." Only those three rooms, because of the ghost, were not desired by people. But it is not suitable for your residence, Zhao replied. "That is enough for me. I have my own way of dealing with it." On the same day, he opened the door, connected the three rooms into one, set up a bed in the center, and slept with a sword as a pillow. Just as the night watch was starting, the woman suddenly appeared, wearing beautiful and fresh clothes, standing in front of Zhao. Zhao asked, "Who are you? Why are you here?" She smiled but did not speak. He asked her repeatedly, but she gave no response. Zhao quickly got up and embraced her. She was very embarrassed and fearful, as if trying to leave. In a moment, like smoke and mist, she dispersed. There was nothing in his arms. Since then, it was peaceful. Zhao lived there for over ten years, and no longer saw anything.

Zhenjiang Wine Cellar Ouyang Xiu, a descendant of the officials in charge of the wine cellar at Zhenjiang, once served as the wine official there. Because the wine cellar was in a dilapidated state, he bought several houses from the people and demolished and rebuilt them there. At that time, Mr. Wang from Changsha was summoned and passed through Zhenjiang. He was an expert in dealing with ghosts and demons, without needing talismanic water or incantations, as he could perceive them silently. Ouyang Xiu met him at the government office and went to visit him. He invited him to the newly built residence and prepared a meal for him. Ouyang Xiu asked, "Are there any ghosts here?" Mr. Wang replied, "There are two ghosts. One died by fire, and the other hanged himself. However, they are all afraid of you and dare not come out. But there is a snake that died unjustly, and I do not know the reason. I will let you see its form." Those who heard were terrified. After the meal, Mr. Wang told the host to look at the wall. When they looked, the snake's shadow was as large as a beam, ten feet long, moving from east to west. Then they carefully questioned the officials, and they replied, "One was a wine maker who fell into the fire while steaming wine, and the other was a warehouse supervisor who hanged himself after stealing official money. But they could not remember the snake's story." These three events are all from Ouyang Xiu's eloquent accounts, and this is his father's story.

**胡教授母** The mother of Professor Hu from Chuzhou. She died at the age of ninety. Two days before her death, someone came to speak with her and instructed her to tell the world: "The great drum does not sound, the deep water does not flow. In June, frost falls, reeds sink and stones float. The cold spring is separated, a high mountain is just a small hill." She also said that the underworld sends messengers everywhere to inform the world. Uprightness is the highest virtue; do not steal land, money, or goods. Seeing and dealing with such matters. There are several more words, but they are considered too sensitive to be recorded. This occurred in the year 8 of the Gandao era. It was said by He De Yang. **戴世荣** Wu Yi Lang Dai Shirong, a wealthy man from Xincheng, Jianchang. His residence was very grand and magnificent. In the thirty-second year of the Shaoxing era, strange and ominous events began to occur in his home. Whenever the door was opened, cups, trays, and dishes were often seen arranged on the ground, with dogs standing around them. Fires frequently broke out in the chests, burning half of the clothing and bedding, yet the chests remained undamaged. His wife, Zhao, felt as if someone was breaking several clay pots near her bed, causing the entire room to shake and a thick cloud of dust to rise. Soon after, she fell ill. Sometimes, bricks, stones, and other objects would be thrown from the air, continuously hitting the doors, windows, and pillars, producing a sound that was both loud and pleasant. Upon examination, the marks left by these impacts were clearly visible, resembling the size of chestnuts. A doctor named Huang Tongli arrived with medicine, but the object seized the medicine and threw it away. The doctor fled in panic, and a flying stone struck his head, killing him instantly. A shaman named Tang Faxian performed rituals, but a stone hit his ankle, forcing him to crawl away. A monk named Zhi Tong chanted the Filth Removal Sutra and set up an altar, but before he could finish, something struck him, causing him to flee.

Shi Rong encountered several dou of wet sand, which blocked his head and neck, almost to the point of being unable to avoid disaster. Relatives who came to inquire about his illness were worried about potential harm and damage, so they all walked facing the wall. A hundred kinds of rituals and offerings had no small effect. Zhaoshi used the talisman he received from Zhang Tian Shi, placed it on the canopy, tore it, and threw it to the ground, but it still did not rise. Shi Rong had a small boil on his foot and encountered strange occurrences even more severely. He then took fishing nets several feet off the ground and spread them throughout the room to avoid the harm of thrown stones. Still, things were thrown incessantly below the nets. Nearby homes within twenty to thirty li had no bowls, plates, or pottery left, and no one knew why they had been lost. It was probably the things struck day and night. Shi Rong's illness became severe, and he saw a strange object standing in the courtyard—a creature with a horse's head and red hair, over ten feet long. In a moment, the head gradually lowered, and it let out a loud roar, flying away into the air. Not many days later, the boil burst, and he died. The family then declined. Shi Rong, although the son of a wealthy family, was known in the village as a good person. He could not understand why such strange occurrences happened. This was recounted by Zhaoshi's elder brother, Shan Zai.

The Snake in the Fields of Jingxi** In the Hezhong Prefecture, there was an old veteran named Hu De. When he was young, he went to Jingxi to capture bandits. One day, while passing through the countryside, he encountered a large snake in the wheat fields. The snake raised its head and moved swiftly, flattening the wheat as it went. Several soldiers grabbed spears and killed it. The snake was about a zhang long and was divided into more than ten pieces, each of which was carried away. Hu De took the snake's head and hung it on his spear. He did not travel far when a village woman saw him and embraced him, crying, "Who made my child go out lightly to die quickly?" She led her family to pull Hu De to their home, where they mourned and complained. They also bought the snake's head to bury it. Another guest, on the Dragon Boat Festival, entered a farmer's house to ask for water. When the farmers were all out cutting wheat, he just stood there for a moment. He heard a sound of "kakak" from the side of the house and rushed to see. There was a snake sitting on the roof, hanging its head between the eaves, and dropping blood into a basin. The guest knew it was definitely poisonous and silently thought to himself that he should remove the harm for people. So he took all the blood and put it into the family's pickle jar. He then went to a neighboring inn to wait. After a long time, the family's elders and children returned carrying wheat. They were all thirsty and tired and rushed to the kitchen to drink the pickle juice. The guest finished eating and passed by the door again. Then the entire household died. My father-in-law, who was a teacher in Hezhong, said that Hu De was a gatekeeper and told this story. **The Fish in the Well of Jianchang** On the fifth day of the seventh month in the year of Wuzi during the Dazhao era, a large well in front of the Jianchang Military District's post station had water that smelled so foul that it was undrinkable for several days. The residents dug and cleaned the well and found a fish about three fingers long. The fish resembled a carp but had red stripes on its eyes that were golden in color. It had two small, hard horns on its head. The people stored the fish in a large bucket and bought paper money to send it to the river. By evening, strong winds and heavy rain blew down countless trees. Everyone suspected that it was a dragon.

The Chancellor of the Imperial Household, Shi Min, completed his term as the governor of Jiankang and returned to his former residence at Yancha Bridge in Lin'an. He left behind only one of his attendants, a Yuhou. Once, they went out together to the market, where they encountered a vendor selling a type of duck that greatly resembled the former cook named Wang Li. The Yuhou also remarked that there was no significant difference. At that time, Wang Li had been dead for a year. Shi Min, while in office, had still given money to help with his burial. Suddenly, Wang Li appeared before him and bowed. He said, "I met you unexpectedly on your mission and did not have time to send a formal greeting. I followed you back and presented a duck that remained from a tray." Shi Min asked, "If you are not a human, how can you walk around the capital city during the day?" Wang Li replied, "Since leaving my previous post, I have been here. In modern-day Hangzhou, one-third of the population consists of people like me. We can be officials, monks, Taoists, merchants, or entertainers. We take on all kinds of roles and interact with people just like them. We do not harm anyone; people simply cannot distinguish between us." Shi Min asked, "Is the duck a real animal?" Wang Li replied, "It is also bought from the market. We buy five pairs each day before dawn, bring them to a large workshop, prepare and cook them, and then pay the owner for the cost of firewood and materials. All other vendors do the same. Our daily earnings are sufficient to make a living, but we continue our activities until night."

It was too painful to speak of. With no house to dwell in, they often crouched beneath the meat tables in butchery shops, frequently startled and chased by dogs. Shi found this very distressing, yet there was nothing he could do. Ducks are earthly creatures meant for consumption. Shi gave him two thousand coins to send them away. The next day, he came again with four ducks. From then on, he would come at times. Shi secretly sighed, saying, "I am a human, yet I speak daily with ghosts. Am I not to live long on this earth?" Lì had already known this. He stepped forward and said, "You need not doubt me. Have you not seen your family’s elderly nurse?" He took out two small white stones from his sleeve and gave them to Shi, saying, "Please put them into the fire. You will know my words are not empty." This old woman was the nurse of Shi’s eldest son, having lived in the household for thirty years. Shi, entering into the jest, said, "What would you say if outsiders claimed you were a ghost?" The woman replied, "A sixty-year-old servant like me is truly fit to be a ghost." Though she was extremely angry, she showed no sign of fear. At that moment, the concubine was ironing clothes nearby. Shi tested by throwing the stone into the cauldron. After a short while, the flame arose. The old woman’s expression immediately grew dull, gradually becoming more pale and faint, like a shadow in ink. Suddenly, all was silent and nothing could be seen. Wang Lì did not come again. I have already recorded the story of Li Ji in the *Bingzhi*, and I had already laughed at how similar the ghost’s trick was. This one, however, was slightly different. This was said by Zhu Chunya, who heard it from Shi’s deputy.

In the early years of the Jiaqing era, scholars from Jianzhou, Li Bi, Wang Jie, and Huang Chong, traveled together to the capital, visiting Xiangguo Temple. At the time, there was a skilled physiognomist who could predict a person's fate using just a few words, and his predictions were as accurate as a deity. The three scholars consulted him, and he said, "Li Bi will become famous and reach the rank of an official. Wang Jie must wait for another examination and will succeed in succession. Huang Chong will be successful after three more examinations, and his rank will be the same as Wang Jie's." Soon after, Li Bi and Wang Jie achieved success as predicted, but Huang Chong wasted time and delayed for exactly nine years before finally passing the preliminary examination again. When he returned to the capital, the physiognomist was still there. Upon seeing Huang Chong, the physiognomist shouted loudly, "Why are you here?" Huang Chong talked about past events and mentioned that Li Bi and Wang Jie had already passed the imperial examination. The physiognomist said, "People come and go like a tapestry; how could I possibly remember. Let's consider your current situation. According to the rules, you should become a high-ranking official, but why do you engage in unjust actions, plotting to steal and kill? You have already incurred severe retribution. You should return home quickly and do not need to take the provincial examination." He then asked how many children Huang Chong had, and Huang Chong replied, "Three." The physiognomist said, "Your fate is also sealed." Huang Chong left in a gloomy mood, and indeed, he failed the examination and returned home. Within a year, he died.

Three of Chong's sons died young. His wife remarried, and thus his descendants were extinguished. Initially, after Chong's mother had passed away, his father, who was over sixty years old, bought a concubine who was already pregnant. On the verge of childbirth, Chong was studying in the prefectural school. The father and Chong's younger brother discussed the situation: "Suddenly in old age, there is this." The father felt very ashamed and asked, "Now, shall we not raise it? Or give it to someone? Otherwise, let's raise it and wait until it grows up. What about letting it become a monk?" The younger brother replied, "This is also a common occurrence. Only as the master wishes. It is better to raise it and let it live." When the elder brother returns, there must be a way to handle it. The concubine then gave birth to a boy. Just send a message to Chong. Chong immediately returned, bowing to his father in the hall. The father informed him of the previous events and ordered to bring out the infant. At the midpoint of autumn, in Fujian, every household makes wine. They drew water to fill several large buckets and placed them in the courtyard to test for leaks. Chong reached out his hand to take the child and directly threw it into the bucket, drowning it. The father wiped away his tears and did nothing more. The Huang family's wealth was slightly abundant, and Chong feared that the child would grow up and would surely demand division of the property. Therefore, the situation ended like this. It is appropriate that he suffered such a fate and his descendants were extinguished. Li Bi served as a Cao Feng Lang and Doctor of the Imperial Academy. Weng Jie served as a Cheng Yi Lang and the Deputy Prefect of Taizhou. The diviner can truly be considered a master.

**Chen Tongpan's Daughter** Chen Zihui, from Xinghua, was awaiting the vacancy of the Southern Xiong County Magistrate. He lived in the countryside. One summer night, his family gathered for a drink. His wife looked at the eldest daughter and asked her to play music. The music was out of tune, so he angrily scolded and sent her away. The daughter did not come out again. After the drinking session, they found her in an empty room at the back of the house. She was holding a needle and was stupid, unaware of what was happening. They took her back to bed, but she was already dead. Although her breath had stopped, her heart was still slightly warm. Doctors and shamans tried to save her but failed. She languished for one hundred twenty days. They heard that there was a Taoist in Quanzhou who was skilled in holding or maintaining the law. He was summoned and came. First, he used a magical seal to stamp her body, then he summoned her soul. He said she was recorded by the Great Temple in Zhangzhou. Two nights later, she suddenly moaned and made a sound. By dawn, she could open her eyes a bit. The next day, she bent her right foot and cried out in pain. When they looked at it, one finger was broken and bleeding. At noon, she could open her eyes a bit more. The next day, she began to say that her maternal grandfather had called her. Her maternal family was in Zhangzhou, but she had never met him before. However, she described his house very accurately. She said that her maternal grandfather had married her to the Great King as a concubine.

Received as a dowry, two pairs of gold and silver hairpins. One pair wrapped around the arm. Ten sheets of silver. A thousand strings of coins. Silk fabrics beyond count. Twenty pigs and twenty sheep each. Several dozen barrels of wine. I entered the royal palace. The great king showed great kindness. Every day, meals were taken with three people sitting together. He also ordered the training of the young maids in music. I stayed for a long time. Outside, it was reported that my family wanted to take me back. He did not want to return. The king also sent them away. In the two days, it was reported that someone held official documents. The demand was very strict. The king became angry. He sent soldiers to resist them. The envoy was about to set the palace on fire. The flames reached my entire body. The king wanted to be close but could not. The officials dragged me out. The king ordered a sedan chair to send me off. The sedan chair bearers were afraid and ran away. I had no choice but to walk alone. The mountain road was dangerous and rugged. My waist and thighs were both exhausted. After passing the mountain pass, a small stone injured my foot. I fell to the ground for a while. I still feel pain that is unbearable. From then on, my spirit was as good as before. But every time it rained, my lower abdomen would definitely hurt. Later, I married Di Gong, a man named Guo. In the year Xin You, we were married in Nanxiong Prefecture.

### Four-Eyed Dog In Jianyang, Huang Dewan bought a dog that was pure black, with two white spots under its eyes that resembled eyes. Because of this, he called it "Four Eyes." After three years, the farm servant Chen Liu came to report that the master's many dogs had often bitten and killed sheep. Huang verified this and found it to be true. The household had a total of six dogs, and Huang ordered that they all be killed, with none left behind to teach each other. Five dogs were killed, but only "Four Eyes" escaped. After two nights, the dog came to Huang's wife in a dream and said, "The officials want to kill all the dogs. I am truly innocent. I never bit sheep in my life. I only guarded the back door at night against thieves. I wish to be spared from death." The wife told Huang about the dream. The next day, Huang investigated further and found that "Four Eyes" indeed did not mix with its kind. He wanted to spare it, and it had already returned. Since then, it truly stayed under the back wall. It still lived for another seven years. ### Teacher Yi's Debt from a Past Life In Jianyang, the medical monk Teacher Yi had a habit of accumulating debt. He once borrowed ten thousand coins from the county clerk Liu He, but despite repeated requests, he refused to repay it. Liu became angry and said, "I will let you pay this debt in your next life." From then on, Teacher Yi stopped talking about it. Five years later, Teacher Yi died. Two years after that, Liu's mother dreamed that Teacher Yi came, as he usually did, and bowed to her, saying, "I once owed you ten thousand coins. Today I am here to return it." Then he left. The mother woke up and told Liu about the dream. "What does this mean?" Liu asked. The next morning, the farm servant came to report that the white cow had given birth to a calf last night at three in the morning.

Zhang One's Debt Repayment A villager from Jianyang named Zhang One borrowed two thousand coins from Xiong Si Lang. The principal and interest doubled. After a year, Zhang refused to repay. Xiong, tired of demanding repayment, said to him, "No longer calculate the interest. Just return the principal, can you?" Zhang felt ashamed and apologized, giving him a small amount. In the end, he still owed eight hundred coins. Xiong did not demand it back. Three years later, Zhang died. Four years after his death, Xiong dreamed that Zhang came to repay him with eight hundred coins, placing them on the ground. They were all small coins. Zhang left them there and sat with Xiong, drinking tea before leaving. Upon waking, Xiong was telling his wife about the dream when a servant knocked on the door and said, "The ox gave birth to a very large calf. We urgently need wine for a blessing." Xiong was very happy. Just ten days later, the calf died suddenly. A neighbor butcher came to buy it. Xiong asked for two thousand coins. The butcher laughed and said, "How could it be worth that much? After peeling and selling everything, how could it not reach this amount? But only the name of selling the ox remains. First, offer wine and a cup of porridge to the village headman, then offer to the four neighbors, and then take the profit. Now there are only eight hundred coins. Please give them to me, or it's over." The butcher unbuckled the bag from his waist and threw it on the ground, exactly where Zhang placed the coins in his dream. Clearly, they were small coins. Xiong finally understood the previous events and hurriedly gave them to the butcher.

A Woman's Concubine In the fifth month of the year Jiazi during the Shaoxing era, there was a great flood in Jiangsu, Zhejiang, and Fujian provinces. Hundreds of families in Huangting Town, Chongnan County, all fled and climbed up to Koubing Temple to take refuge. The entrance, corridors, halls, and temples were all filled. Wu Hui from Jianyang married Lan Shi from Huangting. On the Dragon Boat Festival, his wife returned to her parents' home, coinciding with a flood disaster. Together with his concubine, they took shelter in the bell tower of the temple with her parents. When they woke up, they heard the rooster crowing and found themselves in a pine forest on the mountain. They had no idea how they got there. By morning, they observed that it turned out to be the hill behind the temple. The concubine was also there, but her parents and family were not seen. Out of the thousand people who came to the temple, only one in ten survived. It seemed as if someone in the void had sent them out. The temple buildings were all swept away by the water, and the ground itself was no longer there.

In the twelfth month of the twenty-first year of the Shaoxing era (1151 AD), the governor of Jiankang, Wang Shendao, sent a fast runner to Mount Mao's Yuanfu Palace with a very fast return deadline. The runner returned to the middle of the journey and encountered a very cold night. Seeing a fire lit in the courtyard of a house at the foot of the mountain, he hurriedly went to it. Upon arrival, he found seven or eight villagers surrounding and guarding a corpse, saying, "This person hanged himself in this room. We, the village officials and neighbors, are afraid the body will be damaged by insects and rodents, so we are guarding it together until the magistrate of Jurong arrives." Some were sitting, and some were sleeping. The runner did not dare to stay long and left alone. The moonlight was dim, and as he walked forward, someone followed him. He spoke to the person, and they responded. After walking about two miles, he came across a ditch and leaped over it. The person following him could not jump and fell into the ditch. The sound was loud, and the runner turned back to help him. By the time he returned, the person was dead. He ran to a nearby house and knocked on the door, telling the owner, "I want to return to the capital. There is someone from the village guarding the corpse who followed me. He fell into a ditch and seems to be in danger. Please tell the others to come and see." The owner of the house went with a lantern and saw several people running in panic, saying they had lost the corpse. Upon hearing this, they followed the owner. They found the body and carried it back to the room, placing it in the noose. The next day, the magistrate of Jurong, Xiong Ruonan, arrived. It turned out that a strong spirit had attached itself to the corpse with the intent to cause harm. The runner was indeed in great danger.

Jing Shan Zhuang Bronze Jar When the Qin family was in power, the landholdings in Jinling were very wealthy. There was an estate called Yongning, which was presided over by Liu Wen, a Baoyi Lang. Another estate called Jing Shan Zhuang was presided over by someone named Chen. In the year Ren Shen of the Shaoxing era, Liu Wen visited Chen's estate on official business and stayed overnight. In the evening, he went to the toilet and saw a group of pigs circling a jar and drinking rice water. The jar was rubbed by the pigs, slightly revealing a yellow color. When tapped, it was copper. He returned to visit Chen and said, "Recently, the pigs had broken several clay jars or wooden troughs used for feeding. Last year, a farmer found this in the ground. I obtained it with five dou of rice." The jar's quality is hard and heavy, perhaps it can last a long time. Liu Wen said, "I want to buy it and take it to Juzhou. Can it be recast into a decorative object?" Chen replied, "It's a trivial matter." Liu Wen paid two bolts of silk and ordered his servant to take it back. The jar was polished and cleaned, shining brilliantly. That year, he brought the rent to the Qin mansion and tried to present it to the Chancellor. The Chancellor looked at it and realized it was indeed gold, made from natural gold from the Han era, weighing twenty-four catties. He immediately reported it to the Imperial Treasury and generously rewarded Liu Wen with money and silk.

A Dog from the Qian Family Qian Qi, while serving as the commander of the Jiankang military, had four men in his unit who were skilled thieves. They stole clothes during the day and chickens and dogs at night, not a single day passing without them committing theft. Qian ordered the squad leader to warn them and pardoned their past offenses, saying, "Do not repeat the offense." Qian's family had a dog with a black body and white feet, named Silver Hoof. The dog would follow commands and bow down, making it very lovable. Suddenly, the dog went missing. Qian posted a notice to offer a reward for its return, and after two days or so, an old soldier reported that the four thieves had just killed the dog and were eating it. Qian immediately sent someone to verify the situation. The dog was already cooked, but its fur and skin were still intact. Qian ordered the military officer to oversee the burial and also stamped the ground with ashes to prevent it from being stolen. He thoroughly investigated the details and found that indeed, the four had conspired together. Two of them used a rope and hook to catch the dog near the eastern gate of the city. Qian called out to the squad leader and rebuked him, still considering it a minor matter and instructing him not to punish severely. The squad leader flogged the accomplices fifty times on the back and the main thief was flogged one hundred times. Within ten days, those who were flogged all died. One night, inside Qian's gate, they heard the sound of a dog scratching, which was very similar to Silver Hoof. The family all laughed and said, "Is it a ghost dog?" When called, it responded. When the door was opened, it wagged its tail and entered, biting clothes and bowing and jumping. Its joy and happiness were beyond description. The next day, they checked the burial site. The ash stamp was still intact, and the soil was also not disturbed, but the hole was empty. They suspected that the dog they killed might belong to another household. They then described the dog's appearance in detail and posted notices throughout the area, offering a reward for recognition, but no one came forward to claim the reward. They finally realized that this was a consequence of their past misdeeds. Silver Hoof lived for another ten years before dying.

The Weihuai Temple Deity In Gaizhu Village, twenty miles from Jianyang County, there is a Weihuai Temple, renowned for its miraculous responses. When Chen Xiugong was a young man, he lived in extreme poverty. His friends encouraged him to take part in the local imperial examination. Although he went, he was unwilling. Passing by the temple, he entered to pay respects and cast yarrow stalks, saying, "I am poor. Today, without spending thousands, I cannot proceed. I have no way to obtain the necessary funds. I dare to make a decision based on the response from the Weihuai Lord." He cast the yarrow stalks three times, and all three times the result was negative. His unhappiness increased. His companions forced him to continue. After entering the city, he had a dream in which someone told him that the Weihuai Lord from Gaizhu Village was coming to meet him. He was invited out to meet the deity, and they greeted each other with the proper etiquette. The deity stood up and thanked him, saying, "You considered me at the time. I was just returning from a feast at Anshan. My wife did not properly verify the situation and mistakenly cast three negative outcomes. If you had followed through, you would have passed the imperial examination and become a chancellor." He woke up in shock. Later, he went to the temple in a state of fasting and secrecy to pray, and he received favorable omens, as predicted. He was indeed recommended for the imperial examination, and the following year, he passed the imperial examination and became a chancellor during the Xining period.

### 灵泉鬼魋 King Wang Tian Gong, a native of Fugan Jianyang, lived within the county boundary of Lingquan Temple. Before the temple, there was a field, and in the field, there was a mound. On the mound stood over ten large trees, each with a diameter of several feet, entwined with vines. The residents regarded these trees as ghostly thorns, and the place was shrouded in a mysterious and solemn atmosphere. Some people offered sacrifices to them during festivals. King Wang intended to cut down the trees for firewood and called upon the field servants to bring axes. All the servants were too afraid to go. Enraged, King Wang threatened to beat them, and they had no choice but to proceed. They only chopped a few strokes when blood began to flow from the trees. Frightened, the servants stopped and returned to report the incident. King Wang beat the two leaders, saying, "It was just the sap of the old tree; how could it be blood?" The group of servants realized they could not avoid it and bought paper money to burn as an offering. They cut the trees with disheveled hair, calling out each time they swung their axes, "King Fugan made me chop." They finally cleared the forest and obtained three thousand bundles of firewood. It was the thirteenth year of the Shaoxing era (1143 AD). After a month, King Wang developed a boil on his back and claimed to have seen a supernatural object. After his death, the supernatural presence still did not leave. The people planted trees in another place to appease it. Now, the area has grown into a lush forest, and the supernatural presence finally ceased. ### 鱼病豆疮 The magistrate of Lizhui County, Huang Dewan, patrolled to Gaochun Town and saw several fishermen mooring their boats along the shore. The fishermen had no nets and looked troubled. He asked them why, and they replied that this year, the yellow catfish had been afflicted by an epidemic, all suffering from boils. For several days, they had not caught a single fish. Huang ordered them to fetch some fish to verify the claim. When they pulled up the nets, they found several fish with boils, which were indeed suffering from the same ailment as humans. Some fish had boils all over their bodies, while others had them on their heads, tails, or around their mouths. After over ten days, the fish recovered, but the locals feared the fish might be poisonous and dared not eat them.

The Chilong of Shiji Lake In the Shiji Lake of Lizhui County, there is a shallow area with an official paddy field stretching eighty-four li, covering a thousand acres, known as the Yongfeng Paddy Field. Since the Zhenghe era, it has been granted to the families of three high-ranking officials: Cai, Han, and Qin. In the twenty-third year of the Shaoxing era, in the fourth month, it was destroyed by the river. The imperial court ordered the mobilization of thirty thousand people from four prefectures in the east of the Yangtze River to repair it. At that time, the Qin family was in power, and the prefectures and counties carried out the orders with great diligence. In the following year, on the twelfth day of the fourth month at noon, suddenly a huge creature appeared, floating down from the Xuan River, causing waves and covering the river. It raised its head and swam leisurely, resembling a dragon or a crocodile with horns. The old and weak villagers gathered on both banks, shouting and clamoring, competing to bring nets, baskets, and other fishing tools to catch fish along the shore. The county magistrate Huang Dewan happened to be overseeing the work and saw it. He asked the people, and they all said it was a chilong. Some said it appeared once a year, others every two years, or every three to five years, but it would definitely appear once. Its body was covered with sweet and pungent mucus, so the fish followed and ate it. However, when it approached the shore, the fishermen caught nothing less than a hundred catties. On that day, the creature passed through Danyang Lake and left. By the end of the year, the water of Shiji Lake converged, and boats could not pass. On the night of the full moon, another chilong moved from the lake to Danyang, making a sound like thunder. The ice cracked open more than twelve feet, and the waves were high. The broken ice did not close for two days, and it was then known that the embankment had been breached by the creature.

陈才辅 在建炎末期，建州的叛贼范汝为、叶铁、叶亮发动叛乱。建阳的士人陈才辅召集乡兵，杀死了叶铁的父母和妻子。叛贼的势力因此更加猖獗。绍兴元年，他们占领了郡城。朝廷任命提举詹时升，派遣使者谢向，共同进行招安。群盗都听从了命令，唯独叶铁不肯投降，他说：“除非报了陈才辅的仇，否则我不会出来。”詹时升为此设立了重赏，悬赏捉拿陈才辅。叶铁选了三十名监守人，给了他们一千钱，并严厉警告他们说：“如果让他逃跑了，你们都要被斩首。”叶铁计划第二天邀请使者，并与各路首领举行盛大的聚会，以实现他的复仇计划。监守人用粗绳绑住陈才辅的脚，将他倒挂在梁上，用大竹篾绑住他的手，周围布满了剑戟，刀刃距离他只有几尺。一把非常锋利的刀被放在他身边。到了二更时分，所有人都醉了。陈才辅默默地祈祷：“我陈才辅原本心怀忠诚和孝顺，为国家和人民，我的老母亲还在家中，怎能让我遭受这样的屠害？如果神明有知，愿这些人都能熟睡，刀子自己靠近，帮我解开绳索，让我逃脱。”过了很久，刀果然自己靠近，仿佛有神灵推拥。陈才辅用手将竹篾折断，双手一旦解脱，便逐渐挣脱了束缚，所有的绳索都被割断。他握着刀向门口走去。一个人在睡梦中问道：“谁在开门？”陈才辅回答说：“是我。”那人不知道是陈才辅，说：“不要让贼跑了。”

Chen said, "Such binding is not worth worrying about." By the time he had gone out, it was already the third watch of the night. He walked through a back alley for about one li. He heard shouts from the other side, "The thief has escaped." Chen became increasingly窘迫. He looked at a ditch by the roadside, where bamboo was densely overgrown, and quickly hid there. Suddenly, hundreds of torches were lit, and the search was almost everywhere. Even in the ditch, spears and swords were thrown down, but luckily he was unharmed. The people said they would definitely return to Jianyang or head towards Jianpu, so it was advisable to divide into two groups to intercept. Chen did not dare to choose a direct route, but twisted and turned through the thick forest. The next day, he arrived at the border of Gutan in Fuzhou. He sold his knife and got money to buy food, then went directly to Quanzhou to visit his sister's husband, Scholar Huang. After eight days, ten soldiers arrived with a letter from Master Zhan, and Chen was captured again. Knowing he could not escape, Chen quickly broke his own nose and covered himself in blood, pretending to be about to die. The soldiers blamed each other, saying, "How could we have let it come to this?" They carried him to an inn, thinking he was exhausted and weak, and no longer guarded him. Three days later, Scholar Huang came to visit. Coincidentally, a tea merchant held a banquet, inviting Huang and the ten soldiers. The merchant's place was a bit far away, so only seven went to attend, leaving three to guard. Chen silently prayed as he had before. The three soldiers all drank the wine served and got drunk. One sat in front of the house, one blew fire in the kitchen, and one washed vegetables by the water. Taking advantage of the moment, Chen took a stick and struck, killing them immediately. He fled south to Zhangzhou and finally escaped. The next year, General Han Qiu pacified the bandits, and Chen became an official due to his previous achievements.

Zhang Qintong Zhang Yongnian lived in the capital. It was late winter, and it was snowing heavily. The family was having a feast. He sent a young servant named Qintong to carry sugar-coated crabs and sea delicacies to relatives within three miles. The young boy was agile and not afraid of hard work. He went back and forth several times in the snow. His feet froze, turning purple and black. His mother, standing at the door, saw and worried about him. She called him in and had him soak in warm water. The frostbite was severe, and he felt no pain. After a while, all eight fingers fell into the basin. The mother looked at them, and the blood inside the skin had turned to ice. When the warm water was poured over it, it broke due to the shock. This record is all said by Huang Dewan.

Yijian Dingzhi, Volume 64** Andzhou Hairy People During the Xuanhe period, an elderly woman from Andzhou brought two sons. The older one was twenty-six years old, and the younger one was twenty. She claimed that both were conceived for twenty-four months before being born. Their entire bodies were covered in black hair with a lustrous sheen, their eyes were like drops of lacquer, their pale areas were like blue clouds, and their lips were red like cinnabar. Both were skilled in physiognomy. They were once summoned to the capital, rewarded with gold and silk, and then sent back to their hometown. Vice-governor Huang Daru invited them to ask about their fortunes. The older son said, "You can reach the rank of Doctor and govern a state. You will have six sons, half of whom will become officials." Huang called out his eldest son to see him. He asked if he would become an official, and the son shook his head. He then asked how long he would live. The son said, "Bring money," and counted to forty-four coins. He looked at his younger brother and asked, "Is that right?" His brother replied, "Yes," and he gave the coins to Huang. Huang then asked about his eldest daughter, asking if she would receive a fief. She did not answer. He asked how long she would live, and she replied, "Fifty-three coins." He asked about his second daughter, who replied, "Twenty-seven coins." He then predicted the fortunes of several others, all in a similar manner. He did not speak much. Twenty years later, Huang served as a censor, reached the rank of Doctor of the Court, governed Huizhou, and died. He had six sons, three of whom became officials. His eldest son and eldest daughter lived to the ages indicated by the coins they received. His second daughter, in the year Jiazi of the Shaoxing era (1164), accompanied her husband Zhu to serve as a magistrate in Hengshan, where she drowned in the river, exactly at the age of twenty-seven.

Wang Wenqing's Divination Wang Wenqing, a Daoist priest from Jianchang, was renowned not only for his Daoist arts but also for his extraordinary skill in divining people's fortunes. During the Zhenghe and Xuanhe eras, he was invited to Cai Jing's home, where he was shown all of Cai Jing's descendants. Wang merely nodded in agreement to each one. However, he singled out a small child and said, "In the future, the one who will promote Daoism will surely be you." Cai Jing, who loved his youngest son the most, asked again. Wang placed his hand on the child's back and said, "Wait until this child wears gold and purple, and he will rely on your power to restore his official position." Cai Jing was greatly displeased. The child was Chen Jue, also known as Yuan Cheng. His mother was Feng, the niece of Cai Jing, which is why he often entered and exited Cai's mansion. During the Shaoxing era, all the Cai family members were disgraced and dismissed. Chen assisted the main general of Han Qi's military camp and served as a scholar-official in charge of Chizhou. In the year Xin You, Cai Jing's surviving descendants were specially appointed to official positions. The young son mentioned earlier happened to come to Chiyang to handle affairs, and Chen recommended him on his behalf. Chen practiced the Tianxin method and ate vegetarian food, calling himself a true Daoist priest.

The Retribution of Extravagance In the 23rd year of the Shaoxing era, there was a wine official in Zhenjiang. By nature, he was foolish and extravagant, entertaining guests every day, and the food and drink were extremely exquisite and abundant. Even if his colleagues' family provided elaborate hospitality, he still wouldn't eat, insisting on taking food from his own home. He boasted of abundance and competed in extravagance, determined to outdo others through lavishness. Once, he ordered craftsmen to make ten tables, but was dissatisfied with the paint color, so he smashed them with an axe and had them made again. When eating lamb, he only chewed the juice and spat out all the residue. His other habits were similar. The military officer Qi, serving in the army there, tried to advise him every day, but was instead mocked and insulted. Eight years later, Qi accompanied Grand Marshal Liu Qi, the Faithful Uncle, to Lin'an. They visited a nobleman at Yansha Pit. Qi sat in a teahouse when the former wine official walked in, bowed, and greeted him. He wore a patched black cap, a tattered cloth robe, half of which was stained with mud, and walked barefoot. His appearance was unrecognizable. After a long time, Qi remembered him and asked why he was in such a state. The wine official wept and said, "Recently, after completing my term in Jingkou, I went to the capital to seek an official position, but for many years without success. My family is large, and I have never understood how to make a living. My purse is completely empty, and my official documents were also pawned. My wife and children were barely clothed, and I begged for a hundred coins every day, surviving on vegetable porridge." Qi said, "How could you be so destitute?" He replied, "I got money to buy rice but had no money for vegetables. I only picked up discarded vegetable leaves from food stores, and since I had no way to store them, I only put them in my sleeves."

The reason for this state. Qiqi, deeply moved, said, "Do you also remember when we encouraged each other?" He replied, "Heaven has truly tested me. What regret is there to have?" Qiqi invited him to his lodging and offered him sheep and wine. He also gave him several thousand coins to redeem his official position. After that, he was not seen again. There was another man named Guo Xin, who was from the capital. His father was an official in the inner court, and he was the only son. His father loved him very much and sent him to study under Mr. Cai Yuanzhong in Lin'an. Xin rented a room for himself and liked to keep his clothes clean. He would look left and right, and if they were slightly out of order, he would immediately call a craftsman to fix them. He used fine silk and brocade from Wu to make his shoes and socks. If they were slightly soiled, he would throw them away and would not wear them again after washing. Huang Dewan went to take up his post in the year of Gengyin during the Shaoxing era. He just happened to be neighbors with him. He often advised him, saying, "You, being young, do not know worldly affairs. Money is not easy to come by. Although your family is wealthy, it is also not appropriate to waste it. Day after day, later, it might not be easy to continue." Xin did not think so. In the winter of Jiachen during the Longxing era, Huang went to the capital again. He visited his relative Chen Sheng and found Xin there, teaching Chen Sheng's young son. Xin was wearing tattered clothes, his body was cold and shaking, and he received a thousand coins per month. He said his father had died, and there were still three hundred acres of land, and family assets amounted to several thousand strings of cash, all of which were taken over by his stepmother. One night, he left suddenly, and no one knew where he had gone. He did not know where the fields were located, and there was no way to find them. Huang gave him several hundred coins, and he bowed in thanks and left.

Tchen Yuan-yu, a Vice-Chancellor, was from Jianyang. Originally named something else, before he passed the imperial examinations, he had a dream in which he passed through two grand gates, each with golden inscriptions, like those of temples or shrines. One gate was inscribed with "Left Chancellor Chen Xuan," and the other with "Right Chancellor Huang Fu." Upon waking, he immediately changed his name. In the eighth year of Jia You (1063 AD), he ranked second in the imperial examinations. Huang Fu indeed became the Right Chancellor, while Tchen only became a scholar-official of the Longtu Pavilion. In his later years, he told his children, "I rose from a humble background, and throughout my life, I never did anything to deceive my conscience. Now, my position does not match my dream. I often think about why this is so. Many years ago, when I was governor of Hangzhou, a high-ranking official was furious with an old veteran soldier. He ordered the soldier to be brought to the government office and intended to flog him. However, this soldier was over seventy years old, and according to the law, he should not have been flogged. I had already accepted a payment in lieu of punishment, but the high-ranking official sent a mocking letter. I could not help but call the soldier back. The soldier's family fell to their knees, weeping and begging, saying, 'If you flog him, he will die.' I did not listen and immediately ordered the punishment to be carried out. As a result, the soldier died from the flogging, and his body was carried out. To this day, I have never stopped reflecting on and blaming myself for this. Breaking the law out of personal favoritism and killing someone invites censure. This is why I did not reach a high position. You all should be warned. At the time of my dream, the positions of Left and Right Chancellor were merely titles for receiving salaries, but later they became actual positions of governance. It seems that even in the afterlife, it was known."

A Parasitic Worm in the Family of Gao Zong Zhenglang Chen Pu, a native of Jianyang, had a mother surnamed Gao who was over sixty years old. She suffered from a hunger disease, which caused her to feel as if worms were gnawing at her heart whenever it occurred. She would urgently seek food, and only after eating would the sensation subside. This condition persisted for three to four years. She kept a very large cat, which she loved very much, often placing it nearby. Whenever the cat made cute or affectionate calls, she would take fish and meat mixed with rice to feed it. One summer night during the third year of the Jianyan era (1129 AD), she sat out in the open air to cool off. The cat happened to call out, and she ordered someone to get some dried deer meat. She chewed it herself and fed it to the cat, doing this twice. Suddenly, she felt something touch her throat. She reached out and pulled it out, finding it about the size of a thumb. It fell to the ground. She called for a candle to illuminate the object, which was solid. It had a pointed and flat head, resembling a flatfish, and a body like a shrimp, with a shell eight inches long. It gradually grew to the size of two fingers, filled with solid matter. When scraped, the insides were similar to those of a fish. It had eight offspring, born live and wriggling like small eels. No one could identify what the creature was, but it was drawn out by the smell of the dried meat. Gao's illness immediately healed.

Witch Ong Ji Shi In Chong'an County, there was a witch named Ong Ji Shi. She demonstrated efficacy in serving the gods, and rumors spread widely among the villagers. On the first day of September in the year Xinhai during the reign of Emperor Xiaozong, she was just performing a prayer for someone when suddenly she spoke in divine tones, saying, "I must go on a distant journey. Do not casually answer questions or treat illnesses." The witch's family pleaded, "We have relied on your divine power for generations. We have been diligent in our worship and never neglected our duties. We do not know why we are being abandoned. We have asked repeatedly." She then said, "Foreign invaders are coming from the south. The Heavenly Emperor has commanded all city gods and village shrines to lead their troops to guard the river. Therefore, I must go." Someone asked, "When can you return?" She replied, "It is uncertain. Likely around the Winter Solstice." From then on, there was no further communication. Once, a wealthy man fell ill and strongly invited the witch. He performed a strict and clean ritual prayer, throwing the divining calabash a hundred times, but still received no response. On the first day of December, she spoke again, saying, "I have already killed the foreign king. All the gods along the way have been sent back. From that day on, the divine responses returned to normal."

The Pine Tree at Chen's Ancestral Tomb In Jianyang, there was a man named Chen Pu. Beside his ancestral tomb stood a very large pine tree. In the year Ren Shen of the Shaoxing era (1152 AD), the twelve branches of Chen's family collectively sold the tree to a local villager named Wang Yi for thirteen thousand coins. They agreed to sacrifice and cut down the tree the next day. That night, Chen Pu dreamed of several elderly men with white beards who said, "This tree has been in charge for three hundred and eighty years. It should be used for the coffin of the Yellow Censor. How can it be cut down?" Chen Pu asked, "Who is the Yellow Censor?" They replied, "It is the Prefect of Zhaoxian Village, Mr. Huang." Chen Pu said, "He now resides in Xinzhou. Why must he come here?" The old men said, "If you do not believe, you will surely suffer official disaster. Besides, we have protected this tree for many years, and even if they want to sell it, it will not be possible." Chen Pu woke up and told his wife. She said, "Only because of this tree, we often face anger and insults from our descendants. Do not speak nonsense." The next day, Wang Yi brought money, wine, and geese and ducks to offer at the tomb. After the offering, he gathered everyone at Chen Pu's house to drink together. After drinking, each person received one thousand and eighty coins, and there were still forty coins left. Chen Pu took them and said,

The Bull of Yongning Estate In the Yongning estate of Jiankang, there was a herdsman who was reckless and violent. He often rode a large bull and ate people's crops without restraint. The villagers wept and pleaded with him, but he did not change his ways. Occasionally, he would raise his hand to his forehead and appeal to heaven and earth. In the third month of the twenty-fourth year of the Shaoxing era (1154 CE), while the herdsman was eating the young shoots of wheat, wind, rain, thunder, and lightning all came together. Both the bull and the herdsman were struck dead by the lightning. People saw several long figures in the air, all wearing blue cotton clothes. One of them pulled the herdsman away in the flames, while another figure lifted the bull into the void. They drilled a hole in the back of the bull's head, about an inch wide, and its tongue protruded a foot. The fire burned its hair completely. The estate supervisor, Liu Wen, ordered the bull to be carried and thrown into the river. However, people secretly took and skinned it for food. Liu Wen went to the magistrate to report this, and the magistrate advised and persuaded them to stop. A Dog Biting a Man in Green Robes There was another incident involving a dog that bit a man dressed in green robes. This event was also considered supernatural and caused great alarm among the villagers. The man in green robes was believed to be a spirit or a divine being, and the villagers were deeply disturbed by the attack. The local authorities investigated the matter and took measures to ensure the safety of the community.

Chong'an native Peng Ying obtained the title of Jiangshi Lang by donating grain. After receiving the appointment, he visited his wife's family to express gratitude. The family kept seven or eight large and fierce dogs, living deep in the mountains, where officials rarely visited. Peng wore a green robe and was bowing to his wife's mother when suddenly the dogs did not bark but instead all came out at once. One dog first bit his headband, and the other dogs surrounded and attacked him, tearing his face, ears, and nose. He rolled on the ground, and the family, in panic, beat the dogs with a large stick until they retreated. Peng lay exhausted and bleeding, unconscious and unable to recognize people, and died two days later. Dogs bark at what they find strange, and it is indeed true. Zhong Shixian, a Cishi, had only one son who entered officialdom through hereditary privilege. He went to his wife's family to discuss rituals and was killed by dogs, and the events were the same. Jian'an native Ye Defu lost both his parents at a young age and was raised and cared for by his grandmother. He worked hard to make a living. His grandmother once told him, "A fortune-teller said you would become an official. I want to find a cousin's daughter for you to marry." In the third year of Jianyan, because of fleeing from bandits, they moved to the state city, but the city was captured by the bandits. At that time, Ye was twenty-one years old, and his grandmother was seventy years old and unable to walk. She gave all her savings, fifty taels of gold and thirty bars of silver, to him and told him to leave the city first with two slaves. She warned him to say, "Come back and take me out. Do not abandon me. Even if I die, I will accuse you in the afterlife." Ye indeed did not return, and his grandmother died at the hands of the bandits. When the chaos settled, she could no longer be found. Ye used the money to buy land and trade tea, and his livelihood became increasingly prosperous. In the eighth year of Shaoxing, he obtained a recommendation through someone else's help, married into the imperial family, and obtained the title of Jiangshi Lang. The following year, he participated in the selection on July 2nd.

A man named Ye visited Han Cao in Shu to ask about his fate. Han said, "You will certainly become an official; it is possible even without studying. If you wish to inquire about your future, wait until the twenty-second day after the beginning of autumn, and come to visit me again. I will then explain in detail to you." Ye became very angry and almost wanted to beat and humiliate Han. Those present, including Huang Dewan, advised Ye to let Han go. Sixteen days later, Ye became ill and immediately vomited blood. Initially, he thought this was a cause for concern. A local monk who accompanied him came to sell tea and was the same age as Ye. Ye then ordered the preparation of two horoscopes and visited Han again. Han said, "I remember seeing the first horoscope at the beginning of this month. However, you cannot survive the beginning of autumn. If you do not die on this day, I will not discuss your fate." The monk returned home without daring to speak about it. While sick, Ye constantly mourned and said to his mother or mother-in-law, "Tell her I should return the money. I wish to beg for my life to return home. Do not prolong my suffering." He finally died on the day of the beginning of autumn. Ye was unfilial and unrighteous, and the spirits should punish him. Dying as a stranger was not unfortunate. How wonderful was Han's skill!

**The Taoist of Mount Mao** The *Bing Zhi* records an account of Qin Changling’s misfortune, but it is not very detailed. I now have the full story and record it here. In the third month of the year *Gui You* during the *Shaoxing* era, Qin went with his nephew Tun to Mount Mao to attend a gathering of cranes. He invited Huang Dewan, the magistrate of Lishui, and together they visited Liu Suoyi in the forest near Black Tiger Cave. They sat on the ground and drank wine, and Qin sent a young attendant to summon a Daoist who could sing poetry. Soon, twenty such Daoists arrived. By night, they walked in the moonlight, singing songs, and arrived at the entrance of the *Qingzhen* Temple’s Daoist Hall. The gathered group sat down, and each displayed their skill. Suddenly, from the air, someone sang four lines. Magistrate Huang was able to remember two of them: “Forty-three. Forty-three. A full moon falls into the clear pool.” Qin was exactly forty-three years old at the time, and this greatly disturbed him. He questioned all twenty people, asking who had spoken these words. All said they had not spoken a word. They then stopped drinking and returned home. In September, Qin indeed died. The year before, the accomplished Daoist Huang Yuandao told Qin, “You have a grievance against you. You must be very careful of the number forty-three.” Qin earnestly sought a way to understand this. At that time, a child was playing with a magnetic ladle. Huang took one of them, chanted a prayer, and gave it to Qin. When Qin received it, his hand felt as though it were on fire, and he could not help but fall to the ground. Huang picked it up again and sighed, saying, “This is no ordinary thing.” He then turned to the physician Tang Sanyi and said, “You should keep this object. If there is an emergency, pour it out. If you get a green pill, do not take it. But if you get a red pill, you may take it.” Three years later, Tang fell seriously ill with a severe case of typhoid. He tried pouring the ladle and received a single red pill. After taking it, he immediately recovered. To this day, the ladle is still in existence.

A Merchant from Quanzhou A merchant from Quanzhou named Yang had engaged in maritime trade for over ten years and accumulated wealth of two hundred million. Whenever he encountered storms and waves, he would certainly call upon the gods, swear by heaven and sun, and promise to decorate temples and set up water and land offerings as thanks. However, once he reached the shore, he would forget and not remember these promises, nor would he record them again. In the tenth year of the Shaoxing era, he anchored at sea and dreamed of gods coming to demand repayment. Yang said, "I am now heading to Lin'an. When I return home, I will fulfill my vows one by one. I dare not betray them." The gods replied, "How could you have such good fortune? It is all our power. Your wishes do not need to be repaid; just return the goods to us." Yang was greatly alarmed. On a certain day in July, he arrived at the Qiantang River. Fortunately, there was no incident, and he was overjoyed. He moved all his goods and merchandise to the house of Tang Old Man on Baodian Street and stayed at a guesthouse west of Chaiduo Bridge. Tang Old Man hosted a banquet for him. Yang recounted his dream and said, "I estimate that I now have forty million strings of cash. I will temporarily use one-tenth to fulfill my vow to the gods. The rest I will take back to Quanzhou and establish a living, never to go out again." He took out the agarwood, borneol, pearl clusters, and other precious items and stored them in the earth warehouse. Other incense, cloth, and logwood were no less than ten million strings of cash, and all were left outside the warehouse. That night, he drank heavily. The next day, he heard of a fire breaking out outside and jumped up and ran to climb Wu Mountain. He looked at the place where the fire started and it was still far away. In a short while, it had reached Tang Old Man's house. Yang told his servant, "It will only burn the heavy stuff. It will not cause harm." After a while, he saw black smoke rising straight from the earth warehouse. The house immediately collapsed, and the flames reached the sky. After a while, he looked back and saw that all were reduced to ashes. He then hanged himself on the wall of the warehouse. His body was left exposed overnight. His servant reported to the authorities and they verified it. He was then buried properly.

**The Ode of a Monk Transforming into a Dog** Chen, a scholar from Jianyang, was skilled in writing and had gathered dozens of disciples at the Dizang Hall of Kaitong Temple. The monk Dafu, who could recite the Peacock Sutra, strictly upheld the Water and Land Precepts. Chen's disciples caused great disturbance to Dafu, and when their desires were not met, they influenced Chen. Chen then wrote an ode titled "Dafu Transforms into a Dog During the Day" and spread it widely. One part of the ode reads: "Hungry, it gnaws on rice husks, repeatedly pondering over medicine and food. Cold, it sleeps on a bamboo broom, this time abandoning the meditation seat." All the scholars and common people of the county were shocked and came to inquire. The story spread far and wide, and everyone believed it to be true. Dafu, overwhelmed with anger, prepared a memorial to report to heaven and earth. Every day, he climbed the bell tower and tapped the bell with his forehead, performing a hundred bows each day. Soon after, Chen fell ill, covered in sores and filth, and could no longer gather his disciples. He died in a state of exhaustion and despair. The people said that Chen's harmful speech had invited retribution, but the monk's desire for revenge was also excessive.

In the eighth year of the Ganzhao era, the price of mulberry leaves in Xinzhou suddenly soared, with a pound costing a hundred coins. Zhang Liu, an elderly man from Shaxi, had a thousand pounds of leaves and his silkworms had reached the second stage of their growth. Suddenly, he had the intention to make a profit. He told his wife and daughter-in-law, "Our leaves are not enough to feed the silkworms. At the current price, how can we afford to buy more? If the silkworms do not mature, what will we do? We should throw all the silkworm trays into the river and sell the leaves. Not only will we immediately get a hundred thousand coins, but it will also be quick and convenient." Zhang Liu was usually harsh and violent, and his wife dared not disobey. She secretly discussed with her daughter-in-law, fearing that if they killed the silkworms, they would have no silkworms to raise the next year. So they kept two baskets of silkworms hidden under the daughter-in-law's bed. That night, someone was stealing mulberry leaves. Zhang Liu became furious and went out at midnight with a spear to catch the thief. He saw a person standing under a tree and stabbed him through the belly with the spear. The person fell dead immediately. He returned and told his family that he had killed a thief, saying, "Since he entered at night to steal, killing him was not a crime." His wife was startled and suspected that it must be their son, so she went to check and it was indeed their son. She immediately took off her skirt and hanged herself on a tree. Zhang Liu was surprised that his wife had not returned for a long time, so he went to check and found that she had also hanged herself. Only the daughter-in-law remained, and she used a candle to find her husband and saw three bodies. She shouted for help, and the neighborhood head came and tried to take her to the authorities, but she ran away and hanged herself in the mulberry grove. The whole family was wiped out, and they did not even get a single coin. Divine retribution came quickly. This account was also told by Huang Dewan. The *Yijian Zhi* Volume 76, Part 2 The *Dai Loumen House*

A Scholar of the Xianmo Pavilion named Lin Shao, at the age of twenty, went to take the provincial examination and entered the capital. He rented a house in Dailou, which was located in a very remote and desolate area. Many people said that the house was ominous and strange, but he did not care because the rent was cheap. A few days later, he heard the sound of children's voices on both sides of the main room. He called his servant to climb onto the roof to look, but saw nothing. On the next evening, during the third watch, he was staying in the room when a thief came and completely removed the covers and blankets. The doors and windows remained as they were. Soon after, one of the servants lifted the bed mat and found a newly dug hole beneath it, where the stolen items were discovered. On another evening, in the dim light, a large object fell to the ground with a loud noise. By morning, it was found to be several pieces of patterned stone, each several feet long. Neighbors came to see, and someone recognized them as the stones used in the construction of the state bridge. At the time, the bridge was being repaired, and it was confirmed that the stones were indeed from the bridge. They then moved out. **Lin's Daughter's Maid** Lin Xianmo's eldest daughter, upon her initial marriage to a military officer, was once drinking with her husband. She sent her maid to pick vegetables in the garden behind the house. Shortly after, the husband suddenly screamed and fell to the ground, as if he had suffered a stroke. He regained consciousness by morning. The maid also returned, with messy hair and a dirty face, and her clothes were stained. They suspected that she had taken advantage of the situation to do something improper. When questioned, she said that when she entered the garden, she placed a lantern to the side and used a small knife to dig up the vegetable roots. As she lifted one clump, a small child about a foot long suddenly appeared from the ground. She swung the knife at it, and it split into four or five children. The more she cut, the more children appeared, pulling her clothes and overwhelming her. She fell unconscious and did not wake up until the sun rose. She estimated that the strange incident occurred at the same time her husband fell ill. The husband subsequently died of a heart disease. Lin's daughter later married a high-ranking official named Ren Yong.

Wang Hou and the Carrots Wang Hou, the eldest son of Shao, held the position of Jiedushi (Military Governor) and served as a frontier commander. In his later years, he returned to the capital. One day, during a family gathering, dozens of carrots on a plate suddenly stood up. In a moment, they walked across the table. Everyone was astonished. Wang Hou's anger showed on his face, and he gobbled them all up. He vomited immediately and died the next day. His younger brother, Cai, known as Fudao, served as a Vice Minister of War at the beginning of the Xuanhe era. He was subjected to extreme punishment because a heavenly god descended upon his family. People believed it was retribution for Shao's excessive killing in warfare. The Jade Toad of Tian台 Mountain In the military camp west of Cai Prefecture City, there was a temple called the Tian台 Mountain Temple, whose meaning was unknown. Inside the temple, there was a stone three feet high with an eye that had water, which did not dry up even in drought years. It was once dug to measure its depth, and the deeper it went, the larger it became, seemingly without end. There was also a small white toad, snow-white with red eyes, that always remained in the water. Sometimes it appeared in people's homes, where it was considered a sign of good fortune. Zhu Rulong, the chancellor, was a native of the prefecture. In the spring of the fourth year of the Chongning era, he found the toad under the threshold of his house. He covered it with a clean container and sealed it tightly around. He wished, "If it is truly divine, it should return to the temple on its own." By evening, the container was empty. He went directly to the temple to check and found it in the water. That year, Zhu passed the imperial examination.

Jizhou Rebellious Horse In the early days of the Zhenghe era, a foal was born in the home of a villager in Jizhou. After seven days, it grew as large as its mother. On its forehead, it had one eye with two pupils. Its snout was like a dragon, with tiger-like spots on its lips and hooves. Its color was bright red. Its forelegs were raised, resembling flames of meat. One night, it devoured its mother, leaving no skin or bones. It escaped into the fields. The villagers feared it would cause harm and gathered dozens of people to chase and kill it near the inn. An artist drew its image to show others, calling it a monster among beasts. Nanjing Turtle and Snake In the first year of the Jingkang era, during the intercalary month, the northern barbarians invaded Nanjing. The siege was just beginning to tighten. A giant turtle appeared in the city, as large as a cart wheel, three feet high, with nine bony tails. Its shell was yellow like wax, with each scale carved with a character, eight of which could be read, saying "Guo Fu releases life." The rest could not be read. Its gaze could pierce people, and its neck scales were like coins. Its gaze was extraordinary. The governor-general Zhu Lu Gong ordered it to be placed in the City God Temple. People from the prefecture rushed to see it. The governor feared it would confuse the people and said the turtle does not eat; could it be thinking of water? He threw it into the South Lake, and it did not reappear. Later, a large red snake appeared in the Lei Wansheng Temple, coiled in the incense burner, remaining motionless for several days, but sometimes it would lift its head. No one dared to approach it. The governor wrote an offering to it, also saying that since the enemy invaded the city, it did not provide secret assistance, but instead produced strange creatures to frighten people, why is that? That day, the snake disappeared. Despite being under siege for over half a year, the city could not be captured.

**The Chief Minister** Zhang Bangchang, a mid-level official in the imperial bureaucracy, was sent as an envoy to Goryeo. Upon reaching Mingzhou, he visited the Temple of the Eastern Sea. One night, he dreamed that a deity told him, "In the future, you will become a mid-level official in the imperial court, but you must not hold the position of Chief Minister." Several years later, at the end of the Xuanhe era, he indeed received the appointment to a high-ranking position. In the first year of the Jingkang era, on the ninth day of the first month, while the city was under siege, he was appointed as the Vice Premier. He was taken hostage by the enemy camp and forcibly taken back to Yan Shan. The following year, the capital city fell, and the enemy forced him to be crowned as the emperor of the state of Chu. He was subsequently executed for his crimes. **The Private Seal of Zhu Sheng** Chancellor Zhu remained in Nanjing as a留守 (defensive commander). When the enemy invaders attacked, he was busy fortifying the city. One night, while patrolling the city walls, he reached the southern gate and saw a bright light shining on the ground outside the moat, as if lit by a candle. He carefully marked the location and had someone investigate it in the morning. They found nothing. Later that morning, they dug up a square copper seal, about an inch in diameter, inscribed with four characters in ancient seal script: "Zhu Sheng's Private Seal." The seal was deep green in color and very finely crafted. Zhu Sheng, whose name was not Zhu but Sheng, had a seal that read "Zhu Sheng," which was indeed unusual. These eight events are all recorded in the "Record of the Chancellor's Leisure Time in Shu Water."

In the eighth year of the Gaodao era, my second elder brother, who served as the governor of Jiankang, also unearthed a seal. The seal measured one inch and seven parts in diameter, and its inscription consisted of twelve characters: "Seal of the Western Camp Commander of the Army, both land and naval forces." I wished to investigate when it was made but had no time to do so. Great Hu King Xingzu Wenren, style name Yuqing, was from Xiu Prefecture. He was well-versed and talented, tall and strong, bold and unbound by small details. He lived in the suburbs and called himself a farmer of the Eastern Suburbs. He served as the head of the prefectural school and was friends with Lou Ju, the teacher of the prefectural school. In the summer of Dingmao year during the Shaoxing era, Lou Ju died of illness. In the ninth month of autumn, Xingzu Wenren dreamed of a guest visiting his home. The guest was wearing a red robe and riding a horse, with a large retinue. Upon closer inspection, it was Lou Ju. He said to Xingzu Wenren, "Hopefully we will work together." He called for a horse to ride up and said, "This horse can travel a thousand miles in an instant." In a moment, he was already on the saddle. They then rode side by side. Lanterns lined the road like daylight. After traveling several miles, the light gradually dimmed. They arrived at a large official building. Inside was a hall facing south with a curtain hanging. The lights inside the curtain flickered. Officials and soldiers in the courtyard, some sat, others lay down. When they saw two riders arrive, they did not stand up. The two turned east and then slightly north. There was a room with two couches facing each other. The attendants bowed and said "yes."

As the official, Jū, invited him to sit down and said, "This is where I conduct my official duties." Soon, a small child emerged from behind the screen and tugged at Jū's robe, saying, "Your son is already here." Jū replied, "You should go back; I will come to fetch you later." Xīngzǔ was just about to take the reins when he suddenly awoke. The next day, he informed all his usual contacts, suspecting something to be unlucky. Shortly thereafter, he went out to pay respects and passed by Liu's house. He felt a chill run down his spine, and immediately fell ill. He was supported back home and died after two nights. After his death, his cousin, Chen Zhèn, dreamed of him. They spoke as if they were still alive. Zhèn said, "I heard that my brother is an official in the underworld. Is that true?" Xīngzǔ nodded in agreement. Zhèn then asked, "If someone comes to consult with the cup and divination tool, can you tell them the outcome?" Xīngzǔ replied, "The Great Pure King does not like this." Zhèn said, "Then are you an official under the Great Pure King?" Xīngzǔ hastily said, "I spoke wrongly. I spoke wrongly." He cried out in grief and left. Zhèn woke up in shock and still heard his crying sound.

**The Case of Zhang** In the early years of the Zhenghe era, Prince Xun Zhongyu of the imperial family served as the head of the imperial clan. His fourth daughter married the grandson of a high-ranking official named Yang, who had lost his father at an early age. Yang's mother, Zhang, was known for her violent and fierce nature. She frequently quarreled with her daughter-in-law, often using harsh words. Yang himself was a member of the Yuanyou faction, which was in opposition to the current regime, and thus faced political setbacks. His wife was particularly depressed due to these circumstances. Zhang once said to her daughter-in-law, "You think we are part of the Yuanyou faction, so you treat us so poorly. The times will change, and our family should not remain in this difficult situation forever." The daughter-in-law reported Zhang's words to Prince Xun. Prince Xun's second son, Shili, was married to a woman who was related to Wang Anshi, a prominent statesman and reformer of the Song Dynasty. This woman often visited the home of the powerful official Cai Jing. She passed the information on to him, and Cai Jing saw the situation as an opportunity. He ordered the arrest of Zhang and her imprisonment in the Kaifeng prison. The governor charged Zhang with slander against the emperor, citing her harsh and harmful words.
