The punishment was severe, involving dismemberment. Two judicial officials learned about the case and argued that women should not be subject to such severe punishment. They questioned the validity of the charges, suggesting that there was no basis for a crime of high treason. The governor became angry and ordered the officials to be flogged. Both officials died from their injuries. Zhang was ultimately executed on the day of her trial.

The Prince of Xuang was greatly surprised, not expecting things to reach this point. Lili and his two younger brothers went to the market to look around. Not long after, they died one after another. Lili saw a woman covered in blood, squatting behind the screen and curtains. Then she spoke in the voice of a ghost, saying, "I originally did not wish to cause trouble, but the two officials of the law would not let me." Later, Cai Jing fell ill and ordered a Taoist priest to submit a memorial. The Taoist priest's spirit traveled to the celestial gates, where he saw an object like a pile of meat, with blood covering it. A bystander said that the Heavenly Emperor was sitting on his throne, deciding public matters. Soon after, a person emerged and asked the Taoist priest why he had come. The priest explained the reason. The person pointed to the object and said, "Cai Jing caused this woman to suffer the most severe punishment, and she has come to complain to Heaven. The Emperor is now in a state of great anger. How could you have submitted a memorial?" The priest replied, "As a Taoist priest, I was commanded by the Prime Minister. How could I dare to refuse?" The person said, "Do not do this again in the future." He also said, "It just so happened that there was already a decree to send Cai Jing to be stationed in Tan Prefecture. You should return quickly." The Taoist priest woke up and secretly told someone he trusted. Ten years later, Cai Jing died in Changsha. However, the daughter of the Prince of Xuang and Wu Shi both lived to be eighty.

Chancellors Tang and Shi Chancellor Tang from Jinyun and Chancellor Shi from Siming were both in Lin'an in the fifteenth year of the Shaoxing era (1145). Chancellor Tang, as the magistrate of Zhenghe, went to take the literary examination. Chancellor Shi, as a candidate for the jinshi degree, went to take the provincial examination. They both went to consult Han Zao about their fortunes. At that time, Han Zao was renovating his residence, leaving only one seat available. Each guest would stand and talk briefly before leaving. When the two gentlemen arrived, they mentioned their birth years. Han Zao called his daughter to set up a seat, invited them to sit, and served tea. He pondered and sighed for a long time, then bowed his hands and said, "Both of you will become chancellors. You will soon flourish." They both were not bold enough to say so themselves. That year, they both passed the examination. Chancellor Tang rose from the Hanlin Academy and the Imperial Library to the Privy Council and was appointed chancellor in the Ding-Chou year (1147). Chancellor Shi was then a professor at the Imperial College. He often told people, "Han Zao's prediction about Chancellor Tang was indeed divinely accurate. Why did it not apply to me? Now we look up to each other like heaven and earth." In the winter of the Geng-Chen year (1150), Chancellor Tang resigned from his position as Left Chancellor and returned home. Chancellor Shi was then a lecturer at the Imperial College and quickly rose through the ranks due to favoritism. In the spring of the Gui-Wei year (1154), he was appointed chancellor.

Jing Mountain Guesthouse Han Shu, a man from Mingzhou, wandered southward and settled temporarily in Dacheng Village, Yiyang County, Xinzhou. He traveled alone twenty li east of the county, to a place called Jing Mountain, where he opened a wine shop and guesthouse. In the seventh year of the Gandaod era, during the winter season, scholars from the south were traveling to the provincial examination, and the traffic was very heavy. A scholar from Qiongzhou, Mr. Li, stayed at his guesthouse. The next morning, he left, leaving behind a small cloth bag in his room. The store servant brought this to Han Shu. Han Shu said, "I will take good care of it and hand it over when he comes to claim it." Mr. Li walked to Aoye Rock, which was already one station away, and then realized he had left the bag behind. He hurried back to Han's guesthouse, ran directly into the bedroom, and searched under the mats and cushions but found nothing. His face turned as dark as ink, his eyes bulged, and his mouth gaped open; he could not speak. Han Shu asked, "Is it not possible that you have forgotten something?" Mr. Li replied sorrowfully, "My home is far away, five thousand li from here. I only had a few items to provide for the expenses of my journey. If I lose them tonight, I will surely die on the road and never return to my bones." Han Shu smiled and said, "I have recovered it for you. There is no need to worry." He ordered the servant to bring it back. The bag was sealed and marked as before. When Mr. Li opened and examined it, he found forty-four taels of silver, five taels of gold, and a pair of gold hairpins. Mr. Li offered five taels of silver as a gift to express his gratitude, but Han Shu refused to accept it. Moved to tears, Mr. Li left. The following year, a traveler named Fan Wanqing learned about the incident and wrote a poem on the wall, saying, "The loss of the money was a great confusion. The innkeeper's kindness fully returned it. From now on, Yiyang will have another story. Not allowing hidden virtue to be monopolized by Yan Mountain." He also wrote a postscript, stating, "In the world, those who are greedy and act like small people are numerous. Hearing of Han Shu's virtue, one should feel ashamed. Han Shu is still alive today."

**Summer Second Lady** A woman from the capital city, known as Summer Second Lady, appeared in a dream to her son Du Sheng after she had been dead for a year. She said, "While I was alive, I owed a certain alley's Wang family twelve guan of money and a certain alley's Chen family thirty-four guan of money. As punishment, I was demoted to a Wang family's donkey and sold to Chen. The Wang family received enough money to pay off their debt, but Chen has not. I daily carried wheat with it, but one round trip was only worth about thirty-eight coins. Outside of today, I still owe eighteen thousand coins, which cannot be settled in less than two years. In my lifetime, I buried over a hundred taels of silver under the threshold inside the hall. You can dig it up to redeem me." Her son said, "I will go immediately to find out, but what should I look for?" Summer Second Lady replied, "Go to Nanxun Gate in the morning. The first animal you see will be a mule, followed by a donkey, and then me. When you come, I will raise my head to look at you." Du Sheng woke up and dug up the silver. He went directly to Nanxun Gate to wait for it. Indeed, he met a series of wheat-laden donkeys coming. The third one raised its head to look at him.

Du Yuqi. He wanted to drag and bring the donkey back. A servant from the Chen household said, "This is my master's property. What are you doing?" Du replied, "This is my mother. I should return the original price to redeem her." The servant refused. They argued fiercely. The magistrate recorded and sent the matter to the prefecture. The prefect questioned his account and ordered the donkey to be brought before him. He said, "If you truly recognize your son, you can hold onto his tassel." Du responded in sound and so it was. The prefect found it strange. At that time, Liu Yu had usurped the capital. The prefect reported the entire matter to Liu Yu. He called Du into the palace hall and said again, "If you can lift the front two legs onto your shoulders, then it is true." Du responded in sound and so it was. Liu Yu sighed and found it strange for a long time. He wanted the government to provide money. Du bowed and said, "If so, I fear the mother's debt will not be released. I am willing to pay the money myself and beg for the donkey to return." Liu Yu agreed. Du swept a room and carefully treated the donkey. After two more years, it died. He bought a coffin and added clothes and quilts to bury it. Later, when the court regained Henan, Du's son came back and lived in Ganzhou. People told the story like this.

During the Xuanhe period, an official of a certain prefecture in Shaanxi Province was traveling to take up his post and ate at an inn on the road. A monk suddenly entered from outside. The gatekeeper told him to leave, but he refused to listen. Family members who saw him were also angry and tried to chase him away, but only the prefect invited him to ask the reason. The monk only said, "When the respected official passes through Huayin, if you see the small hall, please pay attention. He said nothing else." After speaking, the monk left directly. The prefect wanted to ask for more details and sent someone to follow him, but they could not catch up. By the time he entered the pass, the prefect was floating on the boat upstream along the Wei River and mooring late at night. A subordinate official reported that a minor official was holding a card, claiming to be the small hall of Huayin and wanting to pay respects. The prefect refused because it was not the right time, and the minor official said, "There is something I want to tell the lord. But I am only serving in the county. The next day at dawn, I cannot leave, so I came here during my leave. From here to the county is still a hundred miles." The prefect remembered the monk's words and ordered him to call and board the boat. The minor official then said, "What I said is extremely secret. I do not want others nearby to hear it. Must move to the north bank to be able to." The prefect agreed. The boatman said the mooring ropes were already fixed, moving north without reason, isn't this the plan of thieves? The north is also not a safe place, had no choice but to go. By the time they reached the north bank, the person did not come. The entire family regretted, it has already happened, there is nothing to be done. Before midnight, a strong wind suddenly arose, like the sound of a mountain collapsing and a spring bursting forth. Fish and dragons mourned, waves splashed and surged, rocked and unable to sleep, worried until dawn. Looking at the south bank, it had collapsed several feet. The boats of guests resting and staying overnight all sank without a trace. When they arrived at the county to search for this official, there was never such a person. The whole family was saved from being buried in fish bellies. Strange indeed.

In the governor's residence of Wuchang, Liu Yafu, the governor of Wuchang, first entered the residence. From afar, he saw someone on the hall. When he ascended the hall, he saw a woman standing inside the door frame, with her feet hanging outside. He went to see her personally and realized that she was likely a newly amputated person. Her shoes and socks were both fresh and clean, but her upper body was not visible. She stood without falling. Liu suspected that this was the work of a villain and secretly observed inside and outside the residence, finding it silent and without any traces. After staying for two days, he ordered to bury her. Eventually, the strangeness could not be explained. This story is recorded by Sun Ge.

A Doubtful Lawsuit in Dayu County The wife of Huang Jie, a low-ranking official in Dayu County, named Li Siniang, had long been having an adulterous relationship with someone. When Huang Jie left home, she took their three-year-old son and fled with him. They were not far from home when the child began to cry uncontrollably. Unable to bear it, she abandoned him in the grass. A hand laborer named Li San, who happened to be passing by, saw the child writhing on the ground and couldn't bear to see it. He picked up the child and took him home, and his family was very happy. When Huang Jie returned home, he found his wife and child missing. He searched for them thoroughly. Li San lived a few miles away and was playing with the child when Huang Jie arrived. Li San immediately informed his neighbors, and they captured him and brought him back to the county. Li San was subjected to intense interrogation and falsely confessed, saying that he had no children, so he killed Huang Jie's wife, sunk her body in the river, and stole the child to return home. Now that he was captured, he willingly accepted death without regret. The case was ready to be sent to the prefecture, and Li San was shackled and standing in the courtyard when dark clouds suddenly gathered, and lightning and thunder came. Li San's shackles and chains loosened themselves, and he became stunned and dazed. Gradually, he calmed down and saw that the official who had already died was lying on the ground with red writing on his back, as if saying the case was unjust. Twenty officials all lost their headscarves. The county magistrate was also terrified. After a long time, he called out to ask what Li San saw. Li San said that his vision was pitch black and he didn't know why, but only the chief official was sitting in a green silk tent. The magistrate feared and regretted, and quickly released Li San. However, Li Siniang and her lover were never caught. It was on August 29, 1149 CE. Huang Jie, Li San, and the child are all fine until now.

The *Yijian* Collection, Volume 84, Part 4 The Gateway of Huayang Cave Li Dachuan, a native of Fuzhou, traveled in the Jianghuai region practicing astrological divination. During the Zhenghe period, he arrived in Hezhou. As the end of the year approached, he did not engage in divination. Vulgar speech referred to sitting in a shop selling divination as "gousi," and wandering in the market for divination as "panshu." On New Year's Day, the innkeeper took him to the outskirts, where they saw a waterfall hanging like a curtain, flowing into a cave. It was very beautiful. They climbed a hill to observe where the water flowed. The area was rarely traveled, and the dark moss made the ground slippery. Li Dachuan fell without realizing it, as if it had been two meals' time. He then sat on the grass, with no harm to his skin. Gazing into the cave, it was pitch black like night. He could not find a foothold to climb. He was certain to die. He tried to lift his right hand, but there was nothing to grasp. When he lifted his left hand, it touched a stone wall. He followed the path down, as if there was a narrow path to walk. Gradually, it became brighter as he progressed. On the right side, there was a stone pool with lotus flowers in full bloom. Despite being overwhelmed by hunger and thirst, the flowers and water were unreachable. Soon, it became very bright. Ahead, he encountered a double-stone cave entrance. He hesitated to enter from the right, fearing it would be more distant, so he passed through the left entrance. He did this three times and found himself in a large cave. The flowers and water were absent, and it was completely cut off from the sky and sunlight. However, the light was brighter than in the human world. There was a stone chessboard inside, and he heard the sound of reciting scriptures, but saw no one. Far away, it seemed as if someone was sitting and combing their hair, but upon approaching, nothing could be seen. Soon, he arrived at a large forest, dark and gloomy, chilling the spirit and bones.

Li was frightened and trembling, running quickly. He had emerged into the open countryside. Lifting his head, he saw the sun and rejoiced at being reborn. He began to walk more slowly. He encountered a temple by the roadside and rested at its gate. The monks came out and asked him what had happened. They were all greatly surprised and questioned him closely. Li said, "Give me a cup of water, and I will tell you." They invited him into the temple, prepared a meal, and he recounted all that he had experienced. The monks sighed and said, "It is said that there is a cave in this mountain. It is the rear entrance of Huayang Cave, but no one has ever reached it before." Li asked, "Where is this place?" They replied, "It is in the territory of Chuzhou. What day is it today?" Li answered, "It is the seventh day of the first lunar month." Li said, "I have been falling for seven days, but it felt like only a day." The monks, armed with weapons, invited Li to search for the old path. However, they encountered various strange and ominous phenomena and could not proceed further. Li returned to Hezhou and visited his old residence. By the time he arrived, it was already nightfall, and he knocked on the door. The owner asked who he was, and Li replied with his name. The entire household spat and cursed, saying, "Unlucky, unlucky!" Li shouted loudly, "I am not a ghost! How can you treat me this way?" They then opened the door. Li stayed for several days and then returned home. He often told others about this incident. Someone criticized him, saying, "You are also a fool. On New Year's Day, lotus flowers bloom. Isn't it a heavenly realm? And with two stone cave entrances, how can one know that the right path is far and the left path can lead out?" Li replied, "At that moment, I was only concerned with dying. How could I think of this? Even if I regret it now, it is too late." Li has a son who now lives in Linchuan. Tian E said, "Heard from Dachuan."

Lightning Struck Wang Si In the village of Houxi, Linchuan County, there was a man named Wang Si who was disobedient to his father and often beat and struck him. His father wanted to report this to the authorities, but was always persuaded to stop by relatives. In the sixth month of the sixth year of the Gande era (1170 AD), Wang Si again acted in this manner. Unable to bear his anger any longer, the father went to the county office to report the abuse. Wang Si, holding 200 coins, intercepted him and said, "Take this as the cost of filing a complaint." This was meant to show that he had no fear of legal consequences. The father had not walked half a mile when a sudden thunderstorm broke out. He quickly took shelter in a nearby house. When the rain stopped, he went out and heard that his wicked son had been struck dead by lightning. He went to see it and found that the 200 coins were embedded in Wang Si's rib cage, attached to the flesh. The father searched for the money he had in his pocket, but it was already lost.

**Nanfeng Thunder Goddess** In the home of Huang Shen, the recorder of Nanfeng County, a sudden downpour occurred. The thunder goddess descended into the courtyard, causing chaos and panic. Her hair was short and red, and she had only three toes on each foot. She was mostly human in form. After a long while, the clouds grew dark, and lightning flashed. She then disappeared without a trace. **Footprints in the Mud** One night, during a thunderstorm in the rural areas of Fuzhou, the residents heard hundreds of people laughing in the air. The next morning, a large tree was found uprooted, with its roots completely pulled out. Near the tree, in the mud, were imprints of giant footprints, extremely large. The marks of the waist and arms were deeply embedded in the ground, and the feet were two chi long and equally wide. It was suspected that a divine being had tried to pull out the tree, slipped, and fell, which is why everyone laughed at the incident. **Predicting Ships in Yihuang** People in Yihuang are skilled at predicting the fate of ships, passing down their knowledge from father to son, but there are no books or written records. In the fifth year of the Ganda era, a county resident named Mo Yin built a large ship. He invited a skilled person to examine it with a large sum of money. The skilled person said, "This is a female ship, but its body has masculine characteristics. One board is shaped like a spear and stands prominently in the center. Its fate is already determined. Official matters will arise, and disaster will befall the owner." Mo Yin wanted to make changes, but the skilled person replied, "Fate is already determined; it cannot be altered." Mo Yin then took three million coins to buy salt in the eastern part of the Huai River region. Just as he was about to leave, the state needed a ship to transport money and detained him. When they reached the foot of Dagu Mountain, the masts and sails were broken by the wind. In a hurry, there was nothing to buy, so they cut down pine trees by the shore to make them. Some people said these were sacred trees, but they had no time to worry about it. That night, the entire ship heard a strange and loud sound, and it was impossible to understand the reason.

After passing through DanYang, a thief entered the boat at night. He observed something that looked like dozens of armored soldiers moving back and forth. Yin's family had collected ancient swords for generations, and recently, after nightfall, one of these swords had emitted a glow. Yin was astonished by this unusual phenomenon and took the sword with him. He then took the sword and went forward, encountering one person who was sleeping with his hand across his abdomen. Yin swung the sword repeatedly and severed the man's right arm. The man was saved and did not die. It was the Deputy Commissioner Liu. Initially, Liu believed that Yin was capable and brave, so he placed him in his boat. There had never been any slight conflict between them. Yin was completely unaware of this incident. He was immediately captured and interrogated in the Zhenjiang prison. The official wanted to sentence him to death, but Deputy Commissioner Liu refused, saying, "It was a grudge from a past life; not a matter of this life. I am fortunate to have survived. Why impose the harshest punishment?" He then submitted a petition for a doubtful case, and Yin was spared the death penalty. Instead, he was branded and assigned to the ShaoWu military.

**Jiá Líu Jù Shī** A Giant Louse Growing on the Cheek A man from Linchuan had a tumor growing on his cheek. The itch became unbearable. He would often roast it with fire, and the itching would stop. Soon after, it would happen again, causing him great suffering. A doctor told him, "This is truly a louse tumor. It should be cut out." The doctor took an oil paper and wrapped it around his neck, then applied the lancet. As soon as the tumor was cut open, countless small lice poured out. In the end, two large lice—one white and one black—emerged, both the size of beans. The shells were empty, with no blood inside. They had nothing to do with the cheek. There was no scar left, only the area blocked by the tumor was now white. **Hú Dào Shì** The Taoist Priest Hu Hu Wu was a poor man from Yihuang. Whenever there were village gatherings and games, he would make drums. At such times, he would become a Taoist priest, hence he was called "Taoist Priest Hu." His profession was cooking snails, and he must first peel off their shells before cooking them. When he fell ill, he raised his right index finger and said, "One snail is here." He then used a needle to remove the claw, and blood flowed over his hand. He cried out in pain. A little while later, he removed the claws of the others, until all the nails on his feet were gone. He then died.

Sacrificial Official Zhao In Jianchang, there was a sacrificial official named Zhao who had been suffering from a long-term weak illness. Someone advised him, "Drinking deer blood will cure it." Zhao bought three or four deer. Each day, he took one deer, using a long iron pipe to insert it into the meat. After a short while, the blood coagulated and filled the tube. He then drank it. The deer suffered this pain daily, and when the blood ran out, it died. Zhao indeed became robust and healthy, eating and drinking healthily as he consumed the blood. Later, he fell ill, with strange sores breaking out all over his body, creating holes in the flesh. The itching was unbearable. He had to insert a bamboo tube into the wounds and pour boiling water through it to stop the itching. He was never at peace all day, and he died after two months. The Mad Monk of the Han Dynasty The *Yi Zhi* records the story of Yang Daming meeting someone who turned stone into purple gold by shouting. I obtained this from the *Qiju Zhi*. Now I have also obtained it from the record of Nan Kang County Magistrate Chen Shicai, which has slight differences but is very detailed. Therefore, I am recording it again here. Yang Daming was a scholar from Chenglong Village in Nankang County. After his father's death, he lived in a hut near the grave. The following year, the year was Ren Xu, but the *Yi Zhi* records it as Gui Hai. On the seventh day of the seventh month, in the morning, there was a monk coming down from the mountain. Yang was with three or four students, and one servant was cooking. The mountain was desolate and quiet, with no one living within ten miles in all directions. Climbing vines, they finally arrived. There was no one to talk to. When they saw the guest coming, they welcomed him and sat down. The guest said, "You will have an ordeal in August. Taking my medicine can avoid it." He took out a small gourd from his waist and a pill, instructing Yang to swallow it with water. And he said, "I have a request for you."

Will you give it to me? "What do you want?" The guest pointed to the cloth robe on the shelf and said, "I ask for this." Yang wanted to agree but was somewhat suspicious of its authenticity. He did not immediately give it to him. He asked again. Reluctantly, he gave it to him. The guest rolled it up and put it into a gourd. The mouth of the gourd was only large enough to fit a finger. Yang was surprised and exclaimed, but he silently wondered if it was tricking him. Then he said, "Do I really want the robe? I just wanted to test you." "Since you can give it to me, it is commendable." He took it out of the gourd and returned it. He asked for a bowl of water and put in a pinch of medicine powder. Stirred it for a long time, it formed a red pill like a bullet. Bowed to Yang and said, "Can you take this?" "I am fortunate to be healthy. I don't want to take it." The guest immediately swallowed it. Slowly said, "You have been here for a long time. You must be in need. I have something to leave for you." He called a student and took a handful of earth, as big as a fist. He held it in his hand and blew on it three times. Looking at Yang, he said, "What do you think is in my hand?"

He said, "I do not know." He placed it on the desk, and it was a piece of gold that shone brightly, clearly marked with five finger prints. He said, "You may take this to help with your daily expenses." It turned out that Mother Yang was still alive, and Yang now realized he was dealing with a strange person. He still doubted that the person was trying to test him with wealth, so he refused to accept it. The guest laughed and threw it on the ground, then kicked it with his foot. It turned into a stone. He got up and bid farewell, and offered to stay for a drink, but he declined. The guest casually pointed to the poems on the wall and said, "These are all from my friends. Would you like to write a poem for me?" The guest replied, "If you want a poem, it is possible." He took a worn-out pen from the desk, drew several strokes on the ground, dipped it in a bowl of water, and wrote large on the wall. There was no trace of ink or pigment, and it was very difficult to distinguish. After sending him down the mountain, when looking back, it had turned a light purple color. The poem read: "Yang Jun is indeed a man of integrity. His filial piety is evident throughout heaven and earth. The emperor pities his hardships."

On the Qixi Festival, a message was sent back. Lingered over with fondness for a stubborn stone. A descendant was given as an offering. The descendant had already refused to accept. I will not force you. Dust and wind make it hard to stay for long. Hope that your determination does not change. It will surely be recorded as a moment of hesitation. Achieving high aspirations is as easy as turning over one's hand. The previous inscription read "disorderly Han hermit," with a character size of about four inches. Soon after, it was added with red color, just like written with red earth. The next day, thoroughly asking the villagers, none of them saw the so-called hermit. The scholars of the village together reported to the county. The county reported to the prefecture. The prefecture informed the court. Granted a bundle of silk. Five years later, Shi Cai came from Fuzhou to become an official. Personally saw Yang Lu's talk from beginning to end like this. Visited Cheng Long's hut. The thatched hut was in ruins. All the other poems were worn away. Only the hermit's inscription was fresh and clean like new. The poem says "send back." Suspect that it must be said by Lu Dongbin. During Yang Lu's father's grave during the final mourning, his mother followed and died. (The original text is missing twelve lines.)

A Missing Section of the Harmonious and Auspicious Tower The internal text is missing. Wu Sengqi Wu Sengqi, originally named Wen You, was a monk from Xinfeng County, Ganzhou Prefecture, who belonged to the Wu clan. After leaving his hair unbound, he traveled and built a hermitage on a mountain in Gandian. After a long time, he left that place. He became a guest at Yun Du Jing Temple’s Sengqi Monastery, where he took charge of the monastery’s affairs. Thus, he was known as Wu Sengqi. He pretended to be mad in the marketplace, and no one could understand him. Every day, he would go to the pine forest to question it, saying, "The emperor of the Zhao family, the king of the Zhao family." No one understood his meaning. When he met virtuous people on the road, he would bow deeply in respect. Those who were greedy, violent, and cruel, he would treat as dogs and pigs, showing no deference. Evil young men who were unhappy would often gather to chase and drive him away. Once, he ran to hide in the bamboo grove of a certain man’s garden, calling out for help and striking the bamboo, saying, "The great bamboo forest will become a broom." Within ten days, all the bamboo in the grove withered. This family was indeed a wicked clan, and from then on, they declined. Behind the temple, there was a bamboo grove with one particularly large bamboo stalk. One night, Wu Sengqi arrived at its base, struck it, and sang, his voice echoing far and wide. He did this night after night, causing other monks to lose sleep. They became angry and cut down the bamboo. Soon after, a purple fungus, a foot in diameter, grew on the ground. A county resident named Zeng Detai, who was old and childless, discussed with his wife to invite Wu Sengqi to eat and pray for a son. Before they could summon him, Wu Sengqi came knocking on the door early in the morning. Zeng Detai was greatly surprised and carefully prepared food for him. Before leaving, Wu Sengqi said, "What should I do to repay you?" Zeng Detai replied, "There is nothing but two pearls." Indeed, Zeng Detai soon had two sons. The market in the county used to be located on Nan Zhou, but outside the county government, it was just a wilderness. Whenever Wu Sengqi passed by the gate, he would say, "Money will soon reach the waist." When Nan Zhou sank into the water, the market moved to the south side of the county gate. He once asked a peasant woman for vegetables and instructed her to prepare plenty.

The woman agreed. Upon returning home, the husband was angry at her for spending recklessly. When Wu arrived and begged for fermented soybean paste, he ate it raw. If he wanted to stop and choked repeatedly while eating. The woman said, "Eat until you are full, and then stop. Why must you finish it all?" Wu replied, "I wanted to avoid your husband and wife blaming me." The people were surprised and thanked her. A Buddhist practitioner named Sun Dejun went to Wuping in Tingzhou to visit Master Dingying at Qingyan Temple. The master said, "There is a Buddha in Yuanyu River. Why do you pay respects to me?" Sun asked, "Who is the Buddha?" The master replied, "He is my fellow monk, Sengga." He asked Sun to hold a fan for him to send. When the boat anchored at the shore, Wu had already arrived. He asked, "Where is the fan my teacher sent?" Sun showed him dozens of fans from Tingzhou mixed together, and Wu directly took the original one and left. Because of this, his reputation for madness gradually diminished, and many called him a living Buddha. One night, he visited all the temple gates in the same temple, laid out seating arrangements, and performed a ritual, saying, "Take care, take care." All were silent and gave no response. At midnight, he sat in meditation and passed away. It was the sixth day of the sixth month of the Xining era of the reign of Emperor Zhenzong. On that day, a big merchant from the town was in Sichuan, where he met Wu on a river bridge. The merchant asked where Wu was going. Wu, hunched over and hurrying, said, "Be patient, be patient." The merchant returned and then learned of Wu's death. Upon his death, a strange fragrance filled the room, and it remained unchanged for several days. Everyone agreed not to cremate him, but instead plastered his entire body as a sacred object. In the winter of the Yanyuan era of the reign of Emperor Shenzong, a monk came to the county city, visited the home of Gui Anya, and requested wood to make a niche. Gui asked, "Who is the teacher?" The monk replied, "He is the monk Wu from Mingjue Temple in Yuanyu River, Miaojing Temple." Gui agreed, and when he sent the monk across the threshold, he disappeared. Later, they investigated the reason and found that Mingjue Temple was indeed Sengga. His true body still exists today.

Chancellor He Wenzhen Chancellor He Wenzhen. Originally from Xianjing, he came to the capital. Passing through Zitong, he intended to visit the Zhang Wang Temple but forgot it. He walked ten li before realizing his mistake. He quickly dismounted and turned back to silently pray and bow twice. That night, he dreamed of entering the temple courtyard. The deity sat behind the curtain and threw a scroll of documents outside. He unfolded and looked at it, finding it completely resembled official documents in the world and also contained words. Waking up, he remembered three lines: "I personally examined scholars, selected ten people, and now you are the top candidate." Later, the official title listed the position granted. Chancellor He thought about it: the scholars selected in the palace examination were about five hundred, and he said ten people, probably to play a joke on him. When the results were announced, he indeed topped the scholars. The first place in the first rank was given to nine people. Then, Fu Songqing, who was the provincial top scorer, was promoted to the first rank, thus making up ten. It was referring to the first rank in the dream. The positions granted were exactly the same. Ye Shilin wrote this.

Dingzhou Water-Drawing Woman In Dingzhou, at the Kaiyuan Temple, there were many travelers staying as guests. Several guests sat together at the temple gate and saw a woman drawing water. One guest was skilled in magic and tried to tease her. He lifted the water without moving, but he did not know that the woman herself could perform magic. She turned around and said, "Gentlemen, do not play around." The guest did not respond. After a while, he said, "If so, we must compare skills to see who is better." He threw his burden, and it turned into a small snake. The guest reached into his pocket and took out a piece of powder. He quickly drew about twenty circles on the ground and stood in the middle. The snake could not enter. The woman spat water and scolded it again, and it then grew as big as a beam. It directly leaped into the middle circle and was about to attack the guest. The woman again warned and stopped it, but the guest still did not listen. The snake then wrapped around his feet and neck, and it could not be untangled. Hundreds of passersby gathered to watch, and those staying at the temple wanted to go and report to the authorities. The woman smiled and said, "There is no harm." She reached out and took the snake, throwing it on the ground. It was still just a burden. She smiled at the guest and said, "Your skill is not yet perfect. How dare you? If you encounter others, you will surely die." The guest bowed twice and apologized. Then he followed her home to become her disciple.

Auspicious Cloud Sparrow In the Ruiyun Monastery of Taiping, Shaowu, the chief monk Xian Yong, whose teacher was Pu Wen, was patrolling the hall and burning incense on the sixteenth day of the eleventh month in the sixth year of the Gandaoyuan era (November 18, 1170). As he was paying respects before the statue of a Bodhisattva, a sparrow flew and chirped around, then folded its wings and stood on the incense burner. It remained motionless for a long time, and when he looked again, it had transformed. The villagers followed one after another to see it, and it remained completely unshaken, directly facing the statue. Xian Yong reported this to the county magistrate, Zhao Shan Gang, who wrote a verse at the end of the paper, saying: "Every day flying and chirping to proclaim the profound meaning. The ephemeral glory rising and fading, what doubt remains? Pitiable are the many travelers of the world. Coming and going, deceiving oneself." The temple monk carved the image onto a stone, and even after several years, the sparrow's feathers did not fall off, appearing as if it were still alive. People showing respect from near and far are unending. The incident of a mouse damaging a scripture recorded in the temple's records also occurred at this temple. In the early years of the Shaoxing era, Zong Ben resided in the Danxia Temple in Taiping and also experienced a miraculous transformation of a sparrow. Xian Yong brought the stone carving here.

**Eiji Ding Zhi, Volume 92, Entry** **Tainan Yi Nü** Yang Congshan, a man from the capital, was captured by the enemy in the Yunzhong region. He went to a certain mountain (the exact location is unclear due to the missing character). While drinking at a wine shop, he saw a poem left on the wall, signed as "Yi Nü from Tainan." There was also a short poem, all of which were words of longing for her husband. Recognizing her name and handwriting, he realized it was Wang, the wife of his brother-in-law Han Shihou. Since they had been separated by chaos and strife, they had not heard from each other. Upon closely examining the writing, he noticed the ink was still wet. He asked the wine shop owner, who replied that several women had just come to drink together, and one of them had asked for a pen to write. She had not gone far. Yang immediately got up and followed her. A few people were walking together, one of whom was wearing purple and carrying a gold horse bowl, with a silk scarf around her neck. She looked at Yang in surprise and dared not openly call out to him. She kept looking at him, signaling for him to follow. By nightfall, the crowd had dispersed, and she led Yang to the gate of a large house. She stood there and said, "Recently, with my husband, we fled to the Huai and Si rivers and were captured by the enemy."

The chief named Sa Ba Taiwei wanted to force me. I am unwilling to accept humiliation. I cut my own throat with a knife but did not die immediately. The wife of the great chief, Lady Korea, heard this and pitied me. She immediately ordered me to be saved and treated, and kept me with her. We parted in a hurry. My husband does not know where he has gone. I seem to hear that he is an official in Jiangnan. I always think and cannot let go. This is Lady Korea's house. Just went out with female companions. Moved by emotion, I wrote on the wall. Did not expect my uncle to see it. Wishes to visit me again at a convenient time. If I can get news of my husband, please inform me. Afraid that people inside the house will come out, dare not stay long. Parted with sadness. Though deeply attached in heart, dare not go back again. One day, just went to the wine shop. Looked at and admired the writing. Suddenly saw new writing on another wall, including a poem of mourning. This is what is said about Han Shihou. Surprised and asked who this is. The wine shop owner said, "The Southern Dynasty sent envoys to establish peace. There were four or five people in the inn. Came to buy wine."

This is presumably what he wrote. At that time, laws and prohibitions had not yet been established, and officials accompanying the envoy could still interact with outsiders. Yang hurried to the residence and indeed saw Han, holding hands and weeping with joy. He told him where Yiniang was. Han was startled and said, "I remember when I was captured; I saw her commit suicide with her own sword. How could she still be alive?" Yang insisted on his previous claim and invited him to come with him. Facing a house, they found it empty and unoccupied, overgrown with weeds as dense as woven fabric. They met an old woman outside the wall spinning thread, and they tried to ask her. The woman said, "Yiniang is indeed here, but she is not alive. Yesterday, Lady of the Korean state pitied her loyalty and righteousness, and since then, her bones have been burned. After the Korean state fell, she was buried here with her. She then pointed out the burial place." The two of them climbed over the wall and entered. Suddenly, they saw from the corridor into the room, and all were frightened. But it was already too late; they followed her. It was the memorial hall of the Korean state, with a painting of Yiniang beside it. Her clothing and appearance were exactly as they had seen before. Han grieved deeply and returned to the residence.

He prepared wine and food, wrote an offering, and performed a libation. He intended to take the remaining ashes back. Bowing and praying, he said, "Whether to go or not to go, please communicate through signs or effects." After a long while, a figure appeared. It said, "Thank you for your love and concern. A lonely spirit resides here. How could I not wish to return? But if you go south with me, you will always look after me well, perhaps to comfort the unknown. If you remarry, no longer pay attention to me, then it would be better not to go south." Han was moved to tears and swore never to remarry. He then secretly exhumed the bones and took them back. He arrived in Jiankang, prepared rituals, and divined the burial site. Every ten days, he would go to visit. Several years later, Han had no family and eventually married. However, he became increasingly distant from his former wife's grave. He dreamed of her coming and was very angry and resentful, saying, "I am very comfortable there. You forced me to come. Now you are breaking your vow. I cannot bear to be alone. I must make you share this experience." Han felt ashamed and terrified, falling ill. Knowing it could not be avoided, he died within a few days. Xu Daoshou Xu Daoshou was originally a Daoist from Jiankang. Later, he returned to being a layperson and lived in front of the imperial ancestral temple in Lin'an. He made a living selling incense, imitating the production of dragon's涎 (a type of incense) from Guangzhou. Even though he used high-quality materials like musk, sandalwood, and frankincense, he mostly made them falsely. His mother had been a widow for a long time. Suddenly, she became pregnant and gave birth to two objects. One took the form of a small child, while the other had a cat's head and a crow's head. Disgusted, they killed them. Within a few days, both mother and child died. This was during the first year of the Longxing era.

#### 滕明之 A man from Lin'an named Teng Mingzhi, who initially served as an official in charge of various departments. He lost his position due to some matter and could not support his wife and children. He then began managing official appointments and seeking to make a profit. He also liked to control people's words, good or bad, and demanded without limit. Those who knew him feared and disliked him. In the autumn of the year Dingmao during the reign of Emperor Gaozong (1147), he told his wife, "I just had a dream where I went to the Wàngxiān Bridge and entered a horse's womb. I woke up in fright. What does this mean?" He then fell ill and died. On the night of his death, all the family heard the sound of horses neighing. His wife later became a prostitute, they say. #### 西池游 During the reign of Emperor Huizong, Zhou Qin, an official in charge of the inner wine warehouse, was leaning on the railing of the Fairy Bridge at the Western Pool in the capital city during the spring outings. He threw bread and bait to feed the fish. The fish swam back and forth, and onlookers crowded around. After a long time, everyone dispersed. Only one woman remained. She pulled Zhou's skirt and spoke to him. When she looked at him, it was the wife of Lao Sheng, a former neighbor who sold medicine. Since moving away, they hadn't heard from each other. She was very happy to see him and asked about her husband. With a grimace, she said, "When we were neighbors, he accused me of having an affair with you. You moved away, but he still often beat and insulted me. I couldn't bear it anymore, so I broke off our relationship. Now I live with my aunt. I heard that you have lost your spouse. I wanted to send a matchmaker to arrange a marriage, but didn't get around to it. I didn't expect to meet you here." Zhou was even happier and invited her into a wine shop. They made a hasty agreement and took her as his wife. After several months, Zhou went out of the city and came back, buying food at the market. Lao Sheng happened to pass by with his medicine bag. Zhou, because he had married her former wife, felt ashamed to see him. He covered his face and wanted to avoid him. Lao Sheng hurriedly came in and bowed. Zhou forced himself to speak with him.

And inquire about his family. Luo lamented, saying, "In early spring, she died of an epidemic. I lost my left and right hand. Her sorrowful memory cannot be forgotten." He then wept. Zhou Kuan tried to comfort and persuade him to leave, but he was greatly surprised. He also suspected that Luo was hiding something and making up a story. Zhou immediately went to his old residence and visited the neighbors. All said that Luo's wife had died. Clearly, they said, "We all participated in the funeral." Zhou became even more disoriented, fearing to return home, and did not know what to do. He drank heavily at an inn and got drunk, then fell asleep. Until nightfall, he came out and walked aimlessly, without knowing where to go. He stumbled upon someone lying on the road and fell, getting wet all over. He got up and continued walking, after only a few dozen steps, he heard continuous shouts of "murderer." Patrol officers followed him, seeing his anxious and hurried appearance, with bloodstains on his body. They all arrested him and took him to the authorities, where he explained his movements in detail. But he could not prove his innocence and was tortured to death in prison. Meanwhile, the real thief escaped to the eastern part of the capital and was caught due to other crimes, detailing the murder case in the capital. A document was sent to Kaifeng, by then, Zhou had already died. This can be called an extraordinary disaster. His son, Ziming, was also executed for evil and rebellion.

Shu Mao Raised Eels and Yellow Eels Shu Mao, a man from Lin'an, Zhejiang, made a living by selling fish and rice. He raised many eels and yellow eels in jars, killing and cooking them immediately. One day, when he opened the jars, he lost all the creatures he had raised. He searched everywhere for them, and they were all clinging to the walls of the house. They were piled up and trying to climb, but had nowhere to go. They were entangled in a way that was frightening. Shu Mao was very frightened. He took them and threw them into the river, vowing never to kill them again and changing to making vegetarian dishes. After several months, his income was very meager, not enough to support his family. So he returned to his old ways. Soon he lost them again, not knowing where they were. He saw them tightly coiled in the well while drawing water. He had no time to check on them. He picked them up and cooked them. It was the spring of the fifth year of the Ganda period. By autumn, an epidemic broke out. Everyone in the household died, but Shu Mao did not. He only had sores all over his body. Every time he got a sore, the heads and mouths of eels and yellow eels would protrude. The pain was especially severe. He died a month later.

During the fourth year of the Xuanhe era, a poor seller of fruits in the capital city encountered a woman one night. She was dressed in beautiful makeup and appeared very attractive. She came to talk to him and invited him to a place where they engaged in playful and intimate behavior. He received several gifts of clothing from her. From then on, he saw her every night, and the benefits he gained increased. His clothes became suddenly fashionable, but his appearance gradually declined. Neither doctors nor sorcerers could cure him. There was a guard commander named Duan who fasted and abstained from food, eating only musk and drinking water. He had the ability to control spirits and demons and was known in the capital as "Duan the Perfumed Guardian." The man's parents went together to pray for help. Duan called to see the boy and said, "Is this creature causing the strange phenomenon?" He had long suspected that the boy must be involved in some misfortune. Indeed, it was so. They then went together to the home of a midwife named Chen's wife. At the entrance of Chen's home, there was a carved wooden statue of a woman, decorated with clothes and jewelry. When it became slightly old or dark, it was repainted and its clothes were renewed. This statue had been there since the time of his ancestors, and no one could remember how many years it had been there. Duan lifted the veil covering the head of the statue and ordered the boy to look at it. The boy saw that it was exactly like the woman he had seen at night. Duan then set up an altar at Chen's home and performed rituals. He lit forty-nine torches and burned the statue. The strange phenomenon ceased.

**The Butcher from Hedong** In Lin'an, there was a butcher named Zheng Liu from Hedong. He slaughtered pigs, but he was the leader of all the butchers. Every morning at the fifth watch, he would slaughter pigs in the workshop. After cutting and dividing the carcasses, the other butchers would carry the meat away. Zheng Liu, however, set up his own shop to slaughter pigs. Once, he hung the meat on the hooks of his butcher's block and used force quite sharply. The hooks were very sharp and injured his hand, with the knife piercing the back of his hand. The pain lasted for more than a month before he healed. Another time, while slaughtering pigs by the stove, he seemed as if something pulled and dragged him into a large pot. His wife and children rushed to save him, but half of his body was boiled to death. **Zhang Yanchengjie** During the Xuanhe period, in the Tianhan Bridge area of the capital city, there was an official who removed his hat and head covering and repeatedly struck his head against the railing. Onlookers surrounded him, but mysteriously, no one could understand the reason. They could not stop him, and when asked, he did not respond. After a long time, his blood and flesh were dripping, and he collapsed to the ground. Patrol officers guarded and watched over him. Toward evening, he slightly recovered, groaning in a tragic manner. He looked around and said, "I am Zhang Yanchengjie. I live in a certain district. Please have someone carry me back home." After being carried home, he became severely weakened. His head swelled and溃烂像一个瓮, and he called for a doctor to treat him. After many days, he slightly recovered. His family asked him about the incident, but he was completely unaware. The wound formed a scab but was itchy to the point of unbearable. If he scratched it all over, it would swell and溃烂 again. Like this, it happened three or four times. After a year, he had not fully recovered and was almost skeletal. The whole family worried that he would not survive. They helped him out of the house, and just as an old servant passed by, he was surprised and asked why. Zhang Yanchengjie told him the reason. **Note:** The term "溃烂" is translated as "溃烂" to maintain the original meaning, as it describes a severe and uncontrolled infection or decay. If a more precise English equivalent is needed, it could be translated as "gangrenous" or "infected beyond healing."

I said, "The Supervisor Du from the Water Management Office applies a miraculous remedy for a severe illness. However, he cannot be persuaded to come. You should make an effort to visit him. Perhaps we can witness and administer the medicine, and you can be cured instantly. Relying on me as a guide, hurry to visit him." The young master Du cleared the room and said, "Do you remember where you were last Mid-Autumn Festival night?" I replied, "I have forgotten." Du said, "I can tell you. That year you were in charge of the rice convoy in Jiangxi. You arrived at Dushu Bay and anchored. The moon was shining brightly. You went ashore with a cane. After about a hundred steps, you found a flat and open place. Just ordered wine to admire the moon. Suddenly, it started raining heavily. You ordered the servant to fetch rain gear, angry that he came slowly, resulting in his clothes and shoes getting wet. You threw the cane at him, hitting him on the forehead." I returned to the boat and told my wife, "I was struck by the master. I already contracted tetanus. I fear that I cannot survive. But have nowhere to appeal. If I die, definitely do not tell the truth. Just say that the chronic illness flared up. This far from home, if they do not tolerate you."

How to survive. One should inform the master. Beg for permission to take your son and mother. Join the boat to enter the capital. Still can follow others to wash and provide for oneself. Spoke and then died. By dawn, the wife raised the body and buried it by the water's edge. She wept and bowed to the man, saying, "My husband unfortunately died on the road. I wish to be allowed to accompany him." The man shouted at her, "The boat is full of men. How could a woman without a husband be aboard?" He paid no heed and urged them to untie the ropes. The wife clutched her chest and wept bitterly, saying, "Stranded in a foreign land, also lacking food. No way to advance or retreat. Might as well die. Hold the young child and jump into the river." I have already died by misfortune. Also grieve that my wife and children did not survive. Appeal to the underworld. Allow me to repay this injustice. Last year when you hit the bridge, it was that he had been searching for you for a long time and finally met you. Zhang Zhen was startled and said, "All of this is true. I was just about to beg for medicine. Why bring this up? And how do you know this?" Du said, "I serve as a clerk during the day, serve in the underworld at night. My job is to handle cases of injustice."

This matter is in my hands. I have repeatedly explained it, but he/she did not listen at all. From now for forty-nine days, I will go to settle with you. On that day, you can sweep and clean the quiet room, light forty-nine lamps, and place a high seat to wait for me. At midnight, there will be something to see. Fortunately, the lamp does not go out. His/Her intention is still good. If half of them go out, then it cannot be done. I also try my best to take care of it. But the injustice of the death sentence depends on whether he/she is willing to give up. The authorities cannot force it. There is no need to use medicine. Zhang wept and thanked, then returned. As instructed, on the night when the lamps were lit, he sat alone on a high couch. Family members waited inside the curtain. Near the third watch, the cold wind was fierce. All forty-nine lamps went out. One of them lit up again. The deceased servant had blood on his face. Wife and children followed, still carrying water dripping. Came out from the corner of the room, pulled Zhang and said, "Can you return my life." Immediately fell down. Head retracted into the neck and died.

In the third year of the Ganda period in the Southern Song dynasty, during the autumn, a great thunderstorm struck Lin'an (Hangzhou). Long Ze and his wife, who worked in the military arsenal workshop, and their son, Guo Seng, all three, were struck dead in one room. Initially, Long Ze's father, Quan, had already died. Long Ze's sister, Tie Shi, lived in Bai Gui Chi as a prostitute. His mother only stayed at the girl's house. When he received his salary in rice, she would come and take three dou of rice away. Long Ze and his wife were quite annoyed by her visits and often spoke harsh words. Guo Seng, also insulted and mocked her, calling her a beggar woman. Therefore, they received this punishment. At the same time, there was a man from Yanzhou named Chen Yongnian, who, along with his brother, opened a silver shop in Lin'an. He roamed wildly without restraint. His mother secretly stored ten or so taels of gold, intending to use it for his funeral expenses, fearing that Yongnian would ask for it without limit. She did not let him know. One day, he opened the chest. Yongnian happened to come from outside and saw it. He immediately seized it and ran away. His mother, filled with anger and grief, fainted and died. His brother caught up and fought with him, only managing to get half of it to give back to his mother. His mother then fell ill and lay in bed. That night, Yongnian also suffered from the thunderstorm.

The Qiantang River Tide The tide of the Qiantang River, on the eighteenth day of the eighth month, reaches its peak, a great sight in the world. The majority of the people in Lin'an (Hangzhou) would go to watch it. In the year of the Song Dynasty, ten years after the founding of the dynasty, two days before the tide, some residents living by the river heard a voice from the sky saying, "This year, hundreds will die on the bridge. They are all evil, lustful, and unfilial people. Among them, those who are supposed to be there but have not arrived, will be sent messengers to hurry them. Those not on the list will be dismissed." The residents were shocked and afraid, and did not dare to speak about it. The next night, someone by the bridge dreamed that someone came to warn them, saying, "Do not go up the bridge tomorrow. The bridge will break." In the morning, they told their neighbors, and their dreams were largely the same. They were all in a state of fear. When the tide was about to arrive, the bridge was already full of people. Those who had the dream watched from the side and tried to persuade their acquaintances to get off the bridge, but they all thought it was a superstition and did not listen. Soon, the tide arrived, and it was more turbulent than usual. The surging waves hit the shore, and the bridge shook and broke into the water. Hundreds of people were crushed and drowned. Afterward, the families of the dead came to weep and gather up their loved ones. They pointed out the people who had died, and they were all the ones who had been unruly and wicked. It was then known that divine forces punish evil, using someone else's hand to carry out the punishment, not by chance.

**A Man from Shaanxi** At the beginning of the Shaoxing era, Henan had been occupied by the puppet regime of False Qi. The Shumiyuan dispatched an envoy, Li Zhong, to conduct espionage. Li was originally from the state of Jin; he was bold in manner and fond of making connections. Many people knew him. Upon arriving at the capital, he met an old friend, Tian Xiang. Xiang was a rascal. Upon learning that Li had come from the south, Xiang knew that according to the law, he should be executed. He reported Li to the authorities, for the reward was very generous. Immediately, he seized Li and said, "You once lent me three hundred *guan* of money. You can return it to me." Li angrily replied, "How could that be? I would rather die." A man from Shaanxi named Liu heard of this matter. He told Li that he knew well that Xiang was unjust. "But now you are like a man who has fallen into a trap. How can you argue about right and wrong? Which is more valuable, your life or your money? Moreover, this could ruin a major matter. Why not adapt and bribe him?" Li still doubted that Liu was acting as Xiang’s agent, but he had no choice but to give him half the money. Liu said, "Don’t take it to heart. I will return it to you in due time." Li pretended to agree, saying, "That would be very fortunate." Xiang received the money and used it to buy goods. He intended to go to Jin and Jiang. Liu said, "I also wish to go there. May we travel together?" They then traveled together. They passed through Hezhong Prefecture and briefly rested on the riverbank. Each carried a servant with a bundle.

They sat together on the sand, looking around and seeing no one. Liu asked Xiang about his hometown and age, and Xiang answered him in detail. Liu said, "Then you are a citizen of China. You have eaten the soil and water of the Song Dynasty." Xiang replied, "Indeed." Liu said, "I too am a remnant of the Song Dynasty. Unfortunately, I have fallen into the false land. I often regret not being able to serve. The court often sends people to investigate, and they often gather hearsay and gossip, which cannot get the truth. Fortunately, there are honest people like Li San. We should contribute our efforts to help him. Why instead take advantage of him for profit?" Xiang hesitated and said, "He has certainly betrayed me." Liu said, "I have always known this. And I have thoroughly investigated, and I have gotten a very detailed understanding. I have no grudge against you, but I am afraid of Southern scholars. They might say that all northerners are like you, which would damage my reputation for loyalty and righteousness." So he struck with an axe and killed him, and his servant also killed Xiang's servant. He threw the body into the river and returned to the capital with his belongings, giving everything to Li. Then he told him the reason. Li wanted to give half the value as a reward. Liu laughed and said, "I would not kill someone to seek profit." They bowed deeply and parted. Li returned south and told this story, but lost Liu's name, which is a pity.

**Two Inverse Retributions** A villager named Yao Er from Gusu made a living by fishing. He was cruel and disrespectful to his parents. In the 23rd year of the Shaoxing era (1153), his wife had just given birth to a male infant who was still breastfeeding. Yao Er's mother was holding the baby when, due to her weak hands, the baby accidentally fell to the ground and died. Fearing her son's cruelty, the mother did not know what to do. Yao Er did not consider it a significant issue. One day, he told his mother, "I haven't been to my father-in-law's house for a long time. I caught a big fish by chance and want to go give it as a gift. Can we go together?" The mother was overjoyed and agreed. She packed her belongings and boarded the boat. After traveling several miles, they reached a deserted place. Yao Er stopped the boat and held an axe in front of his mother. Glaring and cursing, he said, "Since you gave birth to me, you should know how to cherish me. Now that I have a child, how can I not love him? Why did you intentionally let him fall and die? You should pay for his life." The mother quickly covered her head and face with a blanket and said, "I will let you do whatever you want." Yao Er raised the axe to attack his mother. The mother knew she was going to die. After a long time, everything became silent. She lifted the blanket to see and did not see her son. But the boat was already at the shore near their home. After returning home, the wife cried and said there was no cloud in the sky. A loud thunderclap struck, and Yao Er was struck dead in the field. His body was covered with large axe wounds. Not knowing how this happened, the mother began to recount the events. She did not hear any thunder and did not feel the boat moving back. The family of Yao Er died out. This account was recorded by an official from Zhongzhou.

Volume Ten, Thirteenth Story of the *Yijian Dingshi* In the county of Dengcheng, there was a witch who could use magical arts to ruin the wine brewed by wine merchants. All those who operated wine shops were afraid of and served her. Every spring and autumn, she would visit all the wine shops to collect annual payments. She would receive twenty thousand coins from each of about ten households, which ensured that their wine would sell well throughout the year. The witch relied on these payments for her own sustenance and had no worries about hunger or poverty. One year, due to other matters, she was in a tight financial situation. She then went to a wealthy household to make a request, saying, "Your family is the richest and most well-off. You have the means to support me. I hope you will not limit me to the usual amount." The owner of the household rejected her request firmly, saying, "Year after year, we give you twenty thousand coins. This has been going on for a long time. How can you suddenly ask for more? We cannot afford to ruin our wine business. Not even one coin can be given to you." The witch laughed and left, stopping at a nearby store. She sent a servant back to buy one measure of wine, which she placed in a small jar. She mixed it with feces and carried it to a forest. There, she performed a ritual by walking in the manner of Yu and reciting spells, circling around several times before burying the jar in the ground and leaving. Just then, a Taoist priest passed by and saw her. He recognized her as an evil spirit but did not know the cause of the disturbance. The witch left in a mocking manner. Soon after, the Taoist priest also came to visit the wine shop, where he saw the owner greatly worried and distressed. He asked the reason for the distress.

The person said, "I have been troubled by a witch." Now, the wine jars are arranged in a row, all emitting the smell of feces. Afraid that the stench will continue endlessly, he wants to go find the trace and beg for help. The Taoist said, "I have also seen this person. There is no need to go look for them. I have a method to treat it. But once it is ruined, it cannot be saved." He immediately burned incense and performed rituals. After half a day, the stench stopped. He also said that anyone who uses this method to ruin grains has committed a great sin with feces. Your family should fast and abstain from worldly pleasures, and should be presented as a petition to the heavens. The family was angry and in a hurry, hastily setting up a ritual banquet. The Taoist lay down in the courtyard. After several moments, he got up. He said, "The Jade Emperor has issued an order. Within a hundred days, he will be afflicted with a karmic disease. But he is not ordered to die." From then on, the witch felt an itch in his ankle, scratching incessantly. Suddenly, a tumor grew, initially like a water chestnut. Over several days, it grew larger, towering a foot in diameter, like a ball. And it was held by only a thin thread. If it is slightly touched by something, then the pain penetrates the heart and limbs. He cannot walk on the ground anymore. His descendants wove bamboo to make a bed, carrying him on a stretcher to beg. Food, urine, and feces mix in the bed. Everyone covers their nose when he passes by. After ten years, he died. Shangshu Hu Shaoji governed the county and still saw him. His son Guo said.

**Xu LouTai** In Dangtu, there was an external medicine practitioner named Xu LouTai. His family had been skilled in treating boils and abscesses for generations. They were therefore famous for their expertise. The family's reputation was marked by a painted pavilion sign at their door, which gave them their name. By the time it reached his grandson, a scholar who had passed the local examination, he had become particularly adept at the family's traditional practice. In the eighth year of the Shaoxing era, a wealthy man named Jiang Shunming from Wax Mountain in Lizhui County suffered from a boil on his back and knocked on the door to seek medical treatment. Xu LouTai said that the condition could be treated and made an agreement with the family, promising 300,000 coins if the patient recovered. For ten days of treatment, the patient's diet and spirits remained normal, and he showed no signs of weakness. However, when he began to feel slight pain and itching, Xu LouTai explained that the abscess was about to burst and that draining the pus would lead to recovery. That night, while the guests watched, Xu LouTai used a needle to prick the abscess and inserted a piece of paper, about five inches long and as thick as a string of coins, into the opening. Jiang Shunming cried out in pain, and the cries grew louder and more frequent.

Meng said, "In addition, reward me with twenty-five taels of silver." He quickly took out paper. The pus began to drain, and the pain was expected to immediately subside. Jiang Yuan became angry and firmly refused to give. He said, "The original agreement is not small. Tonight there is no issue, and I will immediately repay tomorrow." Meng insisted on getting the reward. Jiang's relative Yuan Chuo was also present and told Yuan, "The patient's pain is at its utmost. Why be reluctant to give this?" Then he gave him half. At that time, the paper thread had already been inserted for more than one watch of the night. When it was removed, blood gushed forth like a spring. The patient's cries gradually grew faint. Meng was surprised to think the pain had subsided. The family looked at him and saw that he had died. The pus continued to flow out. Within a year, Meng fell ill with a fever and cried out in agony without ceasing. He only said, "Shunming, do not hit me. I am indeed not. Your son is also not." In this manner, he died after several days. The two sons followed their mother in remarriage, and the family doctor then ceased or died out.

Fu Zhujiao A man from Fuli Town in Xuancheng, Fu Zhujiao, was skilled in treating abscesses. However, his intentions were very unseemly. He was entirely greedy for bribes. Even when a patient's sores were not toxic, he would first use medicine to make them toxic. The hidden evils he committed were too numerous to count. Once, he entered the city to treat someone's illness and was about to leave. On his way back, he went to the market to buy fruit and sat in a shop. Suddenly, a yellow-robed officer appeared before him and said, "Are you Fu Zhujiao?" The underworld is calling you. He showed him a piece of paper in his hand, with two or three characters written in rows. Everyone in the market saw it and suspected that the names on the paper were of those being summoned. Fu said, "Will the messenger allow me to go home?" The officer replied, "I will take you away immediately. Hurry and go home. The deadline is seven days." Then the officer disappeared. The entire city spread the rumor that Fu Zhujiao had been taken by a ghost. When he returned to Fuli Town and was about to board a boat, the yellow-robed officer was already standing on the riverbank. He raised the bamboo stick he was holding and tapped Fu's back. Fu cried out in extreme pain. The officer said, "So you do know pain." The spot he tapped immediately formed a large abscess like a bowl. Fu cried out for seven days and nights before dying.

Doctor Lu Yang of Shuiyang Village In the Shuiyang Village of Xuancheng County, there was a village doctor named Lu Yang, whose courtesy name was Yiruo. He was renowned for his medical skills. During the Jianyan period, a northern person named Zhu Xinlao, who held the title of bianxiu, fled south to avoid the chaos and took his family to live on a boat. His wife was suffering from restlessness and called upon Lu Yang to treat her. The wife explained that she had been weak in blood and qi throughout her life and could not tolerate cooling medicines. Although she was now feeling restless, she did not feel thirsty. Her condition was caused by fear and worry due to fleeing from bandits and losing her appetite. She warned that it would be dangerous to prescribe cooling medicines based solely on the external symptoms. Zhu Xinlao, who had a drinking problem and suffered from thirst, always preferred cooling medicines. She advised that there was no need to discuss this with him. She had a private collection of pearls that could be used as payment for the medicine. She asked Lu Yang to buy good medicine to treat her and wanted him to understand her true condition. She earnestly told him this. Lu Yang diagnosed her pulse and considered it to be a yang symptom of typhoid fever. He boiled a small dose of Zhihu Decoction to bring to her. The woman said, "The fragrance is similar to that of Zhihu. You should examine it carefully. If I take this, I will die immediately." Lu Yang replied, "No, please calm your heart and drink it."

The woman spoke earnestly and urgently again. Lu remained unchanged. As soon as she swallowed the substance, she experienced simultaneous vomiting and diarrhea. She then became exhausted and weakened. She continued to call out, saying, "Lu Assistant Teacher, deal with you in hell." After these words, she died. Several years later, a young man named Li, who was suffering from a wasting disease, summoned Lu. Lu worked on him for several days without success. One day, Lu went out to drink with a courtesan and demanded money and food from Li's family. Li's brother became angry and refused to give him what he demanded. By nightfall, Lu, while drunk, administered a large number of pills to Li. Li said, "The medicine is in my chest, burning like fire." He also said, "When it reaches my stomach, it is still like fire." And he said, "When it reaches my lower abdomen, it is still like fire." Shortly after, he screamed in unbearable pain and fell from the bed due to severe trembling and fear. By midnight, Lu hastily administered a medicine containing aconite and cinnabar, but Li was unable to swallow it. Lu secretly took a boat and fled. Before dawn, Li died. In the ninth year of the Shaoxing era, Lu suddenly fell ill and called out day and night, saying, "Zhu Lady, Li Young Man, stop punishing me. I will go." Ten days later, he died.

Qin Chucai During the Jinghe period, Qin Chucai was presented from Jiankang to the capital. He stayed in a guesthouse by the Bian River. After he had gone to bed, he heard people outside shouting very loudly. They locked all the rooms. He got up and peeped through a hole in the wall. There were dozens of strong men, all dressed in brocade clothes and flower hats, bowing and kneeling before the statue of a god, calling out Qin's name, and throwing the yarrow stalks to ask. In front, they had set up a large pot with melted fat boiling. Qin was shivering with fear, not knowing what to do, and repeatedly told his servant Li Fu, wanting to plan for suicide. It was almost the fourth watch of the night. The strong men prayed repeatedly but did not succeed, then poured the oil on the ground and left. The next morning, the host opened the door and apologized to Qin, saying, "The scholar's future is unpredictable. Otherwise, we would all be imprisoned." He then explained about the wicked young men in the capital, who gathered in groups of tens, sometimes every three years, sometimes every five years, capturing people and immersing them in the oil, cooking them to worship the ghost called the Fierce Stare God. Each time they worshipped, they must take a handsome man. You nearly died but escaped. How dangerous it was! He looked at the other guests in the inn, and each contributed money as an offering. Qin only remembered passing through Suzhou and encountering these dozen or so bandits.

He followed either ahead or behind, eventually arriving at the highest school. He was quite pleased with himself and invited his fellow students to go out for divination. On the way, he met a tattooed hermit carrying a small basket. The hermit bowed to Qin and said, "It has been three hundred years since our separation on Mount Accumulated Gold. We could not find each other, and we mistakenly took the wrong path. Yet here we are!" He had nothing to give Qin, so he took a piece of white gold from the basket and gave it to him, saying, "We will meet again someday." Qin's fellow students happily said, "This is an unexpected thing. It is not appropriate to enjoy it alone." They led Qin to a shop to sell it, to provide money for food and drink. The shopkeeper looked at the gold, repeatedly admired it, and could not put it down. They asked how much it was worth, and Qin replied, "It can be sold at market price." The shopkeeper said, "My family has been making silverware for generations. We have never seen such a thing." They turned to others, and what they said was the same. Qin also realized the supernatural nature of the encounter and did not sell it. Instead, he used it to make cups for wine, spoons for tea, and medical instruments, totaling five items. He used them daily. From then on, for thirty years, he had no illness or suffering. In the sixteenth year of the Shaoxing era, while in Xuancheng, he suddenly fell ill. The five items were lost at the same time, and he knew he would not recover. Indeed, he died after a month. Mount Accumulated Gold is located at the Yuanfu Palace in Maoshan.

In the early Zhenghe era, the schools in Jiankang were flourishing. There was a Buddhist monk who frequented the halls of learning. He repeatedly visited the entrance of the prestigious Yangwang Hall but did not leave. Two scholars named Qian and Fan, who were studying there, approached him and asked, "What do you do, Master?" The monk replied, "Strange things, strange things. The high-ranking officials are all confined to one place. The two major government offices are just as numerous, but they are not very prestigious." One of the officials, a man named Qin, who was known for his long legs, was pointed out by Fan, who had always looked down on him. Fan said, "This long-legged man will become a high-ranking official." A guest said, "Don't speak recklessly. Your fate will be in his hands." Everyone in the room laughed. The guest, astonished, said, "You all shouldn't laugh. None of you can compare to this person." At the time, there were ten students in the same dormitory, and only Xing Zhi was the most talented and charismatic, highly esteemed by his peers. Just as he had arrived from outside, the others asked him, "What will you become?" Xing Zhi replied, "I will become a minor official," with no praise. He then left. Over the next forty years, all the predictions came true. Qin became the Grand Tutor, Fan Zeshan, Wei Daobi, Liang Chen, and three others became high-ranking officials. He Ren, Ruo Wu Zixian, and two others became high-ranking officials in the Privy Council. Qian Duanxiu, Shi Min, Yuan Ying, and Zhou Cai became high-ranking officials. One student's name is forgotten. Only Xing Zhi, who became a minor official, died shortly after.

Master of the Profound Origin Shen Ruojie, from Lin'an, built a hermitage on Mount Mao. He dedicated himself to dispensing medicine.
