During the reign of Emperor Huizong, he was summoned for an audience and bestowed the title of Master of the Profound Origin. Once, he pointed to a gap of land east of Huayang Cave and said, "I must be buried here upon my death." His disciples argued that the terrain was unsuitable, but he did not listen. He passed away in the 15th year of Emperor Gaozong's reign. His disciples carried out his instructions and dug six feet or so into the ground, finding a stone slab. On it were inscribed six large characters: "Master Shen buried his sword here." Those who saw it were amazed, wondering if this wasn't a sign of divine foreknowledge. The saying of the fine tomb and the painted lamp is indeed true. The above six events are all said by Tang Sanyi.

**Heavenly Gate Assignments** In the county of Ningdu, Ganzhou, there is a temple dedicated to Lord Hu. The deity's name is Xiong, and he was a local resident. He was born with a peculiar appearance and could see his own ears. After his death, he manifested divine powers, able to bring misfortune or blessings to people. The villagers therefore built a temple for him. At the beginning of the Chongning era, a scholar named Sun Xie Zhikang dreamed of an old man with white hair inviting him to his home. The old man asked, "How can I receive a title and rank?" Sun thought the old man was the deity and replied, "You should perform good deeds. Do not harm others." The old man said, "I would never harm others. I am assigned duties by the heavenly gate and daily manage the affairs of this region, including bringing misfortune or blessings to people. This is something I hear from those close to me. They might claim it is my doing and cause confusion. You should know that if you can fully assist in saving people from seasonal droughts and floods, the county magistrate and prefect will report this to the court, and you will receive a title and rank immediately." Upon waking, Sun realized the old man was Lord Hu. Five years later, in the fifth year of the Chongning era, a fire broke out in the county. The villagers prayed at the temple, and soon after, strong winds and clouds arose, as if something were driving the fire away, and the fire was extinguished. The county reported the incident to the prefecture, and the court bestowed the title of "Boji Temple" upon the temple. The following year, the deity was posthumously honored as Lingzhe Hou. Ah, the deity, having been appointed by the heavens, still greedily covets worldly fame, fearing not to obtain it. This shows that the bestowal of titles and ranks is indeed a significant matter. Sun Xie Zhikang, in his dream, understood the deity in such a way, truly demonstrating the virtue of an upright scholar. This account was written by Li Xun.

**The Lame Tiger of Dahan Mountain** At the Dahan Mountain Temple in Suizhou, there was a villa known as Luohu Manor. In the 11th year of the Shaoxing era (1141 CE), a monk from the manor sent a message to the elder monk Master Jingyan Suo, reporting that there was a lame tiger on the road, which had been emerging and causing harm to people. Travelers now dared not ascend the mountain, greatly fearing that the supply of offerings to the temple would no longer be sustained. Master Jingyan immediately ordered a sedan chair and descended to the place where the tiger had passed. Arriving there, he got off the chair and burned paper money. He then saw the tiger approaching from a distance and signaled to his attendants and the temple monks, who all retreated. Alone, he sat on a trapezoidal chair and waited. After a while, the tiger approached and crouched beside him, lowering its ears as if listening to commands. At that time, Zhang Teng, the county official and inspector of Zhaoyang and Suizhou, had just received an official summons from the prefectural government and came to the temple to collect taxes from two villages. He also went along, climbing a tall tree to observe closely. He did not know what Master Jingyan had said, but the tiger suddenly moved away. From then on, it never appeared again. This is recorded in *Hanedong Zhi*.

Zhang Taiqing's Poem In the current dynasty, it is customary for Hanlin scholars to draft edicts for chancellors. Sometimes they are appointed to high offices, a practice known as "being brought in." In the third year of Dàguān (1109), on the eighth day of the sixth month, He Qingyuan Zhizhong was promoted to a high office. In the fourth year of Dàguān (1110), on the eighth day of the sixth month, Zhang Wujin Shangying was also promoted to a high office. Both of these edicts were drafted by Zhang Taiqing in the Hanlin Academy. Despite his contributions, Zhang did not receive further promotion or favor. At the time, Cai Jing had been demoted to the position of Grand Tutor to the Crown Prince. Zhang was tasked with drafting the edict for this demotion and criticized Cai Jing very severely. This criticism was widely praised by the literati, but Cai Jing bore a grudge. When Cai Jing resumed his position as chancellor, he immediately sent Zhang to serve as the governor of Hangzhou. The following year, on the eighth day of the sixth month, Zhang hosted a banquet in the Zhonghe Hall. Suddenly, he thought of the two previous years when he was on duty and appointed as chancellor, which coincidentally fell on the same day. He composed a short poem to record the event: Long sky, scattered with afterglow, distant harbor, calm tide, Standing on a high railing, gazing at the riverbank, I recall year after year of honor. Today is no different from those days. Twice I was appointed as chancellor, Frequently writing under the emperor's approval, with grace and dignity, The first time I was given a cup of tea by the emperor, the scent of incense on my sleeves. Returning to the imperial palace late at night, after sealing the document, The light of a candle just began to burn. The emperor spoke earnestly, He had once personally praised me with his robes. Now I merely dream of it, Lamenting the distance between my current life and the life of a high official. Feeling bored, I tried to drown my sorrows with song and wine. Those who heard the poem admired its beauty but were surprised by its final lines, which expressed a sense of disappointment. Soon after, Zhang was recalled to the capital on false charges and died in office at the age of forty. Zhang Taiqing was from Heyang. According to Wu Chuanpeng.

New Prison In the village of Xinxian in Yuzhang, during a summer night, a group of villagers gathered to cool off. Suddenly, a man rushed over from somewhere else, covering his abdomen and shouting, "Someone killed me!" He ran to his home and died immediately. His family reported the incident to the county magistrate. The magistrate arrested a certain man and interrogated him. The man claimed that on that night, he was a guest at a certain place and had no connection with the deceased. The investigation proved inconclusive. The magistrate then arrested all twenty people who had been cooling off and divided them into groups. He made each prisoner describe what they had seen. All said that they had actually heard his words as described, but they knew nothing else. The county magistrate was determined to uncover the truth and subjected the prisoners to unbearable beating and torture. They were forced to make a joint statement to confirm the man's guilt. When brought to participate in the interrogation, the man could not withstand the pressure and was falsely accused and forced to admit guilt. He looked up to the sky and shouted, "I indeed killed someone. I do not dare to escape punishment. If I am wronged, I wish that heaven would make the witnesses die in prison to verify it." Not ten days later, an epidemic broke out in the prison. All ten prisoners died one after another. The county magistrate knew that it was so, but also feared that the criminal would not be captured. He finally did not release this person, who also died in the end.

In the seventh year of the Gan Dao era (1167 AD), Chen Youyi from Jinyun traveled from Fujian to Guangdong to visit his father, who was a maritime customs official. As he passed through Chao Yang, he heard local people say that in recent years, the governor of Huizhou had taken his family from Fuzhou to take up his new post, passing through this place. This area has many wild elephants, which gather in groups of hundreds. During the autumn harvest season, the villagers feared that the elephants would trample and eat their rice, so they set traps in the fields to prevent them from causing damage. The elephants could not find food and became very angry. They gathered together to surround the governor, lasting half a day without resolving. The governor's attendants, numbering one or two hundred, looked at each other without knowing how to help. The governor's family was in distress and fear, and some even died of fright. The village guards realized the elephants' intentions and quickly gathered the crowd to carry rice, piling it around the perimeter. The elephants still ignored it, but they waited until the pile was full before breaking the encirclement to eat. They were thus saved from disaster. The elephants used strategy to get food, attacking where they knew help would come. They are a strange and different species, so intelligent. However, they are a great harm to Chao, not inferior to crocodiles in terms of harm. This is the account of Chen Youyi.

Liu Zuo Wu Liu Zuo Wu, a man from Hebei, came south to a county in Jiangxi. He lived for thirty years and died. Within a few years, his wife and several children died one after another. Only his daughter-in-law and young grandson remained. His family wealth was originally not abundant, and all was squandered by a servant. By the time five funerals were in progress, he could not afford to bury them. His nephew Zong Shui, who was the son-in-law of a local person named Tu, and his brother-in-law Beniu, helped him with a thousand coins and went together to his home to offer condolences. Zong Shui recently followed his father to be the magistrate of Jing'an, bringing a young attendant with him. On that day, he accompanied them. Suddenly, he ascended to the hall and sat at the table, in the manner of greeting guests for Liu Jun. He called out to his servant and scolded, saying, "My family of five have not yet been buried. What is this time? How can you squander my livelihood to bring it to this extreme? It is not because of the kindness of my brother-in-law that I am in this situation. What should I do?" He stood up with clasped hands and approached Beniu, intending to express thanks, but Beniu avoided and did not meet him. He then scolded the daughter-in-law, saying, "It is because of your lack of understanding that we are in this situation. What can I say now?" He then scolded the servant, saying, "You are a foolish person not worth asking. I will not complain to the underworld. The reason I scold you is just to make you know that although the realms of the living and the dead are different, they cannot be deceived." The servant only bowed his head and dared not answer. Zong Shui disliked his long stay and repeatedly scolded and chased him away, and recited the *Tianpeng* spells loudly. He then glared and said, "I will leave in a moment; why do this?" After about five or six moments, he left. The young attendant suddenly stood up, without any sensation. Beniu said.

Volume Eleven, Fourteen Stories of the *Yijian* Series **Tian the Daoist** Tian the Daoist was a man from Hebei who fled south to avoid chaos and settled in Jingkou. Every March, there was a gathering of immortals or Daoists at Mount Mao, and he intended to go with his disciples, but was always stopped by some reason. In the spring of Jiawu (1159 CE), he finally managed to go once. He stayed for more than a month. On his way back, he suddenly developed severe foot pain and could not walk. After stopping, he immediately recovered, but when he tried to walk again, the pain returned. This happened repeatedly. He thought that the cause was related to this mountain and prayed to the gods for a permanent solution. From then on, he no longer got sick. In a dream, a god told him, "This is not your place to live. You have your own hermitage in the mountain. The site facing east is it. You should quickly find it." He thought it was a sign of his thoughts and did not fully believe it. After several nights, he dreamed the same as before, still undecided. He thought about standing naked here. Even if he found the site, even a simple hut would be difficult to build. That night, the god angrily told him in a dream, "If you do not move within ten days, you will surely die here." In the morning, he sought out his fellow Daoists and asked them to help find the site, but they could not find it. Someone said, "I heard that the place where Great Master Mao hid his elixir is called Dansha Hong. It is located to the east. I only know the name, but I do not know where it is. Why not ask the elders?" No one knew either. The deadline of ten days was approaching, and he feared and dared not be lazy. He wandered alone on a narrow path. Suddenly, a villager grabbed his chest, and he was in fear. The person then asked, "Are you not looking for the site of the Dansha Hong hermitage? I know." He led Tian to a secluded place.

A peasant stomped the ground with his foot and said, "This is the place." Tian looked around and saw the vast, embracing forest. It was just right for a house facing east. Tian was very pleased and rewarded the peasant with a hundred coins. The peasant smiled and said, "I am not seeking this. What use do I have for it?" He left without looking back. Tian marked the path and returned the next day. He cut down thorns and brambles, made a hut with bamboo mats, and stayed there. At midnight, a large tiger came and lay outside. It left at dawn. Under the rock, there was a snake, partially showing its back and ribs, as big as a pillar. None of them harmed people. The next day, he hired workers to level the ground with shovels and picks. Under three to four feet of accumulated leaves, he found a flat stone, rough and hollow, several feet long and wide, as if dug by claws. When opened, a stone lotus basin was found, with a block of cinnabar immersed in water, weighing about twenty taels. He took it and stored it. It was where the peasant had stomped the ground the previous day. After that, neither the snake nor the tiger were seen again. It was suspected to be a protective charm of Wei Dan. During the years 1164 and 1165 of the Song Dynasty, an epidemic broke out nearby. Tian ground the cinnabar into powder, mixed it into pills, and used them to treat the sick. Those who took the pills all recovered. He saved thousands of people. They collectively provided wood, stone, money, and grain to build a hermitage in the pond, not far from the Jade Morning Temple. He treated illnesses by spreading qi (energy), and it was often effective. In 1169, Lan Shiji, the governor of the eastern part of Jiang, passed through Mount Mao and personally saw Tian's account. He received three taels of cinnabar. In 1171, he gave the hermitage to the grandson of Yang He Wang and left the mountain with a determined spirit, without saying where he was going.

**Bottle of Peach Blossoms** Shi Meng Chuyi Qufei served as the governor of Chu Prefecture. On the Lantern Festival, he entertained his guests by creating plum blossoms from reed and attaching them to peach branches, which were then inserted into two copper vases that were never filled with water. After mid-spring, the peach branches suddenly bloomed with abundant flowers, which fell after a few days. Shi took great delight in this. In the fall, there was a family tragedy. The following year, he became the governor of Huai Prefecture.

**The Filial Woman of Fengcheng** In the third year of the Gandao era, a great flood struck Jiangxi. Many people living near the river went to other places in search of food. In Fengcheng, there was a farmer who took his mother, wife, and two children and intended to go to Lichuan. Along the way, they passed by a small stream. The husband secretly told his wife: "Grain is now expensive and food is scarce. Our family of five is difficult to sustain together. I will carry the two children across first. You may follow afterward. My mother is already seventy years old, with old age and illness; she is of no use and only burdens others. Just leave her here. He will certainly not be able to cross the water. To reduce our numbers by one is also a fortunate thing." So he crossed the stream and went north. The wife, moved by her grandmother's old age, could not bear to abandon her. She supported her as they walked, and they sank into a marsh. She bent down to pick up her shoe, but a stone blocked her hand. She pushed it aside, and it turned out to be a slab of silver. The woman was greatly delighted and said to her grandmother: "We originally left our home out of poverty, intending to move to another place. I did not expect that heaven would grant us this fortune. It is not only enough to eat, but can also serve as a small means of livelihood. We should return, then—why go away?" She once again supported her grandmother to the shore and crossed the stream alone to inform her husband. When she arrived, she saw the children playing in the sand and asked where their father was. The children said, "He just arrived here and was taken into the forest by a yellow-and-black-spotted cow." She immediately ran to the forest to look for him, and found that he had been eaten by a tiger. Blood flowed and stained the ground, and only his bones and hair remained. The punishment for filial impiety came swiftly upon him. At that time, Lan Shucheng was the magistrate of Lichuan. A guest, the father of Huang Qinbiao, came from Fengcheng and said that the story had been told to him by a local of that stream—it had happened only a few days before. These three events were all told by Lan Shucheng.

The Temple of Li Wei Gong In the eastern part of Wenzhou city, there stands the Temple of Li Wei Gong from the Tang Dynasty. The people of the state often pray earnestly and seek guidance through dreams, and none fails to respond. In the 32nd year of the Shaoxing era, the scholar Mu Daiwen, recommended by the transport commissioner, visited the temple to ask about gains and losses. In his dream, he saw himself wearing a purple robe standing alone in a field. Thousands of scholars were carrying a coffin and running away, all turning back to look at Mu Daiwen. The next morning, he told his fellow student Pan Cheng about the dream. Pan Cheng interpreted it, saying, "You will be the top scholar in the imperial examination. The character for 'coffin' consists of 'wood' and 'official.' You will surely become an official. The thousands who carried the coffin will all come below you." Indeed, it turned out as he said. At the same time, another scholar from the same prefecture, Mu Zi Zheng, also dreamed that a deity told him, "Next year, another top scholar will emerge from this prefecture. His name will be Mu Fēi." Mu Zi Zheng believed this was a divine message and thought he would follow after Wang Shipeng. He changed his name to Fēi. However, when the examinations were held, Mu Fēi did not pass, while Mu Daiwen was admitted. Mu Zi Zheng then realized that the character "木" (wood) could be interpreted as a cross, and by moving two strokes and combining them with the top part of "棐," it formed the character "朋." Below it, there was another "木," indicating that those who followed after would have the surname Mu but not the name Fēi.

**天随子** In the sixth year of the Ganda period, Mu Yunzhi was waiting for the vacancy of the Tongpan position in Nanchang. Living in the countryside, his house was burned down by fire, and all his household affairs were nearly depleted. He composed a poem about enduring poverty, saying, "Enduring poverty is like enduring a painful treatment. Once the pain is over, the disease is cured." Others value a full meal, unaware of death. Ruan Ji cried at the end of the road, and Fan Zhongyan thanked the five ghosts. The hearts of sages and wise men across generations cannot be bound by worries and joys. Studying classics while facing hunger, how great is the spirit of Tian Suizi! One can still be resurrected in the nine original fields. I should till the fields of Fuli. A year later, he had a dream where an elderly man with a dignified appearance appeared and said, "Do you recognize me? I am Tian Suizi. You like to read my writings, and you have written my 'Chǐ Jú Fu' on the wall. Recently, you composed a poem about enduring hunger, which perfectly aligns with my thoughts. Therefore, I came to see you. I will give you a word. I once had four hectares of land, and the harvest was usually sufficient. Only when there was flooding did the crops get submerged and lost. Enduring hunger while reciting scriptures, this is the time I am referring to."

You have a fire disaster, and it is followed by poverty and destitution. It's as if water brought disaster to me while fire brought calamity to you. My land is still there, but it is infested with flies and mosquitoes and cannot be cultivated. There is no one who can drive them away, so I cannot help but trouble you. Upon waking, I did not understand what I had said. In the morning, I happened to organize the books I had read the previous night, and a volume of the *笠泽丛书* was placed on the table. I looked at it and saw that it was the biography of *甫里先生*. I had not read it before. The passage says that the gentleman had ten thousand steps of land. In Wu, one acre is two hundred and fifty steps, and he had forty fewer cows. He had over a hundred laborers, but the land was low and marshy. After a day and night of summer rain, it became indistinguishable from the river, with no difference between one's own fields and others'. The gentleman thus suffered from hunger and poverty, with no stored grain in the granary. This matches the words in the dream, and there were two dead flies stuck on the character for 'field.' I greatly sighed and found it strange, so I wiped them away.

Võng Kiều Huệ Thúc Ascended the Cloud Ladder In the spring of 1179, during the reign of Emperor Xiaozong of Song, Võng Kiều Huệ Thúc from Putian passed the provincial examination but had not yet taken the final imperial examination. In a dream, he saw a ladder floating in the air, surrounded by clouds. He thought to himself, "Is this the legendary cloud ladder that the world speaks of?" Soon, he found himself at the side of the cloud ladder and climbed it. When he reached the upper levels and looked up, he saw a large stone that appeared like a mirror. Fearing it might crush him, he suddenly felt the stone rise slowly and ascend, standing on it. He awoke in surprise and joy but did not understand the meaning of climbing the stone. Later, he became the top scholar in the country. The second place was taken by Shí Qǐzōng from Wēnlíng. Initially, the examiners ranked Shí first, but just before announcing the results, they changed the order. These three events are all part of a larger narrative. The Prophecy of Jin Creek Crossing Jin Creek Crossing in Nan'an County, Quanzhou, is several miles from the county, about a hundred paces wide, with dangerous rapids and deep waters, making it impossible to build a bridge. An old prophecy said, "When Jin Creek allows people to pass, the top scholar will be born." The people of the county wanted to fulfill this prophecy. Many people with the surname Jin changed their names to Jin, and many people with the surname Fang changed their names to Fang. However, none of them fulfilled the prophecy. Jiang Jingshi, a government official, returned to Quanzhou after mourning his mother in the capital. In 1127, he chose a burial site on the south bank of the crossing. The workers faced daily dangers. The county magistrate, Jiang Gongjin, ordered the temporary construction of a small bridge using bamboo rafts, which could only accommodate light traffic. Soon, the bridge was destroyed by the water. That year, Liang Chéngxiàng was born. The prophecy "When Jin Creek allows people to pass, the top scholar will be born" came true in this way.

The Yellow Dragon Creek of Nan'an In Nan'an County, there is a creek named Yellow Dragon. In the fourth year of the Gandao era, the county magistrate of Tiantai, Lu He, hurried back to the prefectural office. As he passed by the school gate, he heard people shouting. The sedan chair bearers all stopped and looked in surprise. He asked them what had happened, and they said that a dragon had appeared on the Yellow Dragon Creek. Lu He stopped his carriage and carefully observed. In the midst of the turbulent waves, a creature several zhang tall emerged, its head and horns clearly visible, appearing and disappearing. Suddenly, a loud thunderclap shook the air, and smoke and fog obscured everything. The creature soared into the sky. Many people saw its tail. Lu He was shocked and astonished, realizing that this place must have auspicious signs. He composed a poem to encourage the students, with the lines: "The chicken's crossing fulfilled the prophecy of that day; the dragon of the creek still shows auspicious signs today." Many scholars and officials joined in harmony. The following year, during the imperial examination, a county resident named Shi Qizong initially ranked first, but later was placed second. The dragon's spirit, it seemed, was not accidental. The elders said that recently, when Zeng Luguang was promoted, a dragon also appeared on the creek. Zeng Luguang ranked first in the imperial examination, but because of a slight limp in one foot, he was demoted to second place. The two events are very similar, as said by Lu Boke. According to my examination, Zeng Luguang's father, Zeng Hui, was second place, and Zeng Luguang was fifth on the list of Cao Xiaobang.

The Scholar of the Cai River A local man named Doc Changchao was in the capital city. He was traveling with two scholars from the江东 region. They had gone out late in the evening after returning from their studies. It was the third month of the lunar calendar, and the road was obstructed by dug ditches and scattered stones. They arrived at a neighborhood where the streets turned, and one of the scholars got lost and separated from the others. The two scholars, who had never sought shelter or lodging before, were too afraid to follow or pursue the lost scholar, so they returned home. After several days, they reported the incident to the school official, who inquired about the place where they got lost. Nothing was found. The school official then sent a document to the Kaifeng Prefecture, which handed the case over to the criminal official, Dou Jian. Dou Jian arrived at the school and inquired about the name of the lost scholar, who was named Sun Xingzhong, with the courtesy name Qiangfu. Sun Xingzhong was seen leaving, tied up his belt and wearing a hat. Dou Jian called his subordinates and sent them to look for and investigate thoroughly. Many people believed that scholars from the江东 region often visited brothels along the Cai River and imitated their attire. The scholars went in different directions to stay overnight. On the first night of the month, one of the subordinates was in a certain brothel. The prostitute got up at the fifth watch to go to the office to meet an appointment, and the subordinate waited for her. When the prostitute left, the subordinate did not sleep again. He saw a small board inside the bed, on which a black silk hat was there. The subordinate took the hat to look at it, and the two characters "Qiangfu" were clearly visible on the hat. The subordinate made an excuse and went out, telling all his fellow subordinates. He hid outside, waiting for the prostitute to return. He and the prostitute's mother took and tied the prostitute and sent her to the prefecture. The prostitute began to say that in the evening there was a scholar named Sun who came alone to buy wine and entertain her. He was dressed in fine and clean clothing, but his behavior was awkward, and he had no companions. The subordinate suspected that he had intended to kill him, threw his body into the river, and sold all his possessions. Only the hat remained, with the inscription "Qiangfu" clearly visible. The prostitute did not expect that the inscription would lead to her downfall. Her spirit was revealed, and there was no escape from death. The mother and son were both executed. Doc Changchao said.

The Ditch of the Treasury in Guilin In the military supplies treasury of Jingjiang Prefecture, the ditch was blocked by accumulated debris, causing water to stagnate. As a result, gold and silk were frequently lost. The origins of the problem were traced, but it was difficult to determine where the thefts came from. The custodian officials suffered greatly from having to pay double compensation. The treasury official reported the issue to the governor, who ordered the ditch to be dismantled and repaired. In the middle of the ditch, two bodies were found lying face up, their heads facing each other. They were already dried out. Nearby, there was a bundle of silk, which was also torn and broken, making it impossible to pick up. Later, it was heard from other thieves that in the past, every thief entered through the outer side of the ditch. The opening was very narrow, barely fitting one person. One had to enter headfirst and use their feet to push forward. In this case, one thief had not yet finished exiting when another, unaware, entered. They met by chance, and neither advancing nor retreating was possible. Thus, they died. At that time, my father-in-law, Zhang Gong, was the governor.

Wang's Wife In the early years of the Song Dynasty, when bandits and rebels were still rampant throughout the empire, a man from Bianjing named Wang, who was an official, took his wife and concubines to Lin'an to be appointed to an official position. He stayed in a lodging with a sword, but found that all the neighbors were brothel keepers, which was not convenient. So, he rented a house outside. Upon returning, he told his wife, "I have found a certain lane and house. It is very spacious and clean. Tomorrow, I will first send the cage and sedan chair, and then come for you in a sedan chair." The next day, he went ahead. After a while, the sedan chair arrived, and his wife also went. After some time, Wang returned to the old lodging to look for her. The landlord said, "You left not long ago. You sent a carriage, and your wife left immediately. I followed her. Could it be that she took a wrong path?" Wang was shocked and in pain and returned, but he lost his wife and could not find her again. Five years later, Wang became a teacher in Quzhou and attended a banquet hosted by the magistrate of Xian. The turtle soup was very delicious, and all the guests were eating heartily. Wang ate a piece and stopped eating, weeping. The magistrate asked why. Wang said, "Remembering when my late wife was alive, she was the best at preparing this dish. Every time she cooked turtle, she would remove all the black skin and cut the pieces into perfect squares. How different it is now."

The woman wept, and she recounted the entire story in detail. The magistrate was also filled with sorrow. Pretending to go change clothes, he entered the house. After coming out, he immediately stopped the drinking and said, "One person crying in a corner makes the whole room unhappy. The teacher is already like this. How can we have the heart to enjoy drinking?" The guests all left. The magistrate invited Wang into the hall and called out a woman. It was his wife. They looked at each other and were overwhelmed with grief, almost to the point of despair. It happened on the night when they were going to move houses last year. A villain secretly heard about it and then deceived a carriage to bring her to the matchmaker's house, selling her to the magistrate. The magistrate received thirty thousand coins and took her as a concubine. Usually, he didn't let her cook in the kitchen. It was just a coincidence on that day, so he then called a carriage to send her to the Wang family. Wang bowed and thanked him, wishing to fully repay the original price. The magistrate said, "Taking the wife of a colleague as a concubine, not being able to thoroughly investigate, the fault is great. Fortunately, there are no children from this, how dare I mention money?" He finally returned her. I recently heard this from Yu Jiang of Qiantang, who was able to tell their names and hometowns. Now I have forgotten them. Like the magistrate of Xi'an, who was so virtuous, not being passed down to the world, especially regrettable.

**Shen Zhong Falls from a Cliff** My uncle's household raised hundreds of sheep, which were released to graze on the mountain. Many of them were eaten by wolves. Once, I sent my cousin by marriage, Shen Zhong, to search for them. It was still night, and the task was not yet completed. He was walking alone when he suddenly encountered Liu Xing, a servant of the household, ahead of him. Liu called out his name playfully. Though Shen Zhong was angry, he secretly was glad to have company. He immediately responded. Liu said, "This path is very dangerous. You should follow me." Shen followed him forward. After only a few dozen steps, he suddenly fell into the cliff. His arm was nearly broken. He screamed in pain. A butcher who lived at the foot of the mountain recognized his voice. He quickly lit a lamp, took a ladder, and helped him up. He was supported back home. His left arm was pierced through the bone, yet he was still able to describe what he had seen. But Liu Xing had never left. Only then did they realize it was a ghost. **Shen Wei Fu** Shen Wei Fu was from Ruian in Wenzhou. He had long studied in the Imperial Academy but achieved no fame. He gave up and returned to his hometown. He made it his business to flatter local officials and offer gifts in exchange for favors. Yet whenever imperial examination notices were issued, he would also take the exams, but always failed. He would inevitably look up and curse people, saying, "Shen Wei Fu is destitute and unsuccessful, laughed at by the people of the village. My innermost being is torn apart. Does Heaven know me as well?" In the sixth year of the Gan Dao era, the county magistrate, Huang, was accused by the people. The imperial envoy sent officials to investigate, and the truth was uncovered. The magistrate was very afraid. He brought wine and food to visit Shen Wei Fu, and day and night plotted ways to escape punishment. One hundred people, accompanied by two courtesans, took a boat to invite Shen Wei Fu to cruise on the lake. As they neared his residence, they had the courtesans serve wine and surround him, saying, "You can sing the song *Ping Di Yi Sheng Lei* as a toast to Master Shen." Shen declined, saying, "If I could be like this, my innermost being would not be torn apart." He immediately knelt and accepted the toast, but had not yet finished drinking.

Clouds and fog swirled together. Suddenly, a fierce storm arrived. The boat was pulled with all its might but could not move forward. It was the eighth day of the second month when the thunder first began to rumble. Soon, a thunderbolt struck the hall of the Shěn family. One pillar flew apart like dust. The roof tiles were completely shattered, leaving no intact pieces. All the documents in the bedroom were burned to ashes. The curtains and screens were torn to pieces. The screens and beds were as if they had been chopped by thousands of axes. Yet, the corners of the room remained completely undamaged. No one knew where the thunder came from. The next day, the townspeople gathered to burn incense and say, "Evil deeds should not be done." They spoke of the thunder that struck the Shěn family, asking if it was a warning. They recalled the saying that the internal organs would be torn apart. Was this the response? On the door of the hall, there were several lines of heavenly writing, visible only to insiders. The magistrate Huang, overcome with fear, developed a heart ailment. After two months, he slightly recovered and went to visit the Shěn family. The Shěn family still raised their hands to their foreheads, saying, "As the master said, it was like a clap of thunder." They remained completely unaware. Three years later, Huang also passed away. This story was told by Chen Chujun, the magistrate of Ruian.

General Ho Six scholars from Wu Xing entered the capital city to take the provincial examination. Together, they bought a hundred bolts of silk, which was carried by a servant. They were walking late on the Bian River embankment when they encountered a tattooed soldier with a bald head and dark skin. He emerged gracefully from the thicket. Seeing the group, he smiled and looked left, then shouted. Soon, several others followed, wielding spears and knives. They had fierce and brutal appearances, and everyone knew they were bandits. Although the travelers were afraid, they could not escape. One of them, Ho, a scholar, was tall, brave, and strong. He was skilled in wrestling and martial arts and was known in his village as General Ho. He told the others not to run and had them stand in a line behind him. Ho alone held a short staff and rushed forward. The bandits laughed lightly, seeing him as easy prey. Ho struck them continuously, always hitting their knees. They all fell to the ground, unable to rise. Only then did the travelers manage to leave. They walked another ten miles and passed a patrol camp. They entered and informed the patrol commander. The commander was greatly pleased, saying, "These bandits have been roaming this area, killing many people. The authorities have offered rewards for their capture, but they could not be caught. How did you manage to capture them in one go?" He invited the travelers to stay a bit longer and led his men eastward. The bandits were clearly lying on the ground, turning and twisting. There were about seven or eight of them. They were all captured, bound, and taken back. The patrol commander escorted them to the governor's office and thanked them greatly. The five scholars said that Ho would not have come with them; he would have fallen into the bandits' hands. Ho replied, "If I had traveled alone, I would have been in the same situation. Although you did not fight, you stood behind me, protecting my rear, and I had no worries about looking back. This is why I was able to win." This story was told by Yan Huichao.

The *Yijian Ding Zhi* Volume Twelve, Sixteen Stories Gong Pi Xian of Shangrao. In the seventeenth year of the Shaoxing era, he passed the local examination. The following year, after the provincial examination, he dreamed of entering a high official's office. He stood in the courtyard, surrounded by several hundred of his followers, all wearing white robes and standing on the western side. The king sat on the upper seat, and the officials called out names one by one, leading them to the eastern side. His cousin Pang was also selected, and Gong Pi Xian followed the call and was about to move east. Suddenly, a judge hurriedly ascended the hall, had something to report, and then descended, entering the eastern corridor. He carried a large bundle of documents and ascended, unrolling it to show the king. The king reviewed it for some time, nodding repeatedly. The judge descended again and pulled Gong Pi Xian back to the western side. Gong Pi Xian woke up with anger, looking disappointed and unhappy. That year, he failed the examination, while Pang alone passed. Gong Pi Xian knew the dream had come true, but he did not understand what wrong he had committed to be punished. From then on, he lost his ambition and struggled for about a decade. Eventually, he passed the local examination and reached the provincial level, finally obtaining a legitimate recommendation. After the imperial examination, he said happily, "It's over now."

He still clung to the list of successful candidates in his thoughts. One night, he dreamed of going to the wilderness, where he wandered and stood still, gazing into the distance. He saw a divine being descending slowly from the clouds, looking at him and saying, "Do you want to see the list of successful candidates?" The being then took out a small scroll from its sleeve and showed it to him. It was a yellow document, with the name "Gong Pi Xian" written in large letters on the front. Below it, in smaller letters, it read, "Due to the improper dispute over marriage, this was delayed for twelve years." He was startled awake and told his close friends, "Evil deeds should not be done." Recently, in his hometown, there was a woman of questionable conduct who had an affair with a wicked son. His nephew heard about it and exposed them. Fearing exposure, the wicked son made an agreement with the woman and hurriedly arranged a marriage by presenting a gift to her family. His nephew also insisted on the marriage. The wicked son, unable to bear it, took the matter to court. His nephew came to him for help, and he advised him with legal counsel. In the end, the nephew got married. At the time, everyone thought it was a good plan, but they did not expect the clear retribution to be so severe. It was too late to regret it. Gong Pi Xian, who served as the magistrate of Yudan, eventually did not achieve success and died.

The Elder Monk Xun Li Sizhi, the Shilang Mi Xun, served as the governor of Linchuan. On the anniversary of his father, the Shao Shi Gong, he went to Shushan to arrange a meal for the monks. There, he dined with the Elder Monk Hang Man. Hang Man was over eighty years old. As they were finishing their meal, he looked intently at Li and said, "Are you the Elder Monk Xun?" Li did not respond, and those around him were startled. After a while, Hang Man said, "This old monk is your senior brother in the same school. Our names, both above and below, are the same. Ever since I heard that you had been appointed as governor, I had already suspected it. Today, observing your speech, laughter, actions, and appearance, there is no difference at all. Could you be my reincarnation? What else could it be?" Li asked him what year he would pass away, and Hang Man replied, "In the Wuchen year of the Yuanyou era," which was the same year Li was born. Li was also moved by this and, upon returning home, named his study "Xiaoyun Hall" and composed a poem: "Old man, how could one thought lead to this? Willing to exchange my official robes for a monk's robe. There is still my senior brother Hang Man to consult with. Even in this world, I shall still praise Elder Monk Xun. Deep-seated habits remain, and I can still perform. Karma has not allowed me to see the flower in my hand. Now, I shall cultivate my pure work and seek my way back. Content and detached, I live like a layman in his home." Li was originally named based on a dream omen, as recorded in the *Jia Zhi*.

Wang Yu, Judge of the Yutang, A man from Jiujiang named Wang Yu. During the reign of Emperor Huizong (Zhenghe era), he served as the magistrate of Jinxian County in Hongzhou. He was about to be replaced. (The original text is missing one leaf) Subordinate Zhang Chun told him, "This is a case of 'Lan Yuan Xue Feng' (a skin condition). I have a medicine that can treat this. It is called the 'Two Hundred Herbs and Flowers Ointment.' The patient was surprised and said, 'Such a medicine! It is not found in the world's medical books. How could it be easily obtained? You must be joking.' Zhang said, 'I just happened to have seen this medicine. I will give it to you.' The next day, he brought a small spoonful of the ointment. It was firm and solidified into a paste. He instructed Wang Yu to take a small amount and put it in his mouth. After one day, the tears stopped. After two days, the swelling subsided. After three days, the pain ceased, and he was suddenly and completely healed. Wang Yu then went to visit Zhang to thank him and asked about the ingredients. Zhang smiled and said, 'It is simply made from a goat's gallbladder. Remove the skin and fat, and fill it with good honey, mixing it thoroughly. When it dries, grind it into a fine paste. Since bees collect nectar from a hundred flowers and goats eat a hundred herbs, I concealed its name to bewilder people.' Some say there are other records of this medicine in different texts.

The Case of Cursing in Tingzhou The people of Tingzhou, the Nie family, cursed with a certain family. After a long time, dozens of members from both families died one after another. Only the son of the Nie family, named Qìng, survived. He followed the elder monk Fahai and lived in Nan Yan Temple. After three years, Monk Fahai was transferred to Tianning Temple. Qìng went with him. On the way, he encountered a malarial disease. He died but revived. He said to Monk Fahai, "It was as if I saw five people come in a dream. They caught up with each other very urgently. They said they have been chasing you for a long time. You are in Nan Yan Temple, I dare not enter. Now you must go to complete the case." He drove and ran quickly. Qìng felt terrified, recited Buddha's name, and begged for mercy and salvation. He reached Ma Tan Ferry. On the road, he met a white-clad rock master. The five people knelt down and held their breath. The rock master told him, "It is not necessary for Qìng. The only one who died is this ear. You are my son, how can you bear to see me taken away?" Zhai Gui sent military officer Zhang You to lead the laborers who were about to be sent to pull. The wood rolled down from the mountain on its own. Its end was broken off by about one zhang. Those who saw it were surprised. In early April, Wen was planting rice in the fields. Suddenly, a strong wind and rain occurred. The lightning struck him down to the ground. His body was split in half from the bridge of his nose. The right side was burned as if by fire. The smoke color was dense and heavy. The left side remained half of the body, but he did not die. Now both mother and son are alive. Zhai Gui said.

The Descendants of Zheng An The internal text is missing Wen Da sold wood, which is missing The internal text is missing Chen Shisi and his father Chen Shisi, a villager of Xingguo County in Ganzhou Prefecture, was extremely disrespectful to his mother. Once, he got into a quarrel with his neighbors and secretly plotted with his wife to drag his mother out to fight. His mother, who had been long ill, blind, and old, could not endure it. He dragged and threw her about, to the point of death. He then falsely accused his neighbors of killing her. The villagers and his sister all testified that it was not true. The county authorities arrested Chen and imprisoned him, but he died before the punishment could be carried out. This was in the sixth year of the Gande era. Three years later, Chen’s wife crossed the stream to see her daughter and was struck by lightning and died in the water. Her son heard about it and rushed to the side of the stream. He gathered a long vine and went into the water to wrap his mother’s body, pulling her body out. People on the bank urged him to carry her body, but he refused to listen. The lightning struck again, and he too was struck dead. His family thus perished. According to the county magistrate Mu Zhun.

The Poem of Xi Jin Pavilion Ye Shaoyun, the Left Chancellor, had just passed the imperial examinations. He was appointed as the magistrate of Dantu, Runzhou. The governor highly esteemed him and ordered him to inspect the collection and expenditure of taxes. The office was located on the Xi Jin Pavilion. Ye often visited the pavilion on his days off, standing alongside the supervisors by the railing and looking down at the river. One day, he saw a boat heading south towards the pavilion, fully loaded with women who were laughing and joking as usual. He thought they were the family of a wealthy and noble person and was about to avoid them, but the boat had already docked. Ten or so people in splendid attire climbed aboard and went directly to the pavilion, asking the clerk, "Where is Scholar Ye?" The clerk was instructed to inform Ye. Ye had no choice but to come out and meet them. They all bowed twice and spoke, saying, "Scholar Ye's excellent reputation is widespread throughout the Jiangnan region. We are courtesans from Zhenzhou. We have always wished to serve you at your table, fulfilling our lifelong aspirations. But we are registered in the entertainment register. In Yizhen, guests come and go like clouds, and there is always a banquet going on. We could never hope for even a moment's pleasure. Today, the governor has a private observance, and all the officials are not gathered. So we agreed to cross the river here, and it seems fate has favored us." Ye consoled and thanked them, ordering them to sit. The officials planned to bring wine and drink with them, but they stood up and said, "Not considering our lowly status, we brought along simple food and wine ourselves."

Daring to offer a cup of wine to extend your life. Wishing to obtain your wise words to take back, to show off to the people of Huai, for infinite glory. My wishes are fulfilled. But the servant brings the wine jug upstairs. The dishes are all refined and clean. Dancing and singing take turns. After several rounds of drinking, the leader presents a flowered note to ask. Ye commands the pen and writes immediately, without any corrections. This is the *Hè Xīnláng* (贺新郎) poem that is now passed down. The poem says: Upon waking, I hear the chirping of orioles. The green moss on the curtain is covered by the afternoon sun. There are countless fallen petals. The remaining flowers are blown away, unseen by anyone. Only the willows sway by themselves. Gradually, the warm mist returns, bringing a light summer. The precious fan seeks the shadow of the moon again. In the dim dust, there is still a woman riding a phoenix. She is startled by old grudges. They persist endlessly. The dream of Jiangnan ends at the riverside. Waves stick to the sky, grape-colored water rises green. Half the sky is filled with mist and rain. The boundless intention of the vast waves before the tower. Who will pick the water caltrop flowers to send? But only look longingly at the leisurely boat. When will the sails of a thousand miles arrive? Sending off a lone goose, with the view cut off by thousands of mountains. Sing *Jīn Lǚ* (金缕) for me again. The final verse records the truth. This poem is widely praised and matched with Su Shi's *Rǔ Yàn Huá Wū* (乳燕华屋) work. Yet Ye Gong himself believed it was not his masterpiece. Few people know this story. Said by Ye Huishu.

The Wife of Ji Hui Ji Hui, the magistrate of Pingjiang in Yue Prefecture, was from Huayang in Tang Prefecture. He first married into the Wang family, whose daughter was the daughter of a high-ranking official named Lun. After her death, he then married Zhang, a woman from the same prefecture. They lived in Changsha. Zhang gave birth to a daughter and shortly after contracted a serious illness that the doctors could not cure. Her mother was deeply worried and invited a witch to investigate. The witch reported that Wang's spirit was standing in front of them, causing much mischief. They were instructed to set up an altar and pray for relief, promising to perform a ritual of atonement. However, the spirit would not leave. The witch told Ji Hui, "You must write a formal document of divorce as if you were human spouses, and only then can she be released." Ji Hui was reluctant to do so, but as Zhang's condition worsened, he could not help but tearfully take the pen and write the document, handing it to the witch. They mixed it with paper money and burned it to send it to her. The witch said, "The woman took the document and read it through, cried bitterly, and left." Zhang indeed recovered. The act of a living person divorcing a deceased wife is something that has never been heard of in ancient times. Zhang and my wife were sisters from the same hall, and she still lives today.

Wife of Hu Sheng Hu Sheng, a wealthy son from Weishi County, remarried Zhang's daughter. She was his sister-in-law. Hu favored a nun and kept her outside for a long time. Zhang found out about it and called her husband back, rebuking him angrily and dragging him. She even wanted to blind his eyes with ash from a stove. Hu let go and ran away, saying, "Rather than being beaten painfully, how could this be allowed?" Zhang became even angrier and threw herself in the courtyard, rolling and yelling. At that time, she was pregnant for eight months and was extremely tired, lying on the ground and sleeping. In her dream, Hu's former wife came and said, "That person is my husband. How can you take over?" The former wife then said, "I will now kill your child." She raised her fist and hit Zhang's abdomen. Zhang was frightened and awoke, beginning to tell what she had seen. She supported herself by pain into the room, already unable to bear it. The place where she lived was forty li from the county. She quickly called a lactation doctor. The doctor had not arrived when the fetus fell to the ground and died.

The Witchcraft of Xie Eye Xie Eye was a man from Ningdu, Ganzhou, who had one eye that was blind but possessed magical abilities. Once, while sitting with a guest at a village inn, he saw several women dressed in new clothes playing outside. He told his guest, "They are wearing splendid attire. I will make them walk barefoot." He stood there with his hands in his sleeves for a long time. The women indeed hesitated and felt embarrassed, and they all took off their shoes and socks, holding their clothes as they passed by. When they reached him, he asked them why they were acting this way. They replied, "The path is muddy and difficult, and it is very inconvenient." Xie Eye smiled and asked them to look back, and they saw that the path was actually smooth and level. One day, a little boy was carrying two baskets of cakes and bread, following his mother to her family home. Xie Eye approached him and asked for one of the baskets. The boy refused, so Xie Eye took a piece of bamboo strip and secretly placed it in the second basket. The boy felt the basket become much heavier and walked more slowly. His mother waited for him several times. Suddenly, the basket became even heavier and he could not lift it. Surprised, he opened the cover and saw small green snakes filling the basket. He was greatly frightened and threw everything away. Another time, a man was carrying a pig's head to pass by. Xie Eye said, "I can get this to serve wine." He silently recited a spell of several words. The man walked down to the foot of the mountain and was surprised by the smell of blood. When he looked, the pig's head had already turned into a human head. Frightened, he ran away, and Xie Eye calmly took it back and returned home with his guest to cook and eat it. Whenever Xie Eye entered a tavern to drink, no one dared not show him respect. If anyone treated him poorly, he would throw a reed staff out, and immediately a snake would appear on the ground. The drinkers would all leave their seats. Because of this, the villagers feared and respected him. Later, when he grew old and sick, he died. After his death, his magic also ceased. This story is told by Chen Xi.

薛士隆，字季宣，曾遭遇九件奇异之事。之后，有人声称有神物降临他的居所，这种现象持续了好几年。在干道癸巳年，他从吴兴太守的职位上辞职，返回永嘉。不久后，他患上了痔疾，被庸医以毒药治疗，最终因中毒而亡。去世数日后，他的儿子薛沄也在病中，听到有人诵读佛教偈语，内容是：“议着即差，拟着即错。挑起杖头，将错就错。鱼鸟飞沉，各由至乐。要知乐处，无梦无觉。”薛士隆学问广博，见解高明、深邃、纯正、刚正且富有见识。他正准备在当时有所作为，却在四十岁左右遭遇了如此不幸。善良的人们都为他感到惋惜。他最高官职是通直郎，等待常州的职位空缺，但未能赴任。 洞庭湖的沙洲。

In the seventh year of the Gandao era, during the eleventh month, Xie Xun, having completed his term as governor of Fenshou, set out on a journey with his family by land. They arrived at Baling and stayed at the Yueyang Pavilion. All their baggage and belongings were placed in two large boats, and one boat was left empty, intended for his own use. They crossed the vast lake, and three days later reached Yue. On that day, the governor of Yue, Wang Xi, prepared a banquet and invited Xie to dine at the governor’s hall. At the same time, the boat was still approaching from the west. The judicial official Lv Fei, whose official residence was near the pavilion, took advantage of the winter solstice holiday and led his wife and concubines up to the slope, gazing out over the lake. In the center of the lake, they saw a long black object, appearing and disappearing intermittently, trailing behind the three boats. At first, they thought it was a dragon. The scholars said, “This is called *zou sha*.” Such creatures are said to exist in the rivers and lakes, though they are rarely seen. After a long while, the object reached the shore. Xie returned to his boat and continued his journey. Lv Fei observed again, and the black object followed as before. After traveling thirty li, they arrived at Jiu Long Pu, where they intended to stay overnight at Dao Ren Ji. As they moored on a sandbank, the water suddenly surged and formed a barrier, gradually extending eastward to the middle of the boat. The boatman became greatly alarmed and entered the boat to inform Xie, requesting that they quickly leave and take refuge. Xie immediately called his family and, leaping from the sand, they all rushed ashore. Shortly afterward, a huge turtle climbed onto the boat. Its body was over ten chi long and wide, and using its head and feet together, it pressed down forcefully on the top of the boat. Those who had heavy loads all sank flat into the water, while only those who had traveled in lighter boats remained unharmed. The items essential for life, along with bedding, curtains, furs, and clothing, were all submerged. The evening cold was severe, and Xie sent word to borrow clothing from Governor Wang. Governor Wang ordered the patrol commander at Dao Ren Ji to recruit soldiers skilled in swimming to rescue them. The water was too deep to measure, and the masts of the boats were several zhang high, yet no sign of the people could be seen. Knowing there was nothing to be done, they stopped. The entire family had barely escaped death. How perilous it was! This account was told by Lv Fei.

A Man from Huaiyin In the 31st year of the Shaoxing era, Ye Ronglianggui of Pucheng served as the magistrate of Huaiyin. There was a scholar who died and then came back to life after three days. He said he was taken to the underworld to the office. The pursuers led him past the eastern wing. He saw a line of ceremonial guards arranged in rows, all used by the emperor. He found it strange and dared not ask. After standing in the courtyard, the official said, "You were not supposed to die. You should return quickly." Then he went out through the western wing, and what he saw was as before. He then asked the people there what this was for. They replied, "The lord is welcoming a new emperor. So he is arranging the imperial paraphernalia." He woke up at the door. Later he told Ye. Ye warned him not to speak about it. The next year, the emperor ascended the throne, and then he recorded the event.

A Woman from Huaiyin A commoner from Huaiyin lost his daughter. It was during the Cold Food Festival. He wished to perform Buddhist rituals to honor her with strictness, but lacked the means to do so. The mother cut off her hair and sold it, obtaining six hundred coins. She went out into the street to find a monk. She encountered five men passing by her door. She greeted and bowed to them, explaining her situation. They all passively avoided her. After a long time, one monk finally agreed to stay. He said, "Today, I did not bring any scriptures with me. Can you go and borrow one?" The woman went to all her neighbors and found a copy of the *Gokuraku-kyō* (Sutra of Golden Light) to give to the monk. As he began to chant, the woman wept profusely. The monk, moved by her sorrow, said, "I did not expect you to be so grief-stricken. I will go to the market to bathe and purify myself, then I will chant sincerely for you." When he returned, he chanted with a pure heart and completed the prayer for the deceased's rebirth. He then handed the money back to her and returned home. On the way, he met the four men he had previously encountered at a tea house. They asked him about what he had received. He invited them to join him in buying some wine. They sat down, but before they could lift their cups, they heard a woman's voice calling from outside the window. Only the monk who had chanted the scripture got up to respond. She cried, "I am the deceased daughter of that family. I have been lingering in the underworld for a long time. I was fortunate to benefit from the teacher's diligent chanting of the scripture, and thus I have been liberated. Yama has already ordered my rebirth. All the documents are prepared, but they have not yet been sealed. If the teacher drinks alcohol and breaks his fast, all the good deeds will be wasted. It is truly a pity. Can you endure and wait until tomorrow?" The monk felt greatly afraid and told the others. They all left in alarm. This was also a matter of the late years of the Song Dynasty.

A Woman from the Li Family and Vinegar The food and drink of ordinary people each have their own records in the netherworld. What is recorded in biographies and in the *Bing Zhi* (a specific historical or literary record) regarding the pork consumed by a certain woman from the Cai family’s younger sister-in-law—all of this is true. The southern part of the sea is a place of abundant marine life and majestic scenery, where vinegar is indispensable in meals. A woman from the Li family alone could not drink even a drop of it. Her brother, because of a dream, entered the netherworld to attend to affairs. On the verge of being released, he passed through the corridors of various departments, saw a sign on the door that read "Food Supply Bureau," and approached to look at it. He found a ledger for Quanzhou. He asked a clerk to check it, and under the entry for his sister, there was a detailed record of what she ate every day, but there was no mention of vinegar. He then took a pen and wrote "half a sheng of vinegar." When he woke up, his illness was cured. From that day forward, his sister began to eat vinegar like a normal person.

### The Record of What I Have Seen, Volume 13, Entry 15 #### Xing Shunjǔ Xing Shunjǔ was an official during the Daming era of Emperor Huizong's reign, having entered government service through the military examination. He served as the patrol commander of the state of Guo. Throughout his life, he was deeply interested in Daoist practices. Anyone who possessed a skill or craft, he would definitely befriend. Once, while walking alone in the outskirts, he encountered a woman wearing a bamboo hat and a Daoist robe. She bowed before him and said, "Are you not Xing Liangfu?" He replied, "Yes." She asked, "What do you enjoy in life?" He answered, "I enjoy cultivating the art of longevity." She said, "You have studied it for a long time, but you have not yet seen its wonders." She continued, "Although you are deeply interested, how can you break free from worldly attachments? I will give you a medicine. Take it with fresh water. Not only will it prolong your life, but it will also cure various illnesses. It is one aspect of cultivating true nature." Xing Shunjǔ thanked her gratefully, saying, "Thank you very much." However, he did not have the time to take it immediately. She then reached into her sleeve and took out a prescription, naming it *Huan Shao Dan*. She gave it to him and said, "Taking this should be beneficial." He began to suspect that she was an extraordinary person and tried to ask about auspicious and inauspicious omens. She said, "It is difficult to discuss one's future prospects. You will face hardships in your middle age, but you will see good things in your later years." He asked about her name and place of residence. She smiled and said, "I have been with you for a long time. Why ask? Don't you remember the painting on the wall? It is me."

Today I am telling you this. Be absolutely respectful and cautious. Do not forget these words. Suddenly, he was not seen. Xing hurriedly returned home and carefully examined the appearance. It was the appearance of the Daoist nun named He Xian Gu, no different from a regular woman. Disappointed and lost, he took water and swallowed the medicine. And as prescribed in the formula for alchemy, he carefully took it. He felt his energy and strength increased, and his complexion became smooth and good. Later, he entered and left the gates of Yue Shao Bao, serving as the military commissioner of Fujian. Due to Yue's affairs, he was demoted and exiled. Not many years later, he also lost a son, and the family's fortunes declined for nearly twenty years. Finally, he obtained the position of military commissioner of Suizhou and governor of Jingzhou. Later, he retired and lived in Xiangyang. Until the year Gengzi of the Dading era, he was eighty-nine years old, with no illnesses or suffering. His eyes were as bright as those of a child, and his hair was not white. He could still ride a horse and gallop, and people pointed to this as evidence of the elixir's efficacy. Three years later, he fell ill, being ill for three months, and had a severe diarrhea. Something like a bucket came out of his abdomen, hard, smooth, and shiny, with no foul odor. Xing spoke to the people around him in a sad manner, saying, "The elixir has been expelled. I have no chance of survival." He died the next day. My younger brother Jing Pei served in Xiangyang and saw him.

**High County Lady** In the 24th year of the Shaoxing era, Li Qi, a Baoyi郎 (low-ranking military officer), served as the supervisor of taxes in the East Gate Town of Hezhou, and his family was quite affluent. There was a high-ranking envoy named Gao, who was sent to take up an official post in Shuzhou. He came with his wife to visit Li, requesting a loan of 50,000 coins for travel expenses, agreeing to repay double the amount upon completing his term. Li lent him the sum as requested. After Gao completed his term, he intended to fulfill the agreement. However, his wife said, "It is not easy to raise 10,000 coins. Fortunately, we are far apart. He may not come to demand it. Let us wait until another day." Gao agreed to her plan. On his way back, he passed through Hezhou, but did not see Li. Three years later, Li became a patrol official in Huangzhou. One day, while napping during the day, he saw Gao’s wife, dressed in a mule’s skin, coming toward him. She bowed in the hall and said, "I have long owed money to your household. I have come to repay it." Before Li could respond, she rushed directly to the horse stable. Li awoke in alarm, and the stable attendant reported that a female mule had been born. Li went to see, and found the mule lying beside its mother, unable to move. He pondered and sighed for a long time, then said to her, "Gao’s wife borrowed money from me. I never took it seriously. How could this come to be? If you truly are County Lady Gao, then rise and walk." At his words, the mule leapt up and took several steps. Li was greatly astonished. He sent a letter inquiring about Gao’s family, only to learn that Gao’s wife had died on that very day. Li raised the mule with care and did not dare to ride it. When outsiders wished to see it, he would slowly lead it out. But they simply called it "County Lady Gao."
The Official and the Ghostly Children During the reign of Emperor Ling, a woman from Luoyang was born with two heads and four arms.

Pan, the Scholar of Hanyang Pan, a scholar from Hanyang, was once drunk in the evening as he stepped out of the school gate. He came to a lotus pond and wanted to pick lotus flowers but could not. He saw a woman approaching from the water's edge, walking very quickly. She asked Pan, "It is already dusk. Why are you standing here?" Pan replied, "Who are you?" She answered, "I am the daughter of Zhang from the east house. My parents are both out tonight. I have long been attracted to you, and this is a good time that is hard to miss. So I came to join you." Pan was very pleased and took her hand, and they entered together. From then on, he would leave in the morning and return in the evening. Within two months, he became emaciated and weak. His fellow students pressed him hard to explain why, but he kept it secret and did not answer. The school official, Ouan, pressed him further, and he finally revealed the truth. Ouan said, "You will die soon. She is indeed a girl from a good family. How can she come out every night and stay at the school? This is either a ghost or a demon. If you want to save your life, you should perform a verification and treatment." Fearing for his life, Pan sought advice. Ouan gave him a needle threaded with red thread and instructed him to secretly apply it to the hem of his clothes. That night, Pan used the strategy. The next day, a schoolmate went in different directions to visit temples and shrines. One of them found, on the wall of the Peach Blossom Temple, a painted image of a fairy holding an incense tray, with a red thread attached to her skirt. He immediately scraped it off with a knife and smashed the wall. The strange occurrence then ceased.

Zhou Sanlang In Yingchuan, Wuyang County, there was a village called Shizhu Village, about ten miles from the county. The road leading to the village was known for its strange occurrences. One day, a villager named Li Shun entered the county town while drunk. As evening approached, he rode a donkey back home. Not far from the village, someone called out his name from behind. "I am your neighbor, Zhou Sanlang. I just returned from doing business in the county town. Suddenly, I developed a foot ailment, and walking is extremely difficult. Can you carry me home with you? I will treat you as a guest and repay you." Although Li Shun was drunk, he still knew that this place had strange things and did not dare to respond. He also did not look back. Zhou Sanlang became angry and said, "We are neighbors. How can you be so unfeeling? I will definitely ride this donkey with you." After speaking, he sat on the saddle behind. Li Shun was very embarrassed. He secretly untied the cord he was wearing, tied it around both of them, whipped the donkey, and hurried forward. As they approached home, the mysterious figure suddenly shouted repeatedly to get off, saying, "I need to go to the bathroom." Li Shun did not respond again. The figure said again, "Please look back at me," and said this several times. Li Shun pretended not to hear. When they arrived home, there was no sound. Li Shun called his son to look, and it was a rotting coffin board. He cut it up and burned it. The strange occurrences on the road thus ceased.

**HanYang Pomegranate** In the early years of the Shaoxing era, there was a widow in the HanYang military district. She had been very diligent in serving her mother-in-law. Her mother-in-law died without any illness. A neighbor falsely accused the widow of poisoning her. The case was brought to the authorities. Unable to withstand the torture, the widow confessed to the crime. As she was about to be led out of the prison, the jailer placed a branch of pomegranate flower in her hair. As she walked to the execution ground, she turned to the executioner and said, "Take this flower and plant it in the crevice of a stone on the slope." Later, she prayed, "I did not actually kill my mother-in-law. If heaven is watching, I wish for the flower to grow into a tree. If I am guilty, then the flower will wither and die today." Everyone who heard this felt pity for her. She was then executed. The next day, the flower had sprouted new leaves and eventually grew into a tree, about three feet tall. To this day, it bears fruit every year. **Temple of Mercy** The villagers of Wuchang jointly established the Temple of Mercy. One shepherd boy found two steamed buns. He wrapped one in a leaf and placed it in the fish basket on his waist. As he was heading home, the sky suddenly became dark and gloomy, with thunder, lightning, and wind. The boy fell to the ground. After a short while, he got up and continued walking. Those who saw this asked him why. The boy said, "I didn't hear any thunder. But I saw hundreds of spirits rushing towards me, very close to me. An old man took my hand and said, 'How dare you put food from the temple in your fish basket?' I replied, 'I wanted to give it to my mother when I got home.' The old man was pleased and immediately waved his hand to make them all retreat."

Kong Laochong Kong Siwen, a native of Changsha, resides in Ezhou. In his youth, he once met Zhang Tian Shi and was taught the Dao. He could also treat the disease of transmitted corpses. Therefore, people call him Kong Laochong. Liu Wuke, a guest from Jingnan, travels between the rivers and lakes. His wife, Dun Shi, and their two sons live at home. One night, they heard someone outside the window ask if Liu Wuke was present. Dun Shi looked around but did not see anyone. She was very afraid and dared not answer. Then the voice said, "When you return, please ask him to convey my message. I am leaving." When Liu returned, his wife told him what happened. They discussed moving to another place. Suddenly, another voice said, "It is not easy for Liu Wuke to travel." Liu shouted, "What kind of strange ghost are you? You keep coming to my house. I am not afraid of you." The voice laughed and said, "I am the Wu Tong Shen, not a ghost." "I have a request for you. If you can worship me, I will make you extremely wealthy for life. You won't need to spend years trading, risking your life in the waves. How much profit do you gain, and how uncertain is your fate? You should carefully consider both options. Whether you agree or not is up to you. Why be angry?" Then it left and did not speak again. Liu, who was naturally greedy, agreed with what he said. He quickly built a small shrine next to the house. Soon, a carriage with four horses came, and someone announced, "Young Master is paying respects." Liu went out to greet. The guest wore a yellow shirt and a black hat, and had a dignified appearance. As soon as he sat down, there was a continuous flow of delicious food and wine. From then on, he came every day, without regard to morning or evening. They played games and laughed, and the neighbors could not tell who it was. Gold, silver, and silk were given in countless amounts. For a year, Liu gave up on traveling as a merchant.

The family greatly regarded it as an unexpected blessing. On another night, they were playing chess and competing to go first. Resenting that Liu did not show leniency, they pushed the chessboard aside and stood up. The next day, Liu checked the box for what it contained, but found nothing left. Unable to control their feelings of regret and anger, they planned to summon a Taoist priest to deal with it. Just happened that Kong was present, and they told him everything. Kong sent Liu back first, then went to the shrine. He lit incense and said, "I heard that this family has a spirit troubling them, is it you?" A voice from the air laughed loudly and said, "Yes. I know that Liu Wu has ordered you to deal with me. What do you intend to do? It’s just a small trick of writing charms. I am a true spirit. What is there to fear about cinnabar?" Kong replied, "I heard that spirits are extremely powerful. Therefore, I have shown respect and sought the truth. Why do you speak of dealing with it?" They questioned and answered for a long time. Kong scolded and said, "I came to see the spirit. I am a guest. Can’t you even offer tea as a courtesy?" In a moment, the tea was already on the table. Kong said, "If you really do not trouble Liu’s house, why not provide a statement to me?" Initially hesitating, afterward he said that providing a statement would not be a problem. After a while, the table was full of fine characters, written like with soot, not very clear. Kong thanked and left, comforting with good words and saying, "Today I definitely know you are a true spirit. Liu Wu falsely accused. Do not worry. Just happened to offend. Dare to ask for forgiveness." Afterward he told Liu, guessing that it would come that night, he used magic to hide, stood with a sword at the left side of the door. Before midnight, the yellow-robed one came over, wearing the same attire as before, and directly entered the house. Kong raised his sword and struck, the spirit screamed and disappeared, only saw half of a yellow rat fall in the blood. In the morning, he traced to the shrine, found the upper part under the dummy—it was a large rat. He destroyed the temple and smashed the images, and the spirit ceased troubling.

**Commander Liang** Liang Xing was the commander of the Selected Vanguard Army of Ezhou. Once, the water vessel in front of the hall ran dry. He called a soldier from the barracks and scolded him. The soldier apologized, but the same thing happened again. Liang became very angry and wanted to punish him. The soldier said, "I fill it with water every day. How could I dare to be careless?" If you do not believe me, I wish you would come and check it yourself at night. Liang then released him, but ordered a cart to bring water and fill the vessel, after which the soldier withdrew. The next day, the vessel was empty again. Liang was greatly surprised by this strange occurrence and ordered someone to watch it carefully. Just after the second watch of the night, a large python descended from the roof and drank from the vessel. It drank it all in an instant. The soldier quickly entered and said, "Liang sent a junior officer to follow its path, and he saw clearly as it crossed the river to the foot of the Dabie Mountains, where it went straight into a deep cave." The local people all said this creature had lived in the caves for many years and had never harmed anyone. People also dared not approach it. The next morning, Liang called Zhao Chun from his tent and led dozens of strong soldiers, armed with powerful bows and poisoned arrows, across the river. He also ordered one man to climb a mountain and blow a flute. Soon after, the python emerged, and Liang shot it immediately and killed it. A few nights later, Liang dreamed of a woman who came to him, looking angry, and said, "What crime have I committed against you that you have wrongly killed me? Now I come to demand your life." She rushed forward to attack Liang. Liang was greatly embarrassed and immediately fought with her. The woman could not withstand him and said, "I will take your eldest son as a substitute." Soon after, his eldest son indeed died. Several of his soldiers also died, and the rest fell seriously ill. Zhao Chun was afraid and burned incense day and night, praying for forgiveness, and barely escaped. Four years later, Liang died. The people of Hanyang referred to the python as a mountain god, hence it was able to take revenge in this way. Yet while alive, it could not protect its own body, avoiding the river water but longing instead for the water in the vessel—why did it bring disaster upon itself?

A Woman with a Tiger's Head In the eighth month of the fifth year of the Ganda era, during the Southern Song dynasty, there was a woman named Li, the wife of a man named Zhao, who lived in the area between the rivers Xiang and Han.
