She suffered from a severe headache that was unbearable, and she cried out in pain for more than ten days. Her maidservants attended to her illness. Suddenly, they heard a very loud roar. When they looked, her head had transformed into a tiger's head. They quickly reported this to Zhao, who arrived and asked her about it, but she could no longer speak. Her children surrounded her and comforted her. She held her young child while crying, showing a look of pity or affection. She ate food but did not seem to notice it. When given raw meat, she grabbed and ate it. After six or seven days, she began to grab her children, as if wanting to eat them. By then, no one dared to approach her. Zhao carried her to an empty room and locked the door. He fed her raw meat every day. He invited his friend Fan San Guan to come and told him the reason for wanting to get rid of her. Fan said it was not advisable. Li was unseemly or improper in character, known by everyone. The sky is showing a warning. If we get rid of her, disaster will surely follow. Why not burn a petition to heaven to ask for forgiveness? Allow her to die after fulfilling her karma. It is also a good deed. Zhao followed his advice and ordered a Taoist priest to perform several rituals. She died shortly after. When alive, she was cruel, fierce, and very jealous. She was disrespectful to her in-laws and harmed her relatives and neighbors. Zhao dared not confront her. By then, no one pitied her. The above ten events are all said by Mei Shi Zhong.

A Son of Zhang the Shangshu Lady Liu, the wife of Zhang Kegong, the Shangshu, gave birth to three sons, all of whom died in infancy. Their conditions were quite unusual: one was born without a tongue, and another was born with ten testicles in his scrotum. Despite having three sons, Zhang Kegong ultimately had no surviving male heir. Lady Liu was harsh to her maidservants and concubines; whenever she became angry, she would lock them in empty rooms without food. In her later years, she could barely eat or drink, consuming only a small amount of rice over ten days. She became extremely emaciated and eventually died. People believed this was a form of karmic retribution. Lady Liu was the sister of the narrator's maternal aunt.

Yan Si Lao A villager from Fangcheng County named Yan Si Lao fell seriously ill. Suddenly, he told his son, "I am turning into a donkey. Watch me kick." He then lifted his foot and arched his back, making exaggerated gestures that truly resembled a donkey. He said, "Grind some fine grass and steam some beans for me. I want to eat my fill and die." His family, weeping, brought him the food. He sat in a tub and ate heartily, leaving nothing behind. After finishing, he lay down again and soon after passed away. Yan Si Lao had been a broker in his village. Ye Keji Ye Keji from Shouchang was ten years old. At the time, he was traveling with his father, a high-ranking official, to Yangzhou. He fell ill with red eyes, followed by bloody diarrhea. After a long time, his bowel movements became blocked. A monk said, "This is due to taking toxic medicine. Your intestines are already damaged. Now we must remove them." The monk went outside, quickly prepared ten or so ounces of medicine, brought it in, and soaked it in wine for him to drink. He warned the family to prepare a bathing tub. Soon, Ye Keji's intestines were in severe pain. He quickly sat in the tub, and something fell out of him. It was his rotten intestines, about a meter long. This happened twice. His breathing was barely detectable. His father and brothers thought he would surely die. That evening, he suddenly groaned and asked for porridge. The next morning, he walked on the ground. The whole family was amazed. Soon, a large abscess appeared on his genitals, and seven holes appeared in his buttocks. Feces and urine came out of these holes, and the smell was so foul it could not be endured. The monk said, "This is beyond the capabilities of ordinary families to care for. You should come with me." After some time, the monk became overwhelmed by the situation and returned Ye Keji to his family. It was about ten years later when Ye Keji, traveling with his brother, stayed in Lanxi. A Taoist practitioner passed by and told him, "You have a very unusual illness."

I can recognize it. But drink my wine. Tomorrow I will treat you. I will not charge you a single coin. Immediately, he took two liters of wine and drank it. He was pleased and said, "Good wine!" How much did you brew? "Five liters," he replied. He instructed him to keep it all and then left. The next day, he came as promised. He burned a red-hot iron rod and inserted it into the tailgate six or seven inches. The patient was calm as if he did not feel it. He then followed with a cold iron rod to apply medicine, applying it several times. He also burned an iron rod and烙ed it on the ulcer, and the skin all burned off. Then he mixed the medicine, filled the six orifices and left one, saying, "It cannot be blocked. If blocked, he will die." My brother, who was present, could not bear to watch and covered his sleeves and got up. After two evenings, the scabs all fell off. The flesh where it was烙ed is now even, and the six orifices are all full and solid. Inside his abdomen, a wooden intestine grew. From then on, he was no different from ordinary people. He drank and ate twice as much as before, and the feces he passed were all like chicken droppings. He then married and had children. After he was over fifty, the ulcer recurred below the navel, and he died after his abdomen was opened. He lived for forty-two years without an intestine. There is no such disease in the world. The two doctors suspected they were all extraordinary people.

#### The Lin'an Man A man from Lin'an suffered from a cold due to illness, and his tongue protruded more than an inch. No one could treat it, so they fed him by inserting a pen tube into his mouth to give him porridge. Each day, he sat at the door. A certain Daoist practitioner saw him and said, "I can treat this. It will be done in a moment." But what to do if the medicine is not available? The patient's family heard and asked, "If we can get the money, we will do our best to find it." The Daoist refused to tell and left. The next day, he spoke of it again. When a high-ranking official finished his duty and returned, he dismounted to see the patient. The Daoist happened to arrive, and his words were the same as before. The official insisted on knowing what was needed, and the answer was "meihua piannao" (a type of medicine). The Daoist laughed and said, "This is not difficult to obtain." He immediately sent a servant to fetch it and gave it to the patient. The Daoist ground it into powder and applied it to the patient's tongue. It immediately retracted. Using two coins' worth, the disease was cured instantly. These two stories were told by Ye Xingji, who was known as Xiaogong. #### The Chicken-Headed Creature Xu Jiqing, a government official, lived thirty miles north of Quzhou. In the sixth year of the Ganda period (1170), during the day, there was a creature standing under the wall. It had a human body with a chicken's head and was about one zhang long. A concubine went out and saw it, and she was startled and died immediately. A strong servant held tiles and stones to strike it, but it seemed to feel nothing. After a long time, it disappeared. Xu's second son was an official in Xiuzhou. A few days later, he heard the news of her death, which was the same day the creature appeared. Xu Jiqing, however, lived a long and healthy life and was not harmed by the creature. The creature could not cause him any harm. This story was told by a relative of Xu Jiqing.

The Fourteenth Volume of the *Yiji Zhi* contains twelve stories. **Wu Zhenren** Wu Zhenren's name was Yuanzhao. She was a commoner's daughter from Xiaoshan, Kuaiji. When she was an infant, if her mother ate meat, she would not breastfeed for the whole day; but if she ate vegetables, she would breastfeed as usual. Her mother found this very strange. As she grew older, it was proposed to marry her to a wealthy man in the county. After the betrothal gifts were exchanged, Yuanzhao was discontented and unhappy. Her mother taught her needlework, and she sat and dozed off. Her mother scolded her, and she apologized, saying, "I am not being lazy. Last night, I dreamed of a golden-armored deity who summoned me to the earth goddess. He accompanied me there, and we entered a vast hall in the clouds. I saw a high-ranking deity sitting on the throne, with jade maidens standing in attendance. He called me to ascend the hall and instructed me, saying, 'You are originally a jade maiden. You were temporarily exiled to the mortal world due to a past transgression. You will return after three decades. You should now return to rest.' I then gave up worldly affairs. Upon waking, I wanted to abstain from food, but my mother insisted. I had another dream where the deity was angry, saying, 'I commanded you not to eat. Why did you disobey me?' He cut open my abdomen, removed my intestines, washed them in a jade basin, and then reinserted them and sealed my abdomen. He then bestowed upon me the *Lingbao Dadao* method and the *Dadao Dafashi Huifeng Hexie Zhenren Yin* (a Taoist ritual and seal) to heal those in the world who are ill. My mother, upon hearing this, was startled and realized, 'My daughter is no ordinary person. I will break off the marriage for her and let her fulfill her aspirations.'

From then on, he lived alone in a clean room, using talismans and water to treat illnesses. People from far and near rushed to request talismans, and sometimes he was invited to visit homes to examine sicknesses. When that happened, he would order two attendants to carry him in a palanquin. He did not bring provisions with him, and when they reached the middle of the journey, his attendants grew hungry. He merely bought two peaches, blew on them, and gave them to his attendants. Each person ate one peach and could travel dozens of miles without feeling hungry. The imperial inspector T Chen lived in Qiantang, where he used the *Tianxin Fa* method to treat diseases. Beside his residence, he built a multi-story building in a garden. The gardener reported that someone was riding upon it. Chen scolded him away, saying, "How could that be?" At dusk, he carried his sword and seal and stayed below. He also heard the sound of a horse. Soon after, his family knocked on the door, urging him to return, saying, "Your young daughter is tied in midair, as if bound by some force." Upon seeing this, it was indeed so. The girl was unconscious and unaware. For many days, Chen went to the building to perform a ritual to subdue the disturbance. Fire broke out on the wall, and the attendants ran down, and the fire also ceased. He then summoned a Daoist priest to deal with it. When he reached the gate, he had lost his hat. The family became even more frightened, and sent a letter to summon Yuanzhao. Yuanzhao, dressed in formal attire, went to visit him. Chen's daughter then rose, greeting him at the door with laughter, as if she had never been ill. Yuanzhao took her to stay in the upper room. For three days and nights, he saw nothing, and the girl was also calm and composed. Han Ziying, a descendant of a Grand Marshal, served in the imperial court.

Once, Han wrote a chapter on his own, intending to present it to heaven, recounting his encounters with the Great Supreme and the rise of auspicious fortune. No one knew about it. He invited Zhao to present it. Han prostrated himself for a long time before rising, reciting the words of the chapter. Not a single word was false. He said, "The Supreme God praised your tranquil and peaceful nature without any desire for fortune." The divine response was, "Be careful to observe the thousand-two-day cycle of the celestial officials and reward your merit." Later, everything happened as Zhao had said. Han had had a foot ailment since childhood. Every time he tried to bend or stretch, he could not. Zhao massaged him, and he felt a hot sensation in his waist. When Zhao massaged his thigh, it also became hot. A warm vapor emerged from his toes, and immediately he walked on the ground. His illness was completely cured. Han's servant stayed in a small, narrow hut beside the cottage. At night, he dreamed of a ghostly figure pressing on his body, shouted, and came out. Just as Zhao arrived, he did not tell him. So they walked there together. Zhao returned to the door and said, "There is someone who hanged themselves in the room." The figure had disheveled hair and a protruding tongue, who saw Zhao and asked for deliverance. Zhao immediately wrote a talisman and ordered the servant to burn it. At night, a person thanked him, saying, "I received the true official's talisman and was reborn. I will not come back again." He opened the door and went out. Han's family prepared a bed to invite Zhao to sleep. No sound of breathing was heard. Gradually, blue clouds rose from his nose. A baby about three inches long, with a color like green jade, appeared.

A light shone on a bed, revolving above the abdomen. After a while, it disappeared. In the household of Zhang Jun, a maid was pregnant but did not give birth beyond the due date. Someone asked the light to come and look. The maids stood together, and the light only looked at the pregnant maid. It exclaimed with sighs, saying, "In a past life, you were a woodcutter who once killed a big snake. Now it is your enemy, eating your five organs in the womb, and will not stop until it is done." Quickly, someone reported to the king to expel it. The king wrote two talismans and gave them to the maid. The maid burned the talismans as instructed and gave it water to drink. She then gave birth to a big snake. The king respected and honored it, wanting to give it gold and silk, but it refused. The light again went to the Han family, staying for more than a year, and wanted to return. The Han family tried to stop it, but it could not be stopped. It cried and bid farewell, saying, "I will not come again." It took a boat to return to Xiaoshan and died without illness at home. Previously, in the town, more than ten families all saw it wearing Daoist robes, each going to their own home to gather and talk, and after a while, they left. A few days later, someone went to visit it at its home, and the family said it had died. Several people from the town arrived one after another, all saying, "Yesterday he just came to my home." The day it visited the people was verified to be the day of its spirit release. It was the eleventh year of the Shaoxing era, and Han Yu said.

**Thoughtful Heart Study Room** Zhào Shànlián and his younger brother lived in Qúzhōu. They studied at a temple within the city walls. They named a small room "Yì Lè Zhāi" (Also Joy Study Room). That year, Zhào Shànlián passed the preliminary examination but failed the imperial examination. Someone told him that the character "乐" (joy) has the same pronunciation as "落" (fall), which scholars deeply avoid. Therefore, it was not suitable to name the room after it. He then changed the name to "Jū Yì Zhāi" (Easy Living Study Room). After a long time, he dreamed of an old man with a tall hat and white beard who came to visit and said, "You changed the name because you thought the '乐' character was not beautiful. But don't you think that '居易' is the name of the Tang Dynasty poet Bai Juyi? The name 'Bai Lètiān' (Bai Juyi) is particularly inappropriate." Zhào Shànlián apologized and asked, "What should I do then?" The old man said, "You should name it 'Chǔn Xīn Zhāi' (Thoughtful Heart Study Room)." Upon waking, Zhào Shànlián changed the name. He then passed the imperial examination in the 5th year of the Gāndào era and was appointed as an official of the Chánggòng provincial government. He told me this story. **Old Man of Mingzhou** In a village called Xiǎoxī Cūn, 50 li (about 25 kilometers) outside the city of Míngzhōu, a wealthy man built a large mansion. The gates, corridors, and halls were all like those of a high-ranking official's residence. Someone advised him that such a mansion was not suitable for a common household, but he did not listen. The mansion was completed, but the wealthy man died before it was fully finished. His son could not maintain the mansion. Meanwhile, Premier Wèi Nánfū, who lived in the city, had no place to live. Upon leaving his post, he found a million in his empty bag and bought the mansion. The family often saw the elderly man coming and going, sighing as if he had a grudge. They told the Premier, and he had a small room built and molded an image of the land god. From then on, the old man no longer appeared. Xu Kang told this story.

**A Dream of Thousands of Chickens** A scholar from Xianan Prefecture dreamed of hundreds or thousands of chickens flying and circling in the courtyard. At the time, he was preparing to take the imperial examinations. He was puzzled by the dream and wondered if it was a good omen. He shared the dream with a close friend, who said, "People say that chickens have five virtues. If there are so many in your dream, it must mean you will achieve great success. You should be happy." Indeed, the scholar passed the examinations and became a scholar-official. This story was told by Luo Jie. **Wu Tangong** Wu Tangong was originally an official in the Buddhist monastic system in Lang Prefecture. He was a heavy drinker and often caused trouble. One night, he knocked on a tavern's door to buy wine. When the tavern keeper opened the door too slowly, Wu Tangong struck him in the chest and killed him. He then fled over the city walls. Eventually, he reached Guoqing Temple in Taizhou, where he introduced himself as Wu Dao Ren and claimed to be skilled in medicine. He healed many people, but his methods were unconventional. He would only treat patients after drinking several liters of wine and getting thoroughly drunk. Despite his drunkenness, his patients always recovered. Later, he traveled to Jiangshan County in Quzhou, where the wife of a local noble, Lady Mao, had a prolonged pregnancy of twenty-four months without giving birth. Wu Tangong, drunk as usual, wanted to examine her. Lady Mao said, "Master Wu is drunk. Can we wait until tomorrow?" Wu Tangong replied, "I am drunk, but the patient is not." He then entered and called for several liters of wine. After drinking, he said, "Your lady is not pregnant. She has been affected by something unusual. Fortunately, the thing she is carrying has not yet emerged. If it takes another fifteen days, it could be dangerous. I will explain the condition. She has a fondness for chicken."

Every time he ate, he would have a maid tie a live chicken in front of him. He would slowly watch it die. At dawn, he would eat a full meal and would not eat again for the rest of the day. Is it really like this? Mr. Yan was shocked and said, "Indeed." Wu negotiated and demanded up to 200,000 coins. Finally, he left a dose of medicine. He instructed his family to prepare a large bowl and a sharp knife, saying, "Once you take the medicine, you will suffer from severe abdominal pain in the middle of the night. Call me then." As promised, he did indeed suffer greatly from the pain. They quickly called him in. When he entered, it turned out that Mrs. Mao had just given birth to something. Wu held a knife and cut it into two, covering it with a bowl. He ordered the maid to support the pregnant woman and walk around the room. The next morning, when they opened the bowl to look inside, it was a large turtle. Its head and limbs were fully formed, and its eyes were open. It was specially wrapped in membranes, so it couldn't move quickly, and thus it couldn't kill anyone. Mr. Yan respectfully thanked him. He wanted to fulfill the original agreement and also offered his wine shop to him. They all laughed and refused, saying, "I was just joking with you." In the Jianyan period, he died in Guoqing Temple at the age of over eighty. The monk Dao Yi from Guoqing Temple, who studied medicine from him, told this story.

A Tale of Kung Dou Kung Dou, a jailer in Raoyang Prefecture, had long been acquainted with a woman from a tavern. One day, as he passed her door, a quarrel arose over some unspecified reason. The local military officer, Xia Sheng, happened to witness the scene. The next morning, the woman reported the incident to the prefectural authorities, and Xia Sheng supported her account. Kung Dou was punished with a beating and harbored a grudge against the woman. Several days later, as he left for the east side of Bingping Market to buy wine, Xia Sheng was already there. Seeing Kung Dou enter, Xia Sheng fled through the back door. Kung Dou returned directly to the military supply depot and reported the private wine to the authorities. The officials then pursued the wine seller and his neighbors, sending them to prison. The case was resolved, and the wine seller was sentenced to penal labor. His property was confiscated, and his house was demolished. The neighbors were equally rewarded with money, and Xia Sheng was also punished. The wine seller was required to pay out a hundred thousand coins as compensation, which he could not afford, leading him to sell his daughter. Unable to bear the resentment, he led his neighbors to the Temple of the Eastern Mountain, fully reporting the private grievances between Kung Dou and Xia Sheng. The transfer of anger had ruined his family, and they prayed to the gods to take charge. That day, Kung Dou was at home, suddenly struck by shock and fear, unable to control himself. He called his wife, children, and villagers to gather and sit together. Past midnight, he said that he had been captured by more than ten people. Relying on the strong support of his fellow villagers, he decided to leave now. Before dawn, he searched for and put on a shirt, saying, "I should go to the prison office." That evening, he did not return home. After five days, someone reported that a man had drowned in Danjin Lake. Kung Dou's wife was suspicious and believed it must be her husband. When the local official fished out the body, it turned out to be Kung Dou. It is said that Kung Dou, driven by a momentary anger, had caused such disturbances and ruin to various families. Therefore, he was punished by the gods. This occurred in the first month of the Chongxi era, the first year of Emperor Xiaozong's reign.

The Divine of Baiya In the Baiya Temple of Shaxi County, Zitong, Sichuan, there is a shrine dedicated to Lord Lu, an official who was said to be surnamed Lu and named Bi. He passed away as the Governor of Luzhou during the Liang Dynasty. The temple's offerings have become increasingly abundant. On the seventh day of the tenth month of the eighth year of the Zhenghe era (1118 AD), Mr. Guo Zhi, a literatus from Sichuan, returned from Changzhou to Linan. He stayed overnight at Laisuan Post Station and dreamed of being summoned by two officials. They led him several miles to a grand and beautiful government office. The hall was adorned with two embroidered mats, and the guards below were solemn and respectful. Soon, ten officials in red and purple robes arrived, accompanied by a divine figure wearing a purple robe and gold belt. They led Mr. Guo Zhi to stand opposite the deity, who stared without speaking. The deity then looked at him and said, "Change your clothes." Mr. Guo Zhi retreated to the west wing. The official said, "The deity claims to have a connection with you from the same year of the imperial examination. You should pay him respects. Why didn't you say anything?" The deity was very displeased. Mr. Guo Zhi asked, "Who is the deity?" The deity replied, "I am the deity of the Xianhui Temple in Shaxi. I was formerly the Pacification Commissioner of the southern part of Luzhou and the Prefect of Yingzhou." His son said, "He is now the Governor of Jianzhou." Mr. Guo Zhi then realized that he was indeed from the same year of the imperial examination as the son. He was very afraid and said, "If so, I would like to decline due to my lack of ability. And bow to him, is that acceptable?" The official said, "Yes." He bowed twice and approached the mat. They exchanged pleasantries and details, and Mr. Guo Zhi bowed again. The deity was pleased and accepted the bow.

A polite greeting was given according to the customs of the world. He then took his seat. The god said, "I have been in Shu for more than two decades. Previously, I passed by the temple of Lord Lu and left behind a poem saying, 'The Prefect of Luzhou was not demoted. It should be that the dragon returned to its old cave.' It was widely recited at the time and was pointed out as a warning. Later, because of speaking out, I incurred guilt, abandoned my office, and withdrew from the world. I have now settled here. I only regret that the Wang clan has no one who knows. I asked you in Jianzhou to tell my son. Zhi respectfully agreed. Six days after waking up, he arrived at Jianchi. He tried to see the governor but was not granted an audience and could not inform. The next year, he passed through Zizhou and again dreamed that the god summoned him. The god reprimanded him for breaking his word. Zhi felt ashamed and apologized. The god said, "This time, you must definitely tell him for me. I have recently done much good for the people. In no time, I will also receive a slight increase in rank and ceremonial honors." Zhi then rushed to Jian, wrote down the dream and delivered it. The governor was moved to tears and searched the family for the poem and found it. The official's name was Xianke, courtesy name Buzhi. He changed from a literary career to a military one and served up to the position of Various Offices Envoy and Governor of Yingzhou, overseeing Luzhou, and died. Wasn't he replacing Lord Lu as the White Cliff God? The matter of the dragon returning to its cave is recorded in the temple's inscription. In the sixth year of Xuanhe, Yuwen Xu Zhong and Yun were both in the Pacification Office of Hebei and wrote an inscription saying.

Compassionate Pearl of the Cigan Temple During the Dazhong Xiangfu period, a man from Huzhou named Shao Zongyi bought a mussel from the market. Upon cooking and cutting it open, he found a pearl inside that resembled a Bodhisattva or Arhat in appearance. The pearl had its right shoulder exposed, a slightly turned head to the left, and detailed clothing patterns. The viewers were both astonished and respectful. The pearl was then taken and presented to the Cigan Temple, where the monks kept it in a chest or box. Whenever visitors came, the pearl was shown to them. Ye Shaoyun wrote a poem about the pearl, which read: In the depths of the nine abysses, a mysterious creature dances, tempting with its allure. In play, who would know that I am supreme? My form does not shy away from being among the unusual. Why should I cling to the source of my existence? Upon returning, I am said to have transformed from the dragon palace. It is only after long residence that one marvels at the existence of the Eagle Peak. This tale requires the wisdom of the old Master Mo. Compassionate and unobstructed, I have no bounds. At one time, many distinguished scholars composed poems in response. Zeng Gongyun wrote: How vast is the shell that contains the universe? It is said to have been formed from the clear waters of Wujiang. It connects to ancient and profound spiritual sources. Even when the moon sinks into murky waters, its luminous and pure nature remains. Like a lotus that rises from the mud, its inherent value and purity are preserved. Its appearance and disappearance are consistent with its original nature. Do not mistake its illusory nature for emptiness. The Cigan Temple is situated by a stream. During the Jianyan period, the official Yang Yingcheng, responsible for judicial and administrative affairs in the Two Zhejiang regions, was showing the pearl to guests when, without realizing it, he stepped over the threshold and fell into the water. The guests were startled, and he immediately prayed to the Buddha. They searched for the pearl in the misty and distant waters and found it with one search. Ge Changzhi wrote a poem about the incident.

Pai and Hao's Wives and Concubines Pai, the son of a certain official with the title of *Dai Zhi*, during the Jianyan era, resigned from his post as magistrate of a certain county in Jin Prefecture and sought refuge in Sichuan. After some time, he was appointed to oversee the salt wells at the Great Ning Supervision. He took his family with him to assume his new post. His wife had a five-year-old son and a three-year-old daughter. They all traveled together on a single boat, but Pai secretly took a woman from outside the household and traveled separately in a small boat to visit her daily. They were often several miles apart, and sometimes they would lose track of each other in the evening. His wife secretly knew about this. One morning, she sent a child with a box to Pai’s place and said, “Madam has sent some snacks for you.” When Pai opened the box, he found the heads of his two children. He was shocked and in pain, as if in a trance. He stopped at the table and waited for her to arrive. When she came, he committed suicide. Pai eventually went to his new post with his concubine, but he conducted himself carelessly. He was investigated by the prefectural governor Wang. His family had much wealth, which they poured out and offered to Wang, and he barely managed to escape punishment. Not long after, Pai also died. Hao Shizhuang, who once served as the magistrate of Dianjiang in Zhong Prefecture, later stayed at a Buddhist temple in Kuifu. His wife died first, and he had one concubine with a son. The concubine took charge of the household affairs. One day, Hao invited people from the same temple to drink with him. Someone pointed at the wall and laughed, saying, “This place has no tiles. It is also smooth and clean. Is it not that the monks secretly climbed over the wall to enter your inner quarters?” Hao believed it to be true and began to scold his concubine day and night, suspecting her in countless ways, even over small matters. The concubine could not bear her grievances and anger. She waited for Hao to leave early in the morning and then killed his son, hiding the knife under her clothes. When Hao heard of the incident and rushed back, he tried to enter the door, but another maid happened to be sweeping in front and blinked to send him away. The wicked concubine knew she could do nothing and also committed suicide. Women, by nature, are fierce and cruel, so she killed her son and committed suicide without hesitation. Such cases may have been recorded in historical texts.

During the Zhenghe era, the Judicial Commissioner of Shaanxi, Guo Yunding, invited the high-ranking official Ye to a banquet. Guo presented his household entertainers to entertain the guests. One of the concubines had lost favor with her master and was skilled at pleasing the guests. While drunk, Ye made a jest about her, saying, "I will ask my master to take you. He will surely agree. I will choose a day and send a carriage to pick you up." The concubine was overjoyed and believed his words sincerely. She waited anxiously day and night, neglecting sleep and meals, and gradually fell ill. Her condition worsened until she could no longer move. When others came to visit her, she would always ask, "Has the carriage from Ye's office come yet?" The following year on the Lantern Festival, she managed to get herself dressed and ready, telling others, "We had a previous agreement for today. And indeed, a sedan chair arrived. I am leaving now." As soon as she took a step, she died suddenly. It turned out that Ye's drunken jest was forgotten almost immediately, and this concubine had become so infatuated with the idea that it led to her death. Both Guo Yunding and Ye were stationed in Hedong Prefecture at the time. My father-in-law, who was a respected scholar, mentioned this incident to me.

A Farmer from Le Ping A farmer from Le Ping returned after planting rice. He was called out by someone. He saw several people outside, with strange and ugly appearances. They commanded him to carry a burden and passed through several villages, including Hongyuan, Shicun, Hechong, and other villages. For each village, they first went to the place of the village god and said they intended to cause an epidemic. They were all refused and not allowed. The strange group said among themselves, "Then only Liu Village and Liu's house can be visited." So they went and entered directly into the guest room under the veranda and stayed there, with no provisions for food, drink, pillows, or beds. The next morning, Liu's son came out. The leader of the strange group told his followers, "Strike this person's right foot." The stick just touched down, and the son immediately fell to the ground. Then an old woman passed by, and they ordered to strike her left foot. The old woman fell as before. They have harmed three people already. But they only stayed in one room and did not roam around. A spy secretly reported that a tiger was jumping forward from the front. It was very frightening. The leader's face remained unchanged.

Two spirits were sent to wait for him, holding staffs. They were instructed to strike his feet twice upon arrival. Soon after, it was reported that the tiger had died under the staffs. Two days later, the scout urgently reported that there was a fire to the north. In a short while, the flames reached the house. The mountain and water came again in large amounts. The spirits looked at each other in panic. They did not have time to take their luggage and rushed alone. They were angry that the farmer was not working hard and pushed him into a ditch. He suddenly got up and found himself lying in bed. His wife and children had been crying around him for three days. The villagers asked D Liu about the matter. He said that his two sons and a maid were simultaneously suffering from an epidemic. He called a shaman to treat them, but they died at the door. He then invited another shaman, who was cautious due to the previous incident and wanted to catch the spirits off guard. He performed a ritual from the back door, holding a knife and blowing a horn. He recited a water-fire wheel spell and entered. The sick ones were all well the next day. I wrote in the *Yi Zhi* about the case of Shi Tian Wang Shi Wu being driven to Xuancheng by a plague spirit, which is quite similar.

**A Dog Struck by Thunder** On the fifteenth day of the sixth month in the first year of the Chongxi era, a great thunderstorm hit Raozhou. In a market shop, a guest had brought along a hunting dog, which had been there for several days. On that day at noon, the dog lay under a tea table. Suddenly, dense clouds obscured the house, and the shop grew dark. The guest's wife called out to the dog, but a man with an emerald face dragged her away. Soon after, the sky cleared. The dog was already dead, with all its fur scorched upright. Many roof tiles were shattered. The dog's sin remains unknown, but the thunder's power was also disrespectful. The Collection of Strange Stories from a Lonely Studio, Volume 15, Sixteenth Story (Original missing twenty-eight lines) Two doctors named Tan and Li are missing.** **The inner text is missing.** **The story of Huang taking gallbladder is missing.** **The inner text is missing.** **The story of Jia Mu having a bad dream is missing.** **The inner text is missing.** **A Dream of a Turtle Giving a Formula A scholar from Jizhou named Xu Pan fell off a horse and fractured his hands and feet, suffering great pain. He ordered a physician to treat him. The prescription required a live turtle. After obtaining it, he had a dream in which the turtle spoke: "I can only relieve pain but not set bones. There is a special formula to share. Please do not harm me." Xu Pan asked about it. The turtle said, "Take one catty of fresh rehmannia, four ounces of ginger, grind finely, mix with one catty of bran, and stir-fry evenly. Wrap with cloth while hot and apply to the injured area. Change when it cools. Firstly, it can relieve pain. Then set bones. It has great miraculous effects." Xu Pan used this method and it proved effective.

**Tian San Gu** She was the daughter of Tian Ruo of Zizhou, and she married the son of Liu, the Censorate Official of You County. Liu had passed away several years before. When Tian fell ill, she sent a servant to Hengshan to summon her cousin by marriage, Zhang Minzhong, intending to entrust him with her affairs. But before he could arrive, Tian passed away. Originally, Tian had a brother who married a daughter of the Liao family from Hengshan. After the daughter died, he took her sister as his wife. Later, the brother also died, leaving only the younger sister-in-law behind. She then went with Minzhong to pay her respects, and they stayed at Zhang’s old home, the Mei Mountain Pavilion. It was the winter of the year Jia Shen in the Longxing era. That night, Liao’s sister-in-law suddenly fell victim to a severe heart pain. After treatment, she slightly recovered. Past midnight, she abruptly sat up, speaking incoherently. Zhang went to check on her, and then her sister’s spirit possessed her. She scolded her sister sharply, saying: “Where is there not a marriage? You must have wanted to share one husband with me. After I died, you did not set up a shrine to worship me, leaving me with no place to rely upon during the seasons. We must go together to the afterlife.” Zhang tried to reason with her, saying, “This is the doing of Tian’s younger brother. It is not your fault, Aunt. We are sisters of the same family; could you truly bear to do this?” A short while later, she raised her hands in a gesture of greeting and said, “May Uncle be well.” Then she said, “Qing Sun, you may sit on the bed.” “Uncle” referred to Tian San’s youngest paternal uncle, Ruo. “Qing Sun” was the name of her young child. Both had long since died. Indeed, the room was filled with ghosts, and those around her were all terrified. Soon, she opened her eyes and changed appearance, speaking in the voice of the Tian family. She looked at Zhang and said, “The magistrate knows that it is Aunt who has come. Aunt had something to say before her death.”

A man from Fanyang named Wang Cheng. His family was quite wealthy. He alone enjoyed fishing and hunting as a pastime. He was around thirty years old. He died in May of the ninth year of the Gandaode era. His wife was a woman from the village named Yu. She gradually took his playthings and gave them to others. Or destroyed them. On the first night of the seventh month the following year, she was in bed but not yet asleep. She felt her entire body tremble. She was frightened and called out to her wet nurse. The wet nurse also felt the same way. Soon, she began speaking as if Wang Cheng was speaking. She scolded his wife saying, "You lowly woman, how long have I been dead? You have already conceived the idea of remarrying someone else." His two sons were around ten years old. The family's wealth was not insignificant. Could you not have waited until the end of the mourning period? I greatly loved the parrot cage. And the double-horned bow. Why did you give them to Uncle Thirty-Five? Uncle Thirty-Five is his wife's brother, Zhong Tao. Who lived right next door. He secretly peered through the wall. Wang Cheng spoke sternly saying, "Why don't you come inside and see me? But instead you were eavesdropping." Zhong Tao was afraid and left immediately. He then asked for his second brother. His brother excused himself with illness. Then he sighed and said, "We were not close when we were alive. I knew he wouldn't come. Can I see my father?" My father is old and ill. He came in, supporting himself with a cane and crying. Wang Cheng bowed and greeted him. As if he were listening obediently. Father, you have spoken. Wang Cheng should leave. The wet nurse then fainted and became silent. After two meals' time. She then spoke to her son and said, "I will leave, but the land spirits are obstructing me. You should prepare a small sacrifice. Speak well on my behalf." The son quickly killed a chicken and fetched wine. He went to the shrine to pray for release. The wet nurse then woke up. The Yu family's guest said at the time.

**Nie Jin, Who Ate Forbidden Foods** Nie Jin was a man from Beijing. His family had long followed Daoism, refraining from eating dog, goose, turtle, garlic, and similar foods. Yet Jin alone took pleasure in eating such things. His father often warned him, saying, "You will stop." But later, he was again as he had been before. At the age of twenty-two, he fell seriously ill with typhoid fever. He saw a man dressed in green clothes come to call him, and so he followed him. They crossed mountains and rivers until they arrived at a great city gate. The gate official asked, "Who is this person?" The green-clad man replied, "This is Nie Jin." The official said, "He has come. You may proceed quickly." They soon arrived at a palace. At the entrance, another official stood, his robe very grand. He raised his voice and asked, "Who is this?" The green-clad man again replied, "This is Nie Jin." The official said, "He has come. The officials have been waiting long. You may enter quickly." Jin was greatly frightened and hesitated, standing beneath the eaves. Someone called out, commanding him to ascend the steps. Jin secretly raised his head and saw three men, all dressed in royal attire, seated at a table. They addressed him, saying, "You love to eat forbidden foods. Although your father and brothers warned you, you did not heed them. What is the reason for this? What is good about these things?" Jin fell to the ground and said, "I now receive this strict decree. From now on, I will definitely stop eating them." The king said, "If you truly can do this, we will allow you to return." Jin replied, "If I again think of this, the crime is punishable by death, and no forgiveness will be given." The king ordered the officials to send him back. Jin traveled in darkness, not knowing where he was going. When he arrived home, he saw his wife and children gathered and weeping. The officials pushed him, and he fell onto the bed, blood flowing from his nose, about two dou in quantity. After some time, he gradually recovered. Later, Jin returned from the north just as he was appointed to an official post. It was the first year of the Chunjixi era (1174 CE), and he was already forty-nine years old. He was appointed as a *Bingyi Lang* and added to the supervision of the wine tax in Fuzhou. He himself recounted this event.

**New Guang You Wang** The Temple of the Newly Recognized Spirit of Protection on the North Dagan Mountain in Shaowu Commandery. According to historical records, it is the spirit of General Ouyang from the late Tang Dynasty. It is twenty miles from the county. Opposite the road stands a small house with several rooms, used to accommodate visitors to the temple. There is a monk named Wang who lives nearby, diligently performing cleaning duties. In the fourth year of the Gandao era, he dreamed of a procession of chariots and horses filling the fields, with grand ceremonial attire and vehicles, as if welcoming a provincial governor or military commander, all coming out from the temple. He asked where they were going, and a clerk replied, "They are far away to greet the new Guang You Wang." He then asked, "Who is this new Guang You Wang, and where does he reside?" The clerk answered, "He resides in Pucheng County, and he is the late Deputy Magistrate of Linjiang, Mr. Chen." Upon waking, he remembered the words spoken in the dream. The next day, he went directly to the place mentioned in the dream to inquire. Indeed, there was a Mr. Chen who had passed the imperial examination and was known for his honesty and uprightness, a man highly regarded by his fellow townspeople. He had died only five days ago. The monk verified the dream as credible and was delighted. He gradually told others about the dream and its fulfillment. He still lives near the temple.

In the southern gate of Fuzhou, on Huangbai Road, lived two brothers named Zhan Liu and Zhan Qi. They made a living by selling and trading fine silk and cloth. Their youngest brother was called Xiaoge. He once lost money gambling and feared his older brothers' beating and scolding. He ran away to another place and did not return for a long time. His mother thought of him more deeply, and both her dreams and divinations were ominous. She simply believed that he had died. During the Zhongyuan Festival, the night before, the Zhan family spread out paper money to await the offering. As dusk approached, there was a faint sigh coming from outside. The mother said, "Xiaoge must have truly died. He is now telling me." She took a strand of money and prayed, "If it is indeed my son, he should be able to pull this money out. If so, it will be a sign. I will seek your help from the spirit world." Soon, a cold wind blew, and someone seemed to reach out and pull the money out. The mother and her brothers cried out in shock. They quickly called a monk to recite scriptures to help Xiaoge's spirit. They no longer hoped for his return. Several months later, Xiaoge suddenly appeared from outside. The eldest brother said, "It's a ghost!" He grabbed a knife to chase after him. The second brother quickly held him back and said, "Not yet." He stepped forward and looked closely at Xiaoge, asking about his death or life. Xiaoge replied, "I was afraid of being beaten, so I ran away and went to Yihuang to take on work. I did not die." They then realized that the previous events were due to a ghost's deception. Chao Duankui Chao Duankui lived in the capital. He was attracted to a young woman in his neighborhood and gazed around, expressing his feelings, but could not marry her. One night, the woman came to him and pulled his clothes, asking to share a bed. Chao was very happy, but she left before dawn. He could not keep her and wondered if she was afraid that the family would know. After she left, there were stains of blood on the bedding, and no one knew why they appeared. Three days later, as he passed by, he heard crying sounds. He knocked on his neighbors' doors and was told that the young woman had died due to childbirth. She had died three days ago. Chao wiped his tears and returned home.

**The Woman on the Water** During the Zhenghe era, there was a magistrate of judicial affairs in the Jingxi Road named Zhou, whose name has been lost to history. He was known for his imposing and upright demeanor, which instilled fear and respect in the counties. Once, while returning from an inspection tour aboard a boat, he saw a woman-like figure on the water, about a foot tall, with flowing sleeves, approaching the boat from below. The boat docked nearby, and the figure had a sad and sorrowful expression, as if she had something to complain about. When the figure was just a step or two away, she disappeared without a trace. The officials initially suspected it was a coincidence. The next day, the same sight was repeated, and her expression became even more sorrowful. Zhou believed it must be the spirit of a wronged person seeking justice. He halted the boat and immediately sent a messenger to a nearby county to find a singer with sharp and intelligent speech. No one understood why they were doing this. Once the singer arrived, the officials donned their formal attire, burned incense, and prayed to the spirit. They said, "If you truly have a grievance, you may rely on this singer to speak for you. We will seek justice for you." Soon, the singer's demeanor changed, becoming solemn and tearful. Her voice sounded like that of someone from another state. She said, "I am a woman from a certain state, county, and family. I was murdered by someone for their wealth. The matter was never reported to the authorities, and I had no way to clear my name. I dare to express my lingering grievance." Zhou recorded her words and secretly issued an order to the neighboring prefecture. They captured the criminal, who confessed everything upon questioning. The criminal was then punished according to the law. Later, Zhou was appointed as the magistrate of Yihuang and was known for his association with the Buddhist monk Liao Ru, abbot of Shushan Monastery, whose name has been forgotten. Some say he was the father of Ma Zhen, a prominent figure in the imperial court.

A Man Named Zhang Gui Revived In the customs of the Jiang and Wu regions, a cold disease is referred to as an epidemic. When a person's breath just stops, they are immediately buried and left in the outskirts, and cannot stay for even a moment. A commoner named Zhang Gui from Linchuan died and was placed in a coffin at Guangze Temple in the west of the city. The temple monk Liutao heard a rustling sound at night and, upon investigating, found it coming from Zhang Gui's coffin. He dared not open it to look inside. Being separated by several miles from the city, he had no way to inform anyone. He could only bow his hands in respect. After a long while, the sound ceased. At dawn, he rushed to inform Zhang Gui's family, who did not ask any questions. By autumn, they were about to cremate him. When they opened the coffin, they saw the body lying on one side with the face covered, and the clothes all torn apart. It seemed that Zhang Gui had revived the previous night but was unable to stretch out. How pitiful! In Fanyang as well, there was a commoner who, in June, worshiped at the temple of the Emperor of Mount Hua and, overcome by the heat, fainted and died. He was quickly buried in a coffin at Putong Tower. The events were the same.

A Strange Encounter of Zhang Ke Zhang Ke, a villager from Yudan, entered the town for trading purposes and stayed at an inn. One night, he dreamed of a woman dressed in bright and beautiful clothes who asked to share a bed with him. When he woke up, she was still beside him. She stayed until morning before leaving. The next evening, when he finally closed the door, the lamp was still burning, and she stood in front of him again. They lay down together once more. She told him where she came from, saying, "I am a girl from a neighboring house. Don't talk much." For several days, Zhang Ke's mood was somewhat distracted, and the innkeeper became suspicious. The innkeeper said, "There was a person who hanged themselves here in the past. Could it be that you are being deceived?" Zhang Ke kept it secret and did not speak, waiting for her to come. When she returned, he asked her in detail, and she showed no sign of shame or embarrassment. She said, "Yes." Zhang Ke became intimate with her and was not afraid. He asked her in detail about the truth, and she said, "I was a courtesan. I had a close relationship with a man named Yang. He took two hundred thousand coins from me and promised to marry me according to proper rites. However, he did not keep his promise for three years. I became depressed and fell ill. Unable to live, my family gradually grew tired of me. Overwhelmed with anger, I hanged myself. My family sold my home to others. Now it is a shop. This room was my former home, and I still miss it and cannot bear to leave. Yang, your fellow townsman, also knows him. Do you know him?" Zhang Ke said, "I know him. I heard that he moved to the market gate in Raozhou, married a wife, and opened a shop. His life is very smooth." The woman sighed for a long time and said, "I will entrust my life and death to you. I remember burying fifty taels of silver under the bed. No one knows about it."

Zhang found gold when he opened the ground, and as said, it was not a false statement. From then on, the woman also came out during the day. One day, she whispered, "Staying here for a long time is of no benefit. Can you kindly take me back?" Zhang agreed, and he wrote a tablet, saying, "Nian'er Lady's position," sealed it in a box, and whenever they arrived at a place, he opened the seal and called softly, and then she would come out to meet. Zhang always followed her, and she finished dressing and left. The innkeeper thought Zhang was deeply affected by the ghostly aura and would surely die on the road, but Zhang did not find it suspicious at all and continued to travel day after day, always together with the ghost. When they reached home, Zhang slowly opened the tablet on the wall, and his wife thought it was a god he was paying respects to, and then the woman came out. His wife questioned her husband, "Who is that person? Don't bring trouble to our family by bringing a good girl." Zhang told her everything, and his wife, greedy for the gold, did not question further. They stayed together for five days, and then she asked to go to the state capital to collect debts. Zhang agreed. When they reached the southern part of the city and were crossing the river, the woman came out and said, "I am greatly ashamed and thank you. How can it be that we are together for such a short time?" Zhang cried, not understanding what was meant. They entered the city gate as usual, but when they reached the inn, they called for her again and again, but could not see her. They hurriedly visited Yang's lodging, which was in great disorder, and a neighbor said, "Yang Yuan had no illness. He just died with blood flowing from all seven orifices." Zhang was frightened and hurriedly returned, and never met her again. Wu Yanzhou from Linchuan used to stay at Zhang's hometown and could talk about the strange events, but did not have time to investigate thoroughly.

Wu Er's Filial Piety In the eastern part of Lichuan, there lived a humble commoner named Wu Er. He served the Five Tong Gods with great effectiveness. All financial gains and losses, he must first secretly inform. Suddenly, the gods appeared to him in a dream and said, "You will be struck by lightning and die at noon tomorrow." Wu Er begged for salvation. The gods replied, "This is a command from heaven. It cannot be avoided." Although Wu Er was a lowly person, he was extremely filial to his mother. Early in the morning, he prepared food to offer. The gods told him, "White clouds will take you away." He asked to temporarily visit his sister's house, but his mother did not allow it. Soon, dark clouds gathered, and at noon, the sky became dark. The thunder roared. Wu Er became more worried about frightening his mother, so he hurriedly told them to close the doors. He went out and sat in the field, awaiting his punishment. Soon after, the clouds dispersed. Wu Er was fortunate to escape the disaster. He quickly returned to comfort his mother. He still doubted whether the god's words were true and dared not tell her. That night, he dreamed again and said, "Your filial piety has moved heaven. Your past sins have been forgiven. You should treat them with greater respect." The mother and son are still as they were.

### Respected Translator of Classical Chinese Here is the final, polished translation of the provided Classical Chinese text: **Du Mo Visits the Temple of Xiang Yu** Du Mo, a scholar from Hezhou, had been unsuccessful in gaining fame for many years. He was naturally bold and unrestrained. One day, he passed by the Wujiang River and entered the Temple of Xiang Yu. At the time, he was heavily intoxicated. After lighting incense and bowing, he climbed onto the altar, sat on the god's neck, and touched the god's head while weeping loudly. He spoke in a loud voice, "Great King, there is something lacking in your fate. Although you are a hero like the great king, you could not conquer the world. Although my writing is like that of Du Mo, I cannot advance to an official position. It's unfair to me." After finishing his words, he wept again, tears streaming down his face like rain. The temple attendant feared he would definitely be punished and forced to help him down. As he was being helped out, he still looked back and sighed, unable to let go. The attendant lit a candle and went inside to check the god's image, which was also still weeping. **The Stone with Turtles and Cranes** Wang Zhongli, while building a house, took soil from a vacant lot and created a small pond. He found a small black stone, about two inches wide. He washed it with water, and on it were white turtles and cranes, their shapes clearly visible. The tails of the cranes and the backs of the turtles were pure black. Initially, he thought they were dyed by previous owners. After a little scraping and polishing, it was clear that they were naturally so. He then placed the stone in a porcelain container and put it on his desk. At first, he did not find it particularly unusual. One day, when the sun was setting and the light shone through the window, it illuminated the stone on the desk. The two animals floated up in the water. When he took them out to examine closely, they were still in their original place. He put them back in the water, and they appeared as they had before. Only then did he begin to treasure and keep it secret. It was the year 1194 AD. Ten years later, in 1205 AD, the stone suddenly disappeared.

Records of the Unusual in the Ding Volume, Volume 16, Entry 19: Hu Feiying's Dream** In the second year of the Chunxi era (1175 AD), a local scholar named Zhang Xi went to take the provincial examination. He visited the Wu Mountain Temple. After the examination, he prepared wine and roasted meat to invite someone to go together. (The original text is missing ten characters here.) He carried paper money to pay respects. (The original text is missing fourteen characters here.) Why not? (The original text is missing six lines and sixteen characters here.) The public edict (The original text is missing fourteen characters here.) His student Lu Chuan then performed (The original text is missing eleven characters here.) a drought in the Hu and Xiang regions. The military governor Zhang Anguo. (The original text is missing ten characters here.) The people of the region said that the stone statue of Guanyin in the East Bright Temple was well-known for its miraculous responses. (The original text is missing four characters here.) It was said that praying to it resulted in rain. Therefore, they discussed decorating the hall to prepare a place for future prayers and visits. Cai Weng happened to be at the back of the hall. He then told his grandson Wei to move it. Wei was happy to take advantage of the opportunity to find a proper place for it. He immediately chose a place and assigned the task. When they opened the coffin and rewrapped the body, the skin and flesh had completely rotted away. Only a swastika pattern appeared on the heart bone, about one inch high, as if carved. Those who heard about it found it strange. Narrated by Wang Shi Yu Qixian.

A title or name is incomplete, and the internal text is missing. The couple named Zheng Sheng. Zheng Yifu, an inner historian, and his nephew's grandson, Guan, became the son-in-law of Lin Cai Zhong, a high-ranking official. They were married for four years and had one son and one daughter. The couple was very harmonious. Zheng entered the capital and met the Lantern Festival. The day before, he planned to visit the Shangqing Palace. He happened to be invited by an old friend for a meal and ate very delicious beef jerky. Arrived at the palace late in the evening and just when he was watching the lanterns in the palace. Suddenly felt a divine curse or enchantment and quickly returned home. Had already gone mad and started speaking wildly, pointing to something in front of him as if he saw something. Said, (five characters are missing), "Kill this person." At that time, there was a man nearby who saw the use of medicine. (Five characters are missing.) The bystander was actually his wife. Called out to ask Lin, who could vaguely remember the person who was poisoned. Scolded him severely. Lin said, "It was not my intention to do this together, why involve me?" The person said, "Why didn't you say anything?" From then on, Zheng often seemed to be suffering from a wind illness, frequently insulting his wife, and no longer had the usual happiness. Could not stay together for a day, and Lin ordered the daughter to divorce and return home. The grudge followed and did not release, and she eventually became a nun. Zheng eventually became a useless person, later left home and wore a monk's robe, and died in a temple in Wuxi County.

Huang An Dao A scholar from Panyang named Huang An Dao studied the *Shijing* (Book of Poetry). He failed the imperial examinations repeatedly. He considered giving up the exams to become a merchant and traveled between the capitals of Beijing, Luoyang, and the Guanzhong region, making a little profit along the way. His desire for profit became firmly established. Just as he was on his way from the capital carrying goods and heading west, the imperial decree was issued. The officials who were in the capital all scolded him, saying, "How can you, who support your parents, not restrain yourself and become a merchant?" Reluctantly, they stayed in the same temple. At night, he dreamed of someone in Taoist robes and celestial clothing, sitting at a table with records and documents in front. The deity called out to him, saying, "This is the list of successful candidates from previous years." Huang thought it was a deity and bowed twice, praying earnestly. He asked for the name. The deity inquired, "Who are you and where do you come from?" Huang replied in detail. The deity then opened the records and turned through many pages, pointing to one named Huang Jia and showing it to him, saying, "It is you." Huang replied, "The surname is correct, but the name is wrong. I fear it is certainly not so." The deity said, "It is so," and repeated it several times. Huang then thought deeply and said, "Then I should change my name to Jia." He thanked the deity and withdrew. The deity said, "The *Dianmo* (Imperial Edicts) and *Xunhao* (Edicts of Instruction) are the texts you will use to pass the exam." Huang woke up and spoke with his fellow villagers, doubting that the texts he studied were different. Some advised him to change his name and the texts he studied. He changed his name to Jia and used the *Shijing* to take the exams. He was recommended and passed the preliminary exams. When he reached the Southern Examination, the second question on the essay section asked about the *Dianmo* (Imperial Edicts) and *Xunhao* (Edicts of Instruction). He indeed passed the exam.

Freed Fishermen of Wu Two common people from Wu, A and B, both engaged in the eel trade, earning three hundred coins per day. One day, A caught some eels but did not sell them immediately. In his dream, a person mournfully called out, "Think of my offspring." The plea was repeated several times. Awoke in shock, he saw nothing. Lighting a fire to search, he heard the sound coming from the bucket. One eel raised its head, making a sound like "think of my offspring." Upon closer listening, it seemed as if the eel was saying, "Think of my offspring." A suddenly realized, "I sold you for profit, which was not a good plan." He then silently vowed to change his profession. The next day, he gave his usual daily earnings to B and bought back the eels he had sold. He released them into the river. The eels faced the water and led the way, following him. They stayed with him for a long time. A prayed, "Because of my poverty, I did not consider the consequences of my actions. Now that I have released you, and you follow me without leaving, isn't there still some resentment?" The eels responded with a sound and disappeared. A returned empty-handed. His wife scolded him, saying, "You must have spent it on drinking and gambling." She persistently questioned him until the end. Finally, A fully told her the truth, but she still did not believe him. That night, he had a separate dream involving dozens of people who said, "If you want to make money, why not go to a certain road twenty miles away where you can find it." Upon waking, he remembered that the place was not a common path. He tried going there. About twenty miles away, in the dense grass and vines, he found something. Upon closer inspection, he discovered twenty thousand old Kaiyuan Tongbao coins, as if they had been hidden for a long time. He joyfully accepted them with a bow and carried them back. He used them as his main business, and the family then became moderately prosperous.

**Title: The Boat Ascends to Heaven** **Text:** Ma Zhongyu, accompanied by his father, served as an official in Jinling. In mid-July, a daughter and a wife of the family climbed a small pavilion behind their house. Around the fifth watch, they gazed upward into the sky. They saw a boat ascending directly into the void, with several Taoist priests sitting in a circle, laughing and talking. In a moment, it reached the outermost part of heaven. The sky opened up, and the color was pure red. The boat directly entered through the opening, and the sky immediately closed without end. But the opening still glowed red like a sunset. Zhongyu heard about it and went to see. But he only saw a single streak of red. ### Explanation of Key Terms and Concepts - **仙舟上天**: The boat ascending to heaven, symbolizing a supernatural event. - **凌虚直上**: Ascending directly into the void, emphasizing the boat's vertical ascent. - **数道士环坐笑语**: Several Taoist priests sitting in a circle, laughing and talking, suggesting a peaceful and possibly ritualistic scene. - **须臾抵天表**: In a moment, it reached the outermost part of heaven, indicating a rapid journey. - **天为之开**: The sky opened up, suggesting a dramatic and supernatural event. - **色正赤**: The color was pure red, describing the hue of the sky or the opening. - **舟径由开处入**: The boat directly entered through the opening, emphasizing the direct path. - **天即合无际**: The sky immediately closed without end, indicating the closure of the opening. - **而开处尚赩赩如霞**: But the opening still glowed red like a sunset, describing the lingering effect of the event. - **忠玉闻而往观**: Zhongyu heard about it and went to see, indicating his curiosity and action. - **但犹见一道赤色耳**: But he only saw a single streak of red, suggesting the fading of the event.

**Thunderstone** Wu Zhipu, the governor of Chongren County in Fuzhou, Jiangxi, was seated in the hall handling official business one evening in late July. Suddenly, a storm erupted, with lightning and thunder striking repeatedly. The thunderclaps followed one after another, and fireballs the size of swallows flew through the air, passing from the imperial edict tower to the south. After a brief pause, the storm subsided. Outside, it was reported that a granary belonging to a villager named Rao Xiang in a village south of the county had caught fire. The granary, located in the fields on the site of an old temple, had been ignited by the fire. The fire raged for over a month before it finally extinguished. The granary and the grain inside were burned and transformed into a substance resembling . Several days later, a merchant who resembled a Taoist passed by the area. He struck the burned granary with a stone, and the charred grain became as hard as stone, turning into a multicolored substance. Some of it resembled insects, animals, or plants, while other pieces still looked like grain stalks. The merchant took several samples and kept them, burning incense and bowing before them. He then ground the fragments into a fine powder, mixed it with water from a stream, and drank it. When asked why he did this, he replied, "This is thunderstone. It can cure any illness or misfortune." He then left. The people of the county, upon hearing of this, used the substance to treat illnesses and misfortunes, and it proved effective. They took nearly half of the substance, and Rao Xiang's family began to treasure it, setting up barriers to protect it. However, the substance did not attract many people. Instead, when viewed from the outside to the center, it formed images of Buddha and attendants, resembling the appearance of Arhats. It was later moved to a residence for veneration. Wu Zhipu sent officials to seek it, but they only found a small amount of the substance in the cracks and crevices. Rao Xiang held a government position as the head of the Ratefu.

**The Wine Worm** During the reign of Emperor Ling, a magistrate named Xi Jinru from the state of Qi invited his close friend, the scholar Zhang Bin, to be his guest. Zhang Bin was an avid drinker and would always place several liters of wine by his bed every night. Whenever he felt the urge, he would take a sip in the dark and finish it; without this, he could not live. One night, he forgot to set it up. In the middle of the night, he became extremely thirsty and could not find the wine. Frustrated and angry, he made loud noises. Soon after, he vomited and spat out something on the ground. There was no light to see by, so he was tired beyond measure and lay down to sleep, sleeping peacefully until dawn. All the students were awake, but Zhang Bin had not yet risen. When they went to check on him, they saw a piece of meat like liver under the bed, with a yellowish color and still slightly moving. The students said, "Master, you did not rise early to seek drink, and thus produced this worm." They took wine and poured it over it, and it made a squeaking sound. Zhang Bin got up and saw it, and it was the same. He finally realized that this was a relic. He ceased his work and gathered all the discarded cow bones and relics, and built a stupa for them.

The Missing Cowshed Relic Tower The inner text is missing. Dream of Chicken Eggs Dong Ying, the father of Dong Jianlao of Dongping, served as the magistrate of Lingchuan County in Zezhou. The county was usually desolate. There was only one shop selling flatbread in the market. He often suffered from a lack of food and drink. Coincidentally, he was about to marry off his sister. The county official sent him thirty dried chicken eggs as a gift. He considered them a precious delicacy. He ate seven and kept the rest, hanging them on the beam inside the hall. Soon after, his sister's husband arrived. The cook's maid asked to use them for breakfast. That night, Dong dreamed that twenty-three children came down from the beam, all begging for their lives. One girl was wearing a shawl and had a limp. In the morning, he washed his face. The maid took the hung items with a fork and found twenty-three. He then remembered his dream and let them go. He searched for hens in the same official's household and carried them away. All of them became chickens, except for one hen that had a lame leg. Dong then stopped killing living beings. These eight events were all told to me by Dong Jianlao, who said they were recorded by his father, the former chancellor. Therefore, they are all distant historical events. Director of the Western Zhejiang Region In the 27th year of the Shaoxing era, Sir Han Zhang, the Director of the Western Zhejiang region, resigned from his position. His subordinate, Officer Zhang, dreamed that someone told him, "Sir, you have regained your old possessions." Another person beside him said it was his brother Jisi Ji. Zhang hurried to tell Han Zhang and congratulated him on his promotion soon. Soon after, the imperial bulletin arrived, appointing him to fill a vacancy at the Imperial Academy. The name was exactly the same. He already admired its accuracy. Sir Zhu was the Chancellor of Fuzhou. On the day of his farewell audience, he was retained as the Right Censor. Xie Jingsi received it, with the same name as Jisi. The ghosts and spirits are so good at playing tricks on people.

Hu Bangning Hu Bangning, a man from Yichun, was a notorious bandit leader in Jiangxi. He operated in the western mountains of Jizhou, eluding capture by soldiers for many years. His activities caused significant harm to the local populace. After many years, he was finally captured and executed in the prefecture of Yuzhang (modern-day Nanchang). His father's tomb was desecrated, and upon exhumation, his body was found to be partially decayed but not entirely rotted. In the heart of the body, there was a termite nest, which resembled a sword but was still in its scabbard. His son, playing with small-scale military forces, should have been punished with the axe and halberd. Strange indeed, these two events are recounted by Han Zhang. Zhu Yue and the Two Swords Zhu Yue from Jinyun had a dream during the spring of the Renchen year of the Gandaos reign period. In the dream, someone informed him that he had passed the third level of the imperial examination. Another person handed him two swords. When the results were announced, he indeed passed as predicted. He was then appointed as the magistrate of Xinjian in Longxing, Guangchang in Jianchang, and Jianpu in Nanjian. He served as a county magistrate in three different places before finally settling in Jianpu. It was then that he understood the significance of the two swords in his dream. The Dream of the National University Wang Anxing, from Jixi in Huizhou, was reassigned to another position. He was sent to govern Shucheng in Luzhou. When his term was about to end, he wanted to go to the capital to seek another position. Upon arriving in the county, he met Professor Lin Wenqian, a fellow examinee from the same year. Lin advised him, "A two-year vacancy is not easily obtained. Why would you seek a change?" Wang then decided to return home. That night, he had a dream in which someone urged him to leave, saying he had been appointed to a position at the National University. Upon waking, he was delighted and told his servant, who also decided to accompany him. Several days later, he received an imperial edict appointing him as a proofreader and reader at the National University. He was given a seal for the lecture hall of the National University. Thus, the position he had been appointed to was as described. Who would have thought that such a small matter was not predestined?

The Three Assemblies at Longhua Wang Anxing, a professor in Qizhou, was examining candidates in Lushan during the autumn of the Xinmao year of the Qiandao era. He found one scroll that was exceptionally well-written and should have been placed in the top ranks. However, he mistakenly used one Kui (the identity of Kui is unclear). When he was a professor in Huangzhou, Xia Jian firmly stated that it should be (the meaning is unclear due to the missing text). There was no way to (the meaning is unclear due to the missing text) the Three Assemblies. (The meaning is unclear due to the missing text) A monk beside him explained, "This is a small matter. And (the meaning is unclear due to the missing text) the two parties were encouraged to write a letter of thanks and leave, and he then realized it was true." The Boat of Ye Rui Jiang Ye Yue, courtesy name Zizhong, was from Yushan, Xinzhou. He traveled from Shaoxing to cross Qiantang, reaching the riverbank. There were two hundred people waiting to cross together. Seventy of them stood on the pier, while the rest hesitated by the water's edge. When the tide was very strong, those by the water quickly returned to the shore, already drowned by the waves. The tide was about to reach the pier, and the crowd looked at each other in confusion, having no way to escape. Suddenly, a boat came from the west, and someone shouted from the side of the boat to urge the boatman to urgently save the people on the pier. Yue immediately boarded the boat, and thirty others followed and boarded. All were saved. Halfway across, Yue thanked and asked for the name. It was the son of Rui Guoqi, the Master of Sacrifices. (The meaning is unclear due to the missing text) Why did you come to save me specifically? Rui said, (the meaning is unclear due to the missing text) Several years later, Yue served his elder brother (the meaning is unclear due to the missing text) The Yangtze River had already been crossed (the meaning is unclear due to the missing text) In chaos and danger (the meaning is unclear due to the missing text) Come (the meaning is unclear due to the missing text) Everyone was in a hurry (the meaning is unclear due to the missing text) All the events were told by Zhu Yangzhi.

Yu Zhen Dao Ren Gao Zimian, who has lived in Jingzhou for generations, was wealthy and fond of entertaining guests. Once, he spent tens of thousands of coins to buy a beautiful concubine and placed her in a separate garden, where he built a bamboo pavilion for her to live in. He called her Yu Zhen Dao Ren. He would spend his days roaming around the garden. When distinguished guests arrived, he would call her to entertain them. When there were no guests, he would close himself off all day and never go out to play at any time. After several years, during the Cold Food Festival, when people visit graves and perform rituals, Gao Zimian invited her to go out with his family. She refused to go. He tried to persuade her repeatedly, but she said, "The master has a command. How could I defy it? This outing will surely be disastrous. This is also fate." Gao Zimian was puzzled by her words and only suspected that she did not want to meet her wife. He finally made her go with him. Yu Zhen Dao Ren rode in a carriage among the crowd. As soon as they left the city, people were busy visiting graves. Just then, a hunter passed by. The sounds of her trembling could be heard outside. Soon after, a pair of eagles flew back and forth outside the curtain, and two dogs pulled her out of the carriage. They bit her throat, and she died immediately. Gao Zimian rushed to save her but was too late. Her appearance was still as if she were alive. When they were about to carry her body back, they noticed that her tail was hanging down. It turned out to be a wild fox. This event is very similar to the story of Zheng Sheng in the Tang dynasty. Wáng Qíxián says so.

Li of Lin'qiong Li, a descendant of the Li Doctor of Qiongzhou, was captivated by a wandering maiden during the Lantern Festival. He followed her without pause, and she frequently turned back to smile at him, as if inviting him to leave the city. Upon reaching the city gate, another man joined their path, a familiar friend. Li thought he had found a companion and secretly rejoiced. They strolled slowly to the riverbank, but the man suddenly left, and the woman did not cross the bridge but instead descended to the water's edge. Li remained fully aware but was quite surprised. He hurried to call out to her, and the woman returned from the water's surface, circling him several times. She then became entranced and did not look back. They held hands and walked on water, crossing into a mountain temple. They quickly entered a small, winding room under the corridor, which was very narrow, with a table pressing against Li's back. He could not bear the frustration and shouted loudly. The temple monk, who knew what was happening, came with a torch. It turned out to be a small room belonging to someone's wife. Li sat and lay down on top of it, as if wanting to enter but not yet succeeding. The monk recognized him, saying, "This is a relative of Master Li Zhongfu." He quickly supported and carried Li to the abbot's quarters, administered medicine, and by morning, Li gradually regained consciousness. He was sent back home, and after a month, he finally recovered. According to Master Sima Hanzhang, Li was the younger brother of Lady Xianyu, the wife of his master.
