Feng Shi Welcomes His Bride The daughter of Wu Pu from Le Ping married Yu Ningyi from Dexing and had a son. She later married Zhang Shi, a woman from Wuyuan. After Yu Ningyi's death, Wu remarried. Ten years later, she also died. One of Yu's old maids had a dream during the day where someone came to her and said, "Madam Wu has prepared a boat in the river. She has sent me to fetch your bride and you." The maid firmly refused in her dream and did not go. The bride alone commanded the carriage, and they followed the messenger onto the boat. Not long after, the bride fell ill and died suddenly. It was the first year of the Chunxi era. The maid still lives to this day. Eji Ding Zhi, Volume 17, Story 12 Gan Tang Loses His Eye A man named Gan Tang from Fanyang had been blind in one eye for ten years. On the first day of June in the third year of the Chunxi era, he dreamed of a monk holding prayer beads and reciting scriptures. The beads were shiny and black, with a beautiful glow. He tried to find them and woke up with one bead in his hand. Four days later, he went to the city on official business and saw a tall, slender man dressed in a yellow cotton robe at Yongping Bridge. The man looked at Gan Tang and walked straight up to him, grabbing his clothes and saying, "Come with me." Gan Tang was both suspicious and afraid and pushed him away, saying, "I have never met you before. Why are you doing this?" The man laughed and said, "Just come, and I will show you good things." Since he could not escape, Gan Tang reluctantly followed him. After walking a hundred steps, they reached the riverbank. A large boat was already tied up there. The man pulled Gan Tang onto the boat. At the bow of the boat, there was a golden plaque inscribed with the words "Imperial Grant of Official Physician." There were several beautiful women dressed in fine clothes on either side, whom Gan Tang had never seen before. The man said, "You have been blind for a long time. Now we have met by fate, and I will treat your blindness." Gan Tang thanked him, saying, "My eye has been bad for ten years."

The pupil had withered. Even if treated, what good would it do? The Taoist monk did not listen. He forced Tang to lie on his back and had four women hold his hands and feet. He took a copper chopstick and probed the eye socket. The pain was unbearable. Tang cried and said, "Grateful for your kindness, I still have one eye. I really don't want to be treated." Then they helped him sit up. One woman poured half a cup of soup from the bottle and gave it to drink. Tang found it quite sweet. Just thinking of resting a bit, they then pulled him back to lie as before. Tang knew there was nothing he could do, leaving his fate to chance. The chopstick was inserted again into the eye socket. Tang felt something like a hook pulling out of the back of his head. Slowly, they covered it with a piece of paper. After a while, it was removed. They held a mirror to look, and then both eyes were clear, without any pain. Tang was surprised and grateful and got up to thank. He asked to return temporarily. When he returned to the inn, he told others about what he had encountered. Everyone was astonished. More than ten people who were curious quickly followed. When they reached the boat, there was nothing to be seen. Tang was thirty-eight years old at the time. He lived in Chongde Village. Since he first got sick, his family recited the name of Guanyin Bodhisattva daily, and they were very careful in their offerings and rituals. This must be the power of the Buddha.

**The Glass Bottle** Emperor Huizong once gave ten purple *zili* gallbladder bottles to a low-ranking eunuch, ordering the craftsmen to cast gold and line the inside of the bottles. The eunuch presented the bottles to the artisans in the imperial garden, but they all were at a loss, saying, "To place gold inside, one must use an iron *jia* to press and heat it, and only then will it fit properly." However, the neck of the vessel was too narrow to accommodate this process, and the glass was also too thin and fragile to withstand manual handling. If one attempted to fix it, the bottle would surely break. Rather than risk punishment, they dared not proceed. Knowing that he could not force them, the eunuch carelessly stored the bottles in a chest. Some time later, while walking through the marketplace, he saw a tinworker shaping ceramic vessels with great skill. He tried one of the bottles on him, saying, "Line it with gold for me." The worker did not hesitate or question him, but simply agreed to come the next morning to retrieve it. When the eunuch returned the next day, the work was already completed. The eunuch said, "I have seen your skill, which far surpasses all the artisans in the imperial garden. Yet you are confined to this humble position. Is it not due to poverty?" He then confided the truth to the man. The worker replied, "It is an easy matter." The eunuch immediately took him with him and reported the matter to the emperor. The emperor also wished to see it himself and went to the imperial garden, summoning all the goldsmiths and having them stand in the courtyard. He questioned each one of them, and they all repeated what they had said before. Only the tinworker stepped forward, took the gold, and forged it into a sheet as thin as paper, then lifted it and wrapped it around the outside of the bottle.

The crowd exclaimed, "If that’s the case, who can’t do it?" I know you are just an ordinary craftsman. How could you possibly accomplish this? The person smiled and did not respond. Soon he peeled off what was wrapped around it, pressed it onto a silver stick, and inserted it into a bottle. He gradually filled it with mercury, covered the bottle’s mouth, and shook it from side to side. After a long time, the gold adhered completely inside, with not a single crack or gap. He slowly smoothed it over with his fingernail. The crowd then stared in astonishment at each other. The person reported, "The container is made of glass. How could it withstand the impact of hard objects? Only mercury is soft and heavy. It enters gradually without causing damage. Although it will eventually corrode gold, it does no harm where it is not seen." The emperor was greatly pleased and generously rewarded and sent him away. I also remember during the Yuan佑 period, the eunuch Song Yongchen was exiled to Shuzhou. The local officials made a large musical instrument. The drum was very ornate, decorated with gold and colorful designs. When it was placed on the stand, a ring on the side suddenly broke. They wanted to dismantle it but were reluctant to incur the cost of dismantling. Song ordered a larger ring to be made, shaped its legs into a locking mechanism, and secured the drum’s belly with iron. He then hammered the ring into the cavity. As soon as it was inserted, the lock mechanism expanded, and it would no longer come loose. All of this was the result of ingenious thinking, surpassing people’s expectations. As previously told by Liu Zisi.

Dan Yang's Yuan Zhongcheng Fu, who served as a Right Censor and was then promoted to an outer court position, later became the Chief Censor of the Eastern Region. He dreamed of someone telling him, "Be truthful but not arrogant, flexible but not submissive. What does this mean?" In his dream, he could not answer. The next day, he told his guest scholar Fan Cuncheng about the dream. Fan Cuncheng replied, "The meaning of the following lines is that you are a great talent of the age, second only to a sage. This is a good dream." Not even ten days later, Yuan Zhongcheng contracted a wind disease and died in office. Those who understood began to interpret it, saying, "The two lines before refer to a poet using objects to convey deeper meanings, and the correct or appropriate speech in the context of the 'Two Elegances.' Your positions and the illness you suffered all align with these descriptions. What kind of clever ghost is in charge of dreams, capable of playing tricks on people like this?" This was during the third year of the Gandaodao era (1167 AD).

阎罗城 襄阳南漳人张腆，居住在县里的雁汊。世代擅长医术。绍兴十八年夏天，他夜里梦见自己从家中向东走了大约二里地，经过固城铺后向北行进。不久进入一座大城，从北门出来，登上溪上的高桥。桥上水中有许多人来来往往。他看到自己的妻子郑氏也涉水登岸。想要上前同行，转眼间已经失散。不久，他又到了另一座城，同行的人数他不知道。张腆已经进了门，回头问守门的人这是哪个郡县。那人说：“这是阎罗城。”张腆知道自己的身体已经死了，非常悲伤恐惧。他徘徊无计，不知不觉又向前走。走到北阶，看到大门有三间，与众人一起进去。走过大约一百步，又来到另一扇门，五间门金碧辉煌。过了一会儿，又经过一扇门，装饰更加华丽。两旁的司局相对排列，正殿非常高大，挂着黄色的帘子。张腆边走边看，来到东厢吏舍的门内，看到屋子里的人都穿着官服，有的穿红色，有的穿紫色。他上前作揖，没有人回应。只有一个穿红衣服的人微微点头。张腆站着等了一会儿，红衣人显得有些同情，用脚拨开一块砖，说可以坐在这里。他刚坐下，妻子忽然站在门外，彼此都漠然相对。过了一会儿，一个人从殿帘后出来，穿着黄色的背子，拱手仰视屋檐，脚步非常缓慢，好像在思考什么。很久后又进去了。张腆问这是什么官，红衣人摇摇头低声说：“这是阎罗天子。”张腆说：“刚才看他样子，与人间所画的不同。”

Yet he was very similar to the True Immortal Qingyuan. Before he had finished speaking, the curtain on the hall was lifted, and the call for the documents was issued. The officials rushed to follow. Below, there were many prisoners. Some were sent to prison, some were shackled for interrogation, and some were immediately released. After about two hours, the people were almost all gone. Tian remained in the office. He saw his wife from afar. She was also sentenced to twenty strokes of the cane. She could only be shocked, in pain, and shed tears. Soon the curtain was lowered again. The officials returned to their office and took off their clothes, half sitting, half lying down. The red-clothed official pointed to Tian and said to his colleagues, "This person has done no wrong, why not let him return?" Everyone was silent. He spoke again. He said, "If you want to send him away, why ask us?" One of them said, "Even if he wants to go, how can he pass through the three gates?" The red-clothed official warned Tian, saying, "If anyone outside asks, just say that the magistrate ordered to call the jailer." Tian humbly thanked and left. Every time he reached a gate, there was someone asking. If he said what he was told, he was spared. He hurried back along the old path. As he neared the bridge east of the house, he fell into the water and woke up. The rooster had already crowed. He called his wife. She also suddenly woke up in shock. They both said their dreams were the same. His wife scolded, saying, "When I was receiving the caning, you were there but paid no attention to me, treating me like a stranger. How can we continue to be husband and wife?" They then slept in separate beds. A few days later, Zheng's waist suddenly had a small cocoon. It then developed into a large ulcer. The pain was unbearable. After ten days, the pus began to drain. Another ten days passed before it healed. Tian, filled with emotion, abandoned his family. He went to Mount Wudang in Junzhou. He sought the Dao from Master Sun. After seventeen years, he died. In Gucheng, the physician Wang Siming, who was friends with Tian, said. Jing Pei's younger brother, who was an official in Xiang Prefecture, said, "He learned this from Wang Siming."

Wang Zhen, the Observing Judge of Yan Prefecture, was from Jidong. Whenever he attended social gatherings, he would not wet his lips with wine. His colleagues all suspected him of being insincere, thinking perhaps he was secretly observing their mistakes while they were drunk, reporting them to their superiors to advance himself. Over time, he began to be seen as the only one remaining sober. Wang Zhen sighed deeply for a while, then said in a somber tone, "I have long wanted to keep this secret. Since you all doubt me, I dare not hold back. I will reveal everything." He unbuttoned his clothes to show his back, which had two scars facing each other, like marks from legal punishment. He spoke slowly and said, "Three years ago, a severe boil broke out on my back. One day, the wound became severe, and I was in a daze, unaware of others. Someone called me out and brought me to the official's office. There was someone sitting at a desk, who questioned me and said, 'Have you ever served as a magistrate in a certain state?' I replied, 'Yes.' He said, 'At a certain time, a certain person should not have been punished for a certain crime. How could you arbitrarily decide?' I replied, 'It was the intention of the prefect.' I held onto my decision for several days, often entering to argue in the petition, until I was scolded and could not change my mind. The beginning and end of the official documents are all there. I regret that there is no way to bring them here. The person in charge ordered those around to say something. A soldier rushed out. In a short while, he already brought the documents. The person in charge repeatedly read and examined them, happily saying, 'You really have no crime. Almost killed you by mistake. Now send you back.' He called the original pursuing official to escort. The official was very kind, and they had friendly conversations along the way. He strongly warned, 'When you return to the world, do not drink alcohol.' When asked why, he refused to say. Upon waking, blood and gore were mixed together. The wound had broken open. I recovered that very day. By nature, I love drinking, but I think of the warning from the underworld official. I cannot bear to hasten death again." Those who heard the story were all filled with horror and regret.

A commoner from Chun'an County in Yanzhou accidentally killed a villager. The commoner's family was unable to sue. The villager had a brother who was a servant of a prominent family named Fang. The Fang family encouraged him, saying, "Your brother was killed by someone, and you cannot seek justice. How can one call oneself a person if they cannot seek justice?" The brother then prepared a formal petition and went to the county office to report the incident. The Fang family, who had a good relationship with the wealthy man, hinted to the servant to come and plead on behalf of the wealthy man. The wealthy man then came and pleaded, saying, "This is my servant. How dare he do such a thing? I should instruct him to stop. He only hopes for a small reward." The servant respectfully listened and apologized, saying he would not do it. The wealthy man then gave the servant 100,000 coins and another 300,000 coins as a gift to Mr. Fang over several months. The servant then announced his intention to continue the lawsuit. The wealthy man, fearing that the matter would be exposed, begged the servant to use the previous strategy and gave him the same amount again.

Fang Jun said, "I just received a message from an acquaintance in Zhongdu who asked to buy two hundred catties of lacquer. In a hurry, I couldn't buy it. If you are kind enough to buy it for me, I will send money to repay the cost." The old man replied, "I have received such great help from you. This is a small matter. My family has it. Why talk about the price?" He immediately sent the lacquer as requested. The following year, Fang Jun wanted to settle the lawsuit. The old man sighed and said, "I mistakenly killed someone. The law does not warrant death. That is why I do not want to go to the authorities. I fear the prison officials who demand bribes without limit, which will destroy my family. Now, I have spent nearly a million in private expenses, and their plot is not yet satisfied. I am old now. There is only death left." Then he closed the door and hanged himself. After three years, Fang Jun became the magistrate of Puqi in Ezhou. In broad daylight, dazed, he spoke in a trembling voice in the hall, facing many officials, "I knew that the old man would come. I have taken money from the old man many times, and ultimately hastened his death. The old man should come here. Without saying a word to his wife and children, he fell to the ground and died." The official reported what he saw, and it was then known that there was a karmic retribution.

Xue Hezhou Zhenren Xue Rui Zhongcang, serving as the governor of Hezhou, was nearing the end of his day's duties when he suddenly felt a drowsy and uneasy state of mind. He rested on a low trundle bed, taking a brief nap. Suddenly, someone bowed before him and asked to proceed. Following this figure, he was led to a certain place. Another official approached and said, "The officer conveys a message to the governor: the appointed time is not far off. If you do not continue to serve in office, your future prospects are still promising." Xue had long lived in Kuaiji with a comfortable lifestyle and a thin interest in official duties. Upon hearing this, he immediately replied, "I wish to retire from office henceforth." The official then departed. Shortly after, he returned and said, "The officer wishes to have a written document as proof." Xue requested paper and a writing instrument, and the official wrote down the message and handed it to him. The official then said, "Since it has been recorded in writing, it cannot be regretted." He left again. Soon after, the official returned and said, "The officer is very pleased. The governor may return." Xue woke up, feeling as if he had just emerged from a dream. On the same day, he submitted a petition to request a position in the temple service and returned to Yue. At that time, it was the third year of the Chunxi era, and he held the title of Chaoying Lang. People said that he would soon rise to the rank of Daifu through imperial favor and the privilege granted to officials' children, and he would resign from office. Two years later, Xue retired.

Sanyazi Town Sanyazi Town is located in a solitary and remote part of Hebei. There is also a Sanyazi Town in Luancheng County, Ruzhou, Henan, with an official stationed there. The official's salary is insufficient to support his wife and children, and his situation is bleak. The area is full of ponds and lakes, and the only available food sources are reeds, lotus roots, fish, and turtles. There is nothing else to buy in the market, and neither the previous nor the current supervisors have ever come here. Once, a transport commissioner passed through the town, and his officials led him through it. Upon entering the town, they found that all the officials had left, leaving no records to investigate. They wandered around the hall and noticed that the writing on the paper screen was still wet. They decided to read it and found a small poem that said: "For two years, I have been emaciated in Sanyazi. With no rice and no money, how can I support my family? Every day, my two meals consist only of lotus roots. My mouth is filled with lotus flowers." The transport commissioner silently laughed and left. Those who heard about it spread the story. Gao Shilu, from Mengcheng, was a supervisor in Pingjiang during the late Southern Song dynasty. The price of lamb in Wu was extremely high, with one pound costing nine hundred coins. At the time, the prefect had left office for the Zhejiang transport commissioner, and Lin Anzai Juren took over the administration. Lin was strict and frugal, suspecting that the officials who bought lamb must be greedy. He planned to check the purchase records. The vice-magistrate Shen Du Gongya told Gao Shilu, "You are from the north and will inevitably eat lamb. Why don't you alter the records to avoid being troubled by the prefect?" Gao Shilu laughed and thanked Shen, recounting the previous story. He also said, "I have also imitated that style and written a quatrain: 'In Pingjiang, one pound of lamb costs nine hundred coins. With my meager salary, how dare I buy and taste it? I only eat fish and shrimp for my two meals. My stomach has become a small pond.'" Those who heard it all laughed. Lin Juren heard about it and dropped the matter. These four stories are all told by Gao Shilu.

In the seventeenth year of the Shaoxing era (1147), Liu Guan, a person from the capital, served as the market inspector of Xu Village in Xiu Prefecture. His son, Liu Yaoju, hired a boat to travel to the prefectural capital to take the examination for non-residents. He was attracted to the beauty of a boatman's daughter and began to speak flatteringly to seduce her. She seemed to have feelings for him, but her parents noticed and became vigilant, never relaxing their watch. When they arrived at the prefectural capital, they stayed in the boat. Whenever the father went out, the mother stayed, and whenever the mother went out, the father stayed, leaving Liu Yaoju unable to act on his intentions. On the day of the examination, he wrote the essay on "governing the world with ease and without much effort" and composed a poem about the blooming of osmanthus in the autumn wind, both of which were topics he had practiced before. However, the rhyme scheme was different, so he needed to revise them. He worked until late at night. The next day, he wrote the essay on the topic of the day and completed it before noon. The boatman thought everything was as usual. Both the father and mother went into town, leaving only the daughter behind. Liu Yaoju went directly to her room and they consummated their relationship. That night, both of Liu Yaoju's parents dreamed that someone brought a list and announced that their son was the top candidate. Another person said, "That is not true. Your son's actions were unjust, and he has been demoted to the second place." They woke up and discussed the dream, finding it strange and worrisome. When Liu Yaoju returned home, his parents scolded him, but he refused to admit anything. Eventually, he was found guilty of a minor offense and failed the examination. Three years later, he served as an official in Huai West, and indeed, he was recommended as the top candidate, but he died without passing the examination.

*Yijian Zhi: Volume 18, Entry 12* Lu Dangke According to the *Bing* section, Liang Zizheng recounted the story of Lu Dangke. He said that Lu's father was the magistrate of Shangshui. Lu Dangke's father, Junbao, was also a magistrate. Therefore, I saw that he had a very accurate method. Teng Yanzhi said that Dangke is his maternal uncle and that he probably learned the method in Shu (Sichuan). But Junbao is his uncle (paternal). Liang Zizheng's account is not correct. Teng once told his inner and outer brothers about his maternal uncle, asking for a simple, everyday trick that could protect him. His uncle said, "How easy it is to talk about it." He added, "I have always been serious and respectful in my conduct, and I dare not for a moment have a disrespectful heart. Yet I still encountered three misfortunes. I will tell you about them." One of these incidents is as follows: Recently, he passed through the villages of Yan Prefecture and visited the home of his old friend Fang. They lingered over drinks and conversation. It was nearly evening when the village leader Ye sent the host to invite him. Ye said that his unmarried daughter had not yet been married and had been bewitched and disturbed. Anyone who came with a method was immediately thwarted and driven away. He dared to respectfully ask Teng to come. Although Teng was already drunk, it would not hinder him from performing the method. As with Ye, he called his daughter out. After she came out, she was extremely beautiful and elegant.

I am silently amazed and envious, thinking it is something never seen before. The woman suddenly rushes forward as if being driven by someone. I am bewildered and change color in fear. Hurriedly, I retreat to the Buddhist hall. The woman chases me to the door and then turns back. I feel trapped by a ghost. Seeking a quiet place from their house, I intend to present a petition to heaven. The host leads me to a small garden on the west side. A hall surrounded by tall bamboo trees, in contrast to where I live, he says it is the cleanest. I take out red vermilion talismans and writing tools, placing them on the table. Not having time to pick up the pen, I soon become unaware of everything. Closing my eyes and listening carefully, I feel myself in虚空 (a void). The place where I sit is shaking and unstable. It seems I am bound by bamboo. After a meal, I return to the original place, and then all the tables, windows, and doors are covered in filth and disorder, making it impossible to stay. Considering it impossible to fight back, I hurriedly call a servant to carry me out. Traveling about ten miles, I just happen upon a Taoist temple and then stay overnight. My spirit gradually calms down. Then I rush into the courtyard, looking down at the constellation of the Big Dipper. I burn incense and bow a hundred times to apologize, then burn the petition. Staying for two nights, it slightly seems to have an effect.

A Daoist practitioner was sent to the Ye family to investigate the matter. Upon returning, he reported that the fire had started yesterday from the hall in the garden, completely burning the bamboo grove and spreading to the high forest behind the mountain. Dozens of houses in front of the gate, along with a small shrine to the land god, were all reduced to ashes. I knew then that the lawsuit had been decided in our favor, so I returned to the house. The whole family, young and old, were joyfully celebrating. The girl said that for years she had been in a trance, unaware of people around her, but today she was like one waking from a drunken sleep. She recounted that last year, while in her room, she saw an old man who came to act as a matchmaker, coming and going several times. After several more days, he brought several sets of gold, pearls, silk, and cloth. Soon after, he brought in a young man and married her to him as his wife. The next day, he took her back to his home and paid homage to his father-in-law and mother-in-law. Others present were addressed as uncles or elder brothers—more than ten in number. The old man was very old, and he called out to the crowd, saying, "My family has served the Ye family with incense and offerings for many generations. You young men, acting recklessly in unjust ways, will bring disaster upon me. Why not take her from elsewhere?" The young man replied, "This is a proper marriage, arranged through a matchmaker and with the offering of a dowry. The wedding rites are clear and proper—what have you to fear?" Later, I heard that several Daoist practitioners had come.

They must unite and work together to resist them. Often, they achieve victory. When the deity known as Road True Official arrives, the old man calls out to the crowd and says, "I have heard that Road True Official's divine power is beyond comprehension, unlike any ordinary person. He is surely not to be avoided." The crowd is also quite afraid. Suddenly, someone calls out to me, saying that the True Official is calling for me. I then go, and the crowd follows behind me. As we approach the academy, he suddenly calls out and laughs, saying, "The True Official praises you highly. Why not go to meet him?" He then pushes me forward. Afterward, the old man asks what happened. He sighs and says, "The matter has reached this point. If he can be killed, it would be a great deed. But the disaster is still here." Just then, we were having a meal when a fire suddenly broke out inside the gate. The smoke and flames filled the sky. The old man clasps his chest and weeps bitterly, saying, "The disaster has come." He pushes me out with his hand and says, "To save you, I will destroy my family." Only then can I return, and the fire subsides. All the buildings and structures I once saw are completely destroyed. The so-called matchmaker is the local god. The beginning and end of this matter are so terrifying. I nearly suffered harm. How could it be something you should learn? At this time, Yan Zhi still had two more things to say, but he did not have time to speak about them before he died.

饶廷直, courtesy name Chaobi, was from Nancheng, Jianchang. He passed the imperial examination and was a man of great courage and integrity. In the seventh year of the Shaoxing era, he passed through Wuchang on official business and had an encounter there. Afterward, he no longer approached his wife and concubines, sitting calmly and composedly, like a hermit. He wrote a poem to commemorate this event, which reads: On the night of the Mid-Autumn Festival in the Ding Si year, he chanced to visit the Yellow Crane Tower. Suddenly, he met a strange person who gave him a secret. He lamented that he was still bound by worldly affairs and could not fully engage in this secret. Therefore, he wrote a poem to remember the encounter. The poem goes: Before the Yellow Crane Tower, the autumn moon is cold, and the river before the tower is vast and shrouded in mist. At midnight, people disperse, and all sounds cease. Standing alone by the railing, he gazes at his own shadow. With a long cry, he clears the heavens, knowing that the person he met is one who lives a life of purity and freedom, a companion who has lived many lives and formed a spiritual bond. They met unexpectedly and talked together, washing away the busyness of the world, feeling as if he were in a place of pure tranquility. Looking back on past events, they seem like a clay pot, while the whole world seems to have lost its way. Alas, I am constrained by my high status, knowing that Lu Ao has not wandered far. In the future, I wish to follow him, to see the clear and shallow waters of the mythical Penglai together. Three years later, in the Geng Shen year, the court regained control of Henan, and he was appointed as the Tongpan of Dengzhou. The Jin people betrayed their alliance, and the city of Deng fell. He hanged himself to death. His coffin was carried back to his hometown, and the bearers felt it was unusually light. However, no one dared to investigate further. Some suspected that his spirit had ascended to immortality. Su Shi wrote a poem about the Yellow Crane Tower, recounting the story of a soldier meeting a strange person told by Feng Dangshi. Wang Dingguo also wrote about this story in his book. It is suspected that this is a similar tale.

The Daughter of Shi A man from Nancheng named Rao Bin. During the Dazheng period, he participated in the preparatory examination and was sent west. He then stayed near the capital, lodging at the home of a scholar named Hu Jia. Hu was also a candidate for the metropolitan examination. One day, they entered the capital together. In the evening, they stopped at a roadside inn. There, they saw an old woman wearing a yellow silk scarf on her head, holding a green parasol as she passed by the door, resembling a servant of a noble family. There was also a beautiful young girl, extremely beautiful, who followed the old woman away. She looked back while walking, and her beauty was captivating. Hu was captivated by her and led Bin to follow behind them. Just a meal's time later, they became confused about their direction. They continued to move forward and traveled about six or seven miles to a wealthy household. They approached the door, and an old man with white hair and a staff came out. He introduced himself as Shi and was very happy to see guests. He greeted them with great courtesy, and there was a statue of Guanyin in the hall. The incense and flowers were treated with great reverence, and the paintings were colorful and vivid, not of human craftsmanship. They stayed overnight, resting their servants and horses outside. The two sons of the host asked to enter and pay respects to the old woman, and he agreed. They were the same as those seen at the inn.

They inquired about the reason. She smiled and said, "I had brought my granddaughter to visit relatives early this morning. We returned at dusk, not knowing that the two of you were there. I failed to avoid you, and I feel deeply ashamed. Soon after, I called my granddaughter out. She is truly a national treasure. Her speech and silence, her gestures and postures, captivated the two of you. In a moment, she was led into the hall. We provided splendid arrangements and served abundant and clean dishes. The host and guest exchanged pleasantries and were very joyful. Half-drunk, Hu attempted to flirt with his daughter. She happily complied. Bin stood up and went to the toilet. The old man sent a wet nurse with a candle to follow. The wet nurse's appearance was also pleasing. Bin went out to wash his hands, using water for play, and they all laughed. After the wine, the daughter served Hu to sleep, and the wet nurse served Bin to sleep. They all fell into a deep sleep. Upon waking, they felt a cold wind blowing on them. They put on their clothes and looked around. The east was already bright. Looking back, they no longer saw the splendid house and large rooms. They were now in the ancient woods of the maple forest. The two of them looked at each other in terror. The servants also did not know why. They returned to the inn in a daze and could not figure out what kind of demon or spirit it was.

The Purple Lady and Blue Porridge Poem The Xie family of Linchuan lived west of the city. They cultivated a garden and planted flowers. Their nephews and nieces gathered there to study. On their leisure days, they welcomed the spirit of the Purple Lady and composed songs, poems, and miscellaneous writings. A friend named Jiang Nan visited them and suspected that the younger generation had fabricated the event to deceive the people. He did not believe it. One day, he returned and saw that those holding the brooms were all young servants. However, the words they spoke were remarkably profound, which somewhat restored his faith. Thus, he silently prayed for a poem. The broom moved slowly and said, "De Lin has never been trusted. Why would you seek a poem from me?" It then casually composed a quatrain: "End beans are urgently needed. Shavings of elm cannot satisfy desire. Likes resemble Zhao and Zhang. In haste to save Wen Shu." The crowd did not understand what was meant. They prayed again, asking the spirit to clarify and inform them. The broom then wrote slowly: "The first line is from the biography of Shi Chong in the *Book of Jin*. The second line is from the biography of Yang Cheng in the *Book of Tang*. The third line is from the biography of Cang Gong in the *Records of the Grand Historian*. The fourth line is from the biography of Feng Yi in the *Book of the Later Han*." They checked the references and found that they all pertained to porridge. It turned out that this was the official courtesan Lan, whose family had a tradition of selling porridge, and people called it "Blue Porridge." Jiang had stayed at her inn the night before, and the spirit used this to play a joke on him. "De Lin" is Jiang's courtesy name.

Liu Sheng Liu Sheng, a man from Nan Cheng, had a secondary residence thirty miles south of the city, in a place called Li Lake. He often visited this place to inspect his finances and grain stores. Whenever he came, he would stay for ten days, lingering and hesitating to leave. Once, he went to drink at a neighbor's house and did not return until midnight. The servant who stayed behind to guard the house was very tired and lay down on the master's bed. Shortly after, he saw a woman dressed in two red shirts. She entered directly from outside, climbed onto the bed, and looked at him intently. She said, "Who are you? Why are you sleeping here?" The servant responded and pushed her away. She quickly let go and fled, turning over and climbing over the wall. The moonlight was bright, and the servant followed her. She transformed into a spotted dog and ran away. The servant then began to suspect the reason for the master's prolonged stay, realizing that he had been bewitched. The next day, he told his neighbors, and they killed the dog that belonged to the Liu family. When they cut open its abdomen, they found something unusual inside. Only then did they realize the strange transformation. Later, the people of the village called her the Dog Grandma Liu.

Zhang Zhennu Zhang Zhennu, whose origins are unknown. Some say she was a courtesan from Wu Prefecture, but this is uncertain. Though she fell into the world of courtesans, her nature was quite simple and unadorned. Every evening, she would wash and change her clothes, light incense, and pray to heaven, fervently praying to be released from her fate. One day, a scholar passed by her home, and Zhennu greeted him. He found her graceful and refined, and treated her with respect. They drank wine together and parted in good spirits. The next day, he returned, and they met several times over the months, but they never engaged in any impropriety. Zhennu was surprised and asked, "I bear the favor of your attention, and it has not been short. Why do you not stay one night longer to fully enjoy our company? Is it because I am lowly and unworthy, unable to entertain a gentleman like you?" The scholar replied, "No, the essence of human connection does not lie in such things. What matters is the mutual understanding of the heart." One day, after they had drunk half of their wine, the scholar asked Zhennu, "What else do you do in your daily life?" She answered, "I have fallen into this life, so what else can I do? But every evening, I pray to heaven, asking to fulfill this debt." The scholar said, "Then why don't you learn the Dao?" She replied, "Pressed by the need for food and clothing, I have no time for this. Besides, how could I find a teacher?" The scholar said, "How about me being your teacher?" She said, "If so, then I am fortunate." She then changed her clothes, lit incense, and took him as her teacher. Afterward, he did not return for several days, leaving Zhennu alone.

He casually wrote down: "I have met the teacher many times, but he never said anything. Until now, it has caused me great distress." While he was writing, a guest suddenly arrived and saw what he had written. The guest laughed and asked, "What is this?" Zhen did not answer but concealed the writing. The guest said, "Showing me will do no harm." Zhen showed it to him, and the guest continued: "There is nothing particularly clever. I will give you a formula. From afternoon to noon, sit and meditate. The energy will flow through the double gates of the spine and pass through the Kunlun. At that time, if you gain strength, think of me." Zhen was greatly pleased and thanked him repeatedly. From then on, he suddenly gained enlightenment and also secretly received teachings, but he did not reveal it to others. He did not know who the guest was. After many months, the guest departed. Zhen held a farewell banquet for him. At the parting, the guest gave him a letter, saying, "Open it after I leave." When he opened the seal, it was a short poem that spoke of cultivation matters. It read: "The trigrams of Kan, Li, Gan, and Dui divide the hours. Just recognize your own ancestral roots. The next line is missing: refining Jia and Geng, and descending the dragon and tiger. The thunder of the earth shakes rain on the mountain top. It must be irrigated to make the yellow sprout emerge. If anyone asks who taught me, just say the master's surname is Lu." He finally realized it was Lu Dongbin. He then observed a fast and thanked the guests, painted his image, and worshiped him devoutly. He followed his teachings and practiced them. After more than a year, he left through a physical transformation.

**Yuan Congzheng** Yuan Congzheng was from Yichun. In the Gengchen year of the Shaoxing era, he passed the imperial examination. He was then transferred to serve as the magistrate of Chen County. Before this, a woman from the Chen family of Shangao in Junzhou had recently become a widow. She came to marry Yuan, and the couple lived in harmony. They once made a vow: "Should one of us die, the other shall not remarry." Soon after Yuan took up his post, before his term was complete, his wife died. He was unable to carry her coffin back to his hometown, so he simply buried her beneath a mountain near a temple by the roadside. Later, he was transferred again to serve as the magistrate of Pingyang in the Guiyang military district. Thus, he broke his former vow and married a woman from the Tu family of Fuxin. Together, they traveled to Pingyang. Their route passed by this very temple. Someone of the same examination year who was in office there prepared a banquet to welcome them. As soon as they sat down, Yuan saw his former wife coming from outside. She pointed at him with her hand and scolded, "What was your lifelong vow? Have you now broken your oath? I will not let you go!" Yuan merely muttered to the empty air, as if speaking to someone. He then called his attendant and ordered him to return the city god's document. The attendant, startled, carelessly replied, "It has already been returned." Yuan remained seated and no longer looked at the host. Without saying goodbye, he rose and left. Upon returning home, he told his wife Tu all the details. In the middle of the night, he went mad and ran away. Tu followed with a candle, but Yuan blew it out. In the end, he leapt into a well and died.

The Poet-Selling Scholar Zhang Jizhi, a native of the Central Plains, was waiting for a vacancy in the漕幕 (river transport administration) in Hubei Province. He lived in temporary residence at Longxing Temple in Yuzhang (modern Nanchang). One day, while napping during the day in a drowsy state, he heard someone clap and laugh, saying, "Rest, rest, Deye Gang. Lying deep in the clouds, basking in the slanting sunlight." Startled awake, Zhang looked around but saw nothing. He lay down again, but heard the laughter once more. Fearing for his life, Zhang dared not sleep. He went out to ask the temple monk, who said, "In the past, there was a scholar who lived here, selling poetry for a living and eventually died in this room. Could it be the ghost of that scholar?" Zhang, standing in fear, immediately resigned his post. Within half a year, he too died. When he was buried in the West Mountain, the place was called Deye Gang. This story was told by Li Shuda.

A Hundred Verses on Qi'an The scenic spots of Huangzhou, including the Red Cliff, Bamboo Pavilion, and Snow Hall, are famous throughout the world due to the traces left by Zhou Gongjin, Wang Yuanzhi, and Su Gong. In the ninth year of Shaoxing, the prefect Han Meizhi and the deputy prefect Shi Yanzhi each composed a hundred verses on Qi'an, intending to publish them in the prefectural hall. Han dreamed of two gentlemen who claimed to be Du Mu and Wang Yuanzhi. They said that most of what the two gentlemen had composed were based on the stories of Su Zizhan. They asked, "Why were we not included when you were in charge of the prefecture? How many things we did are worth recording. Why were we not included?" They asked him to take a look at their two collections. By collecting and expanding upon what was passed down, they would have a complete and perfect work. Han woke up from his dream, feeling both ashamed and afraid. He intended to take the collections of Du Mu and Wang Yuanzhi, the Fan Chuan and Xiao Chu, and compose two hundred verses. However, he was about to be replaced and did not have time to do so. Thus, he did not publish the previous hundred verses either. **Snow Hall of Dongpo** A man from Huangzhou named He Hu, the son of He Jiesi, a disciple of Su Dongpo, lived in exile in Ezh. Every year on the Cold Food Festival, he would return to Huangzhou. In the ninth year of Shaoxing, the prefect Han Meizhi rebuilt the Snow Hall, restoring the old path of Su Gong. At that time, it was mid-spring. He Hu happened to visit, and in a dream, Su Gong told him, "The base of the Snow Hall is about one hundred and twenty steps different from where it was a few years ago. The small bridge and fine willows are not in their original places. You should correct this." He remembered the details vividly in his dream and did not forget them. The next day, he went to inform Han, who followed his advice and made all the necessary corrections. Later, an old man named Tang Deming, who was eighty-seven years old, came from Huangpi to visit. He exclaimed, "This is truly the original site of Su Xueshi." These two stories are narrated by Prefect Han.

Li Ba Encounters a Fairy Li Ba, from Jinan, with the courtesy name Dingguo, resided in a military camp in Lin'an. He supported himself by gathering students. In his spare time, he freely roamed the lakes and mountains. Once, he intended to visit Jingci Temple. Passing over a long bridge, he got lost in a bamboo path. He saw a Taoist dressed in green robes, chopping bamboo shoots in the forest. Li Ba bowed to him. The Taoist asked where he was going. Li Ba replied, "I am going to Jingci Temple to pay homage to the 500 Arhats." The Taoist said, "There's no need to go yet. Come and eat roasted bamboo shoots with me." The food was very delicious. Suddenly, the wind and rain became dark and gloomy, and Li Ba lost sight of the Taoist. Greatly frightened, he hid in the forest. After a while, the rain stopped. He found the path and came out, reaching the temple gate. He felt his body light and spirit free, walking like flying. When he returned home, he no longer ate or drank. His elder brother Da You, who was not teaching in the royal palace, was about to take up his post. He sent a servant to deliver a letter. Upon seeing Li Ba, his face was as red as a peach blossom, and he could abstain from food. Da You was told this. When Da You arrived, Li Ba had already gone on a journey to Mount Mao. Later, it was heard that he had gone to Shu, hiding in Qingcheng Mountain. Da You was the Chief Censor of Zizhou. He sent someone to Mei Prefecture to inquire about Li Ba's whereabouts. The governor of Mei Prefecture replied that several years ago, Li Ba had already ascended to heaven riding on clouds. Now only his portrait remains.

A Woman from the Xiao Family in the Tang Dynasty Ri Li, a patrol soldier under the imperial guard, was poor and enlisted in the military rolls. He was tasked daily with cutting hay for the horses of the commander. One day, he came to a remote and secluded place by the lakes and mountains where he encountered a beautiful young woman who appeared to be from a family of officials. She herself invited him to meet her, and she left him clothes as a gift. From then on, they met daily at this place, and she also provided him with money and silk for his expenses. Ri Li relied on this support to improve his situation slightly. His fellow soldiers became increasingly suspicious of him, thinking he must be a thief. They reported their suspicions to the commander, who secretly investigated the matter but found no other reason for the investigation. They began to suspect that he had some unusual experience. They consulted a skilled practitioner named Song Anguo to investigate further. Song Anguo summoned Ri Li, who arrived. Song Anguo performed a ritual to summon the woman, who also came. She said, "I am not a person of this world. I was a woman from the Xiao family during the Tang dynasty. In a past life, you were the son of a white magistrate. We agreed to marry, but before I could marry, I was taken by a divine being in Luoyang. Our marriage was not consummated. Your good fortune was weak, causing you to fall into a lowly position. However, I have never forgotten my promise. Recently, my connection with the divine being ended, and I gained freedom. I searched for the reincarnation of the white magistrate's son and discovered that you are the reincarnation of the man. I fulfilled our past agreement. I pitied your poverty, so I provided you with support." Song Anguo asked, "Did you not steal the items you gave to him?" She replied, "No, I did not steal them. I took them from wealthy families who had excess." Song Anguo said, "You should leave quickly and not linger. You might cause future problems. I will send a message to Mount Tai to have you reborn." The woman nodded and bowed before leaving. She was never seen again. Ri Li remained poor as before. These two stories are according to the account of Tong Mendezao.

The *Yijian Dingzhi* Volume Nineteen, Fifteen Stories **Liu Hu's Perfume Satchel** Liu Hu, a man from Quzhou, was in his twenties. He passed the imperial examination and became a jinshi. After being appointed to an official position, he returned to his hometown. He often stayed alone in a room. The place was by the water, and the banks were lined with lotus and water lilies. The scenery was unique and captivating. Suddenly, it seemed as though he had encountered something unusual. His family members had no idea what was happening. They were puzzled by the fact that he would immediately lock the door when he entered the room, and he would not come out unless it was for filial duties or when guests arrived. People secretly suspected him but could not ask. Later, when he changed clothes and washed, his family found a pearl satchel in his belt. The pearls were all northern pearls, intricately formed, and extremely round, lustrous, bright, and clean—something that the world could not possess. The silver threads used to string the pearls were soft and shiny, beyond description. The scent inside the satchel was also particularly unusual. When they asked him where it came from, he refused to say. They secretly listened to him, and sometimes they heard the sound of chess pieces being moved. So, they devised a plan to open the door and catch him off guard. Inside, they saw a beautiful woman playing chess. When she saw outsiders, she quickly retreated behind a screen. Upon searching, they saw nothing. His father and elder brothers thought it was a ghost and were deeply worried. They called in Taoist priests and sorcerers, tried various rituals, but nothing worked. However, Liu Hu looked healthy and strong, showing no signs of being troubled by an unusual presence. When he was about to take up his official duties, he suddenly stopped going anywhere. The pearls and satchel that he possessed, his father ordered to be thrown into the sea. Liu Hu remained healthy and free of illness throughout his life. Even in old age, his desires did not diminish. By the time he was over eighty, he still had ten young concubines. He rose to the rank of Zhongdaxia (a mid-level official) and lived to nearly ninety years old. In his later years, when people asked him about what he had encountered, he said, "It was a water deity." At the time, he did not inquire about her name or surname, so it could not be passed on. It is said that he learned techniques for maintaining health and longevity from her. This was told by his great-grandson, Qingqing.

Jinyun's Literary Brilliance The historical accounts of Jinyun's literary brilliance have been frequently recorded, but it was never known for its poetry. Now, two poems have been obtained. The first poem reads: "Night rain continues until dawn, then clears up in the morning. The mountains in front are once again dyed green. The warm sun on the treetops makes the bird sounds smooth and melodious, stirring spring feelings that are不忍 to listen." The second poem, a poem expressing the sorrow of spring's passing, reads: "The east wind suddenly rises, bringing evening rain. The red and purple flowers fall, leaving a fragrant path. Leaning on the railing, there is only a heart full of spring sorrow. The branches are full of deep green, with nowhere to vent the sorrow. Spring light leaves me behind, moving forward majestically. Even if there were gold, it would be impossible to buy back spring. Wanting to ask the remaining flowers about the departure of spring, the flowers also remain silent, indicating that spring has ended." The poetry is remarkably emotional and refined. Some people even consider it divine, as reported by Cai Congfa, the magistrate of Gong'an.

The Wild Man of Huangzhou In the territory of Macheng County within Huangzhou, there is a mountain called Taibei. A man from Shaowu named Huang Zhicong lived there. The area was abundant with lush, secluded forests. Huang often cultivated the land himself. The fruits, millet, and beans ripened, but he often suffered from his crops being stolen by some creature. He secretly observed and saw a being that looked like a man but was covered in fur. When he tried to catch it, it would disappear. He could never catch up with it. He tried every method imaginable, but finally, he tied a rope and placed it in the furrow, and caught it. Initially, it was not very clear what the creature was. After caring for it for several days, it began to speak. It turned out to be a real person. It said, "I am a son of the Chen family from a certain village. I am over forty years old. During the Jingkang Incident, my entire family was killed in battle. I was the only one who escaped. I hid in the mountains. There was a high cliff in the mountains that I could climb using vines. On top of the cliff, there was grass like a carpet that I could cover myself with. When hungry, I ate grass, tree leaves, and wood. When thirsty, I drank spring water. After a long time, I became accustomed to this life. My whole body was covered in fur, but I had no illness or pain. I forgot that I had left my home and was living in the deep mountains. I was also as agile as a monkey. Huang fed me, and he even forced me to marry. After a long time, my skin and fur both fell off. I was no longer light and agile. Everyone thought that if I were to be released back to the mountain, I might not die. They thought it was a pity to make me live on food, drink, and desires. Huang did not agree. At that time, Tong Bangzhi was the governor of the region. His grandson-in-law, Wang Zhonggongchui, accompanied him on his travels and witnessed this event. He wrote a record of the wild man and composed a poem about it.

**The Art of Fate by Shi Yan** Wang Chonggong was from Nancheng. In the year Yichou of the Shaoxing era, he went to take the provincial examination. He heard that the diviner Shi Yan had just gained a reputation, so he went to consult him. Shi asked about his hometown and said, "This morning, four elders from the celestial district of Xian—Li Ding, Zhou Nan, Yu Qubing, and Shi Zhongkan—came to inquire about their fates. Only I did not favor Shi Jun, but the rest were destined to succeed." Shi then asked what classical text Wang was studying. Wang replied, "I study the *Yijing*." Shi said, "Just now, the elder Deng Wei from Nanjian also said he studied the *Yijing*. This person will be promoted this year." After saying this, Shi began to examine Wang’s five elements. He said, "Do not hide what I say. You are not only at a disadvantage this time, but in future attempts, you will not be recommended for the county examination. In the year Gengwu, you will take the examination again, but in the year Xin, you may not achieve success." Wang left in displeasure. Later, the success or failure of all six people were verified. What Shi had said about Wang’s future was exactly correct. After passing the palace examination, Wang went again with his fellow examinee and hometown friend Jiang Bingjun to consult Shi. By this time, Shi had already forgotten the previous matters, and said, "What are the two of you asking again? Is it not to know who is higher or lower?" He then said, "Their ranks were not high. Wang can still be listed in the golden list." He added, "There is another point to make. Jiang was born in the year Yishen, and has a configuration that includes *ge jiao sha*. He must pass through the care of Fang Yiyang." The two looked at each other in astonishment. It turned out that Jiang was originally the son of the Gan family, and had come to be the successor of Master Jiang. When the names were announced, Wang was listed in the fourth tier, while Jiang was in the lowest tier. Such was the precision of Shi’s divination. The above account was all said by Wang Zhonggong.

The Jade Maiden's Spirit Method A county magistrate named Huang from Shaozhou, after completing his term in Taiping Prefecture, temporarily resided in a temple in Jurong County. The temple was adjacent to Mount Mao. One of the daughters in the household, who had not yet married, frequently became pregnant. Her parents suspected she was having an affair with someone, but the girl never left the house during the day, and no men were seen coming to her home. They secretly questioned her, and she wept, saying, "I truly did not meet anyone. But every time I sleep, I am in a state between sleep and wakefulness, and a Daoist priest always comes and takes me to a quiet room, invites me to drink and dine, and engages in intimate relations with me, leading to my pregnancies. I have long borne shame and resentment and dare not speak of it." Her father suspected that this was the work of a Daoist master from Mount Mao. He then made an excuse to prepare a feast and gathered all the Daoist priests within a ten-mile radius. He had the girl peek from behind the curtain, and indeed, it was a tall and well-proportioned Daoist priest from a certain temple. He appeared to be a Daoist master. After arresting and questioning him, the priest confessed. They bound him and brought him to the county. The county magistrate investigated his actions and circumstances, and said, "This is the technique of the Jade Maiden and the Spirit Method." He ordered the addition of shackles and imprisoned him in the jail. The Daoist priest recited several words in a high voice. Before the sound had finished, black mist spread everywhere, and they could not see each other face-to-face. After a short while, the mist cleared, and only five wooden items were scattered on the ground. The Daoist priest was nowhere to be seen. Rao Wen □□.

In the early years of the Shaoxing era, in a guesthouse of the Huang family located fifteen miles north of Xujiang City, there was a monk who passed by their home. He was tall and dignified, and claimed to be of the surname Ding. He stayed for several days and told the host that he would go into the city each day to beg for alms, returning at night. Over several months, he shared the money and goods he obtained with Huang. Huang treated him differently, and as they spent more time together, there was no suspicion of his movements. Eventually, the monk seduced Huang's wife, who was still young and beautiful. She was pleased with the monk's appearance and also because she had received money from him, so she agreed in her heart but pretended to resist. One night, she waited until it was dark and entered the monk's room. Inside, there was no lamp, but the room was naturally bright. The monk was wearing a golden-barred robe and sat on a lotus flower on the wall, resembling the images of Buddha and Bodhisattvas in the world. The wife was surprised and admired, and made a bow. The monk immediately jumped down and said to her, "I am not a worldly person. I will save you. Do not reveal this."

A Woman Left Behind and Caused Chaos From then on, whenever the husband went out, she would go too. Gradually over time, Yellow discovered and questioned her. She dared not hide it. She told him everything truthfully. Yellow became angry. He planned to capture and punish her. He pretended to go out for the night and secretly returned. After the time when people settle down, she went to the monk and said, "My husband wants to catch you. What can be done about it?" The monk said, "Do not worry. Close the door and go to bed." Yellow lay outside the door and listened. He became even angrier. He only shouted and cursed at her. At first, she also responded. But then her voice gradually grew distant. Soon, there was silence and no sound. He broke through the wall and entered. He lit a fire to illuminate it. The room was already empty. The four walls were hollow and empty. The monk, the wife, and all their belongings were completely gone. But the windows, walls, doors, and windows were not damaged in the slightest. He called for the neighbors. They searched until dawn. There was no trace or sound. In the end, no one knew where they had gone. According to the reclusive scholar Huang Yanzhong of Chongzhen in Jianchang.

江南 Wood Guests South of the Yangtze River, the land is mountainous. The local customs involve the worship of ghosts and spirits. These supernatural beings are extremely strange and bizarre, often relying on rocks and trees to build shrines. Every village has such shrines. In Zhejiang Province, they are known as the Five Tong, in Jiangxi and Fujian Provinces as the Three Young Masters, and also as Wood Guests. A variant with a single leg is called the One-Legged Five Tong. Although their names differ, they are essentially the same. According to historical records, these are the same as the mythical creatures mentioned in ancient texts, such as the *Shan Hai Jing* (Classic of Mountains and Seas). Li Shan, in his commentary on the *Capital of the Eastern Han*, mentions eight brothers named Yezhong and Youguang who often cause mischief in the human world. These beings are similar to the fox spirits of the north. They can make people suddenly wealthy, which is why the common people worship them in order to seek undeserved blessings. If one slightly offends their will, they will move on to someone else. During the hot summer, they often trade timber in the rivers and lakes. They appear and disappear irregularly, and people are extremely afraid of them, to the point where they dare not speak of them openly. Worship and rituals are performed with great care, and they especially love lust. Sometimes they appear as handsome young men of the literati class, or they change form according to what people desire, or they only show their true form. They appear as monkeys, dogs, or frogs, and their forms vary. All are agile and strong, cold as ice and iron, with strong and impressive male organs. Women who encounter them are generally extremely miserable, becoming thin and pale.

A sudden loss of consciousness occurred. There was one who transformed into a witch. People pointed to her as a fairy. They believed that those who encountered her and fell ill were suffering from fairy sickness. There were also cases where someone would lie motionless for several days to a month, appearing as if dead and then reviving. She claimed to be in a magnificent house with grand hallways, having intimate relations with noble people. Some were taken away by supernatural means and remained absent for several days before returning. Others, upon meeting her, would immediately go mad, their minds becoming confused and disordered, and were untreatable. Those she had intimate relations with were not all beautiful women. The spirits said that it was fated. Otherwise, they could not approach her. After completing the interaction, she would leave behind a black, ink-like substance. Many became pregnant as a result. She exhibited various strange and alluring behaviors. Now, I will record over ten such incidents here. In the northwest corner of the military barracks in Jianchang City, there was once the home of the official family of Cao, which was confiscated and taken into official possession. Behind the house, there was a small shrine. Many who came were disturbed by it. Zhao Youzhi's daughter, who was already married, accompanied by her husband and father, was bewitched. During the day, she appeared and interacted with her. No form or shape was seen, but only the woman's cries and groans were heard. Her hands and feet thrashed about, and she shouted that someone was forcing themselves on her. Upon leaving and looking back, the fluid left behind was black, soaking through her clothes. The woman ultimately died. Zhao Bunuo's concubine, about thirty years old, had a graceful appearance. She once went to the toilet to relieve herself. Her hair was suddenly caught by a horizontal beam, suspending her on the ceiling. She screamed for help.

A man was released from his punishment. Soon after, he fell ill and died within a few days. The wife of Geng Bian, the magistrate of the southern city, had a haunted pregnancy. During childbirth, she experienced unbearable pain and called a monk to recite the Peacock Spell. After swallowing a talisman, she gave birth to a ghostly offspring covered in fur. A woman from the Chen family became pregnant before marriage. After marrying, she gave birth to a block of flesh wrapped in purple silk like clothing. Fearing this, she buried it, and the woman also died. The wife of Gong gave birth to a child who looked like a human but was extremely ugly. As he grew up, he was not afraid of cold or heat and could bathe in a stream on a frosty day. When Yu, the wife of Wang, was a young girl at home, she already felt a strange sensation. After marriage, this presence continued to come to her. She then saw it in its true form, which was beautiful and graceful like a woman, wearing fine clothes and elaborate decorations. She spoke and laughed with people. When outsiders arrived, she prepared and arranged fruits and vegetables, acting as if they were family. If slightly offended, she would immediately throw sand and gravel, create wind and fire, and place human feces and cow dung in food and drink. No one was not frightened. Later, she was sent back to her parents' home, and the calamity then ceased. The whereabouts of Wang are unknown.

Li Yi's wife Huang and Liu Shiba's wife Zhou gave birth to children who looked like pigs. Their hair was very long, and they could jump immediately after being born. One died, and the other disappeared. That night, Lady Huang encountered a large toad-like creature and was forced to have sexual intercourse, becoming pregnant. She gave birth after a delay, and a green creature resembling its father was born. Lady Huang, wife of Hu, was pregnant but did not give birth. She consulted oracles, and the witch said that the creature had already received good news on the clouds and that the gods wanted to welcome it, making it impossible to do anything about it. Indeed, she died. In the village of Tian, New City, a concubine of the Li family gave birth to a child with a short and small body, a face that looked like a monkey, and fingers and toes only an inch long. The child did not resemble a human. All three of his brothers were the same. They are now in their fifties or sixties. In the village of Jingyuan, Nancheng County, Lady Qi's wife was pregnant for ten years. The child often made sounds inside her belly. When the mother wanted to go out, the fetus would jump around, causing her intense pain. The pain would not subside until she stayed inside. Later, she gave birth to a red monkey, its color like blood. It was abandoned in the wild, and the mother luckily survived alone. In the village of Xiaolao, Yihuang County, a woman from the Yuan family went to draw water from a well outside the city gate and was wrapped by a large snake, which caused her to fall to the ground. She then encountered the snake. It constricted her tightly, and she cried out in distress. Her family was alarmed and summoned a witch. The witch said that it was the work of the wood spirits and that it should not be killed. It would eventually leave on its own. By dusk, the situation was resolved, and the girl was carried back home. Her face was pale like wax, and she was sick for more than a month before recovering. Her appearance never returned to normal, and she became a mad person.

### Ghost Soldiers Crossing the Stream In the year of Xinmao during the Shaoxing era, at a village ferry in Xincheng County. In the bright moonlight, a fisherman tied his boat and was about to return home. He heard someone calling from the other side of the river, wanting to cross. He went over and found them to be twenty or more people with tattooed bodies and carrying weapons. He thought they were camp soldiers. Without time to ask, he loaded them onto his boat. After they had crossed, he reached into his bag and gave them money. They got ashore, thanked him, and left. Usually, soldiers passing by did not do this. The fisherman was both pleased and surprised. The next day, he looked at the money, and it was all paper. He finally realized they were ghosts. Said Deng Zao, the county scholar. ### Mount Longmen Mount Longmen, located more than a hundred miles east of Nancheng County, has a temple at its summit. It is remote, secluded, and desolate, rarely visited by people. Only one monk lives there. A visiting monk passed by and stayed in another room, which was somewhat far from the abbot's room. After he had slept, he heard a strange voice calling from outside the door. He dared not get up. In a moment, the door creaked open by itself. The guest was very frightened and dared not breathe. He quickly got out of bed and wanted to run away. The door was already blocked by a large stone. He shouted for a long time. The abbot finally responded. Just as they were answering each other, the stone suddenly disappeared, and the door opened as it was. The guest could no longer sleep. He went to stay with the abbot and asked about the strange occurrence. The abbot said, "It was done by the mountain ghosts. I have seen this many times before, but they do not harm each other." Said Deng Zao, the county scholar.

A Soldier from Chenzhou Named Tang the Mad During the fifth year of the Xuanhe era (1123 CE), Deng, the magistrate of Chenzhou, served as a local official in charge of household affairs. At that time, there was a soldier named Tang Sheng who was known for his strange and unpredictable behavior. He spoke and remained silent at irregular intervals, acting as if he were suffering from a mental illness or madness. People called him Tang the Mad. He had a mother but no wife or children. Once, he fled due to some wrongdoing and remained gone for a long time. Eventually, he emerged from a cave in Su Xian Mountain, known as the White Deer Cave. He claimed that the cave contained many beautiful places, with landscapes and architecture that were different from those in the outside world. When asked why he did not stay in the cave, he replied that his mother was still alive, so he could not stay away. He added that it was not too late to leave at a different time. When pressed further, he did not answer. Tang enjoyed drinking alcohol and often carried a horsefly and a snake in his clothes. He would go to people to ask for drinks, and if someone called out to give him a drink, even if it was mixed with excrement, he would not refuse it. Once, he brought a venomous snake to the magistrate's office. The magistrate called him to the courtyard and poured a large cup of wine, making him drink it. Tang happily drank it all in one go, then took the snake and ate it, leaving half of it. He said he would keep it to eat later. Every time he was drunk, he would expose his stomach and have people hit it with a hammer, which sounded like hitting wood or stone. His complexion barely changed. Later, no one knew what happened to him. The magistrate's grandson, Zhi, told this story.

The Dragon Pearl of Futang In the village of Futang, Wuning County, Yuzhang, during the year of Jichou in the Ganda era (July 21, 1169), a sudden and intense thunderstorm occurred during the day. Several herding boys were grazing cattle on a hillside when they saw two dragons battling fiercely in the northwest. From the southeast, several thunderclaps sounded, driving the dragons away. The two dragons fled, dropping an object from mid-air. The object was as large as a cart wheel and moved up and down many times without stopping. After a while, red clouds and white clouds swirled around it, but it could not ascend. It then fell into the fields. Its light gradually dimmed, becoming no larger than a duck's egg, round and bright like a pearl. The children competed to take it. Two woodcutters saw the children still fighting over it and struck it with an axe, trying to break it and divide it, but they could not damage it. A nearby wealthy man named Yu heard about it and came to see. He saw the unusual light and color and recognized it as a dragon's pearl. He exchanged it for several coins and looked at it against the sky, seeing a fairy inside. He obtained it. The governor Wu Mingke, upon hearing about it, visited Yu. Yu gave him a fake pearl to avoid trouble, and Wu did not take it either. After this, the area experienced continuous water disasters, followed by drought. No one knew the cause of it. The Rhinoceros Stone of Jiachang In Jiachang County, a wealthy man had a wayward son who often drank heavily. One day, he became so drunk that he fell asleep by the roadside. When he woke up, he saw a stone as large as a bowl, with a jagged and beautiful appearance. Sunlight shone through it, revealing something inside. Upon closer inspection, it was a rhinoceros. He did not think much of it and took it to Jiangzhou. A person named Pan from De'an found it unusual and offered ten thousand coins for it. The stone was taken. Later, the father heard about it and demanded it back, but it was already too late. This happened in August of the fifth year of the Ganda era (1169).

The Wife of Chen A commoner named Chen from Xingan lived in a village called Guoxia in Xiude Township. In the first year of the Longxing era, his wife was bewitched by a spirit. For several years, various methods to ward off or expel the spirit were tried, but none were effective. Her husband asked her, "How many people do you see every day? What are they like?" The wife replied, "First, there was a white-clad person who forced me to share a bed. Whenever I spun hemp, an old woman would come to help me spin. She would also bring two children to cook. There was no day without them. Even my grandmother suffered from this." The husband told her, "If you see her again, report to me." The wife followed his instructions. One day, when the old woman entered the room, she ran to tell the grandmother. The grandmother took a knife and went straight to lift the curtain. The old woman was just sorting the hemp when the grandmother chopped at her. The old woman showed her a bag of gold and said, "I came to make your family rich. Why do you want to kill me?" The grandmother stopped and, in the blink of an eye, the old woman disappeared. Chen said, "This demon is easy to deal with. Sharpen a knife and give it to your wife. When the white-clad person comes, chop at her." The wife followed his advice and raised the knife to hit her shoulder. The spirit fled, and the old woman appeared again. She said, "You have been with me for a long time. Now you want to kill me. How inhumane is that? Make me suffer greatly at home. I cannot move. I do not hate you. I came to ask for medicine." The wife did not respond. The knife was still in her hand. She waited for an opportunity to stab her side. The old woman ran to the big mountain. The wind lifted her skirt, and her fox tail was revealed. Soon, two little girls cried and came. They said, "You have already hurt our husband. Now you have hurt our grandmother. That is very unkind." The wife continued to chop at them, and they all turned into stone. After that, the spirits never came back again.

Xie Sheng's Miraculous Tangerine Xie Sheng, a man from Wenzhou, had a mother who was old and ill and refused to take medicine. In the summer, she longed for fresh tangerines, craving them as if she were starving or thirsty. Xie Sheng, at a loss, had a small garden where he grew these fruits. One night, he knelt before the tree, his knees worn out from the intensity of his supplication. By the next morning, numerous red fruits had formed. He knelt to pick them and presented them to his mother. After eating them, her chronic illness was cured. Those who heard of this spread it as a story of filial piety. Scholars near and far competed to write poems and verses, praising its beauty. It was called the "Miraculous Tangerine Poem Scroll." The magistrate Wang Gai, known for his benevolence, sent letters to other states, exaggerating the story. He regretted not bringing it to the attention of the court and recording it in the historical records, so that it would follow the footsteps of Jiang Shi and Meng Zong, showing that it was immortal. It was in the fourteenth year of the Chunxi era. Xu De and the Wheat Xu De, a man from Le Ping Ming Kou, heard that the price of rice and wheat was high in the city. He ordered his servant Dong De to take a boat to sell the grain. When he arrived, the price had increased again. Dong De mixed sand and gravel with the grain and sold it. For every stone of grain, he gained five liters. In a few days, the goods were sold out, and he loaded money and returned. Just as he arrived home, the weather was perfectly clear, but it turned dark and gloomy. Lightning and wind threw him into the fields, and he was struck dead immediately.

The 15th Item in Volume 20 of the *Yijian Dingzhi* The Wife of Lang Yan Huang Sheng, a painter from Linchuan, was traveling to Guangchang. He arrived at Zhibazhai, where the military officer Lang Yan invited him to stay. One night, a woman appeared under the lamp. She was quite attractive, and Huang, drunk, tried to flirt with her. She agreed happily. When he asked who she was, she said she was the wife of the household. From then on, she came to him every night. Huang sometimes felt financially strained and secretly provided her with money. They stayed together for half a year, and Huang gradually became weak and sickly. Lang Yan asked her what was wrong, but she refused to speak. It turned out that Lang Yan had once hidden with a courtesan, and his wife, unable to bear her jealousy, had committed suicide in the room. Although she was buried, she still appeared frequently. The room was left empty, and no one dared to live there, but Huang stayed there. Lang Yan thought she was a ghost. When he told her the reason, she finally spoke the truth. Lang Yan spat and cursed at the air. He then moved Huang to a guesthouse. That night, she came again. Huang was planning to avoid her, but the woman said, "There is no need to avoid me. I would not dare to harm you. Even if you flee, I will come." Huang had no choice but to stay with her for the night. They stayed together for a longer time, and Huang eventually feared she would harm him. He rushed back to his hometown, resting along the way under a mulberry tree to cool off. The woman came again and said, "This thief is too heartless. We were together for so long, and you have no intention of caring for me. How could you abandon me? You should return quickly." Huang dared not answer, but he prayed to heaven and earth in his heart and silently recited scriptures. The woman suddenly sighed and said, "This is my fault. I should not have been confused and mistaken. Until I encountered this situation today, I lost all prospects and possessions. What is there to envy? Can I not choose another partner?" She then left, and the strange occurrences finally ceased.

Huang Zunshen Huang Zunshen, a scholar from Guangchang, taught in the village of the Wang family, about a hundred steps away from the master's house. A woman claimed to be the master's daughter and came to have an affair with him. After a while, he fell ill with a wasting disease. The master suspected something and, one evening, the younger members of the family saw a male dog carrying a wine vessel, standing upright and knocking on the door. They hid and watched, and Huang opened the door to invite the dog in. Soon, they heard sounds of eating, drinking, talking, and laughing. They did not dare to call out and ask questions. The next day, they secretly inquired, but the family strongly denied it. That evening, the dog ran into the mountains behind the house and did not return. The younger members followed and saw the dog enter a broken tomb, emerge wearing a skull, and run away. They chased it, struck it with a stick, killed it, and dragged it back.
